Original_Search_Title: Looking Forward to a New Day
Headline: Uganda: Looking Forward to a New Day
Body_Text:
Belinda Akuge, aged 20, is breastfeeding her 9-month-old baby. She is
wearing a bright-coloured dress and is spotting a weave on her head and can
be mistaken for a business lady.
Akuge is one of the many Congolese women who followed their partners
when they were returning from the Democratic Republic of Congo.
A statement was issued on September 21st, 2005 by the RCD-LM secretary
general, Jean Louis Ernest Kyaviro that the Congolese Rally for Democracy
Liberation Movement wanted the Uganda government to repatriate the Congolese
women who are in Uganda back to Congo.
According to the statement, they had learnt that these women were living
under difficult situations. When the news was broken to the Congolese women,
some showed anger and pain because of the suffering they are enduring in
Uganda as a result of following the men they loved while others showed
relief and joy because their countrymen are concerned about them and want
them back, meaning that they still have hope for a better future in their
own country regardless of what they have undergone in Uganda.
Tough times
Akuge who is eight months pregnant for instance comes from Kisangani and
has three other children but they were stolen by their father and taken to
Mbarara although she does not know the exact village where the children are
staying.
She was abandoned by the father of the three children and the man who
impregnated her has denied responsibility of the pregnancy.
"I am pregnant but everyday, I have to go to the disco or drinking place
to get a man who will give me money to feed myself and pay rent," she says
with a sad face.
Akuge says because she cannot earn a living, she has now resorted to
prostitution in order to survive. She says she wants to go back to her
homeland and parents where she can get food and someone to look after her
children.
Another lady, Solangi has three children all with different fathers who
abandoned them. She also earns her daily bread through prostituting herself
so that she can put a meal on the table for her children at the end of the
day. "I am sleeping with men whose health status I do not know as a result
of poverty," she says. Solangi hopes the Ugandan government can provide
transport for them since they neither have money nor any other means to go
back home.
After more than five years of suffering, sometimes going without food,
becoming prostitutes, watching their friends die and not being given decent
burials, the Congolese women are ready to return home, begin new lives and
forget what they have gone through while in Uganda.
"Here, we are being stigmatised everywhere we go. People say, "Congolese
have Aids'," says Anachi, one of the Congolese interviewed.
"We have become the laughing stock of people in Gulu, they call us
thieves, some say we have stolen their men and they even laugh at us because
we eat cassava leaves as food," says Tete, who has a 16-month-old baby.
Tete says they have suffered enough and that if they were near Congo,
they would have gone back home by now. She says they are still stuck in
Uganda because they do not have money.
"We have been humiliated by the very men we followed," she says. If the
Ugandan government or even the government back home can give them transport,
then they are ready to go back. But one thing they will not do is to leave
their children with their so called fathers.
"We shall go back with our children, these soldiers did not pay our
dowry so we shall take our children," Solangi continued. "We came with them
but we do not even know their parents or where they come from, so why should
we leave our children with people we do not know?" Anachi questions.
According to Opio Bosco, the secretary for youth, Kasubi central, the
government should find a way of repatriating these Congolese back to the DRC
because they are really suffering.
Most of them have been abandoned by the soldiers so they resort to
prostitution as a means of survival. Opio says the women now sell themselves
to the civilians and to whoever will give them money.
Evelyn Aya, the secretary for finance LC1, Kasubi central, says that the
women have gone to the extent of providing their services to young boys who
have money. "For any amount of money, they will sleep with any man and they
normally ask 'how much do you have?'" Aya says.
Government's stand
When contacted after the release of the statement, the Army spokesman,
Lt.Col. Shaban Bantariza is reported to have said that the women's return
was not Uganda's responsibility.
"Why did they follow us? Were they abducted? Those women were adult
Africans who came to Uganda. They should get in contact with them." He said
that for the Ugandan government to return the Congolese could amount to an
extradition.
Bantariza however denied allegations that the women were living in poor
conditions and is also reported to have said that they are business women on
kyeyo.

Original_Search_Title: SADC Agricultural Production Records Growth
Headline: Southern Africa: SADC Agricultural Production Records Growth
Body_Text:
Luanda — The production of cereals of 653,000 tons produced this year in
the Southern Africa Development Community (SADC) is estimated to grow
by 35 percent.
According to SADC executive secretary, Tomaz Augusto SalomÃ£o, who
was speaking on Friday to the press, in Luanda, these estimates include
mainly the production of maize that recorded an increase from 531,000
to 734,000 tons.
The official stated that among the products that are not part of the
cereals, is cassava that is expected to rise from 6,6 million to 8,5
million tons.
Speaking about the Protocol on free flow of people and goods, signed
this year by SADC member States, he said that in spite of being in an
initial stage, the countries of the community can still set up
bilateral contacts.
Thus, he stressed the need for the SADC national committees to reach the same level of development.

Original_Search_Title: Between Southern Leaders And 2007
Headline: Nigeria: Between Southern Leaders And 2007
Body_Text:
Lagos — The auditorium of Excellence Hotel, Ogba in Lagos, was filled to the brim on Tuesday when Southern leaders held its historical political summit. The aim was to set a new agenda for power sharing in 2007. Writes Philip Ogunmade
For the people of South-east and South-south zones of the country, it is now sunrise. The thick blanket which has hitherto severed these zones from the seat of power began to disappear last Tuesday, when in an unanimous decision, all the ethnic nationalities which conglomerate the entire Southern part of the country gladly conceded the position of the President of the Federal Republic of Nigeria in 2007 to these zones. With this development, either of the zones now has the legitimacy to produce a credible personality who by his antecedents, can command the mandate of the people of Nigeria, to hold the ace at Aso Rock come 2007, depending on the consensus they both reach.
This agreement, which was the first of its kind in the history of the southern region, witnessed the South-west of the country represented by Afenifere, the pan-Yoruba socio-political group and the Yoruba Council of Elders (YCE), allowing common sense to prevail. The zone reasoned with its counterpart zones that it was high time either of them produced the President of the country, a position which has since the 45 years of Nigerian independence, eluded them.
This decision was the by-product of the political summit jointly convened by South-west, South-east and South-south under the auspices of Afenifere cum YCE, Ohaneze N'digbo and South-south People's Assembly. The summit was also the aftermath of the committee meeting held last week by the groups at the Lagos Airport Hotel under the aegis of Southern Leaders' Forum. Led by representatives of each zone which included Chief Olu Falae of South west, Chief John Oyegun and Chief Chukwuemeka Ezeife of South-south and South-east respectively, the committee agreed to strike a common deal that would lead to the emergence of the President from the zones.
The summit held at Excellence Hotel, was no doubt, a mission accomplished. The South-west, without raising any question, conceded the Presidency in 2007 to the two zones, a move well appreciated in an apparent spirit of understanding.
It was no doubt a comngregation of who is who as eminent personalities across the South made it a date to grace the summit . Among the eminent personalities at the occasion were Vice Admiral Mike Akhigbe, Chief Mathew Mbu, Chief Olu Falae, Chief Reuben Fasoranti, Anyim Pius Anyim, Chief John Odigie-Oyegun, Rear Admiral Ndubuisi Kanu, Prof. A. B. C. Nwosu, Dr. Tunji Otegbeye, Commodore Ebitu Ukiwe, Chief Chukwuemeka Ezeife, Alhaji Lateef Jakande, Chief Raymond Dokpesi among many others.
General Adeyinka Adebayo, leader of the YCE and chairman of the partnership summit was unavoidably absent and hence, Mbu, a co-chairman chaired the occasion. All and sundry lamented the level of depravity the nation has been driven into by the activities of unsrupulous and disgruntled elements who have allegedly been piloting the affairs of the Nigeria since independence 1960.
In his speech, Dr. Tunji Otegbeye who spoke on behalf of YCE, expressed utmost happiness that such a summit marked the beginning of progress. According to him, it was high time the idea of North-South dichotomy which has virtually formed the basis for power rotation be jettisoned and consigned into the dust bin of history. For Otegbeye, a renowned political activist since the pre-colonial days, the idea of North-South dichotomy is nothing else, but a creation of the colonial masters, which he argued should have faded over the years. His candid view is that power rotation should be based on zonal classification as against the North-South structure.
Appraising the decision, Mbu said the concession on the Presidency was absolutely essential, because according to him, the hour has come for the people's government with a due sense of service to be constituted. This he believes, will put paid to the numerous woes blighting the progress of this country. Mbu regretted that Nigerians have been accustomed with the idea of watching the status quo with the assumption that problems should rather solve themselves as against confronting problems with a view to getting them solved. Hence, he argued that the idea of power concession was taking the bull by the horns.
"It is time we had the government of our own that would make impact through the programmes of that government. Nigeria is facing miriads of problems. Nigerians believe that there are problems, but that those problems would get themselves solved. This is the view of Nigerians. But they don't get solved. These problems must be addressed," he stated.
Mbu was of the view that the partnership of the three zones as well as its concession to pursue a common goal, was the foundation for addressing the problems confronting the entity called Nigeria. Moreover, he argued that the South east and South-south zones would henceforth, reject the feelings of inferiority complex foisted on them and take their positions as parts and parcels of the Nigerian state. He expressed optimism that either of the zones would produce the next President in 2007.
Former Minister of Information, Professor A.B.C. Nwosu, in his own comment, did not hesitate to laud the the South west for agreeing to concede the Presidency to the zones. He was of the firm belief that the consensus was a sheer manisfestation of truth, fairness and equity, assuring the Yoruba that both zones would exploit the opportunity to set an agenda for the progress and development of Nigeria.
"I salute you, the leaders for rejecting in totality the North -South dichotomy. I salute our friends, brothers and sisters from the South-west for accepting that the President must come from South-east and South-south. This is a meeting of veterans to fight against ineptitude in the system on the basis of truth, fairness and equity. It is on this basis that we have agreed that Presidency must come from the South-east and South-south. We must set agenda for good governance. If people here can't set agenda, who else can?" Nwosu asked.
Nwosu also assured that following the resolve on the Presidency, both zones of South-east and South-south would henceforth strike a common deal as to which of the two should present the presidential candidate, who will be well supported by the entire peopleof the South.
Former governor of Lagos State, Alhaji Lateef Kayode Jakande, in his address, x-rayed the state of the country and concluded that a summit of that kind was only necessary, if only the country would be rescued from the woods.
Jakande absolutely regretted that Nigeria, blessed with both human and mineral resources has been battered by poverty, homelessness and all maners of physical degradation rendering it completely backward in the comity of nations.
Hence, he pleaded with the gathering not to be carried away by mere zonal agreement, but should rather device a way of fashioning out development programmes which would promote the living standard of the people when the South deal eventually yields the expected dividends.
His words : "I thank the organisers of this meeting. We need to move Nigeria forward and not backward. We have passed through different times in the history of this country. We 've experienced things we 've never heard; things we've never contemplated. The degree of poverty is unprecedented. 70 per cent of the people are poor and homeless. Yet day after day, dismissal is going on to join a number of Nigerians who have no work. This should be our focus. This country is blessed. The resources we are blessed with cannot be compared with others. How many countries have oil, cassava? Some people get wealthier and wealthier, while our people get poorer and poorer. We cannot continue to allow that. We talk of N1.8 trillion budget, yet education is in a mess. There is nothing to write home about the hospitals. Most of us will remember the time that if you graduated, you got a car in addition. It is not so nowadays. We have a number of graduates on the streets who have no job. We talk of robbery; there will be robbery. Mr. chairman, I plead with this audience, let's formulate a programme that can carry this country forward. We want progress in this country. We want people who will live on their own, not the people who will live on the country.
"We must have agenda, concrete agenda. I'm not bothered by third term, fourth term, fifth term or sixth term. We want a clear cut programme that will serve as a stepping stone for greater Nigeria, "Jakande advised.
However, the consensus of the South to produce the President in 2007, may signify another battle front as the decision will no doubt cause a stir in the camp of Northern elites, who recently resolved to seize power, come what, come may in 2007. These recent developments foretell that events in the next couple of months as the general elections draw nearer and nearer, will be exciting. Exciting, because the battle line has now been drawn between the North and South. And undoutedly, it is going to be a fierce battle as the zones seem to have teamed up for a common goal. Whereas, South-west, South-east and South-south have now agreed to raise one candidate to break the invincibility of the North, the region is also leaving no stone unturned to raise a consensus candidate to battle the South, and thus implying that the presidential battle come 2007, will be a race between the North and the South. This again, brings to the fore, the battle against the North- South dichotomy which is the major bedrock upon which the Southern leaders' summit is predicated.
The summit's strong argument is that Nigeria is a conglomerate of six-geo political zones. Therefore, a situation where power is only being shared among other zones of the North and the South-west negates the principle of justice and fair play which the summit said it is committed to. The South-south and the South-east with strong backing of the South-west seem set to take their destiny into their hands. Presidency then, come 2007, will no doubt be a do or die affair as the zones have vowed not to be spectators any longer in the affairs of the country, but rather as active participants, dispensing power like other zones have been doing. The summit reasoned that for the North to lay claim to 2007 presidency on the belief that the incumbent President Olusegun Obasanjo from the South-west zone has exhausted the chances of other zones, is ungodly, unholy and alters the principle of fair play.
Little wonder then, when Chief Raymond Dokpesi, chairman of the planning committee, while summing up the resolution of the summit said : "We deplore the current simplistic attempt to polarise Nigeria into a North/South divide, even on issues of national importance, particularly with respect to the rotation of power at the centre. For the principle and process of rotation to be equitable and meaningful, it must be based on the existing six-geo political zones.
"That given the fact that the other zones have dominated the highest office of the land since independence, the South -east or the South-south shall produce the President of the Federal Republic of Nigeria come 2007. This demand is based on justice, equity, fairness, good conscience and historical antecedents."
How much impact this resolution will make remains an issue to watch out for as the election year gathers momentum. Although, the summit is not ruling out any possibility of selling the idea to the North, based on the above clarifications, the question agitating the minds of many is, what measure of efforts can get the North convinced, that now is the time for these zones of the South, in the face of its recent launch to seize power again come 2007?

Original_Search_Title: Weah's Monrovia Strongholds Condemn CDC Violence
Headline: Liberia: Weah's Monrovia Strongholds Condemn CDC Violence
Body_Text:
Monrovia — George Weah of the Congress for Democratic Change (CDC) has reportedly
denied making statements widely reported in local and international media
that he declared himself winner of the November 8 presidential run-off
Sunday night at a rally at his party's headquarters.
Dr. C. William Allen, Minister of Information in Liberia's transitional
government, told reporters Tuesday at the Capitol Building that the CDC
standard-bearer made the denial at a two-hour long meeting with Chairman
Gyude Bryant. The meeting was reportedly held at the Executive Mansion.
Yesterday's meeting followed a declaration by Weah that his CDC was
cheated in the run-off. Prior to the run-off, the CDC also claimed it was
denied victory in the general and presidential elections of October 11.
Despite his denial of his claims to be the 'legitimate' president of
Liberia, credible international news media such as the BBC and Reuter News
Agency, with the corroboration of various local Liberian media, especially
Star Radio, quoted the apparently disgruntled loser of the recent run-off
election as saying that he won the election but, because of widespread
vote-rigging, he was denied the Liberian presidency.
"I am President of this country, whether you like it or not; it will not
change. I told President Mbeki this. I repeat that I was cheated in the
elections," Weah was quoted as saying upon his return home on Sunday from
his visits with the Ghanaian President, John Kufour, and South African
President Thabo Mbeki.
Recordings of Weah's self-declaration as president of Liberia have since
been played on the airwaves of local and international stations. Observers
are eager to know if Chairman Bryant took the denial seriously.
Although the CDC currently protests the election results with ongoing
hearings at the National Elections Commission (NEC), Weah returned
surprisingly from visits with a flat dismissal of the results, regardless of
the ongoing hearings.
Not only has the pronouncement stirred up the country, but it has also
incited CDC supporters to perpetrate violent acts after the Sunday
rally.
Marauding party youths, fresh from the rally, clashed with National and
UNMIL Police, pelting them with stones and bottles. This violent
confrontation resulted in two deaths as of yesterday, serious injuries and
extensive damages to UNMIL and other vehicles. At least fifty arrests have
been made.
On Monday, a cabinet meeting was moved out of Monrovia to Kakata, 36
kilometers northeast of the capital, following intelligence from Government
security agencies that there were plans to stabilize the state.
Throughout Monday, peacekeepers remained heavily deployed around the CDC
headquarters, while helicopters hovered overhead, apparently to detect any
mass gathering of persons not easily detectible by personnel on the
ground.
Weah's denial came about the same time a key member of his party, Milton
Teahjay, appeared on a local radio station denouncing the UP's victory and
threatening to stall the inauguration of President-elect Ellen
Johnson-Sirleaf on January 16.
A CDC official, appearing on a local radio station Monday, argued that
Weah's pronouncement that he won the election was in keeping with the
party's protest that it was cheated on November 8. He also claimed Weah's
Sunday declaration was nothing new.
Minutes before Weah made his Sunday declaration, Milton Teahjay
presented him to the wildly cheering crowd as "the legitimate 23rd
president-elect." Observers say that statement, more than anything else,
incited the CDC partisans to take to the streets on Sunday night.
Many Partisans Disappointed
Since the defeated presidential candidate made the declaration, public
concern over the statement has been mounting. The former United Nations
Goodwill Ambassador has won long-standing respect among locals as a peaceful
man. But many people in Monrovia were shocked that would make such a
pronouncement, which tends to threaten their newfound democracy and the
peace and stability that the international community had done so much and
spent so much money to bring to war-torn Liberia.
For many residents of West Point, a densely populated slum community in
Monrovia, which Weah carried handily during both the October 11 and the
November 8 runoff elections, their standard bearer's pronouncement, which
triggered violence, was counterproductive to lasting peace and
security.
"I urge Mr. George M. Weah and his CDC supporters to gracefully accept
the election results and help move the country forward with its
reconstruction," says one Mr. Blamoh. The 24-year-old Liberian also
emphasized, "We have a huge task of rebuilding our country that has been
extensively destroyed by political fortune hunters over the years."
Amos B. Wesseh, 45 year-old woodcutter, said he was disturbed by the CDC
supporters' violent protests and demonstrations which, he said, tended to
mar the restoration of peace and stability in the country. "I want to
admonish Mr. Weah and his supporters to still seek the legal solutions to
their problems."
Princess Sonpon of West Point had a different view. She believed Weah
did win both the October/November elections. "Look my brother, thousands of
Liberians were at CDC headquarters everyday in Congo Town, yet the National
Election Commission reported that the Unity Party Standard Bearer,
Mrs. Ellen Johnson-Sirleaf, won the election. I'm not satisfied with the NEC
report' Ms. Sonpon pointed out.
A 77-year-old cassava seller, Madam Elizabeth Sonpon Weah, said, "Let my
son, George Weah leave this president business because, the president
business is not for small children. I want my great grand children to have
better education and jobs to take care of me in my last days" Madam Weah
appealed in anger and desperation.
A similar survey conducted at Buzzy Quarter in Monrovia yesterday
discovered that residents of that community are unhappy about CDC's
pronouncement.
An elderly man, Joseph Kolubah, a shopkeeper, noted, "Let Oppong adhere
to the election results and allow peaceful atmosphere to prevail because we
are tired of war and ardently need to rebuild our lives."
Moses Pewu, a schoolteacher, pointed out that Weah's remark was not
healthy for peace and urged him to give up his claims to the presidency. "We
are war-wearied and must do everything to abolish war and strive to move the
country forward," he said.
For his part, Tarnue Gayflor, a student, said, "If Oppong has any
problems with the conduct of the elections, let him seek redress through the
court system instead of instructing his supporters to take to the streets
and cause pandemonium while the matter is still before the court."
Also commenting on the Sunday incident, a Buzzy Quarter resident said
the arrest of over 50 people in the aftermath of the Sunday riot was
necessary to serve as a deterrent for other would-be troublemakers.
Other residents condemned what they called the "unruly behavior of the
CDC supporters. Many young people who claimed to be CDC members out-rightly
condemned the action of their colleagues and stressed the need for justice
to prevail in the case.
In New Kru Town, the opinion is divided among many residents. Nathaniel
B. Nyenkan, a teacher at the New Era School, said "Weah's statement
declaring himself president on Sunday was too naÃ¯ve, because his case is
before the National Elections Commission (NEC) for investigation. So let him
wait for the ruling from NEC. This is my advice."
Thomas Nimely, a 48-year old instructor in mathematics, said "Weah's
statement was derived from the principle that 'justice delayed is justice
denied.' So, I hold the NEC responsible for Weah's statement to declare
himself president. He's right to speak out, but I am not in favor of the
chaos that came out as a result of the statement. I feel that Weah was
cheated, but I am not a troublemaker to support trouble shooting
statements."
A high school pupil, Musa Lumeh, is the CDC's junior mobilization
chairman for the Doe Community. "I don't support Weah Sunday's statement
because I am not in favor of the argument that he is the elected 23rd
president. We want the country to move forward with development, but not
with unnecessary statements that have no basis of truth," he said.
Lawrence Kofa, a 32-year old physics teacher, expected Weah to act more
responsibly since he is often referred to as a peace ambassador. "So, let
him behave in that manner rather than proclaiming himself the winner of
elections that he clearly lost. I therefore want him to remain calm to
maintain his status as a goodwill ambassador. "
For Tony R. Bartu, a businessman, Weah is entitled to his personal
opinion. "I have no misgivings with his statement, but let others not use
that to cause chaos. The police also contributed to the chaos, so they paid
the price. Notwithstanding, let the law take its course," he said.
Jerome Jackson, 37, thinks different. "The statement of Mr. Weah was
reckless, because the elections were free, fair and transparent."
An elderly man, Ansuman Sonny of the Point Four Community on the Bushrod
Island, thinks much the same way. "I did not agree with Weah because the
statement did not go down well with us in times like these when we are
yearning for peace. Let him accept it as it is since he claimed to be a
unifier"
An elderly woman who referred to herself as Ma Nancy said she is related
to Mr. Weah. She described Weah's statement as something made out of
frustration, but called on him to realize that he lost the elections.
Labor Union Frowns Upon Statement
Meanwhile, the Forestry, Logging & Allied Workers' Union of Liberia
(FLAWUL) has condemned Weah for making the statement.
FLAWUL maintained that by conducting free, fair and transparent
elections on October 11 and November 8, respectively, and results widely
accepted by Liberians and the international community, Liberia has won
victory of good over evil of war, political instability and regular killing
and displacement of the populace by day.
"It is therefore prudent for every peace-loving Liberian to accept the
results and begin to work harder for the country and put hands around the
elected government, in good faith, to develop the country and bring
improvement in the lives of Liberians," a release said.
"The awkward position taken by Mr. Weah and his advisors to gang up
hooligans, failed educated people and quark politicians to fight against the
widely accepted results of the election and impose him on the Liberian
people at all cost, has the propensity to undermine the peace and stability
of the state, erode the confidence of the international community, drive
investors away and continue to keep Liberians in the state of unemployment,"
said the release.
The release said the CDC was "gradually tugging the country into another
round of crisis." It said the Union condemned this act in the strongest
possible terms "and declared it unacceptable to workers in their totality."
The Union therefore called on the Liberian people and the international
community, including UNMIL, to take the move by Mr. Weah seriously and
condemn the act.

Original_Search_Title: FG Generates N13bn From Onne FTZ, Escapes WTO Sanctions
Headline: Nigeria: FG Generates N13bn From Onne FTZ, Escapes WTO Sanctions
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Federal Government realised the sum of N12.84 billion from the Onne
Free Trade Zone, Onne Port, Rivers States, in the first six months of this
year.
It also generated the sum of N5.41billion through the operations of the
Nigerian Custom Service at the port while another N752.14million was
generated through the Nigerian Ports Authority at the same port within the
same period of time.
Similarly, the country has cleared its arrears of duties to the World
Trade Organisation. The arrears, arose as a result of the failure of the
country to pay its duties to the world body since 2001, but wih the latest
development,Nigeria has eventually saved itself from facing stiff sanctions
from the World Trade Orgaization.
Minister of Commerce, Ambassador Idris Waziri, disclosed these in Abuja
on Friday at the Annual Ministerial Press Briefing organised by the ministry
of commerce in conjunction with the ministry of Information.
Revenue profiles of government agencies located at the Onne Port have
increased within the last one year due to increased activities of the
enterprises operating in the zone.
Waziri who acknowledged that the Federal Government campaign for the
export of cassava derivatives said commodity export have created a shortage
in both local and foreign market needs thereby leading to sharp increase in
prices.
He charged private investors to explore avenues to invest in the
production of cassava in the country, noting that current demands especially
from China could not be met this year and had been shifted forward to
2006.
My recent visit to China also proved to be worthwhile as our cassava
chips would now attract $170 as against $110. Also, the Chinese Cassava
Starch Processing association has decided to visit Nigeria on a cassava
investment and trade mission in January 2006.
These give a clear indication that a cassava based industrial revolution
has been set in motion by the Federal Government through the presidential
initiative on cassava.
Waziri said the Federal Government approved the payment to the WTO to
save Nigeria the shame of losing certain benefits from the international
body.
Waziri had a week ago raised alarm that Nigeria may be de-listed as
executive member of World Trade Organisation (WTO) if it refused to pay up
her membership due. The MINISTER had warned that Nigeria cannot afford to
lose the Vice Chairmanship position as it lost the slot for the post of
Deputy Director General of WTO urged the Minister of Finance to release
money for the payment of the due.

Original_Search_Title: Micro-Credit to Be Channeled Through Community Banks - Kpakol
Headline: Nigeria: Micro-Credit to Be Channeled Through Community Banks - Kpakol
Body_Text:
THE National Coordinator (NA) of the National Poverty Eradication
Programme (NAPEP), Dr. Magnus Kpakol has said that existing Community Banks
across the nation would be designated Micro Finance Banks and would become
outlets for channeling micro-credit from the federal government and
commercial banks to the poor at the grassroots. He said in Abuja, at the
weekend that the launching of the new micro-credit scheme by the Central
Bank of Nigeria (CBN) at the middle of next month would commence the
implementation of the Multi-Partner Matching Fund which would attract
resources from NAPEP, Commercial Banks, states and local government, as well
as, major private sector operators.
His words, "the CBN will launch the micro-finance scheme mid next month
and we will be their grassroots partners. We also will help them to generate
funds to put into the micro finance banks through our market partners.
"We have told the governors, and said governors, you bring a certain
amount and we bring matching fund and then get commercial banks to also
raise funds for credit to people in those states as part of our on-going
partnership with the private sector operators.
"We have got United Bank for Africa (UBA), Zenith, Oceanic, Trade Bank
(which is now part of Afribank), Global Bank (now part of Intercontinental),
and Equatorial Trust Fund. These are our commercial partners in the
Multi-Partner Matching Fund Scheme for Micro Credit.
"And then we will bring in Local Governments. This can generate N2
million and upward per state to put into these Micro Finance Banks that we
will creating, along with others. This is new. The money will be tapped
through cooperatives. From commercial banks will give funds to the micro
finance banks which will pass the credit to the poor through cooperative
organizations.
"We will be working with the cooperatives on how to qualify for the
credit. We have to be sure that adequate arrangement is put in place, the
kind of activities in which to put te money, for instance the, the
Presidential Cassava Initiative. There must an effective arrangement for
repayment by those who receive credit facilities, otherwise, the whole thing
would collapse.
"NAPEP will work with those at the grassroots to build capacity among
those who receive credit, by helping them to identify organizations to sell
their produce to, as well as, the kind of high yielding species to grow in
other to ensure maximum output. The poor person in the village does not know
this kind of thing. That is the job of NAPEP.
"We want to build NAPEP into a repository of knowledge. A true knowledge
of how to fight poverty, so that NGOs, local governments and other
grassroots organizations could get that kind of value from us".
He stressed the need for micro-credit for poor members of the nation if
the people were ever to move up on the economic ladder, as no amount of
provision of infrastructure facilities could really remove poverty without
an avenue for individual income generation. His word, "if you put an
infrastructure for the people at the grassroots without a visible means of
income, that infrastructure will definite collapse. If the people don"t have
an income to fix the pump, when the pump breakdowns, for instance, it
becomes what in Economics, we call "the tragedy of the commons".

Original_Search_Title: NDDC Chairman Accused of Shunning Due Process
Headline: Nigeria: NDDC Chairman Accused of Shunning Due Process
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Chairman of the Niger Delta Development Com-mission (NDDC),
Amba-ssador Sam Edem has been accused of running the agency like a sole
administrator.
In a letter dated November 7, 2005 and signed by eight states
representatives, the chairman was accused of running NDDC without due
process or regard to the provisions of the Commission's Act and
Standing Orders of the Board.
The representatives who who are members of the agency board said,
"we find it quite strange, to say the least, that the Chairman of the
NDDC has chosen a contra-direction to the level functioning of the
Board of the Commission", part of the letter read.
The was particularly accused of awarding contracts without due
process, adding that this was in utter disregard for the Commission's
Authorisation Manual.
Among the allegations raised by the representatives include:
unnecessary and unconscionable continued stay at Hotel Le Meridien
amounting to wastefulness and unbudgeted expenditure on the part of the
chairman;
Trivialisation of and disregard of board meetings by the chairman,
board meetings have been reduced to mere presentation by potential
contractors and consultants;
General lack of work focus and direction in the affairs of the commission;
shoddy implementation of the cassava project which is lacking in
focus and direction. board members have found themselves at cross-road
on this project and;
Stiffling of the functions of the board and its committees.
The states' representatives also expressed dismay over review of
sharing indices as it affect states in their individual budgets.
Another issue raised against the chairman was over-centralisation of
functions, expenditure, activities and programmes of the commission at
the headquarters to the total exclusion of the states' offices and
board members.
They also pointed the Non-commissioning of completed projects which
according to the letter, was causing deterioration to a state where
further funds may be required.
In the petition signed by Mr. Godwin Eyo, representating Akwa Ibom
State; Dr. Mrs Georgina Nwagha - Bayelsa State; Mr. Ekpo Okon - Cross
River; Mr. Val Arenyeka - Delta State; Mr Mathew Iduos - Edo; Prince
Emmanuel Assor - Imo; Dr. O Omotehinse - Ondo State and Mr. Dimabo
Oruama - Rivers State, the representatives resolved to cease from
attending board meetings or unilateral briefings or any other fora
forthwith.
They however gave the chairman seven days ultimatum to address all
the issues raised or they will be forced to take further action deemed
necessary.
Obasanjo had in April enjoined the board to work for the
entrenchment of "prosperity, progress and development of the NDDC in
line with the Niger Delta Regional Master Plan.
The President had made the appeal at the Presidential Villa as part
of the fence mending meeting occasioned by the earlier crisis in the
Commission in which the Managing Director was sent on compulsory leave.

Original_Search_Title: Resumption of River Traffic a Great Relief to the Public
Headline: Congo-Brazzaville: Congo-DRC: Resumption of River Traffic a Great Relief to the Public
Body_Text:
Brazzaville — Only five kilometres separate the ports of Brazzaville and Kinshasa
but a two-week suspension of river traffic between the two cities made
the distance seem infinite. To the relief of Brazzaville's 856,410
residents, authorities reopened the trade route across the River Congo
on 4 November.
Kinshasa, capital of the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), is the
main supplier of both food and non-food commodities to Brazzaville,
capital of the Republic of Congo (ROC), on the south bank of the mighty
River Congo. The traffic ban, which was imposed on 18 October,
triggered price hikes and shortages in the ROC's capital city.
River traffic between the two capitals was suspended after former
Zairean army troops occupied Brazzaville's riverside port, known as the
Beach, and demanded that they be allowed to return to the DRC.
The troops had fled Zaire to the neighbouring Republic of Congo in
May 1997 after Ugandan and Rwandan-backed guerrillas, lead by
Laurent-Desire Kabila, toppled Zairean leader Mobutu Sese Seko and
renamed the country Democratic Republic of Congo.
The ex-army troops, who were members of Mobutu's presidential
guards, had been trying to return home from ROC after what their
representative, Capt Ambroise Kusa, said were "several tough years in
exile". Authorities in both Congos, however, had blocked the river
crossings, saying there had been no accord between the two governments
for this movement.
After successful negotiations between the two countries, the first
group of soldier returned to Kinshasa on 1 November. Now that the issue
of repatriation has been completed, the trade route has also been
reopened.
Effect of port closure
The ban on river crossings had an immediate effect on Brazzaville's
residents. Some food prices in the market rose sharply: a 30-kg sack of
fufu flour, the cassava-based staple, rose from 18,000 francs CFA to
32,000 francs (US $32 to $57).
Port officials at the Beach reported that during the closure
smugglers tried to keep the lifeline open by supplying small quantities
of some of these commodities via small, motorised canoes but they could
not meet demand.
ROC imports vegetable oil, palm oil, soap, margarine, coffee,
alcoholic and nonalcoholic beverages, brown sugar, biscuits, salt and
salted fish from DRC. In addition, building materials like cement,
concrete, and steel reinforcements also come from the DRC.
Commerce at Brazzaville's Beach contributed "enormously to the
economic development", according to river transport agent Joachim
Lemba. Therefore a repture in traffic between the two cities could
squeeze of lot of people out of work.
"I live from day-to-day thanks to my odd job at the Beach. During
the two weeks [of traffic suspension], it was difficult to provide for
my family's daily needs," declared ArsÃ¨ne Elombila, a self-employed
port labourer.
"On average I make 12,000 francs CFA [$21.41] per day. This enables me to feed my wife and two children," he said.
Importance of the Beach
The Beach is always a hive of activity. Porters rush around loading
and off-loading goods. Under the shady mango trees that dot the port,
moneychangers do brisk business and turn a healthy profit, thanks to
the strength of the ROC's currency.
Merchants on opposite banks of the river transport their goods on
barges belonging to the national river transport companies of both
countries: la Coordination nationale des transports fluviaux (CNTF) in
the ROC and the Office national des transports (ONATRA) in the DRC. The
Beach also serves as a border post and is staffed by immigration,
customs and police agents.
Some 30 percent of ROC's imports come from DRC, and the latter
imports 5 percent to 10 percent of its wood, plywood and wooden
electricity poles from ROC. The ROC Customs Department said it lost 100
million francs ($178,450) in duty during the traffic closure.
Despite being a producer of crude petroleum, ROC depends on its
neighbour for much of its refined paraffin, oil, gasoline and jet fuel.
ROC's foreign trade office, the Direction gÃ©nÃ©rale du commerce
extÃ©rieur, has recorded 30 billion franc CFA ($53.54 million) of
imports from DRC since 2001. This amount represented about one-third of
the country's total global annual imports, making the resumption of
traffic a welcome relief to the public and the nation as a whole.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: A Day in the Slums of the East
Headline: Sierra Leone: A Day in the Slums of the East
Body_Text:
Freetown — From the wharves of Kampala to that of Susan's Bay, there is an ever
growing community, advancing towards the sea; the demographic changes
of the last six years has caused the expansion of these once
uninhabited areas, which are mostly habited by a youthful population.
(Almost 65% of those who live in these areas are youths), ambitious
and desperate for a place to live, but at the same time lacking the
recourses to afford a more decent housing facility. To get the real
side of these communities I endeavor in the direction of lower savage
square [Kampala] up to Big wharf for a full day rendezvous of how
people cope within these environmentally unfriendly area.
As I venture out early in the morning to spend a day in these wharf
communities, I had my first encounter with Lamin Turay, a 29 year old
man. Standing on his recently built mud house at the edge of the sea,
he moaned that as a boy he never thought of anybody living where he
now lives, "it was unthinkable that one would dear live here" the
reality now is that the tiny sea front, back of Bishop's court (Lower
Kampala, adjacent to the Queen Elizabeth II quay) is filled to
capacity. Lamin like many other youths here are unemployed. Lamin
confesses that he was a pirate, but that after seeing the brutal
demise of two of his colleagues at sea, he has now decided to drop
that career. Ask what he may like to do, he replied, I want to fish,
but I don't have the money to purchase fishing nets. Just a stone
throw from Lamin's mud house there is a much livelier atmosphere as
the sounds of reggae music blazed through. Some young men are deeply
asleep on the benches while others are chatting with vigor. As huge
smoke of marijuana filters through the air some of the young men were
bemused by the presence of a stranger, it took me few minutes to
assure them that I am not a police or CID officer.
Though the men were in the majority, there were few women around; one
of them suggested that I buy them five wraps of marijuana for them to
accommodate me.
After doing so they began to talk freely. Isatu, 17, who struggled to
talk to me as she puffed marijuana to the air, told me that since
1997 she has not seen any of her relative's after the war divided
them. Asked what she is doing she was quick to reply: 'me na rarray
gyal' boldly in front of the others, meaning that she is a prostitute
"why can't you go back to your village?" I asked; two things she
replied "I don't know whether my people are still alive and I don't
even have the transport to go back " Osman a 21 year old man who was
awoken by our conversation told me after I asked him why he was
sleeping in mid-day, that he has no place to sleep. The owner of the
ghetto John said that most of the young men here don't have somewhere
to sleep; you only need to purchase marijuana and have a rest. John a
former combatant boasted that he sells the best available marijuana
in town.
The sprawling community of lower Kampala has now been merged with
that of Moa wharf, which has also witnessed a rapid rise in
population over the past six years, a series of settlements recently
created now made these communities to look as one. Only, about ten
years ago the government had frantically removed the relatively small
settlement that of Moa wharf-- caterpillars and bulldozers had
brushed their way through and destroyed all the shabby settlements.
The government forcefully relocated habitants of these areas to the
Far East of the city. Citing environmental risks and dangers the
government in a bid to salvage a bridge and a drainage leading to the
wharf argued that it is unsafe and totally exigent that people be
removed from these areas. The aftermath of the war however, which
witnessed a breakdown of law and order and the destruction of several
houses by the rebels and also the massive movement of refugees and
internally displaced people meant that the very limited and
underdeveloped housing infrastructure could not cope with the ever
growing demands. The result is that most people have resorted to put
up in these socially and environmentally dangerous areas like
mountain zones and wharves. The dangers and negative features of
these communities are not in anyway hidden. They are perceived not
only by outsiders but by the inhabitants themselves. Mamadi Kargbo a
33 years old man has been sick for the past nine months; originally,
he hails from Makeni where he was a primary school teacher. As a
result of a rebel attack in 1998 he was force to flee. Mr. Kargbo has
tried to get a job as a primary school teacher, but because he has no
formal qualification, his efforts were all futile. Since he fell ill,
he has not received any treatment from a medical doctor. Instead he
has only been offered traditional herbs and other local remedies. As
he strives to show me his picture he took six years ago, one can
discern the drop in condition of once a respectful and physique
primary school teacher who was lying with bare bones. Just few meters
from where he was staying about three other young men were openly
easing themselves near the water. One has to be strong to live here,
remarked one of the guys, as I cover my nose with handkerchief. The
little stretch of soil between the settlements and the sea is strewn
and covered with all types of waste, even the sand is hardly visible
as torn clothes, tins, waste paper bags, etc are scattered
everywhere, there are also dead dogs, cats and mouse all mingling
with human feaces. Notwithstanding this odd sight and the offensive
smell filtering through the air most of the people in the wharf are
simply going about their business, laborers are busy offloading
catches of fish from the canoes parked in the wharf. Fish mongers are
also serving out their product to be taken to the market for sale.
Whilst children are playing hide and seek among the debris of refuse.
As I try to leave Moa wharf I met an old man who has stayed in that
community for a pretty long time, when I asked him about his point of
view of the area he told me that he finds it difficult to live in the
area any longer. "If I have another option, I could have moved to
another area". He said to me that most of the young men around here
are menace to the society, some of them are pirates, others are pick
pocketters and some robbed people at night. Please don't name me he
said. He however, noted that this was not the case before; the war he
said has damage our social order. The government must try and do
something about these boys if not "we've another crisis in the
making" he concluded.
If the slums of Moa wharf and Kampala possess a somber scenario, that
of Marbella and big wharf are a much bigger and environmentally
unfriendly area. Most of the houses in big wharf for instance are
build on piles of refuse. The local head of Big wharf, Chief Pa.
Alimamy sum up the condition of his area saying that they are
suffering; there is no water, no health facility, and no toilet.
There is no training facility for the quite a lot of unemployed
youths .Another problem he said is that the community is not
motorable making it difficult to transport goods and other items. The
only job here the chief said is a laborer's job, most of whom tot
wood from canoes to wood shelters. At times I found it difficult to
comprehend my position as chief in such a deprived community. We've
been promised help several times by NGO's and politicians who
sometimes come down here to do surveys. But as you can see there is
nothing like development here.
Even the chiefs affected by the social decadence of his community,
his house is not much different from the others. Like many other
families around here the chief has lost one of his children, infant
mortality rate is very high. Dalton M Koroma whose house was burnt by
the rebels and who now grinds cassava leaves and groundnut for his
survival has lost six out of fourteen children by his two wives, four
of them he said died at birth whilst two others died during infancy.
There is hardly a family here that has not lost a child. The fact
that Sierra Leone has one of the highest infant mortality rate in the
world is more evident in this community more than any other. Those
who are lucky to survive face a rather tough battle in life. Abdul
Mansaray is only 14, but with his mother dead and father paralyzed,
he's out seeking for anything he can tot for the day, he went out of
his way to show me bruises and marks he sustained in his rough trade.
Only a week ago, he fell over with a pile of wood on his head
sustaining a severe injury, all he had was severe beatings and insult
of a lazy lizard for falling with the woods.
Amidst the deprivation and poverty in the Big wharf and Marbella
community there is also the issue of crime. Sergeant 2446 Alikali
Kargbo is the head of the police unit for Marbella and Big wharf, he
told me that crimes like common assault, larceny and pick pocketing
are on the rise, every day we receive various cases of such crimes,
especially during the night hours. But we're working hard to punish
these criminals so as to reduce this trend, he said. Most of the
young people here are not doing anything, so the only way some of
them feel they can survive is by robbing people. At times we're
overwhelmed as this community is much more bigger. As you can see the
place is congested and people are now living at the edge of the sea
front. Prostitution is rife also in this community, two prostitute,
Tima 14, and Mbalu 15, complained that they are being intimidated by
the police to pay what they refer to as their 'regular dues' whenever
we failed to give them money we're frightened and sometime arrested
until we 'cooperate', meaning bribing them before we are allowed to
operate freely. When I asked them about the possibility of them
contracting hiv/aids, they both wave off their arms saying that aids
is a non-existence disease.
If there is any form of entertainment there are only one; the video
centre that shows both Nigerian movies and European football that is
very popular in the area. Another spot frequented by most of the idle
youths is the tiny football field at the edge of Marbella fish harbor
that once housed a thriving fishing industry, which served as the
source of livelihood for hundreds of youths around the Marbella
community. Now that industry is no longer here, its infrastructure
lay in ruins. As it has been replaced by small scale fishing. Instead
the most visible sight is that of an endless set of footballers and
gamblers doing their hobby all day long.

Original_Search_Title: 'It Was Stressful Living in Exile'
Headline: Uganda: 'It Was Stressful Living in Exile'
Body_Text:
Upon arrival at Nairobi's Jomo Kenyatta International airport from
Johannesburg, Dr Kizza Besigye had a chat with Daily Monitor's Simon
Kasyate. They talked about life in exile, what he had missed most Uganda,
and the political game plan. Here are the excerpts.
How have your four years out of Uganda been like?
Ah! Quite busy actually and stressful in other respects, but generally
quiet in that I did not have any disturbances to my person from any
quarter. There were threats that came from time to time. All those threats
came from Uganda, but I must say that I am really grateful to the South
African security system, which made my stay quite secure, and I think
comfortable.
You said stressful? Let's look at some detail here; how stressful
were these days?
Well, stressful in that anybody who has not been forced to live outside
his/her country will not understand especially the emotional stress that is
found in living without a base. You know, when you are in your country, you
travel and you know you are coming back home. If you don't have that
understanding within you that you have somewhere you call home, that by
itself is extremely stressful.
The fact that you have no family, no friends to whom you have been used
to interact with, you are removed from your environment drastically and
indefinitely, you don't know when you can reconnect with your
environment--that imposes, I think, a unique stress which nobody would
relate to unless someone has experienced that kind of situation.
But it was also stressful in terms of the kind of activities that I have
had to engage in mainly travelling up and down with very little means. You
live on a very, very tight budget, you don't know how you'll meet all the
requirements that are imposed on you and all these are quite stressful.
Your escape from Uganda in 2001 was based on certain conditions; have
these now been addressed to such a point that they will not adversely affect
your stay?
These conditions, I think, have actually gotten worse. Since I left,
many of my colleagues, many of our supporters have been arrested, very many
are still languishing in prisons in Uganda on tramped up charges of treason
and of other criminal charges. Some have been in prison for up to three
years without trial. There is a lot of intimidation of those who are not in
prison; some have been sacked from their jobs because of their political
association with us.
Those in businessÃ¢-oenobody would want to openly associate with members
of the opposition because once they do then they are harassed by the tax
men, by the licensing authorities; they cannot get tenders. So the situation
continues to be hostile.
However, one significant change has taken place, which is that as a
result of the G6 action, the constitutional court ruled and declared the
movement system as a one party state. That was a landmark ruling because it
opened the way for other political parties to be registered and to
function.
And as a result of that, our political pressure groups, which they were
until then, the reform Agenda and others, were able to transform themselves
into legal political parties, to open political activities throughout the
country.
We have offices of FDC in every district of Uganda, we have committees,
we have structures up to every village in our country and therefore we now
have an institutional mechanism within which we can continue to struggle for
our rights.
When I left Uganda in 2001, I was struggling as Kiiza Besigye. I did not
have any organisational backup. Today we have an organisation and it is that
organisation, together with me, that have realised that in order to advance
the struggle to another level this is an appropriate time that I should come
back, all the risks and the hostile environment notwithstanding.
We know that the situation is still as hostile or more hostile than it
was but we think that an opportunity exists to confront the hostility and to
fold it back. Some of us may become causalities in the process but the
process will be served, the process will advance through our engagement and
through my coming back and participation in the struggle for further freedom
and for further democratisation of our country.
So, what is your political game plan?
This is a matter that will have to be discussed within the FDC, within
the G6, the group of six political parties that have been co-operating in
struggling against the dictatorship, as a whole. We must together structure
an action plan that we can then follow. It is not a matter for me
alone.
But immediately, what is going to concern me is the delegates'
conference of the FDC, which is taking place from October 28. That will give
us a way to now have a new leadership that is fully empowered to undertake
whatever processes are envisaged subsequently.
If this delegates' conference anoints you to take the FDC mantle, are
you ready for the task?
Entirely! I am available to FDC, I am available to Uganda to undertake
whatever responsibility is necessary for the purpose of making a transition
to a democratic dispensation. And I have made this very clear right from
2001, that my political engagement is only limited to the requirements of
transiting to a democratic dispensation. Once that happens then I am afraid
I wont be available anymore.
And if the conference does not place you at the helm, what role would
you then play and how?
Well, we are in a political engagement as I have said to ensure that we
achieve democratic governance, that we achieve good governance. It is not
that good governance will come only when I am leading. Therefore whoever is
felt to be an appropriate person to lead the process, we must all rally
around that person and offer whatever we are able to offer to make sure that
the process moves forward.
And even if the FDC chose me as their flag bearer, I would still
recommend that the FDC engages in dialogue with the other parties and that
together with the G6 people, we can come out with one person to lead us into
this democratic struggle. And that person may not be the FDC candidate. Even
if it is any other person outside the FDC who is felt to be the best chance
for leading forward, we would all rally around that person.
Are you thus calling for a coalition of all political forces against
the NRM?
Yes! We in the FDC have always advocated for an alliance of all
pro-democracy political parties and this is why we have been very anxious to
promote the G6, to work through the G6 mainly for the purpose of achieving
the democratic transition.
Once a democratic transition has been achieved, once there are equitable
rules, regulations, laws and institutions, once there is equal facilitation
for all the political players, once a political playing field is leveled and
we can have a free and fair electoral process, then we do not have to work
through an alliance.
Every party can now espouse its development programme and the people of
Uganda can choose from them. But the immediate challenge now is not so much
the alternative development strategies; it is having a dispensation that can
allow any strategy whatever it is to function freely and beneficially for
the whole country.
There is rumour that upon arrival, you will be arrested and charged
with criminal offences. What is your take on that?
That will not be surprising at all. I have heard very recently that the
government has been rather uneasy about bringing charges of treason because
they don't have any kind of evidence obviously; but secondly then they would
be under pressure as the offended party to drop those charges. So I hear
that they maybe working on getting someone they can front to raise
complaints as a private citizen, on the basis of which then, they would say
they are obligated to enforce the law and to act in a manner that would then
require me to be arrested.
And I hear that one of the things that they are indeed considering is to
get somebody who could say that I raped her at some stage, and that she
lodges a complaint and on the basis of that they would then arrest me.
On a lighter note, is there anything particularly in terms of food
that you have so much missed that you can't wait to have?
Well, I would like to have my porridge from millet; I would like to have
my cassava for breakfast, all of which are not available where I was. So I
am looking forward to enjoying these and the wonderful fruits that can only
be found in Uganda. You can never find a pineapple like that which you find
in Uganda, or the small yellow bananas, the bwenvus or Kabalagala as we call
them. All these are things one looks up to as they are coming back
home.

Original_Search_Title: Rebranding Nigeria's Image
Headline: Nigeria: Rebranding Nigeria's Image
Body_Text:
Lagos — Nigeria, by virtue of its size, population and enormous natural
endowment such as crude oil, tin, gold, limestone, etc, has always been
expected to play a leading role in African politics.
These potentials were already recognized at independence. Her large
size, huge population and abundant natural resources endowed her with a
promising destiny and natural mandate to be a leader as well as a major
player in international politics.
However, over the decades, events in the domestic scene conspired to
ensure that these potentials remained not more than a dream. The
intervention of the military in Nigerian politics for about 29 years out of
45 years as an independent state had greatly marred the image of Nigeria
especially in the international community.
The arrogant and overbearing posture of the military dictators notably
the Abacha regime and the increasingly gross abuse of human rights, lack of
accountability and widespread corruption further aggravated the negative
image of the country.
These development weakened Nigeria's moral authority in Africa's affairs
ultimately leading it to becoming a pariah nation. Globally, events in
Nigeria were considered bad examples for an increasingly democratic
Africa. In fact, the seizures of power in some West African countries like
Gambia, Niger and Sierra Leone were attributed to the bad influence of
Nigeria's military; more so, a Nigerian was the Army Chief of Gambia's Armed
Forces.
Unfortunately, Nigeria suffered its most humiliating period in foreign
relations after the annulment of the presidential election result in 1993
and the subsequent sanctions imposed on her by the international
community. Again, the height of the Military's intransigence under Sanni
Abacha coincided with the period of global democratic renaissance. The rule
of law, good governance, accountability, transparency and protection of
human rights had become the rallying cry of the international
community.
Indeed, certain developments on the domestic scene combined to
successfully and cumulatively propel the country on the wrong side of
civilized behaviour and acceptability by the international community.
Infact, at no time in Nigeria's history, as an independent nation, had
there been as much international political and economical pressures as on
the heels of the annulment of the June 12 presidential elections. The
annulment was seen as an impudent subversion of the democratic process by
the Nigerian military. Nigeria's predicaments at this stage in her political
life forced it to seek friendship with countries of similar mould.
The last straw that broke the camel's back was the execution of famous
writer and minority rights activist, Ken Saro-Wiwa, in November
1995. Nigeria was expelled from the Commonwealth of Nations, an organization
composed of former British colonies.
In swift successions, the European Union (EU), the USA, and several
other nations imposed sanctions on Nigeria. Even African leaders expressed
great disappointment over developments in Nigeria. This incident made it
impossible for Nigeria to defend the African Nations cup, which she won in
Tunisia in 1994.
However, by some divine providence, in June 1998, General Abdulsalami
Abubaker, who succeeded the late dictator addressed many of the injustice
perpetuated by the former regime, a development that brought Nigeria into
the mainstream of international relations and friendship. The international
community appears to have so far responded favourably to the enthronement of
democracy in Nigeria as well as the policy thrust of the Obasanjo's
Administration, which is private sector driven. To be candid, this
Government has shown the determination and zeal to consolidate its gains in
various aspects of our national life so that Nigeria can emerge as an
economically viable and stable nation.
This determination has manifested in the establishment of anti-graft
agencies such as Independent Corrupt Practices Commission (ICPC) headed by
eminent Jurist and retired Supreme Court Judge, Mustapha Akanbi, Economic
and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC), piloted by committed and dedicated
police officer, Nuhu Ribadu among others. This administration is also
seriously committed to improving the quality of lives of Nigerians by
establishing poverty eradication agencies such as National Poverty
Eradication Programme (NAPEP), National Economic Empowerment Development
Strategy (NEEDS), and New partnership for Africa's Development
(NEPAD). These and the activities of other agencies and commissions
especially the National Drug Law Enforcement Agencies (NDLEA) and National
Agency for Food Administration and Control (NAFDAC) headed by transparent
and hard working Professor of pharmacology, Prof. Dora Akunyili, has placed
Nigeria on the right side of international community.
The recent debt relief granted the country by the Paris Club is an
indication that the world is appreciating the effort of the present
administration towards economic recovery. The prosecution of former Senate
President, Adolfus Wabara, over bribery and corruption with former Minister
of Education, Professor Fabian Osuji, has shown the world that the country
is determined to wipe out corruption at all levels. The on going prosecution
of former police chief Tafa Balogun; the recent indictments of Mrs Amaka
Anajemba amongst others are clear indications of this administration's
commitment to transparency and accountability. This will in turn attract
investors to the country. Unemployment will be reduced minimally, while
armed robbery, political thugery and the godfather syndrome will be a thing
of the past.
As Nigeria is in the fourth decade of her independence, and under
democratic rule, these positive moves by the government will help Nigeria to
build a strong and reputable image by adopting and implementing popular
policies that will transform the lives of Nigerians positively while still
maintaining her role as a leading player in African affairs.
Western or European foreign media organisations especially British
Broadcasting Corporation (BBC), Voice of America (VOA), Radio France
International and popular news agencies such as Associated press (AP),
Agence France Presse (AFP) and Reuters etc which are responsible for the
gathering and disseminating of information globally often focused on
Africa. Many foreign reporters have refused to change with continuously
changing developments in Nigeria and African nations. They continue with
their old and traditional approach to reporting the continent. Rather than
reporting only the negative side of the Continent, which most of the time is
unbalanced with emphasis on conflict and crisis situation, foreign reporters
should seek out causes of conflict and analyse important political and
economic factors, prepare the audience for possible future development. If
this is done, Nigeria would change the bad press the country receives. The
New World Information and Communication Order (NWICO) should be respected
and adhered to by information managers.
The Federal Ministry of Information and National Orientation in
conjunction with communication practitioners and educators have reiterated
their commitment to organising regular seminars to formulate a veritable
communications policy for the country.
The government of virtually every country has one or more departments or
agencies involved in communication with other nations. Seasoned public
relations professionals without any political or ethnic sentiment are given
the opportunity to make positive contributions to the Heat of Africa
project.. Even larger sum should be devoted to lobbying efforts to obtain
favourable legislation for the country's product. For example, Costa Rica
has urged the US Congress to import sugar at favourable rate. The recent
exportation of cassava to China is a step in the right direction. . Much
effort and millions of dollars should be spent on tourism industry,
attracting visitors whose expenditure would aid the country's economy.
Voice of Nigeria (VON), Nigeria Television Authority (NTA) and other
media organisations are meeting up with the challenges of sending broadcast
signals worldwide to foster national interest abroad, disseminate news and
influence the internal affairs of other nations like it did on the
restoration of democracy in Sao Tome Precipe, settling political crisis in
Liberia, Sierra Leone and currently in Sudan. Where she is the chief
mediator and also hosts the warring factions.
Other actions programmes that might be necessarlly include the
establishment of a national information bureau to provide facts and
published statements of favourable opinions about Nigeria in some of the
influential countries in the word like United State of America (USA) Britain
etc. Appointment should be made with key media people and other influential
citizens, including educators, business leaders' government officials and
core Public Relations professionals
For the government to succeed in the implementation of its policies and
programmes and consequently enjoy the respect and continued favour of the
international public, it requires the understanding, the co-operation and
indeed the involvement, as appropriate, of the international public. For the
country to take back its place as the giant of Africa, all hands must be on
deck.

Original_Search_Title: Togbe Afede X1v Calls for Positive Attitudes Towards National Dev't
Headline: Ghana: Togbe Afede X1v Calls for Positive Attitudes Towards National Dev't
Body_Text:
Accra — The Agbogbomefia of the Asogli state, Togbe Afede XIV, has called on Ghanaians to stop complaining, but show positive attitude towards work in order to achieve maximum production for national development.
He said there is the need to inculcate in the citizenry the virtues of hard work, positive thinking and perseverance and the need to understand that success at work requires hard work and the willingness to do more than one is asked to.
The Agbogbomefia said these at this year's Asogli Yam Festival, which also commemorated his second anniversary as the paramount chief of the Asogli, state in Ho.
According to him, praying for divine and ancestral guidance does not necessary assure success, adding "we need to change our attitude to education and understand that its purpose is to provide us with knowledge and to develop our intellect, not merely to provide us with certificates, only then can we be prepared enough to succeed in life and be able to contribute to the nations development."
Speaking on the theme " Attitudinal Change- An Important Prerequisite for Prosperity", Togbe Afede noted that sustainable development cannot take place in the absence of peace and unity. He added that there is the need for greater commitment to ensuring that governance is more participatory, accountable, transparent, and responsive.
He noted that important developmental challenges are not external, neither is debt cancellation the solution to our problems. "Development aspirations will prove illusive unless we change our attitudes", he advised.
He expressed disappointment at the way ideas of good governance in the country were being undermined by selfishness and greed.
"This is why a lot of money is being sunk into projects that are never completed, local examples include the Ho Sports Stadium and the Ho-Kpedze road, into which billions of cedis have been sunk over the past thirty years", he added.
He entreated all leaders to fight against the pervasive culture of dependency through their works, actions and pronouncements and also fight superstition, which is becoming a major factor in our inability to accept the need for change and to accept the responsibility of our failures.
Togbe Afede encouraged his fellow traditional rulers to bear in mind their responsibility to bring development to their people and to lead the fight to change the attitudes of their subjects in order to make their task easier.
He however called on all chiefs to come together to provide the inspiration, strength and the leadership that their people have been yearning for. He also entreated all rulers to unite to confront the urgent developmental needs of their people.
On his part the deputy Minister for Trade and Industry, Honourable Kofi Osei-Ameyaw said his ministry has put in place a new industrial reform and accelerated growth program to ensure the development of an export-led industrialization strategy and a domestic market led industrialization strategy based on import substitution.
He stated that the main objective of the program is to accelerate economic growth and development in the rural communities. "The program seeks to facilitate the development of commercially viable, export oriented and domestic oriented competitive business activities at the district level," he added.
Hon. Osei-Ameyaw who was also the Guest of Honour noted that the implementation of the program calls for the commitment of both residents and those outside the district. He however disclosed that through the ministries sensitisation workshops to usher in the concept, the Asogli state has been selected among others for the processing of cassava into chips and pellets for local consumption and exports.
He appealed to the citizens to take keen interest in the project especially, traditional authorities who own tracts of lands for the cultivation of the raw material, which is critical for the sustainability of the industries.
The Auafiaga of Ho Asogli, Togbe Lakle Adzie Howusu XII, in his welcome address called on all Ghanaians to be disciplined and to change their attitude of selfishness in order to work hard to be able to achieve our goal of development for better future.
"The Chinese have developed very fast over the past ten to fifteen years, from discipline and hard work", he pointed out.

Original_Search_Title: Agbogbomefia Calls for Attitude Change
Headline: Ghana: Agbogbomefia Calls for Attitudinal Change
Body_Text:
Ho — The Agbogbomefia of the Asogli State, Togbe Afede XIV, has called on all
Ghanaians to uphold and improve upon good governance, the rule of law
through greater accountability, transparency, tolerance and good attitudinal
values.
This, he noted, would ensure participatory governance, help attain
required developmental goals by way of rural and national development,
encourage the need for strict application and enforcement of laws that would
also ensure discipline throughout the country.
The Agbogbomefia, made this call on the occasion of this year's Asogli
Yam Festival celebration, which coincided with his second anniversary, last
Saturday in Ho.
Speaking on the theme "Attitudinal Change- An Important Prerequisite for
Development", Togbe Afede stated that, the nation's development challenges
are not caused by external factors and neither is debt cancellation the
solution: But a total transformation of attitudes, depoliticization of
development and upholding views of the minority and most vulnerable in
society; hence the need to take into consideration their situation in the
decision making process.
Stressing the importance of the theme, which he said would be his focus
for the year ahead, he said, " My experiences and analysis point to one
impediment- our attitudes.Our development aspirations will prove illusive
unless we change our attitudes."
He indicated that the ideals of good governance has been undermined by
selfishness and greediness among others, demonstrated by few people who
enrich themselves through misappropriating the nation's scarce resources and
in the long run destroying state owned enterprises at the detriment of the
poor.
According to him, " This is why a lot of money is sunk into projects
that are never completed. Local examples include the Ho Sports Stadium and
the Ho- Kpedze road, into which billions of cedis have been sunk over the
past thirty years". Togbe Afede XIV questioned why Sub-Saharan Africa still
suffers relative stagnation while the rest of the world such as India, China
and Malaysia are fast moving forward in development.
He therefore called for a change in the national attitude to education
through an understanding of its purpose of attaining knowledge and
intellectual development, and eliminate the notion that education merely
provides the individual with a number of certificates.
