Original_Search_Title: Museveni Calls Gen. Bashir Over Kony
Headline: Uganda: Museveni Calls Gen. Bashir Over Kony
Body_Text:
Jinja — President Yoweri Museveni has telephoned Field Marshal Omar Bashir, the Sudanese President asking him to allow the UPDF extend its operations against the Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) rebels north of Juba.
"Last Friday I spoke to President Bashir that we want to operate north of Juba Road. Gen. Bashir said that the Sudan army in Juba can operate against Kony. But I told him that we have experience in tracing him" Museveni said yesterday.
The President was speaking at the graduation of 208 army officers, 50 of them from the LRA. The graduates also included ex-fighters from the Allied Democratic Forces, Uganda National Rescue Front (I and II) and West Nile Bank Front, all former rebels that have fought Museveni's government.
The President flanked by the Army Commander, Lt. Gen. Aronda Nyakairima, commissioned the ex-rebels to a general rank of a Second Lieutenant (2nd Lt). Also passed out after the eight months Basic Officers' Course (OBC) were UPDF officers, who had been promoted without commensurate training. Former LRA spokesman, Sam Kolo, Kenneth Banya and Onen Kamdulu were present and were introduced as 'senior commanders' attracting applause.
Museveni said that Kony has now fled north of Juba-Torit Road, well beyond the area which Sudan had allowed Uganda to pursue the rebels in the vast Southern Sudan jungles.
"As I speak today, Kony has been thoroughly defeated and his group in Uganda killed," the President declared before adding that the war is coming to an end and the extension of UPDF operations will wipe out the LRA. The President told the former LRA commanders now integrated into the UPDF ranks that whereas as rebels their logistics came from "stealing cassava", in the army their provisions come from stores.
"You have been fighting but you did not have the expertise. You are like that the unprofessional cook who cooks malakwang (an Acholi delicacy), but now we said come here and become better cooks, if you have been quack doctors, we have trained you and made you professional doctors," Museveni told the ex-rebels.
On the fight against the killings in Kampala and other areas, the President said he had directed security agencies to keep guns in the armouries instead of their houses except in the north and northeastern Uganda where there is insurgency.
Museveni said the former armies under Idi Amin failed to contain a Tanzania-led invasion by Ugandan exiles because of lack of training. He said, the soldiers used tanks as accommodation for their mistresses.

Original_Search_Title: In Spite of Cholera, Katanga Residents Defy Order to Suspend Sale of Food
Headline: Uganda: In Spite of Cholera, Katanga Residents Defy Order to Suspend Sale of Food
Body_Text:
Nairobi —  None of the over 1,000 families in Katanga has a toilet; some draw
water from a well next to a burst sewer, writes Frank Sserwaniko
KIMWANYI ZONE IN KATANGA is a triangular low-lying area measuring about
500 square metres. It is the dwelling place for some 7,000 people, some of
them foreign students at Makerere University.
Although it is located right in the city, adjacent to three major
national establishments - the Public Service Ministry headquarters to the
south, Mulago Hospital to the east and Makerere University to the north,
Katanga has a serious public health problem and is reputed to be one of the
filthiest parts of Kampala.
Cholera hit the area two months ago, killing six people, while three
others were hospitalised. However, these are among the rare publicised
cases. Many residents conceal the presence of cholera and other sanitary
diseases for fear of prompting a long-standing eviction plan of some 105
families.
The eviction is meant to pave way for the repair of a burst sewerage
pipe. While city by-laws require that property developers leave at least
four metres between their houses and the sewer lines, this has been
ignored.
An attempt to evict the residents last year failed after the central
government intervened, accusing the opposition-led Kampala City Council
(KCC) of harassing its supporters.
Following the cholera outbreak in June, the city council and local
leaders intensified their public health campaign, urging residents to
observe basic hygiene.
However, families continue to draw water from a well adjacent to the
burst sewerage pipe. Matters are not helped when children as young as five
are sent to collect water without the supervision of adults.
This writer witnessed children ignoring the main pipe and fetching water
from the temporary reservoir where the water from the well freely mixes with
the sewage. Some of them were happy to quench their thirst with this
contaminated water as they played with it.
Said the area council chairman Hassan Wasswa, "We were supposed to close
that well, but residents have objected. They wanted us to provide an
alternative clean water pipe in the same location, which we could not do
immediately."
He said the National Water and Sewerage Corporation had extended a clean
water pipe into another location in the area, but some residents had shunned
it because of the longer distance they had to walk to reach it.
Katanga residents have also defied a KCC order to suspend the sale of
food and drinks in the area. The order was announced in mid-June by the city
director of health services Mesak Mubiru to curb further spread of
cholera.
"It is an issue of survival. Most people here get their meals from food
kiosks. They cannot afford meals in more decent restaurants," says Charles
Wasswa, a chapati maker. "While the City Council stand was to suspend the
sale of food items altogether, local leaders discussed with the people and
agreed to improve sanitation."
He said he had improved the cleanliness at his stall, adding that his
sales had not been affected by the cholera threats.
None of the over 1,000 families in Katanga has a toilet, local leaders
say. Residents use three public toilets where adults have to pay Ush100 (5
US cents) to access them. Children don't pay. Many residents find the US 5
cents prohibitive. The same amount can buy a piece of yam or cassava for
supper.
This has forced some of slum dwellers to use polythene bags to dispose
of waste, which is later thrown into the sewage channels.

Original_Search_Title: Civilization And the Penal System
Headline: Ghana: Civilization And the Penal System
Body_Text:
A news summary dated Monday, July 11, 2005, and posted on Ghana's Peace-FM radio's website reported the quite fascinating story of Belgian prison inmates who are reportedly granted a single two-hour conjugal visit from their spouses and partners every month by the system's administrators. What is fascinating about this story is not the simple fact of prisoners being afforded conjugal visits from their spouses and companions; after all, we know this to be the case vis-Ã -vis the U. S. prison system, and also that of Apartheid South Africa during the period of former president Nelson R. Mandela's incarceration on Robben Island and elsewhere around that country.
What is significantly and interestingly fascinating about this story is the fact that this writer never heard of the issue, or question, of "conjugal visits" being once discussed vis-Ã -vis the Ghanaian prison system. In sum, on the preceding theme and score, it is almost as if the Ghanaian prisoner deserved no such "human" right. Or perhaps it existed, except that I just wasn't aware of it, since any talk of prison, at least until the P/NDC era when it became a political commonplace, invariably attracted the kind of unspoken taboo that AIDS and prostitution have attracted in recent times. Still, it would be quite interesting to learn about the "conjugal visit" regime as it pertains to Ghanaian prison inmates.
Chances are that there might not exist any such system or regime at all. For generally speaking, the Ghanaian prisoner is a virtual pariah, a forgotten entity, a veritable nonentity, in fact.
Which largely explains why distinguished political prisoners like Dr. J. B. Danquah, Mr. Koi-Larbi, Mr. Joe Appiah and others were once housed in the Condemned Cell Block (or Death-Row) at Ghana's notorious Nsawam Medium-Security Prison under the Nkrumah regime. And also that President Nkrumah reportedly fumed when he learned, from Inspector-General of Police Mr. J. W. K. Harlley, that Dr. Danquah, as well as other political prisoners, was being fed standard dietary fare, pretty much gives us some useful insight into the Ghanaian mainstream perception of prison inmates and prison culture in general. And here, also, it is rather tragic to recall that President Nkrumah, a self-proclaimed "Prison Graduate," under the British colonial regime, did not appear to have learned, or taken to heart, any meaningful lessons, whatsoever, regarding the deplorable prison conditions experienced by erstwhile Gold Coast radical politicians under the British colonial administration. If anything at all, prison life and culture appear to have fascinated the pioneering premier of post-colonial Ghana to the pathological extent of reportedly prompting the former political prisoner to mete out the harshest forms of treatment - including the institutionalization of protein-deficient menus - to politicians and statesmen incarcerated at his behest.
For instance, in the case of Dr. Danquah, President Nkrumah ordered that the former be fed on a daily diet of salted water and garri - farinated cassava - according to National Liberation Council member and former IGP, Mr. J. W. K. Harlley (see Enquiry into the Detention and Death of Dr. Danquah at Nsawam Prison. Accra-Tema: Ghana Information Service, 1967).
NOVELTY
Indeed, it may have been the widely accepted short-shrift treatment accorded Ghanaian prisoners, generally speaking, which motivated the operators and proprietors of Peace-FM radio to post the story regarding the niggardly allowance of "conjugal visits" to Belgian prisoners on the station's website. For, needless to say, in Ghana, as can be readily fathomed, the idea of affording prisoners "conjugal visits" from their spouses reeks of the outright defeatist and preposterous. In sum, any talk of "conjugal visits" for prison inmates, on the part of the average Ghanaian, sounds like a bad joke and may well verge on outright lunacy. After all, what is the primary purpose of incarceration but to deprive the subject or prison inmate of the normative rights and privileges of non-convicts, of which the right to consensual coition or sexual intercourse with a recognized partner is part and parcel.
Consequently, the heading, or slug, that the Peace-FM operators gave their posted article on "conjugal visits" in Belgium could only be deemed in terms of the sarcastic. Emblazoned in bold print was the caption: "Extra Sex Time For Convicts." Initially, this writer's attention was drawn to the aforementioned story because he thought, perhaps, it was about convicts being sentenced to have marathon sexual bouts in prison - which would have been quite "newsy," for the very fact of its novelty, as he routinely teaches his journalism students. But the brief, two-paragraph story was quite fascinating in other ways. In sum, it dealt with the study of a Belgian Catholic University student on that country's prison system, which concluded that the monthly two-hour "conjugal visits" afforded prison inmates was grossly inadequate, unromantic and outright inhumane, which also gives a fascinating dimension to the theme of the prisoner and human rights.
And here, also, it is significant to observe that the study did not make any distinction between political and non-political prisoners. In other words, the right of a convicted murderer to "conjugal visits" is envisaged as being equal to that of any other prison inmate. And the latter drift is no strange tune to Western prison culture. Indeed, a little over a decade ago, a convicted attempted murderess, Amy Fisher, right here in New York State, was overheard discussing "conjugal visits" with a young man whom the teenage convict believed to be genuinely in love with her. It shortly turned out that the entire episode was a trap set up by the alleged suitor, or boyfriend, for purposes other than pure romance and intimacy, if memory serves this writer accurately. But what is even more fascinating is the fact that Belgium's Justice Minister, Laurette Onkelinx, has requested a copy of that country's Catholic University student's study decrying the woefully inadequate "conjugal visits" afforded prison inmates. The student in question, Ina van Havere, concluded in her study that the niggardly brief "conjugal visits" afforded Belgian prison inmates have the deleterious impact of straining relationships; in other words, such brief sexual encounters allowable to prison inmates and their non-inmate spouses often leave either couple emotionally dissatisfied with the other.
The implication here, it goes without saying, is that rather than cementing conjugal relationships in times of crises, the brief "conjugal visits" actually serve to sever relationships by, quite predictably, forcing the non-inmate spouse, or partner, to seek more emotionally fulfilling intimacy elsewhere.
HUMANITY
The next most logical question becomes: But why should anybody bother whether an inmate's relationship with a partner on the outside is strained or cemented by such brief "conjugal visits"? And the logical answer appears to be that in many a civilized society, the mere fact of one's incarceration, for whatever number of reasons, fundamentally does not detract from one's immutable humanity as well as the need to treat the societally sanctioned as such, a bona fide human being with the right to the visceral expression of one's humanity.
Another dimension of the story and one that is clearly muted, but may yet be its most significant dimension, is the fact that both the student who conducted the study, Ms. Ina van Havere, and the Belgian Justice Minister, Laurette Onkelinx, are women. And in a heavily patriarchal global community, it cannot be gainsaid that women often bring an added and, perhaps, a more humane dimension to the manner and tenor in which proscribed elements of society are perceived. And this is not to be all together unexpected; for having hitherto been unjustifiably proscribed from many avenues of societal endeavors, women, generally and relatively speaking, tend to be more sensitive to questions of injustice than men. Which is why on the issue of romance, Ms. Ina van Havere cast matters in such inimitably deft and delicate terms: "The time constraints [imposed by the niggardly brief conjugal visits] mean [that] couples are forced into bed almost immediately [upon a spouse's visit to a prison inmate, thus] leaving [the couple] no time to talk [and pet]."
And, of course, who said Ghana and Ghanaians could not take a cue from this more humane and civilized treatment of our prison inmates? Even the most criminal elements among us?
*Kwame Okoampa-Ahoofe, Jr., Ph.D., teaches English and Journalism at Nassau Community College of the State University of New York, Garden City. He is the author of ten volumes of poetry and prose, including Sounds of Sirens: Essays in African Politics and Culture.

Original_Search_Title: Cities Deal With Street Children
Headline: Rwanda's Street Children
Body_Text:
Gisenyi — The authorities in Rwanda are encouraging local communities to look
after an estimated 7,000 to 8,000 children who live on the street.
"We never had such a problem in the past," said Anne Gahongayire,
secretary-general at the ministry of gender and family promotion in
Kigali.
"But because of the realities in the family after the war and the
genocide, we have lots of children who have taken to the streets because
their families have... become so fragile.
"You don't have anyone to blame right now."
The street children are in part the legacy of the 1994 genocide in which
some 800,000 Tutsis and moderate Hutus were massacred. But young people are
often made homeless by simple economic pressures, and the tradition in areas
such as Gisenyi and Ruhengeri where men can take several wives, leading to
domestic conflict.
Rwandan politicians continue to develop new ways of helping them, but
the message is clear -local communities, which have the closest ties with
the children, must take on greater responsibility for their well-being.
The principle is similar to the way in which "Gacaca" or community-based
courts, rather than the state judicial system, are being employed to try
those accused of taking part in the genocide, Gahongayire says. "We have to
equip the community," she said. "They know better. They can be trusted
better."
But the non-government organisation Point d'Ãcoute, which has worked
with street kids in Gisenyi for the past seven years - providing
counselling, food and blankets - says many in the area are shunning their
children.
Aloys Kaberuka, coordinator of Point d'Ãcoute, says Gisenye has 65 to 70
street kids aged 12 to 17, the majority of whom have parents who are simply
unable to care for them.
"It's very complex," said Kaberuka. "I have some cases of children
unable to find a resolution. It is possible they'll turn to crime to support
themselves because they're in a bad situation."
In an effort to earn money, children do odd jobs such as carrying loads
or washing cars.
Claudine Muhamawenimana, a petite, unsmiling 18-year-old with short
black hair, started out as one of these street children, but now rents a
room in a mud-brick house.
Wearing a grey Adidas T-shirt, she carries her one-month-old baby Isame
in a makeshift sack on her back in traditional African style. The child is
sick.
Asked to tell how she came to be here, Claudine says, "It's a long
story."
After her father died and her mother remarried, her stepfather refused
to accept her.
Working the streets as a prostitute to make ends meet, she met a
20-year-old homeless man who bought her food, clothing and soap. But under
the circumstances, she was still forced to continue selling her body. When
she became pregnant, the man deserted her.
Point d'Ãcoute has been instrumental in helping her. But there is only
so much financial aid it can provide.
"I have no means to pay the rent, to feed the baby," she said, sitting
on a neighbour's veranda. She wishes she had enough money to start a
business selling cassava, bread and bananas.
The street children are by no means the only headache facing the
government.  It is currently trying to reunite as many as possible of 3,600
children now living in 25 orphanages across the country with their
families.
And there are many children, particularly girls, who have been forced to
assume control of households after parents and other adults lost their lives
to Hutu killers during the genocide, or subsequently to AIDS. UNICEF
estimates that Rwanda has some 100,000 families where the de facto adult is
aged 10 to 18.
The United Nations agency has responded to difficulties in relying on
social workers by offering these young heads of households a mentoring
programme in which community members take them under their wing.
Rwandan politicians are working with international donors to establish a
fund of nearly 11 million US dollars for organisations working with
children. That money should be available by September.

Original_Search_Title: Shell Declares N2trn Income
Headline: Nigeria: Shell Declares N2trn Income
Body_Text:
Lagos — SHELL Petroleum Development Company (SPDC) has declared a net income of about $14.6 billion (about N2 trillion) from an output of 365 million barrels of crude in the 2004 business year.
It also declared payment of $3.8 billion (about N509.2 billion) in taxes and royalties to the Federal Government during the period.
Shell, with over 15 billion barrels of crude oil and condensate reserves in Nigeria, produces nearly half of the country's estimated 2.4 million barrels per day for export.
The company operates the nation's biggest joint venture (JV) and production sharing agreements in the upstream oil and gas sector which sustains the economy.
In Shell's 2004 People and Environment Annual Report, presented to the media by Corporate External Affairs Director, Rev. Precious Omuku, weekend, the company's production chart indicated average daily production of one million barrels for the year.
The crude export graph also presented in the report, indicated an average price of $40 per barrel ($40/bbl) for the year.
Based on a 2000 Memorandum of Understanding (MoU), the company's petroleum profit tax to the Federal Government rose to 83 per cent from $1.2 billion (N160.8 billion) in 2003 to $2.2 billion (N294.8 billion) in 2004.
Royalty to the country also rose 50 per cent from $608 million (N81.5 billion) in 2003 to $904 million (N121.14 billion) in 2004.
The company declared statutory fund contribution to the Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC) at $69 million (N9.3 billion) from the JV account, out of which $21 million (N2.82 billion) came from Shell.
Besides taxes and royalties, Shell stated that it also made significant payments to state and federal governments as employees' income tax and to the Education Tax Fund (ETF).
On local content policy compliance, Rev. Omuku said 95 per cent of the company's 5000 direct employees were Nigerians, besides 7000 contract staff and 20,000 contractors' staff.
He added that Shell awarded $727 million (N97.42 billion) worth of contracts to local companies, 20 per cent of which came from the Niger Delta.
Describing 2004 as good in the company's performance and operational records Rev. Omuku said trends in community incidents substantially went down in the year resulting in significant reduction in deterred production.
This, he attributed to the company's new sustainable community development model which engages stakeholders' collaboration in tackling specific needs of people.
He said the company, through the model, invested over $50 million (N6.7 billion) in cassava production and processing, roll-back malaria, HIV/AIDS abatement and mitigation, and rural telecommunication.
He, however, listed the challenges to include crude theft, spills resulting from vandalisation, and community resistance to personnel access to facilities.

Original_Search_Title: The Caretaker of Hot Springs
Headline: Rwanda: The Caretaker of Hot Springs
Body_Text:
Kigali — With its palm beaches, nice hotels and lakes, Gisenyi has many tourist attractions among them the famous hot springs. Like his clients, Martin Bashakirahe, 65, believes the hot springs cure fatigue and other sorts of ailments. Bashakirahe is keen on his job as a caretaker of the hot springs.
Bashakirahe works with Virunga wildlife clubs of Rwanda. For two years, the hot springs have been the source of his income. Bashakirahe is in charge of security at the hot springs. He receives forty to eighty visitors per day.
Located near BRALIRWA, the hot springs attract many a visitor to Gisenyi. "My existence is attributed to these hot springs," says Bashakirahe. "I cannot go hungry with all these people that frequent this place." His clients include both local and foreign tourists, students and many others.
People neighbouring the hot springs and Bashakirahe himself believe that the hot water in the springs cure fatigue and ailments like flue , bruises and many others.
"I strongly believe in the healing power of this water but many people do not know its importance," says one of the people neighbouring the springs. "Some people bring food stuffs like sweet potatoes, cassava; bananas, eggs, which they drop into the hot water and in a few minutes it is ready for eating.
Bashakirahe at least earns Frw 500 and 1000 a day. He says the wildlife club does not pay him and he only depends on what some visitors to the springs give him. He is also occupied with cleaning the place such that visitors do not find it dirty as it is his source of income, regardless of whatever little he earns.
However, as caretaker of the hot springs, Bashakirahe faces some challenges in his work among them is the presence neighbouring children especially street children who bring a lot of food stuffs to cook or boil and leave then the place very dirty. They do not mind its being a tourist site.
The problem of not being paid by the Virunga wildlife clubs also worries him, language barrier is another problem. He cannot freely communicate with foreign tourists. He does not like it when people come to visit the hot springs and walk away without giving him any coin.
Jealousy and envy from his neighbours in Nyamyumba district is another challenge. Wearing a wildlife identity card has created many enemies than friends, Bashakirahe's neighbours envy him because the card gives him the right to collect and get money from different people that visit the hot springs. He has managed to make few friends who help him chase away people who disguise themselves as caretakers of the hot springs and demand money from visitors.
On a good day, if he gets more money he shares it with his friends. Residents wish and propose that the place should be developed to become an active tourists' destination.

Original_Search_Title: Ambassador Calls for Action On West African Economic Integration
Headline: West Africa: Ambassador Calls for Action On West African Economic Integration
Body_Text:
Abuja — THE Minister of Commerce, Amb. Idris Waziri has called for
non-politicisation of trade liberalisation and economic integration among
countries of the West African sub-region and urged member nations to ensure
concrete actions on all agreements and protocols to that effect.
Speaking at the Regional Trade Forum organised by the ECOBANK Group and
the Export Group of the Manufacturers Association of Nigeria (MAN) in Abuja,
yesterday, he noted with regret that economic integration and trade
liberalisation had suffered much setback in the sub-region due to undue
politics among its members.
"We are wallowing in abject poverty, diseases and hunger for which there
is no need for anyone of them. We have had enough talking. We need actions
now. Our various governments have signed series of agreements and protocols
on trade liberalisation and economic integration but we have seen little of
this due to unnecessary politicising of the issues.
"In Africa, we emphasise too much on politics instead of economy and how
we can better the lives of our people. We are faced with very weak
infrastructure. That should engage our minds. We should address that, we
should address development agenda so we can trade internationally.
"As long as we emphasise too much on politics, we can't have economic
integration and free trade which have become the hallmark of other regions
of the world and we should remember that no region will wait for us. We have
to be honest, hardworking and patriotic." he said.
Amb. Waziri warned that with Africa accounting for a mere 3 per cent of
the world trade, the dream for sustainable economic growth would ever remain
a mirage without extra efforts at promoting intra sub-regional trade.
Earlier, the MD of ECOBANK Nigeria, Mrs. Funke Osibodu, urged federal
government to open its borders to Ghanaian products, particularly, textiles,
cassava starch, cutlasses and plastics, among others.

Original_Search_Title: Popular Sunyani Chop-Bar Under VAT Probe
Headline: Ghana: Popular Sunyani Chop-Bar Under VAT Probe
Body_Text:
Sunyani — The deputy Minister of Finance and Economic Planning, Mr. Agyemang Manu, has directed the Brong Ahafo Regional VAT Service to monitor the daily sales of Good Mark Foods, a popular chop bar at Sunyani, for at least one or two months, to establish whether she actually qualifies to register for the Value Added Tax (VAT).
The deputy minister explained to the Proprietor of the bar, Madam Josephine Brobbey, that the VAT law is not new in the system, and there was a lot of education being carried out.
According to Mr. Agyemang Manu, the government had not intended to kill businesses in the country, but rather create an enabling environment for her citizens to invest.
He debunked the suggestion of Madam Brobbey that the VAT service wanted to collapse her business, stressing, "VAT is not meant to collapse businesses."
The deputy minister who was in Sunyani to persuade the Proprietor of the bar to reach a compromise with the VAT Service, urged her to register with the service to enable them monitor her sales effectively.
About a couple of months ago, the VAT service had engaged in a legal battle with Good Mark Foods on her failure to register and pay VAT.
This legal battle took a different turn when her colleagues in the industry threatened to embark on a nationwide demonstration if the VAT Service did not withdraw the case from the court.
Subsequently, the VAT had to withdraw the case, while negotiations with Madam Brobbey to get her into the VAT net continue.
The acting Regional VAT Officer for Brong Ahafo, Samuel Sackey, explaining who qualifies to register and collect VAT, disclosed that ACT 546, section 5 that talks of threshold, does not apply to service providers.
According to him, the threshold applies to only those in the retailing business. Mr. Sackey said there were lots that a business entity could benefit for registering with VAT and therefore urged Madam Brobbey to take that advantage.
As to whether Good Mark Foods would be exempted or included in VAT, the Brong Ahafo Office is yet to take directives from the Commissioner of VAT. On her part, Madam Brobbey told the minister that her bar faced a lot of problems, such as payment to her cassava suppliers and other items that went into making the business, and that sometimes, rains destroyed her business, because the place was an open one.

Original_Search_Title: Caution on $60 Per Barrel Oil
Headline: Nigeria: Caution on $60 Per Barrel Oil
Body_Text:
Once again, the international price of crude oil has attained a record
height from which it is expected to recede very little. For Nigeria and
Nigerians, it's both good and bad news as we have been through similar
situation in 1973. That was when the Organisation of Petroleum Exporting
Countries, OPEC, first used its power as a cartel to raise crude oil prices
from about $3 per barrel to $40. At that time, the unprecedented price
dictated, not by the market but by fiat, was followed by negative
predictions about its impact on the global economy on the one hand and by
acclamation in the oil producing countries on the other as their economies
presumably stood to benefit.
In the end, neither the worst scenario nor the most optimistic
prediction for OPEC countries occurred. The world economy was in recession
for several years but momentum was restored as several of the OPEC
countries, as net-importers of finished goods, recycled virtually all the
increased revenue from oil. Nigeria's case was even worse. In addition to
legitimate import, most of the oil windfall returned to the western
countries through pervasive corruption without any savings from the oil wind
fall.
A similar thing occurred during the Gulf war. In recent times when
prices go up at the international oil market, it is quickly followed by the
following; increase in the pump price of petroleum products in the domestic
market, certain galloping and imported inflation, reduced purchasing power,
declining aggregate consumption, lower manufacturing capacity utilization,
increased unemployment and the demand for wage increases. The current trend
of crude oil prices may inevitably produce similar consequences unless the
government is willing to enthrone fiscal discipline and ensure that part of
the money earned is saved as the government has done in recent time.
The government must also ensure the improvement in local refining
capacity through the privatisation of existing refineries and licensing of
new ones.
More worrisome is the fact that the monetisation of earnings from and
the clamour for the sharing of excess crude earnings threaten to do havoc to
the National Economic Empowerment and Development Strategies, NEEDS, through
inflationary pressure which as the new official economic mantra, projected
single digit inflation for year 2005.
Before the latest crude oil prices, inflation rate in the first half of
this year hover around 13.5% and the combination of cassava export and bank
consolidation, in addition to the Central Bank's effort at mopping up excess
liquidity from the banks have already signaled that single digit inflation
would be unattainable.
With less than six months to go in the year, there is an urgent need for
fiscal discipline on the part of government in order to keep inflation at
manageable level and maintain the macro economic stability achieved in
2004.

Original_Search_Title: C'ttee to Monitor MDGs Inaugurated
Headline: Nigeria: C'ttee to Monitor MDGs Inaugurated
Body_Text:
Abuja — President Olusegun Obasanjo on 29 June in Abuja inaugurated the
Assessment and Monitoring Committee on the Millennium Development Goals
(MDGs).
The President reiterated the guiding philosophy behind the MDGs as one
devoted to 'Spare no efforts to free our fellow men, women and children from
the abject and dehumanising conditions of extreme poverty to which more than
one billion of them are currently subjected'
Tracing the origin of the MDGs to the Millennium summit held in 2000 in
New York where 180 world leaders met and adopted the millennium declaration
adding that membership of the committee underscores government's unwavering
commitment and faithfulness to the spirit and letter of that
Declaration.
He described the MDGs as a set of minimum deliverable, interdependent,
time bound and quantifiable development goals that will ensure a break in
poverty cycle and chart a path to sustainable socio-economic
development.
He also said these constitute the entry point to focus the nations
limited resources strategically that will stress the urgency in the need to
end poverty and create enabling environment for investment in sustainable
development and economic growth.
The President explained that government resolve has consistently been
demonstrated through policies and programmes such as, Universal Basic
Education, UBE, Water For Life For All, Presidential Initiative on Cassava
and Small and Medium Enterprise Development Agency of Nigeria. The National
Poverty Eradication Programme (NAPEP) was established to coordinate and
monitor poverty eradication efforts.
According to him, the home grown National Economic Empowerment and
Development Strategy (NEEDS) which is replicated at states and local
government levels, is a is a product of the concerted and sustained efforts
at reforming and repositioning of the nations economy which is designed to
enhance stability growth development, transparency, accountability and
Democracy with key elements as,
- Poverty reduction
- Wealth creation
- Employment generation, and
Value reorientation.
He urged the committee to approach the onerous responsibility with the
highest possible sense of duty and commitment to the nation.

Original_Search_Title: The Game is Up for Nebbi Poachers
Headline: Uganda: The Game is Up for Nebbi Poachers
Body_Text:
Kampala — When Leba Oketcha worked with the Uganda Electricity Board in the 1980s as a meter reader, nobody looked at him as a potential poacher.
But, back home in Kitolo village, Atyak parish, Pakwach sub-county in Nebbi district, he had left an entrenched poaching culture and when he retired after eight years of service, he got back to live out what he was taught in his early age-poaching.
Last Sunday, I made a trip to Oketcha's village that shares boundary with Murchison Falls Game Park. there can never be a better poaching village than that.
Uganda Wildlife Authority (UWA) officials were to meet a group of over 100 poachers, who wanted to voluntarily denounce their lifetime activity and were willing to hand over their tools.
The decision had not come easily as almost every male in the sub-county is or was a poacher armed with a spear he probably inherited from the great, great grandfathers. Nobody could remember when poaching started in this area because the animals have always been in their neighbourhood. They seemed to be more familiar with the taste of game meat than goat's meat or beef.
We sat under a huge tree on a few reserved wooden chairs as though we were the supreme powers in the area while others sat on mats laid down on the dusty compound.
Pakwach is a hot place but our venue was just metres away from the Nile so the cool breeze reached us easily.
Participants trickled in one after another each holding buffalo spears, wire snares, hippo spears and knives. They placed them in one area.
But in unique communities like these strange things are bound to happen and this one came in timely for me to witness.
Junga Ocan plucked his adungu to entertain us with traditional hunting tunes. Before we could enjoy, the sound provoked spirits in 61-year-old Simea Nono, a practising witchdoctor, who usually counsels the poaching group on the safety of their trip.
He shot up from his position on the bench with extra ordinary strength while pointing at the tune player and asked for water. He was handed a cupful and the music was stopped to avoid awakening more spirits. Nono moved aside facing the park side, filled his mouth and flashed it out in three angles before cooling down.
I was the only terrified person as others joked about the toughness of the spirits. He reportedly got them from an elephant he killed in the park during one of his poaching trips.
When the meeting started only the grey-haired elders, were asked to introduce themselves by giving a brief account of their poaching experiences, some going as far back as 1940s.
"When I left work in 1980, I became a fishmonger but did not take long before I joined my poaching colleagues," Oketcha started his narration.
"I inherited two spears from my father, which I have killed 10 buffalos, five warthogs, five hippos, three waterbucks and trapped a giraffe."
He was a popular transporter among the poachers because he owned a canoe that they used to cross the Nile into the park.
Poachers do not lose their hunting tools. Oketcha said he lost one of his spears to colleagues whom he transported across to the park but was arrested and the second one was the one he handed over that day to the UWA officials.
He confessed that he was the one who had transported the poachers who had been arrested two weeks back.
Oketcha said he had made up his mind to give up poaching because he was not benefiting much.
"I have come to realise that the park is more advantageous to us. Even the share I always got from the people I transported was small," he said.
However, the story of Kisaw Katyere, the 75-year-old man stunned the audience. He started poaching in 1956 and by last Sunday, he had killed 170 buffalos, 120 hippos, three lions, six elephants, 72 antelopes and 70 kobs with only a spear.
He said he had a traditional herb inserted in his skin that helped him kill all those animals. Each time he killed an animal, he dedicated some portion of the meat to his ancestral powers to keep him going. He claimed the herb blindfolded the game rangers from seeing him. But he kept the spirits at a cost.
"I pay back the diviner by giving him a piece of meat that I return with and if I spend three days without poaching I grow boils around my body," he confessed.
He said traditional doctors had requested for two goats and sh50,000 to bind the charms and set him free.
"Now that I can never to go back I do not know where to get the money for my treatment," he lamented.
Oloya Jagaya, 60, who was nicknamed 'Raleigh' (the ancient strong bicycle) by the poaching fraternity was a fast runner. Jagaya's duty was a daring one.
"My work was to provoke buffalos so that they chase me and I lead them into a trap or a frontline of armed spear-wielding poachers," Jagaya said. He says this was his task since 1961 and no buffalo had ever hurt him.
Ubediyo Onyeng, 58, said his spear was nicknamed Ameyowu, (Alur for I have snatched it from you) because his spear was light and always landed on the animals first.
Of course this meant he would have a whole thigh of meat to carry home. He also earned himself the heart, tongue, liver and tail- symbols of bravery.
Ameyowu spear was responsible for the death of 64 buffalos, two kobs, one giraffe, two rhinos, two hartebeests, 12 hippos and three warthogs. It was handed over to UWA in a proclamation never to go poaching again.
Out of the 103 poachers who attended the meeting some of them were once or twice arrested, others detained and released after months but still did not give up poaching.
For many years the group operated under a well-established system with a director, a planner, a traditional diviner who consulted spirits on safety issues before flagging off the poaching entourage. His decision was final; they would not go on a trip without his clearance.
The coordinator, Musa Okende, said their poaching trips usually took two to three days. Willing participants informed their wives who then prepared roasted cassava, cassava flour, salt and fish for the expedition.
They set off from the landing site into the park by canoe at 8:00pm. The poachers travelled in the night to avoid the game rangers. They would then camp and wait for the sun to rise.
Okende said poaching is done in broad daylight. Before an animal is attacked each person's opinion is sought. Action is taken on the majority's decision.
Once killed, the animal is partitioned immediately. The one whose spear landed first gets the biggest share, a whole thigh of meat, the heart, tongue, liver and the tail while the second person to spear the animal gets the second thigh.
Others share the rest of the animal but the neck is always reserved for the canoe rider. After a successful hunt they would wait for 8:00pm to get back home.
Almost all ranges of animals are edible among the poaching community, including lions whose meat is eaten and the skin sold mostly to traditional and cultural institutions. The women have learnt to smoke the meat dry such that it can be kept for as long as two months.
So when the park authorities realised that the number of animals especially elephants being trapped, was increasing, they agreed on an intensive programme to sensitise the communities bordering the park on the benefits of conservation.
The senior warden, Murchison falls conservation area, Stonewall Kato, and his colleague the Murchison falls head ranger in charge community conservation, Innocent Wakonga, embarked on the programme without special funding.
For the last one year Kato and Wakonga had been trying to meet the communities with no breakthrough since the poaching community had tagged them "enemies". Incidentally, the poachers knew them by face.
The turning point began last year when Kato commissioned Paroketo Secondary School that was constructed with funds from the park.
Kato said two prominent poachers came to him and expressed their desire to denounce poaching after seeing the benefits the park was bringing to their community.
Apart from Puyo, the park also provided funds that constructed Panyimur Secondary School and two community centres in the neighbouring parishes.
Kato said over the last three years the park released over sh110m in cash to communities in parishes in Nebbi District that border the park.
Kato and Wakonga could not afford hiding their joy the day 103 poachers surrendered as their hard-fought campaign yielded its first biggest achievement.
"Today is a very important day. It is one of its kind in the history of conservation where a group of 103 poachers come out voluntarily to denounce their activity. It is a very big achievement for this conservation area that people are coming to realise the importance of conservation." Kato said.

Original_Search_Title: Local Brew Raises Eyebrows
Headline: Rwanda: Local Brew Raises Eyebrows
Body_Text:
Kigali — A locally distilled alcoholic beverage commonly known as 'Baritereta' has raised furore among residents of the Nyabugogo suburb of Kigali city.
According to the Kimisagara Sector Coordinator, Edro Albert, the brew is manufactured from different chemicals that make it highly potent akin to drugs.
He revealed that the beverage was an assortment of sugar, tobacco midribs and other chemicals, whose colour is easily altered.
Reacting to the censure, the producers of the brew, FranÃ§ois Musonera and Alfred Ndahayo revealed that their alcoholic drink is made from banana juice and millet whose shelf life is only a day.
Police spokesman, Inspector Theos Badege stated that once nabbed, such people would be punished according to the country's laws. He said that a person selling a brew that has a bad effect on consumers whether short or long term would be heavily reprimanded.
Badege cited similar cases of traders in Kanombe District who manufacture odd food products to obtain quick money while compromising people's health.
He noted that some suspects in the district were recently apprehended while selling a concoction of cassava powder and powder milk packed in a tin as food formula for babies.
He urged people to be careful of duplicated products and advised them to work in hand in hand with the police.
The residents concerns come hot on heels of recent reports of a killer brew that killed over 52 people in the Kenyan eastern town of Machakos.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo Inaugurates Millennium Devt Goals C'ttee
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo Inaugurates Millennium Devt Goals C'ttee
Body_Text:
Abuja — President Olusegun Obasanjo yesterday inaugurated the Assessment and Monitoring Committee on the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs) in Abuja, saying Nigeria is in the final stages of completing its 2005 report.
The MDGs is an eight-point agenda set by the United Nations (UN) as the basis for development especially in the developing countries of the South by 2015.
He said since Nigeria has achieved the integration of the MDGs into National Economic Empowerment Development Strategy (NEEDS) programme, the countryÃ¢??s development partners should put into effect the global compact of achieving credible partnerships for good governance, trade, debt relief and increased aid, which will go a long way in helping us achieve MDGs.
Ã¢??Our tenacious pursuit of debt relief, which is beginning to look promising is based on the indisputable fact that will impact very directly on the ability or otherwise of most developing economies to achieve the MDGs, considering that the brunt of debt servicing reduces the amount of resources available for poverty reduction programmes,Ã¢?? he added.
Obasanjo said Ã¢??the Federal Government has consistently demonstrated a strong resolve to frontally attack the scourge of poverty in our polity through several poverty reduction and eradication initiatives, and sustained economic empowerment policies and programmes such as the Universal Basic Education (UBE), Water for All, Presidential Initiative on Cassava and Small and Medium Enterprises Development Agency of Nigeria, SMEDAN.
ÃÂ¬The National Poverty Eradication Programme, NAPEP, was established early in the life of this administration as an effective framework for the coordination and monitoring of poverty eradication efforts across the nation.Ã¢??

Original_Search_Title: Killer Meal: Health Workers Storm Markets
Headline: Nigeria: Killer Meal: Health Workers Storm Markets
Body_Text:
Ilorin — Worried by the rate at which amala meals, a local staple food prepared from either yam or cassava flakes have been claiming lives in Kwara State, government is to dispatch its health workers to some major markets to take samples of the products.
Commissioner for Health, Dr Bola Olaosebikan made the disclosure to newsmen in Ilorin andhinted that government would send part of the samples to NAFDAC and other relevant agencies for analysis.
The pronouncement of Olaosebikan followed the death of three persons, the district head of Alapa in Asa Local government area of the state, his wife and one of his traditional chiefs who reportedly died last week Monday after eating the meal. It is the third time this year the food has claimed the lives of its consumers.
The death which has thrown the community into confusion, brought the number of casualty from food poisoning in the state to 13 this year alone.
The deceased, whose names were given as Alhaji Shuaib Ajape Oniso, District Head of Alapa, and his wife, Alhaja Belawu and Alhaji Saidu Akanji, the Mogaji of Alapa reportedly died few hours after eating the meal which was prepared by Belawu, Oniso's third wife.
Belawu was said to have first raised alarm over an unusual stomachache after the meal and while the family was making frantic efforts to rush her to a medical hospital in Ilorin, the state capital, her husband equally began to make a similar complaint.
She reportedly died at the hospital located along Ipata Oloje area of the metropolis while her husband gave up the ghost some hours later at the University of Ilorin Teaching Hospital, UITH.
Efforts to save the life of the Magaji, who was reportedly admitted at another hospital not far from the community was equally fruitless as he also died at about 8pm on Monday.
The district head was aged 90 while his magaji was 75 years as at the time of the incident. All the deceased have been buried according to Islamic rites while members of the magaji's family said they were not asking for any investigations because of the way the incident happened.
A worried council chairman, Alhaji Ibrahim Sheu, had immediately dispatched a team of medical experts to the area and equally released some money for the treatment of those who survived the ugly incident.

Original_Search_Title: NSCIA Scribe Backs S-South On 25 Percent Derivation
Headline: Nigeria: NSCIA Scribe Backs S-South On 25 Percent Derivation
Body_Text:
Asaba — SECRETARY-GENERAL of the Nigerian Supreme Council For Islamic Affairs, Dr. Lateef Adegbite, yesterday, in Asaba backed calls by the South-South delegates to the national Political Reforms Conference for an increase in derivation from 13 percent to 25 per cent.
He spoke to reporters at the official opening of the 35th annual conference of the Nigerian Society of International Law, which kicked off in Asaba, yesterday.
He lamented the on-going scenario at the Confab over the issue of resource control, saying that "it is unfortunate that we have got ourselves to this situation. My own personal view has been that we should stick to the principle of derivation. This was what we inherited at independence but unfortunately, it was changed later. I will be happier if we return to it.
"But what we mean by resource control as you know varies from viewpoint to viewpoint. If resource control means total conduct, management and actual control of resources by states, I will not be part of that because when we are in a federation, we must regard everything that we have as a common asset.
"But at the same time" he went on, "I don't believe that we should have a distinction between one product and another. Whatever is good for cocoa should be alright for oil, should be alright for uranium, bitumen, cassava, groundnut, cotton, rubber and all that."
"Quite honestly, we have to bring about an acceptable formula. If we cannot achieve it today, we must look forward to a time we will be able to achieve it. In other words, because we have gone away from what I thought was a fair principle -- derivation -- for so long, if we have to return to it, let us try to return to it gradually. And in this regard, I am not a supporter of 13 percent. I believe that we must have a fairer percentage conceded to the oil-producing areas. I will favour something like 20 to 25 percent which can, over time, even be reviewed from until we get ourselves back to the situation in 1963."

Original_Search_Title: Letter From Lagos
Headline: Nigeria: Letter From Lagos
Body_Text:
Freetown — Who cares about the problem of Nigeria's
ruling Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) or the potential successor to
Uncle Shege? Certainly not me! Frankly, I've almost spent the entire
part of my per diem buying Nigerian dailies since my arrival in
Lagos. And it looks like I'm returning home without been able to buy
jewelries for my fiancÃ© as gifts from Lagos.
Interestingly, most of the stories I've read from these newspapers
are either centered on the debate over who the next Nigerian
president would be or the political intricacies surrounding the
current leadership problem within Obasanjo's PDP. To the Nigerians,
the ongoing crisis in the oil-rich Niger Delta Region and rampant
reports of routine and fearful armed robberies are no strange news.
These stories form part of the daily customary activities of life in
the country. And many of them are not even worried over such issues.
I arrived in Lagos last Sunday night (June 12) to attend a Regional
Workshop on Economic and Financial Journalism organized by the West
African Institute for Financial and Economic Management (WAIFEM).
Apart from been my first visit to Nigeria, I'm the only journalist
from Sierra Leone attending this workshop.
For sure, I had little knowledge of what Nigeria looks like before my
departure. The only thing I was certain about was the fact that I'm
an African from an African country traveling to another African
country. I had earlier planned not to mention anything relating to my
experience during the course of my journey but I have found it
impossible to do so.
And this occurrence I believe is nothing strange to many people
traveling across Africa.
I was scheduled to leave Lungi International Airport at exactly 1:00
pm that day. The travel agents from whom I secured a Bellview Airline
ticket to Lagos had advised me to check at the airport as early as
possible so as to avert any inconveniences. That was exactly what I
did!
I arrived at Lungi hours before the checking time and deliberately
spent all the local currency on me buying drinks so as to kill time
whilst I await Bellview.
Amusingly, Bellview, which is thought to be the most reliable airline
flying across the sub-region, could only arrive some three hours
after the actual schedule. At some point, I became so impatient that
I was even skeptical over its arrival. But it finally came and we
surely left for Lagos. The pilot and his crew knew that they breached
our confidence and he was bold in tendering apologies on behalf of
the airline.
The apologies didn't interest me much. After all, it typifies African
businessmen. What I was interested in was my arrival in Lagos and I
could not help myself but laugh when the pilot said: "We appreciate
your patronage of Bellview, the preferred airline." His excuses for
the delay were that the rains in Lagos had led to an aircraft landing
on the beach ultimately leading to a temporal closure of the Murtala
Muhamed Airport. Yes, it was true. We spent nearly 40 minutes
hovering around the airport before we could land.
The actual story was that the pilot of Chanchangi Airways, one of the
local aircrafts in the country, had blocked the runway thereby
creating an unnecessary blockage at the airport. The next day, the
Aviation Minister was on TV announcing the suspension of two pilots
for that unfortunate incident.
At this point, I began wondering how many ministers in Freetown
could've taken such a bold decision or even resign when such
malfeasance activities occur in their spheres of control? There is
none! Whichever way, this is Lagos and don't expect things to be the
same everywhere. Everything could be found here -the good, bad, ugly,
worst, possible and impossible- depending on where your interest is.
WAIFEM is located at the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) Training
Centre, Satellite Town in Lagos. It is not an easy drive to the area.
What immediately caught my attention was the porous nature of the
road to the institute. I doubt why the Nigerians allowed such an
important road leading to no other place but the Central Bank be left
in such a dilapidated condition?
The mud and big pot holes filled with streams of water almost made me
to think that Lagos was caught in a tsunami. It is even worst than
the road to Goderich.
The driver who was sent to collect me at the airport realized how
agape I was over such a condition and was quick to say, "the Central
Bank wants to repair this road but the state government is not
allowing it to happen." But who cares about such a silly excuse? Not
me.
All I knew was that I was in Africa and these are the common
characteristics of every country in the continent. To some extent, it
reminded me of home. For sure, there is no need for me to emphasize
the decency of the apartment I was lodged. It is a sharp contrast to
the road. Ironically, I was tongue-tied to be greeted by electricity
blackout two hours after my arrival. I came to realize that Nigeria's
Electricity Power Authority (NEPA), like NPA in Freetown, is the most
useless parastatal in the country. The only difference is that NEPA
is now in oblivion and a new company, Power Holdings Ltd (PHL) is
currently tasked to perform NEPA's function. So if Nigeria, a leading
OPEC country in the continent could not provide basic and constant
electricity supply for its people, who else would? Is this the
so-called African Super Power vying for a permanent seat in the
United Nations Security Council?
For sure, Nigeria is yet to solve its own domestic problems but
personal ego has made the Nigerians to want everything stationed in
their country including Ecowas, African Union, West African Central
Bank and almost everything that has to do with African supremacy. But
under which criteria can they be rated for this? This is why Ghana
and Nigeria have perceived each other as rivalries.
Certainly, I had imagined the WAIFEM financial and economic
journalism course to be an easy ride. But I found everything to be as
rigorous and tough as ever.
In any case, I'm coping very well and whatever it is, the course
itself is aimed at upgrading the skills and knowledge of editors and
senior news producers in reporting and analyzing economic and
financial issues so as to enable them promote a greater participation
of the civil society in national economic management.
The themes covered range from various and multiple areas of modern
economic and financial affairs and they include regional economic
integration, financial crimes and money laundering, financial
intermediaries and other similar issues. Classes run from 8:00 am
right through 18:00 everyday with intermittent breaks for tea and
lunch. It is so time consuming and hectic that there is little time
to even check ones email let alone send feedbacks home. One good
thing is that there is sufficient food and you get whatever you need
except that I've been missing my usual cassava leaf.
Last night, WAIFEM's Director of Admin and Finance, Tapsiru Dankeh, a
former employee of the Bank of Sierra Leone, invited me for a dinner
at his residence. It was a nice experience with a deliciously
prepared potato leaf and rice. We had a lengthy discussion on
political developments back home and ended on President Mbkei's
recent sacking of Vice President Jacob Zuma for alleged corruption.
It was surely obvious that such decisions can hardly be taken in
Sierra Leone. Marine Resources Minister, Okere Adams, for instance,
was arrested by the Anti-Corruption Commission (ACC) for alleged
siphoning of ADB funds but he is still parading as a minister in
Kabbah's cabinet. And now parliament is mounting a probe on ACC's
alleged excessive misuse of funds. What an irony?
Surely, one interesting aspect I have enjoyed most is the numerous
discussions with various colleagues from across the sub-region. Most
of my conversation with Nigerian journalists was on the issue of
Taylor's asylum, and many of them here seem to be supportive of
Obasanjo's decision to house Taylor in Calabar. This is contrary to
what the BBC and other western media organizations have been
reporting.
Indeed, Nigerian journalists hold a grief against Taylor for
assassinating two of their colleagues in Monrovia, and the NUJ
(Nigeria Union of Journalists) is currently having a law-suit filed
against Taylor for this but no follow-up is being made in court. And
they could hardly believe that the newspaper I work for is owned by a
Nigerian.
Truly, there is no secret that Nigeria has very good and prolific
journalists most of whom are university graduates with some having
post-graduate qualifications. This is indeed a sharp contrast to
Sierra Leone where high school dropouts could just acrobatically jump
from a stream of sleep and start parading amateuristically holding a
pen as a journalist without having the basic understanding of what
actually constitutes the country. Whether we accept it or not, we're
far from getting a matured press that would stand to its task in the
country.
This is where SLAJ President, I.B. Kargbo and executive should have
directed energies than embarking on a fruitless legal exercise with a
government that frowns at everything relating to press freedom. The
politicians in our country know the weakness of the press and they
are exploiting it to their own advantage.
For the moment, however, I'm having a field day without going through
the stress and pain of reading and editing reporters' stories or
commenting on the postponed donor conference that was scheduled for
Paris, France this June to discuss President Kabbah's Poverty
Reduction Strategy Paper (PRSP). At least, I may also not be around
to see Anti-Corruption Commissioner, Valentine Collier sweating
before Speaker of Parliament, Justice Edmond Cowan to exonerate
himself from alleged excessive spending of funds. Or to hear Vice
President Solomon Berewa committing additional blasphemy in his
desperate effort to succeed Kabbah. Let alone be a spectator of that
cat and mouse game for now!
The news making rounds in the western media for now is that Ghana has
already qualified for debt-relief under current G8 conditions and I
know His Excellency, our president, would soon rush to Accra for a
lesson from Kufour on how he reached that completion point. But I
pray not to meet him there. When would African leaders boldly face
these powerful western nations and tell them: "we are not going to
pay our debts"? In few days from now, I would equally be in Accra to
discuss plans for the establishment of a Pan-African Information
Service (PAIS) in our country. Till then, this is my letter from
Lagos.
