Original_Search_Title: Kyenjojo Abducters Were Thugs Not Rebels, Says Bantariza
Headline: Uganda: Kyenjojo Abducters Were Thugs Not Rebels, Says Bantariza
Body_Text:
Fort Portal — The gunmen who abducted people in Kyenjojo district on Sunday were thugs
and not rebels, the UPDF spokesman, Lt Col. Shaban Bantariza, has said. "We
have established that they were thugs who abducted the locals as punishment
for not having money on them," Bantariza said yesterday.
Security sources in Kyenjojo said all the people who were abducted
earlier were released towards daybreak. They had been abducted by two gunmen
wielding a sub-machine gun, a panga and axes.
But the "thugs" hit again on Monday night and abducted four more people
in Kamihanda village. The names of the abducted men had not yet been
established by press time.
The LC3 chairman of the Katooke sub-county, Mr Leonard Rwamwenge, said
the men were suspected rebels. He said the men armed with a gun and pangas,
looted drugs from Kyabiranju village in Myeru parish and uprooted cassava
before heading towards Muzizi forest.
Rwamwenge, who described the situation as "alarming," said over 100
villagers had abandoned their homes and run to the trading centres for
safety for fear of more attacks. One of the returnees, Ms Joyce Mbabazi,
said the men totaling about six told them that they were attached to
renegade officer, Col Samson Mande.
The abductees said the men, who spoke broken Swahili, Rutoro, Luganda
and Runyankole, told them that come 2006, they should vote for FDC.
Bantariza refuted claims that the men were attached to the PRA, saying
they were mere "masqueraders." "Kyenjojo is too far away from the PRA
operating zones near the border with DRC. These are just armed thugs,"
Bantariza said.
FDC Executive Coordinator, Mr Sam Njuba, described the claims as made up
stories, "which were bound to increase as we head towards 2006.
The Kyenjojo District Police Commander, James Ocaya, said the situation
was under control.

Original_Search_Title: Peaceful Run-Up to Presidential Election After Shaky Start to the Campaign
Headline: Guinea Bissau: Guinea-Bissau: Peaceful Run-Up to Presidential Election After Shaky Start to the Campaign
Body_Text:
Bissau — Three main candidates are vying for power in Sunday's presidential
election which aims to return Guinea-Bissau to constitutional rule and
stability following a civil war and six years of political turmoil.
Diplomats and local political analysts are expecting a three-cornered
fight between former presidents Joao Bernardo "Nino" Vieira and Kumba Yala
and Malam Bacai Sanha, the official candidate of the ruling African Party
for the Independence of Guinea-Bissau and Cape Verde (PAIGC).
Interim President Henrique Rosa, who has helped to guide this small West
African country back to democracy following a bloodless coup in September
2003, is not standing for election.
With the incumbent stepping down, the campaign has been peaceful and
good-natured.
Early fears of military intervention after Yala and a handful of his
military supporters briefly occupied the presidential palace during the
early hours of 24 May proved unfounded.
The army, which has intervened repeatedly in politics since
Guinea-Bissau achieved independence from Portugal in 1974, has remained
quietly on the sidelines.
The capital Bissau and the other main towns have been plastered with
campaign posters and cars blaring out music and campaign messages from the
rival candidates have been racing round the streets.
The radio waves have been clogged with daily 10-minute broadcasts by all
13 presidential candidates and women have turned out in force to liven up
the campaign rallies with drumming, dancing and singing.
Johan Ven Ecke, a Belgian member of the European Parliament who is
leading a team of over 90 European Union election observers, told local
radio stations at the weekend, that he was broadly satisfied with the way
the elections had been organised and the peaceful and good-natured way in
which the various candidates and their supporters were behaving.
Vieira, an army general who seized power in a 1980 coup and ruled this
former Portuguese colony for 19 years until 1999, has been running the best
financed and highest profile election campaign.
He returned from exile in Portugal briefly in April to register as a
voter and came back again on 4 June to launch his campaign for a return to
power through the ballot box.
Now aged, 67, Vieira still has many supporters in the PAIGC, where he
served as a guerrilla commander during the bush war which led to
independence.
He also enjoys strong backing from President Lansana Conte of
neighbouring Guinea-Conakry, whose helicopter he borrowed for his first trip
home two months ago.
Vieira's supporters have erected posters throughout the country. They
have also been giving away hundreds of motorbikes and bicycles and thousands
of T-shirts to curry favour with the public.
A cargo plane flew in from Dubai last week bringing half a dozen
four-wheel drive cars and a fresh consignment of posters and T-shorts to
boost his campaign.
Although Vieira only arrived back in Guinea-Bissau two weeks before
polling day, he has attracted crowds of several thousand people throughout
the country.
The former military leader was forced out of power in 1999 by a short by
vicious civil war which divided the army.
But now, six years later, he is projecting himself as a figure of
national unity, capable of bringing stability to this cash-strapped country
of 1.3 million people, most of whom grow rice and cassava and earn small
amounts of cash from selling cashew nuts.
"I stand for peace, I stand for stability, I stand for the national
unity of all Guineans without distinction of race, colour or religion,"
Vieira told a rally of about 7,000 people in the northeastern town of Gabu
last week.
While Vieira has tried to project himself as a conciliatory figure with
broad appeal, Yala, who ruled from 2000 until his overthrow in 2003, has
been playing the ethnic and religious card to win a fresh majority.
Although he has avoided mass meetings, Yala and his supporters have been
assiduously canvassing voters door-to-door.
The former philosophy teacher is the founder and historic leader of the
Social Renovation Party (PSR), the main opposition party in parliament. This
draws its support mainly from Yala's own Balanta ethnic group, which
accounts for a third of Guinea-Bissau's population and provides the backbone
of the army.
Yala's overthrow two years ago was widely greeted with sighs of relief
since his government had been plagued by administrative and political chaos
and had left civil servants, teachers, doctors and nurses unpaid for
months.
Now he is trying to make a political comeback by courting selected
powerful sections of the community, rather than Guinea-Bissau's 538,000
registered voters as a whole./b>
Reaching out beyond his Balanta power-base, Yala has appealed for
support from Muslims from other ethnic communities, promising that he will
help more local pilgrims undertake the haj to Mecca.
He has also tried to drum up support in the south by promising to
transfer the capital from Bissau to Buba, a small town in the south of this
flat country divided by wide meandering rivers, where there are plans to
build a deep-water port.
Yala, 52, began his campaign rather bizarrely by briefly occupying the
presidential palace in the company of several military supporters in the
early hours of 24 May. This action raised fears that the army would
intervene to prevent the holding of free and fair elections.
However, Yala and his supporters left the building peacefully after a
few hours.
Several eye-witnesses reported that they had seen Yala inside the
presidency, but the mercurial politicians later denied that he had been
inside the building.
There is still argument as to whether the short-lived occupation of
presidency was a token action to press Yala's claim to be Guinea-Bissau's
legitimate and democratically elected head of state, despite his overthrow
by the army two years ago, or a failed attempt to topple the present
government.
Prime Minister Carlos Gomes Junior, whose PAIGC party won parliamentary
elections in March last year, was quick to accuse Yala of attempting to
stage a coup. However, the government has not so far brought charges against
him and he has been allowed to campaign freely.
The third main candidate in the election is Malam Bacai Sanha, the
official candidate of the PAIGC. A former speaker of parliament, Bacai Sanha
was installed by the army as interim president in 1999 following the
overthrow of Vieira.
He supervised the holding of presidential elections a few months later
that brought Yala to power.
Bacai Sanha, who is in his late 50's came a poor second to Yala in that
poll after making it to a second round run-off vote.
However, the PAIGC has run a well-organised and strongly financed
campaign this time and Bacai Sanha's rallies have attracted large crowds,
similar in size to those drawn by Vieira.
However, local journalists covering the campaign have noted that both
figures attract thousands of curious bystanders, eager for a few hours' free
entertainment, as well as hardcore political supporters. They have warned
that large turnouts at such rallies are not necessarily indicative of strong
support in a particular area.
Bacai Sanha has been keen to undermine the support which Yala has
traditionally received from President Abdoulaye Wade of neighbouring
Senegal.
The authorities in Dakar were appeciative of Yala's swift moves to close
down the bases of a guerrilla movement seeking independence for Senegal's
southern Casamance region, when he came to power five years ago.
Addressing a campaign rally in the northern town of Sao Domingos, close
to the main border crossing into Senegal, Bacai Sanha said: "We must build
good relations with our neighbours. If I am elected president, I will help
Senegal to end the conflict in Casamance through dialogue with those seeking
independence."
Sunday's election will be closely scrutinised by western donors who have
been bailing out the government of Guinea-Bissau for the past two years and
by other African countries who are keen to prevent the tiny nation from
dissolving into fresh chaos, a move that would threaten the stability of
other states in the region.
Besides the EU, the African Union, the United States and the African
Union are also sending observers to the poll. Altogether, about 200
international observers will be spread around the country.
UN Secretary General Kofi Annan has dispatched former Mozambican
president Joaquim Chissano as his special representative to see fair
play.
According to local law, the official results of the first round of
voting must be announced within 10 days. If no candidate wins more than 50
percent of the vote, the two leading candidates will face each other in a
second-round run-off vote three weeks later.
Political analysts say it could therefore be early August before the
final outcome of the election is known.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United
Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Group Clamours for Aba State
Headline: Nigeria: Group Clamours for Aba State
Body_Text:
Lagos — A group, Nzuko Isiala-Ngwa Socio-political Group has commended delegates
to the National Political Reform Conference (NPRC), convened by the Federal
Government, maintaining that its aim is to articulate a new socio-political
order which will enhance and sustain the continued co-existence of the
various ethno-religious groups.
In a statement jointly signed by some members of the group, namely,
Barr. Sam Erugo, Mr. Uzoma Ogbanna, Mr. Alozie Jimonu, Dr. Oluchi Oguma and
Arch. Onuoha, it noted that NPRC will provide an opportunity for various
groups to come together to dialogue in order to find solution to the decades
of subjugation, marginalisation and outright ill-treatment of some groups in
the country.
"Taking cognizance of the aforesaid, we make bold to commend the quality
of representation at the conference, going by their pedigree and urge the
delegates to purge themselves of any socio- political, ethnic and religious
biases as the assignment is Herculean and expectations of Nigerians are
high.
" We therefore urge the distinguished delegates to look beyond the views
canvassed by the large regional groupings like Ohaneze, Afenifere, Arewa
Consultative Forum, and consider the plights of some sub-regional groups as
Ukwa-Ngwa (old Aba Division) who since 1954 have been agitating for the
creation of Aba State", the group stressed.
The group however, said that the agitation for the creation of Aba State
out of the present Abia State needs to be considered and recommended to the
Federal Government by NPRC.
According to the group, the interest of the South-east zone has been
persistently undermined by the imbalance in the structure of the federation,
adding that, the South-east made up of the Igbos who happen to be one of the
three major tribes in Nigeria has the least number of states among the six
geo-political zones in the country.
The group also said coming down to the South-east, the Ukwa/Ngwa people
of the old Aba division has been persistently marginalised right from the
period of the Eastern Central Region to the present day in Abia State.
Noting that the agitation for Aba State has been consistent since 1954
as it was recommended by Sir Arthur Mbanefo's panel for creation in 1990, it
urged the conference to come to the aid of the people of the area and rescue
them from bondage and oppression.
"The Aba State, when created will be self sustaining, as its people are
industrious, coupled with the location of Aba town, the Japan of Africa, in
the new state. Aba is one of the biggest commercial nerve centers in
Nigeria", the group said.
The group added that, Aba State when created will be a major food
producer in Nigeria as the people produce palm oil, cassava, gari, and yam
in commercial quantity.
"The population of the area that will constitute Aba State shall be over
three million, and the area lacks visible federal or state government
presence", the group stated.

Original_Search_Title: Dr. Saqui Malakpai: A Story of Biblical Parallels
Headline: Liberia: Dr. Saqui Malakpai: A Story of Biblical Parallels
Body_Text:
Liberian with disability says his greatest desire is to see people with
disabilities given the same opportunities he had. Doctor has written a
position paper to Liberia's legislative body, appealing for rights to jobs
and other possibilities for persons with disabilities.
By the age of 16, Dr. Saqui Malakpai had completely lost his sight. He
dropped out of 10th grade.
Ask him what he can cook, and he'll ask you what you want to eat. Palm
butter? Cassava leaf? Pepper soup? Take your pick.
"I'm a very good cook, actually," he says laughing.
He laughs a lot.
At 50, he is incredibly boyish - so much so that it's hard to figure out
what he would look like in person. But that youthful personality belies the
respectable gray hair you'll see when you meet him.
Dr. Saqui Malakpai has traveled all over the world. He completed his
first degree in three years at Florida State University and graduated cum
laude with a bachelor's degree in Special Education with a specialization in
Vision. He went on to complete his master's degree there, finishing with a
4.0 grade point average. He completed a second master's degree at Harvard
University in Boston, Mass., earning a doctorate in Educational
Administration, Planning and Social Policy, with an emphasis on
International Development. He also has a law degree.
And oh, by the way, he's totally blind.
Not quite the man born blind, but close...
Dr. Malakpai was born in Wozi, a village about 15 miles from Zorzor in
Lofa County. He was in sixth grade when he started having problems with his
eyes. He was diagnosed with onchocerciasis, also known as "river blindness."
The disease is caused by a parasite found in black flies along rivers in
developing countries, hence its name.
Two Monrovia surgeons agreed that he was too young at the time to take
the treatment; at his age, the treatment could kill him. They agreed he had
to grow at least a year older. That turned out to be the lesser of two
evils. By that time, the parasites had done irreversible damage. By age 16,
he had completely lost his sight. He dropped out of 10th grade.
But he knew some missionaries in Bomi Hills, Bong County, who owned a
typewriter. He asked them if he could use it.
"We can't teach you," they said. "Neither of us knows how." But Saqui
had his own plans.
"I don't need you to," he said. "I'll teach myself."
"I knew typing would be vital to me now that I'd lost my sight," he says
now.
The couple left Liberia in 1971 and sent him a brand new Olympia
typewriter.
Dr. Malakpai, who still has the instrument, says it was and still is the
single best material gift he's ever received. He would use it through high,
school, college, and to write his dissertation.
He used the typewriter to write letters to friends around the country
and to write short stories for the Lorma Literacy Center in Wozi. He
translated news from English to Lorma and vice versa.
"Jesus, thou son of David, have mercy on me!"
In 1972 President William R. Tolbert was to visit Bomi Hills to install
a mayor. When Saqui heard the news, he immediately took to the task of
writing a letter in which he asked the president to send him to Israel to
have his eyes worked on, or else to send him to school. A police officer,
who was a friend of his, delivered the letter to the president. Impressed
that a blind kid had typed the letter, President Tolbert sent for him.
"Rise! He calleth thee"
Sgt. Tulu Hilton picked him up and took him to meet the president.
"You typed this letter?" President Tolbert asked.
"Yes, I did." He replied.
The president ordered the superintendent of Bong County to take Saqui to
Monrovia to see him.
Three weeks later at the Executive Mansion, the president asked him
again, "Did you type that letter?"
Once again, he replied that he had.
"Take him to a typewriter," the president ordered.
"I couldn't see," says Dr. Malakpai, "but I was very sure it was the
president standing behind me." After about three to four minutes, the
president said, "You can stop now."
Liberia's first eye doctor, Dr. Duma Traub, and an Israeli doctor who
happened to be in Monrovia at the time both agreed that since Saqui's optic
nerves were dead, there was no need for him to go to Israel. But President
Tolbert agreed to send him to school. Dr. Traub supported the idea and kept
Saqui at the hospital in Monrovia so that he would not fall through the
cracks.
In 1972 Saqui left Liberia to attend a school for the blind in Freetown,
Sierra Leone.
"I was so excited that the night I arrived, I learned to type from A
through G in Braille."
He learned Braille in three weeks and afterwards began to teach at the
school.
"Go thy way; thy faith hath made thee whole"
In August 1975, Saqui went home to present a statement of gratitude to
President Tolbert , who offered him a scholarship to attend a university of
his choice anywhere in the world. Saqui attended Florida State University
and completed his bachelor's degree in three years. He took heavy loads,
CLEP tests, and graduated cum laude.
With one year left on his scholarship, he began his master's program at
FSU. But the April 12, 1980 coup in Liberia made it a tough year
financially. President Tolbert had been killed. Nevertheless, Saqui finished
his master's program with a 4.0 grade point average and got acceptance
letters from Cornell University in New York, Pennsylvania State University,
and the prestigious Harvard University in Boston, Mass., to pursue a second
master's degree. Saqui chose Harvard and earned his doctorate degree.
"My Lutheran scholarship had been withdrawn, and those were difficult
times," Dr. Malakpai recalls. "I hung in there and finished my degree in May
1986, but very broke. But it's always wonderful to trust in God," he
says.
With no green card, there were only two places he could work: with an
international agency or an institution of higher learning. With an
impressive resume and no experience, he was stuck between a rock and a hard
place.
He found a job with the University of Toledo in Ohio as a professor. And
30 years later, he is a tenured professor at the school.
"I give the glory to God," he says. "I've published books, traveled, and
God has been very good to me."
Equal Rights for the Blind
The University of Liberia does not accept blind students. Fourabay
College in Freetown, Sierra Leone, on the other hand, graduated its first
blind student in 1971.
Nevertheless Dr. Malakpai says his greatest achievement has been his
three beautiful children, Kortoe, 16; Zoebedeh, 12; and Luopu, 11.
"I always leave my future to God to direct," he says. " I could have
left Toledo for greener pastures; but I prefer to stay in Toledo, close to
my children, to watch them grow up," he says.
His wife of one year, Sue, describes him as "a great man."
Dr. Malakpai says his greatest desire is to see people with disabilities
given the same opportunities he had. He has written a position paper to
Liberia's legislative body, appealing for rights to jobs and other
possibilities for persons with disabilities. The University of Liberia does
not accept blind students. Fourabay College in Freetown, Sierra Leone, on
the other hand, graduated its first blind student in 1971.
"It's not a matter of privilege," Dr. Malakpai says. "It's a human
rights issue. With the same opportunities, many can do as well as I have, if
not better."
Driven
Dr. Malakpai says his driving force has to do with his upbringing.
"My father was and still is the greatest human being I've ever known,"
he remembers fondly.
A Zoe in the Poro Society, Saqui's father could neither read not write;
but he was extremely influential in Saqui's life. He died before Saqui
turned 10.
"But there's not a day in my adult life that I don't think of him,"
Dr. Malakpai says. "Of all his children, he said he knew that I would
somehow make it. The confidence he instilled in me was very
invigorating.
"When I lost my sight, my mother cried for days," he recalls. "But I
promised her that I would take care of her and my brothers and sisters."
Dr. Malakpai says he is also driven by his desire to help others. He is
currently the only professor in the state of Ohio who teaches vision courses
to sighted students. He has just written a federal grant request for
$797,000 to recruit students for his vision program full time. The current
$185,000 grant he holds sponsors 12 students.
"... But one thing I do know..."
He said his Christian upbringing and introduction to Jesus Christ played
a big role. He describes his faith in God as rigid. People ask, "How can God
allow these things to happen," he says. "I think it's the work of the
devil. But one thing I do know is that God is a good God."
Dr. Malakpai say he is exceedingly grateful to God and has never once
been bitter about losing his sight.
"When you appreciate what you have, you don't miss what you don't have,"
he says. "Blindness is a very difficult disability; but the greatest problem
is the inability of the sighted to see my normality. The sighted equate
blindness with closing their eyes. But the idea that a blind person is
perpetually longing for sight implies that they are not normal. And that's
not the case. I never ask, 'Why me?' I say, "Praise God."
bbradford@liberianobserver.com

Original_Search_Title: A Bishop's Panacea to Corruption
Headline: Nigeria: A Bishop's Panacea to Corruption
Body_Text:
Lagos — In the years gone by, when prophets of God spoke, their words were
final. You would ignore them at your own peril. Once they said: "so says the
Lord of Hosts", it was mandatory that their words be respected. King
Pharaoh, that stubborn Egyptian monarch who failed to hearken to God's
warning to let the Israelites go, despite the many visits paid to his palace
by Moses to tell him to free them and let them be, in accordance with God's
injunction, brought a lot of calamities upon his people before ending up in
the Red Sea with his army and chariots. Such was the awe that these prophets
of god were known for then.
There are still such prophets these days, although it is also true that
there are more fakes than genuine ones among them. But this is as old as we
can remember. In those days too, there were fake prophets, some of who also
performed 'miracles'. We remember that some of these people who tried to
compete with Moses (whom the Scripture aptly refers to as (Egyptian
magicians) when they used their evil powers to replicate almost every wonder
that Moses performed. They did that successfully until God proved Himself as
the ultimate God before whom there was no god and after whom none shall
spring up.
I do not know who Bishop Emmanuel Ojiyovwi Ojibo is.
But I was fascinated by his panacea to corruption as reported in one of
the national dailies about two weeks ago.
The man, according to the report, is the General Overseer and Spiritual
Head of St. Jonah's Church of God in Ughelli. What is happening in many of
our churches today (remember the bickering over the $4 million crusade in
the country by Benny Hinn and the brouhaha surrounding it?) almost made me
to ignore the good suggestion of Bishop Ojibo, to wit: that President
Olusegun Obasanjo should give a three-month period of grace to public
officials who had participated in the looting of the nation's treasury to
return such illegally-acquired money. What a brilliant idea, even if it is
not particularly new! We should remember that one of our former late Heads
of State, General Murtala Ramat Muhammed, was reported to have done an
almost similar thing upon getting to power. If I still remember correctly,
we were told then that he voluntarily returned everything that he illegally
acquired such that by the time he was killed on February 13, 1976, in the
botched Dimka coup, all we were told he had in his account was some N6,
000.00 representing his salary for the month of February that he was killed
and a two-bedroom flat in Kano. Then, corruption was not as pervasive as it
is today, when our country has continuously maintained a leading position
and our president thinks that arraigning one or two big fishes (among the
legion of thieves) is enough to improve the country's rating.
Bishop Ojibo predicted too that there is a bright horizon and
sustainable good economy for the country if only we would allow the
president's anti-corruption crusade to endure. As a matter of policy, I have
always refused to contest predictions by men of God, no matter how
ridiculous they might seem. This is much more so for predictions that are
general in nature. It is a different thing if someone wants to hide under
the guise of being a man of God to say negative things about me. I do not
accept that because the Scripture is clear about that. For instance, if a
pastor foresees that for me, death lurks, I would quickly reject that as not
being my portion because the Bible says of me that I shall not die
young. The minimum number I expect to live for is 70 years. And, if I must
say, I expect a reasonable overtime in good health too. That is what I
believe my Bible says concerning that aspect of my life. So, there is no
amount of respect that I may have for any pastor that would make me accept
as God-sent, a vision short-changing this portion of mine.
Having said that, I would also not want to query Bishop Ojibo's
prediction about Nigeria doing well under President Obasanjo because I still
remember such a prediction by one of the prophets of old who predicted life
abundantly in a place and the man who was so pessimistic about this
happening was cursed and he never partook in the boom. He was the stepping
stone that people trampled upon when the abundance eventually came. At any
rate, why must I be opposed to the idea of Nigeria doing well, especially
when the man at the helm of affairs is from my town? I would not even wish
that the country does not prosper if the president is of Igbo, Hausa or even
minority extraction.
The problem with most predictions in the country is that we hardly know
when most of these numerous men of God are speaking for themselves as in
expressing personal opinions or when they are relaying to us what God
actually sent them to relay. I say this because when God speaks, he does so
often in a manner that one knows what would happen next. My knowledge about
this aspect of religion I got when about three uears ago, I reviewed a book:
"The Undotted Scriptures". There, George Nkwoji, the author, tried to make a
distinction between what Jesus Christ said and the things said by the
Apostles. When our men of God speak to us, they should try to let us know of
they are expressing personal opinions or they are telling us about the
message as directed by God.
A prosperous Nigeria is something that would benefit the vast majority
of Nigerians. Perhaps only those who believe that the country's assets are
their exclusive right would not wish for such a positive development. I
certainly do not belong to that category, nor do I ever wish to be
there.
As the bishop noted, the good things would not fall like manna from
heaven. He enjoined us to be ready to pay the price, which is genuine
repentance and to love one another. I guess the repentance Bishop Ojibo is
talking about has to do with the president too.
Corruption remains the number one problem of the country and, until it
is fought to a standstill, we would only keep moving in circles. I have
refused to be carried away by the many programmes of the current government
(like the cassava export to China that it believes would fetch billions of
dollars) for the fact that we cannot manage the funds well. We have not seen
enough evidence that many of our leaders are good managers of resources or
that they would not steal much of it.
If only our leaders watched the people's reactions on the television, or
listened to radio or read the papers during the so-called Democracy Day last
week, they would have seen what the people's verdict is. A situation where
they continue to swim in self-assessment and keep patting themselves on the
back for a job well done will worsen their perception not only within the
country, but also outside.
Bishop Ojibo has given what looks like an easy way out for the corrupt
officials. President Obasanjo should try it and those who turn in the looted
funds should, like the prodigal son, be forgiven and enjoined to sin no
more. We were told the president did a similar thing by asking ministers who
bribed members of the National Assembly to come and tell him privately and
be forgiven or risk being sacked if he discovered it himself. He can do the
same again.

Original_Search_Title: Bangui : Hard To Make Ends Meet
Headline: Central African Republic: Bangui : Hard To Make Ends Meet
Body_Text:
Before and after the presidential elections of May 8, the daily preoccupation of Bangui's residents remain the same.
The capital of the Central African Republic, Bangui, has an airy look. The streets are well-paved with mango trees on the sidelines, while giant billboards adorn the streets to sensitise all and sundry of the dangers of HIV/AIDS and why its important to keep the city clean. "Bangui la Coquette, Bangui la Fleurie" are some of the messages on billboards.
But behind this alluring reality of a spacious capital without traffic jams, lies a disturbing phenomenon in a country endowed with natural wealth. The inhabitants hardly expose toothy smiles for one main reason: salary arrears and widespread inflation. The civil servants had suffered from 36 months of unpaid salaries under the previous dictatorial regimes. Under BozizÃ©, this has been reduced to six months. "This is a commendable effort but I can hardly feed my family three times a day. I however praise FranÃ§ois BozizÃ© for one thing, the relative security that he has installed in the capital and throughout the country," says Bemba Yves Itondo, a civil servant.
Besides, most of the foodstuff is imported either from Cameroon or comes from DR Congo (when the border was not closed). Eating once a day is already an achievement for many families who tend to rely most on the private rather than the public sector. Where to eat tasty food at affordable prices is a serious problem. You can't eat your fill for less than CFA 1,000. But Bangui residents are surviving. Each morning, the women don't sit arms-folded. They are engaged in petty trading such as selling puff-puff; cooked cassava and groundnuts. At the "Port Beach" besides the Oubangi river, women sell roasted palatable fish caught from the river which is called "MpÃ©tÃ©" or "Mboto" in the national language Sangho. It is here that most tourists come to observe the scenery across the river.
Banguissois in the private sector (who are in the minority), are paid regularly. But salary or no salary, the bars and other drinking spots are always flocked by Banguissois each evening. Here, beer such as MOCAF and locally made traditional whisky are savoured - even by girls in their teens who also take pride in smoking cheap cigarettes..

Original_Search_Title: Galamsey Casualties On the Rise
Headline: Ghana: Galamsey Casualties On the Rise
Body_Text:
Accra — AngloGold Ashanti (Obuasi) has restated that the increasing galamsey activities on its concession and other mining areas are worrying, which need national intervention in addressing it.
Mg George Osei-Sasu, the Acting General Manager in-charge of Human Resources, who made the statement called for global action against the illegal mining activities.
This was when some management staff of the company led a team of newsmen and police to inspect some of the illegal pits the galamsey operators had constructed in some parts of the company's concession.
The disturbing issue is that most of these illegal pits are close to the company's active mining areas linking up to the various levels underground.
Mr Osei-Sasu said it was in line with addressing the problem globally and not individual mining companies that the Ghana Chamber of Mine was trying to do something about it.
"That is the more reason why the Chamber of Mines, in league with the mining companies should try to make sure we solve the illegal mining activities once and for all," the Acting General Manager stressed.
Mr Osei-Sasu said as part of efforts to end the galamsey operations, the company was prepared to create an alternative livelihood project for them, including the President's Special Initiative (PSI) on cassava but they are not interested because of their lust for quick money.
Briefing the newsmen on the activities of the illegal miners, Colonel Steve Oduro-Kwarteng (Rtd) said at the moment the Company had no freedom to operate on its legally acquired concession due to the activities of the galamsey operators.
"What is disturbing is that they operate where we have out shafts and at times actually confront our miners underground."
The Head of Special Services Division said the Company had, on two or three occasions warned the illegal miners but "as I am speaking now they have gone to re-take the shafts."
Col. Oduro-Kwarteng said the situation could not be allowed to continue since their activities was dangerous to the Company, themselves and the environment.
"We want to give them the warning to pack their equipment in the pit before we cover them up," He said.
The areas where the illegal mining activities were concentrated included the Ellis Shaft of the Obuasi section, the Cut Three and Four of the Sanav section of the mine and the Dokyiwa tailings dam.

Original_Search_Title: 2,000 Homeless in Yumbe
Headline: Uganda: 2,000 Homeless in Yumbe
Body_Text:
Kampala — OVER 2,000 people, the majority of them children and women, have camped
at Lombe primary school in Yumbe district after being evicted by the
National Forest Authority (NFA) from forest reserves in Moyo district.
They were evicted from Eria north and south forest reserves in Metu sub-
county on April 25 by NFA officials, who also cut down their cassava
fields.
The evicted camped at Gborogborochu village, about 34km from Yumbe town
council and lack food, water and shelter.
They are also living under the threat of a cholera outbreak.
The chairman of Aringa Disaster Preparedness Forum, a Yumbe-based NGO,
Habib Moses Amba, said the first batch of homeless people arrived on April
30.
Amba said another group crossed over to southern Sudan as refugees.
The group had lived in the disputed forestland for over 40 years.
"The little food they came with is getting over. Families are now
feeding on mangoes and though the children are looking healthy, in two weeks
they will be malnourished." Amba said. They are also using contaminated
runoff water for domestic use.
Sources also indicated that the nearest health centre is about 10km from
the school and the only borehole is about one and a half kilometre
away.

Original_Search_Title: Hunger May Affect This Year's Likumbi Lya Mize - Ndungu
Headline: Zambia: Hunger May Affect This Year's Likumbi Lya Mize - Ndungu
Body_Text:
Lusaka — HUNGER in Zambezi district is likely to affect this year's Likumbi Lya
Mize traditional ceremony, senior chief Ndungu of the Luvale people of
Zambezi has said.
In an interview in Lusaka on Wednesday, chief Ndungu also said both men
and women were sharing a ward at a health centre in his chiefdom.
He asked government to send relief food to the area because it had
received very little rain, which affected both maize and cassava.
"This will definitely affect the Likumbi Lya Mize. We might not be able
to feed the people who will go to attend the ceremony," chief Ndungu
said.
Likumbi Lya Mize ceremony is usually held at the end of August each
year.
However, chief Ndungu said the Luvale people were planning to host a
very big Likumbi Lya Mize next year when it clocks 50 years.
He said several chiefs from Angola and other neighbouring countries
would be invited to the event, which was likely to take two weeks.
He also said the government in recognition of the ceremony had invited
them to be in Livingstone to support the Visit Zambia 2005 campaign in
August.
"This is where we will have a mini Likumbi Lya Mize because all our
makishi will be there," chief Ndungu said.
However, chief Ndungu bemoaned that his area was not benefiting from the
tourism income despite making the Likumbi Lya Mize very attractive.
He also disclosed that his subjects intend to build a museum in Zambezi
to store cultural and traditional artefacts and heritage.
Chief Ndungu said it was taboo for men and women to share the same
ward.
He said he had brought the problem to the attention of authorities but
nothing had been done to correct it.
Chief Ndungu also said classified daily employees manned some health
centres in Zambezi.
"Government should send the right personnel. I am appealing to the
government, in my chiefdom, both women and men are sharing a ward. We want
to expand it because it was built during Kaunda era," chief Ndungu
said.
Chief Ndungu urged the government not to treat the tarring of the
Mutanda-Chavuma road as a mere political gimmick because the bad state of
the road adversely affects investment in the area.
He also urged the government to work with local chiefs to curb the
movement of cattle across borders to control the Contagious Bovine
Pleuropneumonia (CBPP), which was still in the area.
Chief Ndungu said Angolans were crossing into the district to buy cattle
at K1.7 million per head and feared cattle would be finished.

Original_Search_Title: Dr. Saqui Malakpai: a Story of Biblical Parallels
Headline: Liberia: Dr. Saqui Malakpai: a Story of Biblical Parallels
Body_Text:
Monrovia — By the age of 16, Dr. Saqui Malakpai had completely lost his sight. He
dropped out of 10th grade.
Ask him what he can cook, and he'll ask you what you want to eat. Palm
butter? Cassava leaf? Pepper soup? Take your pick.
"I'm a very good cook, actually," he says laughing.
He laughs a lot.
At 50, he is incredibly boyish - so much so that it's hard to figure out
what he would look like in person. But that youthful personality belies the
respectable gray hair you'll see when you meet him.
Dr. Saqui Malakpai has traveled all over the world. He completed his
first degree in three years at Florida State University and graduated cum
laude with a bachelor's degree in Special Education with a specialization in
Vision. He went on to complete his master's degree there, finishing with a
4.0 grade point average. He completed a second master's degree at Harvard
University in Boston, Mass., earning a doctorate in Educational
Administration, Planning and Social Policy, with an emphasis on
International Development. He also has a law degree.
And oh, by the way, he's totally blind.
Not quite the man born blind, but close...
Dr. Malakpai was born in Wozi, a village about 15 miles from Zorzor in
Lofa County. He was in sixth grade when he started having problems with his
eyes. He was diagnosed with onchocerciasis, also known as "river blindness."
The disease is caused by a parasite found in black flies along rivers in
developing countries, hence its name.
Two Monrovia surgeons agreed that he was too young at the time to take
the treatment; at his age, the treatment could kill him. They agreed he had
to grow at least a year older. That turned out to be the lesser of two
evils. By that time, the parasites had done irreversible damage. By age 16,
he had completely lost his sight. He dropped out of 10th grade.
But he knew some missionaries in Bomi Hills, Bong County, who owned a
typewriter. He asked them if he could use it.
"We can't teach you," they said. "Neither of us knows how." But Saqui
had his own plans.
"I don't need you to," he said. "I'll teach myself."
"I knew typing would be vital to me now that I'd lost my sight," he says
now.
The couple left Liberia in 1971 and sent him a brand new Olympia
typewriter.
Dr. Malakpai, who still has the instrument, says it was and still is the
single best material gift he's ever received. He would use it through high,
school, college, and to write his dissertation.
He used the typewriter to write letters to friends around the country
and to write short stories for the Lorma Literacy Center in Wozi. He
translated news from English to Lorma and vice versa.
"Jesus, thou son of David, have mercy on me!"
In 1972 President William R. Tolbert was to visit Bomi Hills to install
a mayor. When Saqui heard the news, he immediately took to the task of
writing a letter in which he asked the president to send him to Israel to
have his eyes worked on, or else to send him to school. A police officer,
who was a friend of his, delivered the letter to the president. Impressed
that a blind kid had typed the letter, President Tolbert sent for him.
"Rise! He calleth thee"
Sgt. Tulu Hilton picked him up and took him to meet the president.
"You typed this letter?" President Tolbert asked.
"Yes, I did." He replied.
The president ordered the superintendent of Bong County to take Saqui to
Monrovia to see him.
Three weeks later at the Executive Mansion, the president asked him
again, "Did you type that letter?"
Once again, he replied that he had.
"Take him to a typewriter," the president ordered.
"I couldn't see," says Dr. Malakpai, "but I was very sure it was the
president standing behind me." After about three to four minutes, the
president said, "You can stop now."
Liberia's first eye doctor, Dr. Duma Traub, and an Israeli doctor who
happened to be in Monrovia at the time both agreed that since Saqui's optic
nerves were dead, there was no need for him to go to Israel. But President
Tolbert agreed to send him to school. Dr. Traub supported the idea and kept
Saqui at the hospital in Monrovia so that he would not fall through the
cracks.
In 1972 Saqui left Liberia to attend a school for the blind in Freetown,
Sierra Leone.
"I was so excited that the night I arrived, I learned to type from A
through G in Braille."
He learned Braille in three weeks and afterwards began to teach at the
school.
"Go thy way; thy faith hath made thee whole"
In August 1975, Saqui went home to present a statement of gratitude to
President Tolbert , who offered him a scholarship to attend a university of
his choice anywhere in the world. Saqui attended Florida State University
and completed his bachelor's degree in three years. He took heavy loads,
CLEP tests, and graduated cum laude.
With one year left on his scholarship, he began his master's program at
FSU. But the April 12, 1980 coup in Liberia made it a tough year
financially. President Tolbert had been killed. Nevertheless, Saqui finished
his master's program with a 4.0 grade point average and got acceptance
letters from Cornell University in New York, Pennsylvania State University,
and the prestigious Harvard University in Boston, Mass., to pursue a second
master's degree. Saqui chose Harvard and earned his doctorate degree.
"My Lutheran scholarship had been withdrawn, and those were difficult
times," Dr. Malakpai recalls. "I hung in there and finished my degree in May
1986, but very broke. But it's always wonderful to trust in God," he
says.
With no green card, there were only two places he could work: with an
international agency or an institution of higher learning. With an
impressive resume and no experience, he was stuck between a rock and a hard
place.
He found a job with the University of Toledo in Ohio as a professor. And
30 years later, he is a tenured professor at the school.
"I give the glory to God," he says. "I've published books, traveled, and
God has been very good to me."
Equal Rights for the Blind
The University of Liberia does not accept blind students. Fourabay
College in Freetown, Sierra Leone, on the other hand, graduated its first
blind student in 1971.
Nevertheless Dr. Malakpai says his greatest achievement has been his
three beautiful children, Kortoe, 16; Zoebedeh, 12; and Luopu, 11.
"I always leave my future to God to direct," he says. " I could have
left Toledo for greener pastures; but I prefer to stay in Toledo, close to
my children, to watch them grow up," he says.
His wife of one year, Sue, describes him as "a great man."
Dr. Malakpai says his greatest desire is to see people with disabilities
given the same opportunities he had. He has written a position paper to
Liberia's legislative body, appealing for rights to jobs and other
possibilities for persons with disabilities. The University of Liberia does
not accept blind students. Fourabay College in Freetown, Sierra Leone, on
the other hand, graduated its first blind student in 1971.
"It's not a matter of privilege," Dr. Malakpai says. "It's a human
rights issue. With the same opportunities, many can do as well as I have, if
not better."
Driven
Dr. Malakpai says his driving force has to do with his upbringing.
"My father was and still is the greatest human being I've ever known,"
he remembers fondly.
A Zoe in the Poro Society, Saqui's father could neither read not write;
but he was extremely influential in Saqui's life. He died before Saqui
turned 10.
"But there's not a day in my adult life that I don't think of him,"
Dr. Malakpai says. "Of all his children, he said he knew that I would
somehow make it. The confidence he instilled in me was very
invigorating.
"When I lost my sight, my mother cried for days," he recalls. "But I
promised her that I would take care of her and my brothers and sisters."
Dr. Malakpai says he is also driven by his desire to help others. He is
currently the only professor in the state of Ohio who teaches vision courses
to sighted students. He has just written a federal grant request for
$797,000 to recruit students for his vision program full time. The current
$185,000 grant he holds sponsors 12 students.
"... But one thing I do know... "
He said his Christian upbringing and introduction to Jesus Christ played
a big role. He describes his faith in God as rigid. People ask, "How can God
allow these things to happen," he says. "I think it's the work of the
devil. But one thing I do know is that God is a good God."
Dr. Malakpai say he is exceedingly grateful to God and has never once
been bitter about losing his sight.
"When you appreciate what you have, you don't miss what you don't have,"
he says. "Blindness is a very difficult disability; but the greatest problem
is the inability of the sighted to see my normality. The sighted equate
blindness with closing their eyes. But the idea that a blind person is
perpetually longing for sight implies that they are not normal. And that's
not the case. I never ask, 'Why me?' I say, "Praise God."
bbradford@liberianobserver.com

Original_Search_Title: Foodstuffs Prices Soar
Headline: Nigeria: Foodstuffs Prices Soar
Body_Text:
Despite the onset of the rainy season, prices of foodstuffs have continued to rise sharply, a market survey has shown.
Investigations conducted by Daily Trust in some major towns across the country indicates that the escalating prices of food-stuffs have further wor-sened the already deteriorating economic conditions of the people.
Not surprising, most of the traders interviewed in the survey confirmed that there were significant differences in the prices of foodstuffs this year when compared with last year.
While the traders are blaming the rise on the poor economic situation and rising fuel prices, consumers are calling on the government to urgen-tly do something to reduce inflation.
In Abuja where prices of foodstuffs have always been higher than what obtains in other cities except Lagos, a 50kg bag of rice now sells for N6,000 as against last years' price of N5,200.
Alhaji Liman who sells foodstuffs in Wuse market said a bag of beans which used to sell for N8,500.00 last year is now being sold for N10,000.
He said last year, a bag of red garri cost N3,900 but this year, it costs N6,500, while garri sold for N3,000, now costs N5,000.
Mr. Chukwuemeka Ohia-ri who sells crayfish, said people do not buy crayfish any more due to its cost, as one mudu of crayfish now costs N1,200 while the half mudu costs N600.
A bag of fine edible salt is sold in Lagos and Abuja markets for N650 per 25kg bag a crate of egg costs N450 while a basket of onions costs N400.
Another petty trader at Karmo market, Abuja, Mama Ngozi, said a bottle of groundnut oil hitherto sold for N90, now sells for N130, while palm oil sells for N120 against the old price of N80.00.
However, in Lagos, prices of garri have reportedly gone up by over 100 percent in what most traders blamed on govern-ment's recent policy of using cassava in bread production.
The News Agency of Nigeria (NAN), also reports that government's plan to make cassava bread and the massive exportation of unprocessed cassava may have precipitated the increase.
A garri trader at Oshodi market, Mr. Osarota Elsakho, told NAN that the reason stated above had made it difficult for garri producers to have enough to produce for local consumption.
"We learnt they are using cassava flour for baking it has seriously affected our business in that they now buy unpro-cessed cassava, thereby making it scarce for garri producers," he said.
A 20 litre tin of "Ijebu garri" which sold for between N600 and N700 earlier in the year now cost between N1,200 and N1,400.
Soaring food prices in Zamfara state, however, have led poor people to resort to visiting the wealthy in the society to solicit for financial assis-tance or scavenge for food remnants.
Our reporter in Gusau reports that the poor could be seen hopping from one house to the other, or accosting motorists with their family in tow, soliciting what to eat, while some move in groups.
Investigations further revealed that the situation has made it necessary for the poor and down-trodden to go to bed with empty sto-machs.
In a reaction, a source at the Con-sumers Protection Council who prefer-red anonymity, said the council has no mandate to fix prices as "our duty is not to control food prices."

Original_Search_Title: Delta Government Faults Contractors
Headline: Nigeria: Delta Government Faults Contractors
Body_Text:
Lagos — It may have been a bad time for Contractors handling the construction of
a three storey administrative building for Delta State High Court of
Justice, following a recent surchaged place on them by the State government
The state government's descison THISDAY learnt was borne out of the
reason that the said contractor (Odazee Nig. Ltd), unilaterally constructed
an additional floor to the two storey building which was not part of the
project plan.
The State Commisioner of Information, Mr Magnus Onyibe who disclosed
this recently while briefing the press in Asaba said the action of the
contractor changed the original design of the project by government thereby
making the government to review and re-award the job for completion at a
total of N47 millon, in line with Ibori administration, not to leave any
uncompleted project at the end of the administration tenure in 2007.
In another development, the State government has approved a proposal for
establishment of value added industrial processing of cassava and
rehabilitation of the palm oil estate at Nsukwa.
Onyibe who disclosed that the state government will encourage the
cultivation of 6,000 hectres of land for cassava production across the three
senatorial zones, which is also intended to create employment for its
people.
Meanwhile, the state government has said that henceforth, the donation
of land to government by communities for developmental purposes would be the
prerequisite for the siting of government projects in such communities.
This measure is to stem agitations for payment of compesation to
communities, which presently hinders the speedy establishment of projects
that would benefit such communities.

Original_Search_Title: Testing a Quasi Democracy
Headline: Sierra Leone: Testing a Quasi Democracy
Body_Text:
In 1997, a military regime, in friendly combat with the Revolutionary
United Front (RUF) rebels of Foday Sankoh took over power, exactly nine
months after the elected government of President Kabbah had begun a new
democratic leadership.
Nine months later, the coup was over turned using Nigerian soldiers and
Canadian money in return for diamond concessions; a fact few Sierra Leoneans
know.
This gave birth for what we now know as Koidu Holdings; and looking at
the deals involved, it is unlikely that those fighting to abrogate the deals
will get hearing from Tejan Kabbah.
The return of the legitimate government in March of 1998 gave birth to a
court martial for suspected rebel collaborators after which, some 24
soldiers were executed through firing squad. In January 1999, Sankoh's
rebels heaped mayhem on inhabitants of the capital city Freetown, while on
May 8, 2000, some 20,000 civilians barricaded Foday Sankoh's residence in
the West part of the city, to demand that he takes the peace process
seriously. In a fracas that followed, 26 civilians were killed.
These events preceded the 2002 democratic elections, which, like the
1996 presidential and parliamentary elections, were won by the Kabbah led
SLPP by some 76%.
Since then, a few awkward things are happening for which the Sierra
Leone People's Party, SLPP-led government of Tejan Kabbah is being blamed,
rightly or wrongly.
In January 2005, a 24 year-old University student, Emerson Bockarie,
produced a music album that did not seem to have gone down well with
government functionaries. Vice President Solomon Berewa argues that "the
music paints government as being foolish".
The music tries to ridicule the spirit of personal, material and
financial aggrandizement by political tacticians in the ruling government,
against the background of national abject poverty across the country. Next,
was the production of a local film highlighting the on-going debates
surrounding the female circumcision (Bondo Society).its launching was
sabotaged by the national stadium management though some critics claimed
government's acquiesces.
Then followed the arrest and detention of a senior journalist Awoonor
Richie Gordon of the Pee Magazine for an article that only re-stated a state
lodge press statement in support of Marine Resources minister Ibrahim Okere
Adams, after his arrest, detention and questioning by the country's Anti
Corruption Commission, (ACC).
Prior to all these, an eight man delegation from the government had met
two senior journalists at the information minister's office to discuss the
possibility of 'containing stubborn journalists' as well as managing the
'unwanted torments and effects of phone-in programs' on local radio stations
in the city of Freetown.
While allegations are that government stooges at the behest of some
senior government functionaries sabotaged the launching of the local and
traditional female circumcision film "Sebatu's Initiation", using the
national stadium management, for fear of incurring the wrath of female
voters of the party, sources say SLPP activists stormed the stage on the
night of the launching of the local album "Borbor Belleh" music and caused
problems, bringing the entire program to a halt just few hours after
start. Party supporters dismissed it as foolish allegation.
Already, a senior journalists, Paul Kamara is serving a four-year jail
term concurrently, for publishing articles that were deemed to have cast
aspersion on the integrity of the president. Articles that actually emanated
from earlier findings of a commission of inquiry in 1967.
The speaker of Parliament, Justice Edmond Cowan has been quoted as
telling some journalists "forget that cocoon report. Kabbah is not a
convict".
Recently, prisons authorities at the country's maximum security prison
have had to transfer Paul Kamara to a single solitary cell-room where he
cannot communicate with other inmates. This, however, has been denied by
President Kabbah when this writer met him recently.
In the words of a prison warder, 'Paul is being caged in a solitary
cage", so as to stop him from sending out letters to be published in local
tabloids with regards to the terrible inhuman conditions prisoners
endure.
Though the president's civil service records were refused to be tendered
in courts during Paul's trial, a few journalists have laid hand on it,
waiting to begin serializing it when the president shall have exited office
in March 2007. In Sierra Leone, since 1976, no ex president has the peace of
retirement, and it is unlikely that President Kabbah will be different,
considering the present and previous happenings, including the execution of
some 24 soldiers in October of 1998, mass dissatisfaction with his method of
governance, vis-Ã -vis entrenched corrupt practices within government and the
call by the populace for him to reshuffle his cabinet since most of his team
players have either lost respect in society or seen as highly
corrupt. Allegations for which, the president is asking for proof.
From the arrest of journalists to the sabotaging of the launching of a
local traditional cultural film, to the obstruction of the lunching of a
local anti-corruption music, and invitation and scolding of journalists by
Kabbah's Justice Minister Fred Carew, every finger points to the president
and some supporters of what some observers see as an over exuberant system
and a government ready to silence critical critics by any unorthodox
means.
First week of March saw a student demonstration; asking for the
resignation of Education Science and Technology minister, Dr. Alpha Tejan
Wurie, while accusing the police of using maximum force to disperse a
peaceful demonstration. Wurie has however, eloquently defended his and that
of government's positions saying the Academic Staff Association is to blame
for the strike, since government was spending some$1m every three months on
the university. Observers think that the university authorities too must be
careful in distressing government since no one can expect a sound result if
the ACC were to pay a visit to the seats of academia. In Sierra Leone, no
institution, not even the media, is isolated from the dictates and powers of
corruption.
It however took the office of the National Security personnel to
intervene between the police and students, in a matter that could have been
handled by respective line ministries such as Education, Labour, and
Information.
None of these came nearer the meeting. It is however becoming too clear,
that in Sierra Leone, the issue of corruption is usually intermingled and
confused with the dangers of sheer inefficiency in governance. What
actually, sometimes people refer to as corruption in state management, is
simply and uninspired inefficiency.
Sources close to the Sierra Leone Association of Journalists, SLAJ say
that the association is sourcing for funds to go to the supreme court,
asking for a better interpretation of sections 11, 18 and 25 of the 1991
constitution, as well as the Anti media nuisances cause by the 1965 Public
Order Act; with references to the role, functions and freedom of the
Press. In Sierra Leone, judges to the bench are appointed by the president,
with a usual concordance approval of the parliament.
The question is, will the journalists' association get justice form a
judiciary wherein a very good number and suggestions had been that SLAJ
could initiate a private member bill, through the parliament, for repeal of
some of the obnoxious Anti media laws on the statues.
Certain senior members of the media however believe that some of the
legislators hardly understand some of the issues at stake.
"This', for the SLAJ, according to a former Justice Minister and
Attorney General, "will be a fruitless endeavour, considering the present
constitution of the parliament". However, even for the sake of it, perhaps
going to court is not a bad venture at all.
At the moment, parliamentary representation is an over burdened 95
percent sac of infested higher single interest of myopic "poly-tricks",
leaning heavily on the side of the ruling party.
With the state media in shambles and an epitome of embarrassment to even
the government, private media, mainly a representation of a few independent
newspapers and radio stations, are impoverished and threatened entity; the
vociferous private media today is one of the few signs of hope for the
people.
However, sometimes senior journalist-colleagues who, because of the need
to survive, have become mouth-piece, advocates and ambassadors for
unscrupulous politicians; among them, presidential aspirants.
And while the opposition, All People's Congress party is in chaos and in
a dissipating state of disintegration, the private media are, in the words
of an African diplomat "opposition outside parliament".
Against this background is a heavily loaded financial, moral and
political spirit of support form the British government. Her Majesty's
government spends some fifty million pounds sterling to augment the annual
national budget deficit in Sierra Leone, through various programs, not to
mention the heavily financed military, judiciary and other development
projects in the country through British NGOs.
There is already a twenty five million pounds justice project for the
next five years and another ten million pounds and more for the next three
years to address the issues of decentralization and good governance.
So far, the British government does not seem to believe that all of this
must take on board the media for a well-rounded success. Nonetheless,
through the Thompson Foundation in the UK, has offered training through
various dialogues that would have deregulated the national broadcaster-the
Sierra Leone Broadcasting Service, (SLBS). The Sierra Leone government,
however, is not in haste to take its hands-off the SLBS. A recommendation
from SLAJ and the National Privatisation Commission to restructure the SLBS
is still gathering dust on the president's shelves after he was informed
that a certain recommended individual for the job lacks the necessary paper
qualification.
In Sierra Leone, nothing is apolitical; from HIV/AIDS to Oil, everything
is Presidential.
Though it may be the wish of Her Majesty's government to present Sierra
Leone to the outside world as a success story, researches and talking to the
proverbial Sierra Leonean (especially those who do not have link with the
trappings of political power), on the ground has given a different story
altogether. A half cooked cassava is perhaps a better comparison to Sierra
Leone's present democratic system. Some of those in president Kabbah's
government were key players during the 25-year of misrule of a one party
state under the All Peoples Congress Party, APC. Thus there appears to be a
conflict between Kabbah and some of his advisers and ministers within the
government. It is a game of intrigue and supremacy between their
undemocratic stance and that of Kabbah's, with the international community
looking over the shoulders. Appearing tried and loss of interest and new
ideas, it would seem that Kabbah would have loved to leave power few months,
if not years before March 2007. This had been the rumour and the wishes for
many who believe that the honourable president has no more new ideas and
tact to rule a peaceful but highly expectant populace. "we fear a
constitutional crisis and the return of the khaki boys", says a presidential
aide in explaining why the tired president would not have an earlier
exit. And with the opposition APC party in chaos, backed by an unserious
club of political opportunistic cabal in power, it is not clear whether the
emergency of a new political class before 2007 general elections would be
the answer. Already, a club of young and talented individuals hitherto
opposed to the mess within the present status quo, have lined up to take
various positions within the ruling SLPP.
"It is never an issue of if-you-can't-beat-them-join-them. It is a fact
that to effect changes, we may have to use what the people know best. The
SLPP" says a young aspirant. But in the words of a western diplomat, perhaps
the civil society movements, disjointed though they may be, may have to be
the saving grace as we move towards 2007.
See part II next issue.

Original_Search_Title: Why NPP Failed to Win Manya Krobo Seat
Headline: Ghana: Why NPP Failed to Win Manya Krobo Seat
Body_Text:
Odumase-Krobo —  ... Lazy DCE, corrupt activists cited in post-mortem findings
Nearly four months after the New Patriotic Party (NPP) failed to win any
of the three parliamentary seats in the Krobo area of the eastern region in
the last general elections, the Lower Manya Krobo Constituency branch of the
party, still licking its wounds, is yet to come to terms with how the
National Democratic Congress (NDC)'s Michael Teye Nyaunu was able to floor
them for the third consecutive time, despite the gem of candidates it had
presented every successive election year.
In an attempt to find out the cause of the party's shocking defeat last
December, some of the bizarre revelations that popped up had to do with
disloyalty on the part of some constituency executives, bribery and above
all, acts of omission on the part of the District Chief Executive,
Hon. Andrews Kwesi Teye, which, it was believed, the opposition NDC
capitalized on during the campaign.
The findings, some of which were openly discussed at an emergency
executive meeting at the Liberty Hotel last Sunday, nearly caused a stir as
angry members called for the heads of those they believed were responsible
for their downfall.
Describing the DCE as lazy and ineffective, the scores of polling
station chairpersons present said billions of cedis worth of projects
financed and completed by government in the district were not commissioned
for use by the people, and this allegedly made the people believe that they
were indeed NDC projects.
The people were therefore waiting for Professor John Evans Atta-Mills to
assume office as the president to commission the projects for them.
One aggrieved activist and chairman of the Obopah electoral area in the
Middle Belt zone, Mr. Tetteh Kwami, said a nine-bedroom teachers' bungalow
with toilet, kitchen and bathroom , was completed long before the election,
but the assembly failed to commission it for use, while teachers continued
to sleep in bamboo houses.
He cited also a six-classroom block with library meant for the Jakiti
Primary School, which was completed long before the elections but had not
been commissioned till now.
"My brother, the children are still learning in the sun while the new
building is there. The NDC campaigned with it that it was not Kufuor's
project and that was why the NPP would not dare to commission it. The people
believed the message and voted for the NDC," he told The Chronicle.
According to Tetteh Kwami, an expensive cassava-processing machine
brought to the area to alleviate poverty among women was still idle because
the district assembly could not acquire a small plot for its
installation.
Supporting the claim, another member, Angmor, said for several months
now, a half-billion cedis 24-seater water closet toilet facility at the
Odumase-Krobo main lorry park had been completed alongside a 12-seater
toilet at Lasii, but the people had not been told when the masses would be
given the go ahead to use it.
The Chronicle reporter was taken also to another HIPC-funded brand new
12-seater toilet at Manya Kpongunor, which was completed since November last
year, and had been left at the mercy of the weather and reptiles.
Party members strongly believe that if people were made to believe that
they were NPP-initiated projects, the election outcome would have been
different, more so when the gap was a mere 3,000 votes.
Mr. Elias Aduimado, assistant constituency secretary of the party, was
also not happy that two clear months after government had asked for a 30%
upward adjustment of transport fares, Hon. Andrews Teye had not been able to
enforce the directive, and had given commercial drivers a field day to
charge arbitrarily.
He observed that many commuters in the district had been blaming the
president for it and this had therefore made government more unpopular in
the district even after the elections, and fumed that the DCE was
ineffective.
He argued that it was the duty of the president's representative in the
district to explain government's policies to the people at the grassroots
level and also enforce directives.
Accusing fingers were also pointed at some corrupt members who had
allegedly collected bribes from the opposition camps and sold vital
documents, information and strategies to their candidates.
The disciplinary committee and a large chunk of the constituency
executive have, in series of letters, called on the region to bring to
order, the constituency chairman, Mr. Padi Asime, and secretary, Peter
O. Paditey, alias 'Pop Darl' for what it referred to as negligence of
duty.
Responding, Nana Adi Ankamah, eastern regional chairman of the party,
called for calm so that the issues raised would be thoroughly looked
into.
He considered that he was aware before the elections that there was
division among the constituency executive, with only a faction giving its
full support to the parliamentary candidate, Mrs. Difie Dedo Agyarko Kusi's
campaign team.
"If there had been cooperation between the executive here and the
campaign team, the election would have been won in the constituency hands
down," Nana stressed.
Adi Ankamah told the paper in an exclusive interview that he, as the
regional chairman, was more concerned with the reorganization of the party
in the wards, polling stations, constituencies, regions and to the national
level than blaming individuals for electoral defeats.
Asked whether any punitive action would be taken against anyone whose
action or inaction directly cost the party some parliamentary seats, Ankama
said what was important was to identify the problems and make sure they
don't happen again in the 2008 elections.
For her part, Mrs. Kusi thanked members for their support and debunked
rumours that members of her campaign team had built mansions out of her
parliamentary bid.
"I knew there were problems somewhere along the line but we don't have
to blame anybody. Of course, I spent a lot of money, but this is the time to
forge ahead as a party and look towards 2008," she advised.
The Lower Manya Krobo constituency is one of the three in Kroboland,
where the Danquah-Busia tradition had never won any election in the
political history of this country, even though it continued to register
consistent progress.
In 1996, the NPP's Lt. Col. Lawrence Kudjiku polled 6,000 votes, while
Henry Dema-Narh polled 9,000 four years later, but they both lost to
Hon. Nyaunu. For the first time, the party fielded a woman, Dedo, in last
year's election, and even though she polled a record-breaking 15,000 votes
in that 'difficult' terrain and lost marginally, many political observers
felt she had campaigned well enough to have won the seat.

Original_Search_Title: Rumor of Arms Container Causes Stir
Headline: Liberia: Rumor of Arms Container Causes Stir
Body_Text:
Monrovia — As citizens are yet to overcome the apprehension and fear about the
past war years, every unusual incident tends to cause grave alarm as
was the case of a truck belonging to the Bridgeway Corporation, that
arrived at the Paynesville Red-light early morning at a very high
speed.
The truck bearing license plate number 037 with series number 2638
and carrying a 20-foot container labeled BCC-1-20, driven by one Musa
Kamara created serious suspicion by bye-passing the Mt. Barclay check
point at an excessive speed. The situation caused the UNMIL and
Liberian security personnel as well as residents and marketers to
become stunned with suspicion.
Explaining the incident, UNMIL GhanBatt 2 Commander at the Mt.
Barclay checkpoint, Lt. B.B Pantoah said the action of the driver was
what caused marketers and residents to throng the Zone-5 depot 1
police station yesterday morning.
Lt. Pantoah said at about 7:30 a.m yesterday, the truck moving at top
speed passed through the security checkpoint despite their beckoning
of the driver to stop for the regular and momentary check, thereby,
prompting him to send the soldiers under his command after the truck.
He said the truck hit the back of one of the parked vehicles but no
one got killed during the incident and the soldiers pursued him
(driver) and finally apprehended him and turned him over to the
Liberia National Police along with the passengers and the truck.
He said information gathered further revealed that Mr. Kamara while
en route to Monrovia even refused to stop at the Careysburg
checkpoint.
Lt. Pantoah said, "because of the act, the driver (Kamara) slept in
the bush and was using the early house the run through the checkpoint
to reach Monrovia for fear that the truck would be searched and the
Indian hemps would be discovered".
He said even when the driver was caught he could not say the actual
reasons for his act adding "most cars pass through the checkpoint
without slowing down".
He recounted; that a vehicle running at top speed at the double
bridge Killed one of their solder instantly, another vehicle at a
high speed nearly hit them at the Mt. Barclay checkpoint and now we
nearly got killed.
When the driver was later taken out of the cell to identify some of
the items on the truck, he told reporters that he regrets the
situation he had caused.
He said the checkpoint jammed him after passing a deep curb in that
area, with the speed he was moving he could not check his breaks.
Mr. Kamara said the problem was that he along with another truck
driver were racing at high speed and while approaching the checkpoint
he took the left side outside the checkpoint but because of the
action he got afraid to stop.
He said the continued until he reached Red light. Later he noticed
that the security were chasing him along with some angry citizens so
he passed the police depot and went in around the Gerlib Clinic area
where he finally stopped and began to run but he was caught.
Meanwhile, the police along with the help of UNMIL were able to curb
the violence that was about to occur while the truck was taken to the
Zone-5 depot 1 police station in Red light.
The container was later opened in the mist of observers, marketers
and journalists but as was perceived that arms was on board, nothing
of such y but only 120 pieces of planks, 10 empty fuel drums, two
bags of dried cassava, six bags of coal and a traveling bag
(displaced) fill with marijuana (Grass) were downloaded from the
container.
According to the driver, the truck was coming from River Gee County
through Saclepea with two ladies, one identified as Comfort and the
other Cecelia Vaye along with two men.
The truck, passengers and driver are presently at the police central
headquarters awaiting investigation.
Meanwhile the Management of the Bridgeway Corporation has received
information that one of its trucks along with its driver had been
arrested by the Police for evading a security checkpoint while en
route to Monrovia from the Southeast.
Following a thorough check, some narcotic drugs were reportedly
discovered by the police. They also found out that the drugs belong
to an illegal passenger who boarded the truck from the southeast.
The Bridgeway Management wishes to state that the act of the driver
whom police said escaped is an isolated one, which does not represent
its business practice.
The management also wishes to inform the public that besides the
drugs found on the truck, there was absolutely no other material
discovered by the security forces and it must be understood in this
contest.
This clarification has become necessary to dispel rumors surrounding
the arrest of the Bridgeway truck at the Red light Community.

Original_Search_Title: DCE Exonerated of Blowing of ¢billions in Ykda
Headline: Ghana: DCE Exonerated of Blowing of Â¢billions in Ykda
Body_Text:
- Anti-crusaders urged to smoke peace-pipe
AS PRESIDENT JOHN Agyekum Kufuor is about to name the District Chief
Executives (DCEs) to join the board of Positive Change Chapter Two, one of
the DCEs who is likely to be rejoicing and hoping for re-appointment is,
Hon. Christian Kofi Tetteh of the Yilo Krobo District in the Eastern Region,
following his exoneration from accusations of 'blowing billions of cedis.'
Hon. Tettey, who was accused of corruption and maladministration, has
been exonerated by the committee set up by the Commission for Human Rights
and Administrative Justice (CHRAJ) to conduct intensive investigations into
the issues raised by the anti-corruption and maladministration crusader,
