Mr. Alex Narh Adjirakor.
The anti-crusader who doubles as the assembly member, was urged to smoke
the peace pipe with the DCE and the top officers of the assembly in order to
entrench peace and harmony in the running of the assembly.
"The complainant, Alex Narh Adjirakor, and the DCE, Christian Kofi
Tetteh, should be made to smoke the peace pipe in order to entrench peace
and harmony in the work of the Yilo Krobo Assembly," declared CHRAJ.
The CHRAJ report came in the wake of media reports that serialized
stories alleging that the DCE and top administrative officials of Yilo Krobo
were corrupt and inefficient after Mr. Adjirakor, an assembly member
launched a complaint to the CHRAJ.
In pursuance of the directives of Dr. P.E. Bondzie-Simpson, the Director
of Legal and Investigations, a team of investigators comprising
Ms. Elizabeth Kufuor, Senior Principal Investigator and Mr. Derrick
Ewudzie-Odoom, investigator, were designated to conduct investigations into
the allegations of corruption and maladministrative practices by the DCE and
the other top officials of the Assembly.
The two-man committee was of the view that the DCE is expected to remain
in office to continue his good work but with the co-operation of those who
harbour misgivings against him.
The Chronicle learnt that CHRAJ's recommendation flowed from, among
other things, the fact that most of the allegations made by the complainant
were proven to be unfounded while others showed the DCE had acted
legitimately but his actions had been misunderstood .A typical example was
the allegation that the DCE and the District Engineer had forged the
signature of one John Lartey, proprietor of Mpaebo Carpentry Works, to "take
some monies in his name" The DCE "did not collaborate with the District
Engineer to forge the signature of Mr. John Lartey, the complainant may have
to produce evidence to the contrary," declared CHRAJ.
On the allegation that he had coerced some villagers to cede their land
for an assembly project, CHIRAJ says, he (DCE) "did not coerce landlords at
Klo-Begoro to enter into an agreement with Love Enterprises over the mining
of limestone."
Touching on the complainant's allegation that the DCE had awarded the
construction of the Sra Market on contract without putting it on tender, as
required by law, the finding confirmed it, but took note of the fact that it
was not put on tender "because the assembly had been dissolved". On another
aspect of the allegation, that cost of the project was later on varied,
CHRAJ disproved it, saying, ".. .the cost of the project was Â¢11 6million
and there were no variations."
The refurbishment of the district coordinating director's office
building and bungalow was another bone of contention which Mr. Adjirakor had
charged that payment for the job had been effected before completion, as
such, the contract was fraught with corruption.
Though the full payment of Â¢38million was done before the completion,
"there was no ground to accuse the respondents of any wrong doing".
The CHRAJ's investigations into further allegation that Â¢12m had been
withdrawn from the Poverty Alleviation Fund (PAF) improperly, revealed that
it was untrue but was quick to state that the coincidence of all
beneficiaries of the PAF being polling station chairmen of the ruling party,
however "raises eyebrows over the withdrawal."
This paper has gathered that by far, the most serious lapse on the part
of the DCE and his officials, as the investigators showed, had nothing to do
with the renovation of the Old Somanya market. CHRAJ found out that the
contract have been put on tender, an omission considered a contravention of
sub-regulations 2(a) and (b) of Local Government (District Tender Boards
Establishment) Regulations 1995, LI 1606.
The respondents had explained to CHRAJ that they employed direct labour
to reduce cost. And though they spent less than Â¢600million to complete the
project, the AESL, when called to estimate, put the cost at Â¢1
.2billion.
Nonetheless, CHRAJ recommended that the DCE "should be cautioned and
impressed upon to follow laid down procedure in the award of contracts." All
the allegations leveled against Christian Tetteh, his former District
Coordinating Director, District Budget Officer, District Engineer, Former
District Finance Officer and current Finance Officer totaled nine, which
sought to vindicate the DCE.
Meanwhile, the remaining accusations concerning the execution of a
street light project at Somanya, cladding of 12 classroom blocks, a PSI
cassava project and a drainage system under the Village Infrastructure
Project, the last having to do with an alleged payment of a contractor,
"leading to the loss of the payment of withholding tax," the officers in all
these, were either found to have committed no offence, or CHRAJ recommended
that further investigations be conducted or projects executed should be
inspected.

Original_Search_Title: Ghana Within Japanese Foreign Policy: A Basic Outline
Headline: Ghana Within Japanese Foreign Policy: A Basic Outline
Body_Text:
Relations between Ghana and Japan appear to have taken a dramatic
improvement on the already cordial and fraternal interactions that have
existed since the days of independence.
The reasons for this improvement are myriad, ranging from the personal
style of the immediate past Japanese Ambassador to Ghana, Her Excellency
Mrs. Kazuko Asai right to the commitment of the Koizumi-led government to
promote Japanese foreign policy through increased support and interaction
with developing countries.
Within the ambit and parameters of these two extremes, one can also
decipher a determination by the Republic of Ghana to further the cause of
partnership by playing its commensurate and complementary role as far as the
promotion of the Ghanaian developmental agenda is concerned.
The most striking feature of the improved relations between the two
countries is the decision by the Government of Japan to write off the debts
owed her by Ghana. It is important to know that Japan had hitherto, desisted
from providing financial support to countries that sign up to the Highly
Indebted Poor Countries initiative (HIPC) for reasons best known to
itself.
In any case, it does make sense that there was no justification for
extending funds to countries that are highly indebted and still expect them
to pay up.
Moreover, each country is free to determine how to promote its foreign
policy in consonance with its own inclinations, resource endowments and
national interest within the complex and increasingly competitive global
environment. Thus, no one can fault the Government of Japan for choosing to
abstain from supporting countries that go HIPC.
Notwithstanding the above, Japan, having been convinced of the sincerity
of the Government of Ghana to promote the interests of the Ghanaian people
and determined to ameliorate the plight of Ghana as far as the debt burden
was concerned through pragmatic measures, went ahead and made a dramatic
U-turn that resulted in the conversion of Ghana's debts to Japan to grants
and in the final analysis cancelled completely.
It is on record that Japan has so far cancelled $1billion of debts owed
her by Ghana! This is important within the context of the reliefs that have
accrued to the Government and people of Ghana. It is a known fact that today
the Government of Ghana enjoys a respite that allows it to use its relief
funds for the provision of both social and economic infrastructure in the
form of schools, hospitals and roads among other things.
To give a more specific example, the Government of Japan is presently
financing a stretch of the Cape-Coast Kasoa road to the relief of motorists
and by extension, the benefit of the entire West African sub region, given
that the road forms part of the trans-West African route.
This is a tangible demonstration of inter-governmental support.
What makes this even more interesting is the fact that the immediate
past Ambassador of Japan spent her very last weekend in Ghana visiting the
construction site to ensure that both governments get value for money- that
the contractors work to specification.
This goes to confirm that the commitment of the Government of Japan is
genuine and focused as far as the developmental needs and relations with
Ghana are concerned. The question then is to ask what exactly Ghana is doing
to complement these very laudable efforts by its major development partner
Japan?
In order to fully comprehend the enormity of what Ghana must deal with,
one has to examine the socio-political as well as economic cultural norms of
Japan.
POLITICAL CULTURE
Confucianism (its emphasis was on MORALITY, COLLECTIVISM, HARMONY AND
ELITISM by which a leader had to exhibit high moral standards in order to
induce the populace to behave similarly among other stipulations) influenced
Japan although it has some variations as compared to the Chinese model.
The concept of family is different because it is more of a cooperative
entity than one of close bonds. The most striking features include:
OBEDIENCE to authority- there is a submissive attitude of subordinates.
SELF-SACRIFICE- this makes suicide very high especially when failure is
the end result of strenuous efforts to promote success (also known as
Bushido).
HARMONY- the emphasis in Japan is on the fact that everybody should know
his/her role and status in society, which was one of the basis for the
Japanese economic miracle. Unfortunately, Western individualistic tendencies
are creeping into the Japanese culture with alarming rapidity.
HIGHLY MOTIVATED WORKFORCE- the Japanese have a penchant for working
long hours without necessarily demanding more or complaining.
The Japanese run a statist economy that is heavily dependent on the
relations between the businessmen, bureaucrats and politicians. The people
are also known to be savings-inclined, saving up to 30% of their
income.
Alternatively, another opinion, a pessimistic one holds that the spirit
for restructuring is not there since the recession of the 1980's so unless
this is done, nothing positive would come out. Just like the purge of 1.3
million Samurais and Daimyos in the pre-war era and 200,000 officials after
the war all bequeathed fundamental growth, it was expected that a
fundamental transformation would take place.
Reasons for this optimism are hinged on the small business entities that
are creative, innovative and successful enough to compete with others on the
international scene.
In other words, they would lead to an innovation driven rather than a
state sponsored growth for Japan. KEIRETSUS (they are groups of firms coming
together to be financed by one bank) were established with state created
banks advancing loans to the companies without adequate collateral security,
thereby being partly responsible for the economic and financial collapse of
the 1980's.
In 1997-98, Prime Minister Hashimoto implemented the "Big Bang Reforms"
which were meant to reduce the bureaucracy especially in the Ministry of
Finance. Two divisions were created to change the conservative nature of the
Ministry and these resulted in economic recovery.
Foreign direct investment also started to increase to about $10.5
billion in 1998 with new techniques, skills, mergers etc taking place within
the broader framework of global company expansions.
Although some structures have been created to limit the Finance
Ministry's activities, the reforms are supposed to be comprehensive enough
to touch the entire financial sector and not simply the selective centers it
has so far affected. It was also envisaged that the bureaucracy would be
reduced in power to so as to promote a freer market.
However, some pessimists hold that the big government spending as
exemplified in the $1.2 trillion spent between 1992-1998 is what has made
Japan plunge into recession that only a combination of reforms would
ameliorate.
Notwithstanding some difficulties in the full implementation of the new
policy objectives for the Japanese government, it has remained steadfast to
its international commitments and helped foster global development to the
best of its ability and in consonance with its resource endowments.
But international relations also facilitate quid pro quo in several
areas thus placing Ghana in a position to make the best under the give and
take regime.
It is a fact that Ghana has remodeled its foreign policy to engender
friendly relations with its immediate neighbors but more importantly, to
take its rightful place within the comity of nations.
This presupposes that Ghana is very likely to support Japan's bid for
permanent membership of the United Nations Security Council, for the simple
reason that Japan has contributed both its quota and mirth for the
betterment of the lot of the world body without deriving as much in return
comparatively.
There is no gainsaying the fact that there is tremendous prestige
associated with permanent membership of the Security Council moreso when it
exercises veto powers on matters of grave concern.
It remains to be seen however how the dynamics of global politics would
unfold in the near future to facilitate such displays of inter-governmental
and bilateral cooperation between Ghana and Japan.
At the domestic level, it is not far fetched to assert that Japan would
like to see further improvements in the macro and micro economic indicators
of Ghana as justification for the enormous resources they have poured into
Ghana despite our recourse to HIPC.
To put it bluntly, it would greatly help the Japanese tax payer to dole
out even larger sums if evidence can be adduced that Ghana has made good use
of the resources received from across the oceans especially in the health,
water and sanitation sectors among others.
As has been argued by Her Excellency Mrs. Kazuko Asai, Ghana continues
to be a beacon of hope and an oasis of stability in a sea of chaos, thus
poised to display economic growth like some Asian countries recorded between
1981 and 2003 of which Malaysia remains the best quoted example.
If Ghana is to place itself as a viable business partner of countries
like Japan, then it stands to reason that there would necessarily have to be
a radical transformation of attitudes aimed at promoting a truly
business-friendly environment.
It is a known fact that non-Ghanaians remain frustrated by the
proverbial "Ghana Man's Time" by which lateness appears to have become an
acceptable norm rather than an exception.
For people whose claim to fame is their punctuality and workaholic
attributes, the Japanese are more likely to be extremely frustrated by this
worrisome traditional practice of Ghanaians which is tantamount to saying
"we shall develop at our own pace", as if the whole world would be on
mark-time awaiting Ghanaians to finish consuming their last morsels of
cassava based fufu!
Furthermore, knowing how Japan has been in the forefront of promoting
environmental issues, it stands to reason that they would be more than
elated to see how far Ghana has practicalized the tenets espoused in the
Kyoto Protocol while promoting ozone friendly environmental practices.
There is simply no reason why Ghana should lose its greenery and
enviable forests among others ostensibly because of lack of resources: after
all a latent reason for which some of our debts have been forgiven is to
reduce the pressure on the extraction and exploitation of Ghana's natural
resources so as to conserve and preserve our lush environment for our own
sustenance and more importantly, for generations yet unborn.
Far more important in the scheme of relations between Ghana and Japan
would be the recourse of Ghanaians to the spirit of self-reliance. We need
not pretend that in the promotion of foreign policies and national
interests, countries are not wont to have other members of the international
community emulating tried and tested theories with respect to developmental
issues.
Given that Japan has been largely self-reliant right from the days of
the Meiji Restoration of 1869 to the present era, it stands to reason that
this major development partner would be delighted to see the spirit of
self-reliance taking root in Ghana and eventually becoming the organizing
principle around which our developmental program would progress.
This necessarily includes a very strong leadership, perhaps modeled
along the lines of Daimyos and Samurais in their socio-economic capacity,
focused and fully apprised of the needs of the Ghanaian peoples, vis-Ã -vis
the dynamics of the global village so as to maximize Ghana's aspirations
within the shortest possible time.
Such a leadership would have to be decisive in its decisiveness,
resolute in its resolution and determined in its determination to once again
place Ghana as a leader of the African peoples in their quest to assert
their politico-economic independence in this 21st century.
Without any doubt, such leadership must be strong enough as to eschew
any tendency towards the promotion of politically expedient actions that in
the long run would undermine Ghana's developmental program.
It is then and only then that countries like Japan would be unreservedly
inclined to dole out funds that would help Ghana extricate itself from the
throes of poverty and dependency knowing very well that donor fatigue is
indeed setting in.
In any case, we cannot circumvent the fact that the G8 has come to the
inescapable conclusion that dependency as a theory, practice and organizing
principle of whichever world order dominates is anathema to the attainment
of the Millennium Development Goals and at best, a discredited, derelict and
deficient policy best consigned to the dark days of the 20th century.
Whatever efforts the Republic of Ghana would make to maximize its
interests within the parameters of Japanese foreign policy must take
cognizance of the interests of Japanese bureaucrats, Ministry of
International Trade and Industry (MITI) and the several other entities that
are poised to make Japan maintain a lead within the dictates of modern day
globalization.

Original_Search_Title: Ghanaians Overseas Asked to Increase Contribution to Economic Development
Headline: Ghanaians Overseas Asked to Increase Contribution to Economic Development
Body_Text:
Ghanaians in the Diaspora have been called upon to take advantage of the
favourable political climate at home to contribute their quota towards
socio-economic development.
Mr. Alfred Sallia Fawundu, former UNDP Resident Representative in Guinea
Bissau, Benin and Ghana, said Ghana had set the pace for national
development and Ghanaians abroad should therefore mobilise both human and
material resources to invest in various productive ventures to create jobs
for the large army of unemployed youth.
Mr. Fawundu was speaking at a seminar on, "Developing Ghana From Afar",
which was aimed at exploring ways of involving Ghanaians in the Diaspora in
national development. It was organised by the Cambridge University Ghanaian
Society on the University campus.
He expressed regret about the mass migration of Ghanaian academics and
professionals to the so-called greener pastures, explaining that the
practice was being re-enforced by remittances from the immigrants to
relatives and friends at home.
Mr. Fawundu said although such monies were used for the payment of
school fees, hospital bills and other purposes that were not the effective
means of contributing to the growth of the economy.
He said the country required the support of Ghanaians abroad to enable
it achieve the Millennium Development Goals aimed at eradicating poverty,
hunger, diseases, improved education and the prevention of the spread of
HIV/AIDS, among other objectives.
He said sustained commitment of government to economic and institutional
reforms enhanced the country's reputation as an emerging viable investment
destination. It also signals that Ghana was gradually becoming an ideal
place for investment.
Mr. Fawundu said the implementation of the various presidential
initiatives such as those on cassava, salt, palm oil and garment further
indicated Ghana's commitment to diversifying the export base and creating
job opportunities.

Original_Search_Title: Standards: Minister Canvasses More Responsiveness
Headline: Nigeria: Standards: Minister Canvasses More Responsiveness
Body_Text:
Lagos — National standards bodies in Africa have been charged to be more responsive to the material and human needs of the Africa Organization for Standardization (ARSO), the umbrella body for Standards Institutes in the continent in order to empower it to meet the desires of its stakeholders.
The Minsiter of industry, Ambassador Magaji Muhammed who gave this charge recently in a goodwill messega presented at the opening of the 13th General Assemble of ARSO held in Accra, Ghana, stated that the body should be dynamic and proactive to play an effective role of coordination and facilitation of standardization activities in the continent.
He commented the leadership of ARSO and its secretariat for re-engineering through constitutional review, stressing that is was a sure way to secure the future or trade and commerce in Africa through proper coordination of standardization for more effective cross border trade as well as economic integration.
Ambassador Muhammed enumerated NigeriaÃ­s efforts to promote standardization in 2004 alone as including elaboration of over 500 product Stadards including cassava and cassava products, adoption over 4000 standards of the American Society for Testing Methods and over 200 of the International Electro-Technical Commission.
The Minister disclosed that an offshore conformity Assessment programme (SONCAP) of products meant for the Nigeria market form all over the world had just been commenced by standards Organization of Nigeria to reduce the incidence of dumping of sub-standard goods in the country.
He further charged ARSO to ensure greater involvement and participation of Entrepreneurs and the business community in its activities, stressing that the inauguration of the yearly African Standardization forum in Ghana was a commendable first towards this.
Ambassador Muhammed expressed appreciation to the Government and people of Ghana for hositng the ARSO General Assembly open, Vice President of Ghana, Alhaji Aliu Mahama described poverty as not only lack of resources, but also the lack of opportunities and choice to improve quality of life.
Alhaji Mahama stated that AfricaÃ­s attempts at reducing poverty requires a great deal of imagination and recommended that emphasis be laid on standardization in designing initiative on poverty reduction in Africa.
The Ghana Vice President comment the annual Standardization forum inaugurated by ARSO but charge it to identify more ways to promote Standardization and its related activities like Quality Assurance (Confroming assessment Accredtitaion as well as Metrology in Africa.
He then charge Africa Governments to provide the basic infrastructure. Physical and human, to make standardization work for Africa as in other continents.

Original_Search_Title: Three Perish After Eating Cassava Leaves
Headline: Tanzania: Three Perish After Eating Cassava Leaves
Body_Text:
Arusha — Three children died last Friday night at Engarenaibor village of Monduli
district after a Spartan meal of cassava leaves with ugali.
According to Arusha Regional Police Commander (RPC), James Kombe, the
incident happened when the three children had supper prepared by their
mother.
The victims were identified as Ngosangale Naitochi (8), Meresi Naitochi
(7) and Sitata aged three years old. The deceased are from one family.
The children's mother also suffered stomach pains and collapsed. A
neighbour's child identified as Ndibai Pakasi also suffered similar
pains. Both were admitted at Nguambo dispensary and their condition was
reported to be improving.
The RPC said investigations were being held to determine the type of
cassava that caused death.

Original_Search_Title: Fake Ampicillin Syrup Producers Nab Bed
Headline: Nigeria: Fake Ampicillin Syrup Producers Nab Bed
Body_Text:
Kano — Four persons have been arrested by the police in Kano in connection with the fake production of Ampicillin syrup for children.
Police sources confirmed that the four suspects produced the fake Ampicillin syrup by mixing custard powder with alubo (fermented but dried cassava powder) thereby causing harm to little children.
ASP Gambo Mohammed who is police public relations officer, Zone One police headquartres in Kano, who confirmed the report said the label on the Ampicillin bottle indicated that the product was made in Enugu and Onitsha and that the actual fact was that the suspects were using yam flour and custard powder.
He said that the suspects and the buyers of the drug all of who reside in Sabon Gari market would be handed over to NAFDAC for further investigation and prosecution.
One of the buyers said he bought one carton of the syrup and that he suspected the drug was fake and therefore raised alarm.
In his words: "Jude (one of the suspects) sold one carton of the Ampicillin to me at the cost of N4,000. I gave him a deposit of N1,800. Then I noticed that the medicine was fake after one bottle broke while I was trying to lift the carton".

Original_Search_Title: Buying up Nigeria
Headline: Nigeria: Buying up Nigeria
Body_Text:
On the issue of government and private sector, there can be no angel and
devil scenario.
LAST week Monday the logical process to the privatisation programme took
place in Abuja, when a group of well heeled Nigerian entrepreneurs presented
President Olusegun Obasanjo with their blue print to setup a mega
corporation, with an initial value of 14 billion Naira. According to the
leader of the team Mrs. Ndi Okereke-Onyiuke, which also had Festus Odimegwu,
Aliko Dangote, Jim Ovia and Jacob Moyo Ajekigbe, Otunba Funsho Lawal,
amongst others, the aim of the group is " borne out of President Obasanjo's
original idea of a mega corporation built by Nigerians and owned by all
Nigerians with the sole mission of creating a globally competitive
industrial and business conglomerate which will supply world class products
and services to local and international markets."
On paper it sounds like a very good idea. If others are doing it for
their nations, why not Nigerians. But the devil is in the details. Creating
a transnational corporation is an old idea, in fact as old as the practice
of capitalism itself, which is about, if not more than 500 years as an
ideological system. Since Obasanjo came to power in 1999, the administration
had worked tirelessly on the illusion that foreign investments would come
with 'democracy', especially, with him leading Nigeria. For years, the
President had pleaded, demanded, promised, even cajoled his friends to bring
their investment, make money and help create job opportunities and reduce
social pressure to no avail. Now it seems in the second term, a new wisdom
and policy has taken over.
It is perhaps in this regard that the Onyiuke group wants government
concession to handle large projects like the West African pipeline,
Trans-Saharan pipeline, the supply of cassava feeds to Western Europe, it
wants the opportunity to invest in electricity and refineries. The message
is clear: Nigerian capitalists want to own and run the economy of their
country.
If only it is that simple. Those involved in this project are Nigerians
known for their enterprise, but notwithstanding their track record the
issues involved are slightly more complicated, and may not be as straight as
it might appear in the first place. To begin with this government since it
came into office has not hidden its biases for big business, and has earned
the description as government of big business, for big business. So, it is
no surprise that it is in such good company with the promoters of this
project. Dangote was the single highest donor for the reelection of
President Obasanjo and Onyiuke led corporate Nigeria to make the largest
donation, in violation of the electoral law against corporate citizens
making large political donations. Till date nothing has happened to Onyiuke
and her group, rather her relationship with the administration seems to be
getting chummier.
Private sector corruption
The idea of encouraging Nigerians to take up the challenge of developing
the local economy would seem attractive in the face of indifference of
foreigners. Especially, when it is known that anyone who takes the risk to
invest in Nigeria, stands to make enormous profits unimaginable in any other
part of the world. However, the real question is, does that solve the
problem of building an efficient and better performing economy? This is a
moot point and here is why. The Nigerian private sector always makes the
point about its being better at managing things, that may be true within the
context of a Nigerian state system that is venal, vicious and
incompetent. But as everyone knows corruption has thrived so successfully
because in several ways the private sector operators have been a willing
ally. Where it has colluded, it did so conscious of what it is doing. Where
it took advantage, it did so with the intention of promoting its narrow
interest to the detriment of the larger national aspiration.
The fact of the case is that some of the local entrepreneurs are merely
fronts for those in power. Therefore, despite whatever claims the private
sector can make, it has been opportunitistic in taking advantage of
govern-ment's inadequacies to advance its special interest. Thus, on the
issue of government and private sector, there can be no angel and devil
scenario. Both have when it suits them cohabited to shortchange the
people. And on this issue of mega corporation a Frankenstein is about being
created.
To any careful analyst the business of allowing private sector take over
the economy means they are placed at a position to influence how the state
is organised, and what it does. The trouble with this is that to a large
extent the character of business, not just locally but globally is profit
maximisation, at minimum cost. For a fringe economy like Nigeria in a global
system with weak institutions, it is anything goes. Multi-national
corporations in Nigeria, may not have played overt role in shaping policy in
Nigeria, but its influence covertly is awesome.
Since government in Nigeria has failed on several accounts to meet its
obligation, the common view is: "Give it to the private sector, they can do
it better." That seems a sound argument, but cannot possibly be the most
plausible. If government shows will, it can do it. But before any Nigerian
government can find the right aptitude to succeed, it has to be
de-constructed. The Nigeria state system was bequeathed to local operators
by British colonialists in 1960, with a defined agenda, and so far nothing
has changed. It is for the same reason that ideologically, the Nigerian
private sector has the same orientation as the Nigerian state. It sees
Nigeria as the battering ram to milk all the juices, and export to the
metropolitan capitals of the West. Contrary to posturing Nigeria private
sector has thrived amidst the chaos, loopholes and confusion in the system;
many of them cannot survive in well structured and regulated environment,
and they know it.
Examples for elsewhere One of the clever ruses of the private sector is
the mantra of free flow of capitals, or free entry and free exit. Countries
like South Korea and China for instance bar substantial capital export,
notwithstanding the level of investment. An investor in South Korea cannot
export more than 2 per cent of his profits, the rest is invested
locally. That policy has not stopped Western investors from going
there.
To mention such an idea here is to provoke the wrath of the private
sector and the reason is simple: just like the State operatives, they feel
comfortable when the return on their investment circulates around Western
institutions. A particular example of capital flight is in the
telecommunication sector, where vast profits are made, but little of it is
re-invested, rather most of it is taken abroad.
Such a thing cannot happen in China, which is the world fastest growing
economy. The real question to ask is: why is that? The Chinese success is
beyond the scope of this piece, but the point has been made that it is
possible. A focused and patriotic government can move mountains, if it has
the will and vision. And such a government will find the right private
sector to do like wise. Otherwise, the abuses and exploitation of Nigeria
will never end no matter the intention of those in charge of a particular
scheme.
Mr. Odili is a staff of Vanguard Newspapers.

Original_Search_Title: 200 Youths Get Scholarship Awards
Headline: Nigeria: 200 Youths Get Scholarship Awards
Body_Text:
Gombe — NO fewer than 200 youths of Itaka Community in Etung local government area of Cross River State have received scholarship awards from their representative in the State House of Assembly, Hon. Innocent Okey.
Also, women of the community had a garri-processing factory constructed for them by Hon. Okey, thus bringing to an end problems they go through to process cassava for garri.
The benefitting youths groups, and women disclosed these when they paid a solidarity visit to Hon. Okey at the state assembly complex.
They also commended him for an on-going public toilets project constructed for the awards of Etung by the legislator as well as the provision of a power generation set and two hair barbing clippers for the Itaka youths.
Also in the team where the people of Ntighi ward the direct family lineage of the legislator, led by Elder Emmanuel Enya and the secretary, Comrade Emmanuel Etta Ndum, who apart from commending him for effective representative, implored him to influence the emergence of the next councillor from their area.
The people complained that Ntigbi lineage has not produced an elected or appointed councillor since local governments were created, except for Hon. Okey, a member of the state assembly.
They noted that other lineage had produced several councillors and legislators, but that their situation was different.
Responding, Hon. Okey thanked the Itaka people of the love they expressed as well as the encouragement given to him and promised not to betray them.
As chairman of the House Committee on Education, Hon. Okey said he would encourage Itaka youths to be educated so as to qualify for employment opportunities in the state.

Original_Search_Title: Ivorians Scared to Return to Volatile 'Wild West'
Headline: Cote d'Ivoire: Cote D Ivoire-Liberia: Ivorians Scared to Return to Volatile 'Wild West'
Body_Text:
Loguatuo — In this small Liberian village just over the border from Cote d'Ivoire,
one out of every 10 people is a French-speaking Ivorian refugee fleeing the
country's volatile "Wild West."
"Help us please," said Eugenie Lambe, a 24-year-old who last November
fled western Cote d'Ivoire to Liberia along with her grandmother, mother and
eight brothers and sisters, fearing a new flare-up of fighting in the
country's two-and-a-half-year conflict.
The hilly "Wild West" straddling Liberia and Cote d'Ivoire remains a
region of tension and fear.
"Whenever white people come here we tell them of our suffering. They ask
questions, but when they leave they do nothing to help," Lambe said.
While tens of thousands of Liberian refugees are still living in Cote
d'Ivoire despite a year and a half of peace in Liberia, a mass of frightened
people fled in the other direction late last year. A total 3,406 Ivorians
turned up here in tiny Loguatuo, seeking assistance.
By now most have moved on inland to the Saclepea refugee camp housing
thousands of Ivorians and Sierra Leoneans, but 346 are still housed in the
village of 3,000 people, said local Red Cross member Michael Towon.
"They live with families, but people here are poor and there isn't
enough food or medicine to go around," he said.
Eugenie Lambe and her family fled Man, the main town in western Cote
d'Ivoire, when President Laurent Gbagbo launched an air assault on 4
November to pave the way for a ground offensive to dislodge rebels that have
controlled the northern half of the country since the outbreak of war in
September 2002.
"There were rumours Gbagbo was going to bomb Man after he bombed Bouake
(the north-eastern rebel stronghold) and that the rebels would kill anyone
suspected of infiltrating their ranks," said Lambe's 39-year-old mother
Martine Don.
"We couldn't stay there any longer. We walked through the forest for
several days eating fresh cassava until we reached Loguatuo."
Her husband also fled but has not yet reappeared.
Food shortages
"I want to stay here because the people speak Yacouba," Don added. "An
old woman has lent us a hut to sleep in, but we have to work for days in the
fields to get any rice."
However, a lack of food is not enough to persuade the mother and
daughter to return home.
"Even if there's nothing to eat, at least we feel safe here," said
Lambe. "At home you can't feel sure even for a moment. We've seen too many
atrocities. We can't go back now, we have nothing left there."
Loguatuo's only nurse says that 35 percent of the patients seeking care
at the local clinic come from the Ivorian side of the border.
Over the border in Cote d'Ivoire, in the rebel-held region of Danane
that is home to an estimated 385,000 people, only two out of four hospitals
are open and war or staff shortages have destroyed or closed 27 health
centres.
Stalked by war for more than a decade, the Ivorian west is a volatile
place.
There are few men visible nowadays in the rebel-held villages, with
those residents left behind saying the men have either gone to fight, or
fled for fear of being recruited.
The area is also a haven for arms smugglers and unemployed ex-Liberian
combatants ready to work as hired guns.
As recently as the end of last week, UN officials in New York said there
had been reports of gunshots on the eastern side of Danane, and that groups
of men had been milling around across the border in Liberia.
"UN peacekeepers from the UN Mission in Liberia based in Loguatuo, a
town almost opposite Danane on the Liberian side, told the UN mission in
Cote d'Ivoire they saw concentrations of unidentified persons around that
area," Fred Eckhard, the spokesman for UN Secretary-General Kofi Annan, told
reporters on Friday.
Ethnic patchwork
Cote d'Ivoire's "Wild West" is also an ethnic patchwork, where tensions
run deep. The 10,000 international peacekeepers -- from the UN and former
colonial power France -- that monitor the buffer zone between the rebel-held
north and loyalist south have not been able to fully secure it.
Yacoubas on both sides of the border tend to back the rebel New Forces
while the region's ethnic Bete people support Gbagbo, himself a Bete.
The many migrant workers in the area, largely from Burkina Faso,
meanwhile live in fear of attack from pro-government groups.
At the end of February, months of mounting tension in the western border
region erupted into the worse hostilities seen in the West African country
in more than three months, when pro-Gbagbo militia attacked the rebel
village of Logouale.
Eyewitnesses reported at least 15 people killed and UN peacekeepers
captured 85 assailants.
Responsibility for the attack was claimed by the Ivorian Movement for
the Liberation of the West of Cote d'Ivoire (MILOCI), a new militia headed
by a man known as Pastor Gammi.
General Abdoulaye Fall, commander of the UN peacekeeping force in Cote
d'Ivoire, said some of those arrested after the pre-dawn attack said they
had been sent from Abidjan by Charles Ble Goude, the leader of the Young
Patriots, a militia-style pro-Gbagbo youth movement.
It also emerged that the pre-dawn militia attack on Logouale had been
accompanied by an attack on a nearby village inhabited by immigrants from
Burkina Faso who are widely viewed as being sympathetic to the rebels. The
source who visited the scene of the battle at Logouale said the village had
been burned down by the attackers.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United
Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: 'Inland Container Depots for May'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Inland Container Depots for May'
Body_Text:
Abuja — Eight inland container depots planned by Federal Government to ease the movement of goods to and from the hinterlands will take off in about two months time, Nigerian Shippers Council announced yesterday.
The Council's Executive Secretary and Chief Executive Officer (CEO), Capt. Adamu Audu Biu, who disclosed this at a meeting with shippers associations in the North-central zone in Abuja, said the final documents regarding the project would soon be submitted to President Olusegun Obasanjo for approval, to enable the Federal Ministry of Transport and its parastatals sign the final agreements with the preferred operators already on ground.
The meeting, attended by top officials of Nigeria Shippers Council, was meant for shippers associations in Benue, Kogi, Niger states and Federal Capital Territory (FCT).
Government had last year granted concessions to private companies to set up the inland container ports (dry ports) under a build, own, operate and transfer (BOOT) arrangement. Land for the projects under the arrangement will be provided by the beneficiary states after which the states will lay no further claims to the said land.
Some of the depots are to be located in Kano, Kaduna, Jos and Maiduguri.
The council boss said Federal Government's plan to reactivate the railways system was intended to serve as a backborne to the inland container depots.
The council, he disclosed, had appointed a consultant to research on the modalities for exportation of cassava so as to avail shippers the correct information on Federal Government's cassava initiative.
He said the consultant was expected to travel to the different countries in demand of cassava and cassava-related products to collate information on the standards, packaging, uses, quantities, quality and the markets among others, adding that the information which will soon be available will be supplied free of charge to shippers.
For effective dissemination of information to the prospective shippers, Audu announced plans to set up a special Cassava Desk in the council's offices in Abuja with an officer trained to liase with shippers on cassava matters.
"The research we are carrying out on cassava is an initiative of the shippers council and is in view of Federal Government's attention on cassava," he explained.
But officials of the shippers' associations, who variously spoke at the meeting, complained about the red tape by Nigeria Export Import Bank (NEXIM) in processing applications for loans to finance exports.
The Executive Secretary pledged to meet with the management of NEXIM to get correct information on the procedures for loan application and disbursement so as to pass same to the shippers.

Original_Search_Title: Rugunda Condemns Maiming of Women
Headline: Uganda: Rugunda Condemns Maiming of Women
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE GOVERNMENT has condemned the resumption of lip-cutting by the Lord's
Resistance Army rebels.
A gang of marauding rebels mutilated eight women in Ngomoromo, Kitgum
district recently.
"This is a heinous crime against humanity perpetuated by elements
opposed to the peaceful resolution of the conflict in northern Uganda," said
Dr. Ruhakana Rugunda, the internal affairs minister and head of the
Government team negotiating with the rebels to end the 18-year-long war in
northern Uganda.
A ceasefire offered to the rebels ended on February 22, after several
extensions failed to lure the rebels to the negotiating table.
However, top LRA officials like its spokesperson, Brig. Sam Kolo, chief
planner Brig. Kenneth Banya and director of operations Col. Onen Kamdulu
have surrendered to the UPDF.
Between 1990-92, the LRA became infamous for mutilating their victims,
using fear to scare captives from escaping as the rebel numbers
dwindled.
Rugunda warned, "The UPDF will pursue and punish them for these
atrocious crimes against our people. The way forward is to bring a speedy
and peaceful end to this conflict."
In Apac district, the LRA battered to death 11 people from Anyomolyec
camp. Most of them were women who had gone to collect cassava in Wanglobo
village in Otwal sub-county, Oyam North last week.
The UPDF's spokesperson in northern Uganda, Lt. Paddy Ankunda, said the
army had "deployed special troops to track down the rebels and make sure
that they pay for the pain they have inflicted on the innocent women."

Original_Search_Title: Tribute to Ngora's Subtle Beauty
Headline: Uganda: Tribute to Ngora's Subtle Beauty
Body_Text:
Kampala — Down in the gardens, a troop of black and white colobus monkeys jet in from across the gardens to the trees at the bottom. They groom each other while keeping a watchful eye on intruders
AT first light of day, as vivid red and orange rays of sunlight begin to pour over the horizon, I rise to see the sun hover over Ngora county in Kumi district, about 200km northeast of Kampala. The first cock's crow ignites a raucous response from other cocks in the neighbourhood. Today is my second day in a land whose past wave of insecurity and attractions reached me through tales.
For the locals, the day started long before the sun had risen. silhouettes of women and men, ridding bicycles in and out of the trading centre, appear in the hazy morning mist.
Like a huge ball, the orange sun hangs above one of Ngora's numerous rocks. It remains unchanged for 15-minutes.
It is a quiet morning. Not much will change in the day. Ngora is mostly a serene county with a lacklustre roadside town defined by a handful of shops and bars. In the peripheral is a market that will stay deserted until the next market day when hundreds will travel from as far as 30km away.
In the seething afternoon, boys and men lounge under tree shades in the company of their bicycles and motorcycles, the chief means of transport. The dry spell is causing the ground to crackle.
Ngora is not a paradise of green vegetation where wild animals graze nor is it a minefield of attraction. Not many tourists visit. Those who do, dash to the region's principal attraction - the ancient Nyero Rock paintings.
Today, however, I choose to stray away into the maze of rocks that reminds these people of the tribulations they suffered in the 1980s when rebels and karimojong cattle rustlers paid them a visit.
There is a huge rock everywhere I turn. Each is moulded in distinct fashion; one mimics an ox-plough and another the face of an old man with a long beard.
Three boys at nearby trading centre, Samuel Iisa, James Omagoro and John Mwanika, offer to guide me upon two rocks that seem higher than the rest - tank hill rock and Tididiek. It would take a day or two to climb them.
The captivating range of rocks viewed from Tank hill rock, hem in and sometimes tower over tiny trading centres and institutions of learning. Red and black lizards, resting on the rock, scurry into the trees and bushes where sunbirds are suckling nectar from flowers. Down in the gardens, a troop of colobus monkeys jet in from across the gardens to the trees. They groom each other while keeping an eye on their intruders.
Climbing Tididiek rock, about 3km from Tank hill rock, is not the most challenging climb I have done, but it is not easy either. Using our fingers and toes, we move up rock faces. It is exciting for most of the climb until one frightening spot, before the peak. There is no clear-cut point for the finger or toe to grip, but somehow my three guides passed here.
"Where have you guys passed?" They laugh at my bewilderment. Below me is a rock tumbling more than 100 metres. Any movement forward is as good as signing a death sentence.
"How high is this rock?" I ask
"Two hundred metres. It is the highest point in Teso," Omagoro says.
"Two hundred what?" I am not in position to argue so I shut up. Miraculously, the three manage to guide me to the top where a grand beautiful vista spreads before me.
Giant rock boulders in the south form a rugged landscape and to the north, a forlorn dry plain stretches out. At the foothill, men and women crush stones in a stone quarry that borders millet, sorghum and cassava gardens. The extensive sun-dried savannah vegetation creates a monotony that is redeemed by isolated rings of grass-thatched homesteads.
The horizon is hazy. Mwanika says on a clear day, we would have been able to see the Karimojongs, over 80km away, tending to the cattle they stole from them! They vividly recall the Karimojong torture and raid that saw huge herds of cattle stolen. The lives of these people dramatically changed. Caves in the rocks became their homes.
Perhaps the dazzling spectre of the morning sun reflected Ngora's new chapter, an era where these former herdsmen have learnt to survive without that which constituted their principal livelihood - cattle.

Original_Search_Title: LRA Gangs Cut Off Eight Women's Lips
Headline: Uganda: LRA Gangs Cut Off Eight Women's Lips
Body_Text:
Kampala — Gangs of marauding LRA rebels have now turned their weapons to women,
mainly those working in the gardens.
The army has reported that on Wednesday, rebels attacked and cut off the
lips of eight women returning from a well in Ngomoromo village, Lukung
sub-county in Kitgum district.
The RDC of Kitgum, Lt. Santo Okot Lapolo, said one woman, who attempted
to escape, was shot dead and her one-year-old child left with the mutilated
women.
"This is a desperate move by the rebels as the rebellion is fading
away. They want to show the world that they are still strong, yet they are
finished," Lapolo said.
He identified the women who were admitted at Kitgum Hospital as Maurina
Lakang, Agnes Akeny, Angella Ladyer, Scovia Acan, Grace Aduk, Silvia Acan,
Elda Akwero, Santa Akwero and the dead woman as Alimina Adyer.
Lapolo said the rebels mutilated several people between 1990-92 when
they had been weakened and lost support from the civilians.
The regional army spokesman, Lt. Paddy Ankunda, said the army had
"deployed a special troop to follow the rebels to make sure that they pay
for the pain they have inflicted on the innocent women."
Ankunda added that the rebels had resumed full-scale atrocities on
civilians.
In Apac district, the rebels on Tuesday battered to death 11 internally
displaced persons, mostly women and their children in Otwal sub- county,
Oyam North.
James Oloch quotes the commander of Rhino battalion in Otwal, Captain
Moses Musana, as saying that "LRA rebels killed 11 people from Anyomolyec
camp when they had gone to collect cassava in Wanglobo village, seven
kilometres on the Gulu-Apac border, at 5:00pm on Tuesday."
Musana said when the women sat down under a tree to rest, 15 rebels
pounced on them and smashed their heads with hoes.
Survivors said the rebels laughed and mocked the women as they begged
and cried out for mercy.
The former LC3 chairman of Otwal sub-county, Benson Dila, identified the
dead as Ernesta Atigo, Bencamaci Akello, Lilly Akello, Patrick Odwar, Romano
Okello, Milly Awino and her children, Dorcus Aceng and Juspine Odongo, and
two unidentified women from Iceme sub-county.
Dila said Ader, Otwal Railway and Anyomolyec camps had never received
any food aid.
He said the humanitarian situation in the camps was horrible.
Dila said two women escaped and informed the UPDF soldiers in Anyomolyec
camp.
He said the LRA abducted two people in Iceme and killed them at Bar Mogo
near Angolo P7 School in Amukugungu parish.
Musana said the UPDF rescue team was hampered by long distances.
The fresh rebel atrocities come after the expiry of the Government
ceasefire on February 22.

Original_Search_Title: A Constitutional Crisis that Africa Cannot Ignore
Headline: Togo: A Constitutional Crisis that Africa Cannot Ignore
Body_Text:
The sudden death on February 5 of President Gnassingbe Eyadema of the
Republic of Togo, Africa's longest serving head of state, has triggered a
constitutional crisis of succession.
Despite universal condemnation, the Togolese army has attempted to
continue its monopoly of power by installing Eyadema's son, Faure
Gnassingbe, as the successor in contempt for the country's constitution. The
lawful procedure would have been to install the president of the national
assembly as acting head of state, to be followed by an election within 60
days. Instead, the army coerced the national assembly to install Eyadema's
son to serve until 2008 when the late president's term would have
expired.
This "constitutional coup" has been severely denounced both within
Africa and the international community generally. Although Togo is a minor
country with no resources of interest to the global economy, the African
Union appears determined to stop this power grab from succeeding. As a
general proposition, African leaders are trying to persuade the donor
countries, and the international financial institutions, that they are in
the process of correcting their past political and economic mistakes, and
that expanded development assistance will now bring good results after
decades of failure. It is therefore important for the African Union, in the
Togo crisis, to demonstrate that it can discipline its own.
Background
Normally, the Republic of Togo in West Africa attracts very little
attention in the international community. A tiny sliver of a country about
the size of the state of Maryland, Togo has been rather stable since it
gained independence from France in 1960. Togo was prestigious during the
colonial period because the territory's intellectual class served as the
backbone of the French administration in West Africa. Since independence,
its major sources of revenue have been phosphate mineral exports and the
transit trade to and from the West African hinterland through the relatively
efficient port of Lome. Its once thriving cassava exports to the European
Union lost out to competition from Asia. Togo has not suffered any major
humanitarian disasters since 1960, but neither has it done anything to
reduce the level of extreme poverty.
Politically, Togo followed the mainstream West African pattern of the
1960s by falling under the control of a military dictator. In 1967, the
Togolese army overthrew the elected regime and installed Colonel Gnassingbe
Eyadema as the president. Eyadema gained instant notoriety after the coup by
boasting that it was he who assassinated his immediate predecessor,
President Sylvanus Olympio, in 1963. A former sergeant in the French army,
who saw service in Indochina, Eyadema ruled the country with an iron hand
until his recent death.
Until the end of the Cold War, one-man rule in Africa was the
norm. Eyadema was just one dictator among many. Unlike the majority of the
African heads of state, Eyadema was visibly pro-West. He was not
particularly popular with the nonaligned movement, but he was very adept at
maintaining good relations with France, the European Union, the U.S., and
Germany. The latter has special feelings of nostalgia for Togo because of
the pre-World War I colonial relationship. Although he repressed his own
people, Eyadema was perceived as a statesman by his peers through his
aggressive efforts to mediate conflicts within West African states.
Around 1990, the pattern of one-man rule in Africa began to break
down. The expanded African educated class was demanding it, and the
international community was encouraging it. In several francophone
countries, national conferences were organized to bring together civil
society, political groups, economic groups, religious leaders, labor unions
and others in an effort to forge new democratic systems. Togo was one of
them. While these conferences were successful in bringing about the
beginnings of democracy in some countries, notably in neighboring Benin, the
effort in Togo was aborted.
While Eyadema's sincerity about accepting multiparty democracy was
always in doubt, Togo had a particular problem with its vengeful political
opposition. In other countries, the political opposition understood that
their ability to enter a democratic process depended on their willingness to
forget past transgressions by dictators and their corrupt ruling political
parties. In Togo, the political opposition made revenge the dominant theme
of the national conference. This was not surprising since one of the main
opposition political leaders was, and still is, Gilchrist Olympio, the son
of the head of state allegedly assassinated by Eyadema.
Togo's national conference failed to produce a fledgling democracy, and
Eyadema continued to rule through intimidation and rigged elections until
his death. Eyadema became a full international pariah in 1998 when he agreed
to have a free and fair presidential election. The voting was handled
correctly under the control of an independent electoral commission, but
ballot counting was interrupted early on when it became clear that Eyadema
would lose. The minister of interior completed the count and declared
Eyadema the winner by a narrow margin. Eyadema then compounded his problem
by making a public pledge to French President Jacques Chirac that he would
not attempt to amend the constitution to enable him to run for a third
mandate in 2003. He reneged on his pledge and won his last rigged election
that year. Needless to say, Togo was cut off from all development
cooperation from that point.
The Opposition
The unanimous condemnation of the coup, as well as the threat of
sanctions raised by the African Union, caused Faure Gnassingbe to
reformulate his strategy. On February 18, he told Nigerian President
Olusegun Obasanjo, one of the most vocal denouncers of the coup, that he
would hold elections within 60 days as prescribed by the constitution. But a
spokesperson for the Economic Community of West African States
(E.C.O.W.A.S.) quickly said that this was unacceptable. It is clear that the
son is planning to repeat the tactics of the father: hold an election within
60 days as the constitution demands, but then alter the results through
control of the counting mechanism.
The African community is demanding that Togo implement its own
constitution for the first time since the beginning of the dictatorship. If
it fails to restore order in Togo, it will lose credibility as a continent
with aspirations to participate in the global system.
The leaders of the Togolese political opposition can contribute to a
peaceful transition by declaring their determination to focus on the future
of Togo rather than on the human rights violations and corruption of the
deceased head of state. While Eyadema has departed the scene, his remaining
family and the military hierarchy fear the retribution, settlement of
scores, and confiscations that could result from the long repressed
opposition coming to power. They need a clear signal that Eyadema took his
sins with him to the grave and that they will not be punished on his
behalf.
The leaders of the opposition parties are mainly from academia or from
the legal profession. Most of them live outside of Togo. Over the years,
many have been co-opted by Eyadema and have therefore become politically
neutered. A good example is Edem Kodjo, the former assistant secretary
general of the Organization of African Unity (now the African Union). He
started an opposition party, but accepted Eyadema's invitation to become
prime minister, an arrangement that lasted two years. He had hoped to
institute reforms, but Eyadema blocked everything.
Right now, the most popular opposition leader is Gilchrist Olympio, the
son of assassinated President Sylvanus Olympio. If there had been a fair
count in the election of 1998, Olympio would have likely been
elected. Because of his popularity, as the politician who has incarnated
implacable opposition to the late dictator, Olympio has had to live in exile
in neighboring Ghana since 1967. If Olympio is to fulfill his father's
vision for Togo and bring the country into the global system, he needs to
send a reassuring signal to the Eyadema family and the military that they
will be neither pursued nor harassed in the likely event the opposition
comes to power.
The liberalization of politics in Togo would also bring a number of
younger professional men and women into the game, many of whom have quietly
supported Olympio. Their principal demand right now is the restoration of
democratic freedoms of speech, press and political affiliation. There are
very few left from the 1960s and 1970s who believed in Marxism and
scientific socialism. The examples in all of the neighboring countries are
pointing them toward the private sector as the motor of development.
Conclusion
In the event that Faure Gnassingbe ignores Togolese, African and
international opinion and proceeds to consolidate power either without an
election or through a rigged election, the country's isolation will be made
almost total. Even France, the former colonial power that tends to avert its
eyes from the transgressions of its former colonies, will close its doors to
Gnassingbe, as will the European Union and the international financial
institutions. In addition, the use of force by Nigeria, a regional power,
cannot be ruled out. Upon being isolated, only the use of the port of Lome
is likely to continue because it is an important transit center for
landlocked countries such as Niger, Mali and Burkina Faso.
The Power and Interest News Report (PINR)
is an independent organization that utilizes open source intelligence to
provide conflict analysis services in the context of international
relations. PINR approaches a subject based upon the powers and interests
involved, leaving the moral judgments to the reader. This report may not be
reproduced, reprinted or broadcast without the written permission of
inquiries@pinr.com. All comments
should be directed to content@pinr.com.

Original_Search_Title: Asalu: Talking With Mr. Stocks
Headline: Nigeria: Asalu: Talking With Mr. Stocks
Body_Text:
Lagos —  Chief Akintunde Asalu is an icon of some sorts in the Nigerian
capital market. He probably cut his teeth and fame in his poignant
presentations during AGMs, where, often, fellow shareholders and admirers
applauded his briliance.
He is the President of the Nigerian Shareholders Solidarity
Association, (NSSA). A shareholder in a hundred and twenty-six listed
companies of the Nigerian Stock Exchange and a director of several
companies. Recently he launched an autobiography titled: My Life of
Stocks. Last week, Asalu spoke with Affia Okon highlighting the key points a
prospective stock investor should know
When and how did your interest in stock investment begin?
My obsession for stocks dates back to 1961. I was a lower six student in
King's College then. We had one economics teacher S.C Osunkiyesi who had
studied in the UK, he used to regale us in the class about stocks and shares
in relation to mobilizing savings for investment and development. He made so
much sense on my impressionable mind that, today his memory remains one of
the factors responsible for my obsession for stocks. I had no money to
invest in stocks then, it would have been surprising if I had, but, those of
us who were waiting to get into the university could work in the interval,
so I got a job as a teacher at CMS Grammar school in Lagos and in July 1963
Daily Times offered its shares to the public. I remembered my teacher,
Osunkiyesi's advice. I started with 10 shares of the Daily Times. At that
time, that was the much I could buy from my savings as a teacher. But, once
I cut the bug, I could not help it. I began to be on the look out for
opportunities to buy shares. In 1965 Guinness offered its shares to the
public and I bought the shares. My attitude then was to buy anything that
was on offer that I could afford, but gradually I became selective with my
choices and before I knew it, I was totally immersed in the business of
stocks. But I must confess, my major objective in share trading initially
was not economic or money making, it was more of serious political
redressing and drive. I resented the theory of some expatriates that a poor
country like Nigeria would remain poor as they didn't have a savings culture
for investment and development.
What would you say are the key things a prospective shareholder should
look out for in a stock?
The number one thing is management. Who are the people behind this
company? A good management will make a world of difference to a company; a
poor management will also make a world of difference. So it is not how good
a product is but who is managing it the product can be good because you have
a good manager, the product may be good but you have a poor manager,
eventually the product will be bad because the quality of management is also
poor.
So management is the first factor to look at. The second is; how old is
the stock? That is; how long has it been in operation? This will give you an
idea when you look at the figures. With that, you can consider if the
performance has been reasonable enough or not. The third thing to look out
for is; if it has foreign affiliation. Let me confess that companies
operating in this country without foreign affiliation are generally not good
enough, you need this foreign relationship to keep them going and not just
to keep them going but to keep them in tune with current technology and
trends.
Apart from that, you should also look at the balance sheet of the
company how wealthy is it? If there is some good money there, there must be
some good measure to take care of the money to make sure the company does
not lose the money.
You can also look at the political nature of the business. There are
some business that are a little political in nature, take the flourmills for
instance. We know Nigerians eat a lot of bread, but last year the President
said from this year 10% of the components of bread in terms of flour must be
cassava flour, in that case there's a problem. My belief is that, if you mix
cassava flour with wheat flour the product after awhile will be hard, and
for bread that's bad for business.
In all your years of investment which of your stocks will you say has
been the most profitable?
I think the one that has been most profitable is the Nigerian
brewery. It doesn't follow that it is the most profitable for the next man,
but for me its Nigerian Breweries.
Which has been the least profitable?
Ha! They are many. There are some textile companies. There are some that
have never paid me one kobo, but when they hold an annual general meeting
they give us their products and then they serve us lunch, that's probably
more than the dividend they can give. That happens sometimes. There are some
of them that haven't even paid anything for donkey years like: Tate sugar,
it used to be called Tate & Lyle. Infact, now they seem to have
disappeared
You mean you could be a shareholder of a company and not receive
dividends for years?
O yes! It's not compulsory they pay you a dividend. That's the risk in
it. You invest in the hope that there will be some dividends to be paid. You
invest in the hope that the company will do well and they will pay a
dividend. If they don't do well they are not required to pay dividends and
that is when we start to box them. It is not easy. There are so many of them
owing shareholders who are now taking cover.
How would you encourage a first timer into this passion of yours?
It depends on what you have in mind. I will say, you can create what I
call a pension fund for yourself, by buying good stocks and keeping
them. You can also decide to make quick money; by buying stock when the
price is low and when the price is high you sell. That is, you just hold on
to it for the price to rise then you sell, your profit is the difference
between both. If you are smart enough before something else takes that money
off you, you buy another. Because they have different year ends the prices
continues to vary throughout the year and you can always see your way
through and buy, hold and sell and buy another and hold and sell. But there
are some that you just hold. I must also mention too that if you see that I
have shares in ten companies, I don't have the same number of shares in each
of those companies. It varies. I also have favorites among those ten, so the
most likely thing is that the number one stock I have is my favorite, in
other words, I have the largest volume of it and it goes like that to the
last one.
In this business not only must you be prepared to think you must also be
prepared to act fast because if you miss an opportunity to sell or to buy it
will cost you dearly. You need expertise and you need promptness. These are
the factors involved that they will need to know.
Look! The stock market is a business, if you are smart you will make
good money if not, too bad. So when you see people who go to the stock
market for the first time with nobody giving them a lead, they end up with
their finger burnt but that's the only way they can learn. After that they
become quite good at it. Many of us live on the income we receive from our
investments. So its advisable that when you say your prayers you remember to
pray for the companies you have invested in. O' yes because they give you
something to eat. Like THISDAY, it is doing well, one-day it will become
quoted publicly, some people are praying for that and waiting and one day it
will come to the market.
When was your highest moment as a stock investor?
If you are asking when I made my highest return from the sale of shares
there hasn't been any. My activity in the stock market has been
predominantly one way, just buying and holding. I have sold but not on the
scale I mentioned that is, waiting for the price to go up and sell. I sold
because some years ago, I wanted to buy a property and I didn't have the
liquidity and I didn't want to go to the bank so I decided to put some
shares together and sell them. I did and regretted it. What happened was
that, the stock did so well that the person who bought it from me started
asking me if he could get another one like that one from me. So I am very
careful when it comes to selling.
But it's a way of life and life itself has some measure of
gamble. Investing also has its measure of gamble.
How many companies are you a shareholder of?
The number shouldn't interest you. Let me just say that there are over
two hundred companies quoted in the Nigerian stock exchange and I have
shares in about half of them.
Do your children share this passion of stock investment?
Yes, they do
Tell us a bit about your background
I was born at Ejigbo in Osun state. I attended the Baptist day school
Ejigbo, from there I gained admission into the prestigious King's College. I
started college in 1956 and throughout my years there I knuckled down to do
whatever was necessary to be a successful student. My record at King's
College heralded me to the University of Lagos, where I later became
president of the students union. After graduation I took up a paid
employment with West African Portland Cement Company and have worked for
several other companies since then.
What are you doing at the moment?
What I do now is to take care of my investments. I take care of the
shares I have. There is always something to do as an investor. Yesterday we
had a general meeting; we had one again today that KPMG facilitated. We are
always meeting.
What are your views of the stock market since its inception?
The capital market cannot be considered to have done too badly in the
over 40 years of its existence, it is still evolving and developing. The
stock exchange was lucky in its early years because it had the likes of
prince M.A Odedin a no non sense technocrat to lead it. He did a beautiful
job when he left the mantle of leadership on Hayford Alile who headed the
stock exchange for twenty years. In his own way he contributed to the
development of the market; in fact the most memorable part of the stock
exchange was during his tenure when many companies came up for listing. The
stock market itself is dynamic. There have been innovations in the market on
a daily basis not only in terms of technology but also in terms of
product. But I believe the Nigerian Stock Exchange still has room for
improvement I believe that if the exchange is to make good progress it must
invest in human capacity development and be tolerant of other views. The
stock market is not a free orgnisation it is a technical in nature.
I have watched the operations of stock exchange throughout the African
continent especially the performance of the Ghana stock exchange since that
exchange was established; it has succeeded in taking two of its companies to
international market, this feat we are yet to achieve in Nigeria after 40
years of operation.

Original_Search_Title: Stanbic's Sh80m Recovered
Headline: Uganda: Stanbic's Sh80m Recovered
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Police have recovered sh80m of the sh700m robbed by employees of Security Gray recently.
They have also arrested eight suspects who were found with the money. Four are being held at the Criminal Investigation Department (CID) headquarters in Kampala.
According to the Regional Police Commander for Mid-Eastern region, Bob Ngobi, the suspects alleged that Ogola, a key suspect in the case, had entrusted them with the loot.
"The money was found hidden in houses, gardens, homes and cassava gardens in Kisoko sub-county, Tororo district," he said.
A joint operation by detectives from the region and colleagues from CID in Kampala helped recover the money.
Ngobi said the money would be kept at CID as investigations continued.

Original_Search_Title: Curb Accidents in the Public Transport Sector
Headline: Zambia: Curb Accidents in the Public Transport Sector
Body_Text:
MOST accidents either occuring on water or on the roads are caused by inattention spurred by carelessnes. Ferries have capsized simply because the crew ignored basic rules such as the need not to overload.
Ask the Zambia Police Service; it has mountains on the number of accidents that have occured because vehicles have been overlaoded either with passengers or goods.
The remarks, therefore, made by Vice-President Luapndo Mwape in Kazungula yesterday raise hope. Those who ignore wieght limits should be disciplined; they should be dismissed.
Alluding to the September 2003 accident in which six people drowned, Mr Mwape said the pontoon capsized due to human negligence.
The Kazungula saga is not the first one such tragedy where a ferry sank due to carelessness.
Just in 2003 an overloaded ferry from Kasenga Island to Nchelenge in Luapula Province capsized because of overloading. Though some people managed to swim ashore others drowned.
Eyewitnesses later noted that the vessel was not only overloaded by passengers but was ladden with heavy loads of fish, cassava, second-hand clothes and other items. There are a host of examples too numerous to categorise.
It is, therefore, critical that the regulations are not only followed to the letter, but those ignoring the specifications are punished heavily.
Through the accidents not only lives have been lost, but the property too.
The rehabilitated pontoon which the Vice President was commissioning yesterday was rebuilt at US$700,000 with assistance from the World Bank. That is quite a lot of money which could have gone to other developmental projects if the ferry had not capsized.
That money could have, for instance, gone to road rehabilitation or even used to procure an additional ferry.
To save lives and property Government should take similar measures against truckers and passenger bus operators who overload.
They should be slapped with punitive measures including withdrawal of operating licences (in the case of operators) and driving licences from drivers.
Unless there are regulations to curb carelessness, accidents many of which can be prevented, will continue.
We hope that the Engineering Services Corporation (ESCO) will implement the Vice President's directive.
If they do not the officials should be punished themselves as a starting point. Time has come not only to curb accidents in the public transport sector, but to infuse in a sense of responsibility among operators and their crew members.

Original_Search_Title: Commerce Minister Seeks Support for Cassava Export Programme
Headline: Nigeria: Commerce Minister Seeks Support for Cassava Export Programme
Body_Text:
Lagos — CONCERTED efforts have been called for in the implementation of Federal
Government's cassava products export programme as the initiative can
generate about $20 billion yearly if fully implemented.
Minister of Commerce, Amb. Idris Waziri disclosed this yesterday in
Lagos at the opening session of a two-day National Workshop on Company
Administratioin and Practice in Nigeria, organised by the Corporate Affairs
Commission (CAC).
Amb. Waziri who also disclosed that locally sourced cassava flour can
earn the country about N20 billion yearly, insisted that the nation must
imbibe the best ethical practices in company administration in order to
attract foreign investments.
"Nigeria is one of the richest countries in the world, but are we doing
things the right way," he queried, stressing the need for the country to
institute the best legal framework that will ensure that business
thrives.
Decrying the nation's over-dependence on the oil sector, the Commerce
minister reiterated government's commitment towards making the economy a
private sector driven one.
"Business has to be done by the private sector. There is need for both
the private and public sectors the partner and make things happen," he
said.
He also pointed out that commercialising the key sectors of the economy
would ensure more job creation for the teeming population insisting that
commerce should be the hub of economic activities in the country.
Amb. Waziri also revealed plans by the government to promote non-oil
exports in its bid to diversify the economy, create more jobs and fight
corruption.
According to him, the Ministry of Commerce is already working in
collaboration with the Commonwealth to produce an export framework for the
country.
He commended the CAC for laudable achievements it has recorded,
especially in the area of service delivery.
Also speaking, Attorney General of the Federation (AGF), Chief Akinlolu
Olujimi, called for an ammendment of the Companies and Allied Matters Act
(CAMA) to give the CAC powers to deal with erring directors in a more
specific manner.
"It is important to note that of all the functions of the commission,
the most challenging has been enforcement of compliance. A careful perusal
of the provisions of the CAMA as they are show there are time stipulations
for making of returns and other filings. When there is default, the
