commission is to impose monetary penalties and fimes. There seems to me no
authority for the commission to go beyond this.
"It is worthy to note that the penalties and fines in the CAMA have been
overtaken by economic reality. They can no longer serve as deterrence which
they were meant to be", Olujimi explained.
He also called on stakeholders to encourage and support the CAC in
rendering efficient service to the system.

Original_Search_Title: Random Thoughts on National Dialogue
Headline: Nigeria: Random Thoughts on National Dialogue
Body_Text:
With a couple of weeks to the inauguration of the National Political
Reform Conference, the air of uncertainty is still blowing across the
political terrain as only few outside the corridors of powers could tell you
with relative certainty what the conference is actually intended to
achieve.
A child of secrecy, conceived in controversy is most likely to be an
illegitimate child not so much on its paternity but more on its morality and
legality. Here is a major national conference that was neither on the
electoral campaign manifesto of the ruling PDP nor on the lips of its
presidential candidate during the electioneering campaigns in 2003. From the
blues, Nigerians are with a twinkle of an eye asked to come to Abuja to talk
about predetermined issues within a span of just three months.
Several issues kept on raging in my mind any time I thought or
visualized the modality or conduct of this forthcoming conference that only
a few people outside Abuja understand or welcome. These range from its
credibility, legality, objectives and likely outcome. One issue that is
already hitting the headlines is its poor representations, not only
regarding the quality of the representatives but also the flagrant bias
against women. There are outcries all over regarding the non-inclusion of
women by several states. How could a conference said to be organized to
politically reform Nigeria discriminate against such a significant portion
of the population? Secondly, there are also other criticisms regarding the
quality of the nominated delegates to the conference. Majority of them are
men of yesteryears that should not have been delegates but advisors to the
delegates. Considering their advanced ages, it is very unlikely if they
could withstand the rigours of the high politicking and the anticipated
rancorous conference atmosphere. Also at a more fundamental level, these
nominated delegates can rightly be said to only be representing themselves
or the president or the governors that nominated them. They cannot be called
or regarded as the representatives of the people as they never obtained
their mandate. In a democracy that we are practicing, it is very odd to say
the least that the people do not elect the delegates. So whatever the
outcome of the conference, it lacks legitimacy and therefore also lacks
credibility. Also as we pointed out earlier, the issue of this conference
was never part of the PDP manifesto during the electioneering campaign and
could therefore not be regarded as being endorsed through the election of
their candidates. Just like other federal government policies like
privatization of public enterprises that were not part of the 1999 PDP
manifesto were overnight imposed on the polity. Ditto the N25 billion
recapitilization of banks and other serious economic policies.
From the realm of lack of legitimacy of the conference, my mind also
pondered its legality as the over fifty members of the House of
Representatives are asking the court to interprete for them. Given the
serious issues the delegates are expected to discuss, the conference should
have been given legal teeth. With our experience of Oputa Panel still fresh
in our minds where millions of Naira was expended and several hours of
public hearings in five cities for a span of about a year in addition to
tons of exhibits and other documents, the seven-volume report was thrown
into the archives. All the wahala we suffered was turned into a waste for
reasons that neither make sense nor justifiable. Because part of this issue
is already before the court, we cannot discuss it any further but await the
court's ruling. Suffice it to just observe that the opposition from the
National Assembly consist of members of all the leading parties: PDP, ANPP
and AD. In a nutshell, what this means is that as we earlier suspected, this
conference is not a PDP program hence the stiff opposition from its members
in the National Assembly as well as the deep silence from the PDP
headquarters in spite of the knocks their president is getting from their
members at the National Assembly. Iam also worried about the money being
proposed for the conference at a time when poverty continues to ravage the
land. With nearly one billion naira slated to be spent on the conference,
one is certainly entitled to do a cost and benefit analysis and see whether
the end justifies the means
My mind is also equally worried about the time allocated to the
conference to discuss all the issues listed on the agenda all within three
months. Though I believe in miracles, I cannot see how the conference can
conclude its sittings within three months. Secondly, looking at the agenda,
one is equally confused about some of the items. These items like the issue
of onshore/ offshore dichotomy already before the Supreme Court beats our
imagination. If you recall about twenty two state governors have gone to
court to challenge the law that gives revenues from the offshore oil
exploration to the littoral states instead of taking the whole chunk to the
federation account to be shared by all the states of the federation. So, why
bring this issue that is before the court? One can only imagine the acrimony
and bad blood that the matter will generate between the North and the oil
producing areas of the south south. With 2007 around the corner, is the
matter deliberately included so as to create political friction between the
hitherto historical allies? Another issue bothering my mind is even the
contemplation of changing to parliamentary system from the existing
presidential system. The argument I hear most against the presidential
system is that it is very expensive. But the fact of the matter is that no
matter the system we adopt, Nigerians have the capacity to make it
expensive. The issue therefore is corruption that makes the system very
expensive and not necessarily the system per se. In this regard, one wonders
why after practicing the presidential system for several years, including
the military era, someone wants us to experiment with another system that
the majority of the operators now are not familiar with. This is therefore
an invitation to anarchy: it is not the hood that makes the monk.
From here my mind shifts to the likely outcome or consequences of the
conference given the already charged political atmosphere. One of my fears
is that holding this conference at this time would further polarize the
country along ethnic and regional lines. Every section seems to be
strategizing on how to maximize its gains. From some pronouncements of
leading politicians, it would not be an exaggeration to state that the North
is likely to be on the receiving end as the other sections are likely to
gang up against it. The conference from all intent and purposes is taking us
back to the 1950s and 60s where regionalism held supreme. Delegates are more
likely to be more bothered about what is good for their regions rather than
what is good for Nigeria. But as the conference is becoming inevitable, let
every state prepare to fight its own interests and God is there for
Nigeria. Let my Kano delegates fight against the abolition of the
onshore/offshore dichotomy, fight for the setting up of Desertification
Commission and ensure the retention of local government councils. I have the
feeling that the reports of the presidential committee on local government
councils might find its way to the conference. Let them insist on justice
and equity in running the affairs of the nation. Let them insist the federal
government should have programmes to revitalize groundnut, cotton and wheat
just as much as we have similar programmes for cassava and cocoa. Let them
be vigilant and never take anything for granted.
In conclusion, my mind keeps vibrating as to why this conference must
take place now rather than organize it well with the delegates properly
elected and an open agenda with all the issues under the sun placed on the
table? My "satanic thoughts" suddenly shifted to the ANPP and General Buhari
presidential election case pending before the Supreme Court. I suddenly
began to wonder whether this conference and the re-election of the president
to lead the African Union and the court case might have some
relationship. The timing of the conference either by coincidence or design
is coming when the Supreme Court would start hearing the appeal. But as we
know from Justice Chukwudifu Oputa's popular axiom about justice being a
three-way traffic: to the accused, plaintiff and the overall society? Would
national interest therefore not over-ride any other interest with such an
important conference under way against the newly re-elected president of
Africa? How could in this circumstance the 2003 presidential election be
annulled as the minority opinion of the Appeal Court? Only time could
tell.

Original_Search_Title: Tracing And Reuniting Families After 14 Years of War
Headline: Liberia: Tracing And Reuniting Families After 14 Years of War
Body_Text:
Ganta — Shortly after David Wilson left his home in Ganta two years ago to buy cassava in a nearby village, a fierce battle between government and rebel forces broke out in the town behind him.
When the retired schoolteacher finally returned to his house in the once bustling market town on Liberia's northern border with Guinea, there was no sign of his four children.
"I was cut off from my children when the fighting broke out and all along I thought they were dead because the bombing and the gunfire was so heavy," Wilson told IRIN.
For 18 months, he mourned their loss.
In fact, his kids were safe. But thinking their parents had perished in the battle, they had fled over the nearby border to Guinea where they ended up in a refugee camp.
Working on the off-chance that one of their relations might still be alive to recognise them, the International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC) circulated their photos on posters put up in public places in Liberia as part of its campaign to reunite thousands of separated children with their families.
And in November last year, an excited father picked out the faces of all four children; Geekor, David, Cecilia and Davidetta on one such poster pasted on a well in Ganta.
He immediately passed a message to them through the ICRC and they responded. Finally, last week, all four children tumbled out of a Red Cross Toyota Landcruiser at the Ganta border crossing and rushed into their father's arms.
"I never thought I would see my parents again after we were separated by the fighting in Liberia," said 13-year-old Geekor. "I thought they were all dead."
"God answered my numerous prayers when I saw their photos on the ICRC posters of missing children in search of their parents and I was filled with happiness that my children are alive," said his 60-year-old father, weeping with joy.
Since Liberia's 14-year civil war ended in August 2003, the ICRC has managed to put 2,300 separated children back in touch with their families by launching a series of five poster campaigns.
So far more than 600 youngsters have been reunited with their families as a result of such contacts. Most of them, like the four children of the Wilson family, ended up in refugee camps in Guinea.
Vasily Fadeev, the ICRC spokesman in Guinea, said the Red Cross was managing to reunite about 30 Liberian children a month with their relatives.
In fact, the ICRC has even managed to bring home some Liberian orphans who never had any relatives to claim them in the first place.
Six-year-old Gorgboyee Paye was one of these.
He used to live at a church-run orphanage for 54 children in Ganta. But when the battle broke out in March 2003 that led to the Wilson family becoming separated, Gorgboyee found himself caught up in a crowd of frightened civilians fleeing towards the Guinean border.
A woman who was running away with three children of her own saw the lost and nearly naked child and swept him under her wing. Like the Wilson children, they ended up in Laine refugee camp near Nzerekore, the capital of Guinea's southeastern Forest Region.
Reverend Cyrus Saye, the head of the orphanage in Ganta, was there singing hymns at the border to welcome Gorgboyee home. The young boy, decked out in a new denim suit, climbed down from the Red Cross vehicle, speechless, but smiling from ear to ear.
The ICRC said in a statement that its family tracing division was currently working on 716 new cases of separated children, of whom 411 were living at refugee camps in Guinea.
According to the UNHCR, there were nearly 150,000 Liberian refugees in Guinea when the Liberian civil war ended, but many of these have since come home.
Tens of thousands trekked back spontaneously soon after the peace agreement was signed in 2003. And in October 2004 the UNHCR launched an official campaign of voluntary return and resettlement.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Kaduna Fair: Ayoola to Showcase Poundoyam, Odourless Fufu
Headline: Nigeria: Kaduna Fair: Ayoola to Showcase Poundoyam, Odourless Fufu
Body_Text:
Lagos — Ayoola Foods has stated that their showing at the Kaduna International Trades Fair would be a part of the company's plan to formerly introduce their products which are already in high demand in the Northern part of the country to them.
The Managing Director of Ayoola Foods, Engineer Segun Olaye who spoke with THISDAY about their plan put the company's premium products including the Ayoola Poundo Yam, Oudourless Fufu, Beans, beans flour among others on sale at the Kaduna Fair, explained that apart from direct sale of their products at the fair they were also looking forward to engaging distributors who will handle the distribution of their products after the fair.
"Even though our products have penetrated the northern part of the country long before now, we still want to use the opportunity presented by the Kaduna fair to introduce our company to Nigerians as a responsible corporate citizen and to introduce our excellent products, which are the result of many years of experience in the food industry and tireless effort into research and development in the area food processing to them"
Ayoola Foods began making impact in the food industry from the international market scene, being one of the foremost Nigerian companies to process and export Nigerian local staples such as yam, cassava,beans,melon,ogbono,leafty vegetable among others abroad both to feed Africans in diaspora and also to introduce them to non Africans in Europe America and Asia.
According to Olaye the fact that the company had conquered the international market by exporting easily to the United Kingdom, Holland,Germany, Austria,Canada and the United States regardless their very high standards taking over the Nigerian market will also be an goal they can achieve easily.
"the few companies who are in the business of prodiction of Poundo yam for instance are foreign companies who produce outside Nigeria and then import the product to the country leading to unnecessary waste of the hard earned foreign exchange and also products that cannot be easily purchased by the ordinary Nigerians due to the exorbitant prices"
Olaye however stated that these importers of products for which we (Nigeria) have comparative advantage do not stand a chance in the competition with Ayoola foods, which is an indegineous company.
"Producing here in Nigeria and exporting to Europe and America, we have been able to beat these same competitors that have their production base in developed countries and so producing here in Nigeria, we are making our products at world class standards and offering these products for sale at prices that fall within the limits of all levels of Nigerians.
He said that the impact, which their products made on Nigerians, through the Lagos international Trades Fair still was still evident in the rate of sale of their products till date.
"We receive telephone calls every day from people ho stumbled on our products at the fair and want to buy more. At some point, we had to reduce our level of publicity so that we will be able to meet the demand for our products and reduce the risk of stock out cost. But haven't fortified our factory and significantly increased our product output we are going all out to reach the consumers with these products which we know they will love," He said.

Original_Search_Title: Cassava Biscuit Output Underway
Headline: Zambia: Cassava Biscuit Output Underway
Body_Text:
A LOCAL investor will soon venture into the production of cassava biscuit in Mansa district with production equipment worth K269 million and an initial working capital of K50 million.
Mr Abraham Chikonde said he had plans of producing 360,000 packets of biscuits annually worth K1.6 billion.
In an interview, Mr Chikonde, who is proprietor of Lusaka's Procpres Enterprises Limited (PECO), said cassava flour consumption was estimated at 24 tonnes valued at K48 million every year.
He said the sample production of his cassava biscuit had already received an overwhelming demand on the local market.
Mr Chikonde was determined to ensure that his product penetrated the international market in future.
He said with the Southern African regional initiative aimed at commercialising cassava production using modern methods, his project was assured of constant supply of quality cassava flour, a situation he said was key to continuity of production.
Mr Chikonde lamented that though his company came up with a technology to produce cassava biscuits a few years ago, it could not venture into production because of poor quality of cassava flour.

Original_Search_Title: Keep Burning Illegal Guns
Headline: Uganda: Keep Burning Illegal Guns
Body_Text:
The National Focal Point on Small Arms has announced that it will soon destroy another 10,000 guns. These were recovered from illegal users in the Karamoja region.
The guns will be the second batch to be burnt publicly. The first batch of three thousand was set afire by interior minister Ruhakana Rugunda at a ceremony witnessed by many at Nsambya last month. They are mostly SMGs of the AK47 calibre.
Many Ugandans unaccustomed to such an act would wonder why a poor country like ours would destroy arms worth over ten million dollars.
But this is the right way to go. Those small arms have been responsible for thousands of deaths and if left in circulation they will continue causing more deaths.
The government does not equip its security personnel with weapons captured from criminals.
Purchases of equipment for government departments including security is part of a budgeting process, and does not depend on booty seizures.
These weapons must be destroyed publicly for all to learn that the state does not condone illegal possession of firearms.
It would have greater impact if these guns are destroyed at several sites in Karamoja itself, for this is where the idea would work best that possession of a firearm without license is a serious crime.
The people of Karamoja should witness the hills of AK 47 being set afire to realise how serious the government is over disarmament.
In any case, the average Karimojong needs to know that it is government policy not to license anybody for possession of an AK 47, and so purchasing it, whether by using cattle or cash, is a waste of time as it can never belong to you.
Public destruction of guns must continue especially in Karamoja, until the public accepts that it does not pay to buy a gun without authorisation of the Inspector General of Police.
The same should apply to other contraband seized from criminals. Tusks and other trophies seized from poachers and smugglers should be destroyed in public, preferably in areas where the activities are rife.
Similarly, narcotics seized from the airport and elsewhere should also be destroyed publicly.
Suspicions that security personnel help themselves to these 'exhibits', and that strong court safes contain cassava flour labelled as heroin, are not good for the credibility of the government.
Let us light more fires to burn illegal items, from guns, tusks to narcotics.

Original_Search_Title: Govt to Supply China 30,000 Barrels of Crude Oil Per Day
Headline: Nigeria to Supply China 30,000 B/D of Crude Oil....Corporation to Make$800m
Body_Text:
Abuja — PETROCHINA International and the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC) have signed an oil supply pact that will result in the sale and export of 30,000 barrels of crude per day (p/d) to China to help power the fast expanding economy.
It was gathered that the contract is renewable after one year and is expected to fetch the Corporation about US$800 million, given the prevailing international market prices of crude oil.
Si Bingjun, managing director of PetroChina, and Edmund Daukoru, special adviser to Nigerian President Olusegun Obasanjo on Energy and Oil Matters, signed the contract on behalf of their respective sides.
The contract is for a period of one year and is also at the prevailing cost of crude at the international market.
Si said China and Nigeria should continue to have cooperation given the healthy trade and stable development among the two countries.
He said China has submitted bids for two oil blocks in the 2005 licensing opening in August and there are strong indications that the bids may be successful since it has the support of President Olusegun Obasanjo.
President Obasanjo had during a state visit to China last year promised to make oil blocks available to China after the Chinese authorities had made this a precondition for investing in the country's oil and gas industry.
According to him, China is considering building a hydro-station in Mambilla and has already expressed interest to take over the Kaduna Refinery and Petrochemicals as core investors in the event of privatization.
Dr. Daukoru said Nigeria will continue to offer China opportunity to meet its high demand of energy. China's unquenchable thirst for crude oil supplies to power its growing economy has seen oil prices rising in the last one year, with no hope of abating in the immediate future.
The Presidential adviser who has been nominated as Minister, but yet to be assigned a portfolio, said President Obasanjo has approved four oil blocks to China aimed at assisting it to secure more crude to meet the needs of its expanding economy.
Recent years have witnessed fast growth of foreign trade and economic cooperation between China and the Nigeria. In 2004, the bilateral trade between the two countries stood at more than US$2 billion.
The last three years has witnessed dramatic increases in bilateral trade between both countries, with Nigeria exploring new terrain with the export of cassava chips and petroleum, while China maintains its traditional export of fabrics, and remains a ready source of cheaper household utensils and electronics.

Original_Search_Title: Adegbite Opposes Absolute Resource Control
Headline: Nigeria: Adegbite Opposes Absolute Resource Control
Body_Text:
Asaba — Secretary General of Supreme Council of Islam in Nigeria, Alh. Lateef Adegbite has kicked against the agitation for absolute resource control, but rather called for the return to the derivation principle inherited at independence.
He described as "unfortunate" the impasse at the reform conference over the issue of derivation principle, which led to the staging of a walk-out at the conference by South-south delegates.
Adegbite who spoke in Asaba yesterday while fielding questions from newsmen, said "I would be happier if we return to the principle of derivation inherited at independence, but what we mean by resource control varies from view point to view point. If resource control means total management and absolute control of resources, I will not be part of that".
According to him, since Nigeria is a federating state, Nigerians must regard everything they have as a common asset, adding that there should be no distinction between one product and another.
He noted that whatever was good for cocoa should be alright for oil, should be alright for uranium, bitumen, cassava, groundnut, cotton and all such".
Adegbite added that he did not believe in distinction, but advised that an acceptable formula should be fashioned out in the interest of the growth of the country.
He maintained that the country had drifted from a fair derivation principle for so long, adding that if the principle must be returned to, it had to be a gradual process.
The secretary General added that he was not in support of 13 percent derivation fund but a fairer percentage conceded in oil-producing area.
He however suggested 25 percent, which according to him could be reviewed from time to time until "we can get ourselves back to the situation in 1963 to 1966 and that is my own personal opinion".

Original_Search_Title: 'Why Bakers Shun Cassava Flour'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Why Bakers Shun Cassava Flour'
Body_Text:
Lagos — Master Bakers Association of Nigeria, the umbrella body for bakers in the country, has explained why its members remain skeptical about the Federal Government's directive to them to use cassava flour for bread-making, saying that cassava flour can only be mixed with high quality wheat flour for good results.
The association said that in view of the fact that the quality of imported wheat flour varies depending on the country of origin, time and season, its members cannot afford the risky trial with cassava flour, which might result in an irreversible waste in the event that poor quality wheat flour is mixed with it.
The Chairperson of the Lagos branch of the association, Mrs. Emah Eshiet, and author of the book, "Essentials of Bread Making," made this explanation in an interview with newsmen at the launching of the book in Lagos recently. She also regretted the continued use of banned substances, including saccharine and potassium bromate, by some bakers in an attempt to break even in their business.
Reflecting on the use of cassava flour, Eshiet, who doubles as General Manager of the Lagos - based Timfort Bakery rationalised that bakers cannot afford the costly gamble with cassava flour because when mixed with low quality wheat, an event that is beyond the control of bakers, the quality of the resulting paste would be poor and unacceptable as it would be a great loss to them.
"We have tried cassava flour before now and it worked but the only problem is that the quality of wheat flour, which is usually mixed with cassava flour varies depending on the country, time and season. If it falls in the time that a low quality wheat is brought into the country, if you mix it with cassava flour, you would have a poor result, "she said. "So bakers are skeptical, they are just being careful to avoid a lot of waste because it is a perishable product, when it is wasted, the loss cannot be reversed".
She affirmed that there were dangers inherent in the use of saccharin and potassium bromate in bread making, regretting the resort to these ingredients by some bakers at great human costs to the nation.
"Few bakeries do the right things whereas most bakeries are into potassium bromate and saccharine. They use saccharin because it is cheap but they will fool the people that they are using honey. Bread prepared with these ingredients are very sweet but very dangerous too. They want a cheap way of making profit but their profit is affecting innocent people", Eshiet regretted.
The Chairperson also explained why the prices of bread have remained relatively stable and low over time. She said: "The price of bread is still at the minimal level because the bakers' association is trying to meet with millers and companies producing other bread-making ingredients who have been complaining about foreign exchange and so on".
"If we raise the price of bread just like that, there may be riot before you know it. So we are holding on, waiting for the association to finish meeting with stakeholders in the bakery industry and from there, we will decide on what to do with the price of bread", Eshiet added.

Original_Search_Title: Will Faure Refurbish Eyadema's Presidential Resort?
Headline: Togo: Will Faure Refurbish Eyadema's Presidential Resort?
Body_Text:
Lagos — Up on the mountain, 705 metres above sea level in a town called Kouma
Konda is a gigantic structure, the Presidential Castle, which serves as a
resort for Togo's first family and their guests.
In the wake of the recent demise of that country's Number One Citizen,
the late President Gnassingbe Eyadema, world attention has been drawn to the
tiny West African country. Incidentally, this reporter was privileged to be
part of a delegation of Nigerian journalists who visited Togo for an
educ-tour about two weeks before Eyadema's death.
Definitely, Faure, Eyadema's son who has been foisted on the country in
his late father's stead is no stranger to the Presidential Castle. He must
have been privileged to visit it many times with his father (or alone since
he appears to be the beloved son in whom the old man was well pleased). If
the pressure from world institutions abate and the Togolese parliament and
army are allowed to have their way, this is one place the younger Eyadema
may look to have a quiet time for reflection on how to set an acceptable
agenda for the challenges facing his country.
For reasons that are not immediately clear, the Presidential Castle is
slated as one of the attractions tourists in Togo should see. Getting there
may not always be as easy as driving into town, as there are a few security
hurdles to clear. For our visit, officials of the Togolese Ministry of
Tourism, Arts and Handicraft and management of Hotel 2 Feverier that
facilitated the trip had obtained every necessary clearance. At Kpalime, we
made a stopover at the army barracks to pick a soldier who had the key to
the bar against vehicular traffic. As the soldier struggled with the padlock
for a while, it was a clue that no vehicle had come this way for a long
time. This would later be confirmed when we got up to the castle.
The history of the house as relayed by one of its keepers who was there
in the beginning confirms that the building originally belonged to a German
lawyer, Raymond Vial. (Togo was a German colony before the aftermath of the
First World War gave it to France) He started building the house in 1940 and
moved in on December 7, 1944. He lived there with his wife and two
children. After four years , he went to Europe, returning in 1976. By this
time, he was no more willing to live in Africa. He sought for buyers for his
property. President Eyadema bought it.
Togolese architects and artisans spent four years renovating it to a
befitting Presidential castle. Some of the changes made on the structure
includes an additional eight rooms for cabinet ministers accompanying the
president on retreat, a helipad, provision of water and electricity and an
expansion of the road leading up the retreat by Fougerolle. This task was
accomplished between 1979 and 1982. Previously, Vial used horses as a means
of transportation (a collapsing stable which the housekeepers now use as a
store house for their yams and cassava can be seen at the back of the house)
and powered the house with an electricity generating set. Vial reportedly
died in 1980 at 79 years.
The first eminent visitor to the retreat was the late President Houphet
Biogny of Ivory Coast. Former Senegalese President, Abdou Diouf followed in
1984. Since then, Eyadema never found time to go up the mountain retreat
till he passed on.
Over two decades of neglect has taken its toll on the one-storey
house. Portions of the plaster (POP) used for the ceiling is falling
apart. Decay has set into a few doors and windows. The structure is made up
of five rooms. The living room has a 16-seater settee with seven side stools
and glass top centre tables. Abstract carvings of Professor Ahyi of the
University of Benin decorate the living room. One of them was particularly
instructive. It was a human form with nails driven into its ears and
mouth. This could be interpreted to mean that visitors to the Presidential
Castle are obliged to keep to themselves whatever they heard during their
visit.
Expectedly, the climate up on the mountain is cold. It is a vantage
position that allows the visitor ample view of the surrounding
neighbourhood, even as far as the closest Ghanaian village. The road leading
up to the castle is lined with teak and mango trees. From the visitors
register, we noted that the last set of visitors before our group came on
December 28, 2004.
Hotel 2 Fevrier, the Togolese of Handicraft and Tourism Ministry had
been enthusiastic about the gains that would accrue to the country from
expected tourist traffic after the lifting of a 10-year sanction by the
European Union. To launch this tourism drive, they looked to Nigeria-her
giant brother-next door. An elaborate plan had been prepared with the help
of a Nigerian consulting company, Topcomm Limited to bring over Nigerian
journalists, tour operators and travel agents to savour the tourism products
in Togo. Will Togo's attempt to reposition her economy through tourism
suffer a setback following the aftermath of Eyadema's death?

Original_Search_Title: Rebels Kill MP's Brother
Headline: Uganda: Rebels Kill MP's Brother
Body_Text:
Lira — A brother to the Member of Parliament for Erute south county, and two
other people were on Wednesday killed in a night raid by rebels of the Lords
Resistance Army led by Joseph Kony.
David Livingstone Apunyo, 60, a brother to Mr Charles Angiro Gutmoi, was
killed at 10:00pm on Wednesday when about 11 rebels entered the MP's home in
Tekulu village in Aromo Sub County.
MP Angiro told The Monitor that the rebels tortured his brother to
death.
"That is if they are really rebels, they entered my home and killed my
brother last night before crossing into neighbouring Walela parish where
they killed two others," he said.
The two have been identified as James Odic, 30, and Okello Aryono,
25.
The army has confirmed the incident but blamed the deceased for staying
deep in the villages without security.
The UPDF 403 Brigade Spokesman, Lt. Hebert Turyamwijuka, said the
villages were not yet safe.
"Its true the rebels committed atrocities in the area but to a small
extent it's the dead to blame.
The villages are not yet safe for their (civilian) stay," he
warned.
Meanwhile, the UPDF 81st Brigade at Paluda has killed three rebels and
dug up the rebels' supplies in an operation, the army said on Thursday.
The army's 403-Brigade spokesman, Lt. Hebert Turyamwijuka, said
yesterday the forces killed three rebels, captured one and recovered four
sub machine guns.
He said another rebel, Sgt. Opobo reported to the army's detach at
Palabek-kal with one gun and six magazines of ammunition.
He said an operation conducted in Paluda also discovered several medical
and food supplies buried underground.
The items unearthed include: 20 bags of dry cassava, 300 tablets of
Panadol, 8 boxes of sealed PPF injections, 8 bombs for 82 mm, one stick
grenade and 62 rounds of PK gun.
"We are certainly closing in on the rebels and if they dodge the barrel,
they won't escape sicknesses now that we have taken their medical supplies,"
Lt Turyamwijuka told The Monitor.
The army has also reported killing 20 rebels in the past one week.

Original_Search_Title: Gladys Asmah Lampoons NGOs
Headline: Ghana: Gladys Asmah Lampoons NGOs
Body_Text:
... They take undue advantage of poor and vulnerable people
THE OUTGOING MINISTER of women and children's affair, Mrs. Gladys Asmah
has taken a swipe at Non-Governmental Organisations (NGO's) saying they are
doing nothing to improve the lives of the poor in the country.
In an interview with The Chronicle in Accra last Tuesday, Mrs. Asmah,
currently the minister for Fisheries- designate noted that the NGOs take
undue advantage of the vulnerable in the country and take huge sums of money
from donors with the view of improving the lots of the poor.
According to her, these funds end up not doing the required project for
which they were collected. Some NGOs in the country criticized the women and
children's affairs ministry for being a "useless" entity, after it was set
up on the assumption of office of President John Agyekum Kufour in the year
2001.
The ministry was established to improve the livelihood of both women and
children in the country through poverty reduction strategies.
Mrs. Asmah, also the Member of Parliament (MP) for Takoradi noted that
gender activists in the country feared losing their jobs on the creation of
the ministry and therefore saw it as a threat to their NGOs.
She therefore accused them of not caring about the poor in whose name
they acquired the funding from donors, questioning the basis for their
attack on the women and children's affairs ministry.
"They should propose what can be done to solve the problems of women and
not talk only about gender," Mrs. Asmah stressed. The acting minister for
women and children's affairs indicated that following the numerous problems
associated with poverty such as infant mortality, streetism and
child-trafficking among others that affect the human resource of the
country, her Ministry was established to prioritize the needs of women and
children in the country.
She said the creation of the ministry had built the foundation for most
women and children in the country, taking them out of extreme poverty as a
result of micro-financing scheme adopted by the ministry.
Enumerating series of activities undertaken by the ministry in helping
women and children out of poverty in the country, Mrs. Asmah said her outfit
had, under the women's development fund, provided 50 groundnut and shea-nut
processing machines to women in Sakpala in the northern part of the
country.
Additionally, she noted that 220 gari processing machines were provided
to women throughout the country with the exception of Upper East and Upper
West, where cassava production is not effective, stressing that the ministry
was working for an additional 350 machines to be brought in to augment the
number so far distributed.
In Dormaa in the Brong Ahafo region, the MP for Takoradi said about 220
disabled women had accessed credit facilities from the ministry's activities
and they were currently in tie and dye projects.
Currently, the ministry had provided a total amount of Â¢150billion to
women throughout the country through the banks to engage in income
generating activities in order to reduce poverty among the women folk, the
Minister told the paper.
On the domestic violence bill currently before parliament, Mrs. Asmah
noted that the bill had not been passed because it lacked all the inputs
that were required to make it a good and excellent law, with no negative
drawback effects in the future.
According to her, the ministry was till awaiting contributions from the
public to be included into the bill.
She held that her outfit had been sending reports to the United Nation
on violence against children every three months.
The report outlined the legal and institutional frame works and
resources available to address the issues confronting the nation as well as
the role of civil society in addressing them.
The ministry, for the past four years, had developed an early childhood
care and development policy, which outlined the framework, underlying its
principles, goals, objectives and targets as well as implementation
strategies.
This policy is to enhance the survival, growth, development and
protection of all Ghanaian children since they are the foundation of the
nation's human resource development for the future.
Additionally, Mrs. Asmah noted that the ministry of women and children's
affairs had developed National Gender and Children policy to streamline
gender and children's concerns in the national development process.
The document seeks to regulate and conduct all activities entered into
with women and children at institutional and individual levels.

Original_Search_Title: Is Kony Ready to Embrace Peace?
Headline: Uganda: Is Kony Ready to Embrace Peace?
Body_Text:
Kampala — The Lord's Resistance Army is walking a tight rope. Peace talks with the
government are their only way from total annihilation. Emmy Allio analyses
why the rebels have been brought to their knees
Joseph Kony's Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) and the government are in
talks in an attempt to end the 18-year-old war. Last Tuesday (December 28),
chief mediator Betty Bigombe accompanied by Acholi MPs, Acholi district
leaders and diplomats met the LRA delegation led by Sam Kolo. This was a
preparatory meeting to set ground for talks between the government and the
rebels.
The success of the current peace initiative can best be analysed from
the viewpoint of the military. One needs to examine the factors that have
helped Kony to survive for 18 years and whether those factors still
exist.
The current LRA is a skeleton of what it was before the rebels invaded
Teso region. Teso is, no doubt, Kony's waterloo. Since then the LRA lost
most of its commanders and fighters through death, defections and
capture. Factors that have helped the army to break the back of the LRA are:
Resumption of diplomatic ties between Kampala and Khartoum. This severed
the LRA supply line from the Sudanese People's Armed Forces, the subsequent
military accord Sudan and Uganda signed has allowed the UPDF to operate
inside Sudan where the army now occupies the territory that was a safe haven
for the rebels.
The occupation of Kony's safe havens in Sudan has had a direct
effect. It is in Sudan that the rebels would take and train hundreds of
abducted children into child soldiers or sell the girls into slavery. It is
in Sudan that the rebels would retreat to rest and also evade the dry season
offensive by the UPDF in the north. During the dry season the bush is burnt
and there is little room for escaping the military operations. Lack of a
safe haven explains why LRA no longer raids to abduct. They now hunt for
food and drugs.
Acquisition of Mi-24 helicopter gunship has had a direct effect on the
war. This aircraft is fast, fairly precise and deadly. A military source
says the aircraft has killed more rebels in action this year than a brigade
of ground troops deployed in the north. What the army needs now is to
acquire a transport plane to be used in quick deployment of troops to remote
zones.
Decimation of other armed groups. In the past, the LRA roamed the
countryside at ease and attacked civilians at will. Part of the reason was
that the army used to be over-stretched. For instance, in 1995-1997, the
army fought various battles around the country, concurrently, against
Sudan-backed rebels.
These included the West Nile Bank Front (WNBF) of Col. Juma Oris, the
Uganda National Rescue Front, (UNRF-II) of Ali Bamuze, the Uganda National
Democratic Alliance (UNDA) of Herbert Itongwa, the Uganda Mujahideen
Movement in Buseruka in Hoima and later the Allied Democratic Forces (ADF)
of Yusuf Kabanda. Today, these rebels are either decimated or have abandoned
rebellion.
End of Congo mission.
Uganda's mission in the Democratic Republic of Congo had sapped up to
10,000 soldiers. Most of the soldiers formerly in Congo are now deployed to
fight the LRA.
Intensified battle against corruption in the military. The cancer that
has been causing hemorrhage in the UPDF is the large presence of ghost
soldiers, which had adverse effects on army operations. There was also
failure to supply food, uniforms and other logistics on time. This has
reportedly been adequately addressed through the institution of the Contract
Committee in the army.
Role of Sudanese militia groups
Equatoria Defence Forces (EDF) has been an ally of the LRA in Sudan
since 1994. Supplies to the LRA were channelled through EDF one of the 25
pro-Khartoum government militias fighting the Sudan People's Liberation Army
(SPLA). Others are the Janjaweed militias, now causing mayhem in Sudan's
Darfur province.
Last year, the EDF switched alliance to support the SPLA. Along with
SPLA, the EDF are now hunting the LRA which they accuse of raiding and
killing Sudanese.
Civilian military initiative
Kony met the most brutal civilian response in Teso. The Arrow Boys
backed by the entire civilian leadership mobilised themselves to help the
military to fight LRA. In Teso, the LRA were hunted and killed.
The Teso heroism was emulated by the Langi who formed the Amuka
militias. Here again, politicians and civic leadership put aside their
political differences to give the LRA a resounding defeat.
The formation of the Frontier Guards in Kitgum effectively ended the
free movement of the rebels in Kitgum. Even neighbouring Adjumani district
formed the Cock militias to fight LRA.
Over 30,000 militias have been recruited, trained and deployed to
supplement the army's efforts.
These militias blended with the army to guard IDP camps and patrol the
roads. This freed thousands of soldiers to go hunting rebels in the
countryside.
The political and religious leadership in Gulu and Pader must take some
blame for the continued presence of rebels in their areas. These districts
should have allowed formation of militias to supplement the army.
Accusations and counter- accusations between the army, politicians and
religious leaders further explains the complexities in wanting to end the
war through dialogue or military means. Whatever the case, the military
option has been overwhelmingly successful in the 2003-2004 season.
"The militias helped us to get enough manpower to hunt the rebels. We
trapped them at water springs, in the cassava gardens and at vital crossing
points. This weakened them and many surrendered," army spokesman Major
Shaban Bantariza says.
This tremendous military pressure has forced LRA to its knees. For
nearly a year, the rebels had no safe haven where to regroup and plan. With
nearly 50,000 soldiers and militias deployed to hunt less than 1,000 rebels,
the pressure is big. Peace talks are the only way out.
Over the weekend, several military sources concurred that Joseph Kony is
planning mischief. They say that the military has set its target on the dry
season from January to March to clear the rebels. "Kony has ordered Otti and
Kolo to go for peace talks in order to buy time. We have got that
information," Intelligence sources said.
Last Tuesday, Otti and Kolo asked for more time before actual peace
talks with the government can start.
The military says the ceasefire should be restricted to the 100 square
miles peace zone. "Anything rebel found outside this zone will be killed or
captured," the army says.
Whether it is peace talks or peace jokes, time will tell.

Original_Search_Title: Over 20.000 Refugees Return Home
Headline: Angola: Kuando Kubango: Over 20.000 Refugees Return Home
Body_Text:
Menongue — Around 21.227 Angolans, who were exiled in Zambia and Namibia on conditions of refugees, have returned spontaneous to the province of Kuando Kubango during this year.
According to the yearly balance of the provincial management of Social Welfare, Former Soldiers, War Veterans that ANGOP had access on Tuesday, the refugees to reach the region they travelled through Katuituzi (Cuangar commune) and Mucusso, Dirico districts, areas connected to Kuando Kubango.
In the ambit of the organized and voluntarily repatriation process started last July 16th current month have returned to the Maheba camp (Zambia), passing through Cazombo (Moxico) and Osire, Cassava camp (Namibia) about 2.333 Angolans.

Original_Search_Title: Dreaming of the Day I'll Introduce My Boyfriend
Headline: Uganda: Dreaming of the Day I'll Introduce My Boyfriend
Body_Text:
Kampala — It was a Friday evening and Sarah Kagabane was warming up for a Christmas party organised by her boss, Pastor Peter Sematimba.
Sarah Kagabane a.k.a Fiona is an all rounder entertainer. She is a presenter at Super FM, actress with the Afri-talents and sings with Mariam Ndagire's Trends band.
Kagabane is a daughter to Haji Musa Namiti and Ms Aisha Bin Ibrah Namiti of Kansanga Nabutiti zone in Makindye division.
She went to school at Stella Marris Nsuube for her primary and did her 'O' and 'A' levels at Kampala High School.
Kagabane does not want her age disclosed.
Although a Muslim, she fitted so well in a Christian environment that she was appointed head girl of Nsuube, a Catholic oriented school.
"We worked in the school gardens, every morning and evening till we became used to it. Then it turned into fun. Because we enjoyed stealing cassava and mangoes," she says.
Although nuns had trained them to put all their trust in Mother Kevin the saint, Kagabane and her friends, asked her for help in passing their exams, so that parents could visit them or pray to her that they do not get caught in the wrong.
"I remember vividly that we were not allowed to take food to school but we could always sneak in with sugar by sending a certain old man nicknamed Jjajja Biscuit who would bring anything out of the school compound provided he was given biscuits" she says.
Kagabane says her happiest moment in life was the day she got a hug and a Bible from the late Cardinal Nsubuga: "It was during a speech day when I danced my ribs and thrilled the cardinal that he offered me a Bible and praised me.
At Kampala High, Kagabane was to find more challenges.
"My first day was tears throughout, because of the teasing. A gang of big boys came around uttering big words until one girl called Flavia helped me out and we became friends from day one," she narrates.
Although it was a cat and mouse game with the boys, she kept a deaf ear.
As a way of finding leisure activity, she joined a school drama club where she met Mariam Ndagire who became her mentor up to today.
"I recall a funny incident when some boys seated next to me started a fight over desks to a point of shading blood and students thought the fight was about me, my God I felt sick". Kagabane.
She says the incident was only calmed by the police.
Kagabane later joined the Black Pearls, where she met her boyfriend.
"He used to be our fun at Riverside theatre and he tried all he could as a man in order to win me. He did at last. And the first gift I got from him was a silver watch and a pair of ear rings," she recalls.
This man used to praise her too much every time they would meet due to her character on stage.
"One time we went out together and although he was not straight forward he chose me the snack I could eat and it was the best choice," she adds.
Kagabane dreams of the day she will introduce her boyfriend to her grandmother Mariam Mubbi who raised her up.
For role models, Speciosa Wandira Kazibwe, Peter Sematimba and her aunt, Saida Bbumba.
She says those people have inspired her to become what she is today. She is proud of who and what she is.

Original_Search_Title: When the Costs Get High, Get Real
Headline: Uganda: When the Costs Get High, Get Real
Body_Text:
Kampala — It is an undeniable fact that the cost of living in Uganda has gone up,
yet people's incomes remain very low. An average sized bunch of matooke that
used to cost Shs 5,000 a year ago now goes for Shs 10,000-15,000.
It is particularly hard for low and average income earners who must
fight hard to make ends meet as they pay for food, water, medical care,
rent, graduated tax, etc. This is why it becomes necessary to come up with a
plan that will save you from what MSN money correspondent Melanie
J. Mavrides calls "financial purgatory." Let us see how this plan can help
you when buying foodstuffs.
Make decisions more carefully
Look at your expenditure vis-Ã -vis your income to determine how much it
is you will spend in a month, and how much you will save.
It is necessary to find out what compulsory purchases you must make, so
that you separate them from the optional ones. For a housewife, foodstuffs
are a must-buy while ice cream is not, however much the kids may crave
it.
Without a proper plan, chances are that the low-income earner will live
in a situation of constant panic, wondering how her family will pull through
the next day and the next week. Planning saves you from bad financial
decisions like lending the little money you have to a friend without being
sure you have enough to feed your family tomorrow, or buying luxuries such
as candies.
Make a list of the key foodstuffs you need
This is pretty obvious, but I will say it all the same. You need to know
what you really need, and in what quantities. The list could range from hard
foodstuffs like matooke, sweet potatoes, Irish potatoes, yams, cassava,
cassava flour, rice, groundnuts and beans to perishable ones like greens,
tomatoes, onions and fruits among others.
The quantities will depend on whether your budget is mid-week, or full
week, or even a fortnight. Generally, a one-week budget is okay, for it
saves you from the hassle of making daily purchases. A fortnightly budget is
bad because many of the foodstuffs are quite perishable, especially so if
you do not have a fridge. Even when you have one, it might help because
power cuts have become an everyday phenomenon.
Have an inventory of friendly market places
When shopping for foodstuffs, for example, keep an inventory of what to
buy where and when. Kalerwe market might be cheaper when buying matooke and
sweet potatoes, but it might be more costly when buying fruits or
vegetables.
It therefore becomes necessary to do a rough market survey so that you
go to the appropriate places when shopping for particular things. This is
important because the cost of a three-kilogram pineapple at Nakasero market
is not the same at Kireka market or at Bwaise.
Vegetables might be cheap at Kalerwe but costly at Kireka, while fruits
might be cheap at St Balikuddembe market and costly at Bwaise.
Determine the mode and time of purchase
It is necessary to determine the mode and time of purchase.
It is better to buy in bulk, meaning that you buy all the essential
things you will need in a week.
Morning hours (5:30 to 7 a.m.) when the prices charged are wholesale are
the best to shop. Many times the food we buy at say, 4 p.m. is two removes
from the wholesaler. The fellow selling this food to you probably bought it
at 6 a.m. from the trader selling from a pickup, and the pickup man might
have bought it from the one moving around with a lorry.
Ensure no wastage
This is a very important post-purchase issue, given the fact that the
person cooking might not know how much you have spent on it, and therefore
might be wasteful. It is common to see so much food being thrown away,
because the cooks overestimated the amount to be prepared. This is one thing
you need to fight, even if it means rationing the food on a daily
basis.

Original_Search_Title: 20 Perish in Road Accidents
Headline: Nigeria: 20 Perish in Road Accidents
Body_Text:
Onitsha/Asaba — BLOOD flowed freely in Agbor and Onitsha, Delta and Anambra states respectively yesterday when about 20 people were killed in separate motor mishaps.
At least 10 people were feared dead and over 38 seriously injured in an auto accident which occurred in the early hours of yesterday at the Agbor junction of Benin-Asaba expressway.
The Onitsha tragedy, however, struck when a Mercedez Benz 911 lorry carrying yam and other foodstuffs as well as passengers tumbled, killing 10 of them, mostly women.
The Agbor accident which occurred about 8 a.m. involved six heavy duty lorries, two buses and two motorcycles. Among the dead were four men and a boy.
An eye witness said the accident was caused by oil spill on one end of the dual carriage way from a petrol tanker, which was abandoned on the road close to the busy junction.
Daily Champion gathered that the spill forced the vehicles going to Onitsha from Lagos and Benin to skid off the road, hitting road side traders and passersby. Some of the vehicles had somersaulted causing the death of some passengers in one of the buses with many sustaining injuries.
But the Delta State Sector command of the Federal Road Safety Commission (FRSC) said only four persons were confirmed dead in the multiple accidents.
According to the Public Relations Officers (PRO) of the command, Mr. Dan Asanya, only three men and a boy were confirmed dead in the accident.
He noted that the accident was due to diesel spill from the tank which made the road slippery.
Daily Champion which visited the scene of the mishap saw the charred bodies of the victims and scores of sympathisers wailing and attempting to rescue those trapped in the badly damaged vehicles.
The Onitsha accident came less than 48 hours after some 10 people were feared dead at the Ogbaru main market following a clash between two rival groups contending for the leadership of the market.
But according to an eye witness account, the 911 lorry was coming from Edo State where the traders had gone to purchase food stuff including yams and garri when its brake failed while descending the Bridge.
The driver of the vehicle which was heading to Owerri, Imo State capital attempted to control it, but failed.
The vehicle then hit two other vehicles, including a bus carrying a family returning from Abuja for Yuletide and which was stopped by some miscreants allegedly collecting levies and tolls at the headbridge for the state government. But as luck would have it, the bus which was occupied by the family with some children inside, threw out the children to safety.
Eye-witnesses including a police officer attached to motor traffic division of Fegge Police Station told Daily Champion that six died at the spot, while the remaining were taken to Boromeo hospital. Four others were later said to have died, bringing the number to 10.
Following the accident, there was traffic jam on the bridge, especially with increased vehicle and human traffics at this Yuletide. The bridge has been under rehabilitation by Setraco Nig. Ltd for sometime now.
However, people have blamed the accident to the overcrowding of the bridge especially by the Mobile Policemen and some miscreants who allegedly force vehicles on the high way to stop on the pretext that they are collecting tolls and levies for the state government.
The Onitsha Area Commander, Mr. Dennis Anyagafu was not immediately available to confirm the incident, but a traffic police officers attached to Fegge Police Station, Onitsha who was at the scene of the accident confirmed the development.
The Area Commander had earlier directed uniformed bodies and all other organizations including agents of State Government and National Association of Road Transport Owners to stay away from the bridge.

Original_Search_Title: Phantom Coup: the Battle Line Between Atiku And His Boss
Headline: Nigeria: Phantom Coup: the Battle Line Between Atiku And His Boss
Body_Text:
THE country was woken to a very bad tale last week. The Vice
President, Atiku Abubakar told Nigerians on Tuesday that he had
uncovered plots to rope him and a number of his political associates
into an imaginary coup. Of course, going by the history of the country
especially in dying days of military rule in Nigeria, claims that some
forces were out to rope one into phantom coup would not particularly
surprise one. What would particularly be surprising is that a
democratically elected vice president of the country is making the
claim. Perhaps before going any further, a repeat of his entire
statement would suffice at this juncture.
He said, "my attention has been drawn to some documents circulating
in the country about a purported plan to rope me and some of my
associates into certain unconstitutional acts with grave consequences
for the stability of our country and my personal safety. For the
avoidance of doubt, I wish to state unequivocally, that by my
antecedents and democratic convictions, I will never be involved in any
plot to derail our hard-earned democratic system of government, having
uncovered and frustrated a coup plot whose masterminds are already
facing legal trial. I remain committed and faithful to my oath of
office to defend and uphold the Constitution of the Federal Republic of
Nigeria. In this regard, anyone purporting to drag my name or office
into anything contrary to this oath should be regarded as
mischief-makers who do not wish our country well. I will also use this
opportunity to react to insinuations about my continued role in the
present administration.
I am committed to serving out our entire term of office as mandated
by the Nigerian people when they re-elected President Olusegun Obasanjo
and I in 2003, in spite of false insinuations about my imminent
resignation. Finally, I wish to appeal to Nigerians to remain steadfast
and loyal to the government and constitution of Nigeria and avoid any
acts that could derail our cherished democracy." A careful reading of
the statement of the vice president and a study of the manner in which
previous similar phantom coups arose would show that both the vice
president and his boss, President Olusegun Obasanjo have reached the
point of irreconcilable differences.
After the vice president's speech was published last Tuesday,
sources in the Presidency revealed that the President was totally
incensed by it. According to one of the sources, the vice president
action was seen as totally disgraceful and an act that was meant to
undermine the integrity of the President within and outside Nigeria.
Said the source, "you will be very surprised that we knew nothing
about the statement before it was issued. I mean if indeed the vice
president was being truthful and he had actually uncovered what he said
he uncovered, it is expected that he would go to the President and
inform him about it and if he was going to issue a statement over this,
he should have told the President that he intended to do so. "When the
statement came out and talked about attempts to frame him up, everybody
of course knows whom he was talking about and there is no way such a
person can still conscientiously claim to be loyal to the president.
With that statement I can tell you that it was a final straw between
him and the President." Perhaps, the Tuesday statement and the feeling
it elicited in the President must have been responsible for the manner
in which the President treated the vice president in the hierarchy of
protocol last Wednesday at the 2006 budget session. The President had
in opening his speech at the joint session acknowledged the presence of
the Senate President, that of the Speaker of the House of
Representatives and so on without any acknowledgement of the presence
of the vice president who was sitted at the event. Of course, the vice
president was at the event on the invitation of the National Assembly.
But could the President have done otherwise? Hardly not.
Chief Olusegun Obasanjo, a full-star general of the Nigerian Army,
former military head of state, prominent member of the Eminent Persons
Group and a stickler for respect, is a man that brooks no form of
disloyalty or insubordination from any officer under him
notwithstanding his status. So after having uncovered previous acts of
disloyalty on the part of the vice president, he would certainly not
take the allegation of the attempt to frame phantom coup as a bowl of
pounded yam which he relishes so well.
The disposition of the President to the allegation from the vice
president would have been further sharpened by the fact that the
President himself is a victim of similar phantom coup under the
infamous regime of the late Gen. Sani Abacha, another military ruler
who seized Nigeria by the jugular. But for the grace of God, the
President would have been consumed by the Abacha phantom coup as he was
meant to be executed before a divine intervention got him freed.
So it is very curious that such a man could also want to frame
another for similar offence from which God delivered him from and even
rewarded him with the highest office in the land. Making such an
allegation against him would certainly mean foreclosing every possible
means of rapprochement with the President by the vice, however. Does
this mean the vice president cried wolf where there was none?
By his placement within the hierarchy of commands of military in the
country, the Vice President is in no position to uncover such a plot if
indeed there was one against him as the statement pointed out. However,
he could still get the information about such a plot if one takes into
consideration the person that he is. Atiku Abubakar, in his capacity as
the vice president, Atiku Abubakar has been the chairman, National
Economic Council, National Council on Privatization, National Planning
Commission, National Boundary Commission, Presidential Committee on 8th
All African Games, Chairman National Emergency Management Agency (NEMA)
among others.
These positions and responsibilities enabled him to display what
appears to be an inherent virtue in him as he manifested and multiplied
himself variously through hospitality and generosity all the time he
had effective control of his responsibilities within government.
Therefore, it would be doubtful if such a man could find it
difficult getting information of possible plot to frame him up as coup
plotter. Again, that he got it and told Nigerians about it, still
raises the question of whether indeed there was such a plot. Confronted
with this information, the pubic affairs assistant to the president,
Chief Femi Fani-Kayode, denied the knowledge of the presidency in such
a claim. According to him, "the response I gave to your colleagues when
they asked about this during the week was that we know nothing about it
and I still stand by that."
A close aide of the vice president however dismissed the ignorance
claim of Mr Fani-Kayode, nay that of the Presidency as balderdash.
According to the aide, "this is a plan that was real and we had to cry
out before they can go ahead to implement it because you know that
nothing can be taken for granted now. Their passion for third term
could force them to do anything at this moment."
The Federal Government has the responsibility of actually
investigating the claim of the vice president. A man of his position
should know what he is talking about when he talks. If he has claimed
that there are such plots, Nigerians would like to know those involved.
On whose orders were they plotting? When is the coup really expected
to be hatched and in which way was it to be hatched? These are the
questions that must be answered in the interest of Nigeria and
Nigerians.

Original_Search_Title: MASSOB On Rampage in Aba
Headline: Nigeria: MASSOB On Rampage in Aba
Body_Text:
Three Northerners were confirmed dead and eight others, including a
woman, seriously injured after attacks by hoodlums believed to be
loyalists of the Uwazuruike-led Movement for the Actualisation of the
Sovereign State of Biafra (MASSOB) who blocked the Aba section of the
Enugu-Port Harcourt expressway in the early hours of yesterday.
Daily Trust correspondent who visited the scenes, including areas
littered with smashed tankers and the yam and onion market along the
expressway, as well as the Aba Teaching Hospital where the bodies of
the three killed persons were deposited and the injured received
skeletal treatment, reports that the situation has been brought under
control by the police.
Inquiries by our correspondent revealed that the assailants started
their attacks on Saturday night when they broke into the yam and onion
markets and began looting the properties of the Northern traders,
threatening hell for those who did not immediately escape or who
attempted to defend themselves and their property.
Confirming the incident at the Aba central police station where over
one thousand defenseless Northerners had taken refuge, the Assistant
Commissioner of Police and Area Commander of Aba, ACP Sale Tanko,
described the attackers as hoodlums who had capitalised on rumours that
the Igbos were hit in the North.
Tanko described the incident as unfortunate, but added that the
situation has been subdued and that security has been beefed up at the
markets. Measures ware being taken to guard the worship and living
places of the Northern community in the city, he added.
According to him over ten persons have been arrested in connection
with the attacks along the expressway, which, he said, did not extend
to the living areas of the people inside the city.
He discarded rumours that the Igbos were hit in the North and could
not confirm any connection between the assaults and the arrest and
trial of the MASSOB leaders in Abuja.
"You have gone round with us and seen with your own eyes the extent
of the incident. Just this morning we received a distress call that
hoodlums were operating along the expressway. I immediately ordered our
armoured personnel carriers (APC) to the scene, where over ten culprits
were arrested. The situation has been subdued."
"Really, there was no prior information about the attacks. But there
were rumours of attacks on Igbos in the North which was neither heard
on the radio nor television. While we do not work on rumours, we acted
very fast. The arrested attackers are already undergoing interrogation
and we are going further to fish out all the perpetrators of the
dastardly acts," the Assistant Commissioner of Police Mr. Tanko
maintained.
He said seven tankers were smashed, other were properties destroyed,
and markets were looted, adding that security men have been drafted to
guard the areas with a Northern population.
Urging the people to be calm as the police has taken over, he
disclosed that he will dialogue with the leaders of the community to
find lasting measures for the security of the people.
Meanwhile the deputy chairman of the Aba Muslim community, Alhaji
Musa Iheakaram, has called on members of the community to stick to the
teachings of Islam and the tradition of Prophet Muhammad (SAW), both of
which inculcate peace, unity and tolerance, and to remember at all
times that whatever happens to a Muslim is preordained by Allah.
Confirming that the Aba Central Mosque has been surrounded by the
police, he commended the Abia State Commissioner of Police for promptly
visiting the victims at the hospital and ordering their immediate
treatment, and the Area Commander and the Divisional Police Officer
(DPO) for their prompt response to the distress call.
He advised the host community to desist from actions that are
capable of disuniting the country and foiling the hard-earned democracy
the nation has adopted in the last six years.
The Chief Imam of the Aba Central Mosque, Alhaji Bashir Idris,
blamed the heavy loss on the "communication gap" among the community
members, as well as the late response of the police to distress calls.
According to him the police was informed of the attacks at about 10
o'clock on Saturday night but nothing was done until the morning of
Sunday when some properties were already ablaze.

Original_Search_Title: Abuja Carnival: a Deliberate Bid to Celebrate Our Culture
Headline: Nigeria: Abuja Carnival: a Deliberate Bid to Celebrate Our Culture
Body_Text:
An appropriate take-off point for a discourse on the above topic would be to paraphrase Wolfgang Goethe, the German philosopher by remarking that the best of things Nigerian are so admirable at the individual level, but poor in the generality.
Students of Geography would remember that R.J. Harrison Church et al, in the book, Africa and the Islands wrote that Nigeria consists of over 400 linguistic groups, while broadcasts, to have a national spread, have to be carried out in at least 46 of the languages spoken in Nigeria. I answered call-up in the National Youth Service Corps in the 1983/84 session, and in one of the booklets given us, 387 ethnic groupings were listed. It was after this that the Koma people were "discovered."
During my first trip to South Africa some five years ago, a Zulu I met in Soweto boasted to me that his country has many ethnic groups, and that they speak 12 languages, which is why they call themselves the rainbow nation. Proud man, but he nearly fainted when I gave him statistics on diversity in Nigeria. When I added that except for three in our nine-man delegation, the rest could only communicate using English, my friend nearly fainted.
"How do you speak in Nigeria?" he gasped
The import of the foregoing is to underscore the heady, vibrant diversity in cultures and languages to be found within our borders, and the unattended potential that resides with it, which is why one is simply amused about the spate of comments about the on-coming Abuja carnival, including the editorial of an Abuja based newspaper giving slant to some religious misgiving about showcasing animist tendencies during the event. What animism?
Anyway, divergent views on the carnival and its purpose are welcome, especially in a democracy we are trying hard to nurture. However, it must be pointed out that Nigerians are not only looking forward to grand events like the Abuja Carnival, they need the refreshing tonic that year-ender events like this, and the Christmas Carnival in Cross River and others will provide. In a landscape where political tension is almost making it difficult to breathe, and economic spin-offs of policies debilitating, we all need the breather that the carnival will offer.
More than most countries in the world, Nigeria is uniquely blessed culturally. Ours is a diversity beyond the imagination of most with over 387 ethnic nationalities that present different and unique attributes that not only unifies the people but also identify with, and celebrate life. Though universally recognized, Nigeria's cultural assets are yet to enjoy the same gift of global acceptance such as the Chinese or even the French. French wines and spirits could be found and enjoyed anywhere in the world, but can I get palm wine or burukutu outside my locale, even in Nigeria?
This Abuja carnival is a strong statement in the re-engineering process for a new vision socially, economically and politically for divergent cultural assets of our people. Those who attempt to give religious slant to this event are merely trying to be more catholic than the Pope, an impossible feat. Of course, it is conceded that there is the need to watch out for aspects of our culture that may offend the sensibilities of even our own people, and consumers of our cultural assets.
But this need not overshadow the importance of showcasing to the world the majestic splendour associated with our durbars, neither should it diminish the mesmerizing dance steps of our masquerades at new yam festivals.
Indeed, who can dispute that celebrating life within 'life' is not the way of our people? In fact, there can be nothing satanic with our dances or even festivities associated with crowning of traditional rulers and even notable citizens of our great country.
It is sad that Nigerians easily consume cultural products of other countries but would label theirs offensive and animist. The Abuja carnival is expected to restructure our thought perceptions on our culture when it finally comes to an end next weekend. The carnival would engender our collective resolve to dress as Nigerians, eat and celebrate as a people uniquely blessed by God. President Olusegun Obasanjo must be commended for this initiative. The President dresses Nigerian within and outside the country and in so doing, has helped to showcase to the outside world the unique Adire cloth associated with the Yoruba Egbas of Nigeria. At G-8 meetings and other fora, Mr. President is a picture of regal composure in Nigerian dress that never fails to attract the paparazzi.
Can those attempting to put down the Abuja carnival name any city worth its name in the world without a China town, or Chinese restaurants? Can these critics count the number of Chinese restaurants in Lagos, Abuja, Port Harcourt, etc? Driving the streets of our major cities, you see buses marked Lebanese Community School, Deutsche Schule, etc. Why do these foreign communities set up schools here? To inculcate their culture in the young of course! The time has come for Nigeria to practically show some seriousness in driving the wheel of tourism development and promotion through culture. Are there Nigerian schools outside these shores, where our youth born outside are taught to be Nigerian?
We must be able to export our cultural assets, thereby beaming global attention on our near 400 ethnic groups. On the domestic front, Nigerians are bound to accept each other and also come to see the need to live in peace. Economically, a world determined to discover other people on earth, would find Nigeria interesting and would happily spend their money to share in our ways of celebrating life.

Original_Search_Title: Stella: Ndigbo Commiserates With Obasanjo
Headline: Nigeria: Stella: Ndigbo Commiserates With Obasanjo
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Igbo Speaking Community in Lagos State, weekend joined other
Nigerians in commiserating with President Olusegun Obasanjo over the death
of his wife, Stella and victims of the October 22, 2005 Bellview aircrash,
which claimed 117 passengers and crew at Lisa Igbore village, Ifo Local
Government Area of Ogun State.
Also, the group disclosed that it was in view of the tragic event that
the community decided to postpone the New Yam Festival celebration, which
had earlier been slated for October, but was shifted to this month
(November), as a mark of respect for Stella and the 117 passengers.
President General of the group, Chief Jonathan Nnaji JP. said the Igbo
Community received with shock news of the demise of Stella, which it said
was "the Igbo Community in Lagos, condoles with President Olusegun Obasanjo
and the 117 Nigerians that lost their lives in the ill-fated Bellview 210
Aircraft that crashed at Igbore in Ifo Local Government Area of Ogun
State".
Nnaji, who spoke through a statement signed by his Press Secretary,
Mr. Patrick Ibe, said Stella's death was "a big blow to the entire country
not because of her position as the First Lady but as a result of her role in
nation-building as a mother to all".
The group described "the First Lady as a detribalised Nigerian, who sees
the entire country as one indivisible family hence her unreserved love for
the less privileged in our society especially children.
The statement pointed out that "her pet project for the disabled
children speaks volume of this", adding that "her love for all can never be
quantified by anything and that informed the unabated cries that have
greeted her demise".
