Expressing sadness over the tragic loss, Nnaji said Mrs. Obasanjo died,
when the country needed her most, stressing that the vacuum created by her
death will be very hard to be filled.
The President General therefore, stated that it was against this
background that the group prayed that God grants Obasanjo and Nigerians the
fortitude to bear the irreparable loss.
Meanwhile, the group said the New Yam celebration, earlier postponed
will now come up this month in order to allow members and invited guests
have enough time to overcome the shock that followed the death of the First
Lady, Mrs. Stella Obasanjo and that of the 117 passengers of the Bellview
aircrash.

Original_Search_Title: The Bellview Plane Crash: Oh, We Got No Chance for Final Goodbye, Families Lament
Headline: Nigeria: The Bellview Plane Crash: Oh, We Got No Chance for Final Goodbye, Families Lament
Body_Text:
HOW else will you describe the disaster that befell Nigeria last
Saturday when Bellview Airlines Flight B3 210 crashed at Lisa village in Ifo
Local Council of Ogun State, killing all 117 people on board? Is there ever
any compensation for life? How will you compensate a woman who not only lost
her husband in the crash but also the opportunity to say a final goodbye and
give the body a befitting burial? How do you console a woman begging to be
given only a finger or an ear of her late husband to take home as something
to represent the whole body?
What story would calm a woman who had prepared a yam porridge for her
daughter and her son-in-law who were coming in from a honeymoon, but never
got home while the prepared food remained in the freezer for 48 hours after
the young couple had long died?
How do you calm a husband who pleaded with his wife to postpone the
journey till Monday since Saturday and Sundays were no working days to no
avail? Would a young man who drove his own brother to the airport and was
told his brother had missed the flight by fifteen minutes and should wait
for the next one ever comprehend how his brother entered the same flight he
was told he missed after the former had left?
Questions, questions, questions! These were some of numerous questions
that demanded answers as relations after relations of people perished in the
ill-fated Boeing 727 Bellview flight trooped into the corporate headquarters
of the airline at Opebi in Ikeja, Lagos or at the airline's office at the
Murtala Muhammed Airport, Lagos.
It was, indeed, heart-rending as emotions ran high for hours and for
days. Some came crying, others registered their protests as they all
demanded answers from the officials of the airline concerning their beloved
ones who were in the Abuja-bound flight that Saturday night.
The tears, the pains, the expression of anger, the visible apprehension,
the feelings of hopelessness were all natural expectations from people so
anguished by the cruel tragedy.
Who do you calm? The widow, the widower and the orphans whose
circumstances were created by the crash? Would some relations of these
victims ever get over the agony, the psychological trauma of losing their
loved ones? How will parents who lost their only sons or daughters ever
recover from the reality that their wards whom they waited for at the Abuja
end of the airport that Saturday night would never land to their embrace?
Could anything have saved the pilot from flying with his sister in the same
aircraft? What is the future for Faith and Patience Imasuen, the twin
daughters of the pilot who flew the ill-fated Bellview flight 210?
Indeed, there have been no answers. Only questions and questions. It was
a pitiful sight at the corporate headquarters of Bellview as relatives of
the dead got nothing but only insurance forms to fill, with words of
consolation coming from the airline's officials who were also mourning
because even their beloved ones also died in the crash.
Saturday Vanguard was there and at the airport for days and came
face-to-face with the bereaved families. And for all, one common denominator
was the feeling of emptiness by the distinct families that they would get no
chance to bury their dead, even as they did not get any opportunity for the
last goodbye. We bring to you the grief, agonies and views of the affected
families.
'I narrowly missed boarding the flight but my brother died in it'
SOLA and David Oneh, both of Deeper Life Church came to join the shuttle
bus from Bellview office to the sight of the crash. Sola was the person who
accompanied Steve Bayo, (92 on the list of the crash victims) to the airport
that day. Both were to travel together because they had much belongings but
the sight of many passengers at the airport who wanted the same flight made
them to drop Sola. He tells the story.
I cannot get this picture off my head. I saw all the people that boarded
the flight because Steve and I got there early. We had much load and we
spent so much time booking them in. I even helped the white couple and their
son with some of their loads at the booking. There was also another old lady
who boarded the flight whom I also helped with her luggage.
My brother (Steve) had wanted us to travel because his load was too much
but many people wanted that flight because of the time. There were some
people who still missed it and perhaps they never knew how lucky they were
at that point at the airport. I stood with the passengers and interacted
with many of them because I stayed long. I had to wait for Brother Steve to
leave before I left.
We had already concluded plans. We had called the Abuja end and asked
them to bring a bigger bus to the airport to pick our belongings. I waited
till they boarded the airline's bus that took them to the tarmac. I watched
from a distance as they boarded the plane.
I still remember the mood at the airport, the discussions, the laughter,
the phone calls. You know whenever it is time to board flights, people make
calls and we were all standing there. I would have been a victim but
something happened at the airport that made us to buy only one ticket. If we
had come late, perhaps I would have boarded that plane but because we came
early and finished checking in our goods and made arrangements for a bus to
wait at Abuja to pick them, we decided that there was no need for me to
travel with Steve.
What I really want now is to be taken to Lisa Village. We have been
coming since the Sunday that it was confirmed that the plane was sighted
somewhere. They even showed our cheque, the Deeper Life Bible Church
cheque. Brother Steve had died but God knows better. It's unfortunate we had
no chance to say bye to him but we know he is happy with the Lord.
Too sad, he's gone without a final word, mourns Uche Okolo's
brother
Casmir Okolo, elder brother of Uche Okolo who died in the ill-fated
flight from Lagos to Abuja still looked stupefied as he brought out the
particulars of his brother and began to fill forms given to him at the
corporate headquarters of Bellview Airline. He paused, looked up and asked
his companion, Clement who brought him to the place if his brother, Uche,
was now being referred to in the past tense. As a man, he tried not to shed
tears but they were unstoppably rolling down his cheeks.
"So, we're filling forms that Uche is dead, abi? And he is not coming
back? Clement, are we now filling forms about Uche and we're talking about
him in past tense? Uche that missed his flight by 15 minutes! How did he get
into it? He called me to say he missed his flight and would wait for the
next one. Yet, it was the one he missed that crashed and he was in it. How
did it happen?"
Ironically, it was the same Clement who brought Casmir to the premises
of Bellview plaza that also drove Uche to the airport that Saturday
evening.
"I was with him at the airport," he told Saturday Vanguard. "Uche was my
boss and my friend. We got to the airport 15 minutes behind schedule. He had
already missed the flight because the flight was loaded and set to leave
when we got to the airport. The engine of the airplane was running already
when I dropped Uche. The ticketing officers told him to wait for the next
flight and I handed over his personal effects.
"Uche always travelled like that. He said he wanted to come back Sunday
morning. He made many calls at the airport and asked me to tell some of his
friends and associates he had appointment with that weekend to come on
Sunday afternoon. Then, he asked me to leave and I did. We all knew he
missed the flight by 15 minutes and I can't really say how he ended up
boarding that same flight."
According to his shattered brother, Casmir, "we'll all die but how did
Uche die this way, without even saying a word to us?"
We didn't want my wife to travel that night-- Olaniyan
MR. Olaniyan was one of those who insisted on being taken to the site of
the crash. His wife, Mrs. Remilekun Olaniyan was among those who perished in
the crash. Olaniyan went to the site, saw the debris and trembled all the
way back because he was convinced from what he saw that nothing could have
saved the victims.
We didn't want her to travel that Saturday night. We all begged her that
since it was Saturday, that whatever she wanted to do could wait till Monday
but you see, she gave some good excuses why she needed to be there that
Saturday night and that's that. The rest is history.
You and I know the story but I have come to ensure that really there's
no mistake. No, there was none. My wife that left that Saturday evening is
not coming back to us.
'Nkiru was pregnant when she died with her husband'
Oby Oluigbo and her sister Vivian, both wearing dark glasses to cover
their blood-shot eyes came out of the Bellview plaza on Opebi fuming. Their
anger was not directed at anybody but at fate itself that dealt them a
deadly blow. Their younger sister, Nkiru and her husband Chukwuemeka Okoli
were on their way back from Ghana and some African countries where they went
for their honeymoon when they boarded the ill-fated Boeing 727 Bellview
flight that crashed Saturday night.
"Isn't this life incredible?" Oby asked the journalist. "Isn't it funny
how things turn out? Nkiru was my younger sister. She was aged 22 years, so
full of life. A few weeks ago, we were all together, family and friends
during her wedding to Chukwuemeka and everybody was happy. Nkiru was
pregnant with her first child and died with it and her husband. Is this life
not wicked?"
Oby Oluigbo told this paper that her sister and her husband were
returning from honeymoon, the last lap of which they spent in Ghana.
"They called us before they boarded that flight that they were already
in Nigeria and would be in Abuja in the next one hour. They asked us to wait
for them at the airport and asked my mother to prepare yam porridge for them
which she did. We're still waiting for their flight to land.
"Do you know that none of the wedding gifts has been opened? Not even an
envelope or a gift has been unwrapped. Everything is still like that, like
they were given to them during the wedding. Some people even brought their
presents after my sister and her husband had embarked on their
honeymoon. And they were coming back from that honeymoon when it
happened.
"Have I told you about the child? Nkiru was pregnant, her first child
and they all perished. Do you see the way life is? You needed to have seen
my sister and her husband, so full of life, very much in love, so happy and
now this happened!
"Where do I begin their story and where do I end? Do you know that my
mother prepared yam porridge and is still waiting for her daughter (Nkiru)
and son-in-law, Chukwuemeka to come home and eat. The yam porridge is still
in the freezer. My mum is still waiting for this joke to be over. But it's
alright. They are happy wherever they are today. God will take care of
them."
But Vivian would want more explanations. "It's not God's wish for anyone
to die young. Nkiru was 22. Are you saying no one could explain this? Are
they saying this could not have been avoided? Just three minutes after
take-off and it was death. Why did it take Nigeria so long to find where the
aircraft crashed? Did rescue come early enough to try and save some souls?
"Some things have not been properly explained to us and that is what we
are demanding from the government and the airline. What really happened?
Nkiru and Chukwuemeka and all others would feel better in their graves if we
find answers to their death.
"And could you imagine the misinformation? We had been all over the
place, from Kwara to Ogbomosho, Kishi, Shaki and finally to Lisa
village. We've seen the debris and all we know is that Nkiru and Chukwuemeka
are dead. All we want to know is why the happy couple, so much in love and
their unborn child would ask us to prepare yam porridge for them and are not
yet back. We saw the carcass of the plane. We know where Nkiru and
Chukwuemeka are buried. It's so painful we won't be able to give them a
befitting burial. It equally painful that we couldn't meet our sister and
her husband after their honeymoon.
"Is this the end of a story that began happily? Nkiru was younger than
us but she found love earlier and settled down in marriage. Now, is this the
end? Buried in that strange land we went to with body parts scattered all
over the place? It is a sad story. This is nonsense. We want to know what
happened?"
NANM will forever miss Sabulu, says Okoh
COMRADE Linus Sabulu, president of the National Associaton of the Nurses
and Midwives (NANM) had come to Lagos to be part of the Nurses and Midwives
Council of Nigeria meeting. The meeting had lasted from Thursday - Saturday,
October 22, 2005.
Sabulu who hailed from Gombe State had checked out of the hotel and was
eager to rejoin his family in Abuja even though his wife had told him not to
bother returning till the next day. Love for family had an upper hand and
that was how he boarded the ill-fated flight.
According to Frank Okoh, secretary of the Lagos State chapter of NANM
who was with him on Friday said: "Sabulu had thought the meetings would have
ended on Friday but it spilled over to Saturday. I had gone to his hotel
room to discuss some personal and association matters. He was full of hopes
for our association and how to put it on the path of progress. The
association will really miss his astute leadership. He rose from auditor to
financial secretary, deputy president before eventually becoming the
president in the over ten years of his involvement.
"Sabulu was a very nice guy. I am not saying so because he's dead. He
was a result-oriented leader. His driving force propelled the association to
greater height. He was a man always after success. Only God knows why he and
the other passengers of the ill-fated Bellview flight had to die that
way.
"Our association's executives have been meeting and will soon come up
with plans on how to honour him. He was a friend and a wonderful
colleague. We would forever remember him for his positive attitude to
things."
'It's a pity search-and-rescue efforts were slow'
KUNLE Martins is the president of Cabin Crew Members Association of
Nigeria which was formed in 1990. Three crew members who belong to the
association in the persons of Victoria Etim, Queen Ozekhome and Sanni Steve
were involved in the air crash. Martins spoke on what the body intends to do
to ensure that the families of the deceased get their entitlements.
There are those who would point to the risky nature of our job, but I
want to tell you that some people have flown for thirty five years and
nothing has happened to them. But I want to call on the government agencies
involved in this business to take it seriously. I say this because a few
months ago, we organised a seminar/workshop on safety in air transportation
in Nigeria. No one came from FAAN, NAMA.
But Governor Tinubu who didn't even have a stake, sent a
representative. I want the government to put certain checks in
place. Government must check how much money is released to different
agencies and how they spend the money. This administration must look into
these things.
It is also shameful that our search and rescue team could not trace
where the aircraft crashed. I was with the AIT crew when we got to Lisa. I
saw pieces of parts of the aircraft and human beings and could only imagine
what may have happened to the plane. Our lives are not safe. It is not like
that all over the world. We only have to rely on God.
I have been flying since 1993. I started with Okada Air. I see the work
as just another job. An accident does not make you not to drive around
anymore. What must worry Nigerians is the lack of knowledge and inability to
take prompt action when there is an emergency.
The area where we, as a union, will take seriously is monitoring what
happens to the insurance pay cheque of the victims. This is an area where
there is always a problem. I recall the case of one young woman who worked
for one airline and died on active duty. We fought that case until she was
paid her entitlements. I don't believe Bellview Airlines will try to do
anything funny. But as a union, we'll only monitor it to see that the
families receive the rights of our late colleagues who were wonderful crew
members of note in Nigeria.

Original_Search_Title: Condolences for Stella
Headline: Nigeria: Condolences for Stella
Body_Text:
Chief Richard Akinjide: God bless her, receive her into your glory all human are mortal, who are we to question your wish Almighty You know best, You know why.
Chief Paul Lola; Bangbose Martins (Commander de ordem do kio Branco do Brazil): Mourn for the cross of your very dear wife and a patriotic woman leader. May Almighty God console you, Mr. President at this time period when another nation disaster of the Bellview Airline and the entire passengers perished unexpectedly. I grieve because we all did not dream of all these during our banters with the president. (Madoda Silva of Brazil).
Dr. Bode Olajumoke: It's a great shock for the entire nation, she lived an exemplary life of service to the humanity, you can not ask for more even from the eulogies being poured on her nationwide, am certain that her husband and relations, friends are overwhelmed. I am satisfied that she is resting in the bossom of God. She was the mother of the nation, too young a mother to leave at the age of 60.My condolence to the father of the nation and the entire nation would be certainly missing her. Also my condolences to the families of victims of the ill fated plane, like Waziri Muhammed, Mrs Sokenu, Deji Omotade. We pray that God would stop this kind of incident. God has a purpose for everything
Bruce Onobrukpeya (Ben Bruce family): Goodbye our beloved Nigerian First Lady and a role model. Your legacy will continue to lead the country forward to a brighter future.
Lt Gen. Victor Malu(rtd).: Mama, you wouldn't have departed at a worse time. We pray to God to have mercy on your soul.
Kehinde Sofola( Former Attoney-General Lagos) : It's so unfortunate that she had died but God know's best and all depends on all God wants to do. Everything that has a beginning definitely has an end. May her soul rest in peace.
Wahaab Alabi Oba(NUJ president Lagos State Chapter): A tragic loss and monumental disaster for the nation. The country will continue to remember you for your humility and indeed, you're a mummy.
Chief Chide Ayaegbu (Chisco Transport); May her gentle soul rest in peace.
Esan Ogunleye (Registrar/CEO the Chartered Institute of Bankers of Nigeria); With heavy heart, I wish to express our heartfelt condolence. We will miss you. We wish the Obasanjo's family God's help and a fortitude to bear this irreparable loss.
His Majesty De Wheno Alohu Menu-Toyi 1,the Akran of Badagry kingdom. : Will be remembered within and around the epitome of love and absolute purity, humility who worked to uplifts the Nigerian Child.
Eze Ndigbo of Lagos: We love you but God loves you best. may your soul rest in peace mother of all. As a mark of honour to you we suspended this Yam Festival that supposed to take place on the fateful day.
Festus Odusanmi Chairman State House Lagos: Ha mama you left us without notice, you were a rare gem in your chosen profession. We love you but God Loves you most, we shall continue to miss you dearly. African women are yearning for you and your efforts in bringing succour to the less privileged. "Sun re o".Adieu mama your are great.
The Speaker Lagos State House of Assembly, Mr. Jokotola Pelumi : This is one monumental loss that is unmatched in the history of this country, she was the best in all things and to all manners of people who came her way and will be sure missed by all those who appreciates the legacy of children. We have lost the most ardent crusader of one Nigeria and of those incurable optimists who believes in Nigeria experience. Do rest in sheer bliss. Speaking with the media ,Mr. Jokotola said the state government would be considering immortalising her for her outstanding role she had played in the development of women, children and the nationhood.
Chief Molade Okoya-Thomas, Asoju Oba of Lagos : Goodbye to the lady of honour whose hand I had the honour of shaking at wedding on Saturday before her death.
Samson Nwasei( Forum for Peace& Justice): We miss you as a first lady of Nigeria. rest in peace.
Sam Enudi (forum for peace & Justice): We and indeed Nigerians miss you dearly mama(Adieu Mama)
Pator Bernard B. Adi (NBCI): May the Almighty confort Mr. President and all Nigerians in this tragic loss. Amen.
Bola Dada( Ministry of Foreign Affairs): May her soul rest in peace. May it please Merciful God to forgive her sins. Amen.

Original_Search_Title: The Long Vigil for Debt Relief
Headline: Nigeria: The Long Vigil for Debt Relief
Body_Text:
Lagos — I did not realize the power of the Paris club until I arrived the headquarters of the French Ministry of Finance and Industry. Located near the Seine River, the building announces its majesty and power by a somewhat strange statement. There is an eerie silence that hangs around it. It is sombre; it is grim. All around it, there is a strange absence of traffic - of persons and vehicle. Thinking back, the quietude and gloom that grab you as you approach it, reminds of a graveyard. But what is the difference. It may have been a deliberate way to announce its real purport. In the new world order, that building represents a symbolic grave yard, where the Paris Club plays a merciless undertaker. From what I observed about the way its business is done, that building has become the graveyard of many countries, whose profligate, and sometimes natural disadvantages, have put at the mercy of the Paris Club and such other institutions that preside over international capital and development.
Beyond the faÃ§ade, what this building hides is perhaps the lever that drives the balance of economic power. In one of its wings is the secretariat of the Paris Club, that exclusive gang of creditor nations that undermine the fortunes of many developing countries.
As you approach the back gate, two uninformed policemen, and others in plain clothes, usher you into the place. Once inside the foyer of the secretariat, the club's real but understated power hits you. The activity inside the building belies the silence outside. All manner of officials - young men and women - schooled in the politics of international capital and disappear into rooms, clutching thick files, in which unmistakably are laid their tracking of our misfortune. They disappear into meetings where the fate of supposedly sovereign nations are considered with all manner of contempt, and least warmth and compassion. The officials whose great grand-fathers also sat in rooms in Europe and Carved up among themselves distant lands as their possession, carry on in such a confidence that tells you, that they understand their power as creditors, to not only collect debts ensure that there remain debtors all over the world. What happens to a country may depend on the mood and judgement of these people.
It is said that the debtor has no dignity. It didn't make sense until I observed the politics of debt at the club's secretariat. I became thoroughly convinced that no matter how big Nigeria thinks it is, it cannot consider itself a sovereign as long as it was still indebted to the Paris Club. The poignancy of Nigeria's new-fangled drive to exit the Paris club, was so acute, in my mind that I decided to keep a diary of events. In a subtle kind of way, it contributes to the debate about whether Nigeria had a choice in the matter of whether to pay, or not. And also, when and how to pay.
2.20 p.m. The club President Mr. Xavier Muscat, marches into the hall. Finance Minister, Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala, who has by now lost her voice, and was tired staying up all night working on Nigeria's presentation exchange pleasantries with other delegates at the waiting lounge. On this Tuesday, she was barely able to stand.
In the expansive waiting room of the Paris Club, we the Nigerians (usually few may be because it was not a very occasion), were accosted by Mr. Menachem Katz, the representative of the IMF at the negotiations. He inquired whether we have heard the good news. We said we had not. He pulled out his cellphone and began to read the report, the import of that is that the IMF has approved the policy support instrument (PSI) on Nigeria's economic reform programme. The Paris club had requested IMF endorsement as a condition for debt relief. The endorsement meant that the debt deal would go ahead. "This is good for Nigeria," he said.
The minister herself, who this time, was seated in hall, knew Nigeria had moved up in terms of rating. From the way some of the delegates milled around her, she was sure even before her presentation that things might be different this time. The last time the Nigerian delegation was in Paris, they were received formally and coldly. She and her colleagues were made to hang around the corridors for many hours before they were allowed in to the meeting. But on this day, she was in the middle of a large number of delegates, people who had all along insisted Nigeria must pay what it owed them.
2.25 p.m.
More officials' men, women-in-grey and black suits file the room. They were waiting to decide whether Nigeria, their long, recalcitrant debtor since 1964 would be allowed to go home, free.
2.40 p.m.
The President claps his hands and announces the commencement of the meeting. We were left at the waiting room like a man whose wife had been wheeled into a labour room.
2.50 p.m.
Properly seated opposite an intimidating clan of white people, the minister opens her files and adjusts herself ready like a student waiting to defend her thesis. A white man, Xavier Muscat clears his throat and begins to speak in French. When he ended, Okonjo-Iweala, confidently greets the gathering in French, then switches to English. "What I will like to do, is to brief you on the progress made on our economic reform programmes, then the problems and go on to make our request. With slides running, she began to roll out a forest of statistics, graphs and projection that showed Nigeria's progress with the economic reform programme.
3.10 p.m.
The journalists from Nigeria, infact the only ones allowed, were asked to leave the room. To underscore the seriousness of the Paris club, the media does not cover its activities, except it issues statements. On this occasion, we imposed ourselves on them, for we were sure of the politics of debt at home.
3.50 p.m.
A delegation marches into the waiting lounge. We guessed they may be from Latin America. We were right. One of them heard us and with a broad smile walked over to announce that they were from Dominican Republic. "You guys are here today?" he asked. "Yes, they are on our case right now," we told him. "We came here to review the figures with them," he continued. "We wish you guys luck," he said some how unsure of what may happen. "So do we," we shot back. With that, the group shuffles to a corner, to listen to a Paris Club official address them informally, some of them with their hands behind their back.
4.30 p.m.
Okonjo-iweala comes out of the meeting. After the presentation, the President asked the Nigerian delegation to leave so that they can consider Nigeria's request and begin the negotiations. We gather that the countries from which the minister was expecting trouble, appeared swayed by her lucid presentation. Germany, UK and Netherlands asked questions about Nigeria's commitment to pay, sought assurances that the gains of the relief will not be stolen, and plans on how Nigeria will apply the resources to realising the millennium development goalsÃ¢-oe
To our left, the Latin American delegation sat, reviewing their notes. The way they huddled together, you will think it is a study group in an undergraduate class, working on an assignment. But in reality it is the delegation of a sovereign nation, who has come to judgement over debts owed other countries. For those who think it is a fair world, the stay inside the all-marble building tells clearly where the power lies. Forget that heads of governments go to the UN and speak annually, the real power lies with the Paris Club. So you ask: where is the new world order?
4.55 p.m.
The minister walks over to chat with us on her presentation and to down some coffee to keep awake. This time you could see she was physically drained.
5.20 p.m.
She returns to the technical meeting, downstairs with the rest of the Nigerian delegation to prepare for the actual negotiations.
8.30 p.m.
The Paris Club secretary, speaks to us. He reveals nothing but from his enthusiasm, the prospects looked good. It was from him that we gathered an agreement was afoot. But he made sure he let us know it will take a long while. He asked us to return to our rooms , get some sleep and return by 10 a.m. on Wednesday.
9.05 p.m.
The waiting room began to fill up again. The delegates who had gone for dinner return to the chambers. In other rooms, staff of the Secretariat punch away at computers. The Nigerian team return to their room to plot strategies to ensure it does not concede much. We the journalists were at this point persuaded to return to our hotel to get some sleep, since we came to the meeting from the airport.
DAY 11
7.05 a.m. A knock on the door of my room woke me up. There was Paul Nwabuikwu, the Minister's special assistant. He half-dragged himself to the room. "We just left and nobody slept a wink. Those guys kept bringing new demands and trying to squeeze every dime." What of the minister? "She negotiated with them all night," he said. "You know what the minister told me," he asked. "Those guys thought I will get angry. But I was too tired to get angry." With that he shifted to his room, but not before he said. "We are returning there at 10.00 a.m." What that meant is that the negotiation had run from 9.30 p.m. to 6.30 a.m.
10.10 a.m.
The delegates returns to the chambers. Ambassador PB Preware had joined the minister.
10.11 a.m.
The secretary of the Paris club, Mr. Milan, a smooth-talking show man, who can pass for a rock star, stops by our corner - the now popular Nigerian corner. There was Cordelia Ukwuma, and Austin of NTA, Emma Ujah of Vanguard, and a sympathetic Gambian who has come to represent the ADB. We exchange banters and I ask him: "Are we going to have an agreement? Are we going to keep this vigil today? His answer was trained: "I hope not."
10:20 a.m.
The minister and her group go into the meeting.
10:30 a.m.
The club President Xavier Muscat, his secretary and few key heads of delegation leave the entire room upstairs and return to a corner at the waiting lounge for a meeting. They whisper to themselves. We could see there was some debate. This, raised anxiety among Nigerians. Would those people reject Nigeria's proposals after all these?
10:40 a.m.
The President breaks the meeting and returns upstairs for the day's session to begin.
11:30 a.m.
The National Assembly delegation arrives. In the team are Senator Udoma Udo Udoma, Senator Effiong Bob, Hon. Farouk Lawan and Hon. Sanusi Sadiq.
12.05 p.m.
The group meeting in another room came downstairs and walk out of the building. Nobody is sure where they were headed and none could ask them. They are the Paris Club. They were followed by another group of 10 men and women carrying heavy red and blue files, supposedly evidence of Nigeria's past profligacy and recklessness.
1.10 p.m.
The Nigerian delegation leave for lunch. We gather from some of the Paris Club staff that an agreement had been reached, but will be debated after lunch.
4.53 p.m.
An IMF official and an aide of the Minister exchange banters. "Is the white smoke out?" he asked the Nigerian referring to the agreement. "No, the conclave is going well, but the white smoke is not out. However, the Pope is getting dressed." It did not make sense initially, but it later list us. The wait for the deal has been as long as the papal conclave.
11.00 p.m.
The Paris Club sends signal that it was drafting a press release. This is the first indication that Nigeria has a deal. But another long wait begins.
1.32 a.m.
Senator Udoma comes down from the room where the Nigerians were meeting to chat with us. "We will be through in an hour or two," he said. If it ends in two hours that will be was past 3.00 a.m. For people who are booked on a 10.55 a.m. flight to Lagos, that is a hard task. But we have no choice than to wait. Among us, there was a consensus: any sacrifice to ensure that Nigeria does not remain in this indignity was worth it.
3.10 a.m.
The agreement in gleaming red folders are brought into the room. After a long wait, the process of signing begins. The minister signed first. Then the agreement is taken to heads of delegations to sign. It was a slow process, repeated to ensure that two sets of agreements are signed.
4.00 a.m.
Mr. Ambrose Fayolle, grabs the microphone, satisfied that the deal has gone through. "We are pleased and proud of what we have achieved, collectively in this negotiation," he began. "We are extremely convinced that without your involvement and commitment this would not have been possible," he said referring to the minister. "This is a new start for Nigeria and the creditors. This is an exemplary exit strategy and we are proud to be part of your fight against poverty," he told Nigeria.
Once the minister finished her reponse assuring them of Nigeria's determination to continue on the path of reform, handshakes and backslaps erupted. Nigerians wildly congratulated each other. Dr. Manzur Mulitar, the DMO chief was overcome with emotion. The feeling was unbelievable as we made to leave, Mr. Fayolle, announced that the next meeting at 10 a.m. will be with Dominican Republic. Just before she left the secretariat, the Minister said something that ought to guide our policies in the future. She said: "I do not wish that any Nigerian or set of Nigerians will ever come here to face this again." Does everyone agree?
5.50 a.m.
The Nigeria delegation arrive the residence of Ambassador and Mrs. Rewane. His wife had been up all night. She had prepared Nigerian dishes, pounded-yam, amala and egusi soup. We were ecstatic and as the same time hungry. We ate amala at that unholy hour and by 6.10 a.m. we were on the way to our hotels and the airport.

Original_Search_Title: Two Suspected Kidnappers Lynched
Headline: Nigeria: Two Suspected Kidnappers Lynched
Body_Text:
Abeokuta — HELL was virtually let loose in the Sabo area of Abeokuta the Ogun State capital yesterday as two suspected child kidnappers were lynched by irate mob and their corpses set ablaze.
Police had a Herculean task controlling the huge crowd that thronged the area while another suspect was rescued from the mob by the policemen but not without their vehicle being vandalized.
Eyewitness account said that the two men with one of them having his hairs platted had arrived Sabo area yesterday morning asking for alms from the people claiming to be Sango cultists.
However at about noon, the two men were said to have visited a GSM call center where one of them was said to have placed a call to woman at the other end whom he allegedly told that they were on their way back.
He was said to have told the woman that their mission was successful and that they would soon be with her.
The lady at the GSM centre based on the conversation was said to have immediately alerted people around the area telling them of her suspicion that they were child kidnappers.
The two men were subsequently accosted and when searched were found to have various sums of money on them and six tubers of yam which they claimed to have just bought.
After sometime the crowd started beating the suspected kidnappers while they were matcheted and their bodies set on fire.
Police on arrival intervened but the people had been killed at that time while another person found around a Nursery and Primary school in the area was already being matcheted before he was rescued by the police.
The suspect who said he was returning from the hospital where he had gone to receive treatment said that he was accused of lurking around the school and was subsequently mobbed before he was rescued.

Original_Search_Title: Point of Order: Strengthening the PDP
Headline: Nigeria: Point of Order: Strengthening the PDP
Body_Text:
IF you are one of those who have been celebrating the pending demise of Africa's largest rally (quoting Audu Ogbeh in his capacity as the chairman of the Peoples Democratic Party), please stop. Oh yes, stop. Because the PDP is on the way to transforming into a party, from the rally it has been. I wrote a piece in this column on August 4, 2002 with the title of Between Rallies and Parties. I had been discussing the PDP which Audu Ogbeh had described as more of a rally than a party. I said what I believed a rally is, that is a gathering of all sorts, without a soul; and what a party is, that is a group of people who by and large, are united in opinion. I visited the past and spoke of the groupings that would qualify to be parties and those that would be more appropriately referred to as rallies.
I then came to the PDP and said, "The PDP was made up of as many as 105 groups. The only evidence of a soul in the party has been the PDM of the vice president, a heritage from Yar'Adua. But how much of the soul of the PDM is there to give a name to nowadays when the major actors in that only coherent member of the PDP body is a house divided? The oxygen mask of the PDP is being drained of its content and will be a mere husk if nothing is done to focus the party..." (see page 426 of Democracy Watch, A Monitor's Diary, which is a compilation of my contributions to this column over a period of four years, from November, 1999 to March, 2003)
I wasn't done yet with what I was saying. I said that parties have a thing to live for, and a dream to fulfill. The PDP, I said, "is the most dangerous enemy of the PDP." And I referred to "the quarrels, the maimings, the killings" that were going on when primaries were being conducted for local government elections that had to be put off until 2004. And of Audu Ogbeh, I said he was focused and serious-minded enough to turn the PDP from a rally to a party, and added, "but in a rally, there are many who have their reasons for being present. Will those who believe more in rallies than parties let Audu and his team build the PDP into a party, into a body energized by a soul?" (page 427). Time shall tell, I said. And you are all witnesses to what time has told. Audu could not build a party out of the PDP because he was thrown out of Africa's biggest rally.
Those behind Audu's exit must have sat down and revisited the make-up of the PDP. Let me tell you how my friend Alfred explained it to me when I visited him at his beautiful home in Lekki Phase 1, Lagos, last week. He said the G34 was the main group that formed the PDP. In fact PDP was the only party, but there were all sorts of groups. When some leaders of the groups discovered that some incompatibles were in the PDP, they quit and formed the APP. When some analysts discovered that APP was the core Northern party and the PDP was dominated by and large by the Middle Belt and the Igbo, the Yoruba opted for their Alliance for Democracy, and AD was born. The Yoruba were still upset and angry at what had been done to them, with the annulment of the 1993 presidential elections which had been cancelled for reasons they refused to accept...
I told Alfred I knew all he was saying and had even written about them. He said he had never thought that impatience was one of my faults. Would I listen to him or... I apologized because what we were discussing had to do with the problems in Edo State which culminated in the attempted kidnapping of Chief Tony Anenih, chairman of the Board of Trustees of the PDP, at the home of Chief Inu Umoru, the leader of the PDP in Edo North. I had told him that I was worried that Chief Anenih who was known and recognized as Leader by party men the country over, even by the President, could be so harassed and humiliated in the senatorial district which can be said to be the most loyal to him of all the senatorial districts in his areas of influence, including his own Esanland which is the Edo Central Senatorial District.
Alfred told me that this point in time was predictable. The G34 claimed to have formed the PDP. How many of the members are in mainstream PDP today? Why are the Offors and Ubas in charge of PDP in Anambra State where Alex Ekwueme comes from? The strongest group in that PDP was the PDM which Vice President Atiku Abubakar inherited. With the dominant role Atiku had played in the party, and the specific case of the 2003 Presidential elections when Atiku changed his mind to confront Obasanjo during the primaries, was it not a question of time before tough decisions would be taken to stem the Atiku threat? And what is happening now is the acting out of what was scripted after the 2003 elections!
Well, I was not aware of any scripting of anything, but it was predictable that Baba would weaken Atiku's power base during their 2003 ... 2007 tenure. And he had been doing so! Was anything wrong with that if Baba must keep the promise he was said to have made to IBB?
Alfred laughed and I wondered why. He told me to forget who Baba would support or not support, and whether any promises were made or not. What are promises when circumstances change? The preoccupation now is to get hold of the PDP because it is a structure that has control of the honey pot. The aim is to make it a party, not let it remain a rally. And not just that. The plan is to make it a party the President can and should control. Why not? Who would be in power for this long and be stupid enough to let another person control the instrument of power? The decision to do what is happening now was a tough one to take, but if it was not taken, then it would be more difficult in future taking it.
I had begun to listen to Alfred because I know the crowd he moves in, and with. He told me that all talk about reconciling anywhere was no more than war games. He got up and did a waltz with an invisible lady, in illustrating how Okadigbo's official residence was opened by the President and the very next day, Okadigbo was out of office! He did not have to remind me of the pounded yam that the President ate in Audu Ogbeh's house a few days before he was thrown out!
But what had the attack on Anenih got to do with what I was being told? Alfred said the decision had been taken to make the PDP a party. The registration was a step in that direction. It was really nothing than ensuring that those who held the party cards believed in the party and owed allegiance to the President who started out being lent the PDM to climb to power with, and would like to quit, if he does, leaving a party he technically owns.
The implication was scaring but Alfred did not seem to be moved or embarrassed by what was happening. Atiku and his group will be eased out if they don't have the sense to leave in peace! Anenih is the arrow-head. "Has Anenih ever failed to fix anything for Baba since 1999?" Alfred asked me. I did not answer the question because I had been thinking of the consequences of easing out Atiku from the PDP. Let's look at the stakes next week.

Original_Search_Title: Prices of Essensials On the Increase
Headline: Nigeria: Prices of Essensials On the Increase
Body_Text:
As Nigerians groan under the recent increase in prices of petroleum
products, prices of consumables have taken an upward trend.
In a recent market survey conducted by Vanguard in major markets in
Lagos, prices of most essential food items like meat, fish, rice, beans,
garri, tomato, pepper, vegetable oil, maize and frozen foods rose beyond the
reach of the average consumer.
Among the markets visited which included Alaba suru, Idumagbo, boundary
market, Ajegunle, Mile 12, Ketu, Mushin, Oshodi, Iddo.
A bag of rice which before the increase in prices of petroleum products
sold for N5,800 in August 2005 now sells on average for N7,300 depending on
the brand.
A kilo of meat which sold for N400.00 before the fuel increase now costs
N600:00, while 20kg carton of fish sells for N5,800:00 as against N3,200:00
depending on the brand of fish before the increase in pump price of
petroleum products. A bag of beans now sells for N14,000:00 as against
N13,000:00 before the increase in fuel.
In like manner, price of tomatoes took the upward trend for N3,800:00 a
cartoon against N3,500 obtained before the increase in petroleum products,
while raw tomatoes sold for N5,5000:00, but now sells for 5,800:00.
Consequently, a basket of pepper which sold for N2,800:00 before the
increase in prices of petroleum products can now be obtained forN3,500:00
hence the increase also affected semovita which now sells for N800:00 as
against N1,000:00 before the increase in prices of petroleum products. The
decrease in price was attributed to the presence of new yam in the market
Prices of vegetable oil was also affected by the increase as now sales
for between N3,800:00 (local oil) and N4,300:00 (imported), while palm oil
sales for N3,800:00.
Traders and consumers spoken to hinged the current increase in prices of
staples on the recent increase on prices of petroleum products, stressing
that transportation of the products from the hinterland to the city has
doubled thus necessitated the rise in prices of consumables.
According to them, "the government has succeeded in impoverishing the
people, their gimmick is that when they (government) succeeds in making the
people poor, any legislation made will only be accepted since the people
will be totally devastated to protest, but only think of how to feed their
families".
In a related development, Federal Office of Statistics (FOS) now Bureau
of Statistics in the latest Composite Consumer Price Index (CCPI), an
instrument for measuring change in prices paid by consumers stood at 152.9
in July 2005 which represents 7.4 percent over 142.3 recorded in June
2005.
The increase in the index FOS said was caused by rise in prices of some
food items like tobacco and household goods. It said also that the urban all
items monthly index rose by 2.8 percent over the period while the rural all
items index increased by 9.6 percent over the same period.
The office explained that the annual consumer price level as at July
2005 went up by 26.1 percent. This was higher than that observed in June
2005. It stated also that the corresponding urban urban and rural indices
rose by 25.5 and 26. 4 percent respectively.
FOS reported that the percent change in the average CCPI for the 12
months periods ending July 2005 over the average of the CPI for the previous
12 was 14.2. This was higher than that recorded by making similar comparison
in June2005.

Original_Search_Title: Obi Marks 4th Ofala
Headline: Nigeria: Obi Marks 4th Ofala
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Onitsha Traditional Council has announced the 4th Ofala festival of Igwe Nnaemeka Alfred Achebe (CFR), the Obi of Onitsha.
In a statement by the council, this year's Ofala of the monarchwould hold from Thursday, October 6 to Friday October 7. The statement added that in the four years since Achebe emerged as the 21st Obi, "efforts have been concentrated on laying the foundation for the venture to re-invent Onitsha."
It added that this year's Ofala "would signal the commencement of the greater challenge of building on this solid foundation."
Onitsha sons and daughters from far and near gather at the palace of the Obi during the Ofala festival, which was the highpoint of the Onitsha ceremonial cycle that also marked the end of a cycle and the beginning of a new one.
The festival marks the annual re-emergence of the Obi of Onitsha from an enforced isolation of communal mourning and his re-appearance after the isolation. This was essentially a period of mourning not only for himself but his subjects that had passed on in the course of the out-going cycle.
The Ofala holds a very significant cultural landmark because it marked the 15th day of the eating of new yam (owuwaji). The first day of the Ofala was primarily for the monarch and his red cap chiefs (ndichie).
During the ceremony, they pledged their loyalty and renewed faith for a new year. The second day (azu ofala) was a time reserved for the monarch's other subjects, during which they reaffirmed their loyalty and commitment to his reign
Ofala, a spectacular public event that was an outing for the Obi in the arena of his palace before a record crowd that had not seen him in the past year. The Obi presents himself and the people in turn re Ã± unite with him and offer him their cooperation as a renewal of their faith in his administration..

Original_Search_Title: Direct Current
Headline: Cameroon: Direct Current
Body_Text:
Who Is Distorting The History Of The Cameroons?
Some individuals are going around trying to use their Anglophone
brethren to keep their posts by campaigning against the fact that the people
want to remember October 1, the day they got their independence from
Britain.Every year, French Cameroon celebrates January 1, the day they got
their independence from France.
During the celebration, epaulettes and medals are awarded to
soldiers. But on October 1, the same soldiers are sent to oppress and
suppress Southern Cameroonians from celebrating their own Independence
Day.
By celebrating January 1 and suppressing October 1, the Biya regime is
distorting history by implying that January 1 is the day both Cameroons
became independent. And this pack of punks doesn't have the courage to tell
Mr. Biya so, but have effrontery to carry their yam heads to come and remind
the people of the insult.
No one can distort history. The issue, if they had brains, is that their
masters are trying to distort the truth and enhance their dishonesty. For
instance, Southern Cameroons voted alone on February 11, 1961, to become
independent by joining La RÃ©publique du Cameroun on a federal platform of
two equal states.
Honestly, in 1972, only Southern Cameroonians would have voted on the
question of the federal grounds on which they elected to join La RÃ©publique
as an equal state. But French Cameroon used its overwhelming population to
overwhelm the Southern Cameroons' paltry number of voters.
Besides this, calling and celebrating February 11 as a Youth Day is
distorting the truth. What did the youths do on this day? These quislings
were present then. Shame on them!
Some Fons and Chiefs have joined the chorus thinking they would impress
Biya. Ahidjo also scrapped the House of Chiefs through which they would have
had a better voice. That is one of the institutions and legacies Southern
Cameroonians bemoan.
Ahidjo changed the constitution which guaranteed autonomy for the
Southern Cameroons to operate in its legacy of self-rule, transparency and
good governance, which it practised under the British trusteeship and
imposed a system of fear and coercion. Some of these people hired to fight
against their people were there then. That is the truth that should be
told.
Well, these hirelings have a right to choose to be slaves to or in La
RÃ©publique. As someone once said, "no body can remain a slave for a very
long time without his connivance." One of the numbskulls said that Germany,
which incidentally ruled the Cameroons, reunited like the Cameroons, but did
not say that none of the two Germanies is trying to suppress and annihilate
the other.
Senegal and the Gambia came together as Senegambia and later discovered
that it did not work. They did not stick together because they had decided
to come together. Senegal could have used its numerical and geographical
advantage to suppress Gambia from going its way. Senegal acted civilised and
today they are independent nations living peacefully as neighbours.
Should the blacks in America have remained indifferent to their being
treated as sub-humans because they were carried there as slaves and without
their consent?
Can these punks riding on Mr. Biya's payroll explain why development in
the Southern Cameroons has been in absolute abandonment even though the
territory contributes tremendously to the GNP?
Royalties are being paid local councils in La RÃ©publique for the
exploitation of their timber. But nothing is paid to the councils in
Southern Cameroons where their vast lands have been expropriated to swell
the budget for more than half a century.
Can they tell us why assistance has continuously been given to the Nsam
victims who were stealing petrol, but the Lake Nyos victims who were
befallen by a natural disaster, have been abandoned to fate?
They claim that Southern Cameroons' movements have gone from country to
country scaring people from investing in the Cameroons. But it is corruption
and over-taxation by the regime that are scaring foreign investors. These
foreign countries have embassies here that inform their countries. So, the
Southern Cameroons movements simply relate the truth.
They have also chided newspapers for telling the world the truth that
the regime is adamant to dialogue; for telling the world that every other
Southern Cameroonian is, deep down, unhappy with the political arrangement
and economic and social injustice by La RÃ©publique's ruling clique, towards
Southern Cameroonians.
They are wishfully thinking that they can dissuade you - the people -
from following the truth, and enable them secure their breakfast, lunch and
dinner, while most of us can't afford a meal, potable water, health and good
roads. However, thank them for the publicity. A genuine cause never
dies.
Are We Together?

Original_Search_Title: Gas Flaring As Arson
Headline: Nigeria: Gas Flaring As Arson
Body_Text:
Lagos — Thinking back to those early primary school days, one gets the
impression that some of us were cheated.
Of course, we were too young to insist on our proper placements in those
promotion examinations. In a situation where a pupil would score an
aggregate of some 1000 marks while the next pupil in that class scored 500
marks or less, we should have insisted that the first pupil be positioned as
first, second and possibly third while the next pupil is put at a distant
fourth position. We were just satisfied with the announcement that we had
taken the first position with the accompanying clap offering and since that
guaranteed our Christmas rice and a new cloth for the New Year, nothing else
mattered.
We are not prepared to allow our own dear native land to be
short-changed by anybody. Nigeria must be properly placed. In the entire
world, we are number one. In the entire continent of Africa, we occupy the
first, second and third positions. We are talking of the world of waste and
destruction of a natural resource which God graciously blessed this land
with. We are talking of natural gas.
While all eyes are on king oil, very little attention is paid to our
abundant gas resources. Experts tell us that we are blessed with such
abundant gas resources that elsewhere, Nigeria is often described as "an
ocean of gas with a speck of oil". In this essay, we shall restrict
ourselves to what is called associated gas.
What we have is that oil and gas are found mixed up in the same
wells. In order to get to the oil which has been described as the lucrative
driver, what the oil companies do is to set the gas on fire so as to leave
the crude oil alone. In essence, they engage in what can be called in local
parlance "give way" by burning off the gas. This is called gas
flaring. While associated gas flaring has been increasingly frowned at in
other parts of the world, it has flourished in Nigeria. The practice started
with oil exploration.
With crude oil production of about 2.5 million barrels per day in
Nigeria, about 2.5 billion cubic feet of associated gas is produced daily
due to the high gas/oil ratio of about 1,000 standard cubic feet per
barrel. The normal practice would be to gather this associated gas and
reinject it into the ground and so preserve it so that when crude oil which
is a wasting asset gets exhausted, we can fall back on the gas reserves. But
the process of gas re-injection is a bit more expensive and takes a little
more time, the oil companies prefer the path of least resistance by just
flaring the gas.
More gas is flared in Nigeria than anywhere else in the world. In
Western Europe, 99 percent of the associated gas is either used or
re-injected into the ground. The amount of gas flared in Nigeria is more
than that of the second and third countries put together and four times
higher than that of the nearest African country, Algeria. While available
estimates are unreliable, experts say that not less than 2.5 billion cubic
feet of associated gas is wasted every day in Nigeria. This they say is
equal to 40 percent of the entire Africa's natural gas consumption in the
whole of 2001. Again the annual financial loss to Nigeria is colossal. This
is not all. We are told that gas burning has contributed more greenhouse
gases than all of Sub-Saharan Africa combined. The flares contain a cocktail
of toxins that immensely impact on the health and livelihood of local
communities, exposing residents of the Niger Delta region to the high risk
of premature deaths, cancer, respiratory illnesses and asthma.
In a report issued by Environmental Rights Action in February 2004, the
group summarized the Nigerian situation most succinctly: "This is a
monstrous and unnecessary state of affairs. Especially in a country where
66% of the people reportedly live below the poverty line, and where the
benefits of nearly half a century of oil production have gone almost
exclusively to the multinationals and to the corrupt local elite".
The Nigerian Government has tried to compel oil-producing companies to
preserve gas by re-injecting same into the ground. This was done by imposing
sanctions for gas flaring. In 1979, the Associated Gas Re-injection Act was
promulgated. The Act which was further amended in 1984 provides for a fine
of two kobo on oil companies for each 1,000 cubic feet of gas flared. The
Act, however, exempts a company from paying any fine on a field if at least
75 per cent of the gas from that field has been effectively used. Naturally,
the oil companies opted for the payment of the fines than embarking on the
gas re-injection projects. This development resulted in oil companies paying
some 2.21 billion dollars in fines for gas flaring in 1986 alone.
Everyone agrees that gas flaring is evil and that it must stop. There is
even a Global Gas Flaring Reduction Initiative, promoted by the World Bank,
with a main focus on Nigeria. Yet, Nigeria remains the world's worst
offender in gas flaring. We have set various target dates to end this
dangerous practice of gas flaring. If only in the abstract, we have
recommended stiff penalties for non-adherence. As an instance, section 4(1)
of the Associated Gas Re-injection Act, 1979 stipulates: "Where any person
is engaged in flaring, the person concerned shall forfeit the concessions
granted to him in the particular field or fields of its in relation to which
the offence was committed". This has been more in the breach than in the
observance.
We really do not lack laws but the enforcement is what we lack. Hence
Climate Justice has aptly concluded, "Broken promises, shifting commitments,
shady deals and ignored legislation mar the history of flare-out
targets". We keep shifting the goal posts. The new flare-out target is
2008. Will this be met? And how did this date come about? In May 2000,
Dr. Imeh Okupido, Nigeria's Minister of State for the Environment at the
time, held a meeting with representatives of the major oil companies
operating in Nigeria. Each representative was asked to say when it would be
convenient for his organization to end flaring. He got the following
responses: Chevron 2008; Total-FinaElf 2008; Shell 2008; Texaco 2005/6; Agip
2005; and ExxonMobil 2004. Earlier, the Federal Government had set up a
committee which prepared a report called "Vision 2010" and in the report,
the committee had agreed on 2008 as the zero-flaring date.
Based on past experience, it would be naive to think that the 2008
target will be met, particularly against the back drop that SPDC is already
developing cold feet on the issue. It is quoted as saying that its flares
will not be out till the end of 2009.
Fire is man's best friend and his worst enemy. On the credit side, I
wonder what we would have done with those beautiful yams without fire. Eat
them raw? On the debit side, the law sees arson as the crime of setting a
fire with intent to cause damage. Where the common law definition restricts
arson to setting fire to dwelling, statutes have since expanded the crime to
include the burning of other structures such as bridges, vehicles and other
property. Gas flaring is in this category and must be treated as such. The
only difference is that while the average arsonist is hidden and difficult
to find, the gas flaring arsonists is in the open. Why should we continue to
treat the oil companies with kid gloves while we watch them ravage our
environment and our people? After all, the stand of the law is clear: where
death results from arson, the arsonist is guilty of murder, even where he
did not intent to kill. Need we reiterate that many of the people from Niger
Delta region are dying from the satanic practice of gas flaring?
Before we get caught in the web of a sweeping generalization, we must
mention that we are not unmindful of the gas conservation efforts of some of
the oil companies, where, for instance, the pioneering efforts of Nigerian
Agip Oil Company Limited cannot be easily swept under the carpet. All we are
saying is that we have now got to the point where Nigeria is faced with two
imperatives: one, nothing short of zero-flaring will do; and two, our
lawmakers simply have to come up with a viable gas policy for this country,
our country.
Hon. Omorotionmwn is public affairs analyst, based in Benin-City

Original_Search_Title: Petrol Sells for N74 in North
Headline: Nigeria: Petrol Sells for N74 in North
Body_Text:
The recent increase in the prices of petroleum products has driven the pump price of petrol above N70 in some states in Northern Nigeria.
Reports from our correspondents indicate that the product is being sold above the N65 margin approved by government, shooting up the cost of living in those areas.
Petrol stations operated by both major and independent marketers in Borno and Yobe states in North East Nigeria now sell a litre of petrol for more than N70.
The Nigeria Labour Congress (NLC), yesterday called on members of the National Assembly to join well meaning Nigerians to protest the recent increase in the pump prices of petroleum products by the federal government.
President of the NLC, Comrade Adams Aliyu Oshiomhole, who made the call shortly after a meeting of the Central Working Committee (CWC) of the congress in Abuja, said that it was not enough for the legislators to condemn the increase on the floor of both chambers of the National Assembly.
Our correspondent in Maiduguri who went round petrol stations reports that Oando station sells at N71 per liter while it is sold at N73.30 by the same company in neighbouring Potiskum.
The independent marketers are also selling at between N72 and N73 per litre. The increase has jerked up transport costs.
Motorists in Sokoto metropolis were on Friday greeted with a new price of N75.30 per litre as against the previous N58.50. However, while all Oando petrol stations sell at N75.30k, other major and independent marketers sell at between N69.99 and N71.99k respectively.
A survey conducted by Daily Trust shortly before the price increase, shows that apart from the independent marketers who sold the commodity for between N55 and N68 per litre, all major marketers began selling at the old price of N58.50k until about noon when most of the petrol stations were closed down for the prices to be adjusted.
Petrol stations along Gusau road however stopped selling fuel to motorists as from 9.30am with the exception of Umaru Gusta petrol station which was selling the commodity at N68 per litre.
Most of the major marketers along Kano road and Ahmadu Bello Way stopped selling fuel at about 10.30am when they realized that their colleagues along Gusau road had closed.
As at yesterday afternoon, all the petrol stations were open for business but only a few vehicles were seen buying fuel.
While all the major marketers were selling fuel at N58.50 per litre before the new price, the situation is now different as the price varies from one major marketer to the other.
All Oando petrol stations in Sokoto now sell at N75.30k while others like Total; National and Mobil sell at N71.99, N71.00 and N69.99 respectively.
Most motorists who spoke to Daily Trust expressed shock over the new pump price while others said it has become part of the federal government's policy since Olusegun Obasanjo became president of Nigeria six years ago.
At the time of filing this report, transport fares at the central motor park remained the same but a member of the National Union of Road Transport Workers (NURTW) who pleaded anoynimity, told Daily Trust that "nobody can stop immediate hike in transport fares".
Already, motorcyclists popularly known as "yan kabu-kabu," have hiked their fares by N10. A distance of 400 meters which used to cost between N15 and N20 now goes for between N25 and N30.
Traders are also not left out in hiking the prices of essential commodities as a tin of Peak milk which used to cost N60 now goes for N65 or N70 depending on the __cpLocation of purchase.
Other commodities such as sugar, detergents and food items like rice, beans, and yams have all gone up.
It was observed in Kano that while petrol sells for N63 at the NNPC mega station, Oando sells for N74 and AP, N65.
Operators at the unorganized (black) market have equally increased their prices. The increase is however not accompanied by queues at the stations.
The prices of petroleum products have continued to differ in Bauchi, with the premium motor spirit [pms] popular known as petrol costing higher at most of the major marketers petrol stations.
Our correspondent who went round the Bauchi metropolis reports that petrol is being sold at N73 at Total and Mobil petrol stations, while a litre of kerosene and diesel is being sold at N58 and N71 respectively.
However, at Conoil and Texaco, a litre of petrol is selling at N65 per litre while a litre of diesel and kerosene is sold between N70 and N75 respectively.
At most of the filling stations owned by the independent marketers, the PMS is being sold at N70, while a litre of diesel and kerosene is selling at N71 and N56 respectively.
Explaining the differential prices of petroleum products, Alhaji Ali Habu, an independent marketer, told our correspondent that they could not maintain the same price regime with some of the major marketers because "we have to pay them some charges and the cost of transporting the commodity is high. So, as people in business, we've to consider our expenditure and regain what we have lost".
However, despite the increase in the pump prices of petroleum products, the commodities have been available at the petrol stations as there were no queues or any rush to get the commodity except for kerosene which is available at three petrol stations.
Meanwhile, cost of transportation particularly intercity transport has remained the same with only about five percent increase on intra state services.
From Zamfara, our correspondent reports that many motorists are now parking their vehicles because of the new price of N75:30 being charged instead of the approved N65.The present situation has driven activities to a low ebb as people continue to express sadness with the increase. A manager at one of the petrol stations told Daily Trust that they were implementing the new prices reluctantly, saying they are not to blame. The increase has however not caused any civil disturbance.
Our reporters who went round Abuja yesterday said fuel was being sold at N65 per litre in most petrol stations except Oando which is selling at N71.
Following the increase in prices of petroleum products last week by the federal government, motorists in Sokoto now buy petrol at N75.30k per litre as against the previous price of N58.50k.
A survey conducted by Daily Trust in Sokoto metropolis shows that all Oando petrol stations now sell petrol at N75.30k as against the previous price of N58.50 bringing the increase to N16.80k.
However, the lowest price motorists buy petrol now in Sokoto is N69.99k at Conoil petrol stations. At the Total petrol stations where petrol previously sold at N58.50k, consumers now buy the product at N71.99k while Mobil petrol stations also sell petrol at N70 while some of the independent marketers now sell at N70 per litre.
Meanwhile, as a result of the increase in fuel price, transport fares at the Sokoto central Motor Park has gone up to about five percent in some cases while others by two percent or three depending on the distance.
Transport fare from Sokoto to Kano which was previously N1,600 has now been increased to N2,000 while from Sokoto to Kaduna also attracts the same fare. Passengers who previously paid N1, 500 to Zaria from Sokoto now pay N1,700 while from Sokoto to Gusau now attracts N900 as against N700.
Again, drivers plying the Sokoto-Birnin-Kebbi road now collect N700 as against N500 while from Sokoto to Zuru is now N900 as against N700. The fare from Sokoto to Abuja which was before this week N2, 300 is now N2,500 while intra city transport services have also gone up.
Furthermore, motorcycle operators popularly referred to as Kabu Kabu in Sokoto have also hiked their prices.
A distance of about 200 meters which previously attracted N15 now goes for between N20 and N25.
At the Sokoto central market, the fuel increase has already taken its toll as prices of foodstuffs have gone up. A bag of Dangote rice which sold at N5, 600 before this week, now goes for N6, 200 while a mudu of the same commodity which was selling at N350 before now sells at N380.
Other commodities like sugar, milk, beans, yam and detergents have all been affected by the recent increase in petroleum products. Some of the people interviewed over the recent hike in prices of petroleum products said they are paying the price of election rigging where leaders who have no interest for the common man bulldozed their way to power.
A driver at the Sokoto central motor park who gave his name as Mufutau Alao, said the only dividend of democracy Nigerians have witnessed is the incessant increase in the prices of petroleum products and warned that Nigerians will pay for petrol at the rate of N100 per litre before May 29, 2007.
Meanwhile, Oshiomhole has asked legislators to join the coalition of labour and civil society groups to protest the recent increase in order to show Nigerians that they (members of the National Assembly), really share the agony of the Nigerian masses and the abject poverty in the country.
The NLC president also called on the federal government to act on the report of the Independent Consolidation Committee set up to explore measures to cushion the effects of the previous increases in the prices of petroleum products.
He said that it will be difficult for the NLC and its allies to accept government's invitation to any dialogue in view of its failure to act on the reports and recommendations of the Senator Ibrahim Mantu-led committee on cushioning measures.
According to him, the failure of government to implement the report suggests that the committee was set up to fool Nigerians, adding that the "government uses dialogue as a tool of deceit."
Comrade Oshiomhole also told journalists that the recent increases in the prices of petroleum products "are unacceptable, rejected and going to be resisted by all Nigerians."
He also disclosed that the NLC will meet with members of the civil society groups in Lagos between Friday, September 2, 2005 and Monday, September 5, 2005, to take a definite decision on the date and the strength of the planned protests against the recent price increases.

Original_Search_Title: African Writing Still Not At Ease
Headline: African Writing Still Not At Ease
Body_Text:
Nairobi —  The generation that grew up without direct colonial rule inherited a
literary body of work whose structures could not cope with a more complex
world, says David Kaiza
BETWEEN THE PUBLICATION of The Palm Wine Drinkard by Amos Tutuola in
1952 and Tropical Fish by Doreen Baingana in 2005, events in Africa passed
like history on the fast forward button: decolonisation, militarisation,
coups and genocides following each other in quick succession.
African literature's fortunes progressed in a similar manner: the
spectacular beginning, the prompt decline, the long stretch when nothing
happened and recently, a literary rebound.
In 1962, a group of writers gathered in Kampala, Uganda, for the first
African Writers Conference. They included Kenya's Ngugi wa Thiong'o, South
Africa's Ezekiel Mphahlele and Wole Soyinka and Christopher Okigbo from
Nigeria.
In the following years, these writers earned their place in history
through prolific writing. Ngugi's The River Between and Okot P'Bitek's Song
of Lawino were among the many books that demonstrated that African
literature had come of age.
Besides production of books, there were literary journals such as Black
Orpheus, The East African Journal and Transition.
Four decades later, the gains of that era seem to have been lost to a
large extent. Most of its books are no longer in print and their authors,
who are now old, have little energy left to come up with new work.
When they met again in Kampala in 1973, some of these writers had
already been bruised by post-colonial collapse. But Idi Amin had not yet
become president of Uganda and the excesses of Jean Bedel Bokassa's
government in the Central African Republic were still in the future.
Looking at 1962 and 1973 in retrospect, it is clear that what was good
went on to deteriorate. The hope projected in Soyinka's The Lion and The
Jewel becomes, in the 1990s, the dejection projected in the author's The
Open Sore of a Continent. It is as if Africa's condition continued to be, as
Achebe had noted, No Longer at Ease.
If the decade starting in 1962 witnessed the flowering of African
literature, the period after 1973 was characterised by literary
barrenness.
From October 19 to 21 this year, Kampala will host another African
writers' conference. Its organisers, the British Council, say that its
purpose is to revitalise literature on the continent.
Titled Beyond Borders, the conference is expected to bring together
writers such as Taban lo Liyong, Lilia Maple, prose writer from Mozambique,
Helon Habila of Nigeria, Anthony Kwamlah Johnson of Ghana, Osman Conte - a
Sierra Leonean novelist and Brian Chikwava of Zimbabwe. Non-Africa writers
will include Val Bloom, a British children's poet, Dirin Adebayo and
Bernadine Evaristo. The final list will include 45 writers from 19
countries.
THE conference will provide an opportunity to reflect on what became of
African literature between 1973 and 2005. Not that the answers to these
questions are not known. The poverty that followed in the wake of the
political leadership of people such as Idi Amin made the pursuit of writing
a luxury. The breakdown of education systems made it impossible to propagate
another Okot p'Bitek. If he was still alive today, p' Bitek would most
likely be in exile or living in one of the camps in northern Uganda,
courtesy of the never ending LRA insurgency.
But these are the easy answers. A more exhaustive critique of literature
needs to be conducted in the context of the political history of the
continent. Colonialism was a blow from which African literature has not
recovered. The need to condemn, to chase away, and to raise the alarm about
the "white peril" provided a theme so strong that once the immediate threat
was gone, the writers went into withdrawal.
Song of Lawino is a powerful book because it strikes a defiant blow
against the colonial cultural menace. No Longer at Ease and Athol Fugard's
Sizwe Banzi is Dead take the battle for African liberation a notch
higher. Soyinka himself had to write Ogun Abibiman (1976), a long poem
declaring war against white colonial rule in Southern Africa.
Another group of writers - the expatriate - was busy on a different
beat. Without a powerful counter-voice, they hijacked the agenda. The works
of Shiva Naipaul and Paul Theroux together with titles such as Mrs
Carruthers is Black, and The Man with the Key Has Gone, which are a
continuation of Conrad's Heart of Darkness, make unsettling reading. They
are however, an exact reflection of the outsider's attitude - which Ngugi
has referred to as the colonial/racist encirclement.
IN ALL THIS, JUST LIKE THE REC- ent Live8 concert in London, African
writers in English find themselves relegated to the periphery. The Caine
Prize, for instance - which honours writers of short stories in Africa -
continues to be regarded as the junior Booker Prize.
When children cannot share the experiences of the founding fathers, it
is not possible to talk of a literary tradition. Whereas the generation that
grew up without direct colonial rule had a different experience, they
inherited a body of work whose structures could not cope with a more complex
world. And the cruel twist here is that the world the founding fathers
fought so hard to protect from the colonial onslaught was itself mounting a
more powerful onslaught on its own traditions and history.
Many writers went into denial. Those who struggled on had lost it. The
few, such as Soyinka, who could absorb the shock and went on to write books
as beautiful as Ak : The Years of Childhood, barely survived. His later
poems are hard to identify with.
The result is a kind of identity crisis. How can I be an African writer
when I grew up in the city? How do I identify with the proverbs in Achebe's
