books when I never pulled a yam from the soil? Will my "African-writer"
credentials remain credible if I write about an upper-class neighbourhood in
Kololo? How do I say my tribe hates your tribe and therefore we cannot
marry?
THE TONE ADOPTED BY THE new generation of writers - those who presumably
will be remembered as the 2005 writers - is defensive. In an article
published in The Guardian, Baingana, a prolific Ugandan writer, said: "I
have been asked, for example, why I chose to write about a middle-class
family. Why not? 'I have a choice,' I want to scream. My stories aren't that
happy, I promise. I have written about black-white tensions and Aids too."
It was presumably easier writing in the past because the issues were so
black and white. The black writer was hitting at the white master. Writers
of the post-colonial era had to find new modes of shedding light on the
struggles in their societies.
But the truth remains unpalatable. In approaching ethnic strife in
Uganda, the safe thing has been to talk of "tribes out there, the big man's
goons or they were tall and dark". You do not call them Baganda, Langi or
Ankole. In Moses Issegawa's The Abyssinian Chronicles or even in Tropical
Fish, characters are not given "ethnic" names. Because the names Okello or
Kaiza will betray ethnic affiliation, you call a character "safe" names such
as Leticia or Tina. No mention at all of how it is possible for one to lose
a job or a university appointment because of coming from the wrong
tribe. These are the things you reserve for embittered private tribal
talk. In short, the things that the audience could have identified with
remain too sensitive to admit in the open. African writers are complicit in
a shameful cover-up.
It is not new. You could not predict the Rwanda genocide by reading any
of the 1960s literature. None of the books written after the killings -
except for the scholarly Western anthropological ones - have explained what
happened.
This cover-up happens in a number of ways. The first is to launch a
tirade against Western stereotypes of Africa. That shuts them up. Another
way is to use a difficult literary style. A bit of magical realism to
stupefy the audience - as with Ben Okri's The Famished Road - so much that
they can't think.
The standard "avoidance" trick, however, is to take a bunch of
post-traditional characters, make them speak like American teenagers and do
modern things under city lights - there are no more palm fronds in African
literature and the smell of the yam has been banished.
IT AFFORDS LITTLE MILEAGE. THE dialogue is artificial and the characters
are robotic. It is painful reading.
Whatever Kampala's 2005 conference will be remembered for will depend
not on what it says but on whether the new African writers manage to fashion
an aesthetic that addresses the continent's problems without shying away
from "tribe" and "Africa".

Original_Search_Title: It's Cruel to Do This to Our Suffering Masses, Say Balarabe, Akpatason, Anekwe, Others
Headline: Nigeria: It's Cruel to Do This to Our Suffering Masses, Say Balarabe, Akpatason, Anekwe, Others
Body_Text:
NIGERIA, today, hangs precariously on the fringe of another increase in the prices of petroleum products and the attendant altercations and implications. It started first like a rumour but as the days went by, there were evident signs that, indeed, there were plans to raise the prices.
As expected, the Nigeria Labour Congress (NLC) issued a stern warning to the Federal Government that it would not accept any further action that tends to pauperize the citizenry. But nobody could say with any finality that the government was going to hike the pump prices.
Yet, from North to South, dealers began to shut down their petrol stations in anticipation of the imminent increase that was being contemplated because of the "prevailing prices in the international community." And true to expectations, at the recent quarterly meeting of the Petroleum Products Pricing and Regulatory Agency (PPPRA), the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC) was given the go-ahead to reflect the prevailing international market prices.
And as if to confirm that a price hike was inescapable, the Minister of Information and National Orientation, Mr. Frank Nweke (Jnr) told a bewildered nation, Wednesday, after the weekly Federal Executive Council meeting that the PPPRA enjoyed the full support of the Federal Government on fixing prices of petroleum products.
According to Nweke, "We (government) are not unaware of the speculations and postulations on this issue (of price increase) in the last couple of weeks. The current administration has a policy of deregulation and liberalization of the downstream petroleum sector. What it means very clearly is that the operations in that sector will be subject to global practices. It means that whatever it takes or costs to do business or procure fuel in the sector in other places, is what it will operate in Nigeria."
And only on Thursday, the Group Managing Director of NNPC, Engr. Funso Kupolokun was giving reasons as to why a hike was imminent, if not unavoidable.
Thus, the issue today is not whether the prices of these products would go up, but by what margin will they be hiked? Will it be over N70 as it has been alleged? Or more? How do Nigerians cope with the impending development? Though the Senate even protested against any raise, will the government listen? And what are the immediate and larger implications for the Nigerians masses?
We took these and other questions to Nigerians both in the oil sector and beyond, and they were as concerned by what the future holds. Read on.
Akpatason: Govt must not inflict these pains on our people
Comrade Peter Akpatason is the president of PENGASSAN. For sure, he cannot be silent on developments around the oil sector.
Marketers have pointed out that they could not continue with the importation of petroleum products with the prevailing pump prices because of the increase in prices of refined fuel in the world market.
It was also said that the NNPC had been subsidizing the prices of petroleum products, given this hike in the price of crude oil in the international market. But we believe that government owes a responsibility to underwrite the cost for the NNPC but the government is not ready to do that.
What we believe is that the Senator Ibrahim Mantu-led committee report and the position of the Oando managing director will suffice for now. The issue here is that the excess crude be kept to cushion the effect of the international crude on local consumption. We want government to release and implement the report to save Nigeria from the consequences of another fuel hike and its reaction by Nigerians who are under severe economic hardship.
Nigerians are suffering a lot. Therefore, it will be most unfair to subject to further suffering because that can"t be another dividend of democracy. Let us save our masses from these avoidable pains.
Our masses will be badly hit, laments Unongo
Chief Paul Unongo, elder statesman and commentator speaks on the proposed plan to hike the pump price of petroleum.
This hike of the pump price of petroleum is not good for the people. It is a pity that government's economic policy is sold to the approach of IMF on economic recovery. The IMF advice on economic policy encouraged government to end oil subsidy and drew up a paper that then showed it would improve our economy in numbers.
But this paper work did not give a damn about how the people would be affected by government policies. What I want to say is that a government policy that does not take care of the welfare of the people is a bad one.
Increasing the pump prices of petroleum products would hit badly on the ordinary people and those who depend on salaries. What an increase in pump price of petroleum would do is that it would affect not only transportation but the prices of foodstuff and other social services.
We appreciate what government is doing to improve our economy but government should have the people's interest at heart. Where a policy does not favour the ordinary people, I would not support it. So, my sympathy is with the NLC and the civil society.
No, we can"t take it now, says Obiajulu, TUC boss
Mrs. Peace Obiajulu is the President, Trade Union Congress.
We cannot take another hike in petroleum products price now. The economy is in a bad shape and the people cannot contain it because it will worsen their suffering. So, that idea should be jettisoned.
We have been on this issue and it keeps coming up. How many times in a year do they hike petroleum products prices in this country? The economy cannot take it. There is hardship everywhere. No jobs for our youths and graduates. The general state of our infrastructure is not something to write home about.
If the government has people's interest at heart, they should not even think of it. Except if they want another showdown because the working class and the ordinary people cannot afford it and so, we will protest. It is not in the interest of the people to hike the price of fuel. Not now!
The cost of living has gone so high. Not many families can afford three square meals a day and now, the government wants another hike. Do they want to kill Nigerians? No, not now. We will not support it.
This is an insult!--Balarabe
Alhaji Balarabe Musa, former governor of Kaduna State and national chairman of Conference of Nigeria Political Parties, CNPP, is angry that there are thoughts at all about raising the prices of petroleum products.
There is absolutely no need for this increase. We of the Conference of Nigeria Political Parties condemn it in its entirety. This government really surprises us. They are determined to make Nigerians suffer and they are confident that there is nothing anybody can do about it.
This is the sixth time since Obasanjo came into power (in 1999) that he is increasing the prices of petroleum products. Is he the only wise man in Nigeria? Is he taking us as fools? Indeed, Obasanjo is insulting the people and institutionalising poverty. Unfortunately, the people have been so weakened by poverty that they are unable to pose a formidable opposition to government's evil policies. We all have to do something to stop this man from insulting us.
I think this time, Nigerians should do much more than ordinary protest. It has to be a revolution. My fear is that the NLC is no longer strong enough for this. A section of the NLC seems to be supporting government at every significant moment. Now, the government no longer fears NLC and does not take it seriously anymore. You could see that each time NLC embarks on a strike, it readily calls it off before any change is effected and that has been helping government a lot.
So, I am calling on all compatriots, civil society groups to join this bandwagon to stop this government from taking the people for a ride. We should oppose this price hike because this country has enough revenue from oil sales and prohibitive domestic prices of petroleum products to help stabilize the prices of petroleum products in the country.
It's good for the economy -- Nzeribe
By Celestine Okafor Assistant Editor
SENATOR Francis Arthur Nzeribe representing Orlu Senatorial Zone in Imo state says the development in the international oil market justifies the fuel price increase approved by the government..
"The fuel price increase as approved by the government on Wednesday was a good step if we must save our oil industry. Fuel price increase as in most cases in this country is something we argue so much about with our heart and not with our head.
You find out that we have a situation where most Nigerians are very sentimental about it without reasoning properly as regards what gains that are accruable from it. Fuel price increase is a common thing all over the world and not only in Nigeria. It happens in Britain and Brent (oil) price rises everyday.
You drive past a motor way and you find five or six filling stations all putting different prices. Fuel prices depend on the market. It goes up and down and it is not Nigeria that controls it. Crude oil prices go up. It is unreasonable to expect those who refine it (crude) not to jack up the prices. The raw material goes up in price, so how can the finished product remain constant? Now, government realised that and set up a committee led by Deputy Senate President, Alhaji Ibrahim Mantu to provide palliatives.
Okay, prices have gone up, we sell our crude at high price. We are making more money and so there is nothing wrong with paying more money for the finished product. Do you follow. We already have a palliative to soften the effect, that's what we should be talking about and not to be saying no increase. Crude oil prices can not go up and the finished goods not go up equally. It's not realistic. It is just a simple economics. But politically and for the welfare of the people, there has to be a way to cushion the effect. And since we already have a palliative committee, let government give them the wherewithal to minimise the sufferings of the people as a result of that fuel increase
Nigerians, let's fight this increase! -- Labour
By Funmi Komolafe and Victor Ahiuma-Young
The Nigeria Labour Congress has advised Nigerians to shun the new fuel prices even as it vows to resist it as Labour and civil society allies plan massive opposition..
NLC general secretary, John Odah said, Labour was tired of government agencies recycling the old arguments insisting that government ought to have put the refineries in shape since the last six years.
He said the NLC was ready to lead the resistance as it did in the past but that it would have to summon meetings of the necessary organs of the Congress.
The Trade Union Congress also opposed the new prices. It described it as "attempts to leaving the fate of the poor, masses in the hands of capitalists, especially in areas where the people cannot cope with the forces of profit maximization".
TUC general secretary, John Kolawole, said, " in our view, the pricing of petroleum products is abnormal, in the sense that it is based on the international price of oil. It is a fact that local refineries cannot meet domestic demand.
There is a general agreement on the solution to the incessant hike in the price of petrol in Nigeria. Everybody including government agree that the solution to the problem is the establishment of more refineries to accommodate local consumption.
The attitude of government to more refineries is lackadaisical. The TUC does not agree with this. as it cannot be viewed as solely an economic or business matter, but because of the crisis prone nature every fuel hike, it is a socioeconomic development, growth. security and stability concern" Chief Kolawole said, 'Any attempt to increase the price will be resisted, our resistance will start with a mass rally in the 36 states of Nigeria as a warning of our rejection of further price hike on a date to be fixed but not later than 10 days from today.
Suffering of Nigerians on an issue which solutions are known and could have been applied, is a situation which the TUC cannot ignore but join forces with other well-meanng Nigerians to reject"
The National Conscience Party urged Labour and the Civil Society to mobilize against the increase .
The NCP in a statement by Segun Sango said, "the latest hike in the prices of the petroleum products by the Obasanjo regime despite the soaring incomes from crude oil sales shows the utter insensitivity of this regime towards the plight of the poor working masses and the economy". The Party argued:
"Like all previous ones, this increment will instantly hike up the cost of goods and services without corresponding increment in incomes of the overwhelming majority of Nigerians. We consequently call on the working masses, organised labour (NLC, TUC, CFTU), Civil Society Organisations and pro-masses political parties like NCP to immediately come together with a view to working out a joint programme of action to fight this increment".
But the Group Managing Director of the NNPC, Mr. Funsho Kupolokun pleaded for understanding, saying even if the nation's refineries are working at 100% capacity, the nation would still have to import.
He said if the new prices are not effected, NNPC may be unable to cope and we may be back to the days of long queues for unavailable fuel.
Dr. Kupolokun highlighted the benefits of deregulation, saying: " Competition with NNPC is increasing, monopoly of NNPC is giving way, jobs are being created".
Right now he said, NNPC has enough stock to last 32 days saying that all the depots in Nigeria have stock except for Ore which had its pipelines vandalized. Even then he said, Ore would soon be fully functional.
The NNPC managing director said, " Oil prices have never been so high in 30 years; since 1973".
He said currently, Nigeria's neighbour, Benin Republic sells petrol at N105 per litre.
Dr. Kupolokun lamented that even if government writes a cheque to offset the subsidy, the tariff is inadequate. He said, " the structure of the industry does not allow for cost recovery".
He spoke of NNPC plans to introduce mega floating stations in order to guarantee the supply of petroleum products at affordable prices in the oil producing states.
On local consumption, he said: " Even if we produce at full capacity, we'll produce 17 million litres a day but our daily consumption is 30 million litres a day".
Govt will still intervene -- Gbadamosi
CHIEF Rasheed Gbadamosi is the Chairman of Petroleum
Products Pricing Regulatory Authority, PPPRA. He blames the media for refusing to see reason and heating up the polity.
I think the media is beginning to overdo things again. They should stop their wild speculations and the lies they publish and put things in their proper perspective. No price yet has been fixed. The media is hyping the whole thing and have refused to see reason. There are no deadlines for anything.
All we are doing is trying to work out things in a way that would be mutually beneficial to all the stakeholders. No conclusions have been drawn yet and I think the media should slam down and get its acts right. They should stop all these speculations!
Govt must halt this increase, says Anekwe
FORMER Speaker of the House of Representatives in the still-born third republic, Chief Agunwa Anekwe, in this interview, reacts to the latest hike in the price of petroleum products, calling on the government to urgently halt the increase in the interest of the nation and suffering citizens.
"I am one of those who believe that Nigeria is one of the countries where we should not be talking about fuel increase. Our people say that you cannot live on the bank of the river and then wash your body with your spittle. You can not do that.
We are a nation flowing in oil. And so, talking about oil increase because the international price is moving up and we translate it into a high fuel increase in Nigeria is certainly not good for the people and the government of this country. It is not satisfactory to me. I think that even today that they (government) has endorsed that fuel increase is not late for the same government to reverse itself. Government should do something about an industry where we have that advantage of having things in abundance. We should rebuild our existing refineries and evern build new ones. We should throw more manpower in oil and gas industry. That is why we are supposed to have more than enough manpower because by what God has given us in terms of oil wealth, we are in a position to service the whole of Africa with refined petroleum products. It is a shame that the sixth oil-producing nations in the world is still selling crude to even countries that don't have oil.
These countries will refine it and send back to us. I think it is ridiculous. I think that if there is anything that we should do to help ourselves in this country, this is time to start it. We shouldn't be talking about how much they are selling oil in Japan because Japan doesn't have oil so we shouldn't be comparing their oil sales to ours. It's just like somebody growing corn in his backyard and he is worried about how much they sell it in London. We have this thing(oil) here and we should add value to it. It is a pity that we have not been able to do that. Any economic theory that has kept us from adding value to our crude oil is not a sound economic theory.
Our refineries should be working and new ones should be built and manpower should be trained such that we should be able to service the whole of Africa. Nigeria is the only country in Africa with the greatest deposit of oil and God has not put it in Nigeria for nothing sake, it is just for us to help our brothers in Africa. And we don't help them by exporting our crude oil. We help them by refining this product and sending. During the Nigeria-Biafra civil war, this same crude oil was refined in the then Biafra even without the technology and the people used it. So why can't we, for goodness sake, build small refineries if the big ones are too big for us.
The small refineries can be all over the places to ensure the availability and friendly prizes of the products. We then train our people in the oil and gas so that they can service this industry and that they can service this industry and that way we can earn more money an we can service Africa and other parts of the world.
So I am totally against the price increase on the basis of equating it with the prices of the international market. I think it is wrong. We are rather like a big producer of yam trying to measure how much they sell yam in Japan before he knows what to do with his own. We have a lot of yams in Nigeria and we should eat a lot of yam. So my advice to government is to immediately stop the oil marketers from going ahead with this increase. It is ungodly.

Original_Search_Title: Soldiers Drafted to Abakaliki-Yala-Ogoja Highway
Headline: Nigeria: Soldiers Drafted to Abakaliki-Yala-Ogoja Highway
Body_Text:
Calabar — A DETACHMENT of battle-ready soldiers has been drafted to the federal trunk 'A' road: Abakaliki-Yala-Ogoja Highway, which has been blocked for over a month because of the fighting between the South Ukelle people and their erstwhile Izzi neighbours, now in Ebonyi State.
Vanguard gathered, yesterday, that the deployment of the soldiers was sequel to the prolonged fighting and failure of the police to separate the combatants from killing one another and others in the area. The movement of the soldiers was observed by the residents along the stretch of the highway.
Lorries and other vehicles that had been absent on the road for weeks were seen plying the road to the cheering of peasants and other folks around the communities.
Traders were seen jubilating while groups formed to discuss the development. The re-opening of the road is coming on the heels of the planned celebration of the new yam festival in Yala Local Government, an event which apparently triggered the joy in view of the sales which will accompany the opened road.
Travellers along the road from neighbouring Ebonyi State and those from Cross River State waved at the cheering pockets of crowd.
Children of the affected communities who had turned the long deserted road to a football pitch, jumped and waved, perhaps not aware that their former playground had become busy from that moment. Old ladies and other road-side hawkers were seen dusting their mobile kitchen in preparation for renewed business. Fighting broke out between the Ukelle and Izzi people in April this year, over disagreement between the two sides on payment of rent on land.
The Izzi who migrated to Ukelle around 1923 had been paying rent to their Ukelle landlord since then. But the migrants from Ebonyi State allegedly reneged on the agreement early this year, prompting the frowning of the South Ukelle people. The issue dragged on and could not be resolved by both sides until their tempers boiled over. War broke out and the Izzi people who had lived in the area for decades, were chased out of the entire community into Ebonyi State where they came from.

Original_Search_Title: Last Five Months Traumatic for Me, Says Osuji
Headline: Nigeria: Last Five Months Traumatic for Me, Says Osuji
Body_Text:
Owerri — Former Minister of Education, Prof. Fabian Osuji has described the last five months since he was relieved of his appointment by President Olusegun Obasanjo over his alleged involvement in the N55 million bribery scam as the most traumatic period of his life.
Osuji who spoke as a Special Guest of Honour during the new yam festival of the Nguru Uboma community in Aboh Mbaise Council of lmo State weekend noted that a lot had happened to him within this period which had consistently kept him in an emotional state.
He, however, said he had taken solace in the fact that his current travails would constitute a stepping stone towards his eventual elevation which would not be long in coming.
"This occasion is a very emotional one indeed for me, l'm touched because l have not encountered such a gathering in the last five months. l'm touched also because these are the people who did not know me as a Minister or as a Deputy Vice Chancellor, but they knew me as a growing child, and now they have also decided to honour me," he said.
He said he had used the period of his travails to learn a lot, adding that though many might think he was down, he was not completely out.
"We can be minister or another thing, but we can't be God, whatever we do remain in the hands and mind of God," he said. He however, declined to make his donation to the community in public as according to him "we no longer make donations in public because of impending danger".
Earlier, the President General of the Nguru Development Union, Chief Chris Okoro said in his welcome address that the Mbaise people would not tolerate any attempt to destroy the reputation of Prof. Osuji who he described as their son in whom they are well pleased.
He said the Former Minister was a victim of legislative extortionism, adding that he's currently on retreat but would emerge victorious in no distant time.
Meanwhile, a human rights group based in the South-east, the human rights Justice and Peace Foundation has expressed dismay over the continued stay of those indicted in the bribe for budget scandal in the National Assembly.
ln a press statement issued in Owerri yesterday by the President of the Group, Mr Chidi Nwosu, the Foundation described as selective justice a situation whereby some people were relieved of their appointment while others still retained their positions.
Nwosu, therefore, called on the Senate President Ken Nnamani and the Speaker of the House of Represe-ntatives, Alhaji Aminu Bello Masari to immediately suspend those indicted in the scandal from the National Assembly, especially former Senate President Adolf Wabara and Senator Chris Adighije.
He said that should the Senate President fail to listen do this the group would have no other option than to follow other democratic procedures to commence the recall of all the officers of the National Assembly.

Original_Search_Title: CBN, Lower Denominations And the Commonman
Headline: Nigeria: CBN, Lower Denominations And the Commonman
Body_Text:
Lagos — THE planned introduction of the N1,000.00 denomination this month by the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN), the scarcity of the lower denominations of the currency, the directive to banks to accept only driving licence or International Passport from their customers for identification in any form of transaction, and other unfavourable measurers by the apex bank demand that we turn our searchlight on the objects of this bank, popularly called the banker's banker.
But first, should the N1,000.00 note/polymer be? Although the CBN boss is reported to have said that "nothing will halt the move" I sincerely believe that man's action must be reviewed from time to time since man is imperfect, and that reasons must at all times prevail. In any case, is it not correct that man's action is right or wrong depending on when it is made? Introducing the N1,000.00 denomination now will worsen inflation and poverty in the land. The media has been awash with news of the pains of high cost of living. Prices of even common garri, not to talk of staple rice and yam has been going beyond the reach of ordinary Nigerians who constitute over 70 per cent of our population. The sick cannot afford the cost of treatment, and the lonely like widows are further marginalised. This is evidenced by the United Nations Human Development Report which recently placed Nigeria 152nd out of the 175 countries, judged by life expectancy, educational enrolment, adult literacy and per capital income. Against these facts and background, is it economically prudent and humane to introduce the N1,000.00 denomination now, which would (from experience) lead to further rise in prices of goods and thereby pauperise more the already weary Nigerians? Does the CBN desire more sorrows, deaths and burials? What would the common man do? Where does the common man run to? More night vigils?
Again, introducing the N1,000.00 denomination now would be a direct opposition to the recently reconstituted National Payments System Committee. The committee which was inaugurated by Obong Ufot Ekaette, Secretary to the Government of the Federation has the burden of ensuring that Nigerians embrace the use of cheques in their daily transactions. Trust our traders (and they must be factored always), a good number of them compelled to patronise banks for their transactions would deplete, as it would be much easier to carry cash around and they would avoid the many bank charges some of them dubious and made due to their perceived ignorance. The planned introduction (if it holds) would therefore further discourage saving and development of banking culture. Is it not clear that introducing the N1,000.00 denomination now would worsen the cankerworm of bribery and corruption which has been decimating development and the society? How correct was The Guardian in its editorial of Friday, July 8, 2005 titled "Towards a modern national payments system" when it inter alia stated that "corruption is rife but corrupt transactions are executed using cheques. Corruption swims in rash cash." So the battle of the president, the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC), the Independent Corruption Practices Commission (ICPC) and the Police against corruption and crime will be made more difficult. Fortuitously, our brother country Ghana would be saved the oft-employment of the sobriquet "Ghana must go bag" to describe the packaging and delivery of huge bribes in Nigeria, as in the case of the dismissed former Education Minister, Prof. Fabian Osuji. The ghost of the Buhari/Idiagbon evacuation of Ghanaian illegal immigrants would then be laid to rest. Because, it would no longer be necessary to use such huge bags as the biggest brown envelope (sorry again to the members of the fourth estate) would suffice, or even needlessly the bribes could well be tucked in suits or our usually overflowing agbadas and delivered without any suspicion. Unfortunately thieves and robbers will resort more to direct attacks and stripping of persons to achieve their purpose, as there will be reduced carriage of money in bags. And sorry if you don't have much on you! With N1,000.00 note/polymer added to the family of N500.00, N200.00 and N100.00 of the higher denominations and now in preponderance it would further deplete and worsen the scarcity of N50.00, N20.00, N10.00 and N5.00 of the lower currency in circulation, the group of currency most vital for the daily survival of particularly the commonman who constitute over 70 per cent of our population.
The above now leads us to the main concern of this piece -- the dearth of the lower denominations of the currency and the agony of the commonman. Go round the marks, shops, bus stops, etc., whether at Onitsha, Yenagoa, Kano, Lagos or Ibadan and you will appreciate the sufferings, agonies, inconveniences, delays, and troubles the ordinary man has been enduring as a result of the scarcity of the lower denominations of the currency. Avoidable quarrels, fights, losses, delays, frustrations, breach of contract, police cases and even hospitalisations are common daily consequences of this lack. In the past, only new notes were hoarded and sold mainly to party freaks (owambes) and those who want to either impress women or the rural folks, but not anymore. Both old and new notes (if the new ones can still be seen) are now commonly sold particularly at motor parks and occasions, not only to the above group but also to traders/sellers. The problem is very serious. It is a basic principle of marketing and trading that the customer is king. It also conforms to common sense and our custom and tradition that the seller has a duty to give the buyer change. Today, this hallowed tradition is fast dying as many sellers due to the scarcity of the lower denominations expect the customer to always come with change. Listen! You may even be hearing that conductor shouting "Oshodi Oke Oshodi Oke!! wole with your N20.00 change o!!!" The damage and danger to the economy and general insecurity in the country can be appreciated. It can even spark off a tribal row. Dr. Shamsudden Usman (a Deputy Governor of Central Bank) bemoan the high cost of printing currency during a recent Nigerian Television Authority (NTA) documentary on CBN further heightens our fears. Dr. Shamsudden Usman emphasised repeatedly that the cost of printing N1,000.00 note is about the same as N20.00. Should we be hopeful that the present scarcity of lower denominations of the currency would soon be a thing of the past? Your guess as they say, is as good as mine.
Against this background and with ongoing rationalisation, lay off of workers and implementation of the many IMF/World Bank induced policies by the Federal Government, it is important we make a cursory examination of the objectives and duties of the Central Bank of Nigeria. I agree that the CBN must not indulge in waste, but equally the banker's banker was not established for profit making. And it is not waste to make available the lower denominations of the currency. Section 4 of the Central Bank of Nigerian Act states inter alia that "the principal objects of the bank shall be to issue legal tender currency in Nigeria...to promote monetary stability and a sound financial structure in Nigeria" whilst Section 19(1)a of the same Act compels that "notes and coins issued by the bank shall be in such denominations of the naira or fractions therefore as shall be approved by the minister" Section 19(1)b dealing on forms and designs would approve the planned introduction of polymer notes. From the above, it is a dereliction of duty for the CBN to permit scarcity of the lower denominations of the currency and therefore the bank owes Nigerians particularly the poor (the group most adversely affected by the dearth of the lower denominations) an apology and immediate reversal. I am further emboldened in these submissions by sections 16(1)b & (2)b of the 1999 constitution which directs governments and all its organs (including the CBN) to "control the national economy in such manner as to secure the maximum welfare, freedom and happiness of every citizen on the basis of social justice and equality of status and opportunity" and to ensure "that the material resources of the nation are harnessed and distributed as best as possible to serve the common good" Thus from the above, it is important for government and all its organs (including the CBN) to ensure that its policies and actions meet the above prescriptions at all times.
The recent directive by the apex bank to commercial banks to as from hence accept only an international passport or driving licence from their customers for identification in any form of transaction places the bank and the government in opposition to the ordinary Nigerians and shows clearly that the CBN is insensitive to the plight of ordinary Nigerians. How many Nigerians can afford to buy cars and are able to travel abroad? How many even of those who own cars, use them? The apex bank's directive is also a clear disincentive to saving and banking, judged by the level of our development. This directive will also promote corruption in our licensing/passport offices and road accidents as those who must bank, may not exercise the needed patience/tests to acquire the identifications and the temptation to drive once one acquires the driving license may worsen our accident records. It is therefore compelling that this directive be reversed. What the CBN should even do is to direct banks to accept identifications from prominent community leaders/elders, organised churches/mosques in their areas of operation. This will not only promote the much desired banking and saving culture, but also encourage behavioural changes and respect for our elders, culture, laws and traditions.
If one was still in doubt that the CBN is running anti-people policies, the recent writing off of N82 billion indebtedness of eight commercial banks to assist them meet its re-capitalisation requirement despite the hunger, poverty and sickness ravaging the land should clear that doubt. Talk of robbing Peter to pay Paul (sorry, robbing Obi to pay Okey). You mismanage your bank and I now use public money to pay off your debts in the name of enduring stability in the economy! It is rather rewarding and encourging malpractices, misdemeanours and mismanagement in the banking sector. If the CBN had been adequately supervising and regulating the banks, should the debts have risen, at least to that extent? To add insult (or it is salt) to injury as it is said, the apex bank has called for public support of the initiative. We certainly do not give our support but demand a reversal. It is therefore suggested that the Central Bank of Nigeria should concentrate in regulating, supervising and sanctioning of commercial banks timeously including community banks to eliminate cases of failed banks and frauds, thereby preventing depositors and investors from loosing their hard earned money and engendering confidence in our banks and real development in the country. This to me, as section 4 of the CBN Act demands, will be promoting "monetary stability and a sound financial structure in Nigeria." It is well.

Original_Search_Title: 5 Govs Honour Odili in Mbaise
Headline: Nigeria: 5 Govs Honour Odili in Mbaise
Body_Text:
Owerri — State governors led by Governor Peter Odili of Rivers State were among eminent Nigerians who stormed Mbaise, Imo State, yesterday for the 2005 Iriji (New Yam festival) of the community. Governor Odili was at the occasion conferred with the prestigious title of Obinwanne Mbaise (brother's keeper) by traditional rulers of the community.
In attendance were Governors Achike Udenwa (Imo) Dieprieye Alamieyeseigha (Bayelsa), Abdullahi Adamu (Nasarawa), Sam Egwu (Ebonyi) and Ayo Fayose (Ekiti).
Governor Odili, flanked by his wife Justice Mary was accompanied to Ahiazu local government headquarters-venue of the event by former Governor of old Rivers State, Alfred Diette-Spiff, Deputy Speaker of the House of Representatives, Hon. Austine Opara, Deputy Governor of Rivers State, Sir Gabriel Toby, Speaker of Rivers State House of Assembly, Hon. Chibuike Amaechi, as well as a host of lawmakers and commissioners from the state.
The New Yam festival, chaired by former secretary to Imo State Government, Chief Innocent Nwoga, was also graced by Minister of Power and Steel, Senator Liyel Imoke, former Sports Minister, Dr. Tonye Graham-Douglas, Hons. Mao Ohuabunwa and Emeka Ihedioha of the House of Representatives.
Also in attendance were frontline politician, Chief Emmanuel Iwauanyanwu, Chairman of Daar Communications Limited, Dr. Raymond Dokpesi, Managing Director of Oceanic Bank, Chief (Mrs) Cecilia Ibru, Managing Director/Editor-in-Chief of Champion Newspapers Limited (CNL), Mr. Emma Agu, Deputy General Manager, Marketing CNL Mr. Martins Uba Nwamadi, National Chairman of All Progressives Grand Alliance (NGA) Chief Victor Umeh, Federal Commissioner at the National Population Commission (NPC), Chief Joe Ndu, member of Imo State Assembly, Hon. Nnanna Igbokwe (Aliazu constituency) among others.
Climax of the event come when Governor, Odili, in company of his wife, was decorated by all royal fathers of Mbaise as Obinwanne Mbaise.
The occasion, which was devoid of speeches, was however full of colour as cultural displays, bands and other entertainment outfits dotted the Chioma Ajunwa stadium where the event took place.
Another highlights of the ceremony was the cutting of roasted yam by all the governors.
In his welcome address, chairman, Mbaise Council of Traditional Rulers, Eze Edmund Osuagwu lauded Governor Odili for his numerous achievements in Rivers State, noting that he has continued to make Mbaise proud through his dynamism philanthropy, intelligence, simplicity, godliness and political sagacity.
"You have continued to prove that we did not make mistake when we offered you our cherished daughter, your own better half, Her Excellency Justice Mary Odili, in marriage.
"As a worthy son in-law, we want to assure you of the good wishes and prayerful support of the Mbaise people in your political calculations for the future," the traditional ruler of Ngurunweke community stated.
Eze Osuagwu also commended Governor Udenwa for his numerous achievements, particularly his road rehabilitation programme, and the new secretariat complex Port Harcourt road.
He, however, reminded Governor Udenwa of the Oru-Attah-Ukwu Orji road, which he said, has remained neglected by past administrations but which provides the shortest link between Mbaise and their parts of the state.
He also mentioned the Owerri-Mbaise-Umuahia road, which has remained deplorable, especially at the Mbaise axis.

Original_Search_Title: Traditional Rulers to Honour Gov Odili
Headline: Nigeria: Traditional Rulers to Honour Gov Odili
Body_Text:
Owerri — GOVERNOR Peter Odili of Rivers State is expeced to be a special guest to the people of Mbaise, Imo State, to mark this year's Iriji Mbaise (New Yam) festival slated for Monday, August 15, at Ahiazu local government area of the state.
Governor Odili will also be honoured on the occasion with a special title by one of the traditional rulers in the area.
Top government functionaries at the Federal and State levels, including the Presidency and National Assembly, will grace the occasion in which Gov. Achike Udenwa is the chief host.
As part of preparations to accord the august visitors heroic honour, a meeting of Ndieze, the Eze Jis and planning committee members in Mbaise as well as married daughters of the community, was held at the Ahiazu local government council headquarters in Afor Oru on Wednesday to harmonize arrangements.
The meeting, presided over by chairman of Mbaise Council of Traditinal Rulers, Eze Edmund Osuagwu of Nguru Nweke community, was attended by prominent royal fathers in the area. They are Eze Joe Oleka of Oru Ahiara, Eze Isidore Obasi, Eze Stanley Iwu and Eze S. O. Uwaegbu, secretary of the council of Ndi Eze.
Also in attendance were other illustrious sons of Mbaise, including Chief Charles Amanze and Chief Nobert Obasi.
Speaking to Daily Champion, Eze Osuagwu said that Mbaise community resolved to honour Governor Odili in recognition of the good governance he is exhibiting in Rivers State in Port Harcourt and the country in general.
The royal father described the Rivers State Governor as a great achiever and a cherished in-law of Mbaise people.
According to him, this year's Iriji ceremony will be unique as all hands are on deck to ensure that the occasion is a huge success.
"It is a way of showing the Mbaiseness in our culture. We are going to showcase the treasured culture of our people on this occasion," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Factory Earns Over 59 Mln. Birr
Headline: Ethiopia: Factory Earns Over 59 Mln. Birr
Body_Text:
Dire Dawa — The Dire Dawa Textile Factory has reportedly earned over 59 million birr in the previous fiscal year from the sale of its products locally and internationally.
Factory Deputy General Manager Mekonnen Tewolde told WIC over the weekend that the factory earned 53 million 991 thousand birr from one million 910 thousand kilograms of yam and thread and three million 996 thousand metres of textile supplied to the domestic market.
He said the factory has also earned five million 216 thousand birr in foreign currency from 257 kilograms of knitting thread sold at European markets, adding that the factory is making its utmost to get more hard currency by increasing its exports in collaboration with other organizations.
The factory is currently undertaking five- year Industrial Development Strategy Research so as to bolster the growing demand for its products abroad, he said.  According to Mekonnen, employees of the factory are doing their level best to increase the quality and quantity of their produces..
The factory was established in 1938 and has 2,614 permanent and 334 casual employees, it was learnt.

Original_Search_Title: 11 People Hospitalised After Yam Flour Meal in Kwara
Headline: Nigeria: 11 People Hospitalised After Yam Flour Meal in Kwara
Body_Text:
Ilorin — ELEVEN people comprising a family, relatives and friends have been
hospitalised following the consumption of what was largely suspected to be
poisonous yam flour which has claimed about 12 lives in Kwara State since
the beginning of this year.
Vanguard reliably gathered that save for the timely administration of
first aid medical treatment the 11 people who include an Islamic cleric, his
wife and three children would have passed on.
Eye witness account told Vanguard that trouble started last Thursday
when the clericc's wife, Mrs. Taye Abubakar, a nurse, purchased some
quantity of yam flour from a seller at Oja Tuntun [New Market], a short
distance from where the family lives in Agbo-Oba area of Ilorin.
The woman according to the source used part of the flour to prepare
amala food which the family, the relatives who consisted of cousins of the
cleric and a sister-in-law to the woman and her child, and a friend of the
cleric ate with stew and ewedu soup around 4:00pm the same day.
According to Mrs. Abubakar who narrated the ugly incident to Vanguard
when visited at Kidiz Medical Centre where she works and where some of the
victims were hospitalised, since Thursday around 6:00pm, she said that among
11 of them who took the meal, three children, including her twins children,
Taye and Kehinde Abubakar immediately started complaining of dizziness and
moments later, they started vomiting.
She claimed to have treated them with an injection and thereafter asked
her husband, the cleric, Mallam Sheu Abubakar, to pray for them. She said
that while the children were with her husband one of the twins, Kehinde
suddenly started convulsing adding that it was then she suspected that they
had taken poisonous substance.
The woman said she suspected that the yam flour they ate contained
poisonous substance because the hospital where she works recently treated
six patients of same family who displayed similar symptoms when they ate
contaminated yam flour.
She also said that she immediately administered first aid treatment on
Kehinde to calm his brain from abnormal discharge while the 9-year-old was
thereafter rushed to Kidiz Medical Centre at Olorunshogo area of
Ilorin.
Mrs. Abubakar said no sooner than her child was admitted in the hospital
when her brother-in-law, Kunle Abubakar, a 27-year-old student of Kwara
State Polytechnic, Ilorin started convulsing while the rest of them who ate
the food including herself started feeling dizzy.
She said they were all advised by the doctor to take similar first aid
treatment earlier given Kehinde to reduce the effect of the poisonous
meal.
The nurse said her brother in-law was also admitted in the hospital
alongside her child and that they were all diagnosed of suizure disorder
resulting from food poison.
She disclosed that officials of the state Ministry of Health had taken
away samples of the yam flour and water with which the food was prepared,
stew and ewedu soup for laboratory test.

Original_Search_Title: Ghana Should Build Industrial Parks
Headline: Ghana Should Build Industrial Parks
Body_Text:
A few years ago, when Mr. Alan Kyeremateng was the Ambassador to USA, he made a proposal to establish Ghanaian Banks in the US, which was a brilliant idea.
It could have been replicated worldwide to collect most of the $1.2 billion that is sent to Ghana annually. In the 90s, there were a lot of Ghanaian-owned money transfer centers.
I believe they could have formed a solid base for the proposed banking system. Unfortunately, they had to give way to multinational corporations to take over.
The government collects 0.5 % of import duties to benefit the Export Development Industrial Fund, (EDIF) which started with a $40 million loan from a foreign bank. While we have been trying to woo investors, I know what we need most are industrial parks spread across the country. Once we have built the parks, it would be easier for investors to bring in their machinery to set up. Without manufacturing jobs, we would be spinning the wheel and going through a vicious cycle of unemployment and poverty. If the industries are clustered around Tema, this will increase the drift to urban areas, leaving most parts of the country undeveloped. We should look at what is available in our localities that could be industrialized. If tomatoes are grown in Akomadan and get rotten every year, we can put up an integrated industry to produce plastic jars and a processing factory so that we don't have to import canned tomatoes. Has anyone tasted mango juice from Egypt, Coconut juice from Thailand and seen the oranges and other fruits that are heaped on the ground in our markets?
We can learn from the Chinese, who have become a new superpower. They have built the factories and also the dormitories around them.
They build some of the machines and import the rest from Europe. To their advantage, they have an ideology, which has disciplined them; they also have cheap labor and the population backed by a single currency. I was very happy when I read about the introduction of a single currency for five Ecowas states, which was to begin this year. But no, it has been postponed till 2009. Every minute that we spend exchanging currency to do business with our neighbors is time wasted. The Chinese have not performed any magic. We can do the same or even better. We have the raw materials to feed our industries; the same or cheaper labor cost. We only have to increase the minimum wage for the workers to earn a decent living. On my last visit, I saw orange and pineapple juice imported from Spain and South Africa.
Taking the shipping cost of these countries into consideration, any manufacturer can ship such products at a cheaper cost to the Ecowas states, with a population of 280 million, the same as the USA.
MANUFACTURING
For a long time, Ghanaians have underrated Chinese products and regarded them as inferior, preferring American-made products. What they did not know is that, most American products are made in China. The trade deficit between US and China is about $160 billion this year. And by 2010, 500 million Chinese are projected to achieve middle-income status. For a long time, the Reverend Jesse Jackson has been complaining about shipping the manufacturing jobs overseas, mostly Asia and nobody listened. What has happened is the loss of good paying manufacturing jobs in America. I read the report by NAM, (National Association of Manufacturers) from their website www.nam.org talking about this situation. The report entitled "Securing America's Future-The case for a strong manufacturing base", makes interesting reading. I believe it is a bit too late. The greedy multinational companies are not going to bring the jobs back from where they are paying $1.00 an hour or $1.00 a day in Ghana to pay even $6.00 an hour plus health benefits in the US.
The sad part is that, Ghanaians are building mansions instead of industrial buildings to take advantage of the situation.
There is nothing wrong with building houses in our unplanned cities. We plan to settle after retirement or spend a few weeks when we go on vacation. It would have been better if we had a manufacturing business that could sustain us when we go back home. Since we don't have that, we join the buy and sell group of companies.
AGOA, African Growth and Opportunity Act, was passed by President Clinton to enable us export our goods to the US duty-free. But we are focusing on yam, bush meat (akrantie) and smoked fish, which serve only the African market. We have about 6,000 products that we can export under AGOA. There are various Ghanaian Associations from townships in Ghana based in the USA and we should think of forming cooperatives or partnerships to start building industries in our towns. Instead of buying cars and paying huge duties, we can start with used machinery, from closed down factories, that are selling cheaply at auctions all over the world or we would be helping in perpetuating a welfare society in Ghana with our remittances.
The Chinese have a proverb that says, "A journey of a thousand miles begins with the first step." Perhaps, various associations should bring this up for discussions and form exploratory committees. The government has proposed setting up an industry in all the 113 districts. It is also promoting privatization and since they haven't achieved their target, Ghanaians abroad should focus on setting up industrial parks, possibly in partnership with the government. That is what we need most-Industrial Parks.
Good Business Plans are necessary and this is where the professionals should come in. There is software available to help in writing business plans and assistance is available from universities. Community-based projects in Ghana can obtain financing from African Development Foundation, based in the US.
They have a website detailing their requirements. Visit them at www.adf.gov. The future does not begin in any distant time but from now. Those who want to do something in future should start now. There is no time to waste.
The population in Ghana is going to be 44 million in twenty years. You can imagine how Accra would look like- a sprawling city with more people and no jobs. We can stem the tide by developing our towns and villages and also stop the drift to urban areas. If we are planning any projects in Ghana, let us seriously consider building industrial parks.

Original_Search_Title: Bluntly Speaking Visa: Leave Embassies Alone
Headline: Ghana: Bluntly Speaking Visa: Leave Embassies Alone
Body_Text:
In recent times, foreign embassies have come under severe criticisms for what is perceived as a deliberate policy not only to frustrate visa applicants but also to fraudulently take their money to run those embassies.
Before I proceed any further, permit me to lay the foundation for this article.
If you know Mr. Cameron Duodu, then pass over this portion of the article. If you do not know him, then bear with me for a while.
Mr. Cameron Duodu is currently a freelance journalist living in the United Kingdom.
He was at one time the editor of the Daily Graphic.
He disagreed 'editorially' with the stand of the Busia Government over the question of whether to adopt force or dialogue in solving the problem of apartheid in South Africa.
The Government of the then Prime Minister Dr. K.A. Busia opted for dialogue. Mr. Cameron Duodu disagreed and, for his pains, was relieved of his post by a Prime Minister then touted as the very personification of Democracy.
Mr. Duodu was later to settle in Britain. Apart from writing a column in The Mirror, he also wrote a novel THE GAB BOYS.
Mr. Duodu appears fairly regularly on the African Quiz Programme of the British Broadcasting Corporation (BBC).
Sometime ago, Mr. Duodu used the column of the Ghanaian Times to tell the story of how his application for a visa from the Spanish Embassy in London failed.
As I remember the account, he had been invited to take part in some kind of literary or journalistic confab in Spain.
If he thought that it would be smooth sailing (or is it smooth flying?) he was in for the proverbial rude shock.
The Spanish Embassy simply 'dribbled' him with 'go-come', 'go-come' tactics until, frustrated, he gave up.
What is the object of this story about Mr. Duodu, you may justifiably ask? Well, the point is that if anyone who knows Mr. Duodu thinks that he will enter another country and probably go underground as an illegal immigrant, that person should easily make it to the GUINNESS BOOK OF RECORDS as the biggest joker of all time.
But did the Spanish Embassy official in London know this? Highly unlikely. The official probably saw another black person from a poverty-stricken country pretending to be somebody and hoping to become an economic refugee who might try to earn a living through crime.
The Mirror also published a story of a boy threatened with blindness but being refused a visa so that he could have medical treatment in the United States of America. When The Mirror rekindled interest in the boy, he was still in this country.
In the latest case, it has been former World Boxing Association (WBA) champion, Ike Quartey, making an informal complaint to the Minister of Education and Sports, Mr. Yaw Osafo-Maafo about the refusal of the United States Embassy to grant visas to four of his entourage who should have accompanied him to the United States to engage in his latest come-back fight.
It was not as if Ike Quartey was some obscure person who had never traveled to the United States before. Had he not fought Oscar De La Hoya, one-time World Boxing Council (WBC) welterweight champion? That cut no ice with the Embassy officials and Ike had to leave without them.
The question is, why would respectable people like Mr. Duodu and Ike Quartey run into difficulties trying to procure a visa for themselves or their entourage?
In the first place, it should be noted that no person has an inalienable right to travel to another country without meeting certain requirements unless there is a reciprocal agreement that waives those requirements.
The potential traveler may wish to travel to that foreign country for medical treatment, or to engage in some kind of activity, such as a conference, or sports or even for further studies.
Only the government of that foreign country can decide whether the would-be traveler would be permitted to enter that country.
In the second place, quite a number of people applying for visas or on whose behalf applications are submitted are so desperate to leave the shores or airspace of this country that they are prepared to tell lies or present false documents at the embassies, especially those of the United States and Britain.
It is not just ordinary Ghanaians who have destroyed whatever confidence the foreign embassies had in us as a people. Sometimes, it has been society's "heavyweights" in industry, commerce, academia, the highest reaches of government and traditional rulership who have displayed a total lack of moral rectitude by reportedly engaging in visa racketeering and other fraud.
For a fee or some other consideration, total strangers have been presented as blood relations on whose behalf the applications for visas are being made.
William Shakespeare states in his play, MACBETH, "there is not art to find the mind's construction in the face." In other words there is no way you can truly judge a person's character by looking at his face.
When 'big shots' resort to falsehood and fraud, can you blame the embassies for being extra-careful?
Do not rule out the real possibility of hard drugs being smuggled into that foreign country by unscrupulous travelers.
Going by the recent exhibition by the Narcotic Control Board (NACOB), one can see the utmost ingenuity with which smugglers of hard drugs seek to outwit immigration officials, CEPS and the police.
Drugs swallowed, drugs in yams and garden eggs, drugs in fish, in the soles of shoes, inside the anus, etc.
They are coming up with new concealment places all the time, even as they are caught.
Even those who do not smuggle drugs may be people without any qualification or training that should enable them to be gainfully employed. Such persons are likely to resort to doing very menial jobs or even resort to crime for a living. And they too keep pressures on the social services of that country.
Imagine so-called poverty-stricken Ghanaians prepared to pay visa and other fees of between $1,000 (One thousand dollars) out of a patriotic desire to go to Greece to cheer our athletes during the last Olympic Games ! How many would have returned to this country after the Games or even before the Games ended?
Some of our athletes have failed to return after taking part in other Games, for example, the Commonwealth Games. Do we give cause to the embassies and High Commissions to trust us? To be sure, some of us are not happy at the hassle which would-be travelers, even the honest ones, have to go through before securing visas to travel abroad.
Attending a conference, going for further studies, checking into a hospital, even being present at the graduation ceremony of one's child can mean a great deal to the applicant.
Still, while I extend my sympathies to Mr. Cameron Duodu (I know that he does not need one, actually), Ike Quartey, the family of the near blind boy and all those honest and genuine applicants, I cannot but observe that, in most cases, we have brought upon ourselves the predicament of having applications for visas rejected.
The facts simply say that there are too many crooks wanting to travel abroad by a resort to falsehood and fraud.
I ask again, can you blame the embassies for being extra-careful?

Original_Search_Title: Debt Forgiveness: Matters Arising
Headline: Nigeria: Debt Forgiveness: Matters Arising
Body_Text:
Lagos — One good question deserves another: Ideally, when a man borrows money
and he is unable to pay, would people not be right to describe him as
irresponsible? If the default becomes increasingly embarrassing and the
lender then decides to write off the debt at least in order to keep his
ledger folio clean, would that be a cause for celebration? Should the debtor
not just keep quiet or hide his face in shame?
Admittedly, the answer to these questions would reside largely in the
affirmative but the situation in which Nigeria and other developing
countries found themselves was very far from ideal. It was more of a
conquest. We saw in the debts, an active resurrection of the Shakespearean
Shylock. We saw in them, measures that were deliberately calculated to keep
those countries under perpetual subjection and enslavement. Whichever way
one looked at it, one saw a debt scheme that did violence to logic: the more
you pay, the more you owe. It was a death trap.
In essence, forgiveness as applied to the debts carried full weight and
meaning. It was more than the moral equivalence of a state pardon after one
had been sentenced to death, particularly against the backdrop that the debt
overkill was coming down on those who did not take part in the borrowing,
talk less of partaking in its misappropriation and worst of all, there was
absolutely nothing on ground to show for the money borrowed. It was like
being forced to pay, ten times over, the debt you did not owe. It was that
bad.
Even among the iconoclasts, there is no running away from the inevitable
conclusion that the Obasanjo administration has achieved a major feat by
securing this debt relief. Perhaps unknown to many, we were being choked to
death. For sometime, I see us continuing to bask in the euphoria of this
achievement. Those delegations coming from all parts of the country to Aso
Rock Villa are in order. But we think we shouldn't stop just there. We
should send high-powered delegations to Number 10 Downing Street, London and
the White House in Washington, D.C., to thank Prime Minister Tony Blair and
President Bush, for the great assistance they rendered to us during the
struggle. I want to volunteer myself to be included in the
delegation(s). When we get there, we shall present them with some kola nuts
and yam tubers. We should not offer them money or oil, lest they would
conclude that we are not sufficiently serious with our war on
corruption. Come to think of it, one major instrument our creditors saw in
our hands during the struggle was this fight against corruption coupled with
our poise for accountability and transparency.
Anything that affects all must be approached by all. The battle for debt
forgiveness has brought to the fore, the obvious fact that there is enormous
strength in unity. Although ours is a government based on the doctrine of
Separation of Powers, the making of foreign policy is, in fact, a shared
power. It is shared principally by the President and the Senate but in the
particular case of our battle for debt forgiveness, the role played by the
House of Representatives was not any less important. We also realize now,
more than ever before, that for a shared power to be exercised effectively,
those who share it must be pulling in the same direction.
For most Nigerians, it is not always reassuring to see the President and
the National Assembly at odds over important policy issues when the nation's
vital interests are at stake. The two branches need not remain seriously at
odds. For sure, they must differ from time to time, but they must seek a
common ground in ways by which the misunderstanding and their legitimate
differences can lead to constructive ends. They must work together for the
common good.
Foreign loan is a festering cancer. We hear that there are arrears of
some six billion dollars that must be paid immediately to give meaning to
the 60 percent write off that has just been secured. We hear that some
people are of the views that it would be idiotic to empty our purse on the
sale of excess crude or for the oil wind fall in payment for those arrears,
particularly when the people had been told all along that this money was
being kept in trust for them for development. This argument also makes
sense. On balance, though, it is more reasonable to get the loan off our way
before it builds up again. I hope the request for supplementary
appropriation must now be on its way to the National Assembly. Again, the
concept of separation of powers is not really absolute. It is not intended
to produce a situation of complete independence. Rather it is meant to
produce a situation of interdependence.
As we stress the need to have greater consensus, an important
distinction must be made between making and conducting foreign policy. In
this domain, nobody seriously questions the preeminence of the executive
branch of government. As a practical matter the conduct of foreign policy
can be done only by the president and his agents. Thomas Jefferson puts it
rather succinctly when he was the American Secretary of State. "The
transaction of business with foreign nations is executive altogether".
Actually, loans never end. Even on the eve of the current partial debt
forgiveness, we were still borrowing to finance some other projects. In her
explanation, the Minister of Finance, Dr Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala made it clear
that this was a special loan that was almost interest free. Sound
reasoning. Actually, there is no harm in borrowing. Most viable projects in
the advanced countries are based on borrowed money but in our particular
case, the problem has been that our leaders would obtain the loans, but
would not use them for the intended purpose. With nothing to show for the
loans, several poor countries unable to meet the mounting burden of interest
and debt service are forced to reschedule their foreign debts.
All too soon, the amount of debt service exceeds the gross amount that
they received in loans and this money, like any other type of loans travels
in only one direction, up - it climbs and climbs and jumps and jumps. The
lenders now begin to encourage you to service at least part of the
interest. Meanwhile, you are already in a bottomless pit and there is no
getting out. You are trapped. Before you know it the creditors have formed
themselves into a club - the Paris club, the London club, etc., for the
purpose of considering the case of their debtors and what to do with
them. Here we are carved out for philanthropy. Instead of developing the
Abuja club we would rather behave in the way of Father Christmas. We give
handouts to the poorer countries and we even help to finance their annual
budgets.
There is another evil I see around these days. That evil is mainly
prevalent in the southern part of this country. Most cities are littered
with abandoned or haphazardly executed projects that were started with money
procured as foreign aid. The foreign aid wears two faces: they come either
as direct budgetary support to poor nations or they could be tied to
specific projects. Of course, those who give away money can hardly be
expected to be happy to observe that their gifts are not appreciated or
deserved. Once the project has begun, the donor is also committed to it and
he is involved in the innumerable difficulties of implementation but as the
project begins to progress, aid officials soon get caught between the desire
to have the projects succeed and the necessity and of avoiding continuous
direct involvement. Aid officials are therefore likely to intervene just
enough to make themselves unpopular but not enough to get the job
done. Beyond this point, the benefiting countries are quick at regarding the
donor's intervention as unacceptable limits to their freedom of action.
Whereas the donor's original intention was to protect the beneficiary
against himself, the leaders must devise ways whereby the funds will go
directly into their private pockets. Roads that were not too bad at first
now become completely impassable; and although the water used to run once a
week, the taps are now completely dry. The cure is now worse than the
disease.
In sum, the donors cannot avoid error but they might be able to reduce
the cost of making mistakes. Sound policy must be based on helping the best
while not hindering the worst. If foreign aid must be meaningful, we see no
viable alternative to donors participating actively in project monitoring
and implementation. It can't be done otherwise.
Hon. Omorotionmwan is a public affairs anayst, based in Benin-City

Original_Search_Title: Face of Africa: Three Nigerians Hit International Limelight
Headline: Nigeria: Face of Africa: Three Nigerians Hit International Limelight
Body_Text:
Lagos — Since September last year when the search for the 2005 Nokia Face of Africa commenced, interest on the contest continued until the grand finale last Sunday. Three Nigerian damsels made it to finale stage. Clementina Olomu reports on the trio.
As the soon as the search for the Face of Africa was announced, most parents encouraged their female wards to enter for the contest. In doing so they must have recalled the fame that came the way of Oluchi Onweagba, the first winner from Nigeria. Oluchi and others who have won the contest are now world class fashion models.
Even runners up have not been left out. Ethiopia's Liya Kebede was a Face of Africa entrant in 1998 and she is currently the Face of Estee Lauder, a fashion labell. Lenah Zinyama (Zimbabwe) and Pumla (South Africa) both finalist continue their modelling careers in Europe and New York respectively. Phine Camara from the Ivory Coast was a 1999 Face of Africa finalist and she is now based in Paris where she continues to model.
In the just concluded contest the three Nigerian girls, Doyin, Opeyemi and Adeola are now pontential super models walking on the path of fame and fortune
Previous Finalists
It was not only the winners of the contest who have had the chance to work as professional models. Finalists from each season have found themselves in demand and many continue to work as models around the world.
Amongst them are Lerato Moloi (South Africa) who recently modelled at South Africa Fashion Week while Patricia Namayirira (Uganda) and Eva Ndachi (Kenya) are contracted to modelling agencies in Cape Town. Nigerian Face of Africa finalists Funmi Alaiyemola and Ojy Egwuenu are both now based as models in New York though before her move Funmi was named Style Magazine's South Africa Model of the Year. Norchene Cherif was a Face of Africa 2000 finalist from Tunisia and she is now modelling in Milan.
From the 2001 finalists, Hanna Tulonen of Swaziland is now a fulltime model working in Europe.
Also Zimbabwe's Audrey Chiumbu-Maseko a finalist in Ford SuperModel of the World and Ghana's Ama Abrafi now working as models in the United States. South Africans Avumile Qongo and Omphile Direro are also working as models in Cape Town and Johannesburg respectively.
Criteria for Selection
A glance of past models gives the impression that the organizers have a particular physique of girls in mind. To enter for the contest open to only females, applicants must be, between the ages of 17 and 24 and be taller than 1.72m with a maximum hip measurement of 36 inches.
She must posses a passport from an African country. Those who meet these criteria, were directed to MultiChoice offices to collect entry forms. These forms which were also available in True Love magazines, could also be downloaded at www.mnet.co.za/faceofafrica. Even when the deadline for all entries was in December 31, 2004, those who missed it were given another opportunity, as MultiChoice directed those interested to go to LA Porch Corporate Suites in Victoria Island and the Protea Hotel Oakwood , Lekki both in Lagos to pick up forms. By the way members of the scouting team of the competition which visited Nigeria in March did a pre-event casting.
Scouting Visit to Nigeria
The 8-person team, which came from South Africa, held open casting session in these places on Thursday 24th and Friday 25th of March. The purpose of the casting was to assess entrants for the contest. Those who entered for the competition were invited to attend the sessions where they were given the benefit of immediate evaluation.
Ace fashion designer David Kolawole Vaughan popularly known as Dakova who was present as a guest judge provided insight and advice to the selection team made up of Hilda Gabriel who was the head scout, Anton Robert, fashion photographer and the editor of True Love Musi Mahlaba, who is based in South Africa.
This selection team also visited 11 other countries with open casting sessions in 11 cities. The marathon selection tour took 49 days.
Ten African girls out of the original sixteen, with Nigeria topping the list with three contestant made it to the semi-finals These ten eventually made it to the finals which took place at the Super bowl in Sun City, Johannesburg, South Africa..
What You Need to Know
Opeyemi Awoyemi
Resident in Port Harcourt and having just turned 18-years-old Opeyemi Awoyemi was one of the youngest semi-finalists for the Nokia Face of Africa 2005. At 1.83m in height, Opeyemi is also one of the tallest semi-finalists in the group. A student, who speaks English and Yoruba, Opeyemi is a fan of gospel music and also enjoys the music of Nigeria's Style-Plus and 2Face. Her favourite songs include African Queen and Everytime. In her spare time, Opeyemi says she must have a radio on, listening to cool music. She also says, "I play a little piano and I sing a lot!"
Opeyemi is also an Eddie Murphy fan proving her love for comedies. She plays badminton and sometimes, hits the hoops playing basketball. Her favourite place in Nigeria is Lagos and her favourite Nigerian food is yam, eggs and rice. In addition, she said that, "the best thing about people in Nigeria is that they know their rights in everything they do."
Doyin Haastrup
Standing at 1.84m tall, Doyin Haastrup is the tallest semi-finalist for the Nokia Face of Africa 2005. Born in Lagos, the 20-year-old keeps in shape with regular gym classes and by eating good food, which she said, makes her healthy.
Doyin's fashion accessories include perfumes, shoes and earrings. She would love to model in Paris and in New York City and would like to visit Zambia, Ghana, Liberia and South Africa she said. She lists Lagos and Abuja as the places she enjoys most in Nigeria and says she loves the warm West Africa climate of her home.
On the entertainment front, she enjoys the music of Sean Paul (I'm Still in Love), R. Kelly (Happy People) and 2Face (My African Queen).
Adeola Ariyo
Adeola Ariyo 19 is from Lagos State. She said she was ecstatic when it was announced that she was a semi-finalist for the Nokia Face of Africa 2005. "The first person I told was my father, " says the 1.73m tall student.
Proud of her heritage Adeola, who speaks both English and Yoruba, says Nigerians know how to have a good time and that the country has a lot of different places to visit. Given the chance, Adeola would like to see Zimbabwe though she has been to Ghana, Togo and the United Kingdom.
Adeola loves Gucci and Ferragamo and says her best fashion item is shoes! She would love to model in Paris and says she entered the Nokia Face of Africa 2005 "for the experience and to feel what it's like to be recognized. To have fun and at the same time learn a lot more about the fashion world."
Her favourite colour is sky blue and she loves African and Chinese food. She feels more at home with music of Faith Evans, Aaliyah and Usher. Meanwhile Adeola also describes herself as athletic saying she plays the Karate.
The Final Competition
It was a keen competition because all the girls met all the requirements, more so they have been groomed as models to meet international standard. Their skills gave the judges a tough time deciding who the winner would be.
The long journey towards capturing a face, which epitomizes the essence of African beauty, yielded fruit last Sunday and Botswana beauty, Kaone Kario, was crowned the Nokia Face of Africa 2005 in what analysts considered the most hotly contested finals in the history of the competition. The lanky Botswanian elegantly captured the imagination of the African continent and indeed the rest of the world when she was named as the NOKIA FACE OF AFRICA 2005 during a vibrant fashion extravaganza at the Super bowl.

Original_Search_Title: A Governor And His Ways
Headline: Nigeria: A Governor And His Ways
Body_Text:
It was an observation at close quarters, spending the whole day with Governor Gbenga Daniel of Ogun State at work. It was one experience which exposed the intrigues, the sentiments, bias as well as the high wire politicking which goes into governance. It also revealed another side of a political party, the Peoples Democratic Party, PDP, intent on re-creating itself as a party with genuine interest in and serious commitment to the welfare of the people. This report presents a day-at-work in the life of Governor Gbenga Daniel and key players in the governance of Ogun State.
