It was sometime in 2003, just before the general elections of that year. Justus Olugbenga Daniel, then a governorship aspirant, was on the campaign trail. It was a local government rally. A septuagenarian politician (Pa Adewole Ogunbambo), whom an observer described as a weather-beaten individual near his eighties, made a solemn confession to Daniel, who was then still eying the governorship seat in Ogun State. Pa Ogunbambo told his audience in Iwoye village, venue of the rally in Imeko-Afon, LGA of Ogun State, that in his 50 years of political activism, never in his life had he met a gubernatorial aspirant.
They gave their votes he said, but the politicians for whom they voted never came from the city to campaign nor even had the courtesy of coming back to say thank you. Therefore, meeting with Daniel was, to him, a life-changing experience. In fact, he all but committed his entire village to Daniel. It is instructive that politicians in Nigeria, always being the first to acknowledge that majority of the votes are located in the hinterlands, do not make it a point of duty to engage in a proper tour of same with a view to getting out the votes. Pa Ogunbambo prayed the governorship aspirant not to forget his village when his governorship kingdom comes..
Therefore, on July 5, 2005, over two years after that moving testimony by Pa Ogunbambo, Justus Olugbenga Daniel the aspirant, returned to Iwoye village, this time as governor of Ogun State.
It was a leg in the multi-legged engagement of the Gbenga Daniel administration in Ogun State, which seeks to take governance to the people at the grassroots. Although this was not his first visit to the Imeko-Afon local government since assuming office, his presence in Iwoye village on this fateful Tuesday was yet another sign that governance without the active participation of the governed is one bloody waste of time, a fool's errand. But Daniel, not given to foolhardiness, appreciates the value of grassroots engagement.
The day started from the residence of the governor at Oke Igbehin. It was not the first time he would be embarking on such a tour. It had become common-place to dedicate Tuesdays - at least in the last couple of weeks - to touring local governments in Ogun State. So, there was already a routine to it.
However, the routine nature of the tour does not mean it is always a boring affair because it still packs along with it a sense of weighted apprehension as activities for the event of the day lay solely in the domain of the host local government chairman. He determines which projects the governor would commission just as he chooses which site is best for the main village square talk.
From the governor's residence, we drove straight to Imeko, the headquarters of Imeko-Afon LGA
The great pastor, S B J Oshoffa, founder and leader of the Celestial Church of Christ, hailed from, and was buried in, Imeko.
Just after the big bill board of Oshoffa, at a road bend leading into Imeko, the LG chairman, Prince Saka Adetunji Isiaka and council legislators welcomed the governor. With them was another obviously influential party leader, a woman of timber and calibre, Mrs Ogunjobi. After the pleasantries, the chairman and Mrs Ogunjobi joined Governor Daniel in the bus which was conveying the governor and some of his commissioners and advisers.
Right from that moment, you could perceive the sense of unease in the council. Between the Chairman and Ogunjobi, no love was lost. There had been an acrimonious engagement between both and it was carried into the bus.
Ogunjobi was asked if the chairman had started behaving. Her response, while not condemnatory, was suggestive that the chairman is far from there. Governor Daniel had requested that the NTA transmitting station in Imeko would have to be completed soon. He wanted an arrangement whereby some LG councils in the west senatorial district of the state would contribute some money, while the state government augments, with a view to completing the project.
At that point, a matter broke. Between Governor Daniel and his deputy, Alhaja Salimot Badru, what could pass for a healthy argumentation developed. And it was about the money for the completion of the NTA station. It was quite perceivable that while the deputy governor kept insisting that the governor had the final say, and that whatever he decided would be final, she, nonetheless, attempted to make her point that her part of Ogun West Senatorial district, specifically Ipokia, was suffering marginalisation. Governor Daniel, on his part, told his deputy that quite a number of amenities and facilities have been made available to the district.
Beating this argument back and fort, it became obvious that something would give, or, more appropriately, somebody would bow. The deputy did. But governor Daniel kept repeating that by the time Ipokia would take its turn to organise the village square meeting, he, Daniel, would speak - Ee gbo dende oro ti a ba lo si Ipokia lo se Village Square Meeting, meaning, in a manner of speaking, when it is Ipokia's turn to organise a Village Square Meeting, there would be some tongue lashing.
But Badru was not done. She retorted that the governor should not misunderstand her:
"Excellency, e ma si mi gbo (don't misunderstand me) I'm sorry but I wish you would be there when my people accuse and attack me of not doing anything for them. The pressure is so much that my people do not allow me rest. Won lo'wo no council yi now (this council has enough money)", she said.
"You have spoken-o", Daniel kept telling his deputy, with a ting of deliberate mischief.
"Excellency, I'm sorry but please you have to understand what I'm facing from my people", Badru kept retorting.
This went on for another three to five minutes. Interestingly, while this lasted, there was pin-drop silence in the bus and Daniel, sensing this, immediately employed humour when he bluntly accused his deputy of aggravated anger. Because the matter which brought about this argument was the contribution of money for the NTA project in Imeko, Daniel then turned to the council chairman: "Mr. Chairman, I think you have offended the deputy governor-o. But deputy this your anger, I do not understand-o. Why are you angry and combative?" To this there was an uproarious laughter in the bus. And that was it.
On the way to the council headquarters, the governor stopped to commission the 1km road leading to the council office. At the secretariat, the first function of the day took place. It was a family meeting between the governor, Isiaka and his councilors. The commissioners on the trip were also in attendance. At that meeting, the councillors and some party leaders berated the chairman.
From petty insincerity on how council funds are managed, to outright dictatorial tendencies, it was more a session of lamentation. The council chairman took time to defend himself. When Governor Daniel delivered his judgement, he admonished the chairman to turn a new leaf in his own interest. He made reference to a monitoring committee which he instituted with a view to feeding him back on developments in the council. Although he made it clear that the council leadership as represented by the chairman, was a quasi embarrassment to the Peoples Democratic Party, PDP, he however stressed that there was a chance for improvement, a chance he warned the chairman not to fritter. Looking sober, the chairman promised that he would turn a new leaf.
The next phase in the tour was a meeting with the council workers. Here, the workers, led by Mr. Ayo Kolawole, co-ordinating director in the council, appreciated the efforts of the Gbenga Daniel administration but requested that the government could assist in one or two more areas. Stealing the show at that segment of the tour were the members of the Imeko-Afon Student Union who made a passionate presentation to the governor requesting for assistance from the government to support the building of a center in the council area with emphasis on computer education. To demonstrate their seriousness, an encased model of their ideal structure was presented to Daniel along with an award. The governor, in turn, expressed amazement at the presentation of the students and promised to assist.
Next was to be the show proper.
It was the journey to the village square. On this journey, there was a stop over to commission a block of classrooms. State of work at the NTA premises was also inspected by the governor.
Now, as in everything human, there is one big political gambit involved in this aspect of the entire tour. It is the point where the governor will have to face the people. They would ask question (more like favours of him) and he would have to provide answers.
This was the last leg of the tour and the most important for, according to Governor Daniel, "The people who are supposed to come are not supposed to be members of the Peoples Democratic Party, PDP. People who do not belong to our party are free to come and ask questions about governance. If we are having a party rally, it is a different story and then you will see real mobilisation.
This one is not really political and I try very hard because if you are not careful, people would read meanings into it. What we went to sort out is the legislative arm of the local government council. It is just a co-incidence that the PDP has a hundred per cent control of the house. If it had been a partisan house, they would all have had their opportunity to speak. The LG workers too had their opportunity to speak at the larger forum." (Read full interview)
As the bus moved closer to Iwoye village, crowds gathered to welcome him. He would occasionally tell his security men to slide open the window for him to acknowledge the cheers from the crowd.
Once in Iwoye village, the hundreds possibly thousands of party faithful had gathered. In fact, the people of Iwoye had prepared for this day. Their dressing, enthusiasm, zeal to see the governor, and the attempt at impressing him gave away the fact that this was one day that the people had prepared for. The governor arrived the village at about 5:27pm that day. Before he could make his way to the VIP tent, he had to acknowledge the greetings from the people. He waved at them just as they sang his praise.
Now, if 'No Shaking' originated from Enugu State for Governor Chimaroke Nnamani, the people of Ogun State had their own idea of creativity. They had a slogan for Gbenga Daniel - 'No Tackle'. The import of this is that whereas Nnamani could be tackled without shaking, opponents of Daniel dare not even tackle him. Therefore, it should be understandable when shouts of 'No Tackle' rented the air.
Typically, Daniel lapped it all up as the crowd poured out their hearts. The signal was clear.
At the village square meeting proper, over a dozen representatives of all shades of interests and opinions spoke. Even a WAZOBIA angle was introduced as the Hausa and Igbo communities had cause to make their demands known to the governor. In fact, the representative of the Hausa community promised to deliver a cow to Daniel.
The requests from the people ranged from granting licence and taking over a school, building roads, completing the NTA station.
But the request which rang hollow all through was the one which had to do with the market at Obada which had become a matter of contention between Imeko-Afon LG and Yewa North. But Daniel's response was simply that the boundary commission would have to look into the matter. A school was taken over by government as requested by the people and a road would be constructed linking the council headquarters to Iwoye village.
While he was attempting to conclude his statement, the various dance groups took time off to interrupt him. He allowed them. By the time the entire event was over, it was time to have the first meal of the day. Time then was about 7:15 pm.
But the high wire politics on ground was not over. The chairman and the party leader, Mrs Ogunjobi engaged in one game of local political brinkmanship. Whereas the general impression was that food would be served at Mrs Ogunjobi's residence, the chairman cunningly insisted that the governor had to visit the staff quarters. And not one to be accused of not bidding farewell to the council workers decided to oblige. On getting to the staff quarters, it was an ambush. Food had been prepared. And for people (some who had ate nothing since morning), the sight of food was irresistible. With tired legs and weary eyes, people moved into what looked like the guest house and food was served. Even Governor Daniel proved that he was human. He had said openly that he would not eat there.
But when the whiff of the aroma of efo elegusi (vegetable with melon) caught the sense of smell of His Excellency, with a mold of pounded yam to match, he gladly received the plate of food from the lawyer appointee who served him. He betrayed his hunger too - at least he was just being human.
But that was not to be all because Mrs Ogunjobi, having cooked all through the previous night through that Tuesday morning had to be appeased. She had excused herself and the governor had promised to branch off at her residence before leaving Imeko that night. After about 35 minutes at the staff quarters, it was time to go to Mrs Ogunjobi's.
Her residence was actually decked for the occasion with a band stand on hand and a very huge crowd to boot. The governor and his entourage, still tired - now from the weight of their meal - sauntered into the residence of the Ogunjobis where Daniel was treated to some voluptuous dance steps by some women. After some 30 minutes, Governor Daniel moved out into his bus with Mrs Ogunjobi, accompanying him with bowls of food and beef and wine.
The entourage left Imeko-Afon at 8:49pm and entered Abeokuta, the Ogun State capital by 9:38pm.
It was one long day but packed with all sorts of intrigues. But it was worth the trouble. The governor had become used to it, just as his commissioners too had become used to it.
The lesson in it remains the fact that governance should be all inclusive and that was one thing which the governor demonstrated. However, whether the tempo would be sustained is another matter.
Governor Daniel believes he will. Only time will tell.

Original_Search_Title: Shock of High Maternal Death Rates
Headline: Kenya: Shock of High Maternal Death Rates
Body_Text:
Nairobi — One out of every 16 women from sub-Saharan Africa die during child birth for lack of professional health care, a visiting Chinese minister said yesterday.
Baige Zhao, the Chinese deputy minister for National Population and Family Planning said the deaths were almost nil in the developed countries.
"Maternal mortality in the developed countries stands at a ratio of 1 to 2,800," said Zhao.
She was speaking at a Nairobi hotel during the launch of the Youth Action Movement.
Zhao said women in developed countries easily accessed proper health care during pregnancy and delivery.
She said 200 million women in the South do not have access to modern contraceptive services, and make up 72 per cent of unwanted pregnancies globally.
The International Planned Parenthood Federation (IPPF) estimates that 1.5 million women die each year due to unwanted pregnancies.
The federation, which deals with reproductive health, organised the conference.
Zhao regretted that women did not have the authority to choose the number and time of giving birth, a factor that contributed to the increasing rate of abortion.
Also present at the function were Tewodros Melesse, the IPPF Africa regional regional director, Beatrice Mutali, the programmes director in Kenya. Quoting pre-natal experts, Melesse said 99 per cent of the abortions in Africa are high risk.
"Official statistics indicate that 50 per cent to 60 per cent admissions in gyneacology in hospitals are as a result of complications related to high risk abortion," said Melesse.
Mutali asked African governments to provide funding for reproductive health services to reduce maternal and infant deaths. She said IPPF would find ways of reducing the high rates of abortion and safe motherhood throughout the region.
She said the organisation would enable the youth access current information on reproduction and family planning.
Mutali urged for advocacy among policy makers and development partners to support family planning.
"Governments should provide funding for reproductive health services as it has a major influence on the growth of the economy," Mutali said.
Regional Adolescent and Youth officer Holo Hachonda said few peer educators have the necessary skills on youth guidance.
Hachonda said the Government should monitor information peer educators passed on to the youth.
"It has come to our attention that the quality of information given to the youth has been compromised," Hachonda said. He claimed peer educators underwent a one-week training and thereafter enrolled in institutions where they offer diluted information.
"Guidance and counselling is a sensitive subject and requires intensive training," Hachondo said.
The chairman of YAM Nairobi Province, Mr Erick Ochieng, said the organisation would empower the youth on abortion, Aids and also help the career development.

Original_Search_Title: 3 Nigerians Lead 7 Others to
Headline: Nigeria: 3 Nigerians Lead 7 Others to
Body_Text:
Lagos — Ten African girls, led by three Nigerians, out of the original 16 who
made it to the semi-finals of the Nokia Face of Africa 2005 modeling
competition have made it to the finals of the most prestigious modeling
event in Africa holding on July 10 at the Superbowl in Sun City, South
Africa.
The three Nigeria finalists, Doyin Haastrup, Opeyemi Awoyemi and Adeola
Ariyo have been away in Zambia honing their skills ahead of the
finals. Specifically, the girls have been in South Africa from where they
left for the boot-camp in Zambia for their first real training in
international modeling.
However, the journey towards becoming the next face of Africa is not
over yet... Really, it has only just begun as the Nigerian girls are
expected to get stiff opposition from seven other African prospects for the
prize of instant stardom. Below is their dossierÃ¢?Ãoe
ADEOLA ARIYO: 19, from Lagos, says she was ecstatic when it was
announced that she was a semi-finalist for the Nokia Face of Africa
2005. Ã¢??The first person I told was my father,Ã¢?? says the 1.73m tall
student. Proud of her heritage Adeola, who speaks both English and Yoruba,
says Nigerians know how to have a good time and that the country has a lot
of different places to visit. Given the chance Adeola would like to see
Zimbabwe though she has already traveled quite a lot making stops in Ghana,
Togo and the United Kingdom. A true design fundi, Adeola loves Gucci and
Ferragamo and says her best fashion item is shoes! She would love to model
in Paris and says she entered the Nokia Face of Africa 2005 Ã¢??for the
experience and to feel what itÃ¢??s like to be recognized. To have fun and at
the same time learn a lot more about the fashion world.Ã¢?? Her favourite
things: Sky blue, African and Chinese food, the soap Eastenders and the
music of Faith Evans, Aaliyah and Usher. Meanwhile, Adeola also describes
herself as athletic and say she plays the clarinet.
DOYIN HAASTRUP: 20; at 1.84m tall, she is the tallest
semi-finalist. Born in Lagos, she keeps in shape with regular gym classes
and by eating healthy. A fan of the designers Modela and Mon-Ami, DoyinÃ¢??s
fashion accessories include perfumers, shoes and earrings. She would love to
model in Paris and in New York City and would like to visit Zambia, Ghana,
Liberia and South Africa. She lists Lagos and Abuja as the places she enjoys
the most in Nigeria and says she loves the warm West Africa climate of her
home. On the entertainment front, Doyin enjoys the reality show Ultimate
Search and the music of Sean Paul (IÃ¢??m Still in Love), R. Kelly (Happy
People) and 2Face (My African Queen).
OPEYEMI AWOYEMI: 18, hails from Port Harcourt and is one of the youngest
semi-finalists for the Nokia Face of Africa 2005. At 1.83m in height,
Opeyemi is also one of the tallest semi-finalists in the group. A student,
who speaks English and Yoruba, Opeyemi is a fan of gospel blues singers and
also enjoys the music of NigeriaÃ¢??s Styl-Plus and 2Face. Her favourite
songs include African Queen and Everytime. In her spare time, Opeyemi says
she must have a radio on playing cool music! She also says, Ã¢??I play a
little piano and I sing a lot!Ã¢?? Opeyemi is also an Eddie Murphy fan
proving her love for comedies. She plays badminton and sometimes, hits the
hoops playing basketball. Her favourite place in Nigeria is Lagos State and
her favourite Nigerian food is yam, eggs and rice. In addition, she says
that, Ã¢??the best thing about people in my country is that they know their
rights in everything they do.Ã¢?? When she was selected to be a semi-finalist
for the Nokia Face of Africa 2005, Opeyemi called her sister first to share
the good news!
Ã¢?Â¢Find out more about all 10 finalists by tuning in to M-Net on June
19, June 26, July 3 and July 10 to follow the search for the NOKIA FACE OF
AFRICA 2005.

Original_Search_Title: Nerve-Jangling Defilement Cases Hit Accra
Headline: Ghana: Nerve-Jangling Defilement Cases Hit Accra
Body_Text:
Accra — The Women and Juvenile Unit (WAJU) of the Ghana Police Service has revealed that defilement was the second highest number of criminal cases in the Accra metropolis in the first quarter of the year with 94 cases. These cases mostly involved grown ups having sexual intercourse with minors or children below 18 years.
Abdul Aziz, 32, and resident at Teshie-Nungua estate was on June 6, 2005 sentenced to 10 years imprisonment with hard labour for defilement and indecent assault on a 7 year old girl by a Cocoa Affairs court B1, in Accra, presided by His Highness Kwadwo Owusu.
According to the Accra Station Office of the Women and Juvenile Unit (WAJU), of the Ghana Police Service, Chief Inspector Rita Narh, on June 28, 2005, the victim joined her mother to sell her goods.
She said at about 10 am the victim informed her mother that she was attending nature's call. While she was returning, she met Abdul Aziz who took her to a nearby bathhouse at Novotel Lorry Park and bathe her.
Afterwards, he inserted his finger into her vagina and later had sex with her in the bathroom. After satisfying himself, he bathe her again and warned her not to inform anybody.
At 3.30pm, the accused later sent the victim to one Alhaji Bariwu at the Novotel Lorry Park to take care of the victim for a while. Alhaji then interviewed the victim and she revealed her ordeal leading to the arrest of Abdul who was detained at the Nima Police station.
The victim was medically examined and treated. The case was transferred to WAJU and after investigations the accused was charged before court. Abdul Aziz pleaded not guilty to both offences of indecent assault and defilement.
He was however tried and found guilty on both counts and sentenced to three years on count 1 and seven years on count 2.
Another 22-year-old resident of Adenta Sakora, Love Ametordzie, was on Friday June 17, 2005, sentenced to 7 years imprisonment in hard labor by a Cocoa Affairs court B1, for defiling a 14 year old girl.
According to the Accra Station Officer of the Women and Juvenile Unit, (WAJU) of the Ghana Police Service, Chief Inspector Rita Narh, on June 5, 2005 the victim was selling fried yam when the accused, Ametordzie bought Â¢6,000 worth of yam.
She said Ametordzie thereafter gave the victim Â¢20,000 but the victim said she had no change. Ametordzie then instructed the victim to follow him to his house for the money.
When they got to Ametordzie's house, he ordered the victim to wait outside. The accused then entered his room and allegedly brought out a dagger and threatened his victim to enter his room or else he would stab her to death.
The poor victim had no other alternative than to enter the room. Ametordzie therefore forcibly had sex with her for three days. But on the June 7, 2005, the victim managed to escape home when the accused forgot to lock his door with the key.
A complaint was subsequently lodged at the Madina Police Station from where the victim was sent to the hospital for medical check up. The case was later transferred to WAJU leading to the arrest of the accused. When Ametordzie was put before court, he pleaded guilty to the defilement but denied the other offences. He was however convicted on his own plea.

Original_Search_Title: The Senate Code of Ethics
Headline: Nigeria: The Senate Code of Ethics
Body_Text:
Lagos — POINT OF ORDER. Ordinarily, the best time to point a speaker to order is when he is still speaking, not when he has finished speaking and taken his seat. In essence, the best time to point the Senate to order on the Code of Ethics would have been on Tuesday, May 31, 2005 when they were yet debating.
But Shakespeare was essentially right, I think, when he admitted, ÃÂ¢??loveÃÂ¢??s night is noonÃÂ¢??, which also permits the citizenry to comment on the goings-on in high places, just when such goings-on come to their knowledge.
My point of order is constitutional. If the Senators had taken just a little pain to examine our existing laws, it would have amazed them to find the quantum of the code of ethics we already have. The entire Fifth Schedule to the Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, 1999, with copious crossÃÂ¢??references to sections 66, 107, 172, 173, 209, 292 and 318 is devoted to the Code of Conduct for Public Officers. Our Distinguished Senators went on a wild goose chase in an attempt to outlaw sponsored trips. The Constitution has taken care of all that. Many of these items are subsumed under gifts. Actually, whether you are talking of N55 million or a return ticket to New York, gift is still gift.
Too much ethics destroy ethics. Essentially, the Senate Code of Ethics came about in an effort to show the Senators as pious. In the process, however, they set themselves on a collision course with the very Constitution which they had sworn to uphold. ÃÂ¢??Senators threw overboard the rule which barred them from collecting gift items.ÃÂ¢?? (Daily Independent, Wednesday, June 1, 2005, p. A4). Whether the Senators like it or not, the Constitution outlaws the acceptance of gifts and however goes ahead to say, ÃÂ¢??A public officer shall only accept personal gifts or benefits from relatives or personal friends to such extent and on such occasions as are recognised by custom.ÃÂ¢?? The law is liberal enough to ensure that it does not prevent you from accepting a Christmas card and a frozen chicken from your younger one or from accepting some tubers of yam from your constituents when they come from home. Other than these areas of small gifts allowed by law, the Senators have no business beginning to redefine what constitute gifts to them. They are public officers.
There is a common mistake that people make. They refer to the public service as if it were an occupation, at par with law, medicine, accounting, engineering, etc. In justifying the necessity for the Senate Code of Conduct, the Chairman of the Senate Committee on Ethics, Code of Conduct and Public petitions, Senator Olorunimbe Mamora argued vehemently, ÃÂ¢??All professionals have professional ethics and they are bound by the Constitution.ÃÂ¢?? As a matter of fact, the public service is a composite of all occupations, not one for which a separate discipline, a distinctive technical preparation and a special code of conduct exist. It is a composite of occupations many of which themselves embrace definite ethical standards, in whichever context they may operate. As an instance, virtually every occupation known to man has at one time or the other been represented in the Nigerian Senate.
LetÃÂ¢??s take the hypothetical case of a Senator who is also a lawyer. He is found guilty of stealing a goat. By the time the Senate visits any of the five sanctions approved by it last Tuesday on him, I am sure the Nigerian Bar Association and the Judicial Service Commission will not be congratulating and thanking him for a job well done.
Problems of ethical conduct must necessarily arise for the public official by virtue of the power and influence he commands as well as the commitment he undertakes of loyal and disinterested service to the people. In a democracy authority is derived from the consent of the governed. Public administrators must therefore serve the public in a manner that strengthens the integrity and processes of a democratic society. The public servant must do well to always think of the total welfare, the overall good and the long-range effect of the services he renders.
Throughout the Senate deliberations, the Senators were more preoccupied with what the people would think about them. St. Paul makes it abundantly clear that it is not possible or even desirable to please everyone. The administrative demagogue who, in his anxiety to appear friendly and cooperative with everyone seeking his favour, will invariably leave the impression that the pleaders will is to be earnestly followed while failing to explain the countervailing interests that must also be taken into account. Evidently, there are worse evils than mere graft, and one of them is the reduction of politics to an empty process of wheeling and dealing, in which the personal fortunes of the contestants loom larger than the social issues that they evade, straddle and obfuscate in the hope of offending no one.
Undoubtedly, the issue of conflict of interest dominates most discussions of the Code of Conduct for public officials. The 1999 Constitution clearly states that a public officer shall not put himself in a position where his personal interest conflicts with his duties and responsibilities. The problem permeates all branches of government ÃÂ¢?? the legislator who steers through a bill that benefits an enterprise in which he has some investment; the judge who hears a case involving a person with whom he has business dealings; and the Chief executive who has decision-making authority affecting the fortunes of persons or businesses with which he has economic connections. President Obasanjo is yet to speak out on the multi-billion Naira Library Project that he regards as privately owned but into which a large chunk of our tax Naira has been buried, at least from the perspective of State government donations. Whichever way it plays out, in the months ahead, the Library project will provide very good conflict of interest test cases.
Even granting the necessity for spelling out the goals of ethical behaviour and some of the principal misdeeds that should be forbidden, one cannot help but note that there is an undue preoccupation with appearances than with substances and actual implementation. For instance, so much space is devoted to the proscription of the acceptance of gifts. But in whichever direction one looks today, one sees a flagrant abuse of this rule and one wonders why nobody is doing anything by way of implementation.
In the particular case of our Distinguished Senators, a distinguished path of honour and a demonstration of rare courage would have been that in their new status as Born again Senators, those of them who got to the Senate through rigged elections and those who got there through some contrived court decisions, have resigned their membership of the Senate since last Tuesday. Better still, it would have been pleasing to the ear that in furtherance to the honest confession of Senator Chris Adighije (Abia Central) and his passionate appeal to Nigerians for forgiveness in the wake of the N55 million scandal, some Senators have now come forward with the official receipts showing that ÃÂ¢??the giftsÃÂ¢?? they got before being born again have been refunded to the treasury. Or that they have now discovered a cure for the disease called Nigerian elections as Cash and Carry. It should bother the Senators that running for the lowest office of a Councillor is already costing as much as N10 million in some places. And if we must attract the best brains into government, then there must be a total reorientation in our system. It canÃÂ¢??t be done otherwise. These are the stuff of which ethical revolutions are made, not painting old rules in new words.
Again, there is still a failure on the part of the general public to appreciate that a public service or any organisation for that matter, can hardly be expected to grow much higher than the environment in which it exists and that our public service derives most of its temptations for wrong-doing from that environment. Worse still, a double standard is applied to the public officials as contrasted with those in private endeavours. Invariably, we expect higher standards of behaviour from our public servants. In the words of Pendleton Herring, ÃÂ¢??Our admiration goes to the man who makes good in business even though his methods may be ruthless and he may keep within the letter rather than the spirit of the law. The same admiration does not go to the man who makes a full time job of politics in an effort to win power and wealth.ÃÂ¢??
In sum, we must all bear the responsibility for ethical conduct in public affairs. Those who complain of corruption must desist from seeking benefits that are not available to all; and those who complain of non-performance of elected officials must also remember that they are the worst corruptors because when the politician was on his campaign trip, they were more interested in ÃÂ¢??what he broughtÃÂ¢?? instead of assessing him for his readiness for the office. Until such a time when the citizen develops his own moral code which prevents him from debasing himself by procuring corruption of public servants, the problem of corruption and morality in public life will continue to dwell with us. The onus of attaining the highest possible public ethics is not just on those in public employment, it is on us all.
Hon. Omorotionmwan is a public affairs analyst, based in Benin-City

Original_Search_Title: Global Trade is Dignified Aid
Headline: Ghana: Global Trade is Dignified Aid
Body_Text:
"I used to like yams a lot," reasons Kofi Asiedu as he recalls why he
went into the export business. Twenty years ago, he was living in
Nottingham, England, working his way through an industrial engineering
degree and like most Ghanaians living abroad, was eager to get a taste of
home.
"Yams were very expensive and hard to come by at that time in England,"
he says. He saw that he wasn't the only Ghanaian living in the UK that was
craving food from home. Years later, back in Ghana, he packed four or five
suitcases with different types of Ghanaian food, flew to London and started
selling to the shops.
He had a keen business sense and the determination to make international
trade work for him. Today, as he sits in an air conditioned office in
Achimota, the amenities that surround him betray his success. His mobile is
ringing, a TV sits in the corner, and a sprawling desk surrounded by four
chairs grace the room, yet these do not take up even half the available
space.
On the other side of the room, there is a colorful display of canned
goods: garden eggs and palm soup. They are products from his food processing
business he started in the early nineties, using the profit from his earlier
exports. Asiedu believes in the inherent right of all people to be able to
compete in a global market, and dispels the idea that you need money to make
money. "I didn't have the capital at all," he says. "I started from a
suitcase."
On June 1st of this year, Asiedu struck a deal with a Minnesota-based
distributor who called him to arrange a $10,000 trial shipment of a ton of
smoked lates, herring, sardinella and mackerel. If the trial meets
expectations, then Asiedu will begin regular shipments every two weeks. "He
wants to represent me selling my products all over the eastern and western
shores," says the owner of CAN & KAA. So the quality of life for Asiedu
and his family is about to improve more.
The story illustrates the power of trade, and how the right to human
dignity is preserved when economic empowerment is based on the efforts of an
individual rather then on handouts, something that the West Africa Trade Hub
(WATH) is trying to promote in Ghana.
"The goal for Ghana is no different than any other countries. It is to
develop greater trade both within West Africa and to the US and other
markets," says Vanessa Adams, WATH's Head of Export Business
Development.
The project, which began in 2003, is funded by USAID and seeks to assist
producers who are ready to export goods from West Africa. In Ghana, the
initial focus was on hand-woven textiles. Last year, the project started to
focus on shea butter producers. "West Africa is the biggest producer of shea
butter in the world, so it's an obvious one," says Adams.
Other Ghanaian products that are being targeted for export through the
WATH project are processed fish and seafood, handmade home dÃ©cor, and
cashews.
The project provides training, and promotion of Ghanaian products at
trade shows in the US, but does not provide producers with any money. WATH
looks for people like Adiesu, who are already successfully exporting, and
could use a little help to improve an existing business "They must have a
track record of exporting to somewhere. If you haven't exported to any other
country yet, then you are not ready," says Adams emphatically.
"We are talking about the right to compete on an international level,
but to do that you have to be able to compete in an unforgiving
market. American markets don't give you a second chance. You have to be
ready."
She recognizes that it sounds harsh, but also illustrates the potential
failure of sending someone who is not ready abroad. "Some people may end up
at a trade show and end up like a deer caught in headlights," she says. They
need to know how to converse in English, and answer questions about their
products.
But critics say helping those who already have a business up and running
is not fair, and that much needed money does not reach the people who really
need it.
Peter Lovett, a consultant for WATH, works closely with Ghanaian shea
butter pickers and processors who are not in the position to export. He
believes the benefits do trickle down, either directly or indirectly. Those
who do not make the cut can still be invited to "training only" sessions, he
says, where they can be given information and linked up with entrepreneurs
with similar goals.
They can also get links to contacts and buyers in both the US and
Africa. Given this knowledge, hopeful producers may start to lay the
groundwork for a successful exporting business.
This, Adams says, is a good way to encourage participatory
development. If you go to a country that is incapable, and simply dump money
on it, it just reinforces the negative perception of that country, she
says.
"Why do most people not give money to beggars in the US? Because they
ask, what is it that they are doing to help themselves?"
The idea is looking at strengths instead of needs, and building a
positive form of development that may prove to be much more sustainable than
aid in the long run. It raises profit as well as profile.
"We have gone through a lot, it's not easy here," says Asiedu. "People
still think that blacks are living in trees. When they see people like me,
that changes their opinion."

Original_Search_Title: Nigerians in Nokia Face of Africa Finals
Headline: Nigerians in Nokia Face of Africa Finals
Body_Text:
Lagos — Breaking news from the stable of M-Net, continental broadcasters, has it that ten African girls out of the original sixteen who made it to the semi-finals of the Nokia Face of Africa modeling competition have made it to the finals of the most prestigious modeling event on the African continent.
The confirmed news has it that Nigeria leads the pack of finalist with all three of the nation's semi-finalist making it to the grand finale holding later on the 10th of July at the Superbowl in Sun City South Africa. The three Nigeria finalists, Doyin Haastrup, Opeyemi Awoyemi and Adeola Ariyo have been away in Zambia horning their skills ahead of the finals. Specifically, the girls have been in South Africa from where they left for the bootcamp in Zambia for their first real training in international modeling.
However, the journey towards becoming the next face of Africa is not over yet... Really, it has only just begun as the Nigerian girls are expected to get stiff opposition from seven other African prospects for the prize of instant stardom.
Below is the list of all the finalists as released by M-Net. To find out more about them, simply read the biographies below. Then tune in to M-Net on June 19, June 26, July 3 and July 10 to follow the search for the NOKIA FACE OF AFRICA 2005.
Nineteen-year-old Kaone Kario hails from Maun in Botswana, a place she describes quite simply as, "it's beautiful, it's home."
The 1.75m tall student loves action films and she's a fan of The Pacifier, Oceans 11, Bad Boys 1, Bad Boys 2 and Speed. Energetic and a go-getter, Kaone says her Diesel jeans are her favourite fashion item! Eager to travel Kaone would like to model in New York and would like to visit Uganda. A music lover, Kaone has a long list of favourite tracks including those by Bobby Valentino, Mariah Carey and Britney Spears. Asked if she sings as well, Kaone replies, "Only in the shower!"
She entered the Nokia Face of Africa to "live a dream" and Kaone believes she overcame her fear of rejection when she attended a casting session for the project in Gaborone. Delighted to be travelling to Zambia with the other semi-finalists, she says, "I was so excited I wanted to stand on the tallest building and shout and tell the whole world. If I had control over time, I could wipe away the remaining days. I can't wait."
Twenty-one year-old Tingirt Alemayehu works as a part-time model and a secretary in Addis Ababa. She is 1.78m tall and holds a diploma in computer science. She speaks Amharic and English. Her favourite colors are blue and pink and she watches the Oprah show. Tingirt's musical heroine is Celine Dion and she enjoys the ballad "My Heart Will Go On" from the movie Titanic.
She says that her most-loved Ethiopian destination is Lake Langaan and that she loves the sunshine and traditional dress of her country. She regards fashion designers Christian Dior and Dolce & Gabbana highly and would enjoy modeling in France and the US.
Tingirt's hobbies include swimming, handball and playing the guitar. She has not traveled before and her trip to Zambia for the competition will mark the first time she has left Ethiopia.
Photogenic Yordanos Teshager is 1.79m tall and is 21-years-old. She was selected as a semi-finalist for the Nokia Face of Africa 2005 after attending a casting session in Ethiopia. She's well traveled and returns home from New York just one week before she leaves Addis again for the next selection session in Zambia.
Nairobi-based Anne Wambui Maina is 22-years-old and is 1.72m in height. A former student of the Kenya Polytechnic, Anne is currently studying French with the Alliance Franchoise. She also speaks Swahili and English.
Her favourite color is baby blue and like most women, she loves shopping for shoes! She's an accomplished traveler and has already visited France, China, Germany, Nigeria, Ghana and the USA. She'd love to visit South Africa and Egypt if the opportunity arose.
Anne loves watching romantic comedies and adores the music of Whitney Houston. She enjoys all traditional Kenyan food and says that Kenyans are outgoing and ever smiling. She entered the Nokia Face of Africa 2005 after dropping in on the auditions in Nairobi to greet a friend and when she was short-listed she called her younger brother to share the good news.
Hailing from Port Harcourt in Nigeria and having just turned 18-years-old Opeyemi Awoyemi is one of the youngest semi-finalists for the Nokia Face of Africa 2005. At 1.83m in height, Opeyemi is also one of the tallest semi-finalists in the group. A student, who speaks English and Yoruba, Opeyemi is a fan of gospel blues singers and also enjoys the music of Nigeria's Styl-Plus and 2Face. Her favourite songs include African Queen and Everytime. In her spare time, Opeyemi says she must have a radio on playing cool music! She also says, "I play a little piano and I sing a lot!"
Opeyemi is also an Eddie Murphy fan proving her love for comedies. She plays badminton and sometimes, hits the hoops playing basketball. Her favourite place in Nigeria is Lagos State and her favourite Nigerian food is yam, eggs and rice. In addition, she says that, "the best thing about people in my country is that they know their rights in everything they do."
When she was selected to be a semi-finalist for the Nokia Face of Africa 2005, Opeyemi called her sister first to share the good news!
At 1.84m tall, Doyin Haastrup is the tallest semi-finalist for the Nokia Face of Africa 2005. Born in Lagos, the 20-year-old keeps in shape with regular gym classes and by eating healthy.
A fan of the designers Modela and Mon-Ami, Doyin's fashion accessories include perfumers, shoes and earrings. She would love to model in Paris and in New York City and would like to visit Zambia, Ghana, Liberia and South Africa. She lists Lagos and Abuja as the places she enjoys the most in Nigeria and says she loves the warm West Africa climate of her home.
On the entertainment front, Doyin enjoys the reality show Ultimate Search and the music of Sean Paul (I'm Still in Love), R. Kelly (Happy People) and 2Face (My African Queen).
Nineteen-year-old Adeola Ariyo from Lagos in Nigeria says she was ecstatic when it was announced that she was a semi-finalist for the Nokia Face of Africa 2005. "The first person I told was my father, " says the 1.73m tall student.
Proud of her heritage Adeola, who speaks both English and Yoruba, says Nigerians know how to have a good time and that the country has a lot of different places to visit. Given the chance Adeola would like to see Zimbabwe though she has already traveled quite a lot making stops in Ghana, Togo and the United Kingdom.
A true design fundi, Adeola loves Gucci and Ferragamo and says her best fashion item isÃ¢-oe.you guessed itÃ¢-oeshoes! She would love to model in Paris and says she entered the Nokia Face of Africa 2005 "for the experience and to feel what it's like to be recognized. To have fun and at the same time learn a lot more about the fashion world."
What are Adeola's favourite thingsÃ¢-oethe colourr sky blue, African and Chinese food, the soap Eastenders and the music of Faith Evans, Aaliyah and Usher. Meanwhile Adeola also describes herself as athletic and say she plays the clarinet.
Eighteen-year-old Tiyani Maluleke was born in Nkowankowa. She is 1.78m tall and is currently a student at the Cape Peninsula University of Technology where she is studying Bio Medical Technology.
Tiyani, who speaks English and Tsonga, entered the Nokia Face of Africa because she "thought it was a great opportunity to boost my modeling career and be proud of my country." When she was selected Tiyani says, "I was surprised and overwhelmed. I jumped up and down and screamed. I was so excited."
While she directs most of her energy into her studies, Tiyani enjoys walking and hiking which explains why her fashion sense tends towards jeans and sneakers. She also enjoys she spending time with her friends and loves to dance. Her musical tastes tend towards the sounds of Jack Johnson, India Irie and Jill Scott. Her favourite color is yellow and her favourite food is her mom's homemade pap & chicken.
Tiyani is optimistic about South Africa and says that the best thing about South Africans is "out strength and courage, our willingness to build a better future."
She goes on to say, "We have 11 official languages which encourages different cultures to be united under one flag." If the opportunity arises, Tiyani would like to visit New York, Mozambique, Namibia and Malawi
Mirriam Jaulani was born in Lusaka in Zambia and is 19-years-old. She is 1.74m tall and speaks English and Nyanja. She loves comedy-dramas, gospel music and the songs of Matthew Ngosa and Rebecca Malope. Her favourite place is Zambia is at the Victoria Falls in Livingstone, which is where the 16 semi-finalists will be gathering for the competition's next selection phase.
While she doesn't have any specific designer favourites, when stocking her wardrobe, Mirriam loves to buy long skirts and pants. She entered the competition "hoping to win a continental title. This was my real chance of starting my modeling career." Greatmore Chatya (Zimbabwe)
Harare-based Greatmore Chatya flew to Lusaka, Zambia to attend a casting session for the competition. Named a semi-finalist, the 19-year-old says she couldn't believe her good fortune and is now looking forward to the next selection round in Zambia
Standing 1.78m tall, Greatmore speaks both Shona and English. She loves the color pink, traditional food and R&B music. She listens to the music of 50 CENT and Oliver Mutukudzi. Her favourite place in Zimbabwe is Mutare and she says that the best thing about Zimbabweans is that they love their culture. She would love to visit Nigeria and would like to model in South Africa.
Greatmore respects African designers Bongiwe Walaza, Joyce Chimanya and Kutala.
She also enjoys playing basketball and watching television.

Original_Search_Title: Why We Must Not Blame Jay-Jay Okocha
Headline: Nigeria: Why We Must Not Blame Jay-Jay Okocha
Body_Text:
Last Sunday, the Super Eagles played a 1-1 draw with the Wasps of Rwanda
in the World Cup/African Nations Cup qualifier. Although Nigeria sits on top
of Africa's group four, it is by virtue of goal difference, as it ties with
Angola on 14 points.
This obviously is a precarious position to be, especially given that in
matters of football Nigeria rates itself higher than its organizational
input to the game. Moreover, in a football world where Togo is whitewashing
Kalusha Bwalya's Zambia 4-1, Nigerians forget that football is still a game
between two teams of eleven men and not about country statistics and they
expected that once the war-weary Rwandans saw our Eagles, they would fold
their tents and run, especially if our magician, Jay-Jay Okocha were in the
team. Since the result of the Sunday match, Nigerians have been gnashing
their teeth. All manner of excuses have been given, the most common being
the absence of the team captain, Jay-Jay Okocha in Kigali.
I agree with Mr. Kojo Williams, who once made a cameo appearance as
chairman of NFA, that Enyimba of Aba could have drubbed the Wasps of
Rwanda. But I hope he did not forget that Enyimba can only beat the Rwandans
simply because they play together, understand one another and are dedicated
to their role. Preparation, that's just the word that sets Enyimba apart
from the Eagles. If the players who reported to camp had at least two weeks
to prepare for the match the result would have been different. If we keep in
view the fact that Okocha was not the only Eagle that did not report to
camp, we would agree that it is pointless to blame him, although I find the
manner of his excusing himself from matches he did not want to play very
distasteful.
Yakubu Aiyegbeni made a malaria excuse and avoided camp. Julius
Aghaghowa, John Utaka, Seyi Olofinjana and Ayila Yusuf were all absent due
to different reasons, including injury. Now those who reported to camp
reported late from off-season rustiness. Some straggled into camp 24 hours
before departure to Kigali! We all knew this. How then can we blame it on
Okocha?
One thing I find ridiculous is our exclusive dependence on foreign-based
players, worse still some particular stars. It has often been the case that
once any of the stars disappoints us, we come reeling on the ground. Sam
Okwaraji died in the field of play.
Has it stopped Nigeria going to any football competition? It could
happen to any of these boys we preach patriotism to. If Nwankwo Kanu did not
report to camp, we would have blamed him for the poor showing of the
Eagles. He shows up and what does he get? Some yam heads believe that he
wants to use the national team to boost his career! Really? So because we
are used to hiring foreign coaches who sit in European hotel rooms watching
matches on TV all in the name of "monitoring" or players, we really believe
now that Bryan Robson needs to watch the Eagles matches to take decision on
what to do with Kanu?! We take ourselves too seriously.
Let us take our minds back to last year's Unity Cup. Christian Chukwu
was bluffed by the big boys in the Eagles. He, therefore, assembled boys who
wanted to prove a point, boys who had appetite for victory, boys who had the
humility and the zeal to do what the coach wanted. Against the expectations
of those who believed that without them there would be no Eagles, Chukwu's
Unity Cup team played joy into the heart of every Nigerian. That team
re-enacted the zest of the Eagles of Westerhof era. It was clear that the
days of some Eagles holding the nation to ransom was about to end. But the
smart boys quickly reached out to their publicity agents on various sports
desks in Nigeria. And before we knew it, Chukwu began to get threats never
to believe that he could do without "experienced" players.
He must not think that he can replace the Super Eagles. And, of course,
those who hound him on "technical depth" swore that the marvelous showing of
the Eagles in the Unity Cup was no proof that Chukwu knew anything about
football!
What happened was that Chukwu listened to all these and allowed the big
boys to stroll back to camp for the next match. Thus the opportunity to
raise a team of disciplined, humble and eager players was lost. So I blame
our predicament on those publicity agents who create this confusion for our
sports administrators. Of course you could say Chukwu is entirely to blame
if he allowed these publicists to decide for him. And I will agree with you,
because I keep asking myself, would Okocha get away with this behaviour
under Westerhof? But remember that already, the FA is touting Joseph Yobo as
a replacement for Okocha as captain without asking Chukwu what he
thinks. Remember also that two strange names sneaked into Chukwu's list of
invited players.
Those who compare Okocha's patriotism with the Beckams of this world
hardly understand the working conditions of our players in Europe, and I am
making no excuses for them. But the truth is that the working conditions of
Africans in Europe are not the same with those of the Europeans. Racism is
still alive in football in Europe; ask Thiery Henry! European managers still
prefer to field their nationals instead of Africans. This has to do with the
politics of developing your national team at the expense of the country you
work for. That is why England is worried about the preponderance of foreign
players in the Premier League. So, to arrange matches at the time African
players are switching clubs and avoiding injuries is actually a disservice
to our football. On the other hand, Nigeria has a peculiar problem of
incompetence in sports administration. That is why we cannot play a grade A
friendly match when the whole world is playing.
Now, back to Jay-Jay Okocha. I said earlier that I detest his manner of
avoiding camp. He can be man enough to tell his coach when he does not want
to play a match, instead of the silly pranks of "missing his way" in London!
I knew when Okocha started to talk of tiredness, that he was going to avoid
some matches, particularly the Unity Cup. But it seems that Chukwu did not
understand his body language. He has himself to blame for taking Okocha for
granted.
Above all, we are all too soon assuming that Okocha is not human. Here
is a man who lodged one million dollars in a Nigerian bank, (the kind of
money many Nigerians would kill for) and his money went down with the
bank. Notwithstanding that, he gave his all in the Nation's Cup in
Tunisia. He led the Eagles admirably, and I praised him here. Now our
president promised that his money would be paid to him.
Up till now, we are not aware that his money has been paid. Meanwhile,
instead of the people who caused Okocha's anguish doing term in jail, they
are busy playing political godfathers and demigods, feeding thousands of the
poor they robbed, on cakes, ice cream, roasted chicken and semolina for
which they are being regaled on the pages of some newspapers! Come on! Are
we not sick here? And come the time for nomination for African Footballer of
the Year, the Nigerian FA forgot to vote for its captain! Okocha is only
human if he thinks about pacing himself now in order to play for a few more
years to earn what he has lost to Nigerian banks!
If you played back a ten-year old tape on our preparation for major
competitions, you would find the recent Kigali disaster in it.
Where does the fault lie? You can find the answer in the lackadaisical
manner with which we changed our football statute even in the face of
obvious threat of sanctions from FIFA! How can any serious FA waste so much
saliva on "hiring of foreign coach" when they know they don't have the money
to pay one? The irony may be that the Togolese team, handled by Stephen
Keshi who they sacked, may beat Nigeria in the next African Cup of Nations!
Those who believe that we can't raise a team from our national (they
call it local) League and take them on playing tours to give them exposure
are part of the problem with our football. We cannot always depend
exclusively on players we call "tired legs" at one time and cry when they
say their legs are truly tired and deserve rest! From now on, Chukwu should
be man enough to sanction any player that tries to hold him to ransom. The
only way he can do that effectively is to invite players who are hungry for
the national team. He should resign if the FA imposes decisions on him.

Original_Search_Title: The Gulder Ultimate Search 2
Headline: Nigeria: The Gulder Ultimate Search 2
Body_Text:
Lagos — Ten were calledÃ¢-oe
There can only be one Ultimate Man. Finally, that man has emerged after three tough weeks on the hills of Obudu. It can't be news to you that the winner of the Gulder Ultimate Search 2 is Lucan Chambaliss. He wins the main prize of N5m. Lucan was followed by Martin Uro, who finished as first runner up. For his efforts, at least for hanging in till a more desperate Lucan found the helmet, Martin gets N1.2m. Ifeoma Dike, the 19 year-old University of Lagos Psychology undergraduate was the third finalist. But just like the maiden edition of the Search, the last woman standing fell out when push came to shove. It was always going to come to a time when sheer brawn would rule.
The Search on TV
I shouldn't get ahead of the story. Let me share my thoughts about The Gulder Ultinate Search hereafter referred to as The Search. Just like its first outing, viewers were hooked on TV to follow The search. And for three weeks, that was the main topic of conversation. There was the initial confusion over the slight change of TV time. Before the competition began, the information was that three stations: NTA, AIT and STV would all air the programme from 10 pm. STV aired it from 10.30. The other two stations managed to keep to 10pm. This had some advantages. For instance, you return to STV if you missed the opening action on the other stations.
Search 1 vs The Search 2: Which is better?
I don't know why but everyone felt a need to draw comparisons between the first edition of The Search and this second phase. So much that the contestants (or the 'highlanders' according to Cross River governor, Donald Duke) themselves were caught up in it. I couldn't see the reason for this. I know there was some pressure to declare this second search better than the first. But what does it really matter? Even if by some statistical evaluation, it's discovered that the first was better, what's the great advantage? The fact is that they were different editions of the same programme, set in different environments, done by even more totally different people. As for the highlanders saying the contestants of the first edition of The Search wouldn't have made it on Obudu Hill, that point was neither here nor there. If they needed any consolation, their prize money, even the consolation prizes were more than what the contestants of the first Search got.
The Porridge effect
Last year while reviewing The Search, I'd frowned at the austere lifestyles of the contestants. Their living conditions were far from hygienic. I thought it depicted us in a bad light. However, by the second coming of The Search, I wasn't so focused on the return to the Tarzan lifestyle. Of course, I still wondered if truly the water they drank was from the stream. Not that stream water is bad especially the type found all the way up. But your body needs to adjust to this new lifestyle.
My new understanding notwithstanding, I did wonder about all that yam porridge or is it potridge? Since the show was only an hour on TV, it's possible it was just some kind of coincidence that almost every night, the contestants ate porridge or beans and porridge.
Will there ever be an Ultimate Woman?
Let's imagine for a minute that it was Ifeoma who found that helmet, would she have been called the ultimate woman? And how helpful is this to the brand? For years, Gulder has advertised and marketed itself as the drink for the ultimate man. Is this why The Search appears configured to knock out even the most ambitious woman? How could Ifeoma have found the helmet? Even if by some miracle she'd survived to the last day, how could she have run and climbed at the speed Lucan was doing it? Which leads me to draw the obvious conclusion: women are only included for entertainment. And perhaps to cook food! All hope's not lost. Perhaps Nigerian Breweries can still create an all-female version of the Search.
And Vaughan miscalculatedÃ¢-oe
Vaughan became the most talked about member of the group. According to his post-eviction interview, the controversy which he courted was stage managed. The plan he said was for him to become a better person midway into the show. But that he didn't have time, etc, etc. Vaughan wanted to be in the news, good or bad. I guess he succeeded in that regards. After all, am I not devoting space to him? However, when all's said, Vaughan miscalculated.
Chidi's second outing
Just like the issue of food, the presence of presenter Chidi Mokeme was something I adjusted to. Not that my mind didn't wonder why he was so stiff. Why he carried on as if he was a visiting school headmaster? But like Super Eagles Garba Lawal, there must be something Chidi's employers can see we're not so privileged to share. Fortunately for him (and unfortunately for some people), it's the employers who decide who they want to use.
Illegible graphics
Throughout the show, reading on the screen was difficult. I'm not talking about the text messages. These words usually came against a white background that looked like a thin white film. The words were lost in the white background. Even on the last day when viewers were being asked to vote, it was hard reading what was onscreen. I don't know how this can be justified. The Search wasn't a live programme. So whatever minor errors there were could've been corrected. What's the point of posting messages onscreen if not to communicate with your audience?
N50 text messages
How did you find the call to vote for The Search contestants? Especially when you're reminded that SMS costs 'only' N50? I've come to the conclusion that the reason for the rather high cost of sms was to prevent a deluge of text messages which could overwhelm the network. Or what do you think?
All in allÃ¢-oe
In all, I was excited about the venue of The Search. And Donald Duke proved to be a very good host. He was in the mood of The Search all through. I hope the next one will happen somewhere around Yankari Game Reserve, Bauchi State. There's still the issue of time. The Search lasts only one houron TV. Now, it would take some wizardry at editing to fit in activities of 24 hours. Hopefully, Gulder will increase airtime someday?

Original_Search_Title: Enter Gbenga Daniel, the Double Otunba!
Headline: Nigeria: Enter Gbenga Daniel, the Double Otunba!
Body_Text:
Ibadan — IJEBU ODE, Ogun State, will on Saturday, June 11, 2005, explode in
celebration as the governor, Otunba Gbenga Daniel and his wife, Yeye
Olufunke Daniel, will respectively be conferred with the traditional titles
of Otunba Obalofin of Ijebuland and Yeye Otunba Obalofin of Ijebuland.
Though Daniel had bagged over 50 chieftaincy titles including the Otunba
Adeoti of Omu, the Otunba Obalofin of Ijebuland, is the most significant as
it is a traditional Ijebu kingdom title that has its roots in his maternal
forebears, Alayeluwa Obalofin, the sixth Awujale, who ascended the throne at
Ijebu-Ode in 1482.
Before the formal installation of the governor as Obalofin of Ijebuland,
a series of traditional and cultural activities will hold, from Friday, June
3, 2005, which makes the rites similar to the ones performed before the
crowning of royal fathers in Ijebuland.
They include the "Ilete ceremony" (the royal sitting at his residence),
where Daniel will receive traditional chiefs, age groups (regberegbe),
friends, associates and women, coming to pay homage to him at his Ijebu-Ode
residence. There will be a traditional "woro" dance by an assemblage of
women who will have to go to the traditional stream as early as 4am to fetch
the traditional water and then dance round Ijebu-Ode, before ending the
procession at the residence of Obalofin.
On Friday, June 10, 2005, the "Ikokore night" will hold, where "ikokore"
a special delicacy of Ijebus made of water yam, cocoa yam and laced with
plenty of crayfish, assorted fish and a lot of ingredients, will be served
to the people.
The installation, which is the climax, will be performed at the Aaafin
Awujale, Ijebu-Ode at 10am on Saturday, June 11, 2005, by the Awujale Sikiru
Kayode Adetona, Ogbagba II, supported by a cream of royal fathers all over
the country. Eminent Nigerians including President Olusegun Obasanjo,
Vice-President Atiku Abubakar, all former heads of state, serving and former
governors, ministers, senators, members of the House of Representatives,
diplomats and other special guests have been invited.
A kaleidoscope of colours including horse riding and the rich culture of
the Ijebu will add pep to the ceremony, expected to showcase the tourism
industry in Ogun State. Adetona has placed so much premium on the
installation of the Otunba Obalofin and Yeye Otunba Obalofin of Ijebuland,
that he has made it the grand finale of his 45th coronation
anniversary.
The sixth Awujale of Ijebuland, Obalofin's reign, was said to have been very peaceful and it was this paramount
ruler that played host to Ofiowo, the powerful chief priest who brought
the "Agemos" to Ijebuland. The 16 Agemos, including a few additions, have
made the Ijebu history unique, as they are the custodians of the Agemo
festival, which the Ijebus hold the traditional belief that they are the
harbingers of children for barren women, apart from the peace and prosperity
of Ijebu kingdom.
By the declaration made under section 4 (2) of the Chiefs Law (1957) of
the regulation of the selection of the Awujale of Ijebuland chieftaincy,
Awujale Obalofin comes from the Funsegbuwa Ruling House. The other three
Awujale Ruling Houses are Fidipote, Gbelegbuwa and the Anikilaya. Anikilaya
is the ruling house of the incumbent Awujale of Ijebuland, Oba Kayode
Adetona.
The importance of the Otunba Obalofin traditional title stems from a
deep and historical cultural antecedent that is rooted in the evolution of
Ijebuland. The last eminent holder of the Otunba Obalofin of Ijebuland was
the great philanthropist and industrialist, the late Sir Mobolaji Bank
Anthony.

Original_Search_Title: The President's Democracy Speech
Headline: Nigeria: The President's Democracy Speech
Body_Text:
Lagos — Like all anniversary speeches, President Obasanjo's Democracy Day
address to the nation was laden with a litany of what he considers to be
government's achievements in the past six years. This has prompted criticism
of the speech as more of a report card on his government than an assessment
of democratic rule.
But even as an appraisal of his government's performance, the
president's speech contains too many generalisations and claims of
achievements that few Nigerians can really testify to.
The president said that a lot had been achieved in the past six years,
citing as example a reduction in crime rate, a Gross Domestic Product (GDP)
growth rate of over five per cent, an unprecedented strategic grains reserve
of more than 150,000 tons and increased attention to road construction and
rehabilitantion. He awarded his government high marks in investment in
telecommunication, education, health and other social infrastructure.
Admitting that electricity supply is still a problem, the president
said, however, that the passage of a bill to unbundle the ailing National
Electric Power Authority (NEPA) is a major achievement. He said efforts have
been made within the period under review to sanitise the business
environment and curb corruption, citing the establishment of the Economic
and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC), the Independent Corrupt Practices
and Related Offences Commission (ICPC) and the Nigerian Investment Promotion
Commission (NIPC) as steps in that direction. "I can proudly say that
together we have achieved a lot in the past few years," he said.
The president did not end his address without urging Nigerians to
support his economic reforms which according to him have yielded positive
results.
The president's speech is not altogether without a few correct
assertions. In the last six years, the nation has undoubtedly experienced
some economic reforms. The deregulation of the economy has proceeded with
greater vigour since 1999. One immediate benefit of that is the competition
in the telecommunication industry. Also true are the reforms in the banking
industry which are expected to lead to a strong financial sector. The
self-congratulations that the president gave himself in these areas could
therefore be understood.
However, quite a few of the president's claims are at best debatable. As
we have often observed on this page, too much reliance on abstract
statistics in evaluating the impact of government policies can be
misleading. Statistics don't always tell the whole story especially in a
country with questionable capacity for producing reliable figures. The
easiest way to measure the cost of living is by looking at the consumer
price index. In Nigeria today, the prices of major staple foods have gone
through the roof. Indeed, much of the tension that the nation has
experienced in the past few years was rooted in widespread hunger and
poverty in the land.
A few Nigerians may have become richer within the period under
review. Conversely, however, a lot more have fallen into the poverty
net. The evidence is all over the place. Far fewer Nigerians today feed
better than they did 10 years ago. Whereas in 1999 when this government came
into place, a 50-kilogramme bag of rice sold for N2,500, today it sells for
more than N5000. The same is true of beans, garri and yam.
The grinding poverty all too evident in the country today contradicts
the president's claim that things have become better since the return of
democracy. While we agree that the economic reforms may yield better results
in the long term, it must be obvious to the president that a lot of
Nigerians are dying of hunger and poverty in the short run. That should be a
source of worry to the government.
Also while crime rate may have dropped, it cannot be denied that the
incidence of political violence remains high. Hardly a week passes without
news of one political murder or the other in the country. This is enough to
discourage potential investors, and might have influenced a recent United
States Intelligence report predicting the disintegration of Nigeria in 15
years.
Although the president's address was virtually silent on the employment
situation, it is clear that the number of Nigerians who are jobless has been
rising steeply. This must be an indication of the poor performance of the
economy. Economies that are robust create jobs. Despite promises that the
National Economic Empowerment and Development Strategy (NEEDS) would yield
seven million jobs in its first phase, the jobless queue has continued to
grow. It is an irony that many Nigerians can't understand.
Worryingly also, the past six years has seen increasing incidence of
government's disregard for the rule of law. Court orders have been treated
with levity and sometimes brazenly disregarded by government officials. A
typical case is that of the federal government that re-echoes the attitude
of the Swiss authorities whom the president accused of disobeying their
country's Supreme Court by not repatriating Nigeria's stolen wealth. Critics
would be right to say that our president is guilty of the same offence
because several months after Nigeria's Supreme Court ordered his government
to release Lagos State council funds in its custody, he has refused to do
so. These blights are clear threats to the sustenance of democracy.

Original_Search_Title: Muhammad Al-Ghazali
Headline: Nigeria: Muhammad Al-Ghazali
Body_Text:
The emperor strikes again!
Unless Newton's theory on gravity is about to be reversed not a few
Nigerian lawmakers will be kissing the earth very shortly with a heavy thump
and it will not be on their own free volition but that of our all-
conquering Emperor Olusegun Obasanjo. After few weeks of noise making and
chest thumping on their effort to impeach Mr. President, it now appears that
the Kubwa vigilantes have backed down on their threat reportedly after a
visit from the president in search of truce and; receiving the man in his
office the Speaker Aminu Masari could even afford banters on old soldiers
and their bad habits etcetera. Ces't fini? Not a chance by a long mile! As a
matter of fact, any one familiar with the ways of the Emperor and his
vindictive mindset should know for sure that that indeed, may just be the
beginning of the story. The following are a few reasons why:
The late Senate President Chuba Okadigbo, was not only an excellent
orator or a respected idealist but also a man of steel and principles. One
breezy morning during his brief but eventful tenure, the Police then
mercifully under Musliu Smith laid siege on his official residence to
retrieve the mace we were told he removed from the Senate chambers to
scuttle plans for his removal from office.
That was after agents of the presidency had failed in a previous plot to
impeach him. Quite typical of the man, he stood on his balcony and delivered
a stunning lecture on democracy and civility in which he declared that the
Police's attempt to even touch the mace was pure sacrilege. As the siege
lasted the romantic democrats among us were moved to tears by his ordeal but
also quite expectedly, the normally vociferous human rights activists in
Lagos were deafening in their silence and support for the man and his
symbolic action.
Chuba was to survive that scare but only just. Days after, at the
dedication of his official residence, the Emperor simply gate crashed the
party and even danced with Chuba's wife as if all was long and truly
forgotten. Chuba may have had a PHD in Political Science and may have indeed
been an astute political strategist, but in lowering his guard thereafter he
betrayed extreme naivety about the Emperor's character flaws and ultimately
set himself up for the sucker punch, which was swift in coming. The rest is
now history.
The manner of his election notwithstanding, erstwhile PDP Chairman Audu
Ogbeh became intoxicated with the euphoria of his high office and felt he
was man enough to rebuke the Emperor over his sinister handling of the still
lingering Anambra political crises. The Emperor's courtiers quickly
responded by going for his jugular. The normal public outcry followed after
which the Emperor supposedly backed down and even tucked into a sumptuous
meal of pounded yam at the residence of his adversary in supposed
truce. Case over? The last time we heard of Chief Ogbeh he just survived an
armed robbery attack at home in Makurdi. Before then and in between
mouthfuls of pounded yam, he had the common sense to decipher the ominous
message and quickly fled from his office and the capital for good measure
but not before a visit to the FCT Minister Nasir El-Rufai to secure the
title documents of his landed property in the city. He is generally
considered to be lucky, having escaped with his life without a court
appearance and without the indignity of being paraded in handcuffs. The same
of course, cannot be said about Adolphus Wabara, whose major crime it seems,
was in making himself such an easy target and a major obstacle to the desire
of the Emperor to appear squeaky clean before the other majesties in London,
Paris and Washington.
Now, cast your mind back to 1995 and the aborted coup plot to topple a
certain dark-goggled General of diminutive stature. A few notable
individuals including the Emperor were held, tried and convicted by a
military tribunal. The death sentence on the Emperor was however
subsequently commuted to a jail term but not before an intensive debate in
the AFRC. It later emerged that a tall and bulky General from Zuru who also
happened to be the Army Chief insisted that precedence should be followed,
meaning all convicted coup plotters deserved the firing squad like Vatsa
regardless of their pedigree. Alas, events leading onto 1999 ensured that
the Emperor survived and; among the first tasks he accomplished was to jail
Bamayi and company over the alleged murder of Kudirat Abiola the same way he
did away with otherwise laudable projects such as the PTF and the Vision
2010 just principally because they originated from Abacha. The trial of
Bamayi and Almustapha of course has since been reduced to a comical circus
at a time sadistic killers in the mould of Ganiyu Adams can wine and dine at
state functions. Today will mark nearly five years since their arrest while
their trial remains inconclusive.
Again, flash your mind back to 2003 and the Shenanigans involved in the
selection of the PDP Presidential candidate when the Emperor's re-nomination
was generally thought to be in jeopardy due to the interest shown by his
deputy, the sad faced ex Customs officer from Yola who was buoyed by support
from most Governors in the PDP. Only last minute high-level politicking in
which he was made to swallow his pride several times over saved the
Emperor's scalp then but again, his response was quite predictable. The over
ambitious deputy who previously doubled as the Chairman of the National
Council on Privatisation was soon reduced to a bit-part player in state
