affairs only to surface barely two weeks ago at the fundraiser of perhaps
the world's most expensive library where he delivered a stunning speech in
praise of the Emperor! Incredible.
That aside, Nigerians collective are great shock absorbers. But even
they cannot understand what has befallen them since 1999. With the benefit
of hindsight, it is now possible to hazard a few guesses. Perhaps we should
have stormed the Villa to force Abacha out when the Emperor was
incarcerated. Maybe at a time most of his own brethren fled abroad, we
should all have risked our lives by storming the gulag where the Emperor lay
emaciated or; could it be that we failed to invite the Americans to invade
the country in his aid? The posers seem unending, but whatever the answers
may be, some basic facts are as clear as daylight. At no point in our
eventful history have Nigerians been made to endure so much penury, pain,
anguish, despair, assassinations, and ethno-religious bloodshed without just
cause.
In the intermittent period never have we earned so much in hard currency
without anything tangible to show for it, and never did we have as many
qualified Nigerians in search of jobs without succour. We were promised
three million jobs through NEEDS and got the boot instead with a promise of
more to follow; They promised to fix NEPA within six months when they took
over in 1999 but a great majority are still in darkness despite the colossal
sums allegedly spent in hard currency; the Emperor junketed the globe in
search of foreign investments but could not even recover the loot allegedly
stolen by Abacha let alone to obtain debt relief; they sold the nation cheap
on the international scene in search of friends and allies and received
daggers in the back as exemplified by America's continued attempt to
destabilise the country, the Charles Taylor affair or even the attitude of
South Africa over the ambition of a Nigerian to assume the presidency of the
African Development Bank.
So, what do we garner from all these? We remain hostages to a very
terrible nightmare, which is scheduled to last another three years except
there is divine intervention. It is precisely why I grimaced in anguish when
the first pictures emerged from the Emperor's visit to the National
Assembly; an institution whose members his foulmouthed aide had previously
savaged as a bunch of nonentities. If our recent history is anything to go
by, the originators of the impeachment move are about to pay a painful price
for their docility and complacency.
For all we know, the EFCC and the other state agencies of coercion may
already be knocking on their doors. If there is one thing about the Emperor,
it is the manner in which he is easily predicted in the vindictive sense. He
may be poised to strike, once again unless the assembly realises that what
is fake goes beyond a symbolic visit to placate individual egos!
tempthist@yahoo.com

Original_Search_Title: Mobile Restaurants Flourish
Headline: Cameroon: Mobile Restaurants Flourish
Body_Text:
More women now sell cooked food in markets.
Although the economic crises of the 1990's came and went, its impact has not only been negative. At the Douala Central market, many young men and women have understood that instead of waiting for white collar jobs they should indulged in self-employed businesses. Reasons why some of them have opened shops where they do sell or move all day long from one corner of the market. In the course of carrying out their businesses, some of them find it difficult to close their shops to go and feed themselves either because they are afraid of losing customers, the restaurants are far-off, are too busy to eat or don't like the dishes offered in restaurants.
In order to provide a long and lasting solution to the problem, some women in the economic capital have created employment for themselves by moving and selling cooked food at the central market. Everyday as from midday, it is very difficult for one to move an inch without seeing the busy "Mobile restaurants women" with big basins on their heads, food flasks and plastic plates. Those who cook more than they can carry often solicit the aid of truck boys. Though there are many types of dishes that are prepared for sale, the most demanded combinations by customers are, rice and beans, groundnut soap and rice, pounded potatoes and beans, pounded coco yams with palm nut soup etc. The cost of each plate depends on the type, quantity and the accompanying factors such as fresh or dry meat and fish.
For better sales and due to their prominent presence, each of these women has sectors where a great number of her customers are located. Here, services are in two categories. The first category are those who pay weekly, while the second are those who pay from day-to-day. The difference between these two categories of customers is that, the first has a preference in service. He can be served on credit because there are weeks that he also pays in advanced. The second category popularly known in the market by the slogan "Rice na soup" are those who must pay before service. According to one of the sellers, Madam Nga, former worker in a Douala based Perfume Company, "Most people have now welcomed the idea of us cooking and selling food at their business sites. At first, it was difficult because some preferred bread, sardine, and fried eggs or brought food from their homes. With time and due to the quality and type of dishes we offer, many people now solicit us. Most of our customers at the central market like but traditional dishes. For now, the business is very flourishing and I don't think I can easily abandon it for a white collar job."

Original_Search_Title: Deaths On Highways: Is FRSC Sleeping?
Headline: Nigeria: Deaths On Highways: Is FRSC Sleeping?
Body_Text:
Lagos — The number of road accidents and resultant deaths on our roads are just alarming. Frank Kintum looks at what FRSC is doing (if any) to arrest this development and factors responsible for accidents
Lagos. At Alaka bus stop near the National stadium on May 6, at least 14 persons were confirmed dead when a mini commercial bus was crushed by a sewage lorry that lost control and ran to the other lane.
All the passengers in the bus except the bus conductor were killed by the obviously unhealthy tanker, which was believed to have taken off from Lagos Island. Cause of the malfunctioning of the tanker was not immediately ascertained.
Itori village, Ogun State. No fewer than 15 persons died May 2, while six others sustained serious injuries in a multiple auto crash. Eye witnesses say the incident involved an 18-seater passenger bus, a tipper lorry, and two cars.
Bauchi. April 30. Barely three weeks after a ghastly auto accident claimed the lives of 18 people along Bauchi-Jos road, Bauchi town again, lost seven persons to accident at the same spot.
Accounts say one of the vehicles, a Peugeot 505 station wagon used as a commercial vehicle was heading to Jos from Mubi in Adamawa state conveying eight passengers, while the other one, a Toyota Corolla, also used for commercial business was heading for Bauchi from Jos, loaded with boutique materials.
Lokoja, Kogi State. In three separate accidents on the Okene-Lokoja road, 15 persons were killed, including members of the Traditional Medical Practitioners Association who were said to be traveling from Lagos to Abuja for a conference.
Twenty-five persons including three female NYSC members were killed May 4 along the Akure-Owo road following an early morning accident involving an 18-seater bus and a truck conveying tubers of yams.
Reports say nobody survived from the accident which occurred about three kilometers from Akure, the capital city of Ondo, just as the accident left all the bodies of the victims seriously mangled.
From Ebonyi State comes the report that Chairman of Ngbo East Local Government Council, Mr. Emmanuel Eze Onwe, his driver and Leader of the council's Legislature, lost their lives in an accident at Ogbede, near Nsukka, Enugu State.
And one was killed and three others sustained serious injuries when some vehicles on the convoy of President Olusegun Obasanjo on a state visit to Ekiti, somersaulted about two kilometers to Afao Ekiti. These, and many others are pathetic stories about road accidents on our highways almost every day. And nothing seems to be done about arresting the situation before it gets out of hand.
On a general note there are so many factors responsible for road accidents, ranging from the driver of the vehicle, who in many cases is ill-trained, to the ill-maintained vehicles on the road to the state of our roads.
Lagos Sector Commander of the Federal Road Safety Commssion, (FRSC), Mr. Ayobami Omiyale in a chat with THISDAY Motoring, said inspite the criticisms on the commission, it is not folding its hands.
Though Omiyale agreed that "the recent statistics have indicated an upward trend in road traffic accidents, fatalities and injuries, but when you talk about fatality index, that is the number of deaths occurring as a result of road traffic accidents per 10,000 vehicles in circulation, there have been a systematic drop from 2001 to 2004. From 7.84 in 2001 to 6.8 in 2002 to 6.09 in 2003 and to 5.8 in 2004, that is for Lagos State.
"That tells you that the number of people dying is not commensurate to the rate of increase in vehicular population, there is a rate of increase in vehicular population, which is much more higher than the rate of increase in the number of people dying. That is how fatality index is calculated worldwide-that is either death per 10,000 vehicles in circulation or death per 100,000 population. Thereare a total of 6.2 million vehicles nation-wide and Lagos accounts for 1.5".
He gave specific factors responsible, for the soar in the number of road accidents to include "one the economic environment within which the vehicles are operated does not allow every aging vehicles to be replaced by new ones, an aging vehicle is being replaced by another aging vehicle, that is a tokunbo vehicle, which has been used outside the country, and coming in again with tokunbo tyres, they burst, for instance the accident that happened recently at Alaka, (Lagos), it was a tyre that came off a vehicle.
"The other factors are the roads which are not under the control of the FRSC, the third factor is the driver, the driver behaviour, which the FRSC, fortunately, is in a position to monitor, to regulate and to correct, it is the driver who over speeds, granted that the roads are bad, granted that the vehicle is old, the driver ought to know and regulate his speed, it is his behaviour that we try to control by the upward review of fines (road traffic offences fines)".
But how will fines control the behaviour of drivers?, Omiyale argued that when an offence is made prohibitive, a would-be offender realizes that, "even low as those old fines were, we still had people who were coming to plead, to beg that they would not be able to pay the fines, appealing for weavers."
But FRSC was one of those not in support of the high fines charged by the Lagos State Traffic Management Area (LASTMA). "If you look at the FRSC fines, we operate nation-wide, so FRSC would have taken into consideration the economic factors in all the sections of the society nation-wide. But apart from the upward review of fines we also organize public enlightenment, motor park rallies."
He acknowledged that there is a problem with the drivers licence scheme, as "we are trying to revive our discussions with the other parties in the tripartite arrangement, that we have with the Board of Internal Revenue and the VIOs. What we found out that contrary to what use to take place many years ago, drivers are no longer being tested before they are issued drivers licence, and that is a very dangerous trend, there is no amount of education that you want to give to some body who has not been trained, you also have the issue of the driving schools, no standard driving schools".
Meanwhile, some stakeholders in the motoring public have criticized the recent upward review of the FRSC fines as they say it would not help in reducing accidents.
Prominnent among them is Mr. Dave Ofem, who is an expert in road traffic management and a former Zonal Commanding Officer of the commission.
Ofem decried a situation whereby the FRSC is emphasing on the use of fines to punish erring drivers when the cardinal focus of driver orientation has been relegated to the background.
According to him, "driver training has not improved, yet they are increasing fines, certainly, Iam kicking against it, the fines are too high for motorists".
He is also worried that the FRSC has done little or nothing in tackling the recent re-occuring road accidents in the country, as "the traveling public is on the receiving end".
Ofem said a lot need to be done to stop the decay in the FRSC, as according to him, "the FRSC has compromised, all they do is go to the road and collect money, FRSC is not a revenue making body of the government".
Another frontline transporter, said the hike in the fines by FRSC was uncalled for, adding that the FSRC men are hardly seen on the roads.
However, Omiyale stated that the review in fines go hand-in-hand with other education programmes of the commission. In a recent upward review of FRSC traffic offences fines, offenders are now to pay between N1,000 to N50,000 fines depending on the nature of offence.

Original_Search_Title: Commuters Stranded As Police, Lorry Drivers Clash in Enugu
Headline: Nigeria: Commuters Stranded As Police, Lorry Drivers Clash in Enugu
Body_Text:
Awka — BUSINESS activities were paralysed for some hours yesterday on the Enugu-Onitsha express way, Onitsha following a clash between the Police and lorry drivers. Commuters were stranded as a result of the clash. Mostly affected were commuters returning from other parts of the country to Onitsha and Enugu.
They were held up for over ten hours as rampaging lorry drivers blocked the expressway at Umunya Junction, protesting what they called extortion by the police.
Trouble began when the police allegedly shot and punctured one of the front tyres of a Mercedes Benz lorry belonging to the protesting tipper drivers. The police team was one of the many police check-points on the about 40 kilometers Onitsha-Awka road.
Reason for the shooting was not exactly known, but, but the drivers alleged that the lorry was shot at because the driver refused to give to the police unspecified sum of money they demanded.
But the police have denied the allegation, claiming that they could only speak on the cause of the shooting after a thorough investigation had been concluded.
However, in a solidarity with their colleague, other lorry drivers plying the busy road blocked the road haulting traffick from both sides of the road. The drivers were said to have engaged the police men in a free for all fight.
Attempt to pacify the aggrieved drivers failed as they insisted that the police must pay for the cost of the burst tyre and other damage done the lorry. The lorry was said to be conveying yams from one of the northern states to Lagos when the incident occure
The State Police Command, Awka, was said to have despatched a team of senior police officers to the scene in a bid to bring the situation under control.
A police source told Vanguard that both the policeman who shot at the lorry and members of his team at that check-point have been taken to taken to Awka for interogation. The police were said to have provided the sum of N100,000 for the purchase of a new tyre before the irate lorry drivers opened the road.
The Chairman of the Anambra State branch of the Nigerian Lorry Drivers Association, Chief Rogers Uche-Nebuwa,explained that his colleagues went on the rampage because the policeman endangered the lives of the occupants of the lorry.
The Police Public Relations Officer for Anambra State Command, Mr. Kolapo Shofoluwe, confirmed the incident, but denied that it was over a demand for bribe.
"The policeman only shot the tyre. Nobody, not even a fly was injured. The incident had nothing to do with money because it is not possible to discuss money with a moving vehicle.
"The vehicle was still moving when the tyre was shot at. And that is why we brought both sides to the command's headquarters because the police have a story that is different from the one told by the lorry drivers, Shofoluwe said.

Original_Search_Title: A Conference in Pictures
Headline: Nigeria: A Conference in Pictures
Body_Text:
Lagos — Cartoonists' National Conference is the title of the exhibition. This
landmark event, which ushers in an important landmark in the history of the
profession, opened last Thursday at the International Press Centre in Ogba,
Lagos.
It is indeed the first of its kind, corroborates Chukwuemeka Emenike the
President of Cartoonists Association of Nigeria (CARTAN) in an interview
with this writer. "But it's not the first exhibition we are holding," he
says. "[This] is like a continuation [of the first exhibition]."
That exhibition, titled Democratic Satire, held from March 26 to 28,
2002. Its theme revolved around those famous capitalised words of the
Nigerian political lexicon...Transition and Democracy in Nigeria. It
featured mainly works done during the last days of the military rule. "That
exhibition, organised by the Lagos State government in collaboration with
Cartoonists Association of Nigeria, CARTAN, was used to celebrate our
relative freedom in a democracy by bringing [to the] fore 'the lessons of
the past, the travails of the present and the prospect[s] of the future,"
Emenike said in his opening address.
Hence the current exhibition seeks to re-examine this democratic
dispensation with a view to ascertaining whether or not it is working
well. In other words, it is a continuation of CARTAN's efforts at building
and consolidating the nation's nascent democracy. "You will agree with me
that if you are roasting a piece of yam and you allow it ... stay longer on
fire without turning it, it will definitely get burnt," Emenike adds.
The relevance of the Cartoonists' National Conference, according to
Victor Asowata a member of CARTAN, is its desire to drive home the point
about what lessons the country may have learnt. Cartoonists were equally as
militantly active as writers in the efforts aimed at dismantling the
military dictatorship. They consequently suffered as much as the other
pro-democracy activists in the hands of the state security operatives.
It is perhaps the thankless nature of the profession that has led to
many abandoning the cause. The association is giving the thumbs-up to those
who have kept the faith and have continued to plod on despite all
odds. Hence the exhibition also seeks to enhance the professional practices
of cartoonists in Nigeria. "So, its more or less an addition to the
contributions to the development of this profession," Asowata says.
CARTAN acknowledges the contribution of such great names in the industry
as 'Boye Gbenro, Sanya Ojikutu, Josy Ajiboye, Dele Jegede and Cliff
Ogiugo. Some among these names have extended their exploits beyond the
shores of this country.
Asowata speaks wistfully of state-of-the-art equipments in the more
advanced countries, which in his opinion give a shot in the arm to the
professional practice of Nigerian cartoonists. But Emenike alludes to the
more fundamental part of the problem, which borders on seriousness. There
are, for instance, 200 hundred schoolds in South Korea teaching cartoons
while there is none in Nigeria.
He should know. He participates in the country's annual programmes,
which take the form of cartoon festivals and conference. He even had an
honourable mention in one of these events. "Nigerians are beginning to like
cartoons but what will encourage the continuity of the profession is not
there," he says.
In addition, Emenike has participated in cartoon workshops outside the
country, one of which held last two years in Douala. The latter event was
organised by Cartoonists Rights Network International.
Emenike laments the fact that most of the cartoonists in Nigeria
graduated as graphic artists and painters. Hence he said in his opening at
address that "all cartoonists are artists but not all artists are
cartoonists."
The "Conference" is part of the multi-faceted action plan aimed at
sensitising the people. This is in addition to a planned push for the
inclusion of cartooning in the school curricula. Indeed, the association
plans more vigourous reach-out programmes after the exhibition. This will
include not only educational institutions but also public and private
corporate organisations.
A proper cartoon culture in the society, Emenike argues, is a foundation
for animation. This, he observes, has through the many programmes on the
television stations gripped the fancy of the younger generation.
He blames the poor comic culture on a number of factors, which stultify
its growth in the country. Publications generally -- and comic albums or
magazines are no exception -- are fraught with problems bordering on
finance, sponsorship and proper publicity.
"The Political Cartoonist is like a stationed searchlight," the CARTAN
President told the gathering. "The moment you appear in front of that
searchlight, do not blame it for recording your image, see yourself as being
cartoon friendly. As stakeholders in the Nigerian body politics,
accomplished cartoonists in Nigeria since [the] 1950s till datehave been
exhibiting their work[s] in a unique concept that would afford them a
platform for the continued critical contributions to the development of
Nigeria's democratic process."
Among the dignitaries at the event were Chief Segun Olusola (a one-time
Nigeria's ambassador to Ethiopia), Ted Iwere (of The Daily Independent) and
Lanre Aroguntade (the International Press Centre Co-ordinator).
The exhibits, produced by about a score of cartoonists, were done in
both monochrome and colour. They attest to not only the industry but also
the profundity of a growing number of cartoonists. Perhaps among the most
interesting were Kaycee's swipe at the Nigerian's peace-keeping efforts in
Sierra Leone. In the cartoon, a fire man stands on a fire truck pointing a
water hose on a burning building representing Sierra Leone while the house
behind labelled Warri him is burning. The same cartoonist also illustrates
his thoughts on how leaders are chosen in the West and in Africa. In the
former, a figure emerges like a genie from a ballot box while in the later a
rifle-wielding figure dressed in military uniform emerges from a heap of
skulls in the outline of the African continent. Another cartoonist, who
signs his name as Neearo, mocks the greed of Nigerian generals. He depicts
the generals in the United States and the European Union as being
preoccupied with updating their professional tactics while the Nigerian
general is interested in oil bunkering and "how to loot without tears."

Original_Search_Title: 25 Killed in Akure-Ondo Auto Crash
Headline: Nigeria: 25 Killed in Akure-Ondo Auto Crash
Body_Text:
Akure — Twenty-five persons including three female corps members yesterday died along Akure - Owo road following an early morning accident involving an 18-seater bus and a truck conveying tubers of yam.
Nobody survived in the accident which occurred about three kilometers from Akure, the state capital and left virtually all the bodies of the victims seriously mangled.
Eyewitness accounts of the incident revealed that the truck with registration number Plateau 736 QAP was on high speed when a herd of cows crossed the highway.
In a desperate attempt to avert the accident, the vehicle hit one of the cows which swerved the truck to hit the on-coming bus.
The bus with registration number Abuja XCT 619 RSH has about 19 passengers mostly women who all died instantly.
Before their death, the truck dragged the bus for about a few yards where it had hit the black cow (which also died) and finely rested its whole body on the bus.
A combined team of rescue officers made up of the officials of Federal Road Safety Corps, police and fire servicemen later arrived at the scene of the accident.
Sympathisers, who completely broke down, could not help as the bus was completely buried under the truck.
At 7.30am, the state Ministry of Works sent down a crane and three other heavy duty machines to remove the truck.
When bodies were being taken out one after the other, sympathisers who could not stand the ugly scene broke down completely while the younger ones threatened to unleash terror on the cow owners.
For over three hours, traffic stood still as no vehicle could move either way.
People have no choice but to join the wailing crowd and abused the cow owners.
The peak of the agony was when the charred bodies of the female corps members in their uniforms were retrieved but identifying them was simply impossible but men of the road safety corps took a census of the dead before loading them in two vehicles.
They were all deposited at the mortuary of the state Specialist Hospital in Akure around 10.00am.
Commenting, the Director of Fire Services, Mr. Idowu Fatoyinbo, said "we were informed immediately after the incident but because of dearth of equipment we had to seek assistance elsewhere."
"You can see that our people have tried their best. It is unfortunate that these Nigerians have to die this way," he said.
In his own reaction, FRSC Ondo State Sector Commander, Mr. Ahmed Hassan, confirmed the accident and advised drivers against recklessness.
Some of the sympathisers interviewed called for legislation against open "herding" of animals.
They said "if the country could continue to lose its human resources to accident, government must make laws against this practice."
Others advised the road safety corps to emphasise the need for commercial vehicles to always keep manifest of the passengers.

Original_Search_Title: Wada Nas: A Noiseless Departure
Headline: Nigeria: Wada Nas: A Noiseless Departure
Body_Text:
It is the nature of Nigerians to pay glowing tribute to a man they did not love while alive, at his funeral. As one 'Don Williams of Kaduna' sang recently on NTA Am express "give me my due while I am around, not when I am gone" Nigerians have a lot to learn from that hat-wearing young man from Kaduna south.
The news of the sudden death of Alhaji Wada Nas came to this writer as a rude shock. Nas came into national limelight after the death of Gen. Sani Abacha. Even when he was special duties minister under Abacha, Nas was not as popular as he became thereafter. There is no reader of Nigerian newspapers that did not know late Wada Nas.
Nas was the only one that defended the late Abacha till he himself breathed his last. Others dined with the late Abacha but swore that they never knew him (like Peter did to Jesus in the biblical great denial) after his death, Nas never wavered in his defense of his late master. He was a loyal friend to Abacha till his own death. He shared in Abacha's accolades and guilt. Many Nigerians who readily say to their benefactors when the road becomes rough (or is it famished?) bye-bye basket I have carried my apples, have a lot to learn from the late Nas.
The late Wada Nas meant different things to different people. While some dismissed him as a national clown, only useful in offering comic relief, others took him seriously. The Lagos Ibadan self-opinionated men who see everything through their own prisms nicknamed him 'Wada Noise'.
His newspaper commentaries, which were sometimes in-depth and incisive, were derided as views of motor-park commentator, never to be taken seriously. They considered themselves 'deep' analysts while the genre represented by Nas was superficial; their own analysis of issues were intellectual while Nas's was pedestrian. No Nigerian public affairs commentator had been derided by the southwest axis of the Nigerian press like the late Wada Nas. But when all had thought that Nas would chicken out; he would bounce back like a cat with nine lives. Nas was very consistent in his "noise" making which many considered very necessary. He never feared to swim in turbulent waters of controversy. The current occupants of Aso Rock would not forget Nas in a hurry because he was a one-man opposition in the absence of lack of one at the Dome (National Assembly). No one could say if Nas read his political barometer well before blowing any alarm on Aso Rock. He was always quick to alert the nation on the next move by Aso Rock. Some of his alarms were vindicated while others veered off mark. One way or the other, he kept the occupants of Aso Rock on their toes politically.
Many did not love him while he was alive but they could not ignore him. In recent history of Nigeria Nas remained the foremost former minister who commented on national issues through newspaper articles. His articles were compulsory read. He contributed to every discourse intelligently no matter what many might say.
Nas realized that democracy is too large and important a project to be left for the so-called professional politicians alone. Like this writer had argued elsewhere, that would amount to leaving one's yam in the care of a goat. Even a foolish man would not do that not to talk of the one who claims to have some grey matter. Nas realised this early and played his part within the ambit of his understanding of the political issues he commented on. His contributions would not be forgotten by many of his readers. .
His was a voice for the north specifically and the economically oppressed generally. Just before his death, he warmed his way into the heart of many southÂ­ easterners when he said he would support Igbo president come 2007. As always, his critics added this patriotic move to their list of scenes from the Nas 'comedy'.
Nigeria has lost a vital voice. A particularly necessary 'noise' has been taken away from our polity. It is sad that Nas did not tarry till maybe 2007. He quit the scene when he would have been needed most. May his soul rest in perfect peace.
Dr. Akhigbe write from Specialist Hospital Bauchi.

Original_Search_Title: UN Highlights Tide of Human Rights Abuse in Run-Up to Peace Summit
Headline: Cote d'Ivoire: Cote D Ivoire: UN Highlights Tide of Human Rights Abuse in Run-Up to Peace Summit
Body_Text:
Abidjan — The UN peacekeeping force in Cote d'Ivoire has published a detailed and scathing report on recent human rights abuses in Cote d'Ivoire as President Laurent Gbagbo prepares for a face-to-face meeting with rebel leader Guillaume Soro at fresh peace talks in South Africa on Sunday.
"Despite some encouraging improvements in the human rights situation observed during the months of January and February, the overall situation remained worrying," the report concluded.
It noted with alarm "an intensification of the activities of militias and armed groups responsible for acts of violence and revenge" in Abidjan, the de facto capital, and government-controlled areas of central and western Cote d'Ivoire.
Earlier this week, Human Rights Watch accused the Ivorian government of recently recruiting hundreds of demobilised combatants in Liberia, including child soldiers to join its militia forces in the West.
The New York-based organisation said the recruitment of Liberian mercenaries had intensified in March and it had interviewed 13 of them who said the process was being coordinated by a colonel and a sergeant of the Ivorian army.
The army issued a statement on Friday strongly denying this allegation.
The report by the human rights division of the UN Operation in Cote d'Ivoire (ONUCI) chronicled a series of human rights abuses committed during the first two months of this year.
These took place in the government-controlled south and the rebel-held north of the country, as well as the lawless buffer zone patrolled by UN and French peacekeepers that lies in between.
The report, published on Thursday night, cited a long list of extra-judicial killings and disappearances and instances of torture, beatings and extortion by gunmen supporting both sides in Cote d'Ivoire's three-year-old civil war.
It was issued as Gbagbo, Soro and the leaders of Cote d'Ivoire's main opposition parties prepared for a round-table meeting with South African President Thabo Mbeki in Pretoria.
Mbeki, who has been seeking to end the impasse in Cote d'Ivoire's peace process for the past five months, is the latest in a long line of international mediators to seek a negotiated solution to the conflict.
But there are few signs so far that he has made much progress.
Britain announced on Thursday that it was once more closing its embassy in Abidjan in anticipation of fresh trouble in the country. The Foreign Office in London urged the 150 or so remaining UK passport-holders in the country to leave.
British ambassador David Coates told IRIN that the decision was motivated by "a combination of rising insecurity, heightened political rhetoric and also the possibility that the political crisis is not near to being resolved which could result in violence."
The ONUCI human rights report said that in the government-run south of Cote d'Ivoire, "summary and extra-judicial executions and excessive use of force continue to be regularly perpetrated."
It highlighted the execution-style killing of 18 alleged "gangsters" by the security forces in the Abidjan suburb of Yopougon in broad daylight on January 18.
The rebel-held north is not much safer.
The report said at least four civilians were found summarily executed in rebel territory during the two-month period.
It accused the rebels of arbitrarily detaining civilians suspected of sympathising with or working for government forces.
Several detainees in rebel custody had 'disappeared,' while others had been subjected to torture in the homes of rebel leaders, notably at the Bouake residence of Mobido Drabo, a rebel commander known as "Mobio".
He died shortly afterwards in suspicious circumstances. The ONUCI human rights investigators said they had been unable to verify allegations that he had simply become the latest troublesome commander to suffer rough justice at the hands of the rebel leadership.
In the government zone as well as in the rebel-controlled north, "the climate of suspicion and fear of conspiracies [ ] have provoked arbitrary arrests and acts of intimidation targeting individuals suspected by the rebels to be a supporter or a sympathizer of 'the enemy'", the report said.
UN and French peacekeeping troops patrol the Zone of Confidence, which runs as a dividing belt through the centre of the country. But the report said this no-man's-land was equally cause for concern because "the judicial system and the administration do not function according to required norms, which leaves much room for impunity".
It said the Zone of Confidence had become infested with bands of robbers, who blocked roads with tree trunks and stripped passers-by of their money and possessions at gunpoint.
The report highlighted an "increase of cases of theft, racketeering and violence committed by military elements, bandits or militias against civilians who live in the confidence zone or who are crossing it."
The report said crime had risen sharply in Abidjan since 3,858 prisoners escaped from the city's main jail during anti-French rioting in early November. It noted that only 153 of the escaped prisoners had been recaptured.
Every day, Abidjan newspapers publish reports about burglaries, armed robberies and random killings. Many of the thugs appear to be carrying AK47 automatic rifles, the standard weapon used by all the armed groups in the Ivorian conflict.
The UN human rights division warned that militia activity had intensified since January and that armed groups were active in at least seven neighbourhoods of Abidjan, which hosts a quarter of Cote d'Ivoire's 16 million population.
According to the report, one such militia group, which guards a TV mast on the outskirts of the city, appeared to have been responsible for the murder of a mechanic from northern Cote d'Ivoire, whose body was found nearby.
The researchers highlighted dreadful living conditions in two severely overcrowded government prisons in the towns of Daloa and Bouafle in central Cote d'Ivoire.
In Daloa, which lies at the heart of an important cocoa-growing area 400 km northwest of Abidjan, the prisoners lived on one meal per day of rice or yam and were forced to sleep on the floor in unventilated cells, it said.
Conditions were so bad there that on average two prisoners died per week of diseases caused by malnutrition, untreated infections and AIDS, the report said.
It pointed out that the normal mortality rate in prisons was 0.9 percent for 10,000 people, while the mortality rate in Daloa was estimated to bet 6.2 per 10,000 "which is enormous".
Other worrying developments, highlighted by the report were continuing ethnic tensions in western Cote d'Ivoire, racketeering by security forces, which frequently extort money from passers by at roadblocks, and an attempt by Gbagbo to reassert full control over state radio and television. This technically comes under the authority of Soro, the rebel leader, who is Minister of Communications in Cote d'Ivoire's dysfunctional government of national reconciliation.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Bribery: Matters Arising
Headline: Nigeria: Bribery: Matters Arising
Body_Text:
Lagos — As usual, we want to start today with a matter of urgent public importance. Things are changing. The people of Kyrgyz Stan have proved a point - you do not need a gun to overthrow an unpopular government. For all those who are not familiar with Russian history, Kyrgyz Stan is one of the former 15 Soviet Republics with its capital at Bishkek. The president of the country, Askar Akayov has been in office for 15 years. Recently, he organized a sham election which extended his presidency for another term or perhaps, till thy kingdom come. I guess this man must have some African blood in him.
Last Thursday, March 42, 2005, opposition demonstrators, comprising students, market women, the employed and the unemployed youths besieged the Government House with sticks in hand, smashed doors and windows and anything that was on their way. They posed such a potent force that the more than 200 policemen on duty in Government House had to "borrow legs". They sacked government and took over. As I write now, there are no clear indications of the whereabouts of President Akayov. Some say he escaped through the back door and headed for Moscow. A new interim president has since started work. This serves as a big lesson to those addicted to the sit-tight syndrome. Those being persuaded to stay on should have the moral courage to say NO and quit the stage when there is still some ovation, the faintness of such ovation, notwithstanding.
Now to the issue of the day - that sordid affair at the Senate. The National Assembly has developed a new modus operandi, ruling by intimidation. Their grand design is to cow everybody to total submission so they can get away with murder. It is now becoming clearer why it took years together with a load of stubbornness on the part of the president to get the Senate confirmation of the nomination Prof. Osuji's predecessor in office, Prof. Aborishade. By extrapolation, Aborishade may have been a very smart professor but he is not a footballer. Then came the el-Rufai episode. For crying out that a very heavy bribe money (N54million) was being demanded for his confirmation, the Senate wanted his head. One is not even sure that they have left him alone up till now, going by the frequency of his invitations to the Senate committees these days.
The most direct one was the case of Hon Haruna Yerima who said it like it is. For speaking out, he was sentenced to one-month suspension. He is still serving the sentence. That's why it is often asserted that the majority is not always right but while the minority may have their say the majority will always have their way. It is now clear that Yerima's sentence should have been in the reverse: In a society where things are done properly, the majority of the House members would have gone on suspension while Yerima and the few in the minority would have gone free.
It is alleged that some members of the National Assembly are dusting up their records, trying to make impeachment moves against the president. What a smart move. But have we not said it often enough that it is not always smart to be smart? As the war drums are beginning to sound, some of these members are beginning to fear that the wind of corruption will soon blow them down. It is not really that they want the impeachment to succeed but national attention must be diverted from them. Do they ever realize that if they are able to prove that they had enough evidence to impeach the president ever before he blew the lid off their cups and did not do so, they are double offenders? If nothing else, they are engaged in a cover-up.
As a matter of fact, the battle line has been drawn. Since the President has promised the nation that the cleansing war which has just begun will rage in all fronts, if he now develops cold feet because of the threat of these members, some of whom are not very honourable, people may call him a coward and cowardise is certainly not the stuff of which presidents are made.
The president is also accused of selective enforcement. At the end of the N55 million saga Abia State would have lost greatly. It will also likely loose 66.6 percent of its representation in the Senate as the Senate president, Senator Wabara (Abia South Senatorial District) and Senator Chris Adighije (Abia Central Senatorial District) are at the very centre of the entire controversy. This argument soon collapses because in due season, their replacements will also be from Abia State. And assuming Wabara goes, even if his successor may not be an Abian, he will certainly be an Igbo man.
Wabara has no business running from pillar to post, running to Ohaneze Ndigbo, trying to whip up tribal sentiments on this issue. After all, Ndigbo did not send him to go and soil his hands in red oil. On his part, Dr Ezeife, a man that is respected the world over, argues that the offence for which Prof Osuji has been dismissed has been an accepted practice in our budgetary process. In other words, why should an Igbo man become a first victim? But, must it continue to be business as usual? Should this cleansing war not start from somewhere? Is this not a good plank to stand on and constantly remind Obasanjo that since he has promised an all out war on corruption, the same standard must be applied to all - even his friends in government? Let us have an open mind on some of these issues. Corruption in this country is so pervasive that if Obasanjo is able to punish all his enemies engaged in it, this nation shall be far removed from its Number Three position on the list of corrupt countries of the world. The punish-your-enemy approach to crime control is an emerging one. It can be further developed. If Obasanjo now punishes all his enemies who are engaged in crime and the next president is also able to punish all his enemies which may include Obasanjo's friends, then we are moving. On a very serious note, Gen Yakubu Gowon must quickly include in his programme NIGERIA PRAYS the prayer point that institutions like EFCC, ICPC and so on, must not become instruments of oppression in the hands of some people. To be truly meaningful, such institutions must remain truly independent.
The more things change, the more they remain the same. We are yet to see true patriotism. Even the petition from Hon Usman Bugaje which triggered off the whole N55 million saga is all about sharing. Just in the same way that sharing brings problems even within the ranks of armed robbers, this particular one started as a result of inequitable distribution of the loot. In a situation where the Secretary of the Senate Committee on Education, Ngozi Obichere was said to have pocketed N7 million, although she admitted receiving only N1 million from her Chairman, a case of the thief admitting stealing only one row of yams and you may as well assume that the entire barn is gone! In a situation where the Senate president, Senator Wabara was said to have pocketed N10 million; in a situation where Senator Mbata was said to have received N8 million... and you were squeezing N2000 into the hands of some senators and House members who fought election the same way you did and some times harder, what else do you expect? We do not know if Hon Bugaje would have blown any whistle if his own Ghana Must Go had contained some N5 million. In more organized societies, Bugaje would at best have qualified for plea-bargaining with the attendant reduced penalty instead of being made a hero in his own crime.
What is all this noise about begging Wabara to resign? Does he have a better choice? The only other viable option open to him is the prison yard. In a situation where Prof Osuji, the man they pushed into the scandal at the point of blackmail has since reached home after his dismissal with ignominy, what is Wabara still doing running around as a free man? Has he forgotten how he got to the Senate through the back door, through that contrived court order? What type of leadership does he hope to provide the Senate after all these revelations? More importantly, his continued stay in office is an insult on our collective sensibilities. Public officials must learn to be a bit principled. People in places of power and responsibility who choose not to pay the price of personal integrity merely succeed in shifting those costs to society. In our particular case, one price we will continue to pay is the widespread popular cynicism towards politics and the development of a political counter system that operates in the streets and in the popular press.
Some have argued that since the bribe givers and the bribe receivers are, in the main, PDP members, the PDP should not continue to wash its dirty linen in public. I think the PDP's reaction to this is better: Just don't allow the linen to get dirty. A dirty linen can be washed anywhere, even in the church. The important thing is that the entire drama is beginning to unfold properly: where are the Aborishades and the el-Rufais - the men who resisted temptation? They are still in the system. But where are the Osujis and the Wabaras, the men who fell for temptation? They are on their way out. The LCM of it all is that CRIME DOES NOT PAY.
Hon. Omorotionmwan is a public affairs analyst based in Benin City

Original_Search_Title: New look Wuse Market: Traders Decry Poor Sales
Headline: Nigeria: New look Wuse Market: Traders Decry Poor Sales
Body_Text:
When the Abuja Environmental Protection Board (AEPB) embarked on the
demolition and removal of illegal structures and containers in the Wuse
Market most of the traders there believed the exercise would spell nothing
more than doom for the market. Today, as Anas Galadima of the Business Desk
reports, however, many of the traders appreciate the fact that the market
has been tided up to acceptable standard although they are doomed with poor
sales.
Aliyu Amte, 28, is a trader at Wuse market, Abuja. He used to sell food
stuff like rice beans, yams, Irish potatoes and dry fish in his container
shop at the market. Business was good for Aliyu even though, sometimes, like
in every business, he did record some losses. He was optimistic that the
future of his business was bright.
But on that fateful Wednesday night, officials of the Abuja
Environmental Protection Board (AEPB) accompanied by about two thousand
policemen stormed the market, taking strategic positions in preparation for
a two-week demolition exercise of illegal structures like containers and
wooden shops. Aliyu's shop was affected. It was demolished entirely. He lost
all his stock in that exercise. Since that fateful day, life has not been
the same for him. But life must go on. Aliyu tries to look ahead. He has
since stopped thinking about the past. Aliyu now has a new trade. He sells
Okrika (used clothes).
Unlike in the past when he had a shop in which he sold his wares, now
Aliyu hawks around trying to sell the clothes to low income earners. "It's
unfortunate," he said. "I used to sell food stuff in a container shop but
because of the demolition which affected me, I lost my shop and goods. I
cannot stay idle because I have to work to eat. That is why I decided to
hawk these okrika just to manage. Even at that, I try to avoid being
arrested by the police because when they nab you, you pay N6,000 to be free
or else they will lock you up. So if I see the police I just run."
He compared his gains now and before his container was demolished and
remarked. "What I make now" he added, "is not even up to the 10 per cent of
what I used to make in the past."
The story is not any different from that of Sunday Ape, a young man who
sells window blinds. "Times are hard now because our sales figures have
dropped. We don't get many customers and as you can see (pointing towards
the gate), car owners are required to pay between N50 to N300 before they
enter the market. This discourages some people. Only the rich can pay that
amount every time without grumbling. And these rich people are not many so,
as a result, we are not making what we used to make before. I don't know
whether the situation will change in future. But for now, we don't record
much sales compared to what we used to make before the demolition.
Sunday, however, said he liked Wuse market's new environment now because
it is less congested and it looks more beautiful. "But I am more concerned
about my profit which has dropped. Or am I going to eat the beauty of the
environment?," he querried.
Malam Abdullahi, who is a tailor, also complains that his daily income
has dropped drastically since the demolition took place some weeks ago,
forcing him to relocate to another part of the market. "We don't make much
money these days," he complains, "this is because we have been displaced and
our usual customers do not know where to find us. Even those who have
permanent shops who were not affected by the demolition still don't record
much sales because they too have lost a lot of customers.
"The number of people that come to this market these days has
reduced. I'm sure they believe that if they come to the market they will not
find the traders they are used to. And you know that a buyer prefers to by
from a usual trader."
Even the traders who were not directly affected by the demolition
exercise seem to be negatively disposed to the demolition.
Ironically, however, some traders seem to like what the officials of the
AEPB have done in the market.
Franklin Nwankwo sells curtains and table spreads. "I like the
demolition," he said, "although it affected me negatively, but with the
whole parking space now even a pregnant woman can walk in and move freely
without experiencing any discomfort from the kind of crowd that this market
was known for in the past."
Franklin, however, admits that he, like the other traders, does not
record high sales like he used to in the past. "I used to make more profit
before demolition because, that time, we had a lot of customers who bought
from us and the rent we used to pay for our shops was less. But since the
demolition took place, there has been an increase in the demand for shops so
the prices have gone very high and if you try to increase the prices of your
goods the customers will run away."
Franklin, though not happy about the drop in sales figures, believes
that the environment should be clean and decent. He believes that people
should be able to pass freely without the usual congestion characteristic of
the pre-demolition Wuse market.
Franklin is not the only one who holds this opinion. Ikenna Obi, another
trader who sells clothes, also believes that the market should look
decent. "In the past," explained Ikenna, "big men hardly enter this
market. But now they do come in to buy things because the environment is not
as rowdy and congested as it was.
"So I like the way the market looks now. The environment is neat and
anybody coming from any part of the world can come in and shop in this
market. This is not how it was before the demolition."
Ikenna believes that Abuja is the only city that Nigeria can present as
a pride to other counties. So it should not be allowed to turn into another
Lagos.
When confronted for his comments, Segun Ayodele who sells textile
materials, reacted furiously. "Why should any normal human being say he
likes what has happened in this market"? "What do they like about the
demolition?" he queried.
Segun believes that the affected traders were put in a pathetic state
but they later even became better of. This, according to him, is because
some of the traders who were affected are retailers who buy in bulk from
wholesalers within the market. These whole sellers, he said, no longer get
much patronage due to the fact that their customers, who are retailers most
of whom owned container shops that were demolished, are no longer in
business.
Cyril Igwe, is one of such wholesalers who used to sell to container
owing retailers who in turn sell to consumers.
"The demolition has affected my business indirectly," said Cyril, who
sells consumer goods like toilletteries, food drink biscuit and chewing
gum.
"Although my shop is a legal structure and it was not demolished, my
sales record has dropped. As a wholesaler, most of my customers are
retailers within this same market who used to come and buy from me to
re-sell to consumers.
"Now, because of this demolition, most of the retailers who used to buy
from me have been chased out and their shops demolished. So my sales
turnover has reduced drastically."
While many of the traders have different stories to tell, others simply
chose to remain silent about the current market situation. They probably
believe that it is fruitless talking about it since there is very little
they can do about the current situation. Michael Bolaji, observed, "there is
no need to cry over a spilled milk, "what has happened has happened and
people think it is over. But we in the market know that it is not over
because we are still trailed by the after effects of the demolition.
"Before the demolition exercise took place," Michael, who sells
traditional women attires explains, "I used to make a lot of money. I sell
Ashoke and Buba some times I make up to N250,000 in one day. But today, as I
am talking to you right now, the time is already 3:30 p.m. the day is
gradually coming to an en and I have not made more than N25,000. So far,
only three people out of the numerous people that came into my shop today,
bought something from me.
"Most of my customers don't know that I arm here. My former shop was a
container which has been destroyed. This shop I use now, as you can see, is
a half-shop which was rented out to me by the owner. I paid N200,000 for the
shop. If the market situation continues like this how can I make my profit"?
he queried pathetically.
When confronted with the question that N25,000 in a day is not a bad
sales figure compared to that of other traders who make far less than this
amount, Michael was quick to point out that "the N25,000 is not for the
profit. That is just the overall turnover. Out of that my profit will not be
more than N2,000."
"You see the profit level is very low because we have to do it like that
to attract customers. If you want to make much money from every item you
sell your prices will have to be high but that will scare your customers
away. Competition is tight so you have to bring prices down."
Michael believes that there are over 30 people that sell the same things
he sells. These people, he believes are probably experiencing the same
changes..
In the light of low turnover and profit in the post-demolition Wuse
market, what is the way forward? Malam Musa Jibiya believes that things will
change with time.
He opined that most traders have been displaced and the market has a
different look so customers find it hard to locate the usual traders from
whom they buy their needed commodities that is why they don't come to the
market, in large numbers like they used to.
But Joseph Bala, who sells bottled water which has a contrary
belief. According to him, "things may never be the same again because, since
the demolition exercise, there has been a multiplication of markets within
the Abuja city centre has reduced the influx of people to the Wuse
market.
"But I may be wrong," he was quick to add. "There are millions of people
in Abuja. So with time, things may come back to normal. I pray for things to
come back to normal because, my brother, 'things no easy."

Original_Search_Title: 'Language Difficulty Or Not, Germany is Important'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Language Difficulty Or Not, Germany is Important'
Body_Text:
Lagos —  Nigeria and the rest of the world started yesterday to exhibit their
cultural and tourism potentials at the world's largest tourism exchange, ITB
Berlin in Germany. It ends March 15. They would meet investors, learn more
about new technology, new hotels, new services and special offers. Chief
John Olu Faoseke, author and a photo journalist who was in Germany for that
travel market a few years ago, relives his experience to Justina Okpanku
"I have traveled extensively and I can claim that one place is more
important than another. As a photo journalist , my job took me to so many
places. I have been to some African countries. I have also been to the
Philippines, been to England, been to Russia, Hong Kong, India and
more.
In all my trips the most interesting place I have visited is the United
States of America. It's the home of photography. That's the reason freelance
journalists in the US live on photography.
In fact, I have just written a book titled" Practice of Photo
Journalism" after my visit to the US. That country is a photographer's
delight, thanks to its former US Presidents J.F. Kennedy and Abraham Lincoln
who popularised use of visual communication in America. You will enjoy
photography there. You take photograph and nobody disturbs you; you enjoy it
at the airport, at the marketplace and wherever you go.
It's unlike Nigeria where you have to be careful as you are not allowed
to take photographs even at the airport. I remember the Police seizing my
camera for taking photograph in a public place.
(It's good you are talking to me as I may not be able to put my travel
experiences in a book. Thank you very much.).
I have experienced a lot as a frequent traveler. In India, is it
Calcutta? Oh no! You have flies all over the place that you cannot stop some
from going in if you open your mouth too wide (laughter).
I like the way Hong Kong manage their environment. They have small land,
it's about one-tenth the size of Lagos metropolis. Still, they don't stretch
their infrastructure as we do in Nigeria. They obey traffic code and traffic
flows freely. Their markets, blocks of storey buildings, are not rowdy. It's
a tourist delight.
I'm a frequent traveler to Germany. I have been there four or five times
over a short period. I remember my first visit was in the early 80s as a
tourist. I had traveled to Cologne and Dusseldorf. That time I traveled to
London before connecting flight to Germany.
I now fly Lufthansa, the German airline from Lagos since I realised a
direct flight is available. I still remember my first flight on Lufthansa to
Cologne; I enjoyed every minute of the six hours or so flight. I was
comfortable because of the airline's good service and in-flight
entertainment. Lufthansa service is superb. Well, I'm not trying to promote
the airline here. It's just that they make you feel comfortable, especially
when you want to sleep and when they are about to land. They have gone into
automatic landing a long time ago.
You will not know when the plane is landing until it touched down, even
when the weather is bad.
However, each time I travelled to Germany I experienced language
difficulty. It's not easy for non-German speaking persons to visit Germany
because of the language problem. You either communicate using sign language
(pantomime) or write something on paper and show to people. You might be
lucky to find someone who understands English Language to help out.
It's difficult except at the airport or ITB fairground where a lot of
people speak both English and German.
The main problem for immigrants is exchange deal. You will not change
your money inside the city without reference to the German police. The
bureau de change operators would normally refer customers to the police for
interrogation before any transactions are carried out. If you don't have a
valid record or strong proof the police will seize your money and say they
will contact the authorities in your country.
I was a trade visitor to the Berlin tourism exchange in Germany a few
years ago where I was harassed over currency exchange. I was on the
government delegation from Nigeria, so our estacode was tied together. The
problem started when I wanted to change some money which was given to me by
the leader of the delegation. Unknown to me, I required the master list to
show as a proof.
In my case they said they would contact the Central Bank of Nigeria to
verify if the money found with me actually belongs to me. Our Nigeria-based
German guide was almost running into trouble until the head of the
delegation was contacted before the police was satisfied.
It's better you change your dollar to Euro in Nigeria before traveling
to Germany. However, you may not face any problem if you change your money
at any German airport.
Nevertheless, the Germans are hospitable. I realised this when I went to
Dusseldorf for business recently. I was new in the area I was staying, I
didn't know the place much as everywhere was the same. Unfortunately, I
missed my way when I went out one evening. I was stranded. But a young lady
who may have noticed my confusion was very helpful (I feel bad that I forgot
to take her name). But I was advised to write the name of the place I'm
coming from and where I am going as a reminder. Travelers, especially first
time visitors write the name of the place they are going and coming from in
a paper and keep it inside their pocket.
If you are in a bus or in a tram (train), which is the main means of
transport in Germany, you see people looking at their papers. Transport and
everything else is smooth.
What I eat while in Germany is what they call 'dog food'. It looks like
our doughnut, but it's prepared with meat and served with plenty of
vegetables. It's the common food for immigrants because if you eat one or
two you will be okay, as if you've eaten pounded yam with vegetable soup."

Original_Search_Title: Floods Wreak Havoc in Tamale
Headline: Ghana: Tamale Hit By Disaster
Body_Text:
Tamale —  Floods wreak havoc
DISASTER STRUCK the Tamale metropolis on Tuesday March 8, this year as
two of its suburbs were severely flooded by a heavy downpour.
The over two-and-a-half hour heavy rainfall amidst lightning and peals
of thunder, happened to be the third, since the commencement of the dry
season.
The two communities, Kobilmahagu and Kukuo, which were apparently new
developing areas in the metropolis, were said to have experienced similar
disaster two times within a year.
Almost all the completed and uncompleted buildings were indiscriminately
washed down by the windy-rain. About 200 complete houses were razed to their
foundations, whilst others were partly.
Property and food items worth billions of cedis were destroyed. Animals
and foodstuffs like rice, maize, wheat, groundnut, beans, yam and others
were also affected. A large number of animals, which died in the rain, was
butchered forcedly for meals to avoid bad smells.
Meanwhile, no death was recorded, but one man, who, according to
eyewitnesses, was trying to prevent the heavy wind from ripping off his roof
(hanging under the roof and pulling it down) was taken away by the wind
alongside the whole roof of his house, sustaining a knee injury. Newly
wedded couples, who were enjoying their honeymoon, could not fulfill the
traditional rite. The wind exposed them by taking off their roof and all
their expensive properties were destroyed.
The few trees in the area have been wiped off by the wind. Mr. Salifu
Abass, President of the Northern Regional League of Youth Association, also
lost his almost-completed seven-bedroom house.
Speaking to newsmen after going round to console the victims, the
Northern Regional NADMO Boss, Alhaji Mohammed Alhassan assured the affected
people of NADMOs immediate assistance.
He was apprehensive about giving a total cost of the properties and the
amount that would be needed to assist the people to rebuild their houses,
saying, "NADMO needs to meet the people and gather everything before we can
come out with the amount that will be involved."
Alhaji Alhassan noted that, the houses were severely affected by the
rain as the result of substandard building materials used by the
people.
He said the people had known clearly that the place was a marshy area,
which called for better building foundations, but most of them did not lay
foundations before putting up their buildings.
He accused the Assembly of not pursuing human-centered policies that
might prevent or avoid some artificial disasters. The NADMO Boss expressed
regret over how the Tamale Metropolitan Assembly had turned deaf ears to
ensuring proper settlement of the area.
He reechoed that; the NADMO department had, on many occasions called on
the TMA to cooperate with the disaster management organization to ensure
defensive measures to avoid some man-made disasters in the area, yet no
response came.
He, however, advised the Assembly to allocate a little from its budget
to assist NADMO educate the public on disaster management.
The victims, in an interview with The Chronicle, appealed to the
government to come to their aid without any much delay, since where to place
even their heads and their few undestroyed properties, had been a problem
for them.

Original_Search_Title: 2007: Idoma And Issues in Benue Governorship
Headline: Nigeria: 2007: Idoma And Issues in Benue Governorship
Body_Text:
Lagos — Correspondent Ogbu Onah in this analysis, takes a critical look at the intrigues and moves of major ethnic groups and notable politicians in Benue State over who succeeds the incumbent governor of the state, Chief George Akume.
Before the 2003 governorship elections in Benue State, precisely in 2001, prominent politicians from Idomaland elevated the campaign that the state's top office should be zoned to the area to an olympian height.
Governor George Akume, the incumbent governor felt ruffled by the campaign and did everything to puncture it. But that did not make the people to give up the clamour. However, they could not have their way at the end of the day. And Akume was re-elected for a second term in Benue State.
In reality, the Idoma have always accused their Tiv neighbours of marginalizing them especially in the areas of provision of infrastructure and the governorship of the state. These issues have always formed the strongest reasons for Idoma agitation for the creation of Apa State.
Since the creation of Benue State in 1976, the Tiv have produced all the civilian governors including late Mr. Aper Aku, 1979 and 1983; Rev. Father Moses Adasu, 1991 to 1993 as well as Mr. Akume, 1999 to date. The Tiv also had the privilege of producing a military governor for the state in the person of retired Brig. General Atom Kpera in 1983 and 1984 under the leadership of General Muhammadu Buhari (rtd).
Some Idoma politicians felt in 2003 that should Akume succeeded in securing a second term, it would stretch further their being on the ring side.
Some of these politicians were egged on the more by the apparent image problem of governor Akume. Apart from the fact that the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) under whose platform he rode into power in 1999 had become split into two factions, the governor was accused of non-performance by both the factional members of the ruling party and the opposition political parties in the state.
The factions that emerged in 2001 were those that were pro-Governor Akume himself and those loyal to Chief Barnabas Gemade, who was then the national chairman of the party. The thinking among Idoma politicians was that if Akume was going to be shoved aside in 2003 as was popularly envisaged, the obvious choice would be an Idoma candidate.
Moreover, in 2001 there was a face-off between the Federal Government and Benue State Government over the controversial sale of the Benue Cement Company (BCC) Plc to Alhaji Aliko Dangote whom the state accused of not being a core investor in cement business.
There was a strong rumour that for daring to challenge President Olusegun Obasanjo and his vice, Alhaji Atiku Abubakar over BCC, the Federal Government was going to plot the removal of Akume in 2003.
It was envisaged that the Federal Government, supported by the Chief Gemade faction in the state, would use the party primaries to choose another PDP candidate for the 2003 gubernatorial election. But Akume became smarter that them all and did not allow that to happen.
Moreover, Chief Gemade had fallen out of favour with the president and was on his way out as the PDP's national chairman, leaving only the incumbent governor (Mr. Akume) as the president's only linkman to winning his own re-election in 2003 in the state.
Akume was not only suave in that area, he consequently used the instruments of state power to beat the state PDP leadership into line. The expected state gubernatorial primaries could not hold any longer as the governor caused the state PDP leadership to summon an emergency congress in Makurdi where he was unanimously adopted as the party's sole flagbearer for the 2003 gubernatorial race.
The congress was held few days to the 2001 national convention of the PDP at Abuja where Chief Gemade was voted out and replaced by Chief Audu Ogbe, an Idomaman.
Even at that time, Governor Akume was not without support from Idomaland for a second term for an obvious reason. Senator David Mark was one of such persons who rooted for his second term.
According to him, it was morally incumbent on the Idoma to support the governor for a second term so that in 2007 he too would support Idoma.
"Every Idoma man should support Akume for a second term because we might need him to support us in 2007 when it will be our turn.
"Moreover, in the future it will also be the right of Idoma to ask for a second term", he told Idoma politicians at a function in Otukpo.
Senator Mark's position did not go down well with some Idoma politicians who accused him of selling out to the Tiv. They also accused him of pursuing a personal agenda that was not in line with that of the generality of Idoma.
For that reason, he was deserted and even labelled a traitor of the Idoma cause. Prominent PDP members from the area dumped the party for the newly registered United Nigeria Peoples Party (UNPP).
They include retired Generals Geoffrey Ejiga and Lawrence Onoja, Chief Baba Odangla and a host of other minor politicians.
They hoped that their leaving the PDP would ensure the nomination of an Idoma for the first time in the new party to contest with Akume. General Onoja who was fielded to contest the UNPP's gubernatorial primaries, however, lost to a Tiv man from Gboko Local Government, Mr. Mike Mku.
With that hope dashed some of them including General Onoja himself ran back to PDP apparently to wait for the end of the second term of Akume if the party would still honour its zoning principle come 2007.
Already the All Nigeria Peoples Party (ANPP) had earlier concluded its nomination of another Tiv man, Chief Paul Unongo, as its gubernatorial flag bearer foreclosing any gambling again of Idoma aspirants on the platform of that party.
However, after the general election of 2003, the issue of Idoma governorship receded into the back burner of political discourse. More than one year after Akume secured his second term, the Idoma politicians kept sealed lips on the agitation for the governorship of the state.
When asked why the Idoma people were not talking about the governorship again, the Chairman of Okpokwu Local Government and a notable gubernatorial aspirant in the area told reporters last year that the time was not ripe to talk about election for 2007.
"The governor, Mr. George Akume, has just won his second term. It is too early to talk about election of 2007. The Idoma people believe that you don't talk about crossing a river until you get to its mouth".
But the Internal Affairs Minister Dr. Iyorchia Ayu, did not allow the Idoma to get to the "mouth of the river" before he broached the subject of zoning the governorship to Idoma in 2007.
Dr. Ayu, while responding to a reporter's question on the alleged gentleman's agreement between Idoma and Tiv that the former would produce the next governor, he said the PDP still believe in zoning. He was also reminded that in spite of the so-called gentleman's agreement, some Tiv politicians from zone A were already angling to succeed Akume.
His response: "Let me explain something. When we formed the PDP in 1998, I was one of the first five founding members then. We decided that power should shift to the south. But that did not stop people like Alhaji Abubakar Rimi from campaigning for presidency.
"You don't expect that people will not signify interest, but when the time comes, the party leadership in the state would decide on who their candidate would be. So let us not start speculating who will succeed Akume", he said.
That interview generated some controversy especially when one of the reporters came out with a sensational headline in a national daily (not Daily Champion) to the effect that PDP has zoned 2007 governorship to Idoma. The Tiv from zone A who believe it was their turn after Akume started hacking Ayu down on his alleged delivery of governorship to Idoma in 2007, seemingly on a platter of gold.
Former member of the Revenue Mobilization Al__cpLocation and Fiscal Commission and PDP chieftain in the state, Chief Abu King Shuluwa was one of those opposed to automatic zoning of governorship to Idoma.
A gubernatorial aspirant himself, Shuluwa maintained that the governorship seat would be thrown open to zones A and C in 2007 adding that Idoma should forget securing the position on a platter of gold.
According to him, just like in the previous gubernatorial elections in the state, any zone that produces a good and acceptable candidate for the race would be given the mandate to run for the PDP.
Said he: "Idoma should stop hoping that governorship would be given to them on a platter of gold. In 1979, the governorship seat was thrown open which produced an acceptable candidate in the person of late Mr. Aper Aku. In the Third Republic, governorship was thrown open and Rev. Father Moses Adasu emerged as governor. The same thing happened during Akume.
"If in 2007 the Idoma produce an acceptable candidate for the party, there is no stopping them. But I am not aware of any gentleman agreement between Idoma and Tiv on governorship zoning. If there was such an agreement, I should know. Maybe Dr. Ayu was airing his personal opinion", he said.
However, in Idoma area, the interview with Ayu ignited a flurry of political activities as those with hidden gubernatorial ambitions began to come out openly for recognition. Some of them who had personal relationship with Ayu and governor Akume began moves to secure their favour in anticipation of 2007.
It also opened gates for politicians to tout names of people they hope would be able to make the race when the time comes. Already, on ground are traditional names of aspirants including the serious and those on the periphery.
The role call include, Senator David Mark, Chief Steve Lawani, Comrade Aba Moro, Mr. Jerry Agada, Hon. Ralph Igbago, Hon. Benson Abonu and retired General Lawrence Onoja.
However, only two of this crowd appeared prominently in public discourse last year. They are the state chairman of ALGON, Comrade Aba Moro and Chief Lawani.
Comrade Moro did not need to wait to get to the mouth of the river again after Dr. Ayu blew the whistle of PDP zoning. And because of his closeness to governor Akume and his (Akume's) political confidant and ALGON national president, Mr. Baver Dzeremo, he (Moro) was already touted by public opinion last year to be the heir-apparent to Akume.
The state ALGON chairman, who is indeed loved by all segments of Benue people because of his liberal mind, went about his ambition with some level of zeal. He became the subject of attraction and invitation to religious, social, political and cultural functions where he was always made chairman.
He did not disappoint, as he was everywhere donating to churches, orphanage homes, student associations and community development associations. In his giving, there was no consideration for tongue or tribe. He suddenly acquired the appellation of the man of the people and an Excellency in waiting.
But his encumbrance was Dr. Ayu who was said to be in love with the candidature of Chief Lawani who is believed to be very wealthy. Being in partisan politics longer than Moro, Lawani was said to have been in constant touch with his old political team mate, Dr. Ayu at Abuja.
Moreover, Ayu was said not only to prefer an Idoma man who would ensure the protection of his interest in Government House, Makurdi, but had apparent disdain for Moro being his governor.
Ayu and Lawani are said to share a common trait which is elitism and are so choosy of people they want to move with. Already people have predicted that the gubernatorial ambition of Moro would hit the rocks in the presence of Ayu.
During the burial and funeral ceremony of his late mother, Mama Tabitha in October last year, the Internal Affairs Minister appointed Chief Lawani as the chairman, Central Planning Committee. Over a hundred million naira was said to have been disbursed through him for the funeral. He did the job of organization so well that he got public commendation from the chief mourner.
However, in fairness to Comrade Moro he too, did his best at the funeral, which was attended by who is who in the PDP nation-wide including the Vice-president Alhaji Abubarkar Atiku. He did not only make financial contributions from his local government, but also physically mobilized his people with well refined Otukpa palm-wine for the guests at Ayu's residence in Gboko.
It is generally agreed that because of the nature of democracy and the electoral process in the country, Dr. Ayu and governor Akume would hold the political yam and the knife in the state. But will the two be able to sway the minds of majority Tiv people who form over 80 per cent of the states population towards yielding the governorship to the Idoma in 2007? An answer to this question is tied to time.
Apart from the issue of ethnic population, the geo-political composition of Benue State is quite interesting.
