While the Tiv with two senatorial districts otherwise called zones A and B, are homogeneous, zone C which comprises Idoma is not homogeneous. But the major ethnic group in zone C is Idoma followed by the Igede who produced the deputy governor in 1999 and 2003.
Apart from the fact that Idoma politicians have in recent times been engaged in the politics of accommodation with Igede, the relationship between the two have been like that of the cat and mouse. It also had in the past provided easy entrance of Tiv into the zone.
The Tiv have always shown sympathy for the Igede because of their minority status and marginalization within the zone. It explains why the current deputy governor, Prince Ogiri Ajene from Igede was elected again even for a second term.
Even in the current agitation for power shift to zone C, the Tiv have expressed their willingness to give the governorship to Igede people if at all they'll agree for power to shift in 2007.
The governor, Mr. Akume, himself gave an indication to that sentiment in an Airport encounter with the press in Makurdi last year. After seeing his visitor, Chief Lucky Igbinedion of Edo State, off at the Makurdi airport, one of the Idoma journalists stepped forward to ask the governor some questions and the following encounter ensued:
Journalist: "Sir, in the matter of Idoma governorship in 2007 you are like God.
Governor: (Laughs) "I am not God. I can't be God.
Journalist: "But in this matter you have the yam and the knife".
Governor: (Laughs)
Journalist: "Will you give governorship to Idoma in 2007?
Governor: "Are you talking of governorship to zone C or Idoma". (His deputy was standing beside him during the encounter).
But the man who attempted to drill a final nail into Idoma governorship ambition for 2007 was controversial Chief Godwin Daboh. In a press statement issued to reporters in Makurdi last year he had told the Idoma politicians to bury their dream of becoming governor in 2007. Rather he suggested 2025 as their year of hope.
This suggestion angered a group in Idoma called Idoma Redemption Movement (IREM). In its blistering reaction, the organization called Dabo names that were unprintable. In its special press release signed by its Director of Strategy, Chief Anebi Oko, IREM assured Chief Dabo that the Idoma race would no longer tolerate "empty talkative, ranting of his kind.
"Henceforth Dabo and his cabal should be warned and ready to face very serious challenges from Idoma soldiers of redemption. It will now be fire for fire in terms of intellectual, political and physical deployment of resources", the group said.
Daily Champion gathered that most of the prominent politicians in Tiv have not overtly or covertly supported the shift of power to Idoma in 2007. When confronted most of them, especially from the PDP, re-echo their acceptance of the principle of zoning but without emphatically agreeing on power shift.
For instance, in January this year, Chief Barnabas Gemade had cause to give an inkling into how feels about that to the press. When reporters asked him in Makurdi, if he still holds on to his position of Idoma governorship in 2007, he became evasive in his response.
"Well as a politician I believe in the principle of zoning. Zoning is a good thing". He did not go further.
Generally there appears to be sentiment among Tiv against leasing power to Idoma in 2007. This sentiment may not be far from the fall out from the events of the Third Republic when Rev. Father Moses Adasu was governor of the state. It was the period when retired General Ibrahim Babangida was using the politics of state creation to weaken opposition against his policies.
The Idoma people who were highly represented then in the military had totally hoped to get Apa state created for them out of Benue. They accused the Tiv people of marginalizing them, especially the administration of Father Adasu, in the areas of political appointments and siting of industries.
But in their letter of demand for the creation of Apa state, they were said to have blackmailed the Tiv. Unfortunately for them, eagle-eyed Tiv politicians allegedly intercepted and copied the letter whose content they were said to be much angry with.
The anger against that letter spilled over to the Tiv dominated State House of Assembly. The crisis in the House led to the walking out of Idoma members and the pronouncement of their excision from Benue.
The cold war and animosity generated by that incident between the two groups did not abate until the politics of the Fourth Republic in 1998.
For the very first time, the Tiv and Idoma came under the same political party, the PDP. Hitherto, Idoma had always been in the minority party both at the state and federal levels, which compounded their predicament in the sharing of political offices and infrastructure.
Consequently, the sharing of political offices under the PDP government of Akume became very smooth as the Idoma have not had it so good. Even the decision to endorse the candidature of governor, Akume was taken in Senator Mark's residence at Otukpo.
Though Mark is believed to have fallen out of favour with Akume and Ayu, there is no denying that he along with the latter propounded the principle of the new Benue spirit which translated to 'eat and let your brother eat'.
There was a political development in Tiv last year which would have made the emergence of an Idoma governor in 2007 a reality. It was no other than the crisis of annulment of the local government election in Kwande believed to have been won by the ANPP candidate.
The crisis that followed resulted in the massive destruction of lives and property in zone A. Mr. Akume and Dr. Ayu who were alleged to have master-minded the annulment to spite Mr. Paul Unongo who come from zone B. The battle line was almost drawn between the people of zones A and B.
The thinking was that if governor Akume could frustrate the election of a local government chairman from the zone where most of his critics came from, it would be suicidal for him to give the same zone the governorship.
However, following recent fence-mending moves by a Tiv socio-cultural group called MZ0 U TIV between Akume and his erstwhile political enemies from Tiv, the possibility of zones A reaching some kind of agreement with zone B on governorship for 2007 is most likely. Moreover, the MZO U TIV in America is said to be a critical factor in the decision of power shift to Idoma.
Daily Champion learnt that Akume and Ayu may have been overwhelmed by opposition to their initial stand in favour of Idoma. This is why some kind of arrangement is said to be in the offing to give Idoma the position of a deputy governor this time around instead of Igede who are expected to pick the position of either the PDP state chairman or secretary to the state government.
Zone A already has a plethora of governorship aspirants in waiting. Apart from Shuluwa whose interest has been known since last year, there are the House of Representatives member from Logo, Katsina-Ala Ukum federal constituency, Hon. Gabriel Suswan, Senator Daniel Saror, Benue State Commissioner for local government and chieftancy matters, Mr. Emmanuel Udende and the Special Assistant to the vice president, Mr. Shima Ayati.
All these personalities come from Sankera in zone A, which is a sub-section of Tiv in the state. Regarded as the largest sub-section of Tiv ethnic group, they are yet to produce the governor of the state. And they appear to be getting the sympathy of the governor and his political god-father, Dr. Ayu. But the chairman of Okpokwu local government, Comrade Moro voiced his caution over this line of thinking. "We are not talking of sub-sections of ethnic groups in the matter of governorship but zones".
"Zone A has produced the governor of the state. Zone B has now produced one in the person of my dear friend, Mr. George Akume who is running his second term. It is morally justifiable that zone C which has never produced the governor of the state should be allowed to get it next time". He said.
But will his position sell in Tiv area? Is morality a weapon in politics? Will the Tiv willingly zone 2007 governorship to Idoma? These questions and more may come up as the politics of 2007 begins to take shape.
However, one thing is certain. The issue of the creation of Apa state may yet re-surface.

Original_Search_Title: As 'Ali Must Go' Mounts PDP Saddle
Headline: Nigeria: As 'Ali Must Go' Mounts PDP Saddle
Body_Text:
Lagos — The emergence last Wednesday of former Federal Commissioner for
Education, Col. Ahmadu Ali (rtd), a loyalist of President Olusegun Obasanjo,
as the new Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) National Chairman no doubt marks
the final berth of the Obasanjo political ascendancy in the ruling
party. But rather than helping to put the party in proper shape for the
November 2005 national convention, Tunde Rahman, in this report, writes that
the development may likely activate the party's various political camps
already dusted in the succession game to buckle up for the titanic battle
that lie ahead of the 2007 presidential poll
Last Wednesday's inauguration of former Federal Commissioner for
Education, Dr Ahmadu Ali, as the new chairman of the Peoples Democratic
Party (PDP) was an interesting event. The development seems to have marked
the final demolition of all other political tendencies in the ruling party
and the final berth of the President Olusegun Obasanjo political
ascendancy. The move towards that end had been on for some times. But Ali's
inaguration appeared like the final capture of the party that has been
decimated by factional crisis by the Obasanjo camp. From a political orphan
of 1998 with neither political clout nor political structure of his own to
run the 1999 presidential election, President Obasanjo has turned a full
circle. He now calls all the shots in the PDP. The resignation of Chief Audu
Ogbeh as the party's chairman threw up the PDP again for another power
tussle ahead of the November convention of the party. At the onset, the
battle promised to be titanic with all kinds of weapons being amassed but at
the end of the day, the President's man emerged almost
effortlessly. President Obasanjo ensured among other things that Ali of 'Ali
Must Go' fame of the late 70's, took over as new PDP chairman. Ali, a
medical doctor, had served under President Obasanjo's military
administration between 1977 and 1979.
The President obviously has implicit trust and confidence in
him. Perhaps, Ali also hold an additional attraction for Obasanjo. As a
retired colonel, the military command structure thing where a lieutenant
always look to his boss up there may have come into reckoning. Ali is to
hold the PDP till a new national convention to elect new executives of the
party in November.
Faced with the kind of intrigues, and crisis in the PDP in the build-up
to 2007, how well can Ali holds out as party chairman? Can he instill the
much- needed discipline in the ruling party and wedge all the opposing camps
together?
The emergence of Ali, a loyalist of President Obasanjo, will only help
to make the struggle towards the 2007 presidency fiercer. This is because
knowing where the new chairman is coming from and the body language of the
President with respect to 2007, the presidential candidates of the party
particularly Vice-president Atiku Abubakar will really need to gird their
loins to be in contention for the ticket.
The election of Ali however can hardly be faulted. The arrangement
satisfied the provision of the PDP constitution. According to Section 18 (A)
of the party's constitution, "Should a vacancy occur in any of the party
offices, the appropriate executive committees shall appoint a substitute
from the zone where the former office holder originated pending the conduct
of election at the next congress or convention."
Indeed, that Obasanjo has taken control of the PDP should not be a
strange development. What may however set tongues wagging is the way and
manner a vacancy was foisted on the party with the seeming employment of
brute force.
In some presidential democracies, the President is also the de facto
leader of the party. In the United States for instance, one hardly remembers
the chairman of either the Republican Party or the Democratic Party ounce
their presidential candidates emerge. The party's standard-bearer
immediately takes over as the leader of the party, not to talk of when such
a candidate becomes elected as president.
That realization perhaps partly emboldened President Obasanjo to move in
decisively to muscle out Ogbeh as party chairman after the latter's seeming
faux pas. Ogbeh dug his own grave. He chose to externalize an issue he could
have worked up to the President within the Aso Rock Presidential Villa where
he also resides to iron out. The implication of his letter of last December
6 to the President, which was leaked to a national newspaper, was that he
(Ogbeh) had lost confidence in his President over the handling of the
Anambra political imbroglio and that Obasanjo was now on his own. Will the
kind of President the country has take the way Ogbeh stripped him naked in
the public place lying low?
Obasanjo's position on the Anambra crisis seems to be clear enough. He
believes that the Anambra State Governor, Dr. Chris Nwabueze Ngige, and his
estranged political godfather, Chief Chris Uba, were both engaged in
political chicanery and deserved equal punishment. He likened the two to two
armed robbers who after a successful operation disagreed over how to share
their loot. According to some sources, the President has been giving cover
to Uba and company because he believes the system also protects the Anambra
State governor whom he would have loved to see out of office.
Those close to the President said what really infuriated Obasanjo was
the fact that Ogbeh had unlimited access to him. The former PDP chairman was
indeed one of those the President extended the courtesy of seeing out of the
Villa anytime he visited.
Again, by externalizing the issue, Ogbeh was said to be fighting other
people's fight. He was said to be out to demonise Obasanjo and to weaken him
for the battle ahead of the 2007 poll. Ogbeh seems to have chosen the wrong
place to align. In a developing democracy like Nigeria where government's
power of patronage is unequalled, the President will always win any battle
between him and his deputy as long as he remains in power. The nation's
executive presidential system confers a lot of power on the president, which
the occupant will exploit to whip people into line.
On Ogbeh's letter, Obasanjo responded publicly by tongue- lashing the
PDP chairman. In his reply, Obasanjo called Ogbeh all kinds of names. But
those close to the President knew that with that seeming face-off between
the two, it was a matter of time for Ogbeh to be shown the way out of
office. The President hardly forgives nor forgets. At an ensuing National
Executive Committee (NEC) meeting in January this year, which most political
analysts expected to be heated and at which the pro-Vice President Atiku
Abubakar camp were ready for the kill, Obasanjo reportedly blocked all
attempts to discuss Ogbeh's future saying the matter had been resolved
amicably. After the meeting, he followed Ogbeh to his residence for a meal
of pounded yam and egusi soup, his favourite. Some accounts had it that it
was at that place that Ogbeh offered to resign. Not long after however, it
became clear that the President forcefully demanded Ogbeh's resignation
letter.
The seeming costly error Ogbeh had made would soon dawn on him. Very
soon, the same people he fought for will no longer remember him. Ogbeh will
be out alone in the cold, in the political doldrums.
Last Wednesday, apparently in a fit of anger or perhaps to further
underscore his point, Ogbeh was conspicuously absent at the inauguration of
Ali. He will soon come back to the fold. His former predecessors, Chiefs
Solomon Lar and Barnabbas Gemade, however attended the event. Gemade too had
earlier distanced himself from the PDP when he was booted out of office for
Ogbeh to take over.
As they say, the ball is now in Ali's court. He needs to convince people
that he could be his own man. Ali was inaugurated after the acting chairman
of the party who presided over last Wednesday meeting, Senator Ibrahim
Safana, moved a motion for the ratification of his election. The Speaker of
the House of Representatives, Alhaji Aminu Bello Masari, seconded the
motion.
Five other contestants for the post from the North Central zone to which
the party chairmanship was zoned had previously stepped down for Ali. They
are Ambassador Fidelis Tapgun from Plateau State, Alhaji Shaaba Lafiagi from
Kwara as well as Senators A.T.Ahmed, Alex Kadiri and Alhaji Abdullaziz
Farouk all from Kogi state.
A camp of the PDP in Kwara State loyal to Dr. Olusola Saraki had even
placed advertisements in some national newspapers where it washed its hands
off Lafiagi's candidature.
The National Vice-Chairman of PDP North Central zone, Alhaji Abubakar
Mogaji, presented Ali to the NEC meeting. Mogaji paid tribute to the Benue
State Governor, Chief George Akume, for his support in ensuring that Akume
emerged as Ogbeh's successor. Immediately after, Governors Bukola Saraki
(Kwara), Ibrahim Idris (Kogi), Joshua Dariye (Plateau), Akume (Benue),
Abdulladir Kure (Niger) and Abdullahi Adamu (Nasarawa) ushered in Ali amidst
applause from the NEC members. President Obasanjo, Atiku, 16 governors and
virtually all states chairmen of the party attended the meeting. Governors
Bonnie Haruna (Adamawa), James Ibori (Delta), Lucky Igbinedion (Edo),
Rashidi Ladoja (Oyo), Orji Kalu (Abia) and Diepreye Alamiyiesegha (Bayelsa)
were however absent.
President Obasanjo said he knew Ali as far as 1975 when he (Obasanjo)
was the Federal Commissioner for Works stating, "What I know of the new
chairman, I believe he is up to the task." He said his personal encounter
with the new chairman dated back to the era of the university students'
crisis and the slogan of 'Ali Must Go". The President said emphatically
that, "this time around Ali has come to stay. Ali must stay."
In his acceptance speech, Ali said, "As a medical doctor, my profession
teaches me to cure and care after a through examination and
investigation. He said a thorough examination of the crisis and problems of
the party would be done to get solution for the overall benefits of the
party. Ali described his election as an extra-ordinary opportunity to serve
and stated that the process of healing and reconciliation in the party had
just started.

Original_Search_Title: AMA Puts Defaulters Before Court
Headline: Ghana: AMA Puts Defaulters Before Court
Body_Text:
32 traders defying Accra Metropolitan Assembly (AMA) by-laws, have been
put before an Accra District Court.
They were all charged for obstructing public way. Seven who pleaded not
guilty to the charge were each granted bail in the sum of Â¢5 million to
reappear on March 4, while the rest were fined Â¢1 million each, or in
default serve two-month prison terms.
Six juveniles who were arrested for displaying yam for sale on the
Graphic Road were also charged by the same court presided by Justice Olivia
Owusu-Obeng, for obstructing vehicular traffic. They all pleaded guilty and
were each fined for Â¢500,000 or in default serve a month each in
prison.
Mr. Daniel Nana Tawiah, acting Chief Metro Guard of the AMA told the ADM
that the AMA has more than nineteen major markets in the city, "but many of
the traders vacated them and would want to trade only at the Central
Business District".
Some of the markets include the recently rehabilitated Osu Market, the
Zongo Market, and Tuesday Market at Mamprobi, which he said, were all
abandoned by the traders.
He said the AMA would not relent in its efforts to decongest the CBD and
as such urged all traders who have not acquired approved trading spaces to
quit the pavements, or else face punitive measures.
Some traders have complained that the decongestion exercise is being
abused by the AMA officials. Mr. Steve Asamoah a shop owner at the CBD told
ADM that the AMA officials arrested his son and nephew for selling in front
of his shop.
He told the ADM that they were not selling on the pavements at the time
they were arrested but all explanations made to the arresting officers fell
on deaf ears.
He appealed to the authorities to investigate "and see how some innocent
ones do become scapegoats".

Original_Search_Title: An Assemblage of Columnists
Headline: Nigeria: An Assemblage of Columnists
Body_Text:
Abuja — The major work of a writer lies in the conception and incubation of ideas.
WHETHER one is gifted or not, writing is an art that is nurtured before it grows to a level that everybody appreciates. It gives joy to read the works of the masters because of their timelessness. In fact, only the reader who has tried to write can guess the toil that renders writing so smooth and adored with so many statements of sonorous melody that cry out for quotation and tempt the tongue. It is also only those who have attempted to write that understand that writing requires the highest form of discipline to flourish. You need discipline to stay at your desk for hours, to set your brain to task and commit the thought process on paper.
Like artists, writers are temperamentally alike. One of the greatest renaissance painters was once hired to paint a building. He did not do anything for days until the client got annoyed and had to query him. He was at pains to explain that the work of a painter was much more in conception than execution. In other words, it might take him days to have a clear conception of what to draw or paint, but once the inspiration came, it could take just hours to finish the job.
Similarly, it is not so easy, especially to serious writers, to conceive ideas to be expounded. This explains why some writers carry piece of papers and pens about them - to catch ideas in flight. But once the inspiration or the ideas come, you will discover that the pen in motion, sometimes as in Newton's second law, does not stop until compelled by external forces. All I am trying to say is that to a writer, putting one's thoughts down is very simple; the major work lies in the conception and incubation of ideas.
Columnists are people who put their brains to task; the task of conceiving and incubating ideas. Due to the intellectual nature of their job, they deserve to be respected. Ideally, they should be agenda-setters; important components of public discourse. Rather than appreciate their worth, some government officials pour invectives on them as unpatriotic, especially when they set out to point out the shortcomings of those in positions of public trust.
It is a mark of respect that the Honourable Minister of Information and National Orientation, Chief Chukwuemeka Chikelu has for columnists that made the Ministry organise a seminar for them entitled: The Columnist and His Nation at the Bolingo Hotel and Towers, Abuja, on February 9, 2005.
Media as partner
Though one does not have any column yet, I made up my mind to attend, only see the faces of some of those columnists I have long admired for their abilities to play jolly game with grammar. Some are so gifted that they toss words up, juggle them back and front and end up setting them on their feet again. However, there are others who pretend to be columnist even when they clearly do not have any business being one.
Chief Chukwuemeka Chikelu declared the Seminar open.
That man has come to signify something very unique in governance; that government service should not change the disposition of a man who is basically good. Speaking extempore in his opening remarks, the Minister made it clear that the seminar, in a manner of speaking, was made possible because government considers the "media as a partner" and also in recognition of "the roles of the media in shaping of public opinion." He charged participants to be at the forefront of "explaining policies and difficult choices that government has to make and to constantly engage the public by developing debates and getting feed back."
Speaking on the topic, "the Columnist: Social Crusader or Cynic," Prince Tony Momoh brought his vast experience to bear. In fact it was that day that I discovered why he is referred to as "a veteran journalist." Also a lawyer, with ebullient delight, he dissected the law of libel to journalists and column-writers to guide them in their writings. The old war-horse treated the topic as if he was consuming pounded yam with some delicious soup.
On this foundation, Mr. Peter Enahoro (Peter Pan) spoke on "The Columnists - Past and Present." Even to those who were seeing him for the first time that day, the aura that surrounded him was awe-inspiring. Everybody, including the Minister and Prince Momoh spoke about him reverently. Some lesser mortals looked upon him, even longed to worship him, Pharoah - like, as a god of journalism.
The great man took us on a historical excursion, and dropped the development of Nigerian journalism and the history of column-writing in the palms of those present. Listening to him, one had a nostalgic feeling for the past. He summed up present day column-writing in the following words: "Long, tedious treatises too frequently written in convoluted language or in trivialised, pseudo-academic pretensions are published as 'column' writing. The format is grey, black with row after row of solid font and heartbreaking for the absence of any attempt to make the reading easy on the eye." Columnists, literally sitting on the laps of the father, looked on with admiration and astonishment.
Image project
Dr. Okey Ikechukwu, Special Assistant to the Minister of Information was craftily drafted as one of the resource persons, to be able to further enunciate the Image Project to the critical mass of the media. Dr. Ikechukwu spoke on "The Columnist and Nigeria's Image." As he is wont to, he spoke extempore and beautifully. Even doubting Thomases stood convinced on the need for the image project, some out of conviction, some after coming under the sway of Okey's eloquence. Okey is never obscure; from his choice of words one can hardly believe he is a philosopher. He went directly to the point; the vital spot of an idea he wished to enunciate. He gave his sentences that limpid fluidity graceful to the ears. At such a moment, it is only the depth of his logic that betrays his philosophical training.
In his own presentation, which he christened "Leaving Readers Behind," Dimgba Igwe convinced everybody that he is a "Socrates" on the technicalities of column-writing. He evidently gathered his materials from observation, practical experience and inquiry; ordering his thoughts and findings logically. The presentation provoked questions such as: What is column-writing? Who should write a column? Who edits the column? Is column- writing an ego trip? [This reminds me of the puerile practice of publishing all manner of rejoinders sent to the boxes of some columnists. Sometimes one wonders why no critical rejoinders are sent!] Dimgba identified interesting types of columns. His presentation is recommended for the edification of all columnists who desire to improve on their art.
All in all, the seminar was educative and interesting. One of the lessons there from is that a good writer should be able to inform and at the same time entertain. Besides, it was an opportunity to once again come face-to-face with the lions of Nigeria columns: Dr. Rueben Abati, Mr. Uthman Shodipe, Dr. Amanze Obi, Dr. Dele Sobowale, Alhaji Kola Animashaun, Mr. Okey Ifionu, Mr. Sola Fasure, Mr. Dan Onwukwe, Mr. Segun Adeniyi, Mr. Ethelbert Okere and others. My greatest regret, however, is the absence of Mr. Godwin Agbroko of ThisDay. The thirst to see him was one of the forces that propelled me to gate-crash into the seminar even when I am not yet a columnist.

Original_Search_Title: Anglophone Problem Highlighted At Across the Mongolo Launch
Headline: Cameroon: Anglophone Problem Highlighted At Across the Mongolo Launch
Body_Text:
The Southern Cameroons problem of marginalisation took centre stage at
the launching of John Ngongong Ngengasong's latest novel, "Across The
Mongolo", Wednesday, February 9, in Yaounde.
An enthusiastic, mainly Anglophone, audience reacted animatedly to
various comments and reviews on the 200-page novel at the scintillating and
fun-packed launching that held in Amphi 700 of the University of Yaounde
I. Prof Tazoacha Asonganyi, who chaired the launching, said after reading
the novel, he wished that the story were not true. But unfortunately, he was
ashamed the novel was a simulacrum of the stark reality of Anglophone
subjugation in Cameroon.
He spoke about the politics of bilingualism, in which the Anglophone was
getting a raw deal. He said because many Francophone administrators serving
in English-speaking provinces address the local people in French, the
feeling in Anglophone Cameroon is that the territory is under
colonisation.
Prof Asonganyi said Nkengasong's novel echoed the history of Southern
Cameroons and hoped it would "awaken the conscience of the rulers of this
country."
Recalling the rebellious nature of the protagonist in "Across the
Mongolo", Ngwe Nkemasaah, Prof Asonganyi said thousands of Anglophone youths
are feeling that way. He said when these thousands of youths would decide to
take the Anglophone cause as a messianic mission, just like the fictional
Ngwe, it would be unfortunate for Cameroon.
Prof Asonganyi praised the Rector of the University of Yaounde I, Prof
Sammy Beban Chumbow, for authorising the launching on campus, unlike his
predecessors who banned the launching of books written by university
professors.
The Head of the Department of English at the University of Yaounde I,
Prof Alobwed'Epie, said Across the Mongolo is a landmark in Anglophone
Cameroon literature. He likened the story between the two Cameroons to that
of identical twins. He said each identical twin has his own attitudes and
experiences.
Similarly, Anglophone and Francophone Cameroonians have their separate
cultures, history and expectations. He said Nkengasong's novel is unique
because it fictionalises reality unlike traditional literature that cloaks
reality.
Dr (Mrs) Eunice Ngongkum, who did one of the two reviews at the launch,
spoke on "Dream and Reality in John Nkengong Nkengasong's Across the
Mongolo". Dr Ngongkum said the novel is about growth and discovery during
which the protagonist progresses from dreams to the harsh world of
realities.
"Kama [Anglophone Cameroon] is the land of dreams, even dreams fulfilled
while Ngola [Francophone Cameroon] is a world of reality, harsh reality
where dreams are destroyed," Dr Ngongkum said. She said the injustices and
indignities he was subjected to in Ngola fuelled Ngwe's dream.
That is why he founded the Young Anglophone Movement, YAM, as an
"adequate response to this novel dimension of transforming a personal dream
into reality."She said the evolution of Ngwe from a state of innocence to
the mature recognition of the harsh realities of the world prepares him for
his messianic mission - 'that of liberating his people from slavery in a
faraway land.'
Prof Shadrack Ambanasom, who did the second review, traced Ngwe's
academic progress from Anglophone Cameroon (Kama) to his frustrating
experience at the lone Kamangola (Cameroon) University of Besaadi.
"Gradually it dawns on [Ngwe] that his humiliation is not an isolated
fact but one shared by other Anglophones; that the Francophone government in
place has a hidden agenda to annihilate the Anglo-Saxon culture in the
United Republic of Kamangola, with the university as an experimental nursery
ground; and that the Anglophones co-opted into the government are there to
destroy the Anglo-Saxon culture," Prof Ambanasom said.
He added: "Across the Mongolo thematises the struggle for the survival
of the Anglophone identity within the Francophone dominance. It is
significant that, at the end of the novel, Ngwe, though irremediably
frustrated, is nevertheless alive. His survival emblematises the existence
of YAM, and thus symbolises Anglophone hope."
During the launching proper, chief launcher, HRH Chief Nkendem
Forbinake, set the ball rolling with FCFA 100,000. Many other persons,
including dignitaries and students, obtained autographed copies of the
novel.
Writer As Messiah
Dr Hilarious Ambe, who presented the author, said he was so
multi-talented in the literary domain that it was difficult to define
him. He said Nkengasong, who holds a PhD and lectures in the Department of
English at the University of Yaounde I, is a poet, playwright, novelist and
teacher.
He also narrated how Dr Nkengasong has caused heated literary debates in
some of Cameroon's major newspapers. He cited the case of a critique on the
works of Dr Sanki Maimo that provoked a series of articles in Cameroon
Tribune in the early 1980s. That critique also caused Dr Sanki Maimo to
write a book as a response.
Dr Nkengasong equally caused a protracted debate in The Post in the late
1990s when he challenged a critique of Dr Francis Nyamnjoh's works by
University of Buea lecturer, Dr Henry Kah Jick.Nkengasong, in a terse
speech, said the massive attendance was ocular proof that the Cameroonian
public has concern for the writer and his messianic role in society.
He equally thanked a certain King Herod for his achievement. "When my
play Black Caps and Red Feathers was published and some lecturers included
the text in their programmes on their own accord King Herod sent an order
banning all the works in the curriculum which were fresh from print. I am
immensely thankful to King Herod because his ban generated the creative
energies in me, the result which we are celebrating today," Nkengasong
said.
He said it has always been his wish not to tell the tyrant whether he is
right or wrong, but "to bring tyranny closer to the truth about himself and
the deplorable conditions, the misery, the wretchedness and the despair of
the people they govern."
The launch that was co-ordinated by two impresarios, Ephraim Banda
Ghogomu and Tangie Suh Nfor, was attended by literary icons like Prof Bole
Butake and Dr Bate Besong.

Original_Search_Title: Nafdac to Test Samples of Killer Amala
Headline: Nigeria: Nafdac to Test Samples of Killer Amala
Body_Text:
Ilorin — FOLLOWING the mystery surrounding the death of a family of four in Ilorin at the weekend after a meal of amala, the National Agency
for Food and Drug Administration and Control (NAFDAC) on Wednesday took
samples of the yam flour used in preparing the meal.
The sole survivor in the incident, who had been on life support system
since Tuesday evening when she was moved from Tobi Hospital for further
treatment died in the early hours of yesterday at the Accident and Emergency
Wing of the University of IlorinTeaching Hospital (UITH).
Speaking in an interview with journalists, NAFDAC's representative in
Ilorin, Mr. Adebayo Afolabi, said that the collected samples would be taken
to Lagos for necessary laboratory analysis.
He recalled that Kwara State's Commissioner for Health, Dr. Bola
Olaosebikan, accompanied him and other officials of the Agency to the house
when the samples were taken.Other items taken for laboratory analysis,
according to Afolabi, included sample of water used in preparing the meal
and the pot of soup prepared by the late Owoeye with which the deadly amala
was allegedly consumed.
According to the NAFDAC official: "The samples will be taken to Lagos
today for laboratory analysis. So by next week we should be able to tell
Nigerians what went wrong. We also noticed that there are two sources from
which the residents of the house could source water. The deepwell in the
house and a borehole just at a stone throw from the house. We took samples
of both sources for analysis".
Afolabi, also said that the house was still under lock and key when his
team visited, and that it was forced open for them to gain entry and perform
their duty.
Meanwhile, informed sources at the police headquarters told Vanguard
that the woman alleged to be the brain behind the killing was yet to own up
to the crime.
Public Relations Officer, Mr. Markus Gideon, denied the claim that the
police had apprehended the woman who allegedly sold the acid used in
committing the havoc, saying that, "The woman has just been brought to the
headquarters but she had not made any confessional statements."

Original_Search_Title: Nafdac Investigates Killer Amala
Headline: Nigeria: Nafdac Investigates Killer Amala
Body_Text:
Ilorin — National Agency for Food, Drug Administration and Control (NAFDAC), in
Kwara State, has expressed its desire to unravel mystery behind the killer
amala, which claimed the lives of five people.
NAFDAC official, Mr Adebayo Afolabi, told newsmen that the agency took
samples of the yam flour used in preparing the meal for laboratory
analysis.
The test came on the heels of the death of the sole survivor of the
incident, Ayo Owoeye, who passed on at the Accident and Emergency wing of
the University of Ilorin Teaching Hospital {UITH} on Wednesday.
Ayo had been on life support since Tuesday when she was moved from Tobi
Hospital.
Other items taken for laboratory analysis, according to Afolabi,
included sample of water used in preparing the meal and the pot of soup
prepared by the late Mrs Ebun Owoeye, with which the deadly amala was
consumed.
Owoeye, popularly called "Iya Ayo", in the Iyana Alagbede neighbourhood,
had prepared a meal of Amala a week ago, which later turned out to be a
deadly meal.
The state government dispatched officials of the state Ministry of
Health to the house, to ascertain the event and take other steps that may be
required in the circumstance.
Following government's directive, Commissioner of Health, Dr Bola
Olaosebikan, accompanied by the NAFDAC official visited the house when the
samples were taken.
Three members of a family and a two and a half-year-old, child of a
neighbour had died after the meal.
Afolabi said, "the samples will be taken to Lagos today for laboratory
analysis, by next week, we should be able to tell Nigerians what went
wrong.

Original_Search_Title: Police Arrest Multiple Murder's Suspect
Headline: Nigeria: Police Arrest Multiple Murder's Suspect
Body_Text:
Ilorin — THE Kwara State Police Command arrested yesterday the principal suspect
in the mysterious death of a family of four wiped out from the surface of
the earth having eaten a poisoned bowl of yam flour food 'amala' in Ilorin
at the weekend.
Vanguard reliably gathered that the suspect, Mrs Victoria Sunday was
arrested by the police in Gaa Akanbi area of Ilorin following a tip-off by
the police. She is been presently kept at the Ganmo Police station, after
which she would be moved to the police headquarters for further
investigation.
Mrs Sunday, according to investigation was said to have had a protracted
differences with the deceased which was said to have culminated in the
curses she reportedly placed on the deceased that she would not end well
with her family in this new year.
The State Public Relations of Kwara State Police Command Markus Gideon
(DSP) confirmed the arrest with Vanguard saying that the suspect was picked
over the alleged roles she was said to have played in the unfortunate
incident.
Mrs Ebun Owoeye and her two daughters and neighbour's daughter died at
the weekend after eating Yam flour which was suspected to have been
poisoned.
The last of the victims at the intensive care unit of Tobi Hospital
Ilorin died Monday morning following the death of her mother Saturday, few
hours after taking the meal with one of her daughters and that of her
neighbours.
The Kwara State Police Public Relations Officer, DSP Markus Gideon
earlier in an interview with Vanguard had expressed dismay that despite the
magnitude of the ugly incident nobody had formally reported the incident at
any of the police stations in the state and vowed that the suspect would be
arrested very soon.
"We will do our best to nab whoever that was responsible ,the strategies
I won't tell you, but God helping us, within a short time we will find the
killers", he added.
Marcus, however, said that as of now, no arrest has been made adding
that, it is possible to take up any matter without any formal complaint
depending on the magnitude and the consequence on the society. I've been to
the house and met it deserted, nobody was there, it was under lock and
key. and the police can't go and be arresting people within the
neighbourhood for what they possibly don't know, since they are not living
in that same house' he added.
Vanguard reliably gathered that the deceased Mrs Ebun Owoeye, a cleaner
with Michaeal Imoudu Labour Institute, Ilorin was said to have had a long
time outstanding differences with one of her neighbours simply identified as
Mama Ayo in Iyana Alagbede street in Offa garage area of Ilorin where all
the tenants have now deserted for fear of being arrested by the police;
Vanguard also learnt that many differences of the duo which could not be
resolved, despite several mediations, forced people in the area to point
accusing fingers at Mama Ayo, who they claimed placed a curse on the
deceased and her children, that woes would befall them in the new year and
that they would not see the end of the year
The husband of the deceased, Mr Owoeye who in an interview with
Vanguard, has taken his case before God for judgement said
"I am a Christian and a Deeper Life Church member. The all-knowing God
who has allowed this to happen to me will definitely fight for me."
The remains of the deceased have been deposited at the mortuary of UITH
Ilorin.
Contacted, the Chief Medical Director of Tobi Specialist Hospital, Dr
Moradeyo Alabi said he suspected chemical poisoning, adding that "but until
we get to a toxicology in Lagos, since there is none around here, we cannot
say the exact case of poison.
Cases of food poisoning could be very severe, but it depends on how they
are managed. This equally depends on the exact toxin whether it has
bacterial link, then it is easier to manage but the one with the chemical is
the most difficult," he concluded

Original_Search_Title: What's in Store for Dzamesi?
Headline: Ghana: What's in Store for Dzamesi?
Body_Text:
Ho —  As he braces up to occupy 'hot seat'
It is by now crystal clear to every political observer that, whether by dint of hard work or through the permutations of fate and destiny, Mr. Samuel Ahiave Kofi Dzamesi, the 45-year old construction engineer, the New Patriotic Party (NPP) parliamentary candidate for Ketu North constituency in the last elections, and until recently the deputy Volta regional minister, has, 'barring any unthinkable development', assumed the driving seat as the substantive regional minister.
This is because not only has he been nominated by the president and successfully vetted by the parliamentary committee; he has also been approved by the same committee and is only awaiting the swearing-in ceremony by the President.
It will, therefore, only be a matter of days before the Dzodze-born politician takes full charge of one of the hottest regional seats in the country. As if by design, two incidents seem to be welcoming him already as prologues to the myriads of problems that may be in store for him.
The first was the Krachi riots over the weekend in which three people were reportedly killed and property destroyed in a free for all fight between two factions in the Krachi district (Krachi and Zongo youth) after a football match. Normalcy is reportedly returning to the area, but security presence is still high. At the same time, hundreds of Adaklu citizens, all clad in red, demonstrated through the principal streets of the regional capital last Monday in protest against the 'yet-to-be' minister.
He had told the vetting committee in parliament house some days earlier that those taking an entrenched position in the __cpLocation of the capital for the newly created Adaklu-Anyigbe district brouhaha were in the minority. Some of the aggrieved people of Adaklu, many carrying placards with unprintable messages, marched to the Regional Coordinating Council and presented a petition to the president. They told the president, among other things, that they could no longer guarantee the peace and security of the area.
These two frightening spontaneous developments, which could best be described as 'Dzamesi's first baptism of fire', send clear signals of the numerous volatile spots in the region and also paint a clear picture of the enormity of the task ahead of him.
The Nkonya-Alavanyo conflict will soon be 84 years old soon, but there is little commitment from both sides towards finding a permanent solution to it. Very often, politicians also played the ostrich about it and government after government seemed to be at their wits' end. And the fact is that recently, it was fanned several times in the run-up to the last general election as if the conflict had its roots in the Fourth republic.
The same goes for the Peki-Tsito land dispute, in which almost every regional minister had his fare share of insults and blame for each and every escalation, whether the minister at the time of the escalation hailed from any of the factions or not. How different will his administration be in such matters?
The security situation in the region, which could be one of Dzamesi's headaches, also borders on chieftaincy disputes such as those at Krachi and Nchumuru, Anlo after the late Togbi Adeladza, the perennial Ho Dome issue like the one that raised its ugly head during the last yam festival, the potential war of attrition between Akwamufie (Asuogyaman district) and Dorfor (North Tongu district) over who owns Juapong land, just to mention a few. And even though the fact still remains that political heads do not install chiefs and demarcate lands, the frequent occurrence of these problems do not auger well for accelerated development.
In the area of employment, attracting investors to site industries in the region would surely be, as it had been for others, a Herculean task for him. The region currently boasts of only two such companies- the Juapong Textiles Limited and the Diamond Cement Company Limited, with the latter already in a tug-of-war with local residents over environmental pollution.
It is also a fact that bushfire is one of the Achilles' Heels of the Volta. As at the time of filing this report, the hundreds of hectares of land that stretch from Adaklu, Agortime, Ho and Tsito is an eyesore that can best be described as 'sakora', after the land tasted the usual dry season fires, and the bush burning are still on-going like no man's business.
Keeping the region intact, in one big whole, the way he is coming to meet it is definitely going to be one of the problems on the hands of the new administration.
For even though there had been agitations for successions and separations in the past, never had they been more pronounced than recently.
For instance, in the run-up to the December elections, almost all the paramount chiefs in the northern part of the region openly called on the president, during his campaign tour there, to carve a separate region for them. And as if by design, many of the chiefs christened it Oti region, and had petitioned the president to that effect.
But whether the region eventually gets split in two or not, the in-coming minister will have to grapple with how to further improve the image of the region by not only reducing smuggling, but also laying to rest certain security alarms that are fast being associated with the region.
As the head of the Regional Security Council (REGSEC), allegations of kidnapping and injecting of journalists, like what popped up before the elections and seems to be dying very fast, must be looked into at all costs.
Fortunately, his predecessor had called for a full-scale investigation into the allegation, just as the president of the Ghana Journalists Association (GJA) did.
Rumours making the rounds, as a result of the claim, are that media practitioners in other regions are hesitating to accept postings to the Volta, neither because of accommodation problems nor due to the inhospitality of the people, but on account of the unproven allegations of 3rd December last year.
The new minister will do the nation a lot of good if he puts the minds of journalists and the entire nation at rest, not by merely coming out to refute them allegation, but by calling for the outcome of recent police investigations, and the culprits, if any, brought to book. This way, the image of the region, which is dearest to his heart, would be cleansed and no longer seen as the 'Taliban' region of Ghana.
He told 'The Chronicle' in a telephone interview that he would play the role of a father to all and promised to do his best to unite the people. The task may be hard, but with vision, determination and political will, he should be able to make these indelible marks before leaving office.
Fortunately for him, he was deputy for four years, and may naturally be familiar with these problems.

Original_Search_Title: When No One Could Touch Aids Patients Maama Nowelina Did
Headline: Uganda: When No One Could Touch Aids Patients Maama Nowelina Did
Body_Text:
Kampala —  - Without gloves, she bathed the sick women, especially the bedridden
ones and washed their beddings
SHE is humble, perhaps too humble for her role. At first sight, you may
think she is just another catholic woman. But when you hear her story, you
realise behind her softness is a unique, courageous and hardworking
woman.
Nowelina Namukisa caters for a 'family' of over 2,000, they include
people living with HIV/AIDs (PLWAs). This family is bound by an organisation
called Meeting Point Kampala (MPKLA). Started in 1994 by Namukisa and a
friend called Rose, the group has expanded from Namuwongo to cover Makindye
division.
This believer, whose surname (Namukisa) means 'luck', says her work
started as a leisure activity. She says, "I'm a member of a catholic
movement called "Christ is Communion and Life" (CCL), where we share about
our lives and also visit the sick. With the onset of the HIV/AIDS scourge, I
saw people living with AIDS being mistreated by their relatives".
A story is about a mother, who would not touch the cup used to feed her
26-year-old sick daughter. She had a banana fibre tied to the handle and
using a stick, she would hang it on a nail. She made the daughter use the
dirty cup several times before throwing it away and buying a new one.
"Many patients were given little food, like that of a child, as
caretakers feared to clear the diarrhoea. Many were not being bathed, they
were isolated and died lonely," Namukisa says. From this misery, she
developed the urge to give them special attention. She did not have the
money, but was determined to give them a decent life.
Without gloves, she bathed the sick women, especially the bedridden
ones, washed their beddings and feeding utensils. She spared some of the
food she cooked at home for some patients. The relatives started to refer to
the patients as Nowelina's patients. "Omulwadde wo kati asuuse", relatives
would tell her on the next visit meaning "Your patient is recovering." Both
patients and the caretakers started waiting for her next visit. They
referred to her as a mother and saviour.
"The love and care I gave to the patients greatly changed their
lives. The caretakers' attitudes too changed. They started paying more
attention to the patients. I invited some of the patients to our meetings in
the CCL and as we shared our needs, problems and sufferings, we developed a
desire to help each other as people belonging to one family," Namukisa
says.
An Italian priest, Fr Petero Tibon, who Namukisa occasionally invited to
pray for PLWAs realised the great job she was doing and started soliciting
for help from fellow Italians. Up to now, Namukisa says, most of the funds
used in MPKLA come from Italy.
MPKLA was registered as an NGO in 1997. Today, MPKLA caters for 1,208
PLWAs.
The organisation is implementing a distant support programme with funds
from private donors and International NGOs like AVSI. Through the programme,
the organisation offers scholarships to children orphaned by HIV/AIDS in
Namuwongo. There are 967 children studying under the organisation's
sponsorship. Another 430 orphans get informal education at the
organisation's learning centre.
Namukisa has won the admiration of The Uganda AIDS Commission. A
certificate of recognition from the commission hangs on a wall at the
reception of Meeting Point headquarters on Plot 125 on Bukasa Road in
Namuwongo village, Makindye division in Kampala. The activities of the
organisation have enabled members to put behind them the havoc HIV/AIDS has
wrecked in people's lives.
Maria Ayinkamanyire, a resident of Namuwongo zone A, says when her
husband died in 1989, her life was torn apart. "I was left with six children
in a rented room. When Mama Namukisa called on women to join MPKLA to learn
how to read and write, I was one of the first women to use the
opportunity. I have put the misery behind me and I have a house and two
plots, where I grow yams that I sell in the market in Makindye."
MPKLA provides special care for families, offers counselling services
and social, spiritual and psychological support to people by keeping in
contact with them. The organisation offers vocational training.
it also holds seminars to create awareness on AIDS and its consequences
and also trains counsellors and volunteers to take care of persons living
with AIDS.
"We started a special project for women after realising AIDS impacts on
them most. Kyamusa Obwongo Women Project has helped to improve the situation
of women and girls.
Some of their activities include adult literacy classes, training in
crafts, micro credit and promotion of income generating activities,"
Namukisa says.
Some of the orphans live in a foster home at the headquarters, while
others stay at her home.
"I live with eight orphans and I have eight biological children. My home
and office are always full of people who come seeking guidance, counselling
and, financial moral support.
"At times, I feel worn out, but the love I have for my family, MPKLA,
keeps me going. My husband, Patrick Anguzu, is an understanding person and
we work together," she says.
Nowelina's experiences in the fight for the dignity of PLWAs are
many. However the important issue she would like to emphasise is relatives
of PLWAs, who get help from NGOs should not withdraw their responsibility. "
When an NGO starts supporting a sick person, the relatives stand aside
and expect everything from the NGO, even families capable of providing for
their patients will leave everything to the NGO. People need to know we
cannot do everything. We need to share the responsibility," Nowelina
says.

Original_Search_Title: 'Why New Yam Festival is Important
Headline: Nigeria: 'Why New Yam Festival is Important
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Igbo new yam festival has been described as a medium for fostering
love, unity, peace, understanding and oneness among the Igbo and other
tribes in the country.
Eze Charles Obiukwu, the Eze Ndigbo of Ikorodu, said this at the new yam
festival/cultural celebration in Lagos, recently. The occasion also
witnessed the commissioning of the Lagos State Igbo Community Centre in
Okota, a Lagos suburb.
In his words: the Eze said "the new yam festival brings all Igbo
together and unite them in celebration with other tribes in Nigeria.
"The festival plays host to several dignitaries from other parts of
Nigeria, signifying love, oneness of purpose, unity and strength in
diversity for Nigerians."
Advising Nigerian leaders, he said: "People at the helm of affairs
should please for God's sake do everything possible to lead us out of the
woods to the promised land," he pleaded.
Mrs Oluremi Tinubu, wife of Lagos State Governor, who commissioned the
centre, which also houses the palace of another claimant to the Eze Ndigbo
of Lagos, Dr. C. C. Nwachuku, said: "We have the duty to support the
government by performing our civic responsibilities because government
requires the maximum cooperation of every resident of the state for the
successful implementation of its people-oriented programmes."
In commending the Igbos in Lagos for their positive ways of life, she
said: "On behalf of His Excellency, Asiwaju Bola Ahmed Tinubu, I commend the
Eze Ndigbo and all Igbo sons ad daughters resident in the state for your
positive contributions to the growth and development of Lagos State.
"His Excellence has requested me to convey to you his deep appreciation
for your continued support for the activities and programmes of his
administration for the past five years and commends your efforts as a group,
for being very tolerant, law abiding and for maintaining peaceful
co-existence with other fellow Nigerians resident in the state."
Nwachukwu in his own speech thanked Tinubu for governing the state so
well that there has been peace, love, unity, and understanding among all
tribes, including Igbo.
Dignitaries at the occasion include Rear Admiral Godwin Ndubuisi Kanu
(rtd), former Governor of Imo and Lagos states, Engr. Rafiu Aregbesola, the
State Commissioner for Works and Infrastructure, Honourable Abdul-Lateef
Hakeem, representing Isolo Constituency in the state House of Assembly,
Mr. Dele Ajomale, Commissioner for Special Duties, and Chief Chris
Asoluka.

Original_Search_Title: Political Parties And Crisis of Leadership
Headline: Nigeria: Political Parties And Crisis of Leadership
Body_Text:
Lagos — EMBATTLED Nigerians famished by the ordeal of increasing poverty are
today bedevilled by the protracted in-fighting among the major political
parties in the country.
Rather than concentrating on how to improve the lot of the common man,
politicians want to be at the forefront of leadership with all the cozy
perquisites of office and the fringe benefits derived from the national
cake. Jean Jacques Rousseau in his treatise on Social Contract postulates
that the first law of government is the happiness of society. If the
politicians have invested all their energy on selfless and progressive
governance, the crippling scourge of destitution would have been
considerably reduced.
The Alliance for Democracy (AD) adopted everything diabolical in the
political dictionary to improvise factional groupings waging war to
annihilate opposing cabals. Various strategies were employed. They went to
courts for adjudication, used quasi-military political thugs to intimidate
implacable enemies and at last resorted to the Independent National
Electoral Commission (INEC) for arbitration. The absence of the
indefatigable Chief Bola Ige was quite noticeable. It will take the AD many
years to recover from the wounds inflicted by the bitter fratricidal
discord.
The All Progressives Grand Alliance (APGA) soon joined the fray. A
clique organised a convention and expelled the leader of the party. Factions
developed, each one trying to displace the other. Tensions generated shook
the party to its very foundation. Attempts by Chief Odumegwu Ojukwu,
presidential candidate of the party to reconcile the warring groups have not
succeeded. The crisis is still brewing although the leadership seems to be
regaining its power.
The All Nigeria Peoples Party (ANPP) which lately appeared to be growing
in strength as the most powerful opposition party suddenly became engulfed
in internal strife with General Jerry Useni (rtd) spearheading a factional
offensive. As always, the issue was the mismanagement of party funs. Again,
General Muhammodu Buhari, the presidential candidate intervened to no
avail. With accusations and counter accusations, it was often pretty
difficult to determine who was right or wrong.
The most explosive scenario arrived with the conflict in the ruling
Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) between Chief Audu Ogbeh Chairman of the
party and President Olusegun Obasanjo. What sparked off the battle was a
letter by Chief Ogbeh pointing out the deficiencies of government
policies. The President in his well known combat style replied with a
stinker. The stage was set for a fierce confrontation. Those who assumed
that a luncheon of pounded yam had saved the ugly situation were absolutely
wrong. Chief Audu Ogbeh was pressed to resign under duress.
Some factors have precipitated the instability in the existing political
parties. Their formation at the exit of the military was evidently rushed
bringing many strange bedfellows into identical camps. Incompatibility has
been the weak point of the prevailing political parties. None of the
political parties has any profound ideology or defined policy. They
virtually exist on mere exigencies on day-to-day circumstances without
principles.
As a matter of fact, the only binding interest among parties members is
the sharing of the national cake, seeking self centred means of enriching
their private pockets by whatever means. Nigerian political parties have no
noble goals worth writing home about. Their material ends justify the
means.
Prescriptions underlined in the extant constitution regarding political
parties are observed more in the breach than compliance. The financial
regulations are generally discarded. Investigations show that the
Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) has not been given adequate
legal authority to control the political parties as provided in the
constitution.
Action must be expedited by the National Assembly in this respect. The
issue of quasi-military organisations deserves urgent and prime attention
because most of the internal and external political conflicts are
facilitated by the use of political thugs who perpetuate violence as a
matter of course. Law enforcement agencies have a vital role to play in
disbanding thuggery and preventing them from societal disturbances. It is an
open secret that every political party maintains thugs as a power base for
political offensive and defensive.
Opinions have been expressed about the provision of independent
candidates who should contest elections on their own recognition and
personal merits. This proviso in the constitution will prevent the
prevailing combination of incompatible persons in the same political
associations. It will thus avoid unnecessary competition and conflicts among
members who have really nothing in common. The crisis for leadership may not
only be drastically reduced but entirely eliminated in the local political
process.
The slow judicial system must be expedited to facilitated the settlement
of political cases. It is indeed tragic that the Anambra gubernatorial
election tribunal had been sitting since May 2003 thereby exacerbating the
political crisis in that state. If judgement had been given say after six
months, the conflict would have since ended. A deadline must be given to
judicial officers for the settlement of all political conflicts.
The Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) needs urgent reform
to improve the quality of its membership. Some of its present members have
partisan loyalties because of the nature of their appointment. Members of
INEC should be appointed by an independent body such as the National Council
of State. Their terms of office should not exceed four years.
There should be a constitutional provision banning cross-carpeting by
members of legislative houses. However, any legislator who decides to change
his political association must resign and recontest under his or her new
political flag. In addition, the recall system must be encouraged and
reactivated whenever necessary.

Original_Search_Title: Vista:- Chidi Kwubiri
Headline: Nigeria: Vista:- Chidi Kwubiri
Body_Text:
A Painter with many Laureate abroad
Depression has become our national religion, ever since the incumbent administration made incessant increases in the price of petroleum products the focal point of the administration's policy.
As I sat down in the African restaurant in Koln, Germany, I tried to banish thoughts about Nigeria from my mind. But I couldn't. I was seated in the midst of Nigerians, who seemed happy in a foreign land. Our beloved country, ofcourse occupied the front burner of our discussion as we ate pounded yam and egusi soup. In our gathering, was a youngman, who perhaps would have been frustrated in Nigeria if he had remained in the country.
Or perhaps, his dreams would have been aborted by the hardship imposed on the populace by corrupt leaders whose only achievement is the impoverishment of the citizenry. The youngman is Chidi Kwubiri, a Nigerian painter based in Germany - Chidi, is another pointer to the fact that with the right environment, Nigerians can excel in whatever they do. Born on 21st August 1966 in Umuahia, Nigeria, Chidi Kwubiri studied painting under Professor Micheal-Buthe and Professor A.R. Penck at the famous Arts Academy Dusseldorf, Germany.
His accomplishments in Germany would make any Nigerian proud. He has won several prizes. Among the prizes are first prize. "Art collection" Siemens Group in co-operation with Dresdner Bank, Art prize Ko - Gallery Dusseldorf but to mention a few. His works have been exhibited in the church street Gallery Orlando, Florida, U.S.A, Gallery Huyse Molensloot, Antwerp, Belgium, second Independent International Biennial of Graphic, St Petersburg, Russia, Holland Art fair Netherlands, the state Museum Tivat Montenegro. Serbia, UNESCO headquarters Paris, and in Galleries in Germany. Chidi indeed lends credence to the saying that a prophet is without honour in his home, celebrated abroad, yet unkown in his native Nigeria. My visit to Germany couldn't have been complete if I hadn't got an interview with him. Luckily for me, I caught up with him at the "small" party in the African restaurant in Koln to celebrate the opening of yet another exhibition by Chidi Kwubiri.
How did he get into painting?
"It has always been my dream to be an artist. I started painting at the age of seven". Though Chidi confessed that he didn't know one could eke a living from earnings made from the arts, he knew all he wanted to do was be an artist, "it was simply in my blood to be an artist."
It started when I saw a young man in the market in Umuahia, Abia state. He was called Reginald Akuche. I hope he is still painting now.
When I saw how good he was, his work inspired me and I started doing sketches and I became popular in primary and secondary school for my work."
After his secondary education Kwubiri moved to Kaduna in northern, Nigeria, where he made portraits for top military officers. "I made a portrait of the former Governor of Kaduna state, Col Abubakar Umar, Gen. Sani Sanmi, and the former Chief of Army Staff Gen. Salihu Ibrahim."
So when did he get into serious painting?
"To live as an artist in Nigeria then was very difficult, because if you want to make a living from art, you must do a lot of portrait and that was what I was forced to do. I knew that there was something more than portrait when you talk about art." I had to leave the country to discover this, and also look at the country and my culture from a distance, factors that play an important role in my art."
Chidi Kwubiri relocated to Germany, where he applied to different art schools. "I was lucky to be admitted into one of the best art schools in the world, the art academy of Dusseldorf where you have only fifty admission spaces everywhere, but you have more than a thousand people applying for the spaces, my life took off from here."
Chidi Kwubiri's painting was used to open the musical theatre "Les viserable."
His painting was also used during the presentation of the Tennis World Cup won by Boris Becker in 1996, it was used as a connection between Art and Tennis.
"I was in Nigeria on a visit when the mayor of Duseldorlf called me to return to Germany, I didn't know that my painting was going to be picked."
Plans are on the way by Goethe institute to bring Chidi back home for an exhibition. How does he feel about this? "I've been looking forward to bringing my work home because Africa is the continent that helped me mentally to get to where I am now."
How lucrative has Art been for him?
"In Art, the pride of any artist is not money. You allow it influence your work. But it is also important that an artist is making a living from his art. If you ask me how lucrative is it? For the sake of other artistes back in Nigeria, who are about to drop their creativity because of hardship, I would tell them that the sky is the limit.
It is a gradual process. You have to be strong in spirit. It is worth it. The Nigerian Artist should gloablize their arts. They should make it understandable to the world. I am very proud of Africa, my continent, but I make art. I don't make African art, I don't make German art, or American Art. The African artist should make art that is universal. He should be experimental and create what can be consumed internationally."

Original_Search_Title: Reporter's Diary: Now Guobadia Can Stay!
Headline: Nigeria: Reporter's Diary: Now Guobadia Can Stay!
Body_Text:
UNTIL last week I had thought that pressure from some quarters that Sir Abel Guobadia should resign as the Chairman of the Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) was in order.
Indeed shortly after the most popular political godfather in Anambra, Mr. Chris Uba apologized for having rigged his godson, Governor Chris Ngige to power and the verdict of the Court of Appeal on Ogun state election indicting the Commission, published, I was tempted to simply ask the old man to quit.
Yes, my thinking was that if a man as principled as Guobadia presided over an election in which many serious politicians including some good friends of mine were rigged out, he should have no business still occupying the chairmanship position of the Commission. And this idea was to have been reflected in a personal letter I made up my mind to write to him.
But I had one fear: that of having my letter leaked to the press. Perhaps like many Nigerians, I was afraid of re-staging something like the Obasanjo-Ogbeh drama, particularly since I saw myself in the Audu Ogbeh position - the more vulnerable one. I was sure I would be easily victimized. Though the issue of resignation would not have come up between Guobadia and I, because I have nothing in common with him in this regard. But he could successfully have asked me not to appear even 10 metres before the gate of his Commission. The gate men would gleefully have carried out the order. And when this happened, unlike Ogbeh I would not even have had the privilege of treating Guobadia to a good pounded yam lunch in my poor house.
Having read the Commission's defence (it called it clarifications), contained in the well-crafted release concerning the elections in Ogun State and Anambra I was happy that I never wrote the letter after all. INEC "clarifications", which was signed by its director of public affairs, Steve Osemeke, to be sure was plausible enough to convince any Doubting Thomas that the 2003 elections' perfection was absolute.
It is against this backdrop that I wonder why the Commission had to hide that long before opening up to deflate critics' weak arguments and sentiments against its marvelous performance in that election. Perhaps the only convincing reason for this delay could be situated in the fact that while cheap allegations are easy to make, a good defence such as INEC offered always requires a lot of imagination and creativity.
Of course, nothing could be as challenging and demanding as defending yourself against allegations of which you are completely innocent. But we must be careful not to misunderstand the Commission's "clarifications". Which is why I have offered within my limit to explain to critics and skeptics alike who have made it an article of faith not to even see anything sweet in their mother's soup, what the Commission really was talking about.
And because the message was so fantastic, I shall endeavour to quote appropriately. Said the Commission: "the Independent National Electoral Commission as a statutory body was never on trial in the Presidential Election Petition. The Commission was only joined in the petition as an umpire, whose duty was to assist the court in doing justice".
And this is very true! The point the Commission is trying to make could be understood better from this analogy: once an old man organised a wrestling contest in his house. He had categorically ruled out the use of lethal weapons by any of the wrestlers. And at the end of the contest, which he supervised, one of the wrestlers was hacked to death with a machete. When the winner was sued along with the umpire, the latter argued that he was not the man who killed the victim, he only organised the contest.
I think I have forgotten what the court said Another very important one is here, "it is pertinent to make reference to the disparity as regards the voter turn out in the presidential and governorship election in some states. It must be pointed out that there was no state of the federation where the voting figures in the two elections were the same. The presidential election generated greater interest and wider participation among the electorate in many states", said the Commission.
Then INEC was magnanimous enough to give as examples the case of controversial Ogun State election and that of Benue. In Ogun State, out of the l.57million registered voters, 1.3 milion people voted during the presidential election, while over 738,000 people voted in the gubernatorial elections. Though since the examples it provided to substantiate the difference in presidential and gubernatorial polls record were only in the PDP controlled states (Benue and Ogun); and not in any ANPP state for instance, critics were bound to assume that because much more rigging was perpetrated by the PDP, the Commission could easily rely on these "riggers states". But that would be wrong. Damn wrong!
The other big one is that of Chris Uba, who claimed that in a well organised election like that of the 2003, he still found a way of rigging his godson, Ngige to power. "The onus of proof lies on Uba", said the Commission. And that was simply the best way to treat such weighty allegation. But I think that where Uba seems to be having problem is in his arrogance in implying that he alone rigged the election. Without the upport of the Commission. And this is what the Commission is simply asking him to prove. And of course that will be impossible, unless he gives credit also to some INEC officials who helped him to fix the votes for his godson.
Where I was a little bit disappointed with the Commission was in giving the impression that after so many days of hiding it could not provide equally concrete reasons to why, for instance, it refused to appear before the Appeal Court with the certified copies of some result sheets. Here, the electoral umpire simply said, "the reason for the non production is being looked into". But I think it was the cheapest question the Commission would ever have answered. I have no problem with the simplistic nature of INEC's "is being looked into" answer, because that's the most honest way to answer a tricky question. But the Commission might well have said about the certified true copies, this way: "We don't have it! Or we lost it!!" Either is even more convincing that the "is being looked into."
Now, having read the whole clarifications, I wish to state that the Commission and particularly Guobadia have no need to be crucified. The Commission has answered all queries correctly. And honestly too! We only hope that Guobadia will be given another opportunity in 2007. You don't expect that election to be "flawful", do you?

Original_Search_Title: Audu Ogbeh: Who Next?
Headline: Nigeria: Audu Ogbeh: Who Next?
Body_Text:
Lagos — The asphyxiating supremacy battle in Anambra State has just claimed a
prized casualty in the person of Chief Innocent Audu Ogbeh, the gutsy [he
said Baba liked his guts] chairman of Peoples Democratic Party (PDP).
His resignation from the plum office was an anticlimax of sorts. After
weeks of cloak-and-dagger manoeuvring Ogbeh who showed off as the moral
beacon of the Machiavellian PDP finally succumbed to President Obasanjo's
arm-twisting strategy and threw in the towel.
At the behest of the bellyaching President, the recently concluded
National Executive Meeting of the PDP, where the Ogbeh-Obasanjo collision
was billed to top on the agenda was dropped apparently to allow the
pro-Ogbeh forces in the party to let down their guards. The duo even came to
the meeting in the same car. That is the President's modus operandi as
recent events showed.
In July, 2000, Obasanjo commissioned late Dr. Chuba Okadigbo's official
residence as Senate president at Apo parliamentary quarters, Abuja, and even
capped it with a dance with Okadigbo's wife. One month later, Okadigbo came
tumbling from his high place as Citizen No. 3 in a convoluted plot which our
vindictive president is not totally innocent of .
Just like Judas Iscariot's giveaway kiss to Jesus Christ at the garden
of Gethsemane, Obasanjo's parting kiss to Audu Ogbeh came by way of a
pounded yam lunch together at the home of the later. Thus the deal to have
Ogbeh's head on a platter like that of Biblical John the Baptist was sealed
and delivered.
How did Ogbeh suddenly find himself holding the wrong end of the stick
in his hitherto chummy relationship with the president? The relationship
went sour as a result of an innocuous but patriotic letter Ogbeh wrote to
the President on the festering Anambra crisis. Since then the president has
been bellyaching, brooding and sulking, generally behaving like the bull
that has been shown the red flag. He has been baying for Ogbeh's blood.
Ogbeh's letter reminds one of the late sage Chief Obafemi Awolowo's
prophetic warning to President Shehu Shagari during the Second Republic
about Nigeria's ship of state heading for the rocks. Because of petty
politics President Shagari elected to ignore Awo's warning with dire
consequences for the nation. Like Shagari, Obasanjo roundly ignored the
message contained in the letter and went after the messenger with a cudgel,
nay sledgehammer.
If President Obasanjo ever manifested traits of an iron-fisted ruler in
his first (1999-2003), now he is a full blown case. To expect Obasanjo whose
carriage and temperament are so manifestly military and with a dictatorial
wing to fly in a political field that is fluid, dynamic and constantly
changing is to live on the sugar candy mountain. He believes that he is some
kind of apparatus through which God may manifest His intentions for
Nigeria. He thinks he is Solomon for wisdom, Alexander the Great for majesty
and Jesus Christ for godliness. And he really does not like to hear anybody
say anything to the contrary. As far as he is concerned his government's
coin has only one side: his views.
