Once his ambition for the post of secretary-general of the United
Nations was scuttled, the onetime military head of state has been
globe-trotting and grandstanding as a world-renowned troubleshooter. This
show might win him a Nobel prize for peace. But Obasanjo the peacemaker is a
world different from Obasanjo the president. He is a bundle of crisis back
home in Nigeria as the Owu chieftaincy tussle, the Anambra crisis and the
countless internal wrangling in PDP have shown. Instead of working on his
home-front image and taking steps to remedy his image and that of Nigeria,
he continues to carry on as though all is well. In his words, " I dey
kampe."
With this philosophy, the President addresses nearly every
challenge. But kampeism as the president employs it amounts to
oversimplification and overconfidence of no mean proportion.
The President feels he has monopoly of wisdom. But nobody wants an
all-knowing president. The philosopher king was theoretically unrealizable
let alone having one in Obasanjo. The thought of having a president who acts
like the boss in the mechanic workshop in the running of the government is
frightening. Nigeria deserves a functional political machinery for the
production and implementation of quality policies. He prides himself as
having an unimaginable energy for state affairs. But he should be informed
that governance is not about brawn but about rigorous and stout
reasoning. Let him democratise governance and evolve workable economic
programmes.
With his I-know-all disposition spiced with his messianic complex, it is
not really surprising that Obasanjo has been fumbling. He must learn to be
tolerant of views that do not tally with his.
On the Anambra crisis, the one-month suspension slammed on Ngige was in
bad taste and merely diversionary. The crisis has lingered for this long
simply because Obasanjo has decided to play the ostrich. The truth is
whether Ngige collects soup with his greying moustache he was declared
governor. That is all that should concern likes of the president, Chief Uba
and they ought to relate with him as a state chief executive. Chris Uba and
his cohorts must be made to face the music for the only reason baby snakes
are killed promptly is to prevent them from reaching adulthood when they
would become unstoppably deadly.
In any society, triumph of evil according to a philosopher, is premised
on three main features which are intricately interrelated. These are the
inaction of those that should have acted, the indifference of those that
should have known better and the silence of the voice of justice. I would
like to add a fourth which is what I qualify as actions, collusions and
deliberate machinations of those whose interests are served by the triumph
of evil.
In the words of distinguished German poet; "In Germany, they first came
for the communists. And I did not speak out because I was not a
communist. Then they came for the Jews. And I did not speak out because I
was not a Jew. Then they came for the trade unionists. And I did not speak
out because I was not a trade unionist. They came for the Catholics. And I
did not speak out because I was a protestant. Then they came for me. And by
the time there was no one left to speak out."

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria As Cinema
Headline: Nigeria As Cinema
Body_Text:
Lagos — THOSE who say Nigerians don't go to the cinema are right. We don't, really. But who can begrudge us. We are the cinema. Nigeria is a theatre, a moving video. In the market place, village square, government house, home, bus stop, school, banking hall, even in the church and mosque, there is always something theatrical, comical and farcial about us.
Now you can understand why the home video industry is flourishing. At every turn, there is always a movie near you. It is an expansive, convoluted maze of blockbusters. What comes close to the Nigerian model is akin to having your own cinema with over one million movies showing simultaneously.
Yes, that is the way it is. We are actors, actresses, directors and producers. Our offer is a hotch-potch of drama, comedy, thriller, romance, action, scientific fiction, horror, adventure, musical and the downright erotic.
Reproduced here under are excerpts from a few titles of my catalogue of the Nigerian movie matrix:
-Missing in Custody: An action drama starring an illegal oil bunkering ship, MT African Pride, three Rear-Admirals and some Naval ratings. Engaging, intriguing and compelling, the movie set in the Nigerian coastal water takes you into the belly of the Atlantic Ocean and exposes the messy oil deals perpetrated by a hard-to-burst cartel. An illegal oil bunkering ship was impounded by the Navy. After a few phone calls, money transfer and subtle threats both the ship and her occupants disappeared. And then a twist in the tale: two of the three Admirals were dismissed. But one of them insists there is dirty, putrid rump hidden inside the immaculate white uniform of his traducers. You can be sure you haven't seen the end of this drama in the sea. I will keep you posted. Believe me!
-Desperate in Abuja: An ice-cool inter-continental blockbusting thriller starring Joshua Dariye, the once-beaten governor of Plateau State, President Olusegun Obasanjo and the British metropolitan police. A run-away governor who lost his stool in a highly organised political plot was caught in the streets of London lugging big cash in major international currencies. A quick, eagle-eye scan by the metropolitan police revealed other things - the governor was keeping healthy foreign accounts in clear violation of the laws of his country. The governor who was deposed in a crookedly contrived emergency rule by the Presidency soon returned home to face his enemies in Abuja. But the governor rather than show remorse and get a pat on the back for stealing only small cash, was sounding off, threatening to pull down the roof and expose the rot in "all of us". To check the governor from getting too cheeky, Abuja got desperate. Pronto, it assembled the video clips of the governor's dirty deals and got some very important Nigerians to watch it. It is an Aso Films offering with Baba as producer. A must watch for those who want to be politically wise.
-Restless in Awka: This is another Aso Films high-noon comedy starring Chris Ngige (the godson), Chris Uba (the godfather), Abel Guobadia (the umpire) with Audu Ogbeh and Baba as witnesses. The setting is Awka, the Anambra State capital. Awka is not your idea of a bustling capital city. But ever since the movie was premiered, the once sleepy town has lost its innocence. Voodoo, treachery, blackmail and gangsterism were thrown into the fray and thereafter neither the actors, the producers nor the by-standers could sleep with both eyes closed. Trouble.
A diminutive governor, the godson of a barely literate political godfather was shooed into office with the devious assistance of a partisan umpire. Once in the saddle, the savour and flavour of power sipped into his head. He steeled himself to face the challenges of his office but the godfather had other ideas. He wants to run the state while the godson merely swaggers here, there and everywhere as a figurehead governor. No deal, says the godson. And that redirected the course of the plot. In an unprecedented macabre twist, the governor was abducted by the police. He survived that. He was spuriously sacked by a court order. Again, he weathered the storm.
All manner of missiles were hauled at the governor by the godfather with of course tacit support from the Presidency, but he deployed his miniature size to full advantage, ducking some of the hot pellets and warding off the rest with his Mullah-style beard. Awka has since been restless and so is Abuja. The latest is that both the godfather and the godson have been suspended from their Politically Deceptive People (PDP) club. But don't thrash your ticket yet. Keep it for the second part of this made-for-TV movie. Watch out.
-The Fuhrer's Fury: Intrigue, back-stabbing and debauchery were built into this not-for-the-squeamish movie. And if you are the type that would easily squeak and swoon at the sight of any scandal, if you are the moral purist who can't stand the stench of sycophancy, sleaze and sybaritic corruption, please don't bother to see this movie. Two pals, one, the president and the other the chairman of the club and aide of the President suddenly discover they are no longer wearing the same coat of many colours. So they decided to part ways but not without a fight. The chairman wired a letter to the President but did not forget to drop a copy for the prying press. He accused the President of fuelling trouble in Anambra and over-heating the already febrile polity. He prophetically warned the President of the inevitability of insurrection if he doesn't change his ways.
But the President is a war veteran and was not willing to swallow the insult from a man he taught the art of money-making while in the service of the nation. So he picked his pen and exposed in lurid, lewd details the influence of lust and lucre in the club. The President literally got off the handle when he admitted in his letter that in their club of which the whistle blower is chairman, they are all election riggers, hedonists and a band of thieves who dip their light fingers into the nation's till.
Afraid that the brickbat between the twosome would snowball into a volcano whose eruption would expose the innards of the club, members got together to douse the flame. But they never did. They ended up eating pounded yam served on a platter of suspicion. It turned out an anti-climax but the theatrics and hysterics have refused to leave our sub-conscious. The high suspense built into the movie stands it out in the landscape of inanities and absurdities that hallmark our polity.When the dust appeared to have settled, one casualty lay on the floor: the chairman resigns. Preen your ears, more details coming soon.
-Lawless Lawmakers: This is a true life drama which exposes the seething underbelly of the National Assembly where sleaze, sex, assault and battery are never in short supply. A female senator appropriates money meant for her committee. A male colleague, bewildered, asks questions. But he gets a cold snub. She, too, gets a hot macho slap. A week or so later, a female member of the House of Representatives claimed that a male colleague was eyeing her one kind, so she returned the unparliamentary admiration with an uncomplimentary slap. And the plot thickens. The rest is a dog-eat-dog story that advertises the quality of lawmakers running the show at the National Assembly. The movie is co-produced by Senate President Adolphus Wabara and Speaker Aminu Bello Masari.
-The Prisoner: This is a superbly spruced story of vendetta. It revolves around aristocratic Major Hamza Mustapha who was Chief Security Officer to the late dictator, General Sani Abacha. Mustapha was a mean, no-nonsense officer whose loyalty to his master was oiled with blood, human blood. At the snap of their fingers, they could order a whole family killed. And everybody was a suspect. They found the chief suspect in retired General Olusegun Obasanjo, the current President. To get him out of the way, a phoney charge of coup-plotting was levelled against him and a few others. Pronto, he was hauled into jail. But fate pops up in the most unusual places and at unusual times. And it did for Obasanjo. He was released by Abacha's successor whereupon he later became President. It was now his turn to test Mustapha's will and staying power. The highly dreaded major was ferried into prison custody. But aristocrats are not used to confinements of any type so he decided to call some friends. That singular call has since added more fizz to his cup of worries. He is being charged with the same offence upon which he and his master jailed the President. But is it for real? Book your copy in advance for this feel-good action drama about a young military officer too spoilt to curb his excesses.
-Rebels in the Creeks: If you love the staccato sound of AK-47 machine gun, if you love the allure and morbid smell of detonated bomb, then this is your best bet. An engaging action thriller featuring Asari Dokubo, a middle-age freedom fighter who looks more like a Mandingo warrior than a Buguma, Rivers State son, Ateke Tom and a rag-tag army of Niger Delta youths who shot their way through the creeks to the Presidency. This movie captures the grim and gritty struggle among Niger Delta youths for resource control, self-determination and against sundry injustices.
Dokubo, leader of the Niger Delta Peoples Volunteer Force (NDPVF), a band of youths bearing AK-47 machine guns threatens to blow up the nation's oil wells and pipelines if his people's requests are not met. A distraught Presidency called his bluff and threatened to raze the entire region in a mortal adventure of bombardment. But wise counsel prevailed when it dawned on the Presidency that the threat was for real. Global economy was also threatened as the price of crude oil simply broke loose from the grip of OPEC. Pressure mounts on Abuja from everywhere, and a tempered President Obasanjo despatches a Presidential jet to bring Dokubo to Abuja for talks. The rest is woven into this fascinating, seductive and heroic expose on when a government that practises zero-tolerance for the opposition meets it match. Expect more from this battle of wit in the New Year.
Now, you know why Nigerians don't go to the cinema. Going to the cinema is a dead culture here. We don't need a special hall fitted with projector, screen and all. The entire nation is one big cinema hall. And wherever you are, there is always a movie showing. I have just treated you to a few from my 2004 collection of evergreen movies. Expect more this year. It, sure would be worth your while. Happy viewing.

Original_Search_Title: American Embassy Awards Journalists
Headline: Uganda: American Embassy Awards Journalists
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE US embassy has awarded three journalists for their excellent reporting on environment, economic development and conflict resolution.
Radio Simba senior journalist Emma Okella won the inaugural Award for Excellency in reporting on environmental issues.
Machrine Birungi, the Prime FM news editor, won the award for the best economic development story.
Mama FM's F. Luyombya won in the category for conflict resolution.
Okella's winning story was entitled "Kampala's Yams."
The US Embassy and the East African Media institute organised the awards.
The US ambassador, Jimmy Kolker, handed over the awards to the winners at the Nommo Gallery yesterday.

Original_Search_Title: And the President Blinked!
Headline: Nigeria: And the President Blinked!
Body_Text:
It was Dean Rusk, Secretary of State under the charismatic President
John F. Kennedy at the time of the Cuban Missiles crises in 1962 who
enriched us with the famous line: "We were standing eye ball to eye ball and
the other guy just blinked!"
The crises had reached a crescendo precipitating the American Naval
blockade of the island and subsequently the botched Bay of Pigs invasion
planned by the CIA to overthrow Fidel Castro. It was the closest the world
ever came to full-blown nuclear warfare between the then Soviet Union led by
the flamboyant and equally gregarious Nikita Khrushchev and the United
States under the presidency of the youthful and charismatic Kennedy. As the
Soviet ships steamed towards Cuba the world held its breath mindful of the
ultimate consequences of a possible outbreak nuclear of nuclear war, but at
the end of the day the USSR backed down prompting Rusk to utter his now
famous quote. It was a true test of nerve between the super powers but at
the end the world and indeed humanity sighed with relief at the peaceful
outcome.
A few weeks ago Nigerians also work up to be confronted by a missiles
crises of a different sort, only this time, the projectiles had no nuclear
warheads but came in the form of the venomous letters and verbal exchanges
between the president renowned for his short fuse, and Audu Ogbeh his party
chairman. To say that the president was infuriated by Ogbeh's letter will be
an understatement. True to his vindictive nature he immediately went for the
chairman's jugular, using all the resources at his disposal. First, his
yes-men and fixers within the PDP roundly insulted Ogbeh to set the stage
for his removal from office. Second, not a few members of its National
Working Committee suddenly cleared the logs from their eyes and realized
that the man was not only a frequent violator of the party's constitution,
but also imprudent in the handling of its finances. They then invited the
EFCC to apply the Coup-de-grass. It was all so laughable and easily
predictable. It happened to both Na'abba and Okadigbo before Ogbeh only this
time, the EFCC replaced the ICPC as the ideal weapon of choice.
Although Na'abba craftily avoided impeachment and humiliation, the
'system' still ensured he never returned to trouble the president's sleep
again in his second term. In Na'abba's place, we ended up with a certain
sleepy-eyed Aminu Masari who often appeared bored and uninterested by all
the fuss around him. As for Okadigbo, well, he simply died a 'natural' death
aided by a few puffs of gaseous substances he 'accidentally' inhaled not too
far away from where Tafa Balogun's Storm Troopers were deployed to keep the
peace at a rally in Kano. For his intransigence and insistence on the
principle of separation of powers and bravado over the sanctity of Senate's
mace, we ended up with Wabara who was not even on the ballot in his
constituency. How pitiful! Well, before I digress, that was the situation
leading to last week's meeting of the PDP's National Executive
Committee. The president's men were given quality time by state media to
prepare the grounds for Ogbeh's virtual disrobement and even arrest. The
paid columnists were in their full element and truly earned their pay.
Before the event we were gleefully told that president was to throw down
the gauntlet at his party men and force a straight choice between himself
and the Chairman. It was to be the night of the long knives. Ogbeh's fate
appeared sealed, but then suddenly the president not only blinked, he
uncharacteristically backed down! Not a few people were amazed at the turn
of events but I wasn't. Yes, the Vice president might have intervened to
save the Chairman's neck while some Governors we were told, even threatened
to embarrass the president openly by defying him over the matter. But to me,
he had no other option open to him. In my opinion he badly miscalculated in
his misjudgement of the public mood and indeed the apprehension of others
within the party. He forgot that the patience of Nigerians was stretched to
the limit by the Anambra cauldron and indeed his handling and management of
the entire polity. He forgot that Nigerians are a lot wiser than in 2005
than in 1999 when he endlessly sparred with Okadigbo and Na'abba. Then, a
great majority believed the nonsense about his learning process as an excuse
for his failures, and gave him the benefit of the doubt. But more important,
he had become a liability to his party without even realizing it!
They perceived from his handling of the episodes involving Salisu Buhari
and erstwhile strongman of the Ministry of Defence during his first term
that the president had nothing in common with justice or even fair play. The
unprecedented frauds involving the electoral act and the 4/19 elections
coupled with and despicable events in Anambra state further convinced them
that the man cannot be trusted. They know from what transpired at Odi and
Zaki-Biam that is merely a dictator in civilian garb. They are conscious
from his handling of the economy and lack of attention to their basic social
needs that this quite easily, must be the most inept administration in the
history of the nation. As I write, the morgues in Abuja are overflowing with
dead bodies because the doctor's strike is still in force. Surely, these
home truths would have been brought home to the president during his
nocturnal meetings and consultations with the party hierarchy. Indeed a
rancorous ending to the crises would have further inflamed the polity and
jeopardized their stolen mandate. This much was evident from the initial
missile Ogbeh fired at the president, which ignited the sordid affair. In
spite of himself, this truism obviously registered with the president. We
are all being taken for a ride.
To that extent therefore, the 'peaceful' ending to the feud between the
president and his chairman only serves to bide time before the impending
doom. I won't even dwell too much on the decisions taken on the Uba and
Ngige imbroglio because it was comical and diversionary to say the
least. The one-month suspension of the two mutual felons from the party
insults our intelligence and will serve little purpose, and if the police
are fully conscious of their constitutional responsibilities to the Nigerian
state if not the ordinary tax payer, they would not have waited for orders
from the PDP before hauling both men before the law. No, I submit that the
bizarre ending to the dispute was the only option left open to the president
and the party in a nation already wary of their endless shenanigans. A
different ending would have further alienated an administration already
reeling from the negative effects of a questionable mandate.
We were gleefully told that in the new spirit of dÃ©tente the president
visited the Ogbeh at home after the meeting and had a sumptuous meal of
pounded yam. Well, pounded yam can be delicious especially when accompanied
by the right vegetable soup. But if I were PDP Chairman, I will be very wary
of the gesture. In the heat of his face-off with the then Senate president
Chuba Okadigbo, the president not only wined and dined with him at the
special dedication of his official residence, he even danced with his wife
in supposed truce! Unknown to the man, the theatrics were all a photo trick
and part of a grand strategy. Okadigbo made the fatal error in thinking he
was in league with principled men and women of unquestionable character and
integrity. Sadly, at the crucial moment when he needed the support of the
likes of Mantu and Florence Ita-Giwa whom he wrongly assumed to be his
pillars of strength, they simply vanished from his corner without a
trace. The lettered man took the president's knockout blow flush on the jaw
and never recovered until he died. He may have been a Professor of political
science, but this time he was caught unawares by the latest trend in
political bankruptcy and schism. The greatest beneficiaries apart from the
president, was the Ghana-Must-Go industry which reaped bountifully from the
unexpected dividends of democracy.
The rest, as we know, is now history. Mantu was shortly thereafter
rewarded with the post of Deputy Senate presidency for his treachery, while
Ita-Giwa is now the president's Special Adviser on Nation Assembly Affairs!
Who said the president lacked consistency? For someone who was in the tick
of the action at the time dozens of stuffed Ghana-Must-Go criss-crossed the
assembly complex like bicycles through Tiananmen Square, it is not
surprising that with Ita-Giwa as his main liaison with the National
Assembly, the place is now a haven of peace where acquiescence to the
president's whims and caprices is the beginning of wisdom or even
livelihood. Not even the slapping extravaganza could shatter the new spirit
of camaraderie in its ranks. My only fear for Ogbeh at this present time is
when I look around him I see flip-flops and a potential Brutus like Godwin
Daboh in his corner. He may have only ten months to sit out his tenure, but
if he relies on such characters for his political survival, then he may as
well start looking for a new vocation. But whatever the case Nigerians are
ultimately the greatest losers form the impasse. Having barely survived
removal and disgrace, the president surely now has him firmly tucked under
his armpit.
Still, in the end, the entire episode was yet another family affair,
just like the Mafia!

Original_Search_Title: Rumours of Peace
Headline: Nigeria: Rumours of Peace
Body_Text:
Lagos — Reports of the death of the Peoples Democratic Party were grossly
exaggerated - but so also were reports of its survival.
To everyone who is very conversant with the power play in the party, the
politicians and the militicians are only on a retreat. Political players
fight and sheathe their swords to live and fight another day. Those who
don't know when to retreat or surrender get captured. Those who understand
the game very well surrender before they are captured. But to the
uninitiated, it is peace at last. Thank God.
Before last Tuesday's meeting of the National Executive Committee (NEC)
of the PDP, the stakes were sky-high. The PDP was drenched in tension, and
not even the forewarning that it was going to be a one-item agenda could
dissuade those out to grab Chief Audu Ogbeh, the National Chairman of the
party, by the throat and strangulate him, as it were. They had prepared a
dossier on Ogbeh, whom they accused of not planning to leave his exalted
seat "penniless". Ogbeh's accusers itemised allegations of corruption and
abuse of power quite articulately, and they also provided an overwhelming
list of those on their side, starting with the President himself.
The inclusion of Obasanjo's name on the list was credible, though. Using
ink and paper as their weapons, Obasanjo and Ogbeh had waged war against
each other. Both of them painted each other in black ink. The tone was
Anambra, but the undertone was maybe 2007. Former military president,
Gen. Ibrahim Babangida, who is believed to be eyeing a return to Aso Rock in
2007, is said to be very uncomfortable with the chairmanship of Ogbeh which
may give Vice President Atiku Abubakar a headstart in the 2007 presidential
race.
Ogbeh's letter to Obasanjo was about Anambra, but party insiders believe
it was about 2007. It was about using Anambra to determine who is actually
in control of the party. Is it the faction loyal to Obasanjo, that is, the
Uba Group, IBB and co? Or is it the faction loyal to Atiku, that is, Ogbeh
and co? If the Anambra case was favourably resolved in favour of the
governor, Dr. Chris Ngige, then Atiku has carried the day. If it was
resolved in favour of Uba, then the Obasanjo faction is the winner.
That was probably why the president splashed more ink on Ogbeh than
Ogbeh sprinkled on him. Obasanjo tried as much as possible to paint him
black in full measure, and the battle began. The IBB sub-faction quickly
teamed up with Obasanjo. The Atiku faction got ready for the battle. If the
IBB faction succeeded in rooting out the Atiku influence, it would have
taken over the party machinery significantly, thereby diminishing Atiku's
hopes and dreams.
Before Tuesday's meeting, the atmosphere was hazy. The Atiku camp was
cocksure Ogbeh's opponents would not get the majority of support, but the
Obasanjo camp was not too sure of the outcome. An intelligent mental survey
of the situation was necessary. With the majority of the governors backing
Atiku/Ogbeh for whatever motive, the Obasanjo/IBB group decided to
retreat. He who fights and runs away lives to fight another day - if
something else does not kill him.
At the meeting proper, the president was no longer disposed to deposing
Ogbeh. Not that he now likes him because of the joint pounded yam meal they
shared. Not that the "sins" of the National Chairman had been forgiven. Not
that there was rapprochement. Not that Obasanjo wanted to save the ruling
party, the self-acclaimed biggest party in Africa, from further crisis. Not
at all. Indeed, the General only retreated; he did not surrender. No General
wants to be humiliated, not at least, by "bloody civilians". The General's
tactics are close to his chest. There is peace, all right. But while
everybody is saying peace, safety, then the General knows how to engineer a
suddenly destruction.
Former Senate President, Dr. Chuba Okadigbo, is not alive to tell the
story. The Afenifere and the former Alliance for Democracy (AD) governors
are telling their own stories from the discomfort of their sitting
rooms. Alhaji Ghali Umar Na'Abba, the former Speaker of the House of
Representatives, is alive and kicking himself silly for believing in the
peace of the graveyard. And so on and so forth.
At the NEC meeting, Obasanjo tactically killed the move to sack
Ogbeh. We're here over Anambra, he said. A governor reminded him that the
real moral question was his handling of the matter. The principal characters
- Ngige and Uba - were suspended from the party pending investigation into
the rigging allegation. To humour the president and make him feel like he
had not lost out completely, the majority voted for Ngige's suspension. And
even though this was meant to project the impression that there were no
winners and no losers, truth was that there were winners and losers.
Did the president win or lose? That is debatable. But, at least, the
motion for Ogbeh's suspension was not moved (even though it would have been
illegal). So we cannot be too sure if Ogbeh would have remained on his seat
or not, and if the president would have been humiliated or not.
At the end of the day, PDP is now at peace, the kind of peace that is
worse than war.

Original_Search_Title: 2007: the Battle for Aso Rock
Headline: Nigeria: 2007: the Battle for Aso Rock
Body_Text:
Lagos — Robert Greene, author of the best-selling treatise, The 48 Laws of
Power, is right: Everyone has insecurities.
When you show yourself in the world and display your talents, you
naturally stir up all kinds of resentment, envy and other manifestations of
insecurity. This is to be expected. You cannot spend your life worrying
about the petty feelings of others. With those above you, however, you must
take a different approach: When it comes to power, outshining the master is
perhaps the worst mistake of all... Never imagine that because the master
loves you, you can do whatever you want. Entire books could be written about
favourites who fell out of favour by taking their status for granted, for
daring to outshine.
If your ideas are more creative than your master's, ascribe them to him,
in as public a manner as possible. Make it clear that your advice is merely
an echo of his advice... If you are naturally more sociable and generous
than your master, be careful not to be the cloud that blocks his radiance
from others. He must appear as the sun around which everyone revolves,
radiating power and brilliance, the centre of attention. Any attempt to
impress him with your grace and generosity can prove fatal.
It is not a weakness to disguise your strengths if in the end they lead
to power. By letting others outshine you, you remain in control, instead of
being a victim of their insecurity. This will all come in handy the day you
decide to rise above your inferior status. There can be only one sun at a
time. Never obscure the sunlight, or rival the sun's brilliance, rather,
fade into the sky and find ways to heighten the master's intensity.
In the final hours that preceded the 2003 presidential primaries of the
ruling Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), Vice President Atiku Abubakar found
himself making a statement that sounded innocuous, yet one that was filed
somewhere for reference purposes. The BBC had asked him about what his
decision concerning his stake in the process was. The Turakin Adamawa
responded that he had three options before him. One, be his own man and run
for the ticket for the top job for which he had already purchased a
nomination form. Two, tow the status quo, which meant he could be President
Olusegun Obasanjo's running mate. Three, try a new boss by teaming with
Dr. Alex Ekwueme. He said he was yet to make up his mind on the
subject.
Those who were close to Obasanjo revealed that the man was disappointed
but that he kept his calm. The battle for the party's flag was then fierce
and even with the enormous resources and orchestrated goodwill at his
disposal, it was not a time to leave anything to chance. Not a time to let
the opportunity to lead a democratic Nigeria for the second time to slip
through his fingers. But here was someone he had trusted, someone he had
committed several responsibilities to for four years adding to his headache
when he was supposed to be a balm, a ready support in his quest to
consolidate, mobilise and utilise power. It was like a stab.
With a galaxy of PDP governors, who did not mind if a new face entered
Aso Rock, behind the VP, he was easily the star of the convention, a
deciding factor who could greatly influence the outcome of the
balloting. Reports had it that at a point, he led the "rebellious" governors
to Obasanjo where the deal was finally struck to give the president another
chance. By so doing, he killed two birds with one stone. He showed his boss
that he was loyal. Never mind how he defined it. He also showed his
superior, may be not deliberately, that he was indeed a powerful man. The
real man in charge as far as power politics go.
Those who claim to know the president insist that he does not find it
easy to forget the wrongs done to him. If he had secretly nursed any desire
to make it difficult or impossible for Atiku to succeed him, his
"disloyalty" during the ticket hunt was all he needed to put the last nail
on it.
Politics is a science of alternatives. A vocation for rewards,
compensations and, sometimes, revenge. It is a pendulum of power. The
naivety or outright ignorance about the dynamics of power oftentimes
attracts some punishment.
In 1999, after Obasanjo defeated Ekwueme to clinch the party's ticket,
it was largely the machinery left behind by the late Shehu Musa Yar'Adua and
whose leadership fell on Atiku that gave PDP and Obasanjo the day. His role
in 2003 further confirmed his rating as a man of clout, a kingmaker.
But a kingmaker is, after all, not a king. The king is the one who is in
charge, who holds the yam and the knife, the person who is recognised as the
source of the authority that keeps a domain going. Although there are
technical differences between monarchy and democracy, the principle of power
is fundamentally the same everywhere. There are usually no two number one
positions. The buck must always end somewhere. At the end of the day, it can
be hazardous to be on the wrong side of the person who holds the ace at any
given time, for the simple reasons that human beings can and do often play
God.
The climb from the number two slot to the first is a natural, legitimate
goal. But while it is smooth in some cases, it could prove tortuous in
others. Today, it is a known fact that Atiku wants to become Nigeria's
second president in the Fourth Republic come 2007. But his path is
punctuated with obstacles, with roadblocks that he probably had not
anticipated. It would, no doubt, bother him that the man he had, by his own
estimation and that of his followers, worked hard for to make president
would not give him the benefit of using the same ladder. The Chairman of
PDP, Chief Audu Ogbeh, and many governors are said to be on Atiku's
side. Former military president, General Ibrahim Babangida, his real
antagonist, the chairman of the Board of Trustees, Chief Tony Anenih, some
governors and other chieftains of the party are believed to have lined up
behind Obasanjo to shatter the dreams of the former Customs
officer. Everybody is in the game for a stake. Babangida may be making cold
calculations. By helping Obasanjo to neutralize Atiku, that could boost his
own chances in his race back to Aso Rock.
How things eventually play out may, however, depend on the extent to
which the wisdom of old is adhered to. Baltasar Gracian wrote in the 17th
Century: "Avoid outshining the master. All superiority is odious, but the
superiority of a subject over his prince is not only stupid but fatal. This
is a lesson that the stars in the sky teach us - they may be related to the
sun, and just as brilliant, but they never appear in her company." Like
Greene, Gracian may be right. That explain the cat and mouse relationship
between Obasanjo and Atiku; a relationship that may yet define future of
Nigeria democracy. For good or for ill

Original_Search_Title: Pawns On the Chessboard
Headline: Nigeria: Pawns On the Chessboard
Body_Text:
Lagos — Long before he picked the membership card of the ruling Peoples
Democratic Party (PDP) with the symbolic number 007, former military
president, General Ibrahim Babangida's image had already loomed large in the
party.
He became an issue quite early in the life of the party when he was
viewed as the unseen hand that manipulated former military Head of State,
General Abdulsalami Abubakar, to release the incarcerated General Olusegun
Obasanjo from prison and plot ways for him to emerge as the civilian
President of Nigeria.
It is recalled that on April 25, 1998 the late Minister of Internal
Affairs, Chief Sunday Afolabi, in an elaborate ceremony handed the PDP
membership card to Obasanjo. But before then, Babangida had played John the
Baptist (forerunner) when he visited Obasanjo in Abeokuta, soon after his
release to tell the world that he Obasanjo was indeed the type of leader
this country deserved at the moment. Although he did not expatiate on it,
what followed later explained everything. Obasanjo joined the PDP and the
coast became clear for him to emerge the flagbearer in the historic February
14, 1999 national convention (presidential primaries of the party) in Jos,
Plateau State.
But the foundation for the political gap which is fast becoming a gulf
was laid really less than 24 hours after Obasanjo emerged the PDP
flagbearer, precisely on February 15 when a governor-elect of Adamawa State,
Alhaji Atiku Abubakar was unexpectedly picked by Obasanjo as his running
mate ahead of noteable front-runners that included two former governors from
the north, Alhaji Abubakar Rimi (Kano) and Bamanga Tukur (old Gongola).
This choice did not go down well with the emerging political godfathers
- IBB and General Abubakar who although appreciated the laudable role of the
Yar'Adua group a.k.a. Peoples Democratic Movement PDM) in the swift
emergence of Obasanjo in the Jos Convention but were uncomfortable with
somebody outside their own political lineage to occupy the highest political
position to come from the geo-political north.
But Obasanjo thought otherwise, feeling that the injustice would be too
much if the Yar'Adua group were not properly and adequately compensated for
their role.
Since then, it has been a cat and mouse game with each group striving
always to discredit the other so as not to give them the chance to enjoy the
political leadership left for him as Vice President and the highest
political personality in PDP from the north.
The climax of the intrigue became very clear in the build-up to the
second term bid of President Obasanjo when both Atiku and IBB allegedly
agreed that Obasanjo should be stopped from going for a second term in the
interest of both the party and the country. The duo reportedly agreed to
give the ticket to the Chairman Board of Trustees of the party and Second
Republic Vice President Dr Alex Ekwueme. A lot of sensitisation was done
along this line with most leaders of the party, including about 15 of the 21
PDP state governors, falling in line. But right at the tail end of the
intrigue, Atiku backed out after he discovered that some members of the IBB
group were grooming the National Security Adviser (NSA) Major-General (rtd)
Aliyu Mohammed Gusau to be Ekwueme's running mate, a development that would
have finally sidelined him out of scheme of things in the party and the
national politics. He was to make the last minute u-turn that saw him and
his group backing Obasanjo to the detriment of Ekwueme and his unseen
backer, Babangida.
After assuming power for a second term with a better understanding of
the polity and a more solid political platform from his geo-political zone,
the South-West, President Obasanjo became the proverbial king with both the
knife and the yam in his court.
The President's move to exercise this power of political life and death
for any presidential ambitious persons in his party in 2007 is what has
generated all the crises now gripping the PDP. Both IBB and Atiku, through
their weighty political influence, have polarised the party from top to the
lowest ward level. The President's alleged soft spot for IBB rather than his
Vice has also further heightened the tension.
At the national level, right inside the Federal Executive Council, the
division has been glaring with the President and his Vice leading each
camp. Also, at the national hierarchy of the party, the picture is the same
with the National Chairman, Chief Audu Ogbeh, said to be leading a group for
Atiku group while the National Vice Chairman, South-West, Chief Olabode
George, is leading the Obasanjo/IBB group. Among the governors, the picture
is not different as the Atiku group enjoys a sizeable support of the
governors of Delta, Edo, Katsina, Plateau, Anambra, Adamawa, Akwa-Ibom,
Bayelsa, Gombe, Abia, Benue, Oyo, Kwara, Imo, Ebonyi, Kogi and Taraba while
the Obasanjo/IBB group enjoys the support of state governors of Enugu,
Rivers, Nasarawa, Cross River, Kaduna, Bauchi, Ogun, Ondo, Ekiti, Niger and
Osun states.
Recently, the Deputy National Chairman (South) of the party Chief
Shuaibu Oyedokun had to deny allegations of division of the party leadership
in his state, Osun, with him and the Governor, Prince Olagunsoye Oyinlola,
leading each camp. "There cannot be a collision course in a united house" he
said, stressing that 2007 should be left in the hands of God "who is the
ultimate decider." Chief Oyedokun is said to be heading the Atiku group
while the governor is leading the IBB group.
The recent letter drama between Obasanjo and Ogbeh and the attendant
division now apparent in the case, is just a climax of such polarisations
along the duo (IBB and Atiku). When the party experienced a major crisis in
Abia State, the approach of both the party leadership and the President were
guided by where they stood in the IBB-Atiku political equation. Ditto Imo
and the South-South crisis over a replacement for the late national vice
chairman Dikibo.
Perhaps, nothing has brought the division so glaring as the prolonged
crisis in Anambra State. In the first term, Obasanjo group backed the then
Governor Chinwoke Mbadinuju while Atiku backed the irrepressible business
mogul Chief Emeka Offor in a battle that brought governance in the state to
the ground. When Obasanjo group found Mbadinuju an unsellable political
product for the second term, they dumped him for now political foe,
Dr. Chris Ngige, whose tenure has since opened a chapter in the nation's
political history.
In a major political twist, the Atiku group became Ngige's backers as
his original associates now found him a political nut to crack.
When July 10, 2003 abduction case came up and the party leadership
guided by public opinion attempted to deal with the culprits, the Obasanjo
group waded in terming it a "family affair".
In January 4, 2004, Uba, a prominent member of the Obasanjo group was
said to have engineered a court case in Enugu where Justice Stanley Nnaji
(now retired) ruled that the Inspector General of Police should remove Ngige
from office as Governor of Anambra State. That failed as Atiku group through
Governor James Ibori of Delta State allegedly provided him some cover until
the storm cleared.
Few months later, at the National Assembly, the Obasanjo/IBB group
planned to institute a state of emergency in Anambra State but was quickly
aborted by the Atiku sympathisers.
The November 10, 2004 violent assault on major establishments including
Government House in Anambra State provided yet another breeding ground for
clash of the two political groups.
With public opinion clearly in their favour, the Atiku group felt the
latest arsonist attack provided an opportunity to settle once and for all
the vexed issues in Anambra State but their opponents would not see it as
such. For them, it is an opportunity to tackle once and for all a political
foe whose strategic position in the party is indeed an obstacle to their
main ambition in 2007, hence the Ogbeh-must-go schemings.
The January 4, 2005 crucial meeting whose sole agenda is Anambra issue
might turn out to be a titanic battle between the two groups on who controls
the party machinery. Can Obasanjo/IBB group dislodge the Atiku group? Will
this be the final battle for Chris Ngige? In January 4, 2004, Ngige survived
second attempt to steal him out of office, will he be lucky yet another
January 4, exactly one year after?
Either way it goes, the nation's political climate is being gauged by
two men whose ambition to occupy, or to determine who occupies, Aso Rock as
President in 2007 has become just the issue.

Original_Search_Title: Muslims to 'Empower Hungry' With R1.2m Food Aid
Headline: South Africa: Muslims to 'Empower Hungry' With R1.2m Food Aid
Body_Text:
Muslins across the Western Cape have collected R1.2 million worth of
food to be distributed among the poor and destitute for Eid ul Fitr.
The South African National Zakaah Fund (Sanzaf), Islamic Co-ordinating
Council, Mubarak Fitra fund, with 75 mosques and other organisations, have
pooled resources to provide more than 12 000 people with a food parcel
containing basic items to prepare a meal.
All Muslims are obliged to pay fitra, an annual tax the equivalent of
feeding one person for a day at the end of Ramadaan, which purifies their
deeds for the month and ensures the needs of the poor and destitute are
fulfilled on the day of Eid.
Members of the organisations and mosques collected the tax in their area
and jointly purchased or donated much-needed food condiments as well.
At the weekend various mosques and organisations began collecting the
food from centres like the Masjid Ighwatul Islam, the Bridgetown Mosque and
Sanzaf head office in Bridgetown to be packaged in their respective areas
and distributed to those in need.
Sanzaf spokesman Moulana Riedwan Edries said: "It is the perfect
opportunity to galvanise the community. They are able to actively be a part
of distributing their charity."
Cooking oil, rice, chicken, sweets, potatoes and onions are included in
the parcel to encourage recipients to do things for themselves.
"People need to be empowered to prepare their food with care, love and
affection. They are also able to prepare the food according to their own
taste," said Edries.
The food parcels should reach people before Eid on Thursday or Friday, a
day of celebration and the culmination of Ramadaan.

Original_Search_Title: Money Alone Can't Redeem Uganda's Dented Image!
Headline: Uganda: Money Alone Can't Redeem Uganda's Dented Image!
Body_Text:
Kampala — Depending on what happens in the next six months, the bank-breaking
strategy by the Uganda Tourist Board (UTB) to market the country on CNN will
be seen as brilliant or mud.
The idea is certainly a bold and modern equivalent of roadside billboard
that trumpets the beauty of a product to passersby, in this case, a tropical
paradise.
As the global billboard that reaches billions of potential visitors
looking for sweet vacation getaway spots, CNN will run 'Sights and Sounds of
Uganda' from the middle of this month through to mid-April which is
coincidentally the beginning of the tourist season in Europe and North
America.
In fact, the plan to market Uganda to the world employs the 'big bang'
formula used by cities and regions emerging from tough times of war,
epidemic, natural disaster and economic depression. For instance, in the
aftermath of the September 11 terrorists attack on New York, city leaders
moved swiftly to keep people coming to the beleaguered city. Mayor Rudy
Giuliani authorised $2.5m to buy 50,000 theatre tickets at reduced prices to
be given to the families of those affected by the attack and some to
shoppers who spent $500 in city stores. Hard-nosed advertisements throughout
the USA, Canada and Europe beckoned nervous visitors to defy the terrorists
by visiting the still smouldering city.
Many responded and New York is experiencing a big boom in
tourism. Meanwhile, after the SARS outbreak in April 2003 in Toronto which
killed 41 people, hotel occupancy rates dropped to less than 50 per
cent. The overall revenue in Toronto's four billion-dollar a year tourist
industry fell by more than $300m that year.
To jumpstart recovery, the city developed a five-year Tourism
Development Action Plan, and the Ontario government fronted immediate 10.7m
US dollars to Tourism Toronto to market the city as a fun, safe,
top-of-the-line business and leisure destination.
That summer, the largest ever Canadian rock concert in an abandoned
airfield headlined by Mick Jagger and the Rolling Stones, drew an estimated
half million audience. The message to the globe was very simple - Toronto is
open for business.
Hit by four major hurricanes last year and several times this year
including Katrina, Florida began luring scared tourists back to the Sunshine
State by launching a multi-million dollar advertising campaign to reassure
tourists and convention planners that it is okay to come to the state.
So far, the plan is working. By comparison, Uganda suffers a layer of
negative images dating back to the dictatorial years of Idi Amin, through
the mid-1980s images of skull and bone stacked along the roadside in
Luweero, to the current never-ending war in northern Uganda. The pictures of
cute little children scurrying at dusk to town-centres to find temporary
refuge from marauding rebels hardly inspire confidence in tourists. Next
week, Uganda's bleeding image will suffer further body blow on Saturday
October 22 when more than 40 cities worldwide join the Canadian-led Guluwalk
to draw attention to the plight of children in northern Uganda. In fact, the
US State Department - considered the oracle by many Americans and
non-Americans planning travel to world trouble-spots - has a travel advisory
warning would-be travellers to Uganda to "be aware of threats to their
safety from insurgent groups..."
Notwithstanding the argument that the Sheraton Kampala is probably safer
than New York's Waldorf-Astoria from potential terror activity, the average
sun-loving North American, Japanese, Canadian, Australian or European
planning a hassle-free fun-packed vacation, would choose the risk-free break
in Aruba or the Bahamas over Uganda's natural beauty stalked by machine-gun
totting rebels.
What this means is that crafting a spanking new image for Uganda abroad
is no small potato requiring more than the $1m paid to CNN so far. Foremost,
the best boost to Uganda's image abroad will be the swift conclusion to the
19-year northern war that continues unabated.
Moreover, things will brighten considerably for the entire country with
the resettlement to their former villages of citizens currently wasting away
in Internally Displaced Camps in northern Uganda. What better opportunity to
spread the good news of the new Uganda than to invite a beaming Oprah
Winfrey, arguably the most powerful media personality today, and the world
media to bid them farewell as they exit the gate of the camps!
The photo-op alone is worth at least $100m in free advertisement because
its immediate positive message on vacationing middle class Americans,
Canadians and Europeans is that Uganda, once thought dead like Lazarus,
lives again. Secondly, to compliment the CNN advertisement, regardless of
who wins next year's presidential election, Uganda needs a smooth transition
into multi-party politics.
Little will be gained from trumpeting the country's natural assets to
the world while encouraging political violence to run free like the wild
horses of Wyoming.
The bottom line is that with the meagre $1m advertisement on CNN, UTB
has placed a down payment toward the radical makeover of Uganda. To help the
process along, the government needs to clean up the war in the north,
repatriate IDP children and their parents back home, cut down crime and
corruption, and ensure violence-free elections next year. Do that and watch
the world join in the party and fun.

Original_Search_Title: Policeman, Colleague Jailed for Extortion
Headline: Zimbabwe: Policeman, Colleague Jailed for Extortion
Body_Text:
Harare — A POLICEMAN and his friend were yesterday jailed to an effective 18 months for extorting $80 000 and mobile phone recharge cards worth $30 000 at gunpoint.
Magistrate Mr Godfrey Unzemoyo initially sentenced Rutendo Mafunga (29) of Marondera Police Station and Innocent Mutsvairo (28) to 24 months in prison, but suspended six months of the sentence on condition of good behaviour.
Sentencing the two, Mr Unzemoyo said extortion was a serious offence regardless of amounts involved. Mr Unzemoyo added that the moral blameworthiness was high given that Mafunga committed an offence which he was supposed to guard against.
"Such people tarnish the image of the force. Crimes of extortion within the force have increased hence there is need to send a strong message to like-minded people.
"There is need for people to regain confidence in the country's justice delivery system," said Mr Unzemoyo.
The magistrate also noted that the two extorted money and recharge cards from defenceless minor vendors at gunpoint.
He said community service or imposing a fine would trivialise the offence.
Mafunga and Mutsvairo committed the offence on June 7, 2005 at Makoni Shopping Centre in Chitungwiza when they took advantage of Operation Murambatsvina.
They approached three vendors and identified themselves as policemen conducting the operation.
Mafunga went on to produce a gun, reminding the vendors that they were indulging in illegal activities.
They ordered the vendors to pay them
$80 000 and to give them recharge cards worth $30 000 in return for their freedom.
But a taxi driver, who had known their corrupt behaviour, saw them with the vendors and immediately rushed to make a report.
Realising that they had been observed, the two took to their heels but Mafunga was apprehended while hiding between sweet potato beds on the outskirts of the shopping centre.

Original_Search_Title: Third Eye Open: Governing By Impulse is Taking Uganda Nowhere
Headline: Uganda: Third Eye Open: Governing By Impulse is Taking Uganda Nowhere
Body_Text:
Not all chickens are equal. Some come home to roost faster than
others. Nowhere does this ring truer than at the Uganda Tourism Board, which
is hurting from a pound-foolish decision to invest nearly Shs1.9 billion (a
cool $1 million) into a six-month promotion on CNN.
That decision is causing a royal financial headache at the board,
according to an October 7 New Vision article. Understandably, James
Bahinguza, the UTB boss, and Sam Nahamya, the permanent secretary at the
parent ministry, are tut-tutting news that the agency is broke as a result
of the CNN escapade.
The story quotes anoymous insiders. Who can blame them, those prudent
souls? As the Ma'di ancients said, "Amba ni vu konga gbu anziri koce acika":
meaning that if a grownup farts, children wisely never spit in disdain. It's
much safer to blame it on the nearest dog.
One of the nameless UTB sources expressed so well my own misgivings when
I first heard that the Ugandan government was blundering into an expensive
one-off promotional foray on global television.
"We have no updated brochures about Uganda in our embassies abroad,"
he/she told the New Vision. "Taking a leaf from Kenya, other than
advertising, they organised international exhibitions, made information
available on tourism opportunities by bringing in travel journalists and
opinion leaders that is more effective than an advertising campaign."
Mere change
Much as $1 million might sound, in the world of US media buying, that's
chump change that won't penetrate the market. The foot soldiers that drive
the industry's growth, meanwhile, won't get paid because of cashflow
problems that wakina Nahamya and Bahinguza insist aren't related to the
government's image-making profligacy.
It reminds me of a fascinating urban tale. A nicely dressed woman,
coiffed from head to toe in resplendent bitenge alights from a kamunye near
Nakasero Market. Men are almost breaking their necks trying to pull off the
impossible feat of unobtrusively watching this belle swinging her hips this
way and that way. A few car drivers, also mightily distracted by the
spectacle of a sexually provocative kyana crossing the road, narrowly avert
head-on collisions.
But this little drama doesn't end well for the woman. She trips on the
hem of her own kitenge. As she crashes to the ground butt-naked, her handbag
snaps open and its contents spill. Most noticeable is a big piece of roasted
sweet potato. She is so paralysed by embarrassment that she can't even move
to retrieve the kitenge to cover her nudity.
Meanwhile, the sadistic macho lumpens are howling gleefully like a troop
of predatory hyenas. Only a quick-thinking taxi driver mercifully saves her
from the lewd crowd by whisking her off. Despite her tip-top vazi, she
doesn't even have the money to pay the good man.
This story, most likely a hot stew of sexist fable, nevertheless is an
apt metaphor for the government's sophomoric attempts at marketing our
country abroad.
There is an even sadder reality at work. More than past administrations,
Yoweri Museveni's National Resistance Movement has treated Uganda's foreign
missions largely as places to dump apparatchiks who have fallen from grace
or merely to reward lackeys. As a result, far from executing articulate
public relations for our country, many embassies are theatres of a never
ending farce. Loyalists earn their keep by trolling for and crossing swords
with real or imagined opponents of the Movement from among Uganda's exiled
community.
Even then, as my colleague Mulera Muniini mentioned in his latest
installment of "Dear Tingasiga," (see October 10 Daily Monitor) whenever the
Ugandan government wants to do something really crass, like trying to
infiltrate the Ugandan North American Association, they fly in a bevy of
straw men and women straight from Kampala.
Zero achievement
Is it any wonder that even a cursory examination reveals that these
impulsive schemes usually do not show much for all the tax-payer's money
squandered in carrying them out? For example, "taking over" UNAA is only as
good as a comb to a bald man.
Ugandans in Canada and the United States, who for the most part aren't
even members of the organisation, do not take political decisions as a
block. Thus, regardless of who "owns" UNAA, abanakyeyo will go out and put
their money where their hearts lie.
Come to think about it. If intense chakamchaka failed to create the
army-like command structure among wananchi that the Movement's totalitarian
instincts so badly coveted, how can UNAA, an organisation that survives and
thrives entirely on the goodwill of its members coral some of the freest
thinking Ugandans into the NRM kraal?
Quick notes:
Despite earning the revealing sobriquet of Washington's blue-eyed boy in
Africa, Museveni sometimes reprises a jaded nationalistic poise, as he did
at the Independence Day celebrations, whenever his jittery foreign backers
press him over a looming national crisis.
Where on earth do we get the nerve to give Congo an ultimatum to disarm
or else we re-invade when for more than a decade our mighty army failed
miserably to capture even a single strand of Joseph Kony's dreadlocks?

Original_Search_Title: Younger Leaders Can't Handle Mugabe
Headline: Zimbabwe: Younger Leaders Can't Handle Mugabe
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Like a sinner who knows he is in good company, Zimbabwean President Robert Mugabe stomped the ground at the 25th summit of the Southern African Development Community (SADC) in Gaborone, Botswana, last week, with inimitable confidence. With what transpired at the summit, Dr Mugabe, the longest serving SADC leader, must be excused for thinking that he was at an Organisation of African Unity (OAU) summit circa 1984 when the Big Man was really big.
At its most incompetent best, the defunct OAU was known for its hear-no-evil, see-no-evil and speak-no-evil posture. The organisation is no more but its infamous policy of "non-interference in the internal affairs of member states" is being pursued and sustained most enthusiastically by SADC.
At the Gaborone summit, new SADC chairman and Botswana President Festus Mogae let it slip that SADC has rechristened the OAU policy as "live-and-let-live".
"We believe in a policy of live and let live. Those who want to hold periodic elections shall have them, those who want multipartyism shall do so, while those who want to fight shall continue fighting," President Mogae told a media briefing after the summit.
He categorically stated that Zimbabwe was never on the SADC agenda at the summit although Dr Mugabe and his Zanu-PF have run the country to the ground, an issues that should be of grave concern to the bloc.
More sweet music was to come to Dr Mugabe's ears from former Mozambican president Joachim Alberto Chissano, the man chosen by the African Union (AU) chairman, Nigerian President Olusegun Obassanjo, as special envoy to Zimbabwe. The former head of state admitted that none other than Dr Mugabe had convinced him that he does not have a job as mediator in the Zimbabwe crisis.
SADC decided to bury its head in the sand on Zimbabwe despite loud cries from regional civil society organisations gathered in Gaborone for the summit. The NGOs made a passionate plea for the leaders to deal with the Zimbabwe hot potato. But the die was cast when the SADC leaders announced that Zimbabwe would not be on the agenda. So why have SADC leaders shied away from taking on Zimbabwe?
Besides President Mogae's let-sleeping-dogs-lie explanation, Mr Chissano added another dimension to the sad saga with his pointed question at a media briefing: "Who are we to judge Zimbabwe and start naming their problems while we have problems in our own countries?"
However, President Mogae and Mr Chissano were involved in a classic exercise in contradiction. If SADC has a hands-off policy on dealing with the internal affairs of member states, then why did South Africa and Botswana soldiers storm Lesotho in 1998 to restore order? None other than President Mogae authorised the sending of Botswana troops on the mission.
SADC states Angola, Namibia and Zimbabwe also sent troops to meddle in the Democratic Republic of Congo.
It is obvious that SADC is handling President Mugabe with kid gloves because diplomacy, especially the African brand is a veritable theatre of the absurd. It is easy for regional bodies in Africa like Ecowas - the Economic Community of Western African States - and SADC to lean heavily on errant small states while the big ones are allowed to literally get away with murder.
When Liberia and Sierra Leone were burning in the 1990s, Ecowas did well to send in peacekeeping troops with the mandate to fight. The same happened with the Botswana-South Africa-SADC operation in Lesotho. But Zimbabwe is no Sierra Leone. Nor is it a lightweight like Lesotho or Liberia.
President Mugabe is the enfant terrible in African politics, but his liberation credentials and past standing have scared fellow leaders from taking him head-on. He is one the longest-serving presidents on the continent, the only independence leader still in power. Thus he can afford to stomp the ground at the SADC summit with the confidence of a bull in his kraal. He knows the "young men" who took power "yesterday" have no guts to take on him.
Owino Wene is a Kenyan journalist working in Botswana

Original_Search_Title: Vendors Become Charitable After Relocation
Headline: Botswana: Vendors Become Charitable After Re__cpLocation
Body_Text:
Francistown — After re__cpLocation to a less lucrative site, vendors in Francistown have decided to donate their leftovers to the needy. "It is a waste of petrol to go all the way to South Africa to buy small amounts of vegetables enough for selling only.
We just buy full loads like we used to when business was good and give extras to the needy people. This programme is aimed at giving the needy people a truck full of vegetables at least once a month," said one of the vendors and pioneer of the programme, Molly Sekgwama. Already the SOS Children's Village has benefited from the gesture. Next week, the School of the Deaf will also benefit.
The vendors have been battling with the Francistown City Council (FCC) over a suitable place to operate from in the city. They plied their trade in parking lots, in front of supermarkets, at the bus rank and along Haskins Street. This rubbed some business operators and the local authority the wrong way.
FCC relocated them to a place near the Gallo shopping complex, which the traders say is low in business.
"There is absolutely no business here. I threw away four crates of bad tomatoes this morning and as you can see, the truck is still full, which means I am going to lose more than I anticipated. This has been going on since we came to operate in this area," lamented Sekgwama. She explained that customers are complaining that the area is filthy and dusty. She said the situation is worsened by the proximity to the open barbershops and waste bins that incessantly churn out a putrid smell. "A customer once returned her vegetables saying that they had hair pieces. You can just see how dusty this place is. One can make only up to P150 here instead of the P500 we were making at our former selling points," she explained. She said that only those with loyal customers would survive.
Another vendor, Chandapiwa Kelapile who shared similar sentiments with Sekgwama said that she now takes a week to sell her vegetables and by this time some have turned bad. But at her former selling point, it took only three days for a truck full of vegetables to be sold out. "This shopping complex is only for the high class people who do not even look twice at our vegetables. They prefer Pick and Pay supermarket to us. Our customers were the old women and the low-income class who passed by on their way to the bus rank. Here, we are far from them and the hawkers do not even tell them where we have relocated to," Kelapile said.
She explained that they have been forced by circumstances to lower their prices to lure customers. She said she does not sell vegetables that have stayed longer than four days. "When the fourth day passes, I throw away whatever remains and this is a loss to me. I cannot afford to pay my dues now due to this tough situation. I cannot bear the thought of moving to Naledi Motors as it is rumoured that the council is going to move us to the place. Who will buy there if they cannot buy here in town," wondered Kelapile.
Rosemary Johwa who sells sweet potatoes thinks that the problem may be that the customers are not used to their new area of operation. She said the place near Gallo shopping complex is better than Naledi Motors.
FCC deputy Mayor, Rebecca Nshakazhogwe said the council had done its best in trying to find the vendors a place to settle but they were not cooperative. "We opened our doors to those who were willing to go to Naledi Motors but they opted to negotiate with Tati Company (TC) to operate on that land. They are currently paying P1 daily rental to TC," Nshakazhogwe said. She added that currently, there are no ablutions where the vendors are operating from but at Naledi Motors, the FCC was going to supply them with toilets and water for free. "We were going to maintain the place to make sure that it is not filthy, but they declined the offer," she said.

Original_Search_Title: Time Travel At La Fontaine
Headline: Zimbabwe: Time Travel At La Fontaine
Body_Text:
Harare — In Ancient Roman times, a splendid banquet for members of the senatorial and equestrian classes might include dishes made from "sow's paunch, antelope, hare, gazelle, even the tall flamingo".
But times have changed, and the latest winter menu at Meikles Hotel's La Fontaine, provides the perfect repast for an important meal. Whether one is celebrating a family birthday or wedding anniversary, trying to impress an influential colleague or bank manager, or simply wanting an excellent meal in elegant surroundings, La Fontaine should be the first choice.
A few days before the winter solstice, I received an invitation to lunch at the Chefs' Table at La Fontaine, and to sample the new winter menu. This was to be a menu with a difference: to celebrate Meikles Hotel's 90th birthday, La Fontaine would be offering a classic grill menu, similar to the one at the restaurant's grand opening in 1958.
What were the "in" foods in the 50s? I could recall eating out in the 60s, but anything before that seemed wreathed in the mists of time. Vestigial memories of parental dinner parties produced images of salmon mousse and devilled eggs-and perhaps an American-style casserole called Chicken Maryland. I had a feeling that the combined skills of Executive Chef Chris Gonzo and his talented team, assisted by visiting Kitchen Alchemist Peter Goffe-Wood, might come up with something infinitely more exciting.
Over aperitifs at Meikles' Can Can Bar, the suave and ever-courteous Stan Higgins introduced invited guests to Meikles' top management team, our lunchtime hosts. CEO Roy Meiring then led us deep within the belly of La Fontaine's spotless stainless steel and white-tiled kitchen, to the Chefs' Table, resplendent with snowy white damask, gleaming silver and chrystal, and a magical floral arrangement out of which peeped beaded wire butterflies and chameleons. Our congenial group eagerly awaited the Hors d'Oeuvre.
An elegantly presented Mixed Hors d'Oeuvre platter combined an Orange and Grapefruit Citrus Cocktail, Smoked Nyanga Trout, Chicken Liver Wrapped in Bacon, succulent Crumbed Button Mushrooms (in-house speciality) and Steak Tartare, perfectly seasoned. This is priced on the menu at $100.000. Other options included Egg Mayonnaise with Salami (very 50s!) at $40.000, Flamed Snails in Garlic Butter at $95.000 and a retro sounding Seafood Cocktail at $60.000.
A demitasse of classic Chilled Vichyssoise soup ($40.000) followed: other choices include French Onion, Cream of Mushroom Soup, and the current hot favourite, Butternut and Macadamia Soup ($50.000).
A selection from the Entrees and Grills offered generous tasting portions of Beef Tournedos with Sauce Chasseur, Chicken Breast with Garlic and Mustard Sauce, and Rack of Lamb with Mint Sauce.
These were served with a giant melt-in-the mouth Potato Croquette, (French-Fries, Roast Potatoes or rice were options), and top quality seasonal vegetables, perfectly cooked al dente. I would be hard-pressed to say which of these meat dishes with their classic sauces was the most delicious: prices range from $120.000 to $170.000. La Fontaine has regular suppliers of top quality meat and the freshest of produce, which ensures a consistent excellence in their cuisine.
Desserts include 50s favourites such as Sherry Trifle and flamed Crepes Suzettes, and are mostly in the $30.000 price range.
If you have a sweet tooth, for $65.000 you can taste an incredible selection from the dessert trolley, and enjoy Amarula Mousse, CrÃ¨me Caramel and Fruit Tartlets in addition.
Wine lovers will agree that Meikles Hotel's wine list is second to none. The Chamonix sparkling wine from Franschoek, the Welmoed Chardonnay 2003, and the Saxenburg Merlot 2000, reasonably priced, perfectly complemented our meal.
Fortunately one doesn't need to belong to the senatorial class to enjoy a five star meal in Harare: La Fontaine's new menu offers many affordable options to making dining out in style a reality.

Original_Search_Title: Lifting the Lid On Our Bitter Past
Headline: Kenya: Lifting the Lid On Our Bitter Past
Body_Text:
Nairobi — It is that time of the year when Kenyans reflect on the state of the
nation and reassess the values upon which their country is founded. And why
not? On June 1, 1963, we got the mandate to govern ourselves.
More often than not, broadcasting houses go into the history vaults and
rehash old clips, while newspapers trace the surviving freedom fighters and
record anew their memories - their lives in the forest, in prison and at
home.
This year, the Kenya Broadcasting Cooporation had an interesting
documentary, but I saw only glimpses of it.
In it, good old Jomo stands with Mau Mau fighters (must have been at
Ruring'u Stadium in Nyeri). But he seems ill at ease. There is a towering
fighter, dreadlocks and all, holding him by the shoulder while others mob
the future President.
"You just wanted to see a black flag flying over the land," Jomo
says. "You have a black flag..." Indeed, we got uhuru wa bendera (flag
freedom).
One never quite gets the meaning, nor the biting sarcasm, of the
words. But since Kenyans have a deep respect for the dead, we dare not speak
ill of them. So we don't seek the meaning.
Many moons ago, I traced freedom fighters from those 1963 photos to
Embu. They still wore dreadlocks, which now sweep the ground. And they still
clung to the vows they made in the forest 50 years earlier. Their bitterness
was evident, as was their sense of betrayal.
But let's not go there; last week's events give a better meaning to
Kenyatta's uhuru wa bendera edict. We heard striking civil servants singing
those Kapenguria songs - which memorialised the travails of our "founding"
fathers in colonial detention. Now the songs convey the workers'
incarceration, trapped in grinding poverty for all the days of their
lives.
The humiliation of Mrs Mukami Kimathi, widow of nationalist Dedan
Kimathi, at Nyayo Stadium further sharpened this meaning of uhuru wa
bendera.
Picture this: on the day that Kenya celebrates Kimathi's and other
freedom fighters' contribution to the liberation struggle, his widow is
barred from sitting among the waheshimiwa.
The reason is easy to fathom. These old women carrying ciondos could
pose a security risk. They could detonate explosives in the sweet potatoes
that they eat as they cannot afford proper meals.
Worse still, one could break the security cordon and sprint to the dais
where the Rais is standing. She could whisper in his ear the name of that
affluent neighbour who is snatching her land.
Back to the KBC film. Kenyatta was being hailed as a messiah who would
cast a magic wand and heal the rift between races, securing the country's
future. This is colonial propaganda that we continue propounding to our
children.
The truth, as they say, is out there - among the striking workers and
the old women begging to be allowed in. Why are the waters to bitter?
Because our true history has never been told; it just keeps bubbling. Who
shall lift the lid?
Shame of Oliech's own goal
This is a week to reassess patriotism: Dennis Oliech scored an own goal
by declining to travel to Conakry where the national team is on
assignment.
Regardless of the issues that he put forward - and they are legitimate -
one is persuaded to think he should not have come back if he did not intend
to play for his country.
The Kenya Football Federation is cash-strapped. The entire team almost
failed to make the trip. The amount that Oliech is claiming back is his
right, but he should not have used it as a barrier between him and the
country.
Nigerian striker Jay Jay Okocha has been engaged in similar runs with
his country's soccer federation. But he has never declined to turn up for a
national assignment. Oliech could have gone to Guinea, then put his case
firmly after the match. If Guinea was the yardstick of his patriotism, he
lost miserably by scoring an own goal.
Local 'dream girl' strikes it in the UK
Some people have it! Veronica Wangari, mother of the infamous Anne
O'Brien, alias Wangeci or Cherono, among other names, says her daughter is
being framed of improper acts because she bought a home in the exclusively
white Kensington suburb.
This is a serious allegation that would horrify the Commission for
Racial Equality in Britain. More importantly, the commission would want to
know how she afforded it. It is reported she paid £1m cash (Sh146m)
for the house from her earnings as a care worker and, later, hair
salonist.
In my student days in England, I was paid £4.20 (Sh600) an hour to
sort books and put them back on dusty shelves at the University of London. I
worked 15 hours a week. From my toil, I bought food, a book or two, and a
dozen pints of Irish stout a month. That was it. Not a penny left to send
home.
But O'Brien is a dream girl. She dreamt big and made it big, perhaps
working 24-7. She can now afford a lavish lifestyle in England. She also
built an imposing home for her mother in Kenya and bought her matatus that
were christened - without any irony - Dream Girl. This is also the name of
her alleged call service. If she made it this far from the sweat of her
brow, and nowhere else, she should be brought home to help strategise our
recovery programme. She could make many dreams come true, literally.
A man for all seasons
Samuel Wanjama operates in Nyahururu, seeking alms from well-wishers. He
supplements these by selling puppies. Each fetches Sh200.
It is not clear how he delivers the puppies to Nyahururu town. But from
this picture, one of the pets is disciplined enough to stand in the
allocated space. The other might cling on to its master's shirt, as he has
to use his two hands to cycle.
Whatever the case, it is hoped that he has a licence to engage in
business, since his primary aim is to seek alms.
Justice from a police gun
Kenyans are usually enraged by rampant violations by the police, whether
through arbitrary arrest, torture or extortion.
Last week, a uniformed policeman was gunned down by a fellow officer
while on foot patrol in Githurai. He, too, was on foot patrol.
Now comes the question: since the police receive instruction from the
same office, was there no communication that comrades were in the
neighbourhood?
Clearly, someone has a case to answer, but Nairobi police boss King'ori
Mwangi does not think so. He says the matter does not merit further action,
as it was too dark for the policeman in question to see that the man he shot
was also a policeman.
King'ori, tell that to the woman who is freshly widowed by your "mboy's
mbullet".
Trust them at own risk
About that Gallup poll and the public distrust for politicians. Minister
Kipruto arap Kirwa thinks it is "cheap propaganda."
How so? Because 80 per cent of Kenyans say politicians are dishonest. I
am one of them, and I was not interviewed.
This is why: We were promised a new constitution within 100 days of Narc
leadership. It's yet to be delivered. The Health ministry promised salary
