increments to workers, but instead threatened them with the sack when they
went on strike last month. And the President promised to give regular
briefings to the Press, which he doesn't. Does the minister need more
evidence?
Trying to turn back the hands of time
Outgoing British High Commissioner Edward Clay was on TV last week to
deliver his swan-song, a mild version of the loaded speeches with nauseating
imageries of vomit and stuff.
There were many questions fielded by viewers, which he conveniently
ignored, claiming he could not turn back the hands of time because of "law
and common sense."
What law is that, Sir Edward? The most recurrent question that he chose
to side-step, using the cover of time, concerned the Mau Mau and the
callers' demands to Britain to own up to its past atrocities in Kenya.
Sir Edward must know that the British are very faithful to their
history. Anyone who dishonours it commits a grave sin. But he has diplomatic
licence to cheapen the suffering of Kenyans, and consign such episodes to
history.
He used the right words to describe the future relations of the two
countries. He said we were "condemned" to live with each other. As he takes
his final bow, there are poignant words that he may have forgotten, which
were uttered by Anyang Nyong'o: aibu ya maiti, aijuae ni mwosha.
It's a deep statement. A loose translation is, only mortuary workers
know the extent of a body's rottenness.
Now that he is going, he should heed the advise of Nigerian author
Chinua Achebe: don't weep louder than the bereaved. It will serve him well
in future.

Original_Search_Title: Chris Gonzo Unsung All-Time Hero of the Kitchen
Headline: Zimbabwe: Chris Gonzo Unsung All-Time Hero of the Kitchen
Body_Text:
Harare — Chris Gonzo, Exe-cutive Chef at Meikles Hotel, and unsung hero of the kitchen, has much to be proud about.
While working as a night club porter at Avondale's Le Matelot in the 70s, his cookery skills were soon revealed, and he was appointed Le Matelot's junior fish cook in 1972.
Promotion followed promotion until Chef Gonzo's current appointment as executive chef at Meikles, one of the world's leading hotels.
Talented cooks and success stories interest me greatly, so I decided to invite Mr Gonzo out to lunch, to the restaurant of his choice.
An innovative cook, and always willing to "try anything", Gonzo suggested a Chinese meal, so I booked a table for the next day at my old favourite, the China Garden, in Milton Park.
Meikles Hotel is gearing itself up in anticipation of an increase in the numbers of tourists from China, based partly on Air Zimbabwe's flight to Beijing and partly on Zimbabwe's being a recommended destination for Chinese holiday - makers. A foray into the art of Sichuan cuisine seemed appropriate.
Gonzo and I arrived at the China Garden together, and the smiling Lu Li, our bilingual hostess, showed us to a table by the window.
We both ordered Mazoe Orange and soda, and before long, Chris and I were on first name terms. I heard how Chris' parents had brought up their family of three boys and three girls, in the Eastern Highlands village of Penhalonga.
Encouraged by their mother, a gifted cook, the children all took turns in the kitchen, preparing traditional meals, which included rapoko, sorghum, sweet potatoes and maize meal. Chris' speciality at this time was making bread, which he baked in a three-legged pot, heaping coals below, and above on the pot lid. By the age of fourteen, Chris had an inkling that he might like to become seriously involved in cookery.
Moving to Emmanuel Secondary School at Nyanga for his secondary education, Chris discovered that the syllabus required him to study French-a useful skill for someone destined in the not too distant future to excel in French and International cuisine.
Later on, encouraged by le Matelot's Swiss German chef, Joseph Gander, who found it easy to communicate the art of French cuisine to his pupil, Chris rose rapidly through the ranks to become le Matelot's head cook in 1974.
A career move then took Chris to the Jameson Hotel, where he remained for the next fifteen years, working his way up to executive chef in 1987, and assistant food and beverage manager in 1990.
By 1993, Chris Gonzo was sous chef at the Bagatelle Restaurant.
The Millennium found him executive chef at Victoria Falls Hotel where he spent four steamy years, returning to Harare's Meikles Hotel in 2004 as executive chef.
Chris attributes much of his success to the three P's: pride, passion and patience.
Trend monitoring is also vitally important, and Chris is contemplating the demise of the three course meal in favour of a 'tasting menu', with many delicious and beautifully designed small dishes. "Chefs are becoming architects", he says.
Any aspiring young cook, who is lucky enough to train in Chris Gonzo's kitchen, will be assured of a thorough training in cuisine.

Original_Search_Title: Maseruka Was a Fountain of Wisdom
Headline: Uganda: Maseruka Was a Fountain of Wisdom
Body_Text:
Kampala — He had most of the qualities a child would want in a father. Ssalongo Stephen Maseruka who passed away on February 3, 2005 at 93 was a dapper dresser, pious and social animal.
Maseruka had a fountain of knowledge, a workhorse and also had a soft spot for the needy.
He was a family man who loved his clan and country, an ardent human rights activist who appreciated nature and was so contented with whatever God gave him.
Ssalongo loved cracking jokes. Visitors to our home at Manyangwa in Wakiso district always left marvelling at the funny stories Mzee told them.
The worst mistake a visitor would make was to show interest in his funny stories. Mzee would then spice his narrations with a million exaggerations, making the visitor laugh until tears rolled.
I still recall Mzee's high sense of humour. he could never let anyone around him get bored. He knew how to get people's attention by telling hilarious stories like how he poured piripiri (red pepper) at an Asian who abused him 'maali ya nyoko?' (Is it you or mother's property?)
Maseruka was born on January 7, 1912, in Ssekamuli, Luweero district to the late Masiale Mmundu and Rose Yatuwa. He later settled at Manyangwa.
A staunch Catholic, he used to encourage us to be religious. Apparently his father Masiale (as he was known) survived being burnt with the Uganda Martyrs.
Maseruka went to Nandere Junior School , Rubaga Boys and St. Mary's College Kisubi. He trained to become an office clerk with an admirable typing speed of 80 words per minute.
He worked with the ministry of Health in Hoima, Arua and Mulago hospital. On retirement from the public service, he continued work at Kyambogo Technical School and St. Mary's College Kisubi.
Maseruka married Emily nalubega on May 15, 1954 and were blessed with 15 children, two of whom passed away.
They are parents to renown scribes, Maurice Ssekawungu, Associate editor The New Vision, J.B. Waswa, former Managing Editor of The Monitor and his twin sister Josephine Maseruka a reporter with The New Vision.
His piousness earned us some beatings and at times cost us a meal. He would insist that we should never go to bed like pigs, so we had to say the lengthy morning and evening prayers and even before every meal.
Mzee was very strict on academics. He would caution us against repeating classes, which he dubbed 'doubling' and at the end of term he would scrutinise our reports. This encouraged us to study hard and have good comments from teachers.
We grew up in a family of over 30 children most of them needy relatives. We had to follow a strict menu Mzee copied from his white bosses! Matooke and beef were a must on Sunday. I remember one Sunday, Nanteza the maid prepared sweet potatoes and beef for lunch. Our parents were away so she begged Waswa and I to eat up, but we refused arguing that we could not eat such food on a Sunday. so the rest of the children abandoned the meal.
During his old age, Mzee would spend hours singing church hymns.
To mum, this meant Mzee was in good health.
Once during the turbulent times of the 1980s, he paused while leading night prayers and questioned God why he had politically allowed Ugandans to be subjected to such suffering. This was ununsual, but Mzee just couldn't help getting irked by the endless killing then.
Maseruka was quite progressive. He was among the first Ugandans to drive a Vanguard car, which was commonly referred to as vangadi. He was also one of the first people to buy a PYE (pronounced payi) radio on which he would connect loud speakers so the village would keep abreast of the current affairs.
Having many journalists in his family was no coincidence.
Maseruka loved literature and kept all his financial records and diaries in order. He contributed a lot to local papers especially to the letters pages.
As a human rights activist he contributed a lot to the fight against abortion, wife battering and domestic issues.
Maseruka headed a social clan of members from notable families in the area. They would meet every Sunday in the homes of different members as a way of socialising and laying strategies to improve homestead incomes.
Strict as he was, Mzee avoided caning children. In my lifetime I saw him only twice caning our elder sister Jane.
There are many things we shall always remember about Mzee. As we grieve his death we at the same time praise God for the 93 years he was with us.
May his soul rest in peace.

Original_Search_Title: Kitgum's Human Suffering
Headline: Uganda: Kitgum's Human Suffering
Body_Text:
Kampala — I have listened to so many disturbing war stories as well as witnessed some heart rending sufferings of children, women and men affected by the 19-year-old conflict in northern Uganda. But this has been limited to Gulu district only as I hadn't got the chance to visit the other districts affected by the same conflict.
My four months work with World Vision has given me a whole new experience and taken me places to witness how unfair the world can be to some people.
My recent and first visit to war-torn Kitgum of northern Uganda has been one of the most haunting travels of my time with World Vision. What I saw and heard will always remain fresh in my memory!
The 20 minutes flight from Gulu to Kitgum was a bumpy one. The land looked a vast desert of landscape with no sign of any human being. The grayish remains of the wall of burnt huts is all one could see. Feelings of fear, excitement and anxiety triggered inside me.
A few minutes after landing at Kitgum airfield, we were driven to one of the hotels. As we hit the dusty road to the hotel, I could see pitched rotten tents along the road. Very elderly women and children gathered around them on the chilling hot sun. It is their 'home'!
Later that evening I visited the place. I couldn't compare their condition to any; these quiet traumatized people seem barely real, their circumstances too complicated to grasp, and their plight too overwhelming to address.
"We are going to die of hunger here. We are ignored and despised! All we want is to go back home where we can cultivate and have food in plenty," says Mego Ventino Laker. As I left, I imagined that could just be a sample of the many problems.
Driving through the irritating dusty road the following day, we headed to Labuje camp about 3km away from the main centre. The sight of the dilapidated grass thatched huts built very close together is very threatening. A small spark would leave the whole village in ashes!
Although the inhabitants were in dirty rags, they still welcomed us with warm smiles.
I met the vice chairperson local council III Metric Opira Achan who gave me a brief insight into the many problems the camp population of about 20,000 people face.
"The huts are too close to each other and children often fall in fire. Diseases like syphilis, chronic coughs, and diarrhoea make the lives of the people hard. But the Aids pandemic is the most rampant", she said. Shortage of food and water, insecurity and poor accommodation are some of the problems she briefly told me. "Go talk to the people and find the truth," she says.
Sabrina Auma, 35, was to guide me around the camp that was divided into wards. The first one called 'Labongo Yamo' (without air), the second 'lacan ma twal' (poor forever), third; 'dii cwinyi' (be patient) and the forth; 'wi lobo olanya' (the world has mistreated me). Just the names of the 'wards' touched me so deeply.
As we made our way to the first ward, jerricans in a long zigzag line, young women, girls and children surrounded the only borehole around, patiently waiting to fetch some water. Their sight alone held me spellbound... .spellbound because it depicted human suffering.
They were so angry when they saw a camera, but just a mention that I was from World Vision softened their hearts and they allowed me to continue.
"Imagine water which is God's free gift is a problem here. When you write that story, emphasise that we need more boreholes; if we can't get enough food at least we must have water to drink and wash", said one woman.
One 'Mego' (mama) Santino Lamwaka said it is not only shortage of water they face but food as well. She said although World Food Programme gives them food, they only do it once in three months. She said that they preferred maize flour to maize grains as the latter just added to their problems. She briefly told me how the women had to walk 7km looking for firewood, and how hard it is to even get wild food.
"The war has robbed us of everything. It's here to finish us. We have been made beggars in our own land. Salt and soap is only for the rich," she said.
I was left drained and speechless.
We continued into the camp. Barefooted children in clothes dusty and torn excitedly came along. Jesus' words from the Bible in Mathew came to life: "Let the children come to me... .if any one wants to enter the kingdom of my father, they should first be like a child." I looked at them and smiled.
The children complained of hunger and I bought some sweet potatoes and vegetables for them, that they happily took home to their mothers.
One of the children, Denis Okidi, 10, asked me to go see the grandmother before I go.
Reaching their hut the grandmother, Ajulina Arach, 70, was seated outside with four girls whose ages range between 13-15. They all looked very tired, faces pinched and eyes hollow. Denis introduced me as someone who writes about people's suffering. Through tears and heartache, Ajulina narrated to me her suffering. She told me how all her biological children died leaving her with ten grandchildren to single-handedly take care of. "I have nothing to give them, I am a very poor old woman with no husband, someone has to help me out," she cries.
My soul and heart split into pieces when she said that nine of the children who are girls are HIV positive. She claimed some of them were raped by 'mobile' soldiers who normally spend sometime in an area before being transferred to another place, while others sold their bodies to the soldiers for survival.
"It's only Denis who is not sick with this 'slim'. I feel relieved now that I have told you, it was killing me slowly," she says.
One of the girls who couldn't reveal her name, recounted to me the night when the grandmother was so ill and she had nothing to do to save her life.
"A soldier found me crying. When I told him the problem, he asked me to go with him and buy medicine. When we reached his room in a hotel, he forcefully tore my dress and slept with me. He didn't even give any money. I am now very ill," she says crying.
Another girl admitted to 'giving' in her body for money so she could buy food to take home. "That time, we had nothing to eat, I had to sell my body for Shs5,000, so I could save my family from dying of hunger," she admits.
As for Denis, although World Vision was paying his fees, he dropped out of school. He said he couldn't go to school on an empty stomach, dirty, and without a uniform. "It's no use studying when I feel hungry all the time; they would rather give us that money for food, soap and salt," he says.
Those were a few of the many tortured families living in a camp. The depths of the stories were too much for me to absorb. It pained me so much that I could not do anything right away to change their situation. I couldn't help imagining how many other young girls have the virus, and the young boys who could be interested in them... The thought of it alone makes my body go cold!
"The war has disintegrated the entire culture. Parents don't have authority anymore over their children. There is too much promiscuity, the girls being idle, sleep around too much," comments Metric Opira.
World Vision built a school nearby and also gives food to those living with HIV/Aids.
Christine Lapat, the Manager World Vision, Kitgum, told me that their budget is too stringent to support the people. "There are too many problems in Kitgum for our small budget to handle. Yet people always flood our offices seeking for petty necessities like soap, salt, and food," she says.
By the time we left the camp to my hotel, I realized that there are neither words nor graphics fit enough to illustrate the sufferings of the people. Their pain and agony is too great for them to bear! Peace should return so that the social order may be restored.
The writer is Communications Officer-World Vision Uganda

Original_Search_Title: Life Matters
Headline: Ethiopia: Life Matters
Body_Text:
I am sure that we have all come across the swathe of uniformity that plagues our city. If one person does one thing, then it is more than likely that 50 others will follow suit. Perhaps we should ask a couple of people to jump off bridges and see what happens!
I mention this as I cannot seem to avoid noticing supermarkets wherever I go. On a trip along Africa Avenue (Bole Road) I counted 10 supermarkets on the main road alone. It is possible that I missed a few, but adding to this number will only upset me further so I would rather not.
Let us consider the name 'supermarket' itself. No single store in the entire country actually really fits that description.
Supermarkets are stores that carry anywhere between eight to 12,000 lines. What we have here are convenience stores that are playing dress-up. I think the Ethiopian population must have somehow become addicted to shopping over the last few years. But then again, a majority of the people that shop in these stores are not the majority of our population that live well below international poverty standards.
Why then are so many stores opening up, carrying mostly imported products which have high price tags?
I have no real answer, not least because as the supermarkets themselves adhere to no particular standards. Shopping in your local Addis grocery store is like trying to solve a differential equation without ever having taken a maths lessons.
The first problem you encounter upon entering a grocery store in our fair city is the layout. To be honest, there is no decipherable organisation that one can speak of. All the items that are considered to be 'planned' shopping are not at the front of the store where they ought to be. As soon as you enter these places, you come across impulse purchase items.
I personally do not like being greeted with chewing gum when I enter a store. I see that everyday when I stop at a traffic light, and little kids offer it to me. A good store owner will tell you that the shelves, which are at eye-level, are only used for products that are being marketed to sell at a high volume. This does not include chocolates and potato chips.
The lower shelves are supposed to be home-items or heavier products that are well known and used by your customers. The top shelf plays a similar role but the items are not so weighty. I have yet to find a supermarket that actually follows these guidelines: I have come across gum on the middle shelf and food on the bottom shelf of more than two of the stores that I have had the pleasure to shop at.
It is just so aggravating to have to search through different levels because the persons who own the store have no idea in the world what way the shelving should be set-up.
And then, of course, there are the products that these places display for sale. First and foremost, the products are mostly imported. Though this could serve as a benefit for the international community, and the nice people in the Diaspora who have returned home, that is not the case for the locals. These products are over-priced and are often not consistently available. If you buy and enjoy a certain brand of sweet corn this month, you are not guaranteed to find it next month. This in itself is cause enough to lose customers.
There are many imported things that we enjoy that could easily be produced and distributed within the country. If the stores were to carry both the local and the imported version of a product, it is quite possible that they would be able to increase their customer-base as the consumers would have things to buy that are relevant to the foods they eat and the prices that they can afford.
I mean, really, what is the likelihood that a local couple is going to use Paul Newman's French dressing on their salad tonight? Or what are the chances of Antenneh munching on a packet of Pringles while he is watching 120?
Close to none, I tell you! Not because they may not enjoy these products, but rather because they probably are not going to spend the exorbitant prices that they are sold for.
The last pitfall of the stores in Addis is the fact that there are no standards.
With entire rows and shelves full of feminine products and sweets, I stand disappointed at the marketing and the pricing of almost all the grocery stores that I have come across. Although I do enjoy being able to get a Kit Kat once in a while, it still makes no sense to have so many spaces taken up by stores that have nothing unique or reasonable to offer the public.

Original_Search_Title: Movt Fans Vandalise Market
Headline: Uganda: Movt Fans Vandalise Market
Body_Text:
Kampala — MOVEMENT supporters in Kampala took time off on Tuesday to feast and
dance throughout the night at several centres in the city.
At Kamwokya market and Kasalina zone, Bukoto, the party attracted
hundreds of revellers, who vandalised market stalls and soiled shops.
Many of the revellers wore yellow T-shirts inscribed with MMM (Museveni,
Movement and Me) as they flashed the three-finger and thumbs up signs
signifying the third term for President Yoweri Museveni.
Health state minister Mike Mukula, the chief guest at Kasalina zone, led
guests to a dance around the bonfire chanting Movement slogans.
They later feasted on roast meat and three sacks of sweet potatoes.

Original_Search_Title: Only 5 percent of Children Access ARVs
Headline: Rwanda: Only 5% Access Arvs
Body_Text:
Kigali — Startling reports have emerged that only 5% of the 20,000 Rwandan
children infected with HIV/Aids receive anti-retroviral drugs
(ARVs).President Paul Kagame made the revelation while launching a five
year global Aids campaign on children in Rwanda organized by UNICEF. He
said the statistics signify that HIV/Aids is having an enormous impact
on children.
"We all recognize that Aids has a great impact on children; it makes
them orphans when it takes away their parents, it gives them
responsibilities that are beyond their capacity if they are to cater
for their infected parents, they drop out of schools when they lose
their parents and relatives and they are greatly stigmatized when they
lack someone to cater for them," Kagame observed.
"Children are most infected with Aids through mother to child
transmission, but unfortunately there are some who get it from sexual
abuse by adults and strategies should be set to protect children's
rights, through empowerment", said Kagame.
"We have to strengthen the programme of prevention of mother to
child transmission (PMCT) in order to reduce the risks of producing
infected kids," he added, and said that the strategies are in place to
provide ARVs to 80% of infected kids and cater for Aids orphans within
the five years programme.
Meanwhile, the UNICEF country representative, Bintu Keita, noted
that, "Our children have a right to a bright future; they also need our
continued attention, love, care and affection to live in a country fit
for them. We individually and collectively have failed to deliver a
country free of HIV/Aids."
She added that in Rwanda today, about 30% of children are orphans
who need protection and care, and about 8% of pregnant mothers are
infected with HIV/Aids and are likely to infect infants with HIV every
year.
Meanwhile, the Minister of Youth, Culture and Sports Joseph
Habineza, presided over a cultural ceremony of giving milk, honey and
millet flour to about 50 children from all the 12 provinces to
symbolize the Rwandan culture. "This symbol of offering sorghum flour
was used to indicate peace, health and prosperity, and milk was shared
as nourishment, as a blessing and as a token of reconciliation,"
Habineza disclosed.
The UNAIDS 2004 report indicates that the HIV prevalence rate in
Rwanda is 5.1% among adults between 15 -45 years nationally, 13.2 % in
Kigali town, while it is 6.3% in other towns, and 3.1% in rural areas.
Only 23% young women and 20% of men aged 15- 24 years have comprehensive knowledge on HIV/Aids.
There are strategies by the Ministry of Education to strengthen the
school health section to be charged with monitoring of interventions in
schools.
The National Youth Council is also developing a National Strategic
Plan for HIV/Aids, and an implementation, monitoring and evaluation
plan for OVCs has been developed and funds are to be mobilized for
priority actions against HIV/Aids, according to reports.

Original_Search_Title: Luckily, It is Not a Corpse
Headline: Botswana: Luckily, It is Not a Corpse
Body_Text:
Fear and worry gripped Pitsane residents of Mabete ward on Saturday morning when the reliably efficient well refused to yield water. The well is used mainly to water livestock. Curious to find out why the container that is dipped into the well did not move, the villagers sent in some youngsters to investigate the problem.
Within no time the youngsters identified the problem; the container that draws water was stuck between two heavy bags.
The most confounding revelation was yet to come. The Pitsane local police Station Commander, Gogaisakitso Mono, said the bags contained, among others, videotapes, compact disc (CDs) earrings, lipstick and a jacket.
The police officer said that she believed that some unknown person(s) threw the bags into the deep well. They were only discovered in the morning when herders came to water their cattle. She said that stones were put in the bag to ensure that it sank deep in the water.
Mono said investigations were underway and the suspects will be brought to book when they are identified. The station commander however said it was not going to be easy, especially that no complaint has been lodged with her office regarding the discovered items.
Mono said she had also heard that on the previous day a Botswana Telecommunication Corporation coin box was found in another well in Molala ward.
She has appealed to the public to come and identify the newly found items.
Village Development Committee chairman, Simon Kenosi, said they would continue the search to find out if there were any items left in the well. He said he did not know when the bags could have been thrown into the well except that they did not appear to have been there for a long time.
Kenosi said this was not the first time that they had to fish out goods from a well. In previous years, they fished out bags of sorghum, which they believed to have been stolen.
Kenosi said that they have resolved that the wells should be sealed when they are not in use. He regretted that it has not been easy to implement this resolution.
A week ago a woman was found in another an unused well.
According to Kenosi, once a woman was found in the well after four days. A search party found her.
Kenosi says the culprit, hoping that he would get rid of evidence, threw the woman who had been raped into the well. He said luckily the woman was saved because the well was shallow.
By Saturday evening six men were still searching for more items with onlookers hoping it would not be another human corpse.

Original_Search_Title: Ethnic Fears Stalk Mega Dream
Headline: Kenya: Ethnic Fears Stalk Mega Dream
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Trust the soft-spoken Nyeri politician and industrialist, Mr Peter Kuguru, to rebutt a journalist's cynicism with disarming wry jokes.
When asked what kind of people, age group or economic status, he hopes to recruit as members of his new group, Mega, he retorted: "You are already a member by birth!"
That is why, he adds, he has not sent invitations to anyone because community gatherings are open to all members of the community. And saying it, without batting an eyelid, his hoarse voice as calm as ever, one cannot fail to notice the brewer of the potent Sorghum Sake-turned soft drinks magnate has made considerable progress in his latest venture.
After two abortive attempts at the Mathira Parliamentary seat once held by his father, the late Davidson Kuguru, the politician and industrialist has plunged into controversial project that has been interpreted by some as revival of a scary old monster called Gema -the defunct Gikuyu, Embu and Meru Association.
Gema came to the scene disguised as a simple community welfare body in the early 1970s, but soon came to wield enormous political power during the Kenyatta regime. Retired President Moi disbanded it in the early 1980s two years after succeeding Kenyatta.
Many see in the new Mega intitiative, cryptically called Mobilisation of Economic Globalisation of Africa, an acronym not so similar to the old, an attempt under the present regime to bring back something like the Kenyatta ethic power structure.
Mr Kuguru differs: "This is the 21st century, and Mega is a dream of a different generation from Gema's." Mega has suffered hostile press due to fears it has as part of its agenda cultivating President Kibaki's patronage in influencing government contracts and other opportunities for businessmen and politicians from Central Kenya.
Political ambitions
The group has been accused of all manner of other evil. Some view it as a launching pad for political careers. Mr Kuguru, a former Kanu stalwart, still eyes the Mathira seat. A good number of other officials also have political ambitions.
Mr Kuguru says: "We have nothing to hide, we have no political agenda. Any of our officials who intends to join politics must step aside six months before elections." He insists Mega is different from past outfits. Unlike the others which were run by political cliques, he says, Mega is managed by professionals.
"An economic committee is charged with establishing a community bank. We have a cultural committee charged with researching and documenting positive traditions and customs. The association has branches at district and constituency levels," he says. "We start with a community bank. Imagine 3 million people, each buying a share of Sh200. We need to raise a minimum of Sh600 million."
One key plank in Mega's community economic empowerment agenda is to buy state corporations headed for privatisation and invest in other key areas of the economy.
"Don't you think it is immoral for the Kenya government to spend billions of taxpayers' money marketing the country as a tourism destination, but when the visitors finally come, all the dollars they bring are put into a basket and shipped to Saudi Arabia, Europe and Asia because the tour firms and hotels are owned by foreigners?" Mr Kuguru poses.
Cultural revival, he says, is an important tool for teaching young people community codes of ethics and keeping them from vices like drug and alcohol abuse.
Retired Anglican Arch bishop David Gitari was slotted as guest of honour at the launch. Lawyer PLO Lumumba was listed as a guest speaker and was to present a paper on 'Strength in Diversity' while Dr Mohammed Ali from the closely Bohra community was to talk community initiatives.
Among other activities planned for the launch was recognition of community heroes such as marathoner Catherine Ndereba, Nobel Peace laureatte Wangari Maathai, multi-party campaign pioneers Kenneth Matiba and Charles Rubia, and Mau Mau veterans.
Mr Kuguru says: "The list is not exhaustive. We just chose representatives from the various fields, individuals who have made significant contributions to the community, country or extra-ordinary and inspiring personal achievement."
Business networks
In an effort to allay fears of being viewed as a tribal group with sinister motives, Mega officials say they have reached out to prominent political leaders, among them Roads and Public Works minister Raila Odinga and Local Government minister Musikari Kombo.
"We invited the ministers separately to our meetings. We held fruitful discussions about how to create business and cultural networks and interactions among communities.
"They in fact promised once we are established, they would introduce us to professional and business leaders from their communities to share ideas on cultural exchange and economic forums," Mr Kuguru says.
Mr Kuguru says that Mr Odinga met the group at the Nairobi Club on September 22, last year, while Mr Kombo met them at the same venue a month later.
Some key figures in the Kibaki government, including Special Programmes minister Njenga Karume, who was chairman of the defunct Gema, Lands minister Amos Kimunya and National Security minister John Michuki were also hosted by the Mega team in April at Jacaranda Hotel, Nairobi, says Mr Kuguru.

Original_Search_Title: Mkapa's Call
Headline: Southern Africa: Mkapa's Call
Body_Text:
THE call by outgoing Southern African Development Community (SADC) chairman Benjamin Mkapa on the need for the region to form a united front and combat escalating poverty needs following up by those concerned.
Mr Mkapa, who is President of the Republic of Tanzania, is a very senior African statesman speaking with unquestioned authority. His message is simple and to the point. There is so much potential in the SADC region which, if properly tapped, will transform the area into a more vibrant economic giant.
But what is lacking is the will for people to come together and create structures to confront the problems such as hallowing poverty. In these days of globalisation, every one needs the other. The world is so small that it will no longer be fashionable for governments or indeed regions to survive operating on their own.
SADC has one advantage right now that should strengthen the call for the creation of a united front: Most of the problems faced by member states in the region are either similar or just the same. It is, therefore, easier for countries to identify common problems and tackle them in the order or priority.
There is a danger that unless SADC reviewed its position and looked at itself whether or not it would survive operating in the form it is, the regional risks getting irrelevant.
Strictly speaking, the call for a united front is not quite new. There have been numerous others in the past which have been followed by protocols, but after that there has been no action.
That is the attitude that needs to change if leaders, as Mr Mkapa says, will have to fly where there is a sorghum, like his example of the proverbial bird he refers to.
SADC, therefore, needs to be pro-active. There is so much potential for anything here. That potential should be tapped.
Up to now there are still unnecessary restrictions in travelling between countries in the region. The ground remains uneven. The weaker continue getting weaker and the stronger remain mightier, and the cycle goes on.
At the moment there is so much individualism when the rest of the world is moving with times. Leaders in the community have a serious task to begin working towards transforming this important region. A lot has been done so far. What member states need is to look at the weaknesses and see where they can be realigned. In the absence of such a move, calls for a united front will remain a voice in the wilderness.

Original_Search_Title: Maganjo Installs New Sh23m Millet Flour Mill
Headline: Uganda: Maganjo Installs New Sh23m Millet Flour Mill
Body_Text:
Kampala — Maganjo Grain Millers has installed a new millet mill worth $13,000 (about sh24m) to meet the increasing foreign demand.
Ricks Kayizzi writes that Mary Tamale, the managing director, said recently that the new mill would produce a tonne of millet flour per hour.
"The demand for millet flour has skyrocketed mainly among Ugandans abroad and other nationals," Tamale said.
Maganjo produces about 30 tonnes of maize, soya, sorghum and millet flour daily.
Tamale said a goods store with a 1,000-tonne capacity was under construction.
"Our aim is to push up our output to 50 tonnes by the end of next year. The local and foreign demand is projected to have almost doubled by then," she said.

Original_Search_Title: K'jong Mine Limestone
Headline: Uganda: K'jong Mine Limestone
Body_Text:
Kampala — OVER 1,000 Karimojong and Pokot have embarked on mining limestone for
sale to Tororo Cement Limited, reports Nathan Etengu.
The miners, who include armed warriors, have settled with their families
at three concession sites established by Tororo Cement in Katikekile and
Nangirokomol, both in Moroto district and at Anguruma in Nakapiripirit
district.
Tororo Cement pays sh70,000 per 20-tonne tipper-load of limestone, while
the loaders are paid sh10,000 per truck.
"We sometimes make sh140,000 per month while a good deal can even
realise more than that," said Longok Lochiyo, a warrior from Tapac on the
slopes of Mt. Moroto.
Pulukol Erengengole, another warrior, said they could make more money if
the number of tipper lorries that collected the stones were increased.
"Business has slowed down. Otherwise we had got an easy way of making a
living that has made us abandon reliance on cows and rustling. From this
business, I have been able to buy clothes for my wife and sorghum for the
family," Erengengole said.
He said the limestone business had made the gun literally useless to the
people who were engaged in collecting the stones.

Original_Search_Title: A Fool And His Money
Headline: Zimbabwe: A Fool And His Money
Body_Text:
Harare — Delta ($6 200-$7 200) up 16.1%
IN a clear indication of the times, Delta's share price was going up last week in spite of the recent surge in interest rates. It became the first company this year to register a significant volume increase in its product range, a pointer that people would rather use their dwindling disposable income to drink away their sorrows than watch the economy bury them alive.
At the recent AGM, management noted that sorghum and lager volumes had both eclipsed the budgeted and prior year volumes. Carbonated soft drinks volumes continued to decline partly because of the price management from the ministry of industry. The void that resulted from price monitoring has been decisively filled by non-carbonated soft drinks giant Rainbow Beverages, whose demand grew many times over as consumers switched from carbonated soft drinks that have become scarce on the shelves. Non-carbonated soft drinks benefit Delta, the owner of Rainbow Beverages, in many ways than one. Because the "Mr Juicy" brand is locally owned and controlled, the company does not have to pay for any licensing fees to Coca Cola for example. This means they are both bottlers and the owner of intellectual property.
Another benefit is that the company can independently expand this brand into the region. This they cannot do with beer brands since most, save for Zambezi, Bohlinger and a few others are owned by SABMiller which also happens to be the biggest brewer on the continent. After putting in so much money into the brand, the country's biggest company by turnover is now reaping the fruits. The company also sets to continue breaking records of paying dividends.
Management noted that their cash pile is back to half a trillion even after shelling out an unprecedented $150 billion in dividends. Finance income alone has been $83 billion to date, making the Delta treasury bigger than some smaller banks in the country.
Dog
Runaway Price
(read Inflation)
AGE old wisdom would say, "A fool and his money are soon parted". Leonard, a former colleague would elegantly add, "It's now happening to us all". The saying is picture perfect for what has been happening in the past three or so months.
All of us have become fools in that at some point we have helplessly watched our wealth erode. Some like me, in the name of patriotism and hope in the new monetary authorities, shied away from all sorts of "evil" parallel market dealings and invested money in beloved local markets.
At the time, this was more to do with confidence in the new look central bank than patriotism. I really felt I could win big by looking for depressed assets on the local stock market and wait for everyone else to see what I had seen. Alas, my wealth eroded and, worse still, others are yet to see what I saw. I have painfully acknowledged that I might have mistaken visions for vision. It now seems my whole thinking was based on some fickle illusion. Now, close to two years after advising my colleagues not to lock value in fixed assets with low returns and instead to "look East" towards the undervalued stock market that would bring untold riches, I find myself licking my wounds of single mindedness.
To add injury to insult, the same monetary authorities I had put my trust in are saying "well done" to all those who were buying and externalising foreign currency while I was playing clean. They are saying these "scoundrels" can now buy fuel and just about anything else for resale at exorbitant prices under a no-questions-asked policy. It's now too late for me to turn. I just patiently wait for the market to at least give me what I ploughed in. I will not hold my breath for any real growth in this shrinking economy. How quickly my wealth and I have parted!

Original_Search_Title: UN Relocates More Than 2,000 Refugees in Chad Threatened By Rains
Headline: Chad: UN Relocates More Than 2,000 Refugees in Chad Threatened By Rains
Body_Text:
In a race against time with the rainy season already at hand in remote southern Chad, the United Nations refugee agency has already relocated nearly a quarter of the 10,000 needy refugees from the Central African Republic (CAR) who are threatened with being cut off from all aid.
"The rains are already slowing the operation, with convoys increasingly having to cross rain-swollen wadis (dry riverbeds)," UN High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR) spokesman Ron Redmond told a news briefing in Geneva today. "However, we have been able to move people every day since the beginning of the operation and we hope to finalize the movements within the next two to three weeks."
UNHCR plans to relocate the refugees, mostly women and children, who have been living in makeshift settlements on the Chadian side of the remote border since fleeing unrest last month, to an existing camp at Amboko where humanitarian assistance can be more easily provided.
Amboko already hosts 13,000 CAR refugees who fled after a military coup in 2003, but it can accommodate up to 27,000 people. At the camp, the new arrivals are provided with UN World Food Programme (WFP) food rations, including beans, sorghum, oil and sugar, as well as with UNHCR relief items such as mats, jerry cans and kitchen utensils.
Chad is also hosting more than 200,000 Sudanese refugees from the war-torn Darfur region of Sudan who are currently housed in 12 camps in the eastern part of the country.

Original_Search_Title: Moving 10,000 Refugees Threatened By Floods Could Take Three Weeks - UN
Headline: Chad: Moving 10,000 Refugees Threatened By Floods Could Take Three Weeks - UN
Body_Text:
Racing against the fast approaching rains in Chad, slowed by the lack of roads and facing the threat of bandits, the United Nations refugee agency said today it expects to complete within three weeks the re__cpLocation of 10,000 needy refugees from the Central African Republic (CAR) who are threatened with being cut off from all aid.
Since the operation started on Wednesday, the UN High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR) and its partners have so far moved some 325 refugees, mostly women and children, from makeshift settlements in remote areas of southern Chad, where they have been living since fleeing unrest last month, to an existing camp at Amboko where humanitarian assistance can be more easily provided.
"On Wednesday morning, three trucks carrying the first 97 refugees made their way through rain-flooded areas around Betel village, where 3,000 refugees are staying, to Amboko camp," UNHCR spokesman Ron Redmond told a news briefing in Geneva.
"The convoy arrived in Amboko after a five-hour drive. The distance is less than 75 kilometres, so you can imagine the conditions. It was escorted by Chadian gendarmes as there are frequent bandit attacks in the area. Yesterday morning, amid a heavy downpour, we managed to transfer an additional 229 refugees in eight trucks."
The International Federation of the Red Cross and the Chadian Red Cross are also providing trucks to help in the operation. The refugees arrived in Chad last month after fleeing unrest in northern CAR.
Amboko already hosts 13,000 CAR refugees who fled after a military coup in 2003, but it can accommodate up to 27,000 people. At the camp, the new arrivals are provided with UN World Food Programme (WFP) food rations, including beans, sorghum, oil and sugar, as well as with UNHCR relief items such as mats, jerry cans and kitchen utensils.
The re__cpLocation operation is expected to be completed within a maximum of three weeks, Mr. Redmond said.
Chad is also hosting more than 200,000 Sudanese refugees from the war-torn Darfur region of Sudan who are currently housed in 12 camps in the eastern part of the country.

Original_Search_Title: UN Starts Relocating 10,000 Refugees in Path of Flooding
Headline: Chad: UN Starts Relocating 10,000 Refugees in Path of Flooding
Body_Text:
With flooding already hindering movement in southern Chad, the United Nations refugee agency and its partners today began the weeks-long re__cpLocation of 10,000 needy refugees from the neighbouring Central African Republic (CAR) who are threatened with being cut off from all aid during the rainy season.
The refugees, who have been living in makeshift settlements around 17 villages in remote areas in southern Chad after fleeing unrest in northern CAR in June, are being moved to an existing camp at Amboko, where UN High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR) can more easily provide humanitarian assistance.
This morning, escorted by Chadian gendarmes for the five-hour drive through bandit-ridden areas, the first three trucks carrying 97 refugees, mostly women and children, ploughed through rain-flooded areas to Amboko camp.
"Yesterday, we were afraid the refugees would refuse to move, but this morning they were happy to go to Amboko, where living conditions are more acceptable," UNHCR logistics officer Colin Pryce said. On arrival they were provided with food rations like beans and sorghum, as well as relief items like mats and jerry cans.
Amboko already hosts 13,000 refugees who fled CAR after a military coup in 2003, but it can accommodate up to 27,000 people. The Chadian government has provided UNHCR with additional land to build a temporary transit centre.
A second re__cpLocation is set for tomorrow. The International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent is providing seven trucks, boosting UNHCR's transport capacity to 10 trucks.

Original_Search_Title: 'We Don't Persuade Everybody to Take Alcohol'
Headline: Nigeria: 'We Don't Persuade Everybody to Take Alcohol'
Body_Text:
Lagos — Keith Richards, Managing Director of Guinness Nigeria Plc, is a
remarkable man. Last week, while having a time-out with some mediamen, he
broke the news of the forgiveness of the substantial part of Nigeria's
external debts to his guests. That was before the media went to town with
the story; before Nigerians heard it from their own president in a
telecast. A friend who knew how passionate he is about his country of
residence had called him earlier from his home country, UK, to tell him what
was already in the news over there. And he felt he should let someone else
know.
Meet with Keith Richards for the first time and he's sure to come off as
Keith, a friend next door. The warmth he radiates, the flow of conversation
which comes to him naturally and the naturalness, a character devoid of
affectation, all unite to present a man made for marketing.
"In the 1980's when the government banned the importation of malted
barley, Guinness was one of the companies that invested in the new
technology to produce beer from sorghum and maize, materials grown locally,"
he says with ease. "In the 1990's we invested 45 million pounds in a capital
expenditure programme. If we hadn't made that investment then, we wouldn't
have had the capacity to meet the demands for our products now. We made
decisions over 20 years that have borne fruits today."
He then goes into some analyses of the interplay between the economy and
the socio-political life of the country he has come to love. He thinks that
with the world becoming smaller, and with Nigerians traveling more often,
the influences on the consumers of his company's products have also become
complex and more sensitive. That also means that breweries have to be more
alert and responsive to these ever increasing needs and sophisticated
tastes.
As Richards puts it, "influences from Europe and America are now
predominant. Nigerian consumers want new brands with international
quality. And people are excited about it. The products and their packaging
would have to change."
Guinness' newest answer to the dynamism of the industry is Guinness
Extra Smooth. As he speaks about his latest contribution to the bar, his
face radiates the knowledge of and love for the product and also what the
expectations of his customers are.
Why smooth? Does it mean that the ones that came before it - Harp,
Guinness Stout etc. - have rough edges or particles? "No, not at all," comes
a ready reply. "It is about the mouth feel. How does the drink taste in your
mouth? How does it feel as it slips down? Hundreds of thousands of people
here adore Guinness Foreign Extra Stout. But sometimes they say they want a
slightly different taste. They want something mild - Guinness' blackness,
reputation and taste but sometimes they find it a little bit too
bitter. They want something that suits their mood, when they're relaxing or
chilling out. This is where Extra Smooth comes in. The taste is simply
smooth. So is the whole personality of the product. And so is your own
feeling as a drinker, when you drink it."
The brewery industry in Nigeria is hardly depressed in the functional
sense of the word. Somehow, those who drink would always find a way of
satisfying their appetite - in and out of buoyancy. The companies and all
allied operators are usually the better for it. Alcohol is one item whose
abuse has always caused individuals and institutions heartache. The line
between simply enjoying the flavour of alcohol and the sordid world of
alcoholism may indeed be narrower than many people realise.
Encyclopedia Britannica puts this phenomenon succinctly: "The vast
majority of drinkers in most of the world are light, occasional, and
moderate drinkers - normal drinkers who experience no harm from their own
use of alcoholic beverages... Nevertheless, the problem drinkers invoke
sufficient troubles for themselves, their families, their employees, their
occupational or social associates, and their communities and society
generally so that 'alcohol problems' are major causes of disorder and
suffering...
"Although drinking itself is hardly ever regarded as the sufficient
cause of alcoholism, this disease could not arise without the use of
alcohol. Moreover, alcoholics and heavy drinkers are especially susceptible
to the development of some other diseases, not specifically alcoholic, and
are then less able to withstand the vicissitudes of ill health. Alcoholics
and problem drinkers undoubtedly contribute to the deterioration of the
mental health of other members of their families and often to the break down
of family life...
"A special offense related to drinking is alcohol-impaired driving of
motor vehicles and the resulting high rate of accidents, with fatalities,
personal injuries, and property damage... Although people with very low
alcohol concentrations in their blood - the equivalent of one or two drinks
- do not figure in accidents more than those with alcohol, the chances of
being involved in a traffic accident rise precipitously with increasing
blood alcohol concentrations beyond these minimal levels..."
In Nigeria and many other countries, cigarette makers are compelled to
feature in their advertisements warnings to the effect that smokers are
liable to die young or attract health complications. If excessive drinking
of alcohol can cause untold damage to the individual and his society, won't
it be sensible to make it mandatory for breweries to carry similar messages?
Like every top player in the industry, it is Richards' dream and duty to see
his firm's fortunes grow. That could mean more men on the glass, the bottle,
the carton or crate. We've already seen the flip side of decent
drinking. The homes that may break. The minds that may bend. Or the society
whose sense of order could fall on its knees.
Now that he sells Guinness Extra Stout like ice cream, bringing a lot of
innovation into its promotion (A geodesic dome, an African first - 20 metre
diameter, 10 metres high, was used to launch it the other day), will he be a
happier man if more Nigerians take to alcohol?
His response sounds sincere and well considered: "We target people
who're already drinking, people who already have taste for the type of
beverages that we produce. We're not in the business of persuading everybody
to take alcohol. We believe it adds to a full social life but we realise
that there are a number of people who for very many valid reasons do not
take alcohol.
"The reasons may be religious, moral or social. We respect them. We will
not market in such a way as to attack such people. We're very happy to
display, market our brands. This is one avenue for living a full social
life. In that aspect, I'm happy to bring in new consumers, at the right age
- say over 20 years - and encourage them to savour our products.
"We will increase our distribution networks. If the economy improves,
people who drink cheaper beer or drinks like ogogoro, palm wine or akpeteshi
- some of whom are dangerous to health - will patronise something
better.
"We have a marketing code that does not allow us to make claims that
can't be substantiated. We do not try to make people believe in a certain
prowess that is not there. Rather, we are keen to have our standards conform
to the ones obtained anywhere in the world."
As a teenager, Richards was an avid lover and player of rugby. After
each game, he would feel like sipping beer, like the rest. That was how his
drinking life began. "Then, I felt good because I liked to belong to the
in-crowd," he recalls. "I was quite confident, a social young man. I wanted
to have a drink with friends. I used to play with an adult team;. having a
drink with my seniors was very exciting."
At 52 years, his liking for beer is intact. So is his positive,
invigorating attitude to life. And a culture of productivity and pursuits
that is mobile. Come October this year, he will pass on the baton of the
leadership of the management of Guinness to his successor. He tells you
gladly that being on the driver's seat of one of Nigeria's leading breweries
has been a fulfilling experience, that the years from 2002 to date have been
fantastic. Life after Guinness, for him, will mean staying back in the
country and giving more to it while also consolidating on his achievements
so far and making fresh inroads into Corporate Nigeria.
What will he consider his legacy? "As a team, we have tried to change
things. We have tried to ensure that Guinness reflects best practice
globally and remains part of the local community. We've got a balance
between being part of the Biagio global family, having world class practice
and operating here in a friendly environment and with a good sense of
corporate social responsibility. Whatever we do is informed by what our
customers tell us. That's the reason for our success."
When Nigerians in Britain began to clamour for beer from home, the same
way they longed for Nigerian menu, Guinness rose up to the challenge and
started exporting drinks there. As Richards explains, it is better to get it
done professionally than leave it to unscrupulous business people whose only
driving motive is profiteering.
Again, his clinical, albeit realistic, approach to customer relations
comes to the fore. "We need to satisfy them. To me, it doesn't matter
whether my customers are in Mushin, Aba, Abakaliki or in Peckham or
Bronx. All I know is that they're Nigerians and I want to get them the
product they want."
This chat keeps shifting to expressions of his admiration for
Nigeria. "We, multinationals, can be ambassadors of Nigeria. There are many
negative reports out there that are not even true. We can demonstrate to
potential investors that it's a great place to do business.
"I see a great future; a rocky road, no doubt. But the potential of
Nigeria is enormous and the people are aspirational. It's because of the
people that Nigerians have so much hope. It's an incredible
country. Everyday I see something that excites me. Yes, something that makes
me angry or frustrated at times but I know the future is bright.
"Most of the expatriates stay on the Island. But I go into bars, bukas
regularly and talk to Nigerian consumers. I have never ever received
anything but a welcome hospitality. I've never been harassed; never felt
under threat. It's through interaction that we've been able to upgrade and
develop products like Extra Smooth. That way, I've been able to learn about
Nigerians and what they think about patriotism, about life in general."
In a world that could be fiercely individualistic and blindly
nationalistic, it's not everyday one comes across someone who speaks very
glowingly about another country. Especially if the divide is between the
First World and the Third World. This man, Keith Richards, must be a
Nigerian.

Original_Search_Title: Contaminated Alcohol Kills 48 Near Nairobi
Headline: Kenya: Contaminated Alcohol Kills 48 Near Nairobi
Body_Text:
Nairobi — At least 48 people had died by Monday after drinking contaminated
alcohol which was sold by local traders in Kenya's Machakos district,
southeast of Nairobi, medical and police officials said.
Another 84 people had been treated in hospital for suspected methanol
poisoning, the medical superintendent of the Machakos district hospital,
Simon Mueke, said. Twenty-two of those being treated were in serious
condition.
Three people had lost their eye sight, while another five had developed
"eye complications", Mueke said. Most of the deaths, he added, had occurred
on Friday and Saturday.
Methanol, a highly toxic alcohol commonly used as an industrial solvent,
is believed to have been added to a home-distilled gin apparently in an
attempt to make it more potent, according to Mueke.
Permanent or partial blindness in methanol-poisoning, he added, results
from retinal damage caused by intra-retinal metabolism of methanol and the
accumulation of formic acid. Inhibition of normal metabolism in the optic
nerve cells due to the presence of methanol could also lead to blindness, he
said.
"Death is in most cases the result of liver or kidney failure," Mueke
said.
He said doctors were using conventional ethanol, including vodka, to
treat the methanol poisoning cases because ordinary alcohol inhibited
methanol metabolism in the body and enhanced the elimination of the toxic
unmetabolised poisonous compounds.
"We have been using vodka because it displaces methanol from the
receptors in the blood," Mueke said. Those who were very sick, he added,
were being asked to take sips of vodka.
A police spokesman said they had arrested four people, including a woman
bootlegger said to have supplied the killer drink. Some 105 litres of the
drink had been seized and samples would be taken to the government chemists
for analysis to establish what exactly the alcohol was laced with.
Adulteration of home-made alcoholic drinks with industrial chemicals has
become a common problem in Kenya during the past few years.
More than 130 people died in a slum in Nairobi in 2000 after consuming
contaminated liqour.
In 1998, the government banned several locally distilled low-priced
liquors, but the consumption of "chang'aa" - a popular gin distilled from
fermented maize meal or sorghum remained widespread despite frequent police
raids on suspected distillers.
Kenya's internal security minister, John Michuki, told reporters the
woman who supplied the drink was a "well known peddler who has been arrested
on numerous occasions, but the law being an ass, has not been able to
convict her".
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United
Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Cutty Sark Hotel One of the Best Family Outings
Headline: Zimbabwe: Cutty Sark Hotel One of the Best Family Outings
Body_Text:
Harare — THE beautiful and animal friendly Cutty Sark Hotel remains one of the
best family outings that can help release pressure of the hustle and bustle
of city life.
The natural beauty of the place enthralled me upon my arrival. There is
so much beauty that can create memories to those who visit the Cutty Sark
Hotel.
It is not easy to miss the fresh breath of air from the leafy trees
surrounding the hotel. The fresh smell of water and fish blowing from the
shores of Lake Kariba makes the place like paradise.
Tourists booked at the hotel have the opportunity to watch from the
privacy of their deluxe rooms, animals such as baboons, hippopotamus and
elephants when they come to the shores.
The birds that co-exist with the communities around wake up people every
morning before they take their baths.
My first meal was a hot lunch that was served by ever smiling waiters
who made me feel deeply in love with the hotel.
Later, I enjoyed a safari lunch of sadza and game meat. I could have
chosen fresh or dried fish glazed with peanut butter and served with
traditional sorghum sadza.
In the afternoons, local tourists who frequent the hotel relax by the
pool with refreshing beverages while others have their popular candle-lit
dinners with their loved ones at night.
Cutty Sark is an ideal place for school trips as children get the
opportunity to view the wild animals they only view in magazines and on
television.
Air Zimbabwe resumed its flights to Kariba on May 16 after having
suspended them for some time.
Although the Cutty Sark Hotel is still serving food and offering
accommodation, it needs a renovation.
Mr Ronald Sithole, the sales and marketing manager, admitted that his
hotel was going through a difficult time. There has been a decline in the
number of visitors to the hotel resulting in the hotel adjusting rates.
Like any other hotel, the increase in food prices and the
non-availability of some foodstuff has also affected the Cutty Sark. But the
hotel management has crafted programmes meant to lure visitors to Cutty
Sark.
"We have embarked on a massive marketing strategy to lure business
people to hold conferences here," Sithole said.
"Children below the age of 12 do not pay for accommodation because we
want to help our clients to cut down on expenses. We also have facilities to
look after children in the absence of their parents," he added.

Original_Search_Title: Pula Devaluation Draws Further Condemnation
Headline: Botswana: Pula Devaluation Draws Further Condemnation
Body_Text:
Although sections of the economy have welcomed the crawling peg exchange
system that will see the continuous adjustment of the exchange rate of the
Pula over other currencies, others have argued that the 12 percent
devaluation that came with the changes will have a negative impact on the
economy.
Some of the fears expressed in the local money markets are that the
devaluation would have an impact on equity, bond and portfolio markets and
at the same time fuel inflation. The bond market is expected to be the major
loser with the equity markets expected to bear the brunt in the coming
months as inflation takes its toll. Other circles point out that foreign
portfolio inventors would be affected in the short term.
While arguing that the new system could be useful for the economy,
Botswana Confederation for Commerce Industry and Manpower (BOCCIM) said the
devaluation would send negative signals to investors.
"The crawling peg exchange system announced is a useful tool as long as
it is used with due diligence and is responsibly managed," Elias Dewah,
BOCCIM executive director said yesterday in a statement.
However, Dewah said that the devaluation comes with negative results for
the whole economy. Apart from the devaluation going to fuel inflation, Dewah
added that the devaluation would affect investor confidence, while at the
same time foreign investors profit, repatriation will yield lower
returns.
"BOCCIM would like to urge the government to turn these difficult times
into opportunities by making urgent and structural adjustments in the
economy so that the devaluation of the Pula is not the only option at its
disposal in times of cash flow problems".
Asset managers also argued that the crawling peg exchange system would
inform future and well thought devaluations.
"On the changes in the exchange rate regime, I believe it is a positive
development," Kudzani Pickup, an analyst at African Alliance, an asset
management company said yesterday. "The crawling peg should eliminate the
need for Monday morning devaluations and their shock effects through
"continuous adjustments". We move one step away from adjusting the actual
peg to adjusting the rate of the crawl," Pickup added.
However, chief executive officer of Stockbrokers Botswana, Wayne
Osterberg said there is a need for a holistic analysis of how the new system
would fare, although acknowledging that the devaluation would have an
impact.
"We need to analyse the impact of the new foreign exchange mechanisms in
more detail to see the full effects but it appears that the move to a
crawling peg will take the exchange rate determination out of the "market
forces" and into a directly managed environment," Osterberg said, in his
reaction to the devaluation on Monday.
He however, added that the foreign portfolio investors would bear the
brunt of the devaluation.
"The move will be greeted with dismay by foreign portfolio investors,
particularly those in the developing bond market and those holding the
BW001's which mature on Wednesday," Osterberg said in his reaction to the
devaluation on Monday.
The brokerage sponsors many of the companies that are listed on the
Botswana Stock Exchange (BSE). The government bond-BW001 will today reach
its maturity, but government has hinted that it will not be replacing
it.
"For equity investors, the move is relatively neutral in the short term,
but in the longer term it is likely to have a negative effect as the
inflationary pressures grow and the confidence of foreign investors is
dented by a second direct intervention in the exchange rate in 16 months,"
Osterberg added.
Some of the anticipated results of the devaluation include the
anticipated rise of the cost of living. Experts say that the move is
inflationary 'as it will raise the cost of imported goods and services and
the main beneficiary will be the exporters'.
It is believed that through the devaluation government would reduce the
likelihood of a budget deficit through a 28 percent increase in diamond
revenues.
Local traders yesterday reeled from the recent and unexpected
devaluation of the Pula and criticized government's decision saying it "is
not positive for the economy" but has just opened old wounds.
Importers of raw materials from South Africa contend that the abrupt
decision by government to devalue the Pula by a staggering 12 percent has
been a cold and inconsiderate unpopular decision that will not benefit the
Small, Medium and Micro Entrepreneurs (SMMEs).
The decision follows hard on the heels of the 7.5 percent devaluation
that took place in February 2004, which traders strongly protested.
"I have been trying to recover from the previous devaluation, so this
recent one has devastated my business. I have even lost focus," cried Alpha
Moesi, managing director of Phofu Sorghum Milling in Tonota.
Like other disgruntled small and medium scale entrepreneurs, Moesi
decries the unprecedented devaluation citing "lack of protectionist
tendencies" displayed by Monetary Authorities (MAs). Government should roll
out alternatives that will remedy and cushion the devaluation as a way to
support small-scale business people, Moesi said. "These are the people who
have purchasing power." Phofu milling, a medium scale sorghum mill company
imports raw materials (in the form of sorghum and packaging consignments) at
an amount ranging between P500,000 to P600,000 per month. Moesi says she has
been forced to stop production and "might end up retrenching some of my
employees," Moesi has a labour force of 29 people and is saying almost half
of them are "on the brink of retrenchment. There is nothing I can do," she
announced yesterday.
When the Bank of Botswana (BoB) lessened the Pula value by 7.5 percent
in February 2004, there was crisis in Moesi's business as she reported a
P1.8 million loss.
There are many disgruntled Alpha Moesi's in the trade
industry. Importers and exporters alike have described MAs decision as a
sham. Sthamile Sithole, coordinator of Exporters Association of Botswana
(EAB) regretted the decision, saying their export driven business will
suffer as a result of the devaluation. "We are concerned because our clients
get raw materials from outside," cried Sithole adding: "This is a big
mistake. Manufacturers will have a difficult time."
The devaluation came as a heavy blow to the textile and transport
industry, says Sithole.
Chrisma Raja, business spokesperson for CLAN, a freight company
announced yesterday that the devaluation has forced his company to increase
its charges despite fears that they might lose customers. "We don't know by
how much we will increase our charges, " Raja asserted yesterday.
EAB clients include Kgalagadi Breweries Limited, Nortex, B and M
Garments, FedEx and Botswana Export Credit Insurance Company.

Original_Search_Title: Kala, Mwange Refugee Camps to Get Sorghum
Headline: Zambia: Kala, Mwange Refugee Camps to Get Sorghum
Body_Text:
GOVERNMENT will soon start distributing sorghum to Kala and Mwange refugee camps because the country has no sufficient maize.
Home Affairs Permanent Secretary Peter Mumba said in an interview yesterday that the sorghum donated to the country by the United States government would last for three to four months
Mr Mumba said Government would ensure that hunger prevailing in refugee camps was checked and urged refugees to cooperate with the State until the situation was normalised.
He said he could not understand why refugees were reported to be shunning sorghum
"We are not giving them poison, they are refusing to eat because they want maize, we do not have enough maize to cater for them but we can give them Sorghum.
I have been reliably informed that they are refusing to eat, reasons I don't know," Mr Mumba complained.
He said refugees who were refusing to eat Sorghum were frustrating Government's efforts to control the prevailing hunger situation in camps and asked them to stop the culture because it was not assisting in anyway.

Original_Search_Title: Mumias to Export 10,000mt of Sugar
Headline: Kenya: Mumias to Export 10,000mt of Sugar
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Mumias Sugar Company will export 10,000 metric tonnes of white sugar
later this month to meet its European Union export quota.
Information obtained from agents of a vessel chartered to transport the
consignment to the EU market indicates the vessel will be arriving on May 10
at the Port of Mombasa to load the sugar.
According to Captain Abubakar Ali, Executive Director of Seabulk
Shipping Services Limited, the vessel will be docking at the harbour from
South Africa enroute to Europe.
Meanwhile, activity at the ultra modern Grainbulk Handling Limited
facility at Mombasa Port take centre stage with the arrival of grain carrier
vessel, MV. Island Bulker tomorrow.
The vessel will be docking in to discharge 14,800 metric tonnes of bulk
maize and wheat.
Another grain carrier, MV Liberty Spirit, which is a regular caller at
the port will be arriving on May 9, to discharge sorghum weighing 7690
metric tonnes.
Mechanised grain handling at the port has led to efficiency and quick
vessels turnaround.
GBHL was built at a cost of US$ 35 million, with the aim of modernising
the port of Mombasa.
It was commissioned on March 15, 2000 by former President Daniel
Moi.
At the relatively smaller Mombasa Old port, a vessel christened MV Angel
is currently offloading 250 metric tonnes of dried fish from Somalia.
Elsewhere, MS. Royal Star, the only cruise ship whose home port is
Mombasa will be calling at the port today for bunkering services.
The ultra modern vessel owned and operated by the African Safari Club
(ASC) plies the vast Indian Ocean region.

Original_Search_Title: Is Alcohol Consumption a Problem in Our Society?
Headline: Rwanda: Is Alcohol Consumption a Problem in Our Society?
Body_Text:
Kigali — Alcohol consumption in the Rwandan society is as old as the society
itself. Alcohol ranks number one as the beverage offered in welcoming
visitors in nearly all households. Drinking is considered to be a harmless
practice among the vast majority of Rwandans. Even babies are fed on banana
wine (Urugwagwa) or sorghum brew (Ikigage). No one has paused to ask if this
seemingly harmless practice might actually be a big problem to many
unsuspecting Rwandans. Just consider the following facts:
-Majority of youths and adults alike that took part in the Genocide of
1994 had to first get very drunk before indulging in the evil acts of
hacking people to death.
-It is not possible to estimate the number of marriages that have broken
due to alcohol consumption by one or both spouses.
-More than fifty percent of patients at Ndera mental hospital cite
drinking alcohol as the reason that sparked off their various
illnesses.
-It is not possible to estimate the number of individuals that contract
the deadly Aids virus through careless sex after drinking excessively.
-Nearly all the people that have raped or defiled babies were drunk or
claim to have been under the influence of alcohol.
The list is endless and the above social ills are not unique to
Rwanda. The amount of money spent on drinking can be mind boggling
considering our general economic status. For example, a person earning
30,000Frw can daringly drink one or two Mutzigs a day. A simple calculation
indicates this to be about half his income gone on just alcohol
consumption. Can we then admit that alcohol consumption poses a particular
danger to our society? I personally think it does. Apart from the above
general facts brought about by excessive drinking, something in our culture
encourages drinking alcohol without considering its bad effects and
outcomes. I am not qualified to enumerate the medical effects on the brain
and body in general but I hope someone out there in the medical profession
will help.
Generally speaking therefore, alcohol is considered to be a drug and is
addictive just like any other known addictive drugs. The only difference is
that it is freely available for purchase and its brewing is largely
legal. So, when does one know if they are addicted to alcohol or not? This
is difficult to determine as it varies from individual to individual. What
is common among all alcohol addicts are the symptoms. Many claim to be
unable to sleep without drinking. Family life becomes strained for the
married couples. Endless wrangles and violence between partners are quite
common. Generally, the victim's behavior becomes increasingly intolerable by
those s/he lives with. And all along the victim imagines that s/he can
control his/her habit. As we become more affluent, chances are that alcohol
consumption is likely to increase. It is hoped that it does not become a
function of the above mentioned and other social ills.
Before going much farther I should endeavor to define addiction. A
person is considered to be an addict when drugs (in this case alcohol)
control his/her life. S/he is obsessed and compelled to drink regardless of
the visible negative social and physical effects on his person. Experts
believe that addiction is a disease that is incurable but which can be
arrested if the correct steps are taken. They also concur that it is mainly
a symptom of deeper character defects that a victim has not identified or
been able to face in day to day life. It is therefore true that many
drinkers in our society drink to cover up or "forget" some of their deeper
troubles. They lack the courage to face the world without regular "Dutch
Courage".
What can be done to deal with the drinking problem in our society?
Education of the masses on the evils of this habit can partly solve this
social evil. We all probably know but in our ignorance think that we can
stop whenever we want or control the habit. It is simply a matter of
reminding us and emphasizing that it can, in most cases does, render our
lives unmanageable. We should be reminded that it is a drug that addicts
just like any other and many among us are prone to addiction. In more
advanced societies, fellowships that help individuals quit or break the
habit of drinking exist. These are usually twelve steps fellowships such as
Alcoholics Anonymous and others. But these can be useful to a society and
individuals who admit and acknowledge that they have a drinking problem. If
we think we have no problem and should continue enjoying our Mutzig, Amstel,
Karahanyuze etc, it is time we re-examine and evaluate.

Original_Search_Title: Fuel Shortages Return to Zimbabwe's Cities
Headline: Zimbabwe: Shortages Return to Zimbabwe's Cities
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — FUEL stocks ran out in major centres of Zimbabwe, and additional sources
of desperately needed grain were sought this week as the country's economic
decline picked up speed after a temporary slowdown ahead of last month's
election.
Widespread fuel shortages, which eased during elections after five years
of erratic supply, returned to the capital Harare and other centres this
