week. Shortages of electricity and other basic commodities also
returned.
Zimbabwe is struggling to raise enough foreign currency to pay its
import bill.
While the government claims the economy will grow 5% this year and
foreign exchange earnings will rise dramatically, to $3bn, the country is
failing to raise $34m a month to import enough fuel to meet its needs. It
also needs $17m a month to import electricity.
Harare was plunged into darkness last week after nationwide power cuts
that authorities blamed on technical problems.
Zimbabwe's power utility said generators at power stations and a faulty
distribution line connecting Zimbabwe to a power grid in the Democratic
Republic of Congo caused the blackouts.
President Robert Mugabe's new "development cabinet" has not yet made an
announcement on the steps it intends to take to reverse the economy's
downward spiral. Central bank governor Gideon Gono revised his inflation
target from 20%-35% at the end of this year to 75%-85%. Inflation, which
peaked at 622% last year, is now 123%.
Meanwhile, it was reported on Tuesday that Zimbabwe had turned to
Zambia, Uganda and Tanzania for grain imports as the state Grain Marketing
Board tries to replenish dwindling maize and wheat stocks.
Public Service, Labour and Social Welfare Minister Nicholas Goche
confirmed to the IRIN news agency that Zimbabwe had expanded its grain
sources to include these three countries, in addition to major supplier
SA.
"We have been looking for more and more sources of grain. There are
contracts that have been in existence but not in force - we are reactivating
those, and signing up new ones, to ensure food keeps coming," Goche
said.
Goche, formerly state security minister, assumed his new portfolio
following a cabinet reshuffle last week.
The government's admission came as economists said the country needed to
import at least 1,2-million tons of maize, 200000 tons of wheat, and
unspecified quantities of barley and sorghum to meet its immediate
needs.
Although there were no responses from the embassies of the three source
countries, an official at the Grain Marketing Board procurement division was
quoted by IRIN as saying the Zimbabwe-Uganda maize import deal was signed
during President Yoweri Museveni's recent visit.
State Security Minister Didymus Mutasa said he could not divulge any
details beyond what Goche had said. With ZWnews

Original_Search_Title: In Museveni We Are Witnessing a Replay of Obote Lies, Mistakes
Headline: Uganda: In Museveni We Are Witnessing a Replay of Obote Lies, Mistakes
Body_Text:
There are two Acholi sayings that are important to understand this
piece. One says: Kadi ibut ki maru ite pii, lokke wang mo bikati
kamaleng. It means that even if a son-in law and his mother-in-law have
sexual intercourse under the water, the matter will one day surface to the
top for everybody to know.
The second says: Pe ibed ki ngat mo keken ite-lum pien kadi lum bene
winyo-wuÃ¢. Which means that it is taboo to have sexual intercourse in the
bush because the grass will hear you.
In the first ever autobiographical revelation by exiled former president
Dr Apollo Milton Obote, as told from his home in Lusaka, Zambia, to
Monitor's Andrew Mwenda, there are a number of lies (untrue information),
which I should like to correct for posterity, peace, unity and
reconciliation of Uganda.
I don't blame Obote, his most trusted Vice President, Paulo Muwanga
(RIP) nor his minister of state in-charge of security, now waiting to be
hanged at the Maximum Upper Prison Luzira, Chris Rwakasisi. Whether the lie
was deliberate or some body must have passed false information to the two,
which made them to pass wrong information to Obote, is not important.
What is important today is that Uganda is a polarized country along
ethnic divides than the unity and the spirit of nationalism which prevailed
prior to the 1985 coup.
It is engulfed in a brutal, senseless and costly war in northern Uganda,
whose end is not feasible in the near feature. Over 1.4 million people are
displaced, while thousands are exiled. Stagnated developments and economic
activities, soaring indebtedness, dependency syndrome, diseases such as
HIV/Aids, low productivity and investments coupled with corruption are
making us to be re-colonized once again.
The episode recounted by Dr Obote at the Busia border crossing point in
the early morning of Saturday, 27th July 1985, is very telling. Even among
the mutineers, when one of the soldiers tried to stop his convoy, the others
prevailed over him and allowed the fugitives safe exit.
We must really thank God for that because Obote is alive today to tell
the story and is planning to return to his motherland. Acholi spirit is
working here, as I will elaborate later.
I can empathetically say that Obote's woes began to unfold on the day of
the fateful helicopter crash in which the charismatic Maj. Gen. Oyite-Ojok
(RIP) and others died. I was then a trainee in the army, on night guard duty
at Nakasongola Army barracks in one of the homes of our Korean
instructors.
As the only victim (read prisoner) of the mutineers in the early morning
of that fateful day of the coup in 1985, let me recount my version and try
to explain to Ugandans that it was none other than the late Chief of Staff
Brig. Opon-Acaka (RIP) and a handful of others who failed Obote - or shall I
say misled him?
On the morning of Friday, 25th July 1985, my immediate boss at the 50
Brigade anti National Resistance Army (NRA) tactical base located within
Bombo barracks, Maj. L.R. Kizza, detailed me and another intelligence staff
to travel to the front line at the Kafu-Masindi town junction.
On that day there were three different types of news at our
headquarters. The first one was the usual situation report (sitrep ) about
the skirmishes between our UNLA forces in the Luwero Triangle.
The second report we received was that the mutineers from Gulu had
actually reached Masindi army barracks where they were received without
resistance. That news obviously disturbed Col. John Ogole (PSC), the
Commander of the anti NRA insurgency operations. He knew, and rightly so,
that the new threat, especially coming from fellow UNLA soldiers who were
advancing from Gulu, would jeopardise operations against NRA.
Meanwhile, we also got another report of hope from Kampala to the effect
that the late Prime Minister, Eric Otema-Alimadi (RIP) and other politicians
from the Acholi sub-region, were holding meetings with their colleagues from
the Lango sub-region led by the leader of the Lango Community in Kampala,
the late Joel Wacha-Olwol (RIP).
We were praying that the Kampala talks would succeed to resolve the new
conflict and prevent the mutineers from advancing beyond Masindi, so as not
to divert united UNLA from fighting the NRA insurgents and flush them out of
Luwero triangle. That was when my boss detailed me and another operative,
Emmanuel Onega to travel to Kafu front lines to assess the situation and
report back immediately.
Cadet Officers who were undergoing training in Artillery at Nakasongola
barracks were deployed at the Kafu-Masindi junction. The point section was
more than half a kilometre away onto the murrum road branching to
Masindi. We left our car and walked to them, partly to morale boost them
that all would be well.
Ucit uwac bot Opon ni UNLA pe bilwenyo ki UNLA lawote. Wan ki kany
wamato kwete kacel ki luwot-wa ma gia ki Masindi ni. They told us in clear
Luo dialect meaning that go and tell Brig. Opon that UNLA will not fight a
fellow UNLA soldier. Here we drink kwete (a mild local brew from maize flour
and millet or sorghum yeast) with our brothers from Masindi.
By two oclock in the afternoon, we reported back to Bombo barracks and
briefed Afande Kizza accordingly. We found out that the Chief-of-Staff,
Opon-Acak was in Bombo with Ogole and were holding a meeting. I went and
briefed them about our report from the front line.
Later in the afternoon, we began to see some commanders and their troops
who were deployed to fight the NRA entering Bombo barracks, some with
Artillery pieces. At about six in the evening Opon-Acak assembled the troops
and issued orders to them to deploy along the Kampala-Gulu Highway from
Kasana township in Luwero towards Kafu. I was there listening to him explain
why it was a must for them to get rid of the threatening advance from Gulu
first before getting back to fight the NRA. He referred to the new order as
removing maggots from the mushroom.
Then deployment began in earnest. At around ten in the night, as there
was total silence and darkness in Bombo barracks, I was summoned again to
the office of the Brigade Administrative Officer Maj. Charles Mityero by the
Regimental Sergeant. Major RSM W.O I Okwir alias Karak - father to top UPDF
Commander Lt. Col. Paul Lokech.
This time my brief was to escort Mityero to Kampala to deliver the
balance of Operational cash to Ogole at the Nile Mansion in Kampala. I found
that they had already packed the Shs23 million in cash in a military back
pack. Being a private I was, I did not go back to pick my gun. Little did I
know that some of the people who were sending me to Kampala that night were
not pleased with the mission. They conspired to loot the money. When they
tried to block Mityero from driving, he literally panicked and thought we
would be shot there and then, whereby they would loot the money. I took
charge of the situation.
I literally ordered Mityero to drive on and if need be knock anyone who
was standing on our way. I was annoyed; why should they call me if they did
not want to respond to Ogole's order anyway?
The trick worked. They backed off and we drove to Nile Mansions,
arriving about mid-night without any further incident.
Ogole was nowhere to be seen, Mityero called him on phone and he ordered
us to wait for him at the Hotel. We waited, took cup after cup of coffee
until daybreak. On the fateful day, Ogole never showed up.
By about 7.30 a.m. on Saturday morning, I became impatient because I did
not want to be seen in Kampala in Army uniform. Besides, I wanted the
mutineers to find me in Bombo. I was wrong. The mutineers by passed
Nakasongola and entered Bombo barracks that same night.
I requested Mityero to provide me with transport money to allow me
return to Bombo as he waits for Ogole. He pulled out four thousand shillings
from his pocket and drove me to the Wandegeya-Gayaza round about from where
I found a police land rover returning to Luwero from Kampala. Little did I
know that I was actually travelling to heaven had it not been for Acholi
culture which forbids the shedding of blood without just cause. Instead I
became the first and only prisoner.
The rest of the story is documented by The Monitor in the July 2004
journal. Both Ogole and Mityero live in exile in Britain.
The worst Obote could have suffered that day would have been arrest,
imprisonment and later release like the father of the nation he is. He would
perhaps be like former South African President Nelson Mandela who is
enjoying a blissful life in his old age. All leaders should beware of
boot-lickers.
I can visibly see a repeat of Obote's mistakes with President Yoweri
Museveni. The transition saga unfolding is too delicate to be
ignored. Concerns being expressed by those on whose funds we depend cannot
simply be ignored nor be brushed aside by the sycophancy of the voices of
the Ofwono Opondos, Dr Buturos, Maj. Bantarizas.
Sooner or later, the truth behind the trauma of the Kandida Lakony's
((RIP), China Kaitesi's or even Ms. Winnie Byanyima will sprout.
Contact: mutoono@yahoo.com

Original_Search_Title: Public Fails Police in Crime War
Headline: Botswana: Public Fails Police in Crime War
Body_Text:
Francistown — A police van going at full speed slows down to a level crossing at
Gerald Estate. Its doors open and a man jumps out and takes to his
heels. Police officers leap out and start chasing the man, forcing drunk
patrons at a nearby bar to abandon their drinks to watch the spectacle.
It all happened in the twinkle of an eye. "Tiro e, e batla batho ba ba
sebete (This job is not for the feeble-hearted)," a woman declared,
referring to the police officers. No one cared to join the chase. The only
thing that members of the public did was to shout, "Legodu! (Thief!)".
The driver of the police van joined his colleagues and asked: "Where has
he gone?". A tired constable pointed in the direction of a cluster of poorly
constructed houses. He told the Sergeant who was driving the police van that
the suspect could have run in the thickets near a kopje in the estate. Like
football enthusiasts, patrons had lined up on the fence of the drinking
hole, cheering and cursing.
For the Sergeant and his team, an escape by a suspect is one of those
things that they just have to live with. "It's a serious disciplinary
offence to allow a suspect to escape," explained the Sergeant who pleaded
not to be named because the Police Act does not permit an officer of his
rank to communicate with the media. The officers are disappointed that they
let a felon who has been on the run for days to escape in full view of the
public after arrest. "Will they really trust us after all this?" wondered
the Sergeant.
"The suspect escaped near the level-crossing as the police van slowed
down," he explained. A group of people gather around the police van after
failing to assist in apprehending the escaped suspect. The officers are not
amused as some drunkards enquired: "Who is he?" Another sweating constable
guessed that the escaped suspect could be hiding in one of the houses, as
the people were seemingly not interested in assisting them. "I know him,"
shouted a drunken man who nearly splashed one policeman with sorghum
beer. The police ignored him and drove away with hope that the escaped man
will be re-arrested. They were disappointed that the people did not help
them. Francistown CID boss, Superintendent Mosalagae Moseki said the police
cannot operate in isolation and need help from the community. He added that
there is no magic used in the detection of crime. "Police need public
support in effectively combating crime." He lamented that public support in
fighting crime in his beat has declined considerably. "Yes, there are people
who would just watch crime committed under their noses without taking
appropriate action." However, he said there are people who help in combating
crime, though some of them go to extremes. "Mob justice is not the right way
of administering justice as it leaves some people badly beaten. Under no
circumstances should people take the law in their own hands as this could
even spoil possible evidence linking the suspect to a crime."
Moseki asserted that since the inception of the crime prevention
committees in the country in 1984, the police have been preaching the
anti-crime gospel. "Well, there are people who ignore anti-crime tips for
their own reasons. There is a group whose attitude towards the fight against
crime is still wanting. These are the kind of people that we want to
transform." He was concerned that some members of the public only think
about combating crime when they become victims. He said it is an offence for
people to ignore the police when they call for assistance.

Original_Search_Title: Nambrew to Beef Up Presence in SADC
Headline: Namibia: Nambrew to Beef Up Presence in SADC
Body_Text:
Cape Town — Namibia Breweries (Nambrew), maker of beers such as Windhoek
and Tafel Lager, said this week that it was investigating new market
opportunities in the Southern African Development Community (SADC)
region.
Nambrew said opportunities would be explored with partners Heineken and
Diageo in the three-way brandhouse venture in SA to maximise economies of
scale and minimise the effect of currency fluctuations.
The 14 SADC countries include SA, Zimbabwe, Namibia, the Democratic
Republic of Congo, Mauritius, Tanzania, Angola, Malawi, Lesotho, Botswana,
Mozambique, Swaziland, Seychelles and Zambia.
The group was reporting its results for the six months to December,
which showed a small turnover decline to N$525,4m from N$528,2m a year
before. Operating profit after depreciation rose 16%, mainly because
depreciation charges fell as the useful life of assets was extended in line
with global norms. After higher provision for deferred tax, headline
earnings slipped to 18,4c a share from 19,4c. But cash flow from operations
rose to N$73,4m (N$55,7m). An interim dividend of 5,5c (11c) was
declared.
Nambrew grew volumes in Namibia in a competitive market. Tying up with
Heineken and Diageo enabled it to double its marketing spend in SA. However,
exports remained challenging, particularly in Angola. Delays in implementing
bilateral trade agreements hit volumes.
In a recent presentation to analysts SABMiller showed it would make a
formidable opponent in Africa. It is number one in Botswana, with a market
share of about 98%; in Zambia its market share is 100% for clear beer and
50% for sorghum beer.

Original_Search_Title: Police Arrest Two Cops Over Samfya Shooting
Headline: Zambia: Police Arrest Two Cops Over Samfya Shooting
Body_Text:
Lusaka — TWO police officers have been arrested and charged with murder in
connection with last month's Samfya shootings, in which two people were
killed.
Home affairs permanent secretary, Peter Mumba, yesterday confirmed the
detention of the duo, who he could not name, and indicated that they would
appear in court soon.
Mumba further said that investigations, which were instituted by the
Ministry of Home Affairs last month, had also made some revelations that had
compelled the ministry to make some changes in the police command in the
province, particularly in Samfya district.
"We have made headway with the investigations and two police officers
have been arrested in connection with last month's shootings in Samfya,"
Mumba said.
"We have also made some transfers from the area. Some police officers
have been taken somewhere else due to the same incident."
Last month, villagers in Samfya district in Luapula Province rioted
after two people who were being pursued by Zambia Wildlife Authority (ZAWA)
officers drowned in Lake Bangweulu.
The incident occurred when a combined team of ZAWA, Zambia Police
officers and officers from the fisheries department were pursuing them for
illegal fishing.
The villagers became violent and rioted after their colleagues
drowned.
Two people died in the process from gunshot wounds after the police
allegedly fired live bullets to disperse the rioting villagers, prompting
the Ministry of Home Affairs to conduct investigations into the matter.
Meanwhile, on the food crisis in refugee camps countrywide, Mumba urged
the international community to fulfil their pledges before the situation in
the camps became critical.
"The other point is that Zambia this year is expected to have a food
deficit. So donors should put in place measures that will enable them to buy
food for the refugees from elsewhere," Mumba said.
"Currently, World Food Programme (WFP) has been buying maize in Luapula
and Northern provinces, but with the uncertainty that we are facing, it's
really imperative to bring food from elsewhere. There are about 170,000
refugees and most of them are still surviving on handouts."
He said if the authorities continued to give refugees full rations, it
was expected that the food would only last until July.
Mumba said some donors had donated sorghum a few months ago, but the
problem was that refugees, especially those at Mwange and Kala refugee
camps, were reluctant to eat it in preference to maize.
"Some refugees are reluctant to eat sorghum, so we need to feed them
with what they are comfortable with. As for beans and other legumes, we
don't seem to have sufficient quantities," he said..

Original_Search_Title: DR Apraku: 'A Comeback Kid'
Headline: Ghana: DR Apraku: 'A Comeback Kid'
Body_Text:
Why He Deserves It
His name was conspicuously absent in President Kufuor's first batch of
ministers. There were a lot of media speculations as to what might have
caused his removal.
But the president sent all those speculations to rest when the name of
Dr Kofi Konadu Apraku, the former Minister for Regional Cooperation and
NEPAD bounced back into the ministerial scene this time as a minister of
state at the Regional Cooperation and NEPAD Ministry.
Looking at Dr Apraku's track record at the Trade and Industry Ministry
before going to his current post a number of well-meaning Ghanaians
especially Dr Vladimir Antwi-Danso of the Legon Centre for International
Affairs believe that he is the right person to head the Regional Cooperation
Ministry.
It is however not surprising that rumours are making rounds that a
former spokesperson on Foreign Affairs of the opposition National Democratic
Congress (NDC) has stated in private, "Dr Apraku has really championed the
cause of integration in the Sub-region and propagated the objectives of
NEPAD fairly well."
Dr Apraku is an American trained economist, is a Danquah-Busiast by
birth and a believer of liberal democracy. After staying in the US where he
was schooled and taught he came back to Ghana in 1992 and worked closely
with Professor Adu-Boahene to establish the Danquah-Busia club.
An admirer of Dr Apraku told the ADM, "He used his time, energy and
financial resources to promote and assist the club to challenge the
nefarious government of Rawlings and his cronies."
He was a member of the research committee that wrote the 1992 manifesto
and constitution of the NPP during the 1992 elections of which the NDC won
under bizarre circumstances.
He contested the 1996 elections, in the Offinso North Constituency and
won on the ticket of the NPP. He was made the shadow Minister for Finance
and Economic Planning where he floored the NDC party manifesto and economic
messages.
"As he demonished the NDC message especially on his numerous appearances
on GTV's Talking Point, a current affairs programme, the then government of
NDC could not tolerate the pressure and they had no option but to stop the
programme especially when the NDC presented their 1996/97 Budget Statement,"
the admirer said.
It is on record that Dr Apraku re-organised, refocused programmes and
policies and activities of the Ministry of Trade and Industry when he headed
that ministry. Records have it that he placed much attention on the exports
with the establishment of 500 handicraft centres to augment the
non-traditional exports which led to a 25% increase in the volume of the
non-traditional exports in two years.
It is also clear that he managed to secure the AGOA visa which gave
Ghanaian business industries the opportunity to trade and partner with their
US counterparts without any difficulty.
He also worked to operationalise the Garment and Textile Training Centre
at the Accra Technical and Training Centre (ATTC).
During his tenure at the Ministry of Trade, Dr Apraku convinced UNIDO to
support Ghana in the utilisation of sorghum in the brewery industries in
Ghana. In addition, UNIDO funded the operationalisation of the EDIF to
provide support for the country's exporters with priority given to salt,
garment, textile and handicrafts.
He lobbied Parliament to pass the Ghana Investment Fund. He worked
assiduously for the country to establish trade relationship with Libya when
he signed bilateral trade agreements with Libya in the area of trade
promotion between the two countries.
Also, he worked to appoint a Consular General in Mauritius and the
opening of the first Trade House in Mauritius. Before Dr Apraku left that
ministry, he lobbied DFID, a British NGO to assist the ministry to review
the Ghana Industrial Policy whose results would be launched by the president
this week.
Dr Apraku was re-assigned to his current position after the first
cabinet reshuffle. He has since promoted the ideals of NEPAD, ECOWAS and all
issues relating to the development of the Sub-region and the African
continent as a whole.
He was elected chairman of the ECOWAS Council of Ministers where he
served for a two-year term. He was instrumental in the realisation of the
ECOWAS protocols, programmes and visions.
On NEPAD, he led the sensitisation team to all the ten regions of Ghana
to explain the concept to ordinary people. He was able to convince cabinet
to establish the National Governing Council Secretariat on African Peer
Review Mechanism.
Through this Ghana has become a model to other African countries on the
APRM process. It is against this background that I join others to doff my
hat for Dr Apraku, a political genius for his re-nomination.

Original_Search_Title: The Shebeen Queen
Headline: Botswana: The Shebeen Queen
Body_Text:
Francistown — As long as the low-income __cpLocation of Somerset East exists, a popular drinking spot commonly known as Kwa-ga-Pololo will continue to flourish. This is one of the busiest and most frequented drinking holes in town.
Kwa-ga-Pololo is a Shebeen owned by 48-year-old, Annah Pololo Nyemu- a mother of three- who hails from the North-East village of Mapoka. There is no one in Somerset East or Somi, as it is popularly known, who does not know about Pololo's place. Men in blue, beige and white overalls take turns to sip beer before rushing to work or home. They hide in a small building where she keeps her stock. As the Shebeen is very close to the town centre, it is popular with men in particular, who are least bothered that they go to work almost drunk.
Those in the know say that some stubborn employees who have become alcoholics will never go to work without passing through Pololo's watering hole. To be a Shebeen queen, one must have patience. Clients do not respect time as they pop in at awkward hours. "You can have people knocking at your door or beating the gate calling for your attention. Some come in the wee hours requesting for a cigarette and you must open for them." These are just some of the experiences of being a Shebeen queen. There is stiff competition from drinking holes within Somi and outside.
When Somi transformed to a squatter settlement of jobseekers, Kwa-ga-Pololo transformed as well. Somi was a dangerous place then as it was chiefly associated with criminals who terrorised people.
To a first time visitor in the city, the Shebeen is no different from the house next door, especially during the day, when there are fewer people who trickle in. In the evening, the place becomes a real meeting place where people set appointments and enjoy drinks. But in the evening, even some of the who-is-who of Francistown visit Pololo's place. The Shebeen queen keeps a long list of regulars who frequent the place.
Except for a few empty packets of Chibuku, the open space outside the screen-walled spot at Pololo's place is very clean. Except for a short stint at Sally's shops in Selebi-Phikwe, where she was employed as a cleaner, Nyemu has never worked anywhere. She started selling Chibuku beer and other alcoholic beverages in 1976. "I was approached by the duo, Vula and Chimbo, who were initially the only people who could buy Chibuku directly from the Botswana Breweries Limited and sell it to others." She did this while her mother was away in Mapoka. She later home brewed Mokuru or sorghum beer, which made Mma-Pololo's place famous. "We would hire some gunba-gumbas to woo people to the spot so that they can buy beer and dance to the music. In most cases, this method worked for us as we could even realise some profits."
When she moved from her mother's house to her own place in 1980, Nyemu knew exactly what she wanted to do for a living. She had copied her mother's way of living - selling beer. She started off with selling Chibuku and cigarettes until she bought a deep freezer in 1988. She is aware that she is not allowed to sell alcoholic beverages. But she strongly believes that to survive she has to sell the beer. She has been arrested thrice and fined between P100-P300. "I still have a pending case before the Francistown police for selling alcoholic beverages without a licence and beer amounting to P406.25 was confiscated and it is still with the police." A beer can sells at P3.25 and she says it is cheaper than most of the bars and bottle stores in town.
Although the police continue to terrorise Nyemu for breaking the law, she calls it a game of survival. Gumba-gumbas, she says, are good for pulling the crowd. "We used to hire gumba-gumbas and we were doing well in terms of sales. We used to get permits to play music while we sold beer."
She plans to rent a bar and bottle store at the Donga __cpLocation. "I hope that the police will stop troubling me now as I will be selling alcoholic beverages from an authorised place." She said the police do not want to see people working hard and feeding their children. "They seem to derive some pleasure from unnecessarily making us their customers."
Armed robbers have also troubled her in the past. "Out of frustration possibly, they shot wildly and left a deep hole at the corner of the house. They wanted money from me." Her vehicle was stolen from the yard and has never been found. "I bought that Toyota Hilux after losing hope of recovering the other one," she said.
For Nyemu, there is nothing particular about a name. Her clients call her all sorts of names. Mma-sepoto and Shebeen queen are some of her many names. Even when she opens the bar and a bottle store, Nyemu said she will not close the Shebeen that has been her source of income for such a long time.

Original_Search_Title: Priority Assessment Study Completed
Headline: Zambia: Priority Assessment Study Completed
Body_Text:
THE ministry of Commerce, Trade and Industry (MCTI) and the Export Board of Zambia (EBZ) have identified horticulture/floriculture, textiles, tobacco, honey and paprika as those sub-sectors and products with high export potential that could take advantage of the business opportunities in the Multilateral Trading System (MTS).
MCTI and EBZ have done this with the help of two consultants from the International Trade Centre of UNCTAD/WTO and have just completed a Priority Sector Assessment (PSA) exercise.
Other products of high export potential identified are cotton, leather and gemstone products.
The PSA is part of Module IV of the Joint Integrated Technical Assistance programme phase two (JITAP II), a trade-related technical assistance programme jointly implemented by ITC, the World Trade Organisation (WTO) and the United Nations Conference on Trade and Development (UNCTAD).
The objective of JITAP II is to ensure poverty reduction by assisting beneficiary countries integrate further into the Multilateral Trading Systems (MTS).
This comprises capacity building for trade negotiations and implementing of WTO Agreements and related trade policy formulations; building a national knowledge base in (MTS) issues; and enhancing supply capacity and market knowledge of exporting and export ready enterprises to derive benefits from business opportunities presented by trade liberalisation under the emerging MTS.
The PSA is part of Module IV of JITAP II. Module IV pays special attention to the formulation and implementation of product and service sector strategies.
It is aimed at building the capacity of private sector players to derive benefits from the MTS. Work in the module focuses on undertaking the required analysis of products and markets for identifying sectors for which sectoral strategies need to be developed.
The product and sector strategies are intended to bring together product-sector associations, business persons, apex business organisations, and policy makers in an effort to understand better the opportunities for products with export potential, domestic supply capacity, and bottlenecks to be addressed, as well as to map out plans for building export capacity.
Policy constraints affecting appropriate supply-side responses are to be identified and recommendations made for addressing them.
This will in the long run help entrepreneurs and policy makers to optimise trade opportunities that become available on the international markets.
The basis for the PSA was a list of 15 broad non-traditional sectors that EBZ suggested in its Medium Term Strategy as having the highest potential for export growth. These were in line with the Transitional National Development Plan (TNDP) and the Poverty Reduction Strategy Paper (PRSP).
The ITC's market analysis team refined this list to extract 30 product groups. Companies producing the identified products were short-listed and questionnaires sent out to them.
Producer associations were also consulted for relevant information. Visits were made to these producers and interviews conducted with their representatives.
Mr Thierry Paulmier from ITCpresented the draft PSA report on 30 December 2004 to an audience comprising MCTI, EBZ representatives and representatives of some of the producers who participated in the assessment.
ITC's assessment of Zambia's export potential was based on the examination of three major dimensions: 1) world markets 2) the country's current export performance of the sector; and 3) domestic supply.
At the end of the assessment a number of product groups were short listed as having high, medium or low potential. Export Potential was defined as the capacity to expand exports.
The term applies to both those sectors that have an already proven track record (existing export capacity) and those that may perhaps not yet be exporting, but fulfill many of the necessary conditions to be capable of doing so (capability to export).
Sectors/sub-sector and/or products identified as having medium export potential in order of priority, included groundnuts, sugar, soya beans, oil cakes, maize and wheat bran, sorghum and processed fruits, wood products, cheese, handicrafts and wheat flour.
Others were blankets and toweling, beef, maize meal, protein foods, milk and roasted coffee. Those in the low potential category were cotton fabrics, tea, acrylic yarn and maize.

Original_Search_Title: SABMiller Toasts Steady Rise in Sales, Profit
Headline: South Africa: SABMiller Toasts Steady Rise in Sales, Profit
Body_Text:
Cape Town —  Sunny skies and consumer splurge contribute to increase in SA beer
consumption
Global brewer SABMiller's shares traded in a tight range yesterday,
after it released a trading update for the nine months to December showing
it was sustaining trends in growth and profitability that were evident at
its half-year stage.
For the six months to September the group reported total organic growth
in lager beer volumes of 5%, which it said yesterday had continued into the
third quarter. At the interim stage, turnover grew 14% and adjusted earnings
38%.
The group's shares were trading 1p lower at 857p in London and up 44c to
R96,74 on the JSE Securities Exchange SA at midday yesterday.
An analyst said the investment community had been bracing itself for a
deceleration in growth in the US as Miller Brewing was coming off higher
comparative numbers for last year, but the deceleration was less than
expected.
The latest trading update was broadly in line with trends at halfyear,
he said, although the mix was a little different.
SABMiller said volumes of beer sold in SA rose 4% in the third quarter
and the nine-month period. Subsidiary Amalgamated Beverage Industries grew
volumes 6% in the December quarter and 7% compared with the corresponding
ninemonth period in the previous year.
Good weather and robust consumer spending had contributed to the
growth.
According to a separate presentation by management on the group's Africa
and Asia businesses yesterday, SA accounted for 18% of global lager sales
volumes in the 12 months to September, and 39% of earnings before interest,
tax, depreciation and amortisation.
In the US, Miller Brewing's domestic sales to retailers fell 0,3% for
the December quarter, in a weak trading environment, but it gained market
share.
For the nine-month period, domestic sales to retailers rose 1,5% over
last year. Shipments to wholesalers performed similarly, both in the quarter
and the year to date.
Brewer Anheuser-Busch said two weeks ago that its wholesaler sales to
retailers fell 0,3% last year and 3,2% in the fourth quarter.
It increased its US beer shipments to wholesalers 0,4% to 103million
barrels last year, over 2003, based on continuing growth of brands such as
Michelob Light and Bud Lite. But its fourth-quarter shipments to wholesalers
fell 1,5%.
SABMiller said beer volumes in central America rose 3% in the third
quarter and 2% in the year to date, while sales of carbonated soft drinks
fell 8% in the quarter and 6% in the year to date because of tough market
conditions in El Salvador.
In Europe, organic growth in lager volumes rose 6% in the third quarter
and 5% in the nine months, while the Africa and Asia region, which includes
operations in India and China, grew volumes 10% in the quarter and the nine
months.
The analyst said yesterday's presentation on Africa and Asia showed that
SABMiller's strategy in Africa was moving from being driven primarily by
volume and efficiency to encouraging consumers to move up the value chain
from sorghum beer to mainstream and then to premium beers.

Original_Search_Title: 'Bushera' Shelf-Life Improved
Headline: Uganda: 'Bushera' Shelf-Life Improved
Body_Text:
Kampala — OBUSHERA, the brown porridge that is synonymous with western Uganda, is
one that has been enjoyed by many people. The porridge, made out of either
sorghum or millet, is a sure delicacy. The Bakiga enjoy theirs with sorghum,
while the Banyankole love the one of millet.
Over the years, as people leave their villages for Kampala in search of
a better future, the demand for this traditional cold beverage has soared in
the urban centres. The problem however is, this demand has put a lot of
pressure on the bushera makers to produce the product haphazardly.
According to Dr William Kyamuhangire, a food scientist in Makerere
University, the techniques of production are not being followed
correctly.
"In the traditional home of this beverage which is south-western Uganda,
the bushera is fermented for one to two days. The process allows it to get
sour and sweet naturally. However, in the city, we have noticed that they
don't wait for it to ferment, instead they add sugar and other sweeteners to
it so as to satisfy the demand," Kyamuhangire said.
He adds that the product sold in most of Kampala shops is not
genuine. It is adulterated because of the short cuts in its production
process.
The process of fermentation produces anti-microbial agents and allows
the conversion of starch into sugar naturally. These anti-microbial agents
ensure that the product is safe and stable, but if fermentation is omitted,
then the people are exposed to an unsafe product.
Because of this problem, the department of Food Science, Makerere
University has come up with a way of preserving obushera.
The beverage, they say, is able to last between three and four
months.
"We have gone to the villages and studied the fermentation process in
detail and we have also made a survey in the city centre and noticed the
differences between the two places.
We are not just prolonging its shelf life but are also improving the
processing techniques as well as the quality of the product," Kyamuhangire
explains.
"Obushera is one of the most consumed beverages in Kampala. It even
sells more than sodas in some parts mainly because it is cheap."
The preservation involves pasteurisation method of controlled heating at
a specific time and temperature. This is followed by thematic sealing which
ensures that post-product contamination is stopped. It is then kept at room
temperature and can thus be preserved for six months.
The department is also carrying out experiments on having either a ready
to drink bushera or instant flour that can be prepared instantly and
obtained in any supermarket.
Kyamuhangire says the technology should be ready next year.
"Bushera is a whole industry that should be developed. We are therefore
assisting processors to adopt better manufacturing techniques and develop it
at industrial level," says Dr. Kyamuhangire.

Original_Search_Title: Sudan Peace Will in Fact Be a Boon for Northern Uganda
Headline: Uganda: Sudan Peace Will in Fact Be a Boon for Northern Uganda
Body_Text:
Nairobi —  An interview with Mr Sibajuddin Hamid Yousuf, sudanese Ambassador to
Uganda
How will the signing of the Sudan peace accord impact on the
insurgency in northern Uganda?
The problem of the Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) is as old as the last
war fought between the SPLA and the Sudan government. We started mending
bilateral relations with Uganda in 1999 but five years later the LRA is
still fighting. That was due to the fact that even though the government of
Sudan had decided to cease support to the LRA, it was not able to
participate fully in ending the phenomenon of LRA in northern Uganda and
southern Sudan because of the implications of its war with the SPLA.
Now that the peace accord has been signed, a new situation on the ground
emerges where parties on both sides of the border can use their full
strength and stretch their arms in order to end this problem of insurgency
in southern Sudan and northern Uganda.
Is Kony a big problem for Sudan too?
Kony operates from his base in southern Sudan. There are certain factors
that make up optimistic that his insurgency is going to end very
soon. International efforts to rehabilitate the south will need a secure
environment in order to operate.
Among the priorities is clearing the area of landmines and also
constructing roads for purposes of communication and transportation and
securing the safe return of Sudanese refugees in Uganda and other
places. This is why I can say the peace agreement signed in Nairobi on
January 9 will have a tremendous impact on the conflict in northern
Uganda.
What is Sudan's view on the ongoing peace talks between the Uganda
government and LRA?
The ultimate objective of Sudan is to see an end to the insurgency in
northern Uganda whether by military or peaceful means. And this is why we
agreed to give Uganda limited access for the People's Defence Forces (UPDF)
to operate in Sudan to end this insurgency militarily.
But now, since there is hope and some steps have been taken towards
peaceful resolution of the conflict, we have our fingers crossed.
We are also ready to help this peace process succeed to normalise the
situation for the Acholi community and in southern Sudan.
What trade and investment opportunities do you see arising from the
signing of the Sudan peace accord?
Southern Sudan is a huge market. And given the fact that there is no
infrastructure and no meaningful civil administration, there will be a lot
of work to be done. This will require capital, skilled labour and a constant
flow of goods. When people go back to villages, they will need a lot of
services to settle down after a long period of being refugees in
neighbouring countries.
This fact alone gives an idea of the economic opportunities open for
neighbouring countries and for northern Sudan too. And since there is close
proximity between southern Sudan and Uganda, Kenya and the Democratic
Republic of Congo, peace in southern Sudan will have a very positive impact
on all these countries.
Ugandan manufacturers and traders can sell their goods in this new
marketp. Skilled labour can go and help in the process of
reconstruction. This will help northern Uganda when people settle down,
cultivate their land, sell their products across the border and vice
versa.
What kinds of goods are likely to be involved in the trade between
East Africa and Sudan?
From Sudan, you get refined oil - benzene, gasolene, kerosene or
lubricants - sugar, salt and sorghum. We will import coffee and tea from
Uganda. Even now there is a demand for Ugandan fish in Sudan as far north as
Khartoum.
There is also talk of linking southern Sudan to Uganda and Kenya by
rail.
President Bashir declared at a rally in Juba this week during
celebrations of the conclusion of the peace accord that the government would
immediately start on plans to connect Juba with the rest of the country
through railways coming from Wau in Bahr el Gazel and also connect Juba to
Mombasa. I don't know whether that will take place through Uganda or
directly to Mombasa but it's a viable idea.
Why do you think the Sudan peace accord will work?
The accord will work because the Sudanese people are fed up of
war. There is no reason whatsoever to fight. Over what? Over land in the
south? This land is the land of the southerners; God created them there. The
war has prevented the country from maintaining its normal growth because of
the expenditure of $1-$2 million a day on financing the war.
This money should have been used to help people settle down, educate
them, maintain them in good health. Now with the accord it will be one
country with two systems of government - a secular system in the South and
sharia in the north; two houses of parliament, 26 states each with its
Cabinet. And other marginalised areas like Darfur are in a better position
to have the same deal as offered to the South as far as sharing power and
sharing wealth are concerned.

Original_Search_Title: Parliament: Battle Lines Drawn Over Referendum
Headline: Kenya: Parliament: Battle Lines Drawn Over Referendum
Body_Text:
Nairobi — When Parliament resumes in March it will become the next
battlefield for supremacy between Orange team - those who spearheaded
the rejection of the proposed Constitution, and Banana - those who
supported it.
However, with the recent naming of a new Cabinet, the government
side is likely to garner enough numbers to see through its legislative
agenda.
Should a vote of no confidence be moved, the government side is
likely to get the support of 119 MPs, against 95 for the other side.
The battle lines were drawn between the two sides when the President
left out of his new team former Cabinet ministers belonging to the
Orange Democratic Movement, which rallied Kenyans to reject the draft
constitution.
The Orange team emerged victorious by garnering 57 per cent of the
votes, while the Banana side, which had President Kibaki's blessings,
scored 43 per cent.
Former ministers Raila Odinga (Roads), Kalonzo Musyoka
(Environment), Anyang' Nyong'o (Planning and National Development) and
Najib Balala (National Heritage) were kicked out of Government.
Out also went Mr Ochillo Ayacko (Gender and Sports), Mr William ole
Ntimama (Public Service) and Mrs Linah Jebii Kilimo (Immigration and
Registration of Persons).
Riding on the crest of victory at the November 21 vote on a new
constitution, MPs in ODM have gone on the overdrive, telling all and
sundry that they will force a snap election once Parliament resumes.
The movement has, rightly or wrongly, interpreted the results of the
referendum as an indication that Kenyans want new leadership at the top
which, its front-runners believe, can be achieved through a snap poll.
But President Kibaki has ruled out such an election, saying that
Parliament still enjoys the mandate given by Kenyans in the 2002
General Election, and that people calling for a snap poll are wasting
their time.
Under the current Constitution, a snap election may be held only if
the President dissolves Parliament, or if the House itself passes a
vote of no confidence in the Government.
And at least two-thirds of all MPs must support such a motion for it to sail through.
Parliament stands indefinitely suspended and reopen only when the President so directs.
Of the 222 MPs in the current parliament, 100 are now in government,
which translates to nearly 50 per cent of the National Assembly.
Thus, with such numbers and the MPs' propensity to absent themselves
from the House and to be manipulated, it is highly unlikely that a
no-confidence motion will sail through.
Besides, few MPs would savour, let alone willing, to stake their
political fortunes on a by-election whose successful outcome they would
not guarantee.
It is also understood that most MPs have loans running into millions
of shillings, which they would be forced to repay should a by-election
be called.
Thus, they cannot support a move that is likely to make them lose their privileged status merely to participate a snap election.
Nevertheless, when Parliament resumes , it has its job well cut out, with numerous Bills and motions that need urgent attention.
There are 13 Bills pending, but which should have been dispensed
with, since a majority of them are spill-overs from the previous year.
It is during the discussions on the Bills and ministerial votes that
the Government is likely to face a hard time, since the opposition side
will now be boosted by MPs belonging to the Liberal Democratic Party.
With people like Mr Odinga, Mr Musyoka and Prof Nyong'o on the back benches, the House is likely to witness lively debates.
The fourth session of the ninth parliament had been characterised by
poor performance and truancy, with members frequently absenting
themselves and often engaging in trivialities rather than serious
debate.
Many were the times when business had to be adjourned due to a lack
of quorum, or the absence of ministers to answer questions directed at
their ministries. As a result, Parliament passed votes for 18
ministries running into billions of shillings without the required
minimum number of 30 MPs.
But on the positive side, MPs passed several Bills with far-reaching
effects. Just before it went on recess, Parliament passed a motion by
nominated MP Oloo Aringo seeking to introduce a Bill to establish a
parliamentary budget office.
It is hoped that the office will strengthen Parliament's role in
public finance management, and provide the House with information
necessary for economic, budget and other policy decisions.
The office, once established, will help MPs to effectively exercise
their oversight roles over taxes and any other public funds, and to
hold the Executive to account. Other important laws passed by MPs were
the Public Procurement and Disposal as well as the Privatisation Bills.
The former seeks to streamline government purchases and the clearance
of its obsolete equipment and stores, while the latter is to guide the
offloading of public shareholding in parastatals into private hands.
The two Bills were at the centre of demands by donors to the
Government to open up purchases, even those to do with the most secret
security, and stop subsidies to poorly performing state corporations.
Also passed were the Forests Bill and the sessional paper to streamline
the operations of small and micro-enterprises.
An important law awaiting debate is the Sexual Offences Bill by
Nominated MP Njoki Ndung'u, which seeks tough punishment for rapists,
including chemical castration of offenders.
And while opening the fourth session of the ninth parliament,
President Kibaki said the Public Officers Ethics Act was to be amended
to let the public know the wealth of MPs and civil servants. But the
session ended without the amendments being brought before the House.

Original_Search_Title: Awori Wants Dialogue And Forgiveness in the New Year
Headline: Kenya: Awori Wants Dialogue And Forgiveness in the New Year
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The New Year should bring dialogue and reconciliation between the
Banana and Orange referendum teams, Vice-President Moody Awori said
yesterday.
And he asked leaders on both sides not to carry over what he termed as referendum emotions and political heat into 2006.
The calm and peace, he noted, would enable the country to realise a new constitution before the end of the year.
"All competing sides must be sincere, learn from their mistakes and
appreciate that a constitution cannot be made in a hostile climate,"
the VP said.
In his New Year message to the nation in a statement yesterday, he
appealed to every Kenyan to support the President and his government,
saying that it was only by so doing that the development goals would be
achieved.
"Let's jealously guard our unity and ignore those in our midst who
may be bent on causing division or diverting us from the course of
nation building," he added.
He said the referendum campaigns were a testimony to widened democratic space and respect for free expression.
Meanwhile, the Friends Church of Kenya urged the Government to
establish a national constituent assembly to kick-start the
constitution review.
Presiding clerk Jacob Neyole called upon President Kibaki to immediately reconvene Parliament to finalise the review.
In a statement, Mr Neyole suggested that the assembly consist of
three members from each of the 210 constituencies. "The three should
come from faith-based organisations, lobby groups and other interest
groups," he added.
But he said the Electoral commission should take the full responsibility of coordinating the selection of the members.
But he proposed that the out going secretary of the former Constitution of Kenya Review commission be retained in that capacity.
Twenty members belonging to the major political parties, he
suggested, should be represented in the assembly in direct proportion
to their representation in parliament.
"The NCA will be a small team and will produce a document in form of
a draft bill which will be submitted to the parliament to be debated
and enacted into law without making any substantive changes," said Mr
Neyole.

Original_Search_Title: Man Talk - the Oyunga Pala Awards
Headline: Kenya: Man Talk - the Oyunga Pala Awards
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Each year, this column throws barbs and bouquets at certain Kenyans for their deeds and misdeeds
Ladies and Gentlemen, welcome to the second edition of the
self-funded/self titled Oyunga awards of distinction. These special
mentions are reserved for Kenyans from all walks of life who
contributed in some way or the other to the disturbing but sometimes
refreshing nature of our national character.
In this year's time capsule, you will find some public gestures that
were worthy of note, others you may even consider inspirational but the
majority I can safely say, were shenanigans. The range moves from
displays of excellence to what some might term as odd behaviour. The
selection of nominees was random and the lessons are of national
relevance. You will be pleased to note they are not bogged down by
gender as alleged by critics. The cash prizes promised last year are
absent as the anticipated lucrative sponsorship from organisations that
support struggling publicity stunts wasn't forthcoming.
Without further ado, rolling drums, lights and the list of 42...
1. Nameless - the artist formerly known as David Mathenge for losing the bandana. Thank you for hearing our pleas.
2. Wahu the girl who called us 'Liars'- For teaching us that if you
are going to have a grand wedding, hold it off until you become rich
and famous. It makes for a more lasting memory.
3. Esther Wahome - For staying true to her self and leather while paying total disregard to critics and the weather. Kuna dawa.
4. Bishop Gilbert Deya - For demonstrating how a photo opportunity
with the Queen on an official Royal engagement can be exploited to
advance a struggling career.
5. Redykulass - for showing us that though we think politicians are
ridiculous on TV; we prefer to take them very seriously outside the box.
6. Ruffstone - for that timely reminder. The lord helps those who
help themselves. Even gospel artistes need a clothing line, a BMW and a
trace of bling bling to be taken seriously in showbiz.
7. Poxi Presha - for 'otonglo time' and teaching us the often
diluted value of speaking your mind always. Rest in peace my brother.
8. Ann Wambui - for teaching us the importance of paying income tax
especially in the UK. She is the brothel owner in London who lost The
equivalent of Sh44.5 million.
9. Charity Ngilu - for retrogression. A delegation to State House to earn special favours! That's was so unoriginal.
10. Alfred Mutua - for confirming that a statement from the government must be taken with a pinch of salt.
11. Lucy Kibaki- for showing us the importance of discretion.
Slapping an awarding-winning journalist on the world press, in front of
camera isn't a very smart move.
12. Francis Atwoli - for that useful speech tip; your voice
projection should be appropriate to your purpose, audience and
presentation environment. It is possible to get your point across
without raising your voice or popping your eyes out.
13. Kingsting and Bed Bug - for originality. Never underestimate the potential of a corny name in the music business.
14. Wangari Maathai - for revealing that an environmentalist is not
taken seriously in her own backyard. It takes more than a Nobel Peace
prize in the relevant field to head a ministry.
15. Steven Cherono - alias Saif Saeed Shaheen our very own Qatari
for perpetuating the Kenyan legacy in middle and long distance running.
16. Ketan Somaia - for proving that the prison authorities have
nothing personal against smokers. Every tycoon has a right to his
comforts. He was reportedly seen smoking cigars in the hospital
corridors during his incarceration.
17. Chris Murungaru - for illustrating the delusions of grandeur.
Know Who You're Dealing with. Do Not Offend the Wrong Person. The
underdog is not always guaranteed the backing of the crowd.
18. Jeff Koinange - for teaching us never to dismiss a guy in dreadlocks again.
19. Zain Virjee - of the CNN fame. For assuring the legions of doubting men, that there is such a thing as beauty and brains.
20. Mongolo - for proving that looking the part is essential to
success in comedy. There is such a thing as a naturally-looking comic
features.
21. Fai Amario - for staying in the news for all the wrong reasons. The original jack of all trades.
22. Barrack Obama - for giving Kenyans a clear shot at the White
house. We hope the Illinois senator will find time to visit his
offshore constituents in Kenya with bags of dollars for 'development.'
23. Mrs Deya - for juvenile crap. If you are going to pull a scam of those proportions, get your elementary biology facts right.
24. Dennis Oliech - for behaving like a true star and throwing
tantrums at the other Stars. It was so cute. Success is a large screen
TV.
25. Paul Tergat - for proving that age is merely a number. The
dramatic down-to-the-wire finisher during the New York marathon was an
inspiration for couch potatoes.
26. Vincent Makori - of VOA, presenter of the Africa Journal show
for trying too hard. You can always tell when a Kenyan develops a twang.
27. Bernard Lagat - for introducing the disturbing prospect of an
American with Kenyan features winning the 1500m gold in the next
Olympics.
28. The foreign recipient is Thai PM Thaksin Shinawatra for finding
a new home for endangered Kenyan wildlife. Now Kenyan students in Asia
will get a chance to see exotic Kenyan wildlife in Thailand at
discounted rates.
29. KCB rugby club - receives the team award for finally coming of
age. Contrary to some female opinion, excellence in sport has nothing
to do with good looks.
30. The team members of the African Queens get the positive
influence award - for confirming the theory that men are visual
creatures. I hope female footballers are listening. The sudden increase
in the popularity of volleyball among males is not coincidental.
31. Raphael Tuju - for a handy lesson in succession politics. If you are not David, do not go after Goliath with a sling shot.
32. City hall gets the institutional award for behaving like the mob
levying rates, fees and charges for non-existent services. In some
countries, they call it extortion.
33. Maurice Odumbe - for teaching us the importance of keeping
girlfriends out of your personal affairs. There is such a thing as the
wrath of a woman scorned.
34. Uncle Moody Awori - for giving prisoners more privileges. Now
high risk offenders can have a regular dose of crime on TV while
serving their jail terms.
35. Robinson Githae - for a simple reminder. Think before you speak.
36. President Mwai Kibaki - aka the leader banana. Mostly for refusing to keep his word every time he gives it.
37. Ojwang of Vitimbi - for reminding us that hard work and sustained popularity doesn't always pay the bills.
38. David Sadera Munyakei - the Goldenberg whistle blower - for
illustrating the danger of assumption. You may risk your neck for the
country but the country won't notice.
39. Big pin - for showing that a little exaggeration never hurt
anyone. A small almost puny guy can pass for a big bloke with the right
attitude.
40. Swaleh Mdoe - for cracking his own jokes in a foreign language
(Swahili sanifu) in the middle of a prime time news broadcast and
enjoying the thrill of leaving his audience flummoxed.
41. The wooden mace award goes to the current Parliament for
allowing old men to masquerade as stakeholders. All I see are steak
holders
42. The incompetence award was won easily this year by KFF for turning soccer management into a martial art
Who said we lacked role models? It was a long year. Thank you for
keeping me company. The column will resume next year. Be happy.

Original_Search_Title: Anti-Corruption War Has Been Slow to Pick Up
Headline: Kenya: Anti-Corruption War Has Been Slow to Pick Up
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Narc Government arrived under a platform of zero-tolerance for corruption. But the vice seems to have claimed greater space,
If the fiasco that was the police recruitment is an indicator, the war against corruption is far from being won.
The popular perception, in spite of official claims, is that corruption has not gone down since Narc replaced Kanu in 2002.
Previously, recruits parted with between Sh40,000 and Sh50,000 to be enlisted in the Police Force.
But in the last recruitment that was annulled the fee ranged between Sh70,000 and Sh100,000.
Despite the many institutions set up to fight corruption, the war appears not to be coordinated, and the Government stands accused of sending mixed signals.
The establishment of the Kenya Anti-Corruption Commission (Kacc) was seen as the first major move to fight corruption. But the fact that Kacc has no prosecution powers turns it into a toothless dog.
It's at the mercy of the Attorney General who may decline to act on forwarded cases.
Early this year, Kacc accused the AG of not acting on some of such cases.
Kacc itself has been accused of fighting petty corruption cases such us police and public servants taking petty bribes while grand corruption thrives.
Despite many promises, the country is yet to see any high profile corruption case taken to court, and successfully prosecuted.
When former British High Commissioner Sir Edward Clay accused the Government of grand corruption, First Lady Lucy Kibaki said 40 files involving high profile cases were being investigated.
But nothing much has been heard of the cases raising speculation that that was rhetoric to placate donors and assure the public that something was being done.
The creation of the Office of Governance and Ethics Adviser and the appointment of John Githongo to head it, was seen as yet another milestone in the fight against graft.
But when President Kibaki scrapped the office when he named a new Cabinet early this month, the move was seen as a big blow to the fight against graft.
The office had remained vacant for nearly a year after Githongo resigned in a huff and went into self-exile in the UK.
Though he has not officially, and in any detail disclosed the reasons for his departure, the former PS said it had to with corruption.
He said corruption within the Government had frustrated him out of office though he did not substantiate.
But it is instructive that Githongo resigned at a time when the heat generated by the Sh7 million Anglo Leasing Finance Scandal was being felt.
Even though three former Permanent Secretaries and two other senior Government officials have been charged over the Anglo Leasing scam, its ghost still lingers.
It was an issue during the referendum where the Orange team opposed to the draft constitution used it to hit at their rivals in the Banana camp.
Another avenue of massive corruption is public procurement.
Late in the year, there was a standoff between Kenya and development partners who withheld Sh4.5 billion in budgetary support until a new procurement law was enacted.
President Kibaki later assented to the Bill.
In the last two months, the High Court has stopped over four contracts worth millions of shillings following allegations of corruption in the way they were awarded.
The latest one is where Mits Electrical Company was contracted to construct 10 lifts at the Nyanza Provincial Headquarters for Sh69 million, only for the tender to be given to another company.
Though the case is still pending, the judge stopped the construction or any dealings with the contract until the dispute is resolved.
Though the Government has not lived to expectations as far as the fight against corruption is concerned, it has nevertheless covered some ground.
Cases of land grabbing that were the order of the day in the previous regime have declined even though the Government is yet to act on the Ndung'u Land Report.
The report implicated top Government officials, some including ministers and their relatives.
The failure of the Government to act on the report is seen as a weak link in the fight against corruption.
The Government has also failed to release the Goldenberg Inquiry report that is expected to detail one of the biggest financial scandals since independence.
The report also incriminates top current and former Government officials, including some Cabinet ministers.
There is credit though in improved revenue collection.
This also has to do with the popular feeling in 2003 that the people needed to support a popularly elected government.
The Kenya Revenue Authority has each successive year surpassed its targets for revenue collection.
It is estimated that soon, the country might not require donor support as the amount raised in taxes would sustain public spending and investment.
The money disbursed, as Constituency Development Fund, would also rise as the revenue base expands.
The payment of taxes is now in vogue and corporations are outdoing each other on this score.

Original_Search_Title: Brighter Prospects for Textile Exporters in 2006
Headline: Eswatini: Brighter Prospects for Textile Exporters in 2006
Body_Text:
Mbabane — The Swazi apparel industry that once seemed the salvation of the economy has had an extremely rough year, but some insiders say the worst may be over for the surviving garment makers.
"As a small open economy, Swaziland is vulnerable to external shocks," the Central Bank of Swaziland explained in a study of factors that led to 18 of 31 textile companies shutting down since July 2004.
Workforce employment has also been halved, dropping from 31,000 workers in June 2004 to 15,000 today.
"Swaziland's clothing and textile industry, which significantly increased its exports and created thousands of jobs under the African Growth and Opportunities Act (AGOA) [through trade benefits for Swazi goods entering the US], is threatened with the Agreement on Textiles and Clothing (ATC)," the bank noted.
When it came into effect in January 2005, ATC lifted all quota restrictions on clothing and textile exports worldwide, paving the way for more cost-effective producers, such as China and India, to elbow out Swazi garment makers.
Bheki Dlamini, CEO of the Swaziland Investment Promotion Authority (SIPA), explained: "The end of the International Multi-Fibre Agreement by the World Trade Organisation [and its replacement by ATC] opened the floodgates for Chinese exports of textiles and garments into the Western markets, particularly the United States, where Swaziland enjoyed a quota and duty-free exports as a beneficiary of AGOA. As Chinese exports are cheaper for USA buyers, they are therefore preferred."
Robert Maxwell, head of the Swaziland Exporters Association, told IRIN, "The textile industry was hit hard twice in a row, and only the companies that restructured quickly survived. The first hit was when the US Congress delayed approving AGOA II."
While Congress debated AGOA rule changes, Swaziland's apparel makers saw their orders dry up in the face of uncertainty about the treaty outcome.
"For months there was no activity at all. As soon as AGOA II was ratified, the second hit came. China entered the world market, and overnight there was a 50 percent drop in garment prices," Maxwell said.
"The factories that survived laid off workers and cut costs during the AGOA II negotiations. With the advent of Chinese exports, the factories that prepared, survived," he said.
Meanwhile, suppliers, transport and other service providers, which had done good business during the apparel industry's boom years, suffered enormous losses in 2005.
This week a major road transport and freight clearing company shut down, unable to replace lost clients in the garment trade with other customers in Swaziland's struggling economy.
For the same reason, a major global courier service told IRIN it has considered shutting its facility at the Matsapha Industrial Estate, where apparel companies are concentrated outside the commercial hub, Manzini, 30 km east of the capital, Mbabane.
Restaurants and shops serving Matsapha area workers have closed or shed employees - even informal sector services like taxis and sidewalk fruit-vendors are suffering.
"This is the holiday season, and last year I was sold out - now my fruits are rotting in the sun," said 'Auntie' Shongwe from beneath an umbrella shading her selection of apples, plums and bananas.
Ten metres away, Swaziland Railways' 4:10 p.m. freight train began its daily run from the landlocked country to the port of Durban, on South Africa's Indian Ocean coast. Cargo volume is down by half on some trains, but railway officials are proud that they still run on time.
"The garment export situation has been absolutely devastating for all the support industries," Maxwell said.
The exporters' association head, who also runs a garment company, has been the leading voice in articulating the Swaziland garment industry's needs and problems to government and international trading partners like the US.
However, after two years of gloomy assessments, there is now a note of optimism in his view of the situation. "I can safely say the worst is behind us," Maxwell predicted.
"My company is fully booked until mid-2007 - we make high-end sportswear for Russell Athletics in the US - I could take five million more units easily, and give these to subcontractors. The orders are there for us - too many of them," he said.
The problem now is a shortage of skilled people. Trained workers departed Matsapha after the industry shakeout, and some left Swaziland altogether; replacing them will take time.
While the remaining, more streamlined apparel companies are recovering, SIPA has announced that no new investors in the textile business have come to Swaziland to create employment.
"Fifteen thousand garment industry jobs were lost in one year, and there are no replacement jobs on the horizon. We still have a massive unemployment problem. The ancillary businesses that once serviced the textile companies also must find new customers," a source with the finance ministry told IRIN.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Ministers Dismiss Popularity Poll Results
Headline: Kenya: Ministers Dismiss Poll Results
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Two ministers and an assistant minister have dismissed the Steadman
opinion poll that showed President Kibaki's popularity was plummeting.
Lands minister Amos Kimunya and his Water counterpart, Mutua Katuku said
the poll had been overtaken by events and was unlikely to force a change in
Kibaki's re-election strategies. They said the poll, conducted by the
Steadman Group, did not reflect the political reality on the ground.
Wildlife assistant minister Kalembe Ndile said the President still had
the support of Kenyans, who would vote for him in an election.
They said the poll, which placed President Kibaki's rating at 26 per
cent and Kalonzo Musyoka's at 35 among favourite presidential candidates,
had been influenced by "referendum politics".
Katuku dismissed the poll as "propaganda" with suspect timing.
The National Council of Kenya Muslims also dismissed the poll as a
fabrication. The council's coordinator, Nazlin Omar, said Kenyans lost
confidence in the Steadman Group before the referendum when it said the
Banana side would win by a big margin.
On Thursday, Ndile said ODM would not sustain its popularity until the
next general election.

Original_Search_Title: Retail Prices Shoot Up
Headline: Kenya: Retail Prices Shoot Up
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Commodity prices at the Nakuru retail market have doubled ahead of the
Christmas festivities.
A Nation survey yesterday indicated that most of the prices were up by
up to 50 per cent.
Traders recorded booming business as residents rushed to increase their
stocks.
A crate of tomatoes, selling at between Sh500 and Sh600 last month, now
fetches Sh1,000, while a bunch of Ugandan bananas, going for Sh450 last
month, now sells at Sh700.
A kilo of onion that was selling at Sh80 now costs Sh120 and a 200-kg
bag of Meru mangoes is selling at Sh1,200, up from Sh700 last month.
Dundori (Nakuru) cabbages were also plenty, selling at Sh10 each, up
from Sh5 last month.
"Business has been encouraging and we hope things will not change much
when schools reopen," said banana trader Grace Onyango.
An acute vegetable shortage has hit parts of Nyeri and Murang'a
districts as customers start early shopping for Christmas, causing prices to
skyrocket.
At Othaya's open-air market, the carrots and onion prices have doubled,
onion rising from Sh20 to between Sh40 and Sh45 a kilo and a sack of carrots
selling at Sh1,500, from last week's Sh900.
A cabbage that sold at Sh5 now costs Sh10.
At Murang'a's Kiria-ini market, the tomato price had risen from Sh80 to
between Sh150 and Sh200 for a 15 kilo container.
A goat that sold at between Sh1,800 and Sh2,000 is now going for
Sh3,000.

Original_Search_Title: Economic Growth Not an End in Itself
Headline: Kenya: Economic Growth Not an End in Itself
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki's last three speeches has focused on economic growth and development. On Jamhuri Day, in particular, the President elaborated on this idea, emphasising the economic gains made in the past three years, amidst high political infighting and tensions.
The unspoken message in all these is that as long as economic growth continues, then political support will follow. The rationale seems to be that if Kenyans are enjoying development, higher incomes, and less poverty, then they will inevitably support the government and the President, no matter what else is going on.
This is based on the notion that it is poverty that drives people to "opposition" so dealing with it, naturally, will lead to less "opposition" and more political support. This is an argument that one frequently hears from middle and business class Kenyans.
We cannot fault the desperate need for Kenyans to increase their incomes and reduce their gruelling poverty, nor the Government's role in jumpstarting and enhancing this development.
Political and criminal action
And we must recognise that we have made some strides by attaining the 5 per cent economic growth rate.
But imagine how much higher this would have been if concrete political and criminal action had been taken against the Anglo Leasing animal and the resultant corrupt deals that sprung up in its wake after this regime showed that it was "business as usual."
Imagine how well we would be doing if Kroll Associates who found out about the US $ 1 billion stacked abroad had been allowed to get the money back.
Be that as it may, recent world history has shown that alone, development and growth cannot replace the need for accountability, recognition of the struggles and roles of the people, and respect for their desires.
But even in the unlikely event that we could achieve economic development without accountability, there are some lessons to learn from South America and particularly Peru under former President Alberto Fujimori, and Chile under former President Augusto Pinochet.
In both countries, the former leaders delivered high economic growth raising incomes and reducing poverty. But consumed by their own sense of right, they trampled on justice, accountability and human rights, and were soon disgraced.
And closer home, the Ugandans are also proving the fallacy of this thinking with President Museveni using classic "Big Man" hardline tactics to intimidate and bar his strongest opponent, Kiiza Besigye, from contesting presidential elections in March, despite the undeniable economic growth and development that President Museveni has spurred in Uganda since 1986.
Were development the core criteria in determining political support, there would be no need to use criminal charges against Besigye, and Museveni would simply marginalise him through the ballot box as he seeks his sixth (not third) term.
We must hope that this focus on economic growth in Kenya is not accompanied by repression and hardline tactics as a way to muzzle the inevitable resistance that this regime will face in the years ahead.
Sustainable economic growth can only occur where the government listens to its people, where it admits mistakes and where competence, dialogue and accountability are the hallmarks of its approach.
Loyalty in politics is important, but when about a third of the 34 Cabinet members are from one region, then it means that loyalty has trumped accountability. When those who have been mentioned adversely on scandals and others who failed to deliver on the reform agenda are retained in Cabinet, then a sense of impunity is signalled.
In discussions with ordinary people across the board, the message is not that the key Orange leaders should have been in the new Cabinet, but that key Banana fellows should also have been excluded.
They have also been saying that dialogue with all the political players should have been attempted even if it were to be rejected, and that a focus on new, young, and relatively clean blood should have been tried in the Cabinet without retaining those old guards whose words and actions only hurt the presidency.
Recognition and respect
As much as Kenyans want to get out of poverty, they also want recognition and respect. Or as Dedan Kimathi so well put it: "It is better to die on one's feet, than to live on one's knees."
It is about dignity and pride, and this means listening to the people, and where you disagree with them, at least trying to explain the rationale of your decisions.
