It is about ending impunity and understanding the need for accountability in a changed political environment, vastly different from the 1970s when the dictates of leaders was akin to God's word. It is about understanding that the mark of a democracy is what happens between elections, much more than the elections themselves.
So as we head into 2006, let us hope that the spirit of this season will engulf all our leaders. That they will accept to listen to the people and submit themselves to public accountability forums, as the Presidents of Ghana and Rwanda do regularly, and provide chances for ordinary citizens to question, advice and input on how the country moves forward.
And that all the election pledges will be honoured. It is only with innovations such as this, that perhaps the President Kibaki emerging ideology of development may leave a lasting legacy.
Mr Kiai is the director of the Kenya National Commission on Human Rights.

Original_Search_Title: Kalonzo Leads As First Choice for President
Headline: Kenya: Kalonzo Leads As First Choice for President
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Sacked minister Kalonzo Musyoka is now Kenyans' first choice for
President, nine points clear of the man who fired him, according to an
opinion poll published yesterday.
Mr Musyoka, one of the seven Orange leading lights left out of President
Kibaki's new Cabinet line-up two weeks ago, is now leading the list of
favourite Presidential candidates.
He scored 35 per cent among all Kenyans eligible to vote, while the
President himself was in second place with 26 per cent support.
In third place, with 17 per cent is Official Opposition leader Uhuru
Kenyatta.
Only candidates who scored at least eight per cent in the survey, by
pollsters Steadman International, have been named in the list, meaning
scores are not shown for other possible Presidential candidates like
Vice-President Moody Awori, Mr Raila Odinga, Prof George Saitoti, and Mrs
Charity Ngilu.
Mr Musyoka, MP for Mwingi North and one of the key leaders who
campaigned for President Kibaki in the 2002 General Election was seen as a
Cabinet rebel because of his consistent opposition to the Wako Draft of the
proposed Constitution, which was heavily defeated in the recent
referendum.
He and six of his Cabinet colleagues were dropped by the President when
he named his new Cabinet.
The Steadman survey, entitled the Post-Referendum Opinion Poll, was
carried out across 53 districts at the beginning of last week. It also
contains some good news for the President: 52 per cent of those asked
approved of his decision to dismiss his entire Cabinet after the referendum
and said that they had confidence in his new team.
The preference on which constitution draft should be reviewed for a new
constitution
However, the results of the question about Kenyans' preferred
Presidential candidate showed Mr Kibaki's personal popularity see-sawing
from 33 per cent in October, up to 40 per cent a week and a half before the
November 21 referendum, then down to 26 per cent after the big vote.
Mr Musyoka's popularity has shown a steady increase from 20 per cent in
October to 29 per cent in November and 35 per cent now.
On wider issues, the poll shows the Government attracting its greatest
support from Central Province (83 per cent) followed by Eastern (71 per
cent), Rift Valley and Western (each 50 per cent), North Eastern (18 per
cent), and Nyanza (16 per cent).
Similarly, the President's most ardent support comes from Central
Province followed by Eastern Province.
For Mr Musyoka, that would mean a tough battle against the President in
his own Eastern Province. His most solid support came from Nyanza, home of
Liberal Democratic Party leader Raila Odinga.
Mr Odinga, widely perceived as the leader of the Orange campaign and a
close ally of Mr Musyoka, polled four per cent in the Presidential
popularity stakes, while other Orange leaders and former ministers attracted
less than one per cent apiece.
The poll, which took place as a series of face-to-face interviews
between December 11 and December 16 was published yesterday by Steadman's
managing director George Waititu.
The questions were asked at the height of political bargaining over new
Cabinet posts when some ministers declined to take their offered positions
and negotiations ensued between the President and Ford Kenya chairman
Musikari Kombo and National Party of Kenya leader Charity Ngilu.
In a similar survey carried out before the referendum, Steadman
correctly predicted that the Orange No-vote team would carry the day against
the Banana Yes-vote team by a 57 per cent against 43 per cent.
Yesterday, Mr Waititu told journalists: "An opinion poll is not a fact
but reliable estimates. Scientific methods of arriving at estimate works as
was shown by our polls before the referendum."
The poll results show the Liberal Democratic Party has gained popularity
at the expense of the National Rainbow Coalition (Narc), that fronted Mr
Kibaki as its Presidential candidate and drove Kanu out of power in the 2002
General Election.
Narc's popularity has dipped by 10 points since November and now stands
at 26 per cent, almost on equal footing with LDP's which attracts 25 per
cent support. The Democratic Party, Ford Kenya, the National Alliance of
Kenya and Ford People all score less than six per cent.
A clear majority of Kenyans - 74 per cent - want a new Constitution
before the 2007 General Election, the poll also shows, keeping the issue at
the top of the national agenda.
A similar percentage is optimistic that a new Constitution will be in
place by the next election. However, respondents were divided about who
should spearhead the process.
Only 38 per cent support a snap poll against 57 per cent who do not want
one. Five per cent had no opinion.
Following the referendum, the Orange Democratic Movement called on
President Kibaki to dissolve Parliament and pave the way for a snap
election, a move now rejected by a majority of Kenyans.
While 31 per cent feel the Constitution should be reviewed by experts,
22 per cent believe the country should remain under the current
Constitution, 17 per cent think the review should be by Parliament, 17 per
cent want another Bomas-style caucus of delegates while six per cent think
the Orange Democratic Movement should take charge.
A majority favour the Bomas Draft (the original new Constitution written
by delegates to the Bomas of Kenya) as the departure point for future
reviews while 22 per cent prefer the Wako Draft.
Twenty-one per cent would like to use the current Constitution as the
basis for the review, and 10 per cent did not know.
The quest for a Prime Minister's post - one of the key points of
disagreement over the proposed Constitution that was voted down - proved to
be narrowly unpopular, unwanted by 46 per cent of those asked, and supported
by 44 per cent, while 10 per cent were not sure.
But should a Prime Minister's post be created, 72 per cent of Kenyans
would like him or her to be the leader of the majority party in Parliament,
while 19 per cent think the President should decide who should do the
job.

Original_Search_Title: Ministers Vow to Block Snap Elections
Headline: Kenya: Ministers Vow to Block Snap Elections
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Eight Cabinet Ministers and a number of backbenchers yesterday vowed to reject any attempt to force a snap election through Parliament.
The ministers said they will mobilise MPs to reject a planned vote of no confidence in the Government. They were reacting to the weekend reports that the Liberal Democratic Party, together with Kanu and other dissenting MPs, will join hands to push through a vote of no confidence in the Kibaki administration.
The ministers were Newton Kulundu, Soita Standa, John Munyes, John Koech, Mohamoud Abdi, Maina Kamanda, Morris Dzoro and Rashid Shakombo.
As they spoke, the Liberal Democratic Party chairman David Musila clarified that neither his party nor the ODM had discussed or taken a common stand on the proposed motion.
Those expressing their views on the matter were making personal statements, he said. "The matter has never been discussed by either the LDP or the ODM. We cannot say something is happening when we have not discussed it"
And three clerics opposed the snap election bid and called for reconciliation among the politicians. Anglican Archbishop Benjamin Nzimbi said a snap election would derail development and the constitution-making process.
"Lets forget the snap election and wait for the main General Election in 2007. Kenyans should now start working towards realising their main goal, the constitution," Archbishop Nzimbi said in Nairobi.
St Andrews PCEA Parish Moderator, the Rev Patrick Mungiriria, said a vote of no confidence would further polarise the nation, which is just recovering from last month's referendum.
"It will not be constructive, it will be disruptive, we should be careful of the politics of 'let's crown him and let's crucify him', he said. "But as the dust settles, those who identified themselves with the ODM should be appointed to the Government just like those who were in the Banana camp," he said.
A Catholic priest based at the Holy Family Basilica, Fr Henry Kiritu, appealed for unity among all Kenyans during this season of Christmas.
Elsewhere, Vice President Moody Awori called on Kenyans to forget the differences that arose during the referendum and Cabinet appointments, and re-direct their energy in nation building.
"We cannot continue building on the results of the referendum while our people continue to suffer," the VP said.
A number of LDP MPs, however, insisted that they would push for a vote of no confidence in the confidence.
Leading the pack was Narok North MP Wiliam Ole Ntimama, who said: "We will stage a real battle in Parliament. I think it can result in a no confidence vote in this government which has been constituted without regard to democratic ideals through poaching opposition MPs."
Isiolo South MP Abdul Bahari said: "A government should run on a majority mandate. It is important that we review that position. The country does not need a Yes Cabinet and nobody should be penalised for holding different views."
But the ministers said attempts to pass a vote of no confidence will be resisted vigorously. Mr Kamanda of Gender, Sports, Culture and Social Services, dared the ODM to bring the motion immediately Parliament resumes so that it can be dispensed with immediately.
"Let them bring the motion first thing when Parliament resumes so that we dispose it off. I assure them that they will not get enough numbers to push it through," he said.
Mr Shitanda, who holds the Housing docket, said that there was no possibility of the motion sailing through the House. "They are bitter and the sooner they come to terms with reality the better. They are out of Government. There is no way a vote of no confidence can go through Parliament. It won't even attract 100 MPs," he said.
Tourism minister Morris Dzoro said that the President had signed a five year contract with Kenyans which would not be breached due to political pressure geared towards selfish interests of some leaders.
Mr Shakombo, the National Heritage minister, said: "Kenyans have given their MPs a mandate to represent them until 2007 and they should stop insisting on calls for snap elections. What's the hurry anyway?" he posed.
Mr Koech of the Minister for East African Cooperation ministry described the snap election call as a psychological war being mounted by the ODM. Mr Munyes, who is in charge of Special Programmes, described the motion is a pipe-dream.
In addition to the ODM lacking the numbers in the House, he said majority of the MPs owed Parliament loans and were not ready to face the consequences of a by-election.
Dr Kulundu the Labour minister said: "They are only excited about the Orange win in the referendum but that was not about Kenyans' confidence in the government or otherwise. Not many MPs would like to go back."
Regional Development Minister Mahmud Abdi said; "President Kibaki is still a favourite and the results of the referendum do not reflect no confidence in the government. What they are basing their argument on is irrelevant."
Nominated MP Kipkalya Kones, also Ford People chairman said it was premature to start talking about a vote of no confidence in the government.
However, the Nominated MP said he would support any Bill or motion meant to improve the welfare of Kenyans, regardless of which party brought it to parliament.
Also daring the MPs planning a no confidence vote was Kabete MP Paul Muite and his Nyeri Town counterpart, Mr P.G. Mureithi as well as Kinango MP Gonzi Rai.
Nobel peace laureate Wangari Maathai yesterday asked the Orange Democratic Movement to stop threatening the Government that they will force an early election.
Reacting to the weekend's remarks by a number of ODM MPs that they plan to use Parliament as the launching pad for a campaign aimed at forcing an early election.
Prof Maathai said instead, the MPs should call for negotiations with the government to end the political crisis. "By saying they will spearhead an early election, they are threatening the peace of the country by creating political tensions", she said.
Muslim for Human Rights (MUHURI) coordinator Hussein Khalid dismissed the calls for snap elections saying it would cost the taxpayer millions of shillings despite the fact lots of money had been spent during the referendum campaigns.
"It would mean borrowing money from other countries which the economy cannot sustain and we would then remain in limbo," he said.
However, Council of Imams secretary general Sheikh Mohammed Dor said that it was not the ODM that was calling for elections but Kenyans.
He said that since parliament was prorogued, MPs huge pay perks should be suspended and the money used to conduct the snap elections.

Original_Search_Title: Archbishop Confronts Obasanjo Over Third Term
Headline: Nigeria: 3rd Term: Bishop Confronts Obasanjo
Body_Text:
Abuja — There was a roaring applause yesterday eve-ning when the Catholic
Archbishop of Abuja, Bishop John Onaiyekan, spoke vehemently against the
move by some politicians to amend the constitution to ensure a third term
tenure for President Olusegun Obasanjo.
His comment came as Senate Committee on Inter-Parliamentary Affairs
chairman, Senator Uche Chukwu-merije, at the weekend, alerted that hatch-men
of the third term plot had infiltrated the Senate chambers accusing the
leadership of the Southern Senators Forum (SSF) of creating a window for the
option.
Onaiyekan, who officiated the Christmas Carol service jointly organised
by the Nigerian Television Authority (NTA), and the Federal Radio
Corporation of Nigeria (FR-CN), in Abuja and attended by Obasanjo,
admonished the President not to follow the route of other sit-tight African
leaders who turned their countries to banana republics.
With a roaring applause from the congregation, Onaiyekan said recent
reports indicated that some politicians were pushing the anti-democratic
agenda through a manipulated process of constitutional amendment that would
not be in the interest of Nigeria.
The Bishop, however, added that while these politicians and government
officials might be doing so for selfish reasons, the President had assured
Nigerians and the Body of Christ that he would go in 2007 and not seek any
third term.
"We thank God and we congratulate our President, Chief Olusegun
Obasanjo, who has decided to resist the temptation, which has become a
deadly disease of many African leaders who have decided to stay in power
forever by hook or crook," the Archbishop said.
Special Adviser to the President on Political Affairs, Prof. Jerry Gana,
served as the Master of Ceremonies at the service during which he thanked
the President for being a facilitator of the event beamed live to all
Nigerians by the NTA network service.
Meanwhile, Chukwumerije, in a reaction to the position of the SSF that
the South retains the Presidency in 2007 said, "It will be foolhardy to
continue to believe the evasive official denials of a third term plan",
adding that "the body language of the Presidency is very loud and clear."
According to him, "Like the flood of an approaching hurricane, third
term plot is gradually but steadily creeping inwards, softening the ground
and weakening the resolve of all critical joints of our constitutional
democracy.
"The tactic of the linkmen is the use of a simple but shallow logic: -
'there is no third term plan, because the incumbent has not said so'; 'the
Senate should not respond to a development which has not taken place'; 'wait
until you get to the bridge before you cross it,' etc.
This argument, according to Chukwumerije, could mesmerise only the
unwary into a state of inertia because the body language of the Presidency
had been louder than a million words. He said, the deft moves, quiet
contacts, sudden u-turns and new surrenders are going on daily in the dark
ante-rooms of centres of influence.
"What the spin doctors seek to do is to mesmerise us into a state of
stupor and false safety, draw rings of siege around the town, and present us
with a surrender statement in the eleventh hour when resistance is too
late."
Describing the SSF as a "front of Aso Rock," Chukwumerije also asserted
that the manipulations of the forum offered a clear proof, to those waiting
for formal confirmations, that insidious attempts were being made to
manipulate the joints and ligaments of the structure of constitutional
democracy to endorse a continuation of tenure for the incumbent
President.
"If patriotic elements of the National Assembly do not begin today to
rise against this danger, they will get into the chambers soon and hear the
orchestrated yea's! of a suborned majority," he further said.
He accusing the leadership of the SSF of manipulating the communiquÃ© of
its last meeting where decisions were taken on the forum's position on
retention of power in the South, and said the consensus arrived at differed
from what was released to the public.
According to the senator, "From the arguments at the meeting, the
following consensus emerged: the provision of a two four-year tenure for the
executive in the 1999 Constitution should be retained; the Presidency should
be retained in the South in 2007 and the contestants should be limited to
South-east and South-south zones; the South-west should not field candidates
in 2007 Presidential election.
"Other decisions were reached on other issues - creation of an extra
state in South-east zone, review of formula for sharing of VAT revenue,
resource control, payment of outstanding levies in honour of late Senator
Ama Iwuagwu."
Chukwumerije recalled that a committee was set up to draft a
communiquÃ©. "But the draft abridged the truth. It cleverly limited its
report to retention of the Presidency in the South and cunningly omitted the
specific zonal specifications, thereby leaving open both the possibility of
participation by the South-west zone and the materialisation of third term
option in 2007.

Original_Search_Title: Kombo Faces Tough Choices in Ford-K
Headline: Kenya: Kombo Faces Tough Choices in Ford-K
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Some things we know, and some we don't. But this we surely do -
that the recent referendum had more to do with political power-sharing
and positioning than the Constitution.
The first few lines - an adaptation from Joe de Graft's book Muntu -
couldn't have been more apt with regard to reactions by Ford Kenya, the
Liberal Democratic Party, the National Party of Kenya and Kanu to
President Kibaki's recent Cabinet appointments.
It was in the interest of the Orange Democratic Movement, LDP and
Kanu to avoid the "tainted" Kibaki Government like the plague in
anticipation of taking over to power in 2007, while for Ford-K and NPK,
it was an excellent opportunity to bargain for clout.
Whichever way they campaigned - for or against the proposed
constitution - leaders of the the parties did it in the belief that
they would gain some political mileage.
With seven Cabinet posts, up from the previous three, Ford-K which
has more than doubled its political stake in the ruling coalition, is
the biggest beneficiary. However, at a glance, the achievements are the
result of horse-trading, blackmail and cronyism.
For instance, all the party's seven ministers save one - Mr John
Munyes - are Luhyas among whom the party enjoys a huge following.
The party is also trying to influence the appointment of heads of
state corporations, ambassadors as well as permanent secretaries and
other senior civil servants. While MPs belong to political parties, it
is difficult to tell the political affiliations of civil servants
unless this can be established by ethnicity.
But party chairman and Local Government minister Musikari Kombo
attributes the big catch to dialogue and negotiation, which have earned
him praise from party supporters.
The case in all coalitions
Science and Technology minister Noah Wekesa says the party obtained
only what is its right "as is supposed to be the case in coalitions."
For sure, the party leadership deserves credit for, as it were,
hitting at President Kibaki when he was most vulnerable. With LDP out,
it could have been imprudent for him to let go of the party - even if
for the sake of image or numbers. With the shunting of LDP out of the
Government and more than 20 other MPs declining to take up ministerial
jobs, the President was particularly keen to save face by filling up
the positions. But although he may have partly achieved this following
the return of Ford-K and NPK to the Government, 11 positions are yet to
be filled.
When President Kibaki named the Cabinet, Mr Kombo joined hands with
NPK leader Charity Ngilu to reject ministerial positions, claiming that
their parties had been short-changed. This, argued the Ford-K boss on
rethinking the decision, was the principled way LDP should have handled
the matter after the December 2002 elections instead of grumbling from
within.
Dr Wekesa, a chief architect of the Narc coalition, agrees: "The
current development is a big win for Ford-K. Unlike LDP, we have
demonstrated the usefulness of dialogue among coalition member parties
- a tradition that we must all embrace."
But LDP chairman David Musila doesn't see it that way: "The
circumstances in 2003 were completely different. This time around,
however, when Ford-K declined to take up Cabinet posts after our exit,
the President was in a panic and even dispatched a close relative to Mr
Kombo's house to plead with him."
Dr Wekesa's comparison, he argues, is totally misplaced. While
Ford-K negotiated for positions they had been offered but rejected, he
says, LDP had nothing to begin from since the President had already
jettisoned them.
Reflecting on the Cabinet appointments in 2003, the LDP boss
explains that the President was unwell. The party's walk-out at the
time, he says, would have gone against the public mood and interest,
not to mention that "the President himself was incapable of absorbing
any such reaction.
"We considered the action but withdrew it. How about Ford-K? As Mr
Kombo rightly puts it, they were also short-changed, so why did they
not pull out then?"
The Science and Technology minister blames LDP for shooting itself
in the foot by sending what he terms unpalatable signals to the
President.
"They are now shifting blame," says the Mwingi South MP. "The Ford
Kenya leaders are simply dishonest and selfish. They initially wanted
us to talk to the President as Narc members, yet they sneaked back
alone and struck a selfish deal."
Among reasons initially given by the Ford-K and NPK leaders for
walking out on the Government is that the parties were not consulted
over the appointments, and that they would return only after all Narc
member parties were brought back on board for negotiations.
Ford-K and NPK are back; LDP and the Labour Party of Kenya are not.
However, Mr Kombo and Dr Wekesa deny citing the inclusion LDP in the
Government as a condition for their return. However, it is almost
certain that there is no love lost between Ford-K and LDP.
Says Mr Musila: "It could be over between us. In fact, I told my
colleagues in LDP not to celebrate the decision by Kombo and Ngilu to
reject Cabinet posts because I knew they would soon be compromised. I
think I spoke too soon."
Now thoroughly outnumbered, members of Ford-K have to brace
themselves for possible wars within Narc, which they have now to fight
alone. Previously, they occasionally teamed up with LDP, particularly
on issues relating to recruitment drives and the dissolution of
affiliate parties.
While it successfully negotiated for its "fair share" in the
coalition, Ford-Kenya has absolutely no say over over who is to be
incorporated in the party - a factor that may in the long run stand in
its way to realising some goals.
Mr Kombo who has hinted at his party going for the presidency by
stating that "every party exists to seek power and Ford Kenya is no
exception," now looks up to his long-term allies in Central province to
realise his political ambitions.
Only recently, two Ford-K MPs challenged the Central province
residents to support their party for the seat, arguing that Luhyas
supported President Kibaki in 2002.
Lugari MP Enock Kibunguchy said Luhyas and Kikuyus should maintain
the political alliance started by Ford-K chairman and Vice-President
Michael Wamalwa when he decided to support the President.
"We supported you for the presidency and want you to support us when
our time comes," Bumula MP Bifwoli Wakoli told a harambee meeting at
Kinyoho secondary school in Kandara constituency recently.
Should he succeed in wooing the region's electorate who are renowned
for supporting only one of their own, Mr Kombo would score yet another
first. Among those who have trodden this path and returned empty-handed
is former Butere MP Martin Shikuku.
Having helped the Banana camp to claim victory in Kwanza
constituency and parts of the North Rift, Mr Kombo also hopes to make
inroads in the other parts of the area. In fact, he recently divulged
that he was consulting Kanu chairman Uhuru Kenyatta over possible
cooperation.
However, with Kanu firmly in ODM alongside LDP, such an alliance
with it might not come easy unless, of course, the two ODM partners
fall out.
Citing the example of some ministers, Dr Wekesa says two years
before 2007 is such a long time in politics and that one cannot rule
out a Ford-K alliance with LDP or Kanu.
"Having listened to what some of the politicians said in 2002, for
instance, who would have ever imagined that the Karumes, the Nyachaes
and the Koechs of this world would be part of this government?" he
poses.
Then there is ODM, or rather the (former VP Musalia) Mudavadi factor
Ford-K has to contend with in Western province. Having spearheaded the
Orange campaigns into claiming victory in 19 out of the region's 24
constituencies at the referendum, Mr Mudavadi could well stop Ford-K's
"grand march."
But Dr Wekesa is optimistic: "Because of the significant presence of
the party in the Government, Luhyas will realign themselves in its
support ahead of 2007."
When the Kibaki announced his new-look Cabinet, he retained Mrs Ngilu in the Health portfolio.
But as a chorus of rejections met the new appointments, she added
her voice, saying that she would not take up her position until a few
issues were ironed out.
The tide has since changed, and she is sitting pretty at Afya House
again. But many Kenyans are asking questions. For example, were the
so-called pertinent issues ironed out before she accepted the
appointment? What does she bring to the table for President Kibaki and
his government? What else was her reward during the three-day
negotiations with the head of state?
Mrs Ngilu who had cited a raw deal for her NPK, yet again agreed to
rejoin the Government in what has become a characteristic lack of
consistency in the recent months.
News of her turning down the reappointment were received with
scepticism, especially in her Ukambani backyard, with many arguing that
the Kitui Central MP was just seizing the opportunity and the seeming
political confusion to salvage her apparently dwindling political
fortunes.
As she haggled with the President
As she haggled with the President, her appointment rejection was
being dismissed in her home Kitui district as "just a matter of time
before she went back to the President."
Kitui mayor Martha Mwangangi, a long-time political ally, was among
local leaders who described the move as "lacking in tact" and one that
could not surprise anybody.
"I was not consulted in the matter and can neither praise nor
criticise it," he told the Sunday Nation. "All I can do is to watch the
unfolding events."
Since the beginning of the year, Mrs Ngilu has been grappling with a
rapidly changing political scenario in the wider Ukambani region, which
often threatened her standing in the national politics and survival in
Kitui Central.
First, her close allies in the region, including new Water minister
Mutua Katuku and Kathiani MP Peter Kaindi, have established a direct
link to State House, and do not have to go through her. Both and
several others went to Parliament through NPK.
She has found herself increasingly being sidelined by long-time
party loyalists, the same people on whose behalf she claimed to bargain.
Moreover, ODM had beckoned her, but with the caveat, "as long as she
is sincere." Said former Environment minister Kalonzo Musyoka, her
political nemesis in Ukambani: "Politics is a game of numbers and if
she comes in good faith we have no problem."
Observers argue that the minister's source of disaffection with the
new Kibaki line-up was primarily the elevation to a full Cabinet
minister of Mr Katuku, the Mwala MP, her perceived junior in the local
politics.
"She felt belittled by the President's failure to recognise her
seniority in the region by putting her at par with her own foot
soldiers," says Mutito MP Kiema Kilonzo.
Thus, having lost badly in the referendum and with Mr Katuku and
Makueni MP Kivutha Kibwana now in the Cabinet, Mrs Ngilu's dream of
assuming the leadership of the Kamba seems almost dissipated.
So the big question is: Will the Health minister ditch President Kibaki's government again?
- Additional reporting by Kitavi Mutua

Original_Search_Title: Only a New Gov't Can Save Cameroon From Corruption - Hon. Yoyo
Headline: Cameroon: Only a New Gov't Can Save Cameroon From Corruption - Hon. Yoyo
Body_Text:
Hon. Emmanuel Yoyo, Member of Parliament for Ngokintunjia constituency, says the Biya government is incapable of fighting corruption. According to him, if the Biya government really wants to fight corruption, it will be fighting against itself because most of its members are corrupt. In an exclusive interview with The Post recently, Hon. Yoyo, who is one of the questors at the National Assembly, claimed that some government officials who are said to have been detained on charges of corruption, have really been detained because they are political enemies of the Head of State.
While presenting government social economic, financial and cultural programme, the Prime Minister promised to fight corruption more than ever before. Do you believe him?
I don't believe him because government makes such promises every year and nothing happens. We in the SDF Parliamentary Group have been doing everything to assist government in the fight against corruption, but there is a clear lack of political will. We brandished proof that the former Minster of Education, Charles Etoundi, was involved in corrupt practises.
We called for the creation of a parliamentary commission of enquiry but government snubbed us. We earlier provided evidence that FEICOM General Manager was embezzling billions of FCFA of state funds, but government allowed him to continue to cause the damage until he was sacked recently.
When the present Minister of Finance was still Director of Taxes, we equally presented evidence to show that he was involved in financial rackets. But in an apparent reply to our endeavour, the Head of State instead appointed Abah Abah the Minister of the Economy and Finance. How can you keep a rat to take care of groundnuts? How can you keep a monkey to take care of bananas?
This government is not committed in fighting corruption. The general impression I have is that it arrests only those who disagree with the Head of State politically and not embezzlers of public funds.
The Head of State seems to mean business this time because a few hours after he said embezzlers would no longer be tolerated, the former FEICOM GM,Ondo Ndong was sackedÃ¢-oe..
By saying that, does it mean that he used to tolerate corruption before? I know that he once asked Cameroonians to provide proof when they complained that government officials were embezzling state funds. As I said before, we presented proof in parliament and nothing was done to the embezzlers. It means nothing to me when the President says he will no longer tolerate embezzlers of public funds. It is a question of wait and see.
Cameroon this time appears not to have done so badly in the corruption perception index published by Transparency International, what is your view?
I must tell you straight away that I am not comfortable with the people who are leading Transparency International, branch in Cameroon. It is unacceptable that the President of Transparency International Cameroon is moving about with members of government and the Prime Minister in a bid to defend Cameroon abroad, and say the country is not corrupt. You can imagine how much he has been collaborating with government. So, I don't even want to take the corruption perceptive index into account. I think that if the rating was honestly done, we might have had worse results.
The President of Transparency International, Cameroon, Barrister Akere Muna, is a lawyer. What is wrong with him travelling with the Prime Minister to defend Cameroon?
If the Prime Minister wanted to travel with a lawyer, why not take the President of the Bar Council? What criteria did they use in choosing Muna? Is he the only lawyer in the country? If they wanted to take a lawyer with an official capacity, it would have been the President of the Cameroon Bar council. By the way, what did the Prime Minister want a lawyer to go and do in the US? I think it is not a coincidence that the lawyer in charge of monitoring corruption in the country was the one chosen to go about with the Prime Minister.
You are probably also aware that this very President of Transparency International, Cameroon, has been elected Vice President of Transparency International in the word. Iis that not a sign that he has credibility and a sign of confidence that the rest of the world have in him?
Well, it does not mean anything. The rest of the world may have their confidence in him, but I am talking of what we are living in Cameroon. I don't think he was made Vice President because he was found more honest or more capable than all representatives of all other countries. I frown at the fact that our Transparency International President should be moving around with members of government to defend Cameroon's file on corruption
There is much activity in the fight against corruption. There are anti-corruption committees in the ministries yet you claim that government is not committed. Do you think it is all propaganda?
It is big propaganda. I want to tell you that some of those committees may even be more corrupt than some of the corrupt people that we know. We want to see results of the work that they are doing. We don't need to hear that there are anti-corruption committees all over the place.
We want to hear that this or that government official has been detained on charges of corruption in a very objective manner. You know, there is one thing; many Cameroonians, especially of the ruling CPDM party, are aware of this fact that once in that party they are safe and that is why many of them who are corrupt will not leave the party. They are safe until they have political differences with the Head of state
In such a situation, what can the opposition MPs do to fight corruption in this country?
What we have been doing in the past is what we will continue to do. I quoted a few cases to you, where we tried to expose corrupt members of government so that they may be arrested and investigated. We will continue to do that. We submitted a private members bill on the declaration of assets as provided for by the constitution. This bill makes it imperative for people in high places, including MPs to declare their assets. It's unfortunate that our initiative was blocked, but we will continue with our endeavours.
So you were ready to declare your assets?
Of course, when I go asking that people should declare their assets, it means that I am just very ready and happy to declare my assets.
There is talk that MPs, even some opposition MPs are involved in corruption, how would you react to this?
I think what people are saying is very true, it's very correct. I will say that when you find many business people coming to Parliament, it is clear that most of them are coming to hide and use the parliamentary immunity as a shield against prosecution for their malpractices.
We have so many of such people in parliament. I will not be ashamed to tell you that in the last legislature, one MP from my party, the SDF, was caught in a drug racket in Paris. He was arrested with marijuana and we were the ones who put pressure on government to investigate him .We later dismissed him from our party. We don't condone such things.
It is also alleged that some MPs are feymen. Do you sometimes feel embarrassed that such people are your colleagues?
Any MP worth that title should be very unhappy if he/she hears that amongst them, there are people who do not deserve to be called honourable members of parliament because of their activities. So, I am not happy to hear that there are such people in our parliament. We have so many people here who are suspected and declared openly to be feymen, and that really embarrasses me a lot. But there is really nothing we can do about that.
You have travelled out of this country a couple of times, how much esteem do people have for you when they discover that you are a Cameroonian?
Let me tell you that all these things depend on the circle in which you find yourself. I know very well that before, Cameroonians were very highly respected out of this country. I know that for some time now Cameroonians are not respected as before, but I don't want to put all the blames on Cameroonians.
There are many people from other countries, especially neighbouring countries who have involved themselves in things in the name of Cameroonians. They manage and have Cameroonian passports and then when arrested, they are taken for Cameroonians. Verifications in so many instances have proven that those people are not Cameroonians. We Cameroonians have contributed for the bad image that we have outside.
Do you think Cameroon will be able to repair its battered image as one of the most corrupt countries in the world?
Yes, why not? But it needs a team with the political will to fight corruption. But I think the present regime cannot do it. We need a new team because even if this regime wants to truly fight corruption, it will only be fighting itself. Only a new government that has a political will to fight corruption will do the trick.

Original_Search_Title: Residents of Western Dismiss Appointments
Headline: Kenya: Residents of Western Dismiss Appointments
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The inclusion in government of many Ford Kenya MPs from Western
Province has elicited mixed reactions among residents of the area with
some dismissing the jobs as a "joke'.
Civic leaders said that some of the appointments would not benefit them, as they are of no economic importance to them.
The leaders were particularly critical of the Ministry of Housing that given to Malava MP Soita Shitanda.
They said that Ford Kenya chairman Musikari Kombo should have
lobbied for the MPs to be given important ministries that can enable
the MPs to secure jobs and other resources to benefit the residents.
Councillor Joe Serenge of Bukhulunya ward in Kakamega Municipality
dismisses the ministry as a department that cannot bear fruit for the
residents.
In an interview following the appointments, Serenge explained that
it would have been better for Shitanda to remain in the Office of the
President as an assistant minister, instead of occupying a 'hollow'
ministry.
Serenge and fellow councillors Jacob Andala, Fred Wetundu, Richard
Namale and Hamisi Omuganda said it was wrong for President Kibaki to
give in to Kombo's demands that Ford Kenya MPs be given the ministerial
positions later after the line-up had been announced.
The civic leaders said that the additional ministries would not
benefit the Luhya but the individual ministers. Namale and Omuganda
said that the MPs would benefit as individuals as they will earn the
salaries of ministers despite doing little to improve the welfare of
their electorate. They said they were hoping that the President would
carry out a Cabinet reshuffle to give MPs from Western Province MPs
better ministries.
Although the situation is complicated for the MPs, residents in some
areas are trying to bear with the circumstances under which the
positions were achieved. But some civic leaders believe that Kombo has
'scored' for the community.
The party MPs who have been brought into the Cabinet from Western
Province under Kombo's initiative are Shitanda, Dr Noah Wekesa (Science
and Technology) and assistant ministers Dr Enock Kibunguchy (Health)
and Dr Bonny Khalwale (East African Community).
Voters in Khalwale's Ikolomani constituency are happy with the
appointment and feel that the MP will use the position to attract
investors to reduce poverty in the area. Professionals led by Hamisi
Shivachi say that Khalwale's ministry should assist him source for
investors to put up projects in the district.
Ikolomani was ranked among the poorest constituencies in the country in a recent survey.
Shivachi, who is a teacher, explained that the appointment has
proved that Kombo is out to fight for the rights of his community the
same way the former party national chairman and Vice President, Kijana
Wamalwa, did.
Mwanzi however added that Shinyalu MP Daniel Khamasi should also
have been appointed to the Cabinet as he delivered the highest number
of Banana votes in Kakamega, Butere/Mumias and Vihiga districts.
Bishop Simon Oketch of the Anglican Church of Kenya said that the
appointments would not bridge the gap that President Kibaki has created
by sacking all the Orange Democratic Movement leaders.
Oketch of Maseno North Diocese explained that MPs who had rejected
their appointments should have stuck to their decisions as going back
to the Cabinet through the back door showed poor leadership. Namale and
Andala, who are from Kulundu's Lurambi constituency, were of the view
that Kombo had not solved the leadership squabbles in the party despite
having ensured that Shitanda became a minister.
They said they expected Kombo to demand for the tamarcking of roads,
establishment of factories and elevation of some of the technical
institutions to polytechnics and not to lobby for the ministerial
positions that benefit only a few.
In Butere/Mumias district, the residents are disappointed with the
appointments as none of their MPs was given a post despite the area
contributing to the country's economy through the sugar sector.
Political leaders in the area are now accusing Kombo of misusing
Matungu MP David Were to achieve his political and social interests in
the district. Leaders said that Kombo should now de-link himself from
Were because the Ford Kenya chairman has no development agenda for the
district. Civic leaders Patrick Barasa, Wycliffe Kundu and
professionals described Kombo as a dishonest leader because he has not
influenced President Kibaki to appoint MPs from the area to the
Cabinet. They asked Were not to take up the position of assistant
minister for Information and Communications because the slot was of no
value to the residents.
"Kombo told us while campaigning at Mumias Sports Club that he would
ensure that the district benefits from the national cake only to short
change us when presenting his demands to President Kibaki," said one of
the professional from Matungu constituency, Mr Robert Opwora.

Original_Search_Title: Kones Leaves Kibaki Empty Handed in South Rift
Headline: Kenya: Kones Leaves Kibaki Empty Handed in South Rift
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The decision by former Public Works assistant minister Kipkalya
Kones to take up his position in the new Cabinet complicates matters
for President Kibaki in the South Rift.
It leaves Chepalungu MP John Koech, who was re-appointed minister
for East African community as the only prominent figure in the
government from the region.
Koech is the only MP from the Kipsigis districts of Bomet, Kericho and Bureti who is in the government.
Other than Kones, Bureti MP Paul Sang had earlier declined his assistant minister's post.
But rejection of ministerial posts is not new in the region. Ainamoi MP Noah Too had earlier resigned as an assistant minister.
Too, a Kanu MP and a former CID director, was the first Cabinet
minister to resign from Kibaki's government after serving for a few
months.
It surprised foes, friends and supporters of Kones when he declined to take up his position.
He was one of the most vocal campaigners for the Wako Draft in the region but performed dismally.
In so doing, Kones was in a collision course with his people who
voted against the draft almost to a man, leaving him to deliver only
1,510 votes.
But when he announced his resignation, one of the reasons he gave
was that the government had failed to resettle the 10,200 Kalenjins
evicted from Mau forest in Narok South, earlier in the year.
The other reason was that the nominated Ford People MP and former Bomet MP was bowing to public pressure.
Kones broke ranks with Kanu MPs from Rift Valley when they were
pushing for resettlement and compensation for these families. Bomet MP
Nick Salat recently took a shot at Kones asking, "Which people? The
people rejected him during the last General Election. He is my
constituent so he cannot purport to have any following."
Salat told 'The Sunday Standard': "I am disappointed that he has padded the statistics of the unemployed in Bomet constituency."
Bomet Mayor Patrick Ngerechi and county council chairman Michael
Korir jointly said in Bomet: "It is a classic case of too little too
late. He should have taken up the Cabinet post. He has further
alienated himself from the people as a result."
It is the feeling of many of his supporters in the South Rift region
that Kones should have taken up the post and championed for the rights
of the people while in government.
But there are others who say the move was not only justified but was
over due. Among this group are the Ford People South Rift's region
coordinator, Paul Ngerechi and the Bomet and Konoin branch chairmen
Paul Ngerechi, Joseph Chirchir and Cheruiyot Baliach. "Kones made the
right move at the right time. He is going to reach out to the people,
listen to their views and fight for their rights. It is something he
can do better outside than inside the government," Ngerechi says. "The
Kipsigis held him in high esteem, and failure to deliver the Banana
vote made him look like he has lost touch with the people. He needs to
retrace his steps," Ngerchi, a close ally of Kones, told 'The Sunday
Standard'.
The grapevine has it that Kones declined to take up his position
because he was not appointed to a full ministerial position as he had
hoped.
Instead, the party's (Ford People) leader, Simeon Nyachae and MP
Henry Obwocha were the only legilators in the party to get ministerial
posts.
Lumped together with Kones in the assistant ministerial positions were Joel Onyancha and Asman Kamama.
Kones, it is said, felt that as Ford People's national chairman, he
should have been given a ministerial post and was disappointed when he
was left out as the position placed him in the same league with his
juniors.
Another school of thought has it that Kones, once a powerful
minister in the Office of the President during the Kanu regime has his
eyes trained on the future, especially at the constituency level, where
Nick Salat (Kanu) ousted in the last General Election.
Salat, who was nicknamed "the giant killer" after flooring Kones to
the surprise of many as he was considered the underdog, has been
working round the clock to consolidate his support in the constituency
and in the South Rift.
His star shone even brighter after delivering victory for the Orange
team in the referendum. It is instructive that his Bomet constituency
was second only to William Ruto's Eldoret North in the number of votes
cast. Kones lost the Bomet seat in the General Election largely because
he was in the wrong party, having parted ways with retired President
Moi and Kanu.
Another reason being advanced for his loss is that he went round the
country campaigning for Simeon Nyachae's bid for the presidency and
forgot his own constituency.
"He got so involved with Nyachae's campaign that he forgot his
constituents and assumed that he needed only a few days to campaign.
However, Salat had rolled out a door-to-door, well organised and funded
campaign that saw him come from behind to snatch the mantle from
Kones," one of Kones' supporters says.
Going by the referendum results, Kones will have a hard time wooing
supporters and will have to work overtime with a clear mission and
vision to regain his lost glory. Prior to declining his appointment,
Kones is said to have widely consulted politicians and professionals in
Nairobi.
But it remains to be seen whether this will endear him to the people
and if the Orange Democratic Movement team will welcome him the fold.
With the exit of Kones and Sang, President Kibaki's hold on the South rift region has got even weaker.
It is understood that he retained Koech because of their Makerere
link while other pundits say the two have been bosom friends for many
years.

Original_Search_Title: The Old Guard Still Calling the Shots in Kibaki's Govt
Headline: Kenya: The Old Guard Still Calling the Shots in Kibaki's Govt
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Cecily Mbarire's abrupt transfer from the newly-created ministry of
Youth Affairs has only heightened the perception about the tight grip
of old guard politicians in the Kibaki Government. The reason advanced
for her transfer was quite hilarious.
According to the Government, the new ministry and its grand staff of
three - the minister, the PS and the assistant minister - was
"bloated." And this was before the brand new department had even
developed a bureaucracy. The extensive Transport ministry, where she
was taken, and where Ndia MP Njeru Githae is already sitting as
assistant minister, presumably is supposed to be less crowded.
Reportedly, the real reason this infant ministry was destabilised
hardly before it got going is even more amusing. It so happens that Ms
Mbarire, who everybody admits made a perfect representation of the
youth in the current Parliament, had developed a habit from early on in
the Narc regime of speaking out bitingly whenever President Kibaki made
appointments that typically by-passed the young.
When her griping continued, she was quietly called in and told by
the head of civil service Mr Francis Muthaura to tone down, which she
did. It transpires that after she was appointed assistant minister,
somebody in high circles of power remembered her earlier peskiness and
worried that she could just start making noise from her perch at the
new ministry about every future appointment that by-passed people of a
certain age.
The other thinking is that the old guard who are in power may not be
quite comfortable with a ministry which would forever keep calling
attention to their advancing age.
The big signal of Kibaki's new Cabinet is how the old guard have
consolidated and even expanded their power in the government. Despite
the rumoured expectations of Ford-Kenya, Vice-President Moody Awori
remained comfortably as the Number Two. He is 78, though there was
obviously more riding on his retention than just his advanced age.
The big winners, though, are the Central Kenya wazees who retained
their Cabinet positions and in some cases even acquired expanded
powers. Ministers Njenga Karume and John Michuki remain at the apex of
power at the Office of the President. Their counterpart David Mwiraria
remains at the Treasury.
Did rather well
Simeon Nyachae, whose party, Ford People, did rather well for itself
in the new reorganisation, was transferred from Energy to the
high-budget docket of Public Works. His associate, Mr Henry Obwocha,
was made Planning minister.
The exit of Dr Chris Murungaru and the transfer of his friend Mr
Kiraitu Murungi to the relatively low-profile Energy ministry means the
triumph of the wazees, with whom they competed for power, is now
complete.
The retention of Lands minister Amos Kimunya may give some youthful
flair to a power establishment that is decidedly elderly, but from the
point of view of Dr Murungaru's and Mr Murungi's friends, Mr Kimunya is
just a proxy of the old guard around the president.
It was not only Mr Murungi who was exiled from his fiefdom at the
Justice and Constitutional Affairs ministry, but his assistant and ally
Mr Githae was also thrown to Transport. Kabete MP Paul Muite, a very
prominent ally of both Dr Murungaru and Mr Murungi, had been the
subject of intense speculation to the effect that he would be appointed
to the Cabinet, probably at the expense of Mr Karume, who is also from
Kiambu. That was not to be. The forceful Kabete MP had a meeting with
the President last week whose details neither State House nor the MP
are revealing.
With this Cabinet, there was actually very little effort to reach
out to women until Charity Ngilu brought up the matter and pushed for
the likes of Ms Mbarire and Adelina Mwau to be made assistant ministers.
In Nairobi, there were questions why Beth Mugo, the Dagoretti MP,
was denied a Cabinet seat in Nairobi which instead went to a close
Kibaki crony, Starehe MP Maina Kamanda. This was despite Mrs Mugo being
the only Banana MP in the city who delivered the referendum vote in her
constituency.
The only grand gesture made to women was the elevation of Martha
Karua from the Water docket to the Justice portfolio. For Ms Karua, it
was sweet desserts to score one against Mr Murungi considering the
anger that consumed her long ago when the same wazees of the Democratic
Party sidelined her from being the party's top legal functionary in
favour of Mr Murungi, who had just defected from Ford-Kenya.
The accommodation made by the government with Ford Kenya and the NPK
has not shaken the entrenched power of the old guard a bit. Two
Ford-Kenya new-comers to the Cabinet - Soita Shitanda and Noah Wekesa -
were given relatively small chunks of existing ministries: Housing
(formerly under Lands) and Science and Technology (formerly under
Education) respectively.
The Special Programmes docket that went to Ford Kenya secretary
general, John Munyes, may have been a fiefdom on its own, albeit within
the OP, but its significance was not because of its mundane operations
of relief supplies, but rather due to the catchet it acquired as a
result of its former occupant for whom it was created being one of the
president's closest confidantes.
Actually, Mr Karume has been elevated in being taken to the Defence
portfolio. It is the first time since the late nationalist James
Gichuru's long tenure in the Cabinet that Defence is overseen on its
own by a minister.
The head of the civil service has not yet made public the exact
departmental structure of the expanded Cabinet, and therefore at this
stage it is unclear whether Mr Karume's new portfolio is delinked from
the OP. So far the minister's office remains at Harambee House, while
the PS responsible for Defence, Mr Zachary Mwaura, operates from the
Department of Defence headquarters located in the Hurlingham suburb of
Nairobi.
Until the beginning of the Moi presidency, Defence used to be a
separate ministry. It was Mr Moi who absorbed the docket into the OP
where the department came directly under him.
It is a prestigious and highly sensitive docket. When Dr Murungaru
was in charge, the Defence Department was under his portfolio of
National Security, though within the overall umbrella of the OP.
Powerful job description
When Mr Michuki replaced him, it did not escape notice that his
designation reverted to Internal Security, which was minus the Defence
bit. When the Cabinet was recently reconstituted, Mr Michuki found it
necessary to inform the press that his portfolio had been expanded back
to National Security. But with Mr Karume's recent appointment, Mr
Michuki is back to his original, though still very powerful, job
description.
Sources have it that one of the ministries Ford Kenya had been keen to land was Public Works, which went to Mr Nyachae.
One of the reasons some ministries are prized over others is because
of the largesse they are perceived to control, which plays a big role
when the occupant wants to extend patronage or even jobs to friends and
supporters.
Laikipia East MP Mwangi Kiunjuri had initially turned down his
reappointment as assistant minister for Energy. It is understood that
his main gripe was that another assistant minister (Kanu's Joseph
Nkaissery, who immediately declined the appointment) had been assigned
to the same ministry, which led to Mr Kiunjuri getting confined to the
section dealing with electricity matters. The docket dealing with
petroleum-related issues is reportedly more prized.
Anyway, Mr Kiunjuri soon after took up his appointment.

Original_Search_Title: Resign Now, Banana Leaders Dare Raila
Headline: Kenya: Resign Now, Banana Leaders Dare Raila
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Banana leaders have dared former Roads minister Raila Odinga to resign from Parliament if he thinks the people are with him.
Narc director-general Alex Muriithi on Thursday said Raila was hurling insults at Kibaki and behaving as if he was the "alternate president".
Muriithi said Raila and other members of the Orange Democratic Movement (ODM) should resign from Parliament and seek re-election. He said a similar thing happened in 1966 when 30 MPs resigned from Parliament and sought fresh mandate from the people.
Muriithi, who said he was speaking as the director-general of Narc, said the party would start countrywide recruitment from January. He said five political parties had applied to join Narc, but declined to name them, saying it was too early.
He said the national referendum committees of the Orange and Banana teams should meet to discuss the way forward. Muriithi was speaking at the Narc secretariat in Nairobi, at a meeting for parties that supported the proposed constitution. He was accompanied Dr Thuo Mathenge, the national coordinator of Narc.
The parties, which have since changed their name to the Inter-Parties Development Forum, issued a statement read by the secretary-general of the Labour Party, Daniel Rasugu.
Rasugu said Raila could not be trusted because his bickering began when President Kibaki declined to name MPs Otieno Kajwang', Oburu Odinga, Adhu Awiti and Job Omino in his first Cabinet. He said Raila opposed the appointment of Anyang' Nyong'o, Raphael Tuju and Ochillo Ayacko.
Rasugu claimed that before declaring "Kibaki Tosha!" in 2002, Raila had signed a memorandum of understanding with Ford People leader Simeon Nyachae but trashed it.

Original_Search_Title: I Want Cabinet Post, Says MP
Headline: Kenya: I Want Cabinet Post, Says MP
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Newly appointed Co-operative Development Assistant minister David Mwenje now wants a slot in the Cabinet.
Mwenje said he would seek an appointment with President Kibaki when he jets back into the country on December 26.
Speaking to The Standard on phone from Beijing, China, on Thursday, the Embakasi MP said: "I will do what (Musikari) Kombo did to have President Kibaki yield." On Tuesday, President Kibaki bowed to demands by Kombo and minister Charity Ngilu and appointed four more Cabinet ministers and five assistant ministers from their parties.
"I will go and talk to the President. I will not reject the appointment before I meet him, but I want a bigger position," he said.
Mwenje is leading the Parliamentary Security Committee on a working tour of China. During the referendum campaigns, he campaigned for the Orange team only to defect to the Banana side four days to the vote after meeting with the President.
Meanwhile, Lamu East Kanu branch has suspended area MP Abu Chiaba for accepting his appointment as an assistant minister.
Twenty Kanu officials and members led by the branch chairman, Mohammed Hashim, endorsed the suspension and, in a press statement, urged the party headquarters to expel Chiaba from the party.
But in a quick rejoinder, Chiaba, who is a Kanu life member, dismissed the officials as "busybodies who are serving their masters in Nairobi".
"These are bogus Kanu officials picked from Nairobi. They have lost touch with the grassroots," the Fisheries assistant minister said.

Original_Search_Title: Forget Snap Election, President Tells Critics
Headline: Kenya: Forget Snap Election, President Tells Critics
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki yesterday ruled out a snap General Election, saying
Parliament still had the mandate given by the 2002 poll.
Those calling for a new election were wasting time, he said.
But the President was at pains to add that the Government had no fear of
an early poll.
"It's not that we are afraid of elections. No. We are very ready for
elections, very ready," he said.
Mr Kibaki's comments followed repeated calls by leaders of the new
Orange Democratic Movement and its parties - Kanu and the Liberal Democratic
Party - for the dissolution of Parliament to pave way for a fresh General
Election, following the Government's defeat in the referendum for a new
Constitution, on November 21.
Under the current Constitution, a snap election can be held only if the
President dissolves Parliament or if the House passes a vote of
no-confidence in the Government.
The required number for winning a no-confidence vote is a simple
majority of all MPs which would be 112 in the present Parliament.
The House is now suspended indefinitely and will reopen at the
President's pleasure.
Calls for a snap election came immediately the Orange team won the
referendum polling more than a million more votes than the Banana side which
was supporting the proposed Constitution which had the President's
backing.
Speaking at State House, Nairobi, yesterday when he witnessed the
swearing-in of six Cabinet ministers and eight assistant ministers, the
President said the November 21 vote on the Wako Draft was not a General
Election.
"We were elected to Parliament some years ago and what we had the other
day was a special election that had nothing to do with Parliamentary
seats. That referendum is now behind us. That was an election to determine
whether we agreed with one issue or we didn't," the President said.
"Those who are calling for a snap election are wasting time. It's not
that we are afraid of elections. No. We are very ready for elections, very
ready. It's not just those others talking who were voted in. We came from
constituencies where the Kenyans who voted for us know much about us."
Yesterday's swearing-in ceremony was the second after last week's when a
majority of ministers took the oath of office.
It followed days of negotiations between the President and Ford-Kenya
chairman Musikari Kombo and National Party of Kenya leader Charity Ngilu,
over the appointment of their party MPs into the Government.
Mr Kombo and Mrs Ngilu had skipped last week's ceremony and vowed not to
take office until the President gave them extra appointments. Mr Kibaki
yielded to their demands on Tuesday.
There was a surprise when assistant minister Samuel Moroto, who had
previously declined his appointment, arrived at State House ready to take
his oath. His decision last week was announced by leader of Official
Opposition Uhuru Kenyatta.
Cabinet ministers sworn in yesterday were Mrs Ngilu, Mr Kombo, Prof
Kivutha Kibwana, Mr Soita Shitanda, Dr Noah Wekesa and Mr John Munyes.
Assistant ministers were Mr Moses Wetang'ula, Mr Sammy Leshore, Mr Titus
Ngoyoni, Ms Cecily Mbarire, Mr Enock Kibunguchy, Dr Bonny Khalwale, Ms
Adelina Mwau and Mr Ekwee Ethuro.
Immediately after the function, Mr Munyes told reporters he was happy
with the size of the Cabinet because it reflected the nation's
diversity.
Mr Leshore and Mr Ngoyoni missed the first ceremony because they come
from far-flung constituencies - Samburu East and Laisamis.
Embakasi MP David Mwenje and his Budalang'i counterpart Raphael Wanjala,
both of whom have been appointed assistant ministers, did not attend
yesterday's ceremony because they are out of the country on Parliamentary
duties. Both MPs are members of the Parliamentary Select Committee on
Security.
Addressing the newly-sworn in ministers, the President said: "You should
work from the positions you have taken today whole-heartedly and
efficiently. Kenyans want development and they are looking up to you. We
have already done well and the economy is improving. Kenyans are
hard-working and if you do your job well, you will have assisted them a
great deal."
And the President revisited the constitutional review issue, saying that
those with suggestions should table them for discussion instead of talking
from the sidelines.
"We have debated this constitutional issue for long and whatever remains
to be done should be done quickly because Kenyans want and need it. It's
time to work hard, not time for too much talk. You have been involved in
this process from the start and whatever needs to be straightened out must
be done now," he said.
Newly-appointed Defence minister Njenga Karume also dismissed calls for
a snap election, saying those seeking leadership should wait until 2007 when
President Kibaki's first term expires.
"We don't need a snap election. President Kibaki is in control of his
Government," he said, and went on to criticise German ambassador Bernd Braun
for what he said was meddling in Kenya's internal affairs.
Also dimissing calls for early polls was the new Roads minister, Mr
Simeon Nyachae, who said powers to declare elections were vested only in the
President.

Original_Search_Title: New Ford-K Cabinet Jobs Welcomed
Headline: Kenya: New Ford-K Cabinet Jobs Welcomed
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki's award of three more Cabinet posts to Ford Kenya has
been praised in Western Province.
Leaders say it is a recognition of the party's support for the
Government since 2003.
But they described it as "belated and long overdue", saying it should
not have taken pressure from the Ford Kenya leadership to make the
decision.
Kakamega mayor Poyonchi Kunyobo praised party chairman Musikari Kombo
for the three new ministerial seats and urged party members to rally behind
him.
Accompanied by Kakamega county council chairman Samwel Mwanzi, he urged
Ford Kenya MPs, who opposed the Banana camp during the referendum campaign,
to return to the fold and strengthen the party.
"As civic leaders, we know Kombo is sure of our support, but we ask
party MPs to support him," said Mr Mwanzi.
Former Cabinet minister Cyrus Jirongo welcomed the new Cabinet slots,
saying negotiations and bargaining were the basis of coalition
governments.
He said Mr Kombo was right in declining to take up his seat, since
"coalition governments are about bargaining for posts".
In Bungoma, Knut executive secretary Fred Sichangi and branch
vice-chairman Ken Nganga said incessant political feuds should cease to
usher in issue-based politics geared towards economic transformation of the
region.
In Budalang'i, news of the elevation of area MP Raphael Wanjala was
received with joy as residents celebrated in Port Victoria Town.

Original_Search_Title: Coalition Card Helps Kombo Carry the Day
Headline: Kenya: Coalition Card Helps Kombo Carry the Day
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Ford Kenya leader Musikari Kombo has finally pushed for recognition of
the fundamental principle of coalition governments the world over -
bargaining and negotiations.
Mr Kombo achieved the feat of getting President Kibaki to recognise that
he could no longer take his coalition partners for granted.
Instead of going to the political extreme of severing links with the
ruling coalition, he negotiated additional seats for his party with the
President.
Now Ford Kenya, the predominant party in Western Province and parts of
North Rift, has seven Cabinet slots - Mr Kombo (Local Government), Dr
Mukhisa Kituyi (Trade and Industry), Dr Newton Kulundu (Labour), Mr Soita
Shitanda (Housing), Dr Noah Wekesa (Science and Technology), Mr John Munyes
(Special Programmes) and Mr Moses Akaranga (Public Service).
In addition, it has six assistant ministerial posts held by Mr Moses
Wetang'ula (Foreign Affairs), Mr Ekwee Ethuro (Planning), Dr Bonny Khalwale
(East African Affairs), Dr Enock Kibunguchy (Health) and Mr Raphael Wanjala
(Water Resources). Matungu MP David Were (Information) is yet to take his
post.
After President Kibaki named his reconstituted Cabinet, Ford Kenya felt
that it had been short-changed in the new Cabinet. Its MPs declined to take
their positions, and party boss Kombo, joined by Mrs Charity Ngilu of
National Party of Kenya, entered marathon negotiations at State House until
President Kibaki gave in to their demands and awarded more Cabinet slots to
his coalition partners.
Looked at against the manner in which President Kibaki has treated the
other members of the Narc coalition, it was a significant climb-down from
State House.
Unlike the LDP, Ford Kenya did not accept the seats and then start
grumbling from within and constitute itself into an internal opposition; it
simply stood up on principle and declared it would not take up Cabinet
positions until its demands were met.
This was an admirable standpoint at first glance. But it also exposed
the naked horse-trading, blackmail and ethnic politics that prevails in
Kenya.
There are those who will say that what Ford Kenya achieved amounted to
formalising cronyism. When it next comes to sharing the spoils under a
coalition government, it might well be official that each party is entitled
to not just a given number of ministers and assistant ministers, but also
permanent secretaries, provincial and district commissioners, ambassadors,
parastatal bosses and so on.
There is no telling what sort of havoc this will cause in a civil
service machinery that is supposed to be strictly non-partisan.
The situation is made even more bizarre by the ethnic nature of Kenyan
politics. Ford Kenya, for instance, was not just demanding positions for the
party; it was demanding positions for the Luyia! That is why they seized on
the removal of permanent secretary Rachel Arunga to illustrate a supposed
injustice against the party.
However, as Mr Kombo savours his triumph at the national level, one
question begs: Will it percolate down to the party's strongholds and rally
members of the party around him?
Now that President Kibaki has yielded to Mr Kombo's demands, it has, in
a way, given him an edge over his political competitors in the party.
Says Kakamega mayor Ponyochi Kunyobo: "Mr Kombo has proved that, as
chairman, he is not selfish and did not fear to negotiate with President
Kibaki over additional slots."
The move, argues Mr Kunyobo, has strengthened the Webuye MP's standing
as a leader who will not pursue his own interests or those of his native
Bungoma District. "If he had taken his own seat without thinking about the
broader interests of the party, he could have weakened his grasp. But now,
he is a step ahead," he said.
Readers may recall that a day after leading Western Province to give 48
per cent of the Banana votes in the November 21 referendum, a group of Ford
Kenya officials accused Mr Kombo of turning the party into "a clan club" and
started pushing for grassroots elections.
Led by organising secretary Jael Mbogo, they formed an interim committee
to coordinate the elections within six months.
The group had the support of Dr Kituyi, who is considered to be Mr
Kombo's chief rival in Ford Kenya.
Election timetable
Mr Kombo also led other party MPs to a retreat in Mombasa where they
agreed on grassroots and national election timetable.
Come the new Cabinet appointments, and Dr Kituyi, Mr Akaranga and Dr
Kulundu took up the posts but Mr Kombo declined arguing that the party had
been short-changed.
The Wednesday appointments could, viewed in retrospect, mean that Mr
Kombo has turned the tables against his opponents in the party.
However, former Cabinet minister Cyrus Jirongo, Mr Kunyobo and Kakamega
county council chairman Samwel Mwanzi are in agreement on one thing: that Mr
Kombo and dissenting party MPs need to end their wrangles.
"They should sit down and close ranks and I urge Mr Kombo to be more
accommodative," Mr Jirongo said.
He also said well coordinated grassroots and national elections once
held, would give the party new impetus.
Politician Eugene Wamalwa said Mr Kombo should take credit for the way
he led his team in negotiations with the President.
But he also urged Kanu vice-chairman Chris Okemo and former
vice-president Musalia Mudavadi to work with Mr Kombo to achieve Luyia
unity.

Original_Search_Title: Ldp: Kibaki Will Pay Price for Ignoring Us
Headline: Kenya: Ldp: Kibaki Will Pay Price for Ignoring Us
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Liberal Democratic Party has promised to make President Kibaki pay the price for ignoring the majority and choosing to run a crippled minority government.
The party said it was totally unacceptable for the President to lock out people who vigorously campaigned for him in the 2002 General Election when he was in hospital.
Party chairman, David Musila, said a government that defies the free will of the electors couldn't claim any legitimacy to govern them.
"We are telling Kibaki that he cannot afford to ignore this impasse and assume all is well with his administration. We are saddened by his actions," Musila said.
Speaking in Migwani market in his Mwingi South constituency during Jamhuri Day celebrations, the MP said it was absurd to see losers in the concluded constitution referendum being appointed to ministerial positions.
Musila, who is the Deputy Speaker, said the Orange Democratic Movement would step up its campaign to replace President Kibaki with "a person who can cope with reality and ability to resolve crises in the country" when the next general polls are called.
The MP said LDP was no longer interested in Cabinet positions, but in sensitising Kenyans to pick effective and responsive leaders in 2007.
Other LDP luminaries also said the President was running away from political realities, which he would have to face one way or another.
By refusing to sit at table and dialogue, the party said, the Head of State was only postponing an issue that he will have to confront.
Raila Odinga, Kalonzo Musyoka, Anyang' Nyong'o and William ole Ntimama said ODM was strong and enjoyed a majority following and cannot simply be wished away by the President.
The immediate former ministers, sacked by the President when he dissolved his Cabinet, were speaking at the party's Christmas fete at the headquarters in Lavington on Tuesday night.
Kalonzo said the team in the current Government was wishing that the Orange team would break up.
"They are praying that the Orange team breaks up. We will stand together," he added.
The team threatened to force a general election next year as the President would not call a snap election.
Nyong'o said: "Some people are running away from the realities on the ground. The only thing Kenyans have forgotten is Bananas. Orange is the true future of Kenya any time the whistle is blown."

Original_Search_Title: FDC Celebrate As DP, UPC Get Disappointed
Headline: Uganda: FDC Celebrate As DP, UPC Get Disappointed
Body_Text:
Kampala — AS nomination fever gripped the country, supporters of nine of the 18 candidates who were booked for nomination yesterday converged at points scheduled for public rallies.
At Nakivubo Stadium, the Forum for Democratic Change (FDC) supporters boldly marked out their territory.
Supporters sporting FDC T-shits with an imprint of their leader Col. Dr Kizza Besigye's portrait brought business around to a standstill.
Chanted slogans
They chanted slogans and flashed the V sign as party officials arrived at Nakivubo at 2;30 pm, an hour after Besigye had been pronounced a duly nominated presidential candidate.
The stadium's perimeter wall was decorated with banana stems, Besigye's campaign posters, and banners reading 'President 2006- 2011', 'Vote Besigye for a change', and 'Tired of Dictatorship'.
Elsewhere, the Democratic Party (DP) and Uganda Peoples' Congress (UPC) supporters were the most disappointed.
UPC supporters, who had converged at Kyadondo Rugby Ground, were thrown in disarray on learning that their presidential candidate, Mama Miria Obote, had not been nominated. "Due to a few technical problems, we have not been able to get our candidate nominated, but we hope to do so tomorrow (today) therefore we ask all of you to come back tomorrow same time (4:45 pm)," the UPC National Youth leader, Mr Benson Ogwang, told about 100 crestfallen supporters sporting UPC's traditional colours. They started walking away dejectedly.
The dinner scheduled for the launch of Miria's manifesto at Hotel Equatoria was also cancelled.
Plea to scribes
Kampala District UPC Chairman Bruhani Sekibenga pleaded with journalists not to give the party negative publicity.
Sekibenga said: "I am asking journalists not to talk to whoever you see here because they will give you wrong information."
At the Constitutional Square, DP's supporters started arriving at 3:00pm, the exact time their candidate showed up for nomination at Namboole.
The turn out was low however and police were heavily deployed at the venue.
Green and white gowns were very conspicuous as smiling supporters hugged and mixed.
However, the news that their presidential candidate, Mr John Ssebaana Kizito had not been nominated shocked them.
Silence descended on the supporters, who had been chanting party songs as they waited for their leader to address them after nomination.
Ssebaana arrived at the Constitution Square where he was scheduled to hold a big rally at 5:30 but looked crest fallen.
He was speechless although he flashed an occasional smile.
