"Museveni is now trying to fail our only hopeful candidate. But we shall carry on with the fight because he taught us Muchaka-muchaka (paramilitary training)," said Mr Church Ambrose, a DP supporter, who was at the podium.
Ssebaana however, urged his supporters to remain calm saying he still had hope to be nominated. He said he will raise the required signatures and be in time for today's nomination.
However, overall, the situation was calm in town during the day.

Original_Search_Title: Magid's Magic Lives On
Headline: Uganda: Magid's Magic Lives On
Body_Text:
Kampala — Such was the contradictory life of a man so nice and yet so mad, so gifted and yet so flawed, so down to earth and yet so wild, that an immortal has turned out to be mortal after all, with the death of Magid Musisi.
When he breathed his last on Tuesday night, Musisi became only the latest sporting genius whose extravagant and reckless ways should serve as example to youths about how not to live.
Ironic that tragedy should strike here on the back of the death of George Best, but Uganda has had its own long list of those, prominent among them Musisi's former teammates Godfrey Kateregga and Paul Hasule.
But I am not going to join the multitudes of moralists condemning Musisi's lifestyle, not today.
Even monks die. And yet not even the monks in a particularly rebellious monastery (if ever there was one), put together, would indulge in half the excesses Musisi managed in his short life.
However, having watched almost every single step in the man's journey to becoming a legend, and having actually been quite close at one time, I choose to celebrate Magic Magid's magical life the way I prefer to remember him; as the outrageously talented footballer Uganda will probably never have again.
My fascination with the man was such that every single goal he scored, every little touch, every single twist and turn on the pitch between 1986 and 1992 was imprinted on my mind and the memories are as vivid as if they were as recent as the night of his passing.
Which is why, when everybody was raving on about his incredible first minute strike against Iwuanyanwu at Nakivubo and his tie-clinching header away to the Nigerians in the 1991 African Club Champions Cup (now the Champions League), I was looking to another goal as confirmation that Musisi had cemented his place among the greats of all time.
The goal was against little-known Tanzanian side Pamba in the first round of Africa's top club competition that same season.
Villa were underdogs in all the other ties against El Merrieck, El Ahly, Iwuanyanwu and Club Africain as, in a feat of historic proportions, they unbelievably reached the final.
But they were favourites to beat Pamba in this potential banana skin, and the nerves were calmed by a wonder strike from a man whose goals were so much taken for granted at the time that very few will recall.
On one of his customary forays down the right in this game at Nakivubo, Hasule swung a low cross into the area where Musisi had just enough time to shake off a defender, dive to grass level and redirect the ball into the bottom corner of the net in amazing fashion.
At the time, he spotted a long-in-the-middle-but-nothing-on-the-sides haircut made famous by one of the crewmembers of then top rap group Kid 'N Play. But he could not have made more than just a hair's breadth of contact with the ball!
Wonder goal though it was, it was just one of hundreds in 'my collection' from the man since my 'love affair' with him had begun years earlier.
Watching him metamorphose from a striker who used brute force in 1986 and '87 to one who developed the subtleties of a complete player by the time he left for professional football in 1992, I was never in doubt that I was blessed to have seen the great man from close quarters.
As an S.3 student in Masaka in 1988, I remember using up what was left of my 'pocket money' to travel to Kampala aboard a Gaso Bus to watch a Uganda Cup semifinal clash between Villa and Express.
Then Express coach Robert Kiberu (RIP) had promised that the Red Eagles' frustrating losing streak was coming to an end that day and the stadium was so full there was barely enough air for all to share.
With Express playing well enough to win, up stepped (or rather down sat) Musisi to cruelly end the contest. Having failed to make contact with another vintage Hasule cross from the right and lost his footing in the process, Musisi was literally sitting in the Express box when Sula Kato swung the ball back from the left. But the man who at the time deserved the nickname Tyson in more ways than one, still managed to stick out a toe to send the ball into the net for the sucker punch that turned out to be the knockout blow.
As I sat in those infamous Masaka Road Peugeots on my way back to school, I was torn between a sadness for Express' plight and an elation over another piece of Musisi's magic that it didn't matter that I was totally broke and risked missing the after-chapel roll call which I was supposed to conduct as the assistant head prefect at St. Charles Lwanga Kasasa.
I had been sitting on the Express side of the Kirussia that day, but years later I would get to watch Musisi from much closer quarters.
Being a 'villagemate' to Musisi and his teammates in Najjanankumbi and inevitably becoming friends, I was convinced to join them at Villa Park after my S.6 exams at St. Mary's College Kisubi in 1992.
At Villa Park, I was left impressed when then coach Timothy Ayiekoh lambasted strikers Iddi Batambuze, Sam Mukasa and the rest, saying it was a shame that Musisi still arrived at training earlier and put in more work than them yet he was due to leave for France and already possessed a lot more in his armoury than they would ever have.
In an attempt to prepare the team for his imminent departure, Ayiekoh banished Musisi to the second team (referred to as Kakakuona after Tanzania's B team), leaving Batambuze and Mukasa to forge a partnership in the first II, meaning that I partnered Musisi in the second team.
Our Kakakuona team almost always beat the first team and Musisi, without saying much, told me and showed me things that would have made me a good striker but for fate and my inadequacies.
Rather than think about the enhancement of my career though, I was more worried about Musisi making a mark in France with Stade Rennes that, without ever telling him of course, I used to watch him closely to see if he was preparing himself well.
Imagine how pleasantly surprised I was when, while at a party in Najjanamkumbi one evening, Musisi jokingly said to me: " Mark, my coat stinks."
He was dressed in this knee-deep jacket and the 'stench' he was referring to was from a beer bottle concealed in one of the inside pockets.
The shock for me was not that he should have a beer, but that he should (matter-of-factly) construct a grammatically correct English sentence complete with the right tenses! Like he had done with his football, watching, copying and practising all the tricks he saw on videos of Marco van Basten, other European players and even a talented striker from Burundi's Vital'O, Musisi had quietly gone about learning English as well!
That year, Musisi ended up in the top 10 of the FIFA International Goal Getters Competition, finishing just behind George Weah as the only two Africans in an elite list including the likes of van Basten, Jurgen Klinsmann and Jean Pierre Papin.
Although he broke Ugandan transfer records and scored enough goals to finish top scorer at three different European clubs (Rennes and Turkish sides Bursaspor and Dardanelspor), I believe his abilities deserved better and that better luck and a better agent could have got him to a bigger club and Uganda might have boasted an African Footballer Of the Year; he was that good.
After being on the same list as Weah, their careers took different paths.
While Musisi fell in the hands of a an unappreciative coach who said the Ugandan should have scored more goals despite being top scorer, Weah went under the tutelage of the extremely patient Arsene Wenger who stuck with him when everybody else laughed at his poor first touch and raw, unrefined style.
So, as Weah was going places and being named World Footballer Of the Year among other things, Musisi's European adventure was ending as Dardanelspor was relegated to a lower league in which foreigners were not allowed to play.
Back home a while later, dejected, broke and with his personally life in shambles, Musisi met me at Anger Noir one night and said: "Mark nawe wanelabila? Did you also forget about me?"
He was already ailing then and I thought he should not have another drink or be out that late. But he was stubborn and when he insisted on having a warm Guinness, I reluctantly obliged.
That question was not just to me, somebody with whom he shared more than just those evening walks in Najjankumbi with Robert Ssemakula, Adam Semugabi and Charles Ssimbwa back in the day.
It was a plea to the entire country and prompted me to act for the fondness of my memories, for the patriotism in me as well as the guilt.
When I teamed up with Nick Cavell, now at the BBC, to help Transworld Sport do a profile on him in 2000, I thought it was the least I could do.
When I started the Mark My Word column almost two years ago, I had him in mind and wrote as much in the first piece.
When I made a plea to Michael Ezra to start a Hall Of Fame after listening to a speech from USPA founder Fred Sekitto, I had Musisi in mind.
And when I called on our clubs and federation to introduce the culture of testimonial matches, I was thinking about him.
My single-minded support for the revival of Ugandan football and my involvement with groups like NUFFA owes a lot to him, and I was part of the NUFFA meeting at which it was decided that he should get the financial support he did, however late.
He was the first Ugandan to score 100 league goals. He became synonymous with the brush-headed goal in telepathic tandem with provider Sula Kato, and his goals would have taken Uganda to one or two African Nations Cup finals but for club politics.
It wasn't just the numbers however, it was the significance of the goals that will keep them forever in the memory, from the left-footed winner against Zaire in 1991 which led to a collapse of part of the Kirussia as fans celebrated, to the hat-trick against Rwanda in a Nations Cup preliminary game in 1998.
He was one of the greatest that ever lived, arguably the best striker Uganda ever had.
Like it is with all legends, the comparisons will continue. But there will never be another like him, not for me.

Original_Search_Title: Middle Ground Group Calls for Dialogue
Headline: Kenya: Middle Ground Group Calls for Dialogue
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The middle-ground group of MPs is spearheading efforts to initiate reconciliation to save the country from a political crisis.
A leader of the group, Joseph Nyagah, yesterday said the team had convened meetings to salvage the country from further crisis.
"We will continue preaching dialogue between political leaders as we are convinced that this is the only way forward. This is what we were calling for prior to the referendum, but no one gave us an ear," he said on phone.
The Gachoka MP said the group was working with religious leaders and other organisations on how to secure peace to pursue the country's economic agenda.
The MP quit the middle ground for the Banana camp after the group's calls for further dialogue hit a snag.
"The current wrangles in the country are what the middle ground was attempting to evade by calling for dialogue to iron out contentious issues in the proposed constitution before the referendum," Nyagah said.
"We saw the potential of the current crisis if we took the draft constitution to the people without a general consensus. Unfortunately, we were dismissed," he said.
The former diplomat said he would continue championing for dialogue amongst leaders.
He said he was encouraged to see Ford Kenya chairman Musikari Kombo embrace discussion.
Nyagah said he declined his ministerial post last week since the country was too polarized.
The MP, who had been reappointed as the East African Co-operation Assistant minister, declined the post, saying accepting it would compromise his push for dialogue as many would view him as partisan.
But he said he still supported President Kibaki's government and his rejection of the position should not be considered as rebellion.
"All what I want to do is play the role of a peace-maker, especially now that the country is in a crisis," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Profiles of Newly Named Ministers
Headline: Kenya: Profiles of Newly Named Ministers
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Following are the profiles of the Cabinet and Assistant ministers appointed by President Kibaki yesterday.
Dr Noah Makhalang'ang'a Wekesa, the new Minister for Science and Technology, was formerly Assistant Minister for Fisheries.
Dr Wekesa contested for Ford-Kenya chairmanship, coming third after Trade minister Mukhisa Kituyi and winner Musikari Kombo in 2004.
The Kwanza MP had served in the same post during the Kanu regime.
During the referendum campaigns, Wekesa was among the MPs who walked the middle path, only to join the Banana camp later.
And the new Minister for Environment and Natural Resources,
Prof Kivutha Kibwana, often found himself on the receiving end as police battled with protesters during the clamour for multi-partyism. The Makueni MP was then a co-convenor at NCEC.
The former assistant minister in the Office of the President was accused of bias during the Bomas conference.
Kibwana, who handled the powerful Provincial Administration and National Security docket, which is responsible for the police force, has been criticised of ignoring the rule of law by inciting squatters.
A former University of Nairobi lecturer and member of the Third Progressive Force, he at one time forcefully moved to repossess land allegedly grabbed in Sultan Hamud.
Malava MP Soita Shitanda, the new Minister for Housing, has been strongly censured for leading other Ford-Kenya operatives in keeping the Liberal Democratic Party out of Western Kenya.
He once turned the heat on Luhya ministers whom he accused of working against the interests of the community.
Mr Munyes John Kinyonga, the new Minister for Special Programmes, takes over from President Kibaki's long time ally, Njenga Karume, who moves to the newly created Ministry of Defence.
Born in 1966, he was elected the MP for Turkana North on a Narc ticket in 2002. In 2003, he was appointed an assistant minister for Water Resources, Management and Development.
Ms Cecily Mbarire, a Nominated MP, got involved in politics for the first time as a student at Egerton University.
Mbarire, now the assistant minister for Youth Affairs, became the chairman of the university's student union in 1994. She holds a Bachelors degree in Economics and Sociology and a postgraduate diploma in Gender and Development Studies from the University of Nairobi.
Dr Bonnie Khalwale, the Ikolomani MP, a medical practitioner, is the new assistant minister for East African Community.
Ms Adelina Ndeto Mwau is the new assistant minister for Manpower Development. She is a Nominated MP. The Master of Arts in Development Studies gender activist had served as the Regional Programme Officer of Women in Law and Development in Harare.
Mr Raphael Wanjala, the new Assistant Minister for Water Resource Management, is an untrained teacher. He joined politics in 2003.
Wanjala had a dream of becoming a councillor but found himself in Parliament in 1997 after his party failed to nominate a candidate to vie for the seat.

Original_Search_Title: Raila Lays Blame On President
Headline: Kenya: Raila Lays Blame On President
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Liberal Democratic Party leader Raila Odinga yesterday questioned
President Kibaki's ability to resolve the current political crisis facing
the country.
He said the President was out of touch with the reality in the country
judging from the appointment of the new Cabinet and the more than 20
resignations that followed.
President Kibaki's failures, Mr Odinga said, had led the country into
the current political crisis and must now dissolve Parliament and call for
snap elections. "On a day during which we celebrate our having ended
colonial rule through decades of resistance and sacrifice, the joy has been
replaced with bewilderment, anger and deep concern about the ability of the
President to shepherd the people out of a deep political crisis brought
about by his own failures," he added.
In a stinging attack on President Kibaki, Mr Odinga described the new
Cabinet as full of sycophants and lacking in legitimacy. "Twice in a
fortnight, President Kibaki revealed how profoundly out of touch he is with
the country's needs and desires," said Mr Odinga.
"The referendum's results had showed how badly he is out of touch with
reality. The subsequent Cabinet announcement was a stunning indication that
he had no clue on what it takes to put together a government acceptable to
the Kenyan people," he added.
Mr Odinga described President Kibaki's recent decisions as catastrophic,
adding they could not be left unchallenged. The Lang'ata MP who was
addressing a Press conference at the Serena Hotel said LDP was no longer
interested in Cabinet positions but on giving the country a chance to pick
effective leaders.
He said what President Kibaki was doing was "gambling and playing
politics of survival". Although he called for the reconstitution of the
Cabinet, LDP would not take positions in it.
He added: "We found our Cabinet experience very painful and are actually
happy not to be bearing the burden of heavy responsibilities without having
the authority to implement the mandates we were given at elections."
Mr Odinga said ministers had little control over their ministries and
were "small cogs" in a government which was dominated by State House
officials and the secretary to the Cabinet, Mr Francis Muthaura. Mr
Muthaura, he added, directed senior ministry officials to carry out
functions and policies without reference to the ministers. However, there
were exceptions - some "favoured" ministers were free to run their outfits
as they deem fit.
He cited Finance minister David Mwiraria as one who was authorised to
approve expenditures unlike the rest. He wondered why Mr Mwiraria was
retained the Cabinet, when he had admitted having authorised what became the
Anglo Leasing scandal.
However, Mr Odinga said the President should meet all political leaders
to discuss how snap elections could be held. He described the Jamhuri Day
celebrations as "dark and anxious" moments for Kenyans.
Instead of addressing the crisis in the country, Mr Odinga said
President Kibaki only enumerated Government programmes already known to the
public, instead of explaining how he was going to pull the country out of
the political crisis.
The Head of State's speech at Jamhuri Day celebrations at Nyayo Stadium
and the creation of a "hodge-podge Cabinet", Mr Odinga added, was tantamount
to changing of political partners
It was also another indication that the President had not grasped the
magnitude of the challenge that faced him and the nation.
He described Mr Kibaki as a minority President managing a minority
Government.
Mr Odinga said the National Rainbow Coalition was dead in June last year
when the President decided to include Opposition MPs from Kanu and Ford
People in government.
The inclusion of Kanu and Ford People MPs, he added, was meant to
protect the President from his refusal to implement the Memorandum of
Understanding between LDP and the National Alliance (Party) of Kenya.
"Indeed, even in early 2003, the writing was on the wall when the forces
allied to him opposed my nomination as chairman of the Parliamentary Select
Committee on the Constitution and voted instead for Mr Paul Multe - whose
party had opposed Narc in the elections!"
In addition, the administration failed to enact a new Constitution,
punish corruption, create jobs and constitute an inclusive government based
on merit.
Failures of the Government, he added, were reflected in the recent
referendum vote that saw the Orange team get 3.5 million to Banana's 2.5
million.
Mr Odinga hinted that now that LDP was out of the Government, its
members would spill the beans on issues they learnt in the Kibaki
Government. T
He was accompanied at the press conference by MPs Anyang' Nyong'o,
Ochilo Ayacko, Charles Keter, Omingo Magara and Reuben Ndolo.
Mr Odinga's statement was titled: Jamhuri Day 2005 statement on Kenya's
future by Hon Raila Odinga, Party leader LDP. He said the statement would be
sent to the President. Prof Nyong'o warned: "If he [President] does not hear
or see like some animals, he will land in a hole."
Mr Odinga said Narc no longer enjoyed the people's support, which was
now firmly on the Orange Democratic Movement. He added: "We are the
torchbearers of the Narc dream, and we are determined to fulfil that
dream. The ODM has inherited Narc's mantle and mandate and its popularity of
2002."
Mr Odinga said the President and his "coterie of hardline political
advisers" had shown that they were completely out of touch with the people's
desires.
He called for the reconvening of Parliament, which was the only body
that could help the President unlock the present crisis.
Said he: "I want to state emphatically today that I am ready and willing
to propose to my colleagues in the LDP and in the Orange Democratic Movement
that we agree to such a dialogue."
And in an apparent reference to Ford Kenya and National Party of Kenya's
demands for more seats in Government, Mr Odinga said it was
embarrassing. The parties and the President did not care about the issues
that concerned Kenyans.
He called for an overhaul of the civil service, which he said was
dysfunctional.
Mr Odinga listed key issues to be discussed with the President as the
Constitution, corruption, employment and inclusive governance.
He said the Goldenberg report had not been released, Anglo Leasing
scandal cases had been dismissed while the Kroll Associates report - on
recovery of billions stashed abroad - was shelved.
Mr Odinga dismissed as hogwash, the thinking by people in Central Kenya
that the whole country had united against them. He said the Kibaki
presidency had split the country.

Original_Search_Title: Orange Team Sits On Terraces
Headline: Kenya: Orange Team Sits On Terraces
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Orange Democratic Movement leaders squeezed themselves between members
of the public on the Nyayo Stadium terraces to follow President Kibaki's
Jamhuri Day speech.
Eldoret North MP, William Ruto, and former Cabinet minister, Prof
Anyang' Nyong'o, said they sat on the terraces to show that all citizens
were important and should be treated with respect. They entered the stadium
through the VIP entrance and walked to the western terraces close to Gate
Two.
After the function, they dismissed the President's pledge to facilitate
consultation and establish a legal framework for achieving a new
constitution.
"People gave their views to CKRC, there is no need of delaying the
process and that is why we say the speech was nothing but lip service," said
Ruto.
They asked President Kibaki and his handlers to respect the Constitution
and the will of the majority. The only way forward, they said, was for the
Banana team to meet with the Orange group to chart the way forward.
MPs Ochillo Ayacko, Billow Kerrow, Prof Anyang' Nyong'o and William Ruto
sit with ordinary Kenyans during the celebrations.
"What does the President mean by saying he respects the Constitution and
the rule of law when he goes poaching MPs from the opposition to include
into his Cabinet?" asked Ruto.
He threatened to ask the Speaker to initiate action against Kanu members
serving in the Cabinet.
The Orange leaders also criticised the President's call for respect of
divergent views in the completion of the constitution review process, saying
it was a mockery of Kenyans' intelligence because ministers who expressed
divergent views were kicked out of Government.
They said the President's remarks that the newly created Ministry for
Youth Affairs will address their interests were laughable.
"We ask the youth to remember they were promised 500,000 jobs that have
never been realised and judge for themselves the necessity of a ministry,"
he said.
Ruto, Nyong'o, Ochillo Ayacko, Billow Kerrow, Omingo Magara and Joseph
Lagat were cheered wildly as they left from the stadium.
They were mobbed by youth who blocked traffic chanting, Uchaguzi! and
Chungwa!
In the confusion, many vehicles, including those carrying state guests
and security personnel, were delayed in getting out of the stadium.
Magara, who arrived late, was denied access to the area reserved for MPs
and made a phone call to locate his Orange colleagues. Only six other
backbench MPs, G.G Kariuki, Arthur Magugu, Katoo ole Metito, Maalim
Mohammed, Murue Karue and Kivutha Kibwana turned up for the function.

Original_Search_Title: Legalweek - Review Acts Banning Cock And Bull Fights
Headline: Kenya: Legalweek - Review Acts Banning Cock And Bull Fights
Body_Text:
Nairobi — You have to give it to residents of Western Kenya. While the rest of the country was going bananas and oranges, the residents did not forget the simple things in life.
The sport of bullfighting in Western Kenya is legendary. Huge animals fight it out in an arena to the ululation of hundreds of spectators. At that moment when "Terminator" was crushing his opponents, all political, legal and personal differences were forgotten.
The sport is without doubt highly entertaining, with Ikolomani MP Bonny Khalwale stating it should be promoted as a tourist attraction to the Western circuit.
Besides bullfighting amongst Isukha and Idakho sub-tribes of Western Kenya, cockfighting is also a crowd puller. Cocks, like their domesticated counterparts, slug it out in the arena. People bet on the winner while its owner is praised.
Usually, the animal that loses dies. Bulls that are critically injured in the arena are slaughtered immediately.
When Bonny Khalwale was agitating for the sport as a tourist attraction, he must have had in mind similar sport in Spain and the cockfights of Latin America, the Philippines, Vietnam and Mexico.
The MP, however, should quickly check the statute books. His constituents are in breach of Chapter 360 of the Laws of Kenya, namely the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act.
Like many Acts of Parliament still in force in Kenya (let alone the Constitution), the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act should be looked at again in relation to sport. This is line with long standing cultural aspects of entertainment which have a high potential for tourism and revenue creation.
Currently, the spectacular sport is illegal. Section 4 of the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals (Cap 360) stipulates that a person who, a) causes, promotes or assists at the fighting or baiting of an animal; keeps, uses, manages, acts or assists in the management or, premises or place to be so kept, managed or used; or receives, or causes or procures any person to receive, money for the admission of any person to premises kept or used for the purposes, or partly for the purpose, of fighting or baiting an animal, shall be guilty of an offence and liable to a fine not exceeding Sh3,000 or to a term of imprisonment not exceeding six months, or to both.
Definitely, the entertaining scenes of Terminator vanquishing his foes are illegal. But the illegality does not end here, section 11(1) of the same Act prohibits the broadcasting of films, cinemas or any kind of broadcasting where animals are seen to be inflicting pain, either to each other or to, or by, a person, or through the instigation of someone.
Bullfighting and cockfighting are not unique to Western Kenya. In the Philippines, cockfighting or "sabong", as it is known in the local vernaculars, is not only a sport but a lifestyle and a national pastime.
And in Mexico, although extremely popular, cockfighting is illegal ... with specific legislation banning it. But that does not lessen its popularity, albeit in the back streets and alleys.
Whether the famous bull fights of Ikolomani are illegal, a critical look at our laws, and what we want to achieve with them, is important.

Original_Search_Title: Donors Unhappy With New Cabinet, Says German Envoy
Headline: Kenya: Donors Unhappy With New Cabinet, Says German Envoy
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki was ill-advised in forming the new Cabinet, German
ambassador Bernd Braun said on Sunday.
Braun said donors were disappointed with the President for scrapping the
office of the Permanent Secretary for Ethics and Governance.
"We don't want to interfere with internal politics of the country, but
the President has to correct the situation," said the envoy.
He said the fact that 20 Cabinet ministers and their assistants had
declined their appointments showed lack of confidence in the President.
German Ambassador Bernd Braun chats with Deoek Fee of the European
Delegation when they visited The Nest Children's Home in Limuru on
Sunday. Pic by Boniface Okendo
"The President had our confidence but after the Cabinet reshuffle, the
confidence is disappearing," he said.
Braun was speaking at The Nest Children's Home in Limuru during a
Christmas party.
Cabinet ministers who declined to take up their appointments are Ford
Kenya chairman Musikari Kombo, Charity Ngilu and Joshua Orwa Ojode. Others
were assistant ministers from the Liberal Democratic Party, Kanu and Ford
Kenya.
In naming the new Cabinet, President Kibaki also dropped Liberal
Democratic Party legislators Raila Odinga, Najib Balala, Ochillo Ayacko,
Kalonzo Musyoka and Prof Anyang' Nyong'o. Braun said that the Cabinet only
comprised some political parties.
"It's up to the President to decide. He left out the talents that could
have transformed the country into a working nation," he said.
Last week, 12 LDP leaders said the abolition of the post of the Ethics
Permanent Secretary was the most "egregious action" by the Head of
State.
Blow to graft fight
The MPs led by former Cabinet ministers Kalonzo Musyoka, Najib Balala,
William ole Ntimama, Ochillo Ayacko and Prof Anyango' Nyong'o were concerned
that the omission would compromise the fight on corruption.
The position was previously held by John Githongo, who resigned.
At a press conference at Parliament Buildings, the MPs in a statement
read by LDP chairman David Musila said the position had been scrapped and
yet the Goldernberg and Anglo-Leasing scandals remained still
unresolved.
Meanwhile, Maasai leaders regret supporting President Kibaki in the 2002
General Election, an MP said. Kajiado Central MP Maj-Gen (rtd) Joseph
Nkaisserry said his community felt like outsiders in the Government.
"We have been treated like outsiders in our motherland for the last
three years of Kibaki's regime. We have not benefited in any way from his
Government," Nkaisserry said.
The MP who spoke to The Standard on telephone, accused politicians in
the Kibaki Government of hatching a scheme to consign the Maasai community
to the political and economic periphery.
"After the community resolved to reject the Wako draft at the
referendum, greedy people surrounding the President cheated him that we love
him. But the reality is that he betrayed us," Nkaiasserry said.
He said Maasai leaders around the President failed to deliver votes to
the Banana side yet they misled the President on the community's political
stand.

Original_Search_Title: Appointments At the Core of Coalition Wrangles
Headline: Kenya: Appointments At the Core of Coalition Wrangles
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Two accusations continue to ring out against President Kibaki in
the wake of the Cabinet-selection debacle. One is that he has
deliberately mismanaged coalition politics. The other is that contrary
to the spirit of building coalitions, Mr Kibaki refuses to consult with
political party partners.
Actually the real complaint such as by LDP is that President Kibaki
has refused to accede to a collegial government, which was the implied
objective of the 2002 MoU. But to the Kibaki camp, some of the demands
are calculated to make a mockery of his prerogatives as President.
An example is being cited of the quarrel with Ford-Kenya. Actually,
at some point the party abandoned its insistence on the sacking of
Vice-President Moody Awori, well-informed sources say.
There were two possible reasons for this. The party honchos may have
realised that President Kibaki was not going to carry out that move,
knowing the kind of national fallout the dismissal of any
Vice-President portends. (Mr Kibaki himself has been the victim of such
a demotion). Besides, everybody acknowledged the VP had thrown
everything he could for the Banana campaign.
The other reason is that Mr Musikari Kombo's crowd came to
appreciate that sacking Mr Awori would only sharpen divisions among the
Luhya, which the Ford-K leader himself has been trying to heal with
little success.
The point, however, is what Ford-K reportedly turned to next. The
party made it clear to State House that it would take a dim view of the
situation if Mr Kombo's rival Dr Mukhisa Kituyi, and party renegade Dr
Newton Kulundu were reappointed. Specific names had been floated of who
should replace them.
That was something the Kibaki inner circle was not amused about.
What were a President's prerogatives, somebody in that circle asked, if
he can't appoint or hire the ministers of his choice?
In fact, the question of whether the President can appoint anybody
he wishes is at the core of his opponents' complaints. The coalition
partners, particularly the LDP, have all along insisted on
consultations on senior appointments. They have reminded the President
he got into office because of their collective efforts. It is no wonder
the LDP got outraged last year when President Kibaki appointed
ministers from Kanu and Ford-People.
Where President Kibaki's modus operandi looks muddled, that of his
two predecessors was clear. Mzee Kenyatta preferred to operate without
an over-arching political party. For Mzee, the provincial
administration was sufficient to keep everybody in check.
Errant politicians
Mr Moi, on the other hand, was a true believer in a strong Kanu
whose machinery he used against errant politicians. But Mr Kibaki's
attempts to turn Narc into something more than a mere electoral vehicle
have been blocked by his coalition partners. Grassroots elections
aborted twice after LDP and Ford-Kenya spurned the plan. And with the
prospect of LDP and Kanu joining hands in the Orange movement, Mr
Kibaki finds himself in a weak position.
Ford-K's withdrawal was undoubtedly a very huge blow to the
Government. It has reduced its support to the original DP and
Ford-People, essentially. (Safina is with him, too, but has no
appointee in the government). It is possible the President will sweet
talk Mrs Charity Ngilu (NPK) back into the fold. But to bring Mr Kombo
back will require some exacting commitments.
In other respects, President Kibaki has been operating like he has a
death wish, politically-speaking. Many Central Kenya MPs were openly
alarmed at the omission from the Cabinet of Mr Paul Muite. One minister
from the region who is close to the Kabete MP described him as "a real
asset who we need right now."
The omission is being seen as another example of how the Kibaki
inner circle has led him astray by allowing differences some of them
have with Mr Muite to colour their judgment.
Unless something calamitious happens, like a vote of no confidence
against him when Parliament resumes, it is a safe bet that the
President wants to stick it out in office until his term expires in
2007.

Original_Search_Title: Why President Retained 'Uncle Moody' As His VP
Headline: Kenya: Why President Retained 'Uncle Moody' As His VP
Body_Text:
Nairobi — His retention as Vice-President couldn't have been more comforting for Mr Moody Awori.
Smarting from a humiliating defeat in his Funyula backyard at the
November referendum, his sacking would have marked a major anticlimax
in a political career spanning a quarter-century.
The move would have heavily upset the MP who, at 78 and only a
heart-beat away from the presidency, is basking in political glory
after serving as an assistant minister for two decades. Rumours that he
would be replaced by Ford-Kenya chairman Musikari Kombo had been doing
the rounds, especially after the Government's dismal performance at the
constitution review poll in most areas of his home province of Western.
And only hours after being sworn in on Friday, Trade and Industry
minister Mukhisa Kituyi appeared to lend credence to the rumour by
accusing Mr Kombo of "obsession with the post of vice-president," a
claim that the Webuye MP has since denied.
Much as he failed to deliver the Banana vote in his constituency and
the Busia home district - just like most MPs retained in the Cabinet -
Mr Awori was rewarded most probably for his loyalty to the President.
During his two-year stint in office, the VP who endeared many by his
electrifying smiles and dances with prisoners across the country, has
proved to be an asset to the Government, President Kibaki in particular.
One of the busiest government officers at the moment, he has been
criss-crossing the country to represent his boss in presiding over
government and party functions, including parliamentary group meetings.
There are, however, a host of other credible factors that may have
contributed to his retention, key among them political stability.
The vice-presidency - the second highest political office in the
land - is a sensitive post and the notion of replacing its bearer is a
weighty matter. Moreover, replacing Mr Awori would have compromised, to
some degree, political stability and caused divisions among the
populous Luhya community.
Mr Awori, the oldest and most experienced elected MP in Western,
having served in the House since 1983, has an added advantage - his
association with some of the President's closest allies.
For instance, the Funyula MP enjoys a warm relationship with Kiambaa
MP and Special Programmes minister Njenga Karume, who is said to have
the President's ear and eye.
In fact, a week before his appointment to the Cabinet in September
2003, Mr Karume hosted Mr Awori at Gachie in his home turf during a
funds-drive in aid of a Catholic church, at which he praised his guest
for his leadership, saying that he hoped he would be appointed VP.
Further, it is unlikely that President wished to embarrass fellow septuagenarian, barely two years before the end of his term.
In any case, he did not fire anybody for not delivering the Banana vote at the referendum.
Unlike his predecessor, the late Mr Michael Wamalwa who negotiated
the slot after years of strategising from the opposition benches, Mr
Awori was a beneficiary also on account of his community and political
party.
By naming him VP instead of a Ford Kenya MP, President Kibaki's
calculation was doubtlessly that of killing two birds with one stone,
as it were.
It was thought that the appointment would help to consolidate the
Luhya support for Narc and the President and, at the same time, appease
an increasingly belligerent Liberal Democratic Party. In the first
case, being an MP from Busia, Mr Awori's promotion was expected to
mollify the Luhya who had "lost" the vice-presidency following the
death of Mr Wamalwa, also the Ford Kenya chairman, on August 23, 2003.
By opting for Mr Awori, who is technically allied to LDP, the
President may have hoped to soothe the Narc partners who were crying
foul over a dishonoured memorandum of understanding.
Although they voted against the stand of "Uncle Moody" at the
referendum, it seems as if the Busia people did not reject him as a
person.
Most of the people, especially in his Funyula backyard, simply cast
a protest vote against what they considered the Government's decision
to deny their man the wherewithal for their political, economic and
social development.
After all, had they not voted for him for 25 years?

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki, Listen to the Subversive Cartoon Texts
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki, Listen to the Subversive Cartoon Texts
Body_Text:
Nairobi — My study of editorial cartoons in the two major dailies in Kenya
since Narc took over leadership has revealed that the results of the
recently concluded referendum were after all not unexpected.
If the Banana proponents had paid attention to the subversive
cartoon texts appearing in the dailies then their hopes would not have
been too high.
My interest in editorial cartoon texts is a product of my
realisation that the power of the cartoon text lies in its perceived
harmlessness yet its visual signification of the absurdity and the
claptrap of the policies, decisions and utterances of leaders is lethal.
The semiotics of the cartoon texts appearing from January 2003 to
November 2005 went a long way in exposing the thoughtlessness of those
who later became the proponents of the Proposed Draft Constitution.
Editorial cartoons published within this period consistently
endeavoured to deconstruct political ineptitude and expedience that was
alienating the government from the people.
Cartoon texts of this period contain a variety of sub-texts that
reveal the absurd and bizarre political realities albeit humorously.
Interestingly, politicians do not pay attention to this powerful mode of communication.
That is why the cartoon text remains a safe way of presenting a critique of political expedience in Kenya.
Those in power, on which the cartoon texts thrive, dismiss them as
pure humour yet if there is any channel that predictably and accurately
presents public opinion then it is the cartoon texts. They defy the law
of sedition and libel in the way they construct reality without
rendering the artist easy prey to hawk eyed lawyers.
It is easy for those in power to remain presumptuous and not pay attention to editorial cartoons.
But it is unforgivable for scholars not to recognise the power of cartoon in academic discourse.
In the same vein it is foolhardy for the spin-doctors of those in
power not to take note of the signals of this communicative discourse.
It is of literary importance to theorise about the social processes
and structures, which give rise to the production of the cartoon texts
and their power to create meaning and influence public opinion.
Between January 2003 and November 2005 editorial cartoons have endeavoured to divulge the genesis of Narc leadership crisis.
The editorial cartoons appearing in this period fall in three categories.
Those appearing in the first four months of Narc's leadership, those
that appear between May 2003 to early 2005 and those that come from
then to November 2005.
Those appearing in the first three months of Narc's leadership mirror the hopes and aspirations of Kenyans at that time.
The second phase of cartoons shade doubt on the Kibaki
administration's ability to fulfil pledges his coalition made to the
people.
The rabbit that talks on behalf of Wanjiku becomes more vocal and critical of his style of conducting public affairs.
His handlers should have noticed the change in Wanjiku and advised him accordingly.
The third and final phase captures the disillusionment of the people
and depicts the President completely unable to manage the affairs of
the state.
Kiraitu Murungi and Raila Odinga often appear in the cartoons of this period fighting while the President appears unconcerned.
These fights culminated into the Banana and Orange duel.

Original_Search_Title: What Next for Nyachae's Presidential Ambitions?
Headline: Kenya: What Next for Nyachae's Presidential Ambitions?
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The appointment of Simeon Nyachae and a host of his party MPs to
the Cabinet have left many of the new Roads minister's supporters
wondering what became of their leader's presidential ambitions.
While the statures of West Mugirango MP Henry Obwocha and
Bamachoge's Joel Onyancha have been boosted among their constituents by
their appointment to the Planning and Sports dockets, their party
leader has many question marks that are bothering his supporters.
Newly appointed Roads and Public Works Minister Simeon Nyachae
played crucial role in the formation of the Cabinet by President Mwai
Kibaki.
Obwocha's credibility had waned among his constituents over the 15
years he has been in Parliament but his elevation is likely to help him
reclaim lost clout.
With the elevation to the key Planning ministry, he is widely viewed
as a potential successor to Nyachae both in Ford Kenya and in Gusii
politics.
It now means that political opponents of the 52-year-old Obwocha
will have to go back to the drawing board to work out a new game plan
to beat him in the next elections.
Onyancha, despite being a political greenhorn, is now the most
senior politician in Gucha District, which is also home to Bobasi's
Stephen Manoti and Ford People's renegade Omingo Magara.
His elevation was seen as a reward for delivering the Banana vote
from the larger Kisii despite strong opposition that saw heavyweights
among them Nyachae, lose in their constituencies.
Some political observers say that by accepting reappointment to the
Cabinet, Nyachae has boxed himself into a corner politically.
It will now be extremely hard for Nyachae to pull out of government
and go seeking votes for the presidency, especially if Kibaki opted to
run in 2007.
After emerging third in the last General Election, many expected the
Nyaribari Chache MP to stay in the opposition as Kibaki did despite
losing twice to former President Moi.
This way he would have used his influence to emerge as the alternative to the Kibaki administration.
Nyachae, who has often reminded his constituents that he is 73 years
old, will be 75 at the next elections, making him one of oldest MPs in
active politics.
Age will not be on his side if he decided to have a go at the presidency in 2007.
When the question of Nyachae's options was put to Onyancha, his answer was: "Let's cross that bridge when we get there."
But the Bomachoge MP believes that Nyachae still has a political future.
"The country's politics changes within hours. Future governments
will be formed purely through coalitions. As Ford People our options
are still open," he said Onyancha.
Obwocha agrees with Onyancha.
"We are still serving the country through a government of national
Unity. Our loyalty to the president is paramount but we will discuss
the way forward when the time comes."
Meanwhile, the sacking of James Ongwae as a Permanent Secretary
coupled with the failure to consider the abrasive Kitutu Chache MP
Jimmy Angwenyi for any post in the reconstituted Cabinet is likely to
affect the political scene in Gusiiland.
The fact that among those appointed to take up positions in the
civil service also happen to be relatives of Nyachae has complicated
the political situation in Kisii.
Four new PS appointments, Dr Bitange Ndemo, Dr Hezron Nyangito, Alice Kemunto Mayaka, and Onyancha are related to Nyachae.
While Ongwae's exit has dominated the public debate, little is being
said about Kitutu Masaba's Zackary Ogongo, who was a reserved operator
during his career as a DC and PC before being elevated to a PS.
Even before his exit, Ongwae was under pressure to try elective politics.
Seen by many as a respected, influential and approachable civil
servant, Ongwae's dismissal is likely to cause Nyachae and his team a
headache.
Ongwae is credited with assisting many people when he served in the Moi and Kibaki administrations.
Should he decide to vie against Angwenyi, the battle would be interesting.
Angwenyi's constituents are a bitter lot. One of the longest serving
Kisii MPs, having joined the august House in 1996, courtesy of a
by-election occasioned by the death of Dr Zachary Onyonka, Angwenyi
tried to seek consensus on the draft constitution together with
Chepalungu MP John Koech. While Koech was rewarded for his efforts,
Angwenyi was ignored.
Angwenyi, an ally of Nyachae, is yet to react to the changes with
reports indicating that he is out of the country until December 18.
But his supporters in Kisii are bitter that he ignored.
One fact that cannot be disputed is that Angwenyi would not go against Nyachae, at least now.

Original_Search_Title: Leaders Disagree On Cabinet Jobs
Headline: Kenya: Leaders Disagree On Cabinet Jobs
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Critics of President Kibaki's Cabinet appointments dug in their
heels yesterday as accusations of betrayal and opportunism surfaced.
In Machakos town, former Health minister Charity Ngilu's rejection
of her reappointment was dismissed as a gimmick to salvage a career in
decline, while Narok North MP William ole Ntimama warned the President
the country was headed into a political crisis.
Machakos Town MP Daudi Mwanzia said in declining reappointment,
Ngilu was trying to redeem her political career which was tittering.
"She has perennially antagonised senior politicians in Ukambani and
has realised that politicians outside the region were using her," he
said.
Mwanzia said Ngilu had earned the Kamba's wrath for undermining Kalonzo Musyoka, a leading Orange figure.
He claimed Ngilu, Water minister Mutua Katuku and Wildlife assistant
minister Kalembe Ndile were rewarded for their sycophancy to the ruling
elite.
Mwanzia said President Kibaki should dissolve Parliament and call for a snap election.
He said the President had lost the goodwill of many MPs and Kenyans due to poor advice from his handlers.
Mwanzia said the President should have consulted Orange leaders after the Government lost in the referendum.
Meanwhile, former Cabinet minister William ole Ntimama warned of
trouble ahead unless the President initiated dialogue among political
parties.
He also questioned the credibility of the Cabinet, describing it as a government of losers.
"I can tell you that the danger of disintegration is real because the will of the people was ignored," said Ntimama.
He said contrary to the President's promise the Cabinet is not
broad-based. He said the President's appointees are well-connected
people.
"These are people who are possessed with hate, greed, arrogance and hostility to other leaders," said Ntimama.
The Narok North MP said Kenya punishes winners and rewards losers.
He said the reality facing Kibaki's government was that manipulating democracy wouldn't take him far.
"The fact that he has poached a few MPs from opposition parties in a
desperate attempt to build a voting machine only gives him temporary
relief," said Ntimama.
Meanwhile, three civic leaders in Trans Nzoia District have urged
the President to ignore politicians who rejected Cabinet posts, but are
now making frantic efforts win them back.
Three Trans Nzoia civic leaders said in a statement the MPs should
be ignored since their initial intention was to embarrass the President.
"We are baffled by reports that former minister Musikari Kombo,
Charity Ngilu and Noah Wekesa were rushing to State House in utter
panic, intent to have their seats back. We wonder what has changed in
24 hours for the apparent change of heart yet they were emphatic they
would reject the posts because they were not consulted," said the
councillors, led by the chairman of Nzoia County Council's Town
Planning committee, Mr Joshua Werunga
Werunga, a councillor in Endebess in Wekesa's Kwanza constituency,
claimed Kanu blood flows in his veins "like the Niagara falls".
"Even during the referendum vote Wekesa campaigned for Orange and
only made some tactical appearances in Banana rallies to feign cosmetic
support. We are saddened that he could have used his proximity to Kanu
heavyweights to intoxicate the Ford Kenya chairman," the statement said.

Original_Search_Title: President's Frantic Efforts to Win Back Party Leaders
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki's Frantic Efforts to Win Back Kombo And Ngilu
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Pressure was mounting last night on party leaders Musikari Kombo
and Charity Ngilu to return to Government, hours after President Kibaki
witnessed the swearing in of his new Cabinet.
Sources told the Nation that Mr Kombo, the Ford Kenya chairman and
Mrs Ngilu, the National Party of Kenya leader, who both declined to
take up Cabinet posts on Thursday, were summoned to State House last
evening as the Government made frantic efforts to have them rescind
their decisions and lead their parties back to the fold.
But as State House worked frantically to repair the damage caused by
the two parties' departure, six more assistant ministers failed to show
up for the swearing in ceremony at State House.
Also missing at the ceremony where President Kibaki witnessed the
swearing in of 26 ministers and 28 assistant ministers were 16 MPs who
had earlier turned down appointments to the Government after it was
named on Wednesday.
However, the Government got a boost when assistant minister Mwangi
Kiunjuri, who had earlier rejected his appointement changed his mind
and was sworn in.
The six who did not show up yesterday included Nobel laureate
Wangari Maathai, Mr Kipkalya Kones, Mr David Mwenje, Mr Sammy Leshore,
Mr Titus Ngoyoni and Mr Stephen ole Ntutu.
Prof Maathai explained that she did not attend because she had asked
the President to postpone the ceremony to allow consultations with
political parties whose MPs had turned down appointments.
She would have been going against the spirit of reconciliation if she had presented herself to be sworn in yesterday.
But Mr Kones said he would address a press conference today to
explain why he did not wish to be sworn in. However, close allies of
the Ford People nominated MP said he is likely to reject the
appointment.
Government sources said Mr Leshore was informed late and would be
sworn in on Tuesday while Mr Mwenje was leading the parliamentary
committee on Defence on a tour of China.
It could not be established immediately why Mr Ngoyoni and Mr Ntutu did not attend.
Last evening, Mr Kombo was reported to have been accompanied to
State House by Kwanza MP Noah Wekesa and his Turkana North counterpart
John Munyes, also the Ford-K secretary general.
Mrs Ngilu, who was expected to meet the President together with Mr
Kombo, was said to be accompanied by nominated MP Cecily Mbarire.
Sources disclosed that there have been a series of phone calls and
correspondence between State House and the two leaders since Thursday
when they separately announced their decision not to support the
Government.
Their decision leaves President Kibaki's Government severely
weakened because Mr Kombo's Ford-K is the largest remaining partner of
the President's Democratic Party in the ruling Narc coalition after the
departure of the Liberal Democratic Party.
The two party leaders' move prompted President Kibaki to call a crisis meeting of key ministers at State House.
The meeting was attended by ministers Martha Karua, Newton Kulundu,
Mukhisa Kituyi, Amos Kimunya, Raphael Tuju, Kipruto Kirwa, Njeru Ndwiga
and the newly-promoted Rashid Shakombo.
During the meeting, it was said they discussed mistakes made by the
Banana camp during the referendum campaigns and ways of tackling them.
It was understood the President told his lieutenants that Kenyans
had not only expressed their opinions about the new Constitution in the
referendum but also about the performance of the Narc Government.
It was time they changed tactics and started to deliver services to the public.
On Thursday, Mr Kombo led six assistant ministers from Ford-K in
declining positions in the Government, arguing that the party had not
been consulted.
He also accused the Head of State of side-stepping coalition
partners in Cabinet and other key Government appointments. This, he
said, had plunged the country into a political crisis.
Mr Kombo had been re-appointed Local Government minister while the
assistant ministers were Mr Moses Wetang'ula (Foreign Affairs), Mr
Munyes (Water Resources), Mr David Were (Information and
Communications), Dr Noah Wekesa (Livestock) and Mr Soita Shitanda
(Special Programmes).
But three ministers from the party - Dr Kituyi, Dr Kulundu and Mr Moses Akaranga - accepted positions in the Cabinet.
Mrs Ngilu, while announcing her decision not to take up her position
as Health minister, said she would reconsider her appointment if Ford
Kenya and the Liberal Democratic Party were included in President
Kibaki's new line-up.
Yesterday, the Ford-K and NPK leaders were scheduled to meet
President Kibaki sometime after 4 pm but a delegation from Japan
presenting a stone of peace to the Head of State delayed their talks
until 5.30 pm.
Mr Kombo had disclosed that there had been frantic efforts to have meet the President following the party's move.
"There have been efforts including one not more than an hour ago,"
he told the Nation in his office at Continental House shortly before 2
pm.
He confirmed having held discussions with, among others, Mrs Ngilu,
leader of Official Opposition Uhuru Kenyatta and "leading" Kanu
luminaries.
A relaxed and tough talking-Kombo said his terms for holding
discussions with the President would be guided by the principles of
coalition governments the world over.
"The principles and tenets of coalition governments is negotiations and consultations," he added.
The Ford-K chairman, however, did not begrudge three of his MPs who were sworn in at State House yesterday.
He said the President had a prerogative of including anybody in his
Cabinet as long as the slots which Ford-K was eyeing were not affected.
"In coalition-building, the people who the party forwards should be
picked," Mr Kombo said in reference to his terms of negotiations.
He dismissed claims that Ford-K was interested in "goodies" from
State House noting that the party had been short-changed twice - in
2003 and last Wednesday night.
"We want to establish values and principles of coalition governments
in Kenya because this is going to be a way of life," the Ford-K boss
added.
He, however, said if the party failed to agree with the President on
its demands, the MPs will take their rightful position in the backbench
of the Narc government and keep the Cabinet on its toes.

Original_Search_Title: There's No Cure for Loss of Faith
Headline: Kenya: There's No Cure for Loss of Faith
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Spare a thought for President Mwai Kibaki. Looking at him against the backdrop of his Cabinet crisis, it's hard not to compare him to Christina Onassis, the Greek heiress to the massive shipping fortune who ended up a miserable wreck despite tons of money in the bank.
She just couldn't get her love life sorted out, could never tell who her genuine friends were and retreated into drugs, finally dying in 1988 of heart failure induced by a suspected overdose.
She was only 37. Life had been uncommonly generous to her, yet she squandered the opportunity to give something back.
Mr Kibaki started his term on the highest note ever in contemporary Kenya. We voted for him en masse in the belief that we were ushering in a new era of principled political leadership.
No other president has enjoyed so much goodwill. We were exhausted by the politics of destruction by the time he came along, and we just wanted a chance to heal. You would think that was not too much to ask, but peace has proved more elusive than gold dust and his presidency teeters on the brink of an abyss.
He has no one to blame but himself. The Orange dissent was merely the tip of the iceberg. They are out of the picture, but the President's woes persist. He should ask himself why.
We gave Mr Kibaki free rein, yet the engine is now almost empty and dangerously close to grinding to a standstill. We owe him the truth. We can't sugar the pill any more. Nothing short of a miracle will redeem this presidency. Can he hack it?
Democracy might be the preferred form of government but, by God, it is a tough call - especially when you have your back up against a wall.
It need never have come to this. The position he finds himself in is a self-imposed straitjacket. It is about trust, trust and trust. You can be forgiven for missing one or two steps. But when you persistently act out of tune with the mood of the country, there can be only one way to go. There is no cure for loss of faith.
All too many decades ago, I learnt on my mother's knee that promises were meant to be kept - and that you could compromise on anything but your good name. If you find yourself unable to meet your commitments, you return to the negotiating table. You do not try to demonise the other side or run them out of town.
President Kibaki appears to have chosen to go solo - not even consulting his erstwhile partners - regardless of signs of discontent. So be it. But he must be prepared to pay the price.
Extremists at each end of his previous Cabinet were never going to add value to his leadership. That much we all agree on. But getting rid of the Oranges and clinging to the enemy within was not the solution either. To go ahead and reward unbecoming conduct, in an attempt to buy loyalty, was not just an error in judgment but may prove an indulgence too expensive.
Nothing could be more potent in its message than the recent referendum. It was not just about a disinformation campaign and tribal arithmetics, as his routed troops wished to believe. It was a mid-term report on his performance. And it pointed to serious cracks in the palace walls.
For a while there, some of us believed that the President was making genuine attempts to restore and renovate the edifice. Hence the journeys to State House, supposedly in the name of negotiations with key players.
Here was a man giving the impression of simply wanting to get to 2007 as painlessly as possible. First Lady Lucy Kibaki even assured us once that he had the juice to go on until 2012. Yet he has somehow contrived to raise the hackles of virtually everyone who seeks to hold his hand on the journey.
For all the talk of buying time for wide consultations, it is hard to see the footprints of any serious religious or business leaders - not to mention the free advice doled out by some diplomats - in the President's Cabinet fiasco. This was vintage Kibaki.
There is no shortcut to democracy. I can't imagine that the parade of leaders would have advised him to stick with the expensive government he favours. Forty-six assistant ministers, and this a position that has little going for it by way of job description!
We have surely plumbed the depths when we have a government made up almost entirely of people that we voted solidly against by virtue of rejecting the proposed new constitution. But what's new there?
The Kibaki administration signalled from the beginning that it had little intention of sticking to the spirit and letter of the memorandum of understanding that swept him into power. This was the glue that bound the Liberal Democratic Party and an alliance led by the Democratic Party, Ford Kenya and the National Party of Kenya in a pact to lead us out of the political wilderness.
Yet his very first act - appointing his Cabinet - was an exercise in betrayal. He not only shafted the LDP but also failed to live up to his promises to women that they would get one-third of all appointments.
With the death of Michael Wamalwa, it was Ford Kenya at the receiving end. It lost the vice-presidency - twice over. Mr Kibaki also made the ultimate mistake: the so-called government of national unity that was merely a ploy to rope in his old buddies into the Cabinet in a remake of the old style governance that Kenyans spat out.
The President appears not to have read the omens too well. Instead of mended fences, he now has an ever-growing list of groups thoroughly out of sorts with his leadership. The Oranges, Ford Kenya and NPK are out. Kanu is furious. Women are sharpening their knives. How could he make Maina Kamanda a full minister - of gender, of all things - rather than Beth Mugo, who gave the Bananas their only victory in Nairobi?
I want to be charitable and put up the hypothesis that Mr Kibaki is a very lonely man. You can be surrounded by a gaggle of hangers-on and still suffer the Poor Little Rich Girl syndrome.
He comes through as an intensely private man who is rather uncomfortable in the spotlight. It would take a hard person not to sympathise with the bureaucrat in him. But that doesn't let him off the hook.
The economy has chugged along happily without his Cabinet. But everything boils down to politics in the long run, and this is his Achilles Heel.
We probably expected too much too soon, coming as we did from a tortured political landscape. But, as we have learnt, there's simple medicine for this malaise: NO. Say it loud today.
The writer is the Nation's managing editor in charge of magazines.

Original_Search_Title: Ministers And Kibaki Hold Crucial Talks
Headline: Kenya: Ministers And Kibaki Hold Crucial Talks
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki yesterday held a crisis meeting with top ministers and
senior civil servants after a large number of MPs declined Cabinet
appointments.
The meeting was convened after ruling coalition partners Ford Kenya and
National Party of Kenya (NPK) pulled out of Government, throwing the new
team into panic.
Sources told the Nation that calls by Ford Kenya chairman Musikari Kombo
and his NPK counterpart for fresh talks by coalition partners sent shivers
down the spines of advisers at State House.
It was said the Liberal Democratic Party (LDP) and Kanu partnership in
the Orange Democratic Movement (ODM) was out of any future negotiations and
the addition of Ford Kenya and NPK could complicate the issue further.
President Kibaki, it was said, called into the meeting ministers John
Michuki (National Security), Njenga Karume (Special Programmes) and the Head
of Civil Service, Mr Francis Muthaura.
Their discussions, it was said, explored ways of fending off the
unexpected rebellion by Ford Kenya and NPK. The two parties teamed up with
President Kibaki's Democratic Party to form the National Alliance Party
(NAK), which was the base of the ruling coalition.
NAK was later joined by the LDP to form the coalition, which won the
December 2002 General Election.
State House was caught by surprise when they were informed that Mr Kombo
had led his party MPs in declining Cabinet positions even after holding
talks with the former Local Government minister yesterday morning.
Mr Muthaura called Mr Kombo to inform him of the swearing-in date when
the party boss was addressing a news conference about his party's
walk-out.
"This is a political crisis that has been created by failure to
consult. If they had respected our line, there would be no crisis," he said
as he vowed never to return unless President Kibaki respected rules of
coalition governments.
NPK boss Charity Ngilu deepened the crisis in the afternoon when she
rejected her re-appointment to the Health docket. They both called for fresh
talks of coalition partners, which would lead to an all-inclusive
government.
"I call upon the President to open his heart and door to all our
brothers and sisters in Ford Kenya, LDP and National Party of Kenya together
to compromise for the sake of the nation. It is time to laugh and shake
hands again," she said.
The rebellion, which hit the 20 mark and created a crisis for President
Kibaki's preferred government, was underlined early in the day when Mr Kombo
and Mrs Ngilu declined to take up Government vehicles, which had been sent
to their residences.
It was said Mr Michuki and Mr Karume were fearful that the walk-out by
Ford Kenya and NPK would water down the national appeal of the Government,
which was already without the LDP. Although Cabinet ministers Mukhisa Kituyi
(Trade) and Newton Kulundu (Labour) have accepted the offers, State House
doubted their ability to rally the Luhya behind the Government.
It was said they agreed to explore ways to ensure Mr Kombo and Mrs Ngilu
did not team up with the Orange Democratic Movement leaders Raila Odinga,
Kalonzo Musyoka, Anyang' Nyong'o and Najib Balala.
It was said they agreed to explore ways to ensure Mr Kombo and Mrs Ngilu
do not team up with the Orange Democratic Movement leaders Raila Odinga,
Kalonzo Musyoka, Anyang Nyong'o and Najib Balala.
The four former ministers were among the seven key ODM leaders edged out
of Government in the new line-up.
Fully aware that Mr Kombo and Mrs Ngilu would no longer trust State
House emissaries, Comptroller Hyslop Ipu was detailed to call the two to go
and meet President Kibaki at State House.
The calls were however rejected with a firm demand that any talks must
include the LDP leadership.
Newly re-appointed ministers Martha Karua, Newton Kulundu, Mukhisa
Kituyi, Amos Kimunya, Raphael Tuju, Kipruto Kirwa, Njeru Ndwiga and their
promoted colleague, Mr Rashid Shakombo later joined the talks at State House
in the afternoon.
Two options were placed on the table on how to deal with the vacant
positions in the cabinet-either appoint other leaders immediately or wait
until the heat cooled down for discussions with them.
Sources said it was agreed that new ministers and assistant ministers
who had accepted their positions would be sworn in today to ensure that the
Government was working.
The move, they said, will give the President humble time to reach out to
Mr Kombo and Mrs Ngilu-in person- and hear out their proposals and ways of
solving them.
To ensure Government operations were going on well, all permanent
secretaries-old and new-were called to State House in the afternoon for
proper instructions, it was said.
The Nation was told the President's handlers wanted to avoid the
impression which the crisis were creating in the minds of the international
community.
During the meeting, it was said they discussed the mistakes the Banana
camp made during the referendum campaigns and ways of solving them.
It was understood President told his new lieutenants that Kenyans not
only expressed their opinions about the new Constitution but also about the
performance of the Narc Government.
It was time, he allegedly said, they changed tactics and started to
deliver services to the public.

Original_Search_Title: Slim Options for Kibaki As New Cabinet Crumbles
Headline: Kenya: Slim Options for Kibaki As New Cabinet Crumbles
Body_Text:
Nairobi — With his Cabinet constituted only on Wednesday crumbling like a cookie, President Kibaki's immediate and long-term prospects look especially bleak. In fact, the question is not about how bad the situation is. It is whether Mr Kibaki, known for his gross detachment from serious issues, really appreciates the magnitude of his predicament.
The first temptation for the President would probably be to hunker down and hope the storm will calm. The impulse to do this is largely informed by Kenya's political system where parties are, technically speaking, incidental to the exercise of executive power.
Kenya's executive presidency is cushioned in a constitutional cocoon where the tenure of the chief executive has no direct correlation with his strength of support in Parliament. This is unlike countries like Britain or Germany or Japan, where the chief executive enjoys office for as long as he commands a majority in Parliament. Where no majority party emerges, as happens frequently in many of these countries, carefully crafted coalitions are the result.
Theoretically, the Kenyan President's party may have only a handful of members in the House, and the President would be just fine. The critical problem is when disgruntled Members of Parliament from other parties gang up against his party and move a vote of no confidence against the Government. This would necessitate a snap election to be called. For President Kibaki, a General Election coming so soon after a disastrous referendum showing would not augur well for his presidency.
A day after sacking his entire Cabinet, Mr Kibaki prorogued Parliament. But it is becoming increasingly clear that this would be the epicentre of the next stage of the assault against his presidency. Divergent opinion is gradually coalescing around the viewpoint that a Motion of no confidence in the Government is likely to be the priority agenda of the anti-Kibaki forces when Parliament reconvenes.
Initially, government loyalists were complacent about such a Motion making much headway, both on account of numbers as well as the parliamentary perks MPs stand to lose with a premature dissolution of Parliament. But with things happening so fast, and parties like Ford Kenya, which had been open-minded towards Mr Kibaki, deserting him with speed, it is no longer a guarantee that this growing coalition against the Kibaki government will not play this trump card.
This aside, a president whose party is in a minority will still find his hands and feet have been severely tied if he cannot effect any legislative agenda. Government Bills could routinely get kicked out by Parliament. The result: Government gridlock. For the President, matters seem to be quickly running out of hand. He could soon be confronted by a situation where operating in Parliament will be a real headache, perhaps even impossible. He could very well just find he has very few options but to dissolve it - and face the unpalatable likelihood of not getting re-elected.
However, something much greater has been lost by the Kibaki presidency even if by whatever chance it manages to manoeuvre its way through the minefield ahead. The undeniably inept manner this seemingly doomed Cabinet was constituted is playing havoc when it comes to public perceptions of the president's foresight and leadership.
The rapid withdrawal of support by parties he was hitherto allied to like Ford-Kenya and NPK carried one overriding theme: That Mr Kibaki has taken everybody's support for granted and failed to consult. The same theme was earlier captured by Kanu and LDP when they decided to distance themselves from the Government.
Bereft of any credible partners, the Kibaki administration that started off with incredible goodwill in 2002 has been left marooned in a very short span of time largely because of its own insularity.
When he recalls Parliament, probably in March, it will require only 112 MPs out of the 222 to pass a no-confidence vote in his government.
It will then require the President to dissolve Parliament and call a General Election. If not- according to the Lancaster Constitution - Parliament will stand dissolved on the fourth day, after which elections must be held within three months.
And if such a Motion is brought before a by-election is held in Kasipul Kabondo, a seat left vacant following Mr Peter Owidi's death, it will require only 111 MPs to pass it.
According to section 59 (3) of the Constitution, the House needs only a simple majority to pass such a Motion.
The President prorogued the House - sent it on indefinite leave - two weeks ago, just a few days after the Government suffered a heavy defeat in a referendum through which it tried popular support for a proposed new Constitution.
Seven ministers of the ruling Narc coalition and the official opposition party Kanu campaigned against it and bagged 3.5 million votes against the Government's 2.5 million.
Though the Constitution does not bind the President to recall MPs within a specific time, there are government legislative programmes which force the Head State to summon it.
In March, for instance, Finance minister David Mwiraria will require Parliament to authorise his Supplementary Budget to meet the shortfalls in last June's votes.
This way the President will be forced to open the House to get MPs vote on the mini-budget.
And even without the mini-budget, the Government requires Parliament to be in session for Mr Mwiraria to present his Budget two months later and vote on the estimates to keep it functioning.
Such a Motion requires a notice of not less than seven days. An MP can file it soon after the reconvening of Parliament for it to mature even before the House gets to pass the supplementary estimates.
The question is whether or not the MPs who campaigned in the Orange team will marshal the numbers required to pass a no-confidence Motion.
It has been argued that most MPs would not want to cut short their five-year term prematurely owing to financial commitments - mortgages, car loans and the generous perks they voted for themselves.
Going by the pre-referendum numbers, the Orange team had 101 MPs campaigning while the Banana had 117, including the President, with four MPs maintaining that they would not support any side.
If that situation prevails and the Banana side votes for the Government - it would thus appear - the Motion would not pass.
However, the political arithmetic has since changed and some pro-Government MPs might find their way to the Orange camp. It would make President Kibaki's government all the more vulnerable.
But it would not necessarily mean automatic victory for the Orange camp. As expected, new developments and negotiations between now and March could change the political scenario.
Besides such motions, the Government side will have to work hard to ensure that the Orange MPs do not frustrate the passage of Bills in the House.
The uncertainty of smooth business in Parliament will persist until a clear picture emerges on which way the Cabinet will function.

Original_Search_Title: Golden Silence
Headline: Kenya: Golden Silence
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Many months ago, friends and readers had this knack for either calling me or sending me mail to ask whether I was likely to write on the First Lady Lucy Kibaki, especially after she had said anything they figured was worth commenting about.
I never give away trade secrets, and I never used to tell them whether I would write about her or not, but I used to make them feel that their sentiments were taken in.
At the same time, when she was featured, I used to receive phone calls from female readers castigating me for taking on her. I used to tell them that I could not help it. As long as the First Lady decided to exercise her freedom of speech and commented on matters that touched on the lives of Kenyans, I - and many other journalists - had the right to comment on her utterances also. As a matter of fact, she needed journalists more to get her message across, and journalists would be doing the public a disservice by confining themselves to reporting only her speeches.
Even after the First Lady exercised her freedom of expression and burnt the midnight oil at a newsdesk and on national television expressing herself, I let many readers down by not commenting on it, not because I developed cold fingers, but because I figured she did not "make the cut" to be featured here.
But I learnt that whenever the First Lady gave a speech, I got affected because I needed to explain many things to both her fans and my readers who were affected either by my comments or by lack of them.
