After her debut appearance at the newsdesk from where she caught international attention, the First Lady exercised her constitutional right to remain silent and stayed for a long time without speaking in public until a few months ago when she went about berating some minister who had said the "Prisidint" is his friend. During the same function, she said that she had been telling the "Prisidint" to fire those ministers who were rebelling against him. Even then, she still did not make the cut.
The last time I heard the First Lady speak was on the day of the voting on the referendum when she told voters at a polling station in Othaya that Banana, surely! had already won.
Nothing has been heard from her since. Last Saturday, she was not at the Women's Aids Run, a function which she had come to be identified with. Suffice it to say that she has saved me from the trouble of having to tell readers whether or not I am going to write about her or to explain to her fans why I wrote about her.
How I wish the First Lady would always keep her peace like she is doing now - this way, my readers and her fans will be at peace and I will not need to explain anything to them.
Tribal chiefs
Mr Simeon Nyachae
Many a time, I hear people, especially men and women, or boys and girls who are trying to become an item, inquire about one another's tribe. Invariably, the female - and even the male - says that she is a half-caste. "I am a half caste of a Luo father from Kisumu and a Luo mother," the answer would come. Others could even be half-castes of Embus and Mbeeres; Bukusus and Maragolis. And to prove that they sure are half-castes, they speak both tongues.
But if one's ability to speak different languages is the base for their "half-casteness", then I think I am a tenth of nearly every ethnic group in Kenya because I have a smattering of very many local languages, but I am not a polyglot.
Of all the local languages I have a smattering of, the easiest is Ekegusii, whose formula is very simple. Just add an E at the beginning of any acronym or a word in English and it becomes an Ekegusii word.
Thus DVD, becomes EDVD, VCR becomes EVCR, computer is Ecomputer and so on and so forth.
But even though I can transform words and can keep a conversation going in Ekegusii, when it comes to transformation, and even speaking in Ekegusii, I am no match for Simeon Nyachae, the Ford People supremo and Nyaribari Chache Member of Parliament.
The former chief secretary, who was also a provincial commissioner before he plunged into politics, is fast proving that politics can transform one from a well-meaning orator to a person who just bandies words around without any care to what their ramifications are.
Nyachae, who was the chair of the Banana campaign during the just ended referendum, went overboard during the campaigns when he inferred that a fellow politician in the opposing camp could not lay claim to any leadership qualities because he is not circumcised.
Anyway, that is now foreskin in a hospital dustbin, but the statement was such a low blow, a cheap shot from a person who happens to be one of the most successful businessmen in the country and who even wanted to become the President of the Republic of Kenya.
The fact that Nyachae did campaign for a cause he believed in is not in doubt, but he lost the cause and voters in his constituency voted against the Draft. After that, he went quiet for a few days. When he had plucked the courage, he threw a dinner to congratulate the losing side for a job well done. He took this opportunity also to share his thoughts and tell them why he thought his camp lost.
He could not understand why Kenyans rejected the draft yet it called for the disbandment of the provincial administration, through which Nyachae himself became what he is today. "Tribalism was so entrenched in the country that it was difficult to root out and many politicians discovered it was a cancer during the referendum."
Nyachae's Ford-People party has 14 MPs and 10 of them - him included - come from three districts dominated by members of his community. "The vice has infiltrated religious leaders and journalists. This issue of ethnicity is such a serious problem," he said. "Whether it is a religious leader or a journalist, you read ethnicity." He asked: "Did you know that ethnicity was so entrenched in the country before the referendum," and his guests responded in the negative.
Does it mean that all this time Nyachae had not discovered that Kenya has tribes and politicians always align themselves to their communities?
Mr Nyachae, while you are groping in the dark for the answers as to why your camp suffered a heavy defeat, do not accuse the Press. Keep journalists out of your miseries. Journalists did their bit and reported the gaffes and insults as politicians made and traded them, respectively, and cannot be held responsible for Banana's loss.
Relative calm
Last week, I was at the news desk as the late night reporter. On a normal night, late night reporters literally have their hands full with telephone handsets as readers call in to make enquiries or to book appointments.
If a late night reporter is unlucky, he might even have to deal with a prominent visitor or two from Muthaiga or any other leafy suburb, together with her security detail which might even include the Nairobi Provincial Police Commissioner.
But on this night last week, I did not need to change eardrums after getting an earful from readers and visitors alike. There was even no need to turn the other cheek, or take notes while hiding lest I be chided by an unwelcome guest. As a matter of fact, as a print journalist would put, there was no drama that unfolded.
Had the politicians' aides been told that the person on the desk that night did not have political hair on his chest and there was no need of honouring him with a phone call? Was the economy so bad that Kenyans could no longer afford to make calls, or had they just given up on the Narc Government and had stopped to call to complain on the Government's failure to deliver on its promises?
I could not get answers so I asked around and the lady who was manning the switchboard said all was peaceful in the newsroom as was everywhere in the country because there was no Cabinet.
And she was right. Ever since the President fired those men and women who drive around in fuel guzzlers, and issue contradictory statements back and forth, and call each other names at every opportunity, and travel overseas every now and then at the taxpayers' expense, and in general do nothing that can help the general public, this country has been very peaceful.
True, they put us in business by their antics, and their torn socks, but their absence has been a welcome break - not to mention the taxpayers' money that has been saved.
Real Kenyans
For the last two weeks, I have featured two pieces here which define Kenyans and in which I enumerated what Kenyans are likely to do. Many Kenyans sent in mail to say that it is true Kenyans have such qualities and a colleague sent in a list of some of the things that define Kenya.
He says that it is probably only in Kenya where if you stop on the street and point at the sky, a crowd forms around you immediately and starts looking in the same direction and then start explaining what is happening; if someone is being mugged people will not help him but will stop, make sure they get all the drama and then go and tell their friends; people will refuse to board an empty matatu for an overloaded one because the overloaded one will not stop to pick passengers.
It is only in Kenya where it is manly to have spent a night or two in a police cells and it is regarded cool to have a criminal record and it is the only place where women under dress in cold weather and overdress in hot weather while the men over dress in all kinds of weather.
It is only Kenyans who acquire a nasal drawl, or twang when they are talking to a foreigner, especially a Mzungu; they are the only people who, after connecting flights at an airport in the United States or United Kingdom come back with a nasal drawl, or twang but when they live in India for five years they do not come back with an Indian accent; they all know a public figure personally; they drive at 30 kilometres per hour when it is raining; abandon their cars in the middle of the road when scratched by another car and wait for the policemen to come and view the scratches then they drive off.
Lastly, he says that if you have examined yourself and are lacking in all of the above-mentioned qualities, then you are losing your Kenyan touch and you need to pull up your socks.
No Park-ing Here
Last weekend, I saw Greg, the newly-married former roomate of mine who is the manager of Lake Nakuru National Park's Sarova Lion Hill lodge. I last saw Greg during his wedding some months ago, and when he came back from his honeymoon, he told me to go check him out at the Bird Watchers' Paradise, the name Lake Nakuru National Park was given when it was re-branded.
I had planned to tell you all about Sarova Lion Hill, the lodge with a view with 67 chalets that all face Lake Nakuru and the fact that it is a perfect place to experience one of the world's largest ornithological spectacles in the heart of the Great Rift Valley.
I may not tell you all about Sarova Lion Hill, "the beautiful retreat set in Lake Nakuru national park" as I had intended and it is all the fault of Dr Alfred Mutua, the Government Spokesman, who is also my friend.
On Wednesday, Dr Mutua said that the National Parks will be open to Kenyans for free till Monday.
Free entry means that adult citizens will save Sh100 and the children will save Sh50 for that is what is charged at the gate. He did not clarify whether cars will also be exempted since cars have to be paid for too and their rates depend on the seating capacity. All what one needs is either a national identity card or a Kenyan passport to enter the 60 sanctuaries spread all over Kenya.
Kenyans who can drive to the park can as well afford to pay the entry fees. Those who cannot afford this amount may also not drive because they cannot afford cars. According to park rules, people are not allowed to walk because it is dangerous for them. Dr Mutua, unless vehicles are also provided then these acts of tokenism are meaningless and the people will not "enjoy their heritage and wildlife" as you claim you want them to.

Original_Search_Title: Why Ford Kenya Chairman Slapped Kibaki in the Face
Headline: Kenya: Why Ford Kenya Chairman Slapped Kibaki in the Face
Body_Text:
Nairobi — During a Ford Kenya retreat that ended on Tuesday in Mombasa, some MPs were sure that their chairman, Musikari Kombo, would be handed the Vice-President's position in the new Cabinet.
At the Paradise Beach Hotel's conference hall, word went round that Kombo was the V-P in waiting. Some MPs went into shouts of jubilation in the hall, referring to Kombo as V-P.
But sources in the meeting said Kombo was not amused and restrained his lieutenants from congratulating him even before President Kibaki had made his Cabinet appointments official.
The presence of a Kenya Navy vehicle, which was on an unknown errand at the resort, but which some MPs mistook for transport for Kombo to the airport should he be named V-P, did not make matters any better.
As Kombo left Mombasa for Nairobi aboard a 4.30pm flight, some of the MPs were still upbeat, saying he might leave Mombasa as an MP and land in Nairobi as a V-P.
It must have come as a blow to the MPs when President Kibaki failed to elevate their man above his Local Government portfolio.
Massive blow
Kombo, who on Thursday declined the appointment, had gone to great lengths to campaign for the Proposed Constitution.
In October, he announced a massive pay hike for councilors in a move seen as calculated to woe civic leaders to the Banana side. The pay hike would be paid through the Local Government Transfer Fund, whose al__cpLocation Kombo raised to Sh5 billion. But the rise is yet to be effected. Had he accepted the appointment, Kombo would have found himself pressured to implement it.
A month earlier, he had led 13 Ford-K MPs to a meeting with Kibaki at which more promises were made to the region in exchange for referendum support.
All these were seen as part of the freebies the Government was handing out across the country to persuade voters to support the Proposed Constitution.
Kombo repeatedly asked the Luhya community to support him, saying he was best placed to succeed Kibaki as Kenya's fourth president.
At one point, Kombo said he was confident of the succession plan because Kibaki had never let him down.
Among some of the demands that Ford Kenya MPs have been making is that the VP slot be handed back to the party in line with the pre-election memorandum of understanding, which they said Kibaki breached.
Last week, two party MPs, Dr Enoch Kibunguchy (Lugari) and Sylvester Wakoli (Bumula), asked that the VP's slot be given to Kombo.

Original_Search_Title: Uneasy Calm As Vocal Coast MPs Avoid Press
Headline: Kenya: Uneasy Calm As Vocal Coast MPs Avoid Press
Body_Text:
Nairobi — None of the eight Coast MPs appointed to the Cabinet expressed dissent on Thursday, but this may not give President Kibaki peace about the province.
Trouble could be brewing from the composition of the Coast team that appeared to reward those who worked least during the referendum and leave out MPs who did the donkey work on the campaign trail.
The elevation of Suleiman Shakombo into Cabinet and leaving Danson Mungatana and Anania Mwaboza as assistant ministers, was interpreted yesterday as a reward to a man who did little for the Banana campaign.
Shakombo, the Likoni MP, was largely absent from the Coast Banana campaigns as Morris Dzoro (Tourism minister), Mungatana and Mwaboza trudged the length and breadth of vast province.
Shakombo, Dzoro and Transport minister Ali Mwakwere on Thursday gleefully accepted their positions, Mwaboza and Mungatana promised to speak to the media but did not keep their word. It was a telling sign from the vocal politicians.
Kibaki retained the three Cabinet and five assistants from the Coast as they were in the previous government. He dropped Najib Balala and elevated Shakombo, and brought in Abu Chiaba of Lamu East as an assistant minister. Kibaki's treatment of the province sought to keep the same representation in the new Government as the region had become used to.
Shakombo got Balala's Heritage post, while Dzoro retained his Tourism docket. Mwakwere was shifted from the high-profile Foreign Affairs Ministry to Transport. Assistant minister Chiaba joined Boniface Mghanga, Joseph Kingi and Ananiah Mwaboza, who were all retained.
In picking his team, Kibaki did not include any Muslim MP of Arab descent, making it the first time since independence that members of the community are not represented in the Cabinet. In Mombasa, Balala and mayor Taib are the most prominent leaders from that community. Before them was the late Shariff Nassir, all scions of a community that often stays together in politics.
The impact of the new Cabinet and reaction in the Coast largely centred around Balala's sacking and the elevation of Shakombo to fill his slot. There was also talk of whether Mungatana and Mwaboza would decline their appointments.
When he does, the wider impact of his Cabinet choices will be felt.

Original_Search_Title: Cabinet Walkout Was Inevitable, Says Chief Whip
Headline: Kenya: Cabinet Walkout Was Inevitable, Says Chief Whip
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Government Chief Whip Norman Nyaga has said the Cabinet walkout was "inevitable" as the new line-up had failed to appreciate political realities.
He blamed President Kibaki's political advisors for provoking the crisis, arguing they had failed to read the country's political mood and advised him to lock out popular and efficient MPs in a line-up that also ignored stated expectations of key political parties and specifically Ford Kenya.
And in an apparent criticism of the new line-up, Nyaga said some ministers could not be relied on to effectively take care of Government interests in Parliament and had therefore left it more exposed to a hostile Opposition.
Said he: "Any Cabinet line-up must primarily be guided by the need to safeguard government and public interests and especially in Parliament. Looking at the new Cabinet, I'm unable to get the certain leadership calibre desirable for an efficient Front Bench."
It also emerged that despite his office playing an essential role in the Government legislative agenda in Parliament, Nyaga was locked out of "consultations" leading to the drawing up of the Cabinet list despite reportedly making repeated efforts to meet the President.
He had frantically called State House during the two weeks the President took to form his now-crumbling Cabinet, but had not been granted an audience until he learnt of the new Cabinet from the Kenya Broadcasting Corporation.
Locked out
But the Kamukunji MP refused to blame the President for his predicament and instead accused a cabal of senior civil servants who were allegedly shielding the President from the truth and with whom he vowed to fight to the bitter end.
"I learnt of the new list like anyone else-through the TV. I was denied access by some civil servants who have no interest in the Government. They will only alienate him from his true friends and well-wishers. I'm telling them to expect nothing short of war from those who genuinely love the President. We can't allow them to continue misleading the President," he said.
In the Commonwealth practice on which the Kenyan Parliament is modelled, the Government Whip plays a central role in the crafting of a new Cabinet.
As the manager of government business in Parliament, it is naturally expected that the Whip is better placed to know which MP can be trusted to efficiently take care of the State business in a given ministry.
Sources, however, said Nyaga believed the line-up was largely the work of two Cabinet ministers who had dissuaded the President from reaching out to him and others who might have had different opinions.
Inevitable rejections
The two, both elderly and wealthy and who also served in the dissolved Cabinet, are said to have advised for the inclusion of MPs who had not been consulted, making the rejection of the offers inevitable.
Speaking on Thursday, Nyaga said he believed the snowballing crisis in the Cabinet left the President no option, but to reach out to the disgruntled MPs by "opening State House doors".
In apparent reference to the sacking of the renegade ministers in the Orange Democratic Movement, Nyaga said the omission from the Cabinet of MPs commanding genuine grassroots support had exacerbated disillusionment with the President and his Government.
He further challenged the President to reach out to the public, saying his visibility was necessary to assure the electorate that he was keen on following up a number of pledges made during the referendum campaigns despite his Banana camp loss.
In a tacit admission that the Wako Draft was faulty, Nyaga argued the President must urgently reconvene talks on the new constitution.
"We need to revive talks on the new constitution urgently. Kenyans did not say they don't want a constitution. They simply said they did not want what was offered in the Draft," Nyaga said.

Original_Search_Title: Things Fall Apart
Headline: Kenya: Things Fall Apart
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki put on a brave face Thursday and announced that his new Cabinet appointees would be sworn-in on Friday even as three ministers and 16 assistant ministers turned down the jobs.
The President, who spent the day at State House, reportedly went into a subdued mood as the news of rejection of his job offers trickled in. He attended several crisis meetings with his close confidants.
Ford Kenya chairman Musikari Kombo dealt Kibaki a severe blow when he led his party in a mass walkout from the Cabinet. He pulled out of Government with five assistant ministers: Soita Shitanda, Noah Wekesa, Moses Wetang'ula, David Were and party secretary-general John Munyes.
Earlier, Ndhiwa MP Orwa Ojodeh had rejected a ministerial appointment, saying his community had been given a raw deal while Kitui Central MP Charity Ngilu too declined to take up her former position as minister for Health and said she would first consult her party.
By last evening, 16 Assistant ministers had refused to take up positions in the Cabinet the President unveiled on Wednesday night. He had dissolved it a fortnight ago, following the rejection of the proposed constitution his Government was pushing.
In the new line-up, Kibaki dropped all former Orange ministers who campaigned against the proposed constitution in a move that completely locked a key partner in the ruling National Rainbow Coalition - the Liberal Democratic Party - out of Government.
Rejections pour in
All hell seemed to break loose as soon as the President finished reading out his new line-up on Wednesday night, when appointees called into TV stations, one after another, to decline the positions. The rejections continued throughout Thursday, with the three ministers dropping the bombshell.
Among those who snubbed the call to the Cabinet are two MPs from the President's political strongholds, Mwangi Kiunjuri and Joseph Nyagah.
The duo, from Laikipia and Mbeere respectively, are normally considered staunch Kibaki backers and their decision to join the ranks of those rejecting their appointments is certain to deepen the crisis in the Government.
Others who declined to take up their assistant ministers' seats are Kivutha Kibwana, Paul Sang, Joseph Nkaissery, Samuel Moroto, William Boit, Peter Odoyo, Stephen ole Ntutu, David Sudi and Zaddock Syongoh.
And as the Head of Civil Service and Secretary to the Cabinet, Mr Francis Muthaura, prepared to swear in the appointees, it was by last night not clear who would show up.
Impeccable sources said last night that Kibaki and his adviser and PS, Stanley Murage, and close ally Njenga Karume, attended a meeting at State House that discussed the unfolding events. Vice President Moody Awori and Attorney-General Amos Wako were also said to have been present at the consultations.
It was not clear what resolutions were made, but a Presidential Press Service (PPS) dispatch later said the swearing in ceremony of ministers and assistant ministers would be held this morning.
Crisis downplayed
Kangema MP John Michuki, the designate Minister for National Security and a confidant of the President, said to have taken part in the 14-day consultation before the new team was named, called a Press conference to say Kibaki's Government was not crumbling.
Downplaying the obvious crisis, Michuki declared that 24 hours in politics was a long time and added that those who had declined their positions would be replaced.
He said there were many MPs willing to take up the positions left vacant, adding that it was a great honour for one to be appointed a minister out of the 222 MPs in Parliament.
He said majority of those who rejected the positions thought they would be appointed ministers. "I wish I could give up mine and it be given to somebody who can do it better. We are here to serve, not for status," Michuki said.
He took issue with those who declined positions saying they should not have done it through the Press, but through proper channels.
Only one new appointee, Mwala MP John Katuku, showed up at Maji House and walked to the minister's office to take up his seat even before he took the oath of office. Mukurweini MP Mutahi Kagwe, who was appointed Information minister, spelt out his vision also before being formally handed the instruments of office.
And as pandemonium ensued in Government, Kanu chairman Uhuru Kenyatta and the leading LDP lights called for a snap election "to free the country from the dangers of an ineffective and unpopular Cabinet".
He, however, said Kibaki still had the opportunity to listen to the voice of reason and agree to initiate dialogue with all political parties, to chart a way forward for the country, in a manner acceptable to all.
"In the absence of this, I am calling on you, Mr President, to take the next honourable action, and to immediately call elections to bring leadership and direction back to Kenya." He said the country was in a crisis and had entered a dangerous phase of its political development.
"I am calling upon you, Mr President, as a matter of urgency, to take control and prevent the crisis threatening to divide and destroy our nation."
Walkout inevitable
But even as Kibaki waded through his worst nightmare yet in office the Government's Chief Whip Norman Nyaga conceded the Cabinet walkout was "inevitable as the new line-up had failed to appreciate political realities in the country".
He blamed President Kibaki's political advisers for "provoking" the crisis arguing they had failed to read the country's political mood and had advised him to lock out popular and efficient MPs in a line-up that also ignored stated expectations of key political parties, and specifically Ford-Kenya.
And in an apparent criticism of the new line up, Nyaga said some of the new ministers could not be relied on to effectively take care of the Government interests in Parliament and this, therefore, left it more exposed to hostile Opposition schemes.
LDP challenged President Kibaki to call snap elections now to test his popularity. The party convened a Press conference attended by over 25 MPs and declared it was ready to battle out in the snap polls.
Immediate former Cabinet ministers Kalonzo Musyoka, Najib Balala, William ole Ntiamama, Ochillo Ayacko, Prof. Anyango' Nyongo and a host of former assistant ministers attended the Press conference. Langata MP Raila Odinga was absent as he is in Uganda on a private visit.
LDP chairman David Musila said that the President had shown that he was working with the minority Kenyans after the cabinet appointment. They said that 10 out of the 29 cabinet positions were given to people from one community.
Announcing the party's decision to decline all appointments to the Cabinet, Ford-K accused the President of dishonesty and "shortchanging the party".
The announcement by Kombo, who was retained as Local Government minister, came after an urgent National Executive Council-cum-Parliamentary Group meeting at the party's Jaramogi Oginga Odinga House headquarters.
Party not consulted
Kombo said the party was not ready to serve in the Cabinet as it was not consulted before the appointments were made and, therefore, felt shortchanged. "We have been surprised and disappointed that the President decided to take such a momentous decision without any consultation or regard to the view and feelings of my party, its leadership and membership," he said.
He said: "Ford Kenya will not take positions given to them in the Government."
He was accompanied by MPs Enoch Kibunguchy, Shitanda, Bonny Khalwale, Were, Christine Mango, Daniel Khamasi, Sylvestor Wakoli, Wetang'ula, Raphael Wanjala, Munyes and Wekesa. However, party members Newton Kulundu, Mukhisa Kituyi and Moses Akaranga, who were named into the Cabinet, did not attend the press conference. Kulundu later criticised Kombo's decision but the stand of the other two appointees is yet to be known.
Ojodeh telephoned KTN on Thursday morning to declare that he wished to stand by his electorate in Nyanza, who gave the proposed constitution a resounding No.
Ojodeh was the only pro-Orange politician named minister in what was being described as the new "Banana Cabinet". Only one former Banana campaigner, Dr Chris Murungaru, was dropped alongside the Orange brigade.
Raw deal
In declining to take up his appointment, Ojodeh allayed rumours that the Liberal Democratic Party coerced him to quit the Cabinet.
Ojodeh said he opted out of Government because the President gave Luo Nyanza a raw deal. "I expected him to at least replace those removed from Nyanza with MPs from the same area and in ministries with equal weight," said Ojodeh.
He said the ministry given to him was also inconsequential because it could not assist the people he represents. Ojodeh had been appointed minister for Environment and Natural Resources.
The MP was also unhappy that the President allocated 10 prime ministries to MPs from the Mt Kenya region.
"The Mt Kenya clique has hoodwinked the Luo community and as you can see this is a tribal Cabinet," said Ojodeh.
Before the flurry of big Nos to Kibaki's appointments, Eastern Province had the lion's share in the Cabinet, with seven positions, followed by Western Province with six. Coast, Central, and Rift Valley each walked away with four slots, followed by one each for North Eastern and Nairobi.

Original_Search_Title: Ford-K Supporters Applaud Decision to Reject Job Offers
Headline: Kenya: Ford-K Supporters Applaud Decision to Reject Job Offers
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Ford Kenya's decision to pull out of the new Cabinet was applauded by party supporters in Bungoma District yesterday.
Speaking on behalf of the Bukusu Elders Association, patron Lucas Watta said: "President Kibaki seems not to appreciate the support Ford Kenya has given him since 2002 and it was time he was left alone to his devices. We cannot be taken for granted for that long".
Mr Watta also told Vice President Moody Awori, Trade and Industry minister Mukhisa Kituyi and Labour minister Newton Kulundu to follow Mr Musikari Kombo's example by declining their appointments. Mr Kombo is Ford Kenya's national chairman.
Mr Watta said Luyia leaders should focus on promoting unity and winning the next General Election "now that Narc has disintegrated".
He said: "They must not lose time and ground now if they want to continue being leaders of the community because those that opt to stay on will face the wrath of voters and be kicked out of parliament."
Earlier, residents of Webuye had told Mr Kombo not to take up the appointment.
They claimed that the new line-up did not show any appreciation for the community which supported the Banana team during the campaigns for a proposed Constitution.
Pastor David Walukhu of King Jesus Church, said the new Cabinet would strengthen the opponents of the Government rather than weaken them.
"The President has been making mistake after mistake in his appointments since the death of the late Vice President Michael Wamalwa, and this time those rebelling against him will multiply," he said.
Pastor Walukhu accused President Kibaki of being insincere by not going with the peoples' wishes.
He claimed that Ford-K was being weakened silently by the President as the Luyia community drifted apart with little benefit from the Government.
The community, he added, had not been rewarded adequately because MPs who supported the Banana were left out of the appointments.
But councillor John Ngome urged Malava MP Soita Shitanda to take up the appointment and assist his people while in Government.
Mr Ngome said Mr Shitanda should stop asking for non-existent positions but be contented with the one he was offered.
The Webuye civic leader said Ford-K as a party was well represented in Government and people should not show discomfort.
An Elder, Mr Musi Makete, praised the President for retaining the Mr Awori his Vice President. He said the Luyia community was going to be united behind him. He asked Luyias to support Ford-K and the VP to bring them together.
In Busia, party officials and supporters lamented that the President had given them a raw deal.
Led by the district's party co-ordinator, Mr Jack Wambulwa, the officials said that they had been short-changed as they had been promised "lucrative" returns after the referendum.

Original_Search_Title: Orange Team Calls for Snap Elections
Headline: Kenya: Orange Team Calls for Snap Elections
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange Democratic Movement (ODM) yesterday told President Kibaki to call snap elections as the only option to stem the current political crisis.
The movement said there was no need to subject the country to anxiety following the refusal by ministers and assistant ministers to take up appointments in the Cabinet he announced on Wednesday night.
"Since the President refused to initiate any consultation with parliamentary political parties, he now has no choice but to resolve this crisis through a General Election," the movement said in a statement read by one of its luminaries, Mr William Ruto.
However, Mr Ruto said ODM had not entirely shut the door on Mr Kibaki if he wished to engage in dialogue with the coalition partners.
The Kanu secretary-general said the election was the only option for the Head of State "because it has become quite clear that the President cannot form an acceptable government precisely because his perimeter of choice limits him to a small clique, which does not enjoy popular support".
The Orange leaders blamed President Kibaki for the current political crisis for having failed to heed their calls to dissolve Parliament or initiate dialogue immediately after the defeat of the Wako Draft Constitution and instead chose to deal with individual politicians.
At the same time, the ODM postponed a rally, which had been scheduled for tomorrow in Mombasa until political temperatures were lowered.
There was no need, Mr Ruto said during a news conference at Parliament Buildings in Nairobi, to put their followers in confrontation with the police.
Dropped Cabinet ministers Kalonzo Musyoka, Najib Balala, Ochilo Ayacko, Anyang' Nyong'o, William ole Ntimama and Linah Kilimo were among the 32 MPs who attended the Press conference.
Earlier, the Liberal Democratic Party made similar calls, arguing that President Kibaki had lost the required numbers to rule.
The party said President Kibaki "is now a minority president, ruling the country with a minority in power."
LDP chairman David Musila said: "We are calling upon the President to call snap elections...we think this is very clear...he has no option but to do that."
The party said although they had asked President Kibaki to hold consultations with the ODM after the referendum results, he had ignored the calls.
He said the President had given corruption a window with the abolition of the Governance and Ethics department, formerly held by Mr John Githongo.
The abolition of the department had let loose those who were behind the Goldenberg and Anglo-Leasing corruption scandals, which were still unresolved, he said.
And he added: "We shall continue to highlight the fact that in rejecting the Wako draft, the people also rejected the failed leadership the President has given to the nation."
The Mwingi South MP said there was no democratic country where the losers were rewarded and the winners completely sidelined.
The exclusion of the winning leaders from the Cabinet, he said, was a rejection of the people of Kenya.
He said President Kibaki had punished ministers who were allied to the LDP because of their principled stand on the Constitution.
They asked the President to recall Parliament in January to take appropriate action on the crisis and the constitutional review.
The ODM defeated President Kibaki's Banana group in the referendum with those opposing the proposed new constitution garnering 3.5 million to 2.5.
It was during their celebrations rally at Nairobi's Uhuru park that they called on the President to dissolve Parliament and call for snap elections.
Yesterday, LDP leaders, held a one-hour meeting at Hotel Intercontinental hotel before addressing a press conference at Parliament buildings two hours later.
At the morning meeting were former ministers Kalonzo Musyoka, William ole Ntimama, Anyang' Nyong'o, Najib Balala, Ochilo Ayacko, Mr Musila, Joseph Kamotho, Andrew Ligale, Joe Khamisi, George Khaniri, Orwa Ojode and Peter Odoyo. They were also part of the 32 MPs who were at the press conference.
Mr Musila said: "We stand at the lowest point in our democratic history. For the first time in our history, Kenya has a minority government and a minority President."
He said there was no democratic country where the losers were rewarded and the winners completely sidelined.
The exclusion of the winning leaders from the Cabinet, LDP said, was a rejection of the people of Kenya.
He said president Kibaki had punished ministers who were allied to LDP bacause of their principled stand on the constitution.
The asked the president to recall parliament in January to take appropriate action on the crisis and the constitutional review

Original_Search_Title: Maathai: Put Off Swearing-in
Headline: Kenya: Maathai: Put Off Swearing-in
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The swearing-in of the Cabinet today should be postponed until political parties are consulted over the appointments, an assistant minister said yesterday.
In an exclusive interview with Nation, Prof Wangari Maathai welcomed her re-appointment but told the President to consider dialogue with all politicians allied to the Orange and Banana camps.
The Peace Nobel laureate said: "It is very important for me and others to express our gratitude to the President for appointing us to his Cabinet even though we were all not consulted. "I would like to appeal to him to postpone the swearing-in of the Cabinet today. He should, in my view, call all leaders of the political parties that form the Narc Government so that dialogue may prevail and together, they can chart the way forward."
"My colleagues in Ford Kenya have turned down the appointments among others and they all declined on the same grounds,..... they were not consulted. This has been the culture in the past but in the light of what is happening today, I recommend that the parties be given time to consult among themselves and decide who it is that they want included in the Cabinet," added the Environment assistant minister.
"Let the President extend an olive branch to all political parties who form the ruling coalition. Let him call to a round-table dialogue and consultations because we do not want to divide Kenyans along ethnic lines. Let it not be felt that we have left out the Luos because Raila Odinga is not there or that we have left out the Kambas because Kalonzo Musyoka is not with them," she said.
Prof Maathai, who is the MP for Tetu, Nyeri, said she was among those pushing for the postponement of the November 21 referendum to iron out the contentious issues on the proposed Constitution.
"I urge the President to retrace his steps and put this country first now that the referendum is over. Maybe he fell short of choices when trying to conduct ethnic balancing but for now, what I would urge him to do is to consult all those he wants to appoint, even if it takes longer. All of us know the price that has been paid by Kenyans in the run-up to the multi-party democracy. Let us respect people like Mr Kenneth Matiba, Mr Charles Rubia, Mr Oginga Odinga and others who suffered while pursuing this goal by reconciling our differences through consultations," she said.
Prof Maathai said those who declined the appointments were representatives of their constituencies and were acting within their rights.
"Nobody lets you know and you never have an inkling that you will be appointed. If there is a system where people are called and notified, they may be in a position to either say Yes or No before the decision is made public," she added.

Original_Search_Title: The Orange 7 Who Rebelled Against Boss
Headline: Kenya: The Orange 7 Who Rebelled Against Boss
Body_Text:
Nairobi — It was hailed as a mark of the opening up of the democratic space in Kenya.
That seven of President Kibaki's Cabinet ministers could openly rebel against him and campaign against his wishes and still retain their posts, was cited as an example of just how much things had changed in Kenya since Narc came to power.
Former ministers Raila Odinga, Najib Balala, Linah Kilimo, Kalonzo Musyoka, Ochillo Ayacko, William ole Ntimama and Anyang' Nyong'o joined Kanu in leading the Orange campaign, which culminated in the Government's defeat in the November 21 referendum.
Some of them even dared the President to sack them, but their wish was not granted until the entire Cabinet was sent packing on November 23.
Mr Odinga, the immediate former Roads and Public Works minister, was viewed as the leader of the Orange team.
An acknowledged mass organiser, Mr Odinga invokes admiration and fanatical support among his followers. But his enemies view him as a rabble-rouser, who is never satisfied with anything.
The main cause of confrontation between Mr Odinga's supporters and those who backed the rejected Draft Constitution was whether or not to create the post of an executive prime minister.
Mr Odinga held the view that the President should share power with an executive prime minister, but the opponents saw it as a sinister move by the Lang'ata MP to grab power through the back door.
They suggested that anyone who wanted power should subject themselves to the rigours of an election to test their popularity and then enjoy the fruits of their sweat.
Mr Odinga and a group of former Cabinet ministers had abandoned Kanu prior to the 2002 General Election and teamed up with the National Alliance Party of Kenya (NAK) leaders in Narc to remove the former ruling party from power.
He was one of the Orange leaders who those in the Banana camp did not want to see included in the new Cabinet.
Mr Musyoka, on the other hand, has continued to confound both friend and foe because of his rebellion.
Largely seen as a conformist during Mr Daniel arap Moi's rule, Mr Musyoka shocked many political pundits when he stuck with other Kanu rebels to oppose the imposition of Mr Uhuru Kenyatta as the party's flag bearer during the 2002 polls.
Many had expected Mr Musyoka to be among the first to crumble under the persuasion of Kanu operators and return to the former ruling party. But he remained steadfast, at one time even joking that he would outlast those who doubted his resolve to leave Kanu.
In the end, it was Mr Musalia Mudavadi who jumped ship and was rewarded with a promotion to the Number Two slot while Mr Musyoka remained in LDP.
Now being mentioned as a possible LDP presidential candidate in the next General Election, Mr Musyoka has gained the reputation of a political firebrand, publicly challenging President Kibaki during the Orange campaigns.
Another politician who has lately taken the President on is Prof Nyong'o.
In the final stages of the Orange campaign, Prof Nyong'o appeared to have assumed the role of challenging the President's decisions and public conduct.
He was the first member of the dissolved Cabinet to publicly complain that the President should check his language and not use certain words and phrases, which could be interpreted to be swear words.
An articulate politician, he gives the impression of a man who would be more at ease in a university lecture hall than at a political rally.
Prof Nyong'o's Orange campaign statements never once elicited a response from either President Kibaki or his supporters.
Mrs Kilimo's defection from the Banana to the Orange camp came as a surprise to many. She had earlier supported the Draft Constitution, saying it had good proposals, especially for women and children.
The former Immigration minister's decision to decamp was said to have been influenced by the mood in her Marakwet East constituency, which was overwhelmingly opposed to the Draft.
But once she decamped, Mrs Kilimo became a fierce critic of the Government, at times claiming that public service jobs were being given out on tribal considerations. She, however, said she would remain loyal to President Kibaki while campaigning for Orange.
Mr Balala has emerged as a major political force in Mombasa and Coast politics since defeating Mr Shariff Nassir, who died last week, in the Mvita parliamentary race in 2002.
That Coast voted overwhelmingly for Orange at the referendum, is credited to his organisational abilities.
Mr Ntimama was one of the fiercest critics of the Draft, especially the proposals on land which, he said, would deny the Maasai the right to own and benefit economically from national parks and game reserves.

Original_Search_Title: A Mixed Bag of Newcomers At the Top
Headline: Kenya: A Mixed Bag of Newcomers At the Top
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Although he is serving his first term in Parliament, Mr Kagwe's performance as the chairman of the 11-member departmental committee on Finance, Planning and Trade has been commendable.
He's married to a daughter of Internal Security minister John Michuki.
The committee has been instrumental in polishing and ensuring the passage of several economic legislations by MPs.
Born in 1958, Mr Kagwe went to Kihate Primary School and Kagumo High School before proceeding to the University of Nairobi for a Bachelor of Commerce degree which he obtained in 1981.
A Masters of Business Administration holder, Mr Kagwe worked at the Standard Ltd from 1987 to 1991 as the advertising manager and later commercial director.
He is a director of a leading public relations firm in Nairobi.
JOSHUA OJODE (Minister, Environment and Natural Resources)
Born on December 29, 1959, Mr Ojode holds a Bachelor of Arts degree specialising in Environmental and International Business Studies from Ramapo State University College of New Jersey, US. He attended Ratanga Primary School and Maranda High School.
He was first elected MP for Ndhiwa constituency in 1994, a seat he has held to date.
He previously served in the Narc Government as an assistant minister for Lands and later in the Foreign Affairs ministry in the same capacity.
HENRY OBWOCHA (Minister, Planning and National Development)
Was born in Igenaitambe Village, West Mugirango, Nyamira District in 1949.
He was educated at Kianungu Primary School, then Kisii High School and later went to the University of Nairobi from where he graduated with Bachelor of Commerce degree in 1973. Mr Obwocha has served in various positions such as Pyrethrum Board of Kenya as a chief accountant and as part time lecturer at Egerton University.
He was elected MP for West Mugirango on a Ford People ticket in the last General Election and went on to be appointed as an assistant minister for Finance.
GIDEON KONCHELLAH (Minister, Immigration and Registration of Persons)
He served as Health assistant minister before the Cabinet was dissolved.
Mr Konchellah, 51, first went to Parliament after the 2002 polls when he floored former powerful internal security minister Julius Sunkuli for the Kilgoris seat.
Retaining the former army officer in the Government is largely aimed at appeasing the Maasai electorate.
MOSES AKARANGA (Minister, Public Service )
The new minister of State for Public Service, Mr Moses Akaranga, takes over the ministry formerly occupied by William ole Ntimama.
Mr Akaranga is serving his first term in Parliament having trounced former Vice-President Musalia Mudavadi in the 2002 General Election.
Mr Akaranga came to Parliament on a Narc ticket through the Ford Kenya party. He was defeated by the No Vote in the November 21 referendum.
His immediate task would be to deal with the crisis over 1,600 sacked nurses and clinical officers who were employed in June following a strike by civil servants.
MAINA KAMANDA (Minister, Gender, Culture and Sports)
A staunch supporter of the Kibaki Government, the new Gender, Sports and Culture minister is a former Local Government assistant minister. Mr Kamanda is a former councillor at the Nairobi city council and has been a long time chairman of the Democratic Party in the city.
In the November 21 referendum, Mr Kamanda, whose star appears to be on the rise, was the Nairobi coordinator of the Banana team campaigns.
During the Kanu regime, Mr Kamanda narrowly escaped life imprisonment after he was accused of urging that former President Moi be shot if he did not surrender power.
His re-election to Parliament in 2002 was marked by controversy in the nominations after he was allegedly defeated by businessman Jimnah Mbaru.
During the referendum campaigns, Mr Kamanda (Starehe MP) was among MPs calling for the sacking of former ministers aligned to the Orange Democratic Movement.
MUTUA KATUKU (Minister, Water)
The new Water minister is a former assistant minister for Finance. Mr Katuku, 40, holds a Bachelor of Arts from the University of Nairobi and a Masters degree from Kenyatta University.
A former student leader at the University of Nairobi, Mr Katuku was first elected as the Mwala MP in 1997 on a Social Democratic Party ticket which was led by Health minister Charity Ngilu.
In 2002, he was re-elected on a Narc ticket and was appointed an assistant minister for Finance.
However, after his re-election, Mr Katuku appears to have severed his links with Mrs Ngilu and initially appeared to have been picked to spearhead the Banana team campaign in the November 21 referendum in Ukambani where it lost heavily to the Orange Democratic Movement.
Mr Katuku, who holds the philosophy "hard work rewards" is currently a member of the coordinating committee of Narc party.
SULEIMAN SHAKOMBO (Minister, National Heritage)
Mr Suleiman Shakombo first went to Parliament in 1998 when he won the Likoni seat on a Shirikisho Party of Kenya ticket after trouncing Mr Salim Mwavumo in the 1997 General Election.
He recaptured the seat in 2002 on a Narc ticket and was appointed an assistant minister for Livestock and Fisheries Development in February this year.
Mr Shakombo has been a great supporter of President Kibaki since 2003.
He had earlier served in the provincial administration.
MOHAMMED KUTI (Minister, Youth Affairs)
Born in 1964 Mohammed Abdi Kuti joined Parliament for the first time in 2002 on a Kanu ticket. He is the MP for Isiolo North constituency. A medical doctor, he was in private practice in Isiolo Town until his entry into politics in 2001.
He attended Garbatulla and St Kizito primary schools. He joined Isiolo Boys and later Kangaru High School.
He graduated with Bachelor of Medicine in 1990 from the University of Nairobi.
Between 1995 and 1997, he served as Isiolo's medical officer of health.

Original_Search_Title: Delay After Newsflash Causes Anxiety
Headline: Kenya: Delay After Newsflash Causes Anxiety
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Anxiety gripped the country as a newsflash on Kenya Broadcasting Corporation announced that President Kibaki would name his new Cabinet at 7pm.
Kenyans rushed home or the nearest pubs and other entertainment places to catch the historic event on radio or television.
But anxiety turned to disappointment after the Presidential Press Service shifted the naming of the new Cabinet to 7.30pm and again to 8pm.
This is the first time in Kenya's history that the Cabinet has been named at night. During the reign of first President Jomo Kenyatta and his successor, President Moi, the tradition was to announce the new Cabinet at 1pm or 4pm.
Earlier in the day, Kenyans had waited anxiously as the deadline set by President Kibaki to name his new Cabinet fast approached.
Team lost heavily
When he dissolved his Cabinet on November 23, following the announcement of the referendum in which the Banana team lost heavily to the Orange team, President Kibaki promised to name the Cabinet in two weeks. Kenyans had expected the new Cabinet after just a few days, but this was not the case. President Kibaki started holding consultations with various leaders, including former President Moi.
Before naming his new Cabinet, President Kibaki also held consultations with Christian and Muslim religious leaders and foreign envoys accredited to Kenya.
Sources said the delay in naming the new Cabinet was a result of a last-minute problem with Kanu MPs. But State House issued a statement denying rumours that some MPs had turned down appointments.

Original_Search_Title: Why Kenya is Still the Place to Invest in
Headline: Kenya: Why Kenya is Still the Place to Invest in
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Those who went to school in the 1970s may remember a famous line that geography texts used to describe Kenya. The line was: Kenya, the land of contrasts. The country is still a place of contrasts, if not contradictions.
Sample this: Just as the American Government was renewing advice to its citizens not to step into Kenya last month, the Kenya Tourist Board announced that the country would register the biggest tourism boom in the last 16 years.
It was also in the same week that French ambassador Hurbert Fournier declared that his country had no intention of stopping its citizens from visiting Kenya.
His government, the ambassador said," was convinced the country was not only relatively safe, but was also happy with the Kenya Government's commitment to combating terrorism".
Yet it was the same week that protagonists in the Banana and Orange camps were locked in the constitution referendum circus, and threatening to set the country aflame if either lost the vote.
The country isn't burning
Well, the referendum was won and lost but the country is not burning. Indeed, for two weeks, it has had no Cabinet and it is not like the skies are about to come crashing down on us.
At the height of the referendum campaigns, two of the best thinkers in Kibaki's Cabinet who were on opposite sides of the debate, Dr Mukhisa Kituyi and Prof Anyang' Nyong'o, said at different forums that they still had great faith in Kenya's capacity to regain its place as the regional economic dynamo.
And this at a time when their colleagues in the political madhouse were breathing hell and brimstone!
Because this is the festive season, let's talk tourism. The sector accounts for 13 per cent of the country's gross domestic product and 15 per cent of its foreign exchange earnings. It employs about two million people, directly and indirectly.
Last year, the country had its second best tourism year. The sector was the second best foreign-exchange earner after tea, hitting the Sh42.5 billion mark.
And that was a 70 per cent rise from 2002, which was messed up by the terrorist bombing of Paradise Hotel at Kikambala just before Christmas of the same year.
Such a miraculous recovery in just under a year is, indeed, testimony that Kenya is still the place to be.
Mark you, all this has happened at a time when Britain and the US, which provide the third and fifth largest chunks of tourists visiting Kenya, had issued travel advisories against Kenya.
There is a big lesson here for Americans and Britons, and which their ally, the French, have publicly admitted. It is that Kenya is as safe, if not safer, than many other countries.
Granted, no place in the world is safe from international terrorism. This is because, by its very nature, terrorism is an act of ambush. Nobody is quite safe from ambush. We can only prepare and seal as many loopholes that terrorists exploit as possible.
The second lesson for Americans and the British is that instead of imposing travel bans, they could do much better by helping Kenya become safer.
In the recent past, both the British and the US embassies in Nairobi have had occasion to congratulate Kenya on its anti-terror campaigns. One wishes they would go further by doubling or tripling their anti-terror aid to Kenya.
Now that the country has clearly been identified as a flash point for international terrorists - what with the 1998 and 2002 terror attacks that left about 300 people dead - we could do well with just a fraction (yes, a fraction) of what Americans and the British give, say, Israel or Saudi Arabia, to combat terrorism.
And why not? The al-Qaeda is now operating in our next-door neighbour, Somalia. In fact, British shipping corporations are already lobbying that the Somali coast-line be declared a war zone!
But a safer Kenya will not only be for the good of American and British tourists; a safer Kenya will mean a great deal to their business communities and industrialists.
Only last month, the United Nations Conference on Trade and Development said the eastern African region is about to become the second largest trading bloc in Africa after Nigeria.
Direct foreign investment
It noted that despite the many challenges facing the region - low incomes, unemployment, political uncertainty, and of course, terrorism - the good-nature of the people, their work ethic and unexploited natural resources give the area a high ranking in attracting direct foreign investment.
The report was corroborated by the International Chamber of Commerce. The two have since prepared an investment guide for the eastern African region.
The UNCTD/ICC guide on Kenya first discusses the human and unique natural resource base that makes the country a leading tourist choice of destiny. It moves on to tabulate Kenya's contribution to world peace and humanitarian efforts.
Lastly, it discuses issues of trade and investment. This includes, taxation, labour and investor-protection.
It urges anybody with cash to spare to consider Kenya and eastern Africa in general as the place to invest in.
China, the Far and Middle East are also talking about Kenya and the region. If only our politicians saw this country the way the outside world is seeing it!
In the meantime, now that the festive season is here, we can only repeat what every tourist visiting our coast will be reading wherever he or she goes: Karibu Kenya, Hakuna Matata!
Mr Mbuvi is a lawyer and security consultant.

Original_Search_Title: We Are Glad to Be Free, Says Nyong'o
Headline: Kenya: We Are Glad to Be Free, Says Nyong'o
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Former Planning minister Anyang' Nyong'o said it was good that the Orange group had been left out of the Government.
"Now we can focus on important issues with constitutional reform. We thought he would disintegrate us but at least, we are now still intact and that was our greatest fear," said the Kisumu Rural MP, who campaigned against the Wako Draft.
"We are now going to continue with constitutional reforms with a new force," he added.
However, he added, the Orange group would give a comprehensive statement on the matter today.
He spoke to the Nation at the Serena hotel after launching a book on Philanthropy.
Former Immigration minister Linah Jebii Kilimo said the new line-up is not what they expected.
She said the new appointments were aimed at getting rid of ministers who opposed the proposed constitution.
"When I made my decision to join the Orange group, it wasn't disobedience but I was moving with my people's wishes," said Mrs Kilimo.
Mbita MP Otieno Kajwang' termed the new line up as a Banana Cabinet. "I am personally comfortable with it. It is not a reform Cabinet," he said.
He said the ODM had now enough time to concentrate on Constitution review.
Law Society of Kenya chairman Tom Ojienda criticised the new Cabinet, saying it lacked a professional touch and was guided by selfish political considerations.
"There is nothing new in the Cabinet. It is not lean and it is unfortunate," he said.
The President, he added, had "completely misread the mood of Kenyans."
But LSK council member Evans Monari said the fact that President Kibaki left out ODM leaders showed his boldness.
"It is the best thing that the President has done to show that he is in control," he said.
"He has picked those he can trust and work with. It would be good for the unity of the government," said Mr Monari.
Constitutional lawyer Kibe Mungai said the new Cabinet was good.
Mr Mungai added that the President had observed the importance of collective responsibility which will require appointment of those loyal to the government.
Former International Commission of Jurists Kenya chapter chairman Otiende Amollo said some of the appointees were of low calibre.
Mr Amollo said President Kibaki had shown "defiance to the wishes of Kenyans" in so far as he ignored the members of ODM.
Cotu secretary general Francis Atwoli said the naming of the cabinet was the most important thing.
It did not matter who President Kibaki appointed into the team, Mr Atwoli said.
"Even if he was to fill the entire cabinet with the Banana team, it is within his constitutional rights," said Mr Atwoli in reaction to the naming of the cabinet.
Kiharu MP Kembi Gitura was elated at his appointment as Assistant Minister for Production and Marketing
Speaking by telephone he said, "I am very happy at the appointment and am sure we will have a very cohesive government that can work together. I believe the consultation has helped to come up and it is an honour for me and the people of Kiharu."
Assistant Minister Beth Mugo who remains at the Education ministry said, "I am happy to finish what I started but am unhappy that too few women have been considered in the appointments."
Subukia MP Koigi Wa Wamwere who has been appointed into the Information and Communication Ministry, said, "I did not expect to be appointed and I am glad that I have been considered by the President. It is my hope that I will be able to serve Kenyans better and also advance the cause of the poor people."
On the new Cabinet he said," It is my sincere hope and prayer that we will be able to work as a team ad deliver services to Kenyans ."
Machakos Town MP Daudi Mwanzia said he was shocked at the appointments, saying some of those appointed could not deliver.
Keiyo North MP Lucas Chepkitony said he was "surprised" by the appointment, and questioned the quality of some of those appointed.
But Kibwezi MP Kalembe Ndile, appointed assistant minister for Tourism and Wildlife said he was happy for being appointed.
The assistant minister said he did not expect to be appointed, even if he had worked hard to popularise the Banana campaign in Ukambani.
However, he would continue fighting for the rights of the common man regardless of his new status.
Baringo East MP Mr Asman Kamama, now appointed assistant minister said the President did a commendable job under difficult circumstances.
He said the new cabinet was regionally balanced and would deliver since it would be free from wrangles.

Original_Search_Title: New Cabinet Must Show Some Humility
Headline: Kenya: New Cabinet Must Show Some Humility
Body_Text:
Nairobi — In a two-way contest, there must be a winner and a loser. Who lost the
constitutional referendum?
It was Kenyans because they do not have the Constitution they
wanted. Who are the winners?
Those who campaigned with a hidden agenda, on a platform of vendetta and
lies. Otherwise, there cannot be winners when there is no new
constitution.
We now know that the underlying campaign was not about the constitution,
but about settling old scores over the MoU, which ordinary Kenyans were not
part of. And it was about the arrogance of the sacked Cabinet.
Then we must ask: What went wrong? Why were we betrayed after voting on
a reform agenda? And who are the culprits?
Let those who were in forefront rest while we judge them. They are
giving us no space to judge them.
What Parliament did we put in place in 2002? What was the first action
of this Parliament? To increase their salaries and allowances! The second?
To improve their gratuities and pensions! The third? Their medical insurance
schemes. Fourth? House and mortgage allowances, their security, car
purchase, their offices, MP-controlled CDFs, and so on. This list is endless
and shameful.
Kenyan ministers pocket close to Sh1 million a month (over $13,000), and
thus rank in the top 10 most highly paid legislators in the world. Over 55
per cent of our population lives below the poverty line.
In endorsing these payments, they were all united. This unity was based
on selfishness and naked greed. Why then the disunity, when it came to such
an important issue affecting all Kenyans?
Was the campaign about confidence in government or on a new
Constitution? Why all those lies to the public?
Who gave the referendum "victors" the mandate to start a new process
alone? This was not part of the referendum agenda. We can only re-start it
together.
A lot of lies culminated in the debacle of November 21. What happened
has to do with the behaviour of the former Cabinet. From the Opposition,
they became ministers overnight. Before the crowning, they were accessible
and friendly to each other and to the public.
Then came the Cabinet. The cellphone numbers were suddenly changed. They
surrounded themselves with personal assistants, and the secretaries were
taught to say: "We will return your call when the minister is available."
The calls had not been returned by the time the ministers were
dismissed.
This was the height of arrogance and aloofness, one of the causes of the
referendum debacle.
Our colleagues, people we had worked with, could no longer
consult. There was no need for new ideas. Everyone was assumed to be a
job-seeker. Had they been accessible, they would have been warned that they
had abandoned the election manifesto.
The human rights issues, the Nyayo House atrocities, and the ethnic
cleansing of yesteryear were conveniently forgotten, or side-stepped. You
see, the ministers were too busy.
Those chosen in the new Cabinet should remember that there are many
talented Kenyans. These people want to contribute ideas about a Kenya for
all. A Kenya where we work or do business freely, under a government that
addresses poverty and consults its own professionals.
In other African countries (like in Namibia), you will see a prime
minister in a super-market with his family without a convoy of
bodyguards. You will see ministers driving themselves on weekends. Our new
ministers must show great humility.
The Narc victory reflected people's power. It was not meant to create an
un-reachable clique.
Karl Marx once said that Western democracy would work up to the point
where Parliament realises that it can vote the whole Treasury into their
pockets. Kenya's Parliament is about to do just that. Then they will kill
democracy.
Our Orange compatriots should stop chest-thumping. This was not a
referendum about the Government, of which they were part, and therefore
equally to blame.
Our Banana compatriots were not the losers. The people did not read the
document. Ask yourselves what happened to the issue of isolating only
contentious clauses and debating them? Who short-changed who?
Let Kenyans take a short break. Let the tempers cool. Let the lies
sink. Let us concentrate, for a while, on creating the 500,000 jobs a
year.
Prof Kariuki teaches at the University of Venda, South Africa.

Original_Search_Title: Should Botswana Emulate the Swiss War Against HIV/Aids?
Headline: Botswana: Should Botswana Emulate the Swiss War Against HIV/Aids?
Body_Text:
Soft talk and Botswana's smooth war against AIDS does not seem to be getting positive results
With Botswana and Sub-Saharan Africa's anti-climaxing war against HIV/Aids, is it not time for the countries in the region to review their fighting tactics?
SADC journalists who recently visited Switzerland at the invitation of the Swiss government asked this question. Most of them argued that Botswana could learn a lot from the war against AIDS experiences of Switzerland.
Rather than dealing with the AIDS question under the cover of cultural values, the Swiss take a gloves-off approach in their fight against the scourge.
The advertisement campaigns in the Swiss media are like x-rated movies from Hollywood, but the Swiss HIV/AIDS officials are quick to defend the strategy because that is the best way for the message to hit home, they say.
"We are explicit and we are often pressurised and critiqued by the pressure groups, but this is welcome and does not bother us", says Roger Staub, head of the section on HIV/AIDS at the Swiss Federal Office of Public Health.
Staub is the man at the helm of AIDS campaign in Switzerland, a job he has done for the last 20 years.
With a population of around 7.5 million people, there are only about 40 000 people affected by the scourge in Switzerland, according to Staub.
Staub says that the low figures result from the tight stance that they have taken in spreading the message about Aids, which make an impact on the people's behaviour, the authorities here say.
In one of the advertisements that were shown at a special Swiss AIDS exhibition at the Museum Barengasse in Zurich, some of the people in the television advertisements show women cutting off bananas and frying a russian sausage in a pan and at the end of the advertisement, a message goes by; 'you cannot have sex without a condom'.
Usually, a banana or a russian is used to represent the penis. The message struck home despite criticism from pressure groups, according to Staub.
These are some of the channels of communication that would not be accepted by Botswana viewers because of cultural issues as sex issues are not to be discussed explicitly in public.
Botswana is currently one of the worst hit countries in the world and when you mention the AIDS problem south of the Sahara, all eyes look to Botswana. Apart from the hard war on AIDS, Switzerland is a tolerant country. Some of the things that are covered by a culture of denial in Botswana like prostitution are tolerated.
The Swiss community also acknowledges that there is drug problem and they also acknowledge that there are same-sex relationships.
The key fight against the scourge in Switzerland is focused on prevention, which resulted in the formation of Swiss AIDS Federation (AHS) including representatives of the gay organisations.
This is made to improve protective practices in the population without changing sexual behaviour.
There are special ways of dealing with the situation because, like at the exhibition, there were displays of homosexual condoms that are designed specially for the people attracted to people of the same sex. Equally, in some parts of that country, injection drug addicts are taken off the risks of contracting the AIDS virus by selling needles to them in vending machines in parts of the Swiss cities.
These facilities for drug users are put in strategic points in Switzerland, to arrest the chances of attracting AIDS where condoms and injection equipment is provided.
Swiss authorities say that this is meant to help those drug users that are also involved in prostitution.
According to AIDS figures from Switzerland, the disease only began to spread in Switzerland in the 1970s. It initially affected men with same-sex partners and injecting drug users, but among heterosexual partners, it only started spreading in the 1980s.
However, through the use of antiretroviral drugs in 1995, the trend reversed and then it started to rise again after 2002 amongst homosexuals and immigrants from high AIDS prevalence countries.
The same would not be tolerated by the culture of denial in Botswana. Just recently, one column in the Monitor newspaper was perceived to be unrealistic for proposing the introduction of brothels in Botswana to curb the AIDS scourge in the country.
"The benefits of establishing brothels are many and varied. First and foremost, brothels can play a critical role in curbing the spread of HIV. As a way of combating the epidemic, people are being asked to abstain from sex; to be faithful; and condomise (the ABC strategy). Traditionally, our society used to frown upon pre-marital sex for obvious reasons", the column stated.
But weeks later after the column hit the streets; it became a toast for feminists.
"It amazes me that someone can begin to think that the root cause can turnout to be the solution. I am in fact more ashamed than amazed, wondering what can begin to run in the mind of a visitor who has just come into the country if they pick a newspaper and they are hit in their face by a title BROTHELS: ONLY HOPE TO CURB HIV/AIDS-what has happened to morality? What has happened to men who could speak life into the laws of the young?"
One Bella Rammolai later replied to the column. However, for women activists like Joyce Anderson, the nation should respect culture even in the fight against the Aids scourge.
She says showing explicit scenes on television in the name of the fight against AIDs would not be possible in Botswana because of cultural considerations.
"If you insult someone in public in Botswana, people are going lose trust in you, (batho ba tla a go tlhoboga)", argues Anderson.
Despite the ABC Aids crusade in Botswana, the country is one of the hugely affected countries in the Sub-Saharan region with about 300 000 affected by the scourge from the population of 1.7 million.

Original_Search_Title: Our Emperors Have No Clothes
Headline: Kenya: Our Emperors Have No Clothes
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Our placid collective response to the current political situation proves beyond doubt that Kenyans are remarkably easygoing people. We have no Cabinet. Our parliament has been suspended. And yet we continue to go about our daily business with equanamity.
That is, on the surface, at least. We are concerned, even if only vicariously, as we await the president's next move with bated breath, as we would wait for the end of a suspense-filled sequence in an action movie, knowing the film is a fiction that speaks little to our lives. But, still, this is not a normal situation.
We must worry for our future. Our conversations at every gathering over the past week attested to this - whether those gatherings were personal, political or professional. We had, as usual, endless theories as to what happened, what it meant and, most important, what is yet to come.
We expressed curiosity and shock at the president's meeting with our former president. My personal opinion - one not shared by those who think it is a sign of political maturity and statesmanship Ã is that Kibaki's openly seeking advice from Moi is not only a slap in our face, it is a warning that we are in for a fairly rough ride.
So, our president is consulting - publicly with people like Moi and church leaders, and no doubt privately with many others. We should be asking whom he is consulting privately Ã who is in his political think tank at this moment? Third, some constituencies are relatively more prepared for such consultations than others.
Obviously, the Orange and Banana camps will be busy refining their negotiating position, while the (mainstream) Christian churches have institutional, organisational structures that enable them to quickly come together and they continue to hold considerable ethical, moral, and political persuasive power.
MORE SECULAR constituencies are far less prepared. I know, for example, of at least two intellectual groupings that were analysing, consulting and trying to subtly influence the process of the referendum. But their institutional and organisational structures were not of the nature to allow them to promptly put forward a position given this unexpected hiatus the president has put us in. Neither do the ad hoc alliances and coalitions and groupings of so-called civil society, including the women's movement.
For civil society to turn to the so-called Orange Democratic Movement for that persuasive power would be a fruitless endeavour (pardon the pun). The Liberal Democratic Party took the stand it did for personal and political reasons - and I am not denying here the litany of betrayals it has experienced.
Kanu took the stand it did because it is now the official opposition party - we would be stupid indeed to believe that its younger incarnation has truly delivered itself from its past. Truth be told, none of our emperors has any clothes.
And so, in the absence of citizens able/organised enough to express their frustration beyond the vote, the result is that, as usual, the direction of political change will be driven by those with the most personalised and short-term of interests Ã our politicians.
The president will be balancing a Machiavellian approach to his new Cabinet with more principled and technocratic approaches. Naturally, the latter will lose out. What this means is that we can expect even less competence in the next Cabinet - let's face, a few of our former ministers actually had taken hold of their dockets and were trying to deliver. We can also expect fewer (or no?) women in the next Cabinet.
L. Muthoni Wanyeki is the Executive Director of the African Women's Development and Communication Network (FEMNET)

Original_Search_Title: President Learnt Little From Poll Defeat
Headline: Kenya: President Learnt Little From Poll Defeat
Body_Text:
Nairobi — As President Kibaki goes about making the eagerly-awaited decisions about Cabinet and other high-level appointments, he seems to be entirely unaware that the people overwhelmingly rejected a Constitution which would have given him the right to make such appointments without consulting with the political parties which helped win the presidency.
The President has been holding meetings with leaders who opposed Narc in 2002, church leaders who refused to advise their flocks on the Constitution, and other individuals and groups such as foreign envoys.
But he has refused to hold discussions with the true stakeholders - the Orange Democratic Movement - a part of which, the Liberal Democratic Party, helped propel him to victory in 2002.
The President's approach to these appointments violates provisions of the current Constitution, which requires him to consult with parliamentary political parties outside Government should he wish to appoint their representatives to the Cabinet.
To negotiate directly with opportunistic individuals who use political parties solely for the purpose of personal gain contravenes the Constitution.
We in the leadership of the Orange Democratic Movement have clearly indicated in numerous statements that we are ready to hold a dialogue with him concerning the immediate issue of the Cabinet, and the numerous other urgent post-referendum issues.
The most important among these is the need to chart the way forward on a new constitution. In our Naivasha resolutions adopted last Friday, we specifically asked the President to facilitate a meeting between the Banana and Orange groups as a matter of national priority to address the anxiety of Kenyans on the future of the Constitutional review process.
We have also categorically stated repeatedly that we will not accept approaches of the kind that are being made to individual ODM leaders about joining the Cabinet.
Doing so would not only be unconstitutional, it would be a complete betrayal of all those millions who supported us in rejecting the proposed Constitution.
We stated during the campaign that the new Constitution undermined political parties, which are the lifeblood of democracy, by allowing Cabinet appointments made through individual arrangements.
The ODM leadership will therefore, immediately expel from the Movement and the party any members who join the Cabinet through such private arrangements.
The President and his allies seem unaware that a transformed political environment now prevails in Kenya. For the first time in our history, seven out of eight provinces and 152 out of 210 constituencies voted for the same cause. And for the second time in three years, Kenyans have inflicted heavy political defeats on the governments of the day.
In continuing with business as usual, and by unconstitutionally banning our provincial thanksgiving rallies, the Government has once again thrust upon us the kind of situation that preceded the 2002 elections, with one major difference.
