Whereas the two principal presidential candidates in 2002 were both from Central Province and received support from every corner of the country, the referendum results now reveal a deep divide between that province and the rest of the country.
The ODM is determined to avoid such polarisation, brought about by a presidential entourage that is widely perceived to be exclusionist.
That is why, from our very first victory statement, we have reached out to our brothers and sisters in the Banana camp, and offered to work with the Government to chart the way forward.
But those who lost in the referendum continue to avoid seeing reality by minimising the significance of their massive defeat, saying it was merely a verdict on the Constitution.
There is, however, ample evidence to indicate that the November 21 result also reflected people's lack of trust in the Government and dissatisfaction with both its commitment as well as ability to deliver on its promises on jobs and corruption, and on an inclusive and national approach to governance.
The celebrations that greeted the referendum results are not unlike the euphoria that swept the country when Narc won the elections three years ago.
It is universally recognised that the popularity of Narc came out of its representation of the mosaic of the Kenyan people as a legitimate coalition government, crafted by the leaders through the MoU.
But as soon as he took power, the President dispensed with any consultations with his coalition partners and dismantled Narc organs.
At the time, there was strong opposition within LDP to our accepting positions in the Government after the trashing of pre-election coalition arrangements. I counselled compromise, believing that while the President was in a wheelchair, any serious divisions would create instability.
We will not make the same mistake again. But it is still not too late for the President to initiate consultations with us. It is vital that he lead the way towards restoring national as well as international confidence in Kenyans' ability to pull together, as they so clearly showed in the 2002 election and in the current referendum by voting peacefully.
The only way to move forward is on the basis of a collegial and participatory approach among those with the people's mandate.
Mr Odinga is a former minister for Roads and Public Works

Original_Search_Title: Ford Kenya to Punish Rebels
Headline: Kenya: Ford Kenya to Punish Rebels
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Wrangling in Ford Kenya will no longer be entertained, party chairman Musikari Kombo has said.
He also warned that the party would take disciplinary action against those who tried to undermine its leadership.
"Certain elements within the party go beyond the boundaries of divergent opinion to pursue their selfish agenda that can cause disaffection, disunity, disharmony and breach of basic expectations of adherent to party discipline. The party has an elaborate and appropriate machinery to deal with them," he said yesterday.
Mr Kombo said a team to draw up an election timetable and review the party manifesto had been set up.
There was an urgent need for the party to review its party manifesto which, he said, was last reviewed in 1997.
However, the party boss did not give names of the members of the team or their number, but said he expected a report in a month.
"I want to suggest to you that the legal sub-committee be asked to avail a report to me and the secretariat in four weeks from today. Upon the completion of the review, the relevant party organs will meet to roll out an election timetable, the former Local Government minister said.
"It is my wish that this process be finalised as quickly as possible in order to pave way for the much talked about party elections."
Mr Kombo was speaking at Paradise Beach Hotel in Kikambala, North Coast, when he officially launched the party's Parliamentary Group and National Executive Council meeting to ratify the Ford-K's five-year strategic plan.
He said the interests of the party, its cohesion and unity were issues that would be jealously guarded and asked members to be ready to help him in the task.
The move comes in the wake of emerging rifts within the party with members divided in their support of Mr Kombo.
He said the party needed to strategically place itself ahead of the 2007 General Election which, he said, "is just around the corner."
The party's secretary-general, Mr John Munyes, also supported the hard-line stand against party members who had been fighting the party from within.
Former Trade and Industry and Labour ministers Mukhisa Kituyi and Newton Kulundu respectively did not attend. Dr Kituyi is said to have opted for another meeting with some World Bank officials.
Mr Munyes said the party needed to re-energise itself and prove wrong its opponents, who termed it a Bukusu party. He asked: I am a Turkana and in the party. How can anyone insinuate that this is a clan affair?
Mr Kombo said he had already reached out to the party MPs who supported the Orange team during the just concluded referendum on a new constitution "to build bridges".
We should not mourn, cry and quarrel over past mistakes, rather, we should learn lessons from them and move on, he said in reference to the Banana defeat by Orange during the November 21 referendum. He said the process of jump-starting a new constitutional dispensation should be all-inclusive and above board to ensure the process got the support of the majority of Kenyans.
But we must not be held hostage by too many pressure groups jostling for political space and relevance, he warned.
He suggested that a small team of professionals - more than seven and not less than five - be appointed to accomplish the task. "This is because there already exists basic materials and the sticky issues are already known. What is remaining is an editorial structure," he said.
The meeting, attended by 12 MPs and NEC members, was sponsored by the International Republic Institute (IRI).
The resident director of the Washington-based international democracy advocacy organisation, Mr Peter Mackenzie, said political parties in Kenya had failed and only "managed to divide the nation."

Original_Search_Title: MPs Fail to Agree On Party's Role in Orange Movement
Headline: Kenya: MPs Fail to Agree On Party's Role in Orange Movement
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kanu leaders have failed to agree on the party's future role in the Orange Democratic Movement.
The opposition party's joint Parliamentary Group and national executive council meeting held in Nairobi yesterday resolved to leave the decision to other party organs. They also said party members should be consulted before a decision on the role of the party in the movement is reached.
Other organs of the party are the Governing Council and the National Delegates Conference.
However, the party said it was in the movement to stay and was prepared to engage with any other party as a coalition partner - but on the basis of clear-cut issues and matters of national concern.
Regardless of its role in the movement, the party will do all it can to deliver a new constitution to Kenyans, the leaders agreed. It will also reach out to the Banana side, which lost in the November 21 referendum, to chart an acceptable way forward on the review process.
"We are prepared to engage with any other party, including President Kibaki, on the way forward for this country and especially on the realisation for a new constitution," said Mr William Ruto, the party's secretary-general.
The party repeated its earlier request to President Kibaki to recall Parliament in January to pass a law to jump-start the review process.
The four-hour closed-door meeting at the party's headquarters near Yaya Centre, Nairobi, was attended by 38 MPs and all the five council members. Party chairman Uhuru Kenyatta chaired the meeting.
MPs Nick Salat, Moses Cheboi and David Sudi, who are allied to a faction led by Kerio South MP Nicholas Biwott attended the meeting, which Mr Biwott skipped.
However, MPs Guracha Galgalo and Aden Sugow, who were in the Banana team during the referendum campaigns, also attended the meeting.
Mr Ruto said the party had endorsed the Naivasha agreement by the Orange Movement that the issue of a coalition should first be discussed by respective party organs.
The Eldoret North MP said the party would organise a retreat for its top leaders in January to chart the way forward in the movement.
Former Cabinet minister Dalmas Otieno was charged with the responsibility of organising the retreat, which would among other things, discuss coalition building and its implications.
The retreat will also discuss whether the party should join hands with others before or after the 2007 General Election.
Mr Ruto said yesterday's meeting had agreed that President Kibaki should follow the Constitution and established machinery in appointing Kanu MPs in the yet-to-be named Cabinet. He said: "Any other way of appointment without consultation is unconstitutional. The party will disown any MP who agrees to be appointed as minister without consultation."
During the meeting, Mr Kenyatta was at pains to explain to members that Kanu was not a junior partner to the LDP in the Orange movement.
He said the party had played a big role in the Orange win and should be given the respect it deserves.
Tinderet MP Henry Kosgei expressed concern that Kanu was being treated as a junior partner in the movement.
Nambale MP Chris Okemo accused the LDP of trying to hog the limelight after the Orange win, despite the fact that Kanu was still the Official Opposition party.

Original_Search_Title: ODM Says It Will Expel MPs Who Join Cabinet
Headline: Kenya: ODM Says It Will Expel MPs Who Join Cabinet
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange Democratic Movement has warned it will expel any of its members who take up positions in the new Cabinet without consulting.
In one of its toughest stands yet, the ODM said it would immediately dismiss anyone who takes up such appointments in President Kibaki's yet to be named Cabinet.
In a strongly worded statement ahead of Kibaki's eagerly awaited announcement, ODM leader Raila Odinga said the movement was determined to remain intact.
He said the ODM was disappointed that Kibaki had not responded to its call for dialogue.
At its weekend retreat in Naivasha, the ODM had asked the President to enable Orange and Banana groups hold discussions, but nothing came of it.
"We have stated repeatedly that we will not accept approaches of the kind that are being made to individual ODM leaders about joining the Cabinet," he said.
He told ODM luminaries to be wary of promises of lucrative favours, saying they would rock their boat.

Original_Search_Title: EFCC As a Guillotine?
Headline: Nigeria: EFCC As a Guillotine?
Body_Text:
Lagos — We may not approve of the opprobrium that Bayelsa State Governor, Depreye Alamieyeseigha has caused the world's largest black nation with his infamous bail jump in England penultimate Monday. But we must resist being enlisted in the feverish grand design to exploit the gamut of a much touted anti corruption campaign to rein in a crippling civilian dictatorship of the worst form on Nigeria. We must be wary about dancing ourselves lame according to the odious tunes of the moment and avoid the path to insanity.
No doubt, the revelation by President Olusegun Obasanjo that no significant step has been taken to improve the lives of their people, in spite of the increase in states al__cpLocation by 500 per cent since 1999 must be chilling. Equally distressing is the N700 billion also allocated to the local governments in the first four years which flies in the face of the decrepit nature of socio infrastructure at the grassroots. It is not even farfetched that the country has seen a myriad of huge budget estimates without governance.
Perhaps more damning is the report by the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) that Nigeria's political rulers have stolen and mismanaged $400 billion in the last four decades. .This amount is said to equal to all western aid to Africa in four decades. The Telegraph of London says, "The looting of Africa's most populous country amounted to a sum equivalent to 300 years of British aid to the continent". If this process of corruption is unchecked, the country may soon go to the dogs and acquire the status of a banana island.
The move to criminalize governors and local government chairmen over the country's pitiable level of development may be some attempt to oversimplify the reasons why many Nigerian citizens still wallow below one dollar while life expectancy has dipped to 43 years despite the huge revenue from oil. Obasanjo himself cannot play God in this matter. His government budgeted N350 billion for roads, expended more than N300 billion for electricity and an equally staggering amount for poverty alleviation in the first four years. Yet our roads are filled with craters, our homes are perpetually dark alleys while poverty walks tall on the streets.
Besides the rabid implementation of a World Bank/IMF inspired economic programme has left the citizenry in dire straits, as the nation moves precariously to usher in less government and the smothering of social welfare policies. Many have been rendered jobless while the cost of education, health care, housing and so on has become prohibitive. Like someone noted, many literate parents may be compelled to rear illiterate children as the cost of education becomes unaffordable. This may be compounded by the government's resolve to pay $12.4 billion to Paris Club in a spate of six months as part of the deal to get an $18 billion reprieve. Many economists say not even America will agree to pay such a whooping sum to creditors in six months.
However, the country in spite of its frenzy to fight corruption is being girded on a very dangerous path of civilian diktat through the veil provided by EFCC headed by tough talking cop, Nuhu Ribadu. The floating of this hydra headed monster which has virtually usurped the role of the Police, Customs, Immigrations, NAFDAC, NSO and is inching on the role of the courts in its anxiety to wage a phoney war on corruption appear deliberate.
In addition to the powers conferred on the commission by the Economic and financial Crimes Commission (Establishment) Act, 2004, the commission has been invested as the coordinating agencies for the enforcement of the provisions of (a) the Money Laundering Act 2004,2003 No 7, 1995 No 13; (b) the advance Fee Fraud and other Related Offences Act 1995; (c) the Failed Banks (Recovery of Debts) and Financial Malpractices in Banks Act, as amended; (d) the Banks and Other Financial Institutions Act 1991, as amended; (e) Miscellaneous Offences Act, and (f) any other law or regulation relating to economic and financial crimes, including the Criminal Code and Penal Code. This move to fortify the EFCC at the expense of other agencies looks contrived.
The EFCC obviously has shown some resolve to not only bark but bite. We have seen many arrests but very few convictions. Certainly we have seen a lot of motion without movement. Everything appears as a storm in a tea cup. The dramatization of the crimes involving the former Inspector General of Police, Mr. Tafa Balogun notwithstanding, the man got just six month jail term. What is worrying is that some states like Taraba where 10 legislators and sundry senior government officials including Special Assistant to the Governor, Mr. Hilkiah Bubajoda have been held for two months are virtually paralysed.
The arm twisting of the Bayelsa legislators to endorse an impeachment motion against Alamieyeseigha brings to the fore the fear that EFCC may have been conceived more as a guillotine to make opponents of a third term agenda recoil in their shell and buckle under. Lured to Lagos under the guise of complicity in the charge of fiddling with constituency project fund, the legislators became a tool to pull out someone's chestnut from a raging flame as they were ambushed to endorse an impeachment notice against their governor. to detention.
After this circus show, a supposed press conference was organized by the Speaker Peremobowie Ebebi who talked tough. This is the height of abracadabra. Those who think we are on the verge of a fierce anti-corruption war must be a little circumspect. What we are against is certainly beyond an anti corruption war. The EFCC is being used as a tool to make people fall into the line of the third term train.
Short of declaring a state of emergency in Bayelsa state, armed soldiers and police are virtually everywhere as part of the permutation to remove a governor who shrugged off his bail conditions and returned surreptitiously into the country. It is certainly intriguing how Alamieyeseigha got crated back to Nigeria by those who concocted his escape. It is equally enigmatic that with the extensive security network and the ubiquitous cameras in London, the fairy tale of "Queen" Alamieyeseigha's exploits may be taken with a pinch of salt.
What is most disgusting is the attempt to turn Nigeria's constitution on its head as part of the attempt to make the Bayelsa governor swallow the humble pie. Yenagoa, the capital is almost in a state of siege with the government on its knees and the President barking orders that the governor should resign. The Accountant General who went to collect the states al__cpLocation in Abuja cannot be found while the al__cpLocation has been confiscated.
We are now on the dawn of a country without laws where the whim and caprices of the imperial president holds sway. This attempt to resort to jungle justice is likely to imperil our democracy. Even though the Taraba State Governor Jolly Nyame is merely a victim of media trial by the EFCC, both the legislative and executive arm of the government is almost paralysed with the officials being detained for close to two months without any case preferred against them in any court of law. Even the police are not expected to detain any citizen beyond 48 hours. We must be headed on the road to hara-kiri if we allow a government agency to act with so much impunity.
Odunlami, a financial expert lives in Lagos.

Original_Search_Title: Kanu Leaders to Discuss Coalition
Headline: Kenya: Kanu Leaders to Discuss Coalition
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kanu's top organs meet today to decide the party's future role in the Orange Democratic Movement.
The opposition party's Parliamentary Group and National Executive Council are expected to come up with a list of conditions for the party's participation in the yet-to-be structured movement, which also includes the Liberal Democratic Party (LDP).
Today's meeting will be held at the party's headquarters, near Yaya Centre, Nairobi.
It comes three days after the movement's retreat in Naivasha, where the group announced it was ready to soften its hard-line position, and start negotiations with the Banana camp to calm post-referendum anxiety.
The movement was formed in the run-up to last month's referendum on a new Constitution. The Orange team won on the poll held on November 21.
They also dropped its demand for snap elections and instead asked President Kibaki to recall Parliament in a month to restart the constitutional review.
Consider proposals
The meeting also agreed that Kanu, LDP and other friendly parties consider proposals to convert the movement into a coalition to promote democracy, good governance and social progress.
Yesterday, Kanu vice-chairman Chris Okemo said the party's leadership wanted to hear its MPs' views on the movement because they had not been properly briefed on happenings.
Said Mr Okemo: "Concerns have been raised about the status of Kanu in its alliance with LDP in ODM. Definitely, our MPs are yet to internalise the idea of converting ODM into a coalition and what it means to them. That is what we want to hear from them."
The Nambale MP said the party's MPs were anxious to know if they were entering into the coalition as equal partners.
While in Naivasha, said Mr Okemo, it was agreed that each party was to sit down with its MPs to agree on the kind of structure they want before having an ODM meeting.
It was agreed that parties hold meetings for their members to agree on their preferred structure.

Original_Search_Title: Have Talks With US Over Review, Odm Tells Kibaki
Headline: Kenya: Have Talks With US Over Review, Odm Tells Kibaki
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki was yesterday asked to hold dialogue with the Orange Democratic Movement over a new constitution or risk being removed from power in 2007.
The movement's leaders warned of what they termed a second political tsunami should the President reject the olive branch they said they had extended to him.
Former Cabinet ministers Raila Odinga, Anyang' Nyong'o and Ochillo Ayacko led 11 MPs in demanding that the President immediately have "meaningful and constructive" talks with them.
Misrule and dictatorship
The country risked being balkanised should the President fail to do this, they warned.
They described the movement as a national outfit which, they noted, could save Kenya from misrule and dictatorship.
"When I told them (Banana referendum team) that a political tsunami would sweep them and that they should move to higher ground, I meant that they should embrace dialogue for their own good, and the referendum is a good lesson for them," Mr Odinga said.
Prof Nyong'o said Kenyans were longing for a "precise, concise and clear" constitution that would address the country's political and economic problems.
Burial of a pastor
They were speaking at Kanyamfwa, Rachuonyo District, at the burial of pastor Elisha Arunga, the father of Mr Odinga's personal assistant, Mr David Arunga.
Others were former assistant ministers Peter Odoyo and Orwa Ojodeh as well as MPs Philip Okundi (Rangwe), Otieno Kajwang' (Mbita), Odhiambo Omamba (Uriri), Ocholla Ogur (Nyatike), Ken Nyagudi (Kisumu Town West), Owino Likowa (Migori), Oburu Oginga (Bondo), Gor Sunguh (Kisumu Town East) and Adhu Awiti of Karachuonyo.

Original_Search_Title: Cleric Criticises Kibaki, Moi
Headline: Kenya: Cleric Criticises Kibaki, Moi
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The highly publicized meeting between President Kibaki and former President Moi sought to protect their individual interests, retired Rev Timothy Njoya said yesterday.
Njoya said the two had misled Kenyans, who did not get a new constitution even after spending Sh10 billion.
Speaking at a church service in Gaitumbi, Kiambu, he compared State House to the biblical Temple of Solomon, which he said would perish because of poor leadership. "Politicians should lead people to greater life and stop thinking of how to get to State House," he said while delivering a sermon.
He said politicians, who were campaigning for and against the Wako Draft, misled Kenyans for their own selfish gains, adding that all Kenyans lost at the referendum.
He asked citizens to forget the Orange and Banana teams and reflect on what went wrong in the pursuit of a new constitution. He added that the country was under the leadership of people associated with lies, and who have been involved in major scandals.
Njoya and six others had filed a case in the High Court over the constitution making process. They argued that the Bomas Draft could not serve as a foundation of a united nation.
They called for negotiation on the Wako Draft, with the purpose of building consensus.
Yesterday, Njoya accused politicians of using the constitution making process to get power. He said Kenyans should restart the constitution making process, ensuring that only honest leaders are involved.
On the issue of the Cabinet, he said Kenyans have never had one since none has ever delivered to the people. "The Cabinet does not add value to the lives of Kenyans. These are people out to spend our money," he said, and dismissed accusations that the Church did not give direction on how to vote as malicious.
He said the Church asked people to vote according to their consciences and that it was not supposed to join the campaign trail.
He accused politicians of using the referendum as a forum to prepare for the 2007 General Election.

Original_Search_Title: Ford Kenya Party Members in Kilifi for Retreat
Headline: Kenya: Ford Kenya Party Members in Kilifi for Retreat
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Ten Ford Kenya MPs and several party National Executive Council members led by chairman Musikari Kombo yesterday arrived in Mombasa for the party's retreat.
But Kombo's rival, Dr Mukhisa Kituyi, had not yet arrived by last evening.
On arrival at the Paradise Beach Resort in Kilifi District, Kombo maintained that the party officials had come together to chart the way forward.
The retreat will discuss a five-year strategic plan, post-mortem of the referendum and media relations.
Kombo dismissed reports that the Coast forum was called to discuss a crisis in the party, saying Ford-K has since held similar meetings in Nakuru and Baringo.
He said Ford-K was the only party that has led the way by holding elections.
MPs who had arrived by last evening for today's official opening of the retreat were Kombo, Dr Noah Wekesa, Moses Wetangula, Raphael Wanjala, Soita Shitanda, Bifwoli Wakoli, Bonny Khalwale, John Munyes, Daniel Khamasi and Davis Nakitare.
The retreat is sponsored by the International Republican Institute (IRI).
Wekesa said he initiated the Mombasa retreat through the party chairman to serve as an end of year gathering, reiterating that the meeting was planned a long time ago.
IRI director for East African office, Mr Peter Mackenzie, said they have worked with various political parties in Kenya to help nurture democracy.
"This party retreat is timely because it comes at a time of realignment. Parties need these forums to discuss the way forward now that the referendum is over," Mackenzie said.
He said IRI has in the past worked with the Democratic Party of Kenya (DP), Kanu and the Liberal Democratic Party (LDP).
Earlier, Kombo admitted that his party had been split down the middle by the just concluded referendum.
Speaking at Kinyoho Secondary School in Maragwa, Kandara constituency where they were hosted by former assistant minister, Joshua Toro, Kombo said he was aware of the sharp rifts in the party, but said they would be ironed out during the Mombasa retreat.
He dismissed allegations that the party was going to Mombasa to strategise on how Ford Kenya will be awarded Cabinet posts.
"The party planned this meeting a long time ago and it is not true we are going there to talk about how to share the seats. The party has no mandate to decide on who should get posts as this is the prerogative of the President," Kombo added.
He said the meeting had been planned six weeks before the referendum and was to be used as a vehicle of healing the wounds brought about by the campaigns and reconcile the two groups.
He said some of his members supported the Orange movement during the campaigns while others like himself stuck with the Banana side.
He said his association with President Kibaki dated back a long time and that he was not prepared to ditch his ally during his hour of need.
Kombo at the same time dismissed allegations by Kituyi that his party has not held proper elections for the last eight years.
"Those saying we have not held elections have not read the party's constitution. It empowers some leaders to occupy office after being nominated and that is what is happening, but we will deal with this in Mombasa," he said.
On whether Ford-K will field a presidential candidate, Kombo said any political party worth its salt is driven by a quest for power.
Kombo said for 12 years he had served as an opposition MP, but since becoming a Cabinet minister he was determined to be part of the Government.

Original_Search_Title: In President's Court, Battle Lines Are Drawn
Headline: Kenya: In President's Court, Battle Lines Are Drawn
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The waiting game since President Kibaki sent the entire Cabinet packing
is wearing out a lot of nerves.
In anticipation of appointment or reinstatement, MPs have gazed day and
night at the hallowed State House with almost tangible anxiety.
Few of President Kibaki's loyalists are willing to speak their minds in
the fear that they might jeopardise their chances of appointment.  Nearly
all the former ministers perceived to be close to the President are lying
very low - their cellphones have been either permanently out of reach or
going unanswered.
Meanwhile, President Kibaki has been going about his business as if
nothing is the fuss, meeting people and projecting the image of business as
usual.
However, things in the President's corner of the National Alliance Party
of Kenya (NAK) - bringing together Ford Kenya, the National Party of Kenya
(NPK) and the Democratic Party of Kenya (DP) - will never be the same again
after the suppressed faultlines burst open in the wake of a devastating loss
at the referendum on the proposed new Constitution.
Apart from former Health minister Charity Ngilu, who would throw
broadsides whenever she thought DP was gnawing into her NPK territory, the
other NAK affiliates (especially the DP), have struggled to present the
image of a united front. However, a war of attrition has quietly been
simmering between the wealthy and elderly presidential confidantes - the
so-called Muthaiga Club group - and the elected politicians.
Although few are willing to speak on record, elected MPs feel the elders
enjoyed undue access to and influence on the President while they (MPs) were
the ones doing political battle on behalf of the Government.
The simmering tensions have been laid bare in the ongoing blame game
between the two camps over who was responsible for the defeat of the Banana
campaign.
While the elders tended to point at alleged arrogance and antagonistic
tendencies of two former senior ministers, Mr Kiraitu Murungi and Dr
Christopher Murungaru, the politicians place the buck at the feet of the
elders, accusing them of hijacking the Banana campaign and running it
down.
Although the few of Kibaki loyalists who speak will only do so off the
record, it was significant that Mr Murungi chose to issue a press statement
to describe the Banana campaign as "disorganised, uncoordinated, and
ineffective".
As the public face of the Yes campaign, Mr Murungi was effectively
telling the world that the machinery he had earlier promised would shake
each and every corner in the country actually never took off.
The intriguing thing was that Mr Murungi chose to vent his frustrations
through a press statement - almost a confirmation that he no longer had
access to State House.
Subukia MP Koigi wa Wamwere challenged the former Justice and
Constitutional Affairs minister to go further and name "those he thought
were responsible for the defeat if it was not him".
The nerve centre of the Banana campaign was headed by KenGen board
chairman Titus Mbathi. Other key officials were East African Legislative
Assembly member Rose Waruhiu (director of women affairs), Catholic priest Fr
Dominic Wamugunda (director of strategy), Mr John Munuve (director of youth
affairs), Maj (rtd) Humphrey Njoroge (director of logistics) and Ms Cathrene
Zain (director of communications). Ms Zain, interestingly enough, works for
MCL Saatchi and Saatchi, an advertising firm associated with one of
President Kibaki's veteran advisers, Mr Nat Kangethe.
Although Mr Murungi did not name names, his statement was clearly aimed
at the secretariat and those who established it, some of them believed to be
very close to the President.
Whatever plays out in President Kibaki's court, the referendum fallout
may not take long before the battle lines in his camp become clear.
But some of President Kibaki's supporters are already making their
expectations known, among them calls for dismantling of "patronage networks"
and refurbishment of Narc in preparation for the 2007 General Election.
"There is an urgent need to dismantle the patronage networks in public
affairs. In voting against the proposed new Constitution, Kenyans were
making a statement that patronage needs to be replaced by competitiveness in
public leadership. We need a clean break with the past," says Mr Kabando wa
Kabando, the chairman of the Nairobi Water and Sewerage Company.
Mr Kabando is a member of the Vision Yes lobby group, a gathering of the
Young Democrats from President Kibaki's Democratic Party who regrouped in a
vain effort to re-energise the Yes campaign.
Another campaign outfit seen as rivalling the official Yes secretariat
had been established by the President's nephew and political aide, Mr Alex
Kibaki Muriithi, who is chairman of the Tana and Athi Rivers Development
Authority.
President Kibaki has governed without a political organ since he
collapse of the Narc Summit, and lack of a party machinery is blamed by some
for collapse of the Yes campaign.
With an eye on 2007, some of his advisers are urging a revitalisation of
Narc, minus the rebel elements.
Mr Muriithi, sources say, has been tasked with putting in place the
machinery for Narc to launch a membership drive and hold elections later
this year.
According to Mr Kabando, the majority young voters may not have rejected
the Draft Constitution because it was bad, but were making a statement that
the composition of government does not seem to reflect and represent their
aspirations and expectations.
The President should appoint a Cabinet that will help him reorganise
politics in preparation for 2007, Laikipia East MP Mwangi Kiunjuri, the
former assistant minister Energy, says.
"The parties that supported the Yes vote must have woken up to the rude
shock that, much as they wanted to keep their regional identities, and had
regional support, we are doomed to perish if we don't come together and
craft a national vehicle to steer us into the 2007 elections. We should
either refurbish Narc or create another umbrella body. But it should have
clear leadership, which is Kenyans should be made to know well in
advance. One of the reasons we lost was that we had too many party bosses on
the Yes camp but we did not carve out clear leadership" Mr Kiunjuri
adds.
Mr Kiunjuri says that the 2002 General Election pattern of the combined
NAK-LDP campaign that pulled 3.6 million votes against Kanu's 1.8 million
was more less replicated at the referendum - only this time it was with
KANU-LDP winning with 3.5 million votes against 2.5 million votes.
He says: "The 2002 Rainbow Coalition and now the Orange Movement lessons
on organisation and crafting a political machine with clear leadership the
voters can identify with should make nonsense those calling for regional
parties."
But reorganising Narc may be a tough choice, given the controversy
surrounding past attempts to reorganise it and the determination by the Narc
affiliates, including Ford Kenya, to resist the move.
"Parties have just woken up to the reality that they must jealously
protect their identities and strengths. I am happy the secretary-general of
DP, Mr Joseph Munyao, has issued a statement in that capacity. The DP should
also wake up to this reality," says Sirisia MP Moses Wetangula, of Ford
Kenya
Mr Wetangula says the immediate concern of the "Coalition of the
Willing" that supported the Yes vote is to retain a majority in
Parliament.
"Of immediate concern is sufficient majority in parliament to ensure
political order in the country and ensure legislative business proceeds
undisrupted," he adds.
But Budalang'i MP Raphael Wanjala is more forthright on what his party's
expectations are: "Ford Kenya will remain an independent party in the
coalition and no one will force people into things they are not interested
in like Narc recruitment. They should recruit through Democratic Party, not
Narc. Their officials are still alive, and their chairman is the
President. Why are they ashamed of the Democratic Party?" he asks.
But Mr Wamwere warns that revival of the DP would be political suicide
for President Kibaki. It would encourage parties based on ethnic lines.
"Going back to the DP would be a political suicide. It is time the
President took the bull by the horns and called for recruitment and
elections in Narc. Tribal alliances are inherently doomed and I believe true
nationalists will sooner or later venture out on a nationalist platform that
Kenyans can be proud of," he says.

Original_Search_Title: We Are Not Backing Down: Orange Team
Headline: Kenya: We Are Not Backing Down: Orange Team
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange Democratic Movement yesterday said the victory at the
referendum was a dress rehearsal for the 2007 General Elections.
The ODM leaders also denied they had softened their hardline stand over
the post-referendum relationship with the Government.
They declared that they will neither beg nor lobby for positions in
President Kibaki's new Cabinet expected to be named in the next three
days.
Former ministers Mr Raila Odinga and Prof Anyang' Nyong'o were joined by
Kanu Secretary-General William Ruto in declaring the referendum outcome a
precursor of good things for the Orange team at the next elections.
"This is just a rehearsal; we are preparing for a bigger battle and we
ask Kenyans to continue supporting us, said Mr Ruto.
They were speaking at the burial of Kasipul Kabondo MP Peter Owidi who
died two weeks after a long illness. Mr Owidi was laid to rest at his home
in Kotieno village in Rachuonyo district.
Prof Anyang' Nyong'o
Mr Odinga, who was the Roads and Public Works minister in the dissolved
Cabinet, said the Orange movement would remain a strong team up to the next
General Election. He said: "Those who thought we shall break up will be
ashamed."
The leaders said their demands for a new constitution still remained and
would push for talks to realise the same.
Prof Nyong'o said it was unacceptable to claim that the Orange team had
softened their stand over earlier demands they had put to their opponents in
the referendum.
"We still remain firm in our stand and our demands remain as solid as
Kit Mikai," he said. Kit Mikai is a legendary rock in Prof Nyong'o's Kisumu
Rural constituency.
The former Planning and National Development minister claimed it was the
Government side which had hardened its stand over discussions to chart the
way forward.
"It is not the Orange movement softening its stand. Instead, it is the
Government, which has always hardened its stand. We shall remain steadfast
and firm in whatever conditions we gave," said Prof Nyong'o.
The Orange team had put out tough conditions for the Government before
entering into any negotiations over the proposed constitution or composition
of the new Cabinet.
They had criticised approaches to individuals and demanded that ODM be
consulted as a group.
Said Mr Ayacko: "We shall not go to State House or meet anybody from the
Yes camp to beg for Cabinet posts. We are interested in the way forward for
Kenyans."
Mr Ruto, Mr Odinga and Prof Nyong'o, however, said they were ready for
consultative talks that could give Kenyans a new constitution.
The leaders warned that they would not discuss their possible inclusion
in the Cabinet during such consultations.
"We shall not lobby for Cabinet positions because it is not a must that
we are made ministers. I particularly was not born with a ministerial flag
in my pocket," said Mr Odinga.
He added: "We won't be threatened. We shall not at any given time sell
Kenyans in exchange with cabinet positions."
Mr Ruto said the Orange Movement's decision to enter into reconciliation
talks with the Banana side had been misconstrued to mean they might have
been lobbying for cabinet posts.
"We want to put the record straight. We don't want talks on who should
be minister in President Kibaki's next cabinet. Our humble position is that
we ready to talk on other issues which can help us deliver a new
constitution to Kenyans," he said.
The statement by Mr Odinga, Prof Nyong'o and Mr Ruto sharply
contradicted an earlier appeal made by former assistant minister Foreign
Affairs Mr Orwa Ojodeh who pleaded with the Orange team to accept whatever
the Government offered.
Mr Ojodeh had said amid murmurs from mourners that it was important for
members of the Orange Movement to be in Government to monitor how state
resources were being utilized.
Speaking in Dholuo, Mr Ojodeh said: "Ok onego wa kun (we should not
reject what the Government offers)."
At the same time, the Orange team attacked church leaders describing
them as let down to Kenyans.
Mr Odinga said a planned meeting between President Kibaki, Catholic and
Anglican bishops was betrayal to Kenyans since the two churches had declined
to take a decision on the referendum campaign divide.
Mr Odinga said it made no sense for the church leader to make trips to
State House to consult the President when they sat at the time Kenyans
needed them most.
"These church leaders are traitors. They are like a herdsman who takes
his sheep to the field but abandons it when a hyena appears. They told
Kenyans to read the draft constitution for themselves and they refused to
take a stand," the Langata MP said.
Veteran politician Martin Shikuku also criticized church leaders
accusing them of trying to hijack the post-referendum healing process while
they kept off the debate during the campaigns.
"The Anglican and Catholic church bishops abandoned their flock at the
hour of need. They kept quiet throughout the campaigns and should remain
so," he said.
He singled out the NCCK General Secretary Rev Mutava Musyimi, describing
him as a turn court and an opportunist.
"Rev Musyimi was at Bomas but walked out midstream. He started
Ufungamano and abandoned it. I will walk out if I meet him in heaven," he
declared.
The Orange leaders at the same time told the Government not to attempt
banning their rallies countrywide.
They said the rallies were still on course and would attend whether or
not they were licensed.
Said Mr Ayacko: "The rallies are on and we are asking Kenyans to attend
in large numbers. None of the Orange members however spoke about the snap
election, which they had earlier demanded."
The burial was attended by more than 30 MPs from the LDP, Kanu and Ford
People. Also present was the Speaker of the National Assembly Francis Kaparo
and Embakasi MP David Mwenje.
President Kibaki sent a message of condolence to the family, which was
read by Nyanza PC Hassan Norr.
Speakers praised the late Owidi who was serving his first term in
Parliament as a brave democrat who fought for a democratic society.

Original_Search_Title: Is It the Orange Softening Its Position? Or It is Kibaki Hardening His Stand?
Headline: Kenya: Is It the Orange Softening Its Position? Or It is Kibaki Hardening His Stand?
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Buoyed by the convincing victory over their Banana rivals, last
weekend's rally at Uhuru Park, Nairobi, was an ecstatic affair as Orange
politicians tore into the Narc leadership, calling for the dissolution of
Parliament among other demands. The momentum has since died down.
The question on the lips of many is why the Orange Democratic Movement
seems to be backing down from its initial hard-line position?  Or is it the
Government that is hardening its stance?
While most ODM allied MPs interviewed by the Sunday Nation deny there is
a change of heart by the movement, rumours about individuals within the team
being approached for Cabinet posts have doubtless dampened the spirits of
many.
And the confirmation by some, like the LDP vice chairman, Mr Joe
Khamisi, that they will consider Cabinet jobs if offered, has doubtlessly
complicated the matter and probably forced members to soften their
stand.
The LDP secretary for legal affairs, Mr Otieno Kajwang, talks of rumours
of a possible plot being mooted to isolate the party by President Kibaki and
"other senior politicians" from Rift Valley.
"There is a rumour that the whole idea may be to revive the so-called
Kamatusa (Kalenjin-Maasai-Turkana-Samburu) community with a view to
combining its voting bloc with that of President Kibaki's Mount Kenya,"
claims the Mbita MP.
Last Monday, retired President Daniel arap Moi met with President Kibaki
at State House, Nairobi in the heat of the post-referendum loss over an
undisclosed agenda.
But an optimistic Kajwang and Kanu secretary general William Ruto are
treating the LDP isolation talk as a rumour "deliberately peddled by our
enemies to cause friction within the movement".
In fact some of the MPs claim that before his visit to State House last
Monday, Mr Moi met and consulted with Mr Ruto and LDP leader Mr Raila Odinga
- a claim the Kanu official does not deny.
Defending ODM's current position, Mr Ruto maintains that nothing has
changed from the group's initial stand. The movement, he explains, suggested
two options to the president, namely: To dissolve Parliament or enter into a
meaningful and structured dialogue.
"We have simply embarked on the latter option of dialogue since the
vice-president, Mr Moody Awori, categorically responded to our demands by
stating that the president would not dissolve Parliament," says Ruto.
However, Nambale MP and Kanu vice chairman Mr Chris Okemo explains that
ODM has refined its approach because members are more interested in
realising a new constitutional dispensation.
"We have to separate election euphoria and public goodwill. We are
particularly keen on projecting a good image of leadership," says Mr
Okemo.
Noting that no single side of the political divide can achieve much in
the constitution-making exercise in isolation, the Kanu official maintains
that ODM's priority at the moment is giving Kenyans an acceptable new
Constitution.
South Migirango MP Mr Omingo Magara, is more forthright: "The movement
has realised the need for the country to move forward, hence the change to a
less combative and more accommodative tone."
But former Planning minister Prof Anyang' Nyong'o adds an interesting
angle to the story: "Why isn't anybody seeing it my way? We have not
softened our stand, rather it is the Government that has hardened its
stand."
According to Prof Nyong'o, ODM has extended an olive branch to the
Government, which has arrogantly declined to act.
"We have taken serious and transparent steps to reach out to the
Government yet nothing has come up from that end. While Mr (Simeon) Nyachae
(leader of Banana campaign) has indicated his willingness to talk, we expect
a more serious voice from the president to make a follow-up," he told the
Sunday Nation.
Saying that ODM has not given up on the demand for snap elections, Prof
Nyong'o explains that the issue will come up once the president agrees to
dialogue "since he is, anyway, the one who is permitted by law to dissolve
Parliament".
Mr Khamisi asserts that in the face of overwhelming defeat, the
president was in a weaker position and was expected to readily
negotiate.
However, he observes, ODM leaders have discovered that the president was
in charge and was doing things his own way.
"They have certainly chosen the middle ground which is good for the
country because we cannot forever keep people in the campaign mood," the
Bahari MP told the Sunday Nation.
Reacting to silence from the president, Mr Ruto says: "We have opened
the door for him to come in and talk to us, but if he wants to use the rear
door or the window and fish some of our members, then we can only say that
he is crafty and unreliable."
According to a State House source, President Kibaki's camp is willing to
enter into dialogue. But they will not bend to ultimatums.
They are also uncomfortable with the ODM's appointment of Kanu nominated
MP Mutula Kilonzo as their point-man in any talks.
Mr Ruto further explains that the latest demand on the president to
reconvene Parliament in Januray next year simply arises from the fact that
the review could only start when Parliament is in session.
This probably explains why priorities have changed for the ODM.  Indeed,
the decision to put constitutional review ahead of the demand for snap
elections is more prudent as it would give the MPs an opportunity to craft a
workable power sharing arrangement.
Despite the fact that ODM is congested with political heavyweights
including Mr Odinga, Mr Uhuru Kenyatta, Mr Kalonzo Musyoka, Mr Ruto and
former vice president Mr Musalia Mudavadi, the current Constitution provides
for only two key political positions of the president and his deputy.
Going to the polls at this moment would no doubt present a major
power-sharing challenge to both the ODM and their Banana rivals.
Nonetheless, Mr Ruto insists his camp is ready for the polls at any
time.
The only worry to ODM members is the fact that the Government is slowly
making in-roads into the group with a view of dismantling the emerging
opposition.
According to Emuhuya MP Kenneth Marende, who read the ODM statement at
the Naivasha retreat, dialogue was the second option their camp floated at
the Uhuru Park rally. "The first option of dissolving Parliament was radical
while the second one, talks, was moderate. There is nothing new in our
approach," he said.
-Additional reporting by Bernard Namunane

Original_Search_Title: Banana Leaders Reject ODM's Snap Poll Calls
Headline: Kenya: Banana Leaders Reject ODM's Snap Poll Calls
Body_Text:
Nairobi — For Members of Parliament, it is a four-year honeymoon between one
election and the other and reality only dawns in the fifth year, when they
are called to account by the electorate.
So expensive in monetary and personal terms are campaigns for entry and
re-entry into Parliament that the very contemplation of seeking votes from
raia, is so frightening that some candidates end up in hospital suffering
from stomach ulcers or, in the extreme, heart attacks. That is one reason
why the Orange Democratic Movement's (ODM) calls for dissolution of
Parliament and a snap election may not find favour, even within its own
ranks.
In any case, former assistant minister Mwangi Kiunjuri thinks the ODM
brigade is just bluffing and does not even have the numbers to win an
election.
"The referendum was a blessing in disguise, as it allowed us a peek into
LDP's numerical strength," he says. "During the last General Election, Narc
got 3.6 million votes against Kanu's 1.8, while in the referendum a combined
force of Kanu and LDP garnered 3.5 million votes.
"So if 1.8 million are Kanu votes, simple mathematics indicates that
LDP's strength is a mere 1.7," calculates Kiunjuri.
He further explains the equation that saw the original NAK get 2.5
million votes in the referendum against LDP's 'miserable' 1.7 and even
dismisses this block as an unequal coalition partner in Narc. Kiunjuri
reminds us that most MPs have taken loans worth millions of shillings from
Parliament and have barely re-paid half the debt.
"Most owe Parliament over four million shillings. How would they clear
the loans if they were to be jobless today?"
Paul Muite is candid in calling the ODM's bluff and reminds the Orange
luminaries that no President can be expected to use the absolute power
vested in him to dissolve Parliament to the advantage of his opponents.
"It is therefore futile for those who are calling for snap elections to
do so, but if the ODM thinks it can muster the majority in Parliament, they
are welcome to seek a vote of no confidence in the government through a
motion in Parliament. "Otherwise no government in power can be expected to
sit back and allow incitement of the public with the aim of making the
country ungovernable, by instigating chaos to force a snap election," Muite
said, in reference to ODM's 'thanksgiving' meetings. Koigi Wamwere agrees
and says if ODM leaders were sincere, they should by now have written to the
Speaker of the National Assembly resigning their seats "and causing
by-elections in their constituencies".
He dismisses their call for a snap election as a continuation of the
referendum war.
"I am not sure that voting with the Orange group during the referendum
amounted to viewing their leaders as the leaders of the country or
government and the results of the referendum were not meant to negate and
replace the results of the 2002 General Election," he says.
MP Kembi Gitura believes the issue of a snap election does not arise,
but invites the ODM to go through the rigours of causing a parliamentary
vote of no confidence if it so desired.
"Nobody has said they have lost confidence in President Mwai Kibaki and
in any event, the constitution making has been going on for 15 years and no
other president was asked to step aside or call a snap election because a
new constitution was not realised.
Mirugi Kariuki, formerly of the Office of the President, declares that
the Orange group has misinterpreted the referendum, which was "strictly a
simple opinion poll to determine whether the draft constitution should be
adopted".
The people of Kenya, says Mirugi, did not give anybody another mandate
"be it to call a snap election, dissolve Parliament or make another
constitution for them."
The Nakuru Town MP believes that the Orange group is shying away from
the parliamentary process of sponsoring a motion of no confidence in
government, as "they know too well they are the minority".
He adds that the ODM leaders are trying to overcome their frustration by
introducing the "mischievous" twin political agendas of snap elections and
dissolution of Parliament.
Mirugi concludes by warning leaders of ODM that the dynamics of another
election would shock them.
"It is one thing to vote for a document and completely another to vote
for the presidency," he says. The issue being raised by ODM, according to
Mutahi Kagwe, is "void ab initio" and is not unlike seeking compensation
from an insurance company that you had never paid premiums to.
"I can guarantee you that these agents provocateur are bluffing and
trying to create a crisis where none exists. These are people who just want
to politicise issues so that they can remain relevant."
The Mukurweini MP says those calling for a snap election are suffering
from the illusion that their popularity is equal to the number of no votes
cast during the referendum. "In any case if an election were called today,
there would be no ODM as every man would be for himself and so we should
concentrate on the way forward."
The way forward, Kagwe says, is for the 'Yes' and 'No' camps to nominate
experts who can work on a new constitution with the approval of
Parliament.

Original_Search_Title: Save Our Party, Kulundu Tells Kombo
Headline: Kenya: Save Our Party, Kulundu Tells Kombo
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Former Minister Newton Kulundu has joined the Ford Kenya ruckus, asking
party leader Muskari Kombo to shape up if he does not want to continue
leading a tribal outfit.
IT was once described as the party of the future - one on a Grand March
to State House.
Indeed, most of the politicians on the vanguard of what was called the
second liberation of the early 1990s belonged to this party.
They included former ministers Raila Odinga, Kiraitu Murungi, Mukhisa
Kituyi, Anyang' Nyong'o, and Paul Muite.
It was also envied as the only party with MPs from across the
country.
But today, Ford-Kenya, founded in 1992 by the father of Kenya's
opposition politics, the late Jaramogi Oginga Odinga, is a pale shadow of
its former self.
Its fortunes have changed drastically in the last decade. Once the
Official Opposition party, Ford Kenya today boasts only 16 elected MPs drawn
mainly from Western Province.
Out of the 16, only three are from outside the province.
And if the outcome of the November 21 referendum on the Proposed New
Constitution is anything to go by, the party could well count itself out of
Western Province, save for Bungoma District, the home of party chairman
Kombo.
Kulundu yesterday joined the clamour for a change of leadership in Ford
Kenya, adding fuel to the fire already consuming the party after its poor
show in the referendum.
The Lurambi MP called for grassroots elections to save the party.
Kulundu said the party's performance in the referendum showed that it
had lost touch with the masses.
"This goes to confirm the fears entertained by nationalistic patriots
from western Kenya that the party had all along lost touch with the
grassroots as a result of poverty in its leadership, more so at the apex,"
Kulundu said.
In a statement, Kulundu said demands for fresh grassroots elections
should not be ignored.
Last Friday a splinter group led by the party's organizing secretary Mrs
Jael Mbogo formed a committee to conduct to grassroots elections.
The group's meeting turned chaotic after youths allied to party chairman
Musikari Kombo attempted to disrupt it.
Yesterday, Kulundu accused Kombo of leading the party astray and
abandoning the ideals of its former chairman, the late Michael Wamalwa.
"Some of us who are in Narc through supposed membership in Ford-Kenya
have long been aware that a comprehensive and thorough surgery of the
party's leadership through bottom-up election procedure have been long
overdue," said Kulundu.
He charged that Ford-Kenya has been reduced to a political outfit for
one ethnic group in western Kenya due to poor leadership.
"Many Kenyans who joined Ford-Kenya at its pulsating and vibrant best in
the early 1990s, did so on the basis of its nationalistic ideals that have
since been dimmed by parochial, ethnic considerations that can only serve to
politically marginalize a section of the Luhya community," he said.
He questioned why Ford-Kenya leadership had organised a retreat in
Mombasa to ostensibly to undertake an appraisal of the party's performance
during the national referendum.
"Why, in the whole name of fairness and probity, does the party
leadership wish to tear off from Narc and undertake the so-called appraisal
on its own?" he asked.
He said the retreat was another form of canvassing for seats in the
government " in a very vile way."
All Ford Kenya Banana MPs from outside Bungoma lost to the Orange
campaign in the referendum.
The Bukusu, a Luhya sub-tribe, inhabits Bungoma.
Perhaps this is why the Mt Elgon Ford Kenya branch chairman, Mr Fred
Nasila, described it as a "Bukusu community affair."
Ford Kenya was formed in 1992 after the disintegration of the Forum For
Restoration of Democracy (Ford), with Odinga as chairman.
Although Odinga lost to former President Daniel arap Moi in the 1992
election, the party swept all parliamentary seats in Luo Nyanza.
It did well in Western Province, winning all seats in Bungoma and
secured at least a seat in all other provinces.

Original_Search_Title: MPs Blame Aides' Arrogance for Their Loss
Headline: Kenya: MPs Blame Aides' Arrogance for Their Loss
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Three Ford People MPs who supported the Banana camp blame their dismal
performance in the referendum on propaganda by the Orange group and poor
management of their constituencies' affairs.
They say the Orange camp deliberately misinterpreted the draft,
misleading voters, but admit their humiliation had something to do with the
way they have run their constituencies for the past three years, and used
development funds allocated to them.
Henry Obwocha (West Mugirango), Stephen Manoti (Bobasi) and Dr Hezron
Manduku (Nyaribari Masaba) say emotive issues, like inheritance, religion,
employment of teachers, and burial of animals were used to turn the
electorate against the Wako Draft.
"They (Orange camp) beat us hands down in the propaganda war. They
deliberately misled voters to believe that under the new constitution,
Saturday would not be recognised as a day of worship.
Given that the majority of Kisiis are members of the Seventh Day
Adventist Church, they rejected the draft," Obwocha, who was until a week
ago a Finance assistant minister said. Manduku supports this view.
"I lost to a team of liars. Orange campaigners convinced my constituents
that they would be compelled to pay land rates while those who owned less
than two and half acres would not get title deeds.
But the worst claim was that our married sisters and daughters would
claim part of their families' land," he explained.
Manoti says that misinterpretation of a clause touching on teachers'
employment dealt him the deathblow.
"Knut officials cheated teachers that they would be recruited by
councils under the new law. The teachers rallied their families to reject
the draft."
But Ford People supporters interviewed said the party's top leadership
was vanquished in the referendum because of MPs' "inability to read the
political signs of the times".
"That they remained united in support of the draft was good but that did
not mean following them blindly and forgetting the shoddy manner in which
they have run CDF, bursary and Aids committees and constituency road funds,"
said Peter Nyachiro, a resident of Bonchari.
Voters argued that constituency committee members handling public funds
had amassed wealth within three years and were arrogant. "To hit back, we
had to vote against what our MPs stood for. We needed to remind them that
all was not well with those to whom they had entrusted development matters,"
said Mary Nyangate of Kitutu Chache.
The three MPs are aware that the arrogance of the people they appointed
to run the constituency bodies may have cost them precious votes.
We are now being told the vote was not against or for the proposed
constitution but against our politics, they admitted, adding their former
supporters joined the Orange camp, citing arrogance of constituency
committee members.
"We have analysed the referendum voting patterns and are surprised that
we lost miserably in areas where our close political aides come from," said
one of the MPs.
The area Banana campaign leader Simeon Nyachae also appears to have
performed dismally at polling stations around areas where his aides
come. But the three MPs are optimistic they will consolidate their
popularity before 2007. They say they have no regrets about losing the
referendum.
"We will remain united under Nyachae for that is the only way we will
have a stake in any future government," said Bomachoge's Joel Onyancha who
surprised many by delivering majority Banana vote from his area. They
maintain that they did not lose to anybody.
"The referendum did not test our individual or collective performance
but the voters' feelings about the draft constitution," said Manoti.
They argue that if the votes cast were to be attributed to individual
politicians, the incumbents would carry the day.
"Despite losing to a group of 14 opponents whom I beat in the last
elections, I enjoy majority support," Manduku maintained.
"The 14 collectively garnered about 16,000 votes against my 9,000.  They
are cheating themselves when they say they are the kings of Kisii
politics. Mambo bado," he cautioned.
Obwocha's argument is similar. "About 16 seasoned politicians and
amateurs teamed up to spread propaganda against the draft.
They beat us in the propaganda war but come election time, things will
be different."
For the first time, Obwocha shared a platform with rival and former
nominated MP Catherine Nyamato to convince West Mugirango voters to support
the Wako Draft.
"The Orange team is boasting that it vanquished us in our
backyards.
They should be reminded that there was never a contest between political
parties.

Original_Search_Title: Uganda Could Learn From Neighbours
Headline: Uganda Could Learn From Neighbours
Body_Text:
Recent political developments in neighbouring Kenya and Tanzania have
put Uganda to shame. First, on November 21 Kenyans voted overwhelmingly to
reject a new Constitution that was pushed by the so-called Banana side led
by President Mwai Kibaki.
There were several lessons Uganda could learn from the government side's
defeat. The first is a lesson of hope-that it is still possible for
incumbents to lose elections in Africa.
The second is a lesson on political tolerance. The Orange side that won
in the referendum was led by seven ministers in Kibaki's government who
joined the official leader of the opposition, Mr Uhuru Kenyatta.
In Uganda, those "rebel" ministers would have been sacked long before
the referendum. In Kenya, Kibaki waited until after the referendum and
conceded defeat before dissolving his Cabinet. In Tanzania, President
Benjamin Mkapa this week bid farewell to the nation, after completing his
two terms.
Unlike several African leaders, including our own president, he did not
have the Constitution changed to allow him to go for a third term.
For all their problems, Kenya and Tanzania appear to have settled the
transition problem that continues to dog Uganda.
We could learn a few lessons from them.
Govt can do better
The government's outright dismissal of the Pan African Parliament's
appeal to President Yoweri Museveni to effect the unconditional release of
detained opposition leader Kizza Besigye is as inept as it is
disconcerting.
Besigye, the president and presidential candidate of the Forum for
Democratic Change is facing charges of treason and rape before the High
Court. He is also facing charges of terrorism and illegal possession of
firearms before the military General Court Martial.
In making the appeal, the Pan African Parliament was echoing several
calls from world leaders who feel that Besigye's arrest and the conduct of
his trial at this time of political transition is a threat to peace and
stability in Uganda.
But the government spokesman, Information state minister Nsaba Buturo,
could not hear any of that. He said the African Parliament does not have the
mandate to interfere in the way Uganda is governed.
Dr Buturo could have done better.
Nobody is saying that the opposition leader is above the law, or that
Uganda should cede its sovereignty. The government could allay the fears of
those who are concerned about Dr Besigye's trial by promising and ensuring a
quick, fair and transparent trial.
Unfortunately, recent events cast serious doubts on this notion.

Original_Search_Title: Polls Proved That Power is With the Voters
Headline: Kenya: Polls Proved That Power is With the Voters
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Following the outcome of the referendum, one question has been repeatedly asked: "What did it all really mean?"
In my view, basically three things: First, there is no such thing as
a perfect constitution. Even the Bomas Draft, which was supposedly,
"people-driven," and is now at the centre of focus for those who seek
to continue with the struggle for a new constitution, cannot be perfect
in any sense of that word.
A constitution has to be seen as being, in perpetuity, a work in
progress. If this were not the case, then there would never be any need
for constitutional amendments in any country.
So this admittedly historic vote and the subsequent rejection of the
proposed new constitution must be seen as merely an unusually dramatic
episode in an ongoing process.
But of course, by the time the votes were cast, the issues at stake
had long ceased to be those of constitutional principles. Rather it was
all about an open contest for political support between the Orange and
Banana groupings.
And this brings us to the second point: Do not confuse political drama for progress.
Back in 2000, at the time of the political merger between the then
ruling party, Kanu (led then by President Moi) and the National
Democratic Party (led by Mr Raila Odinga), there seemed to have been a
fundamental shift in the politics of the country.
Virtually all political analyst in the country were unanimous in
writing off the then Leader of the Official Opposition, Mr Mwai Kibaki,
as he was considered to have no chance against the invincible political
monolith created by the former President with an eye to the 2002
election.
And yet as we all know now, it was the Kanu-NDP partnership which
broke up, while Mr Kibaki went on to be the presidential candidate for
the united opposition parties. That led him to the presidency, and
ultimately, the historic humiliation of the referendum result.
And what of the man considered by many to be the principal architect
of the president's mortification? Well, the immediate former Roads and
Public Works Minister has also had his share of ups and downs. He is
not the infallible political genius that many of his supporters seem to
believe he is. By mid-2003, when it became increasingly clear that the
pre-election MoU signed by the Liberal Democratic Party and National
Alliance of Kenya (NAK) was not going anywhere, political pundits kept
asking: What was Raila Odinga thinking when he put his pen to that
agreement? How could he have been so naÃ¯ve?
At that time, Mr Odinga was seen to have been very easily duped by
what later came to be known as "Mt Kenya Mafia," and to have served
their goals at the expense of his own.
But now look who is laughing: All those Kiraitu Murungis and Martha
Karuas, and Danson Mungatanas and many others who had grown accustomed
to mocking Mr Odinga with impunity, are now strangely silent.
In just one crushing defeat, all their boasts and accusations have
been proved to be empty. And each must now be somberly considering what
the future might hold, especially if the newly victorious Orange
Democratic Movement makes a power sharing deal with the President.
The greater point is that this referendum business is but the latest
twist in the ongoing drama of the fight for political supremacy.
But it is just that - the latest intriguing chapter. It is not a
clear pointer to the outcome of the 2007 election. And so to the final
point: On the occasion of the American independence celebrations (on
July 4) the US Ambassador, Mr William Bellamy, had this to say about
the continuing grand corruption in Kenya:
"- too many highly-placed elected officials are allowed to pursue
with impunity their money-making enterprises - for the benefit of
themselves, their kin and their clans - at the expense of Kenyan
taxpayers and the Kenyan poor."
This is not a problem that outsiders can or should resolve. Sooner
or later Kenyans will resolve it by demanding clean and accountable
government."
It is not likely that Mr Bellamy could have guessed just how prophetic his words would prove to be.
For that is what happened during the referendum: Kenyans made a demand for clean and accountable government.
Many analysts have pointed out that only an insignificant percentage
of those who voted on that day had the faintest idea about what that
famous document that they were voting on really contained.
Fundamentally, the occasion provided disgruntled voters with an
opportunity to register their discontent. And this discontent is rooted
in the government's failure to deliver on development promises made in
2002.
A president who had genuinely fought corruption and improved people's lives would not have been humiliated as Mr Kibaki was.
So, ultimately, what the referendum outcome revealed was that when
many of us fretted about the possibilities of the new constitution
producing an elective monarchy or an imperial presidency, we had missed
the point.
In a democracy, it is the people who rule; it is the voters who wield imperial power.
In this context, it little matters whom the President invites to
join his cabinet: that is merely the next stage of the old game of
musical chairs.
We now know that what matters is that the President and his team should improve the lives of ordinary people.

Original_Search_Title: Female Speak - Survival of the Fittest
Headline: Kenya: Female Speak - Survival of the Fittest
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A group of my friends were feeling nothing but pity for the
president for his current predicament until we realised that a few
errant members of our group of girls were in dire need of healing and
reconciliation. Earlier this year, a lucky member had miraculously
convinced some bloke that she was indeed marriage material.
Many committee meetings and diets later, we were all ready to walk
our lucky girlfriend down the aisle. But something happened on the way
to marital heaven - a strategic thinker in the group decided that it
was time to up the stakes in the game of love and marriage. We are yet
to discover the exact details of her marketing strategy, but the groom
experienced a sudden change of heart. With no consideration for the
efforts and labours of family and friends, the now ex- communicated
girlfriend stole the groom and has now converted him into her
groom-to-be in the next few weeks.
To make matters worse, she will be walking down the aisle with a
little something in her swollen belly. They say all is fair in love and
war but when you steal your girlfriend's lover, you have effectively
sounded the battle cry.
After careful evaluation of events preceding the man-theft, we
discovered that the aggrieved girl had behaved rather carelessly in
matters of love. She had left herself wide open and created an
opportunity for clandestine love to bloom and blossom between her man
and our friend. The warning signs were there all the time but she chose
to ignore them in the name of sisterly love.
Three is a crowd and any woman who has no issues with occupying the
tabooed third spot is a woman to avoid. It is rather laughable to
imagine that any woman, who is a spare wheel in this kind of
arrangement, wouldn't mind being the main ride.
I fail to understand why a woman who can vote, drive and work on her
own would insist on being accompanied by their single girlfriends on
all her dates. It baffles me that we will not let our house help near
our men yet we will let our buxom girlfriends dance with our mates in a
very intimate manner. Within no time, the experimental body heat
searching instincts of our boyfriends take over. Soon three in one
dates become two in one dates - only you are not allowed entrance. And
then we pretend that we didn't see it coming, who are we kidding?
It is okay to love your girlfriend but if she is single, available
and searching, get her a good movie or a good book as you go out with
your boyfriend. Just do not let her get too close to your man because
chances are, she's envying you every bit and would not mind edging you
out of the scene. Remember that all the propaganda that states that
self-assured sisters are not scared of competition is spread by single
spinsters.
Be wary of girls who are pseudo-experts in relationships and
psycho-analysis. These are the women who are the local shrink, Oprah
Winfrey and your mother all wrapped into one. They have innate powers
of detecting what may be ailing your relationship simply by analysing
the skin colour of your boyfriend. They profess to be gurus on the
workings of the male mind, the female mind and the couple's mind.
These crusaders for global peace insist on being tireless mediators
in their quest for love, healing and harmony between squabbling lovers.
Every time you and your man fight, this girl will bend over backwards
(literally in some cases) to make sure that you find healing in your
relationship. She will act as the tireless peace broker and will even
make late night visits to your man to find a way to thaw your frosty
relations.
She will donate both her empathic ears, her soft non-judgemental
voice and her soft, curvaceous frame to convince your man that true
love is worth one more chance. Only that the true love will be with her
and not with you. She will have snared your man by being the better
contestant; by being less of a nag and more of the dewy-eyed,
enthralled listener. So, if you have one of your girl friends who
insists on mending your relationship every time it breaks, you need to
be careful. Ask her to donate her reconciliation skills to the Orange
and Banana teams, or tell her to apply for a position in Kofi Annan's
peacekeeping force.
Lastly, be wary of girlfriends who look, cook or talk better than
you. If your girlfriend's cleavage pops into higher and greater levels
than your flat chest, you can bet that your significant other
fantasizes about testing just how well her chest would fit in his arms.
If her waist-hip ratio is more impressive than yours, your man might be
harbouring dreams of testing just how compatible they are to his
procreation dreams. If you cannot boil an egg then do not let a girl
who can make some killer 'chapatis' near your man. And if you have two
left feet, you have no business introducing your 'ndombolo' champion
girlfriend to your mate.
How do you prevent a grown man from harbouring dirty thoughts when
faced with a good-looking chick who can cook and rotate her hips at the
speed of lightning? Very few men can resist the allure of some good
food served by a woman who has the potential of being their willing
secret love. Some may call you insecure, but with the scarcity of good
men around, women must hold on dearly to the good catch they have.
They say that we women are our own worst enemies but it is because
we sometimes get too careless in the name of love. Let us not forget
that it is a competitive word, the best woman always wins...even when
she was once your best friend.

Original_Search_Title: Seek Truce On Review, Urges Electorial Commission
Headline: Kenya: Seek Truce On Review, Urges ECK
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Orange and Banana camp leaders should hold talks to end public anxiety, the Electoral Commission of Kenya has said.
"The ECK appeals to all our leaders, including religious leaders, to
put the interest of the nation first before their tribal or personal
interests," said commission chairman Samuel Kivuitu in a statement
yesterday.
The commission also cast doubts on the possibility of restarting the
review process in the face of the present political hatred and
antagonism.
Mr Kivuitu asked political leaders to be cautious about their
language, actions and thoughts and warned that the public was likely to
follow what the leaders said.
The call came as the Orange Democratic Movement (ODM), which won the
referendum by a million votes, softened its hard-line stance and said
it was willing to talk with the Banana camp which had been campaigning
for the adoption of the proposed Constitution.
After a two-day meeting at Naivasha, which ended yesterday, the
Orange leaders also resolved to ask the President to recall Parliament
in January to restart the Constitution review process.
They had earlier demanded that President Kibaki dissolve Parliament and call for snap elections.
Yesterday, Mr Kivuitu said it would be impossible to restart the review process in a charged political atmosphere.
Muslim religious leaders yesterday told the ODM to reduce the threats and stop imposing conditions on the Government.
They called on the team to show willingness to dialogue and work
with the President and other political leaders with divergent views.
They also asked the President to open dialogue and unite the country after the referendum.
Sheikh Khalfan Khamis, the chairman of the Majlis Ulamaa, which is a
collection of Muslim religious scholars, asked the Orange team to show
leadership and tolerance.
"The President has the mandate of the people of Kenya who elected
