him with an overwhelming majority and should therefore take charge of
uniting the country," he said.

Original_Search_Title: We Have Learnt Tough Lesson - Pro-Referendum Team
Headline: Kenya: We Have Learnt Tough Lesson - Banana Team
Body_Text:
Nairobi — More than 50 MPs who supported the Draft Constitution voted down in
the referendum met in Nairobi and unanimously agreed on one thing: The
results had taught them tough lessons.
The leaders, who met for dinner on Thursday night, said the loss had
helped them to reflect on what Kenyans really needed in a new
Constitution.
They had also come to terms with the bitter truth that tribalism was
deeply-rooted in the country, judging from the voting patterns.
The revelation came as the Orange Democratic Movement announced it
had abandoned its demand for snap elections and instead asked President
Kibaki to recall Parliament in a month's time to restart the
Constitution review process.
The about-turn came barely a day after the group announced it was
ready to soften its hard-line position, and start negotiations with the
Banana camp to calm post-referendum anxiety.
On Thursday, the Orange movement said it was "ready, willing and
available" for discussions and President Kibaki should facilitate the
talks as a matter of national priority.
Speaking in Naivasha, the top leadership of the movement said they
were ready for talks and were not placing conditions in entering such
dialogue.
Yesterday, it emerged that the movement had agreed to soften its
stand on various issues for the sake of peace and harmony in the
country.
But the group demanded that the Government lifts the ban on Orange victory rallies planned countrywide.
The movement said Parliament should reconvene in January to quickly
enact laws establishing a constituent assembly and referendum law for
Kenyans to get a new constitution.
The leaders spoke at the end of a two-day retreat at the Lake Naivasha Simba Lodge, organised by the Fredrick Ebert Foundation.
In Nairobi, the Banana campaign chairman, Mr Simeon Nyachae, told
the pro-Government MPs, who included his six former Cabinet colleagues,
that it was puzzling why Kenyans rejected the document yet it proposed
the disbandment of the provincial administration.
He said Kenyans had proposed scrapping of the provincial
administration at Bomas only to reverse their decision during the
referendum.
Mr Nyachae said the challenge for MPs is how to read public mood accurately and respond accordingly.
Tribalism was so entrenched in the country that it was difficult to
root it out even if all politicians in both Orange and Banana camps met
to discuss it.
He explained that many politicians realised that it was a "cancer" only during the referendum poll.
Mr Nyachae, who hosted the meeting, said the vice had infiltrated
religious leaders, journalists and "other professionals" and called for
concerted efforts to reverse the trend.
"This issue of ethnicity is such a serious problem. Whether it's a
religious leader, a journalist or any other professional... you read
ethnicity. It is a cancer.
"Did you know that ethnicity was such deep-rooted in this country
before the referendum?" he asked the legislators to which they
responded "No".
Former ministers present at the Stanley Hotel meeting were Kiraitu
Murungi, Musikari Kombo, David Mwiraria, Newton Kulundu, Martha Karua
and Morris Dzoro.
Government chief whip Norman Nyagah was the master of ceremonies.
Mr Nyachae cited disagreement between Christianity and Muslim over
some contents of the rejected Draft as even more deep-rooted than
ethnicity.
Religious leaders from the two faiths clashed many times over the
status of the Kadhi's courts in the Draft. While the Muslims wanted it
entrenched in the Constitution, their Christian counterparts wanted it
created by an Act of Parliament.
Mr Nyachae suggested that MPs allied to both camps help to reconcile both religions and make them accept each other.
He also called on the two camps to discuss the review once Parliament was reopened.
The Nyaribari Chache MP, however, said the Banana team should lead
the process because "we are in charge and we shall continue being in
charge".
President Kibaki prorogued the House last week following the
referendum results. The Orange group, which opposed the proposed
Constitution, won the polls with 3.5 million votes against Banana's 2.5
million.
Mr Nyachae conceded that the Banana lost the poll partly because the
campaign was not "100 per cent perfect. But you should not regret that
things went wrong, you did a lot of work and you should congratulate
yourselves," he told the MPs.
He asked them to have a sense of identity with the Banana team,
saying they should not hate themselves for being part of the group that
lost.
"Instead of thinking of what has happened, let's now concentrate on
what the people of Kenya want. If we implement what they want, then we
shall be in the front line," he said.
The former Energy minister said those in Government could be hated
by many, citing how he was referred to as "Prime Minister" while he was
chief secretary.
In Naivasha, Kanu's nominated MP Mutula Kilonzo, who read
yesterday's Orange teams' statement asked the President to facilitate a
meeting between Banana and Orange groups.
The group said it would appoint a panel of experts to take stock of
the review process and synthesise the contentious issues. The committee
would also analyse both the pre and post referendum debates in
consultation with other interest groups.
The meeting agreed that Kanu, Liberal Democratic Party and other
Orange supportive parties consider proposals leading to a coalition to
promote democracy, good governance and social progress.
The movement should encourage dialogue, transparency and accountability in politics, they said.

Original_Search_Title: Orange Team Plans New Coalition for 2007 Poll
Headline: Kenya: Orange Team Plans New Coalition for 2007 Poll
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange Democratic Movement concluded its two-day Naivasha retreat on
Friday with a call to constituent parties to consider forming a
coalition.
Orange Democratic Movement MPs, from left, Chris Okemo, Uhuru Kenyatta
and Henry Kosgey consult at Naivasha's Simba Lodge during the group's
two-day retreat that ended on Friday. Pic by Jackson Ngugi
The ODM leaders also asked President Kibaki to recall Parliament by
January to enable it to enact the required law to pave the way for a new
constitution. They want Parliament to formulate a law that will see the
creation of a constituent assembly and a referendum law.
They also demanded that the Government withdraw, "the illegal,
unconstitutional and undemocratic decree" banning ODM's thanksgiving
rallies.
Ironically, consultation on the composition of a new Cabinet, which has
been one of the key demands by the Orange leaders, seemed to have taken a
back seat during the retreat.
They also demanded that all records kept by the Constitution of Kenya
Review Commission (CKRC) be put in the custody of the Attorney General, and
copies given to the National Assembly, National Archives and the University
of Nairobi.
The CKRC is expected to wind up its operations by next month.
Orange team leader Mutula Kilonzo, at a news conference attended by key
leaders Raila Odinga, Kalonzo Musyoka, Uhuru Kenyatta, William Ruto, Anyang'
Nyong'o, Linah Kilimo, Henry Kosgey, Omingo Magara and Marsden Madoka, read
the resolutions. The retreat was sponsored by German NGO, Fredrich Ebert
Stiftung.
The Orange leaders once again asked President Kibaki to facilitate a
meeting between them and their Banana counterparts to discuss the way
forward on a new constitution. They resolved that: " ODM appoints a panel of
experts to take stock of the review process, synthesise contentious issues
and analyse both pre and post referendum debates and, in consultation with
other stakeholders, inform ODM on the way forward".
However, the ODM emerged from the two-day retreat in a more
reconciliatory tone, a clear shift from the earlier militant demand that
President Kibaki dissolves Parliament and calls snap elections.
They had previously maintained that the loss of the referendum vote by
the Banana side amounted to a vote of no confidence in the Government.
But yesterday, they said they had deliberated and considered various
options and other relevant matters in regard to their demands.
They resolved that member parties, through their organs, give proposals
on the building of the coalition that will promote democracy, good
governance and social progress in Kenya, while encouraging a political
culture of dialogue, transparency and accountability.
On the structure of the proposed coalition, Raila said constituent
parties would first of all put forward their proposals for consideration
before its adoption.
Raila denied that Orange had changed its earlier stand, saying the
movement had always been ready for dialogue.
"Our first option was dissolution of Parliament after the Government
lost in the referendum and our second option has always been constructive
dialogue on the way forward," Raila said.
Nyong'o said there was urgent need to enact a new constitution, hence
the need for Parliament to reconvene early to enact the necessary
legislation.
The leaders said for the second time in three years, Kenyans had
inflicted heavy political defeat on the Government of the day.
"They have done so with a unanimity, which has in each case stretched
across virtually the entire nation. Once again, our people have been ahead
of so many of the elected representatives," said Mutula.

Original_Search_Title: ECK Urges Orange Team to Seek Peace
Headline: Kenya: ECK Urges Orange Team to Seek Peace
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Electoral Commission of Kenya has urged the Orange team to put Kenya
first and stop seeking vengeance.
"Let those who won the referendum contest tame their urge for vengeance
for the sake of the nation," ECK chairman Samuel Kivuitu said in a Press
statement.
Kivuitu said those who lost should accept the results the same way
President Kibaki did and come to terms with reality. He sent an appeal to
all leaders, including religious leaders, to put the interest of the nation
first before their tribal or personal interest.
Kivuitu said the glue that holds the country together is not very strong
and that leaders should watch their language, action and thoughts. He said
the referendum was not about the current constitution or Bomas draft but
about the proposed constitution.
Kivuitu said the question asked at the referendum was whether Kenyans
were for or against the ratification of the proposed constitution. He said
it would be "crooked thinking" to argue that Kenyans voted for the current
or for the Bomas draft. He said Kenyans had a document to vote for but they
rejected it even though they had previously struggled at great risk for a
new constitution.
He said by voting, No, they were saying the document is not what they
struggled for and wanted a better document. Kivuitu said constitution making
is a complex process and it must take into consideration wide ranging
views.
Banana welcomes overtures
Meanwhile, the Banana camp has welcomed the Orange team's overtures and
hinted that they were ready to meet and negotiate.
The chairman of the Banana Group Co-ordination Committee, Mr Simeon
Nyachae, said his team found the Orange team's reconciliatory mood
constructive.
"Dialogue is the only way of building a consensus on the way forward,"
Nyachae said in a statement he read yesterday.
He said consultation should take place for a mutual and acceptable plan
of action on the way forward regarding the constitution.
Those present at the Press Conference at Parliament Buildings were
former assistant ministers Soita Shitanda, Moses Wetangula, Mutua Katuku,
Wilfred Machage, Danson Mungatana and Kipkalia Kones.
Others were MPs Bonny Khalwale, Silvestor Wakoli and Wafula
Wamunyinyi.
The Orange team leaders used their retreat in Naivasha to soften their
stand over meeting with the Head of State and their call for snap
elections. They also launched a strategy to revive the Bomas Draft, which
provides for the sharing of power between the President and the prime
minister.
Marende read the statement that lacked the tough language and heroic
rhetoric of the post-referendum rally at Uhuru Park.
Nyachae said the Banana team welcomed the statement in which the Orange
group urged for consultation in order to find a mutually acceptable plan of
action on the way forward.
The Orange camp had appointed Nominated MP Mutula Kilonzo to co-ordinate
all the communications from the Government and the Banana side for a
meeting. The Banana team held a party on Thursday night at The Stanley Hotel
where they agreed to do a postmortem over the referendum loss.
During the party, Nyachae regretted that ethnicity and religious
divisions were entrenched in the country. Immediate former ministers Kiraitu
Murungi, Musikari Kombo, Maurice Dzoro, David Mwiraria and a host of former
assistant ministers, attended the party.

Original_Search_Title: You're Solely to Blame for Defeat, Kiraitu Told
Headline: Kenya: You're Solely to Blame for Defeat, Kiraitu Told
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Former Justice and Constitutional Affairs minister Kiraitu Murungi
was personally responsible for the dismal performance of the Banana
group, his colleagues said yesterday.
MPs Joseph Munyao (Mbooni), Newton Kulundu (Lurambi), Otieno
Kajwang' (Mbita), Koigi Wamwere (Subukia) and a lobby group asked Mr
Murungi to stop exonerating himself and blaming others when he was part
of the Yes team.
However, Mr Mutua Katuku (Mwala) agreed with some of the reasons Mr
Murungi gave as having contributed to the Banana loss during the
November 21 referendum on the proposed new Constitution.
Mr Kajwang' said Kenyans ganged up to oppose the proposed
Constitution because Mr Murungi had led those in central Kenya to
believe that voting against it was removing President Kibaki from power.
Mr Munyao said: "He should have been in charge of organising and ensuring everything went smoothly."
The former Livestock and Fisheries minister said as the Justice and
Constitutional Affairs minister, Mr Murungi should have taken charge as
the law review fell in his docket.
He added: "He cannot cry this late that the campaigns were disorganised. Let him give the country a good reason."
Dr Kulundu asked his colleague to stop the "blame game" and join the rest in planning for the 2007 General Election.
Said the former Labour minister: "We should strategise on how to
beat the Orange team in the 2007 elections if they will still be united.
"Leaders should help lower the tension and divisions that have characterised the country since the campaigns."
Mr Wamwere asked why Mr Murungi was now exonerating himself when he was the minister in charge of Constitutional Affairs.
He asked: "If he was not in charge, who was?" Kiraitu is trying to
blame other people for the failures of Yes campaign. Who are these
people?"
The MP asked Mr Murungi to be forthright and name those who were responsible instead of trying to clean himself.
Although a poor campaign strategy largely contributed to the Banana
loss, said Mr Kajwang', it was Mr Murungi's conduct and manner in which
he handled the review that cost them.
However, the real reason why the Banana team lost, he added, was
because Mr Murungi packaged the Wako draft as a Government project.
Said he: "It is time Mr Murungi realised that he is the biggest
problem in the review. He should come out of his defeat and talk to
those who won in order to craft a new constitution."
Mr Katuku said Kenyans rejected the proposed new Constitution on flimsy grounds based on tribalism and politics.
The former Finance assistant minister said the Orange team employed propaganda and politics in their campaigns.
He also said the division in the Cabinet greatly affected the
referendum results, arguing that some Kenyans were swayed because
ministers campaigned against the document.
The National Convention Executive Council (NCEC) took issue with Mr
Murungi's statement saying he was trying to campaign to return to the
Cabinet.
It said Mr Murungi, and to some extent President Kibaki, were to blame for the failure to get new laws.

Original_Search_Title: Clay Court - All the President's Mess
Headline: Kenya: Clay Court - All the President's Mess
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Many years before the National Rainbow Coalition lost some of its
colours, I went to State House Nairobi. I did not go there because I
wanted the hedge in my father's home trimmed.
Or that I wanted the St Mary's Boys and St Patrick's Girls primary
schools in Ukwala turned into universities, like some people were doing
last month, but I went there to do "colour" during a visit by the then
United States Secretary of State General Colin Powell. I was going
there not to cover his speech, but to cover sideshows.
Going to State House was a welcome idea. I had had a harrowing time
at Jomo Kenyatta International Airport while waiting for the visitor.
The Americans had literally taken over the airport, and local
journalists were confined to a pen on the tarmac where we had been
herded into after a thorough security check.
Some of the US officials had come from their Embassy in Kampala, and
the place was swarming with Federal Diplomatic Security officials in
full SWAT - Special Weapons and Tactics - gear.
I landed at State House, sans the Press badge, but my name had been
forwarded as one of the journalists covering Powell's visit. While I
was being cleared by a redeyed security officer, a certain baby-faced
judicial officer happened by and I was shunted to the sidelines by the
security officer.
I survived and found my way to the front - or is it the back - lawn
of the building from where we waited for the president and his visitor.
After a long wait, they did come and before they could start briefing
the Press, some fidgety security officials - must have been Americans -
started fiddling either with the light switches or were mumbling that
the lighting was not adequate.
President Kibaki must have heard or seen what they were up to, and
as the host, and a leader of 30 million Kenyans, he bellowed out. "I
think the lighting is enough," or something to that effect. That put a
stop to the Americans' shenanigans, and gave me a sense of deja vu.
And, as I concluded in my colour piece, "the President of the Republic
of Kenya was in charge".
A minor incident you may say, but it made me - and many other
Kenyans who read my piece - have faith in President Kibaki. Looking
back at that incident, and many others before that, I keep on wondering
where the rain started beating our Head of State, whose inauguration
speech was delivered in a authoritative voice that gave Kenyans hope
that at least there was someone they could trust, even though he was in
a wheelchair.
Where, just where did President Kibaki start losing it, so much so
that Kenyans could even feel his weakening grip on the affairs of the
State. How did the President let so many things fall through his
fingers that he could not even rein in his own ministers, people he had
appointed to the posts, and people whose political futures depended on
him? Why did President Kibaki give his ministers so much leeway that
they even got the guts to issue statements and he kept mum while
Kenyans were crying foul at every move they made? The most he could do
was to use the P-word to describe people he imagined were not on his
side!
Sometimes I wish the President were as decisive as the security
officer who shunted me aside to make way for the judicial officer.
Whatever happened, and whatever might happen, the President has no
one else to blame, but himself for squandering the public's goodwill
and what he least needs is sympathy - for that is just but half-justice.
Political analysis
Before we start off on this television programme, it will only be
imperative that the viewers know who will be talking to them. I am a
referendum consultant and an expert on the Government's that have
sacked their Cabinets, not to mention that I am also an election
philosopher and a Constitutional journalist.
You did ask what my take on the referendum results is. What I can
tell you is that the fact that many votes were in favour of the Orange
- the team that was opposed to the draft Constitution - shows that many
people voted for the Orange, and that the number of those opposed to
the draft were more than those who were for it.
It is a tricky situation, but that is what my analysis shows. It is
also coming out that since many people did not support the Banana camp,
the Banana camp lost out despite the fact that they intended to win,
just like the Orange side also intended to win.
But I will be quick to add that a referendum is different from a
general election, but at the same time, it is the same in as much as in
both there are losers and winners. It is different in the sense that in
a referendum, there are fewer candidates, in this case there are only
two fruits, Orange and Banana, in which one has to win and the other
has to lose.
It is a tricky situation because the winner in both a referendum and
an election will be that side or party which will have garnered the
highest number of votes, while the loser will be the side or party that
will have garnered lesser votes than the side that will have garnered
more votes.
Another question you asked regarded the situation we have now where the President has sacked the whole Cabinet.
As it is now, since the President has sacked all the ministers,
Kenya does not have any ministers and everybody in Parliament is what
can be referred to as a Member of Parliament, and there are no
ministers.
You also wanted to know what the President's options now are. Well,
the President has no option but to appoint new ministers from the
current Members of Parliament because that is what the law says. If he
decides to appoint the Cabinet from those who are not MPs than he will
have broken the law, and in many other countries, he will be considered
a law breaker. It is quite tricky, but those are the options the
President has.
Cry, Freedom
In its quest to create more jobs, Kenya's triumvirate Cabinet has
created a new ministry: Ministry of Freedom, or Uhuru, whose
spokesperson this week reiterated the three-people Government's
commitment to provide full freedom to all citizens within a time-bound
programme.
Reviewing the great advances the country had already made in this
crucial area, the spokesperson noted that whereas other, less evolved
democracies, continued to foster in their citizens an unhealthy sense
of dependence on governance, Kenya had in effect given its people total
independence in most spheres of daily life.
While in other countries people were still encouraged to look to
support systems to give them such essential services like food,
shelter, basic health care and drinking water, the Kenyan mwananchi had
long discarded these effete crutches and made to learn the virtues of
self-reliance, which was the true essence of freedom.
This great breakthrough had been achieved by the simple but
ingenious method of getting rid of pernicious institutions like
hospitals, public utilities and such which undermined the individual's
right to complete autonomy and bred a slavish mind-set unbecoming a
truly independent nation.
The Ministry of Uhuru was there to ensure that if you wanted medical
treatment, water, food, shelter or whatever else, you were at full
liberty - nothing more and nothing less - to get them yourself, by hook
or crook, in keeping with the high principles of Harambee spirit which
guaranteed every citizen the inalienable right to strife, bribery and
the pursuit of hopelessness.
Ban wagon
The Government's Sunday evening knee jerk reaction banning all the
public rallies by the Orange Democratic Movement - whatever that is -
has brought in to the open other things the Government plans to ban.
Top on the agenda, it seems will be to ban spouses from telling others about the poll results and how they won in the morning.
Such statements make people apathetic towards voting, after all,
they were told that they already won in the morning. Another thing to
be banned are broadcasters who give a one-sided picture making it seem
as if the whole country is green or yellow while the country is Orange.
It has been discovered that such pictures made people rest on their
laurels, confining their outbursts to only one province while the
situation in the rest of the country was different.
Also to be banned are ministers who make Kalembe Ndile lose out when
they open their mouths and those who call political games Government
projects while everyone knew they were not. These careless statements
are responsible for the subsequent heartaches that are being felt all
the way from Othaya to Kibwezi, Kitui Central to Nyaribare Chache,
Funyula and even beyond to Lwanda Kotieno and Ramba polling stations,
where liberators failed to garner even one-tenth of the votes in their
own villages.
The Government's cross hairs are on many things and when it pulls
the trigger, even activists whose spousal status are forever being
denied are likely to fall. Their efforts at giving donations running in
to millions of shillings and shaking their backsides at every market
place did not yield any results. Even State security they enjoy while
many Kenyans live in constant fear of being attacked by criminals, is
not bearing any fruits, bananas or otherwise.
It has also come to the Government's notice that some legislators
have to be banned. Of what use are they to the State if their memories
are so poor that they cannot even remember where their national
identity and voters' cards are. They are not only an embarrassment to
the Government, but also to the people they represent, because if they
have such poor memories, can they even remember the people they
represent?
Others in this category who have to be banned are those who went
ahead and invoked the name of Satan, that it also eats banana, yet the
biggest rally ever was being coordinated by a man of the cloth, who
decided that there is more to life than just giving spiritual
nourishment to an otherwise bored congregation whose immediate need is
physical nourishment.
But while the Government dithers over who should be banned
immediately or later, those who should have been banned like three
years ago are the extremely monied elders who have advisory roles just
because they are high handicappers and the Chief used to spend time
with them on the greens and exclusive clubs - where they were not in
touch with a certain class of people called wananchi.
Kenya Only (1)
Never underestimate the power of a Nairobi blonde. Whenever she
opens her mouth to speak, you always wish she never did. And at times
she does not even have to speak, for a Nairobi blonde always lives up
to the credo that actions speak louder than words.
Experience will teach you that when you are out with a Nairobi
blonde, never give a tip, for the coins will disappear from the saucer
in to her purse faster than you can say Let's go, and she will follow
it up with a snarl. "How can you give her so much money? What did she
do to deserve it?"
Ask globetrotters if blondes in other countries are like ours, and
chances are that ours are in a class of their own. They have perfected
the art of "blonding", for lack of a better word, - and begging - all
in the name of being chic.
But peculiar blondes are not the only things we have which other
countries do not have. Like the Indians who are always "like that only"
when they cannot explain something, Kenyans' war cry of hopelessness is
"Si unajua tu Wakenya" (you know what Kenyans are like). That
expression has become the National Anathema on which the country's
foundation seems to have been built. It is a reflection of Kenyans'
inability to lift themselves up; to change their status in life, and to
do anything about the rot in society.
Ask a Kenyan why we do not raise funds to buy medicine when someone
is unwell, yet we hold a "major harambee to offset the hospital bill" -
a euphemism for feeding mourners - after the person dies, and the
answer will be, "Si unajua tu Wakenya".
Ask a Kenyan why the national football team players can never be
paid their allowances, yet the Government over shoots its revenue
collection budget, and the answer will be, "Si unajua tu Wakenya",
meaning that we were not willing to act to save a life, or in this case
to pay the players so that they can represent us in the regional
championship, and are just a reactive lot, and not a pro-active one.
Unlike the Indians who "are like that only," but can reject
government policies that are anti-people, or are seen to be populist,
Kenyans take everything lying down. "Si unajua tu...."
We have become a nation of hopeless people who are daily oppressed,
stressed and humiliated by politicians, the police, thugs, muggers, the
Government, but, as the Indians are wont to say, "What to do?"
"Si unajua tu...."
But, it is only in Kenya, too, where: - Beauty queens and models wear swimsuits and nightdresses to social functions;
- Parents go to bars with their underage children then blame teachers when they carry alcohol to school;
- Bars and pubs operate for 24 hours and pharmacies and other essential services close down before dusk;
- Curly kit is a hairstyle and not the kit for making the hair;
- The police can recover toy pistols from thugs they have killed after an exchange of fire;
- We rehabilitate roads as well as street children everyday and yet we still have street children and no roads;
- Boxing is not considered a blood sport, and the bloodied face of a boxer can adorn the front page of a family newspaper;
- The face of a rape victim is blacked out during a television
interview then in the subsequent clip, her parents are interviewed on
camera;
- Restaurant workers do not know what the expression "Excuse me" means;
- Accident victims and women in labour are turned away from hospital for lack of admission fees;
- Schoolchildren in uniform can comfortably walk into a bar and buy alcohol and cigarettes;
- Singers who cannot differentiate between a guitar and a flute are called musicians;
- Television reporters sign autographs yet Paul Tergat, marathon world record holder, goes everywhere unnoticed;
- Parents are named after their children (Baba John, Baba Sharon), and not viceversa

Original_Search_Title: This 'Forgiveness' is Bad for Democracy
Headline: Kenya: This 'Forgiveness' is Bad for Democracy
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Archbishop Ndingi Mwana a'Nzeki has just done his duty as a man of
God. He appealed to the Banana and Orange camps to forgive each other
following the heated referendum campaign.
Former Environment minister Mr Musyoka Musyoka, also expressed
similar sentiments a few days ago, saying he had no hard feelings
against his critics, and that he had forgiven them.
Many other politicians have said the same thing about their opponents after elections.
Archbishop Ndingi must have considered it his pastoral duty to urge
forgiveness. Mr Musyoka and other politicians who forgive their
opponents, probably think that by doing so, they are being statesmen
and magnanimous.
But there is something very troubling behind the logic of forgiving
your opponents. That is because democracy, by definition, means, among
other things the following:
- That everyone is entitled to his or her own point of view.
-That, therefore, the right to disagree is inherent in democracy.
- That every citizen has a right to run for office.
- That anyone who exercises the above rights lawfully, commits no offence.
Kenyans spoke
Therefore, if ,for example, Mr Musyoka supports Orange and Mrs Charity Ngilu supports Banana, neither of them wrongs the other.
Either of them, in trying to woo the voters to their side, is entitled to tell the people why the other presents a bad option.
If you think of it, if the Orangeand Banana sides didn't think that
the opposing side represented a bad option, there wouldn't have been
campaigns and a need for a vote on November 21.
The country would have been happy to have President Kibaki sign the
new Constitution into law from the privacy of his office, and we would
all get on fine.
But much as President Kibaki would have done better with that less
messy option, he had the wisdom to throw the issue to the country for a
test on how many people liked the draft Constitution. Kenyans spoke,
rejecting it by 57 per cent against 43 per cent.
By a strict democratic standard, no one could have wronged the
other. Therefore, Archbishop Ndingi doesn't have to urge the rival
camps to forgive each other, as if one has stolen the other's property.
Likewise, it's quite presumptuous of Mr Musyoka to forgive those who
criticised him politically. Implicit in the idea of forgiveness is that
the person whom you are offering it to sinned. But if I exercise my
right to choose, how does that constitute sinning?
The notion of forgiveness is profoundly undemocratic. The hidden
message is that there should have been only one view on the
Constitution.
Secondly, that there was one side in the campaign that represented
good, and the other which stood for evil. For that reason, it entitles
the one that stood for good to pass judgement on, and forgive the evil
one.
But this is not the way democracy works. Kenya has lived most of its
life under Kanu's one-party rule. Kanu faced no opposition from other
parties, so there were no bare-knuckled contests for power like we saw
in 2002 at the height of the multiparty wave, or during the referendum.
The candidate who was favoured by Kanu headquarters won in the majority of cases.
Kenyans rejected that and fought for multiparty politics. The flying
feathers and name-calling we witnessed recently comes with democracy.
To say we shouldn't have it is to objectively argue for a return to
one-party politics.
Politicians forgive their opponents, because the anti-democratic culture still runs deep in Kenya.
During the referendum campaigns, for example, the Orange group
argued that the Banana team wanted the Wako draft because it helped
them keep a grip on power and its wages.
The Banana side, for their part, said the Orange team was out to grab power and was not interested in a new Constitution.
How you get power
It would have been a joke if the two sides weren't serious about
what they were saying. It raises the question: What is politics all
about?
Politics is about one thing, and one thing only -POWER. If you want
to serve the people and bring development in government, there is no
way you can do it except by getting power first. The only thing is how
you get that power.
In many parts of the Third World in the 1970s, 1980s, and 1990s,
many people got that power through coups. People like President Yoweri
Museveni of Uganda, next door, got it by waging a guerrilla war.
In Kenya, you get it first by joining politics, and fighting for
office against others. Politics is about power. Constitutions are about
power. If you don't want earthly power, don't go into politics.
Archbishop Ndingi didn't want it; he wanted spiritual power, and
therefore joined the priesthood. Other people choose the power to shape
our children, so they become teachers. Others elect to fashion peoples'
world knowledge and public affairs mindset, so they become journalists
and control the information that the public gets.
If we aren't going to forgive Archbishop Ndingi for saving a lost
soul and setting it on a heavenly path, we shouldn't forgive those who
joined politics and wanted to take away power from us, or kept it when
we desperately wanted it.
So political healing, yes. Forgiveness, certainly no.
Mr Juma is a consultant on political economy.

Original_Search_Title: Kenyans Have Failed to Keep the Promise
Headline: Kenyans Have Failed to Keep the Promise
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Yesterday was the World Aids Day, and the theme, 'Stop Aids: Keep the Promise', was quite appropriate.
With the referendum behind us, we should gear up for the future -
hopefully as a united people in the fight against the Aids scourge that
knows no bananas or oranges.
As the campaigning went ahead, I watched keenly to see if our
leaders remembered that there were other matters of national concern
besides the referendum.
This is a time that our leaders should really sit back and reflect,
particularly on the promise made by the President in the year 2003 when
he said "Everyone in my Government and myself now dedicate ourselves to
making the defeat of HIV /Aids our number one priority."
I can only say that even as our Government faces one of the biggest
tests of its life with the recent defeat, it must keep that promise and
stop Aids!
Educating the people
A problem like HIV/Aids is not one that leaders can afford to forget
even for a day. They have to spend every opportunity they have
educating the people about new developments relating to this epidemic.
We seem to have become satisfied with the fact that the majority of
our people are aware of the causes, symptoms and common basic ways of
prevention.
We have even been praised in the most recent UNAids reports as one
of the countries that has made great improvements in reducing infection
rates.
However, there are still so many emerging issues that our people do
not know about this pandemic that it worries me sick that our leaders
behave as though we do not have an epidemic of the magnitude that Aids
has been for the last 15 years.
One needs to travel to a country like Britain and see how committed
some of these countries are about the health of their people.
Although the possibility of the bird flu becoming an epidemic in
Britain is still remote, I am amazed by the amount of effort and
research work that has been put in place to understand this virus.
The possibility of an outbreak of the bird flu in Britain has been
treated with more seriousness than I have seen our country treat the
problem of Aids, yet last year alone, it is reported that about 80,000
Kenyans were newly infected by HIV.
In Britain, they have already stocked drugs in case they have a flu
pandemic, and yet reliable sources in Kenya say that we do even have
the capacity to distribute the already available ARVs, while thousands
of our people desperately need them. As Dr Martin Luther King said,
"Our lives begin to end the day we become silent about things that
matter."
We shall never win this war if we do not keep the promise, which
will be to give the fight against Aids top priority. Unfortunately,
this promise cannot be kept in a country where other social epidemics
thrive.
A prominent theologian from Botswana, Dr Musa Dube, strongly argues
that Aids must be seen, not merely as a health issue, but as a social
issue that affects all aspects of human life. This is an epidemic that
thrives when there is social injustice.
Thus, Dr Dube notes that Aids thrives where there are problems like
gender inequality, stigma, poverty, tribalism, discrimination, hunger,
enmity, pointing fingers etc. These conditions are rife in Kenya
currently.
And given that Aids affects everything and everyone, it must become
everybody's business. It is not a problem that can be left to the
National Aids Control Council and the NGOs directly involved with Aids
work. We need to address the following questions:
-How am I affected by HIV/Aids?
-How am I part of the problem - am I aiding in its spread by commission or omission?
-Am I helping in perpetuating stigma?
-How can I become part of the solution?
Another issue that we must all be aware of as we keep the promise is
that women are now nearly half of all the people infected by HIV, and
gender discrimination, poverty and gender-based violence help fuel the
epidemic.
Discrimination and violence
Without strategies that focus on women, there can be no global progress in fighting the disease.
This is because women know less than men about how to prevent
infection, and what they do know is often rendered useless by the
discrimination and violence they face.
Therefore, promoting concrete actions that address the reality of
women's lives and help decrease their vulnerability to HIV is the only
way forward.
Ms Kamau is a doctoral student, University of London.

Original_Search_Title: Catholic Bishops to Meet Kibaki Over Cabinet
Headline: Kenya: Catholic Bishops to Meet Kibaki Over Cabinet
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Catholic bishops are preparing to meet President Kibaki as intense
lobbying continues ahead of the naming of a new Cabinet.
Nairobi archbishop Ndingi Mwana a' Nzeki visited State House earlier in
the week to finalise arrangements for a meeting between the Head of State
and the country's 28 bishops.
President Kibaki, meanwhile, kept the country guessing as to who would
be in his new Government for the eighth day.
Anxious Kenyans called our newsroom to enquire whether the President
would announce his new line-up yesterday.
Confirming his meeting with the Head of State, Archbishop Ndingi said:
"The Church will be more than willing to share with the President on matters
like this when called upon to do so. We will either appoint a delegation or
go all of us.
Constitutional lawyers
"We will be consulting constitutional lawyers to ensure we are
well-informed when such advice is sought from us now and in the future."
On the Cabinet, the archbishop said the President should appoint men and
women of integrity, who will be faithful to him and the Government.
"They should be people who will implement Government policies and work
closely with the Opposition to promote national unity," he said.
Archbishop Ndingi's visit to State House was only the latest in a series
of invitations by President Kibaki who dissolved the Cabinet on November 23,
saying he wanted a new team which would be more cohesive in serving
Kenyans.
The President has met with officials of the National Council of Churches
of Kenya (NCCK), retired president Daniel arap Moi, former Civil Service
head Richard Leakey, five envoys and former attorney-general Charles Njonjo,
among others.
The strategies being proposed include proposals for the President to use
his powers and create the office of prime minister.
This would enable him to have two offices, which can be shared out among
communities with huge voting blocks.
Strategists have suggested that Coast and Western provinces be targeted
to return to the Narc fold together with the Kamba and Kisii communities,
before efforts are made to convince voters in other areas.
Archbishop Ndingi proposed that at least four respectable women be
included in the new Cabinet. He singled out Nobel laureate Wangari Maathai
as worthy of inclusion in the new line-up.
He added: "They should be courageous, ready to take criticism and
champion the cause of marginalised areas, for this country belongs to all of
us."
The archbishop said the head of Civil Service should be a person who was
ready to make the Head of State accessible to people "and must not hide
behind bureaucracy to mystify State House and usurp the roles of the
President".
The Anglican Church petitioned the President to leave out
corruption-tainted parliamentarians in his new-look Cabinet.
The Cabinet should be all-inclusive and support efforts to restart the
constitutional review.
"We appeal to the President to show sensitivity and consider ethnic,
socio-economic and age diversity of Kenyans, appoint men and women of
integrity, not involved in scandals and corrupt practices and who support
him in nation building," Anglican archbishop Benjamin Nzimbi told a news
conference.
He called for a truce between Banana and Orange team leaders.
Anglican bishops opposed calls by the Orange team for a snap election,
saying it would be an unjustifiable expenditure on public coffers.
The NCCK suggested that the President select an "ethnically-sensitive
Cabinet".
The petition was contained in a letter sent to State House and which was
followed by a meeting with the President on Wednesday.
The private meeting was attended by the NCCK chairman, Bishop Eliud
Wabukala, general secretary Mutava Musyimi, Archbishop Nzimbi and the
moderator of the Presbyterian Church of East Africa, the Rev Dr David
Githii, among others.
They said that the review process should not be left to Parliament
alone.
Separately, the Rev Musyimi said he was confident the President would
come up with a Cabinet of national unity by next week.
"Sacking ministers was a bold move he took; many of us were shocked; the
President cannot, therefore, make unworkable decisions; he must know what he
is doing," he said.
At the same time, Ford Kenya is demanding a higher stake in the
eagerly-awaited Cabinet.
The party's MPs said referendum results showed they had a bigger stake
in the Government and are expecting President Kibaki to accord them their
status.
Ford Kenya parliamentary spokesman Soita Shitanda said: "We are
expecting more posts than we had in the dissolved Cabinet."
Although the party's top organ - the national executive committee - had
not met to agree on the list of demands, Mr Shitanda (Malava), Dr Bonny
Khalwale (Ikolomani) and Mr Wafula Wamunyinyi (Kanduyi) said they wanted to
play a major role in the post-referendum government.
The party's five constituencies in Bungoma District and two in Trans
Nzoia voted overwhelmingly for the proposed new Constitution.
And in Gusii, a section of the residents want former Energy minister and
Ford People leader Simeon Nyachae re-appointed to the Cabinet.
The other section is rooting for South Mugirango MP Omingo Magara to get
a ministerial post to make him the community's spokesman.
In Coast Province, some leaders asked the President to address the
perennial land problems in the region while naming his Cabinet.
But Sheikh Ali Idriss, the Mombasa Council of Imams and Preachers
treasurer, said credibility of the leaders to be appointed should be
paramount.
Mombasa Anglican bishop Julius Kalu said Coast people had been peaceful,
loyal and patient for long and it was time they were rewarded with
significant ministerial positions and not just being in the Cabinet.
Elsewhere, coordinator of Muslim Human Rights Hussein Khalid said a big
percentage of the population in the Coast would wish to be protected from
interference in their way of life in the name of fighting terrorism.
And a section of Coast leaders supporting the Orange Democratic Movement
yesterday rebuked two legislators from the area for accepting invitation to
join the new Cabinet.
The leaders, who included former Kaloleni MP Chibule wa Tsuma,
Shirikisho Party of Kenya officials Mwakio Ndau, Joel Ruhu and Mohamed
Salim, described the move as "selfish".
In a press statement the leaders said the stand taken by Bahari MP Joe
Khamisi and his Malindi counterpart Lukas Maitha was a betrayal to the
ideals of the ODM.
But Mr Khamisi's constituents urged him to take the appointment, saying
Bahari constituency has never had a minister and it was time they had
one.

Original_Search_Title: Why MPs Fear Backing Call for Snap Elections
Headline: Kenya: Why MPs Fear Backing Call for Snap Elections
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The majority of Members of Parliament do not support calls for a snap elections for fear of losing their seats and benefits, The Standard has learnt.
Although Orange Democratic Movement leaders have been calling for snap elections, backbenchers only view the calls as attempts to push the Government to the negotiating table.
Some MPs and former ministers who were in the Banana team have also dismissed the calls for a snap election.
Parliamentary sources told The Standard that all 220 MPs had debts amounting to millions of shillings in mortgages that are offered by Parliament.
At the beginning of each term, MPs are eligible for a Sh8 million mortgage payable in the five-year term.
The loan is serviced through a compulsory checkoff system that is uniform for all MPs.
In the event of a snap election, they will lose the mortgage, especially for MPs who will be voted out.
House Clerk Samuel Ndindiri said in the event of a snap election, Parliament will repossess the houses if MPs fail to repay the mortgage.
"I will have no option but to reposses the houses and sell them in order to recover the money," said Ndindiri in an interview.
A legislator said: "MPs are worried because they know the majority of them will be voted out."
He said he was sure that the calls for snap polls did not have the support of majority of MPs since they do not want to lose their huge benefits.
"Majority of MPs have never owned a house and this is a dream come true for them. To tell them to go to the polls and lose their houses is not possible," said the MP.
The ninth Parliament is the first to offer MPs a package that includes a salary of up to Sh500,000, a Sh2 million grant for vehicles, Sh8m mortgage, a contributory medical scheme and over Sh25 million through the Constituency Development Fund.
Laisamis MP Titus Ngoyoni said the majority of MPs feared elections as they were not sure of re-election.
Former assistant minister Moses Wetangula says elections need planning, money and also slow down economic activity.
"Kenyans are also not ready to live in a permanent electioneering process," he said.
Sources say MPs, are treated equally and are free to take the Sh8 million mortgage.

Original_Search_Title: Lock Corrupt MPs Out of New Cabinet, Kibaki Told
Headline: Kenya: Lock Corrupt MPs Out of New Cabinet, Kibaki Told
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Anglican Church and the British Government yesterday asked President Kibaki to lock individuals perceived as corrupt out of his new Cabinet.
They called for a Cabinet that is clean, effective and representative. One, they said, that would achieve results in the fight against corruption.
In a Press conference in Nairobi, Anglican Church head Archbishop Benjamin Nzimbi urged Kibaki not to appoint individuals tainted by scandals and corrupt practices to his new Cabinet.
Nzimbi said the President should only appoint men and women of integrity.
The Archbishop, who visited State House on Wednesday with the National Council of Churches of Kenya General Secretary Mutava Musyimi to meet Kibaki, yesterday told the President to be sensitive to ethnic, socio-economic and age diversity of Kenyans when reconstituting his Cabinet.
"We appeal to the President to show sensitivity and consider ethnic, socio-economic and age diversity of Kenyans, appoint men and women of integrity, not involved in scandals and corrupt practices," Nzimbi said.
In Mombasa, British High Commissioner Adam Wood said the international community was keen on the type of individuals President Kibaki would name into his new Cabinet. Wood said the world expects the President to move fast and reconstitute his Government, but that he must consider important factors like corruption, credibility and performance.
Confirming that various representatives of foreign missions in the country, including himself, went to State House last Friday to meet the Head of State, Wood said the international community - just like the majority of ordinary Kenyans - was eager to see "clean, effective and corruption-free individuals" named as ministers.
During the meeting at State House, Wood said, the issue of credibility and criteria when appointing a new Cabinet were part of the crucial talks they had with Kibaki. "I'm not in a position to disclose everything we talked about, but at least, while bearing in mind and appreciating that it is the President's prerogative to name his own choice of Cabinet, the criteria to be used and the credibility and performance factors were some of the issues that came up".
Last Friday, top American, British, Israeli and German diplomats met Kibaki at State House Nairobi.
Sources privy to the discussions intimated that the new-look Cabinet was top on agenda, with the envoys setting the stage for the type of Cabinet they would like to see Kibaki name.
Yesterday, Wood - speaking at the Mombasa Mayor's Parlour during a courtesy call - urged the President to appoint his ministers speedily to pave way for a more re-energised co-operation between the two countries.
Wood was in Mombasa for a series of meetings with stakeholders in the tourism industry. He met members of the Muslim community and toured various projects at the Coast.
The envoy said the international community respected greatly the fairness and professionalism with which the referendum was carried out, that allowed Kenyans to exercise their preferences freely.
"We commend the Electoral Commission of Kenya, the peaceful calm with which Kenyans voted and the respect for the result by the Banana team. This has helped consolidate Kenya's status as a democratic country".
Wood said Kibaki now has a great opportunity to redeem his Government, saying the people of Kenya were keen to see an administration that can perform well in offering services like health and education, improving infrastructure and achieving results in the fight against corruption.
And speaking separately at the ACK head office at Bishop's House in Nairobi, Nzimbi - who was accompanied by the ACK provincial secretary Bishop William Waqo and the Provost of All Saints Cathedral, the Rev Peter Karanja - dismissed calls for a snap election, saying it was unnecessary.

Original_Search_Title: Orange Softens Stand
Headline: Kenya: Orange Softens Stand
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Orange leaders used their strategic retreat in Naivasha to drop their tough conditions for meeting President Kibaki as well as calls for snap elections. A statement read to journalists at the end of the first leg of high-level closed-door talks read like a lamentation over President Kibaki's silence to their demands for a structured sharing of seats in his new-look Cabinet and a snap election.
Those taking part in the retreat, which ends today, include former ministers Raila Odinga, Kalonzo Musyoka, Najib Balala, Ochillo Ayacko and Linah Kilimo and Kanu chairman Uhuru Kenyatta and secretary-general William Ruto.
In what could be a pointer to the change of tact, the task of reading the three-point statement which would normally have been left to either Ruto or Mbita MP Otieno Kajwang', fell on Emuhaya MP Kenneth Marende.
"We are ready, willing and available for such discussions and we call upon the President to facilitate the same as a matter of national priority in order to address the anxiety of the people of Kenya on the future of the constitutional review process," Marende said.
He read the statement that lacked the tough language and heroic postulation of the post-referendum rally at Uhuru Park while ringed by the Orange top guns at a media briefing during lunch break. The Orange team also launched strategies for reviving the Bomas draft, which provides for sharing of power between the President and the Prime Minister.
The statement read in part: "Even as we seek internal consensus on the way forward, we are fully conscious of the need for the leadership of both the Orange and Banana groups to urgently meet, consult and find consensus on a mutually acceptable plan of action on the urgent desire by Kenyans to have a new and democratic constitution. We are ready, willing and available for such discussions and we call upon the President to facilitate the same as a matter of national priority in order to address the anxiety of the people of Kenya on the future of the constitutional review process.
"As we have stated before, we are equally prepared to discuss any other issues of national concern contrary to allegations in certain quarters of our unwillingness to do so or placing numerous conditions in entering such dialogue. So far we have received no response from the Government to our requests for consultations and dialogue."
The statement ended on a reconciliatory note: "We believe that dialogue, consultations and structured engagement are vital at this stage to ease the political atmosphere and provide a conducive environment for moving the nation forward."
Their position yesterday was a clear shift from their earlier demands that President Kibaki should dissolve Parliament and call snap elections after the Government defeat in the referendum.
They have previously maintained that the loss amounted to a vote of no confidence in the Government.
However, individual members appeared to have been warned that accepting Cabinet appointments would be tantamount to political suicide.
But while the Orange Democratic Movement was on one hand extending an olive branch to President Kibaki and the entire Banana camp, it seemed there were plans to transform the movement into an opposition party.
Kajwang' hinted that Orange could be having plans to evolve into an opposition party. "We might come out with a statement that coalitions can work if managed on broad foundations and well-grounded," he said.
Contrary to earlier statements by ODM that it is either all the former Orange Ministers back to the Cabinet or none, Kajwang' said that was not ODM's problem but Kibaki's. "That is the headache of President Kibaki and it is his prerogative to do so. He can appoint whoever he wants."
However, he maintained that appointments from the Orange camp must be done through structured consultations and not by approaching individuals. "Anybody that wants to go as an individual can go but we are not overenthusiastic in serving in his Government. He can as well form a Banana government and Orange remains as opposition."
Kajwang' said the retreat would critically look at the reason Kenyans voted the way they did and whether it was the Government they did not like or the draft. "What didn't they like? did they vote for the broad coalition called Orange against Banana? and what may we do wrong and make them change their minds against us in future?"
Orange Team leader Mutula Kilonzo - earlier identified as the only person Kibaki should contact for a meeting with ODM - dismissed as untrue reports that some of his colleagues in the Orange camp were negotiating individually with the President or other key allies.
He, however, said those who betray the Orange cause, and succumb to proposition by the Government, would be judged by the "public court" as the will of the public was that ODM remains united. "We are ready to talk but the negotiations must be structured as we do not want to be like Zimbabwe where the opposition won the referendum but Mugabe used all tricks to divide them," he said.
Mutula said personally he would not be willing to serve in President Kibaki's Government as Kenyans had shown that they had lost confidence in it.
He said there were more fundamental issues affecting Kenyans that ODM would like addressed than be gratified with appointments, which would only benefit a few individuals.
Mutula said those who betrayed the Orange course would be committing political suicide and would achieve nothing in the remaining two years.
Tinderet MP Henry Kosgei dismissed as "mere rumours" reports that he had been personally approached for a Cabinet position. "We are willing to negotiate collectively and do not want to talk individually and I can confirm to you that I have not been approached by anybody," he said.
He vowed that he would personally not take a Cabinet if it were to be done selectively and not negotiated through ODM.
Constitution of Kenya Review Commissioners Mutakha Kangu and Abubakar Zein, University of Nairobi professor Peter Wanyande and lawyer Aurelio Rebello were some of the resource persons at the retreat.
Ruto said the retreat did not involve all the Orange MPs but 35 people representing various regions and interest groups.
The Orange leaders at the forum presented various proposals on the constitution review using Bomas as the basic document.

Original_Search_Title: Leaders Raise Sh1.5m for Owidi Burial
Headline: Kenya: Leaders Raise Sh1.5m for Owidi Burial
Body_Text:
Nairobi — MPs from the Orange and Banana camps buried their differences to raise money for their colleague's funeral.
The MPs, including Mr Raila Odinga and Mr Musikari Kombo helped
raise Sh1.5 million for MP Peter Otieno Owidi's funeral. The
fund-raiser was held at 680 Hotel in Nairobi on Tuesday evening.
Mr Owidi, the Kasipul Kabondo MP, died at a Nairobi hospital from suspected food poisoning and will be buried on Saturday.
MPs raised Sh631,000 which was presented to the funeral committee by
Baharini MP Joe Khamisi. The harambee raised another Sh869,000.
Mr Adhu Awiti (Karachuonyo), who spoke on behalf of the MPs, thanked
Kasipul-Kabondo residents for overwhelmingly rejecting the proposed
Constitution during the November 21 referendum.

Original_Search_Title: LDP MPs Quizzed Over State Jobs
Headline: Kenya: LDP MPs Quizzed Over State Jobs
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Two members of the Orange Democratic movement were yesterday
taken to task by colleagues for expressing interest in serving in the
new Cabinet.
A Liberal Democratic Party national executive committee meeting
turned stormy when the members questioned the loyalty of some of them
who had shown interest in joining government if given ministerial
appointments.
LDP MPs from the Coast, vice-chairman Joe Khamisi (Bahari), Bahari
MP Lucas Maitha (Malindi) and their Changamwe counterpart Ramadhan
Kajembe (Changamwe) were questioned by LPD's national executive
committee (NEC) over claims that they were willing to serve in the new
Cabinet.
Sources during the meeting at Rainbow House, the party's
headquarters, said key leaders Raila Odinga, Anyang Nyong'o, David
Musila, Najib Balala and Ochillo Ayacko were angered by Mr Khamisi's
readiness to abandon the Orange Democratic Movement.
It was said overtures to be part of the cabinet had been extended to
the Bahari MP, Mr Lucas Maitha (Malindi) and Ramadhan Kajembe
(Changamwe) and they had shown their willingness to take up positions
if they were offered to them.
Mbita MP Otieno Kajwang cautioned: "There are some people who have
been approached and it seems they are ready to leave. However, they
must know that the same razor which we used to shave the Banana team
will cut them to size."
In response, sources said, Mr Khamisi stuck to his position arguing
that the Coast region was being given a raw deal in the ODM in spite of
its people voting massively against the new Constitution. He told the
party's leadership that it was the new stand of majority MPs from the
region and must be addressed to stem the impending fall out.
Unless a committee was formed to discuss with the Coast leaders
their stake in the ODM, he warned, they were ready to join the
Government side. A team was quickly formed comprising former minister
Anyang' Nyong'o, former assistant minister Andrew Ligale and party
chairman, Mr Musila and tasked to meet the Coast group.
The team met Mr Khamisi and Mr Maitha at Mr Musila's office in
Parliament Buildings last evening to explore ways of stopping the
fall-out. Mr Kajembe was still at the Coast. Said Mr Musila: "We held a
meeting with them (Mr Khamisi and Mr Maitha) to ask them whether what
we had read in the press was true or false. They told us that they had
not been approached."
Mr Ligale said they met with the two Coast MPs to ensure the unity
that propelled the ODM to victory was sustained. "We only talked about
the unity of the movement."
However, another Coast leader, Mr Najib Balala was not included in
the team. The move by Khamisi is the first sign of cracks in the ODM
which overwhelming scored victory during the November 21 vote.
It also comes three days after President Kibaki met former President
Daniel arap Moi in what was believed to be talks to convince some of
the ODM's leading lights to be part of the new Cabinet.
To stem any possible defection, ODM members were made to sign an
undertaking that they will not walk out of the movement after the
victory.
LDP's Parliamentary whip, Mr Reuben Ndolo accused Mr Khamisi of
flouting the party protocol which required him to report any approach
to the leadership. "He signed the agreement not to abandon the movement
but we were surprised about the allegations."
Contacted, Mr Khamisi said the pact was not legally binding and
cannot even be challenged in court. "It does not stop anybody from
doing what he wants."
The top organ meeting at Rainbow House was attended by Mr Odinga,
Prof Nyong'o, Mr Balala, Mr William Ntimama, Mr Kajwang and Mr Ligale.
Others were secretary general Joseph Kamotho, MPs Oburu Odinga, Reuben
Ndolo, Odhiambo Omamba and party official Larry Gumbe. Mr Odinga left
immediately after the meeting for consultations with former Environment
minister Kalonzo Musyoka.
Speaking after the two-hour meeting, Mr Kajwang said any
negotiations with the Government over inclusion in the new Cabinet must
be through ODM leadership. Asked if the LDP had received any overtures
from the Government, Mr Musila said they were yet to be approached but
added that they should be channelled through the ODM.
Mr Kajwang said the ODM was dedicated to giving them a new
Constitution and they will champion a new law to guide the process when
Parliament opens.
The preparatory work for the legislation will start today at
Naivasha where leaders of the Orange team will met to discuss the way
forward after the referendum. In addition, the Naivasha meeting will
also explore ways of setting in place a structure for the ODM in
readiness for 2007 General Election.
"A committee of the Orange Democratic Movement will meet in Naivasha
tomorrow (Thursday) to discuss the way forward for the review process
and cohesion of the movement," he said.
Those expected to attend include all members of the Parliamentary
Select Committee on the Constitution (PSC) who served during the
leadership of Eldoret North MPs William Ruto, They are MPs Raila
Odinga, Uhuru Kenyatta, Kalonzo Musyoka, Mutula Kilonzo, Chris Okemo,
Bonaya Godana, Kajwang' and Kamotho. Also included are former ministers
Linah Kilimo, Najib Balala, Nyong'o, Ochillo Ayacko, Ntimama, Omingo
Magara and Yusuf Haji.

Original_Search_Title: Ford-K Retreat in Mombasa is Called Off
Headline: Kenya: Ford-K Retreat in Mombasa is Called Off
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Ford-Kenya has cancelled a retreat of two of its main
decision-making organs, which was to be held in Mombasa this weekend to
discuss the referendum result.
The secretary for foreign affairs, Mr Ken Wafula, said the new date
for the meeting of the national executive committee and the
parliamentary group would be announced by chairman Musikari Kombo.
He warned members not to use the poll outcome to disorganise the party, saying that some were jostling for leadership positions.
But he said the party would hold grassroots elections on January 7,
8 and 9, followed by a delegates' meeting on January 29 and 30 at which
the national leaders would be picked.
General council meeting
A general council meeting said to have been called tomorrow by Mrs
Jael Mbogo, the organising secretary, was illegal, he said, and urged
the members not to attend it.
An MP had claimed that the meeting was being sponsored by people
bent on sabotaging Mr Kombo's chances of getting a senior post in the
new Cabinet.
Bumula's Mr Sylvester Wakoli alleged that the meeting was meant to topple Mr Kombo from the party leadership.
"In fact, the meeting convened by Mrs Mbogo is unprocedural because
only the party chairman or the secretary-general can call a national
executive committee meeting," he added.
In Bungoma, 32 councillors threw their weight behind Mr Kombo,
saying his efforts had delivered the Banana votes in Western Province
and Trans Nzoia District.
County council chairman James Lusweti told the Nation after a
meeting at the county hall yesterday afternoon: "The vice-presidency
belonged to Ford Kenya prior to the death of Mr Michael Wamalwa."

Original_Search_Title: Kiraitu: Why Banana Team Lost the Constitution Vote
Headline: Kenya: Kiraitu: Why Banana Team Lost the Constitution Vote
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Former Justice and Constitutional Affairs minister Kiraitu
Murungi yesterday broke his silence over the referendum results and
blamed the Banana team's loss on a disorganised campaign.
Speaking for the first time about the Banana team's dismal
performance at the November 21 referendum, Mr Murungi described the
Banana campaigns as "disorganised, uncoordinated and ineffective".
However, the former minister, who was one of the architects of the
Banana campaign that was supporting the Draft Constitution, exonerated
himself from responsibility for the loss, but added that there was no
justification for calls for the Government to resign.
"As the immediate former minister responsible for the review
process, my conscience is clear that I effectively discharged my
responsibility to provide the legal and institutional framework
necessary to facilitate the exercise of the people's choice on the
Constitution matter," Mr Murungi said.
He added: "How they exercised that choice was not my responsibility but theirs."
The former minister saw the rejection of the proposed Constitution
as a "protest vote" by some Kenyans over grievances that had nothing to
do with the new laws.
"History will judge my contribution in the Constitution process with
clearer vision undeterred by political competition and political
opportunism, ill-will or ethnic bias."
Mr Murungi said that the rival Orange team had employed propaganda
and disinformation on the proposed Constitution to win the referendum.
Complaints about the controversial Memorandum of Understanding
between the Liberal Democratic Party and the National Alliance (Party)
of Kenya contributed to the loss.
Power-sharing deal
Mr Murungi said the Orange team used the MoU to mobilise its
supporters to vote against the Government. "Political leaders who have
consistently complained that they were given a raw deal in the
power-sharing agreement after 2002 elections mobilised their
communities in a vote to punish the Government," he said.
Mr Murungi cited grievances which had nothing to do with the
Constitution as the eviction of squatters and demolition of kiosks by
the Government.
In a three-page statement faxed to newsrooms, he cautioned Kenyans
against reading too much into the results, saying a No vote did not
amount to a vote of no-confidence in the Government.
"It merely means that we go back to the drawing board."
He added: "A Yes vote would have had major consequences on
governance because it would have led to the adoption of a new
Constitution."
Other reasons which led to the rejection of the proposed
Constitution included a sustained campaign to misinform the public on
the contents of the proposed new laws.
Voters were intimidated with falsehoods that they would lose their land if they voted for the new Constitution.
There were also allegations that accepting the Draft meant allowing homosexuality.
Civic education providers were blocked from doing their work in some areas while others had their materials torn, he said.
However, the former minister said the referendum results were not as "devastating" as it would appear.
Mr Murungi said a 43 per cent vote on any issue was "reasonable" level of public confidence for a Government in its mid-term.
Clear indication
"Few Governments maintain a level higher than this beyond their
third year in office," he said citing examples from the US opinion
polls.
He said that the effects of the results should not be exaggerated as
it was common for governments to lose in referenda all over the world.
He gave the example of Australia where the Government had only won nine out of 42 referenda between 1906 and 1988.
"Many European governments have lost referenda on the European
constitution. There is, therefore, no reason to interpret the results
to mean that the Government should resign because it has lost a vote,"
he added.
Mr Murungi, however, thanked the President for allowing him to serve in "this important position".
On the way forward, the former minister expressed hope that the Government would give a clear indication.
But he talked of the need for consultations with various interest
groups in forging the way forward in the review, adding that it was
important to cool emotions generated by the campaigns to ensure
"rational and objective" discussions.
Mr Murungi proposed that two constitutional judgements provided by
the courts would give guidance on how the review should be done.
The courts, he added, had clearly said the Constitution can only be
made through a constituent assembly - directly elected by the people -
or through a referendum.
"The Bomas conference was not a constituent assembly and the process
therefore could not produce a legitimate constitution without a
referendum."
He said the recent court ruling that threw out a case by the Yellow
Movement had indicated clearly that there was nothing wrong with the
process adopted by the Government in introducing the new Constitution.
"This landmark decision along with the Njoya decision are the only
authoritative pronouncements on the Constitution process and both
clearly vindicate the Government's management of the process as
adequate," he said.
Validity of delegates
The Yellow Movement case sought to stop the referendum arguing that
the process the Government had adopted was irregular. However, a
three-judge bench threw out the case.
The Njoya case had questioned the validity of the Bomas delegates, arguing that they were not representative.
Mr Murungi said it was the people's right to either accept or reject
the proposed new Constitution and the Government had accepted their
verdict.
He said the proposed Constitution had to go the whole way to the
referendum since the leaders could not resolve certain issues before
then.
However, the Government overestimated the integrity and goodwill of some leaders.
"We expected that they would tell the truth about the proposed
Constitution - that they would put national and public interest before
their own and that they would focus on the merits and demerits of the
proposed new Constitution other than on political competition," the
Imenti South MP added.
The Government, he added, was focused on giving Kenyans a new constitution as it promised in its election manifesto.
He thanked Kenyans for demonstrating maturity by voting peacefully and accepting the results.
Former Energy minister Simeon Nyachae was the chairman of the Yes
campaign team while Mr Titus Mbathi headed the secretariat that
organised the Banana campaigns.

Original_Search_Title: Kiraitu: I Am Not to Blame for Referendum Loss
Headline: Kenya: Kiraitu: I Am Not to Blame for Referendum Loss
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Former Justice and Constitutional Affairs minister Kiraitu Murungi has
broken his silence on the Government's loss in last month's referendum,
blaming it on a "largely disorganised, unco-ordinated and ineffective
campaign."
In a statement faxed to newsrooms, Kiraitu, who was one of the most
vocal members of the Banana team, chose to absolve himself from blame over
the failure of the Yes campaign, which was supported by President
Kibaki.

Original_Search_Title: Ford Kenya to Hold Crisis Meeting
Headline: Kenya: Ford Kenya to Hold Crisis Meeting
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Ford-Kenya chairman Musikari Kombo has summoned a crisis party National
Executive Committee (NEC) meeting this Sunday to discuss the Banana defeat
at the referendum.
But a rival faction led by Organising Secretary, Jael Mbogo, has called
for another meeting this Friday to discuss the poor performance of the
party. Sources said the rival meeting plans to remove Kombo from the
chairmanship. Kombo will be in Mombasa for the meeting on Sunday amidst
reports that a party MP was planning to quit, but would continue to retain
his seat under the ruling coalition Narc.

Original_Search_Title: Lock Opposition Out of Talks, Says Maathai
Headline: Kenya: Lock Opposition Out of Talks, Says Maathai
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Nobel laureate Prof Wangari Maathai has urged Orange and Banana camps to
exclude the Opposition from negotiations.
Maathai, a former Environment assistant minister, said Narc members on
both sides were best placed to resolve the current political tension, as
they were the ones given the mandate to govern by Kenyans.

Original_Search_Title: ACP-EU Cotonou Agreement Five Years After
Headline: Africa: ACP-EU Cotonou Agreement Five Years After
Body_Text:
CEAC journalists meet in Douala to explore ways of rendering it more functional.
A four day seminar aimed at reviewing the somewhat dormant African-Carribean. Pacific(ACP) - European Union(EU) Agreement some five years ago in Cotonou enter day three in Douala today. The seminar- an initiative of the Cameroon National Professional Media Women Association with support from the Friedrich Ebert Stiftung Foundation is attended by some twenty journalists from eight member countries of the CEAC sub-region. The aim of the seminar according to the President of the NAPMEW, Susan Bamuh Apara at the opening last Monday is to gauge the accomplishment of the Cotonou Agreement and examine its vision for 2020 given what she described as the "timid response to the Agreement by some member governments---".

Original_Search_Title: Referendum Showed Power of Alliances
Headline: Kenya Referendum Showed Power of Alliances
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kenyans have developed a habit of humiliating the establishment. This
trend emerged when they voted out the Kanu government in 2002.
I am not saying the government had hitherto enjoyed unconditional
support. From the early days of Independence, Kenyans have never made it
easy for any government. However, in all cases, the government carried the
day through intimidation and manipulation. Until 2002, Kanu had counted on
the usual greed of politicians, a ploy that failed miserably.

Original_Search_Title: Waiting for Kibaki to Amaze Us
Headline: Kenya: Waiting for Kibaki to Amaze Us
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The referendum on the draft Constitution is over. We held our breath
while we voted, half-expecting disintegration into chaos and
violence. Nothing happened. We held our breath as the results started to
come in, in a similar state of expectation. Again, nothing happened. Kenyans
celebrated the draft Constitution's defeat.
The Orange team was immediately magnanimous. The Banana team graciously
conceded defeat. We all heaved sighs of relief. Then, out of the blue, the
president stole the thunder from the Orange team. In an unprecedented move,
he dismissed his entire Cabinet. Next, he proceeded to suspend parliament,
which had been due to open again this week.

Original_Search_Title: Oil Prices Are Not to Blame for Inflation
Headline: South Africa: Oil Prices Are Not to Blame for Inflation
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — CALL me a raving Friedmanite, but I am unable to find anyone who can
give a satisfactory explanation as to why oil-price changes would be
