described as inflationary.
Newspapers have repeatedly talked about oil prices filtering into the
consumer price index, with first- and second-round effects, yet the notion
that such changes are inflationary is false.

Original_Search_Title: State Must Learn From Poll Debacle
Headline: Kenya: State Must Learn From Poll Debacle
Body_Text:
Nairobi — In the past 15 years, Kenyans have witnessed events without precedent. Starting with the agitation for multiparty democracy, demand for a new constitutional order, the constitution-making process and the referendum, to dissolution of the Cabinet, all these events have been phenomenal.
The immediate reaction has been to expect the collapse of the governance structures. Time and again, this has been proved wrong. There are many now who see the nation in a state of impasse. But if the past is anything to go by, all this will come to pass.

Original_Search_Title: Churches Ask President for Fresh Start
Headline: Kenya: Churches Ask President for Fresh Start
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The National Council of Churches of Kenya wants President Kibaki to appoint a 39-member team to start the constitutional review process afresh.
They will seek to meet the President and leaders from both the Orange and Banana teams to try and hammer out a negotiated plan for a new constitution, they announced yesterday.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki's Options in Forming New Cabinet
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki's Options in Forming New Cabinet
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The just-concluded acrimonious referendum on the draft Constitution during which the Orange team scored 57 per cent of the vote against 43 per cent for the Banana proponents has all the ingredients of a three-scene thriller.
The first scene is pre-referendum. This is best described by the story of three men who were arguing as to whose profession was the first to be established on the animal planet.

Original_Search_Title: Maathai Calls for Easing of Tensions
Headline: Kenya: Maathai Calls for Easing of Tensions
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Nobel peace laureate Wangari Maathai yesterday called for truce between
the Banana and the Orange camps.
She asked them to initiate fresh talks to end political tensions.

Original_Search_Title: New Push to Revive Constitutional Talks
Headline: Kenya: New Push to Revive Constitutional Talks
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The top leadership of the Orange Democratic Movement meets in Naivasha
today to consider options for restarting the constitutional review.
National Council of Churches general secretary Mutava Musyimi and other
officials address reporters at Limuru's Jumuia Place yesterday. Photo by
Joan Pereruan

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki Allies Reach Out to Orange MPs
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki Allies Reach Out to Orange MPs
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki's senior advisers have met several Orange luminaries - including the leader of Official Opposition Uhuru Kenyatta - in a bid to woo them into the new-look Cabinet.
Close confidantes of the President have variously and separately met Uhuru - who is the Kanu chairman - and at least four MPs drawn from Kanu, the LDP and Ford People for talks believed to have been centred on their possible inclusion in a new-look Cabinet expected this week.

Original_Search_Title: Banning Rallies Could Backfire
Headline: Kenya: Banning Rallies Could Backfire
Body_Text:
Nairobi — This Government has an infinite capacity to shoot itself in the foot
without the slightest provocation. Take the ban on the Orange Movement
victory rallies - disguised as prayer meetings - planned across the
country.
All the ban achieves is to depict a panicked Government afraid of its
own shadow after the referendum defeat.

Original_Search_Title: Now Kibaki Calls in Moi for Crisis Talks
Headline: Kenya: Now Kibaki Calls in Moi for Crisis Talks
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki yesterday held a meeting with former Head of State
Daniel arap Moi as the crisis over the referendum results continued.
Highly-placed sources said the meeting at State House, Nairobi, was held
at the request of President Kibaki. It lasted for about two hours.

Original_Search_Title: Orange Group to Defy Rallies Ban
Headline: Kenya: Orange Group to Defy Rallies Ban
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Orange group leaders yesterday declared they would defy a government ban
and continue with their planned victory rallies countrywide.
They described as "unconstitutional, illegal, highly provocative and
irresponsible," the ban announced by Vice-President Moody Awori, who said
the planned meetings were a threat to national security.

Original_Search_Title: Don Offers to Mediate in Fallout Over Vote
Headline: Kenya: Don Offers to Mediate in Fallout Over Vote
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A Kenyan scholar who gained international fame by negotiating peace
between rival groups in South Africa has offered his skills in reconciling
the Banana and Orange groups.
Prof Washington Jalang'o Okumu, who in 1994 led negotiations that led to
peaceful elections in South Africa, yesterday said he did not want to watch
his own country degenerate into chaos.

Original_Search_Title: Is New Currency the Solution?
Headline: Mozambique: Is New Currency the Solution?
Body_Text:
Harare — Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe (RBZ) Governor Dr Gideon Gono recently announced that the central bank will introduce a new currency next year, Mozambique is also mulling the same idea. Stephanie Nolen of the Globe and Mail writes about the new currency debate in Maputo.
MOZAMBIQUE'S money is almost worthless, so the country is banking on a wild idea: lopping off three zeroes.

Original_Search_Title: Banana Camp MPs Dismiss Election Calls
Headline: Kenya: Banana Camp MPs Dismiss Election Calls
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Nineteen MPs allied to the Banana camp have dismissed calls for a snap
General Election, saying Parliament should serve its full term.
They also backed the Government ban on Orange rallies to celebrate the
No side's victory in the recent referendum.

Original_Search_Title: Why Kibaki Met Moi
Headline: Kenya: Why Kibaki Met Moi
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki met his predecessor, Mr Daniel arap Moi, yesterday as
part of his strategy to bring the Orange leaders - who are calling for a
snap election after the humiliating defeat of Government in the November 21
referendum - to the negotiating table.
The meeting followed a call President Kibaki personally placed to Moi at
his Kabarak home on Sunday. The two agreed to talk again the next day
(yesterday) for a definite appointment since Moi was travelling to Nairobi
shortly thereafter.

Original_Search_Title: Orange Team Defies Ban
Headline: Kenya: Orange Team Defies Ban
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Orange leaders yesterday defied a Government ban on their rallies and
scheduled a meeting at the Mombasa Municipal Stadium in two weeks.
They said the Government's ban of their meetings on grounds of national
security was tantamount to declaration of a state of emergency at
peacetime.

Original_Search_Title: Leaders in Plea Over Cabinet
Headline: Kenya: Leaders in Plea Over Cabinet
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Leaders from the Meru community have been lobbying to have former
Cabinet ministers, Kiraitu Murungi and David Mwiraria, re-appointed to the
Cabinet.
At least 100 leaders, including politicians, religious leaders and
businessmen, have urged President Kibaki to include the former ministers in
an expected Cabinet lineup.

Original_Search_Title: Nassir Was Accessible And Ready for Interviews, to the Very End
Headline: Kenya: Nassir Was Accessible And Ready for Interviews, to the Very End
Body_Text:
Nairobi — When I had the last interview with the late veteran Mombasa politician Shariff Nassir last Friday morning at his Ganjoni home in Mombasa, he looked frail.
He had three days earlier been discharged from the Mombasa Hospital, which he had been frequenting since he suffered a road accident three months ago near Malindi town. He was always lively when meeting journalists who he knew by name, occasionally joking with them. On this day, he looked tired.

Original_Search_Title: Let Awori Stay As VP, Western Leaders Say
Headline: Kenya: Let Awori Stay As VP, Western Leaders Say
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Western Vote No Forum members from Western Province and the North Rift yesterday called for the retention of Moody Awori as vice-president.
In a statement issued in Kitale, the leaders also dismissed suggestions that former Local Government minister Musikari Kombo should replace Awori.

Original_Search_Title: Don't Shut Orange Out of Cabinet, Churches Say
Headline: Kenya: Don't Shut Orange Out of Cabinet, Churches Say
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Church leaders yesterday urged President Kibaki not to lock out the Orange team from the new Cabinet.
The clerics also called for the appointment of ministers who can deliver, saying the new Cabinet should not be based on regional balancing alone.

Original_Search_Title: Stop Inciting Kenyans, Mugo Tells No Team
Headline: Kenya: Stop Inciting Kenyans, Mugo Tells No Team
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Former assistant minister Beth Mugo has warned the Orange Democratic Movement against inciting the public and fomenting trouble.
Mugo said the movement's calls for snap elections was ill-intentioned and meant to incite the public against the President.

Original_Search_Title: Meru Leaders Urge President Not to Leave Out Kiraitu, Mwiraria
Headline: Kenya: Meru Leaders Urge President Not to Leave Out Kiraitu, Mwiraria
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Leaders from nine constituencies in Meru yesterday urged President Mwai Kibaki not to leave Kiraitu Murungi and David Mwiraria out of the new Cabinet.
The leaders urged the President to retain Mwiraria in his former Finance Ministry but move Kiraitu to the Ministry of Roads and Public Works.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki Finalises List As Kiraitu Avoids the Press
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki Finalises List As Kiraitu Avoids the Press
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki yesterday kept Kenyans guessing for the fourth straight day as Government sources revealed there were strong indications he could announce his new-look Cabinet tomorrow.
"Expect a new line up tomorrow. The President has already worked on his team," said a State House official. However, it was not clear what the President intends to with former ministers in the Orange camp who insist they will only negotiate as a team and not on individual basis.

Original_Search_Title: State Rules Out Snap Poll And Bans All Planned Orange Rallies
Headline: Kenya: State Rules Out Snap Poll And Bans All Planned Orange Rallies
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Government yesterday ruled out early elections and banned all
political rallies planned by the Orange Democratic Movement.
"There is no intention whatsoever to call early elections because there
is no reason for such an action," the Government said in a statement read by
Vice-President Moody Awori last night.

Original_Search_Title: Tricky Equation in Kibaki's Next Cabinet
Headline: Kenya: Tricky Equation in Kibaki's Next Cabinet
Body_Text:
Nairobi — He may well have acted like a wounded lion. However, the decision by
President Kibaki to sack all his Cabinet ministers a day after
conceding defeat at the hands of some of them, was brilliant strategy -
to immediately shift attention from the victorious Orange celebrations.
President Kibaki has tricky options trying to pick new cabinet after he sacked the ministers following the referendum

Original_Search_Title: Using the Same Strategy to Win a Similar Battle
Headline: Kenya: Using the Same Strategy to Win a Similar Battle
Body_Text:
Nairobi — During the heated referendum campaigns, Raila Odinga liked to
quip to colleagues that if former President Daniel arap Moi's 2002
presidential "project" got scuttled, doing the same to President Kibaki
over the referendum project was much easier.
As a matter of fact, Mr Kibaki has repeated Mr Moi's miscalculation.
The latter underrated the blistering rebellion against his preferred
choice of successor - Uhuru Kenyatta - right up to when it was too
late. He also underrated Mr Odinga's capacity to rally the anti-Kanu
forces.

Original_Search_Title: President's Move: the Untold Story
Headline: Kenya: President's Move: the Untold Story
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Some of President Kibaki's closest allies were kept in the dark
about the decision to sack the entire Cabinet - despite having met him
just shortly before the decision was announced last Wednesday.
Some of them were taken by shock because they left State House on
Wednesday afternoon confident that they had his ear on the next course
of action after the defeat in the referendum for the new Constitution.

Original_Search_Title: Let Kibaki Do His Work, Orange Team Told
Headline: Kenya: Let Kibaki Do His Work, Leaders Told
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange team was yesterday criticised for giving the President conditions in the appointment of a new Cabinet.
A cross-section of Kenyans, including church leaders, politicians
and members of the civil society, said the President should be left
alone to decide who to appoint to the new government.

Original_Search_Title: Elections Now, Orange Says
Headline: Kenya: Elections Now, Orange Says
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Orange leaders yesterday declared that they were ready to face
President Kibaki in a snap General Election, which they said must be
called now.
In the climax of their rally, the Orange leading lights said they
were not ready to negotiate with the Government as the "people spoke"
during the referendum.

Original_Search_Title: Options Open to the Orange Movement
Headline: Kenya: Options Open to the Orange Movement
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Orange Movement leaders seen as rebels within the Government had
planned to extend an olive branch to President Kibaki hours before he
sacked the entire Cabinet.
Sources within the group confided to the Sunday Nation that
ministers and assistant ministers (as they then were) from the LDP had
a meeting at the Nairobi Club on Wednesday afternoon, November 23, the
same day the president fired all his ministers.

Original_Search_Title: For Machage, the Task is in Regaining Public Trust
Headline: Kenya: For Machage, the Task is in Regaining Public Trust
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Before the referendum results started trickling in, the Banana team thought they could count on Kuria constituency.
For several years, Kuria has supported the government and was, therefore, regarded as a 'subservient' constituency.

Original_Search_Title: What Went Wrong in the Kibaki Presidency?
Headline: Kenya: What Went Wrong in the Kibaki Presidency?
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The vote against anything Mwai Kibaki stands for was always
coming although the President and his allies never saw it until the
Wako Draft was rejected.
Ever since they voted out Kanu in 2002, Kenyans had been feeling
energised, defiant and restive, an attitude the US embassy once warned
the government to resolve.

Original_Search_Title: Banana Supporters Holed Up in Hotels
Headline: Kenya: Banana Supporters Holed Up in Hotels
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Life is rough and expensive for Luo leaders who visited State
House a few days to the referendum to pledge support for the Proposed
New Constitution.
Due to hostility from their neighbours, some of the leaders have not returned to their homes after meeting President Kibaki.

Original_Search_Title: The Bone That Kenyans Had to Pick With the Government
Headline: Kenya: The Bone That Kenyans Had to Pick With the Government
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The referendum vote has come with different messages for
different people, elevating some to national heroes and reducing others
to villains.
In some areas, voters used the referendum to show the government what they care about and what matters little to them.

Original_Search_Title: Mixed Fortunes for Maoka Maore
Headline: Kenya: Mixed Fortunes for Maoka Maore
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The rejection of the Proposed New Constitution has elevated some
MPs nationally, even as it made villains of some in their
constituencies.
Ntonyiri MP Maoka Maore is a villain among the Ameru who supported the draft.

Original_Search_Title: Ex-AG At Mammoth Orange Rally
Headline: Kenya: Ex-AG At Mammoth Orange Rally
Body_Text:
Nairobi — It was literally a miracle when Charles Njonjo attended the
Orange Democratic Movement's prayer rally at Uhuru Park, Nairobi, and
shared a platform with politician Martin Shikuku yesterday.
The rally was attended by thousands of jubilant Kenyans, religious leaders and more than 70 MPs.

Original_Search_Title: State Admits Hitches Over Cabinet Sacking
Headline: Kenya: State Admits Hitches Over Cabinet Sacking
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Government yesterday admitted that the sacking of the Cabinet would
affect its operations.
Public service head Francis Muthaura told NTV last evening: "The
operations will be affected and the rhythm reduced, but it is not like we
have a paralysis.

Original_Search_Title: It's All Or Nothing, Orange Tell Kibaki
Headline: Kenya: It's All Or Nothing, Orange Tell Kibaki
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The seven Orange ministers sacked from the Cabinet will not accept new
positions in the Government if any one of them remains out of office.
The all-or-none-of-us decision was agreed by key leaders of the newly
formed Orange Democratic Movement at a strategy meeting held on
Thursday.

Original_Search_Title: Where Voters Never Saw Orange, Banana
Headline: Kenya: Where Voters Never Saw Orange, Banana
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Listening the referendum results on radio or TV from the comfort of your room, you may not picture the pain it took to deliver them.
A team of The Standard made it to some of Kenya's farthest islands in Lamu District, two of which appear only on very detailed maps.

Original_Search_Title: Banana Team: Why We Lost Referendum
Headline: Kenya: Banana Team: Why We Lost Referendum
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A top official of the Banana camp said yesterday that the twin issues of land ownership and inheritance of property may have cost it the referendum.
At the same time, President Kibaki's Democratic Party of Kenya (DP) yesterday criticised Orange leaders for claiming they had been mandated by Kenyans to spearhead the constitutional review.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki's Game Plan
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki's Game Plan
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A day after President Kibaki sacked his entire Cabinet, anxiety gripped politicians amidst indications that his ultimate game plan could see at least eight of the former ministers completely axed.
Sources said the President was angry with his close advisors, who apparently kept him in the dark regarding the mood of the country in the run-up to the referendum in which the Government suffered a resounding defeat.
President Kibaki is also said to be rethinking the idea of a Government of National Unity, in which he appointed four opposition MPs ministers.
Another source privy to discussions at State House said the President was keen on getting into his new-look Cabinet people who could deliver on the economic agenda. "Ability of people to perform will be a key consideration," the source said.
The President is also trying to achieve a "balanced country representation" and this could mean bringing in some new faces.
Separately, the Government Spokesperson, Dr Alfred Mutua, said the President would choose a team of people who could work "in harmony to achieve the dreams and aspirations of Kenyans, which is economic growth".
The team to be chosen, he said, would comprise persons that would focus on improving the quality of lives of Kenyans.
Mutua defended the President Kibaki's action as move to quell the wrangling that had dominated his Cabinet for months.
Another source said there would be inter-party consultations, the source said, but would not confirm whether these would involve reaching out to the Orange team that lead the campaign against the proposed constitution.
He only said it would involve finding out "who can work with who."
"The idea is to get a Cabinet that works as one team. This means that if some people are playing in a separate team, they could be talked to see if they can play in a new team," he said.
Former Roads minister Raila Odinga, one of the leaders of the Orange team, however, said the group would not bend to pressure to hold discussions with President Kibaki.
"There have been such moves. But right now we have a focus that isn't going to change," said Raila.
He dismissed the sacking of the entire Cabinet by the President as "too little too late".
"When I talked of a tsunami that would sweep everything in its way they hurled all sort of insults at us (Orange). Now that tsunami has come and it has wreaked havoc," he said by phone.
Asked to comment on reports that the President was considering getting him and a section of the Orange brigade to the negotiation table, Raila said there had been "covert" moves to get him talking to the Head of State. "There have been moves, but negotiating with individuals at a table isn't where I want to go. This thing is about the people of this country and they have spoken. It is the wishes of the people and their interests that concern us."
Between the time the referendum outcome crystallized into a crippling defeat for the banana camp and the time the President fired his entire Cabinet on Wednesday night virtually all politicians from the vanquished camp who were entrusted with the responsibility of delivering the vote had been summoned to State House to account for the loss.
Earlier at a press conference for the Orange team, Kanu secretary-general William Ruto said the overwhelming majority that voted against the Wako draft urgently required establishment of a government with legitimacy to govern.
And Raila said it was worrying that the country had no government because section 16 of the constitution did not allow for a vacuum.
"We now have a Head of State and not a head of government because there is no government," said Raila.
The former Roads Minister warned of grave consequences should anything happen to the President and in event of the country being invaded by foreign forces. He said under the current Constitution, the Cabinet must sanction the armed forces to fight an invasion from foreign forces on Kenyan territory.
Asked if he would take up a ministerial position if he were appointed in the next Cabinet, Raila said the President would have to consult the Orange team first. "We are partners in this Government but we are not going to negotiate individually for any position in Government," said Raila.
He, however, refused to divulge what conditions they would attach to such appointments: "We will state our conditions at the time we are invited for discussion," said Raila.
He said serving in the Cabinet was not as prestigious as Kenyans assumed. Ministers earned only Sh20,000 more than ordinary MPs. get despite the many responsibilities they shoulder.
"I keep repeating that I was not born with a ministerial tag around my neck and, after all, I have been a minister for only four years out of the nine that I have been in Parliament," said Raila.
He said the Orange leaders could not trust the Government because it abrogated the MOU by including MPs from other political parties in the Narc government.
On whether he will vie for Presidency, the Langata MP said the Orange team would pick one of its leaders to vie for the position when elections are called.

Original_Search_Title: Orange Rally Still On Tomorrow
Headline: Kenya: Orange Rally Still On Tomorrow
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange Movement's rally will go on tomorrow despite the dissolution of the Cabinet, Kanu secretary-general William Ruto said yesterday.
Orange camp key leaders Raila Odinga, Kalonzo Musyoka and Najib Balala were among former ministers whom President Kibaki sacked on Wednesday.
Ruto said the dissolution would not affect their plans.
He said Makadara MP Reuben Ndolo was charged with the responsibility of organizing. The police has also okayed the rally and promised to provide security.
Earlier, Police Commissioner Hussein Ali had cautioned Kenyans against engaging in street celebrations or protest after the announcement of the referendum results.
The Orange team beat the Banana team in the referendum by over 1 million votes.
President Kibaki was leading the Banana team in the Yes campaigns for the proposed new constitution.
Tomorrow's rally at Uhuru Park is to be attended by all the leaders who campaigned for the Orange team.
Ruto said the Orange Movement leaders would chart the way forward on delivering a new Constitution to Kenyans.

Original_Search_Title: The Luxuries That Former Ministers Will Have to Forego
Headline: Kenya: The Luxuries That Former Ministers Will Have to Forego
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Life's comforts invariably bring happiness. But the comfort and happiness are enhanced when one is a member of the Government, especially as a Cabinet minister or assistant minister.
Although they may not gripe so loudly, the sacked officials will admit that already, they are missing the good old days when they were part of the Government.
President Kibaki on Wednesday brought to a rude end the luxuries they had been enjoying at the taxpayer's expense - fat salaries and allowances as well as a long list of other entitlements.
The President cracked the whip in the wake of the referendum in which the Banana group he supported lost heavily to opponents of the proposed constitution, and promised to name a new government in two weeks.
Receiving entitlements
Only Vice-President Moody Awori, who will stay in office as the VP but not as the Home Affairs minister, and attorney-general Amos Wako will continue to receive their entitlements.
As the ministers and assistant ministers began packing up yesterday, it was clear, given the divisions that rocked the Government in the lead-up to the poll, that quite a few might not return to the fold. To such, the two weeks mean no luxuries, and the situation will be the same, at least for some time.
Yesterday, they began vacating their posh and well-furnished government offices as a first step to being Parliament back-benchers entitled to only benefits of ordinary MPs.
President Kibaki announces the sacking of the Cabinet and assistant ministers on Wednesday.
Photo/File
They will miss the luxury of those offices and other facilities that the taxpayer labours daily to sustain.
In the plush ambience of the offices, replete with leather seats and state-of-the-art TV sets, Cabinet ministers are entitled to two direct land lines, which cost Sh10,000 a month. The lines are in addition to the general ones in each ministry.
They are each given Sh15,000 mobile phone airtime a month and in their official homes, Sh 5,000 meets their monthly telephone bills.
Assistant ministers, though at a slightly lower level, enjoy the same services. They are entitled to a direct line with an expenditure of Sh6,000 a month.
Some Sh7,000 pays their mobile phone bills, while at their homes, they cost the taxpayer Sh4,000 in telephone bills.
The communication entitlements were much higher before the head of Civil Service, Mr Francis Muthaura, intervened early this year and put a ceiling. The move was meant to cut operation costs to save funds for development.
Mr Kiai
Yesterday, the fired officials also returned government vehicles - top-of-the-range limousines whose makes include: Mercedes Benz, Toyota Prado, Mitsubishi Pajero, Toyota Lexus and Range Rover.
They are each entitled to two official vehicles fuelled by the Government, with a driver and a security man.
But some ministers were early this year accused by Kenya National Human Rights Commission chief Maina Kiai of receiving more than they deserved.
Some of them, Mr Kiai argued, were driving fuel-guzzlers such as Range Rovers.
The criticism was followed up by local NGOs and the general public, and was finally taken up by MPs in Parliament, who overwhelmingly voted to bring pressure to bear on the Government to stop buying vehicles whose engine capacities exceeded 3,000cc.
Mr Muthaura
In addition to the two official vehicles, some ministers, depending on the seniority of their portfolios, have other cars for at least four bodyguards while on official duties.
Although a minister is officially given two bodyguards, the number has been increasing of late.
Rise in number of guards
On October 10, last year, our sister paper, the Sunday Nation, revealed how a sudden rise in the number of official security men had seen a large number of police officers being diverted from fighting crime.
According to the paper's investigations, most Cabinet ministers had between eight and 12 armed policemen at their disposal.
Some were deployed at the ministers' Nairobi and rural homes, while others trailed them in cars.
The increase, some ministers argued, was due to a rising crime rate at the time.
Maj-Gen Ali
The rise in the number of bodyguards and crime this year forced the police commissioner, Maj-Gen Hussein Ali, to sound a warning in a report that he might withdraw his officers from guarding VIPs.
The report proposed that the provision of security services to members of the Government be given to an independent organisation. Unfortunately, the proposal is yet to be implemented.
For two weeks, the sacked ministers will have to hire their own security men in and outside Nairobi.
In addition, they will bear the cost of their domestic staff, including cooks and gardeners.
According to the figures released by the Parliamentary Service Commission, which determines their salaries, ministers earn at least Sh700,000 a month, excluding allowances.
Of this, only Sh200,000 is taxable, which gives them a handsome take-home.
Their pay was raised from Sh50,000 even as the public protested.
The issue of ministers' allowances has occupied the minds of top civil servants at the Treasury. Early this year, Government spokesperson Alfred Mutua hinted that plans were under way to put the perks and salaries into one package.
The Cabinet was expected to debate the issue as proposed by the Treasury, but details have yet to come out.
"The decision of changing gratuity into a form of allowance has not been made since the issue should be discussed in the Cabinet before a decision is arrived at," Dr Mutua said in April.
Cabinet ministers receive a gratuity of 20 per cent of their salaries as compensation for not engaging in business and in appreciation of serving the Government.
In May, they were each paid Sh848,533 in gratuities.
Also on the cards is a healthcare kitty for them and their families, which runs into millions of shillings a month.
Although they are entitled to a house mortgage scheme, the Government has extended its largesse to them to ensure they stay in safe and comfortable homes in up-market Nairobi neighbourhoods.
Cabinet ministers who do not own houses are paid a Sh100,000 house allowance.
But now they will have to wait for two weeks as President Kibaki burns the midnight oil to arrive at a balanced team to help him for the rest of his term.
In the meantime and perhaps forever, the comfort of serving in the Government is gone.

Original_Search_Title: Sacked Ministers Vacate Offices
Headline: Kenya: Sacked Ministers Vacate Offices
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Former Cabinet ministers cleared from their offices hours after they were sacked by President Kibaki on Wednesday evening.
They then handed over their official vehicles Thursday morning.
Most of the former ministers spent the day at their Nairobi homes.
Those in the Banana camp who supported the proposed Constitution which was shot down during the referendum, welcomed the President's move to fire them, saying the Cabinet was too polarised to deliver services.
Their Orange counterparts said they were not surprised by the President's action.
Former Regional Development minister Mohamed Abdi Mahamud handed over his official vehicles in the morning after clearing his personal effects.
He welcomed the dissolving of the Cabinet, adding that it should extend to the civil service.
"There is need to have a focused and disciplined Cabinet that is faithful to the President. It's time the hypocrisy and double-speak within the Cabinet ranks came to an end," said the Wajir East MP.
He added: "It is good enough for all of us to go home to allow the President to pick another team that he can trust and work with as well as campaign for his Government in 2007."
Mr Mahamud said there should be a clean-up of the civil service, adding that some permanent secretaries, provincial administrators and parastatal heads had taken part in partisan politics.
Some, he said, sabotaged the Government from within. "The President should clean the entire system so that the Government can move on a clean slate," said the Kanu MP.
Former Transport minister Chris Murungaru said at his Nairobi home: "I am neither disappointed nor surprised. The Government was facing serious challenges in delivering its promises to Kenyans because of a divided Cabinet.
"The President took the decision with the best interest of the nation. It is his prerogative to hire or fire at whatever time."
The Kieni MP had not handed over by the time he spoke to the Nation in the afternoon.
Former Education minister George Saitoti got information of the sack as he prepared to travel to China for a four-day meeting organised by Unesco.
The Unesco meeting brings together education ministers to discuss and assess countries' performance towards achieving Education For All goals.
His former assistant, Dr Kilemi Mwiria, who was in Germany, was scheduled to return tomorrow, but called his office to say he would return Thursday.
Dagoretti MP Beth Mugo, who was also Education assistant minister, said she was not surprised by the President's decision.
"For him to move the country forward, he must put together a Cabinet that will be ready to serve Kenyans well," she told journalists as she cleared from her Jogoo House office at 11am.
Mrs Mugo, who was to officiate at a gender workshop in Mombasa, said one of her ministry's major achievements in the past three years was the implementation of free primary education.
However, more work was required to address the challenges facing the programme.
Mrs Mugo said she had been surprised by the outcome of the referendum, regretting that it was a loss to women.
The Draft Constitution, she said, had many positive provisions for women.
Other sacked ministers Kiraitu Murungi (Constitutional Affairs) and David Mwiraria (Finance) also went to their offices after the announcement and took away personal effects. Former Planning minister Anyang' Nyong'o was due to open an East African Planning ministers meeting at Hilton Hotel, Nairobi, on Wednesday evening when the Cabinet was dissolved.
He proceeded to the meeting and gave a keynote address.
Vice-President Moody Awori, who was scheduled to open the conference, did not turn up, and neither did former Labour minister Newton Kulundu, who was to chair it.
The VP's speech was read by Home Affairs permanent secretary Erastus Mwongera at 7pm. Prof Nyong'o said the Orange group had expected some action from the President following the poor performance of the Government in the referendum. He said the Government had lost the mandate it had been given by voters to fight corruption and improve their welfare.
Former Roads minister Raila Odinga's driver and an aide handed back his official Mercedes Benz.
The Lang'ata MP later went to his office at 4.30pm and cleared his effects.
At the Ministry of Water, officers said former minister Martha Karua was in the office when the announcement was made on Wednesday. She then packed her personal belongings and left.
Former Lands minister Amos Kimunya was said to have cleared soon after the announcement.
Mrs Linah Jebii Kilimo, former Immigration minister, packed her personal belongings in the afternoon.
She held a meeting with permanent secretary Daniel Mule and senior ministry officials before leaving.
Former Energy minister Simeon Nyachae was said to have sent his aides to carry away his personal possessions.
The former East African Cooperation minister, Mr John Koech, had to cancel a trip to Arusha, Tanzania, where he was to take part in a session at the East African Legislative Assembly.
Former Environment minister Kalonzo Musyoka said he was not surprised by the Cabinet's dissolution.
Mr Musyoka, who was among the seven ministers opposing the Draft Constitution, said even before the dissolution, some ministers had made it clear they would not sit with others in the Cabinet.
"In a way, the move by the President was not unexpected. I only hope that he will not use the opportunity to punish those of us who supported a different stand from that taken by the Government."
Speaking at his National Hospital Insurance Fund office in Nairobi, the Mwingi North MP said the position of the Orange Democratic Movement was that the President should also dissolve Parliament and call for snap elections.
"The President should demonstrate statesmanship and resist the temptation to victimise ministers who had all along told him the truth about the rejected constitution.
"If anyone should be punished," said Mr Musyoka, "it is some four ministers in the President's inner circle, who had made the Government unpopular." He did not name them.
Mr Musyoka claimed he had asked the President Kibaki before the referendum campaigns started to remain neutral.
But he was happy that the Orange team had saved the President from committing "an act of treason by enacting an illegal constitution."
Mr Musyoka recalled it was Vice-President Moody Awori who had said the referendum's outcome would amount to either a vote of confidence for or against the Government.
Since Kenyans had overwhelmingly rejected the Wako draft, it was clear that the Government had failed the confidence test, hence the need to reconstitute it afresh, he added.
Former Energy assistant minister Mwangi Kiunjuri described as godsend the sacking of the Cabinet, saying the country needed a fresh start.
"I am very happy that the President has finally acted on his divided Cabinet. It does not matter if we lose our jobs as long as we have a united, clean and focused Cabinet to revive the economy," he said.
Former assistant Trade and Industry minister Zaddock Syong'oh was caught by the surprise news while in Arusha, attending a meeting on the World Trade Organisation.
Mr Andrew Ligale, a former assistant minister for Transport, asked the President to appoint a leaner and more efficient Cabinet that would be able to deliver.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki Postpones Opening of Parliament
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki Postpones Opening of Parliament
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki last night suspended Parliament, keeping MPs on indefinite leave, even as members of the Orange team demanded that he should dissolve the House and call a snap General Election.
The suspension, called prorogation, came a day after he sacked his entire Cabinet and all his assistant ministers, and only three days after his Government was defeated in referendum on the proposed Constitution.
President Kibaki signed the order suspending Parliament in a special supplement of the Kenya Gazette.
The House was supposed to resume next Tuesday and continue for two weeks following a break this month to allow MPs time off to concentrate on the referendum campaigns.
The President's decision means MPs will remain on leave for probably three months and return only in March. They will continue to draw their pay and most of their allowances.
Had the President instead dissolved the House, the MPs would all have lost their jobs and had to fight for re-election.
Earlier in the day, head of Public Service Francis Muthaura indicated the Government would issue a statement on when Parliament would reopen.
Section 59 (1) of the current Constitution gives the President the power to prorogue Parliament at any time, marking the end of a year's session.
A subsequent clause gives him the power to dissolve Parliament at any time to pave way for a General Election - which must then take place within three months.
Mr Muthaura said normal government activities would continue and that only policy issues that required ministerial direction would be affected.
"All routine public service matters will not be interrupted," he told journalists after presiding over a graduation ceremony at the National Defence College in Karen, Nairobi.
Parliament buildings, Nairobi.
The President's decision came only hours after the Orange group urged him to call a snap General Election.
They said the people's decision to vote overwhelmingly against the proposed Constitution was also a clear indication that they did not have confidence in the Government.
A General Election was the only way for a fresh start by giving Kenya a legitimate government, which could then prepare a new Constitution, they said.
Speaking only one day after Mr Kibaki sacked his entire Cabinet and all his assistant ministers, they welcomed the decision saying it paved the way for the dissolution of Parliament.
In a statement read by Orange spokesman William Ruto, they said the decision by Kenyans from 153 constituencies out of 210 and seven out of the eight provinces to reject the proposed Constitution required a government with legitimacy.
Mr Ruto said: "The people's historic decision to reject the proposed new Constitution by such an overwhelming majority... urgently requires action to establish a Government with legitimacy to govern."
He added: "The Orange Democratic Movement now urges the President to comprehensively address the crisis we face of our national institutions being so grievously out of step with the people."
Speaking after the press conference, former Cabinet minister Raila Odinga said the Orange team's victory in the referendum showed it had the peoples' mandate.
"The people of Kenya have a lot of confidence in this group we call Orange Democratic Movement: 3.5 million votes against 2.5 million is a strong verdict that everybody must take seriously."
He added: "The people of Kenya have said a resounding No to the Banana side and the sooner they realise this, pack up and go home, the better for them."
Mr Odinga said the country had sent a clear message: "The country has said it has no confidence in the Government as currently constituted."
The referendum results showed the country preferred the option presented by the Orange Democratic Movement, he said.
The Government which had supported the proposed Constitution won 43 per cent of the vote, compared with the 57 per cent gained by the Orange No-vote team.
The President said he would name a new Cabinet within two weeks.
There were more than 60 MPs at the Orange press conference, including former ministers Raila Odinga, Kalonzo Musyoka, Anyang' Nyong'o, Najib Balala, Ochillo Ayacko, William ole Ntimama and Linah Kilimo.
Also present were Official Opposition leader Uhuru Kenyatta and Orange campaign leader Mutula Kilonzo, plus Liberal Democratic Party chairman David Musila and party spokesman Joseph Kamotho.
They said Parliament was the main culprit in the Constitution debacle and it was time it was dissolved.
Mr Ruto said action must be taken against the House because it was the "central player in the betrayal of the people's Constitution".
Most MPs, he added, were manipulated to endorse the proposed Constitution that was rejected.
"This Parliament is now discredited and the President must dissolve it to pave way for the people of Kenya to elect representatives with a new mandate," he added.
Mr Odinga, saying he was speaking on behalf of the former ministers in the Orange team, said they did not mind being sacked but would be willing to serve in a new Cabinet only if the President met specific conditions, which they did not identify.
But he hinted it might involve several previous ministers not being reappointed.
Said Mr Odinga: "We represent certain values and certain conditions must be met first... there are certain characters who are responsible for driving us to where we are today and who in our view, do not belong to any Government that would like to provide proper responses in this country."
He said they would disclose their conditions for serving in the Cabinet if and when they were invited to the negotiating table.
"It doesn't worry us even if some of us opposed to the Draft Constitution are not reappointed. I have said many times Raila Odinga was not born with a Cabinet minister's post tied to his neck," Mr Odinga said.
He said he had only served in the Cabinet for four years out of the 13 years he has been in Parliament. "For nine years I was a backbencher, so being in the back bench is not a problem."
The team was speaking at Orange House in Nairobi's Upper Hill. Leaders first met at a secret address before arriving to meet the MPs and the press.

Original_Search_Title: Civil Society to Shame MPs Over Vote Buying
Headline: Kenya: Civil Society to Shame MPs Over Vote Buying
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Six MPs in the Banana camp caught buying votes on referendum day will be shamed, the civil society has said.
At the same time, the Kenya Civil Society Programme will provide Kenyans with a means of achieving a new Constitution next week.
The desire for a new law, did not end with the rejection of the Wako Draft. All should say how they intend to ensure the dream of Kenyans is realised, the organisation said.
The six MPs were caught by the civil society programme observers dishing out money to voters in Kisii, Busia and Kakamega districts.
The coalition of civil society organisations who monitored the referendum and sent out 5,000 people to specific constituencies.
While giving its preliminary report on the referendum, the alliance said allegations regarding the MPs were specific: they were buying votes to influence the outcome in their favour.
At a press conference in Nairobi on Wednesday, the executive director of the Institute for Education in Democracy Koki Muli said they would hand over the report to the relevant organs for action, including the police.
The group, she said, had earlier complained to the police and the Electoral Commission of Kenya prior to the referendum, that politicians from both sides were involved in election malpractices, including vote buying.
There was no action taken by either the police or the electoral commission, she said.
She added: "Since issues of a criminal nature are reported to police, we now want to tell them people who were buying votes."
The report said, would be presented to the MPs involved so that they can defend themselves.
Several other complaints, they said, were also noted.

Original_Search_Title: The Things Power Does to People!
Headline: Kenya: The Things Power Does to People!
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The people have spoken. They are four words to live by, as the prime movers of the defeated "Yes" campaign have learnt the hard way. They could have saved us all the trouble - and the billions of shillings - had they paused for a moment and listened to the still voice of the people.
It was there all along, expressed in traditional Kenyan fashion - in whispers in the privacy of our homes, at public rallies where the people sat glum and stony-faced as politicians put on performances that were more show than substance. It fed off conversations that sallied back and forth between friends and foes.
It is inconceivable that the Government did not have intelligence on this growing disquiet among the people. Why no one took the trouble to act on it is something that history will judge harshly, without a doubt. This story is a work in progress, and there is still the 2007 General Election to reckon with.
We tend to be very forbearing when it comes to politics in this country. We have a long fuse, but it burns everything before it when it blows up.
Retired President Moi learnt in 2002 that just because Kenyans streamed into stadiums to listen to him did not mean that they could not see through his power games.
They humoured him when he played leaders and ordinary citizens against each other as though they were pawns on a chess-board. They sang his praises and lulled him into a false sense of security even as he sunk deeper into the hole he had dug himself into.
Kenyans were never going to behave differently as the bigwigs in the Kibaki regime took liberties with their hard-earned freedoms. We cherish our peace and, more significantly, we learnt in 2002 that the vote is the most effective tool yet to make our feelings known.
Just because there were no street demonstrations, no human rights activists bellowing from the rooftops, and no signed petitions doing the rounds, did not mean that Kenyans could not cut through the noise and home in on the self-serving machinations of the President's inner circle.
Now our pseudo-leaders are eating humble pie, something they are decidedly unfamiliar with. Justice has a way of catching up with even the most wily of politicians.
The men and women who revolved around the Kibaki presidency must take stock of the past three years and their role in alienating the leadership from the people, who have had to suffer their big egos and ineptitude at marketing the nation as a model of democracy and good governance.
We gave them the mandate to remake Kenya. They opted for internecine wars that could only erode public confidence in the leadership.
Push has now come to shove, and they must look inwards with brutal honesty if they are to take away any useful lessons from the bruising battle of the past few months. This is not the time to cut corners and look for scapegoats.
The Banana camp lost the referendum on the proposed new Constitution, not so much because Kenyans were infuriated by the document as the people championing it - and the blatant interests they represented.
Many Kenyans did not even bother to read the draft. It's not that they couldn't do so. Our literacy levels are quite respectable. But while the document was important, there were more pressing issues at stake.
They were tuned in to the political undercurrents and the opportunity they presented to force the hand of the Establishment into getting back on track - if it wishes to.
The Orange team was simply more adept at reading the signs of the times. They latched on to it, fed the flames into a roaring fire, and stuck their necks out at the right historical moment.
They are not out of the woods yet, but they can at least savour the satisfaction of carrying the day in a gruelling battle for the hearts and minds of Kenyans that they joined with distinct disadvantages, courtesy of the self-proclaimed minister for Bananas, Kiraitu Murungi.
The Oranges gave it their best shot, yes, but there is no way round the fact that half their work was done for them by the Banana brigade, which turned the Kibaki administration into a comedy of errors.
Drunk with power, they insulted the intelligence of Kenyans, rubbished the same law they swore to protect, and treated court orders contemptuously. They used offensive language and rubbed us up the wrong way each time they opened their mouths.
These people simply couldn't handle power. The President will be well-advised to let them go, no apologies and no regrets. He might then salvage his own reputation.
This is a critical juncture in President Kibaki's political career. Fate has been kind to him, and he survived both the murderous Kenyatta and Moi regimes with barely a scratch. Even now, when the mud is flying all over the place, there is no shortage of Kenyans willing to give him the benefit of the doubt.
He must retreat to an inner sanctum and search his soul. He can't take anyone along on this pilgrimage. That is the price of being The Leader. He bears the cross alone. Other names will disappear with time, but his will be written indelibly into our history as the third president.
In his search for working solutions for a "working nation", the President will have to rewind the tape and tease out the precious lessons that should guide his journey henceforth. Let him take all the time he needs to grieve for what could have been - and then do what he must without fear or favour.
The choice is stark. He can earn a good report card or an abysmal one. He can cut off his nose to spite his face.
Or he can take all that has happened on the chin, cut his losses and start all over again. No one promised him that being at the top would cushion him from hurting.
President Kibaki must carry the knowledge with him that the world of politics teems with traitors, and they are not always the usual suspects. No, sir.
In his shoes, I would throw in my lot with the people who laid their trust - and their destiny - in his hands in 2002. Anything short of that will be political suicide.
At the height of the civil rights movement, African-American slaves shored up their energies with songs that had one message - keep your eyes on the prize.
There can be only one prize for Mr Kibaki: fulfilling his promises to the people of Kenya. He must now put the sideshows behind him and focus on the aspirations of his people. They have spoken, loud and clear. Is he listening?
Ms Oriang' is the Nation's managing editor in charge of magazines

Original_Search_Title: Clay Blames Yes Loss On Graft
Headline: Kenya: Clay Blames Yes Loss On Graft
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Corruption in high places is to blame for the Government's stunning loss in the referendum on new Constitution, former British high commissioner to Kenya Edward Clay has said.
Sir Edward told an audience at Chatham House in London yesterday: "The result was unexpected and in my opinion had a lot to do with corruption. The vote clearly shows the level of dissatisfaction across Kenya.
"A new Constitution, which generally sought to disperse power, would have stopped irresponsible and unaccountable corruption in the Office of the President in particular, where 50 per cent of the current corruption lies," said Sir Edward.
Mood of the country
He said that President Kibaki and the Banana campaign supporters had badly misunderstood the mood of the country which, he said, was for change and a new beginning. "Everyone knows who is involved in corruption and what is going on," he said.
But in a quick reaction yesterday, the Government described the remarks as "wild", saying Sir Edward knew nothing about Kenyan politics.
Government spokesman Alfred Mutua said that ordinary citizens in rural and urban Kenya knew better than any foreigner why they rejected the Wako Draft.
"The Government is serving the Kenyan people and is not out to please foreigners," he said.
Dr Mutua continued: "Sir Edward is tarnishing Kenya's name and yet he has not provided any evidence to back his claims that some ministers are engaging in procurement with the sole purposes of getting kickbacks. Procurement is done according to priority and when it is necessary."
Kenyan media
But Sir Edward also said that the Kenyan media bore a huge responsibility over the corruption issue. "Where is the inquiring spirit among Kenyan journalists?" he asked.
The retired diplomat also said that the donors had a role to play on the matter. "They need to think carefully about their priorities," he said.
Sir Edward said he was not advocating a withdrawal of British aid to Kenya "which would only affect the very poor at the expense of the sins of the rich," but that the West should not be afraid to push legal action.
But in answer to a question by the Nation, Sir Edward denied that politics was playing a role in the row over the renewal of the agreement over the British army training in Kenya.
"As I understand it, this is a technical problem," he said.

Original_Search_Title: You Have No Mandate to Review Law, Orange Camp Told
Headline: Kenya: You Have No Mandate to Review Law, Orange Camp Told
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange team has no mandate to steer the constitutional reform process, 39 political parties said yesterday. It has to get that mandate from Parliament, they said.
Welcoming President Kibaki's sacking of his Cabinet, the parties including the Democratic Party of Kenya (DP), said the rejection of the Wako Draft during the referendum should not be interpreted to mean that Kenyans had given the Orange team the mandate of delivering a constitution.
"Kenyans were not asked to give anybody that form of mandate and on our part, we do not accept that any such mandate has been given to anyone," the DP national executive committee, led by former Cabinet minister Joseph Munyao, said at a news conference before they converged at the Yes Secretariat in Nairobi to issue another statement on behalf of the parties.
Apart from nine parties that are represented in Parliament - DP, Ford Kenya, the National Alliance (Party) of Kenya, Ford People, the United Democratic Movement, Sisi kwa Sisi, Safina as well as a faction of Kanu and the Liberal Democratic Party (LDP), the rest are moribund and exist by virtue of being registered by the registrar of societies.
They attributed the Banana camp's defeat at the referendum to their leaders' failure to read the public mood and ignoring several clauses in the Draft Constitution, which "our rivals used to mobilise popular support against the draft".
Mr Munyao said the DP's executive council would now concentrate in reorganising the party and building coalitions with willing parties in readiness for the 2007 General Election.
They called on President Kibaki to form a government of national unity in line with the party's manifesto to allow for national healing and reconciliation.
The party called on politicians to give Kenyans time to cool off before leaders come together to chart out how to deliver a new constitution.
Later at an inter-parties' Press conference where the DP was also represented, Yes Secretariat boss Alex Mureithi said those for the Wako Draft took lightly the clauses on land, inheritance and religion.
Such clauses, in addition to others, led to the rejection of the draft Constitution and would need to be looked at afresh, he said. "We had taken for granted clauses on land, inheritance and religion but now we realise that they are serious issues that need to be dealt with afresh," said Mr Mureithi.
Mr Mureithi emphasized the reforms Kenyans were yearning for would not be realised with a divided cabinet.
In a move met to ensure it remains relevant, Mr Mureithi announced the interparties forum had converted itself to a development forum that will be charged with working towards economic, social and leadership development.
Mr Mureithi said the forum would work with the youth, women as well as those with disabilities to ensure President Kibaki delivers in developing the country.
They will further take a leading role in giving information to bodies fighting corruption to rout the vice in the country which he says was being propagated by the old generation.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki Told to Form All-Inclusive Cabinet
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki Told to Form All-Inclusive Cabinet
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki was yesterday urged to name a Cabinet that reflects the national diversity.
He should use the opportunity he has created to appoint a Cabinet that caters for all Kenyans, said Law Society of Kenya (LSK) chairman Tom Ojienda.
Speaking at Nakuru, he said: "The President should not wish away the fact that a majority of Kenyans rejected the proposed Wako draft. Instead he should now set in motion avenues for an all-inclusive constitution."
Mr Ojienda said he was concerned that two weeks was too long for a country to run without a cabinet.
Political vacuum
"This is the first time in Kenyan history that we have a president without a cabinet and our fears are that this will create a dangerous political vacuum," he said.
Mr Ojienda said the referendum, in which the Government's Banana side lost to its No-Orange opposition, was a reflection of the longstanding political divisions in the Narc coalition government that split the Cabinet.
He questioned the dissolution of the Cabinet, saying it allowed the President to work alone without accountability.
Maseno North Anglican bishop Simeon Oketch said the decision to sack the Cabinet was ill-timed and could create divisions.
The President should have sought to heal the divisions in his Government instead of dismissing his ministers.
Bishop Oketch said the the vote was a reflection of Kenyans' dashed hopes in the Government and urged the President to begin addressing the election promises that propelled the National Rainbow Coalition (Narc) to power in 2002.
But Mumias Anglican bishop Ben Salala welcomed the sacking, saying it would give the President an opportunity to reorganise his Government.
The President, he said, should not reappoint the ministers who had abused their positions.
In Nyamira, leaders praised President Kibaki's move and urged him to name a cabinet to unite Kenyans irrespective of their political affiliations.
Nyamira county council chairman Alfayo Ngeresa said the referendum results clearly indicated that the ministers aligned to Banana camp were not truthful to the President and Kenyans.
President Kibaki's decision was also applauded by Nandi leaders, who asked him to pick ministers who can deliver. The leaders included Kapsabet mayor John Maru, Nandi Hills Town council chairman Wilson Lagat, Mr Boniface Korir and trade unionist Joshua Yuga.
In Western Lugari MP Enoch Kibunguchy called on the President to pick a cabinet capable of helping the Government to fulfil its promises.
Dissolved Parliament
Eldoret South MP David Koros said the President should have dissolved Parliament.
By rejecting the proposed Constitution, he said, Kenyans had shown that they had no confidence in the Government.
Aldai MP Jimmy Choge described the President's action as cosmetic and said he had failed to show the way forward.
Anglican cleric Maritim Rirei called the Cabinet's dissolution ill-advised.
Kanduyi MP Wafula Wamunyinyi said the President should now appoint a cabinet that is united.
In Eldoret, Catholic Episcopal Conference chairman said Cornelius Korir welcomed the President's action but said two weeks was too long for the country to be without a cabinet.
"The President has the prerogative to hire and fire the Cabinet but the two weeks power vacuum will not augur well for stability and economic growth," said Bishop Korir of the Eldoret Catholic Church.
His Anglican counterpart Thomas Kogo and North Rift Muslims chairman Abubakar Ahmed Bini and vice-chairman Suleiman Kimorna asked the President to appoint MPs from the Orange team to the Cabinet.
The Central Kenya Parliamentary Group said the ministers' sacking would ensure "President Kibaki comes up with an effective team".
"We are very happy about it," said group chairman Gachara Muchiri. "The President has proved that he is fully in charge."
Cotu boss Francis Atwoli said: "The President has made the right choice. He is justified in dissolving the Cabinet as no President can lead a divided house."
However, he said he was pessimistic that Cabinet changes would restore confidence in the Government.
Dissolution was expected
North Horr MP Bonaya Godana said the Cabinet's dissolution was expected after the Government side was defeated in the referendum.
"The President has to come up with a Cabinet acceptable to the people, he is under no obligation to form a government of national unity.
"As a president, he has the sole authority to appoint his Cabinet but he has to take cognisance of the prevailing national popular mood." he said.
Reports by Henry Nyarora, Tom Matoke, Tony Kago and Nyabonyi Kazungu, Peter Ngetich, Simbi Kusimba, Barnabas Bii and Moses Mwathi.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki Fires His Entire Cabinet
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki Fires His Entire Cabinet
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki last night sacked his entire Cabinet and all his assistant ministers, only a day after losing the Constitution referendum.
It is the first time in Kenya's 42 years of independence that all ministers have been dismissed at one fell swoop.
President Kibaki
He announced the dramatic move in a live broadcast on KBC, the State television service, at 7pm.
The President said in a terse statement: "Following the results of the referendum, it is important for me as your President to reshuffle ministers and assistant ministers for harmony.
"And according to powers given to me by the Constitution I have sacked all ministers and assistant ministers, and will announce a new Government in two weeks."
He added this move was to ensure harmony and unity in service delivery and the functioning of government.
Exceptions to the dismissals were Vice-President Moody Awori, who will stay in office as VP, although stripped of his additional responsibilities as minister for Home Affairs, and the attorney-general, Mr Amos Wako.
The Attorney-General who also sits in the Cabinet is the Government's principal legal adviser and his role is believed to be so crucial to the running of any administration that it is difficult for a President to leave it vacant for even a day.
The rebellion over the proposed Constitution was championed by seven of Mr Kibaki's own Cabinet ministers.
They were Mr Raila Odinga (Roads), Mr Kalonzo Musyoka (Environment), Prof Anyang' Nyong'o (Planning), Mr Ochillo Ayacko (Sports), Mr William ole Ntimama (Public Service), Mr Najib Balala (National Heritage) and Mrs Linah Jebii Kilimo (Immigration).
Joining them were the leader of the Official Opposition, Mr Uhuru Kenyatta and leading lights from the main opposition party, Kanu, including Mr William Ruto and Mr Mutula Kilonzo.
The President's decision was interpreted as aimed at giving him a chance to form a new government which he will work with for the remaining two years of his term of office.

Ministers allied to the Banana Yes-vote group repeatedly urged the President to sack the rebel ministers.
Before dissolving the Cabinet last night, the President had held a meeting with Mr Awori in State House.
The VP, who had campaigned for the Yes camp, flew into Nairobi from his Funyula constituency, in Busia, and drove straight to State House. Details of the talks were not made available, but it was believed they discussed the poll results.
The President dissolved his top team without even having called a meeting of the Cabinet for the past five months.
A State House official would say only that many former ministers were still in their constituencies, where they had gone to monitor the results.
But a few former ministers had arrived in Nairobi by yesterday planning to resume work after the long referendum weekend.
Those who went back to their offices before being dismissed included Mr Musikari Kombo (Local Government), Dr Chris Murungaru (Transport), Mr Odinga and Mr Balala.
Government spokesman Alfred Mutua announced on October 15 that the State had allowed ministers time off to canvass for either the Yes or No votes.

However, ministers in both camps mellowed towards the last week of the campaigns - which saw eight people killed in violence surrounding campaign rallies - and called for peaceful voting and reconciliation.
The current Constitution empowers the President to appoint and fire ministers at will.
