Original_Search_Title: Now All Eyes Will Be On Kibaki And What He Will Do
Headline: Kenya: Now All Eyes Will Be On Kibaki And What He Will Do
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A night of the long knives in the Cabinet was almost inevitable. But President Kibaki pulled a shocker last night. He did not just do the expected and sack the rebellious ministers who handed him an embarrassing defeat at the referendum, but sent the entire Cabinet home. And he will take his time, a fortnight, to reconstitute the Government.
What a deft way to win back the political headlines from the Orange Movement?
The move is unprecedented in Kenyan history. It is also a bold and daring manoeuvre, and if things work out will accord President Kibaki an opportunity not just to discard the disloyal ministers, but also the loyalists responsible for the referendum debacle.
It is also an indication of the bitter divisions within the President's inner circle in the wake of the referendum defeat.
Trailing badly
Even before the poll when it started becoming increasingly clear that the Banana team was trailing badly and required nothing short of a miracle to catch up, sniping had already started within.
One group was using the public rallies to send the message that the disloyal elements, the seven former Cabinet ministers - Raila Odinga, Kalonzo Musyoka, Najib Balala, William ole Ntimama, Linah Jebii Kilimo, Ochillo Ayako and Anyang' Nyong'o - at the head of the Orange campaign would be sacked immediately after the referendum.
But others were pointing the blame at the loyal and hard-line Cabinet ministers who were being held responsible for the debacle. The fingers were pointing largely at Kiraitu Murungi of Justice and Constitutional Affairs and Chris Murungaru of Transport.
Mr Murungi was being blamed for mishandling the constitutional review from the beginning and ultimately crafting the Wako Draft and taking to referendum an unpopular document that had no hope of passage. He also stood accused of creating unnecessary conflict within the coalition that ultimately led to the big fallout between NAK and LDP.
Unsavoury face
Dr Murungaru, generally, was seen as the unsavoury face of the Narc Government and one of the personalities who were no credit to the Cabinet.
Indeed at various rallies, ministers such as Kipruto arap Kirwa, Mukhisa Kituyi and others stated openly that would not want to share the Yes campaign platform in their regions with "tainted" colleagues.
Yesterday morning, some 12 hours before President Kibaki made his shock announcement, Kikuyu language radio stations were dominated by callers not just expressing shock at the referendum outcome and blaming disloyal ministers, but, more importantly, insisting there there must be a thorough clean-up targeting the powerful figures who have been misleading the President.
By then, there was already furious infighting within the President's inner circle over who should take the blame.
One side was insisting that Mr Odinga, Musyoka and company must be shown the door. But others were insisting that even loyalists who messed up should also go.
Yet pointing the finger at Mr Murungi and Dr Murungaru is simplistic. For the fact is that well before the referendum came around, the two had been largely shoved out of the inner circle.
The politicians exercising most direct influence on the President, the ones who could secure appointments at State House and were ever at the President's side were Mr Njenga Karume (Special Programmes) and Mr John Michuki (Internal Security).
Both came to exercise great clout with the formation of the Government of National Unity. That was also the time the unpopular Dr Murungaru was moved out of the Office of the President, swapping placed with Mr Michuki at Transport.
The elevation of Mr Karume and Mr Michuki suggested that elderly moderates and consensus builders were gaining prominence at the expense of an excitable and abrasive younger generation which was wielding power with too much gusto.
The two were known to chuckle the discomforts figures like Dr Murungaru have endured in recent times such as the UK and US visa bans.
Behind the scenes, also, President Kibaki was coming to rely more and more on his old Muthaiga Golf Club set - figures like Mr Joe Wanjui, Mr Solomon Karanja and Mr Nat Kang'ethe - and their man in the Cabinet Amos Kimunya. Another key figure in the power equation is State House-based PS Stanley Murage.
That, for supporters of the Murungi-Murungaru axis, was another group to be blamed. They were accused of exercising power and influence while holding no Cabinet positions, and thus could not be called to account. They were also seen as politically naive and to lack the cunning required to deal with the likes of Mr Odinga.
With so much rancour within, the President must have really agonised over what to do. He appreciated his loyalists even if they made mistakes, yet recognised that sacking the Orange ministers was no solution to the unfolding crisis in Government.
It would only give them a chance to go fully into opposition as they built up on the momentum generated by the referendum result.
The President pulled a move that caught most by surprise. Up to the time he made his live radio and TV announcement, very few were privy to what he was going to do. Even most of the characters who consider themselves insiders were of the opinion that he was simply going to dismiss the rebel ministers.
Instead, he went for a deft blow that will have the effect of grabbing back the headlines and the political initiative from the Orange movement.
Now all eyes will be on President Kibaki and what he will do next. Apart from that is the expected endless analysis about issues such as running a country without a Cabinet and who will be retained and who will be left out and the President will have won himself a needed respite.
For two weeks, he will be the centre of attention. If one of the problems his administration has faced is a Cabinet distracted by divisions and infighting, he has simply grabbed the reins and sent everybody home.
He has the luxury of time to reconsider exactly how to reconstitute the Government, and that might be time for intense negotiations with various political groupings that could be looking not just at who will be in the Cabinet, but deeper issues such as the way forward for Constitution review and how the Government can come out of the political morass.

Original_Search_Title: Sacked Ministers Welcome Kibaki's Move
Headline: Kenya: Sacked Ministers Welcome Kibaki's Move
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Former ministers reacted quickly to the news of their dismissal, with most accepting that the job was the President's to give ... and to take away.
Former Cooperative Development minister Njeru Ndwiga said: "It is the President's constitutional right to hire and fire ministers. It is also his duty to ensure discipline in his Cabinet."
Asked if there had been indiscipline in the Cabinet, the Manyatta MP said: "I think we have been expressing our democratic rights. But as to whether we have exercised that freedom in the right manner, it is for the President to decide."
Former Environment minister Newton Kulundu welcomed the move, describing it as positive.
He said the Cabinet was divided down the middle and was impossible to work as a team.
"The Cabinet was at war with each other and it appeared if there were two parallel governments," he said.
Dr Kulundu said the sacking and appointing of the Cabinet was the prerogative of the President.
Differences
The former minister said he was expected to open a labour conference on Friday in Mombasa, "but due to the announcement this will not be".
The appointment of a new Cabinet would help smooth out the differences, and he would be more than happy to serve in the Government again.
Former Roads minister Raila Odinga, a prominent member of the Orange team, declined to comment except to say, "Let us talk tomorrow."
Also declining to comment was former Office of the President minister in charge of the Public Service, Mr William ole Ntimama, another member of the Orange group.
Former ministers and their assistants allied to the Orange movement will meet this morning to discuss the development, it was believed.
It is likely they will ask President Kibaki to call a General Election.
Another Orange former minister, Mrs Linah Kilimo (Immigration) said it was the President's prerogative to hire and fire members of his Cabinet.
"Of course it is okay; the Constitution allows him to hire and fire," she said on the telephone.
However the sacking contradicted the President's call for reconciliation on Tuesday.
The Head of State, while conceding defeat on the referendum vote, called for a healing process.
The Marakwet East MP said the reconciliation should have started with Banana and Orange ministers.
She said she learnt of the sackings from others.
Mr Musikari Kombo (Local Government) said: "Like a captain, when your team is not playing together, and the passes are not flowing, you call for time out. That is what the President has done because the divisions in Cabinet were so deep."
It was the President's prerogative to constitute or send the Cabinet home.
Mr Joseph Munyao described the President's move as the best thing ever for the country which will allow him to set up a fresh team.
"He had done the right thing because you cannot continue with a divided house. He will now form a team that will stabilise his leadership," he said.
Mr John Koech said the President had been antagonised too much by some members of his Cabinet and he was bound to take the action.
"It was something that was bound to come especially after the referendum," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Yes Ex-Ministers Blame Campaigns
Headline: Kenya: Yes Ex-Ministers Blame Campaigns
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Former Cabinet ministers in the losing Banana Yes-vote group yesterday revealed why they think voters rejected the proposed constitution in Monday's historic referendum.
First they admitted their campaign was poorly organised and had started late.
They also accused their Orange rivals of "whipping up tribal emotions," and they claimed some of the provisions in the Draft had been "distorted."
And they also said they thought the public perceptions of the reputations of some of the ex-ministers in the Banana camp had cost them votes.
The reasons emerged during separate conversations between the Daily Nation and the former ministers Musikari Kombo, Amos Kimunya, Kipruto Kirwa, Charity Ngilu and Newton Kulundu.
Their frank comments came as President Kibaki held a meeting with Vice-President Moody Awori at State House, Nairobi; the first since losing the the referendum vote.
The President had not called a Cabinet meeting since the former ministers were given a break to concentrate on the referendum campaigns five months ago.
But unlike their Orange rivals who met on Tuesday and again yesterday, the core team of Banana leaders has not met to discuss the outcome of the poll or even set a strategy for the future.
And at the Yes secretariat yesterday, agents were protesting they had not been paid their allowances.
Banana team leaders were divided over whether to accept the hand of reconciliation offered by the Orange team immediately after their victory was announced.
The Orange team - those against the proposed Constitution - polled 3,548,477 which was 57 per cent of the vote, against those supporting the Draft who had 43 per cent with 2,532,918 votes.
Leading lights of the Orange team include former Cabinet ministers Raila Odinga (Roads), Kalonzo Musyoka (Environment), Najib Balala (Culture and Heritage) and Anyang' Nyong'o (Planning), plus Official Opposition leader Uhuru Kenyatta and Eldoret North MP William Ruto.
They were joined in their plea for unity by ex-assistant minister Mutua Katuku and MPs Kalembe Ndile and Bonny Khalwale who said the country needed a new start.
Mr Kombo, Mr Kirwa and Dr Kulundu said the Yes team lost because the Orange team had a better campaign machinery.
The No-vote proponents endeared themselves to the public with misinformation, they claimed.
Wherever, the Orange team went, the two added, they picked issues that were dear to the local communities and misinformed them.
Mr Kombo said: "I think the Orange team had a good campaign machinery which politicised everything."
The Ford Kenya leader said people did not vote for the Constitution but for politics. He cited the chapter on land - taken straight from the original Bomas Draft without amendment - and said Orange leaders had used it to play up emotions.
Asked what he thought about negotiations with the Orange team, he said he had been a proponent of negotiations from time immemorial.
However, Mr Kombo and Mr Kirwa said the Government had a country to run and election pledges to fulfil.
Mr Kirwa argued the Banana team lost because the Orange team frightened people, especially in the Rift Valley.

Headline: Kenya: Kibaki Fires His Entire Cabinet

Original_Search_Title: Banana Shocker From Coast
Headline: Kenya: Banana Shocker From Coast
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Of all the referendum results in different provinces, the Coast's were
perhaps the most unexpected. For the region has always tended to support the
Government.
But this week saw the Yes camp losing in all the constituencies. This
new voting pattern cannot be dismissed merely as a momentary passion caused
by the proposed Constitution.
It is, rather, a strong political statement. Most observers expected the
Yes team to fare well in rural Coast, where poverty-stricken masses are
easily swayed by empty promises.
However, as soon as the first rural results started trickling in, it was
obvious that the political haranguing that the voters had received to entice
support for the draft had largely been ignored.
Several issues
Analysts say several issues were responsible for the way the coastal
communities voted, the most important of the issues being land.
During the campaigns, the Yes team repeatedly harped on land, promising
the electorate that if the new Constitution was passed, all squatters would
be settled, mainly on absentee landlords' possessions.
However, it appears that the people were not convinced that the new
Constitution would adequately address this thorny issue.
"There are laws in the current Constitution that can address the
squatter problem but there has never been the political will to apply them
to resolve the issue," said Bahari MP Joe Khamisi.
He added that the recent rush to issue title deeds was not a genuine
government policy but only a cynical attempt to bribe voters.
And while the voters looked at the Government with suspicion, the
politicians' public utterance that the new constitution would give the
Government the muscle to repossess absentee landlords' lands could have had
a negative effect on the vote-getting stratagems.
Idle land
This might explain the large turnout by Kenyans of Arab and Asian
origins, who are perceived as the main owners of such land. They might have
feared that a Yes vote would adversely affect them.
Voting pattern
Mr Khamisi said he believed the voting pattern was a strong political
statement. Coast people, he said, could never again be taken for
granted.
Said the MP: "Whoever wants to be in political partnership with the
Coast communities will have to take this factor into consideration."
According to Mr Khamisi, the other issue that made the Coast voters to
reject the draft constitution was the clause on devolution.
Said Mr Khamisi: "Coast people have always - ever since Ronald Ngala -
wanted a federal system. They put forward a strong case for it at the Bomas
meeting. But their suggestion was replaced with district governments."
Coast communities, he said, were convinced that only through a
federation could resources be distributed equitably. "So they were not happy
when the Wako Draft introduced the District Governments instead of
federalism," he said.
While local fishermen continued to survive on subsistence fishing, the
Government was allowing foreign ships to exploit sea resources instead of
helping its people to prosper, the MP lamented.
Furthermore, said Mr Khamisi, the local communities had never benefited
from tourism because the industry is controlled by foreigners who stash the
bulk of the money abroad.
The people are aware that only through the president's excessive powers
that most of the land was dished out by successive governments to political
allies, he added.
Attack on Kadhi
Asked why he thought the Coast people voted the way they did, Muslim
leader Mohamed Sheikh said the public attack on the Chief Kadhi for his
support of the No camp disenfranchised Muslim voters.
Although some Muslims had not made up their mind on which way to vote,
the moment politicians started insulting the Chief Kadhi, Sheikh Hamad
Kassim, they all decided to reject the proposed Constitution, said Mr
Sheikh, who is the director of the Youth Against Child and Drug Abuse
(Yacada).

Original_Search_Title: Heal Poll Wounds, Urge Leaders
Headline: Kenya: Heal Poll Wounds, Urge Leaders
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kenyans should cultivate unity after the referendum, Kisumu municipal
councillors said yesterday.
"It is time to begin national healing to ensure Kenya remains a united
nation," they said.
The rift created by the campaigns between the Orange and Banana camps
should be bridged immediately to allow economic development.
Led by mayor Priscah Auma, they told journalists at the Kisumu Town Hall
that it was only through unity that a constitution acceptable to all would
be achieved.
"Kenyans have spoken, and the onus is now on the leaders to sit down,
dialogue and provide direction that will lead to a good constitution," they
said.
The civic leaders called on the No and Yes leaders to bury the hatchet
and work together for the national good. "We know some are bitter but this
is not the time for bitterness," the mayor said on their behalf.
"We want to be sober and realise that this country is greater than them
all. Thus, they should sit down and put the interest of the country ahead of
their personal pursuits."
They praised President Kibaki for "leading from the front" by respecting
the wishes of the majority of Kenyans through conceding defeat.
He had shown true statesmanship by heeding Kenyans' wish, they said, and
called on other leaders to emulate him.
"We must thank the President for providing true leadership at a time
when many thought the country could plunge into chaos," the councillors
said. "He remained sober, and we wish to reassure him of our unending
support and loyalty."

Original_Search_Title: Orange Names New Review Team
Headline: Kenya: Orange Names New Review Team
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Victorious Orange Democratic Movement yesterday set up a committee to
spearhead the search for a new constitution.
The team, to be chaired by Kanu secretary-general William Ruto, includes
Orange MPs who were members of the Parliamentary Select Committee which he
led before it was taken over by former Energy minister Simeon Nyachae.
Mr Ruto said yesterday: "This will be the nucleus body to begin afresh,
and we also give a chance to our friends on the Banana side to give us names
(of people) we will work with."
Other members are former Roads minister Raila Odinga and former
Environment and National Heritage Cabinet colleagues Kalonzo Musyoka and
Najib Balala as well as MPs Otieno Kajwang' (Mbita), Henry Kosgey
(Tinderet), Moses Cheboi (Kuresoi), Marsden Madoka (Mwatate) and Yusuf Haji
(Ijara).
Membership open to LSK
Mr Ruto said veteran politician and former Butere MP Martin Shikuku, one
of the remaining veterans of the Lancaster constitutional conference that
brought Kenya's independence, will also be a member.
Membership will be open also to the Law Society of Kenya and other key
players in the review.
The announcement from the Orange secretariat came as the Constitution of
Kenya Review Commission started winding up.
The chairperson, Mrs Abida Ali-Aroni, said: "The conclusion of the
referendum marks the end of the mandate of the Constitution of Kenya Review
Commission."
The staff and the 27 commissioners will either go home or return to
their original jobs after the December 22 deadline set by the review
Act.
Under the Act, the proposed constitution, which was rejected by voters
on Monday, will be part of the records for future reference.
In announcing the Orange team, Mr Ruto promised that details on how it
would work and where it would be based will be released next week.
For the five years the commission spearheaded the review, the Act
provided for an independent committee of Parliament to act as an arbitrator
in all disputes.
The first team was headed by Mr Odinga (1998-2002) - then Mr Ruto
(2002-2003) and Mr Nyachae, who was elected early this year.
Yesterday, Mr Ruto said the Orange group had set up another team to
organise a thanksgiving service and rally at Uhuru Park, Nairobi, on
Saturday.
The three-hour meeting
The three-hour meeting at the secretariat was attended by five Cabinet
ministers, who have since been sacked, and more than 20 MPs.
The leaders received reports from agents in all the 210 constituencies,
which they analysed and found that 153 had rejected the draft, while 57,
mainly from Central province and Central Rift, had accepted it.

Government and one of the personalities who were no credit to the
Cabinet.
Indeed at various rallies, ministers such as Kipruto arap Kirwa, Mukhisa
Kituyi and others stated openly that would not want to share the Yes
campaign platform in their regions with "tainted" colleagues.
Yesterday morning, some 12 hours before President Kibaki made his shock
announcement, Kikuyu language radio stations were dominated by callers not
just expressing shock at the referendum outcome and blaming disloyal
ministers, but, more importantly, insisting there there must be a thorough
clean-up targeting the powerful figures who have been misleading the
President.
By then, there was already furious infighting within the President's
inner circle over who should take the blame.
One side was insisting that Mr Odinga, Musyoka and company must be shown
the door. But others were insisting that even loyalists who messed up should
also go.
Yet pointing the finger at Mr Murungi and Dr Murungaru is
simplistic. For the fact is that well before the referendum came around, the
two had been largely shoved out of the inner circle.
The politicians exercising most direct influence on the President, the
ones who could secure appointments at State House and were ever at the
President's side were Mr Njenga Karume (Special Programmes) and Mr John
Michuki (Internal Security).
Both came to exercise great clout with the formation of the Government
of National Unity. That was also the time the unpopular Dr Murungaru was
moved out of the Office of the President, swapping placed with Mr Michuki at
Transport.
The elevation of Mr Karume and Mr Michuki suggested that elderly
moderates and consensus builders were gaining prominence at the expense of
an excitable and abrasive younger generation which was wielding power with
too much gusto.
The two were known to chuckle the discomforts figures like Dr Murungaru
have endured in recent times such as the UK and US visa bans.
Behind the scenes, also, President Kibaki was coming to rely more and
more on his old Muthaiga Golf Club set - figures like Mr Joe Wanjui, Mr
Solomon Karanja and Mr Nat Kang'ethe - and their man in the Cabinet Amos
Kimunya. Another key figure in the power equation is State House-based PS
Stanley Murage.
That, for supporters of the Murungi-Murungaru axis, was another group to
be blamed. They were accused of exercising power and influence while holding
no Cabinet positions, and thus could not be called to account. They were
also seen as politically naive and to lack the cunning required to deal with
the likes of Mr Odinga.
With so much rancour within, the President must have really agonised
over what to do. He appreciated his loyalists even if they made mistakes,
yet recognised that sacking the Orange ministers was no solution to the
unfolding crisis in Government.
It would only give them a chance to go fully into opposition as they
built up on the momentum generated by the referendum result.
The President pulled a move that caught most by surprise. Up to the time
he made his live radio and TV announcement, very few were privy to what he
was going to do. Even most of the characters who consider themselves
insiders were of the opinion that he was simply going to dismiss the rebel
ministers.
Instead, he went for a deft blow that will have the effect of grabbing
back the headlines and the political initiative from the Orange
movement.
Now all eyes will be on President Kibaki and what he will do next. Apart
from that is the expected endless analysis about issues such as running a
country without a Cabinet and who will be retained and who will be left out
and the President will have won himself a needed respite.
For two weeks, he will be the centre of attention. If one of the
problems his administration has faced is a Cabinet distracted by divisions
and infighting, he has simply grabbed the reins and sent everybody
home.
He has the luxury of time to reconsider exactly how to reconstitute the
Government, and that might be time for intense negotiations with various
political groupings that could be looking not just at who will be in the
Cabinet, but deeper issues such as the way forward for Constitution review
and how the Government can come out of the political morass.

Original_Search_Title: Women Urge Unity Among Politicians
Headline: Kenya: Women Urge Unity Among Politicians
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Women yesterday urged leaders in the Orange and Banana camps to forge
unity for the sake of the country.
The Women Political Alliance, made up of NGOs, also applauded President
Kibaki's decision to accept the people's verdict.
It said it had voted for the Wako draft because of its promises on
youth, women and education.
"Kenyans spoke out on Monday and their decision in regard to the Wako
draft is final. We women of Kenya applaud President Kibaki's acceptance of
the people's verdict which truly reaffirms the prevailing political
maturity, democratic space and good governance in our country," Mrs Kamla
Skand, who read a statement, said.
She said the referendum result did not mean the end of the quest for a
new constitution, and urged leaders to continue with the task.
Rejection of draft
"In our unity call, we are asking for a new beginning on constitutional
dialogue for the country. We call for a process that recognises the
existence of a negotiable quest for a new constitution which should not end
with the rejection of the Wako draft," Mrs Skand said.
Mrs Virginia Kirumba, the national chairperson of the Kenya Business
Professional Women, said: "We and our supporters voted Yes because of the
gains promised on women's issues and we hope Kenyan women will be fully
represented in all initiatives of constitution-making."
Separately, South Mugirango MP Omingo Magara said the Yes team lost in
Kisii because its leaders had concentrated their resources and energy
fighting him.
He said a decision by his Ford People colleagues to camp in his area and
fight him for supporting the Orange camp instead of promoting the draft
constitution cost them votes.

Original_Search_Title: Pollsters Had Seen Orange Victory Coming
Headline: Kenya: Pollsters Had Seen Orange Victory Coming
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Two independent opinion polls conducted just days ahead of the November
21 referendum had accurately predicted the Orange win.
One, by Steadman International, had been conducted between November 11
and 14, just a week ahead of the poll. The other had been commissioned by a
group of donors and was concluded on November 14.
From the 2,010 people who answered the Steadman pollsters, the survey
showed that 57 per cent of the vote would go to Orange and that Banana would
take 43 per cent.
The actual result saw Orange gaining 57 per cent of the votes cast and
Banana 43 per cent, tallying with the prediction.
Apart from the actual results, 26 per cent of those interviewed said
they wanted the current Constitution to remain should Orange carry the
day. Another 35 per cent said Parliament should amend it.
According to the Steadman survey, only 27 per cent of Kenyans would like
the review process relaunched. The remaining 12 per cent did not have an
opinion.
Analysts interpreted this to mean that the majority of Kenyans were
opposed to the political heat generated by the review and would have liked
it shelved altogether or handled by Parliament.
This poll showed a significant change from another published in October,
also by Steadman, which gave Orange a 10-point lead, with 42 per cent, and
Banana trailing with 32 per cent. Another 22 per cent of those questioned
said they had not made up their minds on which way to cast their votes.
These results mean that the aggressive campaign staged by the Orange
team in the run-up to the poll paid off. It wooed the undecided voters to
its side, pushing up the actual vote by 15 points to 57 per cent.
Final results also confirmed what the pollsters had predicted - that the
biggest support for the proposed Constitution would come from Central
Province while Eastern Province would be the most hotly contested.
The poll commissioned by donors was conducted on the same dates as that
by Steadman and painted a similar picture.
They predicted that the Orange team would carry home 60.9 per cent of
the spoils while the Banana team would get 39.1 per cent. The plus or minus
1.79 percentage errors margin allowed means that the pollsters had well
captured the public mood.
Results of both surveys were obtained by the Nation at the time but were
embargoed until yesterday with the pollsters saying they did not want to
tilt public opinion or whip up emotions in the run-up to the voting.
Political analysts said that last minute euphoria could sway the vote
one way or the other but it is now evident that there was no change in the
public perception of the proposed Constitution.
The Steadman poll, which was the more detailed of the two, showed that
39 per cent of Kenyans prefer the Bomas Draft, 34 per cent the Wako Draft,
14 per cent the present Constitution while the rest were not sure.
It pointed to reasons why the Banana team may have been unpopular: 70
per cent of those interviewed did not approve of the use of State resources
in the campaigns.
Slightly over half of Kenyans were pessimistic about a united Kenya
after the referendum but far much fewer thought that the vote would result
in violence.
The Steadman poll, however, showed President Kibaki maintaining his
position as a favourite for the top seat with a 40 per cent score while
former Environment minister Kalonzo Musyoka was second with 29 per cent and
Mr Uhuru Kenyatta was third with seven per cent.
Former Roads minister Raila Odinga retained a high attraction for the
post of prime minister, should the position be created. Mr Musyoka was
second and former Health minister Charity Ngilu and Vice-President Moody
Awori at the tail end with minimal percentages.
The opinion polls were carried out in all the eight provinces. The
districts were selected randomly and with the number of people interviewed
in each region was determined by the proportion of registered voters.
The proportion, the pollsters said, was meant to ensure that they got a
fairly representative opinion from both rural and urban dwellers on what
position was likely to be taken by the public.

Original_Search_Title: Sacked Ministers Welcome Kibaki's Move
Headline: Kenya: Sacked Ministers Welcome Kibaki's Move
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Former ministers reacted quickly to the news of their dismissal, with
most accepting that the job was the President's to give ... and to take
away.
Former Cooperative Development minister Njeru Ndwiga said: "It is the
President's constitutional right to hire and fire ministers. It is also his
duty to ensure discipline in his Cabinet."
Asked if there had been indiscipline in the Cabinet, the Manyatta MP
said: "I think we have been expressing our democratic rights. But as to
whether we have exercised that freedom in the right manner, it is for the
President to decide."
Former Environment minister Newton Kulundu welcomed the move, describing
it as positive.
He said the Cabinet was divided down the middle and was impossible to
work as a team.
"The Cabinet was at war with each other and it appeared if there were
two parallel governments," he said.
Dr Kulundu said the sacking and appointing of the Cabinet was the
prerogative of the President.
Differences
The former minister said he was expected to open a labour conference on
Friday in Mombasa, "but due to the announcement this will not be".
The appointment of a new Cabinet would help smooth out the differences,
and he would be more than happy to serve in the Government again.
Former Roads minister Raila Odinga, a prominent member of the Orange
team, declined to comment except to say, "Let us talk tomorrow."
Also declining to comment was former Office of the President minister in
charge of the Public Service, Mr William ole Ntimama, another member of the
Orange group.
Former ministers and their assistants allied to the Orange movement will
meet this morning to discuss the development, it was believed.
It is likely they will ask President Kibaki to call a General
Election.
Another Orange former minister, Mrs Linah Kilimo (Immigration) said it
was the President's prerogative to hire and fire members of his
Cabinet.
"Of course it is okay; the Constitution allows him to hire and fire,"
she said on the telephone.
However the sacking contradicted the President's call for reconciliation
on Tuesday.
The Head of State, while conceding defeat on the referendum vote, called
for a healing process.
The Marakwet East MP said the reconciliation should have started with
Banana and Orange ministers.
She said she learnt of the sackings from others.
Mr Musikari Kombo (Local Government) said: "Like a captain, when your
team is not playing together, and the passes are not flowing, you call for
time out. That is what the President has done because the divisions in
Cabinet were so deep."
It was the President's prerogative to constitute or send the Cabinet
home.
Mr Joseph Munyao described the President's move as the best thing ever
for the country which will allow him to set up a fresh team.
"He had done the right thing because you cannot continue with a divided
house. He will now form a team that will stabilise his leadership," he
said.
Mr John Koech said the President had been antagonised too much by some
members of his Cabinet and he was bound to take the action.
"It was something that was bound to come especially after the
referendum," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Yes Ex-Ministers Blame Campaigns
Headline: Kenya: Yes Ex-Ministers Blame Campaigns
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Former Cabinet ministers in the losing Banana Yes-vote group yesterday
revealed why they think voters rejected the proposed constitution in
Monday's historic referendum.
First they admitted their campaign was poorly organised and had started
late.
They also accused their Orange rivals of "whipping up tribal emotions,"
and they claimed some of the provisions in the Draft had been "distorted."
And they also said they thought the public perceptions of the
reputations of some of the ex-ministers in the Banana camp had cost them
votes.
The reasons emerged during separate conversations between the Daily
Nation and the former ministers Musikari Kombo, Amos Kimunya, Kipruto Kirwa,
Charity Ngilu and Newton Kulundu.
Their frank comments came as President Kibaki held a meeting with
Vice-President Moody Awori at State House, Nairobi; the first since losing
the the referendum vote.
The President had not called a Cabinet meeting since the former
ministers were given a break to concentrate on the referendum campaigns five
months ago.
But unlike their Orange rivals who met on Tuesday and again yesterday,
the core team of Banana leaders has not met to discuss the outcome of the
poll or even set a strategy for the future.
And at the Yes secretariat yesterday, agents were protesting they had
not been paid their allowances.
Banana team leaders were divided over whether to accept the hand of
reconciliation offered by the Orange team immediately after their victory
was announced.
The Orange team - those against the proposed Constitution - polled
3,548,477 which was 57 per cent of the vote, against those supporting the
Draft who had 43 per cent with 2,532,918 votes.
Leading lights of the Orange team include former Cabinet ministers Raila
Odinga (Roads), Kalonzo Musyoka (Environment), Najib Balala (Culture and
Heritage) and Anyang' Nyong'o (Planning), plus Official Opposition leader
Uhuru Kenyatta and Eldoret North MP William Ruto.
They were joined in their plea for unity by ex-assistant minister Mutua
Katuku and MPs Kalembe Ndile and Bonny Khalwale who said the country needed
a new start.
Mr Kombo, Mr Kirwa and Dr Kulundu said the Yes team lost because the
Orange team had a better campaign machinery.
The No-vote proponents endeared themselves to the public with
misinformation, they claimed.
Wherever, the Orange team went, the two added, they picked issues that
were dear to the local communities and misinformed them.
Mr Kombo said: "I think the Orange team had a good campaign machinery
which politicised everything."
The Ford Kenya leader said people did not vote for the Constitution but
for politics. He cited the chapter on land - taken straight from the
original Bomas Draft without amendment - and said Orange leaders had used it
to play up emotions.
Asked what he thought about negotiations with the Orange team, he said
he had been a proponent of negotiations from time immemorial.
However, Mr Kombo and Mr Kirwa said the Government had a country to run
and election pledges to fulfil.
Mr Kirwa argued the Banana team lost because the Orange team frightened
people, especially in the Rift Valley.

Original_Search_Title: Mwai Kibaki Sacks Cabinet
Headline: Uganda: Mwai Kibaki Sacks Cabinet
Body_Text:
Nairobi — KENYAN President Mwai Kibaki has dissolved his Cabinet a day after
losing a referendum on a new Constitution in a rebellion championed by seven
of his ministers.
The president said in a terse statement last evening, "Following the
results of the referendum, it has become necessary for me, as the President
of the Republic, to re-organise my government to make it more cohesive and
better able to serve the people of Kenya."
He added, "Accordingly, in accordance with the powers conferred upon me
under the Constitution of Kenya, I have directed that the offices of all
ministers and all assistant ministers become vacant. Consequently, the
occupants of the said offices cease to hold their respective offices with
immediate effect."
Kibaki promised that a new government would be in place within two
weeks.
The president's move was interpreted as aimed at giving him a chance to
form a new team, which he will work with for the remaining two years.
The ministers who championed the anti-constitution rebellion were Raila
Odinga (Roads), Kalonzo Musyoka (Environment), Prof Anyang Nyong'o
(Planning), Ochillo Ayacko (Sports), William ole Ntimama (Public Service),
Najib Balala (National Heritage) and Mrs Jebii Kilimo (Immigration).
During the campaign in the lead up to the Monday poll in which 3.5
million voted against with 2.5 in support the country went through a highly
polarised campaign putting the seven ministers in the "No" camp against the
rest of the 30-member cabinet on the "Yes" side.
In the campaigns, ministers allied to the Banana-Yes group repeatedly
urged Kibaki to sack the rebel ministers.
Kibaki did not convene a Cabinet meeting after the referendum.
The Cabinet last met in June before a split emerged over the
Constitution, touching off the highly polarised campaigns that culminated in
Monday's referendum.
Yesterday, Mr Balala, who had been among the seven ministers campaigning
against the proposed constitution, said it would not be wise for the
President to convene a Cabinet meeting immediately after the poll.
He suggested that Mr Kibaki first meet with the Orange ministers to
discuss the future and how the President would handle some of his own Yes
team.

Original_Search_Title: Sacked Orange Ministers to Consult Over the Move, Says Raila
Headline: Kenya: Sacked Orange Ministers to Consult Over the Move, Says Raila
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Lang'ata MP Raila Odinga yesterday said sacked ministers in the Orange
Movement would discuss the dissolution of the Cabinet by President Kibaki
before issuing a comprehensive statement.
Raila, who was until yesterday 7pm the Minister for Roads, described
their sackings as 'heavy thing".
"I don't have my own opinion now. We have to consult over the matter,"
he said on telephone.
Raila laughed for about a minute when asked to comment on the
matter.
Ford Kenya chairman Musikari Kombo yesterday received the news with
humility.
Kombo, the former Local Government minister, said ministers hold Cabinet
positions at the pleasure of the President in trust of the people of
Kenya.
"If you are the captain of a team and the team is not doing well, you
call for a time-out. That is what the President has done. He is a good
captain and he needs to re-organise," said Kombo.
Lurambi MP Newton Kulundu noted that it is the prerogative of the
President to dissolve the Cabinet.
The former Labour minister stated that the Cabinet was not functioning
as a unit.
"Collective responsibility had been thrown out of the window. He wants
to bring back the sense of collective responsibility, which was lacking,"
said Kulundu.
Prof Anyang' Nyong'o said he learnt of the sack through the media and
quickly added that he was tarmacking.
"I was elected by Kisumu Rural people and tomorrow I will move out of my
office and head to my constituency office," he said.
Nyong'o said the 'rebel' ministers who are in the Orange Democratic
Movement would issue a joint communiquÃ.
"We will issue a statement jointly. I do not want to comment as an
individual. Let me reflect on it before I can issue a comprehensive
statement" he said.
Kanu vice chairman Chris Okemo said the referendum results sent a very
strong message to the Government.
"The Government has no legacy after being defeated. It lacks moral
authority. Kibaki should have gone beyond merely dissolving Cabinet and
called General elections to seek fresh mandate," said Okemo.
Wilfred Machage said he was taken by surprise by the President's
move. Machage who was in Office of the Presdent docket, said it was the best
option for the President.
He said the move was necessary to discipline the divided Cabinet.
Bomachoge MP Joel Onyancha said changes in the Cabinet were long
overdue. Kibaki has been patient for too long and should dispense with
ministers who openly defied him in the face.
Onyancha said it would be bad if it turned out the sackings were due to
the Banana team's loss in the referendum
"Kibaki should crack a whip on non performing ministers and those who
tainted the image of his Government."
Nobel laureate Prof Wangari Maathai speaking on local TV show said the
move would help reconcile the warring factions.

Original_Search_Title: How Imams, NGOs Floored Banana
Headline: Kenya: How Imams, NGOs Floored Banana
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange victory in Isiolo, Marsabit and Moyale districts has been
attributed to the role played by Imams and local lobby groups.
The Muslim leaders took the Kadhis Court as their anti-draft bait, while
the community based organisations focused on the land issue.
In Isiolo North, residents put aside their political differences and
voted as a block against the Proposed New Constitution. The Orange team got
6,801 against Banana's 4,938 votes out of 31,028 registered voters.
But most of the Yes ballots were cast in polling stations dominated by
Meru traders.
In Isiolo South, out of 11,609 registered voters, there were 3,212 nays
to 2,127 ayes.
The Banana camp, led by Assistant minister for Public Health, Dr
Mohammed Kuti, with all the State largesse at their disposal, could not lure
majority of the Muslims and the pastoralists to their side.
Sheikh Harun Rashid, the Imam of Rahma Mosque and the secretary of the
Isiolo branch of Council of Imams and Preachers of Kenya, thanked the
faithful for heeding their advice.
The pastoralists from the Boran, Somali, Turkana and Samburu voted for
the Orange, fearing they would loose their land under the proposed National
Land Commission.
Waso Trustland and Mandate the Youth were among the CBOs that campaigned
for the No vote.
"The awareness level among Kenyans including the pastoralists is very
high. They cannot be cheated or bought when it comes to making decisions. We
must thank the NGOs for this," said Hassan Shano, the co-ordinator of
Waso.
In Marsabit and Moyale, even the clan rivalry between Gabra and Boran
following July 12 Turbi massacre could not deny Orange victory.
The Boran, Gabra and Rendille clans voted against the adoption of the
document even though some politicians openly took advantage of the situation
to divide them.
The co-ordinator of Friends of Nomads International, Yussuf Wako, said
his organization, together with local CBOs, camped in Saku, Laisamis and
Sololo in Moyale to
counter the Banana politicians.
"The Boran pastoralists had earlier said they won't vote the same way
with their Gabra rivals because of recent clashes, but when we told them
about the contentious land issue, they changed their mind," said Wako.
But the Yes team faired well in Saku and Moyale where it narrowly lost
by a margin of 1,170 and 679 respectively.
Non of the MPs on the Banana side could be reached for a comment, but
the region's campaign co-ordinator, Shadrack Jirma, conceded defeat, saying
the minds of both the Muslims and the pastoralists had been 'poisoned' by
the No side.
"We are not entirely surprised by the outcome. The pastoralists take it
very seriously when they are told they would lose control of their land. It
was also difficult to assure Muslims that all would be well," he said.
Jirma also blamed poor campaign plans by his colleagues, saying they
joined Banana leaders in other parts of the country and forgot to drum up
support to the draft in their constituencies.

Original_Search_Title: You Have Done Me Proud, Ntimama Tells Maasai
Headline: Kenya: You Have Done Me Proud, Ntimama Tells Maasai
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Former Cabinet minister William ole Ntimama has thanked his Maasai
community for overwhelmingly rejecting the Wako Draft.
Ntimama said: "My kinsmen have done me proud, I love them all and as a
true leader, I will never betray the quest by my community for justice in
the distribution of the national cake."
Speaking separately on telephone, both the Narok North MP and his
Kajiado Central counterpart, Major (rtd) Joseph Nkaissery, told proponents
of the Banana campaigns to prepare themselves for an imminent wrath of the
community come next elections.
Ntimama added that the raging Orange Democratic Movement's wave in
Maasailand was unstoppable, saying "those who betrayed us by going against
the wishes of the community at the referendum will never see Parliament come
2007 General Election".
"That they failed to deliver substantial votes to their Yes side tells a
lot about their political fates," Ntimama said.
And even as the duo spoke, a group of local religious leaders told The
Standard that plans were underway to convene a special feast-cum-prayer
ceremony to thank the entire Maa-speaking community for heeding their advice
and rejecting the Proposed New Constitution.
Ntimama further lashed out at a section of local politicians for
unsuccessfully trying to divide the Maasai votes.
Said the minister: "As I told you earlier, once you fail to read the
people's mood on the ground at the expense of your selfish drives, you
simply alienate yourself into political periphery."

Original_Search_Title: Minister's Plea for Meeting Spurned
Headline: Kenya: Minister's Plea for Meeting Spurned
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange movement will reject any attempt by the Government to divide
them, Kanu Secretary General William Ruto said yesterday.
Ruto and LDP Secretary General Joseph Kamotho said the Orange movement
leaders would be wary of Government ministers from the Banana camp who would
want to have meetings with them.
"If they want to meet us, then they must write officially to the Orange
secretariat and not to meet Orange leaders selectively," warned Ruto.
The leaders were speaking to The Standard over the move by Ford-Kenya
chairman Musikari Kombo to negotiate after the Orange win on the
referendum.
Kombo was reported to have invited Roads Minister Raila Odinga, Kanu
chairman Uhuru Kenyatta and Kanu secretary general William Ruto for a
meeting in Mombasa to discuss the way forward and reconciliation after the
Banana defeat.
The meeting to reconcile leaders in the Orange and Banana camps is set
to be held on November 27 at the Coast. Kombo invited the leaders in his
capacity as Ford-Kenya chairman.
The Orange team won the referendum with 3,548, 477 votes against the
Banana team which managed to get 2,532,918 votes. Yesterday, Ruto said that
they were not ready to meet ministers who are out to divide the Orange
camp.
Kamotho said that any meeting with the Orange movement leaders has to be
structured.
"We meet them for what. Let them tell us why they want to meet us
first," added Kamotho.
A Ford-Kenya official, Yvonne Khamati confirmed plans to hold the
meeting.

Original_Search_Title: Divided Central Province Leaders Lick Their Wounds
Headline: Kenya: Divided Central Province Leaders Lick Their Wounds
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Even before the Electoral Commission of Kenya's ink dried on the poll
returns, the draft constitution once touted as "the best in the world" is
now in the dustbin of history.
We can now reveal how Central Province politicians were divided among
themselves and against other politicians in the Banana team from outside the
province.
Amid the gnashing of teeth and soul-searching, Central Province has
carved itself the position of a political island.
Before the voting, Central Province politicians hyped that residents
were not simply voting for the Government-backed Wako draft.
The Transport minister, Dr Chris Murungaru, said the future was not
worth talking about in the event of a Banana loss.
And even as president Kibaki conceded that Yes had lost, the Banana
leaders were licking their wounds and contemplating the future.
Before the referendum, Cabinet ministers and politicians jostled for
access to State House. It was a battle so intense that a new group, the
Central Kenya Parliamentary Association, was formed under the chairmanship
of the Ndaragwa MP, Muchiri Gachara.
The MPs were unhappy that they were being treated like junior partners
by Cabinet ministers who made sure they never got the President's ear.
But as the referendum "tsunami" gained momentum, another group of Yes
leaders from other parts of the country formed the National Reform
Initiative. Notable leaders included Kalembe Ndile and Katoo ole Metito. As
the campaigns gathered momentum, assistant ministers Kivutha Kibwana and
Mwangi Kiunjuri were co-opted as members.
Shortly after the Orange team kicked off its campaign, there was
confusion over who was in charge of the Banana team.
An attempt by Democratic Party founder members to bring together all
Central Kenya leaders to a meeting failed. After word leaked to the Press,
the meeting, said to have been organised by Rose Waruhiu, was called
off. Waruhiu is an East African Legislative Assembly member.
Murungaru, who tried to revive DP through former officials, was engaged
in open hostility with several leaders, among them Mathira MP Nderitu
Gachagua and Mukurweini MP Mutahi Kagwe.
The rivalry, coupled with misunderstanding over how the Banana campaign
team would be financed, sparked a controversy which remains unresolved.
"We never got the money. We know that each constituency was supposed to
get Sh500,000. I am sure the money was given out, but it was not used," says
Dr Karuri Ndeng'era, the Nyeri mayor. The Nyeri Town MP, Mr Peter Mureithi,
says he was not aware of such money and accused Karuri of fighting him.
Simmering hostilities between the councillors spilled over during the
campaigns. At the grassroots, wananchi were not mobilised effectively in
most constituencies as councillors claimed they were broke.
Promises of means of transport by Mr John Michuki, the Internal Security
minister, and a Narc activist, Mary Wambui, who said the elderly and the
sick would be assisted to reach the polling stations, were not kept. On the
referendum day, scores of old women and men waited for hours to be taken to
their respective polling stations in vain.
In some parts of Nyeri District, younsgsters expected the promised
transport to be sweetened with handouts, but were disappointed after hours
of waiting.
Except in Kiambu District, residents of various Central Province
districts voted predictably like in previous general elections.
Nyeri, Kirinyaga, Nyandarua and Maragwa all voted as a bloc, with an
average turnout of 60 per cent.
In the greater Kiambu region, all the constituencies which in the past
have flirted with "foreign political ideologies" this time joined others in
voting for the Banana.
The "tsunami" in Central saw Uhuru Kenyatta, the 2002 Kanu presidential
candidate who became the Opposition leader, swept aside locally.
The same treatment was meted on controversial veterans Joseph Kamotho,
the Liberal Democratic secretary-general, in Mathioya.
Kabete, where local MP Paul Muite has since 1992 defied the mob by
allying himself with Ford Kenya and Safina, this time respected the
unwritten laws of the land.
But even then, Kiambu and Thika districts registered higher Orange votes
than others, perhaps because of their proximity to Nairobi and their
cosmopolitan nature.
Even as Central Province residents debate whether President Kibaki will
withstand political scheming and open rebellion, Uhuru still faces the acid
test. Although many sceptics believe that he has blown his presidential
hopes, his supporters see him a hero who will replace the old guard led by
Njenga Karume.
Central Provincial Orange co-ordinator Geoffrey Gachagua says Uhuru had
emerged as statesman and a hero who reached out and worked with other
communities.
To his admirers, Uhuru is the man who refused to be led through the
tribal path and risked his political ambitions to forge an alliance with his
perceived arch-rivals. His men see him as the political Moses who will save
his community from a self-destructive path. But some will be watching to see
whether Kenyatta will be accommodated in the Orange Democratic
Movement.

Original_Search_Title: Koigi in Mourning As Defeat Sinks in
Headline: Kenya: Koigi in Mourning As Defeat Sinks in
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Subukia MP Koigi Wamwere has declared a 30-day mourning period after a
humiliating defeat at the referendum.
Koigi yesterday described the loss as a slap in the face of the
Government that had shown willingness to give Kenyans a new
constitution.
Addressing the Press at his Engachura home, the MP said the biggest
loser in the poll were the poor Kenyans whom the Attorney-General Amos Wako
had put into consideration when drafting the document.
"Wako Draft addressed the plight of the landless and how national
resources would be shared," said Koigi, who said his mourning period begun
yesterday.
He regretted that the quest for a new law had ended prematurely, and
expressed doubts whether the Government would provide Kenyans another
chance.
Koigi said renegotiations on a new draft with the Orange team leaders
because they were not genuine, and dismissed the olive branch extended to
the Yes camp as a mockery.
"They are not genuine when they call for unity as their main aim is to
get power either through a new constitution or otherwise. I don't think the
President would accept the truce," said Koigi.
The legislator blamed the loss on low turnout and failure to convince
Kenyans that the Wako Draft was not meant to serve the interest of a certain
ethnic community.
Elsewhere, the Government has been asked to immediately institute
mechanisms for a new draft acceptable to all Kenyans.
Nyeri Town MP, PeterGichohi Mureithi, yesterday admitted that senior
politicians from Mt Kenya region had erred in their pursuit for a new
constitution.
In a candid interview with The Standard, the MP said the leaders had
failed to reach out and initiate dialogue with colleagues from other parts
of the country.
Mureithi faulted the approach taken by the Banana campaigners.
"We took some things and regions for granted. That is why people have
rejected a constitution we thought was good for everybody," he said.
The MP added: "It is true that the draft had some contentious issues
which were overlooked by the Banana said and angered the Orange team. We
should now go back to the negotiating table."
Mureithi said it is only after all leaders learn to talk and listen to
divergent views that Kenyans will enjoy a unifying constitution.
He said the voters should not be blamed for not turning out, saying the
Banana leadership had failed to identify with the people.
The MP spoke as most residents in the region pointed accusing fingers at
their leaders for misleading them into voting for a document which was
unpopular.
Some youths complained the Government had for a long time ignored their
plight, always targeting hawkers and slum dwellers in urban centres.
"That is why Banana lost such places as in Nairobi and other towns. The
small people are angry with the Government and they made their statement
during the referendum," said Peter Mwangi, a driver.
And Central provincial Kanu co-ordinator, Geoffrey Gachagua, said Banana
leaders and the vernercular radio stations have alienated the
community.
Gachagua said even after failing to reach out to other communities to
revolve the constitution impasse, some 'arrogant' Mt Kenya leaders misled
the people.
"They failed to realise that other people were unhappy with the
constitution. This mis-perception was further boosted by vernacular radio
stations," Gachagua said.
He added that it was not too late to rectify the mistakes, adding that
the Leader of Official Opposition, Uhuru Kenyatta, was fit to reconcile the
people.
And a Kericho Kanu official, Paul Maina, also advised the people of
Central Province to rally behind Uhuru and Mathioya MP, Joseph Kamotho.

Original_Search_Title: Voters Reject Proposed New Constitution
Headline: Kenya: Voters Reject Proposed New Constitution
Body_Text:
Kenya's search for a new constitution will continue, after voters
rejected a proposed draft at a national referendum held on Monday, November
21.
The 'No', or Orange, side won with 3 548 477 (57 per cent) of the votes
cast, against the 'Yes', or Banana, side's 2 532 918 (43 per cent).
"We declare that the Proposed New Constitution was not ratified by the
voters as required by the law," the Electoral Commission of Kenya (ECK)
chairman Samuel Kivuitu announced on Tuesday, November 22 in Nairobi.
At the time, poll results from five constituencies had not been
declared.  But Kivuitu said they would not significantly affect the final
tally.
President Mwai Kibaki who led the 'Yes' campaign conceded defeat in a
broadcast to the nation shortly before the official announcement of the
result.
"This referendum, whose objective was to approve or reject a new
constitution, has come out with a clear verdict that the Proposed
Constitution is not acceptable to the majority of Kenyans," he said.
The leaders of the Orange campaign said the people of Kenya had
decisively spoken, and called on the 'Yes' camp to join them "in
expeditiously charting the way forward for a new constitutional
dispensation."
"The Orange leadership has consistently fought for the rights and
welfare of Kenyans to be respected in a new constitution," Official
Opposition leader Uhuru Kenyatta said.
The Orange campaign was spearheaded by the Official Opposition party,
Kenya African National Union (KANU), and the Liberal Democratic Party, a
renegade partner in the ruling National Rainbow Coalition (NARC).
Voting was peaceful countrywide, in contrast to the acrimonious and
sometimes violent campaigns. Nine people died and many others were injured
in clashes between supporters and opponents of the Proposed New Constitution
ahead of the referendum.
While supporters said the draft embodied the wishes of Kenyans,
opponents claimed it retained a strong presidency, yet Kenyans wanted
executive powers to be vested in a new office of prime minister.
The Constitution of Kenya Review Commission (CKRC), appointed in 2000,
gathered the views of Kenyans on the content of a new constitution.
The CKRC reported that citizens wanted, among other things, a reduction
in presidential power, apparently in reaction to misrule under former
presidents Daniel arap Moi and Jomo Kenyatta.
Delegates debated the findings at a National Constitutional Conference
between 2003 and 2004, but could not reach a consensus on a number of
contentious issues. The talks collapsed.
The Proposed New Constitution that was put to a referendum was a revised
version of a controversial draft that emerged from the National
Constitutional Conference.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki Now Sacks All Ministers
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki Now Sacks All Ministers
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki has dissolved his cabinet, only a day after the
Government side lost in the referendum on the proposed Constitution.
In a television address to the nation at 7pm Kenya time, the head of
State said he will name a new cabinet within two weeks.
With all ministers sacked, only Vice President Moody Awori and Attorney
General Amos Wako remain in office.
Mr Awori also loses his docket as Minister for Home Affairs.
Here is the President's full statement:
"Following the results of the referendum, it has become necessary for
me, as the President of the Republic, to reorganise my Government to make it
more cohesive and better able to serve the people of Kenya.
"Accordingly, in accordance with the powers conferred upon me under the
Constitution of Kenya, I have directed that the offices of all ministers and
all assistant ministers become vacant. Consequently, the occupants of the
said offices cease to hold their respective offices with immediate
effect.
"A reconstituted Government will be in place within two weeks."
It took the President less than a minute to read the statement.
President Kibaki and key members of his cabinet backed the Yes (Banana)
side which was pushing for the adoption of the proposed Constitution in
Monday's referendum.
However, they lost the vote managing 2.5 million to the Orange (No)
side's 3.5 million. The constitution review debate brought a major rift in
President Kibaki's Cabinet, with seven ministers opting to lead the campaign
against the constitution.
The ministers who led the Orange campaign were Raila Odinga (Roads),
Kalonzo Musyoka (Environment), Prof Anyang Nyong'o (Planning), Ochillo
Ayacko (Sports), William ole Ntimama (Public Service), Najib Balala
(National Heritage) and Mrs Jebii Kilimo (Immigration).
The referendum outcome and President's sacking of all ministers are
expected to have a major bearing in the country 2007 General Elections.

Original_Search_Title: Orange Team Celebrates Victory
Headline: Kenya: Orange Team Celebrates Victory
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Thousands of Orange supporters celebrated their victory across the country after the Monday referendum.
In Nairobi, scores of chanting and singing youths poured into the
streets. By 7am, many had gathered outside KICC as they waited for the
Electoral Commission of Kenya to announce the final results. The
outcome was confirmed at 3.30pm.
But business was back to normal in most city streets, especially in
River Road, despite the day being a public holiday. Most premises had
remained closed since Monday as the two days had been declared public
holidays.
And a GSU officer's tent remained pitched at Uhuru Park in readiness for any security threat after Monday's referendum.
However, in the Central Business District, there was no much
activity and the streets were almost empty. Hawkers who had taken
advantage of the calm were not spared as City Council askaris patrolled
the streets arresting them.
In Western and Nyanza provinces, a carnival mood engulfed several towns as Orange supporters celebrated.
Carrying twigs and banners, they criss-crossed urban centres singing songs in praise of the leading lights in the No camp.
But they were united in calling for a speedy review of the existing
laws to reduce the powers of the president among other things.
In Kakamega District, the Orange team carried the day in Lurambi, Malava, Shinyalu and Ikolomani constituencies.
Former vice-president Musalia Mudavadi, who led Orange campaigns in
Western Province, thanked voters for heeding calls to reject the
proposed laws.
He said the victory of the No team had given the leaders an
opportunity to re-engineer the review process so that the country can
have a new constitution acceptable to all Kenyans.
"The people of Western Province voted for the country and have given
us an impetus to have the review process steered for the benefit of
Kenyans and not a few individuals," said Mr Mudavadi.
But Cabinet minister Newton Kulundu, who spoke on telephone from
Navakholo, said that the Banana side had lost in Lurambi because voters
were bribed.
In Kakamega, Orange supporters led by Ford Kenya councillor Joe
Serenge of Bukhulunya ward kicked off their celebrations at the
historic Muliro Gardens and held a peaceful demonstration on Sudi Road
and Kenyatta Street.
They later converged at Shirere grounds in Amalemba estate for a
victory party before dispersing. Mr Serenge, the only civic leader
supporting the Orange campaigns in the area, said the victory was a
reflection of the political mood in the province.
"Those who supported the proposed Constitution had other interests
which did not go down well with the voters in Kakamega," he said.
In Lurambi, the Orange side scored 22,333 votes against 14,613 for the Banana team.
The Orange team squeezed narrow wins against the Banana side in
Malava and Shinyalu constituency. In Malava, the Orange camp scooped
16,687 votes against Banana's 16,194 while in Shinyalu they had 10,239
votes against their opponents' 10,168.
In Siaya District, where the Orange won in all the three
constituencies of Ugenya, Alego/Usonga and Gem, there were celebrations
in Siaya Town, Yala, Ugunja, Aboke, Ukwala and Luhano trading centres.
The Orange supporters began celebrating on Monday night as provisional
results started being announced.
And in Nyamira Town, thousands of Orange supporters from the
district poured into bars to celebrate the No win. They urged the
Orange luminaries to sustain the tempo and unity of purpose they had
shown during referendum campaigns until the next General Election.
Former Mugirango North MP Joseph Kiangoi said: "Let us use this win to unite across the country."

Original_Search_Title: Heed the People's Wishes
Headline: Kenya: Heed the People's Wishes
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Three months of hard and aggressive referendum campaigns finally
ended on Monday. Kenyans cast their ballot to put the constitutional
debate to a peaceful end. And they made their verdict loud and clear
and, most importantly, did so calmly and with dignity.
In all this, it is the voters - the ordinary Kenyans - who deserve
commendation. They turned out in large numbers to express their views
and ensure that the national task was discharged successfully.
Now that the Electoral Commission of Kenya has declared Orange the
victor (with 3.5 million votes against Banana's 2.5 million) and Banana
has conceded defeat, it is time for reconciliation and nation-building.
Untold energy, time and other resources were poured into the
campaigns. Emotions and passions ran high. There were acrimonious
exchanges. Tempers flared. Violence left eight people dead and many
injured.
But all that is now behind us. What is critical is how to proceed in
the search for a new governance charter. It is a moment at which to
look back and reflect on the long path to the referendum and to draw
some valuable lessons.
One, the referendum has shown that Kenya's democracy has come of
age. Much more enlightened, the voters are able to make independent
decisions irrespective of what their leaders say or think.
Despite cases of provocation, cheating and intimidation, the people
held their peace and were decisive in their actions and choices.
The second lesson is that the future of Kenya's politics lies on
broad-based coalitions. No single group ethnic or ideological - can go
it alone when it comes to national leadership.
Similarly, leaders must be sensitive to people's feelings and
respect their views. The era when those in authority forced their
opinions on the people is long gone.
Any decision that touches on the people's lives must be discussed
with the people, not pushed down their throats. Brute force, political
demagoguery and abuse of financial might will no longer guarantee
results.
But, after the polls, the big question remains: Where do we go from here? How do Kenyans come by a new constitution?
For the past 15 years, they have fought hard to enact one because
the current one is terribly wanting. After Monday, the desire may prove
stronger and more urgent than ever before, especially given the elite's
extremely self-interested sabotage of the people's earlier initiative.
It seems difficult to imagine that the people will want to continue
with the Lancaster House constitution - one imposed on them by the
outgoing colonial regime in its own exploitative interests and then
systematically ruined by the post-independence governments in their own
tyrannical interests.
That is why the Government must yet again make the first move -
seizing the invitation offered by the the victorious Orange faction -
to the pressing question: What Is To Be Done? How do we deliver
ourselves from the extreme inadequacies of the present Constitution?
After the resounding defeat of the one it proposed in its stead, the
Government is duty-bound to deliver another acceptable to Wanjiku. That
was one of the reasons that Kenyans voted for Narc in the 2002 General
Election.
Fortunately, we are not starting from scratch. There are various
documents, including the Bomas. Again, we have learnt from the past
experience that to rely solely on politicians to shepherd the process
is fraught with pitfalls.
Serious thought must be given to how agreement can be struck on the
contentious sections of the Bomas Draft or what new approach,
negotiated and inclusive, can bring this country to its long cherished
dream of a modern and equitable constitutional order.
The country needs a constitution that is all-inclusive, one that
serves and protects everybody's interests, one that unites the people
and, at the same time, celebrates diversity while fostering economic
prosperity. The referendum was not a competition just between Orange
and Banana. It was a call to the Government to heed the people's voice
on how they want to be governed.

Original_Search_Title: Minister: Why Banana Lost Vote
Headline: Kenya: Minister: Why Banana Lost Vote
Body_Text:
Nairobi — MPs allied to the Banana camp yesterday blamed their defeat in the referendum vote on low voter turnout.
Dagoretti MP Beth Mugo, whose constituency is the only one that gave
the Yes team the highest number of votes in Nairobi, blamed the loss to
the politics behind the campaigns and "the carnival mood among the
Orange supporters that wooed young voters."
Speaking to the Nation yesterday, Mrs Mugo said: "I am happy at
least I was able to deliver the vote for Nairobi. However, I am unhappy
and sad that all the others lost and more importantly, we lost the
opportunity to give Kenyans a new constitution that we had promised."
Mrs Mugo, who is also Education assistant minister, said most voters were misled by politicians.
She said: "This is a big loss to us and in my view, I think the
voters were confused by the politics that accompanied the referendum
and the too many deliberate lies they were told. I would like to
pinpoint what especially was happening in Nairobi and say that many did
not come out to vote because in my constituency, there are 89,355
registered voters and 22,945 voted Yes while 14,895 voted No, making a
total of 36,000, which is less than half of the registered number.
"The Yes team did not have the carnival mood of the Orange team and
this time, it is a lot of young voters who cast the ballots."
The MP continued: "For me the loss by the Kenyan women will take us
back to more than 20 years and I am terrified that the gains made on
women, youth and disadvantaged groups will never be regained."
Mrs Mugo appealed to MPs to end the campaign bickering and continue delivering services to the country.
"I thank the people of Dagoretti and more so, I would like other
politicians to realise that there is no vacuum. The President is in
charge and there is a Government in place and we all should get down to
business and serve the people of Kenya," said Mrs Mugo in a telephone
interview.
Elsewhere, Imenti Central MP Kirugi M'Mukindia accused a section of
Kenyans of betraying the process to enact a new constitutional
dispensation for the country by failing to vote.
He spoke after witnessing the counting of votes for his constituency
at the St Immaculate Gatimbi Catholic Church in which the Banana team
emerged victorious after polling 42,454 votes against the Orange's
1,527.
The Banana team supporter said time had come for the Government to
embark on a continuous civic education process to sensitise Kenyans on
the need to take part in the electoral processes.
Mr M'Mukindia regretted that many people in the greater Meru region
failed to vote, a fact he said contributed significantly to the losses
suffered by the Yes camp in the referendum.
He gave the example of his constituency and Constitutional Affairs
minister Kiraitu Murungi's Imenti Central, where more than 20,000
people in each of the constituencies failed to vote.
Imenti South and Central have 81,712 and 68,229 registered voters respectively.
Juja MP William Kabogo thanked his supporters for giving the Yes
team a lead of 40,055 while the No team obtained 10,754 in a
constituency that has 111,819 registered voters.

Original_Search_Title: Orange Victory Vow
Headline: Kenya: Orange Victory Vow
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The victorious Orange team yesterday invited their Banana group
rivals to join them in discussions about how to restart talks aimed at
creating a new Constitution.
And they insisted: "A policy of national inclusion is the only one
that can hold us together... Please come and join the rest of the
nation in rebuilding a new Kenya."
However, President Kibaki, in his televised speech conceding defeat, made no reference to the possibility of renewed talks.
Instead he commented that the country already had the old
Constitution in place to serve its needs, following the rejection of
the Wako Draft.
The Orange No-vote won the referendum on whether or not to adopt the
proposed Constitution based on the Wako Draft with a total of 3,548477
votes (57 per cent) against the Banana Yes-vote group's 2,532918 votes
(43 per cent).
The Orange group declared the outcome of the country's first
referendum suggested they had a mandate to initiate moves for a fresh
Constitution.
"Now that the people of Kenya have decisively spoken, and in the
spirit of genuine reconciliation, we invite our colleagues in the
Banana camp to join us in expeditiously charting the way forward for a
new Constitutional dispensation for our country," the leaders said in a
statement read by Official Opposition Leader Uhuru Kenyatta.
They added: "Today we are united as a nation in a renewed quest for
a better Kenya. The people have comprehensively and with large
majorities voted to strengthen democracy, limit Presidential power and
devolve power to local communities."
They said Kenyans had supported and shown confidence in the Orange
team to lead the reform of the country's basic law. "We will not let
you down," their statement said.
It was read out amid cheers and ululations from their supporters at
Kenyatta International Conference Centre (KICC) where the results were
being read by officials from the Electoral Commission of Kenya (ECK).
The Orange supporters wore orange T-shirts and black caps bearing the word 'No'.
Others had T-shirts emblazoned Agwambo Disciple; a reference to the
nickname of Energy minister Raila Odinga, one of the key Orange
campaign leaders.
The supporters forced open the main gate to the KICC to gain
entrance into the compound and chanted slogans and waved placards while
awaiting the results. Some cooled off by paddling in the compound pool.
The Orange leaders offered talks as their supporters celebrated
victory in Mombasa, Kericho, Bomet, Migori and other major towns in
Nairobi, Nyanza, Coast, Rift Valley and Eastern provinces.
In Nairobi itself, Orange supporters danced through the city streets
escorted by a mobile disco, while more singing and dancing went on in
city estates.
And the statement came moments after President Kibaki conceded that
the Draft Constitution had been rejected by the voters. His statement
paved the way for the official results to be declared by the ECK.
Chairman Samuel Kivuitu said the ECK was still waiting for results
from Fafi, Turkana Central, Turkana South and Juja whose returns had
been made but the person who brought them did not wait to authenticate
the tally, accounting for a total of 202,246 votes, not enough to alter
the result.
The turnout of 6,081395 - 52.3 per cent of the 11,608,889 registered
voters - was higher than the 5,815896 realised during the 2002 General
Election.
Both Orange and Banana camps said the referendum had strengthened
democracy in Kenya and congratulated people for carrying out the
exercise peacefully.
The Orange leaders announced a thanksgiving celebration at Nairobi's Uhuru Park on Saturday.
They said they were filled with the same joy and celebrations
sweeping through towns, villages and streets in Kenya. "The people have
triumphed and we salute them," they said.
They added: "Even more than joy, we stand here in awe of the great
people of this country. In awe of the power of democracy. In awe of
those who for the last two months fought tirelessly and strategised
with the most meagre of resources to ensure that we are not subjected
to the monster of legalised dictatorship."
They chided their Banana opponents in Funyula, Kitui, Kisii,
Kajiado, Rarieda and Starehe in reference to the Banana team's key
leaders Vice-President Moody Awori, and Cabinet ministers Charity
Ngilu, Simeon Nyachae, George Saitoti and Raphael Tuju, and assistant
minister Maina Kamanda, all whose constituencies voted No.
Their statement read: "We stand in awe of the wisdom of Kenyans, who
from Funyula, Kitui, Kisii, Kajiado, Rarieda to Starehe and other
constituencies defied those with the power and the wealth of the State
behind them and rejected the Wako Draft in the ballot box."
The Orange leaders said they believed Kenyans will never again allow
themselves to return to the dark days of dictatorship and lack of
accountability that are the, "root causes of our long years of misrule,
corruption and national impoverishment."
They said they had consistently fought for the welfare and rights of
Kenyans to be addressed in the Constitution. "The support of this cause
