by six out of the eight provinces demonstrates the unanimity by which
Kenyans have endorsed the national stand of the Orange leaders."
They called on voters in Central Kenya to reject any divisive
leadership which may mislead and isolate them. "We assure you, and
indeed all those who supported the Banana camp, that we want to work
with you to create the Kenya all of us aspire to. A policy of national
inclusion is the only one that can hold us together and ensure that we
meet the very basic aspirations our people have: education for our
children; jobs for the unemployed; health services for all; equitable
sharing of our land our wealth; and empowering women and the
marginalised, vulnerable and deprived groups. Please come and join the
rest of the nation in rebuilding a new Kenya."
They said: "Defying the prophets of doom who warned of chaos and
repeatedly threatened to suppress the violence they themselves were
predicting, Kenyans voted in every part of the country peacefully and
with grace, despite the efforts of certain irresponsible media to
instigate ethnic hatred with a view to instilling fear and despondency."
Later, the leaders addressed supporters at KICC during which Mr
Kenyatta, Mathioya MP Joseph Kamotho and Ntonyiri MP Maoke Maore were
praised for campaigning for the Orange vote in Central Kenya, a
predominantly Banana area.
The vote by each province was: Nairobi Yes: 161,344 No: 210, 908;
Coast Yes: 65,485 No: 264,170; North Eastern Yes: 19,228 No: 44,502;
Eastern Yes: 543,192 No: 497,715; Central Yes: 930,268 No: 63,718; Rift
Valley Yes: 382,973 No: 1,204,900; Western Yes: 238,499 No 364,561 and
Nyanza Yes :114,077 and No 822,188.
President Kibaki said the Government respected the verdict that the
proposed Constitution was not acceptable to majority of Kenyans.
In an address to the nation from State House, Nairobi, he added
there were no winners or losers in the referendum as the objective was
to determine the people's choice and that choice had been made.
He said: "The people have made a choice and as I have always said, my Government would respect the choice of the people."
He commended all voters for taking part in the referendum
peacefully, saying it was a major leap in the consolidation of
democratic governance in Kenya.
By casting their vote in a peaceful manner, Kenyans had shown their
respect for constitutional and legal processes, said the President,
adding the current Constitution would remain in force, and therefore,
there was no vacuum.
"Any future initiatives in this regard will have to be in accordance with the Constitution and the law," he said.
President Kibaki said the debate which had surrounded the referendum
made it clear that Kenyans had accorded high priority to the
development agenda.
A lot of energy and resources had been spent on the Constitution
debate and the politics associated with it. It was now time for Kenyans
to refocus their energies more intensively to development.
The Government would concentrate on delivering its commitments to
development and he urged people to continue with the way they have
lived as Kenyans, which was the envy of many.
Earlier, the chairman of the Banana-Yes campaign committee, Mr Simeon Nyachae, had written to the ECK conceding defeat.
His letter read: "I wish to formally inform you that the Yes Vote
referendum committee, having examined the results which you have
already declared for the referendum, have concluded that the results of
the remaining constituencies will not make a difference in the overall
verdict of the referendum."

Original_Search_Title: We Accept Defeat, Say Yes Ministers
Headline: Kenya: We Accept Defeat, Say Yes Ministers
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Two Cabinet ministers who spearheaded the Banana campaign at the Coast have conceded defeat.
And yesterday Tourism and Wildlife minister Morris Dzoro and Foreign
counterpart Chirau Ali Mwakwere pledged to mend fences with their
Orange colleagues.
They also congratulated the Orange camp for carrying the victory in the province.
Mr Dzoro said he would now seek to work together with National
Heritage colleague Najib Balala and Coast Parliamentary Group chairman
Joe Khamisi to solve the province's "crucial land and unemployment
problems".
Mr Mwakwere congratulated the Orange camp for their victory but
regretted that it would now take longer than expected for Kenyans to
have a new constitution.
"I congratulate Kenyans for making their decision on this matter. It
is, however, painful that we shall continue being ruled under the
current Constitution which most Kenyans never participated in making,"
he said.
Ganze MP Kahindi Kingi also conceded defeat to the Orange team and said that he accepted the results.
In Kisii, there were celebrations in the town yesterday morning as
the results from Monday's referendum indicated that the Orange had
carried the day. More than 500 people held a peaceful demonstration in
town to mark the Orange victory.
In Kuria, song and dance greeted the victory. Similar celebrations
were also witnessed in neighbouring Migori, Uriri, Rongo and Nyatike
constituencies where Orange won. Former area MP Shadrack Manga, a local
Orange coordinator, led the No supporters in celebrating their victory
in Kehancha.
An Orange supporter in Isebania, Mr Josephat Marwa, said MPs who supported the Banana side would be voted out in 2007.

Original_Search_Title: Election Team Pushes for Law On Referendum
Headline: Kenya: Election Team Pushes for Law On Referendum
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The defeat of the Wako Draft is not the end of the pursuit for a new constitution, the electoral commission said yesterday.
Commission chairman Samuel Kivuitu said Kenyans should now pursue a constitution that protects and unites all.
Said Mr Kivuitu, amidst cheers: "It cannot be said that the pursuit
for a new or better constitution has ceased. "It is imperative that we
continue to strive for a better constitution-a constitution, which
substantially addresses our aspirations and fears, a constitution,
which seeks to unite us as equal citizens."
He continued: "A constitution, which is in fact and spirit
recognises the power to govern rests on the people and no one else; a
constitution, which takes into account our diversities in cultures,
religions, localities, numbers; a constitution, which will protect the
minorities from the might of the majorities; a constitution, which will
unite us."
Mr Kivuitu said this during a press conference at Kenyatta
International Conference Centre, Nairobi, where he declared the Orange
team winners of the referendum. It was punctuated by cheers, shouts of
Orange and pumbavu (stupid) when the chairman mentioned anything to do
with the Banana team.
The Orange team-those against the proposed new Constitution-polled
3,548,477 against those supporting the new Draft who garnered 2,532,918.
The results were released without those from Fafi constituency,
which was voting yesterday. The constituency has 14,122 registered
voters.
The results also did not include those from Turkana Central (which
has 49,351 voters), Turkana South (26,884) and Juja (111,889).
Although the results from the Turkana constituencies had not been
received, those from Juja were presented by the returning officer in
the area, who "disappeared".
Mr Kivuitu said amidst laughter that they were awaiting the
returning officer to come back and certify the results before they
could include it in the final tally.
"The results will be gazetted on Friday and a certificate to the No vote will also be issued on the same day," said Mr Kivuitu.
He said people of high integrity and honesty should now be given the opportunity to guide the Constitution making process.
They should consult widely, and should not ignore any community, person, institution or opinion, he said.
He added: "And we as Kenyans should have patience-though not forever-with the process so long as it exists."
The chairman said Kenyans were lucky to have the current
Constitution, the Bomas Draft and the Kilifi Draft, which could be the
base for news laws. With this, he added, Kenyans should be able to get
a new Constitution quickly.
He said Kenya seriously needed a referendum Act to restart the
process "because it is not easy to conduct the referendum without the
Act".
The Press conference was attended by among others Orange team
leaders Raila Odinga, William ole Ntimama, Uhuru Kenyatta, Kalonzo
Musyoka, Anyang' Nyong'o, Najib Balala, David Musila, William Ruto,
Mutula Kilonzo, Chris Okemo, Joseph Kamotho, Gideon Moi, Charles Keter,
Henry Kosgey, Nick Salat and Otieno Kajwang'.
There was commotion when the Orange leaders arrived with their
supporters in tow. They filled the room and attempts by the police and
Mr Odinga to remove them were not successful.

Original_Search_Title: Why Province Rejected New Laws
Headline: Kenya: Why Province Rejected New Laws
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Orange overwhelmingly won in Rift Valley because residents feared
that they could lose their land to outsiders if a commission managed
the resource.
There were also fears that some sections of the draft were included
targeting some of their leaders over alleged past human rights abuses.
Orange scored 1,094,397 votes in the Monday referendum against 384,159 for the Banana.
Cabinet minister Linah Kilimo said the overwhelming No vote was due to vigorous campaigns and civic education.
She said clauses in the Wako Draft such as the national land
commission to manage the resource, were opposed by many in the region.
"They feared that they could lose their land if the commission
manages it. Land matters are sensitive in the area," said the
Immigration and Registration of Persons minister.
Mosop MP John Sambu said voters in the province rejected the
proposed new constitution because it gave the President "excessive"
executive powers.
Mr Sambu said although campaigns and civic education might have
played a role, voters in the region rejected the content of the draft.
Eldama Ravine's Musa Sirma said the recent eviction of about 10,000
families from the Mau Forest indicated to them that the Government
"does not respect the law on land".
He said the draft was made to victimise past leaders from the area
for alleged mistakes. "It's as if the Constitution was made targeting
the community," he said.
The Kanu MP said members of the Kalenjin community were also
protesting at "the sacking of senior government officials who served
under Moi."

Original_Search_Title: Kanu Team in Central Happy With Outcome
Headline: Kenya: Kanu Team in Central Happy With Outcome
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kanu leaders in Central Province yesterday welcomed the verdict passed by Kenyans during the referendum.
Led by the provincial coordinator Geoffrey Rigathi Gachagua, the
leaders termed the exercise a wake-up call for Kenyans to unite.
The referendum had rightly pointed out that no community can stand on its own, said Mr Gachagua in a telephone interview.
The former provincial administrator praised leader of the Official
Opposition Uhuru Kenyatta for sticking with the opponents of the
proposed constitution despite the hostility he faced in the province,
also his home.
However, Mr Gachagua asked President Kibaki to discipline some
ministers who were in the Yes camp, saying they were discrediting his
Government through arrogance and pride.
He named two ministers in the Banana camp who, he said, were to blame for their side's dismal performance at the referendum.
Mr Gachagua said Kanu supporters in the Orange team were demanding
an apology from some FM stations, which he observed preached anarchy
during the campaigns.
While calling for a healing process across the country following the
heated campaigns in which eight people died in violence, Mr Gachagua
said some communities may be isolated by others due to the way they
projected themselves.

Original_Search_Title: Vote Count Error Shocker for Orange Team Backers
Headline: Kenya: Vote Count Error Shocker for Orange Team Backers
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Shock and disbelief gripped Orange supporters in Nairobi's
Kamukunji constituency after a "technical error" initially gave their
rivals the victory during counting of referendum votes.
Returning officer Horace Onyango on Monday night stunned the
hundreds of No supporters who had gathered at YMCA Hall in Shauri Moyo
for the results with a declaration that Banana camp had won with 7,707
votes against 6,465 for the No team.
But anger immediately replaced shock as the supporters, who had all
along been in a celebratory mood, smelt a rat and rose up in protest,
dismissing the tally as a sham.
Chanting tumekataa (We have refused), the irate mob demanded fresh
tallying which-thanks to nearly two gruelling hours of manual
calculation-later yielded 17,995 votes for the No camp and 14,497 for
Yes.
It was an embarrassing moment for Mr Onyango and his juniors as the
crowd turned on them accusing them of trying to rig the vote in favour
of Yes camp whose luminary Norman Nyagah is the MP for the area.
Mr Onyango was at a loss as to what to make of the big gap in the
arithmetic, opting to blame it all on a "calculator error". His
deputies, who had done the actual tallying ending up with the
misleading figures, cowered to the back of the hall, in fear.
Unknown to the Electoral Commission of Kenya official, the Orange
group had already smelt victory after tallies of figures brought by the
camp's agents in all the 19 polling stations in the constituency
indicated Orange had an unassailable lead of about 3,000.
Journalists who had also meticulously done their own arithmetic
using returns by the presiding officers had already dispatched news
briefs on an imminent Orange victory complete with estimates.
Infact, a number of subscribers had received the provisional
Kamukunji vote results through Nation Media Group's mobile phone
breaking news update service.
When a recount was done and new results announced, the hall burst
into wild cheers and broke into the Orange campaign song Bado mapambano
(the struggle continues).

Original_Search_Title: Minister Wants Law Review Term Extended
Headline: Kenya: Minister Wants Law Review Term Extended
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Constitution of Kenya Review Commission's life should be
extended beyond January as part of a healing process after the
referendum.
It would be difficult to harmonise the proposed Constitution and the
Bomas Draft without the commission, said assistant Trade and Industry
minister Zadock Syong'oh.
The commission, appointed through an Act of Parliament, is supposed
to dissolve 30 days after the November 21 poll, in which Kenyans
rejected the proposed laws.
Speaking yesterday, the Gwassi MP said Parliament should extend the tenure as soon as it reconvenes on Tuesday.
He suggested also that a motion to amend Section 47 of the
Constitution be reintroduced to help jump-start the constitutional
review.
The section provides for amending the current laws' and subjecting
the changes to the vote. But to do this, the approval of at least 65
per cent of all MPs, excluding the Attorney-General and the Speaker,
must be obtained.
Amending Section 47 would provide a transition platform from the
current Constitution to a new one. The motion was withdrawn from
Parliament because it generated much heat by MPs on both sides of the
referendum debate.
Mr Syong'oh said: "We still have the motion. It can be reintroduced
as one of the options of giving Kenyans a new constitution by early
next year.
"The fact that Kenyans have overwhelmingly voted against the Wako
draft does not mean they don't want a new constitution," he said.
The assistant minister was among the referendum middle-ground group
of MPs during the campaigns. They unsuccessfully pushed for its
postponement to allow a reconciliation.
He said he supported Kenyans' stand, but called for urgent
"round-table talks" between the No and Yes leaders to save the review.
The MP, who spoke to the Nation by telephone, later left for Arusha
for a Common Market for Eastern and Southern Africa (Comesa) meeting.
Reacting to the Orange win, Ford Kenya chairman Musikari Kombo said
the current Constitution would remain in force as leaders work out
another way forward.
The Local Government minister said the country had not been
operating in a vacuum, and that there was a constitution to guide it.
He called on the Banana and Orange leaders to make sure Kenya
remains united. He said he would continue to fight to bring the Luyia
together for what he called political survival. But he admitted that
the unity had eluded him, although he would not give up the fight.
He said: "The Luo voted to a man. The Kikuyu voted to a man. The
Kalenjin voted as a bloc. It is only Luyia who showed division." Kanu
secretary-general William Ruto said the No team was ready to negotiate
with its Yes colleagues to deliver a new constitution.
But Mr Ruto warned that Orange would negotiate only if Banana showed what he called sobriety.
Speaking after the results were announced for his Eldoret North
constituency, where the Orange team received 52,795 votes against
Banana's 12,061, he said the Yes leaders had been intolerant.
"As leaders of the Orange team, we have been fighting for a good
constitution for Kenyans, but Banana's intolerance has scuttled the
process," he said at Uasin Gishu primary school, where the results were
announced. He said Kenyans had made a wise decision to reject the
proposed Constitution and that the era of political bribery and
inducement was over and "the Government should never underestimate
Kenyans' knowledge". Kenya Episcopal Conference chairman Cornelius
Korir called for dialogue between the two sides on the contentious
issues.
"The peaceful polling is a clear indication that issues in the Draft
involve the future of all Kenyans, whose verdict should be respected,"
Bishop Korir said.
The Catholic Eldoret bishop called for peaceful co-existence among
all ethnic groups, saying that there was no real loser or winner in the
referendum.
Eldoret Anglican Church bishop Thomas Kogo called for an independent team to thrash out the contentious issues.
He demanded reinstating the civil servants interdicted for criticising the proposed Constitution.
Former assistant minister Reuben Chesire said divisions in the
Constitution of Kenya Review Commission were to blame for the rejection
of the proposed laws.
"The officials abdicated their responsibility of sensitising Kenyans
on the entire constitution-making process and concentrating on fighting
along political lines," he said.
Eldoret South MP David Koros said the Orange victory showed Kenyans'
political maturity, and urged them to repeat the show in the 2007
General Election.

Original_Search_Title: No Team Teems With Presidential Talent
Headline: Kenya: No Team Teems With Presidential Talent
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The referendum victory for Orange luminaries marks the beginning
of everything as far as power sharing and the 2007 General Election are
concerned.
And as they gather at Nairobi's Uhuru Park on Saturday to celebrate
victory, there will only be one thing on the minds of the main movers,
Leader of Official Opposition Uhuru Kenyatta, Cabinet ministers Raila
Odinga and Kalonzo Musyoka, former vice-president Musalia Mudavadi and
Kanu secretary-general William Ruto, how to strike an agreeable power
sharing deal in their new-found Orange Democratic Movement (ODM).
Kanu chairman Kenyatta may have failed to carry the day in his
Gatundu South constituency, but he has doubtlessly built himself a
national image - a crucial asset for his political career.
Apart from winning over the hearts of many countrywide, Mr Kenyatta
still remains in focus among members of his Kikuyu community.
That he is electable within his community and the rest of the
country is not in doubt. The Orange movement could front for his
candidature against Mr Kibaki in the 2007 presidential race.
As was the case in 2002, when Narc opted for the Othaya MP with the
view of ruining Mr Kenyatta's chances, the idea would be to split the
populous Kikuyu vote. However, such a move may not be smooth sailing.
Would ODM supporters readily embrace yet another presidential candidate
from the Kikuyu community?
Mr Kenyatta's position notwithstanding, it would have been even more
disastrous had he, as leader of the Official Opposition, abandoned the
Orange crusade to join President Kibaki in the Banana camp.
Having joined Parliament barely four years ago after his nomination by President Moi, Mr Kenyatta's star has been rising fast.
Sources close to his aides indicate that he is determined to have another shot at the presidency.
But Mr Kenyatta's ambition only complicates matters for Liberal
Democratic Party's Musyoka, who has all along been seen as the party's
flag bearer. And more than ever, the Environment minister appears to
have discarded the image of a lukewarm fence-seater.
That he has managed to eclipse his perennial political nemesis,
Health minister Charity Ngilu, is a major bonus for the Mwingi North MP.
As Kilome MP Mutinda Mutiso said, members of the Kamba community
took the Monday poll seriously as "we were out to prove to Kenyans that
we can be relied upon by other political players to deliver the
presidency."
There is the youthful Mr Ruto, who despite serving as Cabinet
minister in President Moi's Government for less than a year, appears to
have taken over the mantle of leading the Kalenjin community from the
retired president.
The Eldoret North MP appears to have made good calculations by
supporting the Orange camp, unlike Mr Nicholas Biwott who appears to
have lost it all after opting to take on Mr Kenyatta (Moi's anointed
choice) as Kanu chairman in the January elections. Mr Biwott also
backed out of the Monday poll.
Mr Ruto is certainly the outright torch bearer of Kalenjin politics,
partly by virtue of being the highly placed official of Kanu, which
enjoys the largest following in Rift Valley Province.
Having brought on table a huge chunk of votes from the region, Mr
Ruto is right in the mix of all power sharing plans within ODM.
But the man who has made the greatest leap back to power is Mr
Mudavadi. The lacklustre performance by VP Moody Awori and Local
Government minister Musikari Kombo in Western Province in the
referendum polls, leaves Mr Mudavadi as the sole political beneficiary
after the vote.
The former Sabatia MP - who lost his parliamentary seat in the 2002
polls on a Kanu ticket - has since joined LDP and alongside Mr Musyoka,
is touted as the party's potential presidential candidate. Indeed. With
a huge Luyia voting bloc behind him, Mr Mudavadi's role in the movement
cannot be underestimated.
It is the question of Mr Odinga, though, that perhaps presents the
biggest challenge to the team. The main engine of the movement and the
referendum campaign, the Roads and Public Works minister is under
immense pressure to take up a "serious seat" in the movement.
The Lang'ata MP is credited more for playing the king maker without
taking up the central roles himself. A case in point was in October
2002 where he made his famed "Kibaki Tosha" pronouncement at Uhuru Park.
Chances are that his supporters in Nyanza Province and other parts
of the country are increasingly getting impatient and want something in
return.
It is in recognition of this pressure that he recently said at a
rally in Nairobi's Kamukunji grounds that he may as well vie for
presidency.
The one sure mistake that Mr Odinga is unlikely to repeat is play
second fiddle to another politician or enter into another unworkable
Memorandum of Understanding as he did with President Kibaki's National
Alliance Party of Kenya (NAK). This makes the days ahead of the Orange
movement even more delicate.

Original_Search_Title: Orange Group Set to Unveil Road Map for Law Reforms
Headline: Kenya: Orange Group Set to Unveil Road Map for Law Reforms
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange team will unveil a fresh plan of action for
constitutional reforms at its Uhuru Park, Nairobi, meeting on Saturday,
its leaders announced yesterday.
Environment minister Kalonzo Musyoka said the meeting would also be
used for thanksgiving prayers for the victory in Monday's referendum.
Addressing hundreds of supporters outside the Kenyatta International
Conference Centre, Mr Musyoka said members of the diplomatic corps
would be invited to the meeting.
Roads minister Raila Odinga said: "We warned them that a political
tsunami will come if they do not give people a good constitution. It
has now swept them."
He also compared the results of the referendum to the biblical David
and Goliath war, saying the Orange team had used less resources than
the Banana team to win people's vote.
The leaders recalled that Constitutional Affairs minister Kiraitu
Murungi had promised that the Government would use resources that would
shake the country in pursuit of a victory.
Others who addressed the supporters included the leader of Official
Opposition, Mr Uhuru Kenyatta, Cabinet ministers Najib Balala and
Ochillo Ayacko, MPs William Ruto, Otieno Kajwang', Nick Salat, Chris
Okemo and Reuben Ndolo.
Others were former Cabinet ministers Sam Ongeri, Musalia Mudavadi and MP Maoka Maore.
The leaders extended an olive branch to their Banana colleagues, inviting them for talks over a new constitution.
"We want a peaceful path to the achievement of a new constitution.
We will finally get it whether opposers like it or not," Mr Odinga said.
Mr Ndolo asked residents of Nairobi not to be divided along ethnic lines in seeking constitutional or economic reforms.

Original_Search_Title: How Key Yes Campaigners Fared in Their Home Turfs
Headline: Kenya: How Key Yes Campaigners Fared in Their Home Turfs
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Tables turned on outspoken Banana campaigners who were expected
to deliver a crucial vote in their home regions and upsetting the
expectations of the Orange team.
There were also those who delivered pleasant surprises in the face
of the loss suffered by the Yes team, but they were few and far between.
Those who suffered losses in their backyards include Vice-President
Moody Awori, who led Yes-vote campaigns in many parts of the country.
In his Funyula constituency, majority of the voters rejected the proposed constitution.
Not only did he fail in Funyula, but also in Busia District, and in his role as the Yes-vote point-man in Western Province.
But fortunes turned in favour of the Banana team, chiefly in Bungoma
District, where Local Government minister Musikari Kombo and his Trade
and Industry counterpart Mukhisa Kituyi delivered a favourable vote.
Mr Kombo, the Ford Kenya chairman, also delivered a vote in the
party's strongholds in Saboti and Kwanza constituencies in Rift Valley
Province.
The tables were also turned on Banana campaigns leader Simeon
Nyachae, who was widely expected by his team-mates to deliver the Kisii
vote. Majority of the vote went to the Orange camp.
Mr Nyachae lost to Orange in his own Nyaribari Chache constituency but delivered a victory in Kitutu Masaba and Bomachoge.
A similar disappointment was seen in assistant minister Danson Mungatana's Garsen constituency.
Mr Mungatana says the Yes team lost in Coast, including his own
constituency, because of a "disinformation campaign by opponents of the
draft constitution".
In Ukambani, Finance assistant minister and Mwala MP Mutua Katuku
led the Yes-vote onslaught but his resounding pitch failed to resonate
with his constituents and those of the wider region, favouring instead
the Orange campaign led by Environment minister Kalonzo Musyoka.
Another key Banana campaigner in Ukambani was Kibwezi MP Kalembe Ndile who was similarly disappointed at home.
He was also among the most outspoken critics of the Orange team, with the sharpest criticism levelled against Mr Musyoka.
But Yes team performance in his Kibwezi constituency was dismissal. Banana managed a paltry 1,054 to Orange's 16,711.
In Nairobi, Starehe MP Maina Kamanda, the co-coordinator of the
Banana campaigns organised successful meetings in all the eight
constituencies and the final rally at Uhuru Park where he was confident
of a Yes win come November 21.
But when the results were announced yesterday, it was only in
Dagoretti constituency, represented by Mrs Beth Mugo, where Banana won.
But it was Mathira MP Nderitu Gachagua who gave the voting a light touch through his whistle-campaign.
Mr Gachagua distributed whistles throughout his constituency and
asked those who got them to blow them early in the morning so that
everyone could come out and vote.
Mathira gave the Banana team 55,000 votes - the highest in the country.

Original_Search_Title: Hate-Filled Speeches Will Long Colour Politicians' Images
Headline: Kenya: Hate-Filled Speeches Will Long Colour Politicians' Images
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Demeaning tribal and racial stereotypes were exploited by
politicians, some amounting to insults, as the referendum campaigns
became increasingly emotion-charged.
Some of the hate-filled speeches will long colour the public images of the leaders who uttered them.
Energy minister Simeon Nyachae asked why people should support Raila
Odinga, "an omoisia (an uncircumcised boy)," rehashing an epithet that
had been used by others in 1992 when Jaramogi ran for the presidency.
Nyachae was not the only politician behaving badly: There were many
others who used abusive language against their political adversaries.
Circumcision is a theme that Cooperatives minister Njeru Ndwiga
returned to from time to time, stating that Environment minister
Kalonzo Musyoka should be shunned for "walking with ivici
(uncircumcised boys).
The issue was not confined to members of one community. Uhuru
Kenyatta, said former Nyeri mayor James Gathaka, may not have been
circumcised since he studied abroad. He ordered that when Uhuru came
calling, he should be stripped to confirm if he was circumcised.
"Raila ni shetani," said Kamukunji MP Norman Nyagah.
Raila in turn dismissed Kibwezi MP as takataka (rubbish). He also
likened Health minister Charity Ngilu to "a greedy dog that carried a
piece of meat, then mistook its reflection in water for another dog".
Exit warrants were issued to communities that have settled away from their ancestral lands.
"If we pass this Constitution, the Orange team should not step in
Meru unless they remove their flags," charged Tigania East MP Peter
Munya.
Musa Sirma responded in kind: "If Yes wins, the Kikuyus should pack their bags and move out of Eldama Ravine."
The epithets used to describe different communities were equally demeaning.
"Hii samaki people hawatosheki" (these fish people are not satisfied), said Kabete MP Paul Muite, in reference to Luos.
Mwea MP Alfred Nderitu said: "They hate us because we are
hardworking. Luos just go fishing and fish is free, and then ask the
Government for relief maize to make ugali."
Other races were not left out. "Delamere kills Africans like dogs,"
said Nyeri Town MP P.G. Mureithi. "The new Constitution will teach him
and the settlers a lesson since their land will revert to the
Government."
Kibwezi MP Kalembe Ndile was to claim that Arabs were to blame for
landlessness at the Coast because they had taken up all land, while
Mukurwe-ini's Mutahi Kagwe saw Yash Pal Ghai, initiated to shape the
early stages of the constitutional review, as nothing but an Asian.
"The Asian who was brought here," he said, "went to Sri Lanka and
wrote a constitution like the one he brought to Bomas. Immediately he
left, people in Sri Lanka were already slaughtering each other."
"We Kikuyus are many and we are found all over, including Mombasa,
Kisumu, Isiolo. We should turn out in large numbers to vote so that we
are not defeated," he added.
Women were similarly demeaned: "We have men and there are those who
are like women because they are fearing to defect from the Banana
camp," said Eldoret North MP William Ruto.

Original_Search_Title: Orange Leaders Praise Kibaki Speech And Call for Harmony
Headline: Kenya: Orange Leaders Praise Kibaki Speech And Call for Harmony
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Orange Democratic Movement leaders yesterday welcomed President
Kibaki's speech accepting the referendum results and urged the Banana
camp to join them in a healing process.
The Orange team, which campaigned against the constitution
proposals, said the time had come for Kenyans to put the referendum
behind them and unite in nation-building.
It would hold a victory rally at Uhuru Park on Saturday and not today as had been planned.
Addressing thousands of supporters outside the Kenyatta
International Conference Centre, Nairobi, soon after receiving the
official results from Electoral Commission chairman Samuel Kivuitu, the
leaders vowed to remain united and to ensure they delivered to the
public a people-driven constitution.
They were Cabinet ministers Raila Odinga, (Roads); Najib Balala
(National Heritage); Mr Ochillo Ayacko (Sports) and Anyang' Nyong'o
(Planning), and Kanu leader Uhuru Kenyatta, his secretary general, Mr
William Ruto, and former vice-President Musalia Mudavadi. Also present
were 20 MPs from Narc and Kanu.
Mr Kenyatta said: "The public has spoken with one voice. In our last
rally, we prayed for peace to prevail during the referendum and for
voters to turn out in large numbers and vote for Orange. We can now say
thank you."
Mr Musyoka praised Kenyans for being patient, peaceful and for turning out in large numbers to vote.
Makadara MP Reuben Ndolo asked the Orange leaders to remain united and re-start the review process.

Original_Search_Title: Moi's Shadow Loomed Large Over Vote
Headline: Kenya: Moi's Shadow Loomed Large Over Vote
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The shadow cast by former President Moi over the referendum vote was too large to be ignored.
Though he played no direct role in the Orange campaigns, he was
often the bogeyman that the rival Banana team invoked in an effort to
scare people off the No-vote bandwagon.
The name of the former President, who still wields a lot of
influence in his populous Kalenjin community and the main opposition
party Kanu, was foremost on the minds of the chief Banana team
campaigners in the run-up to the referendum.
From the beginning of the campaigns, Banana luminaries such Justice
minister Kiraitu Murungi were wont to say to anyone who cared to listen
that the former President was funding the No-vote push.
Kabete MP Paul Muite even went as far as claiming that Roads
minister Raila Odinga had solicited Mr Moi's support during "secret
night meetings", a claim that Mr Odinga dismissed in Parliament.
When they were not saying he was funding the campaigns, the Banana
leaders were making threats to the former President. One, Justice
assistant minister Robinson Githae, suggested that the retired
President could lose his generous State benefits.
In making such threats, the Banana team forgot or chose to ignore
the fact that the former President still has a considerable
constituency around Kenya and such threats are, to say the least,
unhelpful.
To forget or ignore this point is to overlook or disregard that the
former President is still a major player on the political front, even
though he does not necessarily show his hand.
The former President dismissed the Banana team's claims as "false
cheap and malicious", saying if he had the kind of money that they were
saying he had, he would have channelled it into his humanitarian work
with the Moi Africa Institute.
However, despite the former President's denials of being the moving
spirit behind the No campaign, there is ample evidence that he was not
sitting around twiddling his thumbs hoping for the best.
Former President Moi proved that Jaramogi Oginga Odinga was right to
have referred to him as a far-sighted giraffe looking out for the herd.
As far back as July when the Government had announced the mass
evictions of mainly Kalenjin settlers from the Mau Forest, which is
threatened by degradation, the former head of state cracked the whip of
Kalenjin unity. He made even staunch opponents of his allied to the
Kibaki Government to unite and defend the community's interests.
Once he had rallied the troops in this one instance, he did not
release his grip on the reins. Instead, he used his influence to help
draw the Kalenjin back together again. Up until the evictions, the
Kibaki Government had put into use one of Mr Moi's old tricks, the
divide and rule gambit, and had split the Kalenjin into three or four
main groups.
However, once the former president used the evictions to rally the
Kalenjin under a single umbrella, he sank in his hooks. Reeling in the
community during the referendum campaigns was child's play for the old
manipulator.
It was, therefore, no coincidence that the Kalenjin community was
once again largely united. Even hitherto Kibaki and Narc stalwarts,
such as Immigration minister Linah Chebii Kilimo had no choice but to
jump onto the Orange bandwagon.
It must have given the former president particular pleasure to see
his one-time protege , turned bitter foe, since the end-days of the Moi
succession, Mr Nicholas Biwott, reduced to a peripheral figure during
the referendum campaigns.
The former head of state must have been quietly satisfied to see the
people who abandoned him during his hour of need in 2002 back in his
corner.
The final nail in the Government's coffin as far as Mr Moi and the
Kalenjin community were concerned, was driven by the crackdown on the
then relatively unknown Kalenjin-language radio station Kass.
Unwittingly, the Government lent the former president's Kalenjin
unity campaign a hand at the last minute when with less than a week to
go to the referendum vote, they shut down Kass. Though the charges were
that the station was biased and inciting the community to violence, it
was strange that two days later the station was back on air again. If
the community had needed any convincing that they were being
"targeted", what would be better than this move on "their" radio
station?
The Narc government played right into the old man's hands. By the
time they realised what was happening and tried to reverse any damage,
it was too late. The die had been cast.
Of course, the former president would deny having anything to do
with the sound rejection of the proposed Constitution by the Kalenjin
community or anywhere else, for that matter, but those who have watched
him operate through the years will swear they see his prints all over.

Original_Search_Title: So What Follows After the Victory?
Headline: Kenya: So What Follows After the Victory?
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Leaders in the Orange Democratic Movement will now come under focus with referendum campaigns well behind them.
They created euphoria during the campaigns, but questions are now
being raised as to whether they would continue with the same unity that
enabled them win at the referendum to oppose the Wako Draft.
The 11 key members criss-crossed the country canvassing for votes
against the proposed new constitution in a campaign reminiscent of the
2002 Narc campaigns that swept President Kibaki to power.
And when five ministers in President Kibaki's Government announced
before the campaigns that they would oppose the Wako Draft, pundits
thought they were mere rebels.
The first to break rank were Cabinet ministers Raila Odinga, Kalonzo Musyoka, Anyang' Nyong'o, Ochilo Ayacko and Najib Balala.
And in their camp were leader of the Official Opposition Uhuru
Kenyatta, Kanu secretary general William Ruto, Kanu vice-chairman Chris
Okemo and former vice-president Musalia Mudavadi.
They were joined by ministers William ole Ntimama and Jebii Kilimo, who read the mood of their constituents early.
Already there is speculation that the group might have used the
referendum campaigns as the testing ground for the bigger campaigns
come 2007 General Election.
Keen analysts will be watching the next step, especially whether they will move to register ODM as a political party.
If they do, even with unknown politicians as interim officials, it
will be seen as the initial step towards building unity ahead of
elections.The key players in the campaign are being viewed as having
the political ambition to form a political front that would fight in
the next elections.
If this were to happen, they face the daunting task of identifying a
leader among themselves, a possible running mate and keeping the debate
to found a new constitution alive.
They have the option to push ahead as a team or simply retreat to their political parties and bid time for the next elections.
The politicians from the LDP could also go back to the negotiating
table with their big partners in the National Alliance (Party) of Kenya
in Narc.
However, just emerging from victory at the referendum, they will be
hoping that Nak politicians would be the first to send signals to them
for reconciliatory talks than the other way round.
In getting who to lead the team, the names Odinga, Musyoka, Mudavadi, Kenyatta and Ruto stick out in the group.
The prospects of reaching a compromise would only become clear in
the next few months after they give an indication of how they intend to
continue to sustain the tempo.
But another prospect could be that they might want to move in the
direction of opening dialogue with their colleagues who campaigned in
the Banana group.

Original_Search_Title: Vital Lessons From the Orange Win
Headline: Kenya: Vital Lessons From the Orange Win
Body_Text:
Nairobi — What lessons have we learnt from the results of the referendum vote?
First, it is that anybody who wants to rule Kenya in future must be
willing to work with a team.
It is the group that manages to put together the widest multi-ethnic
coalition with leading lights from all regions, especially where you
have a fair mix of the young and the old, that will carry the day.
Chances get better for you when you ride on the crest of popular
grievances and when the overwhelming majority believe that members of
your group have been treated unfairly by arrogant incumbents. This
country will be run by coalitions, forever.
Secondly, the referendum has demonstrated that money and material
can only buy you transitory loyalty. My heart bleeds for Mr Amos
Kimunya who had to criss-cross the country in choppers - from the Rift
Valley Province to Ukambani to Malindi at the Coast - to dish out title
deeds to squatters.
Isn't it amazing that Rift Valley still voted No even after the
resettlement of people in Mau. This society is democratising quietly
but in very fundamental ways.
Mrs Charity Ngilu was the only minister to be allowed the privilege
of being hosted twice at State House. She led two delegations where
members of the Kamba community were promised all manner of goodies by
President Kibaki.
Relief food was distributed to the poor people of Ukambani with
unprecedented generosity. Yet when the time to vote came, the peasants
voted against Banana. They will accept your money and relief food, but
vote with their conscience.
Neither did the salary increases for chiefs and local government
officials, the reinstatement of sacked Health ministry personnel, and
promises of retaining chiefs in the civil service make a difference.
The referendum has also exposed paper tigers. We now know that
politicians will demand so much public attention claiming to speak for
their constituents and regions even when they no longer enjoy the
support of the people they claim to speak for.
We gave people like Simon Nyachae, Moody Awori, Moody Awori and
George Saitoti so much space and attention, believing that they were
major players who would make a major impact in their constituencies.
In the Coast Province, Ali Mwakwere, Morris Dzoro, and Danson
Mungatana have been forced to eat their humble pie having been exposed
as paper tigers who are out of touch with what their constituents want.
In Nairobi, the Government chief whip, Mr Norman Nyagah, was whipped by the people of Kamukunji.
But in terms of those who have suffered major political
embarrassments, Vice President Awori, Prof Saitoti, and Mr Nyachae top
the list.
Indeed, the referendum results may very well mark the beginning of the end of the political careers of this trio.
Mr Awori could not even deliver his own Funyula constituency to the Banana camp. For Mr Nyachae, the stakes were even higher.
A key player in the Banana side, his strongest point all along has
been his influence in Kisii politics. The expectations were that the
community would vote unanimously for Banana on account of his influence
among his people. It did not happen.
In the coming months, it is unlikely that these politicians will get
so much attention from their peers, especially in the political
realignment and new coalitions which are likely to occur as new
grouping start preparing for the 2007 elections.
My message to the leadership of the Orange Movement is the
following: You deserve to celebrate your victory. You fought gallantly
and triumphed. You have made your point.
However, the hard part begins after the celebrations are over. You
have to demonstrate to the people of this country that the victory you
have in your hands will be used for the national good.
This is not the time for you to gloat and to behave as if this
contest was all about scoring propaganda points against your opponents.
In my view, the mandate the citizens of this country have given to
you is to bring the political temperatures down, reach out to your
opponents - negotiate and bargain with them and lead country on a
course of national healing. Indeed, the most crying need of Kenya today
is to undergo a catharsis - a course of emotional cleansing. The
referendum thing has left us deeply divided.
Consider the following. Out of Kenya's eight provinces, only one -
Central - voted with the government. The Islamic constituency in the
country voted against the referendum to a fault.
Clearly, divisiveness is the Aids of Kenya - a disease that is
spreading fast and wide, preying on the public mind and is without a
cure in sight. We also need to work on how to restore inter faith
harmony.
Only a new constitution will reform the dysfunctional political and social order we have at the moment will heal the country.
Orange, Banana and civil society must come together right away to spearhead structured negotiations and chart the way forward.
I say so because under the present circumstances, the alternative is the return to street demonstration and noisy politicking.
Days before the referendum vote, some civil society elements were
already sending notice that they would resume street demonstrations.
This economy cannot afford to go that route. We have been in an electioneering mood for far too long.
The way I see it, the mandate given to the Orange team in the
referendum is to reach out to all these groups and to engage them in
structured negotiations and bargaining.
This is the only way to undercut the opportunistic elements who may
want to take advantage of the popular grievances against the Government
to take us back to the days of street demonstrations.
What is worrisome, however, is the fact that the constitution reform
thing has dragged for too long as to render the country without
credible independent voices.
Civil society in Kenya is today bereft of institutions and
individuals who can command respect from both sides of the divide. Even
the church no longer enjoys the reputation of an honest brokers. More
than ever before, this country needs statesmen.
Mr Kisero is the managing editor of the EastAfrican, a publication of the Nation Media Group.

Original_Search_Title: Referendum Boiled Down to Power Struggle
Headline: Kenya: Referendum Boiled Down to Power Struggle
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kenyans vote in the historic referendum this Monday, but the outcome
is likely to be determined by regional interests and personalities as
opposed to pertinent constitutional issues.
Top officials of both the Electoral Commission of Kenya (ECK) and
the Constitution of Kenya Review Commission (CKRC), conceded that the
two-month civic education failed to sway the public away from political
considerations to vote on constitutional issues.
Instead, voting is likely to be a repeat of the 2002 presidential
election, whose outcome was influenced by personalities rather than the
merits and demerits of the contenders.
Voter turnout is also expected to play a crucial role, with fears
that most Kenyans are likely to view the referendum as any other
by-election and so decide not to bother to go out of their way to vote.
There is little sign that people working in urban areas, especially
Nairobi and Mombasa, who are registered to vote in rural areas, will go
to the trouble of travelling for the sake of voting the referendum.
According to ECK public relations officer Mani Lemaiyan, it is
difficult to tell whether the huge crowds that have been turning out
for rallies are an indication that Kenyans have taken the
constitutional issues seriously or simply a tribute to the drawing
power of political leaders.
"This is a very important time in the history of our country, but we
are not sure whether people know what they are voting for. It is even
more difficult to tell whether the crowds that have been attending
rallies will turn into votes," said Mr Lemayian, who however gave his
assurance that the electoral body was prepared to handle the exercise.
Both ECK and CKRC officials are apprehensive that should Kenyans
vote largely on the basis of ethnic considerations and personalities,
the key objective of the constitution as a contract between the rulers
and the ruled will have been sacrificed at the altar of politics and
ethnicity.
With other areas having been zoned into either "banana" or "orange"
strongholds, the provinces of Western, Coast, North Eastern, Nairobi
and the cosmopolitan North Rift, are set to provide the swing votes
that will determine the results.
According to CKRC secretary, Dr Patrick Lumumba, "The project has
largely been made into a political and ethnic contest and very few
Kenyans are likely to vote on the merits and demerits of the draft."
This is mainly because other than Kenya's ethnicised politics, the
two-month civic education has done little to persuade voters to focus
on constitutional issues. Dr Lumumba says that the ideal situation
would have been to conduct civic education before the start of the
political campaigns.
Although Kenyans have the right to be adequately informed and vote
from an informed position on the draft, the approach to civic education
since the clamour for political pluralism in 1990s, has always bordered
on advocacy, depending on the political mood of the day.
In this case, the major concerns revolved around uniformity of the
civic education provided, the impartiality and the danger of the
exercise being confused with advocacy.
In some areas the civic educators were being forced to compare the
proposed constitution with the Bomas draft and the current
constitution. In some areas, civic educators were harassed on the
suspicion that they were out to swindle voters into taking a particular
stand other instead of assisting them to make an informed choice.
Dr Lumumba conceded that civic education attendance varied from
place to place, and in certain circumstances, CKRC officials were asked
to state their stand on the proposed draft first before they were
allowed to continue with the exercise.
Meanwhile, the head of the Kenya National Commission on Human
Rights, Maina Kiai, complained that two months was too short a time to
carry out serious and comprehensive civic education on a legal document.
In his view, the constitutional review effort shifted from a
constitution-making process to power and ethnic politics. "The
referendum should be about making a decision on a draft by principle,
information and consensus. But we recognise that this was not the
reality we had to deal with," he said.
Besides the politicisation of the referendum process, Mr Lemaiyan is
also concerned that the short rains pounding Western and the Mt Kenya
region are likely to interfere with voter turnout.

Original_Search_Title: Summon Cabinet, Kalonzo Asks President
Headline: Kenya: Summon Cabinet, Kalonzo Asks President
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Environment minister Kalonzo Musyoka yesterday asked President Kibaki to
call a Cabinet meeting.
"It is the time for healing and reconciliation," Kalonzo said.
He said the rift arising from the referendum battle, which left the
country split between the Orange and Banana camps, should be bridged.
"The Cabinet should be summoned back to continue with its work."
The Cabinet suspended its meetings when the campaigns for the referendum
kicked off.
Government spokesman, Dr Alfred Mutua, later announced that the Cabinet
meetings had been suspended to allow the Ministers participate in the
referendum campaigns, which officially started 30 days before November
21.
Kalonzo said Kenyans had voted for Orange and it was time to concentrate
on reconciliation and healing.
"We don't need to go back... Kenyans have made a choice," he said.
Kalonzo was speaking at Mwingi Boys High School after the results for
his Mwingi South Constituency were announced.
Orange carried the day with 28,372 votes against 6,661 votes for
Yes.
In Mwingi South constituency of LDP chairman David Musila, Orange won
with 28,986 with Banana side getting 3,507 votes.
He said the referendum vote was not a contest between President Kibaki
and Raila.
"We won't abandon this constitution reform agenda. A lot of work had
been done in Bomas," he said.
He said the Orange Democratic Movement's agenda was to have a
constituency assembly put in place to jump-start the review process.
"I expected President Kibaki to be above the Yes and No politics on the
referendum. But now we have finished, we respect him and we have to continue
with Government work," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Gloom Engulfs Yes Strongholds Following Heavy Defeat
Headline: Kenya: Gloom Engulfs Yes Strongholds Following Heavy Defeat
Body_Text:
Nairobi — An uneasy calm descended in Gatundu town on Monday night soon after the
results of the national referendum started to trickle in showing the Orange
team in the lead.
Some residents who had jammed pubs and other public places to monitor
the outcome of the polls booed as radio announcements showed the Orange team
was in the lead.
The results dampened the spirits of the revellers and some abandoned
their drinks and went home cursing, but others opted to remain behind.
"I can't stomach those announcements any more. I would rather go home
and sleep," shouted a middle-aged man as he walked out from a local bar,
sending his colleagues into laughter.
In Gatundu South Banana emerged victorious with 19,848 votes against
Orange team's 11,032 votes.
The local MP Mr Uhuru Kenyatta, who is also the Leader of the Official
Opposition, was in the Orange camp.
Former area MP, Ngengi Muigai thanked the Gatundu South residents for
voting for the Banana.
Meanwhile, two Ford People MPs who delivered the Banana vote in Cabinet
minister Simeon Nyachae's turf, yesterday called for reconciliation among
Kenyans.
MPs Joel Onyancha (Bomachoge) and Mwancha Okioma (Kitutu Masaba), said
for the sake of the country's stability, efforts must be put in place to
unite all leaders.
Onyancha thanked his constituents for supporting the proposed
constitution.
Okioma said it would be wrong for the President to sack ministers in the
Orange camp. Meanwhile placard waving Orange supporters took to the streets
in Kisii yesterday celebrating their victory.
"The outcome has proved to Kenyans that Nyachae and his MPs are not
invincible in Kisii," said a Nairobi-based Lawyer Fred Orora who
co-ordinated the Orange.
Meanwhile, a civic leader collapsed at a polling station in Meru North
District after she allegedly found her name missing in the voter's
register.
The Maua Municipal councillor, Alice Kalkuki Marambo, was admitted to
Nyambene Nursing Home after the shock.
The Banana won in all the four constituencies in the district with
Tigania East constituency delivering 27,339 against Orange's 1,133.
In Ntonyiri Banana had 29,374 and the Orange 2,433 votes.
In Tigania West Banana garnered 27,130 while Orange got 574 votes.

Original_Search_Title: Resounding No
Headline: Kenya: Resounding No
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Orange leaders and their supporters went into a frenzy of celebration
after their resounding victory at the referendum was officially declared
yesterday.
The leaders, who included Kanu chairman Uhuru Kenyatta, Cabinet
ministers Raila Odinga, Kalonzo Musyoka, Anyang' Nyong'o, Ochilo Ayacko and
Najib Balala, thanked God and Kenyans for the victory.
An Orange supporter jubilates after the result was officially announced
in Nairobi.
It was an electrifying moment in the hall at the Kenyatta International
Conference Centre, and among the supporters that were dancing and singing
outside, when Electoral Commission of Kenya chairman Samuel Kivuitu
announced that Orange had carried the day with 3,548,477 votes against
Banana's 2,532,918 - a difference of 1,015,559 votes. This represented a 57
per cent win for Orange to Banana's 43 per cent.
Kivuitu made the announcement that signals a resounding rejection of the
Proposed New Constitution minutes after President Kibaki made a live TV
address to the nation accepting the results.
After months on the energy-sapping campaign trail, the Orange leaders -
standing on the front steps of KICC - extended an olive branch to the Banana
team that lost the bid to push through the draft. They declared that they
would now push for a constitution acceptable to all Kenyans.
They also saluted President Kibaki who supported the draft for
"accepting defeat with honour and dignity".
The leaders told their supporters to attend a thanksgiving and strategy
rally at Nairobi's Uhuru Park on Saturday.
Raila who played the role of master of ceremonies sent out a message of
peace and national reconciliation to the Banana team. "We are not
revengeful, we are have no hatred. We have declared peace and now because we
wrestled you down, give us your hand, we pick you up."
He said the Orange wave was unstoppable and vowed the team would not be
fragmented by outside forces.
Referring to his earlier call on people to climb to higher ground to
avoid an approaching tsunami, Raila said the moment had come to pass and
those swept to the sea were the losers in the referendum who remained on the
ground.
The leaders addressed the frenzied crowd one by one.
"The shareholders of Kenya have spoken and they should be listened to,
we want leaders, not rulers," said Balala.
Kalonzo said the team would never forget the dirty tricks Banana used to
marshal support, including dishing out money to MPs in Parliament and wooing
communities with land and relief food.
"Even the poor and the landless in Kibwezi said No. They would never
gamble with their lives or accept land in exchange for a constitution that
would harm them."
He added: "We shall forgive but never forget."
Earlier in the hall, Uhuru read a joint statement by the leaders in the
hall.
Collectively they appealed to the vanquished: "Please come and join the
rest of the nation in rebuilding a new Kenya."
They vowed to stick together and give Kenyans a new constitutional
dispensation to be negotiated and agreed upon by all, no matter their
background or ethnicity.
"This triumph shows that the future of the country lies not in an
imperial presidency but in an accountable leadership," they said.
The Orange leaders thanked Kenyans for "defying those with power and
wealth of the State behind them". "But even more than joy, we stand here in
awe of the great people of this country. In awe of the power of democracy,
in awe of those who for the last two months fought tirelessly and
strategised with the most meager of resources to ensure that we are not
subjected to the monster of legalised dictatorship."
The leaders appealed to the people of Central Province not to allow
themselves to be alienated from the rest of Kenyans. "We appeal to our
brothers and sisters in Central Kenya to reject any leadership that is
divisive and may mislead and isolate them."
Uhuru noted that the Orange leadership had consistently fought for the
welfare and rights of Kenyans to be addressed in the constitution. "The
support of this cause by six out of the eight provinces demonstrates the
unanimity by which Kenyans have endorsed the national stand of the Orange
leaders," they added.
The Orange leaders assured the Banana rivals and all those who supported
the Yes camp that they wanted to work with them to create a Kenya they all
aspired to have.
A policy of national inclusion was the only way that could hold the
country together and ensure the basic aspirations of Kenyans were met, the
statement said.
These include education for Kenyan children, jobs for the unemployed,
health services for all, equitable sharing of the country's land and wealth
and empowerment of women and the marginalised, vulnerable and deprived
groups.
"Now that the people of Kenya have decisively spoken, and in their
spirit of genuine reconciliation, we invite our colleagues in the Banana
camp to join us in expeditiously charting the way forward for a new
constitutional dispensation for our country," the leaders said.
Uhuru said Kenyans behaved in a respectable manner by defying the
prophets of doom who had warned of chaos and repeatedly threatened to
suppress the violence they themselves were predicting by turning out to
vote.
"Kenyans voted in every part of the country peacefully and with grace,
despite the efforts of certain irresponsible media to instigate ethnic
hatred with a view to instilling fear and despondency."
The Orange team thanked Kenyans for the support and confidence that they
had shown in them to lead the process for a new constitutional dispensation
and promised they will not let them down.
The result was received with brave faces by key Banana campaigners, many
of who suffered humiliating defeats in their own backyards. The Banana
election team chairman, Mr Simeon Nyachae, sent a statement to Kivuitu,
conceding defeat and congratulating Kenyans and the electoral commission for
conducting the poll peacefully.
Lands Minister Amos Kimunya and his Foreign Affairs counterpart Chirau
Mwakwere said they would respect the verdict as a reflection of the voters'
wishes.
But Kimunya, who blamed the Yes loss on electoral "misinformation" by
the draft's opponents, appeared to rule out post-referendum dialogue and a
return to the Bomas draft favoured by the No team arguing the vote had put
an end to the process.
Releasing the results, Kivuitu said the figures would change because
counting was still going on in Fafi and results were yet to be received from
Turkana North and Turkana South constituencies. Results for Juja were also
yet to be authenticated, Kivuitu added, because "the person who brought them
disappeared". However, the margin was too wide to be affected by the
uncounted votes.
In a speech spiced with humour and wildly cheered by the Orange
supporters, the ECK chairman said the struggle to give Kenya a new
constitution should continue. "It is imperative that we continue to strive
for a better constitution which in fact and spirit recognises the power to
govern rests on the people and no one else."
"I wish to commend all of you for participating peacefully in this
historic event. This is a major leap in the consolidation of democratic
governance in this country. The people have made a choice and as I have
always said, my Government would respect the choice of the people. There are
no winners or losers in this process as the objective of this important
exercise was to determine the people's choice and that choice has been
made," he said.
The announcement of the results was a culmination of months of
campaigning that has further widened the split in President Kibaki's
Government. The five ministers broke ranks with their colleagues to oppose
the proposed constitution, sparking a major political war.
And the Government put in its all - including dishing out land to
squatters and creating new districts - to win support.
The No team registered convincing victories in Nairobi, Ukambani, and
the Coast and in large swathes of Kisii and Western Kenya that were expected
to witness a fierce scramble by both camps.
Vice President Moody Awori, and Ministers Simeon Nyachae, George
Saitoti, Charity Ngilu, Government Whip Norman Nyaga and assistant minister
Maina Kamanda were among the Banana luminaries who lost their turfs to the
Orange wave.
The Banana camp lost 7,585 to 8,564 in the VP's Funyula constituency,
19,096 to 22,029 in Saitoti's Kajiado North while Ngilu's Kitui Central
voted 16,415 against the draft with 11,560 backing it.
The No camp also prevailed in Nyachae's Kitutu Chache constituency
trouncing the Yes crusaders 19,977 against 7,756. It also won17, 995 against
15,661 in Kamukunji constituency represented by Nyaga and 23,058 against
22,914 in Kamanda's Starehe constituency.
Although generally billed as a Yes stronghold, Nairobi with an average
turnout of 38 percent went Orange with 208,747 votes against Banana's
144,968.
Information Minister Raphael Tuju braved hostility provoked by his vocal
campaigns for the constitution in an Orange zone to vote in his Rarienda
constituency but ended with a paltry 1,000 for his camp.
But there were overwhelming victories for the camp in Central Province
with an average of 93 percent of the cast votes in its favour and equally
comfortable margins in other strongholds of Meru, Embu and Ford-Kenya zones
in Western Province.
Save for Uhuru who lost in his Gatundu South constituency 11,032 against
19,848 and Joseph Kamotho who conceded Mathioya 28,620 to 756, the Orange
camp was spared harsh voters lessons.
The team easily took Raila's Langata constituency 37,914 against 9,208,
Heritage Minister Najib Balala's Mvita constituency 14,085 against 3,267 and
Musalia Mudavadi's home turf of Sabatia 25,658 to 1,954.
There were also strong showing by Office of the President Minister
William ole Ntimama in Narok North, William Ruto in Eldoret North and Gideon
Moi in Baringo Central.
The No camp made strong showing in Luo Nyanza with record backing of 98
percent of cast votes and in large parts of the Rift Valley Province that
also witnessed high turnouts.
Marakwet East, Mogotio, Baringo North, Marakwet West and Baringo Central
constituency in the province had impressive turnouts of 95.4 percent, 91.3
percent, 91 percent.87.1 percent and 86.2 percent respectively.
There were also impressive voter turnouts in Eldama Ravine, 79.5
percent, Keiyo North, 79.4 percent, Mosop, 78.3 percent and Chepalungu 77.5
percent.
Wajir West experienced the lowest turnout with only 5,366 voters out of
the registered 26,273 participating in the poll. Other constituencies
witnessing poor turnout were Mvita and Dujis with 26.9 percent.

Original_Search_Title: Jubilation As ECK Releases Poll Results
Headline: Kenya: Jubilation As ECK Releases Poll Results
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Jubilation rent the air yesterday as the Electoral Commission of Kenya
chairman, Mr Samuel Kivuitu, declared the Orange camp the winner of the
historic referendum poll.
The Electoral Commission of Kenya chairman, Mr Samuel Kivuitu, announces
the referendum results at the Kenyatta International Conference Centre,
Nairobi, yesterday.
Thousands of Orange supporters, who did not want the Proposed New
Constitution implemented, sang and danced outside the Kenyatta International
Conference Center in Nairobi where the announcement was made.
The Orange camp led the Banana with slightly over a million votes with
less than 100,000 votes not counted.
Earlier, President Kibaki had in a live telecast conceded defeat and
accepted that Kenyans had rejected the proposed constitution.
Orange garnered 3,548,477 votes against Banana's 2,532,918.
Results had not been received from Fafi, Juja, Turkana Central and
South.
To put to rest any fears of controversy or even contests that are known
to characterise such polls, Kivuitu confirmed that the leader of the Banana
team, Mr Simeon Nyachae, had written to the Commission, conceding
defeat.
Nyachae said the Banana team had accepted the verdict of Kenyans.
And happy with the peaceful manner in which Kenyans had conducted
themselves throughout the voting and counting exercises, Kivuitu called for
reason to prevail after the referendum.
Kivuitu said those who will be in charge of delivering a better
constitution must be people of high integrity and honesty.
"They must consult the people in the most effective, transparent and
democratic way as possible," he added.
Urging Kenyans to be patient, Kivuitu said no person, community or
institution should be ignored in the process.
He said Kenyans were lucky to have the current constitution, the Bomas
Draft and the Kilifi draft, which can be the base for a new document.
He said Kenyans need a constitution that addresses their aspirations and
fears seeks to unite them.
"We need a constitution which in fact and spirit recognises that power
rests in the people alone," said Kivuitu.

Original_Search_Title: Fireworks Light Up Coast Skies As Orange Celebrate
Headline: Kenya: Fireworks Light Up Coast Skies As Orange Celebrate
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Orange supporters in Mombasa begun to celebrate soon after receiving
reports of the victory.
Immediately the final results for the Mvita constituency were announced
at Chandaria Hall in favour of the Orange team at around 10pm, Mombasa mayor
Taib Ali Taib and his supporters began celebrating.
The mayor walked out of the polling station with his supporters and
moved around Mombasa town in a convoy of vehicles. His supporters who were
singing Orange victory songs held him high.
There were also fireworks displays, which temporarily turned the town
into a carnival mood with residents coming out of their houses to witness
what was going on.
Taib and his supporters moved around town in a convoy of vehicles until
3am yesterday before dispersing.
Changamwe MP, Ramadhan Seif Kajembe and his supporters broke into song
and dance when the results favouring the Orange team were announced at the
Kenya Polytechnic Hall at around 11pm.
Kajembe and his supporters also moved around town in a convoy of
vehicles and ended up at the Orange Secretariat along Kenyatta Avenue in
Mombasa.
Ready to receive them at the secretariat were Mvita MP Najib Balala and
other secretariat members.
From Kisauni constituency area MP Ananiah Mwaboza's political rival Ali
Hassan Joho moved around town with his supporters when victory for the
Orange team was announced at the Mombasa Women Hall.
Banana camp Coast leaders and there supporters were nowhere to be
seen.
Earlier in Diani, voters chased a councillor from a polling station in
Msambweni after he flashed a Banana, which is the symbol for Yes vote.
Voters at Mwakigena Primary School polling station shouted "Atoke!
Atoke!" (He should leave).
They said the councillor had provoked voters by trying last minute
futile campaigns at the polling station.

Original_Search_Title: MP Concedes Defeat, Admits That Proposed Document Flawed
Headline: Kenya: MP Concedes Defeat, Admits That Proposed Document Flawed
Body_Text:
Nairobi — An MP who spearheaded the Banana campaign in the nine Meru
constituencies yesterday conceded defeat in Monday's referendum.
The Tigania East MP, Mr Peter Munya, conceded that some clauses in the
Wako draft were unpopular.
Munya said Kenyans have spoken strongly against the Wako draft through
the vote.
"To be honest it was very difficult to sell some of the provisions in
the Draft. The most critical of these were land and inheritance which were
very unpopular," Munya said.
Igembe MP, Raphael Muriungi, said the Meru had overwhelmingly voted for
the Wako draft.
Imenti Central, Imenti South and Imenti North constituencies voted as a
block delivering a total of 167,699 votes to the Banana camp. This compares
to 6, 633 votes for the Orange camp in the district. Cabinet ministers
Kiraitu Murungi and David Mwiraria together with legislator Kirugi
M'Mukindia, were leading the Banana campaigns in Meru.
"We played our part by delivering the Yes votes which really shows it
was a block," Muriungi said adding that he was disappointed by the
outcome.
Ntonyiri MP Maoka Maore said: "The Orange victory was
expected. Unfortunately, my people were misled into believing we were at the
top of everything and everybody."
Tharaka, Nithi, Ntonyiri, Igembe, Tigania West, and Tigania
constituencies East delivered over 95 per cent of the Banana votes.
There was a sombre mood in the entire region with the people discussing
in low tones the results of the historic vote.
Although voter turn out was good in the region, the Banana campaigners
were disappointed with the low turnout within Meru Municipality, which falls
under Mwiraria's North Imenti constituency.
Former Central Imenti MP Gitobu Imanyara termed the No win as a new dawn
for Kenya.
"The people have spoken and Kibaki should do the honourable by
quitting," he told The Standard on telephone from a Nairobi hospital, where
he is admitted.
Kiraitu and Mwiraria who with Munya led the Banana campaign in the
region were not available for comment.
They were said to have left for Nairobi early yesterday morning.
In Kangundo, the Banana team blamed its poor show on incompetent
co-ordinators. The group of youths and agents accused the co-ordinators of
using the campaigns to make quick money.

Original_Search_Title: Eastern Politicians in Shock After Tally
Headline: Kenya: Eastern Politicians in Shock After Tally
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A sizeable chunk of Eastern province didn't just vote Orange. It did it
in style, delivering the biggest political upset of recent times by
rejecting senior members of President Kibaki's Cabinet at the
plebiscite.
Orange, rooting for a No vote, garnered 90 per cent of the vote in
Ukambani and won in all the 17 constituencies in the region.
The upper part of the province, the Meru and Embu districts, voted
overwhelmingly for Yes, due to their association with the Central province
group of politicians supporting the draft.
In total, the Orange team in Eastern managed to get 55 per cent of the
cast votes while Banana got 44 per cent of the votes.
Key cabinet ministers Kiraitu Murungi, Njeru Ndwiga, David Mwiraria and
Assistant ministers Kilemi Mwiria, Petkay Miriti and a host of other MPs
from Embu and Meru were campaigning for the Proposed New Constitution.
The ministers from Embu and Meru have also been close allies of
President Kibaki since their days in the Democratic Party and were expected
to swing the vote in favour of the Banana group.
Only Ntonyiri MP Maoka Maore and his Siakago counterpart, Justin Muturi,
had opposed the Wako Draft.
The win for the Orange group is mainly credited to Environment minister
Kalonzo Musyoka, a leading light of the Orange team.
All MPs from Ukambani allied to the Orange team experienced landslide
wins in their constituencies, largely due to Kalonzo's influence during the
campaigns.
Kalonzo and a host of key Orange leaders, including Raila Odinga,
William Ruto, Uhuru Kenyatta, Najib Balala and Anyang' Nyong'o visited all
the 17 constituencies in Ukambani to drum up support for the No vote.
Indeed, Kalonzo, who is perceived in the region as a presidential
contender in the 2007 polls drew support from his many followers after
alleging that the draft was tailored to curtail his presidential
ambitions.
The Orange win in the region set the stage for the 2007 polls, with
majority of Kamba MPs asserting that time has come for the community to have
one of their own ascend to the top seat.
Despite high profile State House visits by delegations from Ukambani,
organised by Health minister Charity Ngilu in a bid to drum support for the
Wako Draft, the loss is a major slap on the face of the indefatigable
politician.

Original_Search_Title: Cold Weather Piles On the 'Yes' Agony
Headline: Kenya: Cold Weather Piles On the 'Yes' Agony
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A chilly weather piled on the agony on dour faced residents of Central
Province who were shell shocked by the outcome of the referendum
results.
After spending more than 12 hours glued to their radios and television
sets many voters could not come to terms with the loss of the vote.
The few who braved the chilly weather to venture into the streets
huddled in small groups as they discussed the result in hushed tones
"I cannot believe what I am hearing. It is not true. I do not want to
venture out I will just stay at home and console myself," a secretary Ms
Mary Nyaguthie said.
Nyaguthie said the only consolation was that she would not be expected
to show up at her place of work.
"I do not know what I would do if today was not a public holiday," I am
just depressed and relieved that I am not working," Mr Peter Karanja from
Mathira said.
In one homestead there was drama after a brother allegedly went
underground and refused to answer any calls from either relatives or
friends.
"A few days ago, I made a bet with my brother, none of us was very
serious but it was understood that if Yes won I would give him Sh200,000. If
No won, the bet would go away," the trader said.
A car washer, Mr Peter Mwangi Njoroge bragged to his friends how he
unwittingly voted for the winning side after he turned up at the polling
station drunk and marked the ballot wrongly.
"I wanted to vote for our side but I realised that I had made a mistake
the moment I put pen to paper. I was not too drunk to realise that if I
tried to change the vote would be rejected. I let the mistake stand and I
have no regrets," Mwangi added.
Early in the morning, scores of residents who were hungry for news
thronged the new stands to grab a copy of their favourite daily, but were
disappointed because none had arrived by 7am.
"There seems to be a conspiracy by fate. There are no papers," Njeri
Mwangi said.
Meanwhile, all the six constituencies in Nyeri District voted as a block
to deliver 230,851 votes did not vote.
Voters in the six constituencies were 360,000 voters .
An estimated 100,000 eligible voters never turned for the vote.
Mathira constituency delivered the biggest chunk of votes with 55,725 of
the 85,295 registered voters, endorsing the Wako Draft.
In President Kibaki's Othaya constituency where the results were delayed
for some time after disputes arose over some boxes, 36,016 voters backed the
draft document while 315 thought otherwise.
In Prof Wangari Maathai's Tetu constituency 30,714 disregarded the Nobel
Laureate's middle ground stand and voted for the proposed constitution.
Maathai who has persistently declined to commit her self publicly to any
side turned up to be counted in an area with 44,359 registered voters where
255 voted for Orange.
Transport and Communication minister Dr Chris Murungaru camped in his
constituency to ensure that the Banana side carried the day with 44,710,
while 754 objected it
In Mutahi Kagwe's Mukurweini, 31,221 voters supported the Wako Draft out
of a possible 46,811 people eligible to vote. However only 279 voters
rejected the proposed constitution.

Original_Search_Title: Signs of Changing Political Fortunes in Gusii
Headline: Kenya: Signs of Changing Political Fortunes in Gusii
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The shocking defeat suffered by Cabinet Minister Simeon Nyachae in the
referendum vote could be a harbinger of a radically changed political
landscape in Gusiiland.
Nyachae, who a few years ago was the man to beat in Gusiiland, a
popularity he used to contest the Presidency at the last general election,
is now under siege. All the seven MPs who lost to the Orange teams in their
respective constituencies may have themselves to blame as they failed to
read the mood of the electorate.
They ignored the strong resistance to the Draft and instead banked on
