Nyachae's popularity to win. The MPs failed to hold public forums in their
constituencies and assumed that the last minute rallies addressed by Nyachae
would save the day for them.
In general, the Orange win in Gusiiland can be attributed to a unity of
purpose among the last general election's parliamentary and civic losers,
who teamed up and rooted for the Orange.
As the Energy minister came to terms with the humiliation, scores of his
political opponents and Orange supporters demonstrated through Kisii town
yesterday morning, bringing business in the busy town to a standstill for
about two hours. Nyachae, a leading proponent of the proposed constitution
who was expected to deliver the Gusii vote for the Banana, lost in his own
Nyaribari Chache constituency and the entire Gusii, except in two
constituencies.
That Nyachae only managed 9,393 votes against Orange's 10,092, must have
come at a wrong time in his political career bearing in mind that his
inclusion in the Kibaki government a year ago was meant to bolster a
tottering Kibaki administration.
The Ford People leader only addressed two rallies at Nyanturago and
Kiogoro playgrounds in his constituency, to drum up support for the Banana
while he spent most of his time campaigning for his party MPs in Gusii and
in other parts of the country. "He took us for granted and assumed that our
respect to him as our MP would translate into referendum votes. We have
proved to him that we needed to be talked to," charged Jared Momanyi, a
constituent.
Many constituents said they voted against the Banana as a protest
against his leadership style. The loss has left analysts wondering whether
Nyachae's clout in the community's politics has ended and whether he should
hand over the political mantle.

Original_Search_Title: Orange Holds Sway in Central Rift As Old Rivalries Persist
Headline: Kenya: Orange Holds Sway in Central Rift As Old Rivalries Persist
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The massive loss the Banana team suffered in Central Rift is a clear
indication that the communities, who occupy 29 constituencies, do not share
a common political view.
Winning only seven of the constituencies must have been a wake-up call
to the Banana side, while the Orange savoured the victory in the knowledge
that it can be translated to votes in 2007.
Banana leading lights had made much ado about the numbers in Nakuru,
Laikipia, Eldama Ravine, Timboroa and Burnt Forest.
They took Nakuru Town, Molo, Subukia and Naivasha, Laikipia West and
East, but the tally paled compared to what they lost in the other
constituencies.
The loss was in Kuresoi, Rongai, Mogotio, Eldama Ravine, Baringo
Central, Baringo East, Baringo North, Bomet, Konoin, Chepalungu, Ainamoi,
Bureti and Belgut.
Others were Kipkelion, Sotik, Trans Mara, Narok North and South, Samburu
West and East, while Kajiado North and Central went orange, leaving the
Banana with Kajiado South.
It is an interesting outcome, former predominantly Kanu zones remained
loyal to their side, as did the former Democratic Party (DP) areas.
The Kalenjin community made the referendum a protest vote in view of the
Narc Government's removal of their key people from Government offices and
parastatals when it assumed power.
The loss was hard for Cabinet ministers Prof George Saitoti, John Koech,
Assistant ministers Kipkalya Kones and Alicen Chelaite of Rongai.
An ambitious man, Saitoti's loss must have returned him to his senses to
ask himself why his Maasai brethren left him in favour of arch rival,
minister William ole Ntimama.

Original_Search_Title: Catholic Bishop Calls for Reconciliation
Headline: Kenya: Catholic Bishop Calls for Reconciliation
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Catholic Episcopal Conference chairman Bishop Cornelius Korir
yesterday called for dialogue to reconcile the different factions in the
country after the referendum.
Korir also appealed to the leaders to accept the verdict of the
people.
He said President Kibaki should unite the Orange and Banana teams for
the sake of Kenyans.
"People have spoken and we must respect that. We must accept the outcome
and go back to the negotiating table," said Korir.
The Catholic Church was actively involved in the making of the
constitution through the faith-led group, Ufungamano Initiative now wants
the contentious issues discussed.
He added that Kenyans should go back to the negotiating table and start
the process that will see Kenyans get a constitution that they want.
"We can not force something unto them (Kenyans). They have made the
decision and all leaders should respect that," said Korir. Korir who was
speaking on behalf of the 27 Catholic bishops, said both the Yes and No
group should discuss the contentious issues in the proposed constitution and
a consensus reached.
The Eldoret Diocese Bishop said all Kenyans should now settle and start
building the nation after the referendum.
He said he was happy that the common man had finally decided and
defended the Catholic Church's neutral stand.

Original_Search_Title: Another Tough Test for Good Old George
Headline: Kenya: Another Tough Test for Good Old George
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Education Minister Prof George Saitoti is now a man under siege
following the victory by the Orange team in his Kajiado North
constituency. Yesterday's defeat of Saitoti's Banana side rekindles memories
of tribulations that have characterised his 16-year career in politics.
That the Mathematics professor, a key strategist in the Kibaki
administration, has failed to deliver a victory for Banana in his
constituency, leave alone the entire Maasailand leaves a lot to be desired
amongst observers.
While the Orange side polled 22, 099 votes in Kajiado North, Banana
lagged behind by 3,033 votes, bagging 19,096 votes out of the total
74,000 registered voters in the constituency.
The minister is unlikely to regain the clout he cultivated prior to the
2002 General Election that saw him garner 33,000 votes against his opponent,
former Olkejuado County Council Chairman Daniel Ole Muyaa's 8,000.
He was carried high in the National Rainbow Coalition (Narc) wave that
swept the country at the time, following his ejection from the Kanu
succession line up and subsequent defection to the Liberal Democratic
Party. The LDP joined forces with the NAK party then led by Kibaki, Health
Minister Charity Ngilu and the late Vice President Wamalwa Kijana to form
Narc.
But soon after Kibaki ascended the throne, Saitoti started warming up to
the NAK side, despite a breached Memorandum of Understanding.
It was then that Saitoti's colleagues in the LDP, including Roads
Minister Raila Odinga and Environment Minister Kalonzo Musyoka saw him as a
political sell-out. Saitoti has declared that he would never betray the
Government by opposing it, as Raila and Kalonzo have done over the past two
years. The defeat at the referendum is likely to open the flanks against the
minister, who has faced severe storms especially revolving around the issue
of his ambiguous ethnicity.

Original_Search_Title: What Options Are There for President?
Headline: Kenya: What Options Are There for President?
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The resounding rejection of the proposed constitution leaves President
Kibaki and his government in an awkward and humiliating situation.
President Kibaki and all his men went into the exercise full throttle
and put everything on the line to push the document through the
referendum.
The President will now need tact, political skill and extreme courage to
handle the deep divisions in his Cabinet and the humiliating defeat.
The President has come out of the referendum wounded, though not
fatally. He will need to face reality and accept that he needs to go through
the process of rapprochement because in the results, the voters are saying:
"This document is not a fair representation of our goals and aspirations."
Apart from the fact that the document had various fundamental flaws,
some of President Kibaki's close ministers who sought to convince the voters
on the merits of the document are seen as sore thumbs in his Cabinet. He may
have to purge his Cabinet to deal with these ministers, some of who have
been tainted by allegations of corruption, which the President has dismissed
without any investigations.
President Kibaki is consistently being seen as reneging on his
pre-election pledge of zero-tolerance to corruption and he may need to
decisively make a break from the past and actually ensure that corruption
suspects, irrespective of their positions, are hauled to court.
Before the vote, the President, with all his men (and women) cheering
him on, threatened to fire the "rebel" ministers who were opposed to the
Wako Draft with the hope that Yes would carry the day at the plebiscite. The
"rebel" ministers - Raila Odinga, Kalonzo Musyoka, Najib Balala, Ochillo
Ayacko, Anyang' Nyong'o, Jebii Kilimo and William Ole Ntimama - have made it
clear that they don't mind being fired for opting to stand with the majority
of Kenyans against the proposed constitution. The Orange win has complicated
this because firing them would be interpreted as a slap in the face of the
majority of voters who said No to the proposed constitution. It would not be
advisable to fire the ministers that opted to support the popular view.
Alternatively, President Kibaki could opt to fire them and learn to live
with the consequences. If he were to sack them, he would then have to
contend with a massive crossover in Parliament of dissenting MPs to the
opposition benches and prepare for bruising battles.
Deep wounds have been opened up not just in the Cabinet, but also across
the country and the President will have to quickly come up with a healing
programme. Trust in the Cabinet is at its lowest and needs to be buoyed. He
will have to play the role of father and statesman to call on his Cabinet to
forgive one another and concentrate on their mandate of serving the people
even if they sharply differed during the referendum campaigns.
The President will also have to rebuild his image of a statesman after
the events of the referendum campaigns that saw him taking on the No
campaigners with his bare knuckles. Initially, he stayed above the din and
noise of the campaigns even though he had declared his support for the
proposed document. But he later hit the campaign trail and to the surprise
of both his critics and admirers, dished out freebies to different
communities and public servants, which were largely seen as bribes to sway
the referendum vote.
The President needs to urgently recruit a strategist. Right from the
onset, the Banana camp failed to portray a national image. By opting to send
MPs back to their constituencies to campaign at the grassroots, they
appeared disjointed, regional and uncoordinated as opposed to the Orange
camp, which portrayed a national image and moved together in massive unity
and divided into various groups with the accuracy of disciplined
troops.

Original_Search_Title: Is Tuju Facing the Sunset of His Political Career?
Headline: Kenya: Is Tuju Facing the Sunset of His Political Career?
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The referendum poll seem to have settled the bitter political rivalry
between Information minister Raphael Tuju and his Roads counterpart, Raila
Odinga.
If anything, the plebiscite went ahead to stamp Raila's political
supremacy beyond Luo-Nyanza to the Abagusii community, previously in the
grip of Energy minister Simeon Nyachae.
The Orange team's overwhelming triumph in Tuju's Rarieda constituency by
23,623 against a paltry 1,310 Yes votes was telling.
The disaffection with the Banana, and by extension Tuju, was witnessed
in his village polling station of Ramba Primary School where he only
garnered 64 votes against 864.
This is the station he shares with Mr Moses Owiti alias Malo Malo, whom
he beat in the controversial Narc nomination at the last General
Election.
At a No campaign rally in Ndori market in Rarieda, Raila revealed that
he helped Tuju after he had lost to Owiti.
Tuju, stung by the revelation, hit back with bribery claims in the
nomination, and challenged Raila to state what he had received for the
help.
But the attack, coming hot on the heels of the death of four people in
campaign violence that also left 40 other people seriously injured in
Kisumu, only dealt him a further political blow.
International Commission of Jurists chairman, Otiende Amolo, and
consultant engineer, Nicholas Gumbo, have spent most of their time in the
area to counter the minister's political gains.
Sports minister Ochilo Ayacko has on several occasions hinted that Tuju
would be replaced in 2007.

Original_Search_Title: Kajwang' Labels Government As 'Unpopular'
Headline: Kenya: Kajwang' Labels Government As 'Unpopular'
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Mbita MP, Otieno Kajwang' said yesterday the poll outcome showed the
Government was made up of unpopular leaders.
There was now need to inculcate in Government, the culture of
consultation and acceptance of divergent opinion, Kajwang' said.
He said the magnitude of arrogance exhibited by top Government officials
during the campaigns, if left unchecked, could plunge the country into
chaos.
He urged them to seek reconciliation. Kajwang' said the verdict of the
people was clear and urged the Government to immediately embark on a process
that would give Kenyans the desired constitutional and political reforms
once and for all.
He said the poll results should not be used as a yardstick to intimidate
certain individuals or communities, but should be viewed as a healthy
declaration by the electorate that they were against an imposed draft.
Speaking to The Standard on the telephone, Kajwang' said the people
should be given the chance to participate in key decision making areas,
especially constitutional reforms, which directly affects their lives.
"The people should be given the chance to make decisions because this
will shun controversies and unnecessary squabbles," said Kajwang'.
He said a good constitution should be the fountain against which
national leadership sprouts.
He added that peaceful co-existence among Kenyans and equitable
disbursement of national resources would be achieved.
Meanwhile, Machakos residents yesterday celebrated with song and dance
after the Orange team won in all the six constituencies in the
district.
The Orange team won with huge margins in Machakos Town, Kangundo, Yatta,
Masinga, Kathiani and Mwala constituencies.
MPs Moffat Maitha, Charles Kilonzo, Daudi Mwanzia and Itwiku Mbai were
in the Orange team, while Assistant minister Kyallo Kaindi and Mutua Katuku
were in the Banana camp.
Jubilant Orange supporters took to the streets in Kangundo, Tala,
Machakos, Matuu, Kathiani and Mwala.
Maitha criticised the police for failing to arrest those accused of
buying voters cards.
Maitha added that poor voter turnout had been experienced in many poling
stations.
Mwanzia said the provincial administration had been used in some parts
to intimidate voters.
He said the elections reflected the wishes of the majority of
Kenyans.
"No leader will now dare to call Kenyans wapumbavu (stupid). Kenyans are
clever and have made their choice," said Mwanzia.

Original_Search_Title: Mwai Kibaki Loses Kenya Referendum
Headline: Kenya: Mwai Kibaki Loses Kenya Referendum
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kenyan President Mwai Kibaki has conceded defeat after a clear majority
voted against a new constitution in Monday's referendum.
The result was a humiliation for Kibaki who led the "Yes" campaign
(Bananas) against a spirited fight from the "No" side (Oranges) headed by
five of his senior ministers, including Roads minister Raila Oginga, and the
Official Opposition leader Uhuru Kenyatta.
Kibaki told the nation yesterday, "The people have made a choice and as
I have always said, my government would respect the choice of the people."
In the address from State House, Nairobi, Kibaki sought to downplay the
defeat, saying there were no winners or losers in the referendum as the
objective was to determine the people's choice. That choice had been made,
he said.
Soon after Kibaki's concession speech, which was broadcast live on TV
and radio, the Electoral Commission chairman, Mr Samuel Kivuitu, announced
that the Orange-No Team had won the referendum with 3,548477 votes (57 per
cent) against the Banana-Yes group's 2,532918 votes (43 per cent).
The turnout of 6,081395 was higher than the 5,815896 realised during the
2002 General Election that saw Kibaki's National Rainbow Coalition sweep
Daniel arap Moi's KANU out of power in December 2002.
The BBC reported that although the referendum was not a vote of
confidence on Kibaki's three-year old administration, "there is no question
that many Kenyans will have voted 'No' in part because they are
disillusioned with the government which has been mired in corruption."
Both the Yes and No camps said the referendum exercise, the country's
first ever, had strengthened democracy in Kenya and congratulated Kenyans
for carrying out the exercise peacefully. A victorious Orange Team yesterday
invited their Banana group rivals to roundtable discussions to restart talks
on a new constitution.
"Now that the people of Kenya have decisively spoken, and in the spirit
of genuine reconciliation, we invite our colleagues in the Banana camp to
join us in expeditiously charting the way forward for a new constitutional
dispensation for our country," the leaders said in a statement read by
opposition leader Kenyatta.
They added: "Today we are united as a nation in a renewed quest for a
better Kenya. The people have comprehensively and with large majorities
voted to strengthen democracy, limit presidential power and devolve power to
local communities."
But opponents accused Kibaki of reneging on promises to devolve some of
the president's powers to the prime minister, a new post proposed in the
draft Constitution.
After conceding defeat, Kibaki commended all voters for participating
peacefully in the referendum, saying it was a major leap in the
consolidation of democratic governance in Kenya. Earlier, the chairman of
the Banana-Yes campaign committee, Mr Simeon Nyachae, had written to the
Electoral Commission conceding defeat.
His letter read: "I wish to formally inform you that the Yes Vote
referendum committee, having examined the results which you have already
declared for the referendum, have concluded that the results of the
remaining constituencies will not make a difference in the overall verdict
of the referendum. "We therefore accept that the majority of the people of
Kenya have rejected the proposed new constitution. We congratulate the
Electoral Commission and the people of Kenya for conducting a peaceful
referendum."

Original_Search_Title: A Resounding 'No'
Headline: Kenya: Politics-Kenya: a Resounding 'No'
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kenyans have decisively rejected a proposed constitution that was put to a referendum on Monday.
According to figures released by the Electoral Commission of Kenya
(ECK), 3,548,477 people voted against the new constitution (57 percent
of those who cast ballots), while 2,532, 918 came out in favour of it.
"This vote has clearly shown that many Kenyans have rejected the
proposed constitution," a dejected President Mwai Kibaki said in a
televised address Tuesday. "This is a giant step towards enhancing
democracy. My government will respect the verdict of the people."
Kibaki and his National Alliance Party of Kenya (NAK) had pushed for
approval of the constitution, prompting a split in the ruling National
Rainbow Coalition. Roads and Public Works Minister Raila Odinga, head
of the Liberal Democratic Party, lobbied for a "no" vote in Monday's
poll -- teaming up with the opposition Kenya African National Union to
convince citizens to reject the document.
Those supporting the constitution adopted the banana as their
symbol, while those opposed to it used the orange -- this to assist
illiterate Kenyans to cast their votes.
Opponents of the proposed constitution claimed that it gave too much
power to the head of state; and, they demanded a return to an earlier
draft which shared executive authority between the president and the
newly-created post of prime minister.
This earlier version is referred to as the 'Bomas draft', in
reference to 'Bomas of Kenya': a cultural venue on the outskirts of the
capital, Nairobi. Here, the National Constitutional Conference --
comprising delegates from government and civil society -- held talks
during 2003 and 2004 on what form the country's new constitution should
take.
Their talks followed nation-wide consultations by the Constitution
of Kenya Review Commission, appointed in 2000 to gather the views of
Kenyans on what measures they wished to see in their new constitution.
The commission reported that citizens wanted a reduction in
presidential power, apparently in reaction to abuses under former heads
of state Daniel arap Moi and Jomo Kenyatta.
The version of the constitution that was put to voters Monday
emerged in July this year when NAK parliamentarians changed the Bomas
draft to maintain strong presidential powers -- and to stipulate a
non-executive prime minister who would be appointed by the head of
state.
Monday's vote took placed largely without incident, in marked
contrast to the campaign for the referendum, which claimed eight lives.
Only one outbreak of violence occurred, in Kibera -- said to be
Africa's largest slum -- located in Nairobi. Chaos erupted in the
Langata constituency of this settlement when a group of youths pelted a
truck driver with stones on the suspicion that he was trying to rig the
vote. This came after the driver defied orders to show what was in his
truck when he was entering a polling station.
Almost 20,000 local and 150 foreign observers -- and close to 60,000
security personnel -- were on hand to monitor the historic exercise,
which saw long queues of voters brave the morning chill to congregate
at polling stations before they opened at 07.00 local time.
"I came here to vote and I believe my vote will have an impact to
this process. I, with my two small children, were here by five a.m,"
Florence Makokha, a voter at Nairobi's Embakasi constituency, told IPS.
Certain irregularities were reported, such as names being absent
from registers or appearing twice. Many people arrived at polling
stations with passports rather than national identity cards, as
required, but were later allowed to vote.
With Kenyans having rejected the proposed constitution, the
country's colonial-era constitution will remain in force until such
time as a decision is made about embarking on another constitutional
review process.
During the announcement of referendum results at Kenyatta
International Conference Centre in Nairobi, ECK Chairman Samuel Kivuitu
warned citizens against allowing constitutional review to be put on the
backburner.
"It is imperative that we continue to strive for a better
constitution, one that seeks to unite us, one that recognises that the
power to govern rests on the people." he noted, amid wild cheers from
hundreds opposed to the constitution who had gathered at the centre to
witness the official announcement of results.
Kibaki initially promised to have a new constitution in place within
a hundred days of assuming office at the end of 2002. Almost three
years later, Kenyans are still waiting.
Irrespective of what occurs on the constitutional front, however,
speculation is already rife that the outcome of Monday's vote will lead
to changes in government -- particularly with elections looming in 2007.

Original_Search_Title: Kenyans Reject Proposed Constitution
Headline: Kenyans Reject Proposed Constitution
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kenyan voters have decisively rejected the proposed Constitution.
Results announced by the Electoral Commission of Kenya indicate that the
Orange camp opposed to the proposed Constitution has clinched 58 per cent of
votes cast in yesterday's referendum.
The Orange camp with 3.5 million votes is ahead of the Yes or Banana
camp by over 1 million votes.
Although not all votes have been counted, the results announced by ECK
chairman Mr Samuel Kivuitu, shows that the Banana camp managed about 2.5
million votes, about 42 per cent of the total votes cast. Mr Kivuitu,
however, says the votes not yet counted are not substantial enough to turn
the tables.
Some 6.08 million people of the 11.8 million registered voters cast the
ballot.
In a Press conference soon after the results were announced, the Orange
camp in a statement read by opposition leader Uhuru Kenyatta said "the
people had triumphed".
He said by voting overwhelmingly against the proposed Constitution
Kenyans had proved that "the future of the country lies not with imperial
Presidency but with accountable leadership."
Mr Kenyatta said the vote had proved that Kenyans will never accept to
return to "the dark days of dictatorship and lack of accountability".
He added: "We appeal to our brothers and sisters in central Kenya to
reject any leadership that is divisive..."
The Orange camp led in all provinces apart from Central Province.
Shortly before the results were announced, President Mwai Kibaki in a TV
address to Kenyans, conceded that the Yes camp had lost in the
referendum.
"Many Kenyans have opposed the Constitution," the President said
After casting his ballot yesterday, President Kibaki had predicted
victory for his camp which he said was winning "in this most important and
historic event."
"We have come to vote and I am optimistic we shall succeed. It's over,"
he said.
In his address today, the President said the country had accomplished a
historical duty and termed the referendum a "major step in bolstering
democracy in our country". He thanked Kenyans for taking part in the
referendum.
The head of State said the country would continue to be governed through
the current Constitution. Future constitution review endeavors will be
guided by this Constitution, he said.
Meanwhile, Kibaki urged Kenyans to focus their attention on nation
building and assured that the Government would continue to play its part in
development.
Thousands of jubilant Orange camp supporters took to Nairobi streets
with song and dance immediately it became clear that their camp was headed
for victory.
The No camp has all along argued that the Presidency would retain
massive powers if the proposed Constitution was approved.
But Kibaki and his brigade in the Yes camp have argued that the powers
of the Presidency would have been dramatically reduced by the proposed
Constitution.

Original_Search_Title: Coast : Poll Officials Slow, Mayor Laments And Alleges Graft
Headline: Kenya: Coast : Poll Officials Slow, Mayor Laments And Alleges Graft
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Polling centres opened on time in Mombasa with few incidents of election malpractices reported.
In Mvita, Mombasa mayor Taib Ali Taib claimed that several political
leaders allied to the Banana camp were bribing voters at the Majengo
Village hall polling centre.
But the centre's presiding officer, Mr Juma Musa, refuted the claims
and said there were no bribery incidents reported at his post.
At the Stadium and Tononoka polling stations, the voting was slow
and Mr Taib protested that this was deliberate to lock out Orange
supporters from voting by the close of the day.
National Heritage minister Najib Balala cried foul over broadcasting
stations that continued to broadcast the President's speech on voting
day.
Security was tight in most of the polling centres as voters slowly trickled in to cast their votes.
In Kisauni, there was tension between Orange supporters and police
manning the Star of the Sea polling centre when the security officers
tried to disperse the No group shortly after they had cast their votes.
Kisauni's Orange team coordinator Ali Hassan Joho said that it was
wrong for the police to disperse voters as it was their right to
oversee the counting process.
At Kagujo polling centre, some of the voters' names were spelt
differently in their identity cards and in the voters' register,
prompting some them to protest before they were allowed to vote.
Kisauni MP Anania Mwaboza cast his vote at 8.30am at the centre and
said that there were propaganda leaflets claiming that voters would be
given Sh500 each by the Banana team to vote in its favour. "The
leaflets have been circulated by the Orange team," he said.
In Lamu West, 10 Banana agents were locked out of Lamu secondary and
primary schools because they did not have properly signed
identification documents from their head agent in Nairobi.
Their problem was sorted out after two hours when a fax from Nairobi
confirmed that they were genuine agents for the Banana camp.
In Lamu East, the ECK officials were provided with a satellite
telephones to monitor the polling in Mangai, Basibo and Marani polling
stations that do not have any other form communication.
At Changamwe's St Mary Primary School, voters and area MP Ramadhan
Kajembe complained that the process was being deliberately slowed down.
In Kinango, the Banana agents did not show up in three stations -
Bonje, Chizini and Mwamdudu. In Voi voter turn out was poor in at least
13 stations with most businesspeople saying that they would vote in the
afternoon.
In another case two people who had been indicated in the register as
having died - one in 1997 and another in 2002, surfaced at Kombani
polling station and were allowed to vote.
In Garsen a vehicle transporting officials and balloting materials
overturned, seriously injuring one of the passengers. However, the
police arrived quickly and secured all the materials.

Original_Search_Title: Nyanza: Security Stepped Up As Voters Troop to Stations
Headline: Kenya: Nyanza: Security Stepped Up As Voters Troop to Stations
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Thousands of regular and administration police officers were spread out in all parts of Nyanza Province to ensure peace.
Provincial police spokesman Albert Waweru said no incidents of lawlessness had occurred and that security would be maintained round the clock.
In major towns like Kisumu, Kisii and Siaya, a heavy police presence was witnessed near polling stations while other officers patrolled major highways. Most business premises in the lakeside town remained closed.
Kisumu Town residents turned out in their thousands to vote amid claims that some names were missing from the voters' register.
Polling stations opened on time. Long queues had formed at the stations as early as 5.30am, the highest turnout recorded at the Aga Khan Hall in Kisumu Town West Constituency.
But confusion reigned at several polling stations when some voters found their names missing. The worst affected were Manyatta, Social Centre and Pand Pieri polling centres, where scores of people were turned away by presiding officers.
Two Orange polling agents at the Social Centre said many people, mostly women, had found their names missing. At Manyatta Primary school, polling agents said that about 100 people had by 11am been denied a chance to vote.
Said agent George Anayo: "We are shocked at what is going on here. Names have disappeared from the registers and we are demanding an explanation from the Electoral Commission."
Two of the affected voters, Ms Elizabeth Otieno and Ms Martha Apiyo, said they arrived at the polling station at 6.30am and were shocked to learn that their names were missing from the registers.
But Kisumu Town East's returning officer, Canon S. J. Owuor, denied the claim, saying there were only isolated cases. In Muhoroni and Nyakach constituencies, voters jammed the stations at the break of dawn and local leaders said they were happy with the turnout.
Nyakach MP Peter Odoyo said: "We have not witnessed any ugly incident. The exercise is peaceful and we expect it to end well." But poor turnout marked the poll in most parts of Kisii and Nyamira districts. Banana team leader Simeon Nyachae - the Energy minister - cast his vote at Nyosia Primary at 9.30am.
He said the Banana team would respect the voters' verdict.
Voting was disrupted for several hours at the Egesieri polling centre, West Mugirango, Nyamira, when a drunken man scared away voters with a dead snake. He was later arrested.
Complaints that some voters were selling their cards were lodged with the district electoral commission officer.
There was a low turnout in most centres visited by the Nation. About 193,000 voters were expected to cast their votes in the district.
An assistant minister's wife was turned away as her name did not appear in the register.
The Electoral Commission of Kenya allowed Mr Wilfred Machage to vote but turned away his wife Mueni at the St Kizito polling centre.
About 50 other people failed to cast their ballots due to similar anomalies.
Dr Machage said the Yes side would accept the verdict and appealed to the Orange camp to accept the outcome if defeated.
Claims of vote buying in parts of Migori District marred the poll.
Uriri MP Odhiambo Omamba said he had received names of people believed to have been buying cards for Sh1,000 each. Similar reports were received from Migori constituency. Confusion reigned at the Oruba and Migori primary school polling stations when potential voters were turned away by the omission.
In Siaya District, voting went on smoothly. Long queues were witnessed at various polling stations as eager voters turned out in large numbers.
Various polling centres visited by the Kenya News Agency in Ugenya, Alego/Usonga and Gem constituencies were peaceful as the voters keenly followed the instructions issued by commission officials.
At the Siaya County hall, a queue almost 100 metres long formed as early as 6am, an hour before the station opened. There was also a high turnout at Sega, Sigomere and Madungu in Ugenya, and Sagam Primary in Gem constituency.
Returning officer Obare Awora said he was satisfied with the arrangements and that the turn out in the morning had been encouraging. He asked the public to ensure peace prevailed.

Original_Search_Title: Central: I'm Hopeful, Says President
Headline: Kenya: Central: I'm Hopeful, Says President
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki yesterday predicted victory for the Banana camp.
The team, he said, was winning "in this most important and historic event."
Addressing thousands of Othaya constituents outside Munaini Primary School, his home's polling station, he said: "We have come to vote and I am optimistic we shall succeed. It's over."
But he accused the Orange leaders of hate campaigns.
He urged Kenyans to go back to work once the result is announced.
The President, who was with most members of his family, arrived at the polling station, about 1km away, just before 11am.
He was received by the local leaders, and immediately went to the voting centre and was taken through the normal procedure.
He gave out his national identity and elector's cards, and his name was checked on the roll before he dipped his finger into a bottle of ink after casting his ballot.
Behind him were First Lady Lucy and their three sons, daughter Judy and a daughter-in-law. Also present was granddaughter Muthoni who, however, did not vote as she is underage.
After voting, the President addressed the residents on the Othaya-Kiriani road.
Mrs Kibaki also addressed the gathering for less than a minute and declared: "It's the Banana team that won in the morning."
The President and his team addressed crowds at several places in the constituency. He promised the Chinga Location residents that the local roads would soon be rehabilitated.
Voters in Othaya turned up in large numbers and, according to the election officials, very few people had not cast their votes by the afternoon.
The constituency has 51,788 registered voters and 80 polling stations.
There was a big turnout in Kieni in the morning and, by midday, there were no queues at most polling stations.
But at Munyu Primary School, where Transport minister Chris Murungaru voted at noon, several people were turned away as they did not register in the area, while others did not have IDs.
Dr Murungaru said 50 per cent of the registered voters had cast their ballots by noon.
Speaking after voting, he said the Banana side would accept the result as "it will be the decision of Kenyans." Most polling stations in Mathioya had registered 70 per cent turnout by midday, except the Muthangari polling station.
But tension was high at Njumbi, (it has 2,308 registered voters - the largest number of registered voters in the constituency) with two elderly women walking out, complaining of poor coordination by the Electoral Commission. Area MP Joseph Kamotho voted at 11am.
Nobel laureate Wangari Maathai regretted that the Government had not postponed the referendum as she voted at Ihithe Primary School in her Tetu constituency at about 10.15am.
"The people were not prepared for the poll since a consensus had not been arrived at between supporters and opponents of the proposed constitution," she said.
She was the 187th voter at the station, which registered 972. Prof Maathai said since her calls for postponement were not heeded, she had no alternative but to exercise her democratic right. She appealed to losers to accept the results to ensure that peace prevailed after the referendum.
Water minister Martha Karua was the first to vote at Mugumo Primary School in her Gichugu constituency at 7am.
She said she was impressed by a big turnout, and urged the residents to be peaceful during the poll. In the Mount Kenya region, voters started arriving at polling stations as early as 5am.
Some stations opened shortly before 7am, and the queues remained long in the morning.
In parts of larger the Meru, the turnout was overwhelming at some stations, forcing the presiding officers to open more booths. In northern Kenya - Isiolo, Marsabit and Moyale - voting also went on smoothly in the morning.
Reports by Wilfred Muchire, Muchiri Gitonga, Waikwa Maina, Mwangi Ndirangu, George Munene and Muchemi Wachira

Original_Search_Title: Show Cards to Vote, MPs Told
Headline: Kenya: Show Cards to Vote, MPs Told
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Three MPs were barred from voting in yesterday's referendum - because they failed to produce proper documents.
In Makueni District, assistant minister Kivutha Kibwana was turned away from Mulala polling station in his Makueni constituency when he failed to produce his national identity card. He told the presiding officer, Mr James Kithu, that he lost the card recently.
Prof Kibwana had arrived at the station at 6.30am. Despite his pleas, Mr Kithu was adamant and referred the MP to the law which states that only a registered voter - with valid voter's and identity cards - could cast a ballot.
The polling station has 815 registered voters. Prof Kibwana left the station and promised to return after sorting out the problem at a higher level. But by 4pm, Prof Kibwana had not returned.
In Mombasa, Likoni MP Suleiman Shakombo had to make frantic efforts to find his voter's and identity cards from his Nairobi home to enable him to vote at Longo Primary School. The MP had been leading the Yes campaign in Coast Province before the vote.
He arrived at the polling station where he admitted that he had forgotten his documents in Nairobi. It was not immediately clear why he had not made earlier arrangements to have the documents brought to him before the polling started.
Mr Shakombo was miffed when the presiding officer, Ms Millicent Achieng, insisted that he had to produce his documents before he could vote. The MP insisted on being allowed to vote because he could remember his identity card number but to no avail.
Mr Shakombo was rescued when he called a relative in Nairobi who despatched the all important documents to him by the 1pm Kenya Airways flight. He voted at 2pm.
In Shinyalu, MP Daniel Khamasi was turned away at a polling station after he failed to produce his identity card.
He told polling clerks at Shamirori polling station that he had lost the card but the officials stood their ground, citing the rules.
Mr Khamasi later went to Kakamega Police Station where he obtained an abstract after reporting the loss of the ID card.
"I have actually managed to sort out the hitch and voted," the legislator told the Nation later in the day.
The returning officer, Mrs Rita Omusolo, declined to give any details when contacted.
In Kakamega Town, several voters got a rude shock when they found the polling registers reflected that they were dead.
One of the affected was a woman at Nabongo Primary School polling station while Mr Charles Ogutu was turned away at Ndani Muslim Primary school.
Accusations of voter bribery were reported at the ministry of Public Works polling station, Bondeni and Christ Church. Banana team agents were blamed for dishing out money to voters outside polling stations.
Electoral Commission officials said they were investigating the complaints.
Keiyo South MP Nicholas Biwott abstained from voting.
Mr Biwott has been calling for the ironing out of contentious issues in the proposed Constitution before subjecting it to a referendum. He said he abstained due to division and acrimony which had dogged the review process.
"Politics took centre stage in the referendum campaign and deviated from issues. That is why I stayed away," Mr Biwott said.
The former minister said he had wanted the No and Yes sides to iron out their disagreements over the proposed Constitution before taking it to voters.
Mr Biwott said Parliament should have gone the 1997 way when MPs - through the Inter-Parties Parliamentary Group - were able to solve a similar stalemate.
After the dissolving of the Middle Ground Group of MPs, which was spearheaded by assistant minister Noah Wekesa, Mr Biwott decided to remain neutral.
Though he did not discourage his constituents from voting, he said the debate on the Constitution had divided Kenyans along tribal lines and the Government should look for ways to end the division after the referendum.
"Leaders from the No and Yes side should sit down with experts and iron out the contentious issues and we move on," Mr Biwott said.
Speaking at his father's home in Mokwo, Mr Biwott said his prediction that chaos would mar referendum campaigns became true when eight people were killed in Kisumu and Mombasa. This could have been avoided if Parliament had intervened as it had done in 1997.
Mr Biwott said he was happy that leaders from both sides saw the need to have peace and there was no chaos in the twin rallies held by the Banana and Orange camps in Nairobi's Uhuru Park and Nyayo Stadium.
"I am calling on Kenyans not to let instability erode what we have achieved," Mr Biwott said.
Reported by Tony Kago, John Kisu, Peter Ng'etich and Benson Amadala

Original_Search_Title: Eastern : Lack of Materials Delays Voting By Five Hours
Headline: Kenya: Eastern : Lack of Materials Delays Voting By Five Hours
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Voting at a station in Kibwezi constituency was delayed for five hours due to lack of materials. Eager voters at Kanaani Primary School waited as Electoral Commission of Kenya (ECK) officials made frantic efforts to get ballot papers, boxes and a voters' register.
Shortly after midday, 100 ballot papers and a box were delivered to the station, with about 126 registered voters.
Area MP, Kalembe Ndile, who had voted at Nguumo Primary School, arrived at Kibwezi Township Primary School with supporters and attempted to enter one of the classrooms.
A crowd heckled him, prompting ECK officials and security personnel to ask him to leave.
The voter turnout in Makueni constituency was high, with most stations visited by the Nation recording more than 60 per cent by 2pm.
More than 2,000 bags of maize were dished out at trading centres across Kitui District on the eve of the referendum. Health minister Charity Ngilu, who spearheaded the distribution, said she went round meeting her supporters but denied that the distribution had anything to do with the campaigns.
Banana agents monitoring voting in North Horr constituency in Marsabit District yesterday failed to take up their places at polling stations after they were warned by their rivals of dire consequences.
The constituency, represented by Orange crusader Bonaya Godana, is mainly inhabited by the Gabra community.
On Saturday, three vehicles the ECK had hired to transport election materials were barred from entering the area because they belonged to the community's rivals - the Boranas, area district commissioner Amos Gathecha said yesterday.
In North Imenti constituency, there was a huge turnout at polling stations.
However, voting at most centres was slow, with officials using one manual register for polling centres with multiple streams of voters.
Some polling station in Tigania East constituency opened half an hour before the official time after enthusiastic voters turned up early.
Area MP Peter Munya cast his ballot at 10.20am at Ncuui Primary School.
Voting took off smoothly in Isiolo North constituency's 74 polling stations. A spot-check by Nation revealed a high turnout at most polling stations. The returning officer Ibrahim Shabo said the referendum started on time in the three divisions of Merti, Oldonyiro and Central with 31,926 registered voters.
The assistant minister for Health, Dr Mohammed Kuti, cast his vote at Isiolo Boys.
Councillor Elijah Mugambi of Isiolo East ward was ejected out of Wabera main voting centre in Isiolo Town as he tried to canvass for votes from the hundreds of voters at the station. The voters told him off, accusing him of having hibernated during the campaign period.
The voters, both men and women who started streaming to their respective polling centres as early as 6am said they wanted to cast their votes before the scorching sun took its toll on them.
A Moyale businessman was lost for words when he found that he was marked dead in the electors' roll.
Mr Roba Guyo, waving his identity and his voter's cards, demanded to know how he died in 1997 as indicated in the register.
However, he was barred from voting by the presiding officer Mr Sharu Muhdin at Moyale Township Primary School.
More than half of the registered voters in Mbeere District had not cast their ballots by 3.30pm.
At Mwondo Primary School, with 900 registered voters, only 300 had voted by that time, while at St Luke's in Gachoka constituency, 400 out of 1,200 registered voters had turned up.

Original_Search_Title: Nairobi: Scores Turned Away Over Papers
Headline: Kenya: Nairobi: Scores Turned Away Over Papers
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Voter turnout was high in all the eight constituencies. But scores of people were turned away when the Electoral Commission officials did not find their names in the official register.
Voters a wait their turn to cast their ballot at Bidii Primary School in Makadara constituencyin Nairobi, yesterday.
Photo/Joseph Mathenge
The officials said they would not allow the people and others without either the national identity cards or passports to vote.
Voting was disrupted in Westlands for more than an hour when ballot papers were found with marks.
The Orange or the Banana symbol against which the voter was supposed to mark, had a dot which was said to have been imprinted.
There was further confusion at Kilimani and Loresho polling stations when more than 80 voters found their names missing.
Kilimani presiding officer Wangari Kariuki said some were turned away for double registration.
The officials were forced to check the manual registers to see if the missing names were included.
But quite a few went back disappointed when their names were not found.
Mr Moses Okemwa was one of those whose names were not in the computer or manual registers, although he had an elector's card for the station.
"I cannot understand why my name is missing yet I voted in 2002," he said.
At Loresho Primary School, more than 30 people had their names missing.
Ms Gladys Sirinji, who was turned away because she was found to have enrolled twice, told the Nation that she voted in 2002 without any hitch, and was shocked that her details were missing.
Westlands MP Fred Gumo stormed the Kenya College of Communications Technology polling station in Loresho where the anomaly was discovered.
Voting was delayed for more than an hour as he tried to sort out the problem with the returning officer, Mr Anthony Maina.
The MP had earlier gone to the media centre at Kenyatta International Conference Centre to complain that about 2,800 supporters had been barred from voting in Kilimani and Lavington primary schools.
A member of the Electoral Commission, Mr Jack Tumwa, asked four officials to escort him to the polling stations to address the matter.
Mr Maina agreed with the MP that all ballot books found with the marks be declared spoilt.
And as tension rose with the MP's supporters claiming that there were attempts to rig the poll, the officials called Gigiri police chief Patrick Lumumba.
As the confusion reigned, the officials held onto the ballot box.
However, Mr Gumo's attempt to help other voters was rejected, and Mr Lumumba asked him to leave the station.
As Lang'ata MP Raila Odinga cast his ballot, he urged Kenyans to maintain peace, saying that he was confident his Orange team would win.
The Roads minister voted at 8.25am at the Old Kibera Primary School where hundreds of voters had turned up as early as 4am.
"I urge Kenyans to exercise restraint and demonstrate that we Kenyans can be peaceful in exercising our democratic rights," he said, and warned that any attempt to rig the poll would be meaningless, arguing that the No team would win anyway.
In Dagoretti, MP Beth Mugo, of Banana, said there were many complaints of voters' names missing. Many others, she said, had presented themselves with driving licences, but were turned away.
At one station, voting was too slow as a polling clerk had a sight problem. However, Mrs Mugo said the hitch was sorted out.
The MP for Embakasi, Mr David Mwenje, who recently defected from Orange, said voters were organised and had turned out in large numbers.
In Makadara, a voter was shocked to find out that he had been marked dead. But he was allowed to vote after producing the identification papers.
Area MP Reuben Ndolo, of Orange, complained that many voters had been turned away for alleged double registration. But the turnout in most stations was high.
Voting in Starehe went on peacefully in all the 17 centres. The people started arriving at 6am, although voting started at 7am.
MP Maina Kamanda, of Banana, arrived at 9am at City Primary School where he cast his vote.
But some voters at Kiboro Primary School complained that they did not witness the opening of a ballot box.
Scores of voters in Kamukunji were turned away for lack of identification papers. But Mrs Fatuma Mohammed Ahmed and the others vowed not to leave the station until they were allowed to vote.
By Jillo Kadida, David Mugonyi, Samuel Siringi, Jeff Otieno, Cyrus Kinyungu, Richard Wahome Thuku, Claire Gatheru, Jibril adan, Mark Agutu, Mike Mwaniki

Original_Search_Title: Is a Break-Up of Narc Now Inevitable?
Headline: Kenya: Is a Break-Up of Narc Now Inevitable?
Body_Text:
Nairobi — It did not require any power of clairvoyance to predict that Narc would break up right from its inception. It was only a matter of time before this would happen. The centre is not holding, and steadily, things are beginning to fall apart.
Even in the post-referendum period, there are indications that there will be no big change, whatever the outcome, and that the coalition would turn out to be a nine-day wonder.
Political coalitions are often informed by shared values, near-identical philosophical persuasions, and an identifiable programme of action at any given time.
Unfortunately for Narc, it was cobbled together out of a hotchpotch of negative values, which included deceit, thirst for power, and a philosophy to the effect that the end justifies the means. It was a confluence of political outfits and personalities that came into existence by default and not by design, and hence did not share an ideological vision.
A ragtag coalition formed at the height of Kanu's apparent invincibility, its existence was held together by a tenuous string - opposition to the former ruling party. Soon after it was clear that Kanu was no longer a danger, things began to unravel.
Since Narc came into power, both the letter and spirit of the power-sharing arrangement arrived at between NAK and LDP were rubbished by the former, even before the ink was dry.
The MoUs have since gathered dust. This is the genesis of the intra-coalition and inter-personality clashes in Narc's political structure, which has taken a high toll of this country's governance.
The Cabinet has been going hammer and tongs at each other. It is divided right down the middle and the doctrine of collective responsibility has been relegated to the backwater.
The ministers opposed to the President have been having a field day. They can even call him names.
On the other hand, ministers closely associated with the President have worsened the situation by threatening their colleagues with sacking, imprisonment or alienation in the allocation of national resources. This is anarchy, not democracy. Democracy is not a tabula rasa; it is freedom premised on defined protocol.
But amidst these high-level internecine and fratricidal shows, President Kibaki has not cracked the whip. His insouciance apropos the rebellion threatening his leadership has baffled many. He has reduced himself to a spectator. He can neither sack, nor rein them in. Why has he been turning himself into a lame duck when he has the constitutional power to effect a virtual Armageddon?
The referendum, whose results have been trickling throughout the night, could well be the final straw that broke the camel's back. The brawl has been insufferable and obnoxious.
Towards the end, the campaign was no longer being informed by issues. The debate from both the Banana and Orange camps degenerated into muck-raking, and not the merits and demerits of the Constitution. It polarised the country on tribal lines and eroded trust among Kenyans.
It is clear, then, that the constitutional review debate was a perfect excuse for both sides to throw caution to the winds. For the LDP group, this was a god-sent opportunity to settle political scores. For the NAK crowd, it was an opportunity to try and get rid of a nettlesome partner.
To this end, therefore, genuine and honest engagement on the constitutional review was sacrificed at the altar of political egoism and power-games. This goes to corroborate that our political leaders do not have the national interest at heart. They can go to any length to achieve their ends - even if it means breaking this country apart.
It is to be noted that despite several meetings, the contentious issues in all the drafts were never thrashed out. Political selfishness proved to be too much.
President Kibaki has several options for changing this state of affairs. He can adopt a pacific approach to addressing issues that have polarised the country, and bring about a national healing after the referendum. He will need to mollify the divided Cabinet by devising a win-win formula.
Secondly, he has a choice of dissolving Parliament and seeking a fresh mandate from Kenyans, upon which he will craft a government based on shared persuasions. This argument may sound too academic, but it is, indeed, plausible.
Thirdly, the President has a choice of sacking the rebellious ministers and constituting a cohesive work-force. At the same time, those ministers who feel dissatisfied with his leadership could, in a civil manner, quit the Government and go their own way. The referendum outcome should determine which camp divorces the other.
Kenyans want to see food on their tables and a lot less of political demagogues who have made a habit of gallivanting around the country shamelessly displaying obscene opulence amidst grinding poverty.
This Government should strive to foster an environment of good governance and political stability because these two are, so to speak, the aphrodisiacs that will arouse and energise the economy.
A robust economy is the antidote for addressing the myriad problems afflicting this country. Kenyans deserve a better economy. Narc must therefore fulfil its pre-election pledge on this score.
Unity of purpose is a must for Narc's survival. In fact, it must be warned: it either hangs together and delivers, or its constituents will be hanged separately upon failing to deliver!
Mr Magutt is a researcher on political economy.

Original_Search_Title: Ford-K Plans Unity Talks for Faction Chiefs
Headline: Kenya: Ford-K Plans Unity Talks for Faction Chiefs
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A meeting is scheduled to try to reconcile the Orange and Banana leaders irrespective of the referendum results.
Ford Kenya chairman Musikari Kombo, whose party is the convener of the meeting, has written to Official Opposition leader Uhuru Kenyatta, Roads minister Raila Odinga, Environment's Kalonzo Musyoka and Kanu secretary-general William Ruto inviting them for the November 27 meeting.
"We shall hold a retreat in Mombasa to discuss the aftermath of the campaigns and the results," Mr Kombo said after he and wife Dorcas had cast their votes at Matulo, Webuye constituency.
Mr Ruto, who spoke for Orange, which campaigned against the Wako Draft, welcomed the meeting, saying: "It is good our colleagues have realised the importance of talking to each other.
"We have been ready to talk since Bomas and that is why we went to Naivasha." The Eldoret North MP had cast his vote in Kamagut.
Mr Kombo said the meeting would seek a way forward after the referendum to enable leaders to bury their differences.
"Whatever the results, Kenya should remain one country and one government under President Kibaki," the minister said. "Your neighbour will remain your neighbour after the referendum."
Saying the letters to the leaders would be dispatched today, Mr Kombo asked his Cabinet colleagues to quit if they are dissatisfied with the Government.
Mr Kombo defended President Kibaki's leadership from criticism over his televised message on the referendum on Sunday saying the Head of State had shown he was a statesman by calling on Kenyans to turn up to vote.

Original_Search_Title: Minister Calls for a Healing Process After Historic Vote
Headline: Kenya: Ngilu Calls for a Healing Process After Historic Vote
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Health minister Charity Ngilu yesterday called for reconciliation between the Banana and the Orange camps after the referendum.
Ngilu said the country needed healing and rebuilding of trust among the leaders to forge development.
Speaking after casting her vote at Ithookwe Primary School in her Kitui Central Constituency, Ngilu regretted the animosity that had been generated during the campaigns. She voted at 8.40am.
"If we had campaigned more responsibly and soberly, the country would not have gone through all this. I am worried about what will happen later," Ngilu said.
The minister said Kenyans had been sharply divided by politicians, adding that there will be life after the referendum.
Voting started on time at various polling stations in Kitui Central, Mutito and Kitui West constituencies.
There were queues of wananchi waiting to vote at Kitui Central Primary School, Muslim Primary School and Ithookwe Primary School polling stations.
At Kavalula Primary School in Mutito Constituency and Matinyani Primary School in Kitui West, few voters were at the stations in the morning.
But no complains or hitches were reported with voting going on smoothly.
MPs Kiema Kilonzo (Mutito) and Mrs Nyiva Mwendwa (Kitui West) voted at Kavalula and Matinyani AIC Primary Schools polling stations.
Meanwhile, an assistant minister Prof Kivutha Kibwana, claimed that some chiefs and their assistants in Makueni constituency were intimidating voters.
They were said to working for the Banana camp.
Kivutha said that the elite members of the constituency had travelled from urban centres were also involved.
"Voters, especially elderly women stayed away fearing chaos at the polling stations after word went round that some people wanted to disrupt the exercise," Kibwana said.
Speaking to The Standard on telephone from his constituency, Kibwana claimed that the situation was rampant in Mbitini polling stations.
In Western Kenya, claims of vote buying dogged a section of polling stations in Western province.
MPs allied to the Banana and Orange Camps pointed accusing fingers at each other but were confident of winning.
Khwisero member of parliament, Julius Arunga who cast his vote at Mulwanda Primary School, urged the Electoral Commission of Kenya to probe vote-buying in his constituency.
"There are Banana agents who are engrossed on buying votes to curtail an Orange win," Arunga said.
Butere member of parliament, Wycliff Oparanya, echoed the allegations alongside his colleagues, Moses Akaranga and George Khaniri. "Dishing out of money will not swing the votes on the Banana side as wananchi have already taken a stand," Khaniri said.

Original_Search_Title: Biwott Backs Out of Exercise
Headline: Kenya: Biwott Backs Out of Exercise
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Keiyo South Member of Parliament, Nicholas Biwott, yesterday declined to participate in the referendum process.
He justified his action, saying he could not compromise his principles at the whims of politicians. He said the referendum vote was not necessary as it was likely to divide Kenyans along ethnic lines.
Biwott, who kept Kenyans waiting and guessing on which side of the political divide he supported, said there was need for a consensus between the Orange and Banana camps. This would be a chance to iron out contentious issues over the Wako draft.
Elsewhere, over 50 voters in Keiyo District yesterday failed to cast their votes due to irregularities.
Anomalies in Keiyo North were noticed at Iten and Kapkesum polling stations while in Keiyo South, they were detected at the Mokua polling station.
Keiyo North MP Lukas Chepkittony, allied to the Orange camp, stormed the Keiyo Electoral Commission of Kenya offices in a bid to get an explanation from the District Co-ordinator, Wilson Rono.

Original_Search_Title: Rude Shock for Awori, Nyachae, Ngilu, Tuju And Saitoti
Headline: Kenya: Rude Shock for Awori, Nyachae, Ngilu, Tuju And Saitoti
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Early results for Kenya's fi rst referendum last night put the Orange team that is opposed to the Proposed New Constitution in an early lead.
The results of the historic vote started streaming in as early as 6pm after vote counting began soon after 5pm in some polling stations. By 11pm, the Electoral Commission of Kenya has released provisional results of only three constituencies Mvita, Limuru and Kisumu Town East.
In Limuru, the Banana team carried the day with 27,374 votes against the Orange team's 3,953. But the Orange took both Mvita (14,085 against Banana's 3,267) and Kisumu Town East with 22,904 against Banana's 592.
The voter turnout was high in most areas, with Kenyans waking up early to make their views known.
Throbbing with excitement and buoyed by a resolution to accept or reject Wako Draft, voters
turned up in hundreds of thousands for the fabulously peaceful referendum.
Had the referendum been a Parliamentary vote, it would have claimed at least fi ve senior members of President Kibaki's Cabinet, including Vice-President Moody Awori, as their constituents defi ed them to reject the draft.
Others are Energy minister Simeon Nyachae, Information minister Raphael Tuju, Education minister Prof George Saitoti and Health minister Charity.
The biggest upset of the evening was for Nyachae, who for the fi rst time in an illustrious political career, was rejected by constituents who have always voted him to Parliament.
But it is the loss for Ngilu that came as a surprise to most observers, having visited the State House twice, each time taking back to Ukambani a bag of goodies that included relief food worth millions of shillings, ahead of the referendum.
Also humiliated were Danson Mungatana of Garsen and Kibwezi's Kalembe Ndile, two of the most vocal proponents of the Wako draft. Early results put the Orange team that is opposing the Proposed New Constitution in an early lead in Nyanza,
Western, Rift Valley and Coast Provinces.
The Banana team was, however, leading with a wide margin in Central Province. In the expansive Rift Valley, the prime vote basket with
2.6 million votes, Sotik, which had 54,063 registered voters gave Orange 41,170 and Banana 837. Narok North, the constituency of Cabinet Minister
William ole Ntimama, who in the in the No team, Orange already had 22,932 votes against Yes team's 3267.
In Emgwen No had bagged 8410 and Yes 593. Out of 35 stations counted in Tinderet Yes had 910 against Orange's 15,100. Baringo Central's preliminary
results gave Orange 15,921 votes and Banana 226.
Yes trailed Banana in Mogotio with 84 against 3,903 already bagged by its opponent. Eldoret South had given Orange 15,183 and Banana only 1124, and in Eldoret
East Orange had 9055 and No 2,002.
In Kilgoris Orange had 23,328 and Banana 2,327. In Kuresoi the No team had 12,435 and Yes 6,337. However, No trailed in Molo with 2,737 against 6,777. Subukia
had given Orange 553 and Banana 7,733.
In Bomet No had 8677 and Orange 290 out of the 66,374 registered voters. Mogotio which has 22,857 registered voters had cast 84 to Banana and 3,903 to Orange while in Naivasha Yes
led with 7,446 against No's 3,594.
Orange had scooped 4,558 from Eldama Ravine's 39,942 registered voters and Yes 1,750. In Kacheliba Yes had only 33 against Orange's 3,526. No also was leading in Laikipia West with 2,473 and Banana 1,658.
In Sigor Yes was yet to get a vote but No team already had 610 votes. In Western Province, results in by 9pm gave Orange an early lead, handing Vice President Moody Awori a major
upset even in his Funyula Constituency. At Moody Awori Primary School, Orange scooped 350 votes against Banana's 204. At the Otiado Primary School polling centre
the No team took 291 votes against Yes team's 273.
But at Wakhungu where the VP voted, Banana topped with 300 against No's 277.
In Sabatia - the base of former Vice-President Musalia Mudavadi -Orange had already polled 23,767 votes in preliminary results against Banana's 1,579. In Ikolomani, out of 20 polling
stations counted No had 3,974 against 3,897 for Yes.
In Vihiga, out of 32 stations counted, No was leading with 16,614 while Banana had 1,108.
In Hamisi out of 41 counted No had 16,000 against Banana's 2,300.
In Webuye the fi rst four counted showed No had 890 and Banana 2076. In Kanduyi No had 2,689 and Banana 1,742.
In Trade Minister Mukhisa Kityui's Kimilili Constituency No had 948 and Yes 3,256. In Lugari out of 41 stations Orange had over 12,000 and Banana slightly over 7,000. In
Butula out of 16 stations Orange had 4,579 and Banana 877.
In Nyanza Province, Orange gave Banana a wide margin in all polling stations counted. In Kisumu East, voters overwhelmingly rejected the draft with 22,904 votes against
Banana's 592.
The story was the same in most other constituencies in Luo Nyanza. In Kasipul Kabondo, Orange had 10,641 votes against Banana's 63 votes while in Karachuonyo, Orange had 7,439 votes against Banana's 67. In Gusii land, Orange also took an early lead, with 2,001 votes out of Banana's 933 in Energy minister Simeon Nyachae's Nyaribari Chache.
And results in by 8pm showed the Orange taking an early lead in the Coast Province. Results from nine of the Coast's 21 constituencies showed Orange with 24,766 votes while Banana
had 6,046 votes. But this was only a small fraction of the province's 900,000 votes. However, in all polling stations so far counted, the Orange prevailed over the Banana with heavy margins.
Earlier, queues across the country were long but the determination of the voters unbreakable as they moved to the booths to immortalise their stand on the draft and 15 years of haggling and frustrating search for a new constitution.
The process was blissful so much unlike Kenya's past elections characterised by abrasive chants and mob fights except for inordinate pockets of violence in Kibera and Kayole. In Kibera an Electoral Commission of Kenya van was pelted with stones as the driver tried to dodge youths demanding to be allowed to inspect the cabin.
Both the Banana team that is supporting the Proposed New Constitution and the Orange that rejected it exuded confi dence they would each win. But they also maintained
they would accept the results and struck a reconciliatory note.
President Kibaki slid his marked ballot paper into the ECK's black box at 10:58am at Munaini Primary School in his Othaya Constituency. The President, who on Sunday went on live television to express his confi dence in the draft, arrived at the school at 10.51am, accompanied by First Lady Lucy Kibaki and members of his immediate family, including sons Jimmy
and Kagai and daughter Judy Wanjiku.
Orange leaders Roads Minister Raila Odinga and Kanu chairman Uhuru Kenyatta also led their supporters in voting against the proposed constitution. Raila, accompanied by his
wife Ida, arrived at the Old Kibera Primary School at 8:20am to a rousing reception by Orange supporters some of whom confessed to have spent the night milling around the polling station.
Uhuru dropped his vote in the box at Mutomo Primary School in his Gatundu South Constituency at 7:20am. The Government on its website reported observers from the Commonwealth,
European Union, the East African Commission and other organisations monitored the vote. A spokesman for the American Embassy separately reported his country deployed 42 observers to various areas.
Retired President Moi cast his ballot at Lena Moi Primary School in Tandui, Baringo Central.
Justice Minister Kiraitu Murungi said the Banana side was confi dent of winning and added that his team believed in democracy and would humbly accept the outcome.
In Othaya, President Kibaki enjoyed a rare mix with his constituents, shaking a hand here and there. Enjoying the homely welcome, the President introduced his family one by one. He caused laughter when he described as "watu wa fi tina" (gossips) leaders opposed to the Wako Draft.

Original_Search_Title: 'No' Camp Triumphs in Constitutional Referendum
Headline: Kenya's Verdict: Orange Triumphs
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Electoral Commission of Kenya (ECK) has officially announced a
resounding victory for the Orange camp kicking off frenzied celebrations by
supporters throughout the country. And President Kibaki, in a televised
speech to the nation said the country is not in a constitution vacuum and
would continue to operate with the current constitution.
While releasing the final results tally, ECK Chairman Samuel Kivuitu put
the Orange team ahead with more than 3.5 million (58.5 percent) votes and to
the Yes team 2.4 million (41.5 percent) in a poll that delivered shock
results in assumed Banana strongholds.
But the result was received with brave faces by key Banana campaigners,
many of who suffered humiliating defeats in their own backyards.
Lands Minister Amos Kimunya and his Foreign Affairs counterpart Chirau
Ali Mwakwere promised to respect the verdict as a reflection of the
votersÂ wishes.
But Kimunya who blamed the Yes loss on electoral "misinformation" by the
draft opponents appeared to rule out post-referendum dialogue and a return
to the Bomas draft favoured by the No team arguing the vote had put an end
to the process.
The No team registered convincing victories in Nairobi, Ukambani, Coast
and in large swathes of Kisii and Western Kenya that were expected to
witness a fierce scramble by both camps.
Vice President Moody Awori, and Ministers Simeon Nyachae, George
Saitoti, Charity Ngilu, Government Whip Norman Nyaga and assistant minister
Maina Kamanda were among the Banana luminaries who lost their turfs to the
Orange wave.
The Banana camp lost 7,585 to 8,564 in the VPÂs Funyula constituency,
19,096 to 22,029 in SaitotiÂs Kajiado North while NgiluÂs Kitui
Central voted 16,415 against the draft with 11,560 backing it.
The No camp also prevailed in NyachaeÂs Kitutu Chache constituency
trouncing the Yes crusaders 19,977 against 7,756. It also won17,995 against
15,661 in Kamukunji constituency represented by Nyaga and 23,058 against
22,914 in KamandaÂs Starehe constituency.
Although generally billed as a Yes stronghold, Nairobi with an average
turnout of 38 percent went Orange with 208,747 votes against BananaÂs
144,968.
Information Minister Raphael Tuju braved hostility provoked by his vocal
campaigns for the constitution in an Orange zone to vote in his Rarieda
constituency but ended with a paltry 1,000 for his camp.
But there were overwhelming victories for the camp in Central Province
with an average of 93 percent of the cast votes in its favour and equally
comfortable margins in other strongholds of Meru, Embu and Ford-Kenya zones
in Western Province.
Save for Official Opposition Leader and Kanu chairman Uhuru Kenyatta who
lost in his Gatundu South consitutency 11,032 against 19,848 and Joseph
Kamotho who conceded Mathioya 28,620 to 756, the Orange camp was spared
harsh voters lessons.
The team easily took Roads Minister Raila Odinga LangÂata
constituency 37,914 against 9,208, Heritage Minister Najib BalalaÂs Mvita
constituency 14,085 against 3,267 and Musalia MudavadiÂs home turf of
Sabatia 25,658 to 1,954.
There were also strong showing by Office of the President Minister
William ole Ntimama in Narok North, William Ruto in Eldoret North and Gideon
Moi in Baringo Central.
The No camp made strong showing in Luo Nyanza with record backing of 98
percent of cast votes and in large parts of the Rift Valley Province that
also witnessed high turnouts.
Marakwet East, Mogotio, Baringo North, Marakwet West and Baringo Central
constituency in the province had impressive turnouts of 95.4 percent, 91.3
percent, 91 percent.87.1 percent and 86.2 percent respectively.
There were also impressive voter turnouts in Eldama Ravine, 79.5
percent, Keiyo North, 79.4 percent, Mosop, 78.3 percent and Chepalungu 77.5
percent.
Wajir West experienced the lowest turnout with only 5,366 voters out of
the registered total of 26,273 participating in the poll. Other
constituencies witnessing poor turnout were Mvita and Dujis with 26.9
percent.

Original_Search_Title: Voters Turn Out in Large Numbers to Decide
Headline: Kenya: Voters Turn Out in Large Numbers to Decide
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Banana and Orange camps in the Coast accused each other of bribing
voters.
In Mombasa, police were given registration numbers of matatus alleged to
belong to Banana agents going round distributing cash to voters.
The claims were reported at around 10am. Mombasa acting OCPD Anthony
Onyango said the registration numbers had been circulated to various police
stations with a view of impounding the vehicles.
In Malindi, one of the Banana co-ordinators, Rishad Amana, reported to
the police a claim that Orange agents were bribing voters at Sir Ali polling
station. Police made a follow up at the venue, but did not arrest
anyone.
In Diani, Orange agents claimed that empty ballot boxes had been found
at Timbani polling station at Likoni.
In Kisauni, Orange camp secretariat chairman, Hassan Joho, said the
bribery claims had been forwarded to the ECK.
Elsewhere, Kisauni MP Anania Mwaboza reported to the police that some
people were distributing leaflets promising a Sh500 reward from him if they
voted for the Banana.
He distanced himself from the claims and urged police to arrest the
authors of the leaflets.
Cabinet minister Najib Balala claimed hundreds of voters were being
transported from up country in buses to vote in Mombasa.
Meanwhile, Environment minister Kalonzo Musyoka yesterday said the
Orange camp would not accept the results if their opponents rig the
polls.
Kalonzo said President Kibaki got 800,000 in the last General Election
in Central Province and that there were 1.5 million registered voters in the
province.
"We will not accept claims that there are currently 2 million voters in
the region," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Ministers, MPs Lead Voters in Casting Ballots
Headline: Kenya: Ministers, MPs Lead Voters in Casting Ballots
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Cabinet ministers and MPs yesterday led their constituents in voting on
the Proposed New Constitution.
Energy minister Simeon Nyachae, who is allied to the Banana team, cast
his vote at Nyosia Primary School at 9.27am.
The Nyaribari Chache MP called for peace and urged both camps to welcome
results of the poll.
He spoke as most polling stations in Kisii and Nyamira districts
recorded low voter turnout.
Nyachae called for peace during the exercise and maintained that the Yes
team would accept the results.
"We will accept the outcome of the poll whether we win or lose, but I
appeal for peace," he said.
Planning minister Prof Anyang' Nyong'o voted at Ratta Primary School
shortly after 9am and was optimistic that the Orange team would win.
Sports minister Ochillo Ayacko cast his vote at Anindo Primary School in
his Rongo constituency shortly after 7am.
Ayacko, accompanied by about 500 voters, exuded confidence assuring his
constituents of a No win.
"The Orange Democratic Movement will from November 23 start mobilising
wananchi for the country's third liberation by endorsing the Bomas Draft,"
Ayacko said.
Voting was calm, but most polling stations in Migori, Uriri and Nyatike
had no Banana camp agents by 9am.
Rachuonyo MP Adhu Awiti voted at Oindo Primary School minutes past
8.30am, saying there would be a revolution after the plebiscite.
Kasipul Kabondo constituents went to the poll without their MP, Eng
Peter Owidi, who is still recuperating in hospital.
Rachuonyo, where sacked poll clerks were reinstated before stations
opened their gates to voters, witnessed a low voter turnout.
Muhoroni MP Prof Ayiecho Olweny led his constituents to vote at 7.50am
at Obago Primary School.
Olweny said voter registers at Obago and Kibigori primary schools listed
some voters as dead when they are alive.
Nyando MP Eric Nyamunga cast his vote at Boya Primary School shortly
after 7.30am, while his Nyakach counterpart, Peter Odoyo, voted at 8am.
Kitutu Masaba MP Mwencha Okioma voted at 10am at Gesima polling centre
while his Migori counterpart Owino Likowa cast his vote at Migori Primary
School at 7am.

Original_Search_Title: Maathai Drops Her Neutrality And Votes
Headline: Kenya: Maathai Drops Her Neutrality And Votes
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Nobel laureate Prof Wangari Maathai yesterday ditched her neutral stand and participated in the voting exercise.
Tetu MP Prof Wangari Maathai casts her vote at Ihithe village. There was speculation that she would not vote, as during the campaigns she had maintained that she was neutral regarding the constitutional debate.
Maathai, who remained neutral during the referendum campaigns, cast her ballot at Ihithe Primary School in her Tetu constituency.
"I have to participate in this exercise as a patriotic Kenyan. I have taken a stand, which I do not have to announce. It is, however, a big pity how leaders have divided Kenyans along tribal lines," she said.
The Tetu legislator said she had decided to participate in the exercise, saying whether she voted for the Orange or Banana was "a big secret that remains sealed" in her heart.
The Nobel Peace laureate arrived at the Ihithe polling station at 10.12 am and cast her ballot five minutes later. There was jubilation when she arrived.
Voters at the station were surprised when the MP arrived and went straight to cast her ballot.
Two weeks ago during a Yes rally in her constituency, the environmentalist declared that she was neutral after her constituents urged her to support the Yes camp.
