After casting her vote, Maathai called on Kenyans to embrace peace and accept the outcome of the exercise.
She added that she regretted that her pleas to postpone the balloting were ignored even when leaders were aware that the campaigns had divided Kenyans.

Original_Search_Title: 'No' Camp in Early Lead
Headline: Kenya: Orange in Early Lead
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Throbbing with excitement and buoyed by a resolution to accept or reject Wako Draft, Kenyans turned up in hundreds of thousands for their first and fabulously peaceful referendum.
And by last evening, results of the referendum had started streaming in from the polling stations after vote-counting started as early as 5pm. The Electoral Commission of Kenya headquarters, was, however, yet to officially announce any results. Results in by 8pm showed the Orange taking an early lead in the Coast Province. Results from nine of the Coast's 21 constituencies showed Orange with 24,766 votes while Banana had 6,046 votes. But this was only a small fraction of the province's 900,000 votes.
However, in all polling stations so far counted, the Orange prevailed over the Banana with heavy margins.
Early results for the region were in from Lamu West, Kisauni, Mwatate, Bahari, Changamwe, Garsen, Malindi, Taveta and Voi.
In Garsen constituency where the Banana had been expected to do well, the Orange started off with 1,400 votes against Banana's 250, with five polling stations counted.
Counting that started immediately after 5pm in most areas of the province was bound to end early this morning, by prediction of most presiding officers. But final results were expected to be communicated late from far flung islands of Lamu District like Pate, Kitayuu and Mande.
Earlier, queues across the country were long but the determination of the voters unbreakable as they moved to the booths to immortalise their stand on the draft and 15 years of haggling and frustrating search for a new constitution.
The process was blissful - so much unlike Kenya's past elections characterised by abrasive chants and mob fights - except for inordinate pockets of violence in Kibera and Kayole. In Kibera an Electoral Commission of Kenya van was pelted with stones as the driver tried to dodge youths demanding to be allowed to inspect the cabin.
Both the Banana team that is supporting the Proposed New Constitution and the Orange that rejected it exuded confidence they would each win. But they also maintained they would accept the results and struck a reconciliatory note.
President Kibaki - slid his marked ballot paper into the ECK's black box at10:58am at Munaini Primary School in his Othaya Constituency.
The President, who on Sunday went on live television to express his confidence in the draft, arrived at the school at 10.51am, accompanied by First Lady Lucy Kibaki and members of his immediate family, including sons Jimmy and Kagai and daughter Judy Wanjiku.
Escorted by Central Provincial Commissioner Peter Raburu, the President was ushered into the classroom by returning officer Mr Joe Ngari.
He fished his identity cards and voter's card from an inside coat pocket and handed them to clerk Lucy Wanjiku Gichingo who confirmed that his name was in the register before allowing him to vote.
Orange leaders - Roads Minister Raila Odinga and Kanu chairman Uhuru Kenyatta --- also led their supporters in voting against the proposed constitution.
Raila, accompanied by his wife Ida, arrived at the Old Kibera Primary School at 8:20am to a rousing reception by Orange supporters some of whom confessed to have spent the night milling around the polling station.
The fans chanting his nickname Agwambo escorted him from the main road into the school compound. Donning a Mexican Sombrero hat a beaming Raila said the Orange team was confident of capturing 70 per cent of the votes cast. He expressed satisfaction with the turnout and security outlay in most polling stations in Nairobi.
Uhuru dropped his vote in the box at Mutomo Primary School in his Gatundu South Constituency at 7:20am.
The Government on its website reported observers from the Commonwealth, European Union, the East African Commission and other organisations monitored the vote. A spokesman for the American Embassy separately reported his country deployed 42 observers to various areas.
Retired President Moi cast his ballot at Lena Moi Primary School in Tandui, Baringo Central.
Last month, the retired President had said he was grappling with regret that the referendum had split the county along tribal lines. He predicted the face of Kenya might never be the same again. "I am very sad to see nationalism has sunk down the pit," he said while adding that national unity should be upheld.
Justice Minister Kiraitu Murungi said the Banana side was confident of winning and added that his team believed in democracy and would humbly accept the outcome.
In Othaya, President Kibaki enjoyed a rare mix with his constituents, shaking a hand here and there. Enjoying the homely welcome, the President introduced his family one by one. He caused laughter when he described as "watu wa fitina" (gossips) leaders opposed to the Wako Draft. He then drove in the jab "sasa watapoa". (They will calm down now)
When the First Lady was invited to "greet the people" she declared that the fight for the new constitution won. "We won this morning," she said amid cheers.
A chief and three sub-chiefs were arrested by wananchi and handed over to the police in Vice President Moody Awori's Funyula Constituency over claims of voter bribery. They were said to have been caught red-handed dishing out Sh50 each to voters.

Original_Search_Title: Kenya Votes in Historic Referendum
Headline: Kenya Votes in Historic Referendum
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kenyans countrywide are casting the ballot in a landmark referendum on the proposed Constitution.
Mr Raila Odinga casts his ballot in Kibera Constituency
Voting began at 7 am but queues had started forming in most polling stations long before they were opened.
Most leaders have called on Kenyans to remain calm during the poll. Voting has so far been peaceful.
However, confusion has reigned in some polling stations in Nairobi
with people bearing old voter's cards or passports instead of
identification cards, being turned away. Some names could not be traced
in the voters' register.
The Electoral Commission of Kenya (ECK) has clarified that passports
should be accepted in the voting process. Old generation voters cards
acquired before 1997 are not acceptable, as they should have been
renewed.
ECK commissioner, Mr Jack Tumwa, said the missing names could have
been occasioned by double registration on the part of the voters.
He, however, said voting was underway in most parts of the country
and was proceeding smoothly. Kenya has 11.6 million registered voters..
Voters have only one question to answer in the poll: Are you For or Are You
VP Moody Awori votes at Wakhungu primary school in Funyula constituency.
Against (the proposed Constitution). There a total of 19,000 polling stations
spread in 210 constituencies.
Vote counting is expected to begin immediately after the close of the polling at 5.00 pm today.
The referendum follows weeks of campaigning, which left nine people dead and scores others injured.
Those in support of the constitution (the Banana team) are led by
President Mwai Kibaki while those in the opposing camp, or the Orange
team, are led by Raila Odinga, an influential LDP member and minister
of Roads, and Kanu chairman Uhuru Kenyatta among others.

Original_Search_Title: Leaders Defy Ban On Campaign
Headline: Kenya: Leaders Defy Ban On Campaign
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Leaders defied the Electoral Commission of Kenya yesterday and pitched for support outside the campaign period.
President Kibaki and key leaders of the Orange team defied the commission's electoral code of conduct which stipulates that the campaigns end on November 19.
The code, released by ECK chairman Samuel Kivuitu, gave the Banana and Orange sides a one-month campaign period from October 21 to November 19, and forbade any campaigns 24 hours before voting.
In a live broadcast from State House, President Kibaki extolled the virtues of the proposed new Constitution and asked Kenyans to vote for it.
He said the proposed new Constitution formed the foundation for a new Kenya.
And at a press conference in Nairobi, the Orange team asked voters to reject the draft, arguing that it was written with a view to protecting those in power.
Turn out
A statement read by Eldoret North MP William Ruto appealed to voters to turn out in large numbers and vote No, saying the team was confident of carrying the day.
They also asked their supporters to maintain peace, law and order during the referendum and ensure that no rigging took place.
The Orange leaders later accused the President of breaking the electoral code of conduct for "issuing a partisan political commercial for the Banana campaign in his live address."
They however welcomed the President's appeal to the nation to come out in large numbers and vote, but regretted that he used the occasion to be partisan.
Ministers Raila Odinga and Anyang' Nyong'o, who addressed journalists after the President's speech, asked the ECK to take action against the President.
But asked about the Orange group's earlier statement, they said they were only explaining to their supporters what was expected of them today.
The Orange leaders had also asked supporters to join them for "a victory party" at Uhuru Park, Nairobi, tomorrow, after the results are released.
In his speech broadcast from State House, the President appealed to Kenyans to ensure that they did not loose "this historic chance.
"I appeal to you all to come out and vote so that the outcome of the vote will clearly demonstrate our common decision," the Head of State said.
Reject violence
He asked Kenyans to reject violence and selfish interests as they move to make the decision that will decide the fate of the Wako Draft.
The Government had taken measures to ensure that the process ended peacefully, he said.
"We should shun violence and respect the rights of each one to have differing views," the President added during the 4 o'clock 10-minute address.

Original_Search_Title: Vote Counting to Start in the Evening
Headline: Kenya: Vote Counting to Start in the Evening
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Polling for the referendum will start at 7am and close at 5pm today, to allow time for counting before dark. Counting will take place at the polling stations and results announced at the end of the exercise.
One will need to present a valid voter's card, a National Identification (ID) card or a valid passport to vote. Expired passports, photocopies, police abstracts or defaced IDs are not acceptable. No other identification is acceptable.
Voters can use any mark such as a tick or a cross or even a line, to show their choice as long as the mark is in the provided space next to their favourite choice.
Electoral Commission of Kenya chairman Samuel Kivuitu has given instructions that ECK officials at the polling stations give priority to expectant mothers, the elderly and the disabled to ensure they do not queue for too long.
He was, however, optimistic that the waiting time before one is allowed to vote will not be long since the referendum has only one question - Are you For or Are You against.
The ECK will use only valid documents to counter-check the identity of the voter against details recorded in the voters register.
If the details are correct, ECK officials cross off the voter's name from the register using a pen.
After the name is deleted, the voter will be given a ballot paper on which he or she will mark against a symbol of their choice.
A valid ballot paper must bear an ECK stamp at the back and front.
In the case of a voter who needs assistance, the ballot will be marked in the presence of agents from both sides.
The voter's little finger will then be dipped in indelible ink to ensure he or she does not vote twice.
Voting will be done through secret ballot or in case of disabled or illiterate voters, the assisted voter rule will be applied.
A voter can bring his or her own person to assist. An authorised ECK official can also assist a voter, but the process must be witnessed by agents from both sides.
An assisted voter's little left finger and the right thumb of the person assisting are then dipped in indelible ink.
Counting will only be witnessed by agents and any other persons authorised and accredited by ECK.
The ballot paper should only have one mark on either the Banana (Yes) or Orange (No).
The ECK poll officials will reject any ballot paper with multiple marks on it or any mark where the intention of the voter cannot be interpreted.
If an agent on either side objects to a rejected ballot paper at any polling station, polling officials write down details of such a ballot and keep it in an envelop before referring it to the Returning Officer at the final stage of polling in the tallying hall.
A petition can be filed by an aggrieved party against the conduct of a voter, an ECK official, agents of either side or a member of the referendum committee.
After counting at the polling stations, the results will be taken to the constituency headquarters, normally divisional headquarters for tallying.
The returning officers will then relay their final results to the ECK headquarters once tallying of all votes in their areas has been done.
They will give provisional results through mobile phones in areas with network and satellite phones, in areas without network. They could also be allowed to fax the results.
However, the commission will only confirm the results once the returning officers produce declaration certificates to the commission as proof of whether the Yes or No camp triumphed in their areas.
Any drunk and disorderly voters will be expelled from the polling area but may vote once they sober up and conduct themselves in an orderly manner. Hooligans who try to disrupt the poll will be arrested by police.
The ECK has banned the sale of bananas and oranges within 400 metres of the polling area.
Similarly, voters wearing T-shirts or carrying posters bearing banana or orange symbols will not be allowed to stay on the queue.
An ECK official at the polling area is empowered to expel such a voter from the station.
All persons who enter polling stations, other than the voters, will require an accreditation badge from ECK.
Agents from the Banana and Orange camps will witness the voting. Others authorised to enter the polling station on ECK accreditation include observers, monitors and the media.

Original_Search_Title: Law Reforms Should Continue After the Poll, Says Lumumba
Headline: Kenya: Law Reforms Should Continue After the Poll, Says Lumumba
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The clamour for constitution reforms should not end with the referendum, Constitution of Kenya Review Commission secretary P.L.O. Lumumba says.
He adds that should Banana win, the Government should continue tackling the contentious issues. And should Orange win, the people should continue demanding more reforms.
"Efforts should be put in place ... through the mechanism of the law reform commission or the office of the attorney-general and Parliament, to ensure the issues are addressed at the earliest opportunity," he told the Nation in an interview. "A lot of efforts have been put in place," he said.
"CKRC has in its custody the views of the people of Kenya. We have the Bomas Draft and the proposed new constitution. All these documents should form the basis upon which we can progress the agenda and ensure that ultimately the people of Kenya get a constitution they deserve.
"A negative vote should not be treated as the final nail on the coffin of constitution making in this country." Dr Lumumba said the timetable of events after the poll should be pegged on the day the result is announced, possibly on Wednesday.
The country's independence Constitution was made at Lancaster House in London in 1963 by a few Kenyan delegates. Since then, there have been several amendments to the document, made in Parliament.
And, says CKRC members Ahmed Issack Hassan, the review is meant to allow the people to participate in making a constitution that aims to "reflect their wishes, aspirations, hopes and dreams for the future."
In his paper, Introductory Document on the whole Process (2001), he says the aim of the review is "to guarantee peace, national unity and integrity of the Republic of Kenya."
There are two scenarios following the poll, he said - whether the No or the Yes vote wins and the result is unchallenged or if either side wins and the result is challenged. In case of the the former, the law allows 14 days from Tuesday to elapse during which the result may be challenged. The result then becomes final on December 7.
The law allows 30 days before CKRC is dissolved, that is January 6. If the Yes wins and the result is similarly unchallenged, the law allows 14 days (from Tuesday) to elapse during which a challenge to the result may be brought. The result becomes final on December 7.
In case of the latter and the No vote wins, the law allows 14 days during which the result may be challenged. The last day for a challenge to be brought up will be December 7, and it must be determined within 45 days.
The result of the referendum becomes final on January 20, after which CKRC will exist until February 20 when it will be dissolved.
The result becomes final on January 20, after which CKRC will exist for the next 30 days, until February 20, when it will be dissolved. If the Yes vote wins, December 7 will be the last day to lodge an objection, and the challenge must be determined within 45 days and the result becomes final on January 20.

Original_Search_Title: Voting Materials Moved to Balloting Centres
Headline: Kenya: Voting Materials Moved to Balloting Centres
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Referendum materials were last night moved from the Electoral Commission's strong rooms to various polling areas in Nairobi.
The strategic materials moved included ballot boxes and papers, ECK and agent's seals and voters' registers.
Other materials included lantern lamps, spirits, indelible ink, notebooks, rulers and pens.
The movement of the polling materials was witnessed by agents of both the Banana and Orange sides.
The ECK officials are by law not allowed to leave any of the poll materials unattended until voting starts.
Speaking at the Holy Family Basilica, Nairobi, Archbishop Ndingi Mwana a'Nzeki said he was happy with the manner in which both the Banana and Orange camps conducted themselves during their final campaign rallies on Saturday.
"I congratulate them and appeal to them to continue being peaceful until the end of the referendum vote," he said.
He called on all Kenyans to conduct themselves maturely and to exercise their democratic right by voting for whomever they choose in the historical event.
Starehe Returning Officer Ms Hamida Kibwana said all arrangements for the poll were in place.
She said each of the 17 polling areas has a minimum of five streams and a maximum of 20.
Starehe polling activities are being coordinated from the Kenya Polytechnic. Results of the final poll for Starehe will also be announced from the polytechnic.
The polling areas include Catholic Parochial, Moi Avenue, River Bank, St Peter Clavers and Pumwani primary schools. Others are Kariokor Social Hall and Park Road, Pumwani, Old Mathare, St Teresa's, Daima, Kiboro, Ndururumo and City primary schools. Mathare Youth Polytechnic, Huruma and the Kenya Polytechnic are also polling areas.

Original_Search_Title: Transporters Protest Over Eck Contract
Headline: Kenya: Transporters Protest Over Eck Contract
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Transporters in Kitui Central constituency earlier promised contracts by the Electoral Commission to ferry election materials yesterday protested at being turned away.
ECK had decided to use school buses instead.
The more than 20 vehicle owners threatened to sue the commission for what they termed a breach of contract, saying that although they had followed the tendering and vehicle inspection procedures and paid a Sh5,000 commitment fee, they had been turned away without explanation.
But area returning officer Joseph Kyavoa said the decision was made to cut costs.
- Residents of Nambale constituency, Busia District, yesterday claimed irregularities in the recruitment of polling clerks, saying many eligible applicants had been left out.
Unemployment to blame
But speaking in Busia Town yesterday, ECK vice-chairman Gabriel Mukele attributed the problem to unemployment countrywide, saying that a similar problem had been experienced in Bumula, Lugari and Sirisia constituencies.
Mr Mukele who is coordinating the poll in Western Province, said, however, that the commission was looking into the matter.
At the same time, he appealed to all registered voters to cast their ballots today, he said, is the right time for all Kenyans to exercise their democratic right by either rejecting or endorsing the proposed Constitution.
"I urge all Kenyans, despite their political beliefs, to turn up and vote today because it is their right to decide how they want to be governed," he said.
He praised the Orange and the Banana camps for maintaining peace during their rallies in Nairobi on Saturday.
Maintain the spirit
"They conducted themselves very maturely and peacefully, and I therefore pray that this spirit be maintained even after the referendum in order to enhance stability in the country," Mr Mukele said.
He added that arrangements for the vote were complete, and that all ballot boxes had been sent to the polling stations.

Original_Search_Title: Churches Pray for Peace During Poll
Headline: Kenya: Churches Pray for Peace During Poll
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Ms Esther Gathoni prays for peace in the country during a special Mass at the Winners Chapel in Nairobi, yesterday.
Photo Stephen Mudiari
Churches throughout the country held special prayers for peace during today's referendum.
The Catholic, Presbyterian and Anglican churches urged Kenyans to turn out in large numbers and cast their votes.
At Holy Family Basilica, Nairobi, Catholic Archbishop Ndingi Mwana a'Nzeki appealed for peace and calm, and asked all eligible Kenyans to vote.
The Catholic Justice and Peace Commission, he said, had drafted a prayer, copies of which were being distributed to all the faithful.
Reads the prayer: "We thank you for your goodness to us in the 16-year struggle for a new constitution; we pray for the unity of our nation and all our leaders, irrespective of different political, religious and gender.
"We acknowledge those who lost their lives in the struggle. We seek your guidance and leadership in this referendum, where we have to accept or reject the proposed Constitution ..."
The churches also prayed for wisdom, patience, happiness, respect, love, tolerance and forgiveness among the people.
Fr Raphael Obetia of the Catholic University of Eastern Africa led the Holy Family Basilica congregation in praying for President KibakI and his Cabinet as well as MPs "to guide and govern the country with wisdom and compassion".
He asked Kenyans to conduct themselves maturely during the vote, saying: "This is a chance of a life time."
At Nairobi's All Saints Cathedral, provost Peter Karanja asked the people to maintain peace before and after the poll. "In crowning the exercise and agitation for a new constitution with the first ever referendum, we celebrate the milestone," he said.
"We take this opportunity to ask Kenyans to turn out in large numbers and vote with their conscience."
The provost urged both the Orange and the Banana supporters to respect the decision of Kenyans at the referendum.
"For this nation to heal, everyone must return his or her dagger to the sheath after the referendum and seek to continue the process of constitution socialisation," he added.
He commended the President saying he had shown restraint and respect for freedom of speech. But he said the President's assurance that no community will be marginalised politically and economically because of a political stand or persuasion taken on the referendum would be important.
At the Presbyterian Church of East Africa's St Andrews Church, prayers were conducted by the Rev Isaack Wanyoike who urged Kenyans to turn out in large numbers and cast their votes.
And Christians were yesterday asked to conduct themselves in a mature manner during today's referendum.
Several church leaders in Kericho said that the referendum was only a day's exercise and that life must continue after the elections.
"Let hatred not be brought about by the election but unity should prevail, " said Bishop Benjamin Kipruto of the Deliverance Church while delivering a sermon in Kericho Town.
At the Sacred Heart Catholic Church in Kericho, vicar-general Samwel Karanja read a Pastoral letter signed by 27 bishops, which appealed to Catholic faithfuls to shun violence during the referendum and to preach peace.
Kenya Episcopal Conference chairman Cornelius Korir said in Eldoret athat Kenyans had yearned for a new constitution for more than 15 years and today is the day they could decide whether they wanted to be led by new laws or not.
The Kenya Assemblies of God led by pastor Sophia Olem and the Abundance Grace Church, led by the rev Joyce Maina also prayed for a peaceful election.
Church leaders in Nyanza also took the referendum campaigns to the pulpit as local politicians disappeared into their constituencies for a last minute scramble for the province's 1.2m votes.
The clerics called for peaceful elections but warned against bribery and incitement at polling stations.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki's Tough Options After the Poll
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki's Tough Options After the Poll
Body_Text:
Nairobi — If Yes wins, will he call a snap election to give himself new full term and stamp authority on enemies, or will he expel LDP to build more support in House in case of a No victory?
Banana supporter sings and dances during the campaign rally at Uhuru Park, Nairobi, on Saturday.
Photo by Joan Pereruan
Even President Kibaki, well-known for often sending the oblique message, has got through with relative clarity that after tomorrow there is going to be a night of the long knives.
"We are going to sack some people," he said in Western Province on Thursday.
In his Banana camp, it seems to be an article of faith that the National Rainbow Coalition is dead and that from tomorrow, the nature of cooperative politics will of necessity need to strike out in a different direction.
Hoaxers are already circulating on the Internet a post-referendum Cabinet line-up in which Roads minister Raila Odinga's Liberal Democratic Party has been scissored out.
If we were a betting nation, it is highly unlikely that there would be a book-maker taking bets on the continued survival of the Narc coalition as it now exists.
As if to encourage the President to do his worst, the Orange ministers have conducted their campaigns against the proposed constitution with bridge-burning confidence. Mr Odinga, their leader, reacted to the shooting of rioters by police in Kisumu by saying he was ashamed to be part of the Kibaki administration.
Generally, the message from Orange has been: "We oppose the proposed constitution; sack us if you dare."
Preparing to rig the referendum
The No ministers' claim that President Kibaki is bribing voters and might be preparing to rig the referendum - in effect accusing their boss of corruption - lends themselves to the conclusion that a certain line has been crossed and that even though this is a generally undisciplined and boisterous coalition arrangement, there might be no going back to the way things used to be.
There is such fury among the President's traditional allies, whose line has always been that LDP seems, in their view, to have brought him nothing but grief.
The party has stood in the way of his legislative agenda, allied itself with foreign missions in criticising the Government's record on corruption, dared him to sack them, on top of running a better organised and seemingly better-funded campaign against a constitution draft on which he has staked his reputation.
In the minds of President Kibaki's allies, therefore, a win for Banana would be an opportunity to reward loyalty and punish "betrayal".
The more moderate voices, however, argue that the President has not kept LDP in government because it is loyal to him; he has done so because he is obligated to. This is a coalition government, and LDP owns it as much as he does. In this line of thinking, a Banana victory should have no consequences for the rebels.
Moderate voices are always in the minority, but should they prevail and the President resist the temptation to finish off his vanquished critics, there could be an attempt at healing and an effort to press on with the coalition business until such a time that the pressures of the 2007 poll make it impossible to go on.
But the tea leaves are not drawing a picture of post-referendum political bliss. So tomorrow is going to be a very long day for very many politicians. Political careers, consuming ambitions and legacies will be in the balance. And, when all the ballot boxes are unsealed, the votes poured out, counted and tallied, tough decisions will be waiting to be made.
This divisive, anxiety-soaked referendum has been a big gamble not just for the Orange brigade and its leaders, Mr Odinga and Environment minister Kalonzo Musyoka, but for President Kibaki as well. At the very least, it has brought out the worst in almost all politicians and, in some respects, Kenyans as well.
In one important sense, it is far easier for a government to lose the support of a poor, struggling population - which is more likely to blame the Government for all its woes - than to win its approval.
The course of a referendum, therefore, has inbuilt risks for those in power.
The question on the minds of Kenyans today is what this country, and its political landscape, will be like tomorrow and next week.
Will there really be a Cabinet bloodbath? Will there be a snap election? Will we revert to business as usual quickly?
A return to normality would require a high level of tolerance and statesmanship than has, unfortunately, been exhibited so far by both factions.
But the shape of things to come will, of course, depend on the outcome.
President Kibaki, though playing a fairly tricky hand, is certainly looking at tomorrow confident that constitutionally, win or lose, he will stay in power.
There will be no constitutional vacuum and there will be no requirement for a change of government one way or the other, though moral and political arguments will, of course, be made.
This, after all, is a referendum on the proposed Constitution, not a confidence vote.
It is unthinkable that the President and his Banana side would reject a No vote. As a matter of fact, they are on record saying they will accept the will of the people.
Matters are more complicated for Orange, however. Given that the constitutional contest is a continuation of their struggle to have more power in the coalition and given that all political calculations are being made with an eye to 2007, a Yes vote would be a serious setback. The possibility that they will be fired, especially if Yes wins with a convincing majority, would make it very difficult for them to accept a Yes win.
As a matter of fact, whereas the No campaigns have talked at length about rigging and what they would do if it happened, an acceptance of defeat is not something they have dwelt on.
It is a safe bet, therefore, that the No side, or significant parts of it, will reject a Yes vote. They have to; the referendum for them might turn out to have been political suicide.
That is not to say that the President will not be facing hard choices. If Banana wins, he will have to make up his mind whether to keep the rebels in his hut or follow the advice of his allies and wield the axe.
Purely for argument purposes, a Banana victory would give the President the opportunity to kill three birds with one stone in a highly risky and unlikely gambit.
Riding the crest of victory, he could call a snap election to give himself a new full term, stamp his authority on his defeated enemies and solidify the legitimacy of his government.
In reality, however, very few people in politics have the money or the energy to immediately go into another season of electioneering. It is, therefore, an almost foregone conclusion that we are unlikely to see a snap poll.
If No wins, the temptation will still be strong to kick out LDP with a view to building more support in Parliament to shore up the Government's chances of survival.
The Orange view does not afford a wide vista of options. If they lose and a new constitution proclaimed, given the difficulty of a legal challenge, they will have three. First, they could resort to mass action to make the country ungovernable and force an election.
Second, they could always go to the President and tell him: "The referendum is over, let us go back and tend our coalition." That way they would buy themselves time to regroup and recover in time for the 2007 elections. Or they could walk out on Narc and into the opposition proper and wage a political guerrilla war on their erstwhile coalition partners and await 2007.

Original_Search_Title: Kenyans Vote
Headline: Kenyans Vote
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kenyans this morning turned up in droves to cast their votes in support
of or against the Proposed New Constitution.
Voting centres opened at 7 am and are expected to close at 5 pm.
Snaking queues of voters who turned up early morning to cast their
ballot at one of the centres in Lang'ata constituency, Nairobi.
There has been no case of violence reported except a few isolated
complaints on voter's card irregularities occasioned by names missing in the
registers, but were handled by the Presiding Officers on the ground.
President Mwai Kibaki has already cast his ballot at his Othaya
Constituency.
Likewise Cabinet minister Raila Odinga led his Lang'ata constituents in
casting their ballot, after which he announced that his Orange team would
accept the people's verdict.
Raila accompanied by his wife Ida arrived at the polling station at 8.20
am to a rousing reception temporarily disrupting operations.
He said that he was optimistic that Kenyans would vote out the Wako
draft as it does not represent their views.
His sentiments were echoed by his Orange camp colleague Uhuru Kenyatta
who cast his ballot at his Gatundu South Constituency.
And the Banana luminaries are oozing with confidence and have expressed
optimism that Kenya would have a new constitution after 15 years of
struggle. Ministers Njenga Karume and Kiraitu Murungi said there was no
doubt that the 'Yes' camp would emerge winners. The two cast their votes in
their respective constituencies of Kiambaa and Imenti South.
In Starehe constituency in Nairobi voting started well in time with the
area MP Maina Kamanda casting his vote at the City primary.
Various polling centres in the constituency recorded high turnout with
eagerness by the voters to cast their votes. Others turned up long before
the stations were open.
"I was here by 5 am to vote before I could embark on other issues. I am
happy to see other people turn out in large number," a voter said.
Kamanda too said he was happy with the turnout in his area.
Security was tight with most of the centres having at least four
policemen manning them.

Original_Search_Title: The Constitution Referendum in Kenya
Headline: Kenya: The Constitution Referendum in Kenya
Body_Text:
Today, November 21, the people of Kenya are going to the polls in a
refendum to endorse or reject a draft constitution for their country.
The so-called "Wako Constitution" (named after the country's current
Attorney General),contains amendments to an earlier draft which had
emerged out of an assembly of political groups opposed to the
dictatorship of former President Daniel arap Moi.
The choice of two fruits as symbols, with those for the draft
constitution symbolised by a "Banana"; and those against it by an
"Orange", appears to have failed to hold back the tribalisation of
debate. An FM radio station which broadcasts in Kalenjin (the language
of former president Daniel Arap Moi),was closed down by the
authorities, one week to voting, for urging violence against the Kikuyu
(President Mwai Kibaki's ethnic group). Eight members of parliament
from the area accused the government's regulatory body of "harassing
the (Kalenjin) community".
But there have also been very positive aspects to the referendum
campaign. President Kibaki has, for example, restrained himself from
dismissing cabinet ministers who are in opposition to the Wako draft, a
rare attitude in African governance. As a foretaste for the potential
benefits of Section 86 of the draft constitution (which would "order
Parliament to give land" to the landless, and offer loans to others to
buy land), President Kibaki distributed land titles to 4,500 households
in one site and 12,000 households in another and 10,000 households in
another area. He thereby turned to using his incumbency positively
rather than turning to rampaging violence against his challengers as
being exibited by Preside Yoweri Museveni in neighbouring Uganda. The
protestations by his Minister for Land that he "would have prefered to
issue the allotment letters after the referendum", would fool but a few
people.
The Kenyan Police also deviated from the sad familiar road elsewhere
in Africa. It restrained the Electoral Commission from banning further
rallies, after two major incidences of violence, by arguing that doing
so "will cause insecurity" since the ban would fuel urges to flout the
directive.
Various groups exploited the situation by visiting President Kibaki
with petitions for him to grant them districts of their own in "a bid
to extract maximum favours in exchange for backing the referendum" by
ticking the banana symbol on the ballot paper. Civil society have also
learned the ropes of exchange in the chemistry of politics. The
opposition is led by an alliance between Raila Odinga (son of Vice
President turned opposition leader to President Jomo Kenyata) and Uhuru
Kenyata (son of Jomo Kenyata). One is a Luo and the other a Kikuyu, two
of Kenya's major ethnic blocs; thereby blunting the fierce flames of
ethnic passions in the referendum vortex.
The main issues in the contest have been four. While the government
favours giving the president a free hand to appoint as large a cabinet
as patronage politics would dictate, the opposition want a fixed
cabinet of 25. The government side wants to protect the president from
easy impeachment by "a rouge parliament", and insists on a 75 per cent
pro-impeachment vote in parliament. The opposition says this is an
impossible hurdle and will protect presidential dictatorship. The
government favours two tiers of government administration: at the
national and district levels. The opposition wants "devolution" into
national, provincial, district, and local government structures; a
system which they argue would take resources away from corrupt
officials at the centre. Finally, the government will accept only a
"Prime Minster" who is an appointee of the President and the leader of
"government business" inside parliament. The opposition wants an
independent centre of power who cannot be "easily fired by the
president". The clarity of the issues has allowed the people of Kenya
an opportunity to discuss and fight over their constitution with an
intensity and broad involvement of the common people that is close to
that of South Africa after the end of white racial dictatorship in
1994. This is a plus for the growth of democratic politics in Africa.
The Kenyan debate is strongly influenced by the destructive
dictatorship of 24 years of Arap Moi's reign. A brutal struggle against
that regime gave birth to the democracy which put Mwai Kibaki in power
in 2002. A cardinal element in that struggle was demands for safeguards
against the use of centralized power to entrench corruption and the
despoilation of the economic welfare of the majority of Kenyans by an
alliance of greedy and brutal cabals who manipulate ethnic loyalties.
There are also memories of other ethnic groups being excluded from the
dividends of governance. Whatever the results of the referendum, Africa
expects Kenyans to avoid the horrible roads to civil strife taken in
Rwanda in 1994 and Cote d'Ivoire since 2000.Both sides should hear and
adopt durable messages from the other camp. Both should export to other
polities in Africa core values and tools for engineering nation
building and healthy durable societies which have been developed from
this campaign whose prosecution has gulped down vast amounts of time,
collective imagination and financial resources.
Kenya, after all once held out to the whole of Africa a symbol and
agent of freedom named "Jomo Kenyata."Critics insist that Kenyata
betrayed this mission in Kenya, but cannot deny the good use to which
others put them.

Original_Search_Title: Who Funded Yes, No Campaigns Rallies?
Headline: Kenya: Who Funded Yes, No Campaigns Rallies?
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Apart from the excitement, violence and emotions the Orange and Banana
campaign teams have so far generated, their source of funding has also been
a major issue.
From the print, radio and television advertisements to the massive
rallies literally awash with oranges and bananas, it is evident that
millions of shillings have gone into the campaigns. The big question is, who
bankrolled the campaigns?
There have been claims advanced by the Banana team that the United
Nations Development Programme and ActionAid funded their Orange rivals. But
both the No team and the two agencies have dismissed these claims.
On the other hand, the No crusaders have accused their rivals of using
Government resources to campaign for the Banana camp. It has been difficult
to draw the line between the campaign for the referendum, as a Government
project, and the campaign for the Banana team.
The Orange team says it has mobilised its resources through donations by
Members of Parliament in the No camp.
The team has also held several mini harambees to boost its kitty.
"We contributed at least Sh60,000 each and that is what we use to hire
public address systems, pay secretariat staff and other general expenses,"
Kanu Secretary-General William Ruto said recently.
The Eldoret North MP said he could not believe Yes MPs were grumbling
that Sh500,000 promised to them was too little.
About 102 MPs are said to be in support of a No vote. Contributions from
these members alone - others gave more than Sh60,000 - yielded about Sh6
million.
And whenever the team has had to hire choppers for their rallies, each
MP contributed towards the bill, Ruto added.
The politicians also fueled their own cars throughout the period.
Colourful shirts and caps became the hallmark of the Orange team.
Clothes Designer Fundi Frank did brisk business by coming up with fancy
and colourful shirts and baseball caps emblazoned with an orange and No
slogan on them. The shirts were selling for Sh2,000, while the caps went for
Sh1,000.
Music producers have also come up with songs in praise of the Orange
team. JamNazi of the "I'm not sober because I have taken Guinness" has
changed the lyrics to "I'm sober because I have taken Orange".
The Banana team has not been left out. It has released several tunes
urging Kenyans to vote Yes. The latest is the adaptation of Bwana nipe pesa,
which chides those in the Orange camp and urges Kenyans to shut them
out.
Things have been obviously different for the Banana team, whose campaign
is better endowed. For example, the team has been able to distribute
campaign materials like T-shirts and caps to supporters free of charge.
The campaign is unlikely to take a toll on the pockets of the Yes
campaigners, who were advised by a Cabinet minister to collect Sh500,000
each from the camp's secretariat.
It has been a luxurious campaign for the Yes team, whose radio adverts
are more frequent than those of the No camp.
That they have a bigger kitty is also evident from the abundant use of
helicopters in the campaign. Although the team has been using choppers
belonging to the Police or Government corporations, the leaders have stated
that the planes are fully paid for.
The Government has indirectly footed part of the campaign bill,
especially the transport and security costs.
Most ministers have ignored calls to stop using the fuel guzzling
Government four-wheel drive vehicles and drivers. Therefore, they have
enjoyed free transport.
The Electoral Commission of Kenya and members of the diplomatic corps
are among those who have demanded that the ministers stop using public
resources.
Apart from ministers and assistant ministers, the provincial
administration officials and security officers have used their official cars
for both the Banana and Orange rallies.
Taxpayers have paid for the security officers' transport as well as the
ammunition and teargas canisters used to break up rallies or disperse
crowds.
Delegations hosted by President Kibaki at the various State Houses have
also cost the taxpayers money in terms of food.

Original_Search_Title: Moment of Truth for Constitution
Headline: Kenya: Constitution: Moment of Truth
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kenya went into meditation and prayer for peace yesterday as it prepared
to confront its destiny at today's referendum on the New Proposed
Constitution. Barriers fell as faithful flocked into churches and mosques to
pray for a peaceful conclusion of a process that has taken 15 turbulent
years.
United by a common destiny, faithful packed churches and mosques with
clerics calling for unity and peace at this important time in the history of
the country.
To take part in the exercise one has to produce a national identity
card, be a registered voter, and be armed with a voter registration
card. The ballot paper has only two symbols - Banana for Yes and Orange for
No.
The wave of prayer swept across the country as the Police Commissioner,
Major General Hussein Ali, warned against celebrations or street protests
over the outcome.
Close to 60,000 security personnel from the regular police,
Administration Police, Prisons and Kenya Wildlife Service will be mobilised
to ensure there is peace. Ali assured Kenyans sufficient security personnel
had been deployed to the 19,000 polling stations countrywide. He spoke as
Orange leaders led by by Roads minister Raila Odinga and Kanu
Secretary-General William Ruto converged on their headquarters to ask their
supporters to congregate at Nairobi's Uhuru Park on November 23 "to devour
the fruit 0f victory and begin the process of national healing".
In a statement read by Ruto at the Orange House yesterday, the Raila
group told its supporters to attend the November 23 rally to celebrate,
because "we are confident of a landslide victory."
Once the Wako Draft is rejected, they said, "We shall embark on a
consultative process that will culminate in new constitution reflecting the
wishes of all people of this country" before 2007.
Ruto said that the proposed constitution had ignored "the popular" views
of Kenyans expressed at the Bomas National Constitutional Conference.
Raila who read the Kiswahili translation said: "We are confident we will
win the vote. We call for the celebration to unite Kenyans in the future."
However, Ali stressed that police would not allow street celebrations or
protests because they would compromise security, adding, "the force has
instructions to ensure no crowd mills around any polling station."
The road Kenya has walked to date is replete with blood, tears,
acrimonious debates and deaths, eight alone in the last one month.
The years have been turbulent; campaigns tailored along ethnic
lines. Leaders have gone for each other hammer and tong to have a new
constitution enacted since the infamous 1988 mlolongo elections. But today
presents an opportunity for Kenyans to put all these behind them and rebuild
the country.
The Catholic Church through its highest decision-making body - the Kenya
Episcopal Conference - urged Kenyans to remain peaceful during and even
after the results are announced.
Through its chairman, Bishop Cornelius Korir, the conference that brings
on board all the 27 Catholic bishops, urged Kenyans to accept the outcome of
the exercise.
At the All Saints Cathedral, the Provost Rev Peter Karanja urged
President Kibaki not to be "vindictive and vengeful".
He asked him to embrace his opponents and give them another chance to
help him build the nation. "We must as Kenyans persuade the President to
continue with the same restraint and not be vindictive and vengeful after
the referendum. He must give his opponents yet another chance to build the
nation with him."
The provost spoke against a background of mounting calls on the
President to sack Orange ministers - Raila, Kalonzo Musyoka (Environment),
Najib Balala (Heritage), Ochillo Ayacko (Sports) Jebii Kilimo (Immigration)
and Prof Anyang' Nyong'o (Planning).
The prayers went on as the President made a last-minute pitch for the
Banana team through a televised address even as Vice President Moody Awori
took the campaign to Teso, despite the fact that the campaign period
officially ended on Saturday.
Karanja showered praise on President Kibaki for exercising restraint,
this far "even in the face of provocation. "We ask people around the
President to share in his magnanimity and nationalism, recognising that as a
nation, we are making a transition into a new national ethos and not merely
having political contests."
He also asked the coalition of opposition parties "to read the mood of
Kenyans as they wanted to get on with their lives and do less politicking."
"For this nation to heal, everyone must return his or her dagger back to the
sheath after the referendum and seek to continue the process of constitution
making inclusively rather than exclusively."
Muslim leaders in Kwale also conducted prayers for a peaceful
referendum. Hundreds of residents attended the prayers led by Council of
Imams and Preachers (CIPK) south Coast chairman, Sheikh Khamis Banda, at
Jomo Kenyatta Primary School grounds in Msambweni.
Banda said the prayers had been called to ask God's guidance for Kenyans
to vote peacefully. He called on Kenyans to accept the outcome of the
referendum and embark on building the country.
"We are praying for a peaceful referendum and acceptance of results. We
want peace during voting and after the results are announced," Banda
said.
He appealed to Coast residents to refrain from chaos and at the
referendum. Banda said CIPK was concerned over the recent referendum
campaign chaos at Likoni where four people were shot dead by police.
In Nakuru, Catholic Bishop Peter Kairu urged ECK to closely monitor
today's referendum vote to avoid rigging.
Speaking during the Sunday mass at Christ the King Cathedral, the cleric
said Kenyans were anxious about the outcome of the referendum.
Praying for peace and stability after the outcome of the polls Kairu
urged leaders to accept the outcome because the people would have decided
whether they want a new constitution or not.
Special prayers for a peaceful referendum were also conducted at Our
Lady of Peace, Consolata Cathedral in Nyeri where Archbishop Nicodemus
Kirima told the faithful and political leaders to exercise restraint.
Kirima was optimistic that peace would prevail.
"We are not rejecting this draft because of politicians, but because it
does not uphold the morality of the country," Bishop Margaret Wanjiru of
Jesus is Alive Ministries told her congregation in Nairobi.
She asked Kenyans to reject the document, but also advised: "The process
has taken so long and so many insults have been traded by the rival camps
but this is the time for healing and forgiveness."
Wanjiru called on both sides to respect the outcome of what she called
the peoples' choice.
Kirima said whether the Orange or the Banana side wins, Kenyans should
remain united. He also advised Kenyans to reject leaders dividing them
across tribal lines.
In Western Kenya, three clerics - Rev David Kodia (Anglican) Rev Kenneth
Wachianga (Anglican) and Simon Makokha (Legio Maria) - called on Kenyans to
peacefully cast their vote.
It is through peaceful and transparent voting, they said, that either
the Banana or Orange camps would accept defeat.
While delivering a sermon at St Stephen's Cathedral in Kisumu, Rev Kodia
called on his flock to rise above ethnic interests. He urged them to vote in
big numbers to usher in a constitution founded on the principle of
negotiation and consensus but not shrewd political games characterised by
corruption, ethnicity and killing.
"Let us not accept to continue living like slaves, like people trapped
in the middle of confusion and political machinations of a clique of selfish
individuals supporting the proposed constitution, free yourselves people of
God," said Kodia.
The head of Legio Maria in East Africa, Bishop Simon Makokha, appealed
to his followers to freely choose which side to vote, saying he had no
powers to influence anyone.
He, however, said he would continue advocating for a Yes vote since the
country had for the last 15 years yearned for a new constitution. "I wish
Kenyans could take the draft as it is then we can amend it later because
going back to the drawing board means another round of wasting time and
resources," said Makokha.

Original_Search_Title: It's D-Day for Referendum
Headline: Kenya: It's D-Day for Referendum
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Ballot papers and other election materials are already in all polling
stations, the Electoral Commission of Kenya (ECK) has said.
The ECK vice-chairman, Gabriel Mukele, said: "It is all systems go for
today's voting in the referendum."
The materials were transported to the stations on time, he said.
Chairman Samuel Kivuitu at the weekend inspected the commission's
operation centre at the Kenyatta International Conference Centre.
Several ECK commissioners accompanied Kivuitu during the inspection of
the centre where the results will be announced.
Kivuitu said he was confident that the results would be announced by
tomorrow.
"Let us remain united despite who wins the referendum," he said.
Kivuitu said the ballot paper is simple in that it has only two spaces
for putting a cross either against the Orange or Banana symbols.
He said observers would be in all polling stations to ensure a free and
fair process.
Kivuitu said before voting for Yes or No Kenyans should ask themselves
whether the document contains their aspirations.
He also urged people to avoid violence.
The results from constituencies will be received at room two at the KICC
where the commission has set up its Press centre.
Yesterday, election officials, led by returning officers, started their
operations in all the centres across the country.
The returning officers will operate from Divisional Headquarters in all
the 210 constituencies.
The referendum on the proposed new constitution is the first since
Independence to be conducted by the ECK.
Kivuitu said the losers should continue with their struggle to achieve
what they stand for, peacefully.
Kivuitu warned that any results announced by the media before ECK should
be ignored.
He said: "No media house counts the votes therefore any other source of
results should be ignored," he said.
Kivuitu said Kenyans would not be voting for any person, but for how the
country should be governed.
"How that power will be shared between the Central Government and your
districts authorities, between the Central Government and Parliament," he
said.
Kivuitu said Kenyans would also decide the rights and privileges of the
minorities and the marginalised.
"Whatever the case do not rely on destructive thoughts like your
tribes," said Kivuitu. He said voters must think of Kenya as one country
shared among several tribes.
"If we care for our tribes or hate or despise other tribes then it means
we do not want Kenya," said Kivuitu.
The ECK boss said Kenyans must vote with clear conscience.
"Banish all evil thoughts and vote without tribe or religion in mind,"
he added.
He said voters should assist ECK to detect cheating.
Kivuitu said some ECK officials might deliberately ignore to check if
the finger index has been dipped in ink and voters should be alert to point
this out.
Kivuitu also said people with special needs like the physically or
visually challenged and the elderly will be given preference to vote
today. He said for the first time the referendum officials would also cast
their votes.
Kiviutu said expectant mothers and those carrying babies, and the sick
and would not queue.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki's Televised Speech Causes a Stir
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki's Televised Speech Causes a Stir
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki yesterday made the final pitch for the Proposed New
Constitution through a televised address to the nation, which was roundly
condemned by the Orange team.
The President, who last evening flew out of Nairobi to take part in
today's historic vote at his Othaya's constituency, endorsed the document,
saying it is "acclaimed to be one of the most modern and progressive in the
world."
He asked voters to turn out in large numbers and vote. "Tomorrow's vote
will impact, not only this generation, but generations to come. I urge you
all to come out in large numbers so that, together, we can make Kenya an
even greater country," he added.
However, in a swift rejoinder, the Orange team led by Roads minister
Raila Odinga and Kanu Secretary-General William Ruto asked the Electoral
Commission of Kenya (ECK) to take action against the President for
campaigning outside the official period.
Raila, accompanied by Planning colleague Prof Anyang' Nyong'o, said the
President's address to the nation was illegal as it went against ECK
regulations.
Ruto - who spoke to The Standard on the phone- said the President's
address confirmed that he was above the law. "His action disqualifies him
from convincing anybody to vote for the Proposed New Constitution," said
Ruto.
Ruto said the address also confirmed fears that the Banana team would go
to any length, including breaking the law, to win the referendum vote.
Kalonzo said the President had intimidated the Electoral Commission of
Kenya. "What the President did amounts to campaigning out of time." The
minister said the military or the police should not intimidate Kenyans when
it comes to security as the President had alluded.
Before the President's address, Vice President Moody Awori was in
Amagoro constituency campaigning for Yes though the campaign period ended on
Saturday.
Awori described the draft as a pro-poor document that guaranteed a
better future for the disadvantaged in the society.
He argued the document has created the office of the public defender who
would ensure that poor Kenyans pursue justice freely whenever they were
aggrieved.
He was speaking at Amukura divisional headquarters in Teso district
during a tour of development projects in the district. He was accompanied by
the area MP, Sospeter Ojaamong.
The Draft Constitution that has provoked heated debate and sparked
sporadic violence that has so far claimed eight lives offered an ideal
platform for economic growth, the President said in a televised speech at 4
pm.
The President later flew to Nyeri, before proceeding to his Othaya
constituency where he is expected to cast his vote for what he described as
the "product of our common endeavour".
"This Proposed New Constitution is acclaimed to be one of the most
modern and progressive in the world. I am therefore confident that we have a
constitution that creates a very good environment for faster growth
throughout the country," he said.
However, the Orange team said the ECK regulations required politicians
not to use State resources to campaign for either Banana or Orange.
Addressing a Press conference at the Orange Secretariat, Nyong'o and
Raila said the President had issued a partisan political statement for the
Banana campaign from State House, abusing his position as Head of
State.
"The President should have gone before wananchi yesterday at Uhuru Park
and put his case as torch bearer of the Banana campaign," said Nyong'o.
The two leaders said the points the President tried to put across in his
television and radio address were not only weak and overstated, but flew in
the face of a close analysis of the Wako Draft.
The President, in the speech that read like a summary of the main points
in favour of the draft, cited a clear separation of powers between the arms
of the State, the creation of district governments and enhanced
representation for women in Parliament as among the draft's strongest
advantages.
The Orange team criticised the President for "persisting" in seeking to
convince Kenyans to vote for the proposed constitution using material
incentives.
"Such promises are good for party manifestos and not for addressing the
merits and demerits of the Draft Constitution. Kenyans should realise that a
constitution is not good only if it is capable of dishing out goodies to all
and sundry," said Nyong'o.
He added that the Wako Draft called for the scrapping of unviable
districts, saying the Finance Permanent Secretary, Joseph Kinyua had already
announced that the districts created recently by the President would be
established once a study was done to establish whether they were
viable.
Mwea MP Alfred Nderitu, who is in the Banana team, welcomed the
President's televised address. "The President has a double role as MP for
Kenya and Othaya and none of those opposing his stand has a similar right."
Environment minister Kalonzo Musyoka accused the President of
intimidating Kenyans in his address to the nation.
Kibaki expressed confidence that the country was well equipped to absorb
the accumulated tensions of the 15-year-long process. It had seen off
greater challenges, the President noted, while urging voters to reject
incitement to violence by people he said were motivated by selfish
interests.
"Let us remember that Kenya is bigger than any one of us. Let us all
therefore strive to ensure the common good of our country. Let us reject
violence and the pursuit of personal interests that endanger the unity of
our nation," he said.
He added: "Let us shun violence and respect the rights of others to have
differing opinions. We are a great country that has overcome many challenges
in the past. We have a duty to ensure that we come out stronger, united and
prepared to face the future with confidence."

Original_Search_Title: Kenyans Set for Referendum
Headline: Kenyans Set for Referendum
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kenya's government and the opposition accused each other of plotting
polling day violence when they held rallies on Saturday in a last push
for votes ahead of a referendum seen as a dry run for the 2007
elections.
The opposition camp, seeking rejection on Monday of a proposed new
constitution for the east African country, scored a boost to morale by
attracting more than 30,000 supporters compared to about 12,000 at the
government meeting.
Shops and offices were boarded up across the centre of the capital
of three million amid fears of clashes between the rival camps. But
both gatherings passed off peacefully.
Riots convulsed Nairobi for three days in July when the constitution
text was published, killing at least one. Another eight have died in
riots at campaign rallies in past weeks. The proposed constitution is
intended to boost democracy following decades of often autocratic rule
but campaigning has degenerated into a bare-knuckled fight for power
ahead of 2007 rather than a debate on the complex issues in the charter.
Norman Nyagah, an ally of President Mwai Kibaki, said the opposition
was trying to foment unrest to derail efforts by the government to
secure approval for the new draft charter.
"Deep down their hearts I know they want chaos: they want to create mass protests and disrupt peace," he said.
Kibaki ally and local government minister Musikari Kombo said: "This
referendum is a vote between those who like peace and those who like
violence." The opposition levelled the same charge at the government.
"They are planning to incite people so that they could rig," said
Raila Odinga, a cabinet dissident who has teamed up with the opposition
to lead the "No" campaign.
His colleague, environment minister Kalonzo Musyoka, predicted an
easy victory. "We can't lose. If they rig, then you'll have Ukraine
here, Musyoka said, referring to people-power demonstrations in Ukraine
earlier this year.
Kibaki, 74, is leading the campaign to approve the new constitution under the symbol of a banana.
The main opposition party and a dissident faction from Kibaki's
ruling National Rainbow Coalition (NARC) are opposing under the symbol
of an orange. The fruits were chosen by the electoral commission to
help illiterate voters.
Many Kenyans say the result of the referendum will in effect be a
verdict on the performance of the president, who took office in late
2002 promising to end the graft, poverty and crime associated with his
strongman predecessor Daniel arap Moi.
Disappointment with Kibaki is widespread, and many Kenyans estimate
that a significant portion of the country favours a reduction in the
powers of the head of state. But Kibaki's own large Kikuyu community is
expected to support him.

Original_Search_Title: 'Yes' Team Expects Change in Fortunes
Headline: Kenya: 'Yes' Team Expects Change in Fortunes
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Major political realignments are expected in the post-referendum period and key political players who staked their fortunes behind the Yes vote are keeping their fingers crossed.
Key figures in the Banana campaign predict a dramatic change in political fortunes and formation of new alliances ahead of the 2007 elections.
Whichever way the vote goes, President Kibaki's loyalists are also sharpening their knives for the final fallout with their Liberal Democratic Party coalition partners "who have been more belligerent and displayed greater opposition to us than the opposition itself," as assistant Foreign Affairs minister and Sirisia MP Moses Wetang'ula puts it.
The Banana campaign is being backed by the National Alliance (Party) of Kenya (NAK) faction of the National Rainbow Coalition (Narc), which brings together Ford Kenya, the Democratic Party of Kenya (DP) and the National Party of Kenya (NPK).
All the groups rallying behind the President in the Banana campaign have their own expectations.
Dr Noah Wekesa, an assistant minister for Livestock Development, does not mince words about where Ford-K should be in the wake of a Banana win.
But the bigger prize will be the place of Ford-K in the Banana coalition.
"Ford Kenya threw its support behind the Banana campaign before everybody else. We made up our minds and announced our positions without making any demands on President Kibaki. Others decided to make some bargains before declaring their support. If Banana wins, it will be a huge boost for Ford-K and our leader, Mr Musikari Kombo, whom we expect to be the running mate for President Kabaki in 2007," Dr Wekesa told the Sunday Nation.
He added: "If we win, the National Alliance Party of Kenya will have shown Kenyans once again that we are focused, and we know where we are going. We will have led the way in delivering a constitution just like we did in 2002. NAK was the first to announce its leadership and unveil its line-up to the public. The move galvanised our supporters and small parties behind us. This was the turning point that inspired other opposition parties to coalesce around us, which culminated in the formation of Narc behind candidate Kibaki," he said.
A Banana win would create the impetus to move forward towards implementation of the new Constitution and preparation for the 2007 elections, Dr Wekesa said.
"We will once again have become the pace-setters around which others could join towards 2007."
But then there is the possibility of losing. "In case we lose," says Dr Wekesa, "it will simply mean we backed the wrong horse and the country will be back to square one with the constitutional reform process. In that case, it will mean negotiating an acceptable position with the winners. In which case we will need sober-minded persons and we have to exclude extremists in our midst like Mr Kiraitu Murungi and others."
Mr Murungi, the minister for Justice and Constitutional Affairs, is seen as one of President Kibaki's most powerful hard-liners and architects of the proposed new Constitution.
The minister insists Kenyans have been anxious to give themselves a new Constitution and they will vote Yes.
"The first beneficiary will be the entire country, which has been fighting for a new Constitution for 15 years. A Yes win will enable us to move forward to implement the new Constitution, attend to development agenda and consolidate peace and stability. Second, there will be major political realignments. The Banana campaign will keep the spirit up and burning and consolidate its position," he says.
Mr Murungi says a win will greatly strengthen the Government of National Unity, with constituent parties getting stronger and forming a formidable political force to push the agenda forward towards 2007.
"However, I am also expecting a lot of pressure on President Kibaki to sack rebellious ministers. There are high expectations in the Banana camp that ministers Raila Odinga and Kalonzo Musyoka and others will be shown the door. If not, the President should expect protests from Banana supporters expressing dissatisfaction. We can't go through all this and expect the rebels will get away with it," Mr Murungi says.
He does not envision a Banana loss and declines to comment on the Government's options in case the No-vote camp carries the day.
On demands that will be made by the parties backing the Yes vote, Mr Murungi says:
"Ford Kenya made a strategic calculation. Mr Nyachae is chairman of the Banana team. He and Mr Kombo will be among the top leaders in the new dispensation."
Mr Wetangula puts it this way: "We proceeded to the referendum as a Government of National Unity and in a coalition of the willing. I don't see a situation where members of the Orange movement will continue being in government. They have been more belligerent and displayed more opposition than the Official Opposition itself."
In the event of victory, says Lands and Housing minister Amos Kimunya, there will be "overwhelming pressure" on the President to reorganise the team that is expected to implement the new Constitution.
"A Banana win will be a golden opportunity for this government to embark on implementation so Kenyans can enjoy what they have been asking for. The win would also give us the impetus to forge a formidable political force to drive us into 2007 with confidence," Mr Kimunya says.
The minister, however, does not delude himself that losing is not an option.
"If the Banana campaign loses, the Government will still continue. We will have to sit down and do a postmortem to find out the reasons for the loss. A lot of people's problems have come out in a strong way and we shall seek to address these concerns ahead of the 2007 elections."
He predicted political mischief and disintegration in the Orange movement, whichever way the referendum goes.
"More mischief will play out in the Orange movement. To start with, Kanu will want to reassert its role as the Official Opposition party, whose role has been hijacked by the the LDP partners. Various players will emerge as heroes and villains, depending on how many votes each manages to mobilise. In any case, they have already launched an Orange campaign trophy. This will be the beginning of the end for Kanu bigwigs Uhuru and Ruto. LDP's Kalonzo and Raila will face the same internal divisions. Don't forget Kanu is yet to punish Raila for walking out on them in 2002," Mr Kimunya says.
Subukia MP Koigi Wamwere has no illusions about a loss for the Banana campaign: "If Banana supporters lose, they will have no choice but to stick together like mud, for the onslaught on them will be vicious ahead of 2007.
"Of course, it will be tragic because if the Orange campaign carries the day, the real victors will be Kanu who support the status quo and always fought the new Constitution."

Original_Search_Title: A Laudable Political Display
Headline: Kenya: A Laudable Political Display
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Tomorrow is the day Kenyans have been eagerly waiting for. It will see a sizeable number of the nearly 11 million registered voters troop to the polling stations around the country to cast ballots which will determine whether or not we should adopt the proposed new Constitution.
It is the culmination of a long, rocky road to the referendum, with numerous campaign rallies by the Banana camp, rooting for the new document, and the Orange group, rejecting it. There have been ugly skirmishes at some rallies, the worst of which resulted in the deaths of nearly 10 people. However, many will be pleased to note that the biggest parallel meetings by the two groups went on peacefully at Nairobi's Uhuru Park and the Nyayo National Stadium.
This has confirmed that the fear that the two final rallies would have degenerated into the worst violence since the Electoral Commission of Kenya blew the whistle for the official start of the campaigns a month ago were unfounded. The good news is that the various speakers were able to sell their sides without any hindrance.
Granted, debates on the proposed Constitution have been furious, with some of the politicians resorting to incitement and the use of unsavoury language against their opponents. And the disagreements have sometimes turned violent, resulting in bloodshed.
However, going by the political maturity displayed during the simultaneous rallies, it would appear that we are back on the track to political maturity. That such huge political campaigns could have been held peacefully within a short distance of each other is a plus in our organisational ability.
Once again, Kenyans are showing the East African region and the international community, who have been keenly watching as we try to forge a new constitutional dispensation to improve our governance, that we may shout at one another but we have a pretty good idea about where we want to go.
Building on this momentum, we hope tomorrow's voting will equally be conducted in a peaceful, free, fair and transparent manner. A huge turnout will not only confirm the premium attached to the whole process, but will also help yield a clear verdict that we can work on for the good of the country and for our mutual benefit. Kenyans should continue to demonstrate a high degree of democratic spirit and mutual tolerance.
And, when the results are announced, we hope, the true will of Kenyans will emerge. It is, therefore, noteworthy that the leaders of both camps have pledged to accept the verdict, whatever the result.
Again, we have a chance to show that our people can compete vigorously but are also gracious both in victory and defeat. Bad losers have caused turmoil in other countries. We cannot afford to go that way.
Our major goal is the bigger picture of unity, peace and stability. It is the tolerance, of especially the key players in the process, the politicians, that will redeem us, as a nation. After all, divergent views need not necessarily create disharmony.
And we must commend the police, who have in the recent past come under severe criticism for preferring brute force to civilised policing, for the exemplary job done yesterday. This confirms that with a little more forethought and proper planning, they can keep the peace without resorting to bashing a few heads.
We hope the same high level of policing will be employed during the voting and counting of the ballots and also at future functions. The trick is that there is no wisdom in waiting for the situation to get out of hand and move in with batons and live bullets.
We have heard what the leaders had to say, and in peace. The verdict now rests with the voters, whose will must be accepted by all.

Original_Search_Title: This is How the Provinces Are Likely to Vote
Headline: Kenya: This is How the Provinces Are Likely to Vote
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Tomorrow, Kenyans troop to polling stations across the country to participate in a historic exercise to either adopt or reject the proposed new Constitution. Sunday Nation writer, OSCAR OBONYO takes a critical look at the likely voting patterns in each of the country's eight provinces.Central
In the 2002 presidential polls, voters in Central province were split between Kanu's Uhuru Kenyatta and Narc's Mwai Kibaki. But this time around, they appear to have solidly rallied behind the President.
Mr Kenyatta and the Liberal Democratic Party secretary general, Mr Joseph Kamotho, who are pushing for the Orange vote in the region, have made little progress.
The biggest challenge, though, for the Banana campaigners is ensuring a good voter turn-out which would assure them of the lion's share of the province's 1.5 million voters.
However, the Orange team is also destined to claim pockets of votes in urban areas within the province, especially in Thika and Kiambu districts where Mr Kenyatta still enjoys significant political clout.
Coast
When the 880,00 registered voters in Coast province show up at polling stations, one key issue will influence how they vote - land.
The land problem, which cuts across all districts and ethnic groups in the province, is an emotive one and has featured prominently in referendum campaign rallies.
The problem of squatters is widespread in Mombasa, Kwale, Kilifi, Malindi, Taita Taveta,Tana River and Lamu districts.
However, there is no indication of a clear cut winner between the Orange and Banana camps. Also, the province is notorious for registering one of the poorest voter turn-outs in the country.
Eastern
The battle for Eastern province's 1,734,209 voters - the country's second highest after Rift Valley - promises to be the most competitive with both the Banana and Orange camps starting off on an almost equal footing.
Home to 13 districts, the province is politically demarcated into three voting blocs, namely: Ukambani, Embu-Meru and the Northern bloc which comprises Moyale, Isiolo and Marsabit districts.
Ukambani's four districts - Machakos, Makueni, Mwingi and Kitui -boast the largest voting bloc in the province of 905,208 registered voters (according to official statistics prior to the 2002 General Election). Ukambani region is largely inclined to the Orange camp, courtesy of the push from Environment minister Kalonzo Musyoka.
The Embu, Meru and Tharaka districts, whose tally is 721,889, are inclined towards the Banana camp. The main movers here are Justice and Constitutional minister Kiraitu Murungi, and Co-operative Development minister Njeru Ndwiga.
The Northern bloc, which technically belongs with North Eastern province, but is geographically in Eastern province, has 107,112 registered voters.
With intensified campaigns in Ukambani, the perceived Orange stronghold in the province, the Banana camp could easily carry the day in the province. Mr Musyoka's team, on the other hand, has concentrated more on consolidating the Ukambani vote at the expense of venturing in the Embu-Meru-Tharaka Banana strongholds.
The belated but active entry of Health minister Charity Ngilu into the campaigns could well boost Banana's chances in Ukambani, thereby tilting the provincial scale in favour of Banana.
Nairobi
