The cosmopolitan Nairobi province is literally split down the middle. And even judging from the sitting elected MPs, they are divided into two halves with four supporting either side.
Although the ethnic divide element has over the years played a big role in determining the Nairobi vote, both Orange and Banana campaigners are banking on the literacy and awareness levels among city residents to woo the 961,295 registered voters.
North Eastern
With a paltry 216,336 registered voters, the province is the least lucrative vote-hunting zone in the country.
Nonetheless, it has attracted great attention from key figures in both camps, sending mixed signals on its position in the referendum vote. Initially, residents appeared hostile to both groups, pelting them with stones and disrupting rallies organised by either side.
Since coming to power, Narc has been wooing area residents. Among the initial indicators was the appointment to the Cabinet of Wajir East MP Mahamud Abdi and Fafi MP Adan Sugow, both of Kanu, as Regional Development minister and assistant minister for Water Development respectively. The two are spearheading the Banana campaigns in the region.
Labour assistant minister Khalif Ahmed and MPs Billow Kerrow (Mandera Central), Yusuf Haji (Ijara) and Abdulahi Ali (Wajir North) are in the Orange camp.
With most Kanu legislators supporting the Orange camp, the No campaigners start off as favourites.
Nyanza
That the Banana campaigners have staged spirited campaigns in all parts of the country other than Luo Nyanza is quite telling - that the area is strictly Orange and that it is as good as lost to the Banana schemers.
Roads minister Raila Odinga is the man to beat in the area. In fact, the recent formation of the People's Progressive Party by Information minister Raphael Tuju must have been one of the frantic efforts to outmaneuver the Langata MP at the referendum ballot.
However, the most formidable challenge for the Orange camp comes from Energy minister Simeon Nyachae, with whom they could share the 1.5 million voters in the province.
Kisii, Nyamira, Kuria and Gucha districts are likely to go the Banana way - if residents follow Mr Nyachae's stand as they did in the last General Election when he vied for the presidency on a Ford People ticket.
This notwithstanding, the lakeside province is expected to return a convincing No vote. The margin could be even higher considering the fact that the Luo Nyanza districts are comparably highly populated.
Rift Valley
The province is home to retired President Daniel arap Moi, who has declared his opposition to the proposed new Constitution.
And although he has not actively participated in the pro-Orange campaigns, his nod for the group has greatly boosted the team's campaign in the region which boasts the highest number of voters in the country - 2.5 million.
Although he briefly lost his clout in 2002 after annointing Gatundu South MP Uhuru Kenyatta as Kanu's presidential candidate, he appears to have regained formidable influence in the region. Most of the politicians who fled to Narc at the time, including former State House Comptroller and Nominated MP Franklin Bett, are now in the same camp with Mr Moi.
Apart from the highly populated Kalenjin community, the Orange camp is expected to make considerable gains from Samburu, Kajiado and Narok districts among the Samburu and Maasai pastoral communities. Cabinet ministers William ole Ntimama and Prof George Saitoti are the key figures in the campaigns among the pastoralists for the Orange and Banana camps respectively.
The Banana camp has its biggest catch in Nakuru and Laikipia districts, especially in the populous Nakuru and Naivasha towns.
The Orange camp starts off as favourites in the expansive province.
Western
The one man who best captures the political mood in the province is Trade and Industry minister Mukhisa Kituyi. During President Kibaki's recent visit to the region, he told him to his face that the Luhya felt short-changed following their support for Narc in the last General Election.
"We would like to get closer to the Government and feel the warmth," said the Kimilili MP. And he conceded that the Banana team had experienced problems in their campaigns because of this factor.
The problem areas for the team include Butere-Mumias, Vihiga, Teso, Mt Elgon and Busia districts, which are largely inclined to the Orange camp. However, the Banana camp enjoys popularity in Lugari and the populous Bungoma and Kakamega districts.
That Vice-President Moody Awori and Local Government minister Musikari Kombo have literally camped in the area during the last week, is a pointer to the fact that all is not well.
Only two weeks ago, Mr Awori's rally in Busia town was met by a hostile crowd in his home district.
Western province voters might just cast a protest vote against Mr Awori for his perceived dismal performance, thereby handing victory to the Orange in the same manner they did to Mr Musalia Mudavadi in 2002, when he was Vice-President.
Yet still the Awori-Kombo group might pull off a last minute surprise. Their trick has been to play the tribal card - that they should remain united as their Luo neighbours.

Original_Search_Title: Movers, Shakers And the Mwenje Factor in Polls
Headline: Kenya: Movers, Shakers And the Mwenje Factor in Polls
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Perhaps the most dramatic development in the referendum campaigns was the defection of Embakasi MP David Mwenje from the Orange to the Banana camp six days to polling day.
A few weeks earlier, the fire-breathing legislator had declared his candidature for the presidency come 2007, ostensibly to square it out with President Kibaki. This was after he was arrested and confined in police cells for a weekend for disrupting a Banana strategic meeting at a hotel in his constituency.
A few days before announcing his "official defection" at Parliament Buildings, he was dancing excitedly on the podium at Kamukunji grounds, Nairobi, assuring his audience that contrary to rumours he was not about to defect.
Nonetheless, Mr Mwenje finally succumbed to the whims of the very man he had sworn to challenge in 2007. The move may have come late, but it certainly helped to re-energise the Banana campaign, particularly in Nairobi.
However, MPs on both sides of the divide have reservations over Mr Mwenje's defection. While welcoming his re-appearance in the Banana camp, Dagoretti MP Beth Mugo said: "We shall only celebrate his contribution to the Banana camp if he stays on until Monday (tomorrow)".
The Education assistant minister, however, maintained that the move was a victory for the Yes team.
"This is just like the parable of the prodigal son in the Bible. He has returned home. We are very happy and welcome him to the Yes team to work with us for the remaining days," she told Sunday Nation.
The move to woo the Embakasi MP was part of the Banana camp's strategy to cripple the Orange campaign. And judging from Makadara MP Reuben Ndolo's reaction, the defection may have dealt his team's campaign in Nairobi a major blow.
"I am particularly hurt that after giving me firm assurances, (Hon) Mwenje finally opted to abandon me. It is unfair and a wrong decision because the two of us have suffered greatly under the very establishment he has now gone to support," he said.
Mr Ndolo and Mr Mwenje were arrested last month and charged with malicious damage to property. They spent three nights locked up in different police stations, following some fracas at a city hotel. The legislators, who were charged at Makadara law courts, are out on bond.
"He told me severally that DP-allied politicians literally camped at his house every night, pleading with him to cross over. He was restless and I knew that soon he would give in," the Makadara MP told this paper.
However, Mr Ndolo maintained that Mr Mwenje's move would not significantly impact on the performance of the Orange camp in Nairobi.
Apart from Mr Ndolo, three other MPs -- Mr Fred Gumo (Westlands), Mr William Omondi (Kasarani) and Mr Raila Odinga (Langata) -- are key Orange campaigners in the city, although the Roads and Public Works minister has been sucked more in the national Orange campaigns.
One man, though, who must be experiencing a sigh of relief following Mr Mwenje's defection is Mr Irshad Sumra. A major political rival of Mr Mwenje, Mr Sumra had increasingly become less visible the moment the Embakasi MP shifted camp to Orange.
During the last elections, Mr Sumra contested the Embakasi seat on a Safina ticket, coming second to Mr Mwenje. He has since decamped to LDP and is one of the main Orange campaigners in the city.
Reacting to Mr Mwenje's defection, he said: "Huyu mtu ametusumbua sana hapa Embakasi. Hata ni vizuri ameenda sasa (It is good riddance for this man who has been troubling us in Embakasi)".
Another behind-the-scenes crucial player in the city campaigns is Mr Njee Muturi, a personal assistant to Leader of Official Opposition Uhuru Kenyatta, who is one of the Orange luminaries. Efforts by the team to get businessman Mr Jimnah Mbaru on board flopped.
That the Orange campaigns and rallies have been quite dramatic and interesting is not in doubt. Conceding this fact, Nominated MP Ms Njoki Ndung'u, one of the leading Banana camp's campaigners, opines that the colour that Orange has brought to rallies could fail to translate to votes.
"On the contrary we adopted a less colourful interactive campaign, which is quite effective," said Ms Njoki, who is in charge of Women and Youth campaigns in the Banana camp. Most of her operations have been based in the city.
But not everything about the Banana has been colourless. The colourful Ms Orie-Rogo Manduli has been gracing the Yes rallies in the city. Having initially supported the Bomas Draft, many expected Ms Manduli to push for the Orange position.
A sizeable number of Nairobi civic leaders, led by current and former mayors, Mr Dick Wathika and Joe Aketch, respectively, are actively involved in the Banana campaigns.
Other legislators spearheading the Banana campaign in Nairobi are Mr Norman Nyagah (Kamukunji), Mr Maina Kamanda (Starehe) and Nominated MP Mrs Betty Tett.
According to the LDP secretary-general, Mr Joseph Kamotho, the return of Mr Mwenje to the Banana camp is tantamount to "committing political suicide."
The Mathioya MP warned Mr Mwenje that he would be disappointed if he campaigned for the adoption of the proposed new Constitution.
Mr Mwenje may well have lost part of his political clout, according to the Orange campaigners, but the Banana camp has reclaimed a crucial vote.

Original_Search_Title: Music, Humour Abound At Banana Rally in Nairobi
Headline: Kenya: Music, Humour Abound At Banana Rally in Nairobi
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A musical and yellow affair it was, the final banana rally at Nairobi's Uhuru Park.
Pulsating and throbbing music filled the park as thousands of banana
supporters, draped in yellow attire and brandishing miniature banana
symbols, danced to a rendition of the hit song Nikiwa Ndani by Prince
Adios.
The hit song brought out both the young and not so young to their feet, as they sang recanted words of the catchy tune.
And so they sang : "Ukiwa ndani, wapiga kelele, ukitoka nje,
unanungunika, halafu unasema mimi sikupendi, nawe kumbe hupendeki" (If
you are inside, you make noise, when you go out, you complain that you
are not loved, but it is because it is hard to love you).
And as the crowd danced to the music, a group of young people near the VIP dais had something else in mind.
They danced slowly and seductively with a purpose towards the dais,
where a several bunches of ripe yellow bananas had been placed on the
table.
Within minutes, a free for all ensued as the group surrounded the
bananas and started feeding on them, to the consternation of Kamukunji
MP Norman Nyagah, who was the master of ceremony.
It took Mr Nyagah some time to restore order, and by then, there were no bananas in sight.

Original_Search_Title: No Camp Puts Up Brave Face Over Sack Threat
Headline: Kenya: No Camp Puts Up Brave Face Over Sack Threat
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The day is Wednesday, November 23, 2005, and Electoral Commission
of Kenya Chairman Samuel Kivuitu has just delivered the eagerly awaited
poll results. The victors, he declares, are Orange enthusiasts.
But even before he divulges details of the tallies, crowds at the
Kenyatta International Conference Centre, now hosting the polls
coordinating centre, erupt into thunderous applause. The ecstasy is
replicated in major towns across the country, and in some ugly
instances, with violent reactions from opponents.
Amidst the unending celebrations, Orange luminaries, including
Leader of Official Opposition Uhuru Kenyatta, Cabinet ministers Raila
Odinga, Kalonzo Musyoka, Anyang' Nyong'o, William Ole Ntimama and Najib
Balala and former Vice-President Musalia Mudavadi hold a series of
"thank-you" rallies mocking their Banana rivals and charting the way
forward come 2007.
But stung by the humiliating defeat, the Government operatives opt
to use the last weapon available to them - handing the errant ministers
a sack. But this action only pours fuel on the already burning fire as
it accords the victims enough time and freedom to intensify their
agenda.
This is just one of the scenarios that may come to pass on
Wednesday, the day set by ECK for official announcement of referendum
poll results. Indeed, the anticipated sacking of ministers,
witch-hunting and retaliation by Orange sympathisers rank high on the
list of fears expressed by some leaders of political parties within the
Orange camp, interviewed by the Sunday Nation.
"With the President having taken sides on this matter (referendum),
I can assure you it is going to be very hectic for ministers in the
Orange camp to operate under him," says the Liberal Democratic Party
chairman, Mr David Musila.
The Mwingi South MP observes that even if the President, in his
characteristic way, plans to move on with his ministers, their sulking
colleagues will engage in witch-hunting, rendering the situation
intolerable.
"While we do not expect the President to sack LDP-allied ministers,
considering that we are equal partners in the coalition, the sack talk
has been around for long and we have always anticipated it," says Mr
Musila.
Such an action, he adds, will give the party enough time to mobilise
the masses without fear and strategise ahead of the 2007 General
Election.
In the event of an Orange win, part of the strategy according to
Kanu's national vice-chairman, Mr Chris Okemo, is to toil on a legal
avenue that will bring back the Bomas Draft back to Parliament.
The Nambale MP explains that the strategy is to have the document
tabled in Parliament, discussed and enacted ahead of 2007. It is,
though, based on the hope that the Orange-allied MPs will get the
numbers in Parliament.
"Once we win the referendum then most MPs will most likely cross
over to our side, which is the people's choice. This will be the
natural thing to do, except for those who want to say goodbye to
Parliament," says the Kanu official.
The Bomas Draft, Mr Okemo explains, offers room for power-sharing -
a factor that will ease congestion and power struggle in both Banana
and Orange camps. Same as the proposed new Constitution, it provides
for the posts of President, Deputy President, Prime Minister and two
deputies, unlike the current one, which only has President and
Vice-President. Presently, the Orange team is a full-house of political
heavy-weights, including Messrs Kenyatta, Odinga, Mudavadi, Musyoka,
Balala, Okemo, Ntimama and Dr Bonaya Godana, among others.
And as the Kanu vice-chairman aptly observes, going to the 2007
polls under the current Constitution would pose a real power-sharing
headache, not only to the Orange team but the Banana one as well.
However, leader of the Labour Party of Kenya, Dr Julia Ojiambo, does
not foresee a major rift or misunderstanding over power-sharing within
the Orange camp.
"We have learnt a lot by working together under the past very
challenging and stressful moments. To us, it has been a healing process
resulting from the politics of hatred among ourselves and I can assure
you we are ready to work through this again as a team," she says.
However, the former assistant minister and Funyula MP, stresses the
need of strengthening political parties within the Orange camp.
According to Dr Ojiambo, the camp will hold even more if it operates as
a movement but gives room for political parties to flourish.
But before the Orange movement think seriously about forming the
next Government, says Dr Ojiambo, there are "minor" problems that
MPs-allied to it have to deal with - key among them, salvation of the
Bomas efforts.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki Faces Tricky Post-Poll Options
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki Faces Tricky Post-Poll Options
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Tutawafuta-a-a. Hakuna kitu wanafanya kwa serikali bali kupiga mdomo
tupu (We'll sack them. There is nothing they are doing in government except
talk)." President Kibaki said it rather casually, but the import was not
lost to his listeners. He was speaking to a delegation of Yes supporters in
Western Province when he visited Kakamega this week. It was indeed the first
time the President was saying openly that he would sack the Orange members
in his Cabinet.
Cabinet ministers Raila Odinga, Kalonzo Musyoka, Najib Balala, Anyang'
Nyong'o, Linah Jebii Kilimo, Ochillo Ayacko and William ole Ntimama want
Kenyans to vote against the proposed law at the referendum. They have teamed
up with the main opposition party Kanu to form the Orange Democratic
Movement, which is rallying the public to reject the proposed
Constitution.
The fate of the dissidents is no longer an if; the pressure from
government loyalists for them to be dispensed with soonest can no longer be
ignored. In fact, the President risks an unprecedented rebellion from within
his ranks if, this time, he chooses not to act. Bumula MP Sylvester Wakoli
was hardly talking in jest in Kakamega when he told the Head of State "to
get the courage to sack these people" this time round. As a matter of fact,
Mr Wakoli's sentiments can be taken to be mild compared to those coming from
the Central Kenya loyalists.
Nominated MP Njoki Ndung'u is one of those who see Cabinet dismissals as
being inevitable. If this does not happen, she warned that "the loyalists in
government are going to make it known that can't work with them," meaning
the Orange ministers. Measuring her words carefully and deliberately, she
asserted that "the President will have to choose between working with the
loyalists or the other group."
In all probability, the fate of the LDP bandwagon in the Cabinet would
have been sealed long before the referendum hostilities broke out if it were
not for a group of elderly, old-time advisers of Kibaki - together since the
early days of the Democratic Party - who would often urge moderation.
Speaking to the Sunday Nation recently, one of the President's advisers
(he did not wish to be named) intimated that he would not be surprised if
President Kibaki ignored the dissidents and left them to their own devices,
as he has always done. But even he acknowledged that the anger of the Yes
ministers with their Orange colleagues had reached a level that could only
be placated with something decisive.
In any case, the pressure has been coming from even more influential, if
unofficial quarters. When touring Othaya and Karatina a few weeks ago, First
Lady Lucy Kibaki revealed that she had been pressuring her husband to act on
the rebel ministers. For some reason she had particularly bad words for Mr
Musyoka, whom she excoriated as somebody with "bad manners."
The successive crises that have plagued President Kibaki's
administration have each been marked with calls from the loyalists to ditch
the dissidents, which by and large, the President had ignored.
Yet the crises have been unceasing, from the initial LDP anger over the
first Cabinet composition in early 2003 to subsequent quarrels over the
power-sharing agreement.
Then came the war of words over the proposed Narc elections, which never
took off. But all these were small potatoes compared to the unrelenting war
over the Constitution.
It was ostensibly in an effort to check the persistent rebellion in his
government's ranks that President Kibaki brought into Cabinet figures from
Kanu, Ford People and the LDP. Though the majority of these co-opted
ministers have loyally supported President Kibaki's line, some like Mr
William ole Ntimama have broken ranks with him over the Wako Draft.
It was at the height of the battle over the leadership of the
Parliamentary Select Committee on the Constitution this year when the LDP
brass sought to make a definite move. They had been incensed with the
elevation of Mr Simeon Nyachae to the chairmanship of the committee and the
removal of key LDP members.
There is a very real possibility that the Orange ministers may opt to
bolt before Kibaki moves. If so, that would happen just before or
immediately after the referendum.
In fact, the defiant declarations by Mr Odinga and Mr Musyoka that they
are "ashamed" to be in this government could be a pointer that they could
jump ship before they are pushed. So far, however, the Orange chiefs have
chosen to be coy about what they will do.
That the die may have been cast is indicated by the fact that the
so-called Old Guard group that would previously seek a truce when
Establishment hawks wanted the LDP ministers ejected seems to have gone
hardline too.
Special Programmes minister Njenga Karume, who is a leading player in
this group, has severally warned the Orange leaders at referendum rallies
that they have tested the government's tolerance to the limit. Internal
Security minister John Michuki has been expressing similar
exasperation.
The breaking point seems to have come with the recent controversial
utterances by Mr Musyoka and Mr Odinga about a likely coup d'etat, remarks
which the two later complained were taken out of context.
Yet matters go beyond sacking dissidents. Ever since the referendum
campaigns began, one of the running jokes of the season was: Whichever side
wins, President Kibaki comes out on top. It is the classic "heads I win,
tails you lose" situation; if the No team wins, the current Constitution is
retained and the President gets to keep all his present powers.
If the Yes team is successful, he fulfills his pledge to deliver a new
Constitution while ceding some minor powers to Parliament and a probable
PM.
For a long time, one of the points of debate has been the survival of
the Kibaki presidency after the referendum. All the President's men (and
women) have been urging him to sack the dissidents.
Speaking in Kibwezi on Thursday, Lands and Housing minister Amos Kimunya
said: "Whichever way the referendum will go, the Banana team will not work
with ministers in the Orange camp."
The President has steadfastly refused to sack any of his ministers.
After Wednesday's statement in Kakamega, it looks more than likely that
whatever the outcome, heads could roll.
This raises the question of the President's real options.
If he sacks the entire Orange team, who will he replace them with? What
will such a "night of the long knives" mean for the Kibaki Government?
Will the Government benches in Parliament be depleted if all the Narc
rebels cross to the Opposition side?
What will be the fate of the already shaky Narc coalition?
This sort of speculation could continue endlessly. The only thing that
remains quite obvious is the fact that the Cabinet will find it extremely
difficult, if not downright impossible, to work together after the
referendum if the hostilities exhibited during the campaigns are anything to
go by.

Original_Search_Title: Leaders' Call for Peace Pays Dividends
Headline: Kenya: Leaders' Call for Peace Pays Dividends
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Nairobi province security committee monitored activities as
the two rallies got under way barely a kilometre away from each other.
Led by provincial commissioner Francis Sigei and area police boss
King'ori Mwangi, CID chief Sammy Githui as well as security
intelligence head S. Kamau, they coordinated the activities to ensure
the rival groups of supporters did not meet and that no rioting or
looting took place.
After going around the city in a police helicopter for more than an
hour as the rallies went on, Mr Sigei thanked both sides for being
peaceful.
He attributed the peace to appeals by leaders from both sides to their supporters not to cause chaos.
"The meetings are going on peacefully, thanks to Nairobians," the PC
said at one point. "We have, however, taken enough measures to make
sure all is well. The leaders should lead the way and guide their
supporters during these trying times.
"More important is next week when people should go and vote without fear and go back to their work places peacefully."
The city centre and and the residential areas were under
surveillance by police who reported to their command post in case of an
incident.
Mr Mwangi and Mr Githui said no ugly incident had been reported.
All police officers in the city, including those at the CID
headquarters, were on Friday afternoon ordered to report to work as a
normal day.
About 4, 500 of them - uniformed and in plain clothes - were
deployed at various parts of the city as the rallies went on. They
would be on stand-by duties until Tuesday when the referendum results
will be announced.
But despite the heavy police presence, they did not impose the
half-kilometre buffer zone on Uhuru Highway between the stadium and
Uhuru Park, the venue of the Yes rally, as earlier announced.
Mr Mwangi on Thursday declared the stretch between Bunyala Road and
the Haile Selassie Avenue roundabouts a no-go zone during the rallies.
But by 4pm yesterday, traffic was flowing along the highway unrestricted.
Police also kept vigil at the City Mortuary roundabout, the planned
deviation point for vehicles using Ngong Road to access the Banana
rally, the Orange one or those carrying non-partisan people headed for
the city centre.
But all roads at the roundabout were open despite Mr Mwangi's
directive that only those heading to the Yes rally use Valley Road,
those to the Orange one take Mbagathi Way and those to the city centre
go to Haile Selassie Avenue.
And the police "listening post" at Vigilance House continued to
monitor radio and television transmissions for any inciting broadcasts.

Original_Search_Title: This Propaganda War is All About Wit
Headline: Kenya: This Propaganda War is All About Wit
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Some of the adverts placed recently in the national newspapers by the two teams in an effort to appeal to the voter's mind.
As the referendum campaigns came to a close, I found myself idly
analysing the political advertising that has of late flooded the media.
Everywhere you turn there is an "advertisement" on the radio,
television, press, billboard, poster, T-shirt or body paint urging you
to sample either fruit for a better future.
By the time of going to press, media monitors Steadman and
Associates had recorded gross media spends on political advertising for
the period September to November at Sh35.5 million. This, of course,
does not include billboards, banners, yellow and orange T-shirts and
shirts, caps, bandanas and body paint!
In the first few weeks of the referendum campaigns, there was
virtually no theme advertising from either camp. The Orange team was
busy traversing the countryside in their trademark orange shirts,
lambasting their rivals in the Banana camp while the latter were, well,
presumably organising themselves for a campaign that would "shake the
whole nation."
Today, though, the future is oranges, and "Just say yes" no longer refers to an extinct brand of mobile telephony.
As the public rallies of both camps literally took over the media in
terms of news and events covered, the real Yes and No ads started
running in earnest.
The Bananas released the television campaign about women's rights
which makes use of the illiterate to sell bananas to the literate.
There is the woman with a baby who speaks endlessly in her vernacular
about how grateful she is for free education and why she will vote
banana. Then there is the other one who talks about inheritance of land
by women, and so on. All the ads, of course, end with a very cultured
male voice over urging you to vote yes for the education of our
children; for women in Parliament; inheritance, etc.
A good stroke, however, is not something you would say about the
first Orange thematic television ad. Coming after several non-starters,
the very colourful 45-second animated graphics ad opens with an orb
that turns into a ripe orange.
This is grabbed in mid air by a child who divides it into two. He
holds one half aloft and it produces an orange glow that projects into
an orange sunset - all this to some song about the colour of our future
by an African-American sounding Kenyan singer whose name I have
misplaced. As the sunset threatens to blind you with its brightness, a
voice over takes over. "One people, One purpose," it booms. "Our future
is orange. Vote No."
Beautiful wordplay, you think. Until you hear the Swahili version
which employs the same music by the same singer, but now in Kiswahili
in order to reach the illiterate. It is an unrepentant direct
translation of the English - Siku zijazo ni za Machungwa (the future is
oranges). The same soundtrack is used on radio ads in both languages -
with the same effect.
What I thought was a good touch for the Oranges - lest someone pelts
me with rotten ones! - was the "con.tradiction" ads on TV where old
footage of Kiraitu Murungi, John Michuki and others, before they were
in power, is contrasted with current footage.
Unfortunately, the good point the ads try to make is spoilt by the
visual clatter of words and diagrams introduced, presumably to drive
several points home - points that would have done well to make other
ads.
This, I suppose, is the ad the Bananas have hit back at with the
newspaper ad that asks the rhetorical "Do you want this (a rotten
orange crawling with worms) or this (a nicely ripe banana)?"
Other notable ads include the Orange one that begins beautifully
with lots of drama and the statement "They had a dream... what's your
dream?" with successive images of Mau Mau being hunted, pro-democracy
Kamukunji rallies and 2002 Narc rallies, followed by a text
presentation of Article 31(3) of the draft Constitution - with a rushed
voice over running over it - and ends with a big red X across the text
on the screen and the voice of a "wise old man" urging you to remember
the old saying that "he who is hit with a club returns, but he who is
hit with justice never comes back. "
The Bananas too have a song. It begins with chants of "Banana,
Banana," has a window for a message and ends with "Say yes, yes, Just
say Yes, Say Yeees," and a voice over that implores you to vote Yes for
"a new Constitution, a new Kenya. "
The ads are in various languages and use the window to explain the
reason why the new Constitution is good for you; or where to go for the
next campaign, or even to wish all Muslims Idd Mubarak before
(cleverly!) inviting them all to the next venue for a Banana party.
Mr Igane is an advertising practitioner

Original_Search_Title: Friends Or Foes?
Headline: Kenya: Friends Or Foes?
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Mwai Kibaki and his Roads Minister Raila Odinga are
strange bedfellows. So strange that they have separately been forced to
insist that they are "friends".
But are they?
When Kibaki was in Kisumu a few months ago, he paid glowing tribute to Raila for helping him win the elections.
But the two soon parted ways just after the publication of the Wako Draft.
Kibaki had earlier graced the wedding of Raila's daughter and those are the rare photo opportunities that the two have had.
Kibaki and Raila are rarely seen together in public and Raila is not
one of the insiders in Kibaki's Cabinet and is neither a confidante.
One of the reasons is that Kibaki loves to hang out, not with political
buddies, but some of his old friends in the golf circles and from
Makerere University and Kenyatta government days.
Thus Raila would never match Njenga Karume, Matere Keriri or Dr Joe Wanjui in search of space inside State House.
Certainly Raila, an avid soccer fan, is not any of these and is
never seen in golf circuits where Kibaki's loyal lieutenants are found.
The two are also from two ideological groupings and Raila is seen as
a reincarnation of his father, Jaramogi Odinga, spirit in resistance
while Kibaki is a moderate whose speeches hardly move crowds.
A trained engineer from former East Germany's Madgeburg College of
Advanced Technology, Raila's political orientation was for a long time
thought to be communist but has of late re-engineered himself as a
democrat although his critics would love to paint him as the opposite
of Kibaki: non-calculating and bent on causing chaos.
During the run-up to the 1997 General Election when Raila vied for
the Presidency on a National Development Party ticket his main
criticism was directed at Kibaki rather than Moi.
Although he polled 667,886 votes against Kibaki's 1,911,742 the two simply had no time for each other.
Rather than join either Kibaki or Kijana Wamalwa who was fourth with
505,704 votes, Raila decided to start cooperating with Kanu.
Interestingly it is this group that has coalesced around the Orange
campaign once gain since it has most of the Kanu veterans and the
Kanu-breakaway group, the Liberal Democratic Party which is a major
partner in the ruling Narc.
For Kibaki, Raila is a new entrant in Kenyan politics and although
he has not attacked him in person there is a sense of betrayal in both
camps.
Raila, and the LDP felt betrayed by the NAK counterparts after they
failed to honour the Memorandum of Understanding reached between the
two parties ahead of the 2002 General Election.
At the moment Kibaki feels betrayed by Raila for leading a section
of cabinet ministers to oppose the Wako Draft. Although Kibaki has not
mentioned Raila by name in his speeches his key supporters have based
their campaign on personal attacks on Raila who they want sacked.
A man who joined national politics at the dawn of Kenya's
independence as Kanu Executive Director, Kibaki is today the longest
serving member of parliament and might have not much to discuss with
Raila.
But Kibaki admits that Raila has a solid block of votes an issue
that NAK strongly considered prior to last General Election when they
decided to woo him with an MoU into their camp.
Raila had by then felt betrayed by then President Moi for proposing
Uhuru Kenyatta and campaigning for him as Kanu's presidential candidate.
There was fear then that if Uhuru did not get a solid Kikuyu candidate to oppose him, Kikuyus would vote for Kanu.
Thus Kibaki, as the senior most politician in the NAK-LDP alliance was ahead of the pack.
Observers say they were friends of convenience brought together by two common purposes - all informed by different backgrounds.
While Raila was angered by Moi's failure to back him as Kanu's
candidate, Kibaki had never forgiven Moi for demoting him as number 2
shortly after the 1988 General Election.
But rather than join the radicals within the emerging Forum for the
Restoration of Democracy which had Jaramogi Oginga Odinga as its
leader, Kibaki surprised many in the then opposition by founding a new
party - Democratic Party of Kenya.
Ever since, until the last General Election, Kibaki and Raila have always been on different paths.
That the two are diametrically opposed to each other has been noted as the Banana-Orange campaigns got underway.
The incompatibility of Raila and Kibaki is borne of their
characters: one is a hotheaded, no-holds barred risk-taker while the
other is a cautious, conservative politician derided by critics as a
fence-sitter.
Raila's critics also say that a man on another destructive power
seeking mission which he denies saying that his is the democratic path.
The fallout between Kibaki and Raila happened after Kibaki announced
his first Cabinet and LDP felt short-charged in that it had not been
treated as "an equal partner" in the sharing of seats.
The MoU had also set up the Summit of eight, drawing four members
each from LDP and NAK but this again has flopped putting a distance
between the two leaders.
As the two leaders go to vote tomorrow Kenyans will be watching
keenly on how the two titans will treat each other after the Wako Draft
fallout.
There is already talk that Kibaki might reshuffle the government.
The President himself has of late been warning those "bent on causing chaos" that he does not fear anyone.
Whether the time has come for the two to call it quits will be known after tomorrow.
But certainly, the Orange and Banana campaign has brought out a fact: Kibaki and Raila are not the best of political friends.

Original_Search_Title: The Economics of Bananas And Oranges
Headline: Kenya: The Economics of Bananas And Oranges
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Tomorrow you are expected to go and vote on the Proposed New Constitution.
For this reason alone news, analyses and politics of the Banana and
the Orange symbols are now ubiquitous. That is what readers are
demanding.
So, to be the true, faithful writer - and one trained in economics,
I must add - I shall supply what is in demand. In keeping with the
current lingo, let's call this people-driven writing!
We have been flooded with Banana and Orange politics. Let's for a
moment, if only for a break, consider the economics - some witty
analyses of the business side of what began about 15 years ago, so we
are told.
First, consider the proliferation of organisations and NGOs in the last 10 years, set up to agitate for a new constitution.
Hadn't we had a flawed constitution, some expenditure, and
employment, in this industry would not have taken place. I am not
downplaying or taking lightly the role this sector of the economy
played.
The point being made here is that the Change-the-Constitution sector
was perhaps one of the few that did not decline throughout the 1990s
when the economy was in the doldrums.
Second, while still on proliferation of organisations, the
constitutional review process and the need to conclude it led to the
formation of a ministry soon after the last elections.
Even though this just consisted of a single, relatively small
ministry, it did surely add to the fiscal problem that Kenya has been
trying to deal with: a swelling public sector but which delivers less
services and value for money.
Third, let's turn to bananas and oranges - proper.
Thanks to the creativity of Mr Samuel Kivuitu, Cabinet ministers and
politicians started addressing press conferences with these fruits
spread everywhere from conference tables to their necks. Of course, one
would not expect a minister to pay Sh5 for a banana or an orange as I
do at the kiosk next to my house. They must have been paying top dollar.
The traders who were in the orange and banana business in
'Marikiti', for the last couple of weeks, must be laughing all the way
to the bank. Somehow, Wanjiku had to benefit from the katiba process,
regardless of the tricks Mr Wako might have played in Kilifi.
In this regard trickle-down economics has worked for the common man!
Fourthly, consider this fact: we are being told that the two camps
are spending billions in campaigns, ranging from publicity and travel
to - let's admit it - bribery.
A bribe can take many forms, as recent visits to State House have
shown. (Aside: I am comfortably enjoying a hair cut last Sunday
evening, and the barber quips: "Na sasa, wataita vinyozi lini huko
State House"! Mind you, it's not to shave Mr Kibaki or Mr Kabira.)
Now, when there is too much money being pumped into the economy,
people fear that there might be inflation, as was the case in the early
1990s when money was printed to finance similar campaigns. I want to
dispute this and argue the opposite.
The billions being spent by both camps are likely to be what economists call 'fiscal stimulus'.
The British economist John Maynard Keynes, who led the effort to set up the Bretton Woods, structured the concept.
Basically, a fiscal stimulus is public expenditure aimed at
kick-starting the economy, to initiate and encourage business and
private spending.
The reason why excess money might fuel inflation is that it is being
poured into the economy and not going into real production of goods and
services.
In the case of the bananas and the oranges, hundreds of thousands
are being spent on T-shirts, caps, fuel for cars and other publicity
materials. Overall, this money will start multiplying and the Sh100
spent initially adds another Sh500 in the economy in the final analysis.
This is called the multiplier effect.
Consider this example. The paper you are reading probably has 20 per
cent additional pages in order to allocate more space for Banana and
Orange full-page ads, more analysis and notices from the ECK.
Also, you probably bought two Sunday papers, as opposed to the one you buy other times.
The person who supplies the Standard Group with paper is therefore a
little richer, has probably employed more hands, while newspaper
vendors are making an extra buck.
T-shirt and poster factories worked 24 hours, employing more workers.
These workers will now be able to take "a kilo" home, enabling the
butcher to place a bigger order from the Dagoretti slaughterhouse.
The slaughterhouse in turn orders more beef cattle from Turkana, making the livestock trader a little wealthier
Fifthly, the Banana said that the proposed constitution is a
government project on which public resources would be spent. That's bad
for our budgetary process.
In essence, if the said resources were not provided for in 2005/06
Budget, that action has eroded Parliament's oversight role in the
budgetary process.
This will also destroy the democracy that Banana purports to seek to deepen.
Finally, the big one. Consider the fact that the Banana and Orange
camps are employing thousands of observers, campaigners, agents, and,
we are told, hooligans.
In 2005, the economy might not just grow at twice the 4.3 per cent
it did last year; we might additionally create those famous 500,000
jobs.
At least that will be one election promise kept, thanks to the economics of bananas and oranges!

Original_Search_Title: Matiba Has Been Steadfast in His Quest for Church-Led Way
Headline: Kenya: Matiba Has Been Steadfast in His Quest for Church-Led Way
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The debate is over - or is it - and Kenneth Stanley Njindo
Matiba, the man who gave the clamour for multi-party politics a new
lease of life has been missing in action.
Although Matiba's absence was not noticed it is his consistency on
how to draft a new constitution that has endeared him to his waning
supporters.
Matiba only emerged when the Wako Draft was published and surprised
many when he dismissed both the Bomas Draft and the Wako Draft arguing
that the entire process was flawed.
The former Kiharu MP, who in 1992 almost crippled Kanu's hold onto
power, has all along argued that religious groups should have been left
to draw up a new constitution and that the Bomas parley was not
representative of the Kenyan populace.
Matiba will not be voting for or against the Draft since he is not a registered voter.
That is why he had to surrender his Kiharu parliamentary seat during
the 1997 General Election insisting that the elections were flawed and
that the then Moi Government lacked political legitimacy to call
elections.
A man who once enjoyed a near fanatical support of the populous Gema
vote - apart from Meru and Nyeri, which are traditional Kibaki
supporters - Matiba has moved from an enigma to near irrelevant.
It was Matiba who dared Kanu by calling an unregistered rally in
Kamukunji (the famous Saba Saba Rally) and refused to apply for a
permit.
But in the quest for a new constitution Matiba was a lone ranger and
usually was at loggerheads with groupings that coalesced around the
National Convention Executive Council (NCEC), which was the main
machine that sustained the clamour for a new Constitution.
It was only Matiba's Ford Asili Party that decided to boycott the
1997 General Election protesting the failure to institute a level
playing-ground through comprehensive constitutional changes.
Although a Ford Asili faction led by Martin Shikuku went ahead to
nominate candidates it perished at the ballot box with all its
candidates.
Only Njeru Kathangu won a seat on a Ford Asili ticket as Matiba's
loyal lieutenants led Paul Wanyange and Ngengi Muigai founded a new
party Saba Saba Asili which was housed in Matiba's Marlborough House in
Westlands.
Despite his failing health, a result of a stroke he suffered in
detention, Matiba has from this base opposed moves led by either
parliament or civil society to write a constitution.
His famous take was: "Hii maneno tuachie makanisa".
Matiba has always played solo game. When Kibaki, Mr Kijana Wamalwa
and Mrs Charity Ngilu formed the National Alliance for Change before
the 2002 elections they decided to approach Matiba to give them a jolt.
But rather than listen to them at his Riara home Matiba accused them of
operating on undemocratic principles.
But it was because the three supported the minimum reforms that were
proposed by Moi ahead of the 1997 elections much to the chagrin of
Matiba.
They had also refused to join him in boycotting the elections.
There was a feeling then that Matiba was the only man who can
galvanise the Murang'a and Kiambu voters to vote its way instead of
running away with Uhuru Kenyatta then emerging as Kanu's candidate.
As Bomas went underway Matiba dismissed it as a "big joke" and said
that certain politicians were using the constitutional review to create
executive positions for themselves, "through the back-door".
"Observing what is happening at the Bomas of Kenya, one is moved to
ask for divine intervention - a great tragedy is in the making for the
country," said Matiba.
Matiba's arguments were that Bomas was dominated by politicians and
groups, whose main interests appeared to be "self-aggrandisement and
aggressive and shameless jostling for power and personal positions".
He said it was "catastrophic for this country that the spirit of the
conference is galvanised by arguments about positions in government
that politicians promised each other before elections To base a
constitution on such interests surely spells doom for the country."
Matiba has always questioned the composition of the delegates and
their selection criteria, saying the conference was not representative.
On the district delegates Matiba would say that some delegates came
from "illegal districts". Moi had created several new districts but had
not gazetted them as required by law.
Although he had always said he would lead Kenyans in rejecting the
Bomas outcome Matiba has missed a chance to offer a third dimension to
the on going Banana and Orange campaign.
But it is his consistency all through the 15-year clamour for a new constitution that will always be remembered.

Original_Search_Title: 'Yes' Ministers Makelast Pitch for the Draft
Headline: Kenya: 'Yes' Ministers Makelast Pitch for the Draft
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Three pro-Banana ministers yesterday appealed to Kenyans to vote for the proposed constitution, arguing it was the best.
Speaking at Uhuru Park, Nairobi, Education minister Prof George
Saitoti said the Teachers Service Commission would employ, discipline
and pay teachers if the new constitution is enacted.
He advised teachers to vote for the proposed constitution because
the Kibaki administration had revised their pay packages and ensured
they were paid without delay.
"This is the best document that this country has ever produced in the 21st century," said Saitoti.
He said the youth would enjoy many privileges if the new
constitution were enacted because it would create leadership
opportunities.
He said the physically challenged, women and the aged would also benefit from the document.
The minister said Kibaki's government would have performed even better were it not for sabotage by rebel ministers.
He said Kenyans had now realised that the euphoria created by the Orange team was misplaced.
The minister said the free primary and pre-primary education
programmes were entrenched in the constitution to avoid interference by
a rogue president in future.
And Finance minister David Mwiraria urged Kenyans to vote for the
proposed constitution because they had spent a fortune on the process.
He said over Sh10 billion had been spent since the process began in 2000.
"Please vote wisely because we have spent what it costs the country to fund free primary school each year," said Mwiraria.
He said the laws in the current Constitution were oppressive because
they did not offer opportunities to the disadvantaged in society.
The minister thanked Nairobi residents for turning up in large numbers for the rally.
Their response, he said, was testimony that they had rejected propaganda by the Orange team.
Mwiraria said the Ameru community had decided to vote for the constitution unreservedly.
"They joined hands across party lines and now those from Kanu, Narc,
LDP and even the Njuri Ncheke are behind the proposed constitution," he
said.
Water Resources minister Martha Karua, clad in a yellow designer
dress, said the new constitution was tailored to benefit marginalised
communities.
She asked the middle class and the poor to open a door of opportunity by giving the country a new constitution.
"It is for your personal benefit and development of the nation
because you will get education, health, water and other services," said
Karua.
The minister said squatters and the landless would be allocated land under the proposed constitution.
She said two Orange team leaders were sons of former Presidents and did not therefore know the plight of the poor.
She said another was the scion of an influential family because his father served has previously been Kenya's Vice-President.
She added the three leaders and a former MP whose father enjoyed
immense power in the Moi government only wanted to continue enjoying
privileges that come with leadership.
"They cannot understand how one can lack school fees and would
wonder if you had misplaced your store keys when you tell them you are
hungry," he said.
The Gichugu MP reminded Orange leaders that there was no short cut to power.
"They started opposing the government after we exposed their attempts to grab power through the new constitution," she said.

Original_Search_Title: President Skips Last Banana Rally
Headline: Kenya: President Skips Last Banana Rally
Body_Text:
Nairobi — It was an anti-climax amid song and dance for the Banana camp yesterday as President Mwai Kibaki skipped its last rally.
At Nyayo National Stadium, the Orange rally was attended by all leading lights of the 'No' campaign.
By the time Vice President Moody Awori took to the podium shortly after 3pm, the Banana crowd at Uhuru Park started leaving after realising the President was not coming.
Also missing in the Banana rally were Cabinet ministers Raphael Tuju, Charity Ngilu, Joseph Munyao, Amos Kimunya and Kivutha Kibwana.
Yesterday, we exclusively reported that the President, who is spearheading the 'Yes' campaign, would skip the camp's final rally.
While the Orange rally attacked the government's record on the delivery of a new people-driven constitution, the Banana rally attacked Raila Odinga, Gideon Moi and Uhuru Kenyatta.
"If you told them you have slept hungry they will ask you: Have you lost the keys to the food store?" said Martha Karua.
But at Nyayo Stadium the Orange team ended its case against the Wako Draft with a harsh audit of President Kibaki's performance, his ability to keep promises and what they termed as his failure to hold together a nation that was united behind only two years ago.
The leaders narrowed down their battle against the Draft to the powers given to the President.
To prove their case, the team, with Mbita MP Otieno Kajwang in charge of the session, played tapes of President Kibaki's speech before the Constitution of Kenya Review Commission ahead of 2002 elections.
The leaders then took turns to read what the Orange team called, "Our case against Wako Draft."
The leaders included Raila, William Ruto, Uhuru, Musalia Mudavadi, Dr Julia Ojiambo, David Musila, Linah Kilimo and William ole Ntimama.
Mutula Kilonzo, Prof Anyang' Nyong'o and Najib Balala also read parts of the case, then handed over to Kalonzo Musyoka to close the chapter.
In speeches as diverse as the speakers, the leaders all agreed on two things: That tomorrow is an historic day, and that Kenyans must cast their ballot against the Wako Draft.
Balala, invited by Ruto, set the ball on the audit on Kibaki rolling.
"They want to create a king and a monarch.
But I say even the Arab monarchs and dynasties were defeated at the Coast.
They want to create an imperial President. But we tell them that Kenyans defeated even the colonial empire. I assure you we will defeat the black emperor they want to create," Balala said.
Then he declared that the race for 2007 began yesterday, and dared Kibaki to sack ministers in Orange.
"Chris Murungaru has asked the people of Central Province that this is their constitution and that they should rise and defend it.
Kombo has said the Draft is good because it will help a person from Western Province take the presidency. Kiraitu said it is a government project. We alone, in Orange, are looking for a people's constitution. The Wako Draft has its owners," Ruto said.
Kalonzo Musyoka, began his speech with a declaration that God had given the Orange team a huge victory over Banana. Then he delved into the treacherous path that the search for a new constitution has been.
"The Banana team has caused an abortion of the Kenyan Dream. That was a powerful dream we had in 2002," Kalonzo said.
"The dream is in pieces now, but we have decided to pick the pieces and march on," he said.
The minister claimed Minister John Michuki had asked chiefs to restrict movement of people from November 20 but asked Kenyans to defy him.
Kanu chairman Uhuru Kenyatta said:
"We have been everywhere, from Coast to Kisumu, Mandera to Namanga. People have rejected this draft done by a few, and they have rejected it wholeheartedly."
State House said Kibaki will address the nation today at 4pm.
Both camps exuded confidence that they will win the referendum tomorrow with Internal Security Minister John Michuki, warning that security would be enforced in case of chaos.
"In case you are doubting you have seen a helicopter hovering around," said Michuki as he gave his mobile number to the crowd to report any incidents to him.
Local Government Assistant Minister Maina Kamanda - the only one who spoke about it - said people should not equate Raila with Kibaki.
Vice President Moody Awori urged Kenyans to keep peace and asked them not to be divided along tribal lines. Awori, who arrived at 3pm interrupting Nairobi Mayor Dick Wathika's speech, said: "The referendum should not be used to divide people. In the proposed constitution we have made it hard for anybody or parliament to remove an elected president."
It is a theme that had been picked earlier by Water Minister, Martha Karua who said that the proposed constitution has "no short cuts to power". By this time parts of the crowd had started leaving after they realised that Kibaki would not turn up.
Awori, who arrived in the company of Special Projects Minister Njenga Karume and Limuru MP Kuria Kanyingi did not make any sterling performance.

Original_Search_Title: Vice President's Chopper in Another Mishap
Headline: Kenya: Vice President's Chopper in Another Mishap
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Tension reigned in Kapenguria when a military chopper carrying
Vice-President Moody Awori failed to take off after three attempts.
The incident occurred as residents of athlete, Tegla Lorupe's Siyoi
village and the provincial security team led by area DC Stephen Ikua
and OCPD David Kerina, watched.
The VP had visited West Pokot to officially flag off Lorupe's peace
race at Makutano before laying a foundation stone for a Sh300 million
academy.
The VP was reportedly heading for a Banana rally in Nairobi.
Kapenguria MP Samuel Moroto and his Sigor counterpart Philip Rotino,
who had accompanied Awori to the event, were not at the scene.
But a senior security officer dismissed the incident, saying the chopper's pilot was testing its power.
Earlier, the VP had asked the Pokot to vote 'Yes' tomorrow. He said
those opposing the draft were doing so for their own selfish gain.
Awori praised Lorupe for her efforts to bring peace to the Pokot,
Karamojong and Turkana and challenged other athletes to emulate her.
Moroto and Rotino praised the government for creating East Baringo
District and demanded that Kenya Army personnel who have pitched tent
at Makutano Stadium be withdrawn.
The officers have been involved in a security operation. And a
technical hitch yesterday caused the cancellation of a UK- bound flight
at Mombasa's Moi International Airport.
The charter plane operated by First Choice Holidays had arrived in
Mombasa early in the morning with 258 passengers from Gatwick
International Airport, London.
A leak was detected in one of the hydraulic tanks which power the
Boeing 767's brakes, according to First Choice area manager, Jon Hilton.

Original_Search_Title: How Politicians Have Changed Tune Over the Years
Headline: Kenya: How Politicians Have Changed Tune Over the Years
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The road to a new constitution has been long and tortuous, and the end is not yet in sight regardless of tomorrow's outcome.
From a historical perspective, constitution making and re-making may
be said to have started in the early 1960s just before Independence.
Over the 40-plus years, many political personalities have left their
imprints on the process. Foremost among them is former President Daniel
arap Moi.
He was a prominent proponent of majimboism as advocated by the
moribund Kenya African Democratic Union (Kadu) in the early 1960s.
But when Kadu wound up in 1964, Moi changed tune to embrace the
pro-unitary government, a position he tenaciously held onto even in the
1990s when calls for a federal system of government gained currency,
especially in the Rift Valley.
Accused by political competitors as the stumbling block to a new
constitution, particularly after the onset of multiparty democracy in
1992, Moi is now supporting the 'No' campaign.
There are a number of Moi's contemporaries who participated in the
1962 Lancaster House Conference and are today playing significant roles
in the forthcoming referendum.
These include Martin Shikuku, who was Kadu's Secretary General.
Being a Kadu official, Shikuku was on the same side with Moi on
federalism in the early 1960s.
However, throughout the 1990s, Shikuku became a ferocious Moi critic especially on matters constitutional.
Today, however, Moi and Shikuku find themselves on the same side, opposing the adoption of the proposed constitution.
While veterans Moi, Shikuku and Dr Taaita Towett (Labour and Housing
Minister at Independence) are all championing the 'No' vote, Ramogi
Achieng Oneko, who attended the Lancaster Conference and later became
Information Minister, is on the 'Yes' side of the referendum campaign.
Oneko's position is interesting in that his 'Yes' position is
contrary to that taken by Roads Minister Raila Odinga. Yet Oneko was a
soul mate of Raila's father, Jaramogi Oginga Odinga - a fact that has
led to charges of betrayal by Luo-Nyanza MPs.
Of the Lancaster Conference generation of leaders still alive, two
of those conspicuous for their silence on the raging constitutional
debate are John Keen and Jeremiah Nyagah. The latter is however more
than sufficiently represented by his sons Norman (MP, Kamukunji) and
Joseph (MP, Gachoka). Norman's position as Government chief whip has
been proposed for elevation to cabinet status in the Draft
Constitution, a development 'No' campaigners have criticised as meant
to benefit him individually.
Be that as it may, Norman is an ardent supporter of the 'Yes'
campaign, while his brother, Joseph, is rather quiet about his
preferences.
In the 1970s James Njenga Karume, today the Minister for Special
Programmes in the Office of the President, led the
Change-the-Constitution campaign with the intention of stopping Moi
from succeeding the then ailing President Kenyatta.
Interestingly at that time, Moi had in current President Mwai Kibaki
an ally against the Change-the-Constitution group of politicians whose
nexus was the Karume-led Gikuyu, Embu and Meru Association (Gema).
Though Moi and Kibaki share the fact that they have held the vice
presidency and presidency, they are in mutually exclusive positions as
far as the current constitution review is concerned. And if the 'Yes'
vote sails through, Karume would have achieved his objective of the
1970s three decades later.
It would have been interesting to see which side of the debate
Karume would have been had official Opposition Leader and Kanu Chairman
Uhuru Kenyatta won the 2002 elections, given that Karume was his strong
supporter during the last elections before switching allegiances after
the polls.
One of the Change-the-Constitution proponents who seem to be missing
in action in the current debate is former Defence Minister and Kenyatta
physician Dr Njoroge Mungai.
One of the milestones in constitutional reforms was the 1982 parliamentary amendment that made Kenya a one-party State by law.
That amendment was passed in record time with Kibaki, then the Vice President, fronting the motion.
In subsequent years, Kibaki would rail at the same constitution for
among other things having been "messed" up through amendments.
One of the beneficiaries of the 1982 constitution change is Energy
Minister Simeon Nyachae, who ascended to the Chief Secretary slot that
had just been created.
As the current chairman of the Parliamentary Committee on
Constitution Review, Nyachae is today a major cog in the 'Yes' campaign
machinery, a position that puts him in the same camp as Kibaki, whom he
contested against for the presidency during the 2002 General Election.
The divergent positions that Kibaki and Raila have taken, with their
followers in tow, is like a new chapter in their chequered political
story.
In February 1999 for instance, Kibaki, then the leader of the
Official Opposition, and Raila, then leading the NDP, released a
stinging statement accusing the Kanu government of seeking to have the
upper hand in the constitutional review process.
The statement, read by Kibaki at a joint press conference, said: "We
urge the AG to discharge his functions without fear because the current
(sic) constitutional crisis has been partly caused by his fear of
intimidation by Kanu."
Not long after this Kibaki and Raila parted ways when the latter led
NDP into co-operation with Kanu and was soon made chairman of the
Parliamentary Committee on Constitution Review.
One of the more dramatic relationships attending the constitution
making politics has been that between Raila and former Ugenya MP James
Orengo. Both politicians were popularly dubbed the Young Turks in the
early 1990s as they campaigned for the repeal of section 2(a) of the
Constitution. They parted ways after the death of Jaramogi Oginga
Odinga and for over two decades trod divergent political paths.
Today, however, Raila and Orengo are sharing the Orange campaign platform.
At a rally in Mombasa recently, Orengo in fact had some endearing things to say about Raila.
Indeed an audit of the allegiances of the Young Turks of yesteryear
yields the fact that they today find themselves on opposing sides of
the referendum debate.
However, more of the no-longer-so-young politicians (it's a decade
since 1992) seem to be on the 'Yes' side of the divide and include,
Trade and Industry Minister Mukhisa Kituyi, Water Minister Martha
Karua, Constitutional Affairs Minister Kiraitu Murungi, Local
Government Minister Musikari Kombo and Kabete MP Paul Muite.
One of the otherwise vocal politicians, who seem to be taking a
rather low profile on the referendum debate, although he is thought to
be on the Orange side, is nominated MP Peter Oloo Aringo.
In 1990 at the onset of the campaign for constitutional reforms,
Aringo, then Kanu chairman was quoted rubbishing Jaramogi for calling
for a change to the constitution to allow multipartyism.
A few months later in 1991, he was on the Ford Kenya campaign trail strategising for Odinga.
In 1999 when NDP went into co-operation with Kanu, Aringo again took
a view critical to Raila that might have caused him his Alego-Usonga
parliamentary seat.
Since his appointment as the Attorney General in 1992, Wako has been
at the centre of the turbulent constitutional review process.
Throughout the 1990s and up to 2002, Wako had characteristically
toed the Kanu line on the constitution. Come 2003 and with Narc in
power, Wako has shifted into an anti-Kanu position if only as matters
constitutional are concerned.
As the chief drafter of the constitution that carries his name, Wako
finds himself in a situation that runs counter to the person who
appointed him - Moi.
On the flipside, Wako may well be said to be doing the bidding for
the Banana team in whose ranks are politicians such as Kiraitu, whom he
has had many run-ins with over the constitution in the past.
The image has that has been repeatedly used by TV stations is one
where a beaming Wako was handing copies of the Proposed New
Constitution to President Kibaki on the doorstep of State House. Less
than three years ago, the two men couldn't have been further part.
One could go on and on enumerating the shifting of positions by leading politicians over time.
Indeed, it will not be entirely surprising to see politicians make
unexpected about turns today ahead of the referendum tomorrow.

Original_Search_Title: It's Bananas Versus Oranges in the Constitutional Referendum
Headline: Kenya: Politics-Kenya:It's Bananas Versus Oranges in the Constitutional Referendum
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The campaign for Kenya's constitutional referendum entered its home stretch this weekend, with supporters and opponents of the draft constitution holding a final round of rallies ahead of voting on Monday.
President Mwai Kibaki and his National Alliance Party of Kenya (NAK) have lobbied citizens to approve the draft, meant to replace the constitution that has been in place since the country obtained independence from Britain in 1963.
NAK is part of a coalition government that also includes the Liberal Democratic Party (LDP) led by Roads and Public Works Minister Raila Odinga. The LDP and Odinga are fiercely opposed to the new constitution.
Those calling for a "yes" vote in Monday's referendum have chosen the banana as their symbol, while the "no" camp is using the orange. As much as the fruit symbols are being employed to help illiterate Kenyans make sense of the poll, they also served to turn this weekend's campaign gatherings into colourful affairs.
At the concluding rally of referendum supporters, held Saturday at Uhuru Park in the capital of Nairobi, bunches of bananas decorated the podium while many in attendance wore white T-shirts emblazoned with the word "Yes".
Despite the efforts of various entertainment groups, however, the crowd showed little excitement -- in contrast to the final rally of those opposing the constitution, who were meeting less than a kilometre away at Nyayo National Stadium. Here, thousands gathered, wearing orange T-shirts, ribbons, scarves, caps, neck ties and hats. Others came adorned with real oranges.
Both meetings were reasonably peaceful, dispelling fears of a repeat of the violence that marred earlier rallies.
Last month, four people -- including school children -- died in the western city of Kisumu following confrontations between police and crowds opposed to a campaign in support of the new constitution led by Information Minister Raphael Tuju. Kisumu is considered a stronghold of the "no" camp.
Earlier this month, another four people died in the coastal city of Mombasa during clashes between police and those attending a rally against the draft constitution. This came when police disrupted the meeting after it had already got underway, withdrawing its license. Officials were reported as saying that rallies could not proceed in Mombasa until Kibaki, also in the city at that time, had left.
These outbreaks of violence prompted expressions of concern from the Election Commission of Kenya (ECK), which has taken issue with the conduct of both "yes" and "no" campaigners.
"The national leaders of these sides expressly and publicly demonstrated their denunciation of these unwelcome activities (incidents of violence) in a meeting with the ECK. That was an oath they took. Yet, violence still persists," ECK Chairman Samuel Kivuitu said in statement on Nov. 17.
At the heart of divisions over the proposed constitution lie concerns about the power it gives Kenya's president.
Those who oppose the new constitution say it allows for an overly powerful presidency. They have called for the reinstatement of an earlier draft of the document which watered down presidential authority, and vested a share of executive power in the newly-created post of prime minister.
The earlier draft emerged from discussions held by the National Constitutional Conference, a body representing government and civil society, at 'Bomas of Kenya' -- a cultural venue located on the outskirts of Nairobi.
The position which the earlier draft of the constitution (dubbed the 'Bomas draft') took on the presidency reflected views gathered by the Constitution of Kenya Review Commission.
This body was appointed in 2000 to ascertain what provisions Kenyans wished to see in their new constitution. It found that citizens wanted a reduction of presidential powers, apparently in reaction to the abuse of executive authority under former heads of state Daniel arap Moi and Jomo Kenyatta.
However, the "yes" camp says there are adequate measures in the latest draft of the constitution to keep the executive in check. The document that will be put to the vote Monday came about after NAK parliamentarians altered the Bomas draft to make provision for an executive president and a non-executive prime minister.
Initially, parliament was obliged to accept or reject the Bomas draft in its entirety. However, NAK legislators succeeded in passing the Consensus Act last year, enabling parliament to alter the Bomas draft so as to retain presidential powers.
While the "no" camp maintains the Bomas draft more accurately represents the views of Kenyans, its support of this draft may also be linked to a reported agreement between Kibaki and Odinga. The two men apparently reached an understanding that Odinga was to be given the post of prime minister in exchange for supporting the Kibaki-led coalition -- the National Rainbow Coalition -- during the 2002 poll that swept it to power.
But, little of this dÃ©tente was visible on Saturday, when Odinga reportedly accused the president of distributing political favours in a bid to buy votes -- echoing previous claims to this effect made by Kibaki's critics.
A poll conducted last month by the Steadman Group, a research company, put support for the proposed constitution at 32 percent, with 42 percent of voters rejecting the draft.
In another poll undertaken by the British Broadcasting Corporation, "no" voters featured even more strongly (81.5 percent). About 18 percent of those interviewed said they would vote "yes".
Should the electorate give the green light to the new constitution, it will enter into force on Dec. 12 with the signature of the president. If the "no" camp triumphs, then the present constitution will remain until such time as a decision can be taken on launching another constitutional review process.

Original_Search_Title: Party Mood at Pre-Referendum Rallies
Headline: Kenya: Party Mood On the Final Wrap
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A banana is a burden this Yes supporter is determined to carry at the rally on Uhuru Park.
A carnival atmosphere dominated the two major rallies in Nairobi
yesterday as the Banana and Orange teams wound up their campaigns ahead
of the referendum.
The rallies went on peacefully despite earlier fears of violence,
marking the end of a frenetic three-month campaign as the two rival
teams sought to woo 11 million voters to either back or reject the
proposed new Constitution.
The two rallies at Uhuru Park (Banana) and Nyayo Stadium (Orange)
were attended by all the key leaders form each side. They were marked
by strong political speeches amidst music and a riot of colour put on
by supporters of the two teams.
The rest of the city was largely quiet with people staying at home
and some shops in the Central Business District shuttered against fears
of violence.
Apart from a few isolated cases of skirmishes after the twin
rallies, the day passed remarkably peacefully, with the thousands of
police officers drafted to maintain law and order having a relaxed time.
At Uhuru Park, the Banana group, which is pushing for a Yes vote,
made a final pitch by reaching out to women, teachers and marginalised
communities.
The leaders, who included vice-president Moody Awori, six Cabinet
ministers and 26 MPs told their supporters to ignore what they
described as lies by the Orange group which is urging Kenyans to vote
No to the proposed new Constitution.
And at Nyayo Stadium, the Orange group, led by Pubic Works minister
Raila Odinga, Environment minister Kalonzo Musyoka and Opposition
leader Uhuru Kenyatta, read in turns a 10-point manifesto on why
Kenyans should reject the proposed law.
A necklace of oranges round his head, a No supporter screams out his loyalties during the Nyayo stadium rally yesterday.
The rallies, both well-attended and characterised by song, dance,
colour and excitement, went on without ugly incidents. Activities at
both venues started at 10 am. The Yes rally ended at 4 pm and the No
one 20 minutes later.
The rallies caused no significant disruptions to pedestrian and vehicles traffic.
Meanwhile, State House announced President Kibaki will today address
the Nation at 4 pm. The address will be broadcast live on radio and
television.

Original_Search_Title: 18,000 Locked Out of Vote in Coast
Headline: Kenya: 18,000 Locked Out of Vote in Coast
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Electoral Commission of Kenya was yesterday putting final
touches on tomorrow's referendum with thousands of voters in Mombasa
facing disqualification for double registration.
The ECK Mombasa district coordinator, Mr David Mwakesi, said 18,000
voters will be locked out because they registered more than once.
In Central Kenya, presiding officers and polling clerks were undergoing training yesterday in preparation for the vote.
The returning officer for Isiolo North, Mr Ibrahim Adan Shabo, said
many of the polling officials in far-flung areas like Merti had left by
yesterday afternoon with the materials.
In Saku constituency, ballot papers arrived on Friday. The returning
officer, Mr John Chawe, said that enough vehicles had been provided
adequate security personnel deployed.
At least 140 presiding officers in Mathioya constituency would be
given poll materials today, the returning officer Mr John Wanjau said.
In Nakuru, police boss Titus Yoma said he had deployed 600 policemen
to patrol the town after the killing of two people there over the past
three days.
Another group of officers had been dispatched to escort ballot papers and boxes to various places.
The district elections co-ordinator, Ms Veronica King'oina,
confirmed 718 presiding officers and their deputies had been recruited
for the exercise.
Returning officers for Baringo East and Baringo Central said the
election material had been sorted out and presiding officers were
expected to collect them today.
In North Eastern Province, polling officials and campaign agents
were trained and sworn in yesterday. Government vehicles have been
mobilised and extra transport hired to transport the staff and
materials.
In Kajiado, presiding officers have been asked to conduct themselves with decorum and not provoke voters.
In Bomet, ECK coordinator Isaac Kipkenbun said all voting materials
had been received in the district and were to be dispatched immediately.
Polling material had been received for all the polling centres in Nyandarua district.
In Kilifi district, there was confusion on when the ballot papers
would arrive. The district ECK coordinator, Mr James Mwatsama, could
not confirm when the commission would deliver the ballot papers. The
district police chief Charles Okweya said that his security team was
prepared for the referendum.
The Malindi district ECK coordinator, Ms Christine Karani, confirmed receiving ballot papers for the exercise on Friday evening.
In Mombasa, the district ECK office yesterday organised a security meeting in preparations for the Monday poll.
In Kwale district, police chief Patrick Mbarire said they were
finalising security arrangements. All station commanders had converged
in his office for final briefing.
Confusion reigned in Ndhiwa constituency ahead of the referendum as
polling clerks and agents threatened to boycott the exercise over
payment.
Polling agents for both Banana and Orange camps were demanding
clarification on whether it is the ECK or their respective campaigns
secretariats to pay them.
In Mt Elgon, ECK coordinator Loise Psengel and returning officer Ben
Ngeiywa said four donkeys, seven tractors and 40 vehicles have been
hired to transport materials in the rough terrain.

Original_Search_Title: Rest of City Quiet On Day of Twin Rallies
Headline: Kenya: Rest of City Quiet On Day of Twin Rallies
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The areas surrounding the venues for both the No and Yes rallies
were peaceful for the better part of the morning as people walked to
the rallies of their choice.
There was little activity in the areas including the upper part of
the Central Business District with most parking lots remaining empty.
Traffic on roads leading to both Nyayo Stadium, where the No rally
was held, and to Uhuru Park, where Yes supporters met, was well
controlled by police officers.
As early as 7 am orange supporters had started streaming into Nyayo
stadium. The Orange rally started earlier than the Yes one at Uhuru
Park when its leaders entered the stadium to deafening cheers from the
huge crowd.
Meanwhile, there was not much activity at Uhuru Park for the better part of the morning.
Kamukunji MP Norman Nyagah was at the venue as early as 9 am,
constantly on his cell phone probably coordinating his colleagues on
the way to the venue.
The master of ceremony kept appealing to the crowd to leave the dais
to make room for the dignitaries when they arrive as groups from
various parts of the country belted songs under the scorching sun.
Unlike the Orange supporters who trekked to Nyayo Stadium, those
arriving at the Uhuru Park were mostly elderly people who were ferried
from outside the city in country and school buses.
The dais was well arranged and decorated with green and yellow
balloons. Also in plenty at Uhuru Park were boxes containing copies of
the proposed new Constitution and bananas.

Original_Search_Title: Theatrics That Capped the Banana Rally
Headline: Kenya: Theatrics That Capped the Banana Rally
Body_Text:
Nairobi — They came in buses, on bicycles, hitchhiked or trooped to Uhuru Park on foot as 'Vision Yes' swamped the city.
The crowd had started arriving as early as 9 am but had to wait
until 1.30 when key banana leaders started arriving for the rally, the
