last leg of their campaign to drum up support for the Wako draft.
Leaders would either arrive incognito- like Dr Chris Murungaru when
prayers were on- or with a set of dancers like Petkay Miriti who was
accompanied by Chuka Traditional Dancers with their cow hide drums.
Unlike other final political rallies at Uhuru Park that have always
attracted convoys from upcountry, there was little of that -- reducing
the rally to a Nairobi affair.
But some organised groups upcountry groups could be seen sharing posters, T-shirts, caps and other campaign material.
A carnival mood engulfed the grounds as the crowds sang, danced,
howled and wailed, all in the name of backing the Proposed New
Constitution.
Acrobatic shows, chants, and a music band kept the crowd busy - at times charged.
But it was unlike the last major rally at Uhuru Park when Kibaki was
sworn in billed by all standards as the largest rally Uhuru Park has
ever held.
Even the elegant Orie Rogo Manduli was there, and she captured the hearts and minds of Banana supporters with her witty talk.
They waved banana stems and leaves, and those with vehicles placed banana parts on bumpers, bonnets, and boots.
Others had a banana stem hanging from his car window.
At one time, the crowd sang and danced their way to the dais before pouncing on the ripe bananas on display.
But Kamukunji MP Norman Nyaga quickly summoned the police to restore order.
A car pulling a trailer on which sat a giant three metre-long banana
was driven round the park as the sea of humanity in yellow garments and
white caps cheered.
Jane Njeri Muchira, who came all the way from Maua, drew the attention of many.
She had a bunch of giant-sized bananas, which she said she brought to show that bananas could feed the nation.
"I'm happy to be here because I know Banana will win. One like this
can feed you the whole day," said the grinning 70-year-old woman, her
voice drowned by the frenzied drumming of Njuri Ncheke dancers who
accompanied Tigania East MP, Petkay Miriti.
At one point, Uhuru Park turned into a cacophony of sounds as dance
troupes tried to outdo each other, providing free entertainment in
various dialects.
Isukuti, Mwomboko and Kamba dance music, Meru war songs and
different choirs even made it difficult for one to make head or tail of
the music.
The venue was bedecked with yellow and green colours of ripe and raw
bananas and posters pinned all over proclaimed: "Katiba Mpya Kenya
Mpya" and "Do what is right for Kenya"
On one side of the dais, bedecked with yellow banners, were 60,000
copies of the proposed new constitution that were given to the crowd as
the rally ended. At one point, the crowd scrambled for the documents.
"Hii ni yangu, ingine ni stock (This one is mine and the rest are
stock copies)," said a young man, carrying some 20 copies of the draft.
From the onset, there was an upbeat feeling of high energy and
anticipation long before the leaders even arrived. The crowd eagerly
staked out the best positions under the few trees on the terraces for
viewing the action. But unlike other banana rallies this was not as
charged and was low key compared to their other rally at Kamukunji but
was marked by good nature but little energy.
When the speeches began, they too were full of hope and optimism
that finally Kenya would have a new constitution. Each successive
speaker called by the master of ceremony Norman Nyaga, the Kamukunji
MP, provided added reasons why the doom and gloom of the proposed
constitution was not the reality.
Perhaps it was the sun or perhaps they had heard the message before.
But they just remained attentive and the pregnant excitement did not
reach a crescendo. Not until Rogo-Manduli, who christened herself Mama
Katiba, took the microphone and for the first time the crowd roared
with laughter and exuberance.
Two former Uhuru Kenyatta key allies, Prof Karanja Njoroge and
Kimani Ngunjiri led the Kanu for Yes convoy that was received with
ululations that Ngunjiri had to be invited to greet the crowd.
But it was the time factor failed the day. There were no powerful
and moving speeches since all leaders, apart from Simeon Nyachae,
George Saitoti and Moddy Awori others were limited to two minutes.
Although some took their sweet time above the two minutes master of
ceremony had to skip several leaders as the crowd started murmuring --
picking them at random. At times he would be seen consulting with Maina
Kamanda.
It was Nairobi Mayor Dick Wathika who set the tone of the rally when
he taunted the Orange leaders: "They always say 'we are the people'
kwani sisi ni Wanyama" and later he alleged that in some Orange areas
people are being threatened with death.
Michuki in his speech paid tribute to Kibaki saying he had led the country " brains and not with a flywhisk or rungu"
Despite reports that President Kibaki would skip the rally, many
still expected him to show up. Police, with sniffer -- dogs were on
hand to secure the presidential dais.
And at the back of the dais, the red carpet rested in a pick-up, just in case Kibaki changed his mind.
Vice-President Moody Awori, who led the team of 35 MPs, arrived at
3.54pm, late and sweating, but better late than never. To the crowd's
disappointment, about 30 Banana leaders did not address them because
the Master of Ceremony, Nyagah, ensured the meeting adhered to the 5pm
deadline.

Original_Search_Title: Where Swing Votes Are Likely to Come From
Headline: Kenya: Where Swing Votes Are Likely to Come From
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A survey by a key government agency has shown that opponents of
the Wako Draft have a strong hold across the country ahead of
Referendum Day.
A survey dated November 3 and copied to Permanent Secretary Stanley
Murage shows the 'Yes' team is strong only in Central Province.
The 'Yes' camp's presence is also felt in Kwanza, Laikipia West and Laikipia East constituencies in the Rift Valley.
GG Kariuki, a Banana campaigner, represents Laikipia West in Parliament.
Assistant Minister Mwangi Kiunjuri, another Banana campaigner, represents Laikipia East.
Kwanza is represented by Assistant Minister Dr Noah Wekesa who
joined the 'Yes' team belatedly after the Middle Ground Group he
supported collapsed.
The three constituencies, plus some five others in Nakuru District,
are the only ones where 'Yes' has a fighting chance in a province the
report says the 'No' camp has support in all the districts.
The report comes in the middle of intense distrust between the
Banana and Orange camps. Orange, in a letter to the Electoral
Commission two weeks ago claimed that there is mischief in the
referendum.
The camp claimed the government is scheming to disenfranchise Kenyans.
The Sunday Standard established that the Orange team, fearing that
the government could be interfering with the voters' register, has
asked for and got a copy of it from the ECK.
Mutula Kilonzo, the head of the Orange National Referendum
Committee, said he acquired a copy of the voters' register after
"suspicious activities" which made his team fear that the government
may be planning to delete or insert names in the register in the last
days.
"When people go for relief food in Ukambani, they are asked to
surrender their voters' cards and to give their ID card numbers. We
feared somebody will insert or delete names," Kilonzo said.
"We demanded, and we got hard and soft copies of the voters' roll. We are not taking chances," he added.
Banana campaigner in Ukambani, Assistant Minister Mutua Katuku,
however dismissed the claims, describing them as "the kicks of a dying
horse".
"If there was vote buying or alterations to the voters' roll, Orange
should have reported them to the police by now. Orange has no issues
for the region, and I request Kilonzo to stop playing cheap politics,"
Katuku said.
"Ukambani is swinging towards Banana because even the MPs opposing
the Draft say it is good, but they only don't want to offend Kalonzo
Musyoka.
The only issue Kalonzo is raising is that the document wants to stop
him from being President, which is really a cheap way of looking at
things," he added.
According to the government's survey, in Central Province, the 'No'
team is strong only in Githunguri, represented in Parliament by Arthur
Magugu and Gatundu South, whose MP is Kanu chairman Uhuru Kenyatta.
The pattern seems to agree with another the Orange secretariat poll released last week.
An opinion poll on the forthcoming referendum released by Steadman
Group late last month showed the Orange camp leading with 42 per cent
against the Banana camp's 32 per cent.
The Steadman Poll, which both Orange and Banana dismissed as flawed,
showed the Draft's opponents scored highest in urban areas where it
took 48 per cent of the vote against the Banana team's 28 per cent.
According to the poll, in the rural areas, the 'No' team still takes
the lead, but with a small margin. It scores 39 per cent against the
'Yes' team's 34 per cent.
Last week, the Orange secretariat released the results of a poll it
commissioned, which showed it was leading Banana by 63 per cent to 34
per cent.
It showed Nyanza Province as a stronghold of the Orange team with
more than 80 per cent of respondents saying they would vote against the
Wako Draft.
The Orange poll showed the group had more than 76 per cent support in Coast and more than 70 per cent in Western.
Orange according to the poll released last week, enjoys 60 per cent
support in North Eastern and about 55 per cent in Eastern Province.
Eastern is the home of key Banana supporters including ministers Njeru Ndwiga, David Mwiraria and Kiraitu Murungi.
In the government's secret survey, which The Sunday Standard
obtained, Orange shows a strong presence in close to half of the 36
constituencies of Eastern Province.
Orange has a strong hold in North Horr, Laisamis, Ntonyiri, Mwingi
North and South, Kitui West and South, Masinga, Yatta, Machakos Town
and Mwala. It also has a stronghold in Mbooni, Kaiti and Makueni.
All the districts in Nyanza favour Orange, according to the survey, except two constituencies - Nyaribari Chache and Rangwe.
Energy Minister Simeon Nyachae, who also chairs the 'Yes' National Referendum Committee, represents Nyaribari Chache.
Philip Okundi of the Liberal Democratic Party represents Rangwe, a
surprise inclusion in the list of possible seats for the Banana camp.
At the Coast, the survey puts Orange ahead in close to half of the 21 constituencies.
Orange is strong in Mvita, Changamwe, Likoni, Kinango and Kaloleni.
It is also strong in Magarini, Taveta, Mwatate and Voi constituencies.
In the Rift Valley, the Banana is seen to have support in five
constituencies in Nakuru District. These are Naivasha, Nakuru Town,
Kuresoi, Molo, Rongai and Subukia.
The district has a strong presence of voters who trace their origins to Central Province, the bedrock of Banana support.
The survey gives Banana a fighting chance in Busia, Western
Province, where it says Orange has taken over the rest of the districts.
Lugari, Kakamega, Vihiga and Bungoma districts are Orange zones, according to the survey.
Of the 11 constituencies that make North Eastern Province, all are listed as pro-Orange, except three.
The eight constituencies supporting Orange are Duji, Lagdera, Fafi,
Ijara, Wajir West, Wajir East, Mandera Central and Mandera West.

Original_Search_Title: Electoral Commission 'Ready' for Referendum
Headline: Kenya: We're Ready for Voters, ECK Declares
Body_Text:
Nairobi — It is all system go for tomorrow's historic vote on the
referendum, Electoral Commission of Kenya (ECK) Chairman Samuel
Kivuitu, declared.
Kivuitu said yesterday that ECK staff would be deployed to all
polling stations today to facilitate the referendum, the first since
independence.
Addressing the final press conference before tomorrow's voting on
the Proposed New Constitution Kivuitu said the commission was ready for
the exercise.
"Voting materials have been delivered to polling stations and ECK
expects the exercise to begin on time," said Kivuitu, adding: "ECK is
appealing to registered voters to cast their ballots."
Kivuiti said it was a waste of time for one to register as a voter then decline to vote.
"It is a mark of responsibility and patriotism to vote if you are registered," he explained.
Kivuitu said the ballot paper has a simple format with only two spaces to mark.
He explained that adjacent to the two spaces, on the left, are the
pictures and names of the two distinct referendum symbols - Banana and
Orange.
"You cannot miss them," he said, adding, "You put any mark in your space of choice except for names and signatures."
He said polling stations would open at 7am and close at 5pm.
"ECK would like to count the votes when its still daylight so you return home before nightfall," added Kivuitu.
Kivuitu called on voters to produce their original national identity and voters cards.
Kivuitu added ECK is confident that the referendum results would be out by Tuesday and appealed for calm.
"Whatever the results let us remain united. Violence will take us nowhere. If anything, it would take us to hell," he added.
Kivuitu challenged Kenyans to examine the Wako Draft carefully before the referendum.
"Before voting 'Yes' or 'No' ask your self if it encapsulates your
aspirations and the chances of deficiencies being remedied," he
cautioned.
Kivuitu said losers should continue to struggle for what they
believe in a peaceful and responsible manner and appealed to the public
to ignore results announced by the media and not authenticated by ECK.
"No Media house counts votes," he said, cautioning the media against inciting the public by biased reporting.
The ECK boss said Kenyans would not be voting for individuals but deciding how the country will be governed.
"You will also be deciding how power will be shared between the
central government and district authorities and between the central
Government and Parliament," he explained, adding the draft takes into
account proposals made in the Bomas, Naivasha and Kilifi drafts.
"Do not vote 'Yes' or 'No' because your tribe will suffer or gain.
If we cared for our tribes and hate or despise other tribes, it means
we do not want Kenya," he cautioned.
He appealed to voters to assist ECK in detecting fraud during
polling and appealed to the public to report any malpractice in polling
stations to police and referendum officials.
He said observers had been posted to all polling stations.

Original_Search_Title: Minister: We Are Prepared to Counter Leaders' Threats
Headline: Kenya: Minister: We Are Prepared to Counter Leaders' Threats
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Government is prepared to deal with insecurity in Rift Valley
Province in the wake of a hate campaign targeting some communities.
Internal Security assistant minister Mirugi Kariuki said yesterday that
no effort would be spared to ensure peace. The Government was also
investigating the source of leaflets circulating in Eldoret Town after the
Kass FM radio station was closed on Tuesday.
He said some leaders were fanning ethnic animosity to scare some local
residents away from voting. He urged them to ignore the leaflets and take
part in the poll.
Some Narc activists in Baringo Central yesterday urged the local
residents to vote Yes to "usher in new leadership and spur development."
In a statement, Mr Jackson Cheburet Bartunen and Mr Evans Kangogo said
democratic space had been enlarged since the coalition took over power three
years ago. "It is time we joined the rest of Kenyans in charting the way
forward for the country," they said.
Banana supporters in Embu have taken what they call steps to ensure a
high voter turnout on Monday.
All non-essential services will be stopped, key campaigners said
yesterday.
Addressing a rally in Embu Town, they said all businesses, including
shops and hotels, would be closed. The move follows claims of voter apathy,
especially after half of the registered voters failed to cast their ballots
in the last General Election.
Councillor Nelson Rintari said his team was determined to make sure this
did not happen on Monday.
Local Government minister Musikari Kombo asked all Luhyas to vote Yes,
saying that doing so would boost their national political bargaining
power.
He rejected the creation of the office of an executive prime minister,
saying that two centres of power could cause conflict.
Constitutions all over the world are never perfect, he said, and it
would be wrong for Kenyans to expect no flaw in the proposed laws. He was
addressing a Yes campaign rally at the Budalang'i shopping centre in Busia
District on Thursday evening.
He was with Information assistant minister David Were as well as local
MP Raphael Wanjala and colleagues Bonny Khalwale (Ikolomani) and Daniel
Khamasi of Shinyalu.
Mr Were said some Orange ministers "used to being in the opposition" had
rejected the proposed constitution even before it was published.
He said he did not regret being the only Butere-Mumias MP in the Yes
camp. Mr Wycliffe Osundwa (Mumias), Mr Wycliffe Oparanya (Butere) and
Khwisero's Mr Julius Arunga are in the Orange group.
Mr Wanjala assured his constituents that the proposed constitution, if
adopted, would not lead to their displacement as claimed by some No
campaigners. Dr Khalwale said some politicians planned to "grab state power
through the back door" after failing to convince Kenyans that the proposed
constitution would not serve their interests.
Three Banana ministers reassured the Maasai that their land would be
safe if the proposed laws are passed.
Prof George Saitoti (Education), Martha Karua (Water) and Njeru Ndwiga
(Cooperatives) were addressing a rally in Trans Mara District.
Reports by Watoro Kamau, Simon Siele, Philemon Suter, Silas Nthiga,
Dennis Odumga and KNA

Original_Search_Title: Give Peace a Chance
Headline: Kenya: Give Peace a Chance
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Electoral Commission of Kenya chairman Samuel Kivuitu.
Both sides in the referendum campaigns united for the first time yesterday to send a common message to all Kenyans: Give peace a chance.
"The final stages of the campaigns, the referendum day on Monday, and the period immediately afterwards should be a time for Kenyans to maintain peace and not be divided," said President Kibaki.
But he warned that those who rejected his plea and instead turned to violence would be dealt with as severely as the law allowed.
The President was speaking while meeting a delegation from Luo-Nyanza at State House, Nairobi.
And echoing the theme, for the Orange No-vote side, Cabinet minister Raila Odinga said: "We want the rallies to be peaceful. We are calling on people to conduct themselves peacefully."
He added that Orange supporters should "arrive on time and listen attentively to the message that will be delivered, and take the message back home and prepare for the vote on Monday."
The heartfelt show of unity came as leaders from both the Banana Yes-vote and Orange No-vote camps formally signed an agreement to keep the peace during their rallies and on voting day itself, and to accept the people's verdict in the referendum, "subject to any challenges as is provided by the law." They also agreed to condemn any acts of violence, intimidation, bribery, cheating and other malpractices.
The signing of the code of conduct by the campaign committees was overseen by the chairman of the Electoral Commission of Kenya, Mr Samuel Kivuitu.
Signing for the Banana team were Narc official Alex Mureithi and Narc nominated MP Njoki Ndung'u, while signing for the Orange camp were Kanu nominated MP Mutula Kilonzo, and former Cabinet minister Isaac Ruto.
The signing was also attended by Mr Odinga of the Orange group and the chairman of Chama Chama Majimbo na Mwangaza, Mr Leslie Mwachiro, for the Banana team.
The two camps also agreed to condemn any media house which incited or created divisions among the public.
In their formal declaration the two sides agreed to "call on all Kenyans to remain united in peace and to pursue activities which will enhance and promote stability and prosperity in the country, irrespective of the results of the referendum."
And Mr Odinga later spoke out separately about the need for peace when he urged all Orange supporters attending today's rally at Nyayo Stadium to stick to the route allocated to them to avoid encountering Yes supporters.
Speaking at Pipeline grounds in Embakasi Estate, he went on: "We want all of our supporters to wake up early and fill the stadium to capacity. This will be the mother of all rallies and is very important to us and our supporters. We will make our submissions during this last meeting in which we will tell the people of Kenya why we are rejecting the Wako Draft as we rest our case and wait for their verdict on Monday.
"We want to show the Banana team that the capital city has its owners and that we are serious people."
Then he added: "I urge you all to maintain peace. We are in the Super League and we do not want any division, violence and chaos so that the Government may send the police and other armed forces to quell the violence.
"I ask you all to stick the route assigned to us by the Nairobi police boss, to avoid causing any mayhem. Let them know that Orange supporters are peaceful and do not want any deaths or destruction."
As the Orange supporters meet at Nyayo Stadium today from 10am, a parallel rally will be held by the Banana team at Uhuru Park, with the crowds separated by a half-kilometre no-go zone stretching from the Bunyala Road roundabout to the one at Haile Selassie Avenue.
It will be the second time Nairobi will be hosting two political rallies in the last three years.
The first was when the then ruling party Kanu and the Opposition parties in Narc held rallies at Nyayo Stadium and Uhuru Park shortly after formal nominations of their presidential candidates before the 2002 General Election.
Both rallies ended peacefully.
For the Banana team yesterday, Local Government minister Musikari Kombo asked supporters to go to the park in their thousands to show the world their determination to put in place a new Constitution.
The lies and propaganda had finally been unmasked and the meeting would be the climax of the Banana campaign to win the referendum, he said.
He too urged supporters to shun violence, which, he said, was the preserve of those who were not sure of the outcome.
Banana chief agent Norman Nyagah asked the Yes team to turn out in large numbers, and to be peaceful and civil.
The turnout, he added, should be reflected on Monday, the voting day, to ensure the Yes team triumphed.
Orange spokesman William Ruto also asked the No supporters to turn out in large numbers for the final rally. He also asked them to be peaceful.
The politicians spoke as Muslim and Christian leaders held prayers for the exercise to end peacefully.
At mosques in Nairobi and in major towns and also at churches nationally, imams, priests and pastors called for peace.
Close to 70 religious leaders from the Pentecostal churches in Nakuru District yesterday led their congregations in prayers and urged politicians to accept the outcome of the referendum.
During the prayer meeting at Afraha Stadium, they urged Kenyans to cast their votes as the referendum was a critical exercise which was bound to determine the future of the country.
They also urged Kenyans to maintain peace during the exercise to enable voters discharge their responsibility.
Meanwhile, the ECK boss announced they would release Sh189 million to pay some of the agents for the Banana and Orange campaign teams.
One agent for polling station from each side would receive a daily allowance of Sh1,000 from the ECK for three days starting on Monday. The camps are allowed a further two agents for each polling station but they would have to be paid by the campaign teams.
The chairman announced that the Government had finally agreed to release the Sh189 million, after some donors who had earlier pledged to chip in pulled out at the 11th hour.
At the same time, the ECK boss reminded all civil servants, who include members of the provincial administration and the police, that they were barred from being partisan during the referendum.
Mr Kivuitu told chiefs and the police to ensure they wore civilian clothes while voting to avoid being accused of intimidating or influencing voters.
He also denied earlier claims by Mr Kilonzo that some serving police officers had been recruited by the Banana team to man polling stations in Nakuru's Bondeni, Bahati and Shauri Moyo, on their behalf.
But the director of operations at police headquarters, Mr David Kimaiyo, said that only one of the four names submitted by the Kanu MP was of an officer, who had retired.

Original_Search_Title: Minister: We Have Backing Countrywide
Headline: Kenya: Minister: We Have Backing Countrywide
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange Democratic Movement will be a full-fledged political party after the Monday referendum, Environment minister Kalonzo Musyoka said yesterday.
"Our movement has overwhelming support countrywide and that is why we have decided to make it a political party after the referendum," he said.
Speaking at Ekerenyo Division, Nyamira District, Mr Musyoka said the party would field a presidential candidate in the 2007 General Election.
The minister was accompanied by former Cabinet minister Sam Ongeri and former assistant minister Joseph Kiangoi.
Mr Musyoka claimed ministers Kiraitu Murungi and Chris Murungaru had used Energy minister Simeon Nyachae as a rubber stamp to make an unsuitable Constitution.
Mr Musyoka alleged there was a plot to sack Vice-President Moody Awori and ministers opposed to the proposed Constitution.
Speaking at Marani, Kiogoro, Keumbu and Keroka shopping centres in Nyamira and Kisii districts, Mr Musyoka said the Orange ministers were not scared of being sacked.
"We shall continue fighting for a better of Kenya even if we are sacked. I don't feel happy with threats."
He said the Banana team was plotting to have Mr Awori sacked if he fails to deliver Western Province.
Prof Ongeri said the Kisii community would overwhelmingly vote against the Wako Draft.
Five armed gangsters went to the Mombasa home of National Heritage minister Najib Balala on Thursday morning but did not steal anything.
Speaking in Lamu yesterday, Mr Balala claimed the suspects went to his home at 8am soon after he flew to Lamu to address Orange rallies.
The minister said the incident was the third in one week and he was viewing it as politically motivated.
Mombasa deputy police boss Anthony Ogutu confirmed that police had received the report.
Residents of Pate Islands were yesterday urged to reject the proposed Constitution because it does not guarantee them land.
Sheikh Mohammed Idriss, the national chairman of the Council of Imams and Preachers of Kenya (CIPK), said the people of the Islands should follow the decision made by the Chief Kadhi to reject the proposed law.
Five MPs allied to Orange team took campaigns to Mt Elgon and Trans Nzoia districts yesterday where hey alleged the proposed Constitution favoured communities in power.
Immigration minister Linah Kilimo and Kanu secretary-general William Ruto urged people to turn up in large numbers and vote against the document, saying it catered for only bigger tribes.
Mr Ruto urged Kenyans to ignore a pledge that the document would be reviewed, saying the Government would have amended the 20 per cent said to be defective before the referendum.
Mrs Kilimo said affirmative action for pastoralists, women, youth and minority groups which had been provided for in the Bomas Draft had been deleted, and called on women to reject the document.
Orange supporters at the Coast have claimed the move by the Government to deploy large numbers of security officers in polling station was aimed at scaring away Orange voters.
Changamwe MP Ramadhan Kajembe said since Independence, voting had been conducted with at least two police officers per station and wondered why this time round so many were to be deployed.
Addressing an Orange rally at Mikindani Social Hall in his Changamwe constituency yesterday, Mr Kajembe urged Kenyans to come out in large numbers and vote without fear.
And Mombasa mayor Taib Ali Taib claimed Banana supporters at the Coast did not have any agenda for the region.
Orange leaders from Tindiret constituency led by Kanu officials Kiplimo Lagat, Samuel Ngetich, Joseph Simotwo, former MP Ezekiel Barngetuny and trade unionist Joshua Oyuga will be attending today's rally at the Nyayo National Stadium.
The leaders said Orange supporters from Nandi South had finalised travel plans.
In Nandi North, Orange supporters led by Cllr Michael Limo and women leader Judy Kosgei said several officials of the team from the district would attend the rally.
It was not immediately known if Banana officials from the two districts would attend the Uhuru Park rally.
An MP supporting the Orange team yesterday protested bitterly at the use of relief food and the provincial administration to campaign for the Banana.
The MP claimed 500 bags of relief maize had been transported to the area on Monday, but the provincial administration was ordered to hold them until a Yes team arrived and addressed rallies in several trading centres.
Mr Musila has been campaigning for the past week in the area against the proposed Constitution.
"The Government is using relief food and the provincial administration in Ukambani to drum up support for the Yes team. I'm condemning the actions in the strongest terms because this is an election offence," Mr Musila said on phone before joining the Orange team to fly to Kisii and Trans Mara districts for rallies.
Bishop Margaret Wanjiru has not changed her Orange stand.
Ms Wanjiru of the Jesus is Alive Ministries said she was opposed to the proposed Constitution because it has 58 weaknesses yet Parliament lacked the capacity to amend them as claimed.
Reports by Henry Nyarora, Angwenyi Gichana, Bozo Jenje, Juma Namlola, Bernard Kwalia, Tom Matoke, Odhiambo Orlale

Original_Search_Title: Section of Highway to Be No-Go Zone
Headline: Kenya: Section of Highway to Be No-Go Zone
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A section of Uhuru Highway - between Bunyala Road and Haile Selassie roundabouts - is a no-go zone today, police will maintain it as a buffer zone between Banana and Orange groups.
The City mortuary roundabout will be the deviation point for those using Ngong road to access Banana rally at Uhuru Park, the Orange one at Nyayo Stadium or the non-partisans headed for the city centre.
People heading to the Banana rally will use Valley Road only to access the venue, supporters of the Orange group will follow Mbagathi Way and those to the city centre will proceed to Haile Selassie Avenue.
All supporters of the two groups will be banned from the city centre today.
Haile Selassie Avenue will be open to the public who do not wish to attend the rallies.
Nyerere and Muthithi roads will be converted to parking lots for Banana and Orange motorists respectively.
Aerodrome Road is exclusive to those headed for Nyayo while Cathedral and Lower Hill roads should only be used by those going to Uhuru Park.
Railway Club football pitch and basketball courts, the Children's Park, open space at the main dais and also the Freedom Corner will be main parking lots for the Banana group.
The parking bay at Nyayo Stadium will strictly be for Orange motorists and also the adjacent parking lots.
Open space at the Nairobi West shopping centre will provide extra parking space for those at the stadium.
Kenyatta Avenue, from the Nyayo house to to Panafric Hotel may also be closed later in the day if need arises.
Police urged members of the public to follow instructions and directions given by police officers who will be deployed on the routes.

Original_Search_Title: Politicians Sing a New Tune But...
Headline: Kenya: Politicians Sing a New Tune But...
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Majimaji of the Gidigidi-Majimaji rap duo whose 'Unbwogable' became Narc's campaign anthem in 2002
During the Banana and Orange campaigns, politicians turned into singers and made a messy job of it. You saw them choose a song, do the lyrical appropriation and use it to praise themselves or ridicule their opponents as the crowds roared in appreciation.
Mwangi Kiunjuri picked the evergreen Umekwisha Potea and in place of the original lyrics that condemned excessive beer drinking, the assistant Minister has been warning against eating of oranges.
Kiunjuri is not alone. Makadara MP Reuben Ndolo has also used songs from the zilizopendwa series to drum up support for the NO campaign.
But the pioneer in this in "genre" is Trade Minister Mukhisa Kituyi who used the music of the late John Mwale to a devastating effect against Kanu luminaries.
Now lawyer and theatre personality Stephen Mwenesi, who is also a copyright expert, warns that the politicians using the songs without consent of the composers could be sued for breach of copyright.
According to Mwenesi, the politicians are affecting the perception of the artistes by the public which sees them as supporting a particular political course when the opposite could be the case or, when they may as well be neutral.
"Singing the songs in campaign rallies as has been the case, constitutes a public performance and requires a licence from the relevant administrator of rights of the affected song or the composer. Short of that, the composer has every right to sue the 'infringer' and seek compensation for the unauthorised use of their intellectual property," says Mwenesi.
Efforts to get comment from the Music Copyright Society were fruitless but Mwenesi, who has served as representative to the Performing Rights Society, says the unauthorised use of songs for campaigns is an indication of the abuses that artistes have endured over the years, worse still from law makers who should know better.
Even in highly developed democracies, politicians have drawn the ire of composers for using songs without authority. Fortunately the artistes have been quick to stop the violation.
Last November, the Bush presidential campaign had to apologise for unauthorised use of John Hall's Still The One after the composer issued an open letter protesting the use of the song without his consent.
In the letter, Hall explained that his concern was as a songwriter who made his living writing songs and would have reacted the same way had it been used by the Democratic Party.
Makadara MP Reuben Ndolo shakes a leg. His penchant for changing Kiswahili golden oldies into political tunes has landed him in problems
"I have political beliefs which I discuss in private with my family and friends and when I vote, I will do so privately in a booth. But the use of the song has created the impression that I support the Bush campaign," said Hall.
The Bush campaign team apologised and stopped using the song.
It was not new in the US presidential campaigns. Earlier, Bob Dole's campaign was forced to stop using Dole Man which was an appropriation of the 1960s soul classic Soul Man by the Sam & Dave duo.
In Kenya, the use of existing songs for political campaigns started in 2002 with Narc adaptation of the GidiGidi-MajiMaji's Unbwogable which became the anthem at all alliance rallies.
But the alliance followed the due process and paid to use the popular hip-hop track to enliven their meetings.
Previously in 1963, Kanu adapted the tune Kanu ya jenga nchi, said to have composed by the late Tom Mboya, as its anthem.
Over the years, music has featured in campaigns but mostly as adaptations of gospel songs. Unbwogable's impact has shown the great rewards possible from use of a popular pop song.
Two months ago, the Orange team hoped to replicate same impact through adaptation of Awilo Mike's I'm not sober for its campaign and followed the due process.
The adaptation of pop songs for political campaigns has created a new dimension in politics and is a talking point among new age researchers in political campaign strategies.
Overseas, hit songs are hot property at major political campaigns.
Last year the Centre for Political Songs at Glasgow Caledonian University did an assessment of most favoured political campaign songs. Researchers interviewed prominent personalities who gave a rating of their most favoured campaign songs.
Former US Secretary of State Collin Powell ranked the African American civil rights song We Shall Overcome as his favourite and offered the basic ingredients for an effective campaign song: "It has the simplicity and eloquence that ensures easy adaptability as a mass rendition at rallies". The former British Chancellor of Exchequer Kenneth Clark cited Paddy Ryan's 1939 composition The Man Who Waters the Working Beer for its line "For a strong and healthy working class, is the thing I most fear. So I reaches my hand for the watering can and I waters their beer".
There is also Labbi Siffre's Something Inside So Strong for the line "The higher you build your barriers, the taller I become".
But Janis Mcnair head of the centre of political songs at Glasgow favours James Brown hit I'm Black and Proud for its liner "I'd rather die than be living on my knees. Say it loud I'm black and proud".
For Americans especially, the culture of protest songs provides an easy choice of music for political campaigns.
Last year, Democratic presidential candidate John Kerry had an appropriate choice picking Bruce Springsteen's No Surrender whose title was a good rallying call even after he lost. But not so especially in Kenya where this genre of politically inspired music has dwindled and politicians have to re-word the songs or contract artistes to write songs specifically for the campaigns.
But an existing song has obvious advantages because the audience is familiar with the tune.
That more of popular songs will be used in future campaigns is not in doubt.
John Katana of Uyoga says Kenyan musicians have been so badly bartered economically that they may not have the resources and energy to pursue matters legally.
But Mwenesi thinks that composers need to start looking at copyright violations seriously as they deprive them of income. "It is a clear breach and any court should be able to offer due compensation and stop use of the song, if asked to do by the artiste".
Mwenesi adds that that copyright violation is a rights issue that touches on the business of composers and also their political rights.

Original_Search_Title: 8 Injured As Rivals Clash in Crusades
Headline: Kenya: 8 Injured As Rivals Clash in Crusades
Body_Text:
Nairobi — At least eight people were injured yesterday when Orange and Banana supporters clashed in Mt Elgon and Bungoma districts.
In Mt Elgon, five people are admitted to Kapsokwony hospital after they were allegedly attacked by Banana supporters.
The victims were attacked on Kimobo-Kapsokwony road.
The victims were heading to Kapsokwony Town to attend an electoral agents' training at Kapsokwony county council hall.
Those who were seriously injured included Mr Moses Masudi, Mr Amos Kipsang, Mr Benjamin Cheptoishak, Mr Paul Kisos and Mr Geoffrey Masobo - all from Kopsiro Division.
The victims recorded statements with the police.
Area MP John Serut is leading the Yes campaign in Mt Elgon while former MPs Wilberforce Kisiero and Joseph Kimkung are campaigning for the Orange.
In Bungoma Town, chaos erupted in the morning.
Three people suffered injuries when both camps supporters clashed outside the Kenya Commercial Bank's premises on Moi Avenue.
Five people were arrested when the two camps clashed in Webuye.
Orange team leader in the area Simon Majani sustained deep cuts on the head when he was attacked by youths he alleged to be Ford Kenya's.
He claimed that his attackers stole his Sh50,000.

Original_Search_Title: Poll Boss Warns Camps Against Intimidation
Headline: Kenya: Poll Boss Warns Camps Against Intimidation
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Electoral Commission of Kenya yesterday warned both the Banana and Orange teams against intimidating it to announce referendum results.
ECK chairman Samuel Kivuitu said they would not condone any derogatory attacks from politicians.
"No one should intimidate us to announce fake results you must wait until everything has been verified and confirmed," said Kivuitu.
He was addressing the Orange and Banana national teams at County Hall yesterday morning.
Present were the Orange team leader Mutula Kilonzo, Isaac Ruto (chief agent) and campaign leader Raila Odinga.
Nominated MP Njoki Ndungu and Narc Secretariat leader Alex Mureithi represented the Banana team.
Kivuitu said ECK would quit and leave the exercise to any person who will try to interfere with the announcement of the results.
Both teams signed a resolution binding them to conduct the election peacefully and in accordance with the law.
"We will go home and leave you to carry on with the exercise if you try to intimidate us," warned Kivuitu.
The Orange team yesterday claimed the Banana camp was buying their agents so that they can rig the referendum.
Mutula Kilonzo, who is the national referendum committee chairman, asked ECK to investigates the matter.
The Commission said it will only pay one agent per polling station for both the Banana and Orange teams.
The announcement dealt a major financial blow to both camps.
They will now have to seek for funds to pay an extra agent because both groups insist they require two agents per polling station.
"We only have Sh189 million and that is enough to pay the 19,124 polling agents," said Kivuitu.
Kilonzo said they would require more time, probably until tomorrow to communicate to ECK their position on the extra agent.
He said it was important that each team gets two agents per polling so that they can alternate when one takes a rest.
The Banana team opposed a demand by the Orange team that ECK keeps police officers out of polling stations.
They said the officers would protect voters from getting intimidated by rowdy youths in some areas.
"We need police officers to protect our voters from violent people in areas that are allegedly zoned of off for the orange team," said Njoki.
Kivuitu said police should be stationed at a strategic corner within the precincts of the station and be called by the returning officer when there is trouble.
He also warned chiefs against appearing at polling units in their uniforms.
He said no voter would be intimidated during the exercise at any polling station in the country.
Kivuitu said the Communication Commission of Kenya should have alerted ECK before shutting down a vernacular radio station.
"We have also received numerous complains about one presenter on Radio Citizen and deployed someone to monitor that broadcast," said Kivuitu.
He also announced that all the voting materials have been transported to the districts in readiness for the exercise.

Original_Search_Title: VP Will Be Sacked With Us, Claims Kalonzo
Headline: Kenya: VP Will Be Sacked With Us, Claims Kalonzo
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Environment minister Kalonzo Musyoka yesterday alleged the Banana team planned to have Vice-President Moody Awori sacked along with Orange leaders in the Cabinet.
Musyoka said the Cabinet changes were imminent regardless of the out come of the referendum.
Kalonzo said the ministers targeted in the Orange camp were himself, Raila Odinga, Najib Balala, Ochilo Ayacko, Prof Anyang' Nyong'o, Jebii Kilimo, William ole Ntimama and a host of assistant ministers allied to the Liberal Democratic Party.
"We in the Orange are privy of the impending changes in the Cabinet targeting us and the VP but that will not change our stand," he said.
Awori, who is also a member of LDP, supports the Wako Draft.
But Kalonzo said healing the wounds among ministers was necessary after the referendum was concluded.
"Soon after Orange are declared winners, we will ask the President to summon a Cabinet meeting to reconcile the warring factions for the interest of Kenya," he said.
The minister said if Orange won, President Kibaki would complete his term in 2007 as per the current Constitution "before he retires with the old guard".
"I want to dispel fears that we in the Orange team will overthrow Kibaki's Government if we carry the day. We respect the law which allows him to remain in office till 2007," he said.
Kalonzo was speaking during a campaign at Marani, Kiogoro and Keroka trading centres in Simeon Nyachae's home turf as he drummed up support for the No vote in rejection of the Wako draft.
He was accompanied by former Cabinet minister Prof Sam Ongeri and other Kisii leaders.
Kalonzo said if the Orange won, they would turn to the Bomas draft and subject it to public scrutiny and amendment before it is passed as a constitution.
Kalonzo said Orange team will not impose the Bomas draft on Kenyans but will give them a chance to improve it.
He also dismissed calls that ministers who were unhappy with the Government should resign.
"We cannot resign, for we have a bigger stake in this Government than those calling for our sacking. People like Nyachae found me there," he said.
Kalonzo called for peaceful elections on Monday and urged Orange supporters to keep vigil at polling centres to guard against rigging.
He described Nyachae as a forthright leader. "I promise to back and console him after Orange wins this referendum. Our aim is to stop their pride and ego," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Orange Leaders Go to Defector's Turf
Headline: Kenya: Orange Leaders Go to Defector's Turf
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange team took their campaign to former colleague David Mwenje's Embakasi constituency in Nairobi in a bid to counter the impact of his defection to Banana.
Led by Roads and Public Works minister Raila Odinga and Planning minister Prof Anyang Nyong'o, the team alleged that the Yes camp would this weekend spend millions of shillings in a last bid to convince Kenyans to vote Yes.
Makadara MP Reuben Ndolo, Information assistant minister and Westlands MP Fred Gumo and Kasarani MP William Omondi, Mwenje's perennial rival Irshad Sumra and former MPs Gerishon Kirima also addressed the rally.
"We are aware that Banana MPs have been given a lot of money from State House in a desperate bid to buy votes," said Raila.
He asked the voters to take the money and demand more and then vote No on Monday.
Raila claimed there were plans by the Banana camp to unleash violence countrywide to give the Government reason to deploy military personnel during Monday's vote.
This he said was aimed at intimidating voters in Orange strongholds and ensure they do not turn up to vote.
He called for peace and order during today's Orange rally at Nyayo Stadium and asked Orange supporters to access the venue through the designated routes.
"Let us wake up early, walk peacefully to Nyayo stadium and ensure we fill up the place by 9 am," he said.
Raila accused the Banana camp of whipping up tribal politics to antagonize the Kikuyu against Luos by claiming the vote was a showdown between him and President Kibaki.
He said the Orange line up clearly demonstrates it was not a tribal outfit but Kenyans out to ensure their country gets the constitution they asked for.

Original_Search_Title: Uhuru Wants Observers to Be Vigilant
Headline: Kenya: Uhuru Wants Observers to Be Vigilant
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kanu factional chairman Uhuru Kenyatta has asked observers to be vigilant to ensure there is no rigging in the Monday referendum.
"As the ECK chairman (Samuel) Kivuitu has already indicated, we fear rigging and would like to appeal to observers to assist in countering the cheating during the polling," he said.
He was speaking in Gatundu, in Thika District, during the final rally in his constituency to lobby for the Orange vote.
Kivuitu was reported to have said that the Electoral Commission of Kenya anticipates cheating during the Monday vote on the referendum as names of dead and double registered persons may be used to vote.
The ECK chairman said cheating was possible in areas dominated by either Banana or Orange sides.
He asked the observers to be vigilant and assist the commission by immediately reporting any malpractice.
Yesterday, Uhuru also said the referendum vote was not a fight between President Kibaki and Roads minister Raila Odinga.
He said other areas where President Kibaki had visited had benefited from the goodies the Government had dished out to influence a "Yes" vote while nothing come to Central.
"The President dished out Amboseli National Park to the Maasai, increased councillors' allowances, in Coast he gave squatters land and went to Western to give new districts. Central never got any thing," said Uhuru.
"The referendum is not an issue of fighting President Kibaki. We are looking for a constitution for posterity," said Uhuru.
He asked members of the Kikuyu community to vote for Orange because the Wako Draft was defective.
Uhuru said members of the community should also reject tribalism and join other Kenyans in voting for Orange.
At the same time Uhuru said the outcome of the Monday referendum would be a wake-up call to President Kibaki's leadership.
He also said today's Orange rally would be the grand finale in their month-long campaign against the Wako Draft.
He hit out at the Banana team for threatening to fire Cabinet ministers in the Orange camp.
"They (Banana) are definitely not democrats, the constitution is for the people of Kenya and not a Government project," he said.
Uhuru was speaking after addressing an Orange rally in Gatundu.
Raila was also reported to have indicated that today's meeting would be the mother of all rallies where they would submit their final case against the Wako Draft.
Yesterday, Uhuru said they would go to the meeting with confidence that majority of Kenyans supported the Orange camp.

Original_Search_Title: President to Skip Final Showdown in City
Headline: Kenya: President to Skip Final Showdown in City
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki will skip the Banana team's campaign rally in Nairobi today, entrusting his deputy and ministers with the task. The Orange group has meanwhile lines up all its luminaries for what will be the final showdown between the two camps before the referendum on Monday.
The parallel rallies will provide the final platform for the two camps to persuade voters to adopt or reject the proposed constitution.
The President has not attended a single rally since the campaigns began.
However, he has hosted several delegations at State House and State Lodges, and appealed to them to support the Proposed New Constitution.
Yesterday, he had been widely expected to give the Banana team a boost at its rally at Uhuru Park, but sources in the camp said Vice-President Moody Awori would lead the troops, backed by Cabinet ministers Simeon Nyachae, John Michuki, Kiraitu Murungi, among others.
Hundreds of Banana supporters started arriving at Uhuru Park yesterday afternoon in preparation for the rally.
In the Orange camp, Leader of Official Opposition Uhuru Kenyatta, Cabinet ministers Raila Odinga, Kalonzo Musyoka, Najib Balala and Anyang' Nyong'o, and Kanu Secretary-General William Ruto are among those lined up to address the rally at the Nyayo National Stadium.
The Police Commissioner, Major General Hussein Ali, announced yesterday said plans for security for the two rallies were in place.
He warned that stern action would be taken against anyone who attempted to disrupt the meetings.
A source in the Orange team said a "major" announcement would be made at today's rally that may give a clearer picture of what to expect come the 2007 general elections.
On its part, the Banana camp said it would concentrate on constitution matters.
For the second time in les s than three years, Nairobi will be hosting two parallel and potentially explosive political rallies. The meetings will be held within less than two kilometres of each other.
They are reminiscent of the of the Narc and Kanu rallies which were held before the 2002 General elections after Presidential contenders Mwai Kibaki and Uhuru Kenyatta presented nomination papers to the Electoral Commission of Kenya.
Kibaki, the Narc candidate, held his meeting at Nyayo Stadium while Uhuru held his at Uhuru Park.
For today's rallies, police have set up a buffer zone to ensure order. Supporters of each group will use designated roads while the city centre will be a no-go zone for any of the groups.
Lang'ata Road will be exclusively used by Orange supporters and part of Kenyatta Avenue, stretching from Nyayo House, will be for the Banana team.
There will be a buffer zone between the two rallies - on Uhuru Highway between Bunyala Road and Haile Sellasie Avenue.
And as the clock ticked towards Monday, the Orange team told of plans for a major post-referendum purge from Government, with Environment minister Kalonzo Musyoka saying even Awori was being targeted.
He said those to be sacked after the referendum were himself, Raila, Balala, Sports minister Ochilo Ayacko, Nyong'o, Immigration minister Jebii Kilimo and Office of the President minister William ole Ntimama.

Original_Search_Title: Kenya Now to Have 107 Districts
Headline: Kenya Now to Have 107 Districts
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kenya will have 107 districts after President Kibaki announced plans to
create 33 new administrative areas countrywide.
The President also gave legal status to 25 districts created during
President Moi's era.
Nairobi, which was both a province and a district, has three new
administrative centres - Nairobi East, Nairobi West and Nairobi North.
Coast Province has three additional districts, raising the number to
10. The Government intends to have Kilindini, Kinango and Kaloleni as new
districts.
The President gazetted the new areas under the Districts and Provinces
Act of 1992 which gives him power to gazette plans for creating new
districts and which would later be debated by Parliament to become
valid.
In creating the new districts and rationalising boundaries, some
traditional names will disappear, such as Kakamega, Kisumu and Kiambu, among
others.
The districts are part of the pledges that President Kibaki has been
making to Kenyans as he campaigns for the proposed Constitution.
However, the President has dismissed allegations that the districts are
meant to woo the public to vote Yes at the referendum.
According to a legal notice published in the Kenya Gazette on Wednesday,
the expansive Rift Valley Province got eight more districts and Eastern got
seven. Central, Western and Nyanza provinces got four districts each.
Nairobi was given two, Coast Province three and North Eastern one
additional district.
In Rift Valley, Turkana, Trans Nzoia, Uasin Gishu and Laikipia districts
were each divided into two.
There will be Turkana North and Turkana South; Trans Nzoia East and
Trans Nzoia West; Uasin Gishu North and Uasin Gishu South; and Laikipia West
and Laikipia East districts.
East Pokot will be hived off from West Pokot to become a new
district.
From Nakuru, the Government will create three new districts - Naivasha,
Subukia and Molo.
In Eastern, Meru North District has been divided into two entities with
new names - Imenti North and Imenti South. Two other districts - Igembe and
Tigania - will be created in Meru.
In Ukambani, Kitui District has been split to create Kitui North and
Kitui South while Kangundo and Yatta districts will be hived off the larger
Machakos District.
In Central Province, Kiambu, Nyandarua, Thika and Nyeri districts will
be split into two each - Kiambu East and Kiambu West; Nyandarua North and
Nyandarua South; Nyeri North and Nyeri South. Gatundu will be a district on
its own, created from Thika.
In Western Province, Kakamega and Vihiga districts were divided into two
each while two new districts were created from Bungoma.
The province will now have Kakamega North and Kakamega South;
Vihiga/Emuhaya and Hamisi/Sabatia; Webuye and Sirisia as new districts.
In Nyanza, Kisumu District has been split into Kisumu North and Kisumu
South while Rongo District was hived off Migori District. In Gusii, South
Kisii District has been created out of Kisii Central and Gucha and another
called Masaba.
Details of the new districts emerged as President Kibaki warned that the
Government will deal firmly with perpetrators of violence on the referendum
polling day as teams of Orange and Banana campaigners criss-crossed the
country in a bid to get the swing vote before official campaigns end
tomorrow.
Addressing a delegation from the North Rift at Eldoret State Lodge, the
President declared: "They should not think they are above the law, we shall
deal firmly with them."
The 21 days given by the Electoral Commission for both sides to canvass
for votes ends tomorrow to pave way for the big vote on Monday in which a
possible 11.5 million registered voters will cast ballots in favour or
against the proposed Constitution.
On Monday, voters are expected to turn out in some 20,000 polling
stations spread out in 210 constituencies around the country between 7 am
and 5 pm to vote for the Banana-Yes or Orange-No.
They will vote only at the centres where they were registered and need
to carry their voter's card, an Identity Card or a valid Kenyan
Passport. The law does not recognise driving licences or employers identity
cards.
After the closure of the polling stations, vote counting will take place
on the spot, then results will be taken by agents and ECK officials to the
divisional centres to the Returning Officer.
There, they will be tallied to get the winner and then relayed to the
ECK headquarters in Nairobi where chairman Samuel Kivuitu and his officials
will be compiling a national tally to establish the winner.
And today, the Catholic Church begins three days of prayers for the
country ahead of the poll.
In a paid up advertisement elsewhere, the Church states: "Whatever the
outcome, let us accept results."
Yesterday, Yes campaigners led by Cabinet ministers Joseph Munyao,
Charity Ngilu and assistant Minister Mutua Katuku made whistle-stop tours in
Kitui, Machakos and Mwingi districts.
Roads minister Raila Odinga led the Orange team to campaign in Maralal
while other teams campaigned in Western Province, Coast Embu and Meru.

Original_Search_Title: Stop Abusing Each Other, Urge Awori And Kombo
Headline: Kenya: Stop Abusing Each Other, Urge Awori And Kombo
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Political leaders were yesterday asked to stop trading accusations and
associating the referendum with personalities.
Vice-President Moody Awori and Ford Kenya chairman Musikari Kombo said
the proposed Constitution was above any individual and must not be reduced
to a supremacy contest.
"The Constitution is not about President Kibaki, a legislator or any
other leader," he said. "The trend of linking the referendum with
individuals must stop. Focus must turn to issues of substance.
Addressing a Yes rally in Matungu constituency, Mr Awori appealed for
sobriety during the vote and said deceit and force had led to undue
animosity. The public should shun "political hecklers", he said.
Mr Kombo asked Kenyans to exercise their democratic right and cast their
ballots on November 21 to shape their future.
People to show the way
"It will be the people to show the way by expressing their preference
during this very important task," said the Local Government minister.
Mr Kombo also urged the Luhya community to emulate their Luo
counterparts who exercised unity on various political issues. "Our
neighbours have the upper hand over us," he said.
"This is because they have an unrivalled unity and face issues as a
bloc, unlike in our case, where individuals insist on individual dreams thus
evoking undue supremacy contests.
The leaders later led separate campaign rallies with the VP moving to
his backyard in Funyula while Mr Kombo pitched camp in Ikolomani
constituency.
They asked the public to turn out in large numbers for the
referendum.
- Monday's poll will be free and fair, three Cabinet ministers said
yesterday.
George Saitoti (Education), Martha Karua (Water) and Njeru Ndwiga
(Cooperatives) said the Government had an effective machinery to guard
against violence and rigging.
"We want the poll conducted in transparency, as is the wish of all
Kenyans," Prof Saitoti said.
They were speaking at Kilgoris Town during a Banana campaign.
Assistant Health minister Gideon Konchellah claimed the Orange group had
printed extra ballot papers to be used in rigging the Monday vote and
planned to collude with some returning officers but that their attempts
would be thwarted.
- About 5,000 squatters at five settlement schemes in Kibwezi
constituency have been given land.
They received the allotment letters yesterday from Lands minister Amos
Kimunya during a Yes campaign rally.
Modern scheme
He asked them not to sell the land because the Government intended to
turn the area into a modern scheme.
Mr Kimunya told Kenyans not to worry that roads were not being built
because "this will be resolved after November 21".
He told the Orange ministers the Government would be reorganised after
Monday, and that "some ministries are vacant and need to be filled."

Original_Search_Title: Aircraft to Monitor Poll, Says ECK Boss
Headline: Kenya: Aircraft to Monitor Poll, Says ECK Boss
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Air Force and police helicopters will be used to monitor the
referendum.
Electoral Commission of Kenya chairman Samuel Kivuitu said yesterday his
team had set up an elaborate arrangement to ensure that even their own
officials who may be tempted to cheat on behalf of the Yes or the No team
did not succeed.
Mr Kivuitu said the Government had given the ECK an undertaking to
provide several police and Air Force helicopters for the historic
exercise.
About 11,608,889 voters are expected to cast their votes and decide the
fate of the proposed Constitution by voting either Yes or No.
Airlift papers
The helicopters will also be used to airlift counted ballot papers and
signed results announced by presiding and returning officers from far-flung
districts.
Mr Kivuitu identified the areas to benefit from the helicopters as North
Eastern Province, which has 10 constituencies, and parts of North and South
Rift Valley which are inaccessible by road, including Samburu, Turkana, West
Pokot and Narok districts.
He regretted that the Orange and the Banana camps' secretariats had not
provided his commission with the full list of their agents who will man the
polling stations around the country.
The Yes has no agents in 20 constituencies while the Orange lacked in
five.

Original_Search_Title: No-Go Zones Set Up for Rival Rallies in Nairobi
Headline: Kenya: No-Go Zones Set Up for Rival Rallies in Nairobi
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Hundreds of armed police officers will tomorrow impose a buffer zone
between Uhuru Park and Nyayo Stadium in Nairobi, as the capital sees rival
Banana and Orange group campaign rallies.
And on Monday, the day of the referendum, 4,000 police will be deployed
in Nairobi alone, backed by 1,000 members of the National Youth Service and
prison warders.
Throughout the country, all police, prisons and youth service leave has
been cancelled to ensure maximum security for the poll.
Tomorrow's half-kilometre safety corridor will be between the Bunyala
Road and Haile Selassie roundabouts to prevent clashes between supporters of
the rival camps.
And all supporters of both sides will be banned from the city
centre.
Security will be tightened to ensure they do not meet as they arrive or
leave the rally venues.
The Banana Yes-vote team will be holding its rally at Uhuru Park from
10am to 4pm while the Orange No-vote group will meet from 10am-5pm at Nyayo
Stadium.
Timings for the ends of the rallies were staggered so supporters of both
sides could be cleared from the area without meeting.
After the rallies, all campaign activities will be banned for the rest
of the day and police warned that anyone "flashing partisan symbols or
engaging in acts that would provoke their rivals" would be arrested. Police
last night released elaborate security arrangements and issued motorists
with alternative routes to follow to avoid being caught up in the
crowds.
"The area between Bunyala Road and Haile Selassie Avenue roundabouts
will be the buffer zone, a no man's land secured by police officers,"
declared Nairobi provincial police chief King'ori Mwangi.
Supporters of each group will use certain designated roads while the
city centre will be a no-go zone for any of the supporters groups, said Mr
Mwangi.
The restrictions were imposed after consultations and endorsement by
secretariats of both groups through their representatives, he said.
Representatives of the Yes and No camps attended Mr Mwangi's press
briefing at Nairobi provincial police headquarters.
Lt Gen Humphrey Njoroge led officials from the Yes side while Makadara
MP Reuben Ndolo led the No camp.
The last time the two venues hosted parallel rallies was on November 18,
2002, after Presidential contenders Mwai Kibaki and Uhuru Kenyatta presented
nomination papers to the Electoral Commission of Kenya.
Mr Kibaki, the Narc candidate held his meeting at Nyayo Stadium, while
Mr Kenyatta of Kanu held his at Uhuru Park.
On tomorrow's rallies, Mr Mwangi said security officers will work
closely with campaigners to ensure only Banana supporters would be allowed
to Uhuru Park and their rivals to Nyayo Stadium.
Major roads will be closed to traffic and converted to parking
lots. Nyerere Road for the Banana supporters and Muthithi Road for those
going to the Orange rally.
However, main parking for the Banana group will be at the Railway Club
football pitch and basketball courts, the children's park, open ground at
the main dais and also at Freedom Corner.
The parking bay at Nyayo Stadium will be strictly for the Orange
motorists while adjacent parking lots will also be used by those attending
the Nyayo Stadium rally.
Open space at Nairobi West shopping centre will provide extra parking
space in case the stadium area is not large enough.
Aerodrome Road will be exclusively for those heading to the stadium
while Cathedral and Lower Hill roads will be for people heading to Uhuru
Park from 9am.
A section of Ngong Road - from City Mortuary to the Haile Selassie
roundabout, will be closed to anybody or vehicles heading for the two
venues.
From the mortuary roundabout, police will direct Orange supporters via
Mbagathi Way, while Banana supporters will head down Valley Road. Banners
will alert motorists to the routes.
Haile Selassie Avenue will be open to members of the public who do not
wish to go to the rallies.
However, Kenyatta Avenue from Nyayo House to the Panafric Hotel would be
closed later in the day if need be, said Mr Mwangi.
Mr Mwangi added there will be a heavy police presence around the city to
ensure that ugly scenes do not occur in the estates. "Police will ensure
that people will reach their destinations safely after the meetings," he
said.
He continued: "Four thousand police officers and 1,000 from the National
Youth Service and prison warders will be deployed in Nairobi on Monday."
After hosting the Banana campaign rally, Uhuru Park will on Monday,
polling day, become the operating base for the police.
Central commands will be arranged and issued from there and even police
helicopters that will maintain surveillance over the city will depart and
land from the park.

Original_Search_Title: Case Against Campaigners Now Dropped
Headline: Kenya: Case Against Campaigners Now Dropped
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A Nairobi court yesterday dropped a criminal case against two people
from the Orange and Banana camps.
Trial magistrate Rosemell Mutoka accepted attorney-general Amos Wako's
intentions to discontinue the case against Hamisi MP George Khaniri and the
director of the Yes campaign secretariat Titus Mbathi.
She discharged the two, who had been sued by the Kenya National
Commission on Human Rights with offences related to violence in the
referendum campaign.
The commission filed a private prosecution against them, saying the AG
had failed to investigate the two and take action against them despite its
complaints.
Following this, chief magistrate Aggrey Muchelule issued summons against
the two to appear before him and plead to the charges on November 9.
However, this did not happen because when they appeared for the case on
November 9, the AG made an application to take over and discontinue the
case.
Public prosecutions director Keriako Tobiko informed the court that the
AG had directed police commissioner Mohammed Ali to investigate the two,
together with other politicians with a view of charging them if found to
have committed an offence.
He argued then, that if allowed to proceed, the private prosecution case
would prejudice the investigations.
Mr Tobiko said no one would be spared from prosecution if found to have
committed an offence.
He had also said the AG was at liberty to prefer the same charges
against the two, depending on the outcome of the investigations.
But this, the commission strongly opposed, saying there were no
proceedings before the court for the AG to purport to take over and
terminate.
The commission had argued that proceedings only start when an accused
has pleaded to the charges.
Through lawyer Neville Amollo, the commission sought for an order to
temporarily halt the proceedings for the purposes of referring the case to
the High Court for constitutional interpretation. The commission wanted to
challenge the constitutionality of the AG's action to terminate the
case.
Mrs Mutoka said once a complaint was filed, criminal proceedings were
deemed to have started and the AG could terminate it.
The magistrate further said that she was not even empowered to ask the
AG why he had decided to take over the case and terminate it.
She ruled that even though the commission had the right to reply in the
case, she had to accept the nolle prosequi presented by the AG.
The court declined to grant orders to have the matter stop temporarily
pending reference to the High Court to challenge Mr Wako's decisions.

Original_Search_Title: Leaders Condemn State for Gagging FM Station
Headline: Kenya: Leaders Condemn State for Gagging FM Station
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Government's decision to close a vernacular radio station was
yesterday met with a chorus of condemnation.
Rift Valley Orange leaders said the action against the Nairobi-based
Kass FM, which broadcasts in the Kalenjin language, was unwarranted as there
was no evidence of wrongdoing by the station.
The only crime it committed, they said, was to campaign for the Orange
side in the referendum.
Speaking at a rally in Mount Elgon, Immigration minister Linah Kilimo
said she was surprised that the Government could take such a decision.
"Why did they have to single out Kass FM while we know of certain
stations which have come out strongly to campaign for Banana?" she
asked.
Kanu secretary-general William Ruto accused three ministers from the
Rift Valley of being behind the closure.
"If you have failed to convince your people to follow you, do you have
to gag them?" he asked.
On Wednesday, the Communication Commission of Kenya (CCK) closed the
station for one week.
Speaking by phone, MP Jimmy Choge of Aldai said there was no
justification for the Government to close the station because it had not
incited anybody.
In Bomet, mayor Patrick Ngerechi and Mr Stephen Sang of the Kenya
National Chamber of Commerce and Industry condemned the gag order.
They said the station had been informing Kenyans about the referendum
but had never advocated violence. Narc nominated MP Franklin Bett said the
closure flew in the face of freedom of expression and of the Press.
In a telephone interview, Mr Bett said that if the Government were fair,
it would have closed all the vernacular radio stations. The Supreme Council
of Kenya Muslim's deputy national chairman, Mr Abdullahi Kiptonui, Kericho
mayor Paul Chirchir, Kipsigis County Council chairman Richard Samoei and
Londiani Town Council chairman Jackson Kikwai asked the Government to reopen
the station immediately.
In Nandi, Orange team leaders also demanded that the station be
reopened, assuring non-Kalenjins that they would not be evicted because they
have a constitutional right to vote for or against the proposed
Constitution.
In Nairobi, the National Convention Executive Council said it was
disturbing for a Government that purports to guarantee press freedom to
close a station. In a press statement, the council said the closure was
uncalled for and unfortunate and amounted to an attack on the media.
The pressure group also accused the Government of discrimination by
silencing voices of dissent in the referendum debate. National Development
minister Peter Anyang' Nyong'o said he had written to President Kibaki
urging him to order the reopening of the station.
The Release Political Prisoners lobby group also condemned the action,
terming it retrogressive. It demanded to know the content of the
transmissions that led to the closure.
Kass FM was expected to surrender to CCK by yesterday all the tapes
containing material broadcasts in the past 90 days. But Government spokesman
Alfred Mutua said the station's officials had told CCK that they had not
recorded all their broadcasts for that period.
According to CCK regulations, radio stations are supposed to record and
save all their broadcasts for 90 days.
Dr Mutua said the station was closed after investigations had revealed
that it was inciting people to violence.

Original_Search_Title: Now Nation All Set for Historic Vote
Headline: Kenya: Now Nation All Set for Historic Vote
Body_Text:
The City of Nairobi will on Saturday host two final rallies by the
supporters and opponents of the Proposed New Constitution ahead of the
national referendum on Monday, November 21.
President Mwai Kibaki will lead the last 'Yes', or Banana, rally at
Uhuru Park between 10 00 am and 4 00 pm local time.
Six ministers of the 'No', or Orange, side will address their supporters
at Nyayo National Stadium from 10 00 am to 5 00 pm.
Police issued elaborate guidelines on the movement of vehicles in the
city, and declared a buffer zone between the two venues that are less than
two kilometres apart.
The government has declared Monday and Tuesday public holidays. The
result of the poll is expected on Tuesday, November 22.
Some 11 608 889 are expected to cast their ballots to decide the fate of
the Draft Law, 15 years since the start of the clamour for a new
constitution.
Over 5 000 security officers will be on patrol in the city on Monday.
Security will also be beefed up across the country.
At least nine people died in referendum-related violence in various
parts of the country. Several others were injured.
The Catholic bishops called for three days of national prayers for the
referendum from November 18 to November 20. They asked Kenyans to accept the
outcome of the poll.

Original_Search_Title: Controversy Mars Countdown to Constitutional Referendum
Headline: Kenya: Controversy Mars Countdown to Constitutional Referendum
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Controversy, invective and violence have marred the run-up to the
first-ever referendum in Kenya, which will either reject or ratify a
proposed new constitution on Monday.
More than half of the 11 million registered voters are expected to turn
out at polling stations across the country.
The referendum debate has bitterly divided this East African
nation. Since the start of the campaign nearly three months ago, political
rallies across the country have been the scene of violent clashes between
rival camps and police.
Thus far, eight people have died and scores were injured in rallies in
Mombasa, Kisumu, Kakamega and Garissa.
The two sides of the referendum question have traded blows, insults,
lies and propaganda, prompting church leaders to hold prayer services for a
peaceful outcome. Some Nairobi-based diplomats also fear that the country
might plunge into chaos after the vote.
In the last few days, however, there has been a tentative calm, with
tough police measures in place.
A divided nation
The fight over the proposed new constitution has polarised the two
blocs, identified by the banana symbol for "Yes" and the orange for
"No".
President Mwai Kibaki supports the "Yes" camp, while his roads minister,
Raila Odinga, and five other cabinet ministers are leading the "No" campaign
in what looks like a cabinet revolt.
The referendum also seems to pitch two major ethnic communities against
each other, with the Kikuyu largely behind the "Yes" vote and the Luo
supporting the "No" vote. None of the other ethnic groups are allied as
solidly to a specific side, and many people now see the referendum as a
popularity contest between Kibaki and Odinga.
The proposed constitution, popularly known as the Wako Draft after the
country's attorney general, is intended to replace the independence
constitution, which was negotiated in London in 1963, when Kenya gained
independence from Great Britain. The document was conceived by Kenya
nationalists but without the participation of the Kenyan people.
Both camps agree that the existing constitution is outdated and
oppressive but have failed to reach consensus on the new one, which has been
the subject of debate since 1997.
The Wako Draft is the final result of a complicated "people-driven"
process to rewrite the constitution, which began with the enactment of the
Constitution of Kenya Review Act of 1997. One of the objectives of the
review was to reduce the excessive powers vested in the presidency. Another
was to decentralise the government and bring it closer to the people.
These objectives and other rights-based issues were incorporated in the
so-called Bomas Draft. The draft, however, was by no means a consensus: Some
of the delegates to the Bomas assembly walked out in disagreement before the
final document was put to vote.
Later subsequent initiatives, aimed at consensus and reconciliation,
produced the Naivasha Draft and finally the Wako Draft, the subject of the
referendum on Monday. The Bomas Draft has been consigned to history, though
it remains the reference document for those opposed to the Wako Draft.
Members of the "No" camp argue that the document does not reflect the
wishes of the people of Kenya as manifested in the Bomas Draft. In
particular, they say, the draft has glorified the powers of the president,
making him a "king".
They are also unhappy with the devolution of powers, the conditions and
voter thresholds for electing a president, the powers of the prime minister
and the appointment of the cabinet.
Odinga stated that the Wako Draft seeks to reintroduce in Kenya a
presidential powerhouse of the kind that prevails primarily in the US where,
he argued, it works well only because of its long history, strong system of
checks and balances, and extraordinary level of devolution.
"If introduced in Kenya, such a system would be a recipe for
dictatorship," he said.
Kipkalia Kones, a nominated member of parliament and supporter of the
proposed constitution, disagreed, calling Odinga's statement "one of the
biggest lies that politicians opposed to the proposed new constitution have
peddled since the referendum campaigns began."
Kones maintained that the new constitution does not increase the power
of the president: "The draft expressly states that all power and authority
ultimately belongs to the people. Therefore presidential power can only be
exercised for the well-being and benefit of the people."
The Yes camp point out that the presidential powers to prorogue and
dissolve parliament have been taken away and that parliament will control
its calendar. The authority to allocate land has also been handed over to an
independent National Lands Commission. Presidential appointments under the
new constitution will be subject to parliamentary approval.
"The powerful president Kenyans are being cheated about does not exist
