in the proposed new constitution. Nor does the king or monarch," said
Kones.
The "Yes" camp argued that the Wako Draft was agreed upon by parliament
in subsequent initiatives and largely reflects the Bomas Draft and the will
of the people.
The "No" side, however, strongly disagreed.
The "No" campaign argued, for example, that the document merely
delegates but does not devolve power.
"In the Wako Draft, devolution has been thrown to the dogs while
delegation has been canonised as the preference of the Kenyan people. This
deception must be exposed and rejected with a big No at the referendum,"
said Anyang' Nyong'o, minister for planning and national development.
Musikari Kombo, the minister for local government, countered that the
new constitution will ensure equality of all communities in Kenya.
"The proposed new constitution recognises the right of local communities
to manage their own affairs. Under the new constitution, they will make
their own decisions regarding their resources. This is what self-government
is about," he said.
Attorney General Amos Wako has defended the proposed constitution,
saying it captures the views of wananchi (citizens) as manifested in the
Bomas draft. "My role was to use Bomas draft as a benchmark and incorporate
some amendments passed by parliament and that is what I did," he said on
Wednesday.
In the run-up to the referendum, the Yellow Movement, a civil-society
group allied to the "No" camp, challenged the proposed constitution in
court. They sought a declaration on whether the constitution-making process,
including the 21 November referendum, was valid.
The constitutional court ruled on Tuesday in favour of the referendum,
arguing that neither parliament nor the courts had the authority to stop the
referendum because it involved the constituent power of the people to make a
constitution.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United
Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: We Will Sack Some People, Warns Kibaki
Headline: Kenya: We Will Sack Some People, Warns Kibaki
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Politicians and civil servants who have failed to toe the official line risk dismissal, President Kibaki has hinted.
The signal, sounded on Wednesday, appears to target members of the Orange team who are spearheading the campaign against the new Constitution.
Without naming the politicians and the civil servants directly, President Kibaki said the leaders and civil servants who had chosen to pursue a separate line would no longer be allowed to serve in the Government.
"If you are part of Government and you do not toe the line, and you think your colleagues are dreaming, that is useless. We will sack some people," he said.
President Kibaki dropped the hint in Kakamega when he took his Yes campaign to Western Province five days to the referendum.
President Kibaki's Cabinet has been split by the referendum campaign, with some members opposing his call to back the Wako Draft.
Cabinet meetings, which were held weekly since Narc came to power following the December 2002 General Election, have not taken place since June.
Cabinet ministers Raila Odinga (Roads and Public Works), Kalonzo Musyoka (Environment and Natural Resources), Najib Balala (National Heritage), Anyang' Nyong'o (Planning), Linah Jebii Kilimo (Immigration and Registration of Persons) and William ole Ntimama (Office of the President) want Kenyans to vote against the proposed law on Monday.
They have teamed up with Kanu to form the Orange Democratic Movement, which is rallying the public to reject the proposed constitution.
Cabinet ministers in the Banana team have asked the President to dismiss their colleagues in the Orange team for going against the Government's determination to give Kenyans a new Constitution.
In response to calls of dismissal, Mr Musyoka, Mr Balala and Prof Nyong'o have said they are not afraid of the sack.
But they have also stated that they will not quit the Government they helped put in place.
Addressing a delegation of leaders from all the districts in Western Province at Kakamega State Lodge, President Kibaki said violence should not be allowed to undermine the wishes of the majority during the referendum.
In attendance were Vice-President Moody Awori, Cabinet ministers Musikari Kombo, Mukhisa Kituyi, Newton Kulundu, John Michuki, and assistant ministers Moses Akaranga and David Were. MPs from the province also attended.
President Kibaki said: "There are people who are on their way out. You may just be hearing of, but not aware of the list. The list is there."
He added: "If a person is not carrying out a job he was employed to perform, he or she must go."
President Kibaki said the Government would no longer spend its money paying salaries of civil servants who were not delivering service. He said: "There is no reason to continue paying somebody who does not work."
And President Kibaki told the public: "We do not want people to cause violence during the referendum. We want Kenyans to vote freely."
He was optimistic that Kenyans would endorse the proposed law.

Original_Search_Title: Banana, Orange Go for Final Twin Rallies in City
Headline: Kenya: Banana, Orange Go for Final Twin Rallies in City
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Banana and Orange leaders yesterday wound up plans for their parallel rallies in Nairobi tomorrow, as police declared a buffer zone between the two venues and restricted access to the city centre.
President Kibaki, who will lead the Yes rally at Uhuru Park, in what is billed to be the biggest gathering in the city since the sea of humanity that turned up to witness his swearing-in ceremony in 2003, expressed optimism in Eldoret that his team was headed for victory.
He also warned he was not afraid of anyone and vowed deal firmly with anyone trying to mar the Monday referendum. "They should not think they are too big to be arrested. I am not afraid of anybody," said Kibaki, only four days to the final showdown with six members of his Cabinet who shall be addressing an Orange rally at the Nyayo National Stadium.
But speaking on the phone, Roads minister Raila Odinga, one of the leading lights of No team, said their side was ready for the showdown. "This will be the mother of all rallies. We will then rest our case and wait for the voters to decide through the ballot."
Raila added: "We are going to give out the final submission of our case against the Wako Draft."
The other leaders in the No rally programme are Cabinet ministers Najib Balala, Ochillo Ayacko, Kalonzo Musyoka, Anyang' Nyongo' and Linah Kilimo.
Nyong'o yesterday circulated a notification to newsrooms that he will today release a statement on the Sh7 billion twin Anglo Leasing scandals whose perpetrators he claims several ministers in the Banana camp know.
The other top Orange guns are Kanu Secretary-General William Ruto, LDP Secretary-General Joseph Kamotho and former Vice-President Musalia Mudavadi.
For the second time in less than three years, Nairobi will be hosting two parallel and potentially explosive political rallies.
The rallies, to be held within less than two kilometres away from each other, are reminiscent of the Narc and Kanu rallies, which were held 72 hours before the 2002 General Election and to which Kibaki and other members of the ruling coalition rode on an open truck as a sign of solidarity.
The Orange team will end their rally at 4 pm while their Banana counterparts will leave the venue an hour later.
None of the two groups of supporters will be allowed to pass through the city centre in a bid to avoid chaos.
Nairobi Police boss King'ori Mwangi advised the public to avoid the city centre after the rallies and revealed a special force would be at work and that the city had already been demarcated and a no-go zone as well as exit points for the two groups drawn up.
"We are set to handle the rallies, referendum and the period thereafter. I don't expect any Kenyan trying to sort out another. Let us be mature and responsible all through. We have a special force, which will team up with us all through."
Mwangi, who held a Press conference to release plans for tomorrow.
Over 4,000 security personnel will be patrolling the city.
The officers will team up with regular police, General Service Unit and the Administration Police to ensure peace prevails and that there is no contact between the two teams.
A police helicopter will be in hand to ensure rapid response. Mwangi, who addressed the Press in his office in the company of rally organisers from both teams, said they would not allow any of the supporters into the city centre after the rallies.
Some roads will be closed in the city to avoid a clash.
He said no Orange supporter would be allowed to use Landhies Road and Haile-Sellassie Avenue to access the venue.
Similarly, no Banana supporter will be permitted to access Uhuru Park through Ngong Road and Haile Selassie Avenue.
He said they decided to license both rallies after a guarantee by the organisers there would be no chaos.
He said Banana camp supporters will be required to park their cars at Railway Club, Freedom Corner and Uhuru Park.
The Orange group will be required to park their cars outside the stadium and Nairobi West. "If you know you are an Orange supporter do not go to anywhere near Uhuru Park and similarly I do not expect any Banana supporter to go to Nyayo Stadium," said Mwangi.
Makadara MP Reuben Ndolo led the Orange team at the Press briefing while Lieutenant General (rtd) S. W. Njoroge headed the Banana group.
The Banana rally co-ordinator, Father Dominic Wamugunda, said that the "Presidential rally" would be the biggest ever witnessed since the start of the referendum campaigns.
Wamugunda said the rally, which will kick off at 11 am was going to be "a big day as we head towards the conclusion of a hard time of campaigns."
Government Chief Whip Norman Nyaga and the Nairobi Yes co-ordinator and Starehe MP, Maina Kamanda, led the Banana organisers on a tour of the venue.
The Orange Secretariat's communications manager, David Makali, said the rally would start at 10 am. Entertainment will be offered by local reggae star, Mighty King Kong, Rumba's Jamnazi Africa of the I am not sober fame among other artistes.
The Orange leaders will address the gathering from 2 pm and end at 4pm.
Nairobi Mayor Dick Wathika said the city council would this morning fill up the potholes on Processional Way, which cuts across the park.
The council workers will also clear the grounds of any stones "except those lined up to control soil erosion on the terraces."
Inter-Parties Forum Spokesperson, Alex Mureithi, later addressed a Press conference and threw a hint the President may not be attending the rally despite the upbeat mood in the Banana camp.
Muriithi said Vice President Moody Awori, who is the "Yes campaign patron, would address the rally. He explained that the chairman of the Yes campaign team Cabinet Minister Simeon Nyachae would lead other ministers to the rally.
Trade and Industry Minister Mukhisa Kituyi said: "I am confident that we are going to win. I could have attended the final rally, but I will be out of the country on official duty."

Original_Search_Title: Orange: We'll Respect People's Verdict
Headline: Kenya: Orange: We'll Respect People's Verdict
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange team has dispelled fears that the country will plunge into chaos after the referendum by asserting it will accept the verdict of the people.
Kanu secretary general William Ruto said whichever way the vote goes, the Orange side will respect.
He however warned " The referendum is almost lost to my feeling. The Banana side has no chance of winning". Ruto was speaking a during the second debate on the referendum over the proposed new Constitution hosted by the Media Owners Association (MOA) and broadcast live by all television and radio stations in the country. The debate brought together protagonists in the proposed constitution.
The Orange side was represented by Ruto, Mutula Kilonzo and Otieno Kajwang' while Cabinet minister Martha Karua, assistant ministers Danston Mungatana and Moses Wetangula appeared for the Banana side.
It was moderated by television talk show hosts Louis Otieno of the Standard Group and Jullie Gichuru of the Nation Media Group.
Karua welcomed the statement by Ruto saying for the first time the Orange side had acknowledged that there is a Government in place.
However, the Orange and the Banana camp had a heated debate on the devolution chapter.
Ruto said " A district Government minus national resources is nothing".
" The function of the district Government will be reduced to providing permits when you want to bury a pet" added Ruto.
"We went to Naivasha and said that the chapter on devolution be rewritten so that we can have a clear formula of distribution of the national resources. And we had recommended that a percentage of the national resources go to the district Government" said Ruto. He said " But my colleagues went to Kilifi and did a terrible thing"
Ruto warned that many district Governments will fold after five years.

Original_Search_Title: State Justifies FM Station's Closure
Headline: Kenya: State Justifies FM Station's Closure
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Government yesterday attempted to justify the closure of a local FM radio station, even as leaders and Kenyans continued to express outrage over the move.
Government Spokesman Alfred Mutua claimed Kass FM, a station that broadcasts in Kalenjin, was a threat to national security.
He said the Communications Commission of Kenya had received complaints that the station was inciting people to violence.
"Subsequent investigations revealed alarming threats to national security," he said.
Said Mutua: "It had issued a war cry, directing its listeners to prepare themselves for a violent war after the November 21 referendum vote," he said.
He added that the station had "refused to hand over some recording to the Communication Commission of Kenya" and that it had now been asked to explain in writing why they had been unable to do so.
But even as he spoke, the station was handing over three tapes of recordings for the last three weeks to CCK.
The tapes containing debates on the ongoing campaign referendum by Orange leaders Raila Odinga, Joseph Kamotho and William Ruto were handed to the CCK's licensing manager, Mr Stanley Kibe.
They also contain debates by Banana camp's Kipkalya Kones, John Koech and Kipruto Kirwa.
Yesterday, Information minister Raphael Tuju said the station's closure followed complaints to his office from listeners that it was airing inflammatory statements.
"There is no political motive in the closure. We received complaints that incitement remarks were being aired and we took the step," Tuju told a local FM station.
At the same time, the station's managers moved to court after handing over a protest letter to CCK over its closure.
Kass FM's legal adviser, Moses Kurgat, said the decision to close the station was illegal and unreasonable.
Meanwhile, there was tension in Uasin-Gishu and Nandi North Districts following the closure.
But church leaders in the region have appealed for calm. Eldoret's Anglican Church of Kenya Reverend Maritim Rirei asked the community to exercise restraint "to avoid rubbing the Government the wrong way".
"This is a plan to put the community on the offensive," he said.
On Wednesday, there were protests in Cheptiret, Moiben and Ziwa.
Scores of people were slightly injured in the fracas. Business partially came to a standstill at Cheptiret as traders locked their premises for fear of looting.
And the Government came under heavy criticism from the Kalenjin community in Kericho, Bureti and Bomet districts for the action.
The Law Society of Kenya Kericho branch said the closure was a violation of freedom of expression.
Branch secretary Gideon Kipkoech Mutai said the station was crucial to the Kalenjin community adding that the move to shut it down when the referendum was around the corner left no doubt that there was a sinister motive.
Ainamoi MP Noah arap Too said the Government was trying to give Chamge Radio a leeway to campaign for the Banana Yes vote.
In the South Rift region, hundreds of residents and leaders condemned the Government's move, saying it amounted to muzzling the Press.
Civic leaders from Bomet, Bureti, Narok, Kericho and Trans Mara districts defended the station against claims that it had incited people to violence.
Led by Bomet Mayor Patrick Ngerechi and county council chairman, Michael Korir, the leaders accused the Government of selective application of the law and seeking to gag media houses that do not support the proposed new constitution.

Original_Search_Title: We'll Adopt Bomas Draft After Win, Say Orange Leaders
Headline: Kenya: We'll Adopt Bomas Draft After Win, Say Orange Leaders
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange camp plans to adopt the Bomas Draft through another referendum if they win the Monday vote.
"We will amend it by changing some areas properly and ensure that it is subjected to a referendum for Kenyans to adopt it," said Ruto.
Orange spokesman William Ruto said the Bomas Draft would be the way forward after the November 21 win.
"We are sure of the No win on Monday. People of Kenya will have said they want a people-driven constitution and that is what we intent to pursue," said Ruto.
The Kanu secretary-general was speaking at the Orange Secretariat on Wednesday after a meeting attended by Roads minister Raila Odinga and Kanu chairman Uhuru Kenyatta.
It was also attended by Cabinet ministers William ole Ntimama, Lina Kilomo, Najib Balala, several MPs and former vice-president Musalia Mudavadi.
The meeting was for all the Orange polling agents in the November 21 referendum from all the provinces.
"Constitution is about people. By voting No on Monday, the people will have said that they want the process to start a fresh to have a people-driven constitution," said Ruto.
Ruto said the Orange team would move to Parliament and make the necessary legislation, which will provide the legal framework to discuss the Bomas Draft.
He said that Kenyans should not listen to results, which will be announced by a radio station because it was biased in favour of the Banana camp.
"The issue we are raising here is not far-fetched. It has happened. They have jammed one of the radio stations," said Ruto.
Kilonzo said the judgment delivered by the three judges Justice Joseph Nyamu, Anyara Emukule and Roselyn Wendo on Tuesday was a recipe for disaster.
"While we do not entirely agree with the judgment of the constitutional court, we are nevertheless glad that Kenyans can now make their will known in the next five days," he said.
Kilimo said women had nothing to benefit from the proposed constitution.
Ntimama said Kenyans must remain firm and vote No on the proposed constitution on Monday.
The minister said the campaign would be sustained until 2007.

Original_Search_Title: 47 New Districts Now Gazetted
Headline: Kenya: 47 New Districts Now Gazetted
Body_Text:
Nairobi — It is now official; 47 districts will be created in a new-look Kenya in the single largest review of administrative units since Independence.
In a "Special Issue" of the Kenya Gazette obtained by The Standard, the Government has given notice of the creation of 31 districts and renaming of 16 others, effectively pushing the total number to 100.
Dated November 16th and signed by President Kibaki, the notice also proposes the renaming of some districts and the gazetting of 25 "political districts" created after 1992 and whose legality has been contested in courts.
Nairobi is to be split into three districts - Nairobi East, Nairobi West and Nairobi North - while Rift Valley will be split into eight and and Western province into four respectively.
Eastern Province will get six more districts, Central and Nyanza four each while Coast has three extra districts. The splitting of Wajir district into Wajir North and Wajir South is the only change in North Eastern Province.
Giving the breakdown of the expected districts, the Notice of Intention states: "It is notified for the information of the general public that, in accordance with the Constitution of Kenya, the Government, through appropriate legislation intends to:
a) Establish the administrative areas specified in the First Schedule as districts; and
b) Legalise the establishment of the areas specified in the Second Schedule, which were administratively created on diverse dates between 1992 and 2002.
In Coast Province, the Gazette proposes the creation of Kilindini district from Mombasa, Kinango from Kwale and Kaloleni from Kilifi to push the total number of districts to 10.
In Rift Valley Province, Nakuru district has been split into three namely Naivasha, Subukia and Molo while Laikipia has been split into two, Laikipia West and Laikipia East.
East Pokot has been hived off West Pokot district while Turkana, Trans Nzoia and Uasin Gishu districts have each been split into two.
In Western Province, Kakamega district has been divided into Kakamega North and Kakamega South while from Bungoma district, two more units; Webuye and Sirisia have been created. Hamisi/Sabatia district has been created from the renamed Vihiga/Emuhaya district.
Meru Central and Meru North districts in Eastern Province have each been split into two. The former will now have Imenti North and Imenti South while the latter has been renamed Igembe and Tigania districts.
Ukambani has four new districts. Yatta and Kangundo, have been curved out of Machakos district while from Makueni, a new district, Kibwezi, has emerged. Kitui has been split into Kitui North and Kitui South.
In Nyanza, two new districts South Kisii and Masaba have been created while Kisumu has been split into Kisumu South and Kisumu North districts. Rongo will be a new district hived from Migori district.
In Central Province, Nyeri district has been split into Nyeri North and Nyeri South while Nyandarua has also been split into two. Kiambu has been divided into Kiambu East and Kiambu West districts while a new district called Gatanga has been created from Thika.
Although majority of the new creations merit it on the grounds of expansive sizes and/or dense populations, the timing of the announcement is likely to be interpreted as intended to influence Monday's referendum poll.
The proposals are based on the recommendations of a task force appointed to look at the issue, but this is unlikely to stop critics seeing the hand of senior politicians in the Government in the exercise.
They are also likely to be seen in the light of the proposed Constitution, which ties the presidential race to the districts by requiring a successful candidate to garner at least 25 per cent of the cast votes in at least half of the districts.
The three districts in Nairobi for instance are hard to fault on the account of population, as is the proposed Kilindini in Mombasa.
Tourism minister Morris Dzoro and Assistant minister Joseph Kingi who are staunch supporters of the Draft represent Kaloleni and Kinango, which are currently constituencies.
Wajir district is home to the only North Eastern Province minister, Mohammoud Mohammed of Regional Development.
The four districts of Meru, which enjoy large populations have traditionally been loyal to the President even in his Opposition days and are home to Justice minister Kiraitu Murungi and his Finance colleague, David Mwiraria, who both wield considerable clout in the Government.
Health minister Charity Ngilu, who is another key player in the Banana camp, hails from Kitui while Kalembe Ndile, another Yes crusader is the MP for Kibwezi.
Central Province, which is generally crowded, is considered sympathetic to the President, as is the larger Nakuru district.
The new districts will also be seen as partly rewarding Western Kenya, which is bedrock of Ford-Kenya and its chairman, Local Government Musikari Kombo who is in the Banana camp.
The two new districts in Gusiiland are likely to be seen as the President's quid-pro-quo to Energy minister Simeon Nyachae and his Ford-People supporters who are supporting the draft.
The intended gazetting of the 25 existing districts is intended to cushion them from another legal challenge.
In 2001, Internal Security minister John Michuki, then an opposition MP for Kangema, went to court seeking to have the districts declared illegal, as they had not been gazetted.
The Government conceded in Parliament in April that the districts were technically illegal, but declined to outlaw them.

Original_Search_Title: Electoral Commission Warns of Cheating, Says Poll Results Out Tuesday
Headline: Kenya: ECK Warns of Cheating, Says Poll Results Out On Tuesday
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Electoral Commission of Kenya (ECK) anticipates cheating during the referendum because the names of the dead and those who have registered twice could be used, chairman Samuel Kivuitu warned yesterday.
Kivuitu said voter cheating may occur in areas dominated by either sides of the Banana or Orange through collusion among the clerks and other electoral officials sympathetic to their camp.
He said the problem has been compounded by lack of agents for both sides.
The ECK has 11,608,889 registered voters. The list includes those who are dead and those who might have registered twice.
Kivuitu was speaking at the Kenyatta International Conference Centre where he briefed international and local observers on the referendum.
And the referendum results may be announced on Tuesday (November 22) and not Wednesday because the returning officers will be airlifted to Nairobi to deliver the results for immediate tabulation.
"I urge you to assist us and observe carefully, if you go to a place and see the clerk is not watching, report that immediately," he said.
"To counter cheating, the commission will dispatch an additional number of ballot papers above the number of registered voters in polling stations".
"Tell the clerk if a person is voting for the second time," he asked the observers.
"It is natural, the clerks and even the agents can be tempted to collude and favour their sides. We will check these and deal with the matter," he added.
He told the observers that they were not the monitors of ECK and warned them against interfering with the work of the commission.
"You were accredited because you are going to be impartial, you must remain so." Kivuitu said the Government had agreed to provide military and police helicopters to airlift returning officers from the constituencies.
"The helicopters will take the returning officers from the rural or hardship areas to the nearest airstrip where they can board the next flight to Nairobi or fly direct," said Kivuitu.
Kivuitu said that the returning officers would be airlifted immediately back after announcing the results, which will also be pinned on doors of the polling stations.
He said that the voting would start at 7am and end at 5pm so counting can take place at daytime.
The areas listed as hardship include North Eastern Province, Turkana, Lamu, Samburu and Narok districts among others.

Original_Search_Title: Voters to Follow the Advice of Leaders
Headline: Kenya: Voters to Follow the Advice of Leaders
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The proposed constitution's legal jargon has prevented many Kenyans from understanding it.
As a result, many of those who spoke to the Nation in a survey
yesterday said they would vote according to what their leaders have
told them.
Few have read the proposed laws and understand them sufficiently well to make an informed choice on Monday.
The Government printed four million copies of the laws in English
and 50,000 in Kiswahili and translated them into 16 local languages in
programmes broadcast on State-owned KBC radio.
In Nairobi, Mr Chris Wamisiko, a security guard, said: "I saw the
version published in newspapers. I tried to read it and found it
complicated. I have taken a position based on inheritance issues - I
believe women will supersede men."
Different interpretations
Mr Kinya Alfayo, an hotel waiter, said: "I have read the draft but I
do not understand the contents because the writers used difficult
language. I have not seen any civic educator to explain to me what I
need to know. Every politician is having his own interpretation and so,
I will not vote."
In Nyeri, Mr Benson Kagera, 40, said he had been unable to go through the draft "as the language used is too complex for me".
A mason at Kamakwa on the outskirts of Nyeri Town, he would follow
the politicians' advice when he casts his vote. "The (politicians) are
the people who will influence my vote," said Mr Kagera.
But Ms Grace Wangui Ndung'u, 31, a businesswoman in the same town,
had a different story. She had read the draft and "I can't listen to
the gossip by politicians," said she.
"I have heard them tell all sorts of lies. They have been misleading the public and I feel bad over it," says Ms Ndung'u.
"Unlike most people, I will not follow the word of politicians who have been going around telling lies," she said.
In Kisumu, Mr Gabriel Auka alias "Nera", an electrician, says he got
a copy of the draft from the district officer's office at Kibuye. After
studying it, he had made a choice to reject it, not being happy with
land rights and the powers of the presidency.
Mr Juma Musa, a businessman and chairman of the Supreme Council of
Muslims in Nyanza, had obtained a copy from the district commissioner's
office.
He had read it several times and decided the document would not serve the country and the future generations.
Ms Elizabeth Odhiambo, 24, who runs a food kiosk at Kisumu, had
borrowed a copy from a neighbour and read it. She objects to the way
the land, religious courts and devolution issues have been treated, and
favours a return to Bomas.
Eldoret businessman James Kamau said that, after attending civic
education, he had compared the Lancaster Constitution against the Bomas
draft and the proposed constitution and favoured Banana because he
believed it would buttress the Government's position.
En route to Kitale from Eldoret was Mr Eric Wafula, 28, a Moi
University graduate, who said he would vote No because he had thought
he would get a job under the Narc Government but none had materialised.
Mzee Samuel Tiongik, 64, of Eldoret North constituency said he would
vote No because a politician had said his land would be under threat if
the proposed constitution goes through.
He said he had attended civic education but that the educators had left without telling him whether to vote for or against.
Businesswoman Wanjiku Mwaura said she would vote No because of the religious courts.
But John Masinde, a vendor in Eldoret, said that, although he had
not read the document, he would vote for it "to silence those
threatening others".
He said some people were saying they would evict others from their areas if the proposed constitution carries the day.
In Nakuru, Mr Jacob Njau, a shoe trader, said he had not read the
document but that, last week, he had received divine intervention and
he would vote Yes.
Mr Ibrahim Ngamau, a 34-year-old businessman, said he had neither
read the document nor attended civic education classes, but that, going
by what he had heard, he would vote in favour.
"The general feeling is that the document is good and should be accepted," he said.
Mr Alex Rubu, a 26-year-old salesperson of mobile phones, has not seen the proposed constitution.
"The way it was distributed was not fair, he said. "Many people, who
include lawyers, have had more access to the document than the ordinary
people."
In Mombasa, curio businessman Mohammed Abdalla, 57, said he had read
the proposed constitution and would vote No "because it has so many
chapters that are negative to my religion, Islam, and the coastal
people and also against African culture.
Not read document
Mr Juma Salim, 68, a retired fisherman, said he had not read the document because of his poor eyesight.
"I will, however, most likely vote on the referendum day based on
what the politicians are saying - and the advice I get from my children
and grandchildren.
Ms Kashi Ali Majaro, 35, a hotelier, said she had not read the proposed constitution because it is too complex.
"According to my husband and most politicians, the draft is flawed
and will be bad for future generations. So my vote most likely will be
No."

Original_Search_Title: MPs Face Off in Big Debate
Headline: Kenya: MPs Face Off in Big Debate
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Banana-Yes and Orange-No Teams last night made a last ditch
television and radio campaign to convince Kenyans to vote for their
side during Monday's referendum.
Six panellists, three from either side, engaged in a one-and-a
half-hour live debate on three issues: The Executive, Parliament and
Devolution.
In the second Big Debate organised by the Media Owners Association,
MPs Moses Wetangula, Martha Karua and Danson Mungatana for the Yes side
and Mutula Kilonzo, Otieno Kajwang' and William Ruto for the No
campaign engaged in a sober debate as they either defended or
criticised various clauses in the Wako Draft.
Except for a brief moment when the issue of the size of the Cabinet
brought heated exchanges between the two sides, the rest of the debate
addressed real issues in the Wako Draft.
The point of contention was the altering of the Naivasha proposals
setting the maximum number of Cabinet posts at 25 but which was removed
by the Kilifi group to allow a limitless number of ministers.
Yesterday, both sides gave the country hope that they will accept the verdict of Kenyans on Monday.
They recognised that if the Yes side loses, the country will
continue with the current Constitution, and there is a government
running the nation.
If the No side loses, the MPs admitted that the country will move
ahead and implement the new supreme law under the proposed Draft.
The emotional exchanges which characterised the first Big Debate on October 18 were absent this time round.
On the contentious issue of Devolution, the two sides presented
different arguments with the Orange team raising questions on the
viability of district governments.
The Banana side defended the two-tier system, with a national and
district level of government, arguing that it was less-cumbersome than
the four levels recommended at Bomas.
The arguments on the legislature centred around Parliament's ability
to check excesses of the Executive. The Yes side argued that provisions
for presidential appointees to be vetted by Parliament were a good
check on the presidency, while the No side said the President could
still manipulate MPs to endorse his decisions and appointments.
The No side felt that the 75 per cent requirement to impeach a
president was unattainable, while the Yes team said it was necessary to
safeguard the office and authority of the President from malicious
designs by MPs.
Yesterday's debate was moderated by NTV's Julie Gichuru and KTN's Louis Otieno.
At the beginning of the debate, each of the panellists was given 30
seconds to explain why they supported or opposed the proposed
Constitution.
Ms Karua said: "I support the new Constitution because it contains
all the needs of ordinary Kenyans such as free primary education,
provision of free health, water and land. However, what is not provided
in this proposed Constitution is the short-cut for people who are power
hungry and who are seeking power."
Mr Wetang'ula said: " I support the proposed Constitution because it
breaks from the old order to anew order and it carries what, in a
nutshell, I will call social security for Kenyans."
Mr Danson Mungatana, Mr Moses Wetang'ula and Ms Martha Karua, who apperaed for the Banana (Yes) side in the debate.
Photo by Joan Pereruan
Mr Mungatana added: "I support this draft Constitution because it
speaks for the ordinary citizen especially section 86. It states that
Parliament must be ordered by the Constitution to give squatters land
and respect. Those living in slums must also be given dignity and
respect where they live. There will also be a fund to assist ordinary
citizens to be given land.
In his turn, Mr Ruto said: "I oppose this new Constitution because
it fails in many aspects to capture the will, views and aspirations of
Kenyans as contained in the views collected by the Constitution of
Kenya Review Commission and represents the whims and interests of a
clique.
Mr Kajwang: "This document fundamentally fails to address the
aspirations and hopes of Kenyans which were collected and collated in
the Bomas draft. It proposes a new constitution alright but doesn't
give us a new constitutional dispensation.
Mr Kilonzo: "Fellow Kenyans, since this document was published in
August, I have repeatedly said it is a bad document. Your time has
come. My reasons for rejecting it will emerge in the course of the
debate tonight but I wish to appeal to you to reject it peacefully for
the rallies you are holding on Saturday and for the polling you will do
on Monday, the November 21st. Please do so peacefully in the name of
God and for the sake of this country. It is not worth fighting for.
What your judges said yesterday is that this is a document you can
replace at will".
The two sides were then given five minutes to present their position and state why they had taken them.
Ms Karua said the proposed Constitution contained the wishes of
Kenyans as collected and collated by the Constitution of the Kenya
Review Commission.
Foreign Affairs assistant minister Moses Wetang'ula described the
proposed Constitution as the most negotiated and comprehensive document
ever seen in Africa.
Mr Mungatana said the Draft took special concern about the needs of
the common man and mentioned Section 76 which provides ordinary Kenyans
with equal rights before the law.
He said the Draft was a document for the common (wo)man which
safeguarded them from the excesses of Parliament and the Executive by
ensuring that it can only be amended by Kenyans.
Eldoret North MP William Ruto said although the Bomas Draft was a
product of the collective will and views of Kenyans, the Government
changed it to suit their needs.
"It had good chapters, recognised the Almighty God, preserved the
supremacy of the Constitution, natural values, principles of
accountability, transparency and culture. But it took the schemes and
machinations of a few to dismantle it to give the executive excess
powers," he said.
The Kanu secretary general said the Draft gave the president
sweeping powers to appoint any number of ministers to negate checks and
principle on the presidency.
"This destroys multi-party democracy and devolution which involves sharing of resources," he said.
Responding to Mr Louis Otieno's query on powers vested on the
executive, Mr Kajwang said the Draft had vested too much power on the
presidency.
Mr Kajwang said the country risked being turned into a monarchy or dictatorship if executive powers were not trimmed.
"Too much powers vested on the executive is dangerous for anybody, any country," he said.
He said under the Draft, the President could even appoint the Leader of Official Opposition to be prime minister.
Mr Kajwang, a lawyer by profession, said even with an executive
president the problems facing the health and water sectors would
continue.
He said an executive presidency could result in continued corruption, destruction of institutions and the economy.
"The executive (in the Draft) is worse than in the current Constitution," Mr Kajwang said.
Mr Wetang'ula said contrary to allegations by the Orange team,
presidential powers in the proposed Constitution had been reduced
drastically and would be checked by Parliament.
The president will not be able to give out public land, appoint
judges and ambassadors without the approval of Parliament. He will also
not be able to sign any international treaties or appoint public
servants without seeking the approval of Parliament.
"I do not understand where these imperial powers of the president
the Orange group has been saying are coming from," Mr Wetang'ula said.
Wetang'ula Ms Karua said in all democracies, executive power rests with the president.
She said: "The buck must stop somewhere... We cannot have a
situation where we do not know where the final authority of the
Government rests."
She gave the examples of America, Uganda, Ghana, Senegal and
Tanzania as countries where executive powers were vested in the
president.
Kenya could not have a situation where executive power was vested in
an unelected prime minister as had been suggested by the Bomas Draft as
it would end up creating two centres of power.
She wondered why the Orange group was now backing off since even
Naivasha had agreed that executive power must rest with the president.
Mr Mungatana accused the Orange team of dishonesty since not one of
them had cited even one clause that conferred excessive powers to the
president.
Likewise, the president had been barred from giving roadside
declarations as they must all be in writing, signed and in a Government
seal or letter head.
"The truth is that the Orange team does not have a single thing to
show that the proposed Constitution is bad. They are like the Galatians
who were told by Paul that they have eyes yet they cannot see, and have
ears but cannot hear!" Mr Mungatana said.
The section says that the prime minister will be appointed by the
president and shall be the leader of Government business in Parliament
and perform other duties that he may be assigned by the president or as
laid out in the Constitution.
But Mr Kilonzo took issue with these provisions saying the
president, under the proposed Constitution, would easily use them to
"fire a prime minister at will".
He also criticised Section 155(7) of the draft which spells out
measures that would lead to the impeachment and removal of the
president from office. The 75 per cent parliamentary vote threshold
spelt out under this section, he observed, makes removing the president
difficult as it was nearly impossible to marshall.
But this provision was defended by Ms Karua who said it would be
risky to have a president that can easily be impeached by a rogue
Parliament.
She added: "A rogue Parliament can easily overthrow a president very quickly."
Mr Wetang'ula said cushioning the president from impeachment was the trend in most democracies both English speaking and French.
He added that the constitution gives the people of Kenya the
opportunity to "impeach their president every five years" when the
General Election is held.
On the size of the of the Cabinet, Mr Ruto questioned why the proposal to fix the Cabinet size at 25 was deleted from the Draft.
"There was a definite provision for the size of the Cabinet. But you
went and brought in the old provision that Parliament will decide. Why
was this provision on the size of the Government removed?"
Mr Kilonzo further said that Section 164 spells no limitation to the
size of the Cabinet, leaving it at the discretion of the president.
Defending the role of the president in appointing the Cabinet, Mr
Wetang'ula said the Naivasha accord, which "Mr Ruto chaired",
determined that the president retains his role as the Chief Executive
of the country.
On the legislature, Ms Karua said she is happy with the proposed law
as it did not provide for two chambers, adding that the creation of the
Senate by the Bomas Draft amounted to duplication of roles.
She said the chapter on Parliament provided for special constituency
seats for women. This, she noted, would see more women going to
Parliament than if each district elects one.
On his part, Mr Mungatana cited Section 116 of the proposed
Constitution, saying it provided for special sets for women, the youth,
workers and the disabled.
Article 116 describes the composition and membership of Parliament
and provides for the election of an MP to represent a constituency.
Political parties will have to reserve special seats for the youth if the document is passed, he said.
If the Wako Draft is enacted, he said, Parliament would be a people's House.
However, Mr Kilonzo opposed them, saying if there was any good
reason why the proposed Draft should be rejected it was because of the
structure of the legislature.
Quoting clause 116, he argued that the structure was confusing as it
was not clear how the one-third women in Parliament would be elected.
He said the number of MPs under the proposed laws were likely to be between 1,000 and 1,6000, according to his Orange team.
He, however, said the 74 women MPs were more as provided for in the
Bomas Draft than the one-third of the House under the proposed laws.
He said it was not possible, even with the use of computers, to know
the number of women going to the House under the proposed law.
He said although presidential appointments were vetted by Parliament
under the proposed Constitution, the Head of State could appoint a
third person to be premier automatically after the first two are
rejected.
Ms Karua denied claims that Parliament will be easily manipulated
under the proposed Constitution saying voters will have themselves to
blame for electing MPs who lacked integrity.
"I ask women to know that they are better off under the proposed
Constitution and they should therefore vote for it on Monday," Ms Karua
said.
Mr Wetang'ula accused the Orange team of "selectively reading the
draft" over the controversial issue of reserved seats for women in the
legislature.
Mr Kajwang scoffed at the claim that the legislature would check the
executive saying that under the draft that was wishful thinking.
"If the President is given the powers to appoint a one party leader
to be premier it will reduce the party leader to a figure head," Mr
Kajwang said.
Mr Ruto opposed the draft saying it had too many grey areas on very pertinent issues that affect the separation of powers.
Mr Mungatana said under the new Constitution Parliament will carry
its business in public and there will be no room for underhand and
shady deals.
"I don't believe the President will use the Constitution to lord it
over the MPs and other arms of Government," Mr Mungatana said.
"This is the best draft we can ever have," the Water minister said.
Mr Wetang'ula said the transfer of power under the Constitution would not happen with a bang but would be a gradual process.
However Mr Ruto said the sharing of the resources would be
determined by Parliament and would not be equitable as there was no
formular for implementing it.
On the aftermath of the referendum vote, Ms Karua said if the draft
was accepted, the Government would embark on implementing it. She said
in the event of a No vote, the Government would continue to administer
on the current Constitution.
Mr Ruto said there will be no winner whichever side won.
"It is only the margin of the loss either way that we are waiting for," Mr Ruto said.
Mr Kajwang said Kenyans will start agitating for a new Constitution
if Yes wins with a small margin. And if No wins, a legal framework will
be put in place to revert to Bomas Draft.
Mr Mungatana said the Government will continue to function normally
if Yes wins or not. He said the struggle (mapambano in Kiswahili) will
continue.
Mr Kilonzo said the proposed Constitution was not implementable and was likely to attract numerous litigations in court.
The Wako draft, he said, cannot be contested for validity or
legality and asking Kenyans to accept it was tantamount to being asked
to take poison.
He said if No wins in the referendum, there would be no vacuum since
President Kibaki was elected in a democratic way and would therefore
continue to govern.
Ms Karua said she was happy that the Orange side had at last
recognised that there would be no vacuum even if No carried the day on
Monday, but warned that there would be no shortcut to power. Mr
Kajw'ang asked Kenyans to reject the Wako draft because the powers
vested in the president would overrun all other institutions in the
country therefore undermining democracy.

Original_Search_Title: Plan for a Fresh Law If Orange Carries the Day
Headline: Kenya: Plan for a Fresh Law If Orange Carries the Day
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange team will push for a review of the Bomas draft if it wins Monday's referendum vote, leaders said in Nairobi.
They said they would table a motion in Parliament seeking to base the new constitution-making process on the older document.
Mr William Ruto and Mr Mutula Kilonzo were speaking as they met team
leaders and chief agents from constituencies at their Upper Hill
headquarters.
"Once Kenyans have voted No to the flawed proposed constitution, we will start the process afresh," said Mr Ruto.
The clamour for a new constitution, said the Eldoret North MP, was
the people's and Parliament was under obligation to fulfil the popular
wish.
And the Bomas draft, which was amended by Parliament before being
placed before the public for a vote, was the document on which to base
a new constitution, he said.
"The Constitution is about the people of Kenya, and the Bomas draft
is a true reflection of their wishes," he said. "It will be a good
starting point to give them a new constitution."
Questions continued to surround the fate of the quest for a new supreme law should the public reject the Wako draft.
The Constitution of Kenya Review Commission (CKRC) Act, which has
shepherded the process, will lapse 30 days after the referendum.
The Orange leaders, however, exuded confidence that they would carry
the day on Monday, pledging to give Kenyans a people-driven
constitution.
Mr Kilonzo, the Orange national team leader, urged supporters not to
be influenced by inducements but stand up to intimidation and reject
the Wako draft.
"The Government's going to shower you with money, intimidate you,
use State machinery, cancel our rallies, block you from voting, give
you more districts, increase your salaries and even promise you jobs.
Take the goodies, but vote No on Monday. Don't give away your
democratic rights," he said.
The Orange headquarters was abuzz as gents filled in forms and collected T-shirts and caps in readiness for the final stretch.
The meeting with the agents focused on how to stop any attempts by the Banana team from influencing the Monday vote.
"The meeting noted various logistical and financial challenges on
the ground, the Government's efforts to manipulate the voters, and the
persistent attempts to link the No campaign to violence," said Mr
Kilonzo.
To clear its name, he said, the team had written to the Electoral
Commission of Kenya pointing out the irregularities which might affect
the vote.
The agents resolved to ensure the task is conducted freely and
fairly, to stop any rigging, to guard the results, to mobilise people
to vote and to rally support for the final rally at Nairobi's Nyayo
stadium on Saturday.
Mr Ruto and Mr Kilonzo claimed the Banana team was planning to use a
media house to announce fake referendum results on Monday and jam other
stations supportive of the No campaign.
"They want to use its TV and radio to announce fake results and we
urge our supporters not to trust the results from a station which is
tribal and biased towards the Banana campaign," said Mr Ruto.
He claimed the station had jammed the frequencies of other media houses before and could be used by the Government once more.

Original_Search_Title: Fm Station Closed As Kibaki Pledges New Districts
Headline: Kenya: Fm Station Closed As Kibaki Pledges New Districts
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Radio regulators yesterday ordered an FM broadcasting station to be shut down for seven days.
The order came as President Kibaki held out the promise of four new
districts in western Kenya... if the proposed Constitution is passed in
Monday's referendum.
They could be hived off from Kakamega, Vihiga and Bungoma, the
President hinted, but only after a nationwide review of all district
boundaries.
The station ordered closed was Kass FM, which broadcasts in Kalenjin language from Nairobi.
The station's closure sparked fighting between members of two
communities in Nandi North during which three people were seriously
injured.
One group was accusing the other of instigating the closure of the
station because they supported the Banana Yes camp in the referendum
campaigns.
Managers at the FM station were ordered by the Government to submit
recordings of their programmes for the last 21 days to the
Communications Commission of Kenya.
The closure came because the station had called for people to engage in violence, said Government spokesman Alfred Mutua.
"The station has for the last three weeks been asking its listeners
to engage in acts of violence. We feel this is dangerous in view of
what happened in Rwanda," he added. Those injured in the Nandi fracas
were rushed to the Kapsabet District Hospital.
Traders from one of the communities selling secondhand clothes and
other goods at Cheptiret trading centre were attacked for allegedly
instigating the closure of the radio station.
As we went to press, the trading centre was deserted with all shops closed. Police patrolled the streets.
Earlier, members of a community around Cheplaskei, about eight
kilometres from Eldoret Town on the Eldoret-Nakuru road, said they had
been warned that they would be evicted from the area if the Yes vote
won.
They appealed to the police to mount regular patrols in the area to prevent the planned attacks.
A senior police officer at the Eldoret police station said they had
received the reports and some officers had been sent to patrol the area.
Efforts to get a comment from Nandi North police boss Julius Narangwi were fruitless.
In Nairobi, Dr Mutua said the Government would not allow people to preach violence.
But the station's closure was immediately condemned by MPs from Rift
Valley, who said it was political and aimed at silencing a station that
had supported the Orange No-vote campaign.
Belgut MP Charles Keter said the closure was meant to give a new
Kalenjin station, Chamge FM owned by Royal Media Services, which
supports the Yes campaign, a head start in the run-up to the Monday
poll.
And Mr Billow Kerrow, MP for Mandera Central, joined the fray,
saying the closure reflected Government bias because Kameme FM and
Citizen radio, which both supported the Yes campaign, had been left off
the hook.
Kass FM is owned by Mr Joshua Chepkwony, who is also its executive director.
The station's managers have been summoned to a meeting with the CCK
next Wednesday when the regulator will ask why its frequencies should
not be withdrawn altogether, according to a statement from CCK.
The closure order came as the Banana Yes-vote and Orange No-vote
teams traversed different parts of the country in a final dash to sway
voters before Monday's referendum on the proposed Constitution.
At the same time, the Government declared Tuesday a public holiday
in addition to the Monday voting day to allow people time to travel
back to work.
Winding down his tour of Western Province, President Kibaki hinted
that Kakamega and Vihiga districts would be split to create two new
districts, while Bungoma would also get two new districts.
But that would only be done after the proposed Constitution was
passed and a national review of district boundaries, the President said.
Mr Kibaki, who received delegations at Kakamega State Lodge, said
densely populated districts would be split to enhance administration.
"We are thinking of increasing, not decreasing districts. For
example, isn't Bungoma District too large?" he asked, in a response to
several MPs who had asked for additional districts.
Since the referendum campaigns started, the President has promised
several new districts to various parts of the country. Among regions
promised new districts were, Ukambani (three), Kisii (two) and Nakuru
(two).
The President left Kakamega for Eldoret last evening.
The Orange-No team said it would push for a fresh start to the review if it won Monday's vote.
A motion would be taken to Parliament seeking to restart the
process, using the original Bomas Draft as the basis of a new
Constitution.
Orange group leaders William Ruto and Mutula Kilonzo made the pledge
as they met team leaders and chief agents from all constituencies at
their headquarters, in Upper Hill, Nairobi.

Original_Search_Title: ECK Rules Out Banning Campaigns
Headline: Kenya: ECK Rules Out Banning Campaigns
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Electoral Commission yesterday ruled out banning the campaign
rallies, saying that police had argued that this could cause chaos.
The chairman, Mr Samuel Kivuitu, made the announcement in his
Nairobi office when the commission started distributing ballot papers
ahead of the Monday vote.
He said police had asked him not to stop any rally despite the
violence that had so far left eight people dead. Police, he said,
feared that either camp could defy the order and cause mayhem.
"Police explained to us that cancelling the rallies will cause
insecurity and there will be instability in the country," he said.
"They explained to us that it was not the best thing to do."
ECK had warned of banning the campaigns after four people were
killed in Kisumu on October 29, and that should there be another
killing, it would go to court to seek the ban.
Vice-chairman Gabriel Mukele had warned: "If there is any more
violence the commission will seek a court order banning both sides from
campaigning."
And after the Friday violence at Likoni, Mombasa, in which four
people were killed when police stopped an Orange rally, the polls body
wrote to the police asking why it should not be allowed to ban the
campaigns.
It also wrote to the Banana and Orange camps asking for details of the chaos that also saw a district officer's office burnt.
But yesterday, according to Mr Kivuitu, police said the ban could cause more insecurity.
At the same time, he told the Nation that ballot papers to be
distributed countrywide arrived in the country on Monday and Tuesday
and were in the commission stores at Nairobi's Industrial Area under
police protection.
Yesterday, the commission's Anniversary Towers office in Nairobi was
a beehive of activity as returning officers collected their allowances
and went to the Industrial Area to pick the ballot papers under the
watchful eye of the police.
The 15 million papers bearing the Orange and Banana symbols were
being distributed to all the 210 officers who will oversee voting at
19,000 polling stations.
Mr Kivuitu said that before the commission took action against
either side it would consider its powers and duties under the law, and
also the possible consequences.
In an advertisement in the Nation today, he argues that before the
commission takes action it will consider if the law is on its side and
if the camps will respect the decision.
Before any action is taken, the commission will also consider if a ban will hurt the public.

Original_Search_Title: Mwenje Abandons Orange And Reaches for Banana
Headline: Kenya: Mwenje Abandons Orange And Reaches for Banana
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Embakasi MP David Mwenje yesterday ditched Orange and decamped to the Banana side.
He said he made the switch after an assurance by President Kibaki
that contentious issues in the proposed Constitution would be amended
in Parliament should the Yes team win.
But Orange team leaders dismissed his defection as "inconsequential"
to their campaign. Saying the No campaign was confident of winning the
referendum, Eldoret North MP William Ruto claimed Mr Mwenje had been
under pressure from the Banana camp to quit the No team.
"We know Mwenje has just submitted to the whims of the Banana team,
but he is a free citizen who can choose where to support. It is his
democratic right," Mr Ruto said.
Yesterday, Mr Mwenje, at a press conference at Landmark Hotel,
Nairobi, cited the chapters on land, religion and human rights as some
of the contentious issues which had earlier made him oppose the
proposed Constitution.
Under the law, all land will be under the National Land Commission
whose members will be appointed by the president but vetted by MPs.
"On human rights, I'm against reviving investigations on the murders
of J.M. Kariuki, Tom Mboya, Pio Gama Pinto among others since this
could easily cause conflict among Kenyans," Mr Mwenje said.
Accompanied by his Limuru counterpart, Mr Kuria Kanyingi, Mr Mwenje
said he had decamped after "a lengthy meeting with his constituents" on
Tuesday. The move came a week after he had had talks with President
Kibaki at State House, Nairobi.
Speaking at the City Stadium yesterday, assistant Education minister
Beth Mugo and her Local Government counterpart, Mrs Betty Tett, said
the move was a victory for the Yes team.
Mrs Mugo said: "This is just like the parable of the prodigal son in
the Bible. He has returned home. We are very happy and welcome him to
the Yes team to work with us for the remaining days."
And Mrs Tett said: "We do not want to lose a single vote to the
Orange team." The MPs were addressing women's representatives from
Nairobi.
Nominated MP Njoki Ndung'u said: "We, as women of Kenya, will make
our presence known on Monday as we take our rightful place. We are the
majority and will be the biggest losers if we vote against the new law."

Original_Search_Title: Orange to Use Mobile Phones 'To Curb Rigging'
Headline: Kenya: Orange to Use Mobile Phones 'To Curb Rigging'
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The orange camp will receive results from its agents in polling stations through mobile phones to curb rigging.
The arrangement was announced as Roads minister Raila Odinga and
Kanu chairman Uhuru Kenyatta claimed that the Banana team was planning
to instigate violence to instill fear and scare voters from going to
the polling stations on Monday.
Raila said the Orange team would establish a tallying centre at
their secretariat, which will compare the results with those released
by Electoral Commission of Kenya (ECK) at its KICC operation centre.
Raila said cellphones will be used by all Orange camp polling agents
to communicate vote count in every station to curb rigging on the
referendum.
"We want all our agents to provide their cell phone numbers so that
they can communicate the vote count and we tabulate the total votes on
our side to ensure that wrong figures are not announced," said Raila.
Raila and Uhuru were addressing the Orange agents at the Orange secretariat in Nairobi.
He said the latest opinion poll indicated that Orange had 61 per cent lead over Banana's 39 per cent.
After the meeting, nominated MP Mutula Kilonzo read a statement to the press.
"The agents will guard the results of the poll and scuttle any interference with the out come," he said.
"Poor turnout at the referendum will be an advantage to our opponents. We must turn up and vote No," said Raila.
Uhuru said Kenyans should turn up in large numbers and vote for the Orange.
"We must translate our big numbers into votes and victory will be on our side," added Uhuru.
He said the Banana team was planning to use the police to intimidate voters "so that we can have a poor turnout".
Raila said the Orange team would launch door-to-door campaigns to ensure they win the referendum.
"We'll submit out final case against the Wako Draft during the
Saturday rally at Nyayo Stadium, the mother of all the rallies," he
said.

Original_Search_Title: Vernacular Radio Station Shut
Headline: Kenya: Vernacular Radio Station Shut
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Government yesterday kicked off a storm after it dispatched
engineers to shut down a local FM station broadcasting in the Kalenjin
language.
Bomet Member of Parliament, Nick Salat , and Kass FM Managing
Director, Joseph Towett, shortly after the radio station was closed by
the Communications Commission of Kenya yesterday.
The closure of Kass FM 89.0 caused a stir in the political arena,
with nine MPs calling a Press conference at Parliament Buildings to
condemn the action which the Government said was triggered by
complaints on the nature of the broadcast messages.
Engineers from regulator Communications Commission of Kenya (CCK)
drove to the station's premises with a letter slapping a seven-day
off-air order on the station. They asked to be shown the general
manager, Mr Joseph Towett, whom they gave a copy of the letter. The
engineers - who refused to talk to journalists - gave the station five
minutes to cease transmission.
The station will remain closed until November 23 - two days after
the referendum - when its management is scheduled to appear before CCK
to "show cause why your frequencies should not be revoked".
After the switch-off, the engineers walked to the satellite room where they disabled the broadcast equipment before driving off.
The letter, dated November 16 and addressed to executive director, Joshua K. Chepkwony, was received at 4:38 pm.
Writing on behalf of the CCK Director General, Michael Waweru, S.K.
Kibe said: "The Commission has received some complaints regarding the
nature of broadcasting messages being transmitted by your M/s Kalee
(KASS) FM sound broadcasting service.
"The Commission also hereby summons you for a meeting in our offices
on November 23 at 9am to show cause why your frequencies should not be
revoked".
CCK called on Kass FM to submit transmission recordings for the past
21 days, which must be received by noon today. The MPs, eight from Rift
Valley Province, said the Government was running scared ahead of the
referendum.
They were Mr Nick Salat, Mr Charles Keter, Mr Bonaya Godana, Mr
Billow Kerrow, Mr Sammy Ruto, Mr Gideon Moi, Mr John Sambu, Mr David
Sudi, Mr Franklin Bett and Mr Paul Sang'.
They accused the Government of trying to influence the people of Rift Valley in Monday's referendum by closing the station.
They said the closure was triggered by a combination of business and
political rivalry. A new station broadcasting in Kalenjin opened two
days ago.
"CCK has infringed on the law by shutting down the station for a week without issuing a warning. That is the law," said Keter.
Kass FM general manager Towett said the Government action
contravened the freedom of the press, adding that it was an act of
intimidation.
"The hurried manner in which the Government has dealt with this
station is political and aimed at harassing the community because of
its stand in the coming referendum," he said.
Towett said the station had not received any verbal or written
complaints from the Government concerning the broadcast content and
programmes.
The station's employees were caught by surprise and could be seen frantically making calls.
Baringo Central MP Gideon Moi said the closure of the station was an
exercise in futility. "We know which stations propagate hate campaigns.
The act will not influence the referendum," he said.
Last month, Information minister Raphael Tuju tabled a statement in
Parliament on FM stations said to be propagating tribal hatred, and
Kass FM was not one of them.
Yesterday, CCK's letter referred to the station as one of those the minister wanted investigated.
But Salat accused the Government of supporting a rival competitor to the station.
Some Orange leaders had hours before the closure claimed that the Banana Group was jamming some stations.
Kanu Nominated MP Mutula Kilonzo said: "They (Banana) are jamming our communications".
Mutula was speaking at a press conference after a meeting of Orange
leaders led by Cabinet minister Raila Odinga and Kanu chairman Uhuru
Kenyatta.
Added Mutula: "They are also planning to use certain radio stations
to announce fake results and to jam the frequencies of media that do
not support their schemes".
Another MP, Charles Keter, said the Government wanted to influence
the referendum by shutting down the station, which has been "carrying
out civic education in Rift Valley'.'
Salat said the Government should have shut down FM stations fanning
ethnic tensions. "There are many FM stations fanning propaganda on
referendum. They are preaching ethnic hatred and nothing has happened
to them."
Mr Sammy Ruto said the Government was interfering with press freedom.
Godana termed the move an act of bias. "It is motivated by nothing
other than its influence on the (outcome) of the referendum. CCK is a
public body, which should operate above board. They are either
incompetent or acting under duress," added Godana.
Kerrow said the action reflected Government bias in the referendum.

Original_Search_Title: MP Joins Yes Camp, Says Kibaki Will Keep His Word
Headline: Kenya: MP Joins Yes Camp, Says Kibaki Will Keep His Word
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Embakasi MP David Mwenje announced yesterday that he had defected from the Orange to Banana camp.
Mwenje said he abandoned the Orange camp after President Kibaki and
other politicians assured him that the contentious issues would be
dealt with after the proposed constitution was passed.
"I now give guidance to my people and urge them to vote Yes at the referendum on 21 November," Mwenje said at a Nairobi hotel
Limuru MP Kuria Kanyingi, who had organised the State House meeting where Mwenje met the President, accompanied him.
The MP said although he had a lot of respect for those campaigning
against the Wako Draft, he had to act according to his conviction.
He said he believed the President and some politicians, whom he
declined to name, would stick to their words and ensure the contentious
issues were amended.
As an MP, he said, he would ensure the issues were looked into so that Kenyans can have a constitution that is fair.
Mwenje's defection came a day after Narc activist Mary Wambui received his supporters hotel who had abandoned the No camp.
Wambui told the defectors at a Nairobi hotel that it was just a matter of time before Mwenje returned to the Banana camp.
Mwenje said in his capacity as the national treasurer of Democratic Party (DP), he had to join the Yes camp.
Some of the areas in the proposed constitution that Mwenje said he
was assured would be amended include treating all religions equally.

Original_Search_Title: Seven Reasons Why Nigerians Must Oppose Sit-Tight Leadership
Headline: Nigeria: Seven Reasons Why Nigerians Must Oppose Sit-Tight Leadership
Body_Text:
Lagos — As many times as it recurs, presidential aides are quick to
debunk it. Even President Olusegun Obasanjo himself has come out on
some occasions to lay to rest speculations that he was behind the
sinister campaign to tamper with the nation's constitution to enable
him go for a third term in office or at the least spend two extra
years. At a retreat held in Abuja for key government officials
recently, Obasanjo assured his audience that he would keep faith with
the constitution by quitting in 2007 when he would have spent the
maximum eight years allowed by law. Perhaps to leave no room for
misconceptions, he said he had "a little more than one-and- a-half
years to be in office."
Ordinarily, in societies governed by law, it would be taken for
granted that the President would go at the expiration of his tenure.
But this is Africa where leaders would rather die in office. Especially
since there are always political scavengers who would argue that
without the incumbent, the nation would crumble like a pack of cards.
The speculations that Obasanjo may be planning to extend his current
tenure of two terms of four years each started late last year and
gained momentum at the National Political Reforms Conference (NPRC). A
draft constitution which sought in an indirect manner to re-configure
the presidential tenure in such a manner that it would be possible for
the incumbent president to return in 2007 suddenly emerged from the
blues. And Aso Rock loyalists made the world to believe it was a
genuine document. That singular act fuelled suspicion that the
president, or at least people close to him, had a hidden agenda. But it
helped to rally opposition against any such agenda. With the exception
of a few presidential aides and some members of the ruling party,
Nigerians have stoutly opposed any such plot.
While the president was presenting the report of the NPRC to a joint
session of the National Assembly, it became obvious that the lawmakers
were not prepared to act on any script that would extend his tenure or
that of any other elected officer.
When the Speaker of the House of Representatives, Alhaji Bello Aminu
Masari, spoke on that occasion , he minced no words in saying that it
would be unconstitutional to extend the tenure of the administration.
The overwhelming acclaim that Masari's speech received was indicative
of the stand of Nigerians on the matter. But from what has been
happening in the political landscape of recent, nothing is certain
anymore were our Abuja legislators are concerned.
Denials of a third-term bid or tenure extension by presidential
aides and apologists notwithstanding, many Nigerians, including
politicians, academics, clerics, civil right advocates and especially
traditional rulers have begun consultations to arrive at a common
position to stop Obasanjo in his tracks.
But against the backdrop of subterranean moves to get an
extra-constitutional third-term by a legislative sleight-of-hand being
concocted by the Senator Ibrahim Mantu, below are seven of the several
reasons why the move will not be in the interest of Nigeria or for that
matter, that of the President.
The Seven Points
It will destroy the reform agenda as people will think that Obasanjo
deliberately initiated the reforms in order to beg for more time to
implement them. Also people who said the reform agenda and the
anti-corruption war of the president was selective, arbitrary and
tailored towards an expected end would have been justified.
Generals Ibrahim Babangida and Sani Abacha attempted it and failed
and there is nothing to suggest Obasanjo could pull it off either,
regardless of whatever lies opportunists may be telling him. While
Babangida was disgraced out of power, Abacha died in mysterious
circumstances.
Already, politicians, businessmen and women, academics, clerics,
civil right advocates and traditional rulers have begun consultations.
All these would culminate into a strong national resistance that will
lead to instability and violence in the polity. And this will create
unnecessary tension that could encourage extra-constitutional
agitation. It could even serve and an opportunity for people to canvass
for military rule which will ultimately not be in the interest of the
nation.
There is no-gain saying the fact that if the third term agenda
scales through, it will make Nigeria look like a banana republic and a
laughing stock within the international community. It will not only
threaten peace and security in the West African region but the whole of
Africa. Take for instance, when there was violent overthrow of
government in Sao Tome and Principe and Mauritania through
unconstitutional means recently, Nigeria and Obasanjo in his capacity
as the Chairman of the African Union (AU) stood up firmly in support of
the rule of law. This same Obasanjo and Nigeria cannot be seen as going
against they the preach
Lately, Nigeria has intensified her campaign for the United Nations'
Security Council seat. The only way she can attain that status is to
the show the world that we are nation whose leaders respect the rule of
law
Obasanjo will destroy all that he spent his life to build. There is
no time the history of Nigeria would be told correctly without the
mention of the achievements of Obasanjo's cumulative years in
government. And all these will crumble like a pack of cards if he
attempts to subvert the democratic process just because he wants to
perpetuate himself in office. The consequences will not be worth the
effort, at least not for him though it will pay the political
scavengers.
Finally, Nigerians may never recover from the problem that will
result from agitation that would definitely follow the third term bid.
It will further raise mutual ethnic suspicion, and the trust of
leadership in the country will vanish.

Original_Search_Title: Developing Story: Kenya Debates Draft Law
Headline: Kenya: Developing Story: Kenya Debates Draft Law
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The second edition of the 'Big Debate' on the Proposed Constitution is currently underway, with the Yes and No sides arguing the merits of the executive, legislature and devolution clauses.
At the beginning of the debate, each of the six panelists was given 30 seconds to explain why they supported or opposed the draft constitution.
Those representing the Banana team are Martha Karua, Moses Wetang'ula and Danson Mungatana whereas proponents of the No team are William Ruto, Mutula Kilonzo and Otieno Kajwang'.
Water Minister Martha Karua said: "I support the new constitution because it contains all the needs of ordinary Kenyans such as free primary education, provision of free health, water and land. However, what is not provided in this proposed constitution is the short-cut for people who are power hungry and who are seeking power."
Mr Wetang'ula: "I support the Proposed Constitution because it breaks from the old order and carries what in a nutshell I will call social security for Kenyans."
Mr Mungatana: "I support this draft constitution because it speaks for the ordinary citizen especially section 86. It states that Parliament must be ordered by the constitution to give squatters land and respect. Those living in slums must also be given dignity and respect where they live. There will also be a fund to assist ordinary citizens to be given land."
The Orange representatives, on the other hand, lined up their key reasons for wanting Kenyans to reject the proposed law.
Mr Ruto: "I oppose this Proposed Constitution because it fails in many aspects to capture the will, views and aspirations of Kenyans as contained in the views collected by the Constitution of Kenya Review Commission and represents the whims and interests of a clique."
Mr Kajwang: "This document fundamentally fails to address the aspirations and hopes of Kenyans which were collected and collated in the draft. It proposes a new constitution alright but doesn't give us a new constitutional dispensation."
Mr Kilonzo: "Fellow Kenyans, since this document was published in August, I have repeatedly said it is a bad document. Your time has come. My reasons for rejecting it will emerge in the course of the debate tonight but I wish to appeal to you to reject it peacefully for the rallies you are holding on Saturday and for the polling you will do on Monday, the November 21. Please do so peacefully in the name of God and for the sake of this country. It is not worth fighting for. What your judges said yesterday is that this is a document you can replace at will."
The debate is organised by the Media Owners Association and is still underway at Nairobi's Grand Regency hotel. It is being broadcast live on all television and radio stations across the country. The first edition of the debate took place on October 18.

Original_Search_Title: Eviction Threat Claim By MPs Being Probed, Declares DC
Headline: Kenya: Eviction Threat Claim By MPs Being Probed, Declares DC
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Government has launched investigations into claims that
residents of two villages in Kwanza and Saboti constituencies have been
threatened with eviction if they vote for the Banana during the Monday
referendum.
Trans Nzoia district commissioner Christopher Musumbu said a team of
criminal investigation officers led by inspector Alex Mutie had been
dispatched to the area to investigate the reports and carry out arrests.
Mr Musumbu confirmed that he had received complaints from assistant
minister Noah Wekesa (Kwanza) and his Saboti counterpart Davies
Nakitare, that their constituents in Kimondo, Endebess and Matumbei had
been threatened with eviction if they vote for the Wako Draft. The two
made the claim on Monday at a Banana rally in Kwanza.
"We have to finish our investigations and we shall not leave any
stone unturned," said the DC and added that the district was a
