cosmopolitan area where everybody had a right to exercise his/her
democratic rights.
Nzoia county council chairman Christopher Makokha also said that he
had received death threats for being in the forefront in supporting the
Banana camp.
Earlier, the DC had told journalists at his Kitale office that the
district security team had met with the Electoral Commission of Kenya
and had finalised their work ready for the referendum.
He said police would be stationed in all the 291 polling stations in
the district while another team of the General Service Unit (GSU)
personnel would patrol the area to ensure that the voting was peaceful.
"We have deployed GSU officers on the Kenyan/Uganda borders to ensure peace during the voting."
Others, he said, would patrol the West Pokot/Trans Nzoia borders.
Present at the security meeting were Kitale police boss Augustine
Kimanteria and district criminal investigations officer Julius Sunkuli.

Original_Search_Title: Security Intensified As Kibaki Arrives in Western
Headline: Kenya: Security Intensified As Kibaki Arrives in Western
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki arrived in Western Province last evening to help
drum up support for a Yes-vote. The Kenya Air Force plane carrying the
President, First Lady Lucy and Internal Security minister John Michuki
touched down at Kakamega airstrip at 4.17pm where he was received by
local leaders led by Vice-President Moody Awori.
Another military aircraft carrying attorney-general Amos Wako and assistant minister Joshua Toro had arrived 15 minutes earlier.
The President then went straight to the Kenya Airports Authority's
office where he signed a visitors' book before holding a five-minute
session with the VP, Mr Wako and the ministers present.
He then joined teams of traditional dancers at the airstrip where
local leaders, among them Cabinet ministers Musikari Kombo and Newton
Kulundu and assistant minister Soita Shitanda as well as MPs Sylvester
Wakoli and Bonny Khalwale. A local band played songs promoting the
Yes-vote.
The President's motorcade left the airstrip and snaked through
Kakamega Town's streets where people - including school children in
uniforms - had lined up to welcome him on his way to the Kakamega State
Lodge. They sang in praise of the Banana.
The President was expected to start receiving various delegations from the province last evening.
Security was tight throughout the route and at the State lodge where cars were subjected to thorough checks.
Earlier at the Kakamega Golf Hotel, youths supporting the Banana
campaign, who were issued with Yes T-shirts to go to the airstrip to
welcome the President, openly argued on whether or not they should ride
their bicycles in a procession. Security patrols were intensified in
the town's streets before President Kibaki's arrival.
On each street, there were not fewer than two plainclothes police
officers. The situation, however, was calm throughout and residents
went about their normal business uninterrupted.
A secretariat set up by the Banana team at Golf Hotel was bustling
with activity as officials fine-tuned preparations for the visit.
Security checks were also intensified on all roads leading to and from the town but no incidents were reported.
On Sudi Road, which joins the main Kenyatta Avenue, youths donning
Orange T-shirts converged near Somken Petrol Station and said they
wanted to demonstrate in support for their side.
The youths appeared on the streets around mid-day but remained peaceful.
Taxi operators in Kakamega snubbed a request to join residents of
the town in welcoming the President during his two-day visit to the
province. They said they did not want to be involved.
Meanwhile, Kakamega was in a carnival mood as groups of Banana
supporters arrived to prepare for President Kibaki's two-day visit.

Original_Search_Title: Why Judges Back Vote On Proposed Constitution
Headline: Kenya: Why Judges Back Vote
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The referendum on the proposed Constitution will take place next Monday as planned.
Three judges yesterday gave the poll and the entire review a clean bill of health.
Courts, they said, had no business interfering with the review and
stopping the poll would amount to challenging the people's right to
make a Constitution.
Judges Joseph Nyamu, Roselyne Wendoh and Mathew Anyara Emukule,
unanimously threw out an application questioning the validity of the
referendum and seeking to have it declared unconstitutional.
"We find and declare that the process is not flawed. In our view the
real judges of the process are the people of Kenya in view of their
past involvement with the process," the judges proclaimed.
The review and next week's referendum were political processes and
the court's intervention would be meddling in politics, the judges
declared in their four hour ruling that ran to 176 pages.
There to hear it read out in a packed Court No 1 at the High Court
in Nairobi were the leading lights of both the Banana Yes-vote and
Orange No-vote camps.
Banana bigwigs included Justice minister Kiraitu Murungi and MP Paul
Muite, while Orange leaders included Raila Odinga, Uhuru Kenyatta,
Najib Balalala, Mutula Kilonzo, William Ruto and Julia Ojiambo.
The judges said the referendum gave people a sense of ownership,
identity and attachment to the outcome. "The overwhelming public
interest should not be ignored but must be upheld," they declared.
They also defended the roles of Parliament in passing the Consensus
Act on which the review hinged and the Attorney General in drafting the
proposals as being perfectly in order and within their mandate.
Seeking to halt the review wand have the poll referendum outlawed were nine members of the pressure group the Yellow Movement.
They had accused Parliament of usurping the people's mandate to make
a Constituon by illegally passing the Consensus Act and through it
delegating powers to "various agents".
Their application to the Constitutional Court was described in court
by their lawyer John Amendi as the most important since the trial of
Dedan Kimathi.
But the judges found that as long as Parliament, the Executive and
the AG did not monopolise the reform, their role in helping to process
the review was within the law.
Also, challenging their roles was time-barred, they ruled, because
the process had been taking place since 1997 and the application had
been overtaken by events.
The Electoral Commission was defended against accusations by the
Yellow Movement that it was overstepping its constitutional mandate by
organising the referendum, because all it was empowered to do was to
conduct Presidential and Parliamentary polls.
The judges said:"The orders and declaration sought against the ECK
have been disallowed on the ground that the existing Constitution and
the law sufficiently empower it to undertake the intended the mandate
or function."
The judges also refused to declare illegal the plans to pass and
make known the proposed Constitution,on the the ground that the legal
structure for the transition gaves no room for any gap or break.
The proposed Constitution would come into force and be made known to
the public after any possible challenge to the referendum result had
been disposed of, they said.
"Under the relevant provisions, it is the proposed new Constitution
which shall automatically repeal the existing Constitution on the
effective date," they said.
And in a key passage of the judgment, they announced:
"We hold, find and declare that in the hierarchy of authority the
people rank first because it is the people who give birth to
constitutions. We reject the invitation to interfere with what will be
put to the people in referendum."
And that power cannot be stopped, inhibited or muzzled by any of the
organs of modern government including any existing Constitution,
especially where the review took place done under an existing
Constitution which is fixed and does not provide for its death, they
said.
The court also noted that no constitutional proposals could claim
purity or legitimacy as had been claimed by the 13 applicants from the
Yellow Movement because the nature of constitution making was to
receive proposals from at least major stakeholders or political
interests.
Local politicians had ample time to offer proposals and it was not
the function of the court to approve or disapprove any of them, except
where there was interference with the constituent power.
Constitution writing itself was yet to happen because the people's referendum had not taken place, they said.
The judges said the court lacked the mandate to stop the referendum
and the constituent power of the people could not be challenged.
"....This court would like to declare in no uncertain terms that the
exercise of the constituent power cannot be stopped, injuncted or
interfered with except under any of the grounds set out in Act and the
regulations facilitating it," the judges said.
The constituent power, they said, was primary and not subject to any external power.
They held that power could be exercised either by a duly elected and
representative constituent assembly or directly through a referendum.
And it is for that reason that the Kenyan process cannot be legally faulted.
During the hearing it was argued the court had no jurisdiction to entertain the case.
In their ruling the judges did not agree they lacked jurisdiction
but emphasised that the court respected the doctrine of the separation
of powers and would not therefore interfere with the decisions of
Parliament or its standing orders.
The judges held that it was the duty of the court to exercise its original jurisdiction in full which has not been limited.
"We find that this court should not restrict itself or limit its
jurisdiction because in many matters parties look to the court as the
final frontiers in the defence of justice, liberty and equality," they
said.
The court however could not undo an Act of Parliament because it
contained and reflected the decision of Parliament which has in turn
had the power to legislate.
The process leading up to the publication of the new Constitution
could not be challenged in court, firstly due to the political nature
of the process and secondly because the succession of events could not
now be reversed.
And whether the proposed Constitution was valid or not was a
separate question because it was speculative and at this stage there
was no controversy that could be challenged in court before the courts.
President Kibaki's power to make known a new Constitution, the
judges said, was not subject to an existing Constitution but was
contained in the Consensus Act.
It was significant, the judges said, that President Kibaki was
elected by the people and in announcing a new Constitution he would be
acting as people's agent, after they had enacted it through the
referendum.
The judges added: "It is utter rubbish to suggest that there shall
be two parallel Constitutions at any time when the new one is enacted
by the people."
In law, the judges said, it is the people who shall enact the new
Constitution at the referendum, if a majority vote is attained.
They declared the process of bringing in a new Constitution was as follows:
-The enactment takes place by the people if a simple majority vote is attained at the referendum;
-Article 289 of the proposed Constitution provides that it shall
come into force upon its public declaration by President Kibaki.
- The repeal of the existing Constitution.
While dismissing the case, the judges blamed the Constitution of
Kenya Review Commission and the state law arm for failing to give
proper legal guidance to the reform process.
"Considerable anxiety, confusion and division was created by those
who had the responsibility of giving the government legal services from
1997, including the CKRC with nearly a third composition of lawyers who
regrettably failed to advise in good time or at all concerning the
people's involvement in the exercise of constituent power," they said.
On the challenge of the role of Parliament, the judges said they
regarded the consultation over the draft proposals was one structured
process and Parliament as any other facilitator had the right to
originate and debate constitutional proposals.
They said: "The Bomas Draft was not an enacted law, they were proposals."
The judgment, which started at 10.35am and ended four hours later
attracted a huge crowd that thronged the narrow and winding corridors
of Nairobi Central Law Courts.
Half an hour before the judges arrived, there was no standing room
left in the Constitution Court with all seats, including those on the
balcony already taken.
Judiciary public relations officer Dola Indidis had a difficult time
organising the crowd and directing them to the designated zones within
the court.
He had to warn the public of the risk of been cited for contempt for
misconduct, including making or receiving phone calls while the court
was in session.

Original_Search_Title: Lobby Group's Birth And Its Turbulent Past
Headline: Kenya: Lobby Group's Birth And Its Turbulent Past
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The fate of the Yellow Movement's bid to stop the poll was nearly sealed three weeks ago when key litigants and their lawyer withdrew from the case.
Four lead plaintiffs, including Mr Patrick Ouma Onyango and lawyer Pheroze Nowrojee, pulled out, nearly bringing it to a halt.
The group, which included Mr Boaz Waruku, Ms Rebecca Kitana and Mr Henry Maina, also took back the main documents.
The documents - the originating and the chamber summonses - sought to stop the referendum and civic education from being conducted by the Constitution of Kenya Review Commission.
The move then left the High Court to deal with only side issues concerning the review that has divided the country into two political camps.
Mr Onyango's withdrawal surprised lawyer John Amendi, for the disputants, who asked for more time.
But it was Ford Asili that applied immediately through secretary-general Njeru Kathangu to be enjoined as an interested party.
The former Runyenjes MP also applied to be enjoined in person, saying that he had greatly contributed to the struggle for a new constitution.
"I stood in court seeking to be enjoined in the case and pledged to draw new documents," he said yesterday. "I was given 24 hours to present them." The hearing bench - Mr Justice Joseph Nyamu, Mr Justice Mathew Emukule and Lady Justice Roselyne Wendoh - later rejected the application.
"This is a matter of serious public interest, and we will not create any gaps in the proceedings unless for very compelling reasons," judge Nyamu said.
The judges instead ordered the hearing to proceed on a daily basis to its conclusion to beat the November 21 deadline.
The original 13 plaintiffs were Mr Onyango, Bishop Stephen Karemu, Mr Michael Gitahi Ngunyi, Mr Boaz Waruku, Ms Rebecca Kitana, Ms Catherine Wairimu, Mr Samson Owimba, Mr R. Achoki, Ms Monica Amolo, Mr Paul Angwenyi, Mr John Oduor, Mr Henry Maina and Mr James Kabutu.
The movement, a lobby group pushing for the enactment of the Bomas Draft, was the embodiment of differences between Parliament and other parties in the review, especially after the national delegates conference at Bomas of Kenya, Nairobi.
The bone of contention between the lobbies and Parliament after the conference was the MPs' taking over of the review, blocking other parties.
"It was a union of major civil society organisations to fight the parliamentary takeover of the review process after the Bomas conference," says National Convention Executive Council (NCEC) boss Cyprian Nyamwamu.
Mr Kathangu agrees and argues that the Bomas Draft - a product "a wide spectrum of Kenyans"- should not have been changed by MPs. "The civil society held a meeting and decided to ensure the Bomas Draft went through unadulterated," he says.
The movement was formed in November, last year, to mobilise the public against Parliament's move to pass the Consensus Act, which gave MPs the authority to amend the draft.
It brought together lobbies which were represented at the Bomas talks, among them the Chemi Chemi ya Ukweli (fountain of truth), 4Cs and NCEC.
The groups met at Mbagathi, Nairobi, last year, and formed the movement to lobby and use peaceful demonstrations to stop Parliament from usurping the role of Kenyans in making the new constitution. They chose the name to show that they were giving the Government a "yellow card" (warning) on the review, and that should it ignore their voice, they would flash the red one.
Most members, notably Mr Onyango and Mr Amendi, were delegates at the Bomas conference.
The movement may also be considered to be a second effort by former NGO Bomas delegates to agitate for the adoption of the draft after the once-vibrant Katiba Watch lobby seemed to waver.
Katiba Watch, which brought together former delegates Martin Shikuku, Leslie Mwachiro and Suba Churchill and others, gradually edged out of the limelight after its rallies countrywide became chaotic.
Some of its leaders pulled out and are now active members of the Orange and Banana campaign teams.
"Our aim was to fight for people's participation in the review-making process till the end," recalls Mr Nyamwamu.
To achieve their goals, they demanded a representative forum to tackle contentious issues in the Bomas Draft and the creation of another in which all players would negotiate a draft agreeable to all.
"But the Government was so determined to go ahead with the Kiraitu (Justice and Constitutional Affairs minister Murungi)-driven process without other stakeholders," he says.
To their chagrin, Parliament not only passed the Consensus Act, but also amended the Bomas Draft in Kilifi and presented it to attorney-general Amos Wako for publication.
Both occasions were characterised by the movement's demonstrations at which some members were arrested.
The suit may have been thrown out, but the Yellow Movement is not giving up. Says Mr Nyamwamu: "Our options are not limited for we are fighting for national interests. There is a lot (more) to come."

Original_Search_Title: Reject Banana, 10 MPs Tell Ogiek
Headline: Kenya: Reject Banana, 10 MPs Tell Ogiek
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Ten MPs have asked the Ogiek to vote against the proposed Constitution
on Monday.
About 12,000 members of the community were recently issued with land
title deeds by President Kibaki. But the legislators, who included
Immigration minister Linah Jebii Kilimo, asked them to reject the proposed
Constitution saying the issuance of the title deeds was not "a favour but a
right".
The MPs were led by Mrs Kilimo, Kanu secretary-general William Ruto and
Mbita MP Otieno Kajwang'.
"It is a constitutional right for one to be issued with a title deed
once you acquire land and hence you should not waver during the November 21
referendum and vote Yes," said Mrs Kilimo.
The No team held their rally at the Olenguruone DO's camp - where
President Kibaki issued the title deeds last month. They said the proposed
Constitution was flawed and aimed at dividing Kenyans.
MPs Noah arap Too, Paul Sang, Charles Keter, Franklin Bett, Musa Sirma,
Moses Cheboi and Nick Salat said should the Banana camp win, "one tribe
stands to benefit". They said the move to revisit past human rights
violations was targeted at victimising a certain community.
According to them, the Akiwumi report on land clashes will be revived to
allow the Government to pursue those who were perceived to have committed
human right abuses such as land clashes that rocked parts of Rift Valley and
Likoni at the Coast.
Mr Kajwang' said Kenyans should expect a new political dispensation
after the No camp wins "because we shall go back and draft a new
constitution that is popular with all Kenyans using the Bomas draft".
He criticised remarks he attributed to Justice and Constitutional
Affairs minister Kiraitu Murungi that Central Province should protect
President Kibaki's Government. He said the remarks amounted to whipping-up
tribal sympathy.
"We gave President Kibaki a five-year contract to rule, and if Mr
Kiraitu now feels that a certain community should rise up and defend his
government by voting Yes during the referendum, then other communities
should rise up too and vote No if the proposed draft is unpopular with
them," said Mr Kajwang'.

Original_Search_Title: When the Court Proved That It is Not Toothless
Headline: Kenya: When the Court Proved That It is Not Toothless
Body_Text:
Nairobi — At the end of the four hours, a warning by Judiciary Spokesman
Dola Indidis that had been so thoroughly ignored earlier, rung so true.
'This court is not toothless this is not a market place. It is not a place for politics," he said.
The spokesman was apparently annoyed that Justice minister Kiraitu
Murungi was enthusiastically booed by a section of the audience when he
arrived for the ruling.
Rarely has a decrepit, crammed room controlled a country's political pulse like yesterday.
No sooner had the three bewigged and bespectacled judges with sagely
airs complimented by tonnes of references, read their verdict than
there was little doubt that the court had the teeth and the willingness
to bite.
Having taken the trouble to define Wanjiku whom they said should
have essentially remained "on or near the driving seat" of the rocky
review path, the judges quickly settled down to demolishing pleas that
the referendum should be stopped.
Doing so, would be unfair because "in the hierarchy of power, the people come first".
The court would not, they said "injunct, stop or restrain" the
referendum because doing so would be "unfair, unjust and prejudicial"
to the review process that is set to climax with Monday's poll.
Quoting extensively from precedents and while comparing the Kenyan
protracted review process specifically to India, South Africa, Ireland
and Australia, the judges agreed that the Yellow Movement case had some
merit, but that was nevertheless insufficient to put the brakes on the
referendum.
In some cases, the justices felt the plaintiff's pleadings were
"retrogressive and utopian" and they especially retained harsh words
for the claim that allowing for a referendum could lead to "two
parallel constitutions" describing it as "utter rubbish."
"Sound Judicial law must be founded on the bedrock of common sense
legal theory must give way to practical policy," they concluded.
And with such pronouncements, Justice Joseph Nyamu, Roselyn Wendoh
and Anyara Emukule effectively saved from potential waste the energies
and millions of shillings so far committed to referendum wars.
It was a judicious ruling delivered in that monotone common with
judges and that appeared to have been made deliberately lengthy and
convoluted to tire the unusual guests who had jammed the Constitution
and Judicial Review Court. Hours to the ruling, crowds had gathered in
and around the court significantly upping the tensions around the case.
Activists, politicians, lawyers, journalists and idlers jostled for
limited space even as Indidis shouted another of his ignored warnings
for those standing to leave the chamber. Besides Kiraitu who sat next
to one of his sworn political adversary, Kasarani MP William Omondi,
two more ministers, MPs and other dignitaries were also present.
Roads minister Raila Odinga, his Heritage counterpart Najib Balala,
Leader of Official Opposition Uhuru Kenyatta, Tourism assistant
minister George Khaniri and MPs Paul Muite, William Ruto, Mutula
Kilonzo and Julia Ojiambo sat through the judgment.
Save for Kiraitu and Muite, the rest arrived late to a noisy
commotion in an apparent borrowing from Robert Greene's idea of how to
maximize one's sense of importance. Raila and Uhuru who both wore
flashy shirts arrived in the company of Balala 20 minutes into the
judgment with Ruto arriving after another 40 minutes.
But in a testimony to the hypocrisy of political leaders, the Banana
and Orange luminaries occasionally exchanged hearty banter and shook
hands with the camaraderie of true friends after the ruling even as
their respective supporters traded insults and threats around the High
Court gate.
The potential implications of the case had clearly outrun the
Chamber space and no one was in a mood to listen to Indidis poignant
reminders that the court had the power to deal with those who "held it
in contempt" or those who chose to display their Yes or No loyalties in
court.

Original_Search_Title: Banana Team Describes Judgment As Excellent
Headline: Kenya: Banana Team Describes Judgment As Excellent
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Banana (Yes) leaders welcomed the judgment, saying it had conferred
legality on the referendum.
Kenyans, they said, could now vote for or against the new Constitution
on Monday knowing full well that the process was within the law.
Speaking outside the High Court immediately after the historic judgment,
Justice minister Kiraitu Murungi described the decision as "excellent, well
researched and visionary".
He said: "This is the most important judgment in Kenya's history since
that on freedom fighter Dedan Kimathi. It has effectively cleared all the
cobwebs and doubts over the entire process."
It was now upon Kenyans, he said, to either vote for or against the
constitution without any hurdle.
Mr Murungi said the case, brought by the Yellow Movement, had been an
Orange (No) attempt to sabotage the referendum because they feared facing
the judgment of Kenya's masses.
"All the noise, propaganda and hullabaloo you see and hear from the
Orange side are symptoms of internal fear," said Mr Murungi.
He said he had no doubt that, come Monday, Kenyans would overwhelmingly
vote for the proposed Constitution.
Mr Murungi said November 21 would be history repeating itself since
those rooting for the constitution had defeated their opponents both in
Parliament and in the corridors of justice. Bananas would win in "the
people's court", he said.
Asked whether he was surprised by the ruling, Mr Murungi answered that,
in the courts, anything could happen.
"Whenever you come to the courts, you should expect
anything. Personally, I was not sure which way the case would go."
For his part, Kabete MP Paul Muite described the ruling as "entirely
satisfactory" since the three judges had touched on practically all the
technical issues on the review process and the referendum.
These included "the fact that it was not necessary" to review Section 47
of the constitution to promulgate a new one.
The judges, he said, had rightly ruled that only Kenyans, through either
a constituent assembly or a referendum, could change the Constitution.
He said: "Personally, I feel vindicated since this is the position I
have all along held since Bomas. But my views were dismissed by those who
are now opposed to the referendum."
Mr Muite, who chairs Parliament's committee on the Administration of
Justice and Legal Affairs, said that, even in Bomas, he had argued that the
delegates discussing the process had no mandate to do so since they were not
elected through universal suffrage.
Mr Muite said: "Two thirds of the delegates at Bomas had been appointed,
and not elected. There was no way they could have been representing the
views of Kenya."
Nor could the Bomas conference be described as a constituent
assembly.
Mr Muite also dismissed as "propaganda" earlier sentiments by Eldoret
North MP William Ruto that the Banana team was trying to use the court
process to stop the referendum.
He said the Yellow Movement, which had moved to court to have the
referendum stopped, had always been part of the Orange Movement.

Original_Search_Title: Yes, No Camps Praise Historic Ruling On Draft
Headline: Kenya: Yes, No Camps Praise Historic Ruling On Draft
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Politicians from both the Banana and Orange camps were yesterday
united in their praise of the ruling by the three-judge-bench, which
removed the final hurdle on the road to the Monday plebiscite.
However, an element of suspicion, doubt and even a dose of
apportioning blame were evident in the reactions, underlining how
deeply it has polarised Kenyans and poisoned the political atmosphere.
"That is good for us, it is just as we expected," Planning minister Prof Anyang' Nyong'o said.
Nyong'o said the decision proved that the courts were independent.
Nominated MP Mutula Kilonzo termed the ruling a disaster.
Mutula said the position taken by the three judges was "legal hogwash".
Kanu secretary General William Ruto said the saving grace of the
judgment was that it was up to Kenyans to decide whether to vote Yes or
No during the referendum. He expressed confidence that Wako Draft would
be rejected and hoped that a constituent Assembly will come up with an
accepted document.
Ntonyiri MP Maoka Maore said the exercise had gone too far to be
stopped and accused the Government of being behind the Yellow
Movement-sponsored case.
Ikolomani MP Bonny Khalwale said it was good because the Orange team
would have cried wolf that the Government had interfered with the
referendum through the courts.
"What we wanted has happened we are longing to beat banana hands down and the time is Monday." Maore said.
Former Vice President Musalia Mudavadi said the Government had
committed so much resources to the process and it would have been a
pity if the courts stopped it.
"Postponing the referendum would have been a recipe for chaos and we are happy that the process is on course," Mudavadi said.
"It has been a gruelling exercise.
"We want to get over it and embark on nation building. Let us go to the vote and put the matter to rest," George Khaniri said
Eldama Ravine MP Musa Sirma said the ruling was an indicator that the Judiciary had come of age.
"The three judges read the mood of Kenyans and allowed them to forge
ahead. It was a fair ruling," said Sirma, who is in the Orange camp.
Assistant minister Alicen Chelaite of the Banana camp, said she was
happy with the ruling, because the referendum will go down to the wire.
The Rongai MP said Kenyans have been accorded the right to make the decision on the future of the nation.
She said the ruling was in favour of the people of Kenya and not the Yes or No side of the campaigns.
Naivasha MP Jane Kihara said she was glad with the outcome adding
that it would have been an anticlimax had the court halted the process.
The legislator said Kenyans want to get done with the constitutional
process and embark on nation building.
Kuresoi MP Moses Cheboi said it would have been unwise to stop the
referendum at such a late stage and said the Orange side had wrapped up
its campaign. The Kanu legislator said it was ill advised for the
Yellow Movement to seek to stop such an historic process.
Molo MP Macharia Mukiri of Banana, while praising the ruling, said
the Yellow Movement comprised people who never wanted Kenyans to have a
new constitution.
Malindi MP Lucas Maitha for Orange said the referendum should go on
to enable Kenyans give their verdict at the plebiscite. He said Kenyans
had worked very hard and it would have been disastrous to stop the
referendum.

Original_Search_Title: Obscure Art At Its Best At Design Agenda
Headline: Uganda: Obscure Art At Its Best At Design Agenda
Body_Text:
For an art exhibition branded 'Love', you would be forgiven to expect that most of the pieces on display depict contemptible images of sinful love. But regardless of your expectations, you would still be pleasantly surprised at what you see.
At Design Agenda Gallery, where an exhibition featuring five artists was opened last Friday, it is graceful. Paul Ndema, George William Semaganda, Stephen Mubiita, John Mary Mukiza, and Kyomukama Pius will constitute the gallery's attraction for one month.
Speaking as chief Guest, socialite Dr Ian Clarke, while mincing words on issues of art, still managed to show that he can appreciate art, at least to a certain minimum. Earlier, while viewing the paintings, Clarke had fallen in love with a watercolour painting by Semaganda, titled Equal Rights. Equal Rights is a well-imagined painting that captures the sight of a bellied man and a tall, slender woman who both want to pass through a narrow corridor at exactly the same time.
Semaganda depicts the man as heading towards the narrower part of the corridor and the woman to the wider, bringing forth the suggestion that in today's volatile world, the woman is assuming more power than ever before. Equal Rights may have gone for only Shs200,000 but its story is worth so much more.
Ndema's abstract paintings are a beauty to view, but only if you can unlock them. His Joseph is an image of the biblical son of Jacob's coat, the beautiful hood that placed him in an enviable position before his brothers.
At the exhibition, Ndema makes blues and greens typical of his palette. The problem is that though his work is the type that would end up in European museums, it lacks cultural relevance.
Mubiita paints images that can electrify viewers. He says he prefers to paint semi-abstract forms because then, viewers can have a fair chance to interpret them. And his Jilted Lover validates him on that.
Kyomugisha does a decent job with banana mosaics.
Mukiza's Humility, which features an image of a rosary falling from the heavens tells the lesson that there is need be down to earth. His combination of dull hues is magical. With the exception of the experienced Mubiita, the rest are promising young artists eager to impress.

Original_Search_Title: Court Throws Out Case Against Referendum
Headline: Kenya: Court Throws Out Case Against Referendum
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The High Court has thrown out an application by the Yellow
Movement lobby group that would have seen to the calling off of
Monday's referendum on the Proposed Constitution.
A bench of three judges said courts have no power to call off the
referendum. They said not even parliament could stop the referendum
because it was an exercise of the people's constituent power.
Thousands thronged the law courts including leading politicians from the Banana and Orange camps.
Constitutional Court judges Joseph Nyamu, Roselyne Wendoh and Mathew
Anyara Emukule reached a unanimous descision to throw out the
application by 13 activists of the Yellow Movement.
The activists had sought to have the process leading to the referendum declared illegal.
They argued that the proposed Constitution is an illegal document
because it is a product of a flawed process. They had also claimed that
it was made in total disregard of the principles of democracy and
sovereignty of the people.
The attorney-general, the Constitution of Kenya Review Commission
and the Electoral Commission of Kenya are named as respondents.
Lawyers for both parties concluded their final submissions last Wednesday.

Original_Search_Title: Yes Or No, We Can Still Smile
Headline: Kenya: Yes Or No, We Can Still Smile
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kenya has one week to go before its first ever referendum. Its first
ever. One in which all Kenyans of voting age are to actively contribute to
determining how we are to be governed. All Kenyans of voting age - not only
the 'representatives' of ethnic and political constituencies who negotiated
our independence constitution. Nor only our elected parliamentarians.
This is meant to be an achievement, a sign of political progress-
despite the racist and implicitly sexist violence that has marked the
campaigns. For the referendum will mark another slight shift, however
unintentional, away from representative democracy to participatory
democracy.
Another achievement: The referendum sparked off a public debate on the
use of public resources in partisan political campaigning. The efforts of
the Kenya National Human Rights Commission, the Kenya Human Rights
Commission and others to bring this to our attention should be lauded.
The arguments invoked in self-defence, particularly by the Banana side,
have been so tortuous as to be absurd. We heard, for example, from no less
than a Cabinet minister that the referendum was a government project that
needed to be supported with government resources. We heard from another
Cabinet minister that his official car was deemed to be on official duty as
long as he was in it.
We laughed incredulously and shook our heads. They continued doing their
thing. But the point was made, and we took note.
ACHIEVEMENT NUMBER three: What can only be termed electoral bribery was
scaled up from crass handouts of money to individuals to a relatively more
sophisticated form of communal appeasement. Indeed, one commentator noted
that if this sudden responsiveness to what are, at heart, valid
developmental and governance concerns, was due to the referendum, then the
more referendums the better.
It's a brilliant idea. Except for this not entirely trivial caveat. All
developmental and governance demands must be balanced against one another in
the interests of development and governance overall. They must also be based
on farsighted, rational planning. Without that balance, without that
planning, "participation" is not only empty populism, it leads to bigger
development and governance problems down the line.
Yes, the dishing out of development and governance trinkets has been
part of our coercive political culture in the past. I am just saying that we
did not expect it of this particular regime, and that too on such a
phenomenal scale. The new culture already seems to have replaced, in the
public eye at least, the more individualistic, monetised bribery of previous
electoral campaigns.
Achievement number four: The debate on the Electoral Commission of Kenya
has also changed. To go by the increasingly fervent mutterings of its chair,
our concern now should be less with the ECK's independence than with its
power to enforce its mandate..
This is small comfort for most of us, disappointed as we are at the way
things have turned out since the last election. These achievements are only
relative, and the notion of "incremental" political progress is a dodgy one
at best. But... I think we have evolved.
AND I suppose we can console ourselves with the reminder that it took
millennia for monkeys to evolve into (wo)men. Yet look at us today: monkeys
are for the national game parks, while (wo)men are for holding
referendums. So smile.
L. Muthoni Wanyeki is the Executive Director of the African Women's
Development and Communication Network (FEMNET)

Original_Search_Title: Kivuitu Blames the Police Over Rally Violence
Headline: Kenya: Kivuitu Blames the Police Over Rally Violence
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Electoral Commission yesterday blamed the police for the violence
that left four people dead in Mombasa last Friday.
The law does not give police powers to licence meetings and the reasons
for denying one such a permit are spelt out in law, said commission chairman
Samuel Kivuitu.
Instead, police are required to accept or decline with reasons any
notice to hold referendum rallies, said Mr Kivuitu.
The law guiding political rallies says one can only be denied a permit
if another group has a similar meeting at the same venue.
Denial could also be on security grounds - that those at the rally will
be in danger.
Mr Kivuitu, who was commenting on the Likoni violence, said police could
only bar a political campaign on grounds provided under law.
The rioting broke out at Caltex grounds, Likoni, after area police chief
Wilfred Mbithi snatched a microphone from Heritage minister Najib Balala
when the rally was already under way, and announced the meeting had not been
licensed.
He said the rally was cancelled and ordered the crowd to disperse.
Said the ECK boss: "There is no law that empowers the police to issue
licences for public political campaign rallies.
"The ground he (Mr Mbithi) based his reason for denying the holding of
the rally is alien to law."
But Mr Kivuitu also criticised Orange leaders at the rally for failing
to calm their supporters. The commission said although it did not have full
information on what Orange leaders told the crowd, it was clear that they
did not tell it to stop the violence.
At the rally were Cabinet ministers Raila Odinga and Mr Balala and MPs
Suleiman Kajembe and Billow Kerrow.
"Their failure was in breach of the Electoral Code of Conduct, which
they subscribe to.
"They thus contributed to the mayhem which followed," Mr Kivuitu
said.
The commission had information from a returning officer that although
Orange leaders in the area had notified the police of the meeting, the area
police chief rejected their request, said Mr Kivuitu.
The returning officer had earlier held a meeting with agents of both
Banana and Orange teams.
The commission boss said the police should have then used other channels
such as the radio and public address systems to warn Orange supporters from
attending the rally.
Mr Kivuitu argued that the officer who went to stop the rally could
"validly claim that he did not foresee that this action would lead to
violence".
Mr Kivuitu also accused the Banana supporters of attempting to stop
Orange supporters from attending the rally. He blamed both camps for
resorting to violence.
The commission yesterday wrote to agents of both camps, asking them to
give information on the violence.

Original_Search_Title: Six MPs Quizzed Over Remarks
Headline: Kenya: Six MPs Quizzed Over Remarks
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Six MPs, including an assistant minister, yesterday recorded statements
with the police over provocative and inciting statements they are alleged to
have made during referendum campaign rallies.
Assistant minister Njeru Githae and Mwea MP Alfred Nderitu recorded
their statements at the Murang'a police station, while four other MPs from
Kericho and Bureti districts reported to the CID offices at Kericho.
The four were Mr Noah Arap Too (Ainamoi) Charles Keter (Belgut), Paul
Sang (Buret) and Sammy Koech (Konoin). They recorded their statements before
the Kericho CID boss, Mr Abdi Salat.
They were alleged to have said that some of the Orange campaign leaders
would be assassinated. The statements were reportedly made during a rally
held at Chepseon in Kericho district last Tuesday and followed raids at the
homes of assistant minister Peter Odoyo and Cabinet minister Najib
Balala.
The leaders claimed that the burglary attempts could have been
politically motivated and warned that they would retaliate if any of the
team's luminaries were targeted.
Mr Githae and Mr Nderitu had been summoned by the police over utterances
allegedly made at a rally at Kahuro trading centre, Kiharu constituency, in
Muranga last Thursday.
Mr Nderitu was reported to have told the Banana campaign rally that some
800 youths were being trained to cause chaos in the country during the
referendum period. He had asked the government to investigate this.
Mr Githae, who also addressed the meeting, is said to have supported Mr
Nderitu's claims. After recording the statement yesterday, Mr Githae
demanded that the police also record statements from Cabinet ministers Raila
Odinga, Kalonzo Musyoka and Kanu Nominated MP Mutula Kilonzo.
Mr Githae said: "As a ministry of Justice and Constitutional Affairs
official, I found it necessary to show that people should heed to police
instructions irrespective of their status." He said the police should not be
seen to take sides in the matter and urged them to summon the two Cabinet
Ministers and a Nominated MP. "Raila, Kalonzo and Kilonzo have in the past
made utterances bordering on subversion of the Government. They, too, should
also be made to record statements," Mr Githae said.
He arrived at Murang'a Police Station at 12 noon and went straight to
see the district criminal investigations officer, Mr Harrison Meme. He
stayed in that office until at 1.10 pm.
"I found it necessary to clarify that there was no way I could issue a
statement amounting to a breach of the law. All that I have told the police
is that investigations should be carried out on the issues, which were
raised by the MP (Mr Alfred Nderitu) and they should not be taken lightly,"
he told reporters.
But Mr Nderitu who spent four hours at the police station declined to
talk to journalists. He was accompanied by his Kiharu counterpart Mr Kembi
Gitura who is also a lawyer.
In Kericho, the four MPs demanded that the police provide evidence about
their alleged inflammatory utterances.
Talking to journalists after meeting the police, Mr Salat said the
summon showed that the government was panicking, adding it was intended to
intimidate them.
"They are already sensing defeat and that is why they are intimidating
us," Mr Salat said. He accused Justice minister Kiraitu Murungi of
trivialising the constitution campaign by implying that the document was
only for the benefit of one community.
The MPs travelled to the CID offices in a convoy with one of the
vehicles mounted with a public address system which blared messages in
support of the Orange campaign. They attracted a crowd which gathered at the
Uhuru Gardens, which they addressed for about half an hour before they were
dispersed by police
Addressing the impromptu gathering, Mr Keter alleged that some of their
leaders and activists were targeted for arrest next Friday to ensure they
did not vote on Monday. Mr Too, Mr Keter and Mr Sang arrived at the CIDs
offices at at 9.30 am and left after half hour, while Mr Koech arrived at
round 11.15 am.
Other MPs who joined the group were Moses Cheboi (Kuresoi) Anthony
Kimetto (Sotik) Nick Salat (Bomet) and Sammy Weya (Alego Usonga).
Additional reporting by Solo Kiragu

Original_Search_Title: You Will Get Title Deeds, Two Yes Ministers Tell Squatters
Headline: Kenya: You Will Get Title Deeds, Two Yes Ministers Tell Squatters
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Title deeds will be issued to land owners in Kwanza constituency once
the proposed Constitution becomes law, two Cabinet ministers in the Banana
camp promised yesterday.
Mr Musikari Kombo (Local Government) and Dr Mukhisa Kituyi (Trade and
Industry), also said that hundreds of squatters living in the constituency
would soon be considered for allocation of land.
The two said that most people in Kwanza lived in market centres and
needed land to cultivate if their economic status was to improve.
Speaking when they toured Kolongolo, Kapkoi, Namanjalala and Kenyatta
stadium where they arrived in a helicopter to drum up support for the
Yes-vote, the group said that it was determined to win the November 21
referendum.
Their host, Dr Noah Wekesa and Saboti MP Davies Nakitare, complained
that some people in Kwanza, especially in Endebess Division, had been
threatened with eviction if they voted for Banana.
But Mr Kombo said that President Kibaki's Government was alert and asked
Trans Nzoia district commissioner Christopher Musumbu to investigate leaders
who issued inflammatory statements.
"I want these people arrested as we shall not allow anybody to threaten
people at any particular time," said the Ford Kenya national chairman.
Dr Wekesa had named Matumbei and Kimondo areas as places where the
Banana supporters were being targeted.
Lugari MP Enoch Kibunguchy also demanded the arrest of those issuing
threats to the locals.
Earlier at Namanjalala trading centre, Mr Kombo condemned the violence
that occurred in Busia on Sunday during a rally attended by Vice-President
Moody Awori, saying those who did so had no respect for leaders.
Mr Kombo blamed the Orange team for allegedly fanning violence and told
its members to desist from politics of defiance.
Dr Kituyi asked Kwanza people to avoid being cheated by the Orange
supporters that they would win the referendum, saying the locals needed the
new Constitution to help them get land after living for years as
squatters.
Earlier, there was drama at the meeting when the Kwanza constituents
demanded to be addressed by a brother to the former Vice-President Michael
Wamalwa, Mr Eugene Wamalwa, after Dr Wekesa introduced him.
Mr Wamalwa told them to vote for the proposed Constitution.
Speaking separately, Orange supporters in the district led by
coordinator Jeff Wabuge dismissed the meeting, saying it was not true that
all the Trans Nzoia people were in the Banana camp. They claimed the Banana
team was "lying to the locals."

Original_Search_Title: Yes And No Camps On Show in Big Debate II
Headline: Kenya: Yes And No Camps On Show in Big Debate II
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kenyans will have a chance to watch politicians from the Banana
and Orange camps argue their positions in the second Big Debate on the
proposed constitution tomorrow.
The discussion, dubbed Big Debate II, will be broadcast live by all
television and radio stations affiliated to the Media Owners
Association (MOA).
Those outside the association were free to air the debate as well,
announced the chairman of the editorial committee of the Big Debate, Mr
Wachira Waruru, at a press conference at the Grand Regency Hotel in
Nairobi.
Three politicians each from the No and Yes camps will discuss three
chapters - on the executive, the legislature and devolution - of the
proposed constitution.
The debate comes just four days before Kenyans go to the vote on
Monday to decide whether to accept or reject the proposed constitution.
Those representing the Yes or Banana team are Water Minister Martha
Karua and assistant ministers Moses Wetang'ula and Danson Mungatana.
MPs Mutula Kilonzo, William Ruto and Otieno Kajwang' will speak for the
No or Orange team.
The debate will be held at the Grand Regency Hotel and will have two
moderators - newscasters Julie Gichuru of NTV and Louis Otieno of KTN.
The Big Debate II will be held between 7.30 pm and 9 pm. Journalists
will not take part in the second Big Debate to allow the politicians
more time to articulate their positions, said Mr Waruru, who is also
the managing director of the Kenya Broadcasting Corporation.
The first debate was held on October 18 and was moderated by Ms Rose
Kimotho, the MOA chairperson. Four journalists were allowed to ask
questions.
Cabinet ministers Mukhisa Kituyi and Raphael Tuju and Kabete MP Paul
Muite of the Banana camp faced off with Cabinet Minister Anyang'
Nyong'o and MPs Chris Okemo and Mutula Kilonzo of the Orange camp.
Present at the press conference yesterday at the Grand Regency Hotel
were MOA vice-chairman Tom Mshindi, Mr Ian Fernandes, the chairman of
the technical committee of the Big Debate, and Mr Cyril Nabutola, the
marketing director of the Nation Media Group.
Mr Mshindi said the association had decided to focus on the three
issues - the executive, the legislature and devolution - because they
were at the centre of governance.
"We want to focus on topics that revolve around governance because
we believe that at the bottom of all the issues is how we are going to
be governed, how power is going to be transferred and how resources
will be controlled," Mr Mshindi, who is also the managing director of
the Standard Group, added.
Unlike the first Big Debate, where many issues considered to be
contentious were tackled, Mr Mshindi said the association had decided
to focus on the three to give both sides time to argue their points.
"This is an opportunity for us to go deeper into those areas...
because of the breadth of the issues we were not able to cover all
issues with sufficient depth (in the first debate)."
The public has been asked to send comments, opinions or questions through debate@uunet.co.ke
The debates were initiated as a way to allow politicians in the two
camps to articulate their positions on various issues in the proposed
constitution.
Mr Mshindi said: "We believe the panel is strong, we don't expect the public to be disappointed."
MOA said the first debate was well received.

Original_Search_Title: Minister Hints At Cabinet Reshuffle
Headline: Kenya: Minister Hints At Cabinet Reshuffle
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A cabinet reshuffle is looming, Finance Minister David Mwiraria has hinted.
Mwiraria, a close ally of the president, also urged Kenyans to pray for peace ahead of and after the referendum.
The minister, who was speaking during a Banana campaign rally at
Githongo market in Imenti Central constituency, accused some members of
the Government of fighting to bring it down.
He accused the Orange team led by Raila Odinga and Kalonzo Musyoka of dividing the country in pursuit of political power.
He predicted that changes in the Narc government line-up soon after referendum would inevitable to "restore sanity".
"Some of the senior members of the Government have committed
unforgivable political blunders. Their only objective is to achieve
power through any means," he claimed.
The minister asked Kenyans to dedicate special prayers for the
country so that it could go through the referendum without any more
bloodshed after what was witnessed in Kisumu and Mombasa.
He predicted the political downfall of those opposing the enactment of the proposed new constitution.
Present at the rally were assistant ministers Kilemi Mwiria, Petkay
Miriti, MPs Peter Munya, Francis Kagwima and the host, Kirugi
M'Mukindia.
Separately, Yes campaigns chairman Simeon Nyachae said yesterday
that the verdict of Kenyans at the referendum will be final and that
the Yes camp will concede defeat if beaten.
Nyachae, who is the Energy minister, speaking at Magonga in Kitutu
Masaba constituency asked both Banana and Orange camps to accept the
outcome of referendum.
Nyachae reiterated there was no bad blood between him and Roads
minister Raila Odinga and the other members of the Orange camp. "What
we differed over are ideas even in your home, you differ with brothers
and sisters. But we remain Kenyans," Nyachae said.
"We have clearly said we will concede defeat and respect the verdict
of Kenyans. We have no power over what Kenyans will decide but follow
it. This is like a competition there must be a winner and a loser,"
Nyachae said after the rally.
"We must begin the process of preparing Kenyans for the verdict and
remind them that this is not the first referendum in the World. People
vote and return to nation building," he argued.
Major differences on the proposed constitution, he said, centred around devolution and the Executive.

Original_Search_Title: All Set for Live Tv, Radio Showdown
Headline: Kenya: All Set for Live Tv, Radio Showdown
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The second live debate on the referendum sponsored by the Media Owners Association (MOA) will be held tomorrow.
Protagonists in the Proposed New Constitution will get their last
chance to sell their stand as the country cruises towards the historic
plebiscite.
MOA, which has made changes on how the debate will be conducted, has promised a more lively and incisive show.
"We do not expect the public to be disappointed. They will be
treated to a lively debate," said MOA vice-chairman and Standard Group
chief executive, Tom Mshindi.
Unlike the first show that was aired from the Nation Centre, the
second 90-minute debate will be broadcast from the Grand Regency Hotel.
The show, which will run from 7.30pm to 9pm, will be aired live on all television and radio stations in the country.
Six politicians, three from each camp, have been invited, the
chairman of the editorial committee and managing director of the Kenya
Broadcasting Corporation, Mr Wachira Waruru, told a Press conference at
the Grand Regency Hotel.
The Banana side will be represented by Cabinet minister Martha
Karua, and assistant ministers Moses Wetangula and Danston Mungatana,
who will face off with Orange proponents Kanu secretary-general William
Ruto, and MPs Otieno Kajwang' and Mutula Kilonzo.
Two journalists, Louis Otieno of Standard Group and Jullie Gichuru
of Nation Media Group, will play the duo role of moderating and firing
the questions.
Mshindi said the choice of the two was based on the fact that they
had been involved in tackling similar debates on their TV programmes.
MOA chairperson and proprietor of Kameme FM, Rose Kimotho, moderated the first debate.
Waruru said the debate would not be open to the public, but asked people to send questions through e-mail (debate@uunet.co.ke).
Mshindi said although the last debate was well received by the public, it could not cover all the key issues in the Wako Draft.
The second, he said, would go deeper and will mainly focus on the Executive, Legislature and devolution.
He appealed to the politicians to stick to the issues and take the opportunity to educate the public.
Wachira said the participants would attend a dress rehearsal today to acquaint themselves with the rules of the debate.
Mr Ian Fernades of NMG said the time allocated was adequate, and commercial breaks would only take nine minutes.

Original_Search_Title: NRM Chiefs Battle for Positions
Headline: Uganda: NRM Chiefs Battle for Positions
Body_Text:
Kampala — NATIONAL Resistance Movement (NRM) top leaders have rushed to the
party electoral commission offices to return their nomination forms to
beat tomorrow's deadline.
National Political Commissar Dr. Crispus Kiyonga submitted his forms
yesterday at 1:52pm for the position of secretary general to NRM
electoral commission (EC) official, Zam Zam Nagujja at their office in
Kampala.
Health state minister Capt. Mike Mukula, vying for the eastern
region vice-chairperson slot, handed in his forms to NRM EC chief Dr.
Ruhakama Rugunda at Namboole stadium, where the party has set up camp
ahead of its November 15-20 national conference.
Mukula's competitor, Parliament deputy Speaker Rebecca Kadaga, said she would return her forms today.
Education minister Namirembe Bitamazire who is facing off with
security minister Betty Akech for the women's league, had also returned
her forms. Others were Okot Ogong for the party presidency.
It was not clear whether defence minister Amama Mbabazi, Tororo
woman MP Dorothy Hyuha and Vice-President Gilbert Bukenya had submitted
their forms for secretary general. Nagujja said they would be coming
out with the final list tomorrow.
President Museveni's fate remains unclear as the deadline draws
closer. Mukula said he would today meet district chairpersons to pass a
resolution for Museveni to stand.
Several ministers and aspirants for various posts were seen trying
to make last minute impressions on the delegates who had assembled at
Kololo Airstrip where they are being registered and accredited.
Akech and Bitamazire were seen talking to the northern and central
region delegations respectively, while Kadaga was meeting the eastern
delegation.
Brig. Matayo Kyaligonza, Uganda's ambassador to Kenya, was
distributing campaign leaflets for vice- chairman western. The National
conference kicks off today with a meeting of special interest groups.
Yesterday, thousands of NRM delegates from the 72 districts started
arriving at Kololo Airstrip grounds as early as 8:00am for registration
and accreditation.
Temporary registration centres had been set up. About 11,000
delegates were expected yesterday, while the rest will report tomorrow.
The NRM has invited close to 16,000 delegates
Close to 20 buses belonging to several transport companies and
draped in dry banana leaves and yellow Movement bandanas were packed at
the grounds.
Eight medium-size tents were set up to accommodate the registration
centres, with each region having its own tent. A special tent had also
been set up for NRM historicals and several individuals who contributed
to the struggle of the bush war that propelled NRM to power in 1986.
Several dance groups entertained the delegates as the registration
process went on. Namboole has been refurbished in preparation for the
conference.
The President will open the conference today with special interest groups before the main conference on Wednesday.

Original_Search_Title: Leaders Tell Off Rights Boss On Court Threats
Headline: Kenya: Leaders Tell Off Rights Boss On Court Threats
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Key politicians in the Banana camp have scoffed at threats by a
commission to take them to court for reportedly using inciting language
in their campaigns.
During a Banana rally in Kabete constituency, a group of 10 MPs, who
included Cabinet minister Chris Murungaru (Transport), denounced the
Kenya National Commission on Human Rights and its chairperson Maina
Kiai for lining them up for possible prosecution.
Among those named were ministers Murungaru, Njeru Ndwiga
(Co-operative Development), Amos Kimunya (Lands), Raila Odinga (Roads),
Simeon Nyachae (Energy) and Najib Balala (National Heritage) for what
it described as inflammatory remarks made by them.
The names of the ministers and others who were mentioned by the
commission have been forwarded to the Attorney-General for action.
Mr Kiai said if the AG failed to prosecute the leaders for
incitement or hate campaign, the commission, a Government agency, would
institute private prosecution against them.
Mr Muite asked Mr Kiai to come clean on his intentions, saying the chairperson had diverted from the commission's objectives.
The rally was also addressed by assistant ministers Njeru Githae,
Maina Kamanda, Betty Tett and Mirugi Kariuki as well as MPs, Patrick
Muiruri, Norman Nyagah, Kembi Gitura and Elias Mbau.
And in Nanyuki, seven MPs condemned the commission and another - the
Kenya Human Rights Commission - describing them as malicious and bent
on discrediting MPs.
Energy assistant minister Mwangi Kiunjuri, who had been named among
those misusing Government vehicles, produced the vehicle's logbook,
road licence and insurance certificate and displayed them to the media.
He said the vehicle, a Lexus, was his and told KNHCR to provide
evidence to the contrary.
Those at the Nanyuki rally were MPs Koigi Wamwere (Subukia), Mutahi
Kagwe (Mukurwe-ini), Macharia Mukiri (Molo) Jayne Kihara (Naivasha) and
GG Kariuki (Laikipia West).
At Kabete, Dr Murungaru claimed: "When the Orange team sensed
defeat, they started telling blatant lies but now they have started
using violence."

Original_Search_Title: Angry VP Blames Raila As Youths Attack Banana Rally
Headline: Kenya: Angry VP Blames Raila As Youths Attack Banana Rally
Body_Text:
Nairobi — At least three people were injured when police used teargas to
disperse rowdy youths who attempted to disrupt a high-powered Yes
campaign rally hosted by Vice-President Moody Awori yesterday.
The Yes leaders, who included seven Cabinet ministers, immediately
pointed an accusing finger at Roads minister Raila Odinga, claiming he
had hired youths to disrupt the meeting in Busia.
Mr Odinga could not be reached for comment.
The VP and the ministers flew to Busia Town in three helicopters -
landing one after the other at the nearby Busia Polytechnic - and were
driven straight to Busia Stadium where they addressed the rally as the
police battled the youths and contained them near the Kenya-Uganda
border.
The busy Busia-Kampala highway was blocked for more than two hours
by the youths who lit bonfires using old tyres after they were repulsed
from the rally venue.
The incident took place as Internal Security minister John Michuki
dispelled rumours that the Government had stopped all campaign rallies
organised by the Orange team that is opposing the new Constitution.
"This Government believes in democracy and has no wish to stop
anyone from holding a rally as long as correct procedures are
followed," the minister said in a statement at the Jomo Kenyatta
International Airport on arrival from Mombasa.
Yesterday, the Orange team took its campaign to Kericho with Mr
Odinga repeating his call for the resignation of Mr Michuki and Police
Commissioner Hussein Ali over the referendum violence in which eight
people have lost their lives.
Addressing a rally at the Kericho Green Stadium, the Roads minister
also repeated that he was ashamed of serving in the Narc government.
"I feel disheartened serving in a government which is killing
innocent Kenyans on issues that are not of their making and this should
be stopped forthwith," he said.
In Busia, a visibly angry VP accused Mr Odinga of allegedly having
ferried "hooligans" from Kisumu to disrupt the rally, saying Busia
people cherished peace and would not engage in lawlessness.
Not a tribalist
"I'm not a tribalist and I was born here. I cannot accept a person from Nyanza to take over Busia," he said.
He added: "You are the people to ensure this does not work. You are men..."
An unusually agitated VP took the podium before his Cabinet
colleagues and expressed disappointment at the turn of events, saying
the culture of violence must stop immediately.
Mr Awori said the Government will deal firmly with those promoting violence across the country.
The VP had been joined at the rally by ministers Musikari Kombo
(Local Government), Mukhisa Kituyi (Trade), Njenga Karume (Special
Programmes), George Saitoti (Education), Martha Karua (Water) and
Joseph Munyao (Livestock and Fisheries).
At one point the youths hurled stones into the stadium as the rally progressed, but the police repulsed them.
After they were pushed back, they retreated towards the Busia-Kisumu road where they lit a bonfire.
However, this did not deter the Yes campaigners from addressing the crowd for about three hours.
Western Province, the VP's home turf, is being targeted by both the
Banana and Orange teams and is believed to provide the swing vote that
could help either side win on November 21.
Kanu vice-chairman Chris Okemo and nominated MP Julia Ojiambo, a
long-time political rival of the VP in his Funyula constituency, are
spearheading the Orange campaign in Busia.
Yesterday's rally was significant for Mr Awori and the Banana team
as it was taking place days ahead of a scheduled presidential visit to
Western Province this week.
The trouble in Busia happened as police in Nairobi fired in the air
to disperse rowdy Orange supporters in Kibera who wanted to disrupt
another Yes rally hosted by assistant ministers Maina Kamanda, Beth
Mugo and Kamukunji MP Norman Nyagah.
It was sparked by Mr Nyagah's remarks referring to Mr Odinga as "mjinga" who had disturbed the country for a long time.
The Orange crowd, which had gathered about 100 metres from the venue
chanting anti-Banana slogans, started throwing stones at the Yes
supporters forcing police to intervene. The rally continued.
During the Busia meeting, Mr Awori's claims that the youths were
organised by Mr Odinga were supported Dr Kituyi, Ms Karua and assistant
minister Moses Wetangula.
Learned people
Dr Kituyi in his address said: "We have no quarrel with the Luo
community, which has very intelligent and learned people, but the
problem lies with their premier leader, Raila Odinga."
"If Jaramogi Oginga Odinga woke up today and found young Luo men
being misused to draw a wedge between them and their neighbours, he
would be ashamed with the son he left behind in the name of Raila," Dr
Kituyi said.
In a brief address, Mr Kombo called for an immediate stop to stone-throwing at political rallies.
Mr Wetangula said: "It is only in Luyialand where foreigners come
and burn tyres as the locals watch. We know these boys are from Kisumu;
they are our mechanics."
RELATED NEWS
Orange leaders hoping for arrest, claims Michuki

Original_Search_Title: Laws Ensure Development, Says Awori
Headline: Kenya: Laws Ensure Development, Says Awori
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The proposed Constitution would assure the country of development, said Vice-President Moody Awori.
Addressing a Yes rally in Gichugu Constituency, Kirinyaga, at the
weekend, the VP said free primary education had been entrenched in law.
Mr Awori said in the draft, presidential powers had been drastically cut.
Rejecting the proposed Constitution would be insensitive because the
country had spent Sh7 billion in the review, the VP told the rally
hosted by local MP Martha Karua and attended by MPs Daniel Karaba and
Kalembe Ndile.
He described as self-seekers, those campaigning for the draft's rejection.
"The Orange crusaders know the Constitution is good, but they are opposing it because of their own selfish interests," he said.
Braving the rain
A crowd attending a Banana rally at Kinoru Stadium on Saturday braved heavy rains to wait for Mr Awori.
The rains stopped just before his military helicopter landed at 4pm.
Mr Awori said a constitution is not changed at whim, justifying the
clause in the proposed laws requiring a million signatures to amend it.
In Nairobi, three Banana MPs accused advocates of the No vote of mounting a hate campaign.
Speaking at a rally in Makadara hosted by Mayor Dick Wathika,
Kamukunji MP Norman Nyagah and assistant ministers Maina Kamanda and
Betty Tett accused the Orange camp of inciting violence after
reportedly sensing defeat.

Original_Search_Title: Resign Over Chaos, Ministers Are Told
Headline: Kenya: Resign Over Chaos, Ministers Are Told
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Orange team leaders yesterday demanded the resignation of three Cabinet ministers.
They said Mr John Michuki, Mr Kipruto Kirwa and assistant minister
Stephen Tarus should resign, claiming they were being used to unleash
violence on Orange supporters in Rift Valley.
The also demanded the immediate arrest of a politician from Aldai,
adding that Mr Kirwa and Mr Tarus were behind the violence which rocked
Kobujoi in South Nandi. One person was shot and another one seriously
injured.
Led by Roads minister Raila Odinga, leader of the Official
Opposition Uhuru Kenyatta, Kanu vice chairman Henry Kosgey, its
secretary-general William Ruto, Aldai MP Jimmy Choge, MP David Koros
(Eldoret South), MP John Sambu (Mosop), MP Joseph Lagat (Eldoret East)
and former MP Ezekiel Barngetuny the Orange team leaders demanded the
transfer of South Nandi district commissioner Lydia Muriuki.
They said the DC was being used by the Banana team to campaign
against the Orange camp and told the DC to stop underrating the
intelligence of the Nandi, who had decided to vote for No.
Addressing an Orange rally in Nandi Hills Town a day after four
Banana ministers held a rally, Mr Raila declared that the Orange
rallies all over the country would continue until October 19, when
there would be a major one at Nyayo National Stadium in Nairobi.
The Roads minister said Mr Michuki was frustrating the team by using
the police. He cited the Likoni meeting, where the President's Coast
tour was used to cancel the Orange rally.
Mr Raila accused the commissioner of police and Mr Michuki, saying
he would not apologise for saying he was ashamed of serving the Narc
government following the Kisumu violence.
He claimed that on sensing defeat, some Banana team ministers went
to former president Daniel arap Moi over proposal to put off the vote,
but Mr Moi turned them away.
Mr Ruto accused the government of bias in investigating rally disruptions.
Mr Kenyatta said Kenyans should vote out the proposed law, saying
Kenyans wanted a Constitution for posterity. And Mr Mudavadi denied a
report that he had defected from the Orange team, saying he will not
make the mistake he made in 2002.
Mr Kosgey said the proposed law was unfit to Kenyans, while Mr Choge
said the Government was using violence to harass his constituents.
Mr Koros and Mr Sambu said Kenyans should not be intimidate by the use of guns.
Addressing another rally in Kericho, the Orange leaders said police commissioner Hussein Ali should be sacked.
Immigration minister Linah Kilimo dismissed those saying the proposed law was in the interest of women.
"Kenyans need a constitution that will unite all of them, not for
one community to stay in power until Jesus comes," she remarked.
Mr Salat warned the Government against rigging the elections, saying
that Orange supporters would guard all polling centres against any type
of rigging.

Original_Search_Title: Orange Leaders Hoping for Arrest, Claims Michuki
Headline: Kenya: Orange Leaders Hoping for Arrest, Claims Michuki
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Orange leaders were provoking the Government to arrest them so that
they could gain sympathy from the public, said Internal Security
minister John Michuki yesterday.
Even with the provocations, the time to arrest the leaders had not come, added Mr Michuki.
Said he: "The conduct of these leaders and their activities, which
border on treason, are intended to provoke the Government... The time
has not yet come for that action."
Mr Michuki was speaking at a press conference at Jomo Kenyatta
International Airport after his arrival from Mombasa with President
Kibaki.
Asked if the Orange leaders would be arrested after the referendum,
Mr Michuki said: "We will cross the bridge when we reach there."
Mr Michuki also said the Likoni rally that left four people dead on
Friday had been cancelled because it was disrespectful to the President
who was in Mombasa.
Although the Government regretted the deaths, he said the officers
shot at rioters, who were burning down the district officer's office,
in self-defence.
Mr Michuki said what happened in Likoni was a defiance of the law because the meeting had not been permitted by police.
"If you decide to operate outside the law, you cannot expect to
receive protection under the same law - you have chosen to be a
renegade."
Police officers were going on with their normal duties, when they
were suddenly surrounded by a mob, attacked and on the verge of being
burnt alive, claimed the minister.
"This is when they had to use force to free themselves from the
burning DO's office. This led to the deaths and injuries of the
attackers and injuries of the police officers," he said.
The officers wanted to retrieve 14 guns and ammunition that were in the office, explained the minister.
Mr Michuki claimed that a prominent Orange leader advised Heritage
minister Najib Balala to ignore police who asked the team to postpone
its rally.
Mr Michuki called on the Electoral Commission of Kenya (ECK) to take action against those who were fanning violence.
ECK vice-chairman Gabriel Mukele said the commission was collecting
information on what had happened and would make its step known today.
He denied the Government had banned Orange rallies in Nairobi and
Western provinces saying, the claims were untruthful.
He said the President would be in Kakamega this week, but the No team could hold rallies in other towns.
Planning minister Prof Anyang' Nyong'o and former Vice-President
Musalia Mudavadi claimed in Kisumu on Saturday that the Government had
cancelled all key rallies by the Orange team in the final week of
campaigns.
Mr Mudavadi said the Orange team first applied to hold a rally at
Uhuru Park but the Banana camp got a permit for the same venue.
"The Orange team then opted for Nyayo Stadium and now we have been
informed that the facility has been booked for another function on the
same day," he said.
But Mr Michuki said the group should have looked for alternatives if
they found that Uhuru Park or Nyayo Stadium had been booked.

Original_Search_Title: Kenyans Won't Accept Flawed Laws, Say MPs
Headline: Kenyans Won't Accept Flawed Laws, Say MPs
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Seven MPs allied to the Orange camp have warned that any attempts
to enact a flawed Constitution could lead to Kenya's disintegration.
Speaking during a No rally in Usenge Town in Bondo District at the
weekend, the MPs cautioned their Yes counterparts against imposing an
unpopular constitution on the people.
"People will resist it the way Ugandans resisted during the time of
the former President Milton Obote, who tried to impose a flawed
document on them," said the host, Dr Oburu Oginga.
The Bondo MP said the Wako Draft did not contain the people's
contribution and added that it could lead to the people resolving to
turn against the Government.
"How do you expect people to react if a document cannot be amended easily?" asked the legislator.
The meeting was addressed by assistant minister Peter Odoyo, MPs
Eric Nyamunga (Nyando), Ken Nyagudi (Kisumu Town West), Gor Sunguh
(Kisumu Town East), Jakoyo Midiwo (Gem), Prof Ayiecho Olweny (Muhoroni)
and Luo Council of Elders chairman Riaga Ogallo.
Mr Sunguh cautioned police commissioner Major-General Hussein Ali
that he would one day be held responsible for the ills committed
against innocent citizens "who clamoured for a better Constitution".
Mr Odoyo warned that any scheme by the Government to rig the referendum would be met with stiff resistance.
Mr Ogallo expressed fears that the country was headed for anarchy due to "trigger-happy police officers".
"Let us vote (during the referendum) and decide what is good for our
country today and tomorrow," Mr Ogallo said, and called for peaceful
campaigns.
Mr Nyagudi said it is only by casting their vote that Kenyans will get a document that they want.
Prof Olweny asked Rarieda MP Raphael Tuju to be in the forefront in
condemning incidents of thuggery that were witnessed in Kisumu Town
recently during a Banana rally.
Mr Nyamunga called on Luo Nyanza people to come out and vote,
expressing fears that the public from the area did not take voting
matters seriously.
- Forty lawyers from south Nyanza region want the proposed Constitution rejected "because it is ambiguous".
Speaking at a one-day workshop at Fountain Inn in Kisii Town at the
weekend, the lawyers among them Kituo Cha Sheria's Harun Ndubi, said
the proposed Constitution would be difficult to implement, if passed.
"Several clauses have been left hanging, which will make
implementation difficult," said Mr Ndubi adding that the proposed
Constitution was tailored to favour interests of the rich.
"Interests of the youth have been ignored in this draft. The
participation of youth in national and constituency politics is not
defined," said the Law Society of Kenya's Nyanza branch vice-chairman,
Mr Keroi Ondieki.
The lawyers criticised a clause on women's nomination to Parliament,
saying it was not clear how the process to fill the slots would be
carried out.

Original_Search_Title: Police Raid Slums in Hunt for Weapons
Headline: Kenya: Police Raid Slums in Hunt for Weapons
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Police raided several homes in three slums to search for weapons
allegedly bought by people planning to unleash violence on residents
ahead of the referendum.
But the Saturday night five-hour operation by Nakuru officers, under
the command of area police chief, Titus Yoma, did not yield anything.
"We did not find weapons, and no arrests were made in connection
with the planned violence. But we will intensify patrols in the area
for any eventualities," said Yoma.
The impromptu search in the sprawling Kaptembwo, Kwa Rhoda and Ponda
Mali slums followed claims that there was a group planning to attack on
November 19 and 20.
The attackers, sources said, had bought pangas and allegedly warned those opposed to the Wako Draft to leave before November 21.
Residents have been retreating to their houses as early as 7pm for the past one week as word on the planned spread.
The area was in the news after the last General Election when
violence, allegedly perpetrated by members of the outlawed Mungiki
sect, rocked the slums.
More than 20 people were killed and scores injured in the skirmishes said to have been politically instigated.
Former Nakuru Town MP, David Manyara, and 19 youths were arrested
and charged with the murders, but were later released by the High Court
for lack of evidence.
Yesterday, Yoma warned that the police would deal firmly with those planning to cause chaos.
Last Friday, three MPs campaigning for Yes vote in Subukia
constituency claimed there were plans to unleash terror on residents
should the Banana team win the review plebiscite.
Subukia legislator, Koigi Wamwere, Jane Kihara of Naivasha and their
Molo counterpart, Macharia Mukiri, said politicians opposed to the
Proposed New Constitution were inciting people to violence.
They alleged of plans to evict a certain community from the area should the Wako Draft sail through.
But police said none had reported the allegations, and told the public to treat the talk as rumours.

Original_Search_Title: Referendum Campaign Enters Final Stage
Headline: Kenya: The Final Sprint
Body_Text:
Nairobi — With less than 168 hours to go before the November 21 referendum,
