both the Banana and Orange teams yesterday entered the final sprint
with over 15 rallies across the country.
The two teams intensified their campaigns as pockets of violence were witnessed.
Chaos at the Busia Bus Park threatened to spill over to Vice President Moody Awori's rally as police tear-gassed rowdy youths.
Later, a vehicle he often uses was pelted with stones. The driver,
who was not in Awori's motorcade as he left the border town, reported
the incident to the police.
Awori's meeting progressed as Orange leaders led by Roads minister
Raila Odinga and Kanu chairman, Uhuru Kenyatta, campaigned in Kericho,
Kuria and finally Nandi District.
With anxiety rising to fever pitch, both camps vowed they were
headed for a win as Raila declared the Orange leaders would be in court
tomorrow to listen to a ruling on a case which seeks to stop the
referendum.
In Nairobi there was stone throwing and more teargas when the
Government Chief Whip, Norman Nyaga, took three assistant ministers to
a Yes rally at Raila's Langata constituency. Police repulsed youths who
threw stones at the dais where the chief campaigners were seated. They
were: Beth Mugo (Dagoretti), Betty Tett (Nominated) Maina Kamanda
(Starehe) and Koigi Wamwere (Subukia).
Present at the Busia rally were Cabinet Ministers George Saitoti
(Education), Mukhisa Kituyi (Trade), Joseph Munyao, Musikari Kombo
(Local Government), Martha Karua (Water) and Njenga Karume (Special
Projects).
"The Orange campaign has been re-energised like the River Nile and
will sweep anything that stands on its way to the Mediterranean Sea,"
Raila said at the Kericho Green Stadium.
"Courts are not above the law. They are answerable to Kenyans. They
must not be used by anybody to create room for shelving the vote on the
proposed Constitution," Raila added.
A contingent of about 60 regular policemen and prison warders lobbed
teargas and fired in the air to contain pro-Orange supporters as Awori
asked non-Luhya communities living in the province not to undermine the
goodwill of the local community.
"Western Kenya hosts all communities from Kenya, but this should not be abused or taken for granted," he warned.
The first Air Force plane carrying Awori, Kombo and
Wetangula landed at midday while Saitoti, Karume, Karua and Munyao
arrived an hour later in a private plane. The third aircraft, an Air
Force helicopter dropped Kituyi and two Western MPs. Booing and
heckling started when the last team arrived.
Another Banana rally attended by 14 MPs led by Cabinet Ministers
Kiraitu Murungi and Chris Murungaru was held in Othaya, President
Kibaki's constituency.
Kiraitu appealed to voters from the Central Kenya region not to let
go a government that "we have painfully sought for many years".
He reiterated that the Government was under siege by power-hungry
politicians, adding that the campaign for the proposed constitution was
only a test of how strong the government was. "We are no longer writing
constitution, this war is aimed at overthrowing President Kibaki's
Government and if there are people who can salvage it, then it will be
people from this region," Kiraitu said.
"I am beseeching you not to let this government go to people who are only power hungry," he said.
Murungaru claimed after failing to get a chance to attain power
through creation of a powerful premiership, Raila, had vowed to
destabilise the Government.
Awori said democracy should be allowed to prevail to ensure that there was no intimidation and harassment in their rallies.
Karume urged the Orange team to respect the Head of State, saying it
was disgusting that despite being warned by the police not to hold a
rally as it would coincide with that of the President's, their
"strong-headedness and lack of respect had forced them to go on.
"Let Kenyans go about their own business. The police are under firm
instructions to maintain law and order. We will not be cowed by a few
individuals who want to threaten the peace in the country."
Kombo said that matters related to the next general election should
be delinked from the referendum campaigns to curb confusion in the
review exercise.
Kituyi said he was laying strategies to woo former Vice President Musalia Mudavadi to join the Yes team.
In Kericho, three Cabinet ministers an equal number of assistants
pitched camp at Londiani, a stone-throw away from the Orange rally, to
campaign for a Yes vote in the referendum.
The ministers were John Koech (East African Co-operation), Karua
(Water) and Amos Kimunya (Lands). The assistant ministers were Kipkalya
Kones, Alicen Chelaite and Mirugi Kariuki.
Kimunya dismissed as "mischievous" claims by some Orange leaders
that there was a move by the Government to repossess land held by
members of the Kalenjin community.
"This is incitement of the highest order. As the Lands minister, I
assure the Kalenjin community that this would not happen during the
leadership of Narc government and I don't believe it would ever happen
even if another regime comes to power."
He assured the Ogiek community from Sorget in Londiani and their
'cousins' in Nandi and other parts of the country that their squatter
cases would be looked into after some pending forest cases in the
jurisdiction of the Ministry of Environment were dealt with.
Speaking in Kericho, Uhuru claimed that those in government
supporting the Wako Draft had pressurised Kanu to deliver a new
people-driven constitution, but were now propelled by a thirst for
personal gain.
"All Orange supporters must wake up at dawn and cast their vote
against the Wako Draft on November 21 because it adulterated the views
they gave at Bomas."
Uhuru said those in the ruling coalition had sidelined
key partners from the Liberal Democratic Party (LDP) and
instead co-opted Kanu MPs into Government to rubberstamp their agenda.
Raila challenged Internal Security minister, John Michuki, and
Police Commissioner, Major General Ali Hussein, to resign over the
manner they handled the violent rallies in which eight people had been
shot dead by police.
"I repeat, I am ashamed to serve in a Government that kills its own
people. I regret the character of the our regime is wanting," Raila
added.
Last evening, Michuki said the Orange rally in Mombasa could not have been allowed to go on as the President was in town.
Baringo Central MP Gideon Moi said; "In order to put to shame the
proponents of the draft, you should all turn out in large numbers, vote
against it and stay put waiting for the counting process."
In Kuria, Raila and Uhuru said they had their eyes on the prize and
nothing would deter them. They asked area residents not to be
hoodwinked to vote 'Yes' and pave way for the "re-introduction of
autocratic system of governance."
They vowed to continue with their campaigns countrywide despite plans by the Government to gag them.
The team, which arrived in Kuria in a helicopter, was given a warm
welcome by an enthusiastic crowd chanting No slogans as they sung the
popular tune of "Bado Mapambano".

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki Tour Begins As Orange Defy Ban
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki Tour Begins As Orange Defy Ban
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki heads to Western tomorrow to intensify the
Banana campaigns in the battle over 1.2 million votes in the province.
Police have cancelled all Orange campaign rallies in the area, a senior officer said.
But Orange leaders have said they will defy the ban. The police said
the rallies were cancelled because President Kibaki will be campaiging
in the area for the Yes vote.
Vice-President Moody Awori, Ford-Kenya chairman Musikari Kombo and Trade minister Mukhisa Kituyi will be hosting the President.
The President has just concluded a tour of the Coast Province where
four people were killed after police broke up an Orange rally in Likoni.
Kombo and Kituyi confirmed the President's visit to the area after a
recent incident where a mob burnt Ikolomani MP Dr Bonny Khalwale's car.
Five suspects were arrested and charged in court over the incident.
The Orange team led by former vice-president Musalia Mudavadi protested at the cancellation of their rallies.
Musalia and Kanu vice-chairman Chris Okemo have teamed up with some
Ford Kenya MPs, including Wycliffe Osundwa (Mumias), Wycliffe Oparanya
(Butere), Julius Arunga (Khwisero) and Maxwel Shamalla of the East
African Legislative Assembly to campaign against the Wako draft.
The Orange leaders said they would defy the cancellation of their
rallies. Planning minister Prof Anyang' Nyong'o and Kanu vice-chairman
Chris Okemo said they would not be cowed into calling off their rallies.

Original_Search_Title: ECK, Leaders Condemn Violence At Campaign Rallies
Headline: Kenya: ECK, Leaders Condemn Violence At Campaign Rallies
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Electoral Commission of Kenya has called a crucial meeting today to decide the fate of the referendum campaigns.
This is after the bloody clashes in parts of the country that have left at least 10 dead.
At least six people were killed during a Banana rally in Kisumu three weeks ago and several others seriously injured.
On Friday, four people were shot dead in referendum clashes in Mombasa, with several others injured.
The ECK vice-chairman, Mr Gabriel Mukele, said yesterday that a
report over the violence in Mombasa would be tabled during today's
meeting.
The commission had, after the Kisumu chaos, threatened to stop all referendum campaigns if there any more deaths occurred.
And Orange camp leaders Raila Odinga and Uhuru Kenyatta claimed that
the violence was targeted at their supporters to intimidate them.
Speaking at a rally in Kamukunji on Saturday evening, Raila and
Uhuru said the police were to blame for the Mombasa violence as they
came and started beating up wananchi at a meeting.
"The commissioner of police must come clean on the matter. We want to know who issued the order to shoot people," he said.
He accused the Banana team of inciting Kenyans, adding that the Orange team would trounce them on November 21.
Uhuru and Raila were speaking at Kamukunji grounds in Nairobi where they addressed a crowd of Orange supporters.
Three people were shot dead in Mombasa at the Orange rally by the
police, while four other people died in referendum violence in Kisumu.
The rally was also addressed by MPs David Mwenje, Amina Abdala,
William Omondi, Ochieng Mbeo, Fred Gumo, Reuben Ndolo, Billow Kerrow,
Mwenda wiro Mhanga, Ken Marende, Joseph Kamotho and former MP James
Orengo among others.
Raila and Uhuru said Kenyans should reject tribalism on the issue of the referendum.
Raila said he did not ask for the Prime Minister's post and that
leaders in the Banana team, including President Kibaki and Justice
Minister Kiraitu Murungi, suggested it but changed their minds once
they tasted power.
"Who said Raila cannot run for the presidency?" he asked.
Uhuru said he has been accused by Banana leaders of supporting Raila when the referendum was above personalities.
Orengo said the Judiciary should remain independent in their ruling
over the case on the constitution to be delivered on Tuesday.
Kamotho said assistant ministers Robinson Githae, Mwangi Kiunjuri
and MPs Alfred Nderitu should substantiate their allegation that the
Orange team planned to use university students to cause chaos.
Meanwhile, the National Council of Churches of Kenya (NCCK) has
condemned the use of brute force to control referendum related violence.
NCCK secretary-general Mutava Musyimi condemned the killing of four
people in Mombasa and other incidents of violence during the campaigns.
He said the use of live bullets by the police was unacceptable no
matter the nature of provocation.
Speaking during a press conference at the Jumuia Place, Nairobi,
Musyimi said the police must exercise restraint whatever the
provocation.
"Even in the face of violence, they should use only non-lethal
control methods to calm the riots. We plead for more restraint on their
part whatever the provocation," Musyimi said.
He said the police should borrow a leaf from France where violent
riots have been going on for two weeks but nobody has been shot.
He said the violence witnessed during the referendum campaigns was a sign of bad governance.
At the same time Musyimi has asked the Government to treat both sides of Orange and Banana equally.
Meanwhile, German ambassador Bernd Braun has expressed concern over
the violence which rocked an Orange rally at Likoni, Mombasa on Friday.
Braun, who spoke in Mombasa shortly after the riots that claimed
three lives, said the Government had an obligation to protect life and
property.
The envoy made the remarks during a dinner party to mark the 15th
anniversary of Day of German Unity at the Mombasa residence of honorary
consul Sidiq Ghalia.

Original_Search_Title: High Stakes Game As Rivals Schedule Their Final Meetings
Headline: Kenya: High Stakes Game As Rivals Schedule Their Final Meetings
Body_Text:
Nairobi — With only a week to the referendum, the race is now on the rocky
yet decisive homestretch leg of a poll that promises radical shifts in
the country's politics, regardless of its outcome.
Key players in both the Banana and Orange camps are confident of
victory at the lapse of the next 168 hours, ending a seven-year
exercise that has cost Sh10 billion.
Two presidents - President Kibaki and retired President Moi - have
so far staked their political legacies to the delivery of a new
constitution, and many careers may suffer irreparably from an
acrimonious and bloody process that has claimed scores of lives.
Behind the faÃade of confidence in the Banana and Orange teams are
anxieties and concerns belying fears that the game is far from won.
Yesterday, Heritage minister Najib Balala of the Orange team, and
Kabete MP Paul Muite, a key strategist for the Banana group, boasted a
minimum win of 65 per cent.
Yet the two, who were equally contemptuous of the rival side, talked
of adopting "secret strategies" to seal the win while eroding their
opponents' support.
Balala said the No team was worried about voter apathy and had turned to a door-to-door campaign to persuade them to turn up.
"We are concerned that this not being a general election, many
people may not bother to come out and vote. We hope to correct that
with a silent door-to-door campaign," he said.
The team was also adopting an undisclosed "counter-strategy" to
contain what he claimed was "Zanzibar-like intimidation and harassment"
from the police and other Government agents.
Muite was more economical with details of how the Yes camp intended
to maximise the final week, saying its game plan was deliberately
secret.
"We are certain of 65-70 per cent win. But we're not relaxing our
campaigns. However, I cannot divulge our strategy for obvious reasons.
We don't want to play into our opponents' hands," he said.
The poll outcome could be a political Armageddon, thanks to the
emotive buildup to the referendum. The Yes-No battle has transcended
the original and straightforward call for rejection or acceptance of
the Wako Draft into a popularity contest between sides divided into
crudely balanced regional/ethnic and personality blocs.
President Kibaki heads the Yes team that has also Vice- President
Moody Awori and the majority of Cabinet ministers, including Kiraitu
Murungi, Simeon Nyachae, Musikari Kombo and Chirau Ali Mwakwere.
The No camp has Roads minister Raila Odinga, Opposition Leader Uhuru
Kenyatta and at least six other ministers, including Kalonzo Musyoka,
William ole Ntimama, Lina Kilimo and Balala.
A No win could be potentially embarrassing to the President who has
wagered his political career by publicly campaigning for the draft.
Having committed the entire Government machinery to whip up support
for the document and showed unrestrained generosity with State freebies
like land and more districts pledges, the least Kibaki and his team can
suffer in a No triumph is a loss of face.
But there would certainly be a bigger issue of public confidence for
President Kibaki to grapple with, as a rejection would easily be
interpreted as a loss of voters' faith in his Government in general and
him in particular.
Orange leaders have publicly dared their rivals to commit themselves
to resigning in the event of a Yes loss and such calls will likely be
echoed louder.
However, a convincing Banana win would place their rivals, and
especially the "renegade" ministers, in a politically precarious
position judging by the potency of venom that has been mutually traded.
Two ministers are currently facing off in the courts over
accusations made in referendum campaign rallies, and a Yes victory
would make reconciliations almost impossible in a Cabinet that is
already reeling from intolerance.
Lands minister, Amos Kimunya, has sued his Planning counterpart,
Anyang Nyongo, for defamation after the latter linked him and three
others to the Anglo Leasing tender fraud.
In a further reflection of how sour the Cabinet relations have
turned, Nyongo over the weekend accused President Kibaki of ruining his
Government's reputation through police repression.
He claimed the Government had increasingly become tyrannical under
the President watch, a factor he attributed to the Banana camp
apprehensions over "imminent" loss at the referendum.
"The Government is increasingly becoming repressive. The Banana camp
has not only sensed, but seen defeat," the minister said in Kisumu.
Even before the D-Day, main Yes members have openly called for the
sacking of their colleagues whom they regard as quislings who have
teamed with Kanu to wreck the same Government they serve.
So frosty have the relationships between the ministers become that
the President has not held a Cabinet meeting for months, and the
referendum outcome is unlikely to mollify the kind of anger that saw
Raila and Kimunya trade insults and sensational claims in Parliament.
Outside the Cabinet, the referendum effects are likely to last into
the next elections with some incumbent MPs and councillors being
punished for backing the "wrong".

Original_Search_Title: FDC Will Fight Back, Musumba Tells Govt
Headline: Uganda: FDC Will Fight Back, Musumba Tells Govt
Body_Text:
Butaleja — THE Forum for Democratic Change (FDC) does not believe in
violence, but will not sit back as its supporters are being beaten, the
party vice president, Ms Salaam Musumba, has warned.
"We shall not be beaten like chicken. When provoked, we shall fight
back," she said while addressing FDC supporters and Busolwe town
residents on Friday, shortly after opening the first party offices in
Butaleja district.
FDC supporters and leaders from various districts in eastern Uganda
attended the opening which started with a peaceful kadodi dance
procession within the town.
The supporters, who moved around town several times, ignored
provocative youth who were carrying President Yoweri Museveni's posters
and dry banana leaves.
She said the government has harassed opposition supporters in the
previous elections, but this time the FDC will counter the intimidation.
Stand for rights
"We should stand for our rights. Let no one intimidate us, it's our
right,"Musumba said. Earlier, Tororo County MP Geoffrey Ekanya, who had
addressed the same gathering, said FDC was prepared for Maj. Roland
Kakooza Mutale, the presidential advisor on political affairs, who runs
a paramilitary group Kalangala Action Plan.
The group was accused of beating opposition supporters in 2001
elections. "Today would be a day of celebrations for us, but our 2001
victory was stolen. I want to tell you I did personally chase Mutale
from the east in 2001 elections, he should not intimidate you. He is
just human like any of us and can also be caned," he told a cheering
crowd.
Musumba assured the people of Busolwe that FDC is the party which will relieve Ugandans of their problems.

Original_Search_Title: Face Your Trial, Presidency Tells Alamieyeseigha
Headline: Nigeria: Face Your Trial, Presidency Tells Alamieyeseigha
Body_Text:
Abuja — THE Presidency has charged the embattled Governor Dipereye Alamieyeseigha of Bayelsa State to concentrate on freeing himself from the allegations of money laundering levelled at him in London as it, yesterday, dismissed insinuations of political persecution raised by the governor against President Olusegun Obasanjo.
The presidency position was a rebuttal to a newspaper interview where the governor accused the president of persecuting him (Alamieyeseigha) on account of his political inclinations to Vice-President Atiku Abubakar and his leading role in the resource control crusade.
Reacting to the allegations yesterday, Senior Special Assistant to the President, Mr Femi Fani-Kayode said the allegations were all lies lacking decency. Besides, the presidency described the allegations by Gov. Alamieyeseigha as the utterances of a drowning man.
He said: "The allegation that the President wanted to have him assassinated is far-fetched and ridiculous. This is not a banana Republic and neither is our president a murderer. People say all sorts of things when they are in a tight corner. It is in that light we begin to see this. As regards that aspect of his being prosecuted because he was a supporter of the Vice President, I will not dignify that submission with a response. That is an old story.
"I think that it would be better if Alamieyeseigha faces the charges that have been levelled at him in London by the authorities rather than suddenly spreading lies and say this is why, this is why, this is why."

Original_Search_Title: Stop Chasing Shadows, Presidency Tells Alamieyeseigha
Headline: Nigeria: Stop Chasing Shadows, Presidency Tells Alamieyeseigha
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Presidency yesterday asked the Governor of Bayelsa State,
Chief Diepreye Alamieyeseigha, to concentrate more on defence of
allegation of money laundering leveled against him by the British
police in London instead of accusing President Olusegun Obasanjo of
victimizing him.
Reacting to the governor's insistence, in a national newspaper
interview published yesterday, that his travail in London was the
handiwork of the President who was vilifying him for political reasons,
the Senior Special Assistant to the President on Public Affairs, Chief
Femi Fani-Kayode, said Alamieyeseigha's claim was a mere subterfuge
that was meant to becloud the substantive issue of his involvement in
corrupt practices.
"The allegation that he is being politically victimized is an old
story that should not be dignified with a response," he said,
explaining that the President was determined to deal with anyone found
to be corrupt irrespective of his status in the country.
The governor, who is currently restricted to London by a London
court for alleged money laundering, in an interview published by a
national newspaper yesterday alleged among other things that he was
being punished for his support for Vice President Atiku Abubakar, and
accused the Federal Government of plotting to assassinate him. He also
said in his effort to nail him, the President descended so low to grant
audience to British police constable that came to Nigeria to
investigate the case.
But Fani-Kayode said Alamieyeseigha's accusations were far off the
mark, contending that they were wild. "The allegation that the
President wants to assassinate him is far-fetched and ridiculous. This
is not a banana republic and neither is Mr. President a killer," he
said.
According to the presidential assistant, the governor's claim that
the fight against corruption was selective was the stereotype of
corrupt persons who were out to discredit the battle against graft.

Original_Search_Title: Kamba Banana Politicians Plan a Second Call On State House
Headline: Kenya: Kamba Banana Politicians Plan a Second Call On State House
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The visit to State House Health Minister Charity Ngilu organised
for Ukambani leaders early last month, came with 'rewards', especially
for civic leaders in the delegation.
Sources said each councillor got Sh50, 000 to run the 'Yes' campaign in their wards with promises of more.
Tomorrow, another delegation from Ukambani will be in State House to
reassure President Kibaki of the region's support for the Proposed New
Constitution.
Kangundo Town Council chairman Peter Kimonyi who was part of the
State House delegation said that the civic leaders complained of poor
pay, which the President promised to address.
Local Government minister Musikari Kombo has already gazetted a pay rise for councillors.
Kimonyi said the Kamba were also promised additional districts.
Kibwezi MP Kalembe Ndile, who was in the delegation, said the visit
was for the President to meet his "people and know their needs".
Ndile says that Ukambani has myriad problems, among them, clean water and land.
He said the visits are pegged on the referendum where each community has its own demands.
"There is nothing wrong with Kamba councillors going to State House
and making their problems known to the President," explains Ndile. The
Kibwezi MP disputes claims by Orange leaders in Ukambani that State
House visits are driven by greed for handouts.
Visitors only received a token amount for "lunch".
But immediately after the State House visits, murmurs of discontent
have emerged among some delegates, with some accused of being in the
list to swell numbers.
"Some of the people I saw at State House as part of the delegation
were not civic leaders," says a Makueni councillor. Ngilu, while
leading 200 councillors, asked for, among other things, to be entrusted
with the leadership of the Banana campaign in the region.
Despite being a key member of the government, Ngilu's involvement
has been overshadowed by political lightweights like assistant
ministers Kivutha Kibwana and Mutua Katuku, and MPs Adelina Mwau and
Ndile.
"Though the President did not give Ngilu the nod to lead the
campaigns, the message that all was not well was sent," says a Machakos
civic leader who requested anonymity. But it is clear that the 'Yes'
team in Ukambani needs a captain and that's what Ngilu wanted.
"Despite the State House visit, Ngilu's impact on the ground is not
being felt due to the rift between her and some MPs who are not willing
to work with her," said a councillor.
Though Ngilu is an indefatigable campaigner she has not been visible in the 'Yes' rallies.
Even before the State House visit, Ngilu had been sulking over what
political observers say is the control of the 'Yes' campaigns by her
political juniors.
But Ngilu says she is more committed to ensuring that her people have food and drugs.
She reiterates the State House visit was not meant to auction the
Kamba to support the proposed constitution but to seek an audience with
the President on local issues.
"Our people need food. That was one of our demands," says Ngilu.
She defends her absence from Banana campaigns, saying she is committed to basic needs.

Original_Search_Title: Banana Campaign Yet to Kick Off in Nyeri
Headline: Kenya: Banana Campaign Yet to Kick Off in Nyeri
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Two weeks to the referendum, daggers are drawn in President
Kibaki's Nyeri backyard, and the Banana camp is yet to hold any
significant campaigns.
Some MPs and civic leaders are wondering about the fate of the
Sh200, 000 set aside for each constituency by the Banana secretariat
for mobilisation.
Residents have been treated to acrimonious exchanges among leaders
with rallies being called off. In some instances, political rivals have
planned parallel meetings, only to call them off.
A week ago, after sensing the hostility among elected leaders,
President Kibaki sent the First Lady Lucy Kibaki to the troubled area.
In campaigns that took her to all divisions in the district, except
Tetu, the First Lady asked locals to support the Banana camp as leaders
fell over each other to be counted as supporting her.
It was not lost on Nyeri residents that the First Lady arrived in a military jet, sometimes used by the President.
Doubling as a compassionate mother and a 'Yes' campaigner, Lucy
visited children's homes and maternity wards in hospitals where she
donated equipment and money.
She also hit out at opponents of the Banana camp when she stopped to
greet people in Nyeri, Karatina, Othaya, Mukurweini and Kieni.
But as Lucy was visiting health institutions, Narc activist, Mary
Wambui, was receiving Kanu leaders supporting the proposed constitution.
It was not lost on observers that the two women had their functions
on the same day, October 31, at the same time, but different venues.
Before the two women electrified the area with their campaigns, Nyeri had been abnormally quite.
Long before the Banana camp side set up a secretariat in Nairobi to
co-ordinate national campaigns, bad blood had emerged between Transport
minister Dr Chris Murungaru and three area MPs.
The enmity arose after Murungaru appeared to support some district's DP officials, who are former MPs.
Murungaru, who is viewed as the senior-most local politician after
President Kibaki, began planning meetings with logistical help from
former MPs Matu Wamae (Mathira) Muhika Mutahi, (Mukurweini) and Gikonyo
Muya (Tetu).
The former MPs who still call the shots in DP were given the mandate
to determine who would attend key political meetings in the district.
MPs Nderitu Gachagua (Mathira), Mutahi Kagwe (Mukurweini) and Tetus
Prof Wangari Maathai were enraged. The MPs distanced themselves from
the minister's meetings. Murungaru had insisted that he has President
Kibaki's authority to unite the district.
The MPs accused Murungaru of plotting their downfall by working with the people they defeated in 2002.
When Murungaru called a meeting at Outspan Hotel two months ago to
plan how to mobilise the district's 360,000 voters to support the
Banana camp, only one MP attended. Nyeri Town MP PG Mureithi on sensing
the rift between his colleagues offered to arbitrate, saying they were
letting the President and their electorate down.
Former Nyeri Town MP, Wanyiri Kihoro, has distanced himself from Banana campaigns, saying he would not follow any camp blindly.
Murungaru has on three occasions attempted to organise rallies in
each of the district's constituencies, including Othaya, but has met
with strong resistance.
The other leaders feel he has no mandate to organise the rallies, as
he is not a member of the national secretariat in charge of national
campaigns.
On October 19, Gachagua organised a civic education session in his
constituency and invited Water minister Martha Karua who said Murungaru
had no basis organising 'Yes' rallies.
Political differences and voter apathy could diminish the numerical
strength of the 'Yes' side, saying it is imperative that all leaders
convince locals to vote.
Kagwe is unhappy with his rivals' attempts to hold rallies in his
constituency without his involvement. It is against this background
that some rallies were cancelled. Each MP is organising meetings
without involving others.
Last weekend a Tetu 2007 aspirant, DK Munene, organised a caravan to signify the start of 'Yes' campaigns in Tetu.
But the banners were found in Wamagana, which is outside Tetu.
Prof Maathai who has declined to take sides was boxed into a corner, as some leaders demanded that she supports the Banana side.
At one point, some leaders tried to force Maathai to eat a banana to symbolise her acceptance of the draft but she declined.
She has declared that she is ready to lose her seat rather than take sides in the debate, which is divisive.
She says the referendum is not a do or die matter and Kenyans need not behave like suicide.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki Cut Down List of Demands From Northern Frontier
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki Cut Down List of Demands From Northern Frontier
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The list of demands by delegates from northern Kenya when they visited State House recently, is arguably the longest.
A section of the delegation from North Eastern Province that paid a
call on President Kibaki at State House, Nairobi. Many of them are
disappointed that some of their requests were not approved.
But the President largely ignored the requests by delegation leaders
minister Mohammed Abdi Mohamud, on behalf of North Eastern Province,
and Dr Mohammed Abdi Kuti for the upper part of Eastern Province.
The reasons, political pundits say, is because the communities
represented by the two leaders have small populations and are unlikely
to affect local political allegiances.
The demands included an airport, tarmac roads, abattoirs, new districts and jobs for locals.
By yesterday, the region had only received a few tons of maize and
beans. But Kuti said there was ample relief food in the district and
Treasury had released Sh40 million, through the Arid Lands Resource
Management Project to buy residents' livestock.
The livestock programme was scheduled to take off two weeks after
the President's October 27 visit, and critics say it may be shelved as
the rains started last week.
It had also been alleged that the Banana team wooed MPs from the
region, promising that they would be co-opted into the government if
'Yes' wins.
Each of the MPs was said to have received between Sh3 million and
Sh5 million, to support the Banana camp. But Kuti denied that the MPs
were bribed.
"We did not get any money. We are there because the Wako Draft is
good for us," he said. He said the only money he got was for transport
for 150 delegates who visited State House on November 2.
MPs Abdi Sasura (Saku), Titus Ngoyoni (Laisamis) and Dr Guracha
Galgalo (Moyale) also led a similar number of delegates to the function.
Sources say the region was promised an additional Cabinet post, and at least three assistant ministers' position.
Two MPs from the region were also heard during the meet-the- people
tours of their areas urging residents to vote 'Yes' in the referendum
to secure Cabinet appointments.
But Cabinet ministers Raila Odinga and Najib Balala maintained the
community was duped when they took the Orange campaign to Mandera,
Moyale and Marsabit on Monday and Tuesday.
The ministers said the Kanu and Ford People leaders in the Banana
team were tricked when they were told LDP ministers would be sacked and
replaced with the defectors.
"Your MP will not be appointed to replace me as he was not there
when we campaigned for the Narc coalition. And in case I'm sacked, he
won't be the one to get the post. The President is more interested in
areas with big populations," said Balala in Moyale.
The demand list presented by the delegation from Isiolo is similar
to the one they presented to the President on October 27, when he
visited Isiolo Girls' Secondary School to launch the Nepad-e School
Initiative.
The President ignored protocol and kept donors waiting for more than
five hours after he went into a meeting with local leaders to drum up
support for the proposed constitution.
During both meetings, the leaders asked the government to construct
an airport in Isiolo and rehabilitate the 510km Isiolo-Moyale road.
They also asked for an abattoir, rehabilitation of Garba-Tulla High
School, a livestock take-off programme, and relief food.
At the meeting held at the DC's compound, the President rubbished most of these requests.
"Why do you need an airport and you are poor? Better ask for roads, they will help you," Kibaki told the crowd.
On rehabilitation of Garba-Tulla High School, a national school that
collapsed in 2001, but reopened a year later, the President told the
leaders: "You destroyed it and it's your responsibility to rehabilitate
it. The government is providing free primary education."
Kuti said the President pledged that the district would get six more
boarding primary schools during the October 27 meeting. The Isiolo
North MP believes the government would fulfil the pledges even if
Orange won on November 21.
"The government is serious with its development plans. Already, it
has done much for us. The airport and construction of the Isiolo-Moyale
Road is not politics," he said.
Wajir South MP Abdirahman Hassan Ali said he defected to the Banana
side on principle, after realising that the proposed constitution was
good for his constituents.
During this State House visit earlier this month, NEP delegates asked for at least three districts, but the plea was denied.
Kibaki also rejected minister Mohamud's request that university and
other college entry cut-off points for students from NEP be lowered.
"How are they going to compete with others if they had failed their
examinations? Better ask for more teachers, equipment and facilities,"
the President advised.
But area residents say they deserve additional districts. Wajir, for
example, is the third largest district nationally, about 65,000sqkm,
and about 500,000 people.
Mandera Central MP Billow Kerrow said the government had given NEP a
raw deal. He wondered what criteria the President used to give Turkana
an additional district, yet it was not different from Wajir or Mandera.
"This shows that this government is biased against pastoralists and
Muslims. It is time leaders who sell their people realise this," the MP
said, during an Orange rally in Moyale.

Original_Search_Title: Minister Claims State Gagging Team
Headline: Kenya: Minister Claims State Gagging Team
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Government has cancelled all key rallies the Orange team was
to hold in the final week of the referendum campaigns, a minister
claimed yesterday.
Police violence that rocked Mombasa on Friday killing four people
was part of a scheme to gag the Orange team, Planning Minister Prof
Anyang' Nyong'o and former Vice President Musalia Mudavadi claimed
yesterday.
The two claimed the Government had cancelled the 'No' rally scheduled for Nairobi on November 19.
Mudavadi said the Orange camp first applied to hold a rally at Uhuru Park but the Banana team got a permit for the same venue.
"The Orange team then opted for Nyayo Stadium and now we have been
informed that the facility has been booked for another function on the
same day," he said.
Mudavadi said the Orange team, which was scheduled to hold rallies
in Western Province starting today, had been informed that the meetings
would not take place as the President would be taking the 'Yes'
campaign to the region.
"These people are trying to frustrate our efforts to hold rallies.
This is not a General Election, but a referendum on document that
concerns all Kenyans," Mudavadi said.
Meanwhile, more violence was anticipated in Mombasa after the
Government refused to license an Orange rally that was to take place in
Bombolulu today.
The Orange camp insisted it would go on with the rally despite being denied a permit by police.
The team said it would go on with the rally this afternoon at Khadija Primary School grounds.
Orange leaders yesterday claimed the Government was scheming to stop
them from holding rallies across the country in the final days to the
referendum.
Addressing a press conference in Kisumu, Nyong'o and Mudavadi said
the move was aimed at intimidating voters ahead of the November 21 poll.
The leaders claimed a plot had been hatched to ensure an Orange
rally scheduled for Nairobi did not take place. The group's campaigns
in Western Province would also be frustrated, they claimed.
Nyong'o said the 'Yes' team was asked by foreign envoys to furnish
them with campaign schedules but refused, saying they were too busy.
"Now they are interfering with Orange campaigns."
Nyong'o accused police of brutalising 'No' supporters to intimidate
voters. He wondered why the police were violently dispersing 'No'
rallies, yet they had been furnished with schedules of the meetings.
"We condemn the killing of innocent people by police. This is unfortunate. It is excessive use of State power," he said.
Nyong'o challenged President Mwai Kibaki to campaign as an
individual and not as the President, and accused the Head of State of
abusing Government resources.
International Commission of Jurists chairman Otiende Amolo said
there was no need for licenses for rallies once the Electoral
Commission of Kenya blew the whistle for campaigns.
Otiende said police action in Likoni, Mombasa on Friday, was meant to cause chaos.

Original_Search_Title: President Confines Business to State House
Headline: Kenya: President Confines Business to State House
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki's expected campaign blitz at the Coast turned into a two-day session at the State House, Mombasa.
The President's itinerary changed from rallies to meetings with
groups of delegates, leaving residents of areas he was expected
disappointed.
Ahead of Kibaki's arrival on Thursday, Banana MPs had asked the
region to anticipate a massive 'Yes' rally in Mvita, the constituency
of minister Najib Balala, who is leading the Orange movement at the
Coast.
Banana MPs were looking forward to countering Balala in his own turf.
According to the original unconfirmed schedule, the President was to
visit Kilifi, where Lands minister Amos Kimunya had done the ground
work a week earlier by issuing title deeds to squatters.
Kimunya had camped in Mombasa for a week and was seen as preparing the ground for Kibaki to announce goodies for Coast Province.
The Malindi Banana team had also told a local church that the President would be their guest in a fund-raiser this morning.
But as Kibaki jetted into Mombasa on Thursday, it became clear that he would be confined in State House.
The earlier mentioned rallies were not listed in his programme on
Friday and yesterday. Instead, on Friday, the President met Muslim
delegates led by Supkem Mombasa chairman Sheikh Juma Ngao.
Yesterday, the President had a long session with delegates from all districts at the Coast led by Banana MPs from the region.
He instructed Attorney General Amos Wako to hasten the issuance of
ID cards and birth certificates and announced the government would take
up major development projects.
But he was missing in the streets of the coastal town where hundreds had earlier lined up to meet him.
The delegates' meetings appeared to be fully-fledged rallies within
State House grounds but locals could only follow the proceedings on TV.
Kibaki's entourage was small, consisting of Internal Security
minister John Michuki and Roads assistant minister Joshua Toro and MPs
who met Kibaki at State House.
Banana politicians could not explain why the President had decided
not to hold rallies but some argued the meetings with delegates would
bring him closer to the Banana team in the region.
Some of the Banana MPs said they too were surprised by Kibaki's decision to remain within State House.
"We had prepared the people to expect the President. Now this visit
looks low key and it is the last before the vote," said an assistant
minister in the Banana camp.
The Banana team at the Coast expects to hold at least four rallies before November 21.
Meanwhile, First Lady Lucy Kibaki has urged Kenyans to vote for the proposed new constitution.
Addressing wananchi at Mwembe Tayari, Mackinon and Kongowea markets
in Mombasa yesterday, the First Lady said the new constitution would
ensure national resources were distributed equitably.
"We need to adopt the draft constitution which will guarantee a
better future. Appropriate systems will ensure wananchi are treated
equally irrespective of their socio-economic status," the First Lady
said.
She stressed that by voting for the Wako Draft, Kenyans would help the government fulfil its pledge of a better constitution.
The First Lady said opponents of the proposed constitution were
involved in the production of the document right from the beginning but
had now changed their minds because they discovered it would not serve
their interests.
Said the First Lady: "These leaders opposing this document are not
genuine. It seems they have had a change of heart after realising the
new document will empower wananchi."

Original_Search_Title: President's Date With Teachers Flops
Headline: Kenya: President's Date With Teachers Flops
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The much-publicised meeting between President Mwai Kibaki and
primary school head teachers turned chaotic after the Head of State
failed to show up.
The more than 18,000 disappointed teachers, who had travelled from
various parts of the country for the official launch of the Kenya
Primary Schools Head Teachers Association said the President had
disappointed them.
The launch was to be presided over by Kibaki, who is in Mombasa.
Even Education minister Prof George Saitoti was absent.
Education assistant minister Beth Mugo tried to calm the teachers,
assuring them they would meet the President at State House tomorrow.
The meeting ended after Mugo inaugurated the head teachers' association.
Most of the teachers travelled back to their homes after realising they could not stay in the city over the weekend.
But some of the teachers' representatives, remained.
Most of the teachers had travelled to Nairobi overnight and hoped to meet the President.
Mugo denied claims the Government had invited the teachers to Nairobi to woo them for the 'Yes' vote.
"It is a coincidence that the inauguration comes just days to the
referendum. The Government has no plans to woo them to vote for 'Yes',"
she said.
Amid shouts of "Ndizi", she told the teachers the President was held
up in Banana campaigns at the Coast and would see them on Monday.
But the teachers demanded to be refunded for the money they had used for transport to State House.
"We are disappointed. The chairman assured us that the government
would refund the money. He assured us that the President would address
us," said Sammy Kitur from Nandi North.
Most of the teachers said they would return to their homes as they had no money for accommodation.
Officials of the union convened an impromptu meeting to discuss the issue.
They said they had been promised everything would be catered for, yet they were not even given lunch.
All they got was a copy of the Wako Draft. Some vowed to reject the Proposed New Constitution.
"Why should they (officials) lie to the President that we are 'Yes'
supporters when we are firmly in the Orange camp?" posed Joseph Obadha
from Nyanza.
The disgruntled teachers said they had hired vehicles to ferry them
to the venue after promising owners they would pay after the trip.
They said they were assured they would receive "envelops' from the Presdent.
The teachers, who had a list of demands for Kibaki and Saitoti, said
they were demoralised as the Government was not keen on addressing
their plight.
Waithaka said the teachers were concerned over their pay, which he
said was not commensurate with their work. "Teachers feel demoralised
by their low pay, yet they are expected to handle large classrooms
following the introduction of free primary education," he said.
He asked the Government to include Kepsha representatives in the provincial and district education boards.
Some of the teachers said they suspected the meeting with Kibaki was
a ploy by the government to use them to influence their stand on the
Wako Draft.
"Why should the President invite us to State House if it is true
this launch is to address professional issues," said a teacher from
Kirinyaga.

Original_Search_Title: Movers, Shakers And the DP Factor in Local Politics
Headline: Kenya: Movers, Shakers And the DP Factor in Local Politics
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Meru has become famous as the homeground of many of the most powerful individuals in President Kibaki's government.
Kenyans normally focus on Justice minister Kiraitu Murungi and
Treasury boss David Mwiraria when it comes to powermen from Meru. There
is no question that the two have a lot of clout as well as enjoying the
ear of the President. But there is another very powerful player as well
who, by the nature of his job, prefers being in the background: Head of
the Civil Service Francis Muthaura.
Unlike Mr Mwiraria, Mr Murungi was not in the Democratic Party (DP)
at the beginning. He was originally firmly in Ford-Kenya; an active
member of the cosmopolitan Young Turks that included Paul Muite, James
Orengo, Michael Wamalwa and others. He only joined the DP after
leadership tussles in Ford-Kenya turned ugly and forced others like Mr
Muite and Prof Anyang' Nyong'o to flee as well.
Even then, the DP had that stuffy and geriatric feel, comprising
conservative men who formed a sort of a country club set. Mr Murungi
represented a different breed, younger and more aggressive. The fact
that the DP was desperate for this kind of makeover caused him to be
received with both arms.
Mr Mwiraria is the typical DP man: Low-key, conservative,
technocratic. In fact, the minister has a background as a bureaucrat
and a technocrat. A statistician by training, he worked for many years
with the East African Community in Arusha and later on in high-ranking
capacities in the civil service, eventually rising to permanent
secretary in the ministries of Education and Home Affairs. He is an
alumni of Makerere University like President Kibaki and the two have
been friends for a long time.
Though it is Mr Murungi who normally catches the headlines
nationally, the person chairing the Banana campaign in the Meru region
is Mr Mwiraria. The minister also chairs the caucus of Meru Narc MPs.
While Mr Murungi has acquired a reputation of making controversial
gaffes, to the extent where the Orange camp has gleefully come to
demonise him, Mr Mwiraria will never be caught speaking out of turn.
Such are his connections that when the Anglo-Leasing mess spilled over
into the public domain last year and the Treasury PS, Mr Joseph Magari,
and his Home Affairs counterpart, Mr Sylvester Mwaliko, got sacrificed,
Mr Mwiraria was spared.
Mr Muthaura is the archetypal bureaucrat - silent, self-effacing and
inscrutable. But even if his powerful hand is quietly directing the
Banana show nationally, as has sometimes been alleged, it has been near
impossible to pin a finger on him.
For many years, he served as ambassador in several postings, before
former President Moi unexpectedly tapped him to become the first
secretary-general of the East African Community when it was revived in
the 1990s. Once his term in Arusha was over, he was recalled and
promoted to permanent secretary.
When President Kibaki came to power, he inherited from the Moi
regime Dr Sally Kosgei as Head of the Civil Service. The new government
retained her for a while, before bringing in Mr Muthaura.
Meru has become the envy of other regions because of the top-notch
appointments that have been made from there. In addition to the two
ministers, the area has no less than four permanent secretaries. They
are Gerishom Ikiara (Transport ministry), Erastus Mwongera (Home
Affairs), Kiriinya Mukira (Lands and Housing), in addition to Mr
Muthaura.
The remarkable thing is that all of them come from Meru Central.
Incidentally this is where Mr Mwiraria and Mr Murungi also come from.
No other district in the country, including the President's own Nyeri,
has such a high concentration of the government's top cream.
Ironically, this lopsided concentration of top men has caused a
great deal of resentment within the other Meru districts - Meru South,
Meru North and Tharaka. In particular, the person who has been
articulating the resentment most forcefully is Ntonyiri MP Maoke Maore,
who happens to be the sole Meru MP in the Orange camp.
His beef is that the other three districts have had a raw deal in
the distribution of government appointments. Meru Central district is
dominated by the Imenti, the most populous and well-heeled of the Meru
sub-tribes.
Out of the nine MPs from the greater Meru region, Mr Maore is also
the only one who is in Kanu. He was formerly in the DP, before jumping
ship in 2001 to join Kanu when every indication was that Uhuru Kenyatta
was going places.
There are two other MPs who were technically not in Narc. One is
Tharaka MP Francis Kagwima (Ford Asili) and Tigania East MP Peter Munya.
Both, however, have staunchly been pro-government and totally in the Banana camp.

Original_Search_Title: Religious Courts to Influence Region's Vote
Headline: Kenya: Religious Courts to Influence Region's Vote
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Unlike other regions where political affiliations are determining
views on the proposed new Constitution, religion has emerged as a major
issue in the referendum campaigns in what used to be called the
Northern Frontier district - North Eastern province - including Isiolo,
Marsabit and Moyale districts in Eastern province.
Cabinet minister Mahamud Abdi Mohamed and assistant minister Dr Adan
Kuti - leading lights in the Banana campaign in Nep - admit that
religion is a major issue in the referendum campaigns. But they claim
that Imams and Sheiks have been incited to mislead the Muslim faithful
against the proposed new Constitution.
Divisions among Muslim leaders and their followers have spilled into
the public arena in a manner uncommon amongst Muslims and their
spiritual leaders.
The altercation culminated in the Chief Kadhi, Sheikh Hamad Kassim,
engaging in public exchanges with Cabinet ministers Chirau Ali Mwakwere
(Foreign Affairs) and Mahamud Abdi (Regional Development) when he urged
Muslims to reject the proposed law.
Mr Mwakwere was so incensed he publicly called for the sacking of
the Chief Kadhi. Mr Mahamud was more restrained, only urging the Chief
Kadhi to restrict himself to adjudication of disputes and
interpretation of the personal law, and keep off politics.
At the centre of the dispute is Kadhi's courts that opponents of the
proposed new Constitution argue have been downgraded from the proposals
in the Bomas Draft.
On the other hand was a rebellion led by evangelical Christian
Churches who were preaching scare sermons about the threat of Sharia
law.
A compromise was eventually reached to retain the Kadhi's courts as they have always been.
"Muslims have good grounds to reject this document. At Bomas,
Muslims asked for the expansion of the Kadhi's courts to include an
appeal court. The courts are very important to Muslims, especially
women and children who are the main beneficiaries. Instead, we were
lumped together with Christians, traditional and Hindu courts" says Ms
Nazleen Rajput, of the National Muslim Council of Kenya.
Ms Rajput adds that provisions on gender equality are also
unacceptable. "Sharia law empowers women and protects them from
exploitation. The provisions are going to take away some of these
protections," says Ms Rajput, an activist who runs one of the largest
Islamic NGOs.
However, Mr Mahamud called on Muslims to ignore the NGO, claiming it
was being financed by Americans through the United States Agency for
International Development (USaid), "and the American's attitude towards
President Kibaki's government is well known".
Ms Rajput dismisses attempts to cast aspersions on her connection
with USaid as diversionary. "We have heard this before. We are a
development organisation and we decide what help we want. USaid does
not tell us what to do."

Original_Search_Title: Ethnic Conflict Spills Over to Campaigns in the North
Headline: Kenya: Ethnic Conflict Spills Over to Campaigns in the North
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Religion and ethnicity are likely to influence significantly how
the people of Upper North Eastern Kenya - Isiolo, Marsabit and Moyale
districts - vote in the referendum.
The recent skirmishes that hit Marsabit district caused a major
poltical shift, in which all the four MPs from the Borana community in
the region moved to the Banana camp. Mr Abdi Sasura (Saku), Dr Guracha
Galgalo (Moyale), Dr Mohammed Kuti (Isiolo North) and Mr Mohammed
Bahari (Isiolo South), teamed up with Mr Titus Ngoyoni (Laisamis), from
the minority Rendile communuty, to isolate former Cabinet minister Dr
Bonaya Godana (North Horr), who is a Gabra. Dr Godana, the deputy
Official Opposition leader, has been a key political figure in the
region in the last few years.
Mr Bahari has since moved back to the Orange camp. His political
rival, former MP Abdulahi Wako, has been busy on the campaign trail for
the No vote. The shifts indicate the political underrcurrents since the
Turbi massacre.
The vast but sparsely populated region comprises Marsabit, Isiolo,
and Moyale districts in Eastern province. It has six constituencies
with 108,920 registered voters. Marsabit, the largest district, has
Saku, Laisamis and North Horr constituencies while Moyale is a district
constituency. Isiolo is divided into Isiolo North and Isiolo South
constituencies.
Majority of the inhabitants are Borana, followed by the Gabra and the Rendile.
There is a considerable population of Burji in Marsabit and Moyale.
Other minority communities in the region include Gari of Moyale and
Sakuye of Isiolo. There is also a big population of the Meru people in
Isiolo as well as Samburu and Turkana.
The Borana and Gabra have been embroiled in conflict over control of
scarce resources for their livestock in Marsabit. This resulted into a
war that heightened in July this year. More than 90 people were killed
at Turbi trading centre in North Horr.
When the Wako Draft was released, the four Borana MPs and Mr Ngoyoni called a press conference to declare their support for it.
Given this background and the turn of events, how are people in this region expected to vote?
With the Borana MPs supporting Yes, all Gabras have vowed to vote
No. Gabra occupy North Horr, which has 17,633 voters. There is a small
population of Boranas in the constituency.
Despite the stand taken by their leaders, not all Boranas are
supporting the proposed Constitution. Many of them profess the Islamic
faith and may follow the path set by their religious leaders.
It is also notable that the Orange camp has been bolstered by two
former MPs from the Borana community. Mr Charfano Guyo Mokku, a former
Isiolo North MP, is the Orange point man in the district, while Mr
Jillo Falana who lost to Mr Sasura in Saku is campaigning for a No vote
in Marsabit. Mr Falana is remembered for being a key player in the
parliamentary initiative that gave birth to the Constitution review.
Nevertheless, the Banana group is still a force to reckon with. In
Isiolo North, for instance, there is a large Meru population among the
30,926 million voters. Indeed, the constituency was between 1983 and
1988 represented by a Meru. Saku, with 15,881 registered voters, is
also pretty cosmopolitan.
In Laisamis with 15,594 voters, the community leaders in the area
occupied by the Rendille, Samburu and Turkana are in the No group. They
attended the Suswa meeting where Maasai leaders urged the pastoralists
to reject the Draft.
Muslim preachers and leaders have cite the lumping together of
Kadhi's courts with other religious courts, including traditional
courts, and provisions on equal property rights as unfavourable to
their faith. But those supporting the Draft say it is progressive.

Original_Search_Title: Bribery And Tyranny Rife, Says Nyong'o
Headline: Kenya: Bribery And Tyranny Rife, Says Nyong'o
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A Cabinet minister has accused President Kibaki of allowing his government to "degenerate into tyranny and bribery".
"The President has lost ground and his rule is fast degenerating
into one of tyranny," Prof Anyang' Nyong'o of Planning and National
Development told a news conference in Kisumu yesterday.
"He no longer pays any regard to the rule of law and presides over an increasingly flawed system of governance."
The minister said the recent incidents of shooting of opponents of
the proposed Constitution and the creation of additional districts
showed an "insensitive leadership that no longer has a place for
democratic principles."
This "bad precedent" had ruined the image of Mr Kibaki's presidency,
he said, and urged him to separate his function as head of state from
that of a Banana campaigner.
The minister's remarks came as the Orange team claimed that there was a plot by the State to frustrate their campaigns.
Former Vice-President Musalia Mudavadi said several rallies lined up
for Western province and Nyayo Stadium, Nairobi, had been outlawed by
police for unexplained reasons. Former Cabinet minister Chris Okemo
said the Government had "questionably" released about Sh200 million to
some contractors to boost its campaigns.
International Commission of Jurists chairman Otiende Amolo said it
was unlawful for police to break up a rally after the Electoral
Commission declared the official campaign period early this month.
Assistant ministers Andrew Ligale and George Khaniri and MPs Oburu
Odinga, Ken Nyagudi, Eric Nyamunga, Ayiecho Olweny and Jakoyo Midiwo
also attended the press briefing.

Original_Search_Title: Accusations Fly As Coast Rally Death Toll Rises
Headline: Kenya: Accusations Fly As Coast Rally Death Toll Rises
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The death toll in the Mombasa referendum campaign violence rose to
four yesterday as the rival Yes and No teams traded accusations over
who was responsible for the chaos.
Two more victims of the violence died at the Coast General Hospital.
One died on Friday night while the second one died yesterday morning.
The administrator of Coast Provincial General Hospital, Dr Khadija Shikely, confirmed the deaths.
President Kibaki expressed disappointment at the violence that
rocked Likoni where two people were shot dead and a DO's office burnt
down, saying that those who resorted to violence had gone astray.
"Burning down a DO's office is just a waste of time because the
Government has resources to build a new and better one," he told the
delegations from all the seven districts in Coast Province who went to
State House to declare support for the proposed new Constitution.
Even as the camps traded accusations, a man was shot and injured
ahead of political rally attended by three Ministers in Nandi yesterday.
The Banana and the Orange teams clashed at Kobujoi trading centre in Nandi South.
The rally was attended by Cabinet ministers Kipruto Kirwa, Njeru Ndwiga, Amos Kimunya and Assistant minister Stephen Tarus.
Mr Joash Koskei, a casual worker with Wu Yi Construction Company, said he was shot by a local politician.
National Heritage minister Najib Balala consoles Mr Patrick Onzere
at the Coast General Hospital. Mr Onzere sustained bullet wounds during
the No campaign rally at the Likoni Caltex grounds. Photos/Gideon
Maundu and Jack Owuor
Addressing the rally, the politician said he did so to save his life.
He said the police watched as the Orange supporters stoned them and he had no alternative but to use his gun.
At a press conference in Nairobi, Orange campaign leaders and the
Law Society of Kenya blamed police commissioner Hussein Ali for the
Mombasa killings.
Orange leaders, who included Cabinet ministers Raila Odinga, Kalonzo
Musyoka and Najib Balala and Leader of Official Opposition Uhuru
Kenyatta, said the police commssioner should be held responsible.
But at another press conference in Mombasa, Government Spokesman
Alfred Mutua blamed Orange leaders for the violence, saying they "have
blood on their hands."
Dr Mutua, who addressed the media from the burnt-out shell of the
Likoni DO's office in Mombasa, said an estimated 10,000 stones were
thrown into the building. He said at least 10 police officers were
injured.
Asked why the police again used live bullets, Dr Mutua claimed that the rioters were trying to break into the armoury.
"The Orange supporters broke into the station and threw burning
tyres inside in an attempt to kill the officers. They also tried to
break into the armoury and steal guns, and the police shot back in
self-defence and to protect the guns from getting to the wrong hands,"
he claimed. Dr Mutua said that several police uniforms were stolen.
The Orange leaders, led by Mr Odinga and Mr Balala, held peaceful
rallies in Kinango, Kwale, Matuga, Msambweni and Ukunda townships
earlier in the day before police dispersed their rally in Likoni.
"The Government will take stern measures against the politicians who
organised this violence, and the public should be aware that the police
put their lives on the line every day to protect us from criminals and
should not be villified against such criminal acts," said Dr Mutua.
The Central Organisation of Trade Unions also backed calls that Maj-Gen Ali should be held responsible for the killings.
At their press conference at the Cabanas restaurant in Nairobi,
Orange leaders asked why their rallies were being cancelled on the
pretext that the President was visiting certain areas.
In a statement read by Kanu nominated MP Mutula Kilonzo, the leaders
said: "This is the time for the Electoral Commission to not only
condemn but also to order for the arrest of not only police officers
involved but also the Commissioner of Police himself."
"After the Kisumu killings the Commissioner himself said he had no
apologies to make ... once you tell your troops you have no apologies
it is understandable they will continue shooting," the statement added.
Also present were MPs Joseph Kamotho, Fred Gumo, Billow Kerrow,
Kenneth Marende, Reuben Ndolo, Amina Abdalla, David Mwenje and William
Omondi. Eldoret North MP William Ruto also signed the statement.
At a separate press conference, LSK chairman Tom Ojienda said
Maj-Gen Ali should resign or be sacked if he is unable to control his
officers.
Mr Ojienda, who was speaking at the College of Insurance, asked the
commissioner to arrest and charge Mombasa police bosses with murder,
causing grievous harm, assault and destruction of property.
Yesterday, ECK vice-chairman Gabriel Mukele said the commission was
collecting information on what had happened in Likoni on Friday and
would make its step known tomorrow.
Last week, the commission warned that it would go to court to stop the campaigns if another Kenyan died.
Mr Mukele gave the warning while giving a ruling on the Kisumu
violence that left four people -including two schoolboys - dead and an
incident in Kakamega that left Ikolomani MP Bonny Khalwale's car burnt.
Yesterday, the Orange leaders said the Government planned to cancel
their rallies this week in Western Province because President Kibaki
would be in Kakamega. The rallies are set for Bumula, Lugari, Webuye,
Sirisia and Bungoma.

Original_Search_Title: Lessons Our Leaders Ought to Learn
Headline: Kenya: Lessons Our Leaders Ought to Learn
Body_Text:
Nairobi — This referendum business is causing a lot of people to lose their
heads. In particular, the recent behaviour of two ministers has become
a matter of some concern. I am talking of Environment Minister Kalonzo
Musyoka and Special Programmes Minister Njenga Karume.
Let's start with Mr Musyoka. There is something the aspiring
presidential candidate needs most to remember: wise people know when to
hold their tongues. He especially needs to guard against airing
confidences shared to him by other public figures which can only place
those same figures in very embarrassing positions.
Nothing illustrates this more perfectly than the minister's
"disclosure" - at a public rally in Mwingi last week - of a
conversation he claimed to have had with Attorney-General Amos Wako
sometime ago. The gist of what Mr Musyoka said was that Mr Wako had
approached him to complain about "these people" (obviously meaning the
Banana power circle of ministers) who supposedly had tampered with the
draft constitution by inserting a clause that bars a presidential
candidate from contesting simultaneously for a parliamentary seat.
My concern is not whether Mr Wako actually said those words , though
the A-G was afterwards quick to deny there was any late "tampering"
with the draft. What bothers me is when a senior public figure blithely
exposes confidential conversations he had with other senior figures at
public barazas. It shows a dismaying lack of concern for the other
person. Irrespective of what Mr Wako may feel of his current political
masters, he certainly has no wish to hear those reservations narrated
carelessly at a political rally. It raises unnecessary suspicions about
him and his trustworthiness at those levels higher up. That is not
something any public figure who is keen on playing it safe wants to be
burdened with.
As it were, Mr Musyoka is already saying that the clause in question
was specifically inserted to frustrate him. He believes he is the
person the State House crowd actually fear. The Banana bigwigs were
reportedly more amused than irritated by all this. The fact of the
matter was that this clause was there even in the Bomas draft, long
before the later "tampering" Mr Musyoka complains of occurred.
It is on record that Mr Musyoka again went public - in Thika
recently - with yet another "revelation" about a confidential
conservation he claimed he had with, of all people, Special Programmes
Minister Njenga Karume sometime after President Kibaki came to power.
In a nutshell, Mr Karume purportedly told him to speak to President
Kibaki on his (Karume's) behalf and tell him the amended version of the
constitution the government was keen on was unpopular. Mr Karume has
not bothered to comment on these assertions.
Whatever the case, it is difficult to believe Mr Karume would have
at any time sought Mr Musyoka as his intermediary to get to President
Kibaki. Certainly not even after they parted ways when Mr Karume joined
Kanu. I recall that one of the very first private functions President
Kibaki attended soon after coming to power was the funeral in Kiambu of
Mr Karume's wife. That was long before the President brought him on
board in the so-called government of national unity (GNU).
And now we can turn to Mr Karume and his latest, inexplicable
pre-occupations. I am at a loss as to why he thought it necessary to
meet and solicit Banana support from former Mungiki head honcho Ndura
Waruinge. Mr Karume prides himself in being an accomplished Mr Fix-It
who President Kibaki can rely on at critical moments. What the elderly
gentleman perhaps needs to know is that some groups must always be kept
at arm's length, even when a situation is desperate. There is no value
in tying yourself to such groups. Whatever political mileage expected
is illusory.
Mr Karume may not know that Germany is reeling from a "hung"
election, where no party was able to muster a decisive parliamentary
majority. But none of the "respectable" mainstream parties were willing
to break the stalemate by getting into a coalition with the party of
the Left that absorbed the communist remnants of the former East
Germany. It was a question of political principle. Never mind that
those leftists are nowhere as controversial as Mungiki.
Mr Waruinge now professes to be a Christian pastor. But in the public mind his old associations keep recurring.
Another verbal gem from Robert Mugabe: "The man's name is Dell. When
I think of it I spell it with a "H" and that is where he should go."
Christopher Dell is America's ambassador to Zimbabwe

Original_Search_Title: Yes Ministers Dare Rivals On Protest Threat
Headline: Kenya: Yes Ministers Dare Rivals On Protest Threat
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Banana team yesterday dared their Orange rivals to make good their threat to stage protests after the November 21 poll.
Cabinet ministers Chris Murungaru, Charity Ngilu, Njenga Karume and
George Saitoti said the Government would counter any move aimed at
causing anarchy.
A Yes supporter wears a hat of bananas to show his support during a campaign rally in Keroka, Nyamira district.
Photo by Sylvester Onyango
"The Government will not take lightly threats made by the Orange
group that they will call their supporters to protest at Uhuru Park,"
they said.
They were speaking at Ngong Stadium in Kajiado North constituency during a campaign rally.
Said Dr Murungaru: "This is a government that was elected by the
majority during the last General Election, and we will not allow anyone
to bring it down."
The Kibaki administration, he said, had a "strong foundation and cannot be shaken by a few selfish people".
He said in Kiswahili: "Kama ni wanaume na wajaribu tuwaonyeshe (If they are real men, let them try; we'll show them)."
Prof Saitoti recalled that innocent lives were lost during the 1982
coup attempt and said that Kenya should not witness a similar incident.
The leaders were apparently referring to remarks made recently by
Roads minister Raila Odinga and his Environment counterpart Kalonzo
Musyoka that passing the proposed laws could usher Kenyans into a
culture of revolt. Mr Karume accused the No group of being behind the
Likoni violence to ridicule the President who was visiting Mombasa.
"These people lack discipline; they should first learn to behave before they fight for the presidency," he said.
The Special Programmes minister asked the Government to question
Orange ministers over the violence. Mrs Ngilu accused Mr Musyoka and
other Orange ministers of undermining the Government.
"Who can be led by these people who are only self-seekers? They
should wait for the 2007 General Election and fight for their
parliamentary seats first, which I am sure they will not get," she said.
Elsewhere, Energy minister Simeon Nyachae attributed opposition to
the proposed Constitution to a sense of bitterness over the memorandum
of understanding signed before the 2002 elections.
Addressing a Yes rally at Marani shopping centre in Kitutu Chache
constituency, Mr Nyachae claimed the broken promise that Mr Odinga
would be given the prime minister's job was the driving force behind
the No group. Water minister Martha Karua, addressing a rally in
Londiani, Kericho district, asked Mr Odinga to respect President Kibaki
the way he would have wished to be respected if he were the President.
She was accompanied by ministers Amos Kimunya and John Koech as well
as assistant ministers Alicen Chelaite and Kipkalya Kones. In Kakamega,
Local Government minister Musikari Kombo condemned the Likoni violence
and accused some leaders of loving anarchy. He spoke after receiving a
group of Kanu leaders from Vihiga district who said they had had
defected from the Orange camp.

Original_Search_Title: Ngilu Bounces Back in Door-to-Door Campaign
Headline: Kenya: Ngilu Bounces Back in Door-to-Door Campaign
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Health minister Charity Ngilu yesterday went on a door-to-door
campaign and described her long absence from referendum rallies as a
"strategic move."
"I had gone back to the drawing board to strategise and counter the
lies that are being peddled by the No team, especially in Ukambani,"
she said.
Mrs Ngilu declared that she was firmly in the Banana camp and sought
to allay fears that she had decamped to the Orange side following her
absence from the referendum campaigns.
"A lot of lies have been peddled that I am about to ditch the Banana camp for the Orange one...
"This is just mere propaganda and wishful thinking by those scared
by my popularity, especially in Ukambani, and other parts of the
country."
In her campaign in Makueni and Kitui districts yesterday, she appealed to residents to vote for the proposed Constitution.
Speaking to the Nation, Mrs Ngilu said: "This is a battle that I'm
confident the Yes team will win with ease since the No team has lost
ground for peddling lie after lie (on the proposed laws) to the
electorate during their rallies."
Energy minister Simeon Nyachae yesterday said the Orange camp was the stumbling block to a new Constitution.
Speaking at a Banana rally in Nyamira District yesterday, Mr Nyachae
said the Orange campaigners tried to block the country from getting a
new constitution while they were in the Kanu administration.
The minister said the Bomas Draft was written by appointees of the
former regime to serve the interests of the former president's cronies.
The proposed constitution represented views and interests of youths, women and the disabled, he said.
He added that teachers were being misled by the Kenya National Union of Teachers to oppose the new laws.
Nyeri politician Mary Wambui yesterday took her Banana campaign to
Nyamira District, where she accused Orange crusaders of fanning
violence in referendum campaigns.
"They have sensed defeat and are now using youths and those who
served in the past government to cause chaos across the country," she
said.
Speaking at Uhuru Garden, Ms Wambui urged Kenyans to shun the orange
team, saying it was after the leadership through the backdoor.
She urged youths to be wary of politicians who were out to use them for political gain.
Residents of Mochongoi Settlement Scheme in Baringo district would
be issued with title deeds within the next month, said Lands minister
Amos Kimunya.
"Whether you vote Yes or No (in the referendum) does not matter but
it is the duty of the Government to settle its people...no one should
cheat you that the Government will evict you," he said at Mochongoi
trading centre yesterday.
On Thursday, Mr Kimunya said the Banana team was ready to hold talks with their Orange rivals.
Speaking in his constitujency on Thursday, Mr Kimunya said the
Banana team was ready for reconciliation talks with the Orange group.

Original_Search_Title: Ministers Might Face Incitement Charges
Headline: Kenya: Ministers Might Face Incitement Charges
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Key figures in the Orange and Banana camps will be taken to court for using inciting language in their campaigns.
At least five Cabinet ministers could find themselves in the dock,
the Kenya National Commission on Human Rights said yesterday. Another
minister is cited for using unsavoury language.
The ministers are Dr Chris Murungaru, Mr Njeru Ndwiga, Mr Amos
Kimunya, Mr Raila Odinga, Mr Simeon Nyachae and Mr Najib Balala.
However, Mr Odinga and those who used unsavoury language are not lined
up for court.
The names of the ministers, their assistants, members of Parliament
and councillors have been forwarded to the attorney-general for action.
Commission chairman Maina Kiai said that if the AG failed to
prosecute the leaders for incitement or hate campaign, the organisation
would privately prosecute them.
Mr Kiai's organisation, which is appointed by the Government, is
being assisted by the Kenya Human Rights Commission to compile the
list. The latter is an NGO.
Yesterday, however, Mr Odinga and Mr Balala dismissed the commission's report, saying they had done nothing wrong.
Mr Odinga said he had referred to Mr Nyachae as an "old primitive
man" because the Energy minister had used the word "uncircumcised" to
refer to some Orange leaders.
The Roads minister said he had used figurative language to compare
some leaders' behaviour to that of animals, but did not mean they were
beasts.
The commission accuses Mr Odinga of equating the behaviour of some leaders in the Banana camp to that of a dog.
Mr Odinga is quoted as saying: "...is like a dog which drops its
piece of meat thinking that its image in the water is another dog with
a bigger piece".
He said yesterday: "I didn't say they are dogs... that is figurative speech."
Mr Odinga and Mr Balala accused the commission of being partisan.
They both said they had no apologies to make and Mr Balala said he was ready to face the court.
Mr Balala is accused of of saying: "Funds allocated for roads in Coast Province had been diverted to Mt Kenya."
The Mvita MP repeated claims that Nyeri and Meru Central districts
received more funds than other regions. He gave the example of
allocation in water - Sh500 million for Nyeri and Sh495 million for
Meru Central - whereas other constituencies received Sh5 million each.
Dr Murungaru, the Transport minister, is accused of saying that
Nyeri should be at the forefront in the Yes campaign and any rebel
should be dealt with accordingly. "Anybody who wants to behave like a
rebel should be folded. We have no time to flatter demons whose work is
to ruin the scheme of things."
He allegedly uttered the words on October 17 at Runyenjes in Embu District.
On his part, Cooperative minister Ndwiga is cited three times for
using unsavoury language and hate speech. During a rally in Maua
