Stadium, on October 8, he said: "(Mr Kalonzo Musyoka) is lost, Kambas
do not walk with uncircumcised men."
Mr Kimunya (Lands) is quoted asking squatters to continue occupying the late JM Kariuki's land, a court order notwithstanding.
Others named are MPs Mwangi Kiunjuri, Kalembe Ndile, Peter Munya,
Reuben Ndolo, Samuel Moroto, Musa Sirma, Norman Nyagah, Paul Muite,
Alfred Nderitu, Mutahi Kagwe and Gideon Moi.
Mr Kiai said the language used by these leaders was likely to make Kenyan communities turn against each other.
According to Mr Kiai, the utterances were intended or calculated to
promote feelings of hatred or enmity between different races or
communities in Kenya.
The two commissions cited specific instances and places where the
utterances were made. All were captured on tape and officials have
sworn affidavits to this effect.
They cited 13 cases of incitement, 14 incidents of hate speech, and 14 cases of use of unsavoury language.
Some of the remarks border on warning one community of dire consequences depending on the outcome of the referendum vote.
An MP said: "If we pass this Constitution, the Orange team should not step in Meru unless they remove their flags."
Mr Kiai said the statements could incite ethnic clashes similar to what happened in 1992 and 1997.
"We are putting these politicians on notice that such derogatory
speech along ethnic and racial lines is categorised under the list of
offences known as subversion contrary to Section 77 (3) (e) of the
Penal Code and is punishable with a prison term not exceeding three
years," Mr Kiai said.
He was speaking at a news conference accompanied by KHRC director Wanjiku Miano and officials Catherine Muma and Tirop Kitur.
The commission said it had documented the statements in rallies and from the media.
He added: "In each of the cases we are releasing today, we have firm
affidavits from our field monitors attesting to the statements made,
and/or tape recordings of the speeches."
Already, he said, the utterances were creating cracks in society,
and could lead to loss of life, injury or destruction of property.
He told the leaders to take Rwanda as an example of a country where hate speech led to loss of life.
Mr Kiai said the mark of democracy was the tolerance of different and competing views within the citizenry.
"While it is the right of politicians to campaign, and indeed
campaign vigorously for the causes they support, it is imperative that
their campaigns do not cross the line into incitement to violence, hate
speech, unsavoury and distasteful language."
He said it was possible to make a point without inciting or denigrating others.
Said Mr Kiai: "It is particularly disturbing to see the impunity
with which political figures from both the Yes and No sides of the
campaigns have engaged in hate speech deliberately intended or
calculated to promote feelings of hatred and enmity between different
races and communities in Kenya in order to solicit for votes for either
camp."
Last week, the commission released a list of ministers and MPs who misused taxpayers' resources.
Already the commission has taken two figures from the Yes and No
camps to court over incitement and abuse of office. Assistant minister
George Khaniri and Yes secretariat boss Titus Mbathi are yet to plead
to the charges, although the AG has applied to take over the cases.
Mr Kiai said the commission did not harbour malice nor did it wish to cause any politician undue embarrassment.

Original_Search_Title: Two Shot Dead At No Rally
Headline: Kenya: Two Shot Dead At No Rally
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Mombasa police boss Wilfred Mbithi (centre) and his officers use
chairs to shield themselves from stones after stopping a No campaign
rally at Likoni,
Two people were shot dead, scores more injured and a DO's office
burnt to the ground in a new outbreak of Constitution campaign violence.
Chaos erupted after police disrupted an Orange No-vote rally, then engaged the public in running battles.
They fired in the air and used tear gas canisters to disperse
thousands of Orange supporters in Likoni, Mombasa, after declaring
their rally illegal.
Coast Provincial Police boss Gatiba Karanja confirmed the deaths
last night but could not immediately say under what circumstances they
were shot.
One of the dead was identified by Dr JW Daluma of Sau Medical Clinic
as Tobias Ochieng Gwada. His body was loaded onto a police Land Rover
also carrying another person believed to be a victim of the violence.
A third man with head injuries reportedly received during the fracas was admitted at Gorofani Medical Clinic.
And another man with gunshot wounds was treated at Sau Health
services, two kilometres from the rally venue, before being transfered
to Coast General Hospital.
By late last night, an uneasy calm was slowly returning to the area
which was being patrolled by General Service Unit personnel and
anti-riot police.
Two houses and two cars as well as the local district officer's
building plus lines of roadside kiosks were burnt at the height of the
rioting.
Roads minister Raila Odinga, who was driven away by his security team as
Mombasa police boss Wilfred Mbithi stops Cabinet ministers Najib
Balala from addressing the No rally and right, a passenger in a matatu
shouts at the Likoni crowd.
chaos enveloped the rally, later accused the police of using
excessive force, as they had in Kisumu when four people were killed.
The rioting broke out at Caltex grounds, Likoni, yesterday after
area police chief Wilfred Mbithi snatched a microphone from Heritage
minister Najib Balala when the rally was already underway, and
announced the meeting had not been licensed.
He said the rally was cancelled and ordered the crowd to disperse.
Mr Balala retorted that all Orange campaign meetings had the
blessing of the Electoral Commission of Kenya, but as the argument
intensified, the crowd reacted first by heckling, then by throwing
stones, causing the police to fire tear gas canisters to disperse them.
Youths barricaded roads with burning tyres and fought the police who reacted with more tear gas and clubs.
Another contingent of police was called in to reinforce the security team as a fierce battle began.
At one point furious youths surrounded the police, who fired in the air to scare them away.
As the fighting broke out, Mr Odinga and MPs Billow Kerrow, Ramadhan
Kajembe and Lukas Maitha were whisked away by their security details
who sped off towards the Likoni channel.
Traffic to the ferry from the South Coast was disrupted as police
reinforcements were called in, and stalls lining the road caught fire
and burnt to the ground.
Earlier, the Orange team had forced its way into the Caltex grounds after police tried to prevent them.
A convoy of five police vehicles had blocked the entrance to the
grounds and the Orange convoy was intercepted by Mr Mbithi, who tried
to convince them not to go ahead with the meeting.
Mr Balala and others in the convoy got out of their vehicles at the
police cordon and marched into the grounds accompanied by hundreds of
supporters.
One of the rally conveners, Mr Hisham Mwidani, and councillor Ali
Mwakunyapa unsuccessfully tried to ask the police to clear the road for
the Orange team.
Mr Mwidani and Mr Mwakunyapa said their rally was licensed more than
two weeks ago and they did not understand why the police were
attempting to prevent it. The Orange team was forced to calm its
charged supporters, who were taunting the police due to the stand-off.
For about 30 minutes, vehicle movements on the Likoni-Lunga Lunga road was blocked by a huge crowd of Orange supporters.
Mr Balala was first to address the meeting, saying the Orange team
was engaged in a peaceful campaign calling on local residents to reject
the proposed Constitution in the November 21 referendum, because it did
not address their interests.
He said they respected President Kibaki but, unlike the Banana
Yes-vote team which had resorted to bribing voters in State House, the
Orange team was going to people directly.
It was at that point that the police chief seized the microphone and announced the rally was illegal and had been cancelled.
Later, Mr Odinga, accusing the police of overreacting and claiming
that two people had been shot, said Mr Mwidau had notified the police
of a change in the rally programme which they had acknowledged.
The minister said police had told him the Likoni rally had been
cancelled because it had been decided that no rally should be held in
Mombasa until President Kibaki left the town for Nairobi, on Sunday.
He added: "But Likoni is far away from State House and we do not see how we were going to interfere with the Head of State.
The Orange team will today address rallies in Ganze constituency of
assistant minister Joseph Kingi. The rally has been organised by former
area MP Noah Katana Ngala.
Mr Kerrow, Kanu's finance spokesman, claimed the Banana team planned
to saturate the countryside with Sh2 billion just days before the
referendum to influence the outcome.
The money would allegedly be distributed the last 48 hours of the
campaign to confuse voters to back the Draft, he alleged during an
Orange rally in Kinango, Kwale District.
Mr Odinga supported the claims, saying MPs in the Banana camp had
been given money by the Government and accused their rivals of dishing
out the money aimlessly.
"If they come to you, please take the money but on the voting day, choose the Orange," said Mr Odinga.
He said the Draft gave the President excessive powers. "He will be
like a king who has powers even to snatch one's wife," said Mr Odinga.
Meanwhile, a section of Coast Muslim leaders who met President
Kibaki at the Mombasa State House, declared support for the proposed
Constitution, saying it catered for Muslims.
They said that contrary to earlier reports, the majority of Muslims
in Coast Province agreed that the Constitution reflected their
interests and they would vote for it during the referendum.
The Muslim leaders, drawn from all districts in the province - led
by Supreme Council of Kenya Muslims Coast chairman Sheikh Juma Ngao and
senior Muslim scholar Sheikh Mohammed Mussalam - called for sober
reflection away from emotions.
Muslim youths and women representatives said the Constitution was
good for them because it empowered them and assured them of their
rights.
Responding to issues raised at the meeting, President Kibaki
reassured them that the Government was streamlining the issue of birth
certificates and national identity cards in the province.
On squatters and absentee landlords, President Kibaki reiterated
that the Government had identified idle land where landless people
would be settled.
The President also gave pledges on how education in the area will be improved.
The much talked about Coast University would become a reality when the budget allowed, he said.
He also reaffirmed the Government's commitment to the revival and
rehabilitation of stalled projects in Coast province, including the
Kenya Meat Commission at Kibarani, Kilifi Cashewnut factory, Ramisi
Sugar Factory in Kwale and the Kenya Cooperative Creameries factory at
Mariakani.

Original_Search_Title: A Woman of Substance
Headline: Kenya: A Woman of Substance
Body_Text:
Nairobi — BERTHA KANG'ONG'OI has a chat with Nyamko Sabuni, the soft-spoken Swedish Member of Parliament
It's 2.30 Tuesday afternoon, at the Association of Media Women in
Kenya's offices in Hughes building, Nairobi. We are waiting for a
Swedish female Member of Parliament. But that's not any news, the
notable thing about this particular MP is that she is African - born
(and partly bred) on the continent. She is Congolese but also now a
citizen in Sweden.
It's about a quarter to three when a slightly-built woman, in a
striking red blouse and white linen pants walks in, accompanied by a
man. She looks... well, ordinary, like the girl next door. She is
simple in dress, though with a good sense of style but it's hard to
imagine that she is the European legislator that we have been waiting
for.
Well, it turns out that she is, indeed, the Swedish Member of
Parliament. My imagination and expectation of a larger-than-life,
do-you-know-who-I-am personality are dashed.
Nyamko Sabuni finds her seat, places her banana fibre handbag on the
floor and then places her arms on the table as though to say, 'here I
am. Fire the questions.'
36-year-old Nyamko Sabuni is soft-spoken but eloquent. She is doing
her first four-year term as an MP in the Riksdag, the Swedish
parliament, on a Liberal Party ticket. She sits on the parliament's
trade and industry committee. This is a great achievement for a girl
who only went to Sweden for the first time in 1981.
"My family and I went to Sweden in 1981 on exile," she says when she
finally starts to talk about herself. "I was only 12 years old then.
Both my parents are from Congo and Sweden was not the first country
that my family had taken refuge in," she adds as a by-the-way remark.
"I was actually born in Burundi where the family had fled to at that
time! My Dad had controversial political leaning and was imprisoned in
the Congo at some point. He thought it wise to have his family in
Burundi away from all the turmoil back home."
A group of about 10 of us has gathered around a table, listening and
asking questions though at first we all seem to take our time to
reconcile this small woman to the big title!
Sabuni prefers to speak in Swedish, having the man accompanying her
interpret, though when I later sit down with her, she talks to me in
English. "I express myself better in Swedish, although I can speak
English and a little Swahili," she explains to excuse herself, toying
with her gold necklace.
In 1981, the Sabuni family was able to get asylum in Sweden. "For
security reasons, my Dad was cautious about having the family settle in
a minority groups neighbourhood so we settled in a predominantly white
suburb and only later did I realise how much that helped shape me for
my current political career."
Sabuni learnt the Swedish language early and easily and so
assimilation was easy for her. "Those around me made me feel like one
of them. I was never made to feel that I was an African, that I did not
belong. It was only much later in life that I found out that some
people in Sweden were actually marginalised and discriminated against
because of their race."
Perhaps that is when Sabuni's dream of becoming a lawyer was
conceived. "I love to agitate, argue and convince others of my point of
view," she says with a smile. "Perhaps that's why studying law came
naturally for me."
But she was in law school for just two years. "I was offered a job
by a PR firm. The deal was too good and I decided to quit school and
start working."
It was while working at the PR firm that she became actively
involved in an Afro-Swedish association. "I was the chairperson of the
association. This exposed me to many prominent people in Swedish
politics. I was also involved in a lot of debates that affected the
minorities in Sweden. Eventually, I joined the Liberal party and that
was just the beginning of my career in politics."
Sabuni admits that her position is one that is a great inspiration
to Africans and minorities in Sweden. "But at the same time, I do not
want to be viewed as a politician who just talks about segregation and
inequality, as much as that is important. I want to fully participate
in the policy-making issues in my party, issues that affect every
Swede. This is how I can grow politically, not by wallowing in
self-pity and feeling disadvantaged. I do not want to be limited to
just one kind of politics."
Unlike other women MPs, even in Sweden, Sabuni says that she goes
out of her way to work with her party leaders and male colleagues who
are the key policy makers in the Riksdag. "I am not just satisfied with
sitting in parliament. I want to influence my party's policies, and you
can only do this by working closely with other people," she says.
"But the world is the same everywhere. When the boys are having a
beer or coffee, I go ahead and sit with them, laugh and chat. Of course
getting too friendly with the party leader will have people talking and
others asking what we are up to but that does not bother me at all."
She tells why she does not worry about others talking about her: "I
know what I want, and that is to influence policies made by my party.
And so I go out to get what I want, not giving my time to worrying
about what everyone is saying!"
Worrying too much about what everyone is saying becomes an excuse to
never grow or never keep learning which are my main desires in life"
She pauses for a while and then says, "But I am not shy, I am tough...I
grew up in a house with five boys anyway!"
Sabuni's tough side begins to break through the surface as she
starts to talk politics and issues close to her heart. It's easy to see
her now campaigning or delivering a speech...convincingly...
"We are lucky in Sweden that the government has mechanisms that
ensure balanced representation of both male and female, young and old,
majority and minority. Perhaps the lack of these mechanisms is what
ails women's politics in Africa, and women are not given a chance to do
and become what they want to become."
"I am even luckier that my husband Allan understands me and the
nature of my job well," says Sabuni. "I do not have a problem balancing
my different roles as a wife, a mother of four year-old twins and a
politician. When I am away on duty, like now, Allan stays home with the
kids. He knew what kind of a person I am when he married me."
But Sabuni also relents that maintaining the balance can be tricky.
"I have to make a decision every time on what's important at that
particular moment. Some times it's my husband, or my kids and at other
times it is my job. I let each moment define itself."
The legislator has a life outside politics. 'I love going out with
friends for a drink," she says with an air of excitement. "Champagne is
my favourite drink and going out with friends is my hobby!"
When parliament takes the summer break, Sabuni, her husband and
children visit their cottage in the Swedish country side." It helps us
to get away from it all."
Sabuni's husband is a travel and safari agent. Together they come to
Tanzania and Congo to visit relatives. "But we go to Greece or Spain
for holiday and for the sun."
But Sabuni says that she feels more at home in Africa. "I often say
that my roots are where my parents are, and right now that happens to
be Sweden, but I always feel so much at home when I am in Africa. I am
more proud of being African than of being Swedish."
Sabuni will admit that by orientation, she is more Swedish than she
is African. "I even think and dream in Swedish. I have to think twice
and again before expressing myself in Kiswahili. My parents and I and
all my family in Sweden speak to each other in Swedish!"
It is no surprise then that her greatest ambition is to become
Sweden's prime minister. "I know that sounds impossible but it is
possible. If I can win my party election to become the leader of my
party, then the next thing I will do is to vie for the prime minister's
post. But it all depends on whether I remain active in politics or not."
One can tell that Sabuni is very confident of herself, and is not
afraid to say it. So I ask what she likes most about herself. "I am
spontaneous, I am a happy person and I am also very generous," she says
with a smile. "But my weakness is that I am very lazy! When I lie on my
couch at home, I just lie there and forget to worry about anything
else. I am not the kind of person who finds pleasure in cleaning house,
doing the dishes or laundry!
But perhaps my weakness also helps me relax because I never get the
nagging feeling that I should be somewhere else doing something else
instead of just lying down! When I lie down, I just enjoy the moment!"
Someone comes in the study to tell Sabuni that she is needed
somewhere else. But she cannot leave before she tells me some principle
she believes in or which she lives by. Without much thought, she says:
"Never look down on yourself and think that you cannot make it because
you are a woman or because you are a foreigner. That will only keep you
down where everyone else has put you."
As we walk outside the Hughes building, I watch her walk away. She
takes out her designer sunglasses and strides off confidently.

Original_Search_Title: Misuse of Official Cars is Relative
Headline: Kenya: Misuse of Official Cars is Relative
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Theft is theft, even if it's a piece of cake that has been
stolen. But you would not want to spend your valuable time stalking a
cake thief while somebody else is stealing your cow. Nor would you, if
you are a hunter, want to use up your arrows to pin down a porcupine
while you could be bagging an antelope. Nor would you use a sledge
hammer to kill a fly.
But it is sometimes appropriate to chase the cake thief, use up all
your arrows to hunt down small game, or use a sledge hammer to kill a
fly. In any of these scenarios, you would be expending much effort and
resources for small or doubtful catch. But you would be making a point.
But if you are results-oriented - and not interested in public
bravado - you would probably consider the opportunity costs, the
proportional merits and relative value of your exploits. These are some
of the considerations that come to mind when you think about the
relentless campaign by the Kenya National Commission on Human Rights to
pursue ministers who use government vehicles at referendum campaign
rallies.
Commission chairman Maina Kiai has a very clear mind on this. He
started off the campaign one day in September by telling Ministers
Raila Odinga and Anyang' Nyong'o to their face that they should not
have used Government vehicles to the launch of their referendum
campaign.
Since then the commission has netted some eight ministers and seven
assistant ministers in both the Orange and Banana camps and has
promised that if the Attorney-General does not he will prosecute them
himself. In addition, the commission has set up a 24-hour telephone
hot-lines and an e-mail address to receive information on violations
from the public. It has also sent out the so-called monitors to
political rallies across the country to see which ministers are using
government vehicles. The Public Officer Ethics Act does not allow the
use of public resources for private purpose, and the Government has no
stand in the Constitution and no group should use State resources, Mr
Kiai has argued.
The problem, however, is the difficulty of defining the misuse of
government vehicles. There is also the issue of the seriousness of the
misuse. In broad terms, using a government vehicle for private purposes
is corruption. There is petty corruption and there is grand corruption.
It is the degree that really matters. There is a great difference, for
example, between using a government vehicle to ferry some fish from
Kisumu while returning from an official trip to using the vehicle to go
on holiday in Mombasa. For the purposes of understanding the problem
and devising remedies, it is important to keep a sense of
proportionality.
Further, the prohibition against using a government vehicles for
personal purposes does not mean that no personal benefit may result
from the official use. Personal benefit could be incidental. Indeed
much of the misuse occurs when the personal benefit conferred by its
use is not merely incidental. And this is not limited to government
vehicles. It includes other resources and equipment such as telephones,
computers, fax machines, etc.
There is also the question of the nature of the authorisation to use
a government vehicle, which could include incidental personal use.
Moreover, what constitutes or does not constitute misuse may also
differ, depending on the eye of the beholder. The commission is now
facing a challenge by Regional Development minister Mohamed Abdi
Mohamed and assistant ministers Mwangi Kiunjuri and Peter Odoyo to
provide evidence that they have misused government vehicles.
Because of the above reasons one could argue that the resources
expended by the commission on checking whether ministers are misusing
government vehicles could be more usefully spent investigating more
serious human rights abuses. Besides, isn't the commission invading the
territory of the Kenya Anti-Corruption Commission?
What emerges from the use of government vehicles controversy is the
object lesson that the commission should not pursue cases that are
difficult to prove and enforce, or are relatively minor infractions.
Since the commission does not have the resources to pursue all wrongs,
real or imagined, everywhere, it should exercise a sense of
proportionality. May be it should just stick to its core mandate of
furthering the protection and promotion of human rights, however widely
interpreted. Stalking ministers in rallies to see if they are using
government vehicles is certainly not a core business. It could lead
some people to think the commissioners are busybodies who have nothing
better to do.

Original_Search_Title: Orange Team Expected in Busia Today
Headline: Kenya: Orange Team Expected in Busia Today
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange team torch-bearers in Western Province take their campaigns to Busia District today.
Kanu vice-chairman Chris Okemo, who is also the Nambale MP, has been
in the constituency for the last two days trying to lay the groundwork
for the campaigns.
Vice-President Moody Awori has been pitching tent in the district
and has already campaigned in support of the proposed new Constitution
in Nambale but Mr Okemo yesterday said the VP "made no impact."
Mr Okemo said he would team up with former vice-president Musalia
Mudavadi and other Orange leaders in the province today and take their
campaigns to Budalang'i, Funyula, Butula and Nambale.
Mr Okemo, a former Finance minister, was optimistic that the No team
(those opposed to the proposed new Constitution and whose symbol is an
orange) would win in the province during the November 21 referendum.
"We will certainly win," the only MP, apart from Mt Elgon's John
Serut, who was elected on a Kanu ticket in Western Province in the 2002
elections, said.
However, he said the Orange and the Banana teams could share the
spoils in Bungoma, the Local Government minister Musikari Kombo's Ford
Kenya stronghold.
"We (Orange) enjoy huge support especially in Bungoma Town although
all local MPs are in the Banana team. We can share the votes 50-50,"
said Mr Okemo.
Mr Okemo denied reports that he had disagreed with Roads minister
Raila Odinga, a leading Orange team luminary, for allowing Mr Mudavadi
to steal the limelight during the campaigns yet he (Mr Okemo) was the
top-most Luhya leader in the No team by being Kanu's vice-chairman.
He said: "Right now, our common objective is to ensure the Draft
Constitution does not pass. We have not talked about the power struggle
or even hinted about it. This is a matter for Luhyas themselves to
decide and I cannot blame Raila."
Mr Okemo criticised ministers Simeon Nyachae (Energy), John Michuki
(Internal Security), Njenga Karume (Special Programmes) and David
Mwiraria (Finance), accusing them of misleading President Kibaki on the
"mood of Kenyans" about the proposed Constitution.
"These old politicians have no regard for the younger generation and
that is why they are misleading the President about the feelings of the
majority Kenyans," said Mr Okemo.
He made the remarks at Mungatsi market in his Nambale constituency where he addressed an Orange rally.
He at the same time dismissed Mr Kombo (Local Government),
describing him as an "over-ambitious man eyeing the post of
vice-presidency currently held by Mr Awori".
Noting that all political parties were involved in the referendum
campaign, Mr Okemo added that some people had narrowed the debate to
party levels, something he said was likely to cause divisions among
Kenyans.
The Orange team yesterday took its campaign to Kuria District where
they asked the local community to reject the proposed Constitution.
They told the Kuria community to join the rest of Kenyans in demanding a people-driven Constitution.
The team included Foreign Affairs assistant ministers Orwa Ojodeh,
Mbita MP Otieno Kajwang' and his Migori counterpart, Mr Owino Likowa.
A senior official of the Mombasa branch of the Supreme Council of
Kenya Muslims(Supkem) yesterday accused the branch chairman, Mr Sheikh
Juma Ngao, of misleading President Kibaki on the true position of
Muslims in regard to the proposed new Constitution.
Branch treasurer Sheikh Abubakar Awadh said they were not party to
people who were afraid of telling the President that Muslims were
against the proposed laws.

Original_Search_Title: Let Us Think Beyond November 21, Please
Headline: Kenya: Let Us Think Beyond November 21, Please
Body_Text:
Nairobi — There is no doubt now. This referendum is not about the Constitution at all. It is about power and ethnic politics.
It is about unfinished political squabbles, reinvention of
politicians with skeletons in their closets and the support for
politicians engaged in new corruption. It is about the Banana side
maintaining power and the Orange side wresting it.
And we, the majority non-politician Kenyans, are the cannon fodder,
the pawns in this zero-sum contest for power between a very small
clique of political players with a knack for manipulation for power,
self-interest and illegally generated wealth.
We have all heard, via the media or directly, statements from the
political leaders on both sides that leave no doubt in the mind that
this whole referendum has moved past the Constitution and is about
power and ethnicity.
The Yes side is not discussing the merits and demerits of the
proposed Constitution so that voters can make up their own minds. They
are using public servants - who should be neutral - to run their
campaigns.
Can there be any doubt when the No side bashes the Government - not
the Draft - and asks Kenyans to "teach the Government a lesson" by
voting No? Or when their most prominent member publicly declares that
he is ashamed of being in the Cabinet? Yet that same person decides
that staying in a position of power is bigger than his shame. Many of
those supporting Yes are doing so because they do not like one or the
other of the leading lights in the No side; do not like the history of
some in the No side; or on ethnic basis.
Similarly, many of those supporting the No side do so because they
don't like the arrogance of one or another in the Yes side; want to
teach those currently exercising power a lesson; or believe that, by
voting No, their ethnic group will come to power.
Of course, there are some on both sides who believe - for whatever
reason - that their side is right. And there are others with strong
convictions either for or against the Draft, but find the politicising
of the process discomforting for various reasons.
Yet, there are many of us who don't like either option; who don't
want to be part of the machinations and manipulations of the political
class and are reluctant supporters of either side simply for lack of
alternatives.
And that has led to confusion and frustrations, because we know that
this process is not about what the political class is telling us. And
we are struggling to find appropriate "space" that can validate the
disgust we have with the process and the political elite.
We are frustrated since it seems we are heading into the confusion of November 21 referendum without brakes.
We know that the referendum should be about making a decision on a
draft document marked by principle, information and consensus, but
recognise that this is not the reality we are dealing with at present
and will likely stay this way till November 21.
Ideally, a constitution reform process is meant to bring a country
together on basic values that identify it as one country with agreed
principles and aspirations.
It sets the ground rules for governance, accountability and justice,
affirming the responsibility and limits of government and hereby
creating a social contract between the governed and the government.
Our process - especially since Bomas in 2003 - has been everything
else but that. It has been about maintaining or getting power. It has
been about emphasising the national divisions - whether ethnic or
religious - as a means of capturing or maintaining power, contrary to
the expectations of civil society and religious groups that championed
this cause in the 1990s.
And it has been about political payback with little room for principles, values and the aspirations of Kenyans.
Moreover, the deepening divisions based on ethnicity and personal
power politics are worrying to many Kenyans looking beyond November 21.
The inability - on both sides - to articulate a vision of unity,
tolerance and respect after November 21 has raised sky-high the stakes
in this referendum.
How do both sides intend to start healing the rifts they are
creating with their abusive language, the use of ethnicity as a tool
for gathering votes and by abusing power?
And let us not think that chaos is impossible. Just last week, Addis
Ababa was rocked by clashes, violence and riots from the discontent and
divisions stemming from the elections in May, six months back!
With our economy on a recovery path now, what would be the impact
on, say, tourism, if we have chaos in Nairobi after November 21? What
price will local shopkeepers, hawkers and others pay if we are unable
to manage our divisions in a civilised manner?
So is there anything that we can do? I believe so, even if in a
small way. First, we must keep focusing on the need for peace,
tolerance and stability after November 21 even if the political leaders
don't seem concerned to reach out to one another in a spirit of
tolerance and compromise.
If ever there was a time to pray for Kenya and for continued
improvement in our economy, this is the time. Any chaos after November
21 will clearly have been precipitated by political leaders on both
sides, intent on showing each other who is the "man" and leaving the
ordinary Kenyan to suffer the consequences.
And we should resist - as strenuously as possible - the selfish
attempts by the political leaders to create "ethnic kingdoms" that
"speak with one voice."
Nothing could be more dangerous for our fledgling democracy as this
trend, as it portends, eventually, five or six men - the ethnic kings -
deciding the fate of the entire country any way they want through
horse-trading based on their individual desires.
And that will eventually mean more corruption, more ethnic conflicts
and more tensions in Kenya, whether we pass this draft or not.
In practical terms, here are some ideas of what we can do:
-Start two weeks of prayers, fasting and/or meditation from November 19, for peace, stability and good governance;
-As Kenyans of goodwill and peace - notwithstanding our stands in
the process so far - let us wear something green or white as a symbol
of peace till Christmas (or after). Green reflects "new beginnings" and
is a neutral but strong colour while white stands for peace in our flag.
-Every day at 5pm from now till the end of December, hoot all
vehicle horns for two minutes as a message to politicians that they
must start listening to us, the ordinary Kenyans. And if you have no
access to a vehicle, blow a whistle, bang on a sufuria, or whatever,
for two minutes.
-Call on our churches, mosques, temples and pagodas to set up tables
where we can collect signatures on our desire for peace and tolerance
within the existing political leadership with the goal of presenting at
least one million signatures to our political leadership expressing our
views on the current divisions.
Yes, these are small efforts, and even if we don't get the political
class to change before November 21, let it be said that all
peace-lovers played their part and that when the country counted more
than the selfish power interests of the political class, we stood up
and were counted.
Mr Kiai is chairman of the Kenya National Commission on Human Rights

Original_Search_Title: Do Not Dare Join Banana, Mwenje Told
Headline: Kenya: Do Not Dare Join Banana, Mwenje Told
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Embakasi MP David Mwenje would be "committing political suicide" if he joined the Banana camp, an Orange MP said yesterday.
Liberal Democratic Party leader Joseph Kamotho said the MP risked losing his clout if he defected.
Mr Kamotho, who talked to the Embakasi MP before and after he
visited President Kibaki at State House, Nairobi, said it was too late
for Mr Mwenje to decamp.
Also at State House was Limuru MP Kuria Kanyingi.
He urged him not to "make the mistake," arguing that Kenyans would think he had been compromised.
"Having said everything that there was to say, both political and on
the Constitution, if he turns back public perception will be that
Mwenje has been bought," he said. But Mr Mwenje would not talk about
the matter yesterday.
The Mathioya MP said he would be disappointed if Mr Mwenje
campaigned for the proposed Constitution. He would be opening himself
to ridicule if he moved camp, Mr Kamotho said.
He added: "Hon Mwenje did tell me the President was looking for him,
and I advised him as a citizen that he could not tell the President
that he would not see him. So I told him: 'Just go; I know you as a
senior politician you are not a novice in politics. Go and state your
case as a senior politician and listen to him.'"

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki Wants Issuance of Ids, Birth Cards Hastened
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki Wants Issuance of Ids, Birth Cards Hastened
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki has promised to address issues raised by Coast people.
The President yesterday instructed Attorney General Amos Wako to
ensure all Muslims at the Coast without Birth Certificates and ID cards
get them within a month.
The President surprised Malindi MP Lucas Maitha, who is in the
Orange team, when he gave in to his demands before he commented on
those presented by Banana leaders.
This was on Thursday when he met all area MPs.
Malindi District will benefit from several presidential orders that
could enhance tourism, end squatter problem and create job
opportunities.
Yesterday, Kibaki told Wako he wants the issuance of birth
certificates and identity cards fulfilled before his next visit to the
Coast at the end of the year.
Kibaki was speaking when he met a delegation of about 1,000 Muslim
leaders led by Supkem, Coast branch, chairman, Sheikh Juma Ngao, at
State House, Mombasa.
Kibaki demanded to know from the PS and the Attorney General why the
issuance of the papers in the Coast and particularly to Muslims was
still a problem.
Kibaki told the two to stop giving lame excuses over the delay.
"You must start the process tomorrow. You have no excuse for not
giving these people their rights. They were born in Kenya, why should
they lack such vital documents," said Kibaki.
Kibaki also promised the Muslims to follow-up on the issuance of
soft loans to thousands of fishermen so that they can buy fishing
equipment easily.
The President also promised to revive of stalled factories and tackle absentee landlords issue.
He met several groups at State House, with a Banana rally that was
earlier expected to take place in Mvita being dropped from his
programme.
The State House visit by Muslim leaders was criticized by the
Council of Imams and Preachers of Kenya and Chief Kadhi Hammad Kassim
of the Orange camp.
The Head of State also gave in to their request to have at least
three Muslim directors at the Board of Governors of Mombasa Polytechnic.
On Thursday evening, Kibaki gave a wide range of promises to tackle
problems presented to him by Coast MPs during a meeting with some of
them that lasted late into the night.
Kibaki directed the Ministry of Transport to immediately embark on
the search for land adjacent to the Malindi airstrip to begin its
expansion.
The President also directed that the Mombasa-Malindi road be repaired.

Original_Search_Title: Name Recipients of Cash, Minister Tells UNDP
Headline: Kenya: Name Recipients of Cash, Minister Tells UNDP
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Transport minister Chris Murungaru wants the United Nations
Development Programme (UNDP) to name the recipients of cash it gave for
civic education.
Murungaru challenged UNDP to name the Non-Governmental Organisations
that benefited from the funding to prove the world body is not funding
the Orange team.
He also urged his constituents to vote for the proposed new
constitution to save the Government that, he alleged, was under siege
from hungry power seekers.
"We are no longer in the constitution writing process. This is a
fight against President Kibaki's Government and I am urging you to turn
out in large numbers if you want to protect this Government. This is
your government and if you want to retain it, show up on November 21 to
vote for the Banana (side)," said Murungaru.
The minister alleged that the funding from UNDP, allegedly for civic
education, was diverted to the Orange team for campaign purposes in a
larger scheme to ensure the proposed constitution does not pass.
"We have been asking UNDP to tell Kenyans the NGOs that benefited
from their funding to conduct civic education on the draft constitution
but they cannot answer," he said.
He was speaking at Nairutia in his Kieni constituency after drumming up support for the proposed constitution.
Murungaru was accompanied by a group of professionals from Kieni in a convoy of several vehicles.
He said the Orange team was being funded to reject the draft
constitution by Western nations on the pretext that the funds were
meant for civic education.
He also dismissed claimsthe Banana team was planning to rig the referendum results.

Original_Search_Title: Six Ministers On Violence List of Shame
Headline: Kenya: Six Ministers On Violence List of Shame
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Six Cabinet Ministers are among 40 politicians whose names have
been presented to the Attorney General for prosecution over violence in
the referendum campaigns.
And the Kenya Human Rights Commission and the Kenya National
Commission on Human Rights that compiled the list have vowed to lodge a
private prosecution against the politicians should the AG fail to act.
The ministers are Najib Balala (Heritage) and Dr Chris Murungaru
(Transport), Simeon Nyachae (Energy), Amos Kimunya (Lands), Raila
Odinga ( Roads) and Njeru Ndwiga ( Co-operatives).
KNCHR chairman Maina Kiai and the Executive Director of KHRC, Mr
Miano, released the "list of shame" at a press conference in Nairobi
yesterday.
The two human rights organisations have enumerated the politicians'
utterances, which, they say, amount to incitement to violence.
Nyachae is accused of incitement to violence by saying: "Wale
hawajatahiri peleka jandoni (Those who are not circumcised should be
taken for a circumcision ceremony)."
The Nyaribari Chache MP said this at Ndumberi Stadium on October 15 during a Banana campaign meeting.
Raila on his part said: "Since Nyachae is an old man, the brain is
equally old and he should go home". He made the statement on October 8
in Bungoma.
Kimunya is accused of incitement for telling squatters to continue
occupying the late J M Kariuki's land, this notwithstanding a court's
order to the contrary.
The human rights groups say he did this on October 24 at Ol-Kolau market in Nyandarua District.
Ndwiga will face the charges over his remarks: " These people are
opposing the constitution yet they were part of the process. Isn't that
what we call ubiici? (Behaving like one who is not circumcised)".
Ndwiga allegedly uttered the words on October 5 at Kerugoya Stadium in
Central Province.
Balala is cited for incitement was in Kilifi on October 15 and said:
"Pesa ya barabara za pwani zimepelekwa Mt Kenya Region. ( Funds
allocated for roads in Coast Province have been diverted to the Mt
Kenya Region).
Murungaru is accused of having said Nyeri should be at the forefront
in the Yes campaign and any rebel should be dealt with accordingly.
The Transport minister is also accused of having added that:
"Anybody who wants to behave like a rebel should be folded. We have no
time to flatter demons whose work is to ruin the scheme of things".
The Kieni MP is alleged to have made the remarks on October 21 this year at Nyamachaki Hall, Nyeri.
"We are putting these politicians on notice that such derogatory
speech along ethnic and racial lines is categorised under the list of
offenses known as subversion, contrary to Section 77 (3) (e) of the
Penal Code" said the human rights organisations in a statement.
The human rights groups accused the politicians of incitement to violence, making hate speech and unsavoury language.
If the politicians are found guilty, they could be jailed for up to three years.
Kiai and Miano said the AG should investigate and, if necessary, prosecute the politicians.
Others in the "List of Shame" are immediate former Ndaragwa MP
Thirikwa Kamau, who is accused of asking people to physically resist
encroachment by the Orange team into Nyandarua. He is alleged to have
made the statement on October 10.
Kapenguria MP Samuel Moroto is named for saying: "Naambia Kombo
alete hao chokora councillors Kapenguria ndio ajue kwa nini punda
hakuna mapembe (I am daring Kombo to bring those destitute councillors
to Kapenguria and he will understand why donkeys have no horns)".
He is accused of uttering these words on October 15 at Iten Stadium.
Machakos Town MP Daudi Mwanzia is on the list for saying:
"Wakisimamisha pension ya Moi, then Wakalenjin na Wakamba watavamia
State House (The Kalenjin and Akamba will storm State House should
(former President) Moi's pension."
He was said to have made the remarks on October 15 at Iten Stadium.
Makadara MP Reuben Ndolo is named for uttering the words: "Kombo
anakula pesa ambayo iliuwa Mbai. (Kombo is "eating" the money that
killed (university lecturer Odhiambo Mbai)".
He said this on October 23, according to the human rights groups.
Assistant Minister Mwangi Kiunjuri is accused of having said on
October 28 at Kabazi Open ground in Rift Valley that " People should
prepare for war if NO wins".
Eldama Ravine Kanu MP Musa Sirma is accused of saying "If Yes wins,
the Kikuyu should pack their bags and move out of Eldama Ravine.
He added: "Kikuyus from Shauri, Maji Mazuri and Timboroa will not be issued with title deeds if they vote Yes".
He allegedly committed the offence on November 6 at Eldama Ravine.
Tigania East MP Peter Munya allegedly said: "If we pass this
constitution, the Orange team should not step in Meru unless they
remove their flags."
Others on the list include assistant minister Maina Kamanda. He is
accused that on September 30, he said: "Amepigwa na akina mama
amemalizika na wewe ukisha pigwa na akina mama umefika mwisho ( He has
been beaten up by women and once you have been beaten by women. You
have reached the end)", referring to David Mwenje after the City
Cabanas chaos.
Kibwezi MP Kalembe Ndile is accused of saying: "Wenda wazimu wanatuambia tuseme NO (These mad people are telling us to say NO)".
Ndile is also charged with saying: "Mombasa, Mombasa nzima watu wote
ni masquota kwa sababu ya Warabu kutoka Zanzibar (All the people of
Mombasa are squatters because of the Arabs)."
Kamukunji MP Norman Nyaga is accused that while at Maua Stadium, in
Eastern Province, on October 8, he said: "Raila ni shetani (Raila is a
devil)".
Kanu Secretary General William Ruto is in the list for saying: "We
have men and there are those who are like women because they are
fearing to defect from the Banana camp." He made the remark in Marakwet
on October 8.
Kabete MP Paul Muite is named for referring to Raila Odinga as a
"mla samaki" (fish eater) who sneaked the Prime Minister who is
powerful than the President in the Bomas draft".

Original_Search_Title: Soludo's Benevolence?
Headline: Nigeria: Soludo's Benevolence?
Body_Text:
He comes across as a no-nonsense banker. If his words do not have
the ring of sacredness of his American counterpart, the outgoing,
Federal Reserve Chairman, Alan Greenspan (who will be replaced in
January by Ben Bernanke described as one of the world's best monetary
economist) to financial markets, at least it stings and could get the
Nigerian Financial Community marching on their toes.
Ever since the Central Bank governor of Nigeria announced the 25
billion capitalisation for the banks and followed with a deadline to
meet the target, the industry has been shaken up to its bones. He has
subsequently since then, threatened, cajoled and bullied them to
restructure, merge, consolidate or simply die.
The results have been all of these and lots more. Job loses at the
banks have been massive. And even at the Central bank, where Mr. Soludo
holds sway, he has announced that an axe dangles over more than a
thousand employees who will have to take their skills to the street for
whoever might want to engage them.
If Mr. Charles Soludo does not impress you with all his tough talks
and actions on the banking revolution, he is orchestrating, he will
surely touch you with his kind of generousity that is unprecedented
with any known Central Banker across the world.
The recent death of the wife of the President, Mrs. Stella Obasanjo
revealed that the Anambra state-born Central Banker can be kind
hearted. He charted flights for all the "big" men and women across
Nigeria who can of course buy their own plane ticket to travel, to
Abeokuta the home town of President Obasanjo where his wife, who had
died earlier in a Spanish hospital was finally interred. It is not a
bad thing to help people attend the funeral or the last burial rights
of the first lady. But how does it square that a deficit busting hawk,
a strict enforcer of fiscal discipline, stringent and puritan advocate
of bank consolidation could be so generous with public funds to the
most unusual benefactors. The truth is that the beneficiary of Mr.
Soludo's free air ride to Abeokuta could more or less individually or
in groups charter the flights themselves. Mr. Soludo has claimed in
several public fora that the main critical element in the distortion of
the economy is the rentier behaviour of the state in doling out favours
to a parasitic elite class. He claimed that the administration's
economic reform was essentially designed to reduce or eliminate elite
dependency on state's patronage. His latest largesse to the same
community of parasitic clique in airlifting them to Abeokuta, when they
could easily airlift themselves there, is one classical case of the
bankruptcy of this administration's reform elite who say the kind of
things that would please their international finance patrons, but could
hardly do a worthy thing even by their own standard.
The Central Bank Governor has earlier this year caused a stir, when
he doled out, about 50 million naira to parliamentary banking
committee, which he and officials of the Central Bank described as
'Social responsibility'. Again, social responsibility" to people who
are already socially inoculated against any shock of hunger, poverty,
unemployment squalor and other real menaces that torture the mass of
the people on continuous basis.
Mr. Soludo appears very clearly to be keen in building sufficient
political bullet proof around himself than any serious endeavour to do
his job. He appears to recognise the banana peel on which he stands and
seems to be doing every thing he can to prevent a premptive fall. But
this is a shame for an academic, a reformer and more importantly a
central banker.
Running a country's monetary policy cannot be combined with a
surreptitious desire to build a political base. It is difficult to ask
a senior public official to ignore political matters around him, but to
go about it in the way of buying political favour is plainly
unfortunate.
The Central Banker has earlier acquired reputation for a
serious-minded public official that cannot be compromised by political
expediency, but his recent benevolence has been in the direction that
could be nothing but political inducements and even corruption. If Mr.
Charles Soludo prefers to run an aids agency than the country's central
bank, he is welcome to spare the undue assaults on the collective
psyche and intelligence of Nigerians.
However, what Mr. Soludo's act of charity shows is that the reform
process is a smokescreen that hides the perfidy of the new conquering
elites with their foreign partners and collaborators. The economic
reform slogan is a plain white-wash and literary demagoguery that has
no content in policy options, sincerity and even elementary commitment.
The declining quality of life for most Nigerians will remain
unabated while a tiny cabal rides roughshod on sterile, arcane and
bankrupt reform slogans. Currently, a pernicious trinity of neo-liberal
technocrats in government along with primitive and corrupt wing of the
political class in active collaboration with the contractor wing of the
so-called organised private sector are in concert to foist the
administration of General Obasanjo beyond the constitutional term of
two tenures of four years each, on the country.
If this clique succeeds, it will not be, because they are clever,
intelligent or invincible but because of the complacency of the rest of
us.

Original_Search_Title: U-17, Others Awarded
Headline: Gambia: U-17, Others Awarded
Body_Text:
Banjul — In collaboration with Wohobi FC on Monday night, Serrekunda West
Sports Organisation (SEWESO) awarded players and officials of The
Gambia National U-17 side and veteran players of the zone. In this
first-ever ceremony, at least 30 awards were disbursed.
The award ceremony, which took a form of fund-raising show with
Senegalese mbalakh star, Fallou Dieng and local rap groups, attracted
thousand of sports lovers across the country. Conferred were the
following U-17 players: Ebrima Sohna, Momodou Ceesay, Abdoulie Njie,
Babucar Suso, Pa Modou Jagne, Abdoulie Mansally, Alhagie Amadou Ngum,
George Cole and Pierre Gomez. Included in the award list were the
physiotherapist and equipment manager of the U-17 team, Pa Matarr Ndow
and Sheriffo Suso respectively. Each of them received a medal and
certificate.
The zone awarded individual awards to Ebrima, Sohna, Momodou Ceesay,
Abdoulie Njie and Pierre Gomez for promotion they have given to their
area.
Meanwhile, 14 other veteran players and sports administrators who
contributed to the development of the zone were also awarded
certificates of reorganization and appreciation. They are Ya Sainey
Jobateh, late Dodou Secka, Lamin Owens, Joseph Bakury, Ebrima Jobe
alias Mbat, Badou Jasseh, Abdoulie Faal alias Gainde park, Babucarr
Jarjue alias Banana Jarjue, late Pa Kasory, Secka Jobe, Alhagie Biri
Biri Njie, Harriet Owens, Alhagie Njie VP and the American Basket ball
player Jerrod Mustaf.
The awards were presented by SoS Samba Faal and Neneh Macdoll-Gaye,
Omar Ndow Gamtel Managing Director, Francis Gomez KMC Deputy Mayor and
Amadou Scattered Janneh.
SoS Faal Tourney Final In Niumi
Soccer fans have finally got what they have been waiting for - the
final of a trophy being sponsored by the SoS for Youth and Sports,
Samba Faal which kicks off at Kerr Mama village in Upper Niumi this
Sunday, November 13th.
Organised by the Upper Niumi Sports Committee, the soccer competition was competed by five villages in the district.
After months of intensive competitions, Mass Sosseh of Kerr Mama and
Wallidan of Fass Saho are ready to battle it out in this grand final.
Mass Sosseh of Kerr Mama, the defending champions, made it to the final
after beating Kerr Chebo 3-2 in the semis while Wallidan of Fass Saho
tamed Kerr Demba 3-0.
The Sunday final will obviously be a revenge for Wallidan, for they
lost to Mass Sosseh 2-1 in the group stage. However, having a match at
home has made Mass Sosseh favourites.
Meanwhile, according to an official of the organising committee, Ba
Dodou Jallow, the winner goes home with a glittering trophy and a cash
prize of D5, 000 while the runner-up receives a consolation prize of
D3, 000.
The sponsor of the tournament, Samba Faal who is expected to grace
the occasion alongside important dignitaries in the likes of SoS
Yankuba and National Sports officials, is billed to take the kick-off.
Super Nawettan Progresses At last, the 2005 Super Nawettan
championship kicked off at the Independence Stadium in Bakau last
Wednesday.
The eight GFA recognized zones are taking part in the championship.
They are divided into two groups of four teams. Group A comprises of
the defending champions Bakau, Gurjur, Banjul and Serrekunda East while
Group B encompasses Serrekunda West, Sukuta, Brikama and Lamin.
The competition's cost is being footed by the national football
association and the overall champion goes home with D25, 000 while the
runner-up D18, 000. Each competing team receives D4, 000 and D6, 000 as
preparation costs and consolation prizes respectively.

Original_Search_Title: Kalonzo Taken to Task Over Presidency Claim
Headline: Kenya: Kalonzo Taken to Task Over Presidency Claim
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Banana team said yesterday that, contrary to No claims, the Bomas
constitutional conference was what barred presidential candidates from
contesting parliamentary seats.
It was misleading, they said, for the Orange group to claim that the
clause had only later been sneaked into the proposed Constitution.
Yes leaders Musikari Kombo, John Koech, Newton Kulundu, Danson
Mungatana, Maina Kamanda and Njoki Ndung'u described the allegation as one
of the many "lies" the No team was telling the public.
Said Local Government minister Kombo at a Nairobi press conference:
"Kalonzo (Environment minister Musyoka) himself knows the truth. Let that
truth guide him to tell the public that we agreed at Bomas that presidential
candidates should not vie for parliamentary seats."
It was time the Orange leaders argued their case without distorting the
facts, the Ford Kenya chairman said.
Mr Kombo, who will be bidding for the presidency in 2007, said other
contenders had accepted the clause.
"Why is it that some of us are not scared of the clause? If you are as
popular as you want the people to believe, why fear the clause?" he
asked.
Addressing a rally at Mwingi on Tuesday, Mr Musyoka said the clause had
been aimed at stopping him from contesting the presidency.
The requirement, he said, was meant to favour President Kibaki in the
next General Election. "It is very malicious that they are using the
proposed constitution to protect one person."
The Mwingi North MP said attorney-general Amos Wako had told him the new
document had been changed before it was made public. "I met Mr Wako at Moi
International Airport, Mombasa, where he told me: 'Steve, look at what these
people are doing, even Simeon Nyachae will realise it later.'"
Yesterday, Mr Koech, the Regional Co-operation minister, said Mr
Musyoka's remark had been made in desperation. "They have now realised that
we (the Banana team) are winning and have decided to poison the minds of
voters," he said.
Labour minister Kulundu urged Mr Musyoka to stop making embarrassing
remarks in the belief that he is popular.
Assistant Lands minister Mungatana said the proposed constitution was
not tailored to any person and assistant Local Government minister Kamanda
said it was meant to ensure presidential candidates are popular.
The clause had been agreed on at Bomas and no leader had challenged it
at the Naivasha and Kilifi retreats, said Nominated MP Ndung'u.
At another meeting, Co-operative minister Njeru Ndwiga said the document
was prepared for posterity and not to suit a person's interests.
He said Mr Musyoka had kept changing his position on the
presidency. "Hon Kalonzo is behaving like a chameleon, sending different
signals over his presidential bid, and I would like him to come clean on the
issue, he said.
He was addressing a Yes rally in Gilgil, where assistant Finance
minister Mutua Katuku said people opposed to the proposed constitution had
grabbed huge tracts of land and feared that they would lose it if the
document sails through.
Laikipia West MP G.G. Kariuki said government corruption had been to
blame for the delay in signing the Procurement Bill.

Original_Search_Title: Bishops Call for Three-Day Prayers for Referendum
Headline: Kenya: Bishops Call for Three-Day Prayers for Referendum
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Three days of prayer should be held before the referendum on the
proposed Constitution, as an antidote to the violence that has marked the
campaign so far, the Catholic Church said yesterday.
Twenty seven bishops also called for a high voter turn-out and urged
Kenyans to remain united after the vote.
They said the referendum "will be an expression of the maturity of our
nation and of our sense of patriotism."
And in a pastoral letter they went on: "This day should be one of pride
for every citizen.
"We remind Kenyans that we have to remain united after the
referendum. We must therefore maintain peace before, on the day of the
referendum and after the referendum. It has taken long to build peace in our
country. Let us not destroy it."
Both the Banana Yes-vote and Orange No-vote teams should accept the
voters' verdict and forge ahead united to develop the country, they
said.
The three days of prayer should be from November 18 to 20, leading up to
the vote on November 21.
Campaigning so far has been characterised by violence leading to the
deaths of four people - two of them schoolchildren and one a teenager -
during rioting at a Kisumu rally. Ikolomani MP Bonny Khalwale's car was
burnt to a shell during clashes in Kakamega Town.
An inquiry by the Electoral Commission blamed both sides for the
violence, but particularly the Orange camp for contributing to the Kisumu
rioting by "getting into the way of the Banana side".
The disciplinary hearing also concluded Dr Khalwale had contributed to
the Kakamega violence by failing to notify the police of his plans to hold a
procession for the Banana side in the town.
The ECK did not mete out any punishment to the campaign teams, but
warned them that if the violence continues, it will seek a court order to
stop any further campaigning before the big vote.
The bishops said in a pastoral letter that the referendum, "will be an
expression of the maturity of our nation and of our sense of patriotism."
It went on: "This day should be one of pride for every citizen. We
remind Kenyans that we have to remain united after the referendum. We must
therefore maintain peace before, on the day of the referendum and after the
referendum. It has taken long to build peace in our country. Let us not
destroy it."
The letter was issued after three days of deliberations and prayers by
the bishops and one diocesan administrator who met at the St Mary's Pastoral
Centre in Nakuru.
The bishops said it was important that all those entitled to vote did so
on the basis of their conscience because the referendum would determine the
destiny of the nation. No one should be forced or prevented from acting
according to his or her conscience.
The referendum debate had at times degenerated into violence, ethnicity,
personality politics, power struggles, a culture of lies and public
exchanges of derogatory remarks.
On the positive side, it had opened up the democratic space in the
country through freedom of expression and people had become more aware of
their rights, the law and the mechanism of how they are governed.
The bishops urged Kenyans to accept the outcome of the referendum,
saying the country must remain united.
"After some 10 years of debate, meetings and conferences and more
recently acrimonious accusations and even violent rallies and after spending
Sh8 billion, there are only 10 days left before the landmark decision when
Kenyans will cast their vote on the proposed New constitution," the bishops
said.
"While reaffirming our consistent position on this issue, we call on all
Catholics and people of good will to hold three days of prayer beginning
November 18, 2005 and culminating on Sunday 20, 2005, the Feast Day of
Christ King. For us Jesus Christ is Lord," the bishops stated.
Archbishop Ndingi Mwana a' Nzeki, Archbishop Zacchaeus Okoth and Bishop
Philip Sulumeti read the letter to the media in turn in the presence of 24
other bishops who have been attending the three-day episcopal meeting.
Only the Bishop of Ngong, the Rt Rev Cornelius Schilder, who was injured
in a road accident on Monday while on his way to Nakuru, was absent from the
meeting.
"We Kenyans find ourselves at crossroads. For the first time we are
going for a referendum on a proposed Constitution which will decide the
destiny of our nation. It is therefore the duty of all who are entitled to
vote to do so on November 21, 2005. All should seize opportunity to take
part in this historical exercise," the bishops said.
They urged people to read and become informed about the document.
"Previously we highlighted what we considered as its strong points and
weak points. Now is the time for the people of Kenya to make a decision,"
the bishops stated.
The bishops asked those with influence to focus on truth and speak with
respect for others and not to use inflammatory language.
People should not sell their votes and instead vote in an informed
manner and appreciate people who have different opinions.
"Whatever the outcome, let us accept the result. We have assurance that
God is with his people," they said.
They said conscience was a judgment of reason by which each human being
decides whether an issue or an act which he was going to do was right or
wrong.

Original_Search_Title: VP Assures On Districts Created By Moi
Headline: Kenya: VP Assures On Districts Created By Moi
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Thirty one districts created by the former President Moi will not be
phased out, Vice-President Moody Awori said yesterday.
He made the assurance at Amagoro in Teso District, during a meeting with
area leaders including MP Sospeter Ojaamong.
The VP said the districts, which were created during the last 15 years
of the Kanu government, would be gazetted ahead of those whose creation has
been announced by President Kibaki.
Mr Awori said that the Alupe Medical Research Centre in the district was
poised to become a constituent college of the Western University College of
Science and Technology when the latter becomes a full-fledged
university.
The Funyula MP said a number of professionals from the district had been
identified and would soon be given government jobs.
The VP visited St Joseph Kocholia District Hospital where he gave a
donation of Sh4,471,942.50 to Mr Ojaamong who handed it over to the medical
officer of health, Dr Melsa Lutomia. The money is the initial government
contribution for building of wards at the hospital. The project is expected
to cost Sh20 million.
The VP also gave out anti-malaria drugs and treated nets for
distribution to expectant mothers in the area.
Mr Awori's team, which included Ford Kenya chairman Musikari Kombo and
three MPs, appealed to Mr Ojaamong and civic leaders from Teso to vote for
the proposed Constitution.
Mr Awori commended Mr Ojaamong for hosting the Banana leaders although
he was in Orange camp.

Original_Search_Title: Leaders Divided Over Kibaki's Coast Visit
Headline: Kenya: Leaders Divided Over Kibaki's Coast Visit
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki arrived in the Coast Province to drum up support for
the proposed Constitution yesterday as Muslim leaders announced they would
not visit him at State House.
The Council of Imams and Preachers of Kenya said it would not honour an
invitation to meet the President tomorrow, saying it would be unnecessary
since the group had decided to reject the reform proposals.
A meeting with the President would be misinterpreted to mean the council
had changed its mind and was now campaigning for a Yes-vote.
"As Muslim leaders, we cannot visit State House because we have already
decided to vote against the new Constitution and the President is on the
other side," organising secretary Sheikh Mohamed Khalifa told journalists
outside Mbaruk Mosque.
"We respect the President and we do not want a situation where we would
be forced to argue with him," he added.
The President's arrival in Mombasa also coincided with Orange campaigns
in Lamu and Mpeketoni - about 500 kilometres away - spearheaded by Cabinet
ministers Raila Odinga (Roads), Najib Balala (National Heritage) and Anyang'
Nyong'o (Planning) and leader of Official Opposition Uhuru Kenyatta.
But as the Imams remained adamant, the local branch of the Supreme
Council of Muslims of Kenya led by Sheikh Juma Ngao said they would be
taking a delegation to State House. The split on the proposals has also
divided the province's MPs with 11 in support, nine against and one
neutral.
The Orange campaigns in the province are being spearheaded by Mr Balala,
while the Banana team is being led by Tourism minister Morris Dzoro.
Although government officials were tight-lipped about the President's
itinerary, Mr Mungatana said the President was on a campaign mission to seek
support for the proposals.
"We have already done most of the groundwork for the Banana team to win
in the referendum and the President's visit is just meant to consolidate
it," he said.
Sources said the President had originally planned to hold a meeting with
delegations from all over the province on Saturday morning.
The Banana team at the Coast has been engaged in a flurry of activities
ahead of the President's tour.
Last week the group opened an office in the up-market Kizingo area to
coordinate the campaign activities.
Earlier at the airport, angry youths supporting the Yes-vote heckled
mayor Taib Ali Taib, demanding that he leaves the scene since he was in the
No group.
It took the intervention of Provincial Commissioner Mr Cyrus Maina to
stop the youths from harassing him.

Original_Search_Title: Yes And No Sides Scoff At ECK Verdict
Headline: Kenya: Yes And No Sides Scoff At ECK Verdict
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange and Banana teams were yesterday united in dismissing the
Electoral Commission's verdict that both were to blame for the Kisumu and
Kakamega violence.
They said the commission was toothless and that its role in the poll was
becoming useless.
The Yes leaders who talked to the Nation, included Labour minister
Newton Kulundu, Finance assistant minister Mutua Katuku and Ikolomani MP
Bonny Khalwale, whose vehicle was burnt in Kakamega.
Those in the Orange team were Kanu secretary-general William Ruto,
assistant minister Orwa Ojodeh and MPs Mutula Kilonzo, Charles Keter, Reuben
Ndolo and William Omondi, who accused the commission of favouring the Banana
side.
However, the two groups blamed each other over the chaos that also left
four people dead in Kisumu.
"It was clear youths allied to Orange were causing mayhem to block the
Yes rally," Dr Kulundu said, adding that because the No side was intolerant
of Information minister Raphael Tuju, its supporters barricaded roads and
stoned vehicles.
Mr Katuku said the ECK should have banned the Orange team from the
campaigns, arguing that some of its leaders had planned the October 29
Kisumu violence. Some leaders should have been taken to court, he
added.
In the Kisumu riots, pupils Paul Limera, 14, and Vincent Otieno Owuoche,
15, as well as Mr Hillary Ochieng, 17, and Mr George Ogada, 32, were
killed.
Former Cabinet minister William Odongo Omamo was injured.
But Mr Ojodeh said: "This is the beginning of rigging...if the
commission cannot hold the Yes team accountable for the killings."
Mr Kilonzo, who was addressing a news conference at the Orange
secretariat in Nairobi, described the ECK decision as a farce.
"What took place is a farce, and we consider it a public relations
exercise," he added. "The commission admitted not only that it was
under-funded, but also didn't want to seek the truth."
The Kanu nominated MP said the Orange team was dismayed by the decision
not to call witnesses from the Banana side it wanted cross-examined. The use
of live bullets on civilians, he said, was against UN conventions, and the
culprits should have been punished, he added.
Mr Ruto said the ruling showed that the commission "feared" the Banana
team.
Dr Khalwale said the commission was "hiding in a state of ambivalence."
Parliament, he said, should enact a law to govern the referendum so that
incidents of violence were punished severely.
In its ruling, the commission accused both sides of causing the
mayhem. But the larger share of the blame went to the No camp, ruled the ECK
disciplinary committee, presided over by vice-chairman Gabriel Mukele, who
argued that Orange supporters got in the way of the Yes group.
On the Kakamega violence, while warning the camps, he said: "There has
been no proof before the committee of the organisers of violence. The MP was
a victim of it and suffered severely. We symphonies with him."

Original_Search_Title: Criticos' Land Will Be Yours, Squatters Told By Minister
Headline: Kenya: Criticos' Land Will Be Yours, Squatters Told By Minister
Body_Text:
Nairobi — More than 5,000 squatters will be given former Taveta MP Basil Criticos'
land once the proposed new Constitution comes into force, an assistant
minister alleged yesterday.
It was unfair, said Mr Danson Mungatana (Lands), for the former MP to
hold such a vast chunk of land - 180,000 - acres while the locals suffer as
squatters.
Thousands of acres
"It is regrettable that thousands of acres of Taveta land are in the
hands of Criticos and the Kenyatta family," he told a Banana rally in
Taveta.
He said the nagging squatter problem in Coast Province and especially in
Taita-Taveta was "a historic injustice brought about by the current
Constitution, drafted at London's Lancaster House with the one aim of
protecting ill-gotten land by a few wealthy people".
Mr Mungatana said that, under the proposed constitution, each Kenyan had
the right to acquire and own land.
Get title deeds
"The new constitution will not only ensure that Taveta people get land
but also provide them with an opportunity to get title deeds," he said.
"Once the proposed constitution comes into force, the likes of Criticos
and Kenyatta will not have the leeway to own huge chunks of land as the new
law has specified the minimum and maximum land size such individuals can
own," said the Garsen MP.
He said some rich landowners and absentee landlords at the Coast were
opposed to the proposed constitution because they feared losing some of the
land.

Original_Search_Title: Race for Votes in Two Crucial Districts As D-Day Advances
Headline: Kenya: Race for Votes in Two Crucial Districts As D-Day Advances
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Orange and Banana teams have targeted Bungoma and Uasin Gishu
districts to provide the swing votes in Western and Rift Valley
provinces.
Both regard the two districts - Bungoma with 309,000 voters and Uasin
Gishu with 237,795 - as the litmus test in the run-up to the vote, 10 days
away.
And as the D-day draws closer, they plan to target Eldoret North, with
the highest voter registration of 110,116 in Uasin Gishu and Kimilili,
leading in Bungoma with 74,446 voters.
The No team will find it easy going in Eldoret North, represented by Mr
William Ruto, one of the leading lights in the Orange campaign, while in
Kimilili, represented by Trade minister Mukhisa Kituyi, the No team could
face an uphill task.
Nonetheless, the two districts are key to both camps. Voters are both
from urban areas and the settlement schemes where different ethnic groups
settled after independence.
In Uasin Gishu, both teams will also intensify campaigns in Eldoret
South of MP David Koros, which has 63,903 voters. Eldoret East, represented
by Mr Joseph Lagat, has 63,776 registered voters.
While the three MPs in Uasin Gishu are in the Orange camp, there are
supporters of the Yes camp canvassing for votes.
But the Orange team sees itself as having the upper hand. Still, the
turnout on voting day could determine who wins.
The Banana campaigners are placing their hopes in Bungoma, where all MPs
are in the team. Apart from targeting Kimilili, they are going for Sirisia,
whose MP is Foreign Affairs assistant minister Moses Wetang'ula. It has the
second highest voter registration of 66,249.
Both teams could intensify campaigns in Webuye, the turf of Ford Kenya
chairman Musikari Kombo, which has 64,095 voters.
Bumula, represented by Mr Sylvester Wakoli, is fourth with 53,259 voters
while Kanduyi, represented by Mr Wafula Wamunyinyi, has 51,004. The Banana
team is keen to deliver the district vote by erasing gains made by the
Orange group.

Original_Search_Title: Mwenje Holds Talks With Kibaki
Headline: Kenya: Mwenje Holds Talks With Kibaki
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Embakasi MP David Mwenje's support for the Orange campaign appeared to
waver yesterday after he met President Kibaki.
After the State House meeting, Mr Mwenje said that he would make an
announcement whether he would join the Banana team that is campaigning for
the proposed Constitution.
During the meeting, President Kibaki asked him to reconsider his
decision to back the Orange campaign, the MP revealed. However, Mr Mwenje
said the meeting should not be misconstrued to mean he had defected. "I am
still pondering..." he said.
However, people close to the MP said the decision to join the Banana
side was as good as done.
The Embakasi MP said: "The President called me and I met him... we
discussed many things, among them my position in the Constitution debate."
"I am currently considering what the President told me... but I have not
defected yet."
The MP said there was nothing wrong with him meeting the President since
he (President Kibaki) was the Democratic Party of Kenya chairman and Mr
Mwenje the treasurer.
Last evening, he was holed up in a meeting with Mathioya MP Joseph
Kamotho, a key leader of the Orange team, and his Limuru counterpart Kuria
Kanyingi.
Mr Kanyingi was at State House with Mr Mwenje, although it was not clear
whether they went there together.
The meeting with the President, the Nation learnt, was held at Mr
Mwenje's request. The MP, however, said it was initiated by State
House.
Mr Mwenje, it was understood, requested to see the President two days
ago but this was not possible because of the visiting Thai Prime Minister,
Dr Thaksin Shinawatra.
He finally met the Head of State shortly after Dr Shinawatra's departure
and before the President flew to Mombasa.
Mr Kanyingi told the Nation that the meeting should not be viewed as
strange. "After all they belong to the same party."
Appeared uneasy
He asked: "Is it wrong for people in the same party to meet?"
Last evening, Mr Kamotho arrived at the Panafric Hotel and said: "I want
to see Mr Mwenje."
Earlier, Mr Mwenje had made a call to Mr Kamotho telling him he was at
the hotel. The Mathioya MP arrived a few minutes later.
Mr Mwenje, who appeared uneasy answering questions, said: "I will give a
statement on the matter by Saturday."
The MP said he told the President why he had decided to oppose the
proposed Constitution. However, the President in turn told him why he should
support it.
Mr Mwenje, the Nation, learnt was expected to convey his decision to Mr
Kamotho before making an announcement at a Yes rally today at Ngecha, Kiambu
District. Mr Mwenje and his Makadara counterpart, Mr Reuben Ndolo, were
arrested last month and charged with malicious damage to property.
The two spent three nights in different police stations following a
fracas at City Cabanas Hotel where Nairobi leaders were holding a Yes
strategy meeting.
Before their arrest, the MPs spent a night at Parliament
buildings. However, after successfully evading police and obtaining bond at
Makadara law courts, they were seized and taken to the cells.
They are out on bond.
