Before he was charged and after, an angry Mr Mwenje declared he would
take on President Kibaki in the 2007 General Election.
Elsewhere, the Orange team yesterday claimed the Government plans to
create a fuel shortage on polling day as part of its scheme to rig the
referendum.
The group also alleged that ballot papers and fake currency were being
printed at a secret location to be used to rig the polls.
Artificial fuel shortage
Orange campaign chairman Mutula Kilonzo said the Banana team had hatched
a plot to defranchise voters especially in areas where the Orange group had
a big following.
He was speaking at Orange headquarters in the company of MPs Reuben
Ndolo and William Omondi.
Mr Kilonzo said: "Creating an artificial fuel shortage in the country to
deny Kenyan voters who will need to travel upcountry to vote is part of the
plot."
The team further claimed that the State wanted to use one million
national identity cards to rig the vote in favour of the Yes team.

Original_Search_Title: I'll Deliver Vote, Ngilu Tells Kibaki
Headline: Kenya: I'll Deliver Vote, Ngilu Tells Kibaki
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Health minister Charity Ngilu has said she is confident of delivering the Ukambani vote and dismissed Orange leaders as self-seekers.
The minister said the proposed constitution would protect the youth against discrimination and urged them to support it.
The minister was speaking on Wednesday when she hosted some members of the Kamba community at Home-Park Caterers Hotel, Nairobi, to campaign for the Yes vote.
One of them, who spoke after Ngilu, said: "Things are bad on the ground. Let us not cheat Mheshimiwa to please her, we have to campaign door-to-door."
Ngilu said she was in charge of the Banana campaigns in Ukambani.
She said the proposed constitution would cater for the interests of the poor. The delegates were served beer and soft drinks as Ngilu spoke.
Ngilu is facing a tough challenge in the referendum campaigns from Environment minister Kalonzo Musyoka in the Orange team.
Ngilu bragged that she had initiated many development projects in Ukambani unlike other ministers from the region who have been in the Cabinet for the last 20 years.
The Health minister said President Kibaki had given her the mandate to run the ministry and that is why she had initiated many health projects.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki, Raila Teams Fight It Out in Coast
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki, Raila Teams Fight It Out in Coast
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki yesterday took the Yes vote battle to Mombasa as Roads minister Raila Odinga and Kanu chairman, Uhuru Kenyatta, led the Orange team in three whistle-stop campaigns in Coast Province.
The province at the centre of Banana-Orange supremacy war has 939,888 registered voters.
President Kibaki landed at the Moi International Airport, Mombasa, shortly after Mayor Taib Ali Taib, who is in the Orange team, was shoved by Banana youths who wanted him to leave the airport precincts.
The President, who was last evening expected to meet Coast MPs at State House, Mombasa, drove through sections of the town plastered with portraits of Taib and the orange symbol.
Later in the evening, the President retreated to his quarters in State House as Raila went to his room at the Mombasa Beach Hotel.
With only ten days to go before the plebiscite, President Kibaki's campaign programme could this morning cross paths with that of Raila's team.
Kibaki is scheduled to meet Banana delegates at State House, but by last night it was yet to be confirmed if a rally scheduled for Mvita was still on. The Orange itinerary starts at Mazeras in Kilifi. Raila's entourage may encounter Banana delegations as he heads to Mazeras while the latter travels to State House.
The Orange MPs will return to Mombasa Island and head to Caltex, Likoni and Msambweni with a stopover at Ukunda at 2pm.
At 4.30 pm, Raila's team will drive back to Likoni for a final rally. The team will tomorrow visit parts of Kilifi district to wind up their three-day programme in the province.
The Orange team was last night scheduled to have dinner at Mombasa Beach Club where some of them were booked for the night.
The team includes Heritage minister Najib Balala, Planning minister Anyang' Nyong'o, MPs Ramadhan Kajembe and Billow Kerrow and former Vice President Musalia Mudavadi.
In Garsen, Raila claimed the Head of Civil Service, Francis Muthaura, this week met leading Banana ministers to plot victory at the referendum.
He further claimed Muthaura had met ministers led by Kiraitu Murungi (Justice) and he had information that one of the things discussed was how to delay announcement of the results if the No vote prevailed.
Raila claimed the Banana group was plotting to overturn the results because they could not cope with defeat, but he added that the Orange team would not allow the vote to be manipulated.
During the first rally in Lamu Island, Raila claimed there was a sinister motive by Banana ministers to incite the outlawed Mungiki sect to cause chaos if they lose the referendum.
Raila questioned the motive behind the meeting between Special Projects minister Njenga Karume and former Mungiki leader, Ndura Waruinge on Wednesday, saying it smelt of a plot to cause chaos.
"Force will be met with force!" he said, adding that there was no other reason why Karume would seek out the former Mungiki leader-turned preacher.
After Lamu, the Orange entourage moved to Mpeketoni Settlement Scheme where they addressed the second rally.
Raila claimed that the Government was campaigning using State resources. "If this draft is as good as they claim, then why are they giving people title deeds and salary increases?" he asked.
At the airport, Banana activists heckled and shoved Taib as he lined up to shake the hand of the President. When he extended his hand for a handshake with Foreign minister Chirau Mwakwere, he snubbed him.
One of the activists, Mr Nyonga wa Makemba, placed a Banana hat on Taib's head.
Others at the airport to receive the President were Tourism minister Morris Dzoro and Assistant ministers Danson Mungatana, Anania Mwaboza, Joseph Kingi. Others were MPs Abdalla Ngozi, Gonzi Rai and Harrison Kombe.
Coast Provincial Commissioner Cyrus Maina and provincial Police boss Gatiba Karanja blocked the ejection of Taib from the airport.
Mwaboza accused Taib of pasting the town with his Orange posters and said it was imprudent of him to turn out and meet the President.
Taib asked him to differentiate between an official and a campaign visit by the President. "I am here as the Mayor of Mombasa and Kibaki is coming here as the President of Kenya, I have full authority to welcome him."
First Lady Lucy Kibaki , Internal Security minister John Michuki and Assistant minister Joshua Toro accompanied the President.
At the Orange rally, Muslims chanted Allahu Akbar! (God is great) as Raila claimed the proposed constitution did not cater for the Islamic faith.
Uhuru said that the powers of the presidency had not been curtailed in the new draft, but had been elevated to a dangerous level.
Balala, who was the master of ceremonies, cautioned local people against selling voting cards while Nyong'o claimed he had the names of three foreigners who were at the heart of the Sh7 billion Anglo-Leasing scandal. He said the three had wired back the money to the Government when the scandal leaked.
He said their names were with the Government and challenged Kiraitu to name them. "The Government knows the names of these masterminds of Anglo Leasing. Why are their names being kept a secret?" he asked.
As the President pitched camp in Mombasa, Vice President Moody Awori accused Raila of "dividing the Luhya."
Awori, who led ministers Musikari Kombo and Mukhisa Kituyi on a campaign trail of Western province ahead of Kibaki's visit next week, told the Luhya community to be wary of Raila.
Kombo said Raila could not be trusted as he kept changing parties and political stand. They were speaking at Buchenya Grounds in Butere where a former Cabinet minister, Amukowa Anangwe, hosted them.
"We need to be respected and we must not allow anybody to come here and tell us which way to go politically," Awori said.
He refuted claims by Environment minister Kalonzo Musyoka that the proposed constitution was written to bar him from ascending to the Presidency.
"Let us not look at the constitution subjectively. It is not a step to leadership but about the laws that would guide Kenyans through future generations," Awori said.
Kombo described Raila as a jumpy politician.

Original_Search_Title: ECK Ruling is a Farce, No Team Leaders Say
Headline: Kenya: ECK Ruling is a Farce, No Team Leaders Say
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange team yesterday dismissed as "a farce" the decision by Electoral Commission of Kenya (ECK) to blame both Banana and Orange sides for the Kisumu violence two weeks ago.
At a press conference in Nairobi, nominated MP Mutula Kilonzo assured the bereaved families, injured and maimed that atrocities meted on them would be punished.
"Not only did Kenyans die, (but) a sitting MP lost a car in the violence. They cannot just blame sides. We want to assure all that we will not let their lives to go in vain," said Kilonzo, who was accompanied by Makadara MP Reuben Ndolo and Kasarani MP William Omondi.
The ECK's code of conduct and enforcement committee blamed both the Banana and Orange for Kisumu violence, which claimed five people.
The committee, chaired by ECK vice-chairman Gabriel Mukele, found no group guilty of the violence that rocked Kisumu when Information minister Raphael Tuju held a rally at Moi Stadium.
Mutula said the ECK had rejected their demands even after being assured that they would help it to resolve the case. "They would not hold their meeting in Kisumu, where there were more than 700 witness," said Kilonzo.
He told the ECK and the Government that the country was a signatory to international treaties which forbid the police from using live ammunition in a riot.
Ndolo said the Orange team would hold a rally at Kamukunji on Saturday. "It will be a peaceful rally. We urge all peace-loving Kenyans to turn up," he said.
Meanwhile, the Orange team will meet to chart the way forward for the country's leadership if it wins the November 21 referendum.
Liberal Democratic Party chairman, Mr David Musila, said Kenyans opposing the proposed constitution because of they did not trust the current leadership.
Saying he was the first person to save Kibaki after he was involved in a road accident in December 2002, Musila urged the President to read the mood of the country.
"The truth must be said. Kenyans who overwhelmingly voted for Kibaki in 2002 feel ashamed and tired of his administration," the Mwingi South MP said at a No campaign rally at Kabati market.

Original_Search_Title: ECK to Print More Ballot Papers
Headline: Kenya: ECK to Print More Ballot Papers
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Electoral Commission of Kenya will print 200,000 additional ballot papers, it was revealed yesterday.
This means there will be 11.9 million ballot papers against 11.6 million registered in the referendum.
The ballot papers are expected to be in the country a few days before the November 21 polling date.
Sources at the ECK said that the extra ballot papers were for what sources described as "emergency purposes."
They would be used where there are shortages, the source said.
The vote on the referendum will confirm, if Kenya will have a new constitution or not.
ECK Commissioner, Jack Tumwa, said extra ballot papers had been printed when he met some envoys last week.
The meeting, which was called by the Government to brief the envoys
on the progress of plans for the referendum, was held at the Kenyatta
International Conference Centre.
He said the ballot papers would be sealed in bundles of 50 and there could be a shortage in some areas.
Yesterday, sources at the commission said Kivuitu had okayed the move to print more ballot papers.
But, the Orange camp leaders, led by Kanu secretary-general William
Ruto, were reported recently to have claimed that there was a plot to
rig the referendum.
The Orange leaders claimed the names of some voters had been misspelled.
The ECK chairman Samuel Kivuitu instituted investigations to correct the anomaly after the complaints by the Orange leaders.
The ballot papers bear Banana and Orange symbol representing Yes and No vote respectively.
Police officers normally guard the election materials during voting and counting of the votes.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki Tours Coast to Drum Up Support for New Constitution
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki Tours Coast to Drum Up Support for New Constitution
Body_Text:
Nairobi — With eleven days to go before the landmark referendum, President
Kibaki arrives in Mombasa, Coast Province, today where Orange leaders
have also taken their campaigns.
Kibaki was scheduled to arrive on Friday, but his itinerary was changed.
The Orange camp led by Roads minister Raila Odinga are also expected
in Lamu today and other parts of the province on a three-day visit.
The President, who has not been to the Coast since he opened the
Mombasa ASK show in August, is likely to face several demands by Coast
people some made by his local leutenants in the Banana campaign.
There has been talks that wherever Kibaki goes he will dish out
favours from his bag of goodies, especially in form of title deeds to
squatters who are numerous in the Coast.
Lands minister Amos Kimunya has camped in the Coast for a week,
demonstrating a sneak preview of what to expect. Kimunya has held three
rallies in which he promised things that are close to the hearts of
Coast people.
In Kisauni, Kimunya gave a reprieve on payment of ground rent for
over 60,000 residents who have been in arrears and are facing eviction.
In Kilifi on Tuesday he offered 23 titles deeds to squatters in a
settlement scheme and in Taveta yesterday, he was expected to dish out
more title deeds.
Kimunya also revoked the allocation of three major islands in Kwale;
Mpunguti, Kisite ya Juu and Kisite Ya Chini, which are set to revert to
wananchi through the local council.
Kibaki is expected to make major announcements, mostly echoing old
Narc pre-election promises to the Coast, as he seeks support for Banana
votes.
Kibaki is also expected to make major announcements on the collapsed
factories and the issue of absentee landlords especially in Mvita.
"The President will make major announcements which are in the best interests of Coast people," said Mwaboza.
"The President will be here to talk to his people. He is also expected to visit Malindi and Bahari," added Mwaboza.
The President had first been expected to stir the Orange stronghold
of Mvita, Cabinet minister Najib Balala's constituency, with his first
rally, but a surprise twist emerged yesterday with a confirmation that
he has asked to meet all Coast MPs, in the Banana and Orange camps.
The President, who will be in Mombasa for three days, will meet the MPs at State House Mombasa.
Coast PC Cyrus Maina yesterday confirmed Kibaki's visit. Bahari MP
Joe Khamisi who is the chairman of the Coast Parliamentary Group said
he had been asked by the PC to inform his fellow MPs about the change
of programme.
Banana leaders from the province led by ministers Morris Dzoro, Ali
Chirau Mwakwere Ananiah Mwaboza and Danson Mungatana, have been holding
rallies in the area ahead of the President's visit.
They will join Kibaki tomorrow at Mvita Primary School grounds. Kibaki is also expected to visit Malindi District.
However, the Orange side, which will host Raila and top Orange
luminaries in Lamu, Kwale and Ganze, says whether President Kibaki
visits Coast or not, the die on the constitution has been cast.
Mayor Taib Ali Taib who joined the Orange team recently said the Head of State was welcome to Mombasa.

Original_Search_Title: Electoral Commission Blames Yes, No Teams Over Kisumu Violence
Headline: Kenya: ECK Blames Yes, No Teams Over Kisumu Violence
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Electoral Commission of Kenya (ECK) yesterday blamed both the
Orange and Banana teams for the Kisumu violence in which five people
were killed two weeks ago.
Even then, the ECK's Code of Conduct and Enforcement Committee found
no group guilty of the violence that rocked Kisumu during the Banana
rally hosted by Cabinet minister Raphael Tuju.
"While there was breach of the Code of Conduct, there is no
conclusive evidence as to members of the referendum committees that
were involved in the violence," said Committee chairman Gabriel Mukele.
"For lack of conclusive evidence, the committee will not find any
particular referendum committee or member guilty of an offence. The
commission must blame both sides and particularly the Orange for what
took place," he said.
The ECK vice-chairman also exonerated both sides for the Kakamega
violence that resulted in the burning of Ikolomani MP Bonny Khalwale's
car.
"The MP was a victim of it (violence) and suffered severely. We sympathise with him," he said.
Mukele maintained that they would go to court to stop campaigns by either side if one more death occurred.
"The commission would in the circumstances warn both sides to the
referendum and state that if the violence goes on, ECK under Paragraph
9 of the Elecotral Code will go to court to stop the campaigns," said
Mukele.
In both the Kisumu and Ikolomani cases, Mukele said it was the job
of police to maintain law and order. He noted that it was their
responsibility to protect life and property.
"There was total breakdown of law and order. There are issues of why
live arms were used in Kisumu. The evidence was that the police were
dealing with the matter and people had appeared in court," said Mukele.
But Orange team lawyers reacted angrily, terming the ruling a public relations exercise.
The lawyers, led by Tom Kajwang, said they would move to the courts
to petition the Government to set up a judicial inquest into the Kisumu
deaths.
"We have instructions to petition the Attorney-General Amos Wako for
a judicial inquest. We are going to bring to book the perpetrators of
the violence. We will get to the bottom of it. That we assure you,"
said Kajwang.
Mukele said ECK should be allowed to carry out voter education as a
solution to the violence. The exercise is currently being carried out
by Constitution of Kenya review Commission (CKRC).
Condemning violence, he said it was counter-productive even to those who perpetrated it.
Kajwang claimed that the ECK had found a "safe way of running away from reality".
"It has tried to justify its existence in the referendum. We
mentioned names during the exercise and I do not see them mentioned
anywhere," he said.
Josephine Ojiambo for the Orange team called on ECK to ascribe
blame. "We want names. We want to know who was responsible for the
deaths."
Another lawyer, Mr Daniel Maanzo, said ECK had refused to use its
powers under the law. "It is not a toothless bulldog. It has just
refused to bite."

Original_Search_Title: Raila Urges No Voters to Accept Yes Freebies
Headline: Kenya: Raila Urges No Voters to Accept Yes Freebies
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Roads minister Raila Odinga has told voters to take home goodies offered
by the Banana team, but vote for Orange.
The freebies, Mr Odinga said, were theirs, and voters should tell the
givers to bring even more.
Speaking on Tuesday evening during a talk show on a Migori Town-based FM
radio station, Mr Odinga urged Kenyans to remain steadfast in opposing the
proposed law, despite the inducements.
The minister claimed on Kas FM that the Banana side was using all forms
of baits to woo voters after sensing defeat.
And he told chiefs not to be cheated that their jobs were safe.
He said: "Chiefs should be wary of assurances being given by Internal
Security minister John Michuki. The proposed constitution has clearly spelt
out their fate."
Mr Odinga told them to refuse being used to drum up support for the
proposed law and appealed to all eligible Kenyans to vote on November
21.
Said he: "As democrats, we will accept the verdict of the people. But we
will not accept the results if our Yes counterparts engage in election
irregularities."
He said the No team was not a tribal outfit but reflected the whole face
of Kenya. Leaders from all corners of the country, Mr Odinga said, were in
the Orange team.
At the same time, Cabinet colleague Linah Kilimo told President Kibaki
to stop drumming up support for the Banana team.
Saying the Head of State should be neutral, Mrs Kilimo added that only
ministers should spearhead the campaigns.
She said: "Taking sides by the Head of State has created hostilities
among Kenyans and he should have left the task to us (ministers) for a
peaceful Kenya after November 21."
Addressing constituents at Tot trading centre in her Marakwet East
constituency, Mrs Kilimo said: "We (No team) are confident of winning. But
what will happen after we win?"
The Immigration minister asked her constituents to turn up in large
numbers to "kick out" the proposed Constitution.
And at Lomut and Sigor trading centres in West Pokot District, Mrs
Kilimo reiterated that she was still loyal to President Kibaki and would
continue discharging her Cabinet duties diligently after November 21.
She said: "My decision to support the Orange team does not mean I am not
loyal to the President."
A recent remark by Vice-President Moody Awori that the proposed new
Constitution was not a government project had given Cabinet members a free
hand to join the team of their choice, she added.
At Chesegon and Tot in Marakwet East and in Arror, Marakwet West
constituency, represented by MP David Sudi, the No team warned residents of
massive supply of relief food and money to woo voters to the Banana
side.
Mrs Kilimo, Kanu secretary-general William Ruto and 10 MPs censured Ms
Mary Wambui, a political activist, for squandering State resources.
Ms Wambui is scheduled to visit the area tomorrow.
The leaders asked the Kenya Human Rights Commission to take action
against the activist, claiming she was being accorded State security.

Original_Search_Title: Both Sides Guilty Over Campaign Rioting - ECK
Headline: Kenya: Both Sides Guilty Over Campaign Rioting - ECK
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Both sides in the referendum debate were guilty of causing the rioting
that left four people dead, the Electoral Commission said yesterday.
But the larger share of the blame belonged to the Orange No-vote camp,
ruled the commission's disciplinary committee.
For Orange supporters got into the way of the Banana Yes-vote team
during the October 29 rally hosted in Kisumu by Information minister Raphael
Tuju, said the committee chaired by commission vice-chairman Gabriel
Mukele.
And after delivering its verdict, Mr Mukele issued a stern warning to
both the Yes and No teams:
If there is any more violence the commission will seek a court order
banning both sides from campaigning.
There was no evidence to implicate any individuals or committees on
either side to make the ECK impose punishment for the rally deaths,
however.
The ECK is empowered to reprimand, fine or seek court orders banning
public campaigns.
As well as the four people killed in the Kisumu riots, 30 other people
were left nursing gunshot wounds, while another riot in Kakamega earlier in
the same week ended with several people injured and a car belonging to
Ikolomani MP Bonny Khalwale burnt to a shell.
Mr Mukele read two rulings - one for Kisumu and one for Kakamega - for
half-an-hour in a packed conference room at the ECK headquarters in
Nairobi.
The commission condemned the violence in both instances and accused the
Orange team of influencing the Kisumu violence because it had attempted to
interfere with the Banana rally. Mr Mukele said: "While there was breach of
the Code of Conduct, there is no conclusive evidence as to members of the
referendum committees that were involved in the violence.
"But it is reasonable to state that the violence had origin in the two
sides of the referendum with the Orange side getting into the way of the
Banana side."
"People lost their lives and the commission must blame both sides and
particularly the Orange side for what took place."
Announcing the commission would seek a ban of future rallies if violence
was repeated, he said: "The commission would in the circumstances warn both
sides to the referendum and state that if the violence goes on in Kisumu or
other parts of Kenya, the commission will go to court and under Paragraph 9
of the Electoral Code of Conduct ask for an order barring both sides from
campaigning and allow the referendum to proceed without campaigns."
Mr Mukele ruled that those involved in the rioting in both places had
already been charged in separate courts and that the cases would go to
trial.
Although the commission sympathised with Dr Khalwale for losing his car,
it also accused him of not having notified the police of his procession
through Kakamega Town, as required by law.
Evidence to the commission by police and the peace committee indicated
the MP had not informed them of his procession.
Both Mr Tuju and Dr Khalwale failed to back their submissions that
Kisumu violence was carried out by hooligans from Nairobi while that of
Kakamega was committed by youths from Kisumu, the committee said.
Police and peace committee evidence contradicted this, Mr Mukele
said.
Although nobody had complained formally to the commission about the
rioting, it had acted according to the Electoral Code of Conduct by
summoning both sides.
Mr Mukele said his committee ignored pleas from Orange lawyers that
favours were being given to voters as they were not part of the summons. It
also rejected suggestions for it to visit and receive evidence from the
scenes of the rioting as the hearings were informal.
Kisumu police chief Stanley Kilonzi said they had expected problems, but
allowed the rally to go on as they did not foresee violence of that
scale. He said 402 regular police and 34 paramilitary General Service Unit
officers were deployed.
Mr Kilonzi said problems began at night on the eve of the meeting and on
the day of the rally his officers were assaulted, roads were blocked and
drivers were robbed, forcing his officers to use live ammunition.
Evidence from Mr Nyagah and Mr Isaac Ruto - the chief agents of the Yes
and No groups - amounted to them blaming each other.
Orange lawyers Tom Kajwang', Daniel Maanzo and Josephine Ojiambo
dismissed the ECK's ruling saying, the commission did not have "teeth to
bite despite having the law to punish".
The Orange team said they would petition the Attorney-General to start a
judicial inquest into the killings. We will bring to book the perpetrators
of the violence... we will get to the bottom of this matter," Mr Kajwang'
said.
They said despite giving the commission names of those behind the Kisumu
violence, it had failed to discipline them, and they gave a warning that
violence could continue with impunity since the commission had openly shown
its weakness.
They also blamed the commission for failing to reprimand the Banana team
for ignoring the committee's two-day deliberations.
The Banana team did not send lawyers, although its representative Mr
Norman Nyagah, the chief whip, appeared. Also giving evidence were Banana
supporters Mr Tuju and Dr Khalwale.

Original_Search_Title: Orange Leaders in Uproar Over Chiefs Pay Deal
Headline: Kenya: Orange Leaders in Uproar Over Chiefs Pay Deal
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange team yesterday protested at the Government's decision to
raise salaries of chiefs and assistant chiefs.
They wrote to the Electoral Commission to say that the move violated the
referendum campaigns' code of conduct.
Their campaign committee chairman, Mr Mutula Kilonzo, said in the letter
to ECK chief Samuel Kivuitu that the new pay was meant to influence the
outcome of the poll in favour of the Yes group.
But he said the No team did not begrudge the administrators their right
to get a salary increase. "We encourage them to accept the hefty increases
in anticipation of the abolition of their offices should Yes win," he
said.
The gesture and timing of the move, the team said, amounted to an abuse
of the power, privilege and influence of the Banana team, which "comprises
the Government."
Mr Kilonzo said: "As you are well aware, the Orange team has no similar
power or influence over chiefs or government resources and cannot compete
fairly against such violatory conduct towards the Electoral Code."
The letter, copied to Yes team leader Simeon Nyachae and Finance
minister David Mwiraria, said the pay rise discriminated against other civil
servants, "who have been demanding increases in salaries including the
police, teachers and university lecturers."
It termed the move a show of arrogance to and disregard for ECK.
The International Commission of Jurists (ICJ) Kenya section executive
director Samuel Mbithi said the proposed Constitution clearly stated that
the Provincial Administration would be dissolved within six months after it
sails through.
Assistant minister Fred Gumo termed the new salaries a big joke, arguing
that the administrators would be rendered jobless if the draft was
approved. "How do you increase the salary of somebody who is going, unless
you are paying them a golden handshake?" he asked.
Makadara MP Reuben Ndolo challenged the Government to state the source
of the money to pay the chiefs.
"Parliament has not approved any extra money for chiefs, so where is
this money coming from?" he posed.
In Mombasa, chiefs and district officers interviewed said the new pay
was deserved as chiefs were currently earning less than the Administration
Police under them.
Muslims for Human Rights project coordinator Hussein Khalid said that
the move was "a government ploy to hoodwink chiefs into supporting the
proposed constitution."
In Eldoret, Pioneer chief Peter Samoei said the increment was long
overdue as it was promised in 2003.
Eldoret South MP David Koros said the move was a bribe to make chiefs
force the people to vote Yes.
In Siaya, chiefs said it could be a ploy to have them help the
Government to win the poll.
Reports by By Lucas Barasa, David Mugonyi, Mike Mwaniki, Tony Kago, Juma
Namlola, Peter Ng'etich and John Onyango

Original_Search_Title: Human Rights Lobby Starts Probe On Kisumu Violence
Headline: Kenya: Human Rights Lobby Starts Probe On Kisumu Violence
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A human rights watchdog has launched investigations into the violence in
which four people died and 40 others were injured in Kisumu.
The Kenya National Commission on Human Rights has set up camp at the
Kisumu Social Centre from where its officials will interview the police,
politicians and area residents on the October 29 violence.
No stone unturned
Three officials, led by commissioner Khalef Khalifa, arrived in Kisumu
on Tuesday and said they would "leave no stone unturned" to expose those
behind the violence during which two schoolboys were shot dead. They will be
in Kisumu for three days.
Among those to be interviewed are Roads and Public Works minister Raila
Odinga and his Information and Communications counterpart Raphael Tuju.
Police officers, journalists and the public will also be expected to
testify. Yesterday, Mr Khalifa said: "Our mandate is to find out why live
bullets were used and whether the shootings were premeditated or not."
Mr Khalifa asked those with information on the violence to visit the
centre and testify.
Kisumu Town East MP Gor Sunguh has called for the arrest of all the
officers who used live bullets during the riots.
"They should all be charged with murder alongside those who ordered them
to use force on unarmed civilians," Mr Sunguh said.
He accused some senior Government officials of politicising the
investigations into the deaths and challenged assistant minister Mirugi
Kariuki to name the senior politician from Nyanza whom he claimed flew to
Kisumu in a helicopter to plan the violence. The claims were serious and
should be clarified, the MP added.
Last week, Mr Kariuki told Parliament that a minister organised the
youths who tried to disrupt the Banana campaign rally.
The MP said the real impact of the mayhem was just beginning to
emerge. According to him, some people who were beaten by police could have
died in their houses.
Inhaling tear gas
He said he had received reports of children who had been taken ill after
inhaling tear gas.
He said among the children affected was a two-year-old grandchild of
Jaramogi Oginga Odinga. The child's parents had just arrived from Canada and
were staying in a house near the stadium.
Mr Sunguh said he had also received reports that many people who were
clobbered by the police were still nursing their wounds at home.
He could not give the identities of some of the people alleged to have
been killed and their bodies hidden or those nursing wounds at home

Original_Search_Title: We Won't Quit, Says Orange Team
Headline: Kenya: We Won't Quit, Says Orange Team
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Three Cabinet ministers in the Orange team yesterday rejected calls that
they quit government.
They declared that they would not quit a government they helped to
create.
Ministers Kalonzo Musyoka (Environment), Anyang Nyong'o (Planning) and
Ochillo Ayacko (Sports) defended their No stand in the referendum debate. Mr
Ayacko explained that Liberal Democratic Party ministers - a major partner
in the National Rainbow Coalition besides the National Alliance (Party) of
Kenya - will not resign from the Government "because it is us who made
it".
Mr Ayacko said his flag was with the people and did not fear losing his
ministerial docket.
A good constitution, he argued, was much more important than a cabinet
post.
He spoke in Rongo as his colleague Mr Musyoka was campaigning in
Mwingi.
Said Mr Ayacko: "We are not going to resign from the Narc
government. But in case we are sacked let Kenyans know that things will
never be the same again."
Addressing a rally in his Mwingi district home, the Environment minister
defended his colleague Raila Odinga, saying he had every right to feel
ashamed of serving in the Government following the Kisumu riots where four
people were killed.
The ministers spoke as Education minister George Saitoti led the Yes
team to campaign in Kajiado district, where accused some leaders from the
Maasai community of undermining him.
He claimed that the same leaders had been used to throw him out of Kanu
leadership in the run up to the 2002 General Election.
Said the Education minister: "Do not allow yourself to be misled like in
2002 when a section of the Maasai leaders were used to ensure that the
Maasai community was left out of leadership."
On Monday Prof Saitoti and Cabinet colleagues David Mwiraria (Finance)
and Chris Murungaru (Transport) asked Mr Odinga to quit if he was ashamed of
serving in the government.
Prof Nyong'o accused his colleagues in the Banana team of being
preoccupied with sacking and resignation threats at any slight
opportunity.
"These people don't understand democracy. What they know is the
dictatorship that they have lived with most of their lives," Prof Nyong'o
told the Nation on phone.
He defended Mr Odinga's remarks that he was ashamed serving a Government
that killed its people. Mr Musyoka and deputy Speaker David Musila were
addressing a rally at Musila Gardens in Mwingi town where he led more than
10 MPs to campaign against the new Constitution.
Mr Musyoka also alleged that three million ballot papers had been
imported from Poland for the purpose of rigging the November 21 public
vote.
But he was unable to justify his claims when challenged by the
Nation.
In Marsabit, National Heritage Minister Najib Balala said ministers in
the No group could not be intimidated or shaken by those in the Banana
team.
"I know there are many people who are waiting for Balala's flag, but I
will not be intimidated," he said.
Mr Odinga repeated his accusation that State House had been turned into
an "auction ring" where people were given national parks, relief food,
salary increases and districts.
Districts were people's right although they were being used to lure
people to vote Yes.
The Marsabit rally was also addressed by MPs Bonaya Godana, Billow
Kerrow, Chris Okemo, Ahmed Khalif and Moffat Maitha.

Original_Search_Title: Ballot Papers Expected, Says Polls Official
Headline: Kenya: Ballot Papers Expected, Says Polls Official
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Referendum ballot papers are expected to arrive from overseas any day
from today, the Electoral Commission said yesterday.
Vice-chairman Gabriel Mukele would not - "for security reasons" - name
the exact day but said the high security printers had last week completed
and packed half of the 15 million papers bearing the Orange and Banana
symbols awaiting freight.
"Ballot papers are sensitive materials, so they will be kept in a secure
place and be sent only a few days before polling," he said.
He added: "By last week most of them had been printed and packed."
Each has one question in English and Kiswahili: Are You For or Against
the Ratification of the Proposed New Constitution?/Je, Unakubali au Unapinga
Kuidhinishwa kwa Katiba Mpya?
Mr Mukele was upbeat about the November 21 voting date, saying that,
even if the ECK were to be called upon only in the next five days, it would
still be able to conduct the polls.
Although the ECK would not disclose the printer's name, several
companies, including the UK-based Smith and Ouzman which printed the 1997
and 2002 ballot papers and is approved as a security printer, was among the
bidders.
The bidders included Kalamazoo of the UK, Manipal of India, Lithotech of
South Africa, SR Communications of the UK, TWAS of Kenya and Aeroprint of
the UK.
Edward Thompson (UK), Efcon International (Dubai) Hitech (India), Madras
Security (India), Renform (South Africa) and Sahitya also placed bids.
The ballot papers were printed by the overseas firm and at hand were
electoral officials to verify that the details are correct.
"We have already dispatched other (polling) materials to the districts,"
said Mr Mukele.
These include transparent ballot boxes, ink, books containing the
registered voters' names in each of the 20,000 polling centres and other
stationery.
And voting booths have been built in the centres in readiness for the
referendum, 12 days away.
On voting day, polling stations will be open between 7am and 5pm.
Voters would be allowed to vote only at the centres where they are
registered, and each must carry a voter's card and an identity card or a
valid Kenyan passport.
The ECK allows any mark (tick, cross or comma) made by a voter on the
ballot paper provided it is not a signature or a name that can identify the
voter.

Original_Search_Title: ECK to Make Ruling On Violence Today
Headline: Kenya: ECK to Make Ruling On Violence Today
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The fate of the Yes and the No campaign teams over the Kisumu Town
killings and violence in Kakamega will be known today.
The Electoral Commission of Kenya (ECK) will pass its verdict and make
public the masterminds and the perpetrators of the violence on October 29,
in which four people died at the lakeside. They were shot dead during a
day-long riot following a Yes rally organised by Information minister
Raphael Tuju.
The Kakamega violence culminated in the burning of Ikolomani MP Bonny
Khalwale's car.
Yesterday, Lurambi peace committee chairman Mathew Musambi said the MP's
Yes rally-cum-procession, on October 27, had not been licensed and the
latter's car was burnt half a kilometre away from the venue of the meeting
by pro-Orange youths.
The official caused a light moment when he stood up and performed an
Isikuti dance singing khwenyanga Lichungwa (we want the Orange) in Luyia
language.
The official tabled minutes of their meeting on October 30, stating
that: "The MP's procession from Muyala to Kakamega Town was a
meet-the-people tour. The MP did not apply for a permit to conduct the
same."
On Monday, the inquiry heard that Cabinet ministers Raila Odinga and Mr
Tuju, who are on the opposite sides of the referendum debate, met in State
House, Nairobi, before the chaotic Yes rally in Kisumu.
And when they spoke, Mr Odinga promised there would be no violence at
the rally, Mr Tuju had said in his evidence.
The ECK's electoral code of conduct gives it the power to warn, enforce
regulations, fine (candidates) and stop others from campaigns if they engage
in election malpractices.
So far, the violence has been condemned by leaders from both camps, who
are accusing each other of masterminding it.
The Yes team, whose symbol is the banana, is campaigning for the
proposed Constitution to be adopted at the November 21 referendum, while the
No camp, whose symbol is an orange, is campaigning against it.
Yesterday, the ECK deputy vice-chairman, Mr Gabriel Mukele, said they
had concluded their inquiry after receiving evidence from Mr Tuju and Dr
Khalwale among others. Said Mr Mukele: "We have heard a lot of evidence so
far. We will withdraw and deliver a verdict on Wednesday (today) at 2.30pm."
On Monday, the ECK deputy chairman had also warned the two camps that
should there be any more deaths at their campaign rallies before the
November 21 referendum, he would seek a court injunction to stop all
politicians from undertaking further campaigns.
Five people were yesterday charged with incitement and plan to commit a
felony.
Mr Washington Obondo Odongo, Mr Dorington Olando, Mr Festus Murray, Mr
Joshua Rume Ochieng' and Mr Milton Wafula Wakhungu were found with pangas
during an Orange rally at Navakholo market in Kakamega District, a Kakamega
court was told.
The court ruled that they could be released on a Sh20,000 bond each with
a surety in similar amount or a Sh5,000 cash bail. The case will be heard on
January 24.
In the same court, the five people who have been charged with burning
Ikolomani MP Bonny Khalwale's car were each released on a Sh100,000 bail
with a surety in similar amount until January 13 when the case will
heard.

Original_Search_Title: Chiefs Pay Doubled As Draft Row Rages
Headline: Kenya: Chiefs Pay Doubled As Draft Row Rages
Body_Text:
Nairobi — At least 6,000 chiefs and their assistants are to be given big pay rises
- in some cases more than double their present earnings.
A senior chief's salary will double from Sh15,600 to Sh33,384, the same
as a district officer, and the rises do not include housing allowances and
other benefits.
The new increases are contained in a scheme of service released by the
Office of the President last night.
It details new categories which will see the chiefs jump several job
grades but still retain their designation. The scheme also details the job
description of each category of chiefs, which range from the lowest -
assistant chief two to the highest - senior chief.
But the move immediately renewed criticism of the Government for
attempting to bribe the chiefs to ensure they pushed for a Yes vote in the
referendum slated for November 21.
Kanu secretary-general William Ruto, one of the leading lights of the
Orange No-vote campaign, declared the rises to be part of the Government's
"grand scheme of buying support in the referendum."
"The chiefs should not be hoodwinked," he told the Nation. "If they want
to work hard for the Banana team and ensure that the new Constitution goes
through, then they should as well consider it a golden handshake because
they will be jobless after that." He said the pay rises were "the latest in
a series of attempts to win support".
Previously the Government has been accused of trying to buy Yes-votes by
issuing land title deeds to squatters in the Mau Forest, handing Amboseli
national park over to the local Ol Kejuado county council, rescinding the
order that land should not be sub-divided below two-and-a-half acres,
creating additional districts and giving relief food to hard-hit marginal
areas including Ukambani.
In the latter case, the food was distributed only hours after Kitui
Central MP and Health minister Charity Ngilu declared her support for the
Yes campaign.
The increases for the chiefs and their assistants come less than a month
after a meeting at which Internal Security minister John Michuki assured
them all they would keep their jobs - even if with different titles - in
spite of the scrapping of the Provincial Administration required by the
proposed Constitution. They would be needed to run the new devolved district
governments, the Government said.
And only last month, assistant minister Mirugi Kariuki declared that the
Government was not concerned if its critics saw the move as an attempt to
bribe the chiefs to ensure they pushed for a Yes vote in the referendum
slated for November 21.
Mr Kariuki's comment came after he promised that at least 700 chiefs and
their assistants would benefit from salary increases.
The new round of rises comes only months after the chiefs and other
civil servants were given 14 per cent pay increases across the board,
following weeks of acrimonious exchanges which led to a strike which
resulted in the sacking of scores of civil servants.
The new scheme sets out their appointment specifications, duties and
responsibilities.
A senior administrator in Mr Michuki's office, Mr George Natembeya, said
yesterday the new round of rises will come into effect soon.
"It is the first time the chiefs are having their own scheme of
service. They used to stagnate at job group J (Senior Chief) but now a
senior chief can go up to job group N where DCs used to start. We want to
professionalise the job," he said.
Precise figures for the new salaries for the chiefs were still being
prepared.
Under the old system, an assistant chief started at job group E on
employment and rose up to G but now he or she has to start at G and go up to
J where university graduates start.
"If an assistant chief becomes chief, he or she jumps up to job group K
and can rise to N," Mr Natembeya said.
There are about 6,000 chiefs in the country, Mr Natembeya said, adding
that there were many more assistant chiefs.
A new job scheme for district officers who used to start at job group J,
Mr Natembeya said, will be released soon.
"They now have to start at a higher scale," he said.
An assistant chief II will jump one job group from E to G, meaning the
lowest paid assistant chief in this category will be taking home between
Sh8,738 and Sh12,636 a month, up from between Sh6, 287 and Sh7,700.
Most of the promotions have seen the chiefs jump one job group or in
some instances, two groups. An assistant chief I will move from job group F
to job group H where he will earn between Sh10,220 and Sh15,600, up from
between Sh6,697 and Sh8,407.
However, a senior assistant chief has now been promoted to job group J
from job group G. Instead of between Sh8,738 and Sh12,636 paid previously,
this cadre will now earn Sh14,464 and Sh15,600.
Other cadres of chiefs who have moved are chief I and chief II, who have
each jumped three job groups. Chief I will now take home between Sh19,647
and Sh26,259 while Chief II, will be going home more than Sh8,000 the
richer. They have jumped from between Sh8,738 and Sh12,636 to between
Sh17,367 and Sh22,839.
A senior chief who is in job group J will now be able to rise through
the ranks to job groups M and N, which were previously the preserve of
district officers. He or she will now take home between Sh30,819 and
Sh33,384.
The new conversions are expected to take place even though the chiefs
may not have the minimum qualification and or experience stipulated in the
scheme of service.
However, those who hope to be promoted after the scheme of service comes
into place are expected to ensure they meet these qualifications and to have
acquired the necessary experience to be considered.
If employed as an assistant chief, the person will be entering the civil
service at job group G, which means they will be earning between Sh8,738 and
Sh12,636 while a chief will be joining at job group J where salaries range
between Sh14,464 and Sh15,600.

Original_Search_Title: How Nak Bigwigs Failed the Probity Test
Headline: Kenya: How Nak Bigwigs Failed the Probity Test
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Both the Orange and Banana camps - which represent the sum total of the
country's dominant political class - are inherently incapable of envisioning
a grand future for Kenya. It is no wonder that politicians have based the
referendum campaigns on tribes and personalities, and not competing
visions.
This article unmasks the genealogy of the Banana camp to demonstrate why
it has an atrophied brain as a political machine.
Virtually the entire senior leadership of the Banana faction was trained
either in Kanu's autocratic culture or the politics of patronage,
neo-patrimony, and cronyism.
This political culture grips the political elite in such a vice that it
has successfully co-opted outsiders from civil society and Moi-era political
oppositionists.
This is possible because our political parties are not driven by any
political ideologies or economic and social philosophies. They are
intellectually barren receptacles for tribal barons and ethnic
demagoguery.
Let's peel the banana. The leading lights of the Banana camp had a
long-standing feud with the Kanu regime. Their core leadership is drawn from
the Democratic Party, a political class from Central and Eastern provinces
that broke away from Kanu in 1991.
The DP joined with Michael Wamalwa's Ford Kenya and Charity Ngilu's
National Party of Kenya, the other main anti-Moi oppositionists, to form
NAK, the party that engineered Kanu's ouster in 2002.
With the exception of Mr Gitobu Imanyara, Mr Raila Odinga, Mr James
Orengo, Mr Pheroze Nowrojee, and Mr Paul Muite, NAK was home to the largest
number of reformist politicians. It was the pivot of the major
opposition.
Today, the bananas have captured Mr Muite. But perhaps their biggest
coup is the clout added to their ranks by Mr Simeon Nyachae and his Ford
People. Kanu legislators from Central Province have abandoned Mr Uhuru
Kenyatta and hitched their wagons to President Kibaki's Yes camp. These
"deserters" are led by Mr Njenga Karume, a founding member of DP and
long-time Kibaki ally.
A telling addition to the Yes camp is Mr Nicholas Biwott and his
breakaway Kanu faction. Mr Biwott chafed at Moi's choice of Uhuru as the
Kanu leader.
Two other major catches for the Yes team are Vice-President Moody Awori
and Minister George Saitoti, both LDP luminaries who have crossed over to
NAK. Prof Saitoti, a former Vice-President passed over by Moi for Uhuru,
could not reconcile himself to Raila's leadership. The calculation is that
he would bring a substantial Maasai vote.
VP Awori's effective defection from LDP weakens the No campaign and
beefs up the Yes camp in Western Province.
In a plot reminiscent of the schism between the late Oginga Odinga and
Tom Mboya, the two most important nationalists of Luo descent, NAK has
encouraged an increasingly precocious Raphael Tuju, originally an LDP
minister, to undermine Raila's magic spell over the Luo. He has broken free
of Raila and established himself as a rare voice of dissent in Luo
Nyanza.
He may already have eclipsed Prof Peter Anyang'-Nyong'o, the NAK
minister who has defected to the LDP, as the second most important Luo
politician. But his flickering star is unlikely to translate into votes for
the Yes faction.
Clearly, NAK has circled its wagons around the Kibaki
administration. Interestingly, apart from Anyang' Nyong'o, NAK has not
suffered any major defections, although Ngilu's star has plummeted in
Ukambani. Mr Kalonzo Musyoka has capitalised on her mercurial temperament
and self-inflicted woes to give the Orange team the upper hand in the
region.
What then are the engine and glue for the Yes camp? The bottom line for
the Yes team is a win by any means necessary.
President Kibaki has suddenly sprung to life. He is deploying the full
advantages of incumbency to vanquish the naysayers. He has given away land,
food aid, and a game reserve. He has promised new districts and development
projects to wavering supporters.
Procedurally, the banana team has played fast and loose with the
law. Even worse, President Kibaki may promulgate an unconstitutional
Constitution if the banana side triumphs.
The referendum vote, though required by the Njoya case, would violate
Section 47 of the current Constitution, a provision that must have been
amended to constitutionalise the birth of a new Constitution. It remains to
be seen if this illegality can be politically cured by the referendum
outcome.
True, the proposed Constitution is a vast improvement on the
current. Anyone who says otherwise is a liar. But it has major defects as
well. It is shoddily drafted and seeks to ram down the country's throat
NAK's version of the contested issues.
What's worse, the banana campaigners - not unlike their Orange
counterparts - have engaged in lies and distortions. Unbelievably, some
banana leaders have even said that the Provincial Administration will not be
abolished.
Senior banana ministers have used threats of violence against their
opponents. In certain cases - such as in Kisumu recently - the police
unacceptably used deadly force against civilians.
The constitutional review process would have been a golden opportunity
for the NAK team to show its moral superiority over the Kanu
regime. Instead, an incompetent collection of old men and their unprincipled
young cohorts squandered Kenya's best opportunity since independence to
create a true prosperous democracy.
Ethnic chauvinism, the culture of anti-intellectualism, corruption, and
political greed have claimed yet one more African success story.
Prof Mutua, who teaches law at the State University of New York at
Buffalo, chairs the Kenya Human Rights Commission.

Original_Search_Title: You're Unable to Probe Violence, Lawyer Tells ECK
Headline: Kenya: You're Unable to Probe Violence, Lawyer Tells ECK
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange team yesterday said the Electoral Commission of Kenya (ECK) lacked the capacity to investigate referendum violence.
Speaking at ECK's Code of Conduct and Enforcement Committee
hearings, Orange lawyers James Orengo and Tom Kajwang' asked the
commission to ban the Banana team from campaigning in areas where they
have not registered referendum committees.
"This is against the law. They have no right to do so. It is such
places like Kakamega and Kisumu, where there is most violence," said
Kajwang'.
The committee, chaired by ECK vice-chairman Gabriel Mukele, had
summoned Cabinet minister Raphael Tuju and Ikolomani MP Bonny Khalwale
over the Kisumu and Kakamega violence.
Mukele postponed the verdict-that was to be delivered yesterday-to this afternoon.
"We sat until very late, 7.30pm on Monday. We are unable to sit this
afternoon to make a ruling. We will do it tomorrow (today)," said
Mukele as he adjourned the sittings.
Yesterday, the committee heard from Raziah Anyanzwa and Mathew
Musambi, who are members of Lurambi referendum peace committees.
Lawyers for the Banana team were not present.
In a statement signed by Orengo and circulated to the news media at
the hearings, the Orange demanded the setting up of an independent
Judicial inquiry to investigate Kisumu violence.
"ECK does not seem to have the time or the people and space to conduct proper investigations," said Orengo.
He said the Government should take responsibility of the five deaths in Kisumu.
"The Government and the police must take responsibility for what happened in Kisumu," he said.
He blamed Tuju for the violence, saying he used "bad language," thus provoking the violence.
"Tuju had used intemperate language and expressions which
contributed immensely to the violence showing disrespect to the
judgement and conscience of the people. In effect, he was saying that
he was ready to fight," said Orengo.
He exonerated the Orange team from Kisumu and Kakamega violence, saying Khalwale was the mastermind of the violence.
"The story which is not being told is that Hon Khalwale was possibly the author of his own misfortune," said Orengo.

Original_Search_Title: I Want to Be President, Minister Declares
Headline: Kenya: I Want to Be President, Minister Declares
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Ford-Kenya chairman Musikari Kombo has said his ultimate goal is to become Kenya's fourth Head of State.
Kombo asked the Luhya to unite to enable him achieve his dream.
"I really want to be president and I am appealing to my people to
form a formidable alliance with those whom we can trust," he said.
Kombo, who is the Local Government minister, said he would rather work with President Kibaki than Roads minister Raila Odinga.
He said Raila could not be trusted in forming political alliances.
Kombo said Raila had in the past let Luhyas down after he abandoned the late Vice-President Michael Wamalwa in 1997.
"Wamalwa supported Raila's father in the 1992 General Election only
for Raila to abandon him later after his father's death, by quitting
Ford-Kenya to form the National Development Party.
Kombo said Kibaki had not let him down since he was elected Ford-Kenya chairman.
"I became Ford-Kenya chairman when Luhyas had only one Permanent
Secretary, but now we have five. We are still making more demands
because we are senior partners in this coalition," said Kombo.
Kombo said he was confident of cultivating Luhya unity to enable him run for the presidency after Kibaki's tenure.
He said Luhyas had been divided by others and used as political
ladders since 1963. Kombo was speaking in Eldoret during a meeting with
Luhya professionals and businessmen.
He said former Vice-President Musalia Mudavadi, who is in the Orange
team opposing the Proposed New Constitution, is being taken for a ride
by Raila and his group.
"Musalia has no number in the Orange team yet in the Banana team,
Vice-President Moody Awori is number two while I am third," said the
minister.

Original_Search_Title: Cabinet Minister Testifies That Raila Pledged No Violence At Banana Rally
Headline: Kenya: Tuju to ECK: Raila Pledged No Violence At Banana Rally
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Cabinet ministers Raila Odinga and Raphael Tuju who are on the opposite
sides of the referendum debate met in State House, Nairobi before the
chaotic Yes rally in Kisumu in which four people were killed.
And when they spoke, Mr Odinga promised there would be no violence at
the rally, Mr Tuju said yesterday.
His comment came as he gave evidence to the Electoral Commission
disciplinary committee investigating the rioting which saw three boys and a
young man shot dead as well as scores of residents injured.
It was not however disclosed whether the two had met for a pre-arranged
function or at the garden party hosted by President Kibaki as part of the
Kenyatta Day celebrations earlier marked at Uhuru Park.
While giving his evidence yesterday Mr Tuju said: "I met Honourable
Raila on the 20th and he gave me his word that there would be no violence."
The minister refused to name those he suspected were behind the rioting,
saying he had already handed the names to the police for investigation.
He told the committee, chaired by ECK vice chairman Gabriel Mukele, that
his political rivals wanted to ensure he neither campaigned in nor visited
Nyanza, but he said he would continue his political struggle.
In answer to questions from lawyer James Orengo for the Orange No-vote
team, Mr Tuju denied he had used emotive language in the run-up to the
rally.
He said: "I had called a rally for October 22 which I cancelled when my
rivals called for a rally in the same city and I saw all the animosity.
"When I scheduled another meeting, they called for another and I knew
they were hell bent on stopping me. I come from Nyanza province. They want
to make sure that I do not even go to Rarieda constituency. They can forget
it."
It emerged that a senior police officer was seriously hurt and was still
nursing wounds inflicted by stone throwers as he shielded Ikolomani MP Bonny
Khalwale from a mob in separate campaign rioting in Kakamega Town, during
which the MP's car was burnt to a shell.
The Orange team national chief agent Mr Isaac Ruto repeated that the
police were to blame for the violence and demanded that Maj Gen Ali plus
Cabinet ministers John Michuki, Martha Karua and Amos Kimunya should also be
summoned by the commissioners to explain what had happened.
At one point, Mr Ruto and Mr Orengo accused the committee of impatience
in taking evidence.
As the proceedings got underway, Mr Orengo said; "This is like any other
baraza."
This prompted chairman Gabriel Mukele to comment tersely; "I cannot
stomach that. You have to refrain, reframe and rethink your language."
Mr Ruto stated: "Mr Chairman, are we here to placate the public? Those
who we have named should be summoned here. Some of them are public officials
and can be found. You invited me on behalf of Orange and we are prepared to
bring many witnesses to assist you establish what actually happened," Mr
Ruto said.
The committee will rule on whether to receive evidence from them
today.
During the morning session Kisumu police chief Stanley Tito Kilonzi told
the committee that all the police guns used in quelling the Kisumu rioting
had been collected to establish the number of bullets fired.
Supt Kilonzi said police were also auditing the number of firearms
issued and returned by officers on October 29, during the violence, which
surrounded the Yes-vote Banana team's rally held at the town's Moi Stadium
by Mr Tuju.
The audit will also establish how many tear-gas canisters were
used.
Supt Kilonzi said the identity of the people who caused the violence had
yet to be established, although 49 youths had been arrested and charged in
court.
Supt Kilonzi said part of the information about the numbers of guns
issued and the number of officers used was in a confidential document known
as an Operation Order, which he refused to release to the committee.
He told the panel: "I was mandated by the district security committee to
prepare an operation order, after we held a meeting and discussed concerns
raised about possible violence. I then asked for reinforcement from the
provincial security committee because I did not have enough manpower to
handle the situation."
He insisted that they did not use prison officers as had been widely
reported and said 402 officers from the regular police, Administration
police and General Service Unit helped to put down the riot, not the 3,000
claimed by some campaigners.
Mr Mukele told the police chief that the inquiry was not attempting to
try anyone for murder, saying that would be dealt with by the High
court. "We are only interested in knowing whether the Yes or No teams are
responsible for the Kisumu violence and whether the electoral code was
violated, " he said.
Those killed in the rioting were schoolboys Paul Limera, aged 14, and
Vincent Otieno Owuoche, 15, unemployed Hillary Ochieng, 17, and milk vendor
George Ogada, 32.
Mr Tuju and Supt Kilonzi were two of the six people summoned by the ECK
committee.
The others were Dr Khalwale, Mr Norman Nyagah chief agent of the Yes
campaign, Mr Ruto and the Kakamega police chief Charles Mumanthi.
Others summoned in the morning were former Kisumu mayor Grace Onyango,
who is a member of the Kisumu Town West referendum peace committee, and an
ECK official, Ms Rhoda Atieno.
Mrs Onyango said her committee had discussed the threats of violence and
informed the Kisumu police boss, who was a member, about it.
As the hearing began, Mr Orengo and state counsel Edward Mwangi made
submissions, and Mr Mukele ruled that the proceedings would be quasi
judicial, and witnesses would not be asked to take an oath nor would the
advocates be allowed to cross-examine them. This was to save time and deal
specifically with the issues of violence and electoral law, he said.
Mr Mukele agreed with Mr Mwangi that his disciplinary committee would
not undermine investigations by a team appointed by the police commissioner,
Maj Gen Ali.
"Our inquiry is coming in the middle of the on-going referendum
campaigns when issues are very hot. We are only interested in issues dealing
with violence and violation of the electoral code of conduct," Mr Mukele
said.
Sitting with five fellow commissioners, he rejected a request by Mr
Orengo to have the sitting moved to Kisumu Town, and also that the
Commission should prevent President Kibaki from using State House to
campaign for the Banana team.
"There are no rules for ECK to use to bar anyone from visiting State
House; the President is free to invite anyone he wants to State House," Mr
Mukele said.
In his brief evidence, Mr Nyagah, who is also the Chief whip and MP for
Kamukunji, claimed one of the Orange leaders was behind the Kisumu
violence.
The Narc MP said he was quoting from Hansard (the verbatim report of
Parliamentary proceedings), in which Internal Security assistant minister
Mirugi Kariuki, had said a senior member of the Orange team was seen flying
in and out of Kisumu on the eve of the riots.

Original_Search_Title: Crusades Not for 2007 Polls, Says Ufungamano
Headline: Kenya: Crusades Not for 2007 Polls, Says Ufungamano
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Ufungamano Initiative yesterday asked the public to delink the
November 21 referendum from the 2007 General Election.
It was surprising, the faiths-led forum said, for politicians to tie the
referendum over the proposed new Constitution to the elections.
"We take great exception to the lies being peddled by politicians in the
building up to the November 21 referendum. We urge Kenyans to bear in mind
that the issues at hand are on the referendum on the proposed Constitution
and not related to the 2007 General Election as it is being perceived," they
said in a statement read by Nyeri Catholic Archbishop John Njue at
Ufungamano House, Nairobi.
The three-hour meeting brought together representatives of the Catholic
Church, the Anglican Church of Kenya, Evangelical Alliance of Kenya,
Presbyterian Church of East Africa, Seventh Day Adventist, Hindu Council of
Kenya, Friends Church (Quakers), Salvation Army, Methodist Church and the
Organisation of African Instituted Churches.
They were unanimous that Kenyans would take individual responsibility
for the decisions they will make on November 21.
"As churches, we have not absconded our responsibility of guiding our
flock but we have only moved away from the tradition of telling them to do
specific things. Whether they vote Yes or No, they should take
responsibility for their decisions," said Archbishop Njue.
Mainstream churches have chosen not to tell worshippers to vote for or
against the proposed new Constitution and instead are engaging in civic
education on the Wako Draft.
The National Council of Churches of Kenya, the umbrella organisation of
Protestant churches, has suggested the postponement of the referendum to a
later date to pave way for consensus on contentious issues in the
Draft.
However, the NCCK's call has been rejected by both the Orange and Banana
camps, who are determined to subject the new Draft to the public vote at the
November 21 referendum.
The church leaders deplored campaign violence, which has resulted in at
least four deaths and urged both the Banana and Orange groups to avoid
inciting the public to fight.
In particular, they called for politicians to show respect to the
highest authority in the land - the presidency - and to each other as they
conduct their campaigns.
The new Constitution, they stated, would put in place a governance
framework for the present and future generations and urged Kenyans to
vote.

Original_Search_Title: Maathai Vows to Continue Being Neutral
Headline: Kenya: Maathai Vows to Continue Being Neutral
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Nobel Peace Prize winner Wangari Maathai has vowed to remain neutral in
the debate over the proposed Constitution.
Prof Maathai told a rally at Ithekahuno market in Tetu that as a
laureate, she is expected to cultivate peace in the country instead of
fuelling acrimony.
Speaking after various leaders accused her of forsaking Banana
campaigns, Prof Maathai said she would not associate herself with any group
but was willing to arbitrate between the Yes and No camps.
The Tetu MP said she would be travelling to Nyanza Province to spread
the reconciliation message and people in the region would not listen to her
if she declared her support for the Yes team.
"I know Tetu constituency is 100 per cent Banana, but as a Nobel peace
laureate, I have opted to take a neutral stand as I seek reconciliation
between the rival camps," said Prof Maathai.
She repeated her earlier calls for the referendum to be postponed for
further discussions, so that the Constitution can be acceptable to all.
Earlier, Prof Maathai declined to eat a banana offered to her by Nyeri
Town MP P. G. Mureithi.
However, she asked people from central Kenya to turn up in large numbers
at the referendum, noting that during the last General Election only 61 per
cent of eligible voters from the area went to the polls, compared to 91 per
cent in Nyanza.
There was no consensus on contentious issues in the proposed laws
because the constitutional review was never agreed upon by all the
stakeholders, both at Bomas and in Parliament, said Prof Maathai.
Amidst booing from the crowd, she called for the referendum to be
postponed to give more time to working out a near-perfect document that
would be accepted by the majority of people.
The rally was also addressed by Transport minister Chris Murungaru, who
said the proposed Constitution was among the best in the world, particularly
on human rights.
He accused the Orange Movement of collaborating with foreign missions to
secure funding while tarnishing the name of the Narc Government.
He said the new laws would cater for the common man's needs, which
include free primary education and health care.
Dr Murungaru attributed the travel bans issued to him by the US and UK
to his failure to honour what he termed as those countries' selfish trade
deals.
He accused them of trying to bring down President Kibaki's
Government.
Others who addressed the rally included MPs Mutahi Kagwe (Mukurwe-ini)
and Mr Mureithi.

Original_Search_Title: Threat to Cancel Campaign Rallies
Headline: Kenya: Threat to Cancel Campaign Rallies
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Electoral Commission of Kenya (ECK) yesterday warned it would ban Orange and Banana campaigns should there be any more deaths in the scramble for a win in the November 21 referendum.
The ECK's Code of Conduct Management and Enforcement Committee Chairman, Mr Gabriel Mukele, said the national electoral body charged with the task of overseeing the referendum had the constitutional power to bring the campaigns to an abrupt end if the two camps did not refrain from violence.
"We are not going to let Kenyans die. We will go to court and stop any campaigns from both sides. Let ECK and CKRC carry out the vote without campaigns," said Mukele.
Mukele, who is also the ECK vice-chairman, read the riot act to the Orange and Banana leaders as Information Minister Raphael Tuju appeared before the commission in response to last week's summons to answer questions on the loss of four lives in Kisumu when he took the Banana campaigns there.
Tuju fielded questions from lawyer James Orengo, who represented the Orange team, and the ECK team led by Mukele. The minister said the rally was cleared by the Commissioner of Police, Maj Gen Hussein Ali, after a thorough assessment of the security situation on the ground. Ali also promised to provide adequate security for the Banana team.
When Mukele asked if he had sought audience with the No leaders, Tuju revealed he talked with Roads minister Raila Odinga on violence and the Langata MP assured him there would be no problem.
The minister added that he "made a decision the rally would continue irrespective of any form of intimidation" from the No camp.
The ECK held its sitting as ten General Service Unit officers, who were part of the team dispatched to quell the Kisumu violence during Tuju's rally on October 29, appeared before the board of inquiry probing the violence in the lakeside town. The probe team was set up by Ali.
A senior officer has been arrested after it emerged that a report that the police used live bullets to stop the riotous mob from invading a police station was false.
Ikolomani MP Boniface Khalwale also appeared before the Mukele committee over the recent violence in Kakamega in which his car was burnt.
During the hearing Banana and Orange chief agents asked ECK to extend their summons to President Kibaki and Raila for alleged infringement of the electoral code.
Banana Chief agent Norman Nyagah, who is also the Government chief whip, called on ECK to grill Raila and Liberal Democratic Party MPs Jakoyo Midiwo (Gem) and Gor Sunguh (Kisumu Town West) over the Kisumu violence.
"Let us have more people listed to be summoned if you want to do a good job. Raila, Midiwo and Sunguh should be here. We know Raila flew in and out of Kisumu on the eve of violence," said Nyagah.
Orengo accused President Kibaki of engaging in election corruption. He said many people were making a beeline to State House to be "corrupted" ahead of the crucial vote. "It is a corrupt conduct happening during the electoral process. Never before has State House, Nairobi, been used the way it is being used now," he said.
He also argued ECK should have extracted a statement from the President on the loss of four lives in the Kisumu violence. "A statement should be obtained from the President. Law enforcers fall under him," said Orengo.
The Orange team tabled a list of other leaders it wants summoned. They included ministers Kiraitu Murungi (Justice), John Michuki (Internal Security), Amos Kimunya (Lands), Martha Karua (Water) and Joseph Munyao (Livestock). The team also wants Government Spokesman Alfred Mutua, assistant minister Kivutha Kibwana, Maj Gen Ali and the officer in charge of Kondele Police Station grilled.
In his presentation Tuju said he ruled out the possibility of cancelling the rally. Orengo had asked him why he did not call off the rally having taken an oath to protect the integrity of a Constitution that underscores the right to life.
He also said that cancelling the rally would have meant that he was allowing his detractors to block him from visiting his Rarieda constituency.
He claimed some leaders in the No camp had dared him to go to the rally "if I am man enough". Tuju stressed that "nobody should have any kind of imagination they can stop me from going to any part in this country."
The minister was challenged by the chairman of the committee to name those who had "threatened" him but Tuju said he was only "cautioned" and promised he would reveal the names to the police.
He said this even as Mukele said that the hearing was "an informal" tribunal that does not stop the police from prosecuting anybody with murder or any related offence on the Kisumu rally.
Tuju claimed that the No camp was only out to molest them in Kisumu by announcing parallel rallies whenever the Yes camp planned to campaign in the town.
"Orange was not interested in a rally but uchokozi (molestation)," Tuju added.
Orengo asked ECK to institute private prosecutions against those breaching electoral law. "You can undertake private prosecutions. You are not a toothless bulldog as many people say." He called on ECK to transfer its sittings to Kisumu, where the incident took place.
After preliminary issues, the committee members went into camera to make a ruling. When they returned, Mukele rejected the plea to summon the President, among others.
He asked the politicians to give names and postal and physical addresses of those they thought were involved in electoral offences within 24 hours.
On the plea to summon the President, Mukele said the 1998 code of conduct does not govern the conduct of a president. "It will be baseless if we made the ruling because it does not govern his conduct."

Original_Search_Title: Orange Ask Police to Probe Leaflets in Campaign
Headline: Kenya: Orange Ask Police to Probe Leaflets in Campaign
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange team's national referendum committee yesterday claimed that their rivals were printing inflammatory leaflets to discredit their campaign.
The team leader, Mr Mutula Kilonzo, said the leaflets were being printed on Kijabe Street, Nairobi.
Although he did not say who was responsible for the printing, the letter seemed to point fingers at the Banana camp.
The letter, copied to Police Commissioner Hussein Ali and the Officer Commanding Central Police Station, said the leaflets would be circulated in Dagoreti Constituency.
"It has come to our attention that (name withheld) Printers located along Kijabe Street are at the moment printing public inflammatory campaign leaflets advising anti-No Campaign voters in Dagoretti to flee the area before November 21, 2005 or face dire consequences," said Kilonzo.
He blamed "opponents of the No campaign" attempting to discredit peaceful rallies.
He attached a copy of the leaflets which say, "Onyo, Onyo, Jitayarishe kuhama Dagoretti kama wewe sio wa chungwa (be prepared to leave Dagoreti if you are not for orange)".
But in a rejoinder, the Banana team's national co-ordinator, Mr Moses Wetangula, said the claim was "another little ploy by the Orange team after realising they were losing".
"They have spread a litany of lies from the beginning and they are now hanging on every kind of misinformation but unfortunately the public is now informed," said Wetangula.
The assistant minister said the biggest worry for the Orange team was that most Kenyans had read the proposed constitution.
Contacted, the deputy director at the printing company said they had not received contracts from both camps.
Meanwhile, MPs from opposition parties supporting the adoption of the proposed constitution have been promised ministerial posts held by LDP legislators, the Orange team claimed yesterday.
Cabinet ministers Raila Odinga and Najib Balala said the Government had told the MPs mainly from Kanu and Ford People that they would replace LDP MPs in the Cabinet.
Raila said each of the MPs was promised Sh10 million and got a down payment of Sh5 million.
"They cannot reap from where they did not sow. They are the ones who stoned us when we campaigned for Narc in 2002," Raila said.
They were speaking at Moyale Stadium during an Orange rally.

Original_Search_Title: Police Arrest Armed Men At Besigye Rally
Headline: Uganda: Police Arrest Armed Men At Besigye Rally
Body_Text:
Gulu — POLICE on Saturday arrested four men armed with submachine guns and a
pistol who attempted to disrupt Col. Kizza Besigye's rally in Gulu
town.
They were part of about 100 people arrested by riot police as they
demonstrated against Besigye's visit to the town. Police confirmed the
arrests but refused to comment on the guns.
"We arrested people on Saturday. I will not mention whether they had
guns or not," the District Police Commander, Gulu Mr Richard Mivule, told
Daily Monitor on phone yesterday.
Our reporter said the suspects were carrying placards and demonstrating
against Besigye's rally before the arrest. "These people came in a passenger
vehicle. A patrol car rounded them up and took them in police custody. They
were found with three SMGs and a pistol," the reporter said.
The spokesman for Forum for Democratic Change (FDC) Wafula Oguttu told
Daily Monitor yesterday, "We don't know what they (armed men) really
wanted. We don't know whether they wanted to kill somebody or not."
He said the men were moving around in two cars trying to mobilise
people. Other agitators demonstrated at the roundabout at the entrance into
Gulu town. They carried placards reading: "Besigye where is Opoka?" "Besigye
you are not welcome," and "We don't want rebellion."
Two of the demonstrators Ms Mariam Aliga, 80, and Mr Sam Olwedo, 28,
