told Daily Monitor they were demanding that Besigye tells them where Mr
James Opoka is. They said they held Besigye responsible for Opoka's
fate.
Opoka, a former aide to Besigye, was reportedly killed on March 9, 2003
on the orders of LRA rebel leader Joseph Kony. Reports said Kony suspected
Opoka, along with several other people, of establishing a political wing
inside the LRA without his consent.
Besigye has since said he does not know where Opoka is.
Mivule said the police intervened to ensure law and order. "We took the
protestors into custody to allow Besigye's rally to go on. It was a matter
of streamlining things," he said. He declined to say whether the protesters
would be charged with any offence.
"Just know we took them into custody, don't mind about the details", he
said. The arrest was seen by many as a big test for the police under their
new leader, Maj. Gen. Kale Kayihura.
"I was impressed," said a man in the crowd at the roadside waiting to
welcome Besigye. "That is what the police are expected to do but not to
block opposition rallies."
Aruu County MP Reagan Okumu accused the UPDF Intelligence Officer in
northern Uganda, Col. Charles Otema Awany of organising the
demonstration.
"The Movement people organised by Col. Otema Awany wanted to disrupt our
rally. I am grateful the police stopped them. Please continue with the good
work," Okumu said.
Other sources said one of the men arrested with guns is Awany's
brother.
Awany yesterday told Daily Monitor on phone that, he was in Kampala. "I
have been here for the last four days. I have no idea about the arrest." At
the well-attended rally, Besigye urged the people of Acholi not to re-elect
members of Parliament who support the NRM.
"Let us make sure there is no Movement MP coming from Acholi. We must
sweep them with their corruption," the FDC boss said attracting
applause.
His interpreter, Prof. Morris Ogenga Latigo identified the NRM MPs from
Acholi as Ms Betty Akech, Mr Henry Okello Oryem, Mr Hillary Onek and Mr
Santa Okot. Besigye said the government had been getting money from donors
but it was being embezzled as the masses suffer in poverty.
"As long as there is a government full of thieves, the lots of money
given by donors will always vanish in thin air. The problem in the region
will not be solved by people who are benefiting from it," he said.
He said FDC gives the northern region first priority and that was the
reason for starting his tour from Gulu, Lira and West Nile. On claims that
he fled the country in 2001 after losing the presidential polls, Besigye
said it was merely a tactical withdrawal.
"For us in the military, we talk of tactical withdrawal and that is what
I did but I am happy to say I am now back," drawing applause from the crowd
that braved a downpour. He called on the opposition parties to remain united
in order to defeat Museveni in the polls.
"Before I came back, I was told that what used to be a cotter pin is now
dry banana leaves and we only need brooms to sweep it away," he said Several
FDC leaders attended.

Original_Search_Title: Prayers Held for Victims of Riot
Headline: Kenya: Prayers Held for Victims of Riot
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A prayer service was held for four victims of police shooting in last
week's riots in Kisumu with leaders including four Cabinet ministers
demanding an independent probe.
Environment minister Kalonzo Musyoka demanded independent investigations
and the entire security team, including Internal Security minister John
Michuki, be summoned to explain their possible roles in the brutal use of
force that left the four people, among them three children, dead and many
others injured.
"We need an independent outfit that would clear issues to the core
starting with the minister and down to the junior officers who executed the
innocent children," said Mr Musyoka.
The violence followed a Banana rally organised by Information minister
Raphael Tuju in the town.
Minister ashamed
Mr Odinga said he was ashamed of serving as a Cabinet minister in a
Government that is fast losing ground on its human rights record despite
being elected on the pedestal of restoring justice and fairness after years
of tyranny by past regimes.
"I am greatly ashamed to continue serving as minister in Government that
is fast throwing its human rights record to the dogs... What shall we say to
those who elected us on the platform of instilling justice and freedom?" he
asked.
Mr Odinga, however, said the urge to liberate the country from
dictatorship is unstoppable adding that the voice of the Kenyan people is
solidly behind the Orange movement.
The Roads minister hit out at Mr Tuju terming him an errand boy for
powerful officials in Government out to bring politics in Nyanza into
disrepute.
Also at the prayers were Cabinet ministers Anyang' Nyong'o and Ochillo
Ayacko and assistant minister Andrew Ligale. Others were MPs David Musila,
Ayiecho Olweny, George Khaniri, Chris Okemo, Ken Nyagudi, Gor Sunguh,
Wycliffe Osundwa, Eric Nyamunga, Philip Okundi, Jakoyo Midiwo and Julia
Ojiambo. Former Vice-President Musalia Mudavadi, East African legislative
assembly's Maxwell Shamalla and Kisumu mayor Priscah Auma.
During the service led by Anglican Bishop Mwai Abiero of Maseno South
diocese at Kisumu Stadium, tears flowed freely as Dr David Olima read out
the findings of postmortem examinations on the bodies of Paul Limera, 14,
Vincent Otieno Owuoche, 15, Hillary Ochieng, 17 and George Ogada, 32.
According to the reports, Limera, a pupil at Josana Academy was shot in
the head and back, while Owuoche, a Standard Seven pupil at Ogango primary
school was shot at close range, the bullet going through his back and
rupturing his intestines.
Hillary Ochieng' from Ebenezer Rescue home had two bullets in his
shattered left shin. His skull was broken.
Milk vendor Ogada was shot from the back at point blank range.
Three caskets bearing the remains of Owuoche, Ochieng' and Ogada were
placed inside a tent in the stadium.
Seated by the coffins were grieving family members, who demanded reasons
why their loved ones died brutally.
A four-member team appointed by Police commissioner Hussein Ali to
investigate the killings yesterday entered its third day of work in
Kisumu.
A police inspector and a constable at Kondele police post have been
arrested after it emerged that there was a massive cover up to justify the
shootings.

Original_Search_Title: State House Open to All, Declares Kibaki
Headline: Kenya: State House Open to All, Declares Kibaki
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki will continue entertaining delegations from various
parts of the country as part of his open-door policy.
The meetings between the Head of State and ordinary Kenyans provided a
crucial forum for the President to understand issues affecting people in
different corners of the country, a statement from State House said
yesterday.
"Kenyans have the right to talk directly to the President and it is
unfortunate that leaders who claim to be fighting for democracy are opposed
to this," the statement said.
Leaders of the Orange campaign that is opposed to the proposed
Constitution were quoted yesterday accusing the President of receiving
tribal delegations and bribing them to support the new laws.
Roads minister Raila Odinga charged that State House had been converted
into an "auction ring" where Kenyans were being "bought" to support the new
Constitution.
However, the statement said the President had chosen an open-door policy
and those seeking to have State House doors closed to the public will not
succeed.
"There are leaders who are now used to talking down to the people at
political rallies and who have failed to understand that effective
leadership entails listening and responding to issues raised directly by the
people," the statement, released by the Presidential Press Service,
said.
It added: "We wish to remind these leaders that State House is not the
exclusive reserve of ministers. Any Kenyan has a right to visit and hold
consultations with the President who is directly elected by the people."
The head of PPS, Mr Isaiyah Kabira, said it was wrong for anyone to say
that the President had started meeting delegations because of the referendum
campaigns.
The Head of State received delegations whenever he toured the
countryside, Mr Kabira said. "He will continue meeting delegations even
after the referendum is over."
He, however, could not disclose which teams are lined up for State House
before the November 21 referendum.
Promised a new district
The Orange leaders took issue with the State House meetings as the
President was hosting a team from Turkana district in Nakuru on Saturday,
where he promised a new district for the region.
The Turkana delegation was the latest in a series of visits by groups of
mainly grassroots politicians from various parts of the country.
Among those that have visited State House are groups from Maasailand led
by Education minister George Saitoti; a delegation from Ukambani which was
escorted by Health minister Charity Ngilu; a group from North Eastern
Province guided by Cabinet minister Mohammed Abdi; and another from Gusii
which was led by Energy minister Simeon Nyachae.
The delegates have variously been promised extra districts, a university
and land, among other things. The Orange team says the promises are
inducements to vote for the Banana side in the referendum.
Suspend the delegations
Speaking in Butere/Mumias district on Saturday, Mr Odinga, Environment
minister Kalonzo Musyoka and Kanu secretary general William Ruto urged the
President to suspend the delegations until after the referendum.
They asked the public to reject the new districts which they claimed
were being used as an inducement to endorse the new Constitution.
On Friday, Catholic bishops asked the President not to take sides in the
campaigns and also urged him to suspend receiving delegations.
And yesterday, both sides intensified their campaigns in different parts
of the country to woo a possible 11.5 million registered voters for the
referendum poll.
The Yes teams led by Vice-President Moody Awori went to Khwisero in
Butere/Mumias district; Co-operatives minister Njeru Ndwiga campaigned in
Embu district and another team was in Nairobi led by assistant minister Beth
Mugo.
The No teams was in Kakamega district where Mr Odinga led the campaign;
Taveta district where leader of Official Opposition Uhuru Kenyatta
spearheaded the canvassing; and in Bomet district where Mr Ruto was the
pointman.
Door-to-door campaign
In Embu, Mr Ndwiga announced that the Yes team had launched a
door-to-door campaign to mobilise people to turn out in large numbers during
the poll.
Mr Ndwiga and Runyenjes MP Nyaga Wambora said the campaigns will be
carried out in the entire district to ensure registered voters turned out in
support of the new Constitution.
They addressed rallies in Makengi and Manyatta markets where they
criticised the perennial voter apathy in the area.
"While we assure that more than 98 per cent of voters here are for Yes,
our worry is that they might fail to turn up and participate in the poll,"
Mr Wambora said.
In the last General Election, the MP said, about half of the registered
voters in the district failed to cast their ballots.
Mr Ndwiga said that more than 50 grassroots meetings have been planned
to sensitise voters on the need to participate in the polls.
"Our biggest challenge now is to convince our people to vote otherwise
we have won the battle," Mr Ndwiga, the Manyatta MP, said.
In Kakamega, the No campaigners accused some leaders in the Yes team of
corruption.
Mr Odinga and Planning minister Anyang' Nyong'o talked of the Anglo
Leasing scandal and claimed that some of the Yes campaigners were supporting
the new Constitution in a bid to cover up their past misdeeds.
"I challenge my colleagues in the Banana camp to come out clean about
Anglo Leasing before we spill the beans," Prof Nyong'o said.
The Planning minister accused President Kibaki of having turned against
the people who elected by going against their will.
He said: "President Kibaki has refused to listen to the voice of
reason. The people who elected him have said No to this Constitution yet he
is only listening to a few people who are out to protect their interests. I
want the President to change his mind before it is too late."
Mr Odinga said shooting down the draft would ensure misdeeds committed
by certain individuals in government are laid bare and action taken against
them.
The leaders spoke in Mwihila in Ikolomani constituency during a tour to
drum up support for the rejection of the proposed draft.
Present at the rally were assistant ministers George Khaniri and Andrew
Ligale and legislator Wyclife Osundwa. Also present were former
Vice-President Musalia Mudavadi, politicians James Orengo and Martin
Shikuku.

Original_Search_Title: Roads Not Linked to Vote, Says Raila
Headline: Kenya: Roads Not Linked to Vote, Says Raila
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Two key roads in Western Province will be tarmacked to ease
transportation, Roads minister Raila Odinga has said.
But Mr Odinga said the tarmacking of the Ekero-Ebuyangu and
Kisa-Khumusalaba roads was not meant to entice area residents to vote
against the proposed Constitution on November 21.
"I have ordered that these two important roads be tarmacked but it is
not a bribe. We in the Orange movement don't bribe Kenyans to influence
their stand on the Constitution", the minister said.
He said the Budget for the roads had been approved and work would start
immediately.
"I know you had made up your mind to reject the draft Constitution long
before I informed you about the roads. Let nobody link the work on the roads
to the referendum," said Mr Odinga.
He was speaking at Khumusalaba market in Khwisero constituency on
Saturday when he, Environment minister Kalonzo Musyoka and Kanu
secretary-general William Ruto led an Orange campaign in Western
Province.
Mr Odinga also denied claims that the Orange movement was a tribal
outfit formed to destabilise President Kibaki's leadership.
"This movement has brought together all the Kenyan tribes and represents
the face of the country. It is wrong to call us tribalists," he said.
He asked Ford Kenya MPs in the Banana camp to join the Orange
movement.
"Members of Parliament still languishing in the Banana camp are letting
down their constituents. Kenyans have rejected the draft Constitution and
leaders still singing Banana should treat themselves as enemies of the
people," the minister said.
He praised MPs Wycliffe Osundwa, Julius Arunga, Christine Mango and
Wycliffe Oparanya for supporting the Orange camp.

Original_Search_Title: Awori Leads Banana Team to Home of Pupil Killed in Riots
Headline: Kenya: Awori Leads Banana Team to Home of Pupil Killed in Riots
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Vice President Moody Awori yesterday led a team of Banana campaigners to
the home of a pupil killed in the Kisumu riots.
Mr Awori was accompanied by Local Government minister Musikari Kombo and
assistant ministers Mirugi Kariuki and John Munyes among others, when he
visited the home of Master Paul Limera, 14, in Butere/Mumias District.
The pupil of Kisumu's Josana Academy was buried on Saturday in a
ceremony attended by leading lights in the Orange campaign.
The VP, who also attended a special service for the family called on the
clergy to counsel and pray for it to overcome its loss.
Mr Awori called on politicians to desist from utterances that were
likely to cause animosity among Kenyans.
He asked them to educate the public on the clauses in the proposed
Constitution and stop playing politics.
The Banana team later addressed a rally at Khwisero market in the
district.
Speaking at the rally, Mr Kombo claimed that the people who organised
the Kisumu chaos were the same ones behind bloody confrontations that marked
the fight for Ford Kenya leadership in the 1990s.
Reckless statements
Mr Awori said leaders had failed to respect the opinions of others and
resorted to issuing reckless statements and inciting the public.
This, Mr Awori said, had resulted in chaotic campaigns in some parts of
the country.
He called upon the Luyia community to unite and vote for the proposed
Constitution.
Mr Kombo said gangs used in Kisumu chaos a week ago were organised by
people who caused mayhem when the former Vice President Michael Wamalwa was
fighting over supremacy of the party's leadership with Roads minister Raila
Odinga.
He claimed a politician was using the same people to cause chaos in
Kisumu.
Mr Kombo cautioned the No camp against politicising the Kisumu
killings.
Mr Kariuki called upon Kenyans to vote for the new laws, which he said
would benefit the common man.
He asked Kenyans to give the team looking into the shootings of four
people during the Kisumu riots time to investigate.
Mr Munyes accused the Orange side of lying about the contents of the
draft.
Former Cabinet minister, Dr Amukowa Anangwe, argued that the proposed
Constitution was much better than the current one, which Kenyans had
tolerated for more than 40 years.
Central Organisation of Trade Unions secretary-general Francis Atwoli
left the meeting after the crowd jeered when he arrived waving an
orange.

Original_Search_Title: Reject Proposed Laws, Ex-Minister Tells Coast
Headline: Kenya: Reject Proposed Laws, Ex-Minister Tells Coast
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Coast residents were asked to vote against the proposed Constitution
because it does not address the land problem in the region.
The proposed Constitution had provided for the establishment of a
national land commission whose operations had not been clearly spelt out,
said former Cabinet minister Katana Ngala.
"The proposed Constitution will not be able to address the perennial
land problem in the coast region because it has vested powers over control
of land in the Government and not local communities," he said.
Mr Ngala was addressing an Orange rally at Matano-Manne in Vitengeni
division and was with Mr Peter Shehe of the Liberal Democratic Party.
"There are many people in the Coast region who have been landless since
the colonial times while big parcels of land owned by absentee landlords are
lying idle in the area," said Mr Shehe.
Mr Ngala, the former area MP, cautioned the people against denying
themselves a chance to participate in the referendum by selling their voting
cards.
"There are people who will come to you and offer you money to surrender
your voting cards, particularly on the referendum day so that you do not
vote. Please refuse to do so because you will be selling your rights," he
said.
Mr Ngala, a Kanu national vice-chairman denied claims that the Orange
team was being funded by foreign embassies to campaign against the
laws.
"Our opponent, the Banana camp, is now panicking because we are ahead of
them in campaigns. Our gains are making them utter alarming statements
against us to get support from Kenyans," he claimed.
Mr Ngala announced that the Orange team would hold a campaign rally at
Bamba trading centre on November 12 in the morning and at Kilifi stadium in
the afternoon.
Meanwhile the Law Society of Kenya has said it will maintain its neutral
position on the proposed Constitution despite pressure from some members
that it take a position on the issue chairman Tom Ojienda has said.
Mr Ojienda told LSK members in Mombasa that he would not succumb to
pressure to declare the position of the society on the proposed new
constitution since LSK members were divided on the issue.
His comments came two days after lawyers in Rift Valley took him to task
over the neutral position the society had taken on the review debate.
The lawyers wanted to know why the society had remained impartial in a
process that has polarised Kenyans, saying that by being neutral, LSK would
fail in providing stewardship in the review debate.
Mr Ojienda told Mombasa lawyers that the society would not support
either the Banana or Orange team but would continue to debate on the
strengths and weaknesses of the Wako draft.
He also said that LSK would conduct civic education, adding that it
would be acceptable to challenge the outcome of the referendum within 14
days after announcement if the results.
"We are not saying that the whole draft is bad but there are some
clauses that are unacceptable," he said.
The review commission had provided Sh50,000 to used by the Mombasa LSK
branch for the civic education.

Original_Search_Title: Strike Clamps Down On Appetite for Injera
Headline: Ethiopia: Strike Clamps Down On Appetite for Injera
Body_Text:
Sisay Gebre is a mother of two, and expecting a third child. She
is in her 30s, and married to a daily labourer. The family lives in a
rented house in Arada district, in the area customarily known as Aroge
Kera. She sells tomatoes and bananas to complement the family income.
Since November 1, the family, which habitually consumes up to six
injeras per day, has been faced with the reality of doing without fresh
injera, as most injera sellers have stopped selling the product since
disturbances started last Tuesday. In many parts of the city the demand
for injera by people who could not make it for themselves had to go
unsatisfied.
Sisay had a choice of seven sellers from whom she bought each injera
for 50 cents. None have been around since Tuesday. She went farther, to
Arat Kilo, to find other sellers, but was unsuccessful.
"I couldn't even find a single piece for my children," she said.
Melkam Fetene was one of the seven injera traders in the area. On
normal days she sold up to 80 injeras a day, which enabled her to
support her four children. She has been idle over the past few days
because she could not replenish her flour supply. She said she could
buy neither teff, the cereal from which injera is made, nor could she
buy wood. Unable to make new money she said she is now using up money
she had saved for her business.
Other injera sellers in Urael area said that they were still able to
provide the staple food to their customers, but they are beginning to
get worried about the supply of firewood. In an area near Haya Hulet
Mazoria, in Kebele 05 in the Bole District, there are six injera
outlets, of which five were closed during the week. A lady at one of
the closed outlets said that she stopped baking injeras for fear that
people may not turn out to buy them and the food would be spoilt.
The closure of the five has been a bonanza for the sixth. Fitsum
Habtyes, the only injera seller operating at this point, said that
whereas she used to sell up to 100 injeras a day she is now selling up
to 150 at its usual price of one Birr.
In some places prices have also been affected. In Kotebe area the
prices of sergegna (darker coloured) and white injeras were up from 80
and 90 cents apiece to one Birr and 1.20 Br, respectively.
Besides injera, however, other consumable are also showing price
increases and a reduced supply. A single pepper is selling for 25 cents
in some places, and a kilogramme of onion is up from two Birr to 3.50
Br, and neither has the usual freshness. Shoppers in Kotebe area also
complained of paying 30 to 50 Br more for a quintal of teff.
There may be some relief that bread is still available at the usual
prices where stores are open. Sissay is making do with a supply of
dried injera which she has kept for some months. But, hardly satisfied
and already getting hungrier as the supply of dried injera is
vanishing, her children are already setting their eyes on the
neighbours hoping to find something to eat.

Original_Search_Title: Tuju, Senior Officers to Appear Before ECK Over Kisumu Deaths
Headline: Kenya: Tuju, Senior Officers to Appear Before ECK Over Kisumu Deaths
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Information minister Raphael Tuju and a senior police officer are this morning expected to appear before the Electoral Commission of Kenya (ECK) over the Kisumu violence in which four people died.
Tuju and the police officer will face the ECK Electoral Code of Conduct and Enforcement Committee.
It was confirmed yesterday that the committee, which is chaired by the ECK vice-chairman Gabriel Mukele, would handle the matter.
Police Commissioner Maj-Gen Ali Hussein last week also ordered investigations into the killings.
The deputy officer commanding Kondele Police Station, which was allegedly attacked by the shooting victims, is being held at Vihiga Police Station over the matter.
Yesterday, sources at the ECK, indicated that Tuju and the senior police officers are expected at the meeting at 9am.
They are expected to report at the ECK offices together with the leaders of both the Orange and Banana national referendum committees.
It was confirmed yesterday that the Banana team's chief agent, Mr Norman Nyaga, and his Orange camp counterpart, Isaac Ruto, would represent both sides.
Nyaga is also the Government Chief Whip, while ruto is also the Kanu secretary-general.
The ECK has also summoned the Ikolomani MP, Dr Bonny Khalwale, and another senior police officer, over the recent violence in Kakamega during which a mob burnt Khalwale's car.
The police officers summoned by the ECK are Mr Stanley Kilonzi of Kisumu and Mr Patrick Safari of Kakamega. Both are in the rank of Officer Commanding Police Division.
Nyaga received the letter summoning him at County Hall, Nairobi, last Wednesday.
Last week, ECK chairman Samuel Kivuitu, held a meeting with the Banana and the Orange teams in which he announced the formation of the disciplinary committee.
Last week, Tuju told a group of diplomats how he almost lost his life during the violence in Kisumu.
"I almost lost my life. My driver avoided a petrol bomb in Kisumu targeted at me in the car where I was in with my wife. You could be sending condolences now," he said.
A primary school pupil who was among the victims of the violence, Paul Limera, was buried last week.

Original_Search_Title: Ndile Provides Light Moments in Rallies
Headline: Kenya: Ndile Provides Light Moments in Rallies
Body_Text:
Nairobi — When it comes to political comedians, Ukambani region does not appear to
have a shortage of them. Exit Mulu Mutisya, enter Richard Kalembe
Ndile.
More like the late Mr Mutisya (then undisputed Kamba kingmaker) who
poked fun at anyone and anything, Mr Ndile, the Kibwezi MP, has been busy
amusing himself and his audiences at various referendum rallies.
Mr Ndile has flavoured the otherwise serious and tensed up
constitutional debates with light moments. A key Banana campaigner in
Ukambani, Mr Ndile has been electrifying crowds at rallies, tickling their
senses of humour.
Usually sporting outfits with a rainbow of colours, Mr Ndile has been a
spectacle, working up crowds and occasionally joining, and even outdancing,
members of entertainment troupes.
The MP has taken up the burden of translating the Wako Draft, clause by
clause, into Kikamba including speeches made by visiting politicians for the
benefit of his audiences.
"A vote for Yes means that all the poor in our community, including my
mother, shall be entitled to a monthly stipend of Sh2,000!" he
thunders.
Never mind whether there is such a clause in the Constitution or the
viability of such an undertaking for a developing country, all that the
Kibwezi MP cares for is that his sentiments create a lasting
impression.
And he confirmed to the Sunday Nation: "The best way of communicating to
our people is by interpreting the Wako Draft for them by giving local
examples. It is pointless to emphasise on the exact meaning as long as you
capture the general good intentions of the Draft."
"One must be able to read the people's moods, their feelings and dance
alongside them. And my biggest advantage over most MPs is that I am
typically a villager having grown up with the people. I understand their
feelings more than anyone else," he said.
The only problem, though, is that the MP occasionally takes his jokes
beyond the podium. Last month, he was kicked out of Parliament for entering
the chamber wearing a necklace with a ripe banana hanging on it.
Recently, the MP also claimed that the Orange team was misleading the
Kamba people that Kalonzo Musyoka would be their presidential candidate in
2007.
"What is the point of having a weak president without piki piki
(motorbike outriders) given that those would be meant only for Raila
(Odinga) who will be prime minister?" he asked.
Reacts Kilome MP, Mr Mutinda Mutiso: "Kalembe is simply a gun on
hire. He is part of the Banana propaganda machine trying to discredit the
leadership of Orange."
But Mr Ndile thinks otherwise: "I am like my name, Kalembe, which means
a small hoe. Kanaweza kulima chochote na kila mahali. Tena hakavunjiki ovyo
(It is a versatile tool which can be used everywhere. Besides, it cannot
break easily)".
A first-time MP, Mr Ndile was born in April 1964. He worked as a
carpenter after secondary school education and later as an office messenger
at Bayusuf and Sons Company.
From an initial Sh350 monthly salary at Bayusuf to his present job, his
is a true rags-to-riches story.

Original_Search_Title: Koech Blames Raila Over Kisumu Chaos
Headline: Kenya: Koech Blames Raila Over Kisumu Chaos
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Roads Minister Raila Odinga is to blame for the death of four people in
Kisumu during a Yes rally last week, a Cabinet colleague alleged
yesterday.
Mr John Koech claimed that Mr Odinga had organised youths to disrupt
Information minister Raphael Tuju's Yes rally.
Mr Koech said that if the youths had stayed away from the rally no
deaths would have occurred.
Speaking at Mulot trading centre during a Yes rally, Mr Koech ,who is
the MP for Chepalungu, said the police should not be blamed for the action
they took saying they were right to restore calm during the rally.
"The police are law abiding officers and cannot shoot innocent Kenyans
unless they are provoked and they take action for self defence."
The Minister criticised what he considered a move by Mr Odinga to block
the Banana team from campaigning in Luo Nyanza while he (Mr Odinga)
criss-crossed other parts of the country selling the Orange position.
He told local people that the Government had no intention of seizing
land from rightful owners if the proposed law was passed.
He told the Orange campaigners to stop misleading Kenyans on land
ownership.
Mr Koech stated that only leased land would be levied as disignated by
the law.
Bishop Joseph Kiptanui of Church of Christ told the same meeting that it
was the evangelical churches which asked for religious courts in a bid to
defend their rights.
Elsewhere, Mr Haroun Lempaka, a long-time political opponent of Cabinet
minister William ole Ntimama, yesterday accused the latter of misleading the
Maasai over land ownership as stipulated in the proposed Draft.
Addressing a Yes rally at Olopironito, Mr Lempaka said that Mr Ntimama
had interersts in the Maasai Mara which he wanted to protect.

Original_Search_Title: Rights Bodies Release List of State Car Users
Headline: Kenya: Rights Bodies Release List of State Car Users
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Eight Cabinet and seven assistant ministers are on a list of government
officials said to be misusing official vehicles during the campaigns.
According to the list compiled by two human rights bodies, six of the
ministers and five assistants are in the Yes camp, while the others are in
the No group.
They used the vehicles to attend rallies in various parts of the country
in October, and they had their official drivers and security guards.
The list released by the Kenya National Commission on Human Rights
(KNCHR) and the Kenya Human Rights Commission (KHRC) during a news
conference in Nairobi yesterday includes two ordinary MPs in the Banana
group.
But the roll leaves out officials who had used either police or military
helicopters as it could not be established whether they were hired or
not. Ms Catherine Mumma of KNCHR said yesterday that the two organisations
were investigating the use of helicopters.
The organisations promised to release another list of politicians
inciting Kenyans to violence, those giving hate speeches and distorting the
proposed Constitution.
The ministers on the Yes camp said to have been driven in government
vehicles include Mr Kiraitu Murungi (Constitutional Affairs), Mr Simeon
Nyachae (Energy), Mr Joseph Munyao (Livestock), Mr Amos Kimunya of Lands, Ms
Martha Karua (Water) and Mr Mohamed Abdi Mohamed (Regional Development
Authorities).
Ministers Ochillo Ayacko and William ole Ntimama were the only ones from
the Orange team who used official transport.
Assistant ministers from the Yes camp included Mr Mwangi Kiunjuri, Dr
Wilfred Machage, Prof Kivutha Kibwana, Mr Mirugi Kariuki and Ms Beth
Mugo.
On the other side were Mr Peter Odoyo and Andrew Ligale. The two
organisations said they would hand over the list to the Attorney-General for
action.

Original_Search_Title: Campaigns Trail Leads to Kisii
Headline: Kenya: Campaigns Trail Leads to Kisii
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The two sides in the referendum debate yesterday traded accusations,
with the Yes team calling those opposed to the new Constitution fraudsters,
as their opponents said President Kibaki should own up to mistakes in the
Wako Draft.
It was unfair for the Head of State to continue passing blame to others
after overseeing a flawed process in which a few individuals shelved the
will Kenyans, said Eldoret North MP William Ruto .
Mr Ruto said a clique of powerful individuals changed proposals
presented to them by Kenyans, leaving the current proposed Draft
Constitution "shameful document" engineered by greed.
A police officer escorts an Orange team convoy at Suneka trading centre
in Kisii District yesterday after some youths wearing Yes T-shirts started
throwing stones at it. Photo by Sylvester Onyango
"Kenyans had good ideas for their country but some individuals carried
their wishes to Kilifi where they were subjected to serious mutilation. What
came out of the Kilifi talks was nothing close to the people's wishes and it
absurd for the President to cry foul today yet all took place under his
nose," he told a rally at the Gusii stadium in Kisii Town.
On the Banana side, Water minister Martha Karua accused former
presidents' sons of hiding behind the constitutional referendum to conceal
past misdeeds.
Ms Karua said Mr Uhuru Kenyatta and Mr Gedion Moi were out to cover up
their fathers' injustices by maintaining the status quo. She was among
leaders who addressed a Banana rally in Nyamira. Mr Ruto absolved himself
from blame over the impasse on the Constitution, saying "clandestine forces"
frustrated his efforts when he chaired the Parliamentary Select Committee on
the Constitution.
Roads minister Raila Odinga supported Mr Ruto, saying a selfish quest to
cling to power caused certain individuals in Government to enter into
machinations that derailed the wishes of the people.
He said contrary to dreams of a democratic Constitution, some selfish
individuals were trying to force on Kenyans a set of laws that bestowed
immense powers on the presidency.
Mr Odinga told off his Cabinet colleague Simeon Nyachae, accusing him of
causing confusion in the review.
He also accused the Energy minister of digressing into trivial personal
matters in the ongoing debate on the Constitution.
But addressing the Nyamira rally, Mr Nyachae said the Orange team wanted
to use the outcome of the constitutional referendum as a tool against
President Kibaki's Government in the 2007 General Election.
He said he was unhappy that Banana campaigners were being barred from
certain sections of the country whereas the Gusii community listened to both
sides.
Mr Nyachae said Kisii people turned out in large numbers for rallies
called by either side.
He maintained that Orange supporters were out to grab power through the
backdoor.
Among those who attended the rally, at Nyamira Primary School, were
assistant ministers Joshua Toro and Henry Obwocha. Others included MPs Bonny
Khalwale, Jimmy Angwenyi, Mwancha Okioma and Zebedeo Opore.
At Kisii, South Mugirango MP Omingo Magara, who was among 20 MPs who
addressed the No rally, alleged that pro-Banana campaigners were using money
to discredit him politically in his constituency.

Original_Search_Title: Exploiting the Art of Campaigning
Headline: Kenya: Exploiting the Art of Campaigning
Body_Text:
Nairobi — In the heat of referendum campaigns, Lands minister Amos Kimunya used
the October 2 eclipse to make a point.
"You saw the shape of the (irrisidence) around the eclipse? It looked
like a banana! And you saw the shape of the umbra (blocking the sun)? It
looked like an orange."
Kimunya probably meant the 'Yes' team is the torchbearer of truth and
light in the campaigns while the 'No' team are leaders unto "death and
darkness."
But when it comes to the art of campaigning, it doesn't take Steadman to
say that Kimunya and the 'Yes' camp are being eclipsed by the sheer colour,
imagery and flamboyance of the 'No' lot.
"Politics is an art, and like all arts, it has a little bit of truth -
but lots of propaganda," former American President John F Kennedy said, at a
time when his rival for the presidency, Richard Nixon, was tarring him as
inexperienced and under the allegiance of the Vatican. In the referendum
campaigns, the 'Yes' side has so far emerged as a little humdrum. Not so
much because they have failed to drum up support (polls suggested three
weeks ago a third of voters are 'bananas') but rather, in creating an artful
campaign.
The problem may lie in the gallery of their campaigners.
A freeze-frame photo of 'Yes' champions would show men like Dr
Christopher Murungaru and Kiraitu Murungi. At a campaign rally Murungaru
reportedly said, "Even Satan knows the new constitution is a good one".
Using references to Beelzebub isn't likely to endear a politician to a
crowd of Christians. Then there is Justice minister Kiraitu who may be a
brilliant lawyer but doesn't do justice to the 'Yes' campaign by sounding
somewhat miffed, bellicose and belligerent, for instance, by threatening to
withdraw retired President Moi's pension. And as one pundit put it, it isn't
telegenic to stuff a big, yellow banana into one's mouth, however enthused
about the campaign one is.
Internal Security minister John Michuki's threats are alright because
his role is one of "bringing order to chaos", as of when he was sorting out
matatus or now as he tries to curb lawlessness.
But they added a dash of hard grey in the campaigns because campaigning
is more the art of persuasion than the threat of persecution. Ominous
overtones go down as well as onions in poisonous campaigns.
Onomatopoeia seems to be favoured by fiery Coast MP, Danson
Mungatana. The youthful politician has taken to making noises like, "Nyo!
Nyo! Nyo!" as he contorts his features to suggest the inherent negativity of
the 'No' team.
But onomatopoeia, while folklore favourite, emerges a tad
not-all-that-grown-up as a campaign tool when used out of context.
Raila Odinga has made in-roads as the leader of the 'No' campaign by
marshalling African story-telling skills and using proverbs to provide
colour during Orange campaign.
He famously did this during Rainbow campaigns in 2002, exciting the
nation and sweeping Kanu out of power. Raila's metaphors, especially the
running commentary-on-football types, are a crowd thriller. Makadara MP
Reuben Ndolo has turned his solo literary claim to fame, the phrase
"Ukiwaona wao!" to warn wananchi against the Banana side.
The phrase excites the imagination because it combines the adrenaline of
seeing an oncoming foe with the war-like "achtung" of anticipation of
battle.
Throw in David Mwenje's populism and the 'No' side seems to have all
hecklers on its side.
As any soccer fan will tell you, the psychological boost provided by
cheerleader's plays a big role in determining who wins the game. Government
whip Norman Nyagah merely comes across as querulous when he tries to whip up
emotions for 'Yes' while President Kibaki has to stay statesmanlike, even
during campaigns.
While dignity is admirable, decorum seldom rouses excitement in any
forum.
The English say that music soothes the savage beast but it also is a
fantastic campaign tool. Raila and his cronies learnt this when Unbwoggable
by Gidigidi-Majimaji took Kenya by storm in 2002. They now have an 'Orange'
guitarist, James "Demolition" Omondi, a former boxer, who strums away as
Ndolo belts out their theme song Mapambano, first 'released' by Otieno
Kajwang.
'Yes' campaigners should, perhaps, have hired comedian Walter Mongare to
unleash the song Sweetie Banana at their rallies. People are governed by
prose but persuaded by poetry.
Although 'Yes' is doing its best to show Kenyans the substance in the
proposed constitution, style moves people. As the French philosopher
Voltaire said, "All the masses want is a little bread ... and a circus."
The 'Yes' team, being in government, may be providing the bread and
bananas, but it's the Orange caravan that rolls into town with the
circus.

Original_Search_Title: Fight Widens for Decisive Western Vote
Headline: Kenya: Fight Widens for Decisive Western Vote
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The battle for and against the Proposed New Constitution is narrowing
down to a fight for the votes of Western and Coast provinces.
Ford Kenya MPs, who are supposed to spearhead the Banana campaign in
Western, this week presented their budget for every constituency to cover
the last two weeks of the campaign.
Initially, the party's MPs had asked for Sh10 million and four-wheel
drive vehicles for each of them. That was revised down to Sh5 million, which
was to be paid out in bits.
President Kibaki, too, was expected to tour Kakamega District from
yesterday to help boost the Ford Kenya team on the ground.
The tour was, however, cancelled in what an MP said could have been fear
of violence after the recent incident in which protesters burnt Ikolomani
MP, Bonny Khalwale's car. Sources said the President would go to Bungoma
instead, after touring Coast on Friday.
But the going is rough for Banana in Western, forcing its leaders to
invent new ways of campaign. Instead of open rallies, party leaders are said
to be moving from door-to-door, a style usually reserved for the last days
of a campaign.
In Busia, Vice-President Moody Awori prefers to meet delegations at
home, where they leave in Banana colours. His referendum committees round up
people and ferry them to his home in groups. The Banana team, said Dr
Amukowa Anangwe, is operating at the village level.
"Banana prefers small but several rallies a day. We know Orange goes big
in one place, and then it disappears. We prefer small but many rallies,"
Anangwe said. On Friday, the Banana group was in Bumula and was to move to
Hamisi. At the end of the day, the discreet movements were supposed to end
up with about eight rallies.
"We decided not to go the Orange way. We decided to go small but
deeper. You reach more people that way," the former minister said.
"We can address three or four rallies a day. But unless you follow our
campaign trail, you will not see us. We recognised that Orange took off
first and poisoned the people's minds, so we are at the location," he
added. Orange leaders in the region claim that their Banana opponents are
buying voters cards and paying up to Sh500 for each, a claim repeated by
senior government official in Western.
But Anangwe denied Banana is bribing voters. "They have a lot of
money. If one voter could be given Sh500, then you know the kind of money
they have," Butere MP Wycliffe Oparanya said.
The Banana message in Western varies, depending on who is delivering
it. Despite disquiet among Ford Kenya MPs about Awori, Local Government
Minister Musikari Kombo and the VP move together in Western, selling the
Banana message.
But the MPs allied to Kombo are selling the message that Western
Province is headed for bigger things after the referendum. Kombo, they say,
will be VP and a presidential candidate behind whom President Kibaki's
allies from central Kenya would rally in 2007.
But the campaign is discreet. The VP has addressed rallies in Malava,
Bungoma and Sio Port in Busia. The wider Butere-Mumias and Kakamega
districts are yet to witness any visible Ford Kenya activity. Only Khalwale
has held a rally in the neighbourhood, which ended with the burning of his
car about two weeks ago.
Budalang'i, too, remains largely untouched. For long, its MP Raphael
Wanjala was stuck with the middle ground group, until it collapsed. Kombo
has tried to move into Busia, often in quiet unplanned meetings, a move his
opponents say is an indication that the going is rough. His allies, however,
say it is a strategy.
"Kombo's original plan was to hold rallies two weeks to the
referendum. He wanted to talk to opinion leaders first, and then move in for
rallies. That is still the plan, though it has changed due to
circumstances," an MP allied to him said. The Orange team wants to set up
tent in Western. The Orange leadership from the four groups that make the
team was expected in the rallies that began yesterday in Butere, then moves
to Mumias, and Matungu. The leaders will then disperse for the various
regions, before converging in Bungoma.
In Bungoma, Kombo has been concentrating in Webuye Division, while other
MPs from the district concentrate on their constituencies. Still, the going
is tough for the Ford Kenya team that is supposed to deliver Western
Province. The whole of Butere-Mumias has more or less gone to Orange,
largely due to the stand of the MPs.
In Khwisero, the MP Julius Arunga has abandoned Banana and joined
Orange. That was the same time Butere MP Wycliffe Oparanya also joined
Orange. Mumias MP Wycliffe Osundwa abandoned Ford Kenya long ago, and has
been aligned to the LDP and Orange crusade. The only MP in Butere-Mumias
District still in Banana camp is David Were of Matungu. Yesterday, Nominated
MP Julia Ojiambo said the Orange team is targeting either to convert him or
undermine him.
In Vihiga District, Emuhaya Constituency is in the hands of LDP and
Orange's Kenneth Marende. Hamisi Constituency is in the hands of George
Khaniri an LDP MP. LDP's Andrew Ligale represents Orange crusaders in
Vihiga. Only Sabatia has a Banana MP Moses Akaranga, who has to battle it
out with former VP Musalia Mudavadi. The Orange team had a head start here
too, like in Butere- Mumias.
In Busia, Nambale Constituency, under Chris Okemo and Butula
Constituency under Prof Christine Mango is tilted in Orange's favour.
Budalang'i Constituency is under Raphael Wanjala, who began by remaining
in the middleground but has been making brief appearances for Banana after
the middle ground fence collapsed.
Moody Awori is battling Funyula with Julia Ojiambo and it is not clear
how the VP is fairing at home.
Teso District has only one constituency, Amagoro, where the MP Sospeter
Ojaamong' and former MP Albert Ekirapa have rejected the Wako Draft. In Mt
Elgon, former MPs Joseph Kimkung' and Wiberforce Kisiero, who was also a
delegate at Bomas, besiege John Serut, the Banana man.
When civic education providers moved to Bungoma recently, a senior
government official warned them to watch what they say in Webuye and
Kanduyi. The two constituencies, represented by Kombo, the Ford Kenya
chairman, and Wafula Wamunyinyi, the district's party chairman, were said to
be hosting many Orange sympathisers.
For now, Orange is the dominant colour in Western Province.

Original_Search_Title: Nyanza Leaders Rush to Disown Tuju
Headline: Kenya: Nyanza Leaders Rush to Disown Tuju
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A rush to disown Cabinet minister Raphael Tuju gripped Nyanza this week,
with groups and individuals distancing themselves from the minister in his
war of words with Raila Odinga.
Mr George Ochola, who lost the Uriri Parliamentary seat to Odhiambo
Omamba after being denied Narc nomination certificate this week denied he
was rigged out.
Earlier in the week, Tuju had counted Ochola among those who had been
rigged out by Raila. But on Friday, Ochola disowned Tuju.
"I wish to exonerate myself from Tuju's insinuations and I wish to state
categorically that the minister should desist from dragging other people's
names in his uncalled for, ill-informed and misplaced fight against Raila,"
Ochola said, in a Press statement. Ochola maintained that he won the
nomination for the Uriri seat and was given a certificate.
"It is the ECK that knows why my name was omitted from the ballot papers
in the UK and not Raila," he said. Ochola accused Tuju of abusing the
Luo.
In Kisumu, the man at the centre of the controversial Narc nomination in
Rarieda Constituency Moses Owiti yesterday recalled the exercise that saw
Tuju become the MP.
Owiti said he was happy that the truth had finally come out. Quoting
from a Biblical scripture where Jacob stole Esau's birthright, Owiti said it
is Tuju who stole his. "Tuju should know that I won the nomination but he
stole by birthright," Owiti said.
He added: "I am consulting on the matter but for the moment I am happy
that the truth has come out and I will continue to serve the people of
Rarieda in various ways."
Owiti said that in future elections, Rarieda people should be allowed to
elect a leader of their own.
"We should be allowed to campaign for elections without external
interference," he said.
During the Narc nomination Owiti and Tuju had nomination
certificates. The ECK rejected Owiti's certificate.
An angry Owiti later defected to Social Democratic Party where he lost
to Tuju managing only 5,427 votes against 15,949.
Raila, speaking in Ndori market in Rarieda last week, said he helped
Tuju to make it to parliament after Owiti defeated him. He said Tuju has no
political mantle to win any seat. But Tuju challenged Raila to declare what
he got from him and claimed the Narc nomination was steeped in
corruption.
In Nairobi this week, Mbita MP Otieno Kajwang' and a group of
professionals from Rarieda constituency took the issue to another level and
dragged President Kibaki into the war.
Kajwang', branded Tuju "a turn coat" saying he was doing the dirty work
of ministers from Central Province. Kajwang', who is LDP's secretary for
Legal Affairs, said Kibaki, whom he claimed was behind Tuju's defiance, is
an unreliable politician. Kajwang' reminded the President and Central Kenya
leaders to read what Special Projects minister Njenga Karume said about the
President during the 2002 campaigns when Karume defected from DP to
Kanu. "In September 2002, Njenga Karume left DP and moved to Kanu to support
Uhuru Kenyatta. He gave us a free lesson that I now wish we had learnt
from," Kajwang' said.
The MP said Karume stated in 2002 "Kibaki is a thankless man".
"Karume said in 2002 that he had always said that Kibaki is a thankless
man. That is why he fell out with his bosom friend Kenneth Matiba. That
element of thanklessness has returned to haunt the country.
But Karume is now backing Kibaki and he does not see the
thanklessness. Instead, he sees Raila as unreliable. They have sent Tuju to
spread that message," he said.
Kajwang' accused the Banana team led by ministers from Central Kenya of
sending Tuju on a mission to say Raila was not a democrat.
He compared Tuju's nomination to that of Starehe MP Maina Kamanda in
2002. "The public knows that Jimnah Mbaru won the Narc nomination for
Starehe. But Kibaki insisted that the certificate be given to Maina
Kamanda. Did Kamanda give Kibaki something? Was Kibaki a democrat?" Kajwang'
asked.
At another press conference, three MPs, among them, one of those accused
of being Raila's relatives owned up, then claimed Tuju provided adverts for
Kibaki's 2002 campaign portraits leading to his ministerial
appointment.
But Tuju was quick to dismiss the MPs and said: "Let them answer the
question as raised about them and Raila."
The legislators, Jakoyo Midiwo, Gor Sungu and Prof Ayiecho Olweny warned
Tuju that he was on a "self-destructing mission".
Midiwo told a press conference at Parliament Building "Tuju bought the
Narc nomination certificate and bribed his way to the Cabinet".
Ayiecho claimed that Tuju made the posters of Kibaki's portraits as an
advert for Narc presidential candidate during the 2002 election campaign to
influence his appointment.
Midiwo said that, according to 2002 pre-election MoU, Tuju was not on
the list of the MPs who were to be appointed ministers.
The Gem MP said that just like him, Tuju was also related to Raila
through the Rarieda MP's mother.
"I am Raila's blood relative but I was elected to Parliament. I am not
like Tuju who bought his nomination," Midiwo claimed.
But, when asked to respond to the MP's allegation yesterday, Tuju said,
"Let them answer what I told Raila. I stand by that."

Original_Search_Title: Banana to Chief Kadhi: Toe the Line Or Else
Headline: Kenya: Banana to Chief Kadhi: Toe the Line Or Else
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Chief Kadhi was yesterday warned against supporting the Orange camp
or risk unspecified consequences.
The warning was issued as a Coast politician blamed the government for
failing to empower the Chief Kadhi to discharge his duties effectively.
The Chairman of the Coast 'Yes' team, Mr Mohamed Jahazi, told Sheikh
Kassim Hamad Mazrui, he (the Sheikh) has no moral authority to direct
Muslims on political issues.
He was reacting to remarks attributed to Sheikh Kassim asking Muslims to
reject the Proposed New Constitution.
"Let the Chief Kadhi know that it is the same government he is fighting
that pays for his upkeep and he should toe the line," Jahazi said.
Speaking in Mombasa, Jahazi dismissed the Chief Kadhi as "issuing
statements on the proposed constitution that hold no water".
While addressing Muslim faithful during the Idd Baraza at the Treasury
Square in Mombasa on Friday, Sheikh Kassim asked Muslims to reject the Wako
Draft.
He claimed that Muslims were not fully involved in the preparation of
the proposed document and that it had many clauses that were unacceptable to
Islam.
But Jahazi said that it was only the Mufti - a high ranking religious
leader - who is empowered to give political directions.
He gave the example of former Iranian spiritual leader, Ayatollah
Khomeini who was a mufti and whose word on politics was heeded by many.
Jahazi was speaking at the official opening of the 'Yes' secretariat in
Mombasa's Kizingo area. Tourism and Wildlife Minister, Morris Dzoro
officiated at the ceremony.
Assistant Minister Ananiah Mwaboza - who had at one time threatened to
stop campaigning for the 'Yes' team citing interference from wealthy
businessmen - and a former Mombasa civic leader, Khamis Nyundo, also
attended the function.
"I have full confidence that we shall trounce the 'No' team come
Referendum Day," Mwaboza said.
And in Kisauni, Abdalah Maur Bwanamaka said the Chief Kadhi's office
receives little support from the government unlike other judicial offices
like that of the Chief Justice.
He spoke as Muslim leaders took issue with the Chief Kadhi for confusion
in the moon's sighting to end the Holy Month of Ramadhan.
"We accept the Chief Kadhi's apology over the failure to give an exact
date on when to break the fast, but it has become routine and this is not
good," he said.
He made the remarks during a breakfast prayer meeting at the Taqwa
Islamic Mosque in Mwakirunge location, Kisauni.
Bwanamaka said the Chief Kadhi should be paid well, have decent housing,
a car and security; adding that occupants of the office have had no
trappings of power as compared to their counterparts in Tanzania.
He challenged Muslims to speak with one voice and ensure the government
responds to their demands.
"We should be accorded the privileges that other citizens enjoy,"
Bwanamaka said.
Meanwhile, thousands of Muslims drawn from the North Rift region
yesterday held a peaceful procession in Eldoret town to reject the Proposed
New Constitution.
Led by Sheikh Abdikadir Manor from Uasin Gishu, they marched from the
local mosque in town to the Eldoret Municipal Council Hall where they vowed
to reject the Wako Draft.
Manor said they were not sure that the Wako Bill would cater for their
interests saying that there was a chance the Kadhi's Courts could be deleted
if the Constitution is passed in it's present form.
Addressing the Muslims later at the hall, Manor said Muslims had not
been consulted sufficiently during the drafting of the proposed
constitution.
"We don't want to pass a document that will eventually relegate us to
the periphery of government. Why were we not consulted in the first place?"
he asked.
He said the inclusion of other religious courts in the constitution was
a clear testimony that the Kadhi's Courts would no longer enjoy the same
weight in law.
"I urge my fellow Muslim brothers and sisters to come out in large
numbers and vote against the Wako Draft," Manor added.
He said by voting 'No' against the proposed constitution, Muslims will
be safeguarding their rights and property.
He said the Proposed New Constitution had many clauses that were
unacceptable to the Muslim faith.

Original_Search_Title: Origins of 'Odingaism' And Why Tuju's is a Tough Call
Headline: Kenya: Origins of 'Odingaism' And Why Tuju's is a Tough Call
Body_Text:
Nairobi — When riots rocked Kisumu City last Saturday as Information Minister
Raphael Tuju defied odds to hold a pro-referendum rally in the lakeside
town, it was not all about the forthcoming plebiscite.
Tuju had made - some say - the mistake of attacking the Odinga family's
continued dominance of Luo politics or what has been christened
Odingaism.
The chaos and deaths that resulted as police stopped bands of youth from
scattering the Tuju-Banana rally notwithstanding, it was the war of words
between Roads Minister Raila Odinga and Tuju that confirmed what every
politician from Luo Nyanza knows: that nobody gets to parliament these days
without Odinga's blessing.
Raila said as much: that were it not for his personal intervention, Tuju
would not have made it to parliament.
The story and sentimentality of Odingaism runs much more deeper than
many Kenyans understand. On June 18, 2001 in Kisumu Raila was crowned by the
Luo Council of Elders as the leader of the community and promised not to let
them down. It was a role only enjoyed by his father, Jaramogi, until his
death in 1994.
That Raila now controls the direction the Luos take in local politics
has not been lost over the years. That Tuju was going against the grain in
Luo Nyanza politics can only be measured by the minute crowd that attended
his 'Yes' rally and the attempt by youths to stop it. It is also strikingly
reminiscent of the battle for the domination of Luo politics by Tom Joseph
Mboya and Oginga in 1960s.
Last weekend's riots and rally in Kisumu were of political and
historical significance too: it happened only three days after the unmarked
anniversary of the October 26, 1969 killings in Kisumu in which Jomo
Kenyatta's bodyguards opened fire on a defiant Kenya People's Union (KPU)
crowd that had shouted him down as he dressed down Oginga at the official
opening of "Russia", as the New Nyanza General Hospital is known.
Historians have always pointed out that these 1969 killings were of
political significance in that they bonded the Luos together to hunt for
political power as a pack. But they also confirmed Odinga as the de facto
Luo leader - a mantle that Raila took after his father's death.
When he left teaching at Maseno's Veterinary School for a political
career and business, Jaramogi did it in protest. That his mission snowballed
into what scholars now call Odingaism was not by chance.
"I was haunted by the view which other Africans had of the Luo people. I
had been hurt at Makerere by the accusations of fellow students from other
tribes that the Luo were extravagant, self-centred, and exhibitionist the
Luo needed to build a sense of unity, common purpose, and achievement," he
later wrote in his autobiography, Not Yet Uhuru.
The only politician who rose to challenge the hold of Jaramogi on Luo
politics was Mboya. But theirs was a battle that had been taken to another
level. While Jaramogi seemed to lean towards socialism, Mboya was his
capitalist opposite. But it was their battle to run and command Luo politics
that was bruising - and politically lethal.
Born in Sakwa of a woodworker, Odinga had graduated from Makerere
University but was denied a British scholarship to further his
studies. Frustrated about poverty in his backyard he in 1947, he founded the
Luo Thrift and Trading Corporation, a construction and printing business
company, which drew its support from small-time investors hoping to rally
all Luos in East Africa into a giant economic block.
It is from this that Odinga built a trust among the influential Luo
leaders which catapulted him to the position of Ker when the Luo Union was
first formed in August 1953. He had also wrestled the community leadership
mantle from Paul Mboya, a Seventh Day Adventist and author who had
influenced politics in the region.
Some five years earlier, 1948, Odinga had convinced the Luo to back Jomo
Kenyatta's Kenya African Union (Kau) which took him to the centre of
mainstream politics. By that time he had not met Kenyatta and was only
introduced to him in 1952 by Achieng Oneko, who had joined national politics
much earlier.
That Kenyatta was to later betray Odinga has always been a source of
friction especially between the Kikuyu and the Luo elites and a rallying
point for Odingaism. It is a friction that has once again arose as the two
communities find themselves on the opposing ends of the referendum with the
bulk of Luos supporting the 'No' campaign while the Kikuyus are supporting
'Yes'.
By siding with the 'Yes' camp, Tuju is seen as a spoiler much the same
as Mboya, then Kanu Secretary General, was seen when he led Kanu to dethrone
Oginga of political power in March 1966 when the post of party vice
president was formed.
"These provocations were clearly not accidental. They were deliberately
mounted to isolate not only me but the forces I represent in the country,"
Odinga would later write. " my membership in the Cabinet was used to silence
me and to hold the allegiance of my supporters, not only in Nyanza but
throughout the country."
Although in private Oginga described Kenyatta as "too old" the man he
perceived to be his political enemy was Mboya, an ambitious pro-Western
trade unionist, who however did not enjoy the grassroots support Oginga
had.
It is an attempt that Tuju will make only to his political demise,
observers affirm.
Scholars say that Odingaism was borne of Kenyatta era betrayal that
Odinga and most of the Luo elite politicians faced from 1960s and the
negligence of the Luo economy.
Tuju's latest explanation is that he wants to "free" the Luo community
from the past and "offer a development path".
Archival records show that Oginga would have become Kenya's first Prime
Minister and had been invited to Government House by Governor Sir Patrick
Reninson to accept the offer in 1960. He refused and told Reninson to
release Kenyatta - a man the governor had described as "leader to darkness
and death".
Ironically, Odinga would become the second politician to suffer under
Kenyatta (Odinga's key political friend Pio Gama Pinto was killed in 1965)
and he pulled out to form KPU with bedrock of support in Luo Nyanza which
some blame for the negligence that followed.
Although Odinga had pulled the rug from Mboya's feet and withdrawn the
Luo support from Kenyatta's government, there is a general feeling that it
was the community that bore the brunt of punishment after 1969 riots and
which still runs deep.
Replacing Odingaism in Nyanza politics will be a tall order for Tuju
indeed.

Original_Search_Title: Uganda is Yearning for New Policies, Not Faces
Headline: Uganda is Yearning for New Policies, Not Faces
Body_Text:
It was just like clock work. Rtd Col. Dr Kizza Besigye jets in from South Africa Wednesday, paralyses the airport-city highway, registers as a voter, addresses a mammoth public rally at independence grounds at Kololo. Before the week ends, he is elected unopposed president of his party and therefore presidential candidate, at a delegates conference held in a stadium.
"If I am arrested as was widely feared, I wont be just another returnee from self imposed exile," he seemed to have determined.
A sense of urgency has characterised Besigye's return.
In 2001 when he first challenged his comrade-in-arms President Yoweri Museveni, he had also abruptly decided to announce his candidacy just days before his anticipated arrest. Besigye sure knows how to raise profile as if to throw a spanner in the works.
His press conference at Entebbe Airport was defiant, defending the use of force as constitutional. He certainly did not want to return as a coward, an impression he had helped to create when he fled the country shortly after a bruising presidential race in 2001.
Between Entebbe and Kampala, he suddenly changed his mind, for when he spoke at Kololo he looked fierce alright (I was there), but soft on the message, declaring that the era of the hammer was over, as things had changed. He said we were not dealing with a piece of steel any more (the Museveni of 2001 ) but with a bunch of dry banana leaves or essanja in local lingua (the Museveni of 2005).
He thanked the government of South Africa for the hospitality of the past four years. " I am fully backed up," was the actual message. South Africa would be thanked again in a resolution at the end of the delegates conference. Isn't Pretoria (Tshwane) the capital of Africa anyway?
The man who in May this year had opined that elections under the prevailing circumstances were a waste of time, adding that he would never lend his name to such a futile exercise, now thought: "We can win"
The electorate is confused. The signals are simply too contradictory too soon from a man that could be president.
Dr Besigye's return has been hailed by all political forces as good for democracy. The euphoria, though, that has accompanied it has shifted alliances. G6, the conglomerate of six political groups including FDC of Kizza Besigye could disintegrate, while Dr Besigye has emphasized the need for united action; DP and UPC, Uganda's older parties, do not seem very excited.
The official DP was conspicuously absent at Kololo. UPC too did not pitch; spymaster David Pulkol of the Progressive Alliance Party, PAP, not part of the G6 was there, coming right behind Dr Besigye.
His presence was not acknowledged by the organisers. He fumed. PAP had expressed willingness to join the G6 to create a G7, in the past weeks, With some effort it is possible to keep the G6 together, we should believe.
FDC's enviable show of strength is likely to have made the party more confident of winning them before, but could also have made the other parties more determined not to be swallowed in the FDC glory.
Own space
Increasingly, I see DP and UPC wanting to have own space on the presidential ballot paper in 2006, for better or for worse.
Hardliners in both parties see 2006 as their only chance in a long time to organise themselves and gauge their real strengths.
They tend to believe that the whole NRM-FDC scenario is a sophisticated attempt to stage a bloodless palace coup to allow the veterans of the Luwero triangle project perpetuate their hold onto power. Moderates would disagree.
Others view the FDC as a party of the "formers " than a promise of the future. Each day that passes makes the FDC-NRM race look like a fight between two tired men, from the same old school. Besigye seems to have read into the minds of a wary electorate when he promised to serve only one presidential term if elected. He knows he has been part of the sad story for too long.
Many people would want Museveni out, but cannot figure out how that single event can pull Uganda out of a $5bn debt that the NRM has accumulated on their behalf. They want the whole opposition to unite behind one candidate and frustrate the kisanja project once and for all but cannot determine whether it is true that only a Besigye victory would indicate a free and fair election, so as to save the country from the hell one of the candidates is promising should the results not be acceptable.
Younger people, the majority of voters, had hoped for some freshness in the transition, some inspiration from leaders they could identify with. Instead what they foresee is a call to them to participate in a possible perspiration exercise between two "kyankwanzi professors."
If Besigye warned in 2002 that conditions of war exist in Uganda, I warn instead that conditions for an ideological revolution have already been satisfied. Ugandans have tried cosmetic politics before. They would not want to risk it again if they had a choice.
As it looks, the first new party without excess baggage and perhaps nothing to lose that will defy gravity and refuse to play by the old rules of scarecrow politics will be the only relevant force to emerge out of this much hyped transition. Only such a party that can pit itself against 'everybody else' and risk short term isolation by refusing to play by the rules of 'short-cutism politics' will awaken Uganda from her nightmare of the past twenty years. Any political party that will find comfort in the current simplified illusion and wish to benefit from the luxury of sounding correct is, in my view, an undesired irrelevance.
Uganda, we all know, is not simply yearning for new faces at the political parade, She is searching for a new policy. A discourse obsessed with 'issue' not tissue (muscle)." A set of behaviour that discourage sacrificing tomorrow's big needs for today's small wants.

Original_Search_Title: Campaigns Trail Leads to Kisii
Headline: Kenya: Campaigns Trail Leads to Kisii
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The two sides in the referendum debate yesterday traded accusations,
with the Yes team calling those opposed to the new Constitution fraudsters,
as their opponents said President Kibaki should own up to mistakes in the
Wako Draft.
It was unfair for the Head of State to continue passing blame to others
after overseeing a flawed process in which a few individuals shelved the
will Kenyans, said Eldoret North MP William Ruto .
Mr Ruto said a clique of powerful individuals changed proposals
presented to them by Kenyans, leaving the current proposed Draft
Constitution "shameful document" engineered by greed.
A police officer escorts an Orange team convoy at Suneka trading centre
in Kisii District yesterday after some youths wearing Yes T-shirts started
throwing stones at it. Photo by Sylvester Onyang
"Kenyans had good ideas for their country but some individuals carried
their wishes to Kilifi where they were subjected to serious mutilation. What
came out of the Kilifi talks was nothing close to the people's wishes and it
absurd for the President to cry foul today yet all took place under his
nose," he told a rally at the Gusii stadium in Kisii Town.
On the Banana side, Water minister Martha Karua accused former
presidents' sons of hiding behind the constitutional referendum to conceal
past misdeeds.
Ms Karua said Mr Uhuru Kenyatta and Mr Gedion Moi were out to cover up
their fathers' injustices by maintaining the status quo. She was among
leaders who addressed a Banana rally in Nyamira. Mr Ruto absolved himself
from blame over the impasse on the Constitution, saying "clandestine forces"
frustrated his efforts when he chaired the Parliamentary Select Committee on
the Constitution.
Roads minister Raila Odinga supported Mr Ruto, saying a selfish quest to
cling to power caused certain individuals in Government to enter into
machinations that derailed the wishes of the people.
He said contrary to dreams of a democratic Constitution, some selfish
individuals were trying to force on Kenyans a set of laws that bestowed
immense powers on the presidency.
Mr Odinga told off his Cabinet colleague Simeon Nyachae, accusing him of
causing confusion in the review.
He also accused the Energy minister of digressing into trivial personal
matters in the ongoing debate on the Constitution.
But addressing the Nyamira rally, Mr Nyachae said the Orange team wanted
to use the outcome of the constitutional referendum as a tool against
President Kibaki's Government in the 2007 General Election.
He said he was unhappy that Banana campaigners were being barred from
certain sections of the country whereas the Gusii community listened to both
sides.
Mr Nyachae said Kisii people turned out in large numbers for rallies
called by either side.
He maintained that Orange supporters were out to grab power through the
backdoor.
Among those who attended the rally, at Nyamira Primary School, were
assistant ministers Joshua Toro and Henry Obwocha. Others included MPs Bonny
Khalwale, Jimmy Angwenyi, Mwancha Okioma and Zebedeo Opore.
At Kisii, South Mugirango MP Omingo Magara, who was among 20 MPs who
addressed the No rally, alleged that pro-Banana campaigners were using money
to discredit him politically in his constituency.

Original_Search_Title: Two Policemen Held Over Riots
Headline: Kenya: Two Policemen Held Over Riots
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A police inspector and a constable were yesterday arrested in connection
with the shooting to death of four people, including three boys, in last
weekend's Kisumu riots.
Nine other officers are being sought for questioning on the campaign
violence that followed a rally organised by Information Minister Raphael
Tuju to campaign for the Yes vote in the town.
Police commissioner Hussein Ali said the arrests followed claims of a
cover-up by police to justify the shooting.
Initial police reports had indicated that the victims were shot when a
mob raided Kondele police station to free a suspect. The commissioner
defended the use of live bullets, believing that officers were protecting
the police station from raiders who wanted to overrun it.
"We have nothing to hide and the law will take its course. Those who
defied orders not to use live ammunition will pay for it," Maj-Gen Ali
said.
The police commissioner said the board appointed to investigate the
incident would operate independently.
It has emerged that Maj-Gen Ali had personally telephoned all provincial
police bosses warning against the use of live ammunition to contain
referendum chaos. He had later followed up the caution with letters to the
eight provincial police bosses.
One of the victims of the shooting, 14-year-old Paul Limera, is to be
buried today in Butere. He was the son of a police inspector.
The other victims are Standard Seven pupil Vincent Otieno Owuoche, whose
body was discovered on Thursday, and Hillary Ochieng', aged 17.
Vincent's parents found the body at Nyanza hospital mortuary after
searching for him since rioting rocked Kisumu on Saturday. He had been shot
in the stomach.
Hillary Ochieng' was shot in the left leg and then allegedly clubbed to
death by the police.
The fourth victim was Mr George Ogada, a 32-year-old milk vendor shot in
the stomach.
Paul's body was taken from the hospital's mortuary and carried to his
school, Josana Academy, where his school mates and teachers read poems in
his honour. During a funeral service conducted by Pastor Walter Odenda of
the Pentecostal church, the boy's parents called for justice and the arrest
of his killers.
"I know what we are undergoing as a family but I leave it all to God,"
said his father, Inspector Kennedy Limera Mutere, a prosecutor at Kisumu
courts. "It is God who gives and it is God who takes away."
Insp Mutere added: "Almighty God is awake each moment and will always do
unto one what one does unto others."
Paul died when hit by what police described as a stray bullet, which hit
him in the head, killing him instantly.
He collapsed still clinging to his school bag, which was packed with
books bearing his name.
The chairman of the Electoral Commission of Kenya, Mr Samuel Kivuitu,
announced on Wednesday he would summon Mr Tuju and five others for
questioning about the violence by a committee headed by vice-chairman
Gabriel Mukele on Monday.
Others to be questioned include Ikolomani MP Bonny Khalwale, whose car
was burnt to a shell in Kakamega in the same week, Government chief whip
Norman Nyagah, who is the chief agent for the Banana Yes-vote group, and
former minister Isaac Ruto, chief agent of the Orange No-vote group.

Original_Search_Title: President Says Constitution Making Transcends Parties
Headline: Kenya: President Says Constitution Making Transcends Parties
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Mwai Kibaki has said the constitution-making process transcended party boundaries and was not a contest between different political interests.
He was speaking at State House, Nairobi, on Thursday when he addressed a delegation of Kanu officials who called on him.
The President said the process should not be portrayed as a monopoly of certain individuals.
He said: "This document is for all Kenyans now and posterity and should not be trivialised as a contest between competing political interests."
Saying leaders come and go, President Kibaki warned some leaders against deluding themselves about their political invincibility.
The President said in a democracy like Kenya's, every party had the right to operate but cautioned that party interests should not be let to wreak havoc.
President Kibaki said the Government would continue to take development to all corners of the country and no community would be discriminated against on account of voting with the opposition.
The Kanu officials, who were led by Special Projects minister Njenga Karume, distanced themselves from some of their leaders who were opposed to the proposed constitution.
The officials said there was an established machinery for making such important decisions.
Elsewhere, President Kibaki opens a new ground for the Banana campaign today when he meets leaders from the North Rift region in Nakuru.
He will host Turkana District leaders at State House this morning.
Sources said the President will announce the creation of another district in Turkana, most probably Lokitaung, as he continues his campaign for the proposed constitution.
Legislators John Munyes (Turkana North) and David Ekwee Ethuro (Turnana Central) are in the Yes camp, while their Turkana South counterpart Francis Ewaton has yet to declare his stand.
On Sunday, he will meet a delegation from Samburu.
In Samburu, Planning Assistant minister and Samburu West MP Simeon Lesirma are in the No camp, while their Samburu East counterpart, Sammy Leshore, is undecided.

Original_Search_Title: Leaders Dismiss Probe On Kisumu Killings
Headline: Kenya: Leaders Dismiss Probe On Kisumu Killings
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A group of leaders and the Law Society of Kenya yesterday dismissed a board formed by Police Commissioner to probe the killing of four people in Kisumu last weekend.
The Law Society of Kenya (LSK) said Maj-Gen Ali's board appointed to probe chaos that rocked Kisumu last weekend was illegal.
Kisumu Town East MP Gor Sunguh has called for an neutral inquiry into the violence that left four people dead and at least 40 injured in Kisumu last Saturday.
He dismissed the board of inquiry set up by Ali as part of attempts to cover-up the issue.
"How can the police be the investigating officers in a inquiry where they are the accused?" wondered Sunguh.
On Thursday, Ali formed a board of inquiry headed by the Deputy Criminal Investigation Director, Mr Peter Kavila.
The team of four comprises senior police officers Pauline Adhiambo, Albert Ombaba and Stanley Kinuthia.
But yesterday, Sunguh said the move was aimed at intimidating potential witnesses.
Speaking at Parliament buildings, the MP said since Ali had absolved police and said he had no regrets over the killings, he should not be involved in the investigations.
He questioned why police never use live bullets when braking demonstrations in other towns and only target Kisumu residents.
