He recalled the 2003 Saba Saba riots in Kisumu and Nairobi, saying people were killed in the lakeside town yet minimum force was used in Nairobi and no one was injured.
Elsewhere, leaders in Rift Valley also dismissed the formation of an inquest into the killings.
Speaking in Nakuru, the Orange supporters said the Government wanted to hoodwink the public.
The leaders from nine districts demanded an independent judicial inquiry by an impartial tribunal.
Led by a former Cabinet minister Isaac Ruto, they said the inquest formed by Ali was a slap in the face of Kenyans.
The former Chepalungu MP accused President Kibaki of turning State House into a banana campaign nerve centre and wondered why the Electoral Commission was doing nothing to stop the use of Government resources in the campaign.

Original_Search_Title: Civic Leader Whipped in Campaign Violence
Headline: Kenya: Civic Leader Whipped in Campaign Violence
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Hired Ford Kenya goons beat a civic leader allied to the Orange group as Minister Kipruto Kirwa led four of his colleagues to drum up support for the Wako Draft in Trans-Nzoia District.
Councillor Ronald Sawenja was cornered in Kitale town by a group of youths allied to Ford-K national Chairman Musikari Kombo as he was drawing a programme for Orange rallies.
The councillor was whipped before he was left in a comer. A Good Samaritan took him to Kitale District Hospital.
Sawenja was briefly admitted before he was discharged and re-admitted to a private hospital as his supporters promised to revenge.
The incident took place as Kombo was officially opening the Kitale ASK show.
He later hosted a party for all civic leaders in the area at a local private members club.
Orange supporters led by Francis Rono asked the Government to provide security for the group, saying their lives were in danger.
"We want action taken against those who assaulted the civic leader to avoid further confrontations. We have the capacity to hit back, but we want the Government to take the necessary steps," Rono said.
The civic leader lost cash and his cell phone during the incident.
The four Cabinet ministers asked the Bukusu community living in the district to support the draft and President Mwai Kibaki to enable them enjoy national resources.
Addressing a Banana rally at Kiminini on their way to Bumula, the ministers, who included Amos Kimunya, Kombo, Dr Mukhisa Kituyi and Kirwa, said the current draft would enable the locals to perform their duties without intimidation from the provincial administration.
They assured the public that the draft would sail through in the referendum and called on Cabinet ministers allied to the Orange team to concentrate on issues and not personalities.
Kirwa asked the locals to deliver all their maize produce to the National and Cereals Produce Board from next week.
MPs Davies Nakitare, Koigi Wamwere and Sylvester Wakholi attended the rally but Kwanza MP Dr Noah Wekesa was absent.
The MPs accused Roads minister Raila Odinga of allegedly being behind the Kisumu skirmishes last weekend that left four people dead.
"Raila should leave Minister Raphael Tuju to hold his rallies. He should not hire hooligans to prove his popularity in Luo Nyanza," the MPs said.
Kirwa asked the residents to hold peaceful campaigns and listen to everybody to avoid confrontation.
"After the referendum we will remain Kenyans. President Kibaki will remain the President and us ministers," he told the rally as he was cheered by his nickname "Makokha".

Original_Search_Title: Chief Kadhi Urges Muslims to Reject Draft
Headline: Kenya: Chief Kadhi Urges Muslims to Reject Draft
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Chief Kadhi Sheikh Kassim Hamad yesterday told Muslims to reject the proposed constitution.
Addressing Idd baraza at Treasury Square Gardens, in Mombasa, Kassim said Muslims had not been fully involved in preparing the Wako Draft.
He said the documebt had many clauses that were unacceptable to Muslims.
The Chief Kadhi said declared his stand on the Wako draft for the first time after weeks of urging the faithful to read the Wako document.
His declaration boosted the Orange ranks, whose leaders have been calling on Kenyans to vote against the proposed constitution at the November 21 referendum.
"Let us wake up and say a big No to the proposed constitution as our welfare has not been taken seriously in the Wako Draft," said Hamad.
Kassim urged the owners of a Mombasa-based Islamic FM radio station, Rahma, and Nairobi's Radio Iqra FM, to educate Muslims on the dangers of voting for the proposed constitution.
"I urge Muslims in Mombasa and Nairobi to tune to Radio Rahma and Iqra and understand why the Wako Draft was not good for Muslims," said Hamad.
Mombasa Mayor Taib Ali Taib said Muslims have been marginalised because of disunity.
"Our weakness, division and hatred among Muslims, has given the Government advantage to sideline us," he said.
Taib joined the Orange side three weeks ago despite threats from the Banana camp that his nomination as councillor would be revoked.

Original_Search_Title: Banana Team in Secret Meeting With Moi
Headline: Kenya: Banana Team in Secret Meeting With Moi
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Government has approached retired President Moi to seek his support for the proposed constitution, it was revealed yesterday.
Senior members of President Kibaki's Cabinet met Moi at his Kabarnet Gardens home in Nairobi on Tuesday afternoon.
The meeting was reportedly arranged by Special Programmes minister Njenga Karume, an influential political figure in the former regime and close friend of the President.
Liberal Democratic Party secretary-general Joseph Kamotho yesterday told of the secret meeting, which, he said, was attended by Internal Security minister John Michuki and a senior Parliament official.
"We want Mr Michuki to explain to Kenyans why they visited the retired President and what was the agenda. Recently they threatened to strip him of his retirement benefits. Later they go visiting him," Kamotho said.
A source close to the retired President promised that his office would issue a detailed statement on the matter today.
"Mzee (Moi) hasn't denied there was a meeting, neither has Michuki. But that's all I can say for now. I'll get back to you tomorrow."
Sources said the Banana camp representatives asked Moi to use his political influence to either ensure the Government did not lose at the referendum or to force the Orange team into a dialogue.
Moi reportedly told the leaders he was no longer in active politics and was quietly enjoying his retirement.
He is said to have told the visitors to seek dialogue with the Orange team and that he had no control over them.
He reportedly said to them: "This thing has taken a life of its own and it is unstoppable."
Moi has previously publicly declared his opposition the proposed constitution, saying it was not in the interest of Kenyans.
And recently, he issued a statement saying he was not part of the No campaign as was being alleged.
Yesterday, Kamotho claimed the Banana camp had visited the retired president because it had sensed defeat.
Others who spoke at the rally were Cabinet ministers Raila Odinga, Kalonzo Musyoka and Ochilo

Original_Search_Title: New Website for Banana Campaign
Headline: Kenya: New Website for Banana Campaign
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Yes team yesterday launched its website, www.visionyes.com .
The launch came two weeks after the No team started its campaign
website, www.orangenocampaign.com .
Yesterday's new web site gives voters the chance to compare the proposed
Constitution, the Bomas Draft and the current Constitution.
"The website is designed to give Kenyans a 360 degree view of all the
issues in the current and the proposed constitutions, and enables a candid,
fair and equitable discussion of the issues therein," said Mr Kamau Mbugua,
an official of the Vision Yes lobby group.
Banana team leaders, Information minister Raphael Tuju, assistant
minister Danson Mungatana and MPs Njoki Ndung'u, Jayne Kihara and Kalembe
Ndile attended the inauguration of the website at Serena Hotel in
Nairobi.
Vision Yes
Mr Tuju said the web site was the brainchild of youthful professionals
and businesspeople who formed Vision Yes lobby last month.
"People will now be able to read and compare the (three sets of
laws). They have been filled with lies by the Orange team," he said.
On the home page of the website, is the question: "Where is the truth?"
The website also enables an e-mail debate between the public and the Yes
secretariat to take place and posts analytical articles about the proposed
law.
All rallies by the Banana group are posted on the website.
"This was intended to give the Yes campaign more punch and ensure the
Banana team wins at the referendum," said Mr Mbugua.
The Orange team, which wants Kenyans to reject the proposed Constitution
at the November 21 referendum, launched its website two weeks ago, and
adopted Mr Michael Ooko's song, I am not sober.
The Yes team has adopted Prince Adio's (Mr Adio Mohamed's) hit, Nikiwa
Ndani, to discredit their opponents.
Yesterday, Mr Tuju accused the No team of coming up with a "strategy to
stop other people from going to Nyanza".
He continued: "In other areas, their strategy is to galvanise the other
communities against Central Province." Both camps have supporters in every
part of the country, however.
Mr Mungatana, the Lands assistant minister, and Mr Ndile accused No team
leaders Raila Odinga and Kalonzo Musyoka of using their tribes to attain
selfish ends.
"They are after the presidency, and the 20 per cent (flaw in the
proposed Constitution) you hear of does not concern the common man. It is
about power and they should be honest and tell Kenyans," said Mr Mungatana,
the Garsen MP.

Original_Search_Title: Go Liberate the People of Othaya
Headline: Kenya: Go Liberate the People of Othaya
Body_Text:
Nairobi — My dear Raphael Tuju, forgive me for addressing you in such familiar terms, but I like to think of you as my brother-in-law. In my books, a brother-in-law is as good as your brother minus the petty jealousies of sibling rivalry.
Because you have not grown up in the same household, you see the brother-in-law through rose-tinted glasses. You are not all-too-familiar with the low and dirty on them.
In the heat of the referendum campaign, I choose to address you directly for one simple reason: You have packaged yourself as the voice of truth and justice. I respect that in a country where transformation leadership has flown right out the window.
This is a defining moment in Kenya's history and the truth is central to what we become. I would not expect anything less of the Minister for Information and Communication, who should double up as the chief executive's PR adviser.
I particularly like it that you have chosen to stand on your principles, whatever they are. It has become rather difficult lately to define the boundaries between the real thing and self-serving lies, you see.
Truth, it was drilled into us in childhood, sets people free. Now, it seems, it can be so elastic that it makes nonsense of debate. You can tell the truth and nothing but the truth. You can doctor it to suit your need of the moment. You can also manufacture your own version of the truth - and still manage to stay this side of the anti-corruption authorities.
It's all in a day's work when political careers and fortunes are being thrashed out. But let's get back on track. You are on record as saying you are on a mission to liberate the Luo from the Odinga yoke. Ambition, as I might have said in these columns before, should be a compulsory subject in our schools. May the wind be behind you!
I could question your timing, though, considering that saving a nation from itself is such a delicate task. Some would say that it is foolhardy to load it on the back of a referendum that's sure to leave a lot of bloody noses around come November 23.
I have a little trouble with your version of truth too. During the exceedingly messy Kisumu riot that has now become the subject of a police inquiry, you sent out an impassioned plea that you should be allowed to address the people - even if there were only two or three of them, and they then dismissed you as a madman.
I am with you there 100 per cent. Democracy is about giving everyone a hearing on equal terms. Our people need to be told that loud and clear - and I don't just mean the small community that you have set up as the object of your benevolence.
I find it rather amusing, though, that you should take this position from the vantage point of the "Yes" campaign. Your camp declared unashamedly that it would commandeer State resources to give its campaign the extra edge to "shake every village in Kenya".
Your cohorts have declared the Constitution a Government document and not a Government document, all at once. Phooey to those of you who think a constitution belongs to a nation, not the clique that controls the purse strings!
Orange or banana, we all pay taxes and deserve our fair share of resources to ensure a level playing field - the very same thing that you yearn for so passionately that you are ready to lay your life on the line.
I don't recall your principles riding you hard enough to ask your "Yes" colleagues to go easy on the gas. You should have heard me cackle like a witch when your camp put out a full-page, full-colour advertisement in all newspapers with the title, "Why are some people afraid of democracy?"
Methinks you might get greater mileage preaching liberation theology to your buddies in Parliament. Tell them that documents, however important, do not build a nation.
Presidents Kenyatta and Moi sent Kenyans to detention even when the Constitution guaranteed us the freedoms of association, movement and expression. Nothing and no one stopped them.
The leading lights in your camp were all part of those governments, and in key positions too. They stood by and watched these dramas play themselves out. Some even grew stupendously rich out of unjust systems, going back as far as the colonial era. Now they set themselves up as our saviours and mumble about the progenies of the rich, as if being a Kenyatta, Moi or Odinga is a crime!
They have herded us into tribal cocoons - in the true tradition of the governments they served so faithfully - from which they reward and punish us at will.
It's business as usual. You get a district thrown at you or a bullet in the back of the head, depending on where you stand at any given moment. Is there any chance that your village will be declared an independent state within Bondo District? I wait with bated breath.
There are two kinds of leaders, it seems - those who use others like so much toilet paper and those who nurture and build genuine partnerships. I have often had reason to question the wisdom of Raila Odinga, but do tell: how come he was wholly acceptable, warts and all, during the "Kibaki Tosha" days but has now morphed into devil incarnate in the very same circles that gained so richly from his efforts, including yourself?
As Minister for Information, you have presided over the emergence of hate media, yet the same Luo people who are the principal targets today gave President Kibaki 99 per cent of their votes in 2002 out of a turn-out of 97.2 per cent. It is possible, of course, that you have mounted a "high profile" helicopter campaign to liberate us from genocide talk, and I somehow missed it - in which case, please forgive me.
Your People's Progressive Party really must spread its wings, man. I don't see why you should be satisfied with being a big fish in a small pond. When you think of it dispassionately, the Luo are virtually inconsequential when it comes to ambition and power.
Go free the people of Othaya, who religiously vote Democratic Party. Head west to the land of mulembe, where locals are being exhorted to eat banana to the point of constipation - just so that one of their homegrown sons can ascend to power by virtue of being Number Two.
I've never understood why they should settle for second place when everyone else wants to rise to the top of the heap. But there you are. You have your work cut out for you!
Ms Oriang' is the Nation's managing editor in charge of magazines

Original_Search_Title: Body of Third Boy Killed in Rioting Found
Headline: Kenya: Body of Third Boy Killed in Rioting Found
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The identity of the fourth victim of campaign rioting was revealed
yesterday... a 15-year-old boy.
His body was found at Nyanza hospital mortuary by his parents who had
been searching frantically for him since rioting rocked Kisumu on
Saturday.
Standard Seven pupil Vincent Otieno Owuoche had been shot in the
stomach.
He was the third boy killed in the violence accompanying a Yes-vote
rally called by Information minister Raphael Tuju.
The first, 14-year-old police inspector's son Paul Limera, is to be
buried tomorrow in Butere.
The second was Hillary Ochieng', aged 17, who was shot in the left leg
and then allegedly clubbed to death by the police.
The only adult who died was Mr George Ogada, a 32-year-old milk vendor
who was shot in the stomach.
Hospital chief John Odondi confirmed Vincent had died of a bullet wound
and that his body had been dumped in the mortuary after the Saturday
rioting.
He went on: "His body had remained unidentified until his parents
visited the mortuary after a futile search."
Pupils of Josana Academy mourn schoolmate Paul Mwela, another victim of
the riots, during a requiem mass at the school yesterday.
Grieving parents Samuel Owuoche and Emily Achieng' immediately demanded
an investigation into their son's death.
Mr Owuoche added: "Our son did not report back home on the Saturday and
we embarked on a fruitless search that yielded to nothing until now."
He said Vincent, their first born of five children, was in a happy mood
when he left home at around 3pm for a stroll near their Manyatta Estate
home. But he never returned.
At his school, Ogango Primary in Kisumu, headmistress Esther Oduor said
students had been given the Saturday off because staff were attending the
funeral of a teacher's relative.
"It was not until we got the information today from the slain boy's kins
about his death, otherwise we never could have known anything," she
said.
Mrs Oduor described Vincent as a polite boy, whose good manners had led
to his being nominated as a class prefect.
The discovery of his body plunged Kisumu into fresh mourning. "We have
lost two young lives. It is terrible and the Government owes us more than an
apology", said Kisumu lawyer Olago Aluoch.
On the day the discovery of Vincent's body was announced, the lakeside
town was already mourning the death of Paul Limera, shot dead during the
rioting.
His body was taken from the same mortuary yesterday and carried to his
school, Josana Academy, where tears flowed as his school mates and teachers
read poems in his honour.
They formed part of an emotional funeral service conducted by Pastor
Walter Odenda of the Pentecostal church, during which the boy's parents
called for justice in ensuring his killers were arrested.
"I know what we are undergoing as a family but I leave it all to God,"
said his father, Inspector Kennedy Limera Mutere, a prosecutor at Kisumu
courts. "It is God who gives and it is God who takes away."
Insp. Mutere added: "Almighty God is awake each moment and will always
do unto one what one does unto others."
Paul died when hit by what police described as a stray bullet, which hit
him in the head, killing him instantly.
He collapsed still clinging to his school bag which was packed with
books bearing his name.
Car was burnt
The chairman of the Electoral Commission of Kenya, Mr Samuel Kivuitu,
announced on Wednesday he would summon Mr Tuju and five others for
questioning about the violence by a committee headed by his vice-chairman
Gabriel Mukele on Monday.
Others to be questioned include Ikolomani MP Bonny Khalwale, whose car
was burnt to a shell in Kakamega in the same week, Government chief whip
Norman Nyagah who is the chief agent for the Banana Yes-vote group and
former minister Isaac Ruto, chief agent of the Orange No-vote group.
Along with them will be the Kisumu police boss Stanley Kilonzi and
Kakamega police chief Charles Mumanthi.
"Even with our little authority, we will deal with you and Kenyans will
know what we have done," declared Mr Kivuitu.

Original_Search_Title: Orange Leaders to Battle Each Other for Votes Harvest Trophy
Headline: Kenya: Orange Leaders to Battle Each Other for Votes Harvest Trophy
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Orange team leaders have launched a competition among themselves in the
run-up to the November 21 referendum.
Ministers Raila Odinga and Kalonzo Musyoka and Kanu officials Uhuru
Kenyatta and William Ruto will vie for the National Orange Trophy to be
taken home by the politician who garners the highest number of votes.
Mr Odinga and Mr Musyoka also announced that they would give a trophy to
the constituency with the highest number of No votes in their respective
Nyanza and Ukambani regions.
In large numbers
The two, who were addressing rallies in Kangundo and Tala markets, asked
voters to turn up in large numbers on polling day.
"They are using violence to intimidate us, but we shall not be
cowed. You should not agree to sell your right to vote by succumbing to
their threats," said Roads minister Odinga.
Mr Musyoka and Mr Odinga dared Mr Michuki to make good his threat to
charge them in court with treason over their coup utterances. "We cannot be
threatened. Using force will not work because we threw out the colonialist
and Mr Michuki cannot manage us," Mr Odinga said.
The rallies heard that some people in the Banana camp were being bribed
to discredit those in the Orange camp.
Mr Musyoka said MPs were being given between Sh1 million and Sh2 million
under a scheme hatched by Yes camp leaders.
"Some of these MPs have suddenly become very rich and their sources of
wealth are questionable," he told the Nation.
Mr Musyoka, who addressed a series of rallies before being joined by Mr
Odinga and Mr Ruto in Kagundo Town, described the proposed Constitution as
"the devil's advocate". He said: "Voting for it is to kill democracy."
Out to grab
He said those who vote for it would be committing suicide because the
document "will be impossible to amend".
The Environment minister denied claims that the No camp was out to grab
power through the backdoor.
"Mr Kibaki is still the President and will be the President until fresh
elections are held," he said. Earlier, while addressing Central Province
Orange agents in Thika, the minister asked President Kibaki to step down
from the presidential race in the 2007 General Election.
Not seek second term
"President Kibaki promised us under the MoU pact that he would not seek
a second term and I still believe he will keep the promise," the minister
said.
He described the Yes camp leaders as old guards in their sunset years
who were not keen to pave way for the youth who, he said, held the key to
the country's future.
Mr Kalonzo also criticised the Government's plans to create two
districts in Ukambani, Kibwezi and Yatta.
"They want to use the districts as bait and you should not accept to
play into their hands," he said.
Mr Ruto criticised those claiming that Mr Musyoka was being used by
others interested in the top leadership.
Additional reporting by Victor Nzuma.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki Dishes Out Districts And Varsity
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki Dishes Out Districts And Varsity
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki yesterday promised the larger Kisii region two more districts and a university.
The President said Egerton University's Kisii Campus would be
upgraded into a full university once adequate land and facilities were
found.
Addressing a delegation of Gusii leaders led by Energy minister
Simeon Nyachae at State House, Nairobi, Kibaki said that an area with
more than 400,000 people needed a district of its own.
Nyachae's was the sixth delegation the President was receiving at
State House during the ongoing campaigns for the November 21 referendum.
"It is very important. We do it everywhere," he said in reference to the creation of new districts.
At present, Kisii region has three districts - Kisii Central, Gucha and Nyamira.
Kibaki said there were currently 71 districts in the country and more would be created based on population.
At the same time, the Head of State told leaders campaigning against
the Proposed New Constitution to own up and tell Kenyans the truth
concerning the process that culminated in the Wako Draft.
Kibaki said the Orange leaders, who oppose the draft, were being dishonest in their campaigns.
"They should own up and say Nilipitisha lakini ilikuwa na makosa (I
passed it but it had some mistakes) and we will still listen to you,"
he said.
Kibaki said some of the No leaders took part in the Naivasha meeting
where contentious issues on the review were thrashed out and wondered
why they were now against the Wako Draft.
"One of them was the secretary (in apparent reference to Mr William
Ruto, the then chairman of the Parliamentary Select Committee on the
review) and now they think we don't read. We read it even in the
papers. Don't say you were not in Naivasha," said Kibaki.
The delegation of MPs, councillors and grassroots leaders from Gusii
presented a memorandum to the President listing their requests.
In the memo, read by Mr Kennedy Riogi, the delegates asked for - in
addition to two new districts and a full university - for more
allowances and gratuity for councillors.
Kibaki said the Government would consider upgrading the Kisii campus, but asked the leaders to find land for its expansion.
"We will talk to Egerton and do our part. You go and do your part," he said.
However, on the matter of increased allowances for councillors, the
President said the Ministry of Local Government would have to be
consulted, noting that the civic leaders recently received an
increment. He said their request would be considered alongside all
other councillors in the country.
Last month, Local Government minister Musikari Kombo announced a
salary increment for all civic leaders. However, many councils are yet
to begin paying the new emoluments.
The delegation assured the Head of State of their support for the proposed constitution.
While thanking them for their stand, Kibaki urged the delegation to remain firm and vote Yes during the November 21 referendum.
He said the constitution was not an issue to discuss for a long time, saying Kenyans had been on it for the past 14 years.
Nyachae said majority of the people in Kisii backed the Banana
group. "There are, however, some elements who may not be with us".
Nyachae, who is the chairman of the PSC on the review, said he
understood the tradition and culture of Kenyan communities and that he
could not have deviated from this.
He said the Abagusii were fully supporting Government for the sake of development and would therefore not fight the system.
In the delegation were MPs Henry Obwocha, Jimmy Angwenyi, Joel
Onyancha, Geoffrey Masanya, Stephen Manoti, Hezron Manduku, Mwancha
Okioma and Zebedeo Opore.
Others were Internal Security minister John Michuki, Head of Civil
Service Francis Muthaura and Nyanza Provincial Commissioner Hassan Noor
Hassan.
South Mugirango Ford-People MP Omingo Magara was not present at the meeting as he supports the Orange camp.

Original_Search_Title: ECK Summons Tuju As MPs Debate Kisumu Riots
Headline: Kenya: ECK Summons Tuju As MPs Debate Kisumu Riots
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A heated debate on campaign rioting took place in Parliament yesterday,
as the Electoral Commission summoned Information minister Raphael Tuju and
five others for questioning about the violence.
It also emerged that police are investigating the role played by two
former Air Force officers in the Kisumu rioting which left four people dead,
including the 14-year-old son of a police inspector, and 30 others with
gunshot wounds.
The investigation was revealed by assistant Internal Security minister
Mirugi Kariuki, as MPs suspended normal business to debate the causes of the
chaos which erupted outside Kisumu stadium during a Yes-vote rally hosted by
Mr Tuju, and earlier at Kakamega.
Mr Mirugi, who defended the police action in Kisumu in opening fire with
live ammunition, said the two former Air Force officers had been identified
as ringleaders of the chaos, and were now on the run.
He also revealed police were investigating reports that a senior
politician had spent last Friday night in Kisumu to orchestrate the
violence, before flying out of town in a helicopter on the Saturday
morning.
In addition to Mr Tuju, the ECK will on Monday also question Ikolomani
MP Bonny Khalwale, whose car was burnt to a shell in Kakamega, Government
chief whip Norman Nyagah who is the chief agent for the Banana Yes-vote
group and former minister William Ruto, chief agent of the Orange No-vote
group.
Also summoned by the ECK was Kisumu police boss Stanley Kilonzi and
Kakamega police chief Charles Mumanthi.
Questioning the six will be a committee headed by ECK vice-chairman
Gabriel Mukele.
"Even with our little authority, we will deal with you and Kenyans will
know what we have done," declared ECK chairman Samuel Kivuitu who vowed to
crack down on violence in the lead up to the November 21 referendum.
In Parliament, Kisumu Town East MP Gor Sunguh who initiated the debate
wiped away tears with a white handkerchief as he told of the violence that
unfolded around Kisumu stadium.
During the debate, Dr Khalwale was expelled for saying all Kenya's
problems could be traced to the families of three men: former Presidents
Jomo Kenyatta and Daniel arap Moi, and first Vice-President Jaramogi Oginga
Odinga.
Dr Khalwale's remarks sparked protests from MPs who demanded that he
withdrew the remark. He refused to do so.
MPs on both sides condemned violence and spoke, without naming them, of
people they claimed were out to organise chaos.
The leader of the Official Opposition, Mr Uhuru Kenyatta, who supports
the Orange camp, said police were not justified in using live
ammunition.
Mr Sunguh recalled that local leaders had warned of impending chaos and
suggested the Kisumu rally be cancelled.
"What I saw there was a scene from hell. Are the people of Kisumu the
same Kenyans who voted for President Kibaki?" he asked. "Did the rally mean
Kisumu was under curfew? People were attacked at Kondele, Kajulu, Manyatta,
and Nyamasaria estates. What wrong did they do?"
Mr Sunguh had initiated the debate by proposing a motion of adjournment
so MPs could discuss the rioting which he said was a matter of national
importance.
Seconding him, Mandera Central's Kanu MP Billow Kerrow said it was the
Government's duty to protect human rights. He accused Parliament of not
addressing public concerns.
"MPs have failed to make an impact on the Government. And the
consequence of exclusion and powerlessness is discontent and violence," he
said.
The MP went on: "Police brutality has always been built around orders
from above. Whereas the Government ordered the police not to use live
bullets to disperse riotous mobs in 1996, the practice is still on."
Mr Tuju said he had no apologies to make for his rally, and instead
blamed the violence on his political detractors.
The weekend riots were part of a scheme of violence that could be traced
to a history of political violence.
"We cancelled an earlier Yes rally and we were warned not to step in
Kisumu. It was part of a wider scheme to intimidate me not to go to Kisumu,"
he said.
The development by the ECK was disclosed after a three-hour meeting
between the commission and leaders of the Yes and No teams held at County
Hall, Nairobi.
Energy minister Simeon Nyachae led the Banana group who included Cabinet
ministers Musikari Kombo, Mohamed Abdi, John Koech, Martha Karua, plus
assistant minister Moses Wetang'ula, and MPs Nyagah, Ms Njoki Ndung'u and
G. G. Kariuki, among others.
The No team was represented by Environment minister Kalonzo Musyoka,
plus minister Linah Kilimo, and MPs Mutula Kilonzo, Joseph Kamotho, Joe
Khamisi and Justin Muturi, among others.
When they appear before the ECK, Mr Tuju and Mr Kilonzi will be required
to explain the violence.
Dr Khalwale and Mr Mumanthi are to be questioned over riots in Kakamega
in which the MP's car was burned and several people injured.
Mr Nyagah and Mr Ruto - as chief agents responsible for keeping order -
are expected to explain what they have done and what they intend to do about
violence.
The ECK code of conduct enables the commission to warn, enforce
regulations, impose fines or stop any side from campaigning if they engage
in election malpractices.
The commission set a precedent during the 2002 General Election when it
fined two Parliamentary candidates up to Sh150,000 when they were
reprimanded.
Yesterday, leaders from both teams traded accusations over the causes of
the violence.
Mr Kilonzo accused the Banana team of fanning violence in Western
Province and was supported by Mr Musyoka who claimed the Yes team was behind
the violence meted to Orange leaders at Makutano in Meru and Chwele,
Bungoma.
"We were met with machetes in Chwele and (Planning minister) Nyong'o was
nearly hit with a stone in Meru. We should not pretend that violence was
only in Kisumu," he said.
The Banana team's Mr Wetang'ula claimed the No team had hired people
with a history of causing chaos since 1992.
Mr Kombo and Mr Nyachae accused Orange team leaders of being behind the
violence and told Mr Kivuitu to take action against them.
Mr Kombo urged the commission to go beyond the youths and charge the
"real" perpetrators of chaos.
The Yes team was also accused of using State resources during their
campaigns, contrary to the campaign regulations. Mr Kilonzo and Mr Musyoka
cited use of helicopters, sub-chiefs, chiefs and district commissioners by
the Banana team.
But Mr Nyachae said the helicopters were hired by Yes team members and
challenged their rivals to do the same.
He said he had used a helicopter to fly to Kuria and Meru paying
Sh87,600 an hour.
Handing out relief food in Ukambani, the creation of new districts,
State House visits, the transfer of civil servants and intimidation of them
by the Banana team were also condemned.
Mr Kivuitu warned against use of State resources and criticised the
Government for distributing food in Ukambani on the days the No team was
holding rallies.

Original_Search_Title: I Have No Apologies Over Rally, Says Tuju
Headline: Kenya: I Have No Apologies Over Rally, Says Tuju
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Information minister Raphael Tuju told Parliament that he had no
apologies to make for his controversial Yes campaign rally in Kisumu on
Saturday.
He blamed his political detractors for the violence that engulfed the
town and in which four people died.
The minister said the riots were part of a long-running scheme by people
he did not name.
Mr Tuju sent a message of condolences to the families of the people
killed.
But he defended his decision to organise the rally, saying he had given
the police notice after the first one - on October 21 - was postponed for
fear of violence.
Heckled and accused
But some MPs heckled and accused him of shedding crocodile tears.
Speaker Francis ole Kaparo intervened and warned that they would be
kicked out the House for disorder.
Said Mr Kaparo: "For a long time, you have not had a meaningful
debate. But I will congratulate you today on the high standards of debate."
In his five-minute contribution, Mr Tuju said political violence in the
country could be "traced to the same source".
Mr Gor Sunguh (Kisumu Town East, Narc) stood on a point of order, but
the Speaker said he would not allow debate on the Kisumu rally.
The Information minister gave a chronology of events leading to the
weekend riots. He said he met his supporters on October 13 in Kisumu, where
he announced October 21 as the date for the Banana rally.
But he was surprised to learn that the Orange team had applied to hold a
parallel rally.

Original_Search_Title: 54 Diplomats Criticise Use of Force By Police
Headline: Kenya: 54 Diplomats Criticise Use of Force By Police
Body_Text:
Nairobi — More than 54 envoys yesterday criticised the use of excessive
force by the police during protests that marred a Banana rally in
Kisumu at the weekend.
The envoys, led by US Ambassador William Bellamy and his British counterpart Adam Woods, blamed the violence on the Government.
They were speaking when they met Foreign Affairs minister Ali Mwakwere and Mr Raphael Tuju, the Information minister.
The meeting at Kenyatta International Conference Centre was aimed at restoring donor confidence in the Government.
The envoys demanded that the Government lives up to its promise to ensure that referendum campaigns were peaceful.
"We want to know how you are going to communicate to the people to stop the violence," said Woods.
"We would like also to have the Banana campaign rallies schedule," said Bellamy.
Tuju said of the violence in Kisumu: " I almost lost my life. My
driver avoided a petrol bomb in Kisumu targeted at me and my wife, who
was with me in the car. You could be sending condolences now".
Tuju dismissed claims by some politicians that the Government felt
Britain and America, were funding the Orange camp. He said those were
personal statements by the politicians.
"We have appreciated the Government's explanation that the alleged foreign funding is baseless," said Bellamy.
Before the envoys put the Government side on the spot, Tuju had
tried to explain the history of violence in Kenya dating back to 1982
coup attempt.
In an apparent move to clear the Banana team of blame, Tuju said:
"Players on our side change but our opponents remain the same".
"At one time the heat was directed towards the late Vice President Michael Wamalwa, but now it is Tuju," said the minister.
Tuju cited violence in Lugari by-elections where Politician Alfred
Sambu lost his son, Ndhiwa and Kipipiri by-elections, and vigilante
groups like Mungiki and Taliban.
The envoys told the Government that it was wrong for the police to use force on innocent people.
The other envoys were from countries including Australia, Finland, Germany, Canada .
The briefing was attended by the Constitution of Kenya Review
Commission chairperson Abida Ali Aroni and a Commissioner with the
Electoral Commission of Kenya Jack Tumwa.
Tuju said he would inform the envoys of the Banana camp referendum schedule after communicating with the Yes secretariat.
The ambassadors had demanded the "Yes" rallies schedules to monitor the campaigns.

Original_Search_Title: Electoral Commission Summons Minister Over Chaos
Headline: Kenya: ECK Summons Minister Over Chaos
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Electoral Commission of Kenya has summoned Information
minister Raphael Tuju and a senior police officer over Saturday's
violence in Kisumu, in which four people died.
They are expected to report at the ECK offices on Monday next week,
together with the leadership of both the Orange and Banana national
referendum committees.
Sources said the Banana team's chief agent, Norman Nyaga and his
Orange camp counterpart, Isaac Ruto, would represent both sides.
The ECK has also summoned Ikolomani Member of Parliament Bonny
Khalwale and another senior police officer over the recent violence in
Kakamega during which the legislator's car was burnt by a mob.
The ECK issued the summons as envoys from 54 nations reprimanded the
Government over the use of live bullets in Saturday's violence.
The envoys, led by Mr William Bellamy (USA) and Adam Woods (UK),
expressed concern over the widespread violence in the referendum.
The police officers summoned by ECK are Mr Stanley Kilonzi of Kisumu
and Mr Patrick Safari of Kakamega, both officers commanding police
divisions.
Nyaga received his summoning letter at County Hall yesterday morning.
Sources at ECK said police officers from Wajir and some leaders suspected to have fanned violence would also be summoned.
It was, however, not clear if this group would also be appearing
before the disciplinary committee led by ECK vice-chairman, Gabriel
Mukele, on Monday.
Kivuitu yesterday held a meeting with representatives of both the
Banana and Orange camps at which he announced the creation of the
committee.
He asked politicians to stop zoning off areas: "Tuju should be
allowed to hold rallies at any place in this country without being
threatened by anybody," he said.
But he demanded to know what 2,000 police officers were doing in
Kisumu on the eventful day. "I read about the 2,000 police officers and
asked myself, "was it a civil war?"
He called on police officers to treat Kenyans with humility, just
like other members of their families. "They have wives, husbands and
children who are not police officers and they should therefore not use
excessive force on other people," said Kivuitu.
Those present from the Banana camp were Cabinet ministers Simeon
Nyachae, Musikari Kombo and Newton Kulundu, Nyaga, assistant minister
Moses Wetang'ula and DP deputy secretary-general George Nyamweya.
Those from the Orange team included Cabinet ministers Kalonzo
Musyoka and Linah Kilimo, MPs Joseph Kamotho, Mutula Kilonzo and Justin
Mturi and Ruto.
Kivuitu said those who have perpetrated violence at campaign meetings must explain their involvement.
"We have summoned those leaders who were involved in the Kisumu and
Kakamega violence to come and explain what happened," he said.
During the meeting, leaders from the two camps traded accusations over the campaign violence.
Speaking for the Banana team, Nyachae, Kombo, Nyaga and Wetang'ula
laid the blame on the Orange leaders, who they accused of inciting
their supporters.
But Kalonzo, Mutula, Kamotho and Mturi countered that Government agents had unleashed terror on their supporters.
Nyachae defended the Government on the use of police helicopters for the campaigns.
He said ministers paid for the use of the aircraft at market rates.
"I'm asking the ECK to be prepared for all kinds of complaints
because these people never verify facts before opening their mouths,"
said Nyachae.
He challenged the Orange team to visit his office and verify two
US$1,200 vouchers issued when he hired the police and Kenya Pipeline
helicopters recently.
But when he said the Orange leaders had even hired his (Nyachae's)
personal helicopter, Kalonzo insisted that this request had been turned
down on several occasions.
Nyachae also denied Kalonzo's claim that the Government was dishing
out relief food at venues where the Orange team was holding rallies.
The Orange team had demanded to know why Government ministers were personally involved in the exercise.
Nyachae said he started distributing food with the late President
Mzee Jomo Kenyatta in Samburu when he was Rift Valley provincial
commissioner.
Kalonzo asked Kivuitu to ensure that the Kenya Broadcasting Corporation gave equal and quality coverage to both teams.
He also asked the commission to provide the teams with registers of
all polling stations to enable them to prepare a data bank for their
agents.
Kombo denied accusations that nominations for civic leaders from Karatina and Murang'a had been revoked.
"I have spoken with my friend Kamotho over this matter and he knows
that one civic leader died in Karatina while the other breached the law
by taking his local authority to court," said Kombo.
Kivuitu said distribution of relief food at campaign venues was wrong.
The Orange team asked for ECK intervention to stop intimidation,
including the transfer of public servants who support the No team.
Kalonzo also demanded a stop to the creation of new districts as a gift in exchange of votes.
Both groups, however, agreed that the Government should pay and train two agents for each polling station.
They agreed to tone down the language used during their campaigns.
Nyachae said vulgar and inciting language could easily provoke supporters of some MPs into violence.
"Please let us not use language that will incite people because this
country must remain united after November 22," said Nyachae.
Kalonzo then said that Nyachae had recently said that some leaders were not circumcised.
But the Nyaribari Chache MP quickly jumped to his defence, saying he
was referring to circumcision songs the Orange team sung when they
visited Gucha recently.
"I was just inquiring why they were singing songs sang for circumcised initiates when there were none at Gucha," said Nyachae.
And Parliament yesterday suspended its normal business to discuss
the Kisumu violence. MPs condemned the violence, but both the Orange
and Banana groups blamed each other for the mayhem.

Original_Search_Title: Leaders Plan Parallel Rallies in Kisii Again
Headline: Kenya: Leaders Plan Parallel Rallies in Kisii Again
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Anxiety and political tension has gripped Kisii region ahead of
tomorrow's twin parallel referendum rallies to be addressed by Raila
Odinga and Simeon Nyachae.
While the Orange team led by Raila will be staging its final rally
in the larger Kisii at Gusii Stadium, which falls in Nyachae's
Nyaribari Chache constituency, the Energy minister will be seeking
votes from the Nyamira District residents, about 15 kilometres away.
Also expected at the Nyamira rally will be several Cabinet ministers led by Vice-President Moody Awori.
Chief organisers of the two camps, Assistant minister Henry Obwocha
(Banana) and former Cabinet minister Sam Ongeri (Orange), pleaded for
peaceful campaigns.
They both denied they had deliberately organised the rallies with intentions to sabotage each other's meetings.
"We applied ahead of the Orange team. In any case ours will be in
Nyamira, many kilometres from Kisii town. If their meeting fails that
will be up to them," said Obwocha, who will be the host MP.
On his part Ongeri accused the Banana team of fixing the Nyamira
rally to coincide with the Orange one in Kisii with ulterior motives.
"Their aim is to take away some of the Kisii residents who would have wished to attend our meeting.
At the same time, the Kisii OCPD yesterday issued a stern warning to
Kisii politicians and their supporters to "keep off the meetings if you
do not want to listen to your rival".
At a news conference in his office, Donasian Njagi confirmed that
police officers had been brought in from other stations to ensure there
was law and order during the two rallies.
This is the second time that the Orange and Banana teams are holding parallel rallies in Gusiiland within a month.
Three weeks ago both Nyachae and Raila led their teams to Ogembo and Gusii stadiums respectively.
Meanwhile, Nyachae will today lead a delegation of Gusii leaders to
meet President Kibaki at State House, Nakuru, to present, among others
issues, the community's demands ahead of the November 21 referendum.
In the delegation are local MPs allied to the Banana camp, including
Obwocha, Parliamentary Consensus Committee chairman Jimmy Angwenyi and
a host of councillors drawn from the 15 civic bodies in the region.
The meeting was brokered by Nyachae following a request by the
councillors when they paid him a courtesy call at his Nyosia home last
Friday.
Nyamira County Council chairman, Councillor Alfayo Ngereza, who was
yesterday finalising travel arrangements for his colleagues, said they
would demand for two more new districts.
The delegation will also push for elevation of Egerton University's Kisii Campus to a fully-fledged university status.

Original_Search_Title: You Are the Boss, Not Lucy, Raila Tells Kibaki
Headline: Kenya: You Are the Boss, Not Lucy, Raila Tells Kibaki
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki should come out and talk directly about the violence
that rocked Kisumu last Saturday, Roads minister Raila Odinga has said.
Mr Odinga said the Head of State should condemn the violence and condole
with the bereaved families instead of leaving it to the First Lady.
Mr Odinga said: "The time has come for the President to talk to the
nation directly. Four people have been killed and many others injured."
He added: "At least a Head of State and Government has to come out and
condole with the families who have lost their loved ones instead of allowing
people to provoke the nation at a time we are mourning the loss of innocent
lives."
Mr Odinga said it was wrong for those who were killed and injured to be
referred to as hooligans as some politicians and individuals were
doing.
He said remarks attributed to Mrs Lucy Kibaki about the violence were
"unfortunate and heartless".
The minister asked Mrs Kibaki to stop meddling in politics and
concentrate on her role as the First Lady.
The Langata MP said: "I talk to the President as a minister appointed by
him. The First Lady should confine her roles to the ones which are basically
hers."
He added: "I would expect her as a mother to have been on her knees
weeping at the loss of life of an innocent child," the minister added.
But in response, State House absolved President Kibaki from blame with
Presidential Press Service head Isaiyah Kabira saying the Head of State had
condemned the violence and called for peaceful campaigns in the remaining
period.
Mr Kabira said: "He has already condemned it, and in fact almost his
entire speech on Kenyatta Day dwelt much on peaceful campaigns."
He would, however, not comment on the First Lady.
Mr Odinga spoke as Vice-President Moody Awori renewed his call to
Kenyans to exercise sobriety and restraint, as the referendum date draws
closer.
He appealed to politicians to conduct mature campaigns and caution their
supporters against acts or utterances that could threaten peace.
On Saturday, police shot and killed three people during a Yes rally
convened by Information minister Raphael Tuju at Kisumu's Moi Stadium. The
fourth victim died in hospital the following day.
Among the casualties was 13-year-old pupil Paul Limera, who was shot
dead by a stray bullet as he left school.
Commenting on the violence, the First Lady accused some politicians of
inciting youths to engage in lawlessness resulting in the loss of lives and
destruction of property.
"I urge Kenyans not to listen to these dishonest people who have decided
to use all evil means to oppose the Constitution in order to discredit the
Government which they claim to serve," Mrs Kibaki said at a rally in Nyeri
Town.
But speaking at the Orange headquarters in Nairobi, after receiving
Isiolo South MP Abdul Bahari Ali who led a group of councillors and Muslim
leaders in joining the No team, Mr Odinga condemned individuals within and
outside the Government who were making "unfortunate and heartless" remarks
about the violence.
The minister was accompanied by leader of Official Opposition Uhuru
Kenyatta, Cabinet minister Najib Balala, Kanu secretary general William Ruto
and MPs Mutula Kilonzo, Bonaya Godana, Charles Keter, Ali Wario and Omingo
Magara.
His remarks followed his call to Police Commissioner Hussein Ali to
resign and return to the military following the Kisumu deaths.
He spoke moments before the team flew to Homa Bay to canvass for votes
against the proposed Constitution in the November 21 referendum.
They went to South Nyanza as Internal Security minister John Michuki led
the Banana team to rally support for the new Constitution in Kibwezi where
he announced the creation of a new district with the same name.
Mr Michuki dismissed those calling for the removal of Maj Gen Ali,
saying the police boss would continue to be in charge at Vigilance
House.
Receiving the Isiolo MP, Mr Odinga took issue with church leaders for
failing to guide their flock on how to vote on the Constitution.
However, the Mr Odinga and Mr Kenyatta praised Muslim leaders for
guiding their followers.
The Isiolo South MP said the Constitution was about the people and their
future his constituents had decided not to support the new law.
"I formally announce my support and commitment to join the Orange team
in conformity and furtherance of the wishes of my people," the MP said.
Meanwhile, a fund-raising dinner to help the Orange team in the
campaigns has been postponed from today to Sunday.
The organiser, Mr Badi Ali, said the venue had also been changed from
Hotel Intercontinental to Visions Plaza on Mombasa Road.
Elsewhere, the Electoral Commission announced it would meet in Nairobi
today with leaders from the Yes and No camps.

Original_Search_Title: Police Chief in the Spotlight After Four Die of Bullet Wounds
Headline: Kenya: Police Chief in the Spotlight After Four Die of Bullet Wounds
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Police are in the spotlight once again for the manner in which they
handled the recent referendum protests in Kisumu.
What started as a mere protest by youths opposed to Cabinet minister
Raphael Tuju hosting a Yes campaign rally in the town ended tragically with
the death of four people.
According to reports, three people were shot dead on the same day -
among them a 13-year-old schoolboy Paul Limera - while a fourth victim died
the following day after being admitted to Nyanza provincial hospital with
gunshot wounds.
Leaders and residents from the region believe the police were brutal and
used excessive force against the protesters. This view was shared by leaders
of the Orange team which is opposed to the proposed Constitution.
And for the first time since he was appointed police commissioner 14
months ago Maj Gen Mohamed Hussein Ali is being asked to resign or go back
to the military because of the way his officers handled the Kisumu
riots.
One MP went further and threatened that he would push for the arresting
and prosecution of Maj Gen Ali if the Orange Movement, fronted by Kanu and
the LDP, comes to power one day.
Roads minister Raila Odinga, who is spearheading an onslaught against
the police chief, blamed the police action on Maj-Gen Ali's training which
he said was improper for the police force.
Although violence has characterised other rallies, Mr Odinga said, "we
have not witnessed the horrendous brutality unleashed in Kisumu on
Saturday."
Civil society organisations have said the police used excessive
force.
They called on President Kibaki to instruct the police to avoid using
live bullets when dealing with unruly crowds and said the Government was
liable for the deaths and injuries.
In defending the police, Maj Gen Ali said: "The disturbances were not
spontaneous acts, but deliberate deeds of hooliganism by locals to prevent
the Banana rally."
He claimed the schoolboy was hit by a stray bullet and an inquest would
be established to determine the circumstances which led to his death.
Maj Gen Ali said the shooting at Kondele Police Station was justified
because the mob had attempted to rescue a suspect who was being held there
after his arrest earlier in the day.
He said the shooting occurred long after the referendum rally was
over. The police chief said: "The law provides for use of lethal force when
people try to rescue a person being held in custody or to prevent a suspect
from being arrested."
Police used tear gas and batons to disperse people who attempted to stop
Mr Tuju's rally, he said.
Maj Gen Ali praised his officers for using non-lethal measures to
counter groups who threw stones and lit bonfires on major roads in the
town.
He said one of the deaths was not as a result of the shootings.
Unlike in the Western world, most encounters between the police and
protesters in Kenya end in bloodshed or even in death.
In the Kisumu incident, the police, the protesters and politicians came
up with their own versions of what happened.
Blood has smeared Kenyan streets many times before. There have been
deaths reported during university students' riots, the campaign for
multi-partyism and the push for constitutional change.
This is not the first time that the actions of the police have come
under sharp focus. In 1996, former President Moi moved fast to sack police
commissioner Shedrach Kiruki over a furore sparked by the killing of three
university students in two days.
He was sacked because his officers shot dead Mr Festus Okong'o Etaba, an
unarmed first-year student at Egerton University, during a student
demonstration on December 17, 1996. The students were demanding a partial
refund of fees they claimed they were owed by the university.
The following day, police shot dead Kenneth Makokha Mutabi and Eric
Kamundi, both unarmed, who were among a group of students at Kenyatta
University who had gathered peacefully to mourn the death of Mr Etaba and to
protest the use of lethal force by police against student protesters.
After the deaths of his two students, the then Kenyatta University
Vice-Chancellor, Prof George Eshiwani, said he had not called in the police
and that the police had ignored his plea that they leave the campus.
By comparing the Kisumu and Kenyatta University incidents to the protest
which was staged in July against US President George W. Bush and other Group
of Eight (G8) leaders in Scotland, one can say they are, so to speak, worlds
apart.
Masses of pro-debt relief protesters - estimated to be 250,000 -
descended on the meeting venue in Edinburgh for the Make Poverty History
demo and police were able to contain them without ugly incidents.
The numbers assembled were so huge that people queued for hours to be
part of the enormous demonstration winding a circle round the Edinburgh city
centre.
When perplexed marchers asked why they were refused access to one of the
main routes back to the train station, masked police only raised their
shields without warning and charged at them, scattering protesters.
Unlike in Kenya, no stones or other missiles were hurled at the police
and no one was clobbered. Yet some of the protesters in Edinburgh complained
that the police had over-reacted.
The way the police handled the Kisumu riots begs a number of questions:
-Is Maj Gen Ali squarely to blame for the killings?
-Are the police not adequately trained on riot and crowd control?
-Are the police trigger-happy when dealing with hostile crowds?
- Is the police reaction due to the hostility protesters harbour towards
State agents?
- Should the police continue to be answerable to partisan politicians or
be made independent?
From the way the police handle rioters, it is evident they need to be
adequately trained on tactical methods of dealing with different riot
situations that arise during civil unrest.
The use of live ammunition should be as a last resort because police
have riot shields and helmets to shield themselves against missiles hurled
by hostile crowds.
Unlike protesters, the police are trained on riot-control techniques and
have equipment to their advantage.
Every police officer who has passed through the Kenya Police Training
College at Kiganjo has taken lessons on riot control. But few practice what
they were taught.
At Kiganjo, the officers are taught how to use the many modern riot gear
at their disposal.
They are required to use the weapons for the sole purpose of dispersing
or calming down a hostile crowd and can only use live bullets as a last
resort.
The light weapons include foggers, batons special riot weapons, helmets,
non-lethal weaponry like tear gas, gas masks, plastic shields and armoured
vehicles.
The decision to use a certain type of weapon against protesters is based
on the perceived level of threat and the existing laws.
Police using armoured vehicles and water cannons can subdue rioters and
subsequently allow the less heavily armoured, more mobile officers to arrest
those who are unruly.
In the face of a greater threat, the riot police will be backed by other
officers equipped with firearms designed to disperse rioters rather than to
inflict serious injury or death; riot guns to fire tear gas and rubber
bullets designed for crowd control.
The most modern water cannon versions are capable of adding a usually
soluble substance for staining or colouring to mark rioters or adding tear
gas to the water.
Following the Kenyatta University killings, 12 police officers were
charged with the murder of the two students but released a year later for
lack of evidence.
While sacking Mr Kiruki, Mr Moi ordered the police to stop using live
ammunition but rather use rubber bullets.
Kenya's post-independence police commissioners sometimes have trouble
making independent decisions.
Kanu often used the police as party youthwingers to violently crack down
on those opposed to its rule.
At the time, Cabinet ministers were never very vocal about police heavy
handedness.
Among those who were on the receiving end at the time were people like
Mr Kiraitu Murungi, Mr Mirugi Kariuki and Mr Maina Kamanda, who are now
serving in the Government.
While the police were busy man handling the Opposition leaders (most of
whom are now serving in the Kibaki administration) of the time, cronies of
Moi seemed to enjoy it and never protested.
Demands by Mr Murungi and company to overhaul the police force and
delink it from politics fell on deaf ears.
When Mr Kibaki took over power from Mr Moi, he inherited the police
force with the same officers who used to brutalise and oppress them.
Now that his ministers are in a position of power to reform the police
force, minimal reforms have been carried out. Ironically, the same police
officers who used to violently break Mr Murungi's rallies and spy on him are
the same ones being used by the Narc administration to do its dirty
work. And that's the tragedy.

Original_Search_Title: We've No Role in Campaigns, Says America
Headline: Kenya: We've No Role in Campaigns, Says America
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The US government yesterday described as untrue and irresponsible claims
that it was partisan in Kenya's referendum campaigns.
Ambassador William Bellamy said his government respected Kenyans'
freedom to vote for or against the proposed constitution and denied it was
funding those opposed to the document.
The results would not affect US interests in Kenya, he said, adding: "We
are interested only in a peaceful, free and fair vote."
Mr Bellamy was speaking at Embu as he presented milk coolers worth about
Sh800,000 to the Dairy Co-operatives Society. The equipment has been bought
through the envoy's Self-Help Fund.
He said the travel ban to US on Cabinet minister Chris Murungaru had
nothing to do with the referendum, as alleged by some politicians.
Some Banana government officials have accused the American and British
governments of funding the Orange camp, which is against the proposals.
The envoy denied discussing funding with any camp, saying: "Even if they
asked us to fund them, we would not do so."
He defended his decision to host Orange campaigners in his house
recently, saying it was perfectly in line with his diplomatic duties.
He would soon meet with a Banana team, he said and added: "As diplomats,
we are free to meet any group and hear their views. It is part of our
diplomatic mission."

Original_Search_Title: No End to Raila-Tuju Battle
Headline: Kenya: No End to Raila-Tuju Battle
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Two Cabinet ministers have taken their war of words a notch higher -
despite saying they were not interested in fighting each other.
Roads minister Raila Odinga yesterday maintained that his colleague, Mr
Raphael Tuju would not have made it to Parliament without his support.
He also dismissed claims by the Information and Communication minister
that some MPs from the Luo community bribed the Odinga family to get to
Parliament.
"He is like my son and he cannot claim to have gone to Parliament on his
own," Mr Odinga told Orange rallies in Ndhiwa and Homa Bay towns.
On his part, Mr Tuju called a Press conference in Nairobi where he
blamed the state of Luo politics on the Odinga family.
He said that violence against people opposed to Mr Odinga and high
levels of irregularities at the nomination stages were part of the politics
in the region in the run-up to the last General Election.
He said: "There is a lot I shall say about politics in Luo Nyanza. It is
characterised by violence that is unleashed to terrorise anyone who is
perceived to be working against the Odinga dynasty. It is tainted with high
levels of corruption and rigging at the nomination stage.
But Mr Odinga, who spoke from Homa Bay, told wananchi from Nyanza
Province to allow Mr Tuju "to continue addressing empty stadiums" because he
had no support in the area.
Mr Odinga was quoted on Monday as saying that he helped Mr Tuju make it
to Parliament even after he had lost the Narc nomination in Rarieda.
But Mr Tuju said that he won the preliminary Narc nominations which gave
him the ticket to vie for the Rarieda seat.
Yesterday, he said he ran the "most sophisticated" nomination
campaign.
"Raila wants to claim that he assisted me. He believes in it and if he
believes in something so much and you talk about it, you will be stoned," he
said.
He added: "He came in a chopper one morning and campaigned for me like
he campaigned for others and President Kibaki, but he cannot claim the
credit."
The exchanges between the two Cabinet ministers highlighted the rifts
that have emerged among politicians ahead of the November 21 referendum on
the proposed new Constitution.
Mr Odinga is in the Orange team while Mr Tuju is in the Banana
group.
Mr Tuju said it was not his political agenda to fight Mr Odinga but that
he would not allow himself to be intimidated. And he put questions to the
Lang'ata MP which, he said, could bring to the fore secrets which were
played out during the last elections. He listed them as follows:
-Why did Mr Odinga favour his candidature over Mr Owiti as he claims?
-How many of the current Luo MPs are Mr Odinga's blood relatives like Mr
Oburu Odinga who is his brother and Mr Jakoyo Midiwo his first cousin? How
many are related to him by marriage?
-What happened to the following candidates who had been issued with
valid Narc nomination certificates?: Mr George Ochola (Uriri) and Mr Otieno
Kopiyo (Kasipul Kabondo)
-What were the considerations for receiving a certificate for those who
were not blood relatives?
-What are the conditions for receiving a certificate for 2007?
"If he is man enough, let him come out and answer the questions and I
will let out the whole truth," Mr Tuju said and promised to give more
details in a statement he was preparing to read in Parliament this
afternoon.
Former MP Otieno Kopiyo, who had accompanied Mr Tuju, said there was no
democracy in Nyanza.
Mr Tuju said the PPP will fight to restore democracy in the region and
ensure popular leaders were elected.

Original_Search_Title: Those Behind the Fighting Are Cowards
Headline: Kenya: Those Behind the Fighting Are Cowards
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Following last weekend's tragic events in Kisumu where four people died
in pre-referendum violence, all those politicians traversing the countryside
preaching hate and violence should be thoroughly ashamed of themselves.
By fanning animosity and suspicion amongst Kenyan communities with their
irresponsible utterances, they must now bear full responsibility for the
regrettable loss of life and property.
The Kisumu incident should not be viewed in isolation. It is a
culmination of the rising culture of violence and intolerance that we have
witnessed since the Yes/No campaigns began.
It could easily have been prevented if those who did not wish to listen
to Minister Raphael Tuju had kept away from the venue. Mr Tuju was
exercising his democratic right to preach the Banana sermon to those who
wished to listen.
As we mourn the dead and commiserate with the injured, we need to ask
ourselves why we are allowing ourselves to be incited to violence.
Those causing senseless mayhem must bear part of the blame for agreeing
to be used as pawns to further other people's political agenda.
Anyone who pays another to disrupt a meeting is an enemy of democracy
and is clearly unfit to lead in any capacity.
If the issue is the coming referendum, why are some people using
violence to stop their opponents from spreading their message? Why are our
politicians creating no-go zones where only one view is acceptable?
If these leaders are the democrats that they claim to be, why do they
encourage their supporters to disrupt their opponents' rallies while
breaking the law to boot?
Setting people up for a clash with armed police and then going to
'console' them with empty platitudes after they have been injured is not
only cynical, but the height of hypocrisy!
It would be extremely naÃ¯ve for anyone to provoke the police and then
expect a pat on the back. It is also dishonest for politicians to incite
people to violence and then heap the blame on the Government.
For them to then feign injured innocence when things go awry is a clear
abdication of responsibility, and we should not let them get away with
it.
Supporting the Banana or Orange camp is not a licence to perpetrate
criminal behaviour, nor does it offer one immunity from prosecution. The law
must protect both Yes and No supporters.
Those now nursing injuries inflicted during the referendum campaigns
must wonder why none of their leaders are in a similar predicament.
Word in the street is that hired goons are being paid a measly Sh1,000
(food, transport and accommodation are free) to disrupt meetings. Hirelings
from rural areas are being paid even less.
Those on hire must ask themselves if this is enough to die for while
their paymasters are sitting comfortably in their homes with their wives and
children. Surely, life cannot be this cheap!
Lately, we have all heard of dire threats from Ministers John Michuki
and Kiraitu Murungi in reaction to equally dire threats from Ministers
Kalonzo Musyoka and Raila Odinga.
Clearly, a section of our leaders want to create anarchy and make Kenya
ungovernable for reasons that are not too difficult to discern.
There is also a suspicion that some people want to goad the Government
to lock them up so that they can become martyrs and thus get sympathy
votes.
As they casually tell us about legal 'monopoly of violence', coups and
sit-ins at Uhuru Park, we can bet our bottom shilling that their families
will not be put on the front line. These people have the resources to put
their loved ones out of harm's way, and it is Wanjiku and her ilk who will
suffer should the country disintegrate. After we have slaughtered one
another at their behest, these same people who want to rule over us!
This is why we must stubbornly resist to be dragged into political wars
in which we shall be the eventual losers. We should not allow anyone to take
advantage of our children's joblessness by paying them paltry sums to be
their foot soldiers.
Long after the dust has settled on the referendum issue, the dead will
remain dead and the wounded will still carry their scars. Will it have been
all worth it?
Mrs Macharia is an Information Technology analyst

Original_Search_Title: Ministries Allowed to Spend Sh299bn
Headline: Kenya: Ministries Allowed to Spend Sh299bn
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Government ministries and departments can now spend Sh299.2 billion on
staff salaries and allowances until the end of June next year.
MPs legalised the move by passing the Appropriation Bill, 2005.
The procedural Bill contains a raft of expenditure on the recurrent wing
of the State estimates for one financial year.
Yesterday, members allowed the 27 ministries with their departments to
withdraw the money from the Consolidated Fund.
The amount was contained in the 2005/2006 Budget read in Parliament in
June by Finance minister David Mwiraria. MPs authorised the amount on June
22, but its formal nod, in parliamentary practice, awaits the endorsement of
the Appropriation Bill.
Yesterday, MPs debated the Bill for one hour before moving into the
Committee of the Whole House where they went through it with a toothcomb,
and sought explanations from the minister.
Several Opposition MPs criticised the all-in-one endorsement of budgets
for other ministries without scrutiny, through a system called
guillotine.
Kenya's Parliament, in keeping with British Westminster model, debates
votes for at least 10 ministries. The rest are pooled in a basket and passed
at once through the guillotine.
Last week, MPs took issue with the method, saying it had been used by
the Government to hide questionable estimates.
Nominated MP Oloo Aringo (Narc) told MPs that even the British
Parliament had ditched the system to infuse transparency into public
expenditure.
Yesterday, Mr Mwiraria reminded them that the House departmental
committee on Finance already played a role in the initial stages of Budget
preparation.
Parliament will today conclude debate on the Bill that is to legalise
the entire 2005/2005 Budget by including its development expenditure.
The House is expected to pass the Finance Bill, put on hold yesterday
through a supplementary Order Paper that placed the Appropriation Bill
before it.
And an Opposition MP appealed to the Government spokesman, Dr Alfred
Mutua, to stop using his office to place partisan advertisements in the
media.
Mr Omingo Magara (South Mugirango, Ford People) singled out the on-going
adverts which are promoting one side of the referendum campaigns.
Contributing to debate on the Appropriation Bill, the MP said he was
concerned about the adverts which are paid for by the exchequer, but were
partisan supporting the Yes campaign team (Bananas) who want the proposed
Constitution adopted at the November 21 referendum.

Original_Search_Title: Helicopter Flies Back to Rally to Pick Up Forgotten Phone
Headline: Kenya: Helicopter Flies Back to Rally to Pick Up Forgotten Phone
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A helicopter landed at the Karatina stadium - three minutes after taking off - because an MP had left his mobile phone behind.
At first, the abrupt return created a scare during a Yes rally on Sunday, only for it to turn out that there was nothing wrong with the aircraft, with assistant minister Mwangi Kiunjuri and MPs Koigi Wamwere and Macharia Mukiri on board.
The legislators had flown into the stadium earlier in the day, sparking excitement among the crowd. But no sooner had they addressed the rally than they excused themselves, saying they were scheduled to address another rally at Wiyumiririe, in Mr Kiunjuri's Laikipia East constituency, after which they would hop to Kamukunji in Nairobi. They had not gone far into the skies when they made a detour to pick up the forgotten phone. A few minutes after they took off again, Juja MP William Kabogo landed in yet another helicopter - a white one.
It was a busy weekend for the Nyeri skies as aircraft after another landed and took off, bringing in leaders eager to drum up support for the Banana vote.
The first aircraft to land had brought First Lady Lucy Kibaki on Saturday after a test- landing the previous day.
The flying machines have enabled politicians to hop from one place to the next addressing rallies in different parts of the country within hours of each other.
The rally in Mathira was hosted by MP Nderitu Gachagua to win over the more than possible 90,000 registered voters in the constituency.
There were three simultaneous rallies in Nairobi, Wiyumiririe and Ngurumbani in Mwea constituency and the MPs hopped from one to the next, swopping places with ease - thanks to their winged machines.
Some MPs took off from one venue soon after another had flown in from yet another. In the end, 14 MPs attended the Karatina rally but hardly shared the platform.
But unity seemed elusive as local leaders, among them former MP Matu Wamae and some councillors, skipped the Karatina rally.
Another local politician, Mr Peter Kuguru, also protested after he was denied a chance to address wananchi.
Mr Gachagua, the host, said he snubbed local leaders because he feared they would have introduced local politics.
"This issue of the referendum should rise above local politics," Mr Gachagua said.

Original_Search_Title: Careless Talk a Recipe for Disaster
Headline: Kenya: Careless Talk a Recipe for Disaster
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kiraitu Murungi, Raila Odinga, Chris Murungaru, Uhuru Kenyatta, John Michuki, William Ruto, Njenga Karume, Gideon Moi, Simeon Nyachae, Raphael Tuju - How I would like to take the whole lot and caboodle of them, lock them up together in a tiny and stinking single cell at Kamiti without even that bucket in the corner to overflow with human waste - and throw away the key!
Then we can wait and see how they would sort out their differences over the new Constitution without the opportunity to make incendiary statements and incite poor wananchi to senseless violence.
If they choose to bludgeon each other to bloody pulp within those sealed confines, so much the better. The loss will not be felt and wananchi will have been insulated from primitive politics.
Let's face it, the referendum campaigns have led to tragic loss of life. And it is not the campaigns themselves that have become murderous, but the principal figures behind both the Yes and No camps.
It is our so-called leaders, as individuals, who have blood on their hands. Yet in the wake of the tragedy at Kisumu, we are not seeing regret and contrition. We are not seeing any signs of grief. All we are being exposed to is more of the fighting talk, finger-pointing, chest-thumping and base attempts to gain political mileage.
It is sickening to the pit of the stomach the extent to which our leaders will exploit tragedy. Every death becomes a photo-opportunity and a chance for vacuous political statements.
It is sickening the way in which they will shed crocodile tears while spouting more of the inciting speeches that might have led to the deaths of their cannon fodder.
Cabinet minister Raphael Tuju may have been needlessly provocative in insisting on holding a Yes rally in fiercely No territory. The police and the provincial administration may have been irresponsible in allowing the rally to go ahead knowing full well the security concerns.
But that does not change a cardinal principle; Tuju has every right to sell his ideas in any corner of the country. And the police and other security and administrative organs have the duty to provide security.
Let us not forget that even as they pretend to be outraged by the tragic consequences, the Orange leaders, too, have required massive police protection at some of the rallies they have held in hostile territory.
In fact, there is one thing you can say about the police of today. They have been even-handed in providing adequate security for both sides. And they have been even-handed in their application of force, irrespective of whether they are dealing with Orange or Banana crowds.
What we have seen and heard in the past few days should make us all pause and take stock. We have heard MPs, Cabinet ministers no less, make careless remarks about the possibility of a military coup if their side losses the referendum.
We have heard an MP of dubious education and even more dubious knowledge of the "masses" threaten to bring about a people's revolution.
We have heard a Cabinet minister issue grotesque Njonjoesque threats to hang for treason those on the opposite side making such remarks.
We have seen an MP's car, one bought with taxpayers' money, burnt to ashes. And we have seen the violence and tragic death in Kisumu. Will such decline to anarchy force our leaders to pause a little and consider where they are taking us?
Or will it only force a hardening of positions and raise the threat of revenge?
I fear the latter. And as those we look to for guidance and leadership drive us to the brink of the precipice, I surely hope they will stop to consider a few home truths.
We in Kenya have always tended to take our relative peace and security for granted. We survived largely unscathed through the grand theft, political assassinations, murder, ethnic cleansing and torture chambers of the Kenyatta and Nyayo regimes.
