Even in those dark periods, we could afford to smirk at the war and instability all around us; we were the island of peace and stability in a sea of turmoil.
Yet, instead of building on the democratic gains of the last few years, we are hell-bent on bringing everything crashing down. Is it likely that we have been so foolish as to imagine that we are divinely insulated from the curse of Africa?
Many years ago, countries like Sierra Leone, Liberia and Cote d'Ivoire were proudly lauded as models of peace, stability, democracy and progress in Africa. It took just a few reckless leaders to send them spinning into the vortex of coup and counter-coup, death, destruction and civil war.
If we are not careful, there is absolutely no reason why we cannot go the same way. Oh, yes, if leaders will it, as they so evidently will it, Kenya can descend into the chaos and anarchy of Sierra Leone, Liberia and Cote d'Ivoire. Or, closer home, Somalia, Sudan, Rwanda, Burundi, Congo DR and the Uganda of Amin and Obote.
Peace, democracy and stability do not exist by the grace of God. They have to be carefully nurtured and protected.
It can take only a few senseless statements and acts of seemingly random violence to ignite the spark that leads to conflagration.
By all means, let us go ahead with the referendum and resolve the new Constitution impasse one way or the other.
But our leaders must rise to the challenge and conduct themselves in a manner that will not lead to violence and bloodshed.
The political elite can insulate themselves at State House, traipse around the country by helicopter, and on the ground protect themselves with entire battalions at taxpayers' expense.
Yet they will remain blithely uncaring that the masses they are driving to violence and murder have no such luxuries.
That is what we are witnessing in Kenya right now. It is called the curse of Africa - the failure of leadership.
Strange spectacles are being witnessed at the "DSTV pubs" these days. Dispirited Manchester United and Arsenal supporters band together to root for whichever team Chelsea might be playing. Roman Abramovich has surely achieved a miracle way beyond buying success.
Somebody looked at the scenario and reminded me that we have in Kenya an achievement to match. Ex-President Moi tried without success for years to unite everybody else against central Kenya. President Kibaki has achieved that without even breaking a sweat.
Mr Gaitho is managing editor, Sunday Nation

Original_Search_Title: Shun Violence And Try Persuasion, Kibaki Tells Rival Campaign Factions
Headline: Kenya: Shun Violence And Try Persuasion, Kibaki Tells Rival Campaign Factions
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki yesterday struck a reconciliatory note over the
referendum debate with a plea to feuding factions to try persuasion instead
of violence.
In an apparent reference to the weekend violence in Kisumu in which four
people were killed and scores injured, the President said it made no sense
to fight over the referendum.
The protagonists, he said, would need each other after the "one-day
affair". It was, therefore, unnecessary to polarise the country over the
November 21 vote.
"The referendum is important, but other important duties will follow
after it. Let's us not quarrel with those we don't agree with. They are
still Kenyans and even after the vote, they'll still be your brothers and
sisters," he said.
But the President dismissed the Orange camp as "people who are lost" and
who needed to be guided into appreciating the goodness of the proposed
constitution.
He was speaking at State House when he hosted a delegation of North
Eastern Province Banana supporters.
The delegation from Ijara, Mandera, Wajir and Garissa districts was led
by Regional Development minister Mahamod Mohammed and included six MPs from
the region.
The MPs were Water Assistant minister Aden Sugow (Fafi), Isaack Shaaban
(Mandera East), Sheikh Dahir (Lagdera), Abidrahman Hassan (Wajir South) and
Yusuf Haji (Ijara).
Reacting to requests from the delegation, Kibaki announced that work on
the Lamu-Isiolo-Mandera road to link the country to Ethiopia and Sudan would
begin soon.
But the President denied that the announcement was tied to the
referendum, saying it was in keeping up with his Government pledge to
prioritise the region in development.
"That (announcement) is not for any other reason. I'm mentioning these
things to show our commitment to the promises we made earlier. We will
neither slow down nor stop," he said.
He also announced that the Government would expand an unspecified
airport in the region and sink several boreholes to alleviate water
shortage.

Original_Search_Title: Endorse Kalonzo, Voters Told
Headline: Kenya: Endorse Kalonzo, Voters Told
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Rejecting the proposed Constitution would amount to endorsing minister
Kalonzo Musyoka for the presidency, the Orange team has said.
"Our opponents in the Banana camp have said we have another agenda apart
from campaigning against the proposed Constitution and we are here to
confirm that it is true. We want leadership to come to the community," Kitui
West MP Nyiva Mwendwa said yesterday when the team took its campaign to
Makueni and Mbooni in Ukambani.
Leaders endorsed Mr Musyoka as the leader of the Kamba as they made
their way to Wote Town - the Makueni District headquarters.
Mutito MP Kiema Kilonzo said the community had a chance to show it could
produce a leader.
Nominated MP Mutula Kilonzo said anyone spoiling Mr Musyoka's chances to
get the presidency should be cursed.
And the Environment minister himself wondered why some people were
panicking at the prospect of his presidential candidacy.
"The whole of Kenya has agreed to support Kalonzo," he told wananchi at
Kakusywi in Mbooni constituency.
Mr Musyoka also asked the community to reject the proposed Constitution
which, he claimed, only sought to retain one community in power.
A No vote, he said, would amount to restoring their dignity which had
been taken away by those who had wrongly thought that they could buy them
through relief food.
"We shall vote with a lot of bitterness because we are not cheap and
because it is not our wish that we have perennial food shortages," Mr
Musyoka said.
He told Livestock Development minister Joseph Munyao, who is also the
Mbooni MP, to rethink his political stand as "the train of change is
unstoppable and he could be left behind."
The Kamba community will not allow Roads minister Raila Odinga to use
them to attain his political goals, the Yes team said yesterday.
In a veiled reference to Environment minister Kalonzo Musyoka, the Yes
team said leaders from the community stood to lose should they oppose the
proposed Constitution.
Livestock minister Joseph Munyao and assistant ministers Mutua Katuku
and Rashid Shakombo said voting for the proposed Constitution would bring to
an end perennial problems of hunger and lack of water in the region.
Mr Munyao said it was ironical for Mr Odinga to attempt to close up Luo
Nyanza to leaders from other communities while he toured other regions
freely.
"I was in Kisumu the other day (Saturday) and people are eager to hear
our message but some people do not want others to go there," he said.
Mr Katuku claimed Mr Odinga was scheming to use Mr Musyoka as his bait
to attaining power. He asked the community to oppose the plot.
He advised the Mwingi North MP, a key player in the Orange camp, to seek
the presidency with executive powers. "The plan is that Kalonzo gets elected
and gives powers to Raila. We don't want a person who gets power and
transfers it to another one," he said.
Mr Shakombo urged the Luo community not to allow Mr Odinga to isolate
them from the rest of Kenyans.
"What if we also say we are not ready for them to come to our regions
for campaigns? It will be chaos," he said.
They were speaking during a Banana campaign rally in Masinga Town,
Machakos, which was held under tight security. Youths carrying oranges and
shouting the No slogans stood at a distance as police in riot gear kept
vigil.
Mr Munyao told the Orange supporters at the meeting to go home instead
of heckling the speakers.

Original_Search_Title: Michuki Warns of Tough Action to Stop Chaos
Headline: Kenya: Michuki Warns of Tough Action to Stop Chaos
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Government will deal ruthlessly with anyone bent on causing chaos,
Internal Security minister John Michuki warned yesterday.
"(They) should know that there is an elected Government firmly in place
whose cardinal duty is to protect its people," he said.
Mr Michuki was addressing a Yes campaign rally at Kamukunji grounds in
Nairobi.
Without naming names, Mr Michuki asked the crowd where the perpetrators
of violence should be taken, and they replied: "Ndani "(behind bars).
He repeated the question three times and then said: "They should be told
that Michuki is not very far."
Starehe MP Maina Kamanda demanded that Mr Michuki orders the arrest of
Roads minister Raila Odinga for questioning over weekend violence in
Kisumu.
Seventeen MPs, including four Cabinet ministers and five assistant
ministers, attended the rally. They dwelt on the issue of national security
and the proposed Constitution.
Mr Kamanda said his Nairobi colleagues had resolved not to disrupt
Orange campaign meetings and suggested that MPs of areas where violence
occurs be arrested.
Finance minister David Mwiraria said his Cabinet colleagues openly
undermining the Government had betrayed their oath of office. "If they are
men enough, they should resign and fight the Government from outside," he
said.
Local Government minister Musikari Kombo also dared his colleagues to
resign, while Trade and Industry's Mukhisa Kituyi said Yes campaigners would
not be provoked to hooliganism.
In Mbooni, Livestock minister Joseph Munyao appealed to Ukambani voters
to back the proposed new Constitution because people from the community had
been given key Government positions. He said Sh100 million had been spent on
development projects in the area in two years.
The rally, attended by Cabinet ministers George Saitoti and Njenga
Karume, was interrupted several times by crowds chanting Orange
slogans.
Vice-President Moody Awori has appealed to Kenyans to differentiate
between parliamentary elections and the forthcoming referendum. He was
addressing a Banana rally at Ganjala, in his Funyula Constituency, Busia
District.
At a Yes rally in Wiyumiririe in Laikipia East, assistant ministers
Mwangi Kiunjuri, Asman Kamama and Subukia MP Koigi Wamwere asked President
KIbaki to take action on Mr Odinga and Environment minister Kalonzo
Musyoka.

Original_Search_Title: Outrage Over Kisumu Chaos As Death Toll Rises to Four
Headline: Kenya: Outrage Over Kisumu Chaos As Death Toll Rises to Four
Body_Text:
Nairobi — At least 30 people were nursing gunshot wounds in Kisumu as the death
toll from Saturday's riots rose to four yesterday.
The day-long violence drew condemnation from leaders with some accusing
the police of brutality while a top cleric asked President Kibaki to call a
Cabinet meeting to discuss how referendum campaigns should be conducted
before November 21.
One more person died yesterday while 29 others were treated and
discharged or admitted to the provincial general hospital and the Aga Khan
hospital. This followed riots outside Kisumu's Moi stadium where Information
minister Raphael Tuju had hosted a Yes campaign rally.
Five of those admitted at Aga Khan Hospital were students of Kisumu
Polytechnic. Two of them were expected to be operated on.
The acting Nyanza provincial hospital medical superintendent, Dr Juliana
Otieno, said they were treating 25 people who had gunshot wounds.
Two of the eight people admitted died while six will undergo surgery to
remove bullets lodged in their bodies.
The Kisumu district hospital medical superintendent, Dr Otieno Obondi,
said 12 people earlier admitted to the hospital were referred to New Nyanza
General Hospital for surgery.
However, police commissioner Hussein Ali yesterday cleared the police of
blame, saying live ammunition was only used in one incident at Kondele
Police Post where a mob had attempted to rescue a suspect arrested earlier
in the day.
"The law provides for use of lethal force when people try to rescue a
person being held in custody or to prevent a suspect from being arrested,"
he told a news conference in Nairobi.
Mr Tuju and his Lands counterpart Amos Kimunya, who attended the Kisumu
rally, condemned the violence and regretted the deaths.
Addressing a press conference in Nairobi, Mr Tuju appealed to his
opponents to leave him alone to address rallies in Nyanza Province.
He said: "I put the blood of those who died on the hands of those who
organised groups of youths to create mayhem in Kisumu."
He asked: "If what I stand for is so unpopular, why can't they let me
address my two or three people? If they are so popular, why attempt to stop
the rally?"
Nairobi Catholic archbishop Ndingi Mwana a'Nzeki and Presbyterian
presiding bishop the Rev Lawi Imathiu made a passionate call to political
leaders and their supporters to canvass for votes without resorting to
violence.
"My prayer to them all is conduct yourselves peacefully, there will be
winners and losers, how you vote is your choice but this should not divide
us, avoid violence because it does not help anybody," Archbishop Ndingi
said.
He was speaking at the Holy Family Basilica where he joined the public
in voters' civic education.
The Rev Imathiu urged the President to call an emergency meeting of his
ministers to discuss the rising violence and how to ensure the remaining
rallies were peaceful.
Yesterday, the Banana and Orange crusaders continued with countrywide
campaigns ahead of the November 21 referendum to persuade a possible 11.5
million registered voters to reject or accept the proposed
Constitution.
Internal Security minister John Michuki led a Banana campaign rally in
Nairobi while Vice-President Moody Awori was in his Funyula constituency as
other Yes teams canvassed for votes in Mbooni, Kipipiri, Mwea, Laikipia East
and Kinango where Tourism minister Morris Dzoro led a group. There was also
a rally in Karatina.
Four Cabinet ministers in the Orange group - Mr Raila Odinga, Mr Kalonzo
Musyoka, Mr Ochillo Ayacko and Mr Najib Balala - led their team to campaign
in Siaya district.
Before embarking on yesterday's campaign rallies, they visited victims
of Saturday's violence at the hospital where they addressed a press
conference. They also viewed bodies of those killed at the mortuary.
Others in the team were assistant minister Andrew Ligale and MPs William
Ruto, who is also Kanu's secretary general, Philip Okundi, Sammy Ruto, Gor
Sunguh, Ken Nyagudi, Ayiecho Olweny, Oburu Odinga, Otieno Kajwang' and
Kisumu Mayor Priscah Auma.
They talked to the patients who were writhing in pain as they recounted
their experiences.
The leaders appealed to President Kibaki to dismiss Mr Michuki and
Justice minister Kiraitu Murungi whom they accused of allowing police to use
live ammunition to quell the riots.
Mr Raila Odinga claimed that the fracas was part of a bigger strategy to
instil fear among Nyanza people.
"I am ashamed to be serving a Government that does this to its people,"
Mr Odinga said outside the provincial general hospital mortuary.
He wondered why the police used live bullets in Kisumu whereas it used
tear gas when Orange campaigners were attacked by mobs in Wajir, Meru,
Nanyuki and Marsabit.
The Orange team, he announced, had set up a committee of lawyers to
start judicial proceedings against the Government. The lawyers will first
attend the postmortem examinations.
Mr Odinga named the lawyers as Mr Pheroze Nowrojee, Mr James Orengo, Mr
Mutula Kilonzo, Mr George Oraro and Mr Otiende Amolo.
They were mandated to take the case to the International Court of
Justice.
Mr Odinga asked President Kibaki to visit Kisumu and assess the
aftermath of the police action.
"The President ought to come and carry out investigations; he will
realise there was nothing calling for the use of live bullets; I condemn in
the strongest terms possible the action perpetrated on the people," Mr
Odinga said.
Mr Musyoka asked the ICJ (Kenya Chapter) to report the matter to its
parent organisation to hold those who caused the deaths responsible.
The incident, he said, heightened his fears that there was a ploy by the
Government to delay or cancel the November 21 referendum.
He called for the sacking of Mr Murungi on the basis of his words about
brutality last week. Mr Murungi was quoted as having said that, "when it
comes to violence, the Government has an advantage because it had the legal
monopoly of all instruments of violence".
Mr Ruto challenged the President to issue a statement on the
incident.

Original_Search_Title: Ali Defends Officers Over Killings
Headline: Kenya: Ali Defends Officers Over Killings
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Police commissioner Hussein Ali yesterday absolved the police of the
Kisumu killings and instead laid the blame on the people.
"The disturbances were not spontaneous acts, but deliberate deeds of
hooliganism by locals to prevent the Banana rally," Maj Gen Ali said.
An inquest would be opened to investigate the death of the 14-year-old
boy, who Maj Gen Ali said was hit by a stray bullet at Kondele market.
Police shot another man, who later died in hospital. Maj Gen Ali said
the shooting at Kondele Police Station was justified because the mob had
attempted to rescue a suspect who was being held there after his arrest
earlier in the day.
Adding that the shooting occurred long after the referendum rally was
over, the police chief said: "The law provides for use of lethal force when
people try to rescue a person being held in custody or to prevent a suspect
from being arrested."
He continued: "The third person had no visible injuries and so the cause
of death will be known after postmortem examination."
He denied reports that 600 GSU officers were deployed to the lakeside
town ahead of the rally, saying the number was escalated by a personal
opinion.
He asked the media to avoid being "apocalyptic" when presenting news on
the campaigns.
Police used tear gas and batons to disperse people who attempted to stop
the Saturday rally called by Information minister Raphael Tuju.
Maj Gen Ali praised his officers for using non-lethal measures to
counter groups who threw stones and lit bonfires on major roads in the
town.
"The officers stayed unprovoked and prevented further deterioration. The
situation could have been worse," he said.
Maj Gen Ali, who was addressing a press conference at police
headquarters yesterday, said 46 people were arrested during the skirmishes
and would be arraigned in court to face various charges today.
Saying some of those in custody were caught dishing out money to eager
mobs outside the venue of the rally, said the police chief.
He warned people causing violence, saying: "Any threat to public safety
will be dealt with decisively."
Maj Gen Ali once again avoided stating what action police would take
against Cabinet ministers Raila Odinga and Kalonzo Musyoka on statements
they made about the Constitution and coups last week.
He added: "Nobody will take the law into their own hands."
The police chief assured the country that peace would prevail after
November 21.

Original_Search_Title: Don't Rig, Warns MP
Headline: Kenya: Don't Rig, Warns MP
Body_Text:
Nairobi — An MP yesterday warned of a popular uprising if the November 21 referendum was rigged.
Baringo Central MP Gideon Moi said that it would be the people themselves, and not leaders, who could bring down the Government if the proposed new constitution was adopted against their wishes.
Cabinet ministers Raila Odinga and Kalonzo Musyoka have come under criticism for suggesting on Thursday that imposition of the new Constitution could lead to a military coup.
Yesterday, Mr Moi told a No rally at Nakuru's Afraha Stadium that much had been said about the coup remarks.
"This is what I have to say: If you are not planning to rig, you have nothing to worry about, but if you are planning to rig, I will take you down memory lane to Ukraine."
He added: "In Ukraine there are 100 times more security personnel compared to Kenya, there are atomic bombs, some things Kenya doesn't dream about, but Ukraine was brought down by wananchi. It is not us who will bring the Government down. It is the citizens."
Security was tight in Nakuru town and at the venue when No team leaders led by Mr Odinga and Kanu chairman Uhuru Kenyatta arrived.
Mr Odinga and Mr Musyoka said they would not bow to threats and intimidation by fellow cabinet colleagues over their coup utterances.
Cabinet minister Minister Ochilo Ayacko led other Orange team leaders in condemning the violence that erupted during a Yes campaign rally in Kisumu.
They said Cabinet minister Raphael Tuju who convened the rally should be held responsible.
Another MP, the Rev Ken Nyagudi, asked why the government continued to licence Mr Tuju's rallies despite being aware of the volatile and hostile environment.
More than 40 MPs, including some Cabinet ministers, attended the Nakuru rally. They accused the Government of misunderstanding the coup remarks, saying the Yes camp was using diversionary tactics to find excuses for declaring a state of emergency.
Kanu Secretary-General William Rutto denied that the Orange Camp was planning to overthrow President Kibaki's government.
"If Kenyans want change, they can do so through elections as has always happened in the past and that is why we are waiting for 2007 when they will decide how to vote but as for now our main focus is on the proposed draft Constitution," he said.
South Mugirango MP Omingo Magara called on all Kisiis to vote No during the referendum.
Referring to the proposed Constitution, Bomet MP Nick Salat said "If it is dead, it is dead", adding there was no intention of overthrowing the government.
His Eldoret East counterpart Joseph Lagat said. "We have no apology if they (Government) lose. Let them go after they have been voted out by the people."
Belgut's Charles Keter said. "Do not panic as November 21 nears and start peddling lies. We know what we want."
Nominated MP Franklin Bett said he had advice for Mr Michuki . "Any person intending to overthrow a Government does not announce his intentions in a public forum, but does it secretly."
Mr Simeon Lesrima urged civil servants to abide by the Code of Conduct Bill that barred them from obeying illegal orders.
Elsewhere, Yes campaigns in different parts of the country continued the onslaught against Mr Odinga and Mr Musyoka over the coup remarks.
In Kiambu, Meru and Kigumo, more than 10 MPs in the Banana camp yesterday called for removal of ministers on the Orange side.
In Githunguri, Cabinet Minister Njenga Karume warned his colleagues Mr Odinga and Mr Musyoka that they were treading on dangerous ground.
Justice Minister Kiraitu Murungi and Finance minister David Mwiraria asked the President to crack the whip on the leading lights of the Orange campaign whom they allege were abusing their cabinet privileges to incite wananchi.
Transport Minister Chris Murungaru asked the police to record the statements from the two ministers.
In Kigumo, MPs who included Mr Mwangi Kiunjuri, Mr Joshua Toro, Mr Peter Kenneth and Mr Paul Muite told President Kibaki to "clean his house" after November 21 and weed out rebels in the ruling coalition.
In Mumias Labour Minister Newton Kulundu and area MP Wycliffe Osundwa clashed publicly in front Vice President Moody Awori.
Mr Kulundu hit out at the Orange team for allegedly perpetuating lies about the Wako draft during their campaigns in Western province.
Mr Awori cautioned leaders to mind their public speech and respect each others views and opinion.
In Kisii, Energy Minister Simeon Nyachae said the proposed Constitution represents the best chance to women to ensure that their rights are entrenched in the country's laws.
Finance Assistant Minister Henry Obwocha termed the Orange movement an "unholy alliance."
He was accompanied by MPs, Dr Hezrom Manduku (Nyaribari Masaba), Mr Jimmy Angwenyi (Kitutu Chache) Mr Zebedeo Opore (Bonchari), Mr Mwancha Okioma (Kitutu Masaba), Mr Godfrey Masanya (North Mugirango), Mr Stephen Manoti (Bobasi) and Mr Joel Onyancha (Bomachoge).
They said if they lost in the referendum, they are prepared to accept defeat and asked their opponents in the Orange camp to gracefully accept defeat if they lose.
In Subukia, MPs supporting the Yes camp also condemned coup talk.
The MPs were addressing a Yes campaign rally in Kabazi area in Subukia constituency.
The leaders included assistant ministers Njeru Githae, Mirugi Kariuki, Alicen Chelaite and Mwangi Kiunjuri and MPs Koigi wa Wamwere, G.G.Kariuki, Kalembe Ndile and Macharia Mukiri.

Original_Search_Title: Three Shot Dead in Kisumu Rally Riots
Headline: Kenya: Three Shot Dead in Kisumu Rally Riots
Body_Text:
Nairobi — An eight-year-old schoolboy was among three people killed as riots broke out when the Banana campaign went to Kisumu town.
The three were said to have been shot as residents trying to stop the rally called by Cabinet minister Raphael Tuju clashed with police.
More than 40 people, including former Cabinet minister Odongo Omamo, were seriously injured as Orange supporters engaged police in a day-long battle.
Five students from the Kisumu Polytechnic were admitted to the Aga Khan Hospital with bullet wounds. Two of them were in critical condition last night.
The Acting Nyanza provincial hospital medical superintendent, Dr Juliana Otieno, said the young boy and an unidentified man were pronounced dead on arrival at the hospital, while a third person died while being treated.
Earlier in the day, several vehicles were damaged as angry mobs confronted police while trying to block the rally at the town's Moi Stadium.
Trouble started in the morning when groups of youths chanting anti-Banana slogans took to the streets, rampaging through Kondele, Nyalenda, Kibuye, Makasembo and Kachok areas.
Officers from the General Service Unit and regular police confronted the youths and a fierce battle broke out.
Tension heightened shortly after 11.45 am, when Cabinet ministers Martha Karua (Water), Amos Kimunya (Lands) and Joseph Munyao (Fisheries and Livestock) landed in Kisumu for the rally.
Youths barricaded and lit bonfires on all major roads, including those leading to the stadium.
Some took advantage of the mayhem to loot, robbing people of mobile phones and wallets. Some motorists were forced to part with money to be allowed through the barricades.
Dr Omamo's vehicle was smashed with huge stones by a group of youths who confronted him opposite the Kisumu museum. He suffered a deep cut on the head and was taken to the Aga Khan hospital by his driver.
Nyanza provincial deputy police boss Joseph ole Tito said the former minister was treated and discharged.
And as chaos raged, Mr Tuju and his guests were whisked from the Kisumu airport under tight security provided by lorryloads of anti-riot police and escorted to the Yatch Club in Milimani area where they had lunch.
But when the time came to leave for the stadium, the convoy ran into huge mobs of youths who barricaded the highway and lit bonfires.
The pandemonium lasted about 10 minutes before police cleared the road and escorted the vehicles into the stadium.
Speaking during the tension-filled rally, the four Cabinet ministers, two Assistant ministers and an MP claimed that the Orange team was panicking and resorting to violence.
Those who spoke included Ministers Tuju, Kimunya, Karua and Munyao.
Others were Assistant ministers Kivutha Kibwana and John Munyes and the Wajir South MP, Mr Abdulrahman Hassan.
Mr Tuju said the wave of change was unstoppable and no amount of intimidation or fear would discourage them.
"What we are seeing are a few political desperados who do not want to hear the truth uttered within their earshot. It is these people who are funding such violence you are seeing but even in hate, we would want to tell them that we respect them but they also need to respect us and learn democracy," he said.
Prof Kibwana said the proposed new Constitution was "all-encompassing" and addressed the interests of all Kenyans.
During the skirmishes in town, a police officer lost his pistol, which was snatched by a group of youths at the Kachok junction. Several of his colleagues suffered injuries in attacks by stone-throwing youth.
At the Nyanza Provincial Hospital, medical personnel had a hectic time dealing with casualties. Nursing officer Nahashom Omwata told the Sunday Nation two people were brought in from Nyalenda with bullet wounds in their legs. One was treated and discharged.
Eight others brought in by Red Cross officers had heads injuries and were admitted.

Original_Search_Title: 'Yes' Team Still Waiting for Kibaki
Headline: Kenya: 'Yes' Team Still Waiting for Kibaki
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Going by the timetable issued by the Electoral Commission, the referendum campaigns officially were to kick off on October 21. Notwithstanding the fact that everybody was running around long before then, the assumption was that President Kibaki would finally go on the offensive after that date.
That the President is a hands-off actor is now an old story. But presumably even his friends expected from him a more vigorous show after Kenyatta Day. That weekend passed quietly with no word from him, save for reports that he had met in the privacy of State House with delegations from Kajiado and various other places. In the meantime, it was full steam ahead from the Orange camp all over the country.
From every available evidence, the Banana campaign is not looking to be the smooth and earth-shaking machine promised by Justice Minister Kiraitu Murungi. It is not even clear who is in charge. The secretariat headed by Mr Titus Mbathi seems to be in competition with another outfit headed by President Kibaki's nephew, Mr Alex Mureithi. The steering committee of senior Yes ministers seems to be doing its work in the dark. Whatever it is they are up to, the rank and file don't quite seem to know.
There is discontent in the lower ranks, to be sure, much as all the Bananas try to present a united front. Disputes that have led to abrupt cancellation of rallies have been widely noted. There was the tiff in Gatundu when former MP Moses Muihia refused to share the platform with Mr Ngengi Muigai, another former MP. At the Coast, there is loud grumbling by MPs Ananiah Mwaboza and Gonzi Rai about interference in their Yes campaigns by unelected busybodies from upcountry.
Whether we like it or not, the referendum battle was framed right from the start as a contest for power. At any rate that is how the Bananas are selling it.
Presumably, too, that is how President Kibaki is inclined to look at it. The question is, is the President ready to demonstrate the energy it takes to fight his battle? If indeed the stakes are so high, is he really psyched to play to win?
The condemnation by all sides of the recent Steadman poll can be taken as a pointer that the pollsters probably got it right. It showed the Orange were leading, though at the same time it indicated the margin for the Banana was not completely hopeless and that there was time and room to catch up.
It is usual for any team that is playing catch-up to misread the problems besetting it by pointing fingers at the wrong targets. Quite true, word is that the different Banana campaign outfits have taken to blame one another for the disjointed show so far. But if they all got brutally honest, they would admit their main handicap has been the no-show by the person who is supposed to be their main asset. That person is at State House.
Truth be said, the Yes campaign is not really Mr Mbathi's battle, or Mr Mureithi's, or for that matter anybody else's. Frankly, this is President Kibaki's battle. It is him who ultimately has got the most to lose. The most his functionaries can do is to help him win it. But in the end it is him who must take the bull by the horns. It is him who must get on the frontlines.
Hosting delegations at State House may help in roping in important community leaders onto the President's side. Yet at the end of the day, the principal in this battle, who is the President, will have to come out and make his case before the public. That means going out for rallies like his antagonists are doing. It is curious that President Kibaki has so far not organised for any presidential rally, in Nairobi or elsewhere. Even with the reports that he will soon be making appearances in Western and Coast provinces, it is nonetheless a mystery why he should remain underground until then.
As it were, his underlings have been doing their bit. Vice-President Moody Awori has been very busy. So have the other Banana ministers campaigning for Yes.
The giddy atmosphere of the campaign trail can lead to dangerous hubris. The Orange leadership is sliding into that. The talk about impending military coups was very, very ill-advised. The threat that the proposed constitution would be deemed unacceptable even if it was democratically approved implied the referendum will be a meaningless exercise unless it was to confirm the death of this constitution.
Anybody dreaming of coups should know they are not one-way streets. We saw that in 1982. There were some people who paid a very high price indeed.

Original_Search_Title: Politicians Renew Old Rivalries Over Vote in Kibaki Home Turf
Headline: Kenya: Politicians Renew Old Rivalries Over Vote in Kibaki Home Turf
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Even with high possibilities of Central Province voting as a bloc for the proposed new Constitution, the November 21 referendum campaigns have left local leaders sharply divided.
The campaign has rekindled old political rivalries witnessed during the 2002 General Election and triggered political re-alignments among strange political bedfellows.
The referendum has provided political newcomers with an early opportunity to send the message they are set for the 2007 General Election.
In President Kibaki's Nyeri home turf, separate groups of leaders have been trying to mobilise the area's 347,000 voters to support the proposed new Constitution.
One team of sitting MPs - Mr Nderitu Gachagua (Mathira) and Mr Mutahi Kagwe (Mukurwe-ini) - has been drumming up support for the proposed law separately from another group led by Transport minister Chris Murungaru.
However, Tetu's Prof Wangari Maathai has taken a neutral stance over the proposed law.
Narc activist Mary Wambui has also been involved in the campaign blitz in Othaya and Nyeri towns.
Mr Wamae
The main bone of contention among the MPs is apparently Dr Murungaru's option to work with their rivals who include former MPs Matu Wamae (Mathira), Muhika Mutahi (Mukurwe-ini) and Gikonyo Muya (Tetu).
Also in his group are members of the Nyeri District Development Association, a Nairobi-based caucus of professionals and businesspeople. Many of them seem to be harbouring ambitions for some parliamentary seats in the district.
The caucus includes millionaire contractor Ephraim Maina and former Kenya Roads Board boss Isaiah Mutonyi, among others.
A strategic meeting attended by more than 1,200 leaders from the district on October 14 was boycotted by the three MPs and scores of civic leaders.
There have been feelings among the MPs that Dr Murungaru was supporting their political rivals - an allegation that the minister has often denied, arguing that he was working with the ex-legislators because they were the party officials.
According to Dr Murungaru, each constituency had been given Sh200,000 by the Yes secretariat to coordinate referendum committees at the grassroots.
Prof. Maathai
In Tetu constituency, Prof Maathai's local political rivals moved swiftly to capitalise on the MP's public declaration that she would take a neutral stand over the proposed Constitution.
The announcement made on Kenyatta Day provided fodder for her rivals who were quick to claim that the Nobel Peace Laureate was lukewarm in her support of President Kibaki's government and could not be counted on in the hour of need. Prof Maathai had to equally move swiftly to clarify that she, indeed, supported the Yes vote.
The notable 2002 parliamentary hopefuls in Tetu, Mr David Kimanyo Munene (Narc/DP) and Wilson Ndirangu (Kanu), were already on the ground telling the constituents they were campaigning for the Yes vote as their MP had declined to mobilise the people.
Both Mr Kagwe and Mr Gachagua argue that Dr Murungaru and his group were not following the structure laid down by the Yes secretariat, which gives the MPs and councillors, and not party officials, an upper hand in the referendum campaigns.
The usurping of the MPs' roles by party officials and former legislators could impact negatively on the Yes camp, they argue.
"We must follow the structures laid down by the Yes secretariat. The meetings have been set and we must go by that. We shall not entertain anything else," Mr Gachagua says.
Due to these differences, a Yes rally scheduled for October 23 in Mathira was postponed in the last minute due to differences among Mr Gachagua, Mr Wamae and Mr Peter Kuguru on what strategy to adopt.
Mr Wamae, Mr Kuguru and Mr Maina are said to be working together to deliver the Yes vote in Mathira, while Mr Gachagua has been organising his own programme.
It was explained that a group opposed to the area MP had called off the rally at the eleventh hour following a temporary ceasefire.
Local civic leaders, who have previously thrown their weight behind Mr Maina to fight Mr Gachagua, have now vowed to team up with the lawmaker to ensure that the Banana campaign is successful.
They are led by the Nyeri county council chairman, Mr Kega Kang'au, and Karatina Mayor Christopher Kamau. They, however, say the political alliance and ceasefire are only temporary.
In Mukurweini constituency, former MP Muhika Mutahi is quite active on the ground, mobilising the masses to vote Yes in the referendum. Mr Kagwe is conducting his own campaigns, too.
What is interesting is that while the politicians have failed to shelve their differences before the referendum, all of them have chosen to proclaim their support for the Yes vote.
Although these differences appear to be localised, they can be traced to the twin Yes secretariats in Nairobi.
The secretariat run by former Cabinet minister Titus Mbathi is basically dealing with sitting MPs to coordinate the referendum campaigns.
But the other secretariat led by Mr Alex Mureithi is dealing with anyone who has some political influence.
It has not escaped political observers that in many areas, including Nyeri, majority of the sitting MPs had no links with the Democratic Party before their election in 2002.
However, most of the ousted politicians were party officials with grassroots networks. Those in Mr Mureithi's secretariat argue that it was important to involve the ex-MPs in the campaigns as they could use the party network to mobilise voters.
Laxity and indifference by some MPs to take sides over the proposed law has even made it more urgent to involve these former heavyweights.
Some are said not to have even offered to collect campaign materials and funds from the secretariat.
So far there are no Orange supporters to talk about as the region is predominantly Banana.
The furthest that the Orange team led by Roads minister Raila Odinga and Opposition chief Uhuru Kenyatta has been was Nanyuki town where they braved a hostile reception by Banana chanting crowds on October 23.
Earlier, the leaders of the No-vote campaign addressed a rally in Thika, which was marred by violence between their supporters and those of their Banana rivals.
Mathioya MP and Liberal Democratic Party (LDP) spokesman Joseph Kamotho is also yet to take the crusade against the proposed law in a big way even to his own constituency.
However, low-key campaigns by the team have been noted in Mukurwe-ini and Mathira divisions.
There is little civic education in rural areas and many of the rural folk will go to the referendum with a notion that it is another General Election.
The civic education exercise also took off to a controversial start, with several lobby groups accusing the Constitution Review Commission of Kenya of hand-picking trainers who were strangers to their respective areas.
It is likely that the voting pattern for the referendum will follow that of past General Elections - the direction that President Kibaki will take.
Local people are not known to turn up for voting in large numbers and the herculean task for the Banana campaigners will be to mobilise the voters to participate in the referendum.
While the politicians have failed to shelve differences, all of them proclaim their support for the Yes vote.

Original_Search_Title: Violence a Threat to Tourism, Says Hotel Association
Headline: Kenya: Violence a Threat to Tourism, Says Hotel Association
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Hoteliers and tourism stakeholders have expressed concern over the
violence rocking campaign rallies ahead of the constitutional
referendum.
Kenya Association of Hotelkeepers and Caterers Coast branch chairman,
Mohamed Hersi, said yesterday that the chaos could erode the gains made in
the tourism industry.
"The industry was beginning to recover after suffering a jolt following
the 1997 Likoni clashes and the El Nino rains, that rendered many roads
impassable," said Hersi.
He said the increasing violence in the run-up to the referendum was
sending the wrong signals to potential visitors abroad.
The hotelier said at the moment, there is an upswing in overseas
arrivals, helped by new direct charter flights to Mombasa, but the numbers
could dwindle over the referendum skirmishes.
Two new charters, from some of the country's leading tourist markets,
the United Kingdom and Poland, are scheduled to start flying directly to
Mombasa early next week.
Hersi added that one of UK's leading tour operators, First Choice, was
making a return to the town after a six-year absence.
Hersi appealed to politicians to stop inciting their supporters to cause
chaos.
"Violence is a bad recipe for tourism, and a good and sound economy
cannot succeed in such an atmosphere," said the Whitesands Beach Hotel
general manager.
Similar remarks were made by the general manager of Plaza Beach Hotel,
Alfayo Onyango, and Isaac Rodrot, resident manager of Club Temple Point in
Watamu, Malindi.
The referendum rallies have been characterised by violence in some parts
of the country, with people suffering injuries.
Four people were killed in Kisumu on Saturday in the worst incident so
far, during a Banana rally organised by Information minister Raphael
Tuju.
Still in connection with the referendum chaos, the Kenya Union of
Journalists (KUJ) has urged media houses to make politicians account for
their utterances, failure to which they should be given a coverage
blackout.
KUJ secretary-general Ezekiel Mutua said at a Press conference in
Nairobi that journalists should ask politicians to explain utterances they
considered reckless.
"We are appealing to the media houses not only to provide fair,
objective and balanced reporting on the referendum debate, but also give a
blackout to any politician whose utterances are likely to set Kenyans
against each other," he said.
Mutua also urged the media must shun tribal or regional biases and cover
the debate professionally "without fear or favour".

Original_Search_Title: Team Alleges State of Emergency Plan
Headline: Kenya: Team Alleges State of Emergency Plan
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Government is orchestrating violence in order to declare a state of
emergency before the November 21 referendum, the Orange Democratic Movement
has claimed.
Heritage minister Najib Balala said the Banana team was in a panic after
realising that it might lose at the vote, and was now out to cause chaos so
that the referendum could be put off.
"The weekend killing of eight-year-old pupil Paul Mwala and others is a
clear testimony of the Government's panic. This violence could be used to
declare a state of emergency," he claimed during a No rally at Yala in Siaya
District.
Balala was referring to the skirmishes that rocked Kisumu town on
Saturday during a Banana rally headed by Information minister Raphael
Tuju.
The violence left four people dead, including an eight-year-old
boy.
He said claims by some Banana ministers that the Orange team wanted to
overthrow the Government through a coup was part of the plan to declare a
state of emergency.
The minister dismissed the coup accusations as "rubbish and
unrealistic."
Environment minister Kalonzo Musyoka challenged those claiming that he
and his Roads counterpart Raila Odinga were plotting a coup to charge them
in court with treason if they had evidence.
"We did not talk about overthrowing the Government, but said the
implication in the Wako Draft could lead to coups," he said. Kalonzo said
the draft proponents were scared of him and Raila.
"We shall stop at nothing until we bring democracy and a people-driven
constitution to Kenyans," he said.
Balala said the Orange team did not have the machinery to overthrow the
Government as alleged by their opponents.
"Our security is you, people, who elected us," he said.
Raila said the referendum would be a make-or-break situation for the
Orange team and urged the residents to turn out in large numbers to vote on
the D-day.

Original_Search_Title: Taib Takes Campaign to Balala Turf
Headline: Kenya: Taib Takes Campaign to Balala Turf
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Mombasa Mayor Taib Ali Taib yesterday displayed his campaign posters in
his archrival Cabinet minister Najib Balala's Mvita Constituency.
Many saw this as an affront to Balala, who is the area MP.
Balala, who is the head of the Orange secretariat at the Coast, said the
move is not in line with the Orange Movement's campaigns agenda.
Balala said Taib should stop personalising the No campaign.
"I have no problem with Taib putting up banners in support of the No
vote. But his portrait alongside the message is equal to personalising the
campaign," said Balala.
The posters, which were distributed in Mwembe-Tayari, Bondeni, and in
the Central Business District, bear the mayor's colour portrait and Orange
symbol, representing the No vote in the November 21 referendum.
The posters resemble those used in parliamentary and presidential
election campaigns.
They bear the mayor's full names on top, his picture and the slogan
"Vote Orange" at the bottom.
Taib has publicly declared his interest in the Mvita seat. He is
expected to take on Balala in 2007.
Coast Orange secretariat leaders, wondered why Taib only targeted Mvita
yet he had the whole of Coast to campaign for the Orange team.
A truck bearing the posters went round the town yesterday carrying
youths singing Orange slogans and praising Taib. The truck passed through
Jomo Kenyatta and Digo roads in Mvita constituency.
The posters were displayed in Mwembe-Tayari, Bondeni, and in the Central
Business District.
The mayor has so far opted to hold his Press conferences in his parlour,
one time hitting out at leaders in the Banana camp, instead of doing so in
the Orange secretariat, along Jomo Kenyatta road.
Contacted for comment, Bahari MP Joe Khamisi, who is also the chairman
of the Coast Parliamentary Group, said there was nothing wrong with Taib's
decision, if he was sincere.
Khamisi said there were no rules governing the No vote campaigns.
Taib was not available, but his close ally and former deputy mayor Salim
Bajaber defended him.
He dismissed remarks by Balala as baseless.
Bajaber said it was unfortunate that Taib's effort to mobilise support
for a No vote in Coast were being misconstrued by his political foes.
"Taib has used his own money in the campaigns and I do not see anything
wrong with that. He is also a crucial member of the Orange camp at the
Coast, why is this affecting Balala so much," said Bajaber.

Original_Search_Title: MP Urges Women to Support Wako Draft
Headline: Kenya: MP Urges Women to Support Wako Draft
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Women have been urged to support the Wako Draft if they are to win the
struggle for equity.
East African Legislative Assembly MP, Mrs Rose Waruhiu, said in Mombasa
it would be sad if Kenyans reject the Wako Bill and drown the struggle that
started in 1975.
"It has been a painful struggle for women, but they can only achieve
their dreams if they vote for the draft," she said.
Waruhiu was speaking to women groups from Mombasa at Chandaria Hall
where she had been invited by Coast Banana campaigns MPs.
Tourism minister Morris Dzoro told local leaders to support the
women.
Dzoro said women were the best ambassadors and should be supported if
the campaign for the draft was to gain mileage.
The leaders urged the women to restrain their children from being
misused to create violence.
Present were Assistant ministers Danson Mungatana and Annania Mwaboza
and MPs Harrison Kombe and Abdallah Ngozi.
Ngozi said the draft had favoured women more than any group, as a third
of the parliamentarians would be women if the draft was passed.

Original_Search_Title: Kombo Seeks Top Job After Kibaki
Headline: Kenya: Kombo Seeks Top Job After Kibaki
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Ford Kenya chairman Musikari Kombo has declared his interest in the
presidency.
Kombo disclosed he intends to vie for the country after President
Kibaki's term.
Speaking at a Yes campaign rally at Emuhaya, Vihiga on Saturday, the
minister said the shortest way the community could ascend to power was by
voting for the proposed constitution.
The Ford-K chairman was reacting to a challenge by MPs Moses Akaranga
and Bonny Khalwale, and former Cabinet minister Amukowa Anangwe to declare
whether he was interested in the top seat.
Khalwale had said if the Local Government minister would not go for the
seat, Ford-K would look for other candidates, including Trade minister Dr
Mukhisa Kituyi.
Akaranga and Anangwe then asked Kombo to declare his stand to the
community.
"Kombo should declare his position on the presidency.
If he wants it, I will support him, if he doesn't, we shall look for
alternatives," said Anangwe.
Kombo also accused Roads minister Raila Odinga of sabotaging former VP
Wamalwa Kijana's presidential bid, and urged the Luhya to ignore him.
He asked local residents to turn up in large numbers during the November
21 vote to pass the draft.
He said the party was using grassroots campaigns to educate the people
on the content of the document.
The minister said lies peddled by the Orange team had poisoned the
wananchi.
At the same time, Khalwale yesterday vowed to continue with his Banana
crusade despite losing his car.
The Ikolomani MP said the burning of his car was an act of cowardice by
his opponents.
"This was a cowardly move. Why turn their anger on my car instead of
going for me? They burnt my vehicle because they fear me," he said.
Elsewhere, the Banana camp yesterday called for the arrest of leaders
who allegedly hired youths to disrupt a rally in Kisumu, accusing Orange
politicians of lying to Kenyans.
Cabinet ministers John Michuki, Mukhisa Kituyi, David Mwiraria and Kombo
accused the No politicians of ruining the country.
Michuki, the Internal Security minister, called for peace, saying the
country would not develop if campaigns continue to be violent.
He said he would not accept chaos, adding that he would deal with those
responsible for causing chaos at campaigns.
"Kenyans are peaceful people who were not born with stones in their
hands. We want to continue with life as usual," he said during a rally in
Nairobi's Kamukunji grounds.
Others who attended the rally included assistant ministers Beth Mugo,
Betty Tett, Moses Wetang'ula, Maina Kamanda, Joshua Toro and MPs Njoki
Ndung'u, Kalembe Ndile, Jane Kihara, Adelina Mwau and Norman Nyaga.
Kituyi accused the Orange team of misleading the youth, who turned
violent at a rally in Kisumu hosted by Information minister Raphael
Tuju.
He called for the immediate arrest of those behind the violence.

Original_Search_Title: Maathai Calls for Referendum Dialogue
Headline: Kenya: Maathai Calls for Referendum Dialogue
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Environment Assistant minister Prof Wangari Maathai yesterday urged the
Banana and Orange teams to renegotiate the Wako Draft.
Saying her stand on the constitutional debate was neutral, Maathai
warned that if endorsed the way it is, the proposed constitution could
plunge the country into a crisis.
The Nobel Peace Laureate said she would not campaign for any group
contrary to media reports that she is in the Yes camp.
"My stand still remains neutral as I do not want to be seen fuelling
disunity in the country, but if the Orange group comes to Tetu I will let
them hold their rally," she said.
Maathai called on the leading lights of both Yes and No teams to
renegotiate the document to avert future conflicts.
"The document has to be renegotiated by all groups involved. It cannot
be forced upon people or sneaked in because sooner or later it will be
rejected," said Maathai.
She said although the Wako Draft is an improvement of the Bomas Draft,
there were issues, which should be amended. Maathai said the bickering among
leaders has less to do with the contents of the Draft constitution, but
political survival and supremacy.
"Some leaders have made mistakes in the past. This has made some of
their colleagues angry and vengeful. For some of them this is the time to
settle unfinished battles," said Maathai.
Maathai added that it is unfortunate that the referendum exercise has
been used in dividing Kenyans.
Speaking in her Tetu Constituency, the assistant minister said the
referendum should be postponed to pave way for dialogue.
Maathai blamed the rift in the Cabinet on President Kibaki's refusal to
honour the memorandum of understanding.
She said there could be no division in the Government had the President
kept his word to his coalition partners.
Maathai also called on the Government to translate the document into
local languages.
She said more than 80 per cent of Kenyans do not understand English or
Kiswahili and rely on what leaders tell them.

Original_Search_Title: Accusations Traded Over Chaos as Fourth Dies
Headline: Kenya: Ministers Trade Accusations Over Chaos
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A fourth person died yesterday from injuries sustained during Saturday's
chaos over a Banana rally in Kisumu.
And as Kenyans came to grips with news of the deaths, one question was
on everybody's mind: Did these people have to die?
A blame game immediately ensued as Cabinet ministers from the rival
Banana and Orange teams traded accusations, with some calling for an inquest
and the resignation of key Government officials.
Roads minister Raila Odinga reacted angrily to a statement that those
killed were his goons, saying the Government should go for him "instead of
brutalising my people".
And Government Spokesman Alfred Mutua issued a stern warning to
politicians against using children to carry out violent acts during
rallies.
As the chorus of condemnation of the violence poured in, Police
Commissioner Hussein Ali said one of those killed was shot when a mob
stormed a police station to free a suspect. Major General Ali said the
police had no apologies to make over the shooting: "You don't attempt to
raid a police station and expect to go away scot-free. It doesn't matter
what justification you give and the law provides for use of lethal force in
such a case."
Ali said the second fatality, a 13-year-old pupil, was killed by a
"stray" bullet and announced that an inquest would be launched into his
shooting.
Ali said the third victim was found lying a distance away from the
police station and had no physical injuries. He said police were justified
to use live bullets after a mob attempted to raid Kondele Police Station to
free a suspect.
Ali was addressing a Press conference in Nairobi in reaction to the
incident in which several people were seriously injured when police battled
unruly mobs that were threatening to disrupt a Banana rally hosted by
Information minister Raphael Tuju.
Four Cabinet ministers from the Orange team termed the killings genocide
and called for a public inquest.
Raila, Kalonzo Musyoka (Environment), Ochillo Ayako (Sports) and Najib
Balala (Heritage) demanded immediate action from President Kibaki.
They were speaking in Kisumu after viewing the bodies and visiting the
injured at the New Nyanza Provincial General Hospital.
However, Tuju blamed the deaths on people he did not name in the Orange
camp, but whom he said had attempted to organise a parallel rally.
"There is no way I can be comfortable with loss of life, but some people
are so hungry for power that they do not care the number of deaths that
occur in their pursuit," Tuju said.
Tuju said those behind the chaos and deaths were now visiting hospitals
"pretending to console the injured".
He said some leaders in Kisumu had been spoiling for a fight and he had
been forced to cancel the rally once to avoid a possible confrontation after
a parallel one was licensed.
He said he later sought and obtained a licence for the Saturday rally
even though the Orange side had attempted to organise a parallel one.
"I will go back to campaign. Even after the referendum I will be very
active in Nyanza province. I will claim my democratic place," Tuju
said.
"We demand the sacking of Internal Security minister John Michuki and
his Justice and Constitutional Affairs counterpart, Kiraitu Murungi, for the
brutal force of terror used against Kisumu residents," Raila said.
"We take great exception to the use of live bullets to kill innocent
people. The Government must take concrete action and bring to book the
perpetrators," Raila added.
Kalonzo said the Orange Democratic Movement would take up the matter at
the International Court of Justice in search of satisfactory answers. "This
is a crime against humanity. It is a painful loss and a shame for the
Government I serve in," Kalonzo said.
He claimed the brute force was well plotted in advance and executed
under instructions: "Kiraitu said when it came to violence, the Government
had an advantage because it had the monopoly of all instruments of
violence," and demanded an explanation from the minister.
Ayacko said Kiraitu and Michuki had preached hate ahead of the rally,
leaving many questions begging.
Balala said "President Kibaki owes Kenyans an explanation and an apology
over the murder committed under his leadership".
Raila said protests in Kisumu were often met with brutal force and gave
the example of Saba Saba riots last year when four people were shot
dead. "Why does this killing only happen in Kisumu? No report has been
handed over in connection with Saba Saba riots up to now yet they repeat the
abuse of State power," he said.
He said the Orange team had instructed lawyers James Orengo, Pheroze
Nowrojee, Mutula Kilonzo, George Oraro, Olago Aluoch and Otiende Amolo to
pursue the murders in court.
Later in Siaya, Raila said it was unfortunate that a statement had
stated that those who were killed on Saturday were his goons.
"If these people want (to get at) me let them come for me and stop
brutalising my people. They killed an innocent pupil who was on his way back
from school," Raila said.
He said attempts Michuki and Kiraitu to get at him would fail
miserably.
"They cannot manage me. Kenyans have come from far and nobody will stop
us from reaching Canaan," he said.
Kanu chairman Uhuru Kenyatta said he was shocked and dismayed by the
violence, which he said was a reflection of the Government's "blatant
disrespect for human life".
"I strongly condemn the police brutality and especially the use of live
bullets upon innocent children and call upon the Police Commissioner to stop
using selective justice," Uhuru said in a statement.
But Ali defended his officers, saying they had acted professionally to
contain a situation that could have otherwise turned uncontrollable.
The police boss said the shootings took place long after the rally had
been addressed and the conveners had left and commended the officers
involved in the exercise for their efforts to control the situation.
He termed those killed and injured in the incident as hooligans, saying
their actions were facilitated by some local leaders.
He said police arrested 46 people and that several others, including
policemen, were injured. Those arrested will be arraigned in court
today.
Those arrested were found giving out cash after the rally had ended, he
said, warning that police would deal with anyone sponsoring violence.
He also told off those condemning the shooting and termed them
apologists holding brief for criminals.
"Those vilifying police for the action they took are apologists for
criminals to justify their actions. We will continue to do our work and I am
not going to respond to their queries," he said.
Ali said there were 21 rallies across the country on Saturday and
wondered why the Kisumu one turned chaotic.
Tuju said the mayhem witnessed was unleashed by hired youths ferried
from Nairobi and alleged that two former military officers were commanding
the operation.
"It is all about intimidation. They are terrified of the truth. They
know anytime I talk to people in Nyanza the game is over," Tuju said at a
Press conference in his Nairobi office.
And Trade minister Mukhisa Kituyi, assistant minister Mwangi Kiunjuri,
MP Mwandawiro Mghanga and Cotu secretary-general Francis Atwoli regretted
the violence that has rocked the referendum campaigns.
Kituyi said the violence had taken the country three steps backwards in
its quest to improve the economy and woo investors' confidence.
"It is unfortunate for this country, we are now going back to the
drawing board, especially at a time when we are struggling to stabilise the
economy and have investors invest in Kenya," said Kituyi.
He blamed the violence on politicians in both the Orange and Banana
camps, saying they should all be held responsible for the deaths and
injuries.
Kiunjuri, the Laikipia West MP, said the Orange team should be held
responsible for the violence in Kisumu and called on the Government to
prosecute leaders suspected of inciting the public.
"The violence trails in the country are bad for the nation. It has
already created animosity and hatred among us leaders and wananchi and we're
now looking at each other as suspects," said Kiunjuri.
"It is about time leaders in Luo Nyanza came to terms with the wave of
democracy that we are currently living in and shunned inciting violence," he
added.
Mghanga said leaders who were using provocative and abusive language
were the cause of the tension and violence.
He appealed to leaders to create an atmosphere that was conducive for
them to articulate their support or opposition to the proposed
constitution.
"We have become our own enemies as leaders, what do you expect if you
abuse and call me names before my fans, they will definitely retaliate,"
he said.
The International Commission of Jurists condemned what it termed as
excessive use of force by police that resulted in the deaths.
The ICJ Kenya chapter chairman, Otiende Omolo, said it was wrong for
police to unleash violence on innocent people who were in their estates and
houses and who were far away from the venue of the rally.
"It does not make sense why the Government mobilised such a serious
operation that led to the death of innocent Kenyans. We condemn this in the
strongest terms possible," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Orange Leaders Defiant Over Coup Utterances
Headline: Kenya: Orange Leaders Defiant Over Coup Utterances
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange camp yesterday remained defiant over their revolution and
coup remarks as Raila Odinga expressed surprise at President Kibaki's
anger.
The 'No' crusaders said in Nakuru they did not support a military coup
but said Kenyans have an inherent right to revolt against a bad government
and laws.
Speaking in Nakuru, five ministers from the 'No' camp and over 40 MPs
said Kenyans would stage a revolution, should the vote be rigged.
The MPs were received by enthusiastic supporters at the Afraha Stadium
for the first rally of the group opposed to the Proposed New
Constitution.
Minister William ole Ntimama made his second appearance in an Orange
rally and was given a hero's welcome.
His Cabinet colleagues Raila Odinga, Najib Balala and Ochilo Ayacko
equally received heroic cheers at the rally that kicked off at 1.00 pm.
Leader of Official Opposition Uhuru Kenyatta joined the team for a rally
that had been waited for with abated breath since the Banana group took
their campaign to Nakuru three weeks ago.
Raila said he was surprised by Kibaki's anger following remarks he and
colleague, Kalonzo Musyoka, made on Thursday.
The Roads minister said the President should have asked him to explain
the context in which the remarks were made.
Raila said the President had been misinformed about the statements
during the launch of a 'No' booklet, Our Case Against the Wako Draft.
Kibaki, while receiving a delegation of Nairobi leaders at State House,
condemned the coup talk and assured Kenyans they were safe.
The President described the remarks as "ujinga na upumbavu" (foolish and
stupid).
Kalonzo told the cheering crowed the Orange crusaders respected the
President and urged him to reciprocate instead of insulting them.
"I am not a pumbavu (stupid) because I have chosen a different path from
that of the President," he said.
Justice minister Kiraitu Murungi came under criticism for saying the
State has a monopoly over violence. Raila once again cited the Ukranian
revolution that brought down President Loenid Kuchma and said Kiraitu and
Internal Security minister John Michuki had no capacity to threaten him with
treason charges.
He said he was not afraid to suffer for his political stand as he had
survived worse times.
He blamed the two ministers for misleading the President, adding that he
had been charged with treason previously and acquitted after the State
withdrew charges.
The minister told the rally a minority would not threaten Kenyans.
He said the Government's power lies with the people and not a few
individuals, adding voters would decide when to withdraw their support.
Raila dismissed Information Minister Raphael Tuju as a minion with whom
he cannot engage in exchanges.
He said those who plot military coups do so in secrecy and strike when
their adversaries are unaware, but he said he had spoken in public about a
possibility should the referendum vote be rigged.
Sports minister Ochillo Ayacko said wananchi would exercise their
sovereignty if they chose to revolt against a rigged poll.

Original_Search_Title: Three Killed in Kisumu Rally Riots
Headline: Kenya: Three Killed in Kisumu Rally Riots
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Three people were yesterday killed during yesterday's Banana rally in
Kisumu City.
More than a dozen others were shot while scores were bludgeoned by
police officers.
A number of officers had their plastic helmets burst by vicious stones
as others became victims of their own teargas when canisters were swiftly
returned to them by riotous mobs.
Three of those shot and the woman were fighting for their lives at
different hospitals.
Among those seriously injured was former Bondo MP William Odongo
Omamo. The politician was admitted to Aga Khan Hospital, Kisumu, where his
condition was said to be stable.
Police descended on a crowd surrounding the unidentified woman, who was
selling mandazis at Kachok, about a kilometre from the venue of the rally,
and injured her and three others.
She was ferried to hospital in a Red Cross ambulance.
The 7am incident triggered off the day-long running battles between
police and defiant Kisumu residents.
It was in this sea of chaos, echoes of gunfire and plumes of teargas
smoke that a defiant Minister for Information and MP for Rarieda, Raphael
Tuju, addressed a crowd of about 500 people - all ferried to the stadium and
locked in before the protesters took charge of the town and its
neighbourhood.
Tuju's Cabinet colleagues Martha Karua (Water), Joseph Munyao
(Livestock) and Amos Kimunya (Lands), constantly conferred on their mobile
phones.
Also in attendance were Kivutha Kibwana, John Munyes, and Otieno
K'Opiyo.
The vehicles ferrying the Banana luminaries to Moi Grounds, where the
rally took place, abandoned the direct route instead using an alternative
one - to dodge barricades erected by protesters - under tight security, with
GSU officers holding their guns at the ready.
Police arrested scores of people, including at least two Banana
supporters from Tuju's Rarieda constituency who were in possession of
axes.
Tension had been building up in the town from Friday night but got worse
yesterday when word spread that Tuju was ferrying people from outside the
town to attend his rally.
Youths quickly blocked all roads and for the whole day, no vehicles left
or entered Kisumu.
Police were forced to fire in the air and lob teargas at an Orange mob
that had earlier converged at the entrance of the Moi Grounds.
A Form Four candidate at the Kisumu Day School, Moses Odhiambo, was
clubbed and left for dead after he was caught in the fracas that unfolded a
few metres from the school.
After the rally ended after 3pm, the VIPs were escorted out of the
stadium by GSU personnel to waiting cars.
They were taken straight to Kisumu Airport - which was itself sealed off
by security personnel - from where they were flown to Nairobi.
Addressing the rally, Tuju, Karua and Kimunya accused some politicians
of organising "the thuggery" to thwart the function.
"Ignore those people making noise outside. They have been paid to cause
chaos but we are least perturbed," said Kimunya.
Tuju said the youths had been paid by his detractors who are incapable
of initiating development in the region.
"I will not be deterred by the violence and I will return to Kisumu and
hold rallies in various parts of Luo Nyanza," he said.
They declared they had embarked on a campaign to liberate the Luo
community from the yoke of Roads Minister Raila Odinga.
The ministers said it was only through such political liberation that
the community would be able to fight poverty and other economic
disadvantages.
"I formed the PPP to give an alternative voice to the community and
create democracy. This is why we have the 'Yes' rally here today and I am
focused to hold such similar meetings often in the region," said Tuju.
Kimunya said: "You people should know there is life beyond one person
and that is why Tuju should be heard and his ideologies and principles
supported," he said.
Karua said unlike Raila, Tuju does not want to become a king.
"It is high time you people diversified your politics and joined PPP and
become part of Kenya," she said.

Original_Search_Title: Constitution Not State Project, Says V-P
Headline: Kenya: Constitution Not State Project, Says V-P
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Vice-President Moody Awori yesterday asked politicians to stop
misleading voters that the constitution was a Government project.
He said a constitution gives guidelines on how the country should be
governed, and should be de-linked from the government.
"Katiba (constitution) is not a Government project but a document to
guide us and we should be careful when making decisions about it," said
Awori.
The VP was speaking during a funds drive in aid of Anglican Church of
Kenya, Mumias Diocese.
Awori lashed out at Cabinet minister Newton Kulundu for attacking Mumias
MP Wycliffe Osundwa over his stand on the Proposed New Constitution.
The Labour Minister had said Osundwa was a liar who had lost
direction. He also dismissed Osundwa's decision to support the Orange camp,
saying the MP needed prayers.
Cabinet Minister Musikari Kombo, and MPs Soita Shitanda, Moses Wetangula
and David Were, host Bishop Binear Salala, and Legio Maria Archbishop Simon
Makokha attended.
The drama all started when Osundwa told the congregation to reject the
proposed constitution because it was flawed.
The MP likened Kenya's fight for a new constitution to the tribulations
of the biblical Jacob, who toiled for seven years to be given Leah as his
wife but was short-changed and given Rachel.
"This constitution is similar to Jacob, Leah and Rachel's saga in the
Bible," said Osundwa, amid cheers from the congregation.
But Kulundu tore into Osundwa and the Orange camp, saying they were
liars. "My brother Osundwa has lost direction and is being misled by
liars. I am praying for him to see the light," said the Lurambi MP.
The VP said it was wrong for leaders to abuse each other.
"Leaders should respect decisions of the opponents and avoid
name-calling," said Awori.
The VP cautioned Banana and Orange leaders against violence.

Original_Search_Title: Minister Faults Campaigns for Scaring Investors
Headline: Kenya: Minister Faults Campaigns for Scaring Investors
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Investor confidence has dipped because of on-going referendum campaigns,
Trade minister Mukhisa Kituyi has said.
Kituyi told a university conference in Nairobi some investors were
adopting a wait-and-see attitude before setting up bases in Nairobi.
He blamed the Orange and Banana camps for "careless statements".
"It is true that the investor confidence is sagging," Kituyi told a
Strathmore University Politics and Ethics conference yesterday.
He singled out recent coup remarks attributed to Cabinet ministers Raila
Odinga and Kalonzo Musyoka.
But his challenger at the debate Bonaya Godana of Kanu said the media
had misinterpreted the ministers' statements.
"The media are guilty of exaggerating the coup statements this is
irresponsible and intimidation," Godana said.
Kituyi told the gathering a women-only election at the district level
would follow the General Elections, leading to one-third representation in
Parliament for women.
Godana said a good constitution must be clear, with no 'ifs' or 'buts',
claiming women may end up with "zero."
Kituyi said since the law had not yet been enacted, he could not give
details of how the elections would be conducted.
Mutula Kilonzo, while closing the two-day conference said ethics should
guide politics and law making.

Original_Search_Title: A Bumpy Ride Against the Tide
Headline: Kenya: A Bumpy Ride Against the Tide
Body_Text:
Nairobi — It is not hard to understand why Kanu chairman Uhuru Kenyatta's popularity has suddenly plummeted in Central province. A section of leaders from the province claim he is deliberately playing the spoiler role by resisting President Kibaki's push for the proposed new Constitution.
In essence Mr Kenyatta, who is among the Orange campaign leading lights, has turned out to be the bad boy of Central province over the perception that he is undermining a kinsman (Mr Kibaki).
On the contrary, those in the Orange camp argue that the Gatundu South MP's stand has helped project a positive image about him as a national leader beyond regional or tribal cocoons.
In his home province, Mr Kenyatta is being censured by a number of politicians for "working with outsiders" allegedly with a view to bringing down Mr Kibaki's government instead of waiting in the wings to take over as president "when the right time comes."
Claims Justice and Constitutional Affairs assistant minister Njeru Githae: "Uhuru is simply being misused by Raila (Odinga), Kalonzo (Musyoka) and (William) Ruto, who will soon dump him and take over the Kanu party."
And Mwea MP Alfred Nderitu regrets that Mr Kenyatta, whom he describes as "outright presidential material", would have easily succeeded Mr Kibaki only that he is now busy smoking away that chance through his "unholy alliance with Raila".
The Kanu chairman has severally denied allegations that he was being misled by the Roads and Public Works minister or that he was undermining members of his Kikuyu community by disregarding President Kibaki's stated position on the proposed law.
Perhaps his most powerful and eloquent defence came three weeks ago during an Orange rally at Kihara market in Gachie, Kiambu district.
Speaking in Kikuyu language, he moved his audience by stating that the same man (Mr Odinga) they were shunning as kihii (uncircumcised man) helped propel Mr Kibaki to the presidency with his famous "Kibaki Tosha" remark. And Mr Odinga's late father (Jaramogi) accorded his (Uhuru's) father, the late President Jomo Kenyatta, the chance to become president of Kenya at independence.
"Didn't you nickname him njamba (hero)? Why has he suddenly become the enemy? And what will you say if he supports my bid for presidency in 2007?" posed the Gatundu South MP.
In the run-up to independence in the 1950s, the late Jaramogi Oginga Odinga declined to participate in the formation of a new government insisting that Mzee Jomo Kenyatta, whom he described as "the people's choice," be released from detention first.
Upon ascending to the presidency in 1963, Mzee Jomo Kenyatta rewarded Jaramogi by appointing him his principal deputy. The two, however, fell out and Jaramogi quit the vice presidency in 1967.
Nearly 45 years later, sons of the two independence heroes, find themselves in another political marriage that is mainly responsible for Mr Kenyatta's problems in his home-turf.
The real predicament for the Leader of Official Opposition, though, has more to do with competition from the president, who appears determined to take over almost the entire Central province voting bloc.
During the last elections, Mr Kibaki got the lion's share of votes in the province. He scooped 701,916, representing 69.0 per cent against Mr Kenyatta's 308,072 (30.3 per cent).
This was not a particularly poor showing by the newcomer, considering that he sliced off a sizeable chunk of Mr Kibaki's share, who in 1997 claimed 88.4 per cent of the total votes cast in the province.
Mr Kibaki won the presidential race with 3,637,318 compared to Mr Kenyatta's 1,839,575, representing 61.3 per cent and 31.6 per cent, respectively.
Central Province is not only a competetive zone, but home to highly ambitious politicians. Over the years, more politicians from the province than in any other have vied for the country's presidency.
Since the advent of multi-party politics, several politicians among them President Kibaki, Mr Kenyatta, Kenneth Matiba, Prof Wangari Maathai, Waweru Ng'ethe and the late Mukaru Ng'ang'a have vied for the seat.
Since losing the 2002 elections, Mr Kenyatta's political fortunes and clout have been on a decline, especially in his home province. Despite his party winning a paltry six parliamentary seats, mainly in Kiambu and Thika districts, compared to Narc's 21, he has since lost grip of all the others save for his own.
Among the first to ditch him was Kiambaa MP Njenga Karume, who had abandoned Narc for Kanu ahead of the 2002 polls and spearheaded Mr Kenyatta's presidential campaign in the region. He now serves as Mr Kibaki's minister for Special Programmes in the Office of the President.
Members of Parliament on two other tickets from the region - Safina and Sisi Kwa Sisi - Paul Muite (Kabete) and William Kabogo (Juja) respectively, have allied themselves to the Kibaki administration and support the Banana campaign.
"Uhuru has absolutely no foot soldiers on the ground as he is solely dependent on Ruto and Odinga," charges Mr Githae.
The minister claims that Mr Kenyatta is of little significance to the No crusaders and will be kicked out of the new-found Orange Democratic Movement "since he brings nothing on the table".
"Odinga will marshal the Luo vote during the referendum polls, Ruto the Kalenjin, Mudavadi will deliver the Luhyia vote and Musyoka the Kamba vote. What will Kenyatta deliver? Nothing!" claims the Ndia MP.
However, viewed beyond the Central province, Mr Kenyatta's position could well win the hearts of many across the country.
