Observes former Butere MP Martin Shikuku: "If we are talking about national and not regional politics, then those who are opposed to Kenyatta's stand are the ones who are digging their political graves".
Mr Shikuku explains that the Kanu chairman is showing a rare but painful gesture that could correct the impression among most Kenyans that members of his community never support any individual other than their very own.
The seasoned politician enumerates several historical instances including one in 1959 when he was arrested and detained at Kileleshwa Police Post, Nairobi, for demanding the release of Mzee Jomo Kenyatta.
"I was again arrested and detained in 1975 following protests over the death of J. M. Kariuki (slain Nyandarua North MP) after declaring in Parliament that Kanu was dead," he recalls.
Most recently, Mr Shikuku points at a 1992 incident when he mobilised over 600,000 voters from Western province to support Mr Kenneth Matiba's bid for presidency on a Ford Asili ticket.
"After supporting my friend, I thought the Kikuyu electorate would reciprocate in 1997. But I was totally abandoned as they all moved to Kibaki's Democratic Party," he says.
Mr Shikuku believes Mr Kenyatta's position is quite commendable, not to mention that it helps him market himself across the country as a nationalist.
In deed, one cannot ignore these facts considering that this is exactly how Mr Kibaki made it to the presidency after two consecutive failed attempts in 1992 and 1997.
His attempt in 2002 received a major boost after he teamed up with other political heavyweights, including Cabinet ministers Mr Musyoka, Mr Odinga, Mrs Charity Ngilu and Prof George Saitoti and the late Vice President, Mr Michael Wamalwa.
Reacting to Mr Kenyatta's political fortunes in the province, Laikipia MP Mr G. G. Kariuki affirms that he is not in the wrong camp.
"The Kanu party has decided to go Orange and being no ordinary member but its chairman, his decision is perfectly in order," says Mr Kariuki.
And likening politics to the revolving globe, the former Cabinet minister says Mr Kenyatta may have made a wrong decision now but since politics keeps rotating and changing, he may still survive if things change in his favour some time in the future.
"However, at this point in time, Kenyatta has got it absolutely wrong. Foundation is quite critical in politics and what he is trying to do is build a house on sand without a strong foundation," he warns.
To succeed in politics, the MP says, one needs to identify with the people who offer him the bedrock support - his tribesmen.
"Let us not cheat ourselves, Kenyan politics still revolves around tribes and unless you get your tribe solidly behind you, it is difficult to win a national seat," argues Mr Kariuki, one of the key Banana campaigners.
Being a newcomer in national politics, Mr Kenyatta is doing relatively well. His first attempt at the presidency in 2002 was quite impressive.
Retired President Moi nominated Mr Kenyatta, then 41, to Parliament in October 2001 and appointed him to the Cabinet as Local Government minister the following month. A year later, Mr Moi single-handedly endorsed him as Kanu's presidential candidate, a move that led to a last minute walkout by key Kanu politicians to join the then little known Liberal Democratic Party.
The Leader of Official Opposition and President Kibaki have not had a particularly explosive relationship. And much as Central MPs would have wished the two leaders to support one position, such a move would have jeopardised Mr Kenyatta's political career.
The last time there was a semblance of cooperation between the two was early this year when Mr Kenyatta visited State House and declined, thereafter, to lead his Kanu MPs into walking out of the Parliamentary Select Committee on constitutional review in solidarity with LDP-allied MPs who had been axed from the crucial House committee

Original_Search_Title: When Banana And Orange Mixed in Kiambu
Headline: Kenya: When Banana And Orange Mixed in Kiambu
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Two rival referendum rallies in Kiambaa on October 15 pointedly illustrated the political undercurrents in Central Province.
The first, at Ndumberi market, was hosted by Special Programmes minister Njenga Karume, who is also the area MP.
Not very far away at Karuri market in the same constituency was another meeting called by Official Opposition leader Uhuru Kenyatta.
The theme at Mr Karume's Banana camp meeting, where Energy Minister Simeon Nyachae was the chief guest, was that the Orange camp was essentially fighting to "grab" power from President Mwai Kibaki, and by extension consign the people of Central Kenya back into the cold.
Mr Kenyatta's argument at his rally was that Central Province could not afford to isolate itself from other regions, which he insisted was the case regarding the proposed new Constitution.
It is the play on fears expressed at Ndumberi that has fast gained currency throughout the greater Central region. And it is precisely for this reason that Mr Kenyatta was anxious to portray the referendum as not being a contest against President Kibaki.
Passions are such that the Orange proponents from the province are being accused of betraying their people, a tag Mr Kenyatta protests. At the Karuri function, he was seated next to Mathioya MP Joseph Kamotho, who had to endure the troublesome "traitor" tag throughout the Moi years when most of Central Province was fiercely anti-government.
It is easy to conclude from the relatively scattered attendances both at No and Yes rallies in the province that a mood of apathy may have set in. Yet the hype about an alleged plot to take over power is designed to draw out the maximum support for the Yes vote in the populous region.
In Nyeri district, there have been reports of people turning out in large numbers to confirm whether their names are properly maintained in the voter's registers.
Mr Kenyatta's rally had been preceded by a roadshow, which started from Kiambu town, then to Githunguri, Kimende, Limuru and Gachie before finally converging at Karuri stadium. Accompanied by Planning minister Anyang' Nyong'o, Mr Kenyatta's group had a morning rendezvous at a roadside restaurant called Sarivid just outside Kiambu town. Hosting the party was local tycoon Stanley Githunguri, who had made available several choice goats for the guests.
Mr Githunguri is an avowed foe of Mr Karume and has been financing some of the Orange rallies in Kiambu. A potentially ugly confrontation was defused when Mr Kenyatta's party brushed attempts by the police to block them from driving their convoy through Kiambu town.
The Karuri function was actually well-attended, but it was a loud and intoxicated rogue who started shouting as the meeting was breaking up who seemed to connect with the sentiments being quietly expressed from the ground. "Don't be fooled there are no oranges here," he shouted as the convoy was driving away.
At Ndumberi, Mr Karume had brought along virtually all of the Kiambu MPs, including Mr Arthur Magugu of Githunguri who has long kept a low profile. Also present was Limuru's Kuria Kanyingi, who was the last remaining ally of Mr Kenyatta in the district until he joined the Banana trail.
Ndumberi bars did roaring business from people the MPs had ferried in buses for the rally who came sporting bright Yes T-shirts and caps. Another lot of rally-goers were booked to be entertained later at the Nova Hotel in Kiambu town.
Mr Kenyatta was actually well-received when he passed through Kiambu town, but there were hecklers in his wake.

Original_Search_Title: Review Chief Warns Against Inciting Words
Headline: Kenya: Review Chief Warns Against Inciting Words
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Constitution of Kenya Review Commission chairperson yesterday urged politicians to stop making utterances that could jeorpadise the country's peace.
It is unfortunate, Ms Abida Ali-Aroni said, that leaders are threatening the country's peace by making statements that are dividing Kenyans.
Speaking during a civic education session at Eastleigh in Nairobi yesterday, she said the constitution making should unify the nation.
"When we started agitating for a new Constitution, we did it to heal the wounds created by the current Constitution by seeking good governance and rectifying deficiencies in the current Constitution," she said.
The constitution review Act requires leaders "to build confidence and national consensus and to urge Kenyans to desist from actions that would encourage violence," Ms Ali-Aroni said, adding that there was need for all political leaders to obey the law to ensure the review succeeds.
She criticised remarks by two Cabinet ministers that reportedly said the proposed Constitution could create conditions for popular uprising, or even a military coup, saying that they were unfortunate.
Roads minister Raila Odinga and his Environment colleague Kalonzo Musyoka were reported by the media as having said that the proposed Constitution was inviting Kenyans to the culture of military coups.
But they later said their comments were mere caution to highlight the danger of enacting a defective Constitution.
Ms Ali-Aroni said the Yes and the No leaders had deviated from the pertinenet issues and were engaging in divisive politics.
"Politicians have stopped discussing the merits and demerits of the proposed Constitution and are now engaging in a battle of words," she added.
Trade minister Mukhisa Kituyi said Kenya risked losing billions of shillings worth of investment if politicians continued to issue what he called reckless statements.
Dr Kituyi of the Yes camp said that unless the current politicking stopped the country might suffer irreparable damage.
Some investors were now reluctant to put their money in Kenya, arguing that the political climate was not conducive.
He gave the example of Nokia mobile phone company which, he noted, had postponed indefinitely the opening a regional office in Nairobi because of the referendum tension. "There are many more in this category," the minister said.
He was speaking at Strathmore University, Nairobi, where he was a panellist at a public debate on the proposed Constitution.
North Horr MP Bonaya Godana of Kanu represented the No side, while CKRC secretary P.L.O. Lumumba and nominated MP Mutula Kilonzo were moderators.
Dr Godana accused the Banana side of misleading Kenyans and the media of exaggerating politicians' statements.

Original_Search_Title: For Uhuru, Selling Orange At Home is an Uphill Task
Headline: Kenya: For Uhuru, Selling Orange At Home is an Uphill Task
Body_Text:
Nairobi — When the Orange camp launched its campaign against the proposed Constitution in Central Province in Thika, it marked the beginning of Official Opposition Leader Uhuru Kenyatta's tricky hunt for No votes in his home district.
But the Gatundu South MP is finding himself increasingly isolated by fellow politicians at home and in the neighbouring Kiambu district, which voted overwhelmingly for him in the 2002 elections in which he was the Kanu presidential torchbearer.
All the other three MPs in Thika district - Mr Patrick Muiruri (Gatundu North), Mr Peter Kenneth (Gatanga) and Mr William Kabogo (Juja) - have joined the Banana side, along with most of the civic leaders in the Thika and Ruiru municipal councils and the Thika county council.
The councillors in the Yes camp include Mayor John Mutahi (Thika), Wilfred Wamuya (Ruiru) and the county council chairman, Mr Raphael Njenga.
More recently, Uhuru suffered a major blow in the referendum campaign when his long-time friend and political ally, Mr Zackary Mukui, abandoned him and defected to the Banana side.
During the Thika rally, which turned violent as Banana and Orange supporters fought it out with chairs right in front of Mr Kenyatta and key No campaigners, including Roads minister Raila Odinga, no other MP from the greater Kiambu district and, by extension Central Province, was present.
But the latest surprise in the unfolding scenario was politician Ngengi Muigai's announcement last week that he was backing the Yes vote.
More significantly, Mr Muigai, who is Uhuru's first cousin, made the announcement to a fairly large crowd in Gatundu town.
The former Gatundu MP's move seemed to complicate matters for the Kanu national chairman, as he leads his No protagonists in the hunt for the district's close to 300,000 votes.
Mr Muigai, who still commands considerable following in both Gatundu South and Gatundu North, as well as Juja, could help sway the outcome of the vote.
In fact, the politician may be Uhuru's biggest political headache after speakers at the Gatundu rally queried his two-time failure to contest the parliamentary seat and asked him to do so next time.
It emerged during the meeting that water shortage was among the leading concerns for residents who recalled Mr Muigai's projects as area MP in the late 1970s and 1980s. However, Mr Kenyatta still enjoys admiration and support from ordinary people in both Thika and Kiambu districts and his influence may be felt particularly in the two Gatundus.
Besides water, land is another issue in Thika district, more so in Gatundu where land-related murders have been a common feature.
In Kiambu, where again all the five MPs are in the Banana camp, Mr Kenyatta seems to command a sizeable following from residents.
Unlike in Thika where only two out of the four MPs were elected on a Kanu ticket, the former ruling party swept four of the five Parliamentary seats in Kiambu.
The district's MPs who are backing the Yes vote are Mr Njenga Karume (Kiambaa), Mr Paul Muite (Kabete), Mr Kuria Kanyingi (Limuru), Mr Arthur Magugu (Githunguri) and Mr Viscount Kimathi (Lari).
Thika and Kiambu are expected to provide the province's swing vote in the referendum.

Original_Search_Title: Banana Accuse Foreign Missions
Headline: Kenya: Banana Accuse Foreign Missions
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Banana camp has named three foreign missions, which are allegedly interfering with the country's political affairs and "belittling" Kenyans ahead of the referendum.
Eight MPs yesterday told the German and US embassies and British High Commission to stop meddling in Kenyan politics, saying the country was a sovereign State that no one could dictate to.
The MPs, who included Assistant ministers Mwangi Kiunjuri (Energy), Peter Kenneth (Co-operative Development) and Joshua Toro (Public Works), accused the missions of direct interference, saying they were planning to impose leaders on Kenyans.
The group was speaking at Kangari market in Maragua District during a Banana meeting organised by professionals from Kigumo constituency.
The professionals, led by Mr Jamleck Kamau, made the move to counter the No stand taken by the local MP, Kihara Mwangi. MPs Paul Muite (Kabete), Jayne Kihara (Naivasha), Kembi Gitura (Kiharu), Elias Mbau (Maragua) and Daniel Karaba (Kerugoya Kutus) said some foreigners were waging war on President Kibaki's administration because of "flimsy interests".
"The same foreigners have already taken sides in the referendum debate, which is a sign of their strange interests," claimed Kihara.
Gitura said time had come for Kenyans to rule on their own, without acting on "decisions, threats and intimidation" from foreigners.
At the same time, Mbau called for the arrest of Cabinet ministers Raila Odinga and Kalonzo Musyoka for allegedly making coup threats.
"Since there are rules to be followed by all in the referendum campaigns, the Police Commissioner, Maj-Gen Hussein Ali, should arrest those issuing coup threats.
"If nothing is done, they will continue to incite Kenyans," said the legislator.
He said President Kibaki had allowed democracy to prevail but this had been abused by some self-seeking leaders.
Muite appealed to leaders in both the Orange and Banana camps to respect the Electoral Commission of Kenya by adhering to its directives.
He said it was regrettable that some leaders had waged war on the elections body, saying it should be accorded respect and left to operate independently.

Original_Search_Title: Raila Warns State Against Rigging At the Polls
Headline: Kenya: Raila Warns State Against Rigging At the Polls
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Government will live to regret it if it rigs the November 21 referendum on the proposed Constitution, Cabinet minister Raila Odinga warned yesterday.
The Wako Draft was unacceptable to Kenyans and cannot work, the Roads minister told an Orange rally in Kapsabet.
He said the Banana team should accept defeat just as the Orange team would do if the referendum is free and fair.
The minister refuted claims that he and Environment minister Kalonzo Musyoka would seize power through a coup, saying the Orange group respected democracy.
He claimed the Government was stage-managing reports that he and his group were planning a coup after realising that the Banana team will lose.
He accused Internal Security minister John Michuki of lying to provincial administrators that they would retain their positions if the Yes team wins.
Kanu chairman and Official Opposition leader Uhuru Kenyatta said the referendum was not a fight between Mr Odinga and the President.
The Orange team did not support a change of government through coups, he said and added that the three years Narc had been in power had seen corruption rise to alarming levels.
The Government had given MPs in the Yes camp Sh5 million each to campaign for the Yes vote, claimed Mr Kenyatta. He asked assistant minister Stephen Tarus to join the Orange team, claiming he was working in isolation.
He said Kenya needed a constitution that could unite all its 42 tribes and promote democracy.
The crowd at Kapsabet Stadium was told that Kalenjin leaders who support the proposed Constitution will be rejected by the community in the next General Election.
The Orange team received Aldai MP Jimmy Choge, previously in the Middle Ground group, who declared his rejection of the new laws.
Tinderet MP Henry Kosgey said the Wako Draft made the President too powerful while the Bomas one wanted the Prime Minister to have more power.
Mosop MP John Sambu said anyone who betrayed the Kalenjin community would face its wrath.
A lawyer in the Orange secretariat yesterday said lawyers who walked out during a Law Society of Kenya luncheon did not do so because of Mr Odinga, who addressed the function.
Mr Joseph Kiangoi, the convenor of the Orange campaign legal subcommittee said those who left as the minister was speaking were rushing to court.
Of the 180 lawyers who attended the luncheon, he said, more than 170 came out with the minister.
Lawyers who left before the meeting ended did so as the minister was explaining what the Orange team would do to ensure positive aspects of the Constitution were not lost should the No side win at the referendum.
Additional reporting by Mugo Njeru

Original_Search_Title: Police Find Weapons At Scene of Violence
Headline: Kenya: Police Find Weapons At Scene of Violence
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Police have recovered weapons they suspect to have been used when
Orange supporters attacked their rivals in Kakamega Town on Thursday.
Ikolomani MP Bonny Khalwale's car was burnt in the incident.
Area deputy police boss Patrick Safari said yesterday that some weapons had been recovered at the scene of the attack.
But he said calm had returned to the town after the incident,
although no arrests had been made. Investigations were going on, he
added.
"We have handed over the investigations to the CID and have been able to recover two machetes from the scene," Mr Safari said.
In the Thursday attack, taxi driver Simeon Shimanyula was seriously injured when he was hit on the head with a weapon.
The car's burnt shell was towed to the police station yesterday.
CID officers were looking for people they accused of instigating the
violence and a van reported to have transported some armed youths.
Mr Safari said the MP and five other people had recorded statements with the police.
The clash started when a group of Orange youths confronted Dr Khalwale and his supporters as they campaigned in the town.
A group of Isukuti drummers who had accompanied the MP and his
supporters in Yes T-shirts were whipped but the MP fled on foot after
they pelted his car with stones and snatched Yes campaign materials.
Copies of the proposed Constitution, which the MP had intended to
distribute to the residents at the main park, were burnt by the youths.
Dr Khalwale and some supporters were removed from a shop in which they had taken refuge and taken to the police station.
The police shot in the air to disperse the youths when they started pelting the MP with stones as he was being driven away.
Yesterday, Local Government minister Musikari Kombo condemned the
attack, terming it "an act of desperation by the Orange team after
sensing defeat in the region."
He said: "I want to say that the violence in Kakamega will not
change the situation on the ground because people in Western Province
have seen the falsehoods which have been churned out by the Orange
team."
Mr Kombo, who was addressing journalists at the Banana secretariat
in Nairobi, urged the youth not to be misused by leaders bent on
causing chaos.
They should realise that they too are citizens who will lose if the country plunges into chaos, the Ford Kenya chairman said.
"This campaign is about the Constitution for this country, and I
would appreciate if everyone went about seeking votes in a very
peaceful manner," he said.
Other Ford-Kenya leaders also condemned the violence.
Speaking to journalists in Eldoret Town, foreign affairs secretary
Ken Wafula and Uasin Gishu branch secretary Nick Omitto said it was
unwarranted.
They accused senior campaigners in the Orange camp of instigating violence countrywide, and said it was counter-productive.
"It is an act of cowardice and a display of the worst form of
malice," Mr Wafula said. "Engaging in violence is a sign of defeat as
they unleash anger to seek solace."
Mr Omitto said it was unbelievable that such acts could occur when
the people had been given the chance to either support or reject the
proposed Constitution through the ballot.
Any views, he added, should be expressed through the vote on November 21, and not in street battles and arson.
The two officials accused Cabinet ministers Raila Odinga and Kalonzo
Musyoka of inciting the people. It was not a coincidence that as they
uttered the coup d'etat threat, chaos erupted in Kakamega, they said.

Original_Search_Title: Now the War Turns Musical
Headline: Kenya: Now the War Turns Musical
Body_Text:
Nairobi — There is a new musical CD, and its title track is a reworked
version of a popular song, I Am Not Sober by Awilo Mike. The CD, which
is inspired by the November 21 referendum, and supports the Orange, or
"No" camp.
The title track has been redone by the same artiste but instead of
the refrain, I am not sober because I have taken Guinness that has got
Michael Power, the remix goes: I am sober because I have chosen Orange
that has people's power.
While in the original sing, Awilo counts his drinking buddies and
the number of bottles of Guinness stout they bought, in the reworked
song, he substitutes Guinness with the sacks (of oranges) politicians
in the Orange camp have bought. He then goes on to sing about the
leading lights in the Orange camp, among them Raila Odinga, Otieno
Kajwang', David Mwenje, Kalonzo Musyoka, Joseph Kamotho, Omingo Magara,
Anyang' Nyong'o, Najib Balala, Joe Hamisi and William Ruto, adding that
he has chosen Orange because he is not stupid.
Just like in the original song in which he says his friends should
be given stout, in this remix, he names the politicians who should be
given oranges, and even weaves in the name of the former President Moi
as those who should be given orange. Arap Moi, apewe chungua, ni mzee
wa zamani, apewe chungua, goes part of the song.
The other songs in the Orange CD are done in the urban hip-hop
style, with one of the tracks having voice overs by the leading lights
of the Orange camp.
But the banana camp has not been left behind and have released a song titled "Let's Go Bananas."
Nairobi's regular master of ceremonies Big Ted (Ted Kwaka), who
produced the song, says many musicians are trying to capitalise on the
current political mood and have released songs which they hope either
the Banana or Orange camp will adopt as their anthem.
"Many people think they can do a Gidi Gidi Maji Maji and gain
prominence as happened in 2002," he says of the rap duo's song, I Am
Unbwogable, which Narc adopted as its anthem, and which catapulted the
two to the limelight. "There are singers who have even composed songs
in praise of the Orange camp."
Let's Go Bananas is getting airplay only on one radio station and is
being played wherever rallies in support of the Yes vote are held.

Original_Search_Title: The Beehive Factor in Kenya's Politics
Headline: Kenya: The Beehive Factor in Kenya's Politics
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Not too long ago, I had the difficult task of explaining to a
group of foreigners a small news item appearing in one of the local
newspapers.
The news was that a group of men had been arrested by police on
their way to a political rally. What puzzled the visitors was the
reason for the arrest - the men were found carrying a beehive to a
referendum campaign meeting that was being held in a nearby centre.
The obvious answer, that these men were determined to disrupt the
rally, had not occurred to the visitors. Even after I explained, they
still had questions: Why prevent your political opponents from
addressing their supporters? Why disrupt a peaceful gathering by
tossing a hive full of angry bees into the crowd? And, above all, why a
beehive of all things?
It was hard to explain that what they had read actually reflected a
substantial advancement for Kenya. That the level of violence over the
referendum has actually been low compared to that of previous political
battles.
It is always difficult to explain to outsiders that a certain degree
of brutality is actually considered "normal" in the politics of this
country, and that we only worry if things begin to get out of control.
But, leaving the foreigners aside, let me do my bit for "civic
education" and point out a few things that have not been given emphasis
thus far in the referendum debate.
First, I would emphasise that for all the lofty words and phrases so
often heard, a constitution in a democratic country is not primarily
about unity or development or the welfare of generations yet unborn.
It is simply a device for the distribution of power. It essentially
attempts to balance this distribution between the Executive, the
Judiciary and the Legislature; or between national and regional
interests; or between individual rights and the demands of citizenship
in a modern state.
The most insightful comment about constitution-making that I have
read in recent weeks was not made in Kenya, but actually came from an
online report on the Iraqi referendum which ended recently. The writer
expressed the view that "a democratic constitution should be hostile to
the accumulation of power at any one centre." That is the essential
thing, and the rest is detail. It serves to remind us that although
what we in Kenya have learnt to fear most is an all-powerful president,
the evils we associate with excessive powers of the presidency, can
actually be brought about by judges, MPs, or the media.
It was parliamentary tyranny that led to the huge salary increases
and other incredible perks for MPs, at a time when most Kenyans are
striving to survive on less than a dollar a day. Media tyranny played a
big role in the Rwanda genocide.
So if constitution-making and politics are about distribution of
power, it is no wonder that the people-driven constitution (codified as
"the Bomas Draft") was doomed to be a non-starter.
That draft is nothing more than the unrestrained effort by different
sectors of society to grab for themselves all sorts of powers without
any thought of balancing their desires against the claims of others. It
promised everything to just about everybody.
This explains why the Orange group that is opposing the proposed
Constitution has had a upper hand in the campaign, while the Banana
group has had their work cut out for them - selling the new
Constitution.
But if the lessons of the 1992 and 1997 general elections are
anything to go by, then the Orange group may be in for a surprise. For
what the results of those two elections revealed, was that if those who
hold the instruments of government are willing to utilise them well,
they can easily turn the tide of public sentiment in their favour.
In this respect, we may say that at least some good has come of this
referendum process; it has somehow compelled the Government to heed the
cries of various minorities which have hitherto been ignored.
It is not really clear why the Government suddenly finds that it has
ears for the longstanding pleas of the Ogiek, or the Maasai. In a crude
mathematical reckoning, these are not the key voting blocks that will
determine the outcome of the referendum.
But perhaps the reasons behind these recent moves are not as
important as the fact that they are happening at all. And despite the
pretences of various foreigners (including some diplomats) that this
exercise in political patronage is shocking, the practice is as old as
democracy itself.
In one of his books, (based on events that actually took place) the
British 19th century political novelist Anthony Trollope had a passage
which aptly explains how it all works.
An opponent of the Government of the day, seeking to justify a
change of position, starts by declaring that he did not particularly
like the prime minister of the time, but that "the thing now offered
was too good to be rejected, let it come from what quarter it would.
Indeed, might it not be said of all the good things obtained for the
people, of all really serviceable reforms, that they were gathered and
garnered home in consequence of the squabbles of ministers? When men
wanted power, either to grasp it or to retain it, they offered bribes
to the people. But in the taking of such bribes there was no
dishonesty, and he would willingly take this bribe."
This passage might just as well have been written in reference to
what is going on in Kenya right now. And before any foreigners turn red
in the face claiming that the Government is bribing voters, let them
consider the political traditions of their own countries.
Apart from the occasional beehive tossed at a crowd attending a
political rally, we are really not that different from the rest of the
world in our politics.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki Condemns Coup Talk: Raila And Kalonzo Protest
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki Condemns Coup Talk: Raila And Kalonzo Protest
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki yesterday led a wave of condemnation against
suggestions by two of his Cabinet ministers that passing the proposed
Constitution could create the conditions for a popular uprising or even
a military coup.
And the President told Kenyans to relax, because there was no
possibility that the country could experience any of the parallel
conditions that triggered unrest and military coups in some
neighbouring countries.
He condemned the ministers' remarks as "ujinga na upumbavu (foolish and stupid)".
The President's message came as Internal Security Minister John Michuki said the Government was aware of plans to cause chaos.
"This will not be allowed and our security forces have been on the
alert because we have been aware of their intentions," said Mr Michuki.
He added: "We want to assure Kenyans that these individuals will not be allowed to destroy the peace enjoyed in the country."
And his Cabinet colleague, Justice minister Kiraitu Murungi
commented that should chaos occur, the Government had the advantage
because it had "the legal monopoly of all instruments of violence".
The ministers whose comments caused the furore were Mr Kalonzo Musyoka and Mr Raila Odinga.
Speaking at the launch of a booklet Our case against the Wako Draft,
Mr Musyoka, the Environment minister, said: "The Wako Draft is inviting
Kenyans to the culture of military coup d'etats while we are a
democracy. Let us avoid coup d'etats in future by having a Constitution
that is amendable."
Mr Odinga named Cuba and Uganda as places where coups had taken
place because of bad constitutions, and continued: "That is why we
should not push Kenyans to a corner where they have no alternative but
to rise up against an undemocratic constitutional order."
Their comments were published in yesterday's Daily Nation beneath the headline Raila and Kalonzo warn of coup peril.
However, yesterday both Mr Musyoka and Mr Odinga, the Roads
minister, while not denying they had made the comments, said: "The
media must help our country at this crucial time by reporting correctly
and avoiding headlines that are alarmist."
In a joint statement they said their comments were meant to
highlight only, "the inherent danger of enacting a defective
constitution."
"The thrust of our submission was based on the fact that once
enacted, the Draft Constitution will be practically unamendable and
hence the need to reject it in order to avert any possible recourse to
military intervention as the only open option in future as happened in
other countries."
They added: "Prevention is better than cure, and we believe it is
our responsibility as leaders to point out to the Kenyan people the
inherent danger of enacting a defective Constitution."
Mr Odinga went on to refute claims he was agitating for a coup if
the Yes team won the referendum, saying his team wanted only a free and
fair referendum. Speaking at an Eldoret hotel, on his way to an Orange
rally in Kapsabet, Mr Odinga added that he and Mr Musyoka had not
talked of a coup should the proposed Constitution be enacted.
President Kibaki told Kenyans to ignore threats of a military coup if the proposed new Constitution is passed.
Addressing a delegation from Nairobi at the capital's State House,
the President said no one had the capacity to stage a coup in Kenya and
such talk should be shunned by peace-loving Kenyans.
He said: "Kenya is our country. It belongs to all of us. No one should brag that he will do this and this."
He added: "Whom are they threatening? People should not be cheated as nothing will happen. Kenyans should leave them alone."
Describing himself as an old man who had seen much since the
colonial days, President Kibaki said Kenyans did not want or support a
coup and that any team - either the Orange No-vote leaders or those for
the Banana Yes-vote - should accept Kenyans' verdict in the November 21
referendum.
"Why not accept defeat if you lose? The majority should win. There's
no need of forcing people or saying things that are stupid. Some things
are just said to show that you are stupid. Even if you slap your friend
there's nothing you will get," the President said.
He said Kenyans cherished peace adding that the coup threats were just "empty talks", ignited by the referendum.
The vote "is an easy thing and we should just vote then go back to our work."
The vote will be free and fair; no one will be stopped from voting, he added.
"There's no need for some people to cause fear among Kenyans. We have no ill motives," he said.
He, however, warned that the Government was ready to deal with anybody wanting to cause chaos.
President Kibaki said: "If you start chaos we will meet there. No
one will make you stay at home because of chaos. Those who want to
cause chaos thinking they are powerful should know that it is God who
put all the people in this world and He will not allow them to do that."
President Kibaki said the Government was doing well, the economy had
improved, donors had resumed financial support and that petty talk
would not help the country.
Addressing a news conference at Harambee House, Mr Michuki said the
Government was fully aware of plans to cause violence and disaffection,
even before the talk of revolutions was made.
He added: "Yesterday's statements by Hon Kalonzo Musyoka and Hon
Raila Odinga calling for a rebellion have now made public their
intentions. It is clear they are not interested in the Constitution
reform for this country.
"They are agitating people to overthrow the Government and
therefore, it should be clear to them, they are treading on a very
dangerous path."
He accused the Orange team of hatching a plot to incite rebellion.
The No team, he added, intended to cause security problems and put at
risk the peace Kenyans enjoyed.
He said: "This will not be allowed and our security forces have been
on the alert because we have been aware of their intentions. We want to
assure Kenyans that these individuals will not be allowed to destroy
the peace enjoyed by the country."
Elsewhere, Justice minister Kiraitu Murungi said: "I would like to
warn those talking about coups and revolutions that under section 40 of
the Penal Code, any person who plans to overthrow the Government by
unlawful means is guilty of the offences of treason, which is
punishable by death."
Mr Murungi said the Government had the advantage because it had "the legal monopoly of all instruments of violence".
Local Government minister Musikari Kombo described the two
ministers' remarks as, "a very dangerous statement which is bad for the
country."
In Kisii, Ford People leader Simeon Nyachae, a key Banana
campaigner, led eight Kisii MPs in demanding investigations in the coup
remarks.
Kabete MP Paul Muite also called on the Government to investigate
the two ministers. Their statement should be taken seriously, he added.
Kanu chairman Uhuru Kenyatta said the Orange team did not support a change of government through coups .
Cabinet minister John Koech asked the Government to take stern
action against the ministers, saying that unless the Government checked
such inflammatory statements, they would cause panic and despondency.
Women's groups allied to the Yes camp accused the ministers of making statements that could breach the peace.
Convened at a Nairobi hotel under the auspices of the Yes national
secretariat, Ms Rose Waruhiu, the secretariat's director for gender
affairs said the ministers' statements were "very horrifying."

Original_Search_Title: Why Political Leaders On Both Sides Stand Accused
Headline: Kenya: Why Political Leaders On Both Sides Stand Accused
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The language of coups has been creeping into the Constitution campaigns. It was taken to a new level by the Orange team on Thursday, when its leaders said a victory by the Banana side would leave the country with a constitution that is a recipe for revolutions and military coups in the future because, the Orange side believes, the new Constitution would be impossible to amend.
And, as expected, the Government yesterday reacted strongly, warning that it would take the necessary measures to secure Kenya's stability. The Orange team, too, has issued a statement saying its remarks were maliciously distorted. But there is more than just semantics at stake here.
Tragic as the matter is, both sides stand accused. The Banana side has previously accused the Orange team of trying to use the Constitution issue to overthrow the Government. In that sense, both the Bananas and the Oranges are guilty of trying to influence the voters' views by using the bogey of the possibility of overthrowing the Government. The difference is that the Bananas see a coup plot today, and the Oranges see it in the future.
This is a failure of the collective political leadership. Kenya deserves better. The politicians have a responsibility to appeal to our hopes, not to mobilise us through fear.
It also demonstrates a point we have made before - that the debate is no longer about the Constitution. It is, rather, a bruising race for power. In the past we argued for a need to return to the real issues in the Constitution, rather than dwelling on political disasters - real or imaginary - if the Constitution referendum doesn't go one way or the other.
Debate between Bananas and Oranges
It was this view that eventually grew into the recent debate between the Bananas and the Oranges aired live on all the country's TV stations and all, but one, major FM radios.
When the two sides came together for the debate, we, as a country, for one day demonstrated that it is possible to take a different and more peaceful and more civilised path to making a new constitution, rather than the violence-wracked and abuse-laden one that most of the leaders have chosen.
Lest anyone forgets, there is something greater to fight over than a constitution - namely, Kenya. It will continue to be here irrespective of the outcome of the referendum. It might be the worse for the outcome, it might be the better. But it will be here.
So let us be brutally honest about it. While the politicians are tearing out one another's eyes because, say they, the Constitution is for eternity, it is not. Both the present Constitution and the draft are all creatures of the dominant political forces of the generation of politicians at the time.
The next generation of Kenyans might not have the strong prejudices and intolerance that we see in the campaigns today. For they will be the first generation of urban Kenyans - people whose grandparents and parents were born in the city and in towns. They are not hostage to the divisive passions that the older generation has brought from their villages.
Task is actually smaller
It is for this reason that we have argued for a little modesty by the referendum campaigners, because their task is actually smaller than they think. For the coming generation of Kenyans will remake the country in their own vision. Therefore, there is no need to literally bring the country down over this particular document.
In case they thought they were handing down the Ten Commandments, like the biblical Moses, they are wrong. It's still possible to reach a common ground over the Constitution. It is also possible for the Banana and the Orange camps to deploy the more thoughtful and more moderate voices in their camps to speak to Kenyans and discourage the flame throwers from leading the charge.
Narc collectively has allowed its internal divisions to blind it to the real possibilities available over the Constitution. For one, giving Kenya a new constitution is one of the easiest tasks facing it - if only its politicians can stop listening to their own voices, if they would listen to the rest of the country.

Original_Search_Title: No Camp Pledges Peace At Rally But Tells Rivals to Keep Off
Headline: Kenya: No Camp Pledges Peace At Rally But Tells Rivals to Keep Off
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange camp yesterday pledged to maintain peace during its rally at Nakuru's Afraha Stadium today.
More than 100 MPs would be expected, Eldama Ravine's Musa Sirma and four other MPs said after inspecting the venue.
They asked Banana supporters not to organise a parallel rally in the town as this would lead to hostilities between supporters of the rival camps.
The MPs who toured the stadium yesterday included Mr Moses Cheboi, Mr David Koros, Mr Charles Keter, Mr Joseph Lagat and Mr Joseph Korir.
Accompanied by Rift Valley Orange campaigns co-ordinator Oluoch Lule, the MPs asked the police to provide adequate security.
Nakuru police chief Titus Yoma said security measures were in place and that he would not permit any other campaign meeting today.
Keiyo South elders have accused their MP, Mr Nicholas Biwott, of failing to guide them on how to vote in the November 21 referendum.
They spoke on Thursday evening at Kamwosor, where Katiba Watch activist Martin Shikuku and former State House Comptroller Abraham Kiptanui addressed an Orange meeting.
Mr Biwott had initially backed a middle-ground group of MPs calling for postponement of the vote to allow a consensus. But his Kanu faction recently announced that they would back the Yes vote after the MPs gave up.
The two told the meeting to reject the proposed Constitution, saying it had enhanced presidential powers instead of reducing them.
Mr Shikuku said the Bomas Draft was better as it had distributed power to the President, the deputy president, the prime minister and the Cabinet.
National Heritage minister Najib Balala has accused some politicians of spreading tribalism in Coast Province and told them to be ready to take blame should Kenyans rise against one another.
He claimed some remarks made by Yes campaigners could cause animosity between communities.
Said the minister: "This Government is taking for granted the peace we have enjoyed. We want to tell them that we shall not wait for such things to happen."
The Mvita MP spoke yesterday at Fort Jesus, where he was chief guest during a Ramadhan Iftar get-together.
The minister was reacting to comments by Tourism minister Morris Dzoro, assistant Lands minister Danson Mungatana and assistant Immigration minister Anania Mwaboza that members of one community had illegally allocated themselves large chunks of land in the province.
The trio had claimed that the squatter problem had been contributed by absentee landlords and that the proposed Constitution was the only solution.
Mr Balala said the Bomas Draft was the best for Kenyans and the Coast people and asked the more than 100 Muslim leaders present to reject the proposed Constitution.
Council of Imams and Preachers of Kenya organising secretary Sheikh Mohamed Khalifa said Coast people were divided while other communities had united and used their bargaining power to win concessions from the Government.
"We, at the Coast, are still divided along tribal and religious lines, so we shall continue waiting for mangoes to fall from trees, he said.
Controversial preacher Sheikh Ali Shee urged Muslims to attend his lectures in mosques to know why they should not vote for the proposed Constitution.

Original_Search_Title: Victory Party Plan If Yes Team Wins
Headline: Kenya: Victory Party Plan If Yes Team Wins
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki yesterday promised to host a victory party in State
House if the Banana camp wins the November 21 referendum.
"On November 22, we will be waiting for the announcement of the
result. We shall win and come here to celebrate together," he told a
delegation of the Banana team at State House, Nairobi.
He told a delegation of grassroots leaders from all the eight
constituencies of Nairobi to intensify the campaign to ensure the "Yes" camp
wins.
The delegation was the fourth after Ford Kenya chairman Musikari Kombo
took party MPs to State House followed by Health minister Charity Ngilu, who
led Kamba leaders. Recently, Education minister George Saitoti led Maasai
leaders to meet the President over the "Yes" campaigns.
Present yesterday were Internal Security minister John Michuki,
Assistant ministers Maina Kamanda, Betty Tett and Beth Mugo, Nominated MP
Njoki Ndung'u, Narc Chief Whip Norman Nyaga and Nairobi Mayor Dick
Wathika.
Others were chairman of the NGO Co-ordinating Council Orie Rogo Manduli,
Nairobi Provincial Commissioner Francis Sigei and councillors in the Banana
camp from Nairobi.
Kamanda said Assistant minister Fred Gumo, who is the Westlands MP, had
sent apologies because he was out of the country on official duty.
The President said he was confident on November 22, the referendum
results would be announced in favour of the "Yes" camp".
"Nobody wants to rig the polls. And no one will be blocked from voting,"
he said.
He said the decision of the majority on the referendum should be
respected.
He urged Kenyans to exercise their democratic right and turn up to vote,
saying he wanted to see the campaigns on the referendum held in a peaceful
manner.
President Kibaki reassured Kenyans that no one's land would be taken
away and called for truth over the proposed new constitution.
The Head of State said revenue collection had tripled and said all roads
in Nairobi had been surveyed and would be done properly.
"We are going to do all the roads in Nairobi. We have collected enough
revenue and donors will also give us funds," he said.
He said the roads from Southern Sudan to Mombasa would also be
done.
Meanwhile, the Nairobi MPs urged the President to crack the whip on
'rebel' Cabinet ministers.
The leaders also made various requests on land and asked that Nairobi be
subdivided into four districts and more constituencies created.
"The President has tolerated the behaviour of some ministers for a long
time. Former presidents would have fired these ministers," said Tett.
Nyaga said: "The tolerance Kibaki exhibits is God-given. But if I were
to be the President for five minutes "

Original_Search_Title: Muslims Denounce Harassment By State
Headline: Kenya: Muslims Denounce Harassment By State
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Hundreds of Muslim faithful yesterday protested at alleged harassment
and violation of their rights by the Government.
Led by Sheikh Al-Amin Kimathi, they urged the State to stop
indiscriminate intimidation and arbitrary crackdown on Muslims in the guise
of fighting terrorism.
"There has been wanton suffering occasioned by the State-sanctioned
violations of individual rights of Muslims which should be stopped
immediately," Sheikh Al-Amin said.
Muslims, chanting Koran teachings and carrying banners, blocked the road
near Jamia Mosque for about two hours as speakers condemned the Government
for discriminating against them.
They denounced the reintroduction of Terrorism Bill even after being
rejected by Parliament.
The Al- Sheikh said it was unfortunate that the State seems to be taking
instructions from the US government, yet Kenya is a sovereign state.
As prayers continued in the mosque, several police officers in full riot
gear readied for any eventuality following reports that there would be a
march to police headquarters to seek audience with Police Commissioner,
Major-General Ali Hussein.
Al-Amin announced the launch of a countrywide Muslims human rights
forum.
He castigated the recent arrest of a group of Muslims on suspicion of
terrorism, adding that the Wako draft, which does not cater for their
rights, should be rejected during the November 21 referendum.
"The Government is extending draconian and illegal laws by victimising
Muslims in the name of fighting terror," he said.
He appealed for the restoration of all Muslim charity organisations
wound up in the name of combating terrorism.
Amid chants of "Enough is enough!" , Al-Amin said the community would no
longer sit back as their brothers were brutalised by police.
He called for an end to violence and contempt against Muslims in the
northern parts of the country during the recent referendum campaigns.
"We condemn the violence meted on the women in Wajir at the Banana
campaign rally. It is beyond denial that women nurture society and should
not be abused," he said.
The Sheikh said they had launched complaints with the Police
Commissioner and Minister for Internal Security, John Michuki, over the
incident.

Original_Search_Title: Outrage Over Coup Threats By Orange Team
Headline: Kenya: Outrage Over Coup Threats By Orange Team
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Anger - from both the Orange and Banana Campaigns - greeted yesterday's
reports of a coup warning attributed to Cabinet ministers Raila Odinga and
Kalonzo Musyoka.
While President Kibaki and several ministers led the Banana team in
daring anyone to cause chaos and condemning the utterances, the Orange team
said the leaders' utterances had been misconstrued.
Raila and Kalonzo addressed journalists separately in Eldoret and
Nairobi, to react to reports they said implied that they had warned of a
coup if the proposed constitution is passed.
The ministers had both been quoted as warning over the dangers of the
draft constitution, which they said was impossible to amend.
They added that a dictatorial constitution could only be overthrown and
drew parallels from countries where constitutions had been overthrown
through coups.
Yesterday, Raila said "their intentions" had been misconstrued. But the
damage was already done. President Kibaki warned that anybody who tried to
cause trouble would face the full might of the law.
"Who are you threatening? You can not defeat all the Kenyans," said
Kibaki, who was addressing grassroots leaders from all the eight
constituencies of Nairobi.
Present were Internal security minister John Michuki, Assistant
Ministers, Maina Kamanda, Betty Tett, and Beth Mugo, nominated MP Njoki
Ndung'u, Narc Chief Whip Norman Nyaga and Nairobi Mayor Dick Wathika.
"Those who are planning to cause chaos think they have the might. They
cannot defeat God," he said, without naming the ministers' names.
"Atakae leta fujo, tuta onana huko (We will deal with whoever attempts
to cause chaos," he said.
Internal Security minister John Michuki also dared the ministers: " Let
them try. I was appointed to this ministerial position by the
President. They will see."
And Justice minister Kiraitu Murungu warned that Raila and Kalonzo
risked being arrested and charged with treason for their utterances.
"As minister for Justice and Constitutional Affairs I would like to
bring it to the attention of the two ministers that under Section 40 of the
Penal Code any person who plans to overthrow the Government by unlawful
means is guilty of treason, which is punishable by death," he said.
He went on to warn that the Government would deal ruthlessly with any
person who threatened peace and stability.
"The Orange team should therefore tread carefully as they are now
entering a dangerous zone."
Later at a Press Conference at his Harambee House office, Michuki told
Raila and Kalonzo that they were "treading on a very dangerous path."
He said their alleged schemes would not be allowed and "as Kenya's
security forces have for a while been alert and aware of their
intentions".
"If anyone thinks that what happened in 1982 can be replicated, he is
highly mistaken and ill advised," he said in reference to the coup attempt
against the President Moi's government.
Michuki, who was accompanied by PS Cyrus Gituai and Government spokesman
Alfred Mutua, accused the Orange team of hatching a plot to incite a
rebellion in the country.
And Local Government Minister Musikari Kombo challenged the ministers to
stage the alleged coup now.
He said coups had been taken over by events in Africa and any leader
calling for the same was "dangerous."
In Kisii, Energy minister Simeon Nyachae led nine other MPs in asking
Kibaki to probe the ministers' alleged coup claims.
Speaking at Kiogoro in Nyachae's Nyaribari Chache constituency during a
rally to drum up support for the Wako Draft, the legislators petitioned the
President to take the alleged threats seriously.
"If No wins, we shall carry on with the current Constitution and the
government that was popularly elected by the people will remain in
power. This is not a general election," said Nyachae.
"And If Yes carries the day, they should respect the wishes of the
majority by conceding defeat, " he added.
Other MPs present were Henry Obwocha (West Mugirango), Jimmy Angwenyi
(Kitutu Masaba) and Godfrey Masanya (North Mugirango).
As the ministers' utterances continued to generate heat, the Orange
campaign called a press conference in Nairobi, which was addressed by
Kalonzo, as Raila denied the reports in Eldoret.
"The media has totally misconstrued our intentions by implying that we
are planning to bring instability if we lose in the referendum," Raila told
journalists.
"We are democrats and we will accept the verdict of Kenyans if we lose
fairly," he claimed.
He went on: "If there is an attempt to rig the polls and impose an
undemocratic constitution on Kenyans, we will not accept the results." He
did not elaborate but asked his Banana counterparts to do the same if they
are defeated.
"If the Yes team does not intend to rig the poll, they have nothing to
fear. But if they have those intentions, we will join Kenyans to resist such
plans," he said.
And Kalonzo said: "We wish to refute in the strongest terms possible the
insinuation and implications assigned to our remarks."
But Kalonzo said the thrust of what they said lay in the fact that once
enacted, the draft constitution would be practically un-amendable.
"Hence, there is need to reject it in order to avert any possible
recourse to military intervention as the only open option in future as has
happened in other countries," he said.
The said the Orange team's firm belief in democratic ideals and
abhorrence of military dictatorship were unshakable.
And Kanu chairman Uhuru Kenyatta, who accompanied Raila to Eldoret as
the Orange team headed for a rally in Kapsabet, described the claims that
the NO team was plotting a coup as misplaced, misguided and aimed at causing
fear and panic.
Uhuru said the Orange team wanted the country to remain united after the
November 21 poll.
"This is not a general election and the present Government will still be
in place. It will not go anywhere," he said.
Later yesterday, Kiraitu teamed up with his Finance counterpart David
Mwiraria and seven other Meru MPs in vilifying Raila and Kalonzo.
Among the MPs were Peter Munya , Raphael Muriungi and assistant
ministers Petkay Miriti and Dr Kilemi Mwiria.
They spoke in Ntonyiri constituency where they held two rallies at Raare
and Mutuati shopping centres to drum up support for the Wako Draft.
Kabete MP Paul Muite called a press conference in Nairobi at which he
branded Raila "a despot, who is determined to seize power using any means."
Muite was accompanied by Assistant minister Joshua Toro, MPs Patrick
Muiruri, Peter Kenneth, Kembi Getura and the Banana secretariat boss Titus
Mbathi.

Original_Search_Title: It's Nonsense, Kivuitu Tells Raila
Headline: Kenya: It's Nonsense, Kivuitu Tells Raila
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Electoral Commission of Kenya chairman Samuel Kivuitu has dismissed
Roads minister Raila Odinga's claims that the commission is
compromised.
Kivuitu termed Raila's claims as "absolute nonsense", adding that ECK
held a meeting with two ministers for Raila's own good.
"I'm priceless, I'm not such a cheap person who can be bought. I am now
daring him to repeat that on Tuesday when ECK will be meeting with campaign
committees from both sides," warned Kivuitu.
He said they met the ministers because the Banana and Orange camps had
requested ECK for funds from the Government to pay polling agents.
On Thursday, Raila told lawyers in Nairobi he was aware of on-going
talks between the Yes camp and ECK to influence referendum result.
"The Orange team will only accept the results if they will be free, fair
and without any interference from any quarters," said Raila.
Kivuitu warned that he could go to court to protect his integrity from
unnecessary defamation.
He said ECK wrote to the Finance minister to communicate the request for
financial support.
Finance minister David Mwiraria then invited ECK for a meeting.
Also present at the meeting, he said, was Justice minister Kiraitu
Murungi.
"The minister agreed to make a proposal through Parliament for the money
to be allocated from the consolidated fund," said Kivuitu.
The commission, he said, also approached donor agencies in Nairobi to
pay agents after the Orange team came up with the proposal.
Representatives of the European Union, the British government and other
donors promised to discuss the matter with ECK, he said.
Kivuitu said ECK did not have capacity to arrest all
trouble-shooters.
He was speaking after addressing students at Strathmore University. He
blamed local leaders for having no respect for institutions such as the
ECK.
"Here, the only institution they respect is Armed Forces because not
even courts and the legislature are given the respect they deserve," he
said.
He said 80 per cent of voters do not know what they will be voting for
because they have not been educated on what the Wako Draft contains.
He said Kenyan leaders would only become accountable when voters stop
treating them as demigods.

Original_Search_Title: Security Tightened Ahead of Tuju Rally
Headline: Kenya: Security Tightened Ahead of Tuju Rally
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Security was tight in Kisumu yesterday ahead of Cabinet minister Raphael
Tuju's Yes rally today.
Tuju will host six Cabinet ministers to drum up support for the proposed
constitution.
Despite running into hostility for three consecutive days as he
attempted to popularise his People's Progressive Party and shore up support
for the Yes team, Tuju plans to host the ministers at Moi stadium, in
Kisumu.
"Violence is a reflection of fear by those who organise the goons. They
go to my constituency but let them ask themselves why I do not retaliate,"
Tuju said.
Yesterday, the lakeside town remained tense as local leaders and
residents asked the Rarieda MP to keep off the venue.
Police said they would mobilise up to 3,000 uniformed and plainclothes
officers to ensure security for the Banana campaigners.
They said they would adjust the numbers if necessary.
Kisumu Town East MP Gor Sungu, whose constituency is the venue of
today's rally, urged Tuju to keep off and instead campaign in his Rarieda
backyard.
"I appeal to Tuju to take his campaigns elsewhere... Kisumu people have
taken a stand, which is irreversible. It is a big No!" Sungu said by
phone.
Sungu said Kisumu people were bitter with Banana advocates and
supporters who sponsored violence on Orange luminaries led by Raila Odinga
in Nanyuki last weekend.
"I'm not denying him (Tuju) a chance It is his democratic right to hold
a rally anywhere but let him begin in Rarieda," Sungu said.
But Tuju said violence was being orchestrated and sponsored by rivals
terrified by the truth he is preaching.
Kisumu Mayor Prisca Auma said she would have nothing to do with the
Banana rally after police turned down her application for a parallel
rally.
"We are waiting to gauge how popular Tuju is at the end of the day and
this will help us strategise against him," she said.
Yesterday, Tuju did not indicate which ministers would accompany him to
the function, saying he was awaiting confirmation.
Tuju said he was least disturbed by the hostility he ran into in Migori,
Gem and Nyando as he campaigned for the Wako draft early this week.
"I'm not scared at all Not even the burning of my friend Bonny
Khalwale's car moves me. I'm a calm person I rarely panic," he said.
He said those who spearhead violence were afraid of the truth.
"Let Kenyans decide the vote but we must reason and give opponents a
chance," Tuju said.
Nyanza deputy Provincial Police Officer Joseph Oletito assured the rally
organisers of security.
"My officers will ensure the Banana rally goes on peacefully and
successfully. We will not entertain any hooliganism whatsoever," he
said.
Tuju was pelted with oranges in Migori, Mbita and Nyando early this
week.
On Tuesday, his chopper could not land in Gem, Siaya District, after
residents lit bonfires in a field where he was expected to address a
meeting.

Original_Search_Title: Campaigns Not About Elections, Says MP
Headline: Kenya: Campaigns Not About Elections, Says MP
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The campaign on the proposed Constitution is not about the presidency or
the 2007 General Election, an assistant minister said yesterday.
Speaking during a Banana rally at Miu trading centre in Mwala, Finance
assistant minister Mutua Katuku, who is also the area MP said: "We have been
cheated that it is a presidential campaign between Environment minister
Kalonzo Musyoka and President Kibaki ...the truth is that some leaders who
reaped where they did not sow during the past regime are scared of being
told to account for their misdeeds."
Assistant ministers Joshua Toro and Njeru Githae and MPs Koigi Wamwere,
Silvester Wakoli, Katoo ole Metito and Kalembe Ndile, also addressed the
meeting.
Mr Githae, an assistant minister for Justice and Constitutional Affairs,
claimed Transport minister Chris Murungaru's travel bans by Britain and the
US were influenced by people campaigning against the proposed
Constitution.
He predicted that some European Union countries would be next in line to
punish Dr Murungaru.
"We are aware that some ministers have been visiting US and Britain
missions in Nairobi for the sole purpose of tarnishing those close to
President Kibaki," said Mr Githae.
Mr Toro, who had earlier opened Miu Bridge, advised the Kamba community
not to fall prey to lies from politicians backing the No vote.
He said the community had the opportunity to enjoy fast development
because the proposed Constitution would give them all the avenues to realise
their political, social and economic goals.
Mr Ndile accused Mr Musyoka of being a puppet of Roads minister Raila
Odinga, leader of Official Opposition Uhuru Kenyatta and Kanu secretary
general William Ruto.
Mr Metito urged the Kamba community to read and understand the Wako
Draft, while Mr Wamwere cautioned that if the Draft was defeated, then
Kenyans would be accused of giving the President enormous powers used by
past regimes to encourage detention without trial, among other
misdeeds.

Original_Search_Title: Scramble for Coast Votes As Poll Nears
Headline: Kenya: Scramble for Coast Votes As Poll Nears
Body_Text:
Nairobi — With just slightly more than three weeks to go before before the poll,
the Orange and the Banana camps in Coast province are mapping out strategies
to outdo each other.
The Banana campaigners appear to have recovered from an initial scare
caused by their Orange rivals, and have been holding successful rallies in
the past few weeks. With each side claiming to have the upper hand, the
region's votes are still up for grabs, with more rallies on the cards.
But the Yes team has a lot to do in Taita-Taveta district where three
out of the four MPs are rooting for the No vote.
Recent petty differences among the local Banana leaders appeared to be
playing into the hands of the rival group.
But Tourism minister Morris Dzoro told the Nation yesterday that they
had been resolved, and that his team was back on a united campaign
trail.
A leading Orange light in the region, Bahari MP Joe Khamisi, exuded
confidence when he told the Nation that his group had lined up several
meetings to be addressed by the national leaders before the voting day.
"We shall be going to Ganze on November 2 to undo what the Banana team
has done there," he said, adding that other rallies will be held in
Taita-Taveta on November 6, in Lamu on November 10 and in Garsen, Mtwapa and
Kisauni on November 11, 12 and 13 in that order.
The Banana team, led by Mr Dzoro, will from today address three rallies
- in Tarasa of Tana River district today, Matsangoni near Malindi tomorrow
and in Kinango on Sunday.
Although the Orange team will not have major rallies in the region this
weekend, it has devised a strategy that ensures continuous campaigns even
when the leaders are attending rallies upcountry.
"We have groups all over the country whose leaders communicate on a
daily basis with the Orange headquarters in Nairobi for instructions," said
National Heritage minister Najib Balala, who is leading the team's push at
the Coast.
He said that through the network of mosques, they had also been able to
relay the message effectively.
The Yes group said, however, that they were confident of winning the
region's 800,000 votes on November 21.
Mr Dzoro, who is spearheading the campaigns in the region, said the
group's MPs had settled their differences with the local secretariat, which
had threatened to scuttle their vote hunt.
An assistant minister for Immigration and Registration, Mr Anania
Mwaboza, said the other day that he would quit the Banana campaigns if the
secretariat did not stop spearheading them.
The secretariat is chaired by former Mvita MP Mohammed Jahazi and Dr
Edwin Muinga the secretary.
They are assisted by businessman and Narc activist Charles
Karigithu.
"Dr Muinga and Mr Karigithu should cease campaigning for the Yes team
because ... we do not recognise them at all," Mr Mwaboza said.
But yesterday, Mr Dzoro said that they had ironed out the differences
and had taken steps to make sure of a Yes victory.
At the same time, the minister condemned violence that had rocked Yes
campaigns in western Kenya and other parts of the country, terming them
"cowardly acts that portray Orange supporters' fear of defeat.
Even though this sort of violence was expected because the country has
never been through a referendum before, it is unfortunate because the
Constitution is a document that will guide Kenyans in generations to come,
and violence should be avoided in the campaigns, he added.
All our campaign machinery is on course, and we hope that we will be
triumphant and deliver the province's vote to the Banana camp in the
referendum.

Original_Search_Title: MP's Vehicle Burnt After Youths Attack Banana Team
Headline: Kenya: MP's Vehicle Burnt After Youths Attack Banana Team
Body_Text:
Nairobi — An MP's vehicle was burnt and several people were injured when Banana
group supporters ran into a hostile Orange group in Kakamega Town
yesterday.
Police questioned six people after the violence, which started when some
youths confronted Ikolomani MP Bonny Khalwale and his supporters near the
main bus terminus.
The MP's Toyota Prado was set ablaze outside the Golden Inn. Several
other cars parked at the hotel were damaged, their windscreens
smashed. Officers sent to the hotel to protect property were overwhelmed by
the youths, who smashed the hotel's windows.
A taxi operator, identified as Mr Simeon Shimanyula, was hit on the head
with a sharp object and seriously injured. Several other people were injured
by youths pelting motorists and shops with stones.
A group of Isukuti drummers who had accompanied the MP while wearing
"Yes" campaign T-shirts were also whipped by their rivals.
Dr Khalwale had earlier been forced to flee from the market, shielded by
aides, when the youths began stoning and pelting him with oranges.
Police fired in air to disperse the youths who had cornered the MP and
some of his supporters when they took refuge in a shop. Calm was restored
after nearly two hours. The local MP, Cabinet minister Newton Kulundu,
condemned the violence. Dr Kulundu, who spoke from Navakholo, said Dr
Khalwale should have consulted him before campaigning in his backyard.
Dr Khalwale was escorted by policemen when he left the Kakamega police
station after recording a statement.

Original_Search_Title: Raila And Kalonzo Warn of Coup Peril
Headline: Kenya: Raila And Kalonzo Warn of Coup Peril
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Passing the proposed Constitution could lead to national instability
with even a military coup, two leading Cabinet ministers warned
yesterday.
Coups all over the world had taken place because unpopular constitutions
had been imposed on the people, they said.
And ministers Raila Odinga and Kalonzo Musyoka added that Kenyans too
could be pushed into a corner where, in Mr Odinga's words, "they would have
no alternative but to rise up against an undemocratic constitutional order."
Mr Musyoka said: "The Wako Draft is inviting Kenyans to the culture of
military coup d'etats while we are a democracy. Let us avoid coup d'etats by
having a Constitution that is amendable."
They told people to vote No to the proposed Constitution to avoid that
happening.
Government spokesman Alfred Mutua later told Reuters news agency that
the remarks were, "very dangerous."
They presupposed "this country is at the brink of war, at the brink of
military takeover," he said.
Dr Mutua went on: "This leads us to worry, what are these people of No
planning when they lose, because already they are talking about losing,
rigging, trying to bring this Government to the ground."
"For somebody to say passing a new Constitution, and because it requires
people to actually sign a petition for it to be amended will cause our
military to overthrow the Government, is actually inciting the people," Dr
Mutua said.
The Cabinet ministers' comments came only a day after Mr Musyoka warned
that the Orange No-vote team would stage a one-month sit-in at Uhuru Park,
Nairobi, if the Banana Yes-vote team rigged the referendum on November 21 to
ensure the proposed Constitution were passed.
And yesterday, Mr Musyoka who spoke before Mr Odinga said: "Where you
have constitutions that are not amendable the only way to deal with those
constitutions is to overthrow them."
Then came his key remark about "the culture of military coup d'etats."
The two were speaking during the launch of a document Our Case Against
the Wako Draft, at the Orange headquarters in Nairobi's Upper Hill.
Mr Odinga named Cuba and Uganda as examples of states where coups took
place because of bad constitutions.
He said: "That is the reason why we should not push Kenyans to a corner
where they have no alternative but to rise up against an undemocratic
constitutional order."
Mr Odinga said: "World over, history of countries is replete with cases
where imposition of undemocratic constitutions have led to revolutions."
He went on: "It is accepetable now that the taking over power in Cuba by
Fulgencio Batista in 1953 and imposition of undemocratic and dictatorial
constitution gave birth to the Cuban revolution.
"Next door in Uganda, the civilian coup of 1966 that overthrew the
independence Constitution and imposed a new one gave rise to Idi Amin."
And he added: "That is the reason why we should not push Kenyans to a
corner where they have no alternative but to rise up against an undemocratic
constitutional order."
The leading lights of the Orange team spoke out as another ugly
incidents marred the campaigning that has whipped up emotions
countrywide.
Rowdy mobs in Kakamega confronted Ikolomani MP Bonny Khalwale who was on
the campaign trail for the Banana team and set fire to his Toyota Prado car,
reducing it to a shell as mobs fought for nearly two hours before police
restored order.
The violence which temporarily affected business in the town was the
latest example of the violence that has dogged campaigning during the last
two months.
In Nyanza Information minister Raphael Tuju's Yes campaigns have been
disrupted by youths supporting the No team.
Appealing for calm yesterday, the influential Luo Council of Elders
called on the people of Nyanza to shun violence and allow the minister and
other Banana campaigners to sell their views freely.
Invited to comment on the rising violence, the Government spokesman, Dr
Mutua, said security was being beefed up throughout the country.
He said: "The Government has a good idea who is organising the violence
and how it is being done. But I can assure you the people involved will
answer for it".
Mr Musyoka's warning about instability came after he remarked that it
would be very difficult to amend the proposed Constitution once it became
law.
And Mr Odinga said it was unfair of the Government to distribute only
the new Draft and ask Kenyans to vote for it.
It would have been better had they distributed an equal number of copies
of the proposed Constitution based on the Wako Draft, the original Bomas
Draft plus the current Constitution so people could compare them all before
making up their minds.
Mr Odinga said it was like asking the legendary athlete Kipchoge Keino
to run 1,500 metres without a competitor and later declaring he had won the
race.
"Won against who?" Mr Odinga asked. "Kipchoge must compete against
somebody."
He added that a parent could not give his child a meal laced with poison
and urge him to eat because he would be taken to hospital.
The Roads minister also asked how illiterate people were expected to
read the new Draft when MPs, churches and Muslims had needed advice and
interpretation from the Attorney General, Mr Amos Wako.
He asked: "Is it not unfair to give the Wako Draft to an illiterate
woman and ask her to read?"
Both the Banana and Orange teams had sought advice from lawyers, he
said.
Mr Musyoka and Mr Odinga were speaking at the launch of a critique, Our
Case Against the Wako Draft, their new website www.orangeno.campaign and
four CDs of music advancing their cause.
Also present were the leader of the Official Opposition, Mr Uhuru
Kenyatta, Cabinet ministers William ole Ntimama and Najib Balala, and Kanu
secretary general William Ruto and his LDP counterpart Mr Joseph
Kamotho.
Mr Kenyatta appealed to well-wishers to help the Orange team to print
more copies of their 24-page critique so it could reach every Kenyan.
He said the No team had decided to publish what they saw as the faults
in the Wako Draft because the materials being distributed by the
Constitution of Kenya Review Commission were only to help the Yes team
win.
Mr Odinga said the Orange team would now be making its submissions to
the people and they would have a final plea on November 19 before the
referendum.

Original_Search_Title: Raila Taken to Task Over His Group's Plans
Headline: Kenya: Raila Taken to Task Over His Group's Plans
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Some members of the Law Society of Kenya (LSK) yesterday walked out on
Roads minister Raila Odinga over a statement on what his Orange would do
should it win.
The walkout followed a stormy session after a luncheon they had hosted
for the minister at The Stanley Hotel, Nairobi, when he said his team would
have a constituent assembly to review the Bomas Draft.
They wanted to hear from the minister - the leading light in the No
campaign - what undertaking his group would give Kenyans should they
win.
But Mr Odinga tried to parry the questions, including whether the
faction would be trusted by Kenyans after he had said there would be no
vacuum as the country would immediately constitute a constituent assembly to
improve on the Bomas Draft.
"The Bomas Draft was not cast on stone and, after November 21, we can
look at it because there are some issues in it which were passed in a hurry
when some people walked out," he said.
It was then that some lawyers stormed out as he explained that he would
prefer to have members of the assembly nominated from across the country
instead of giving Parliament the task.
The Review Act, which has guided constitution-making, is specific that
once Kenyans reject the proposed laws, the Constitution of Kenya Review
Commission will wind up after 30 days and the country will continue under
the current ones. Should the Yes team win, President Kibaki would, according
to the Act, be required to promulgate the new Constitution within two weeks
after which the commission will wind up.
Mr Odinga, however, assured LSK that his camp would accept defeat should
the Banana team win. "We shall accept the people's verdict should we
lose. We are democrats.
"However, we caution against rigging because the people of Kenya will
not accept it. We have told [Electoral Commission chairman Samuel] Kivuitu
not to agree to be taken to an hotel in Mt Kenya to be convinced to
manipulate the vote."
He claimed there was a lot of talking between the ECK and the Banana
camp "and we have been monitoring it".
But he evaded questions on whether there is any more collective
responsibility by the Cabinet and why ministers do not resign before taking
divergent stands.
He was accompanied at the luncheon by Mrs Rebecca Garang, the wife of Dr
John Garang, the Sudanese First Vice-President who died in a plane crash in
July.
She is the minister for Roads and Transport in the Southern Sudanese
government named early this week.
LSK chairman Tom Ojienda declined to declare his organisation's stand in
the debate, saying he preferred getting such a mandate from members who, he
noted, had not done so.
But he said the body would take a stand on Tuesday when the members meet
at Panafric Hotel, Nairobi, to launch civic education.

Original_Search_Title: We Will Support New Law, 140 Councillors Say
Headline: Kenya: We Will Support New Law, 140 Councillors Say
Body_Text:
