Nairobi — Councillors of the three Gusii districts yesterday resolved to support
the Banana team. This followed a meeting with Energy minister Simeon
Nyachae.
"The Orange campaigners were telling us that councils will be dissolved
if the new Constitution is passed, but the issue has been clarified and we
shall support it," the 140 civic leaders said in a statement read by
councillor Ken Maiko Riogi of Matutu ward.
Accompanied by nine MPs, the Kisii, Nyamira and Gucha civic leaders
announced what they called "the Gusii Declaration", in which they vowed to
campaign for the proposed Constitution.
Some councillors had earlier opposed it. They had consulted for six
hours behind closed doors with the Ford People party leader at his Nyosia
home where they raised several issues, including a demand to meet President
Kibaki next week.
"We have resolved that our party boss organise a meeting with the
President before the referendum for us to give him our grievances," said Mr
Riogi.
They also demanded that the Kisii campus of Egerton University be
upgraded into a fully-fledged university.
The leaders also demanded that two more districts be created in their
area if the proposed Constitution is passed.

Original_Search_Title: MP's Car Burnt As Violence Intensifies
Headline: Kenya: MP's Car Burnt As Violence Intensifies
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Youths opposed to the Proposed New Constitution take to the streets in
Kakamega town.
Referendum campaign violence became uglier yesterday when rowdy youths
pounced on a Member of Parliament, set his Sh3m luxury car on fire and
brought an entire town to a standstill for three hours.
Ikolomani MP Bonny Khalwale ran for dear life and hid behind a shop
counter as the marauding youths bayed for his blood in the 10am incident. He
was later rescued by Kakamega DC Patrick Osare and Officer Commanding Police
Division Patrick Safari, who whisked him to the police station.
The attackers then turned their wrath on the MP's car and other
motorists. They stuffed wooden planks into the metallic silver grey Toyota
Prado TX and lit a fire. Soon, it was engulfed in flames - and then reduced
to a grey shell.
Then the mob started stoning motorists and looting shops, forcing
businesses to close.
Scores of people were injured in the chaos. They included the OCPD, who
was stoned as he shielded the MP from attackers. A taxi driver, Mr Simon
Shimanyula, 24, was injured on the head. His wound was stitched and he was
discharged.
Trouble started shortly after Khalwale had addressed a rally at Muyala
Primary School near the town, where he urged people to vote for the proposed
constitution.
Accompanied by a group of a few isukuti dancers the MP started walking
towards the town using the Webuye highway. But as he entered the town he ran
into a charged group of cyclists chanting Orange slogans and singing war and
circumcision songs.
They started pelting the MP and his group with stones and oranges,
forcing him to flee.
The mob then ran to Golden Hotel where the MP's vehicle was parked and
smashed the windows and windscreen before setting it on fire.
Police watched helplessly from the safety of their car.
Later, a shaken Khalwale said from the Kakamega police headquarters that
he had dispatched his driver because he did not need a car in his
"backyard".
Khalwale said he only learnt about his car being burnt from the
media.
The attack on Khalwale came barely a day after Information minister
Raphael Tuju ran into a rowdy mob a few kilometres from Kisumu Town on
Wednesday when he took the Banana campaign to Nyanza.
The minister fled into his helicopter to escape from the mob that was
pelting him with stones.
Yesterday, the Electoral Commission of Kenya blamed the violence on the
police.
Vice-chairman Gabriel Mukele said in a referendum the ECK could only
take action against members of national or constituency campaign committees
involved in violence.
"We cannot take action against crowds which decide to engage in
violence," he said.
Kakamega Town where Khalwale was attacked is in Cabinet minister Newton
Kulundu's constituency. The minister is in the Banana camp too.
Yesterday's violence broke out ahead of a planned visit by President
Kibaki on November 5 to campaign for the proposed constitution.
The town, popularly known as the "city of mulembe" (peaceful town) was
yesterday treated to menacing violence only comparable to the 2002 campaigns
when angry residents
jettisoned Kanu leaders led by then President Moi from the town with
chants of "Rainbow".
The town was under siege for three hours as the mobs sang the popular
circumcision song Ngahoo...Ngahoo as they danced through the main streets,
harassing motorists and looting from them.
Youths wearing white T-shirts emblazoned Yes were stripped of them and
forced to eat oranges in
public before being forced to chant "Yote yawezekana bila ndizi". ( All
is possible without bananas.)
A cartwheel operator Geoffrey Mshera Indeche said he had tried to
forewarn
Khalwale of the danger awaiting him but he was brushed aside.
"I was with Khalwale in Muyala. He addressed the crowd but as he left
the locals asked him for 'chai' but
he just danced to isukuti as headed towards town," Mushere said.
Acting provincial police officer Alfred Ombaba said no arrests had been
made but that the matter was being investigated.
As calm returned after three hours of
violence, Western PC Abdul Mwasera led a team of security chiefs to the
scene where the car shell stood.
He said the perpetrators of the violence would be brought to book.

Original_Search_Title: Orange Team Launches Blueprint On Review
Headline: Kenya: Orange Team Launches Blueprint On Review
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange campaign yesterday launched its blueprint outlining why it is
opposed to the proposed constitution.
The team declared that adopting such a constitution - that is difficult
to amend - would be a recipe for a revolution.
The blueprint titled Our Case Against the Wako Draft raises issues with
the articles on the Republic, Bill of Rights, Land and Property, Environment
and Natural Resources and Representation of the People. It also cites the
chapters on The Legislature, Executive, Judiciary, Devolved Government and
Amendment of the Constitution and explains why the Orange team is opposed to
each provision.
The campaign team launched its website www.OrangeNoCampaign.com and
several music CDs exhorting the reasons to vote No.
The occasion at the campaign's secretariat in Nairobi was attended by
ministers Raila Odinga, Kalonzo Musyoka, Najib Balala, William ole Ntimama,
Leader of Official Opposition Uhuru Kenyatta, Kanu secretary-general William
Ruto and former vice-president Musalia Mudavadi.
The Orange leaders said the blueprint would bear them out on accusations
by the Banana camp that they were spreading falsehoods.
Nominated MP Mutula Kilonzo, who took the group through the "Case", said
Kenyans had not struggled all these years "just for any constitution" but
for a people driven document.
In asking Kenyans to reject the proposed constitution at the November 21
referendum, the blueprint states: "The Wako Draft singularly fails to be an
enduring document that would give you a constitution that guarantees unity,
peace and prosperity."
Kalonzo said the Wako Draft was bad for Christians, Muslims and the
entire country.
"There is no question of only 20 per cent being bad. The whole thing is
poisonous," he said.
He said it was interesting for the Banana group to claim that "hata
ibilisi" (even the devil) would agree that the Wako Draft is good. "We do
not want an ibilisi constitution which would be very difficult to amend
later," he added.
Kalonzo said short of a revolution Kenyans would not be able to change
the Wako Draft if adopted.
Raila said: " We are now launching our case before the people of Kenya
who are the jury."
He said the Orange team would make its submissions for the "Case"
between now and November 19 when it would make its final submissions at
Nyayo Stadium.
Raila said the November 18 Uhuru Park rally would be "the mother" of all
rallies and it is here the Orange team would rest its case and leave it upon
Kenyans to decide.
The minister said it was the height of hypocrisy to insist that Kenyans
must read and understand the Wako Draft, yet even Banana and Orange teams
and religious leaders had sought the services of lawyers, including the
Attorney General, to take them through the Draft.
"If MPs are seeking the services of lawyers, is it not unfair to give it
to an illiterate person and insist someni! (read)," he said.
Raila said it equally did not make sense to give Kenyans the Wako Draft
alone and not provide them with copies of the current Constitution and the
Bomas Draft for comparison.
"It is like telling legendary athlete Kipchoge Keino to compete against
himself in a race," he said.
He accused the Yes team of trying to impose the Wako Draft on Kenyans by
capitalising on their ignorance.
Raila said imposed constitutions had led to revolutions, and cited Cuba
and Uganda as examples.
"We should not push Kenyans to a point where they have no alternative
but to rise up," he said.
Uhuru said the Orange team had raised consistent arguments against the
Wako Draft and was not merely politicking as claimed by the Banana
rivals.
"We are now launching our own case for Kenyans to reject the Wako Draft
and the blueprint concisely puts our case forward," he said.
He dismissed most of the material being used by the Constitution of
Kenya Review Commission for civic education as being geared towards
influencing Kenyans to say Yes without giving them a chance to look at an
alternative.

Original_Search_Title: Voter Turnout Real Test On Who Will Win
Headline: Kenya: Voter Turnout Real Test On Who Will Win
Body_Text:
Nairobi — With just 25 days to go before the referendum, the Orange and Banana
camps are employing different strategies to carry the day.
But the real test on who will win rests on which side will convince its
supporters to turn up in large numbers to vote on November 21.
The two groups fear that the vote will be hit by a poor turnout,
reminiscent of the many by-elections.
A poor turnout was a real possibility because most people did not
realise what was at stake, said an Orange strategist.
In a country where elections centre around political parties and
personalities, the only regions that may see a large turnout are Central and
Nyanza provinces.
The referendum has no political parties and there are no candidates for
the voters to elect.
But this has not dimmed any hopes for the two groups as they battle to
capture voters, expected to be about five million, if the figures in the
2002 General Election are anything to go by.
Then, the Rift Valley Province had the highest number of registered
voters with 2,415,555, while North Eastern Province, at 216,336, had the
lowest.
The province with the second highest number of voters was Eastern
(1,734,209), followed by Central (1,563,084), Nyanza (1,555,986), Western
(1,202,104), Nairobi (884,135) and Coast (879,741).
Changed numbers
Although the number of registered voters may have changed, it may not be
radically different from what it was in 2002.
It is also noteworthy that only 58 per cent of the 10 million registered
voters turned up during those elections. This figure could be lower at the
referendum.
Strategists in both groups concede that unless the Electoral Commission
of Kenya and the Constitution of Kenya Review Commission go out of their way
to encourage a large turnout, then the number of voters could be lower than
five million.
But Makadara MP Reuben Ndolo, who is in the Orange camp, dismissed fears
that the turnout on the polling day could be poor.
"People are eager to reject this document and they will turn out in
large numbers. There's too much at stake," said the MP.
This view was supported by Nairobi mayor Dick Wathika, in the Banana
camp, who said that voters would turn out in large numbers.
"There is too much at stake and Kenyans know it," said the mayor, who is
eyeing the Makadara parliamentary seat in the 2007 General Election.
Mr Ndolo said his team had a strategy of getting Kenyans to the polling
centres to reject the proposed Constitution.
Even if the referendum is hit by voter apathy, it may not be different
from the parliamentary elections, where only about 40 per cent of the people
turned up to vote in his constituency in 2002.
But Ikolomani MP Bonny Khalwale, who is on the Banana side, feared that
a low turnout could benefit the Orange campaign.
"A low turnout will be a close race to call but if it (turnout) is high,
the Yes team will win resoundingly," said Dr Khalwale.
The race for the more than 10 million registered voters has led the
Orange camp to divide itself into three teams led by Cabinet ministers Raila
Odinga and Kalonzo Musyoka; and Official Opposition leader Uhuru
Kenyatta.
Public meetings
The Orange team is using public meetings to rally support throughout the
country.
The three teams have already done a first round of rallies and are
getting ready for the second one starting this weekend.
The Banana team on the other hand, has adopted a low profile, with
regional pointmen meeting small groups before holding public rallies.
Dr Khalwale said the strategy had worked well in western Kenya where
they are targeting 1.2 million Yes votes.

Original_Search_Title: MPs Abandon Bid to Put Off Vote
Headline: Kenya: MPs Abandon Bid to Put Off Vote
Body_Text:
Nairobi — MPs calling for negotiations over the proposed Constitution yesterday
abandoned their quest to have the referendum postponed and joined different
camps.
They announced that they had given up after party leaders failed to back
their proposals.
"We thank many silent majority supporters for their support and prayers,
but regret we have to stop our current efforts now," they said in a
statement released at a press conference in Nairobi.
Sold idea to Kibaki
The group said they had met and sold the idea to President Kibaki,
Vice-President Moody Awori and Cabinet ministers Raila Odinga and Musikari
Kombo, among others.
Although all listened to them, none was willing to support the idea by
action.
Those present at the press conference at Parliament Buildings in Nairobi
were MPs Noah Wekesa, Joseph Nyaga, Zaddock Syong'oh, Raphael Wanjala and
Hassan Abdirahman. Only Emgwen MP Stephen Tarus was absent.
Dr Wekesa, Mr Nyaga, Mr Wanjala and Mr Hassan joined the Yes camp,
whereas Mr Syong'oh said he would support the No team.
Mr Syong'oh said: "The option we have is we continue with the existing
Constitution until we realise when we want a new constitution."
Dr Wekesa, who read the statement on behalf of the group, said: "Our
efforts have been frustrated by senior party bosses from both Yes and No,
who have refused to give the motion (filed in Parliament by the group)
priority, even though it is one of national importance."
He added: "There is no middle ground from today we as leaders are going
to show the way."
Mr Hassan, who had tabled the motion seeking to postpone the referendum,
withdrew it yesterday.
The motion sought to amend section 28 of the Constitution of Kenya
Review Act to set up a panel to receive views on the reforms and oversee
renegotiation of a consensus.
It also proposed formation of a bench of moderators when the review
commission winds up civic education on the proposed laws.
Such a panel would, through the presiding moderator, collect public
views on the reforms and prepare a negotiation agenda.
However, yesterday, the group said their initiative will be proved right
after the November 21 vote, since the country would need reconciliation,
irrespective of the winners.
They accused leaders of failing to heed the need for talks to save the
country from the current divisions.
"We, nonetheless have to accept the reality that senior Kenyans in our
political process are unwilling to yield and wish to go ahead and have a
political fight," Dr Wekesa said.
The group warned of civil strife and deep ethnic division among Kenyans
once the vote is taken.
However, Mr Syong'oh said although they had wound up, other avenues such
as a presidential emergency powers or a court action could still be used to
suspend the referendum.
He warned that if the Yes team wins, the country would be in a danger of
operating under two constitutions. On the other hand, rejection of the
proposed laws would mean the review starting all over again.
The MPs said they had never sat on the fence as alleged, but wanted an
amicable solution to the differing camps.
Mr Nyaga said the group believed in what it stood for, while Mr Wanjala
said both the Banana and Orange teams had made mistakes in their quest for
popularity.
A government ploy
The Orange team dismissed the middle ground group as a ploy by the
Government to get an exit route after it sensed defeat.
However, President Kibaki declared the referendum would not be put
off.
The group wanted the country to have a new Constitution by June 1,
Madaraka Day, next year.
And Mr Abdirahman, accompanied by 15 councillors and Regional
Development minister Mohamed Abdi went to the Yes headquarters where he was
received by Vice-President Moody Awori. Said the MP: "The people of Kenya
are now at crossroads, not because of their making but due to
misunderstanding and misconceptions created by leaders," he said.

Original_Search_Title: EU Offers to Donate Sh80m for Poll Agents
Headline: Kenya: EU Offers to Donate Sh80m for Poll Agents
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The European Union has offered to donate least Sh80 million to help the
Yes and the No teams to pay their agents on the poll day, it was announced
yesterday.
British high commissioner Andrew Woods made the announcement after a
meeting in Nairobi with the Banana and the Orange groups.
The envoy said the meeting was aimed at ensuring a free and fair
poll.
Mr Wood said: "We in the EU would like to see free and fair polls which
will guarantee Kenyans their democratic rights.
"We are keen to ensure that both sides (of the campaigns) think of Kenya
beyond the referendum date."
Although they did not specify the exact amount the EU was expected to
chip in with, the Orange and the Banana groups have suggested that each
agent be paid at least Sh2,000 on the polling day.
According to Electoral Commission records, there are 20,000 polling
stations in 210 constituencies countrywide where polling and vote counting
will take place.
The two teams will be expcted to provide an agent in each, or 40,000
people, which will translate into Sh80 million or 909,000 euros.
ECK has a referendum budget of Sh3 billion, but says it has a shortfall
of Sh300 million.

Original_Search_Title: Civic Education of a Different Kind
Headline: Kenya: Civic Education of a Different Kind
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kencom bus stop in Nairobi. The lunch time crowd has taken its usual
position around the preacher, but drained of the energy to laugh, they
grimace, displaying rows of uneven teeth.
The preacher, who comes to feed souls of people who cannot afford lunch,
has been replaced by a young man who looks fairly well fed.
And why not? He is fed up with the Narc administration under President
Kibaki, he says.
"When Kibaki took over, he promised to do a Mandela," says he seriously,
"One term, then move on. Now Lucy (the First Lady) is saying Kibaki is
getting younger and younger..."
The last sentence is delivered in an affected shrill voice, accompanied
by a swagger.
The man's white sports shoes squeal joyfully on the asphalt, his blue
jeans sags off his waist and the bold writing on his shirt, Highland 70 ,
glows in the sunshine.
This is civic education with a difference, and the great thing is that
Kenyans are listening. They would like to know how the proposed Constitution
will affect them.
Metres away from the preacher, is a campaign of a different sort. A much
younger man is tearing away bits of wire, then coiling them into a string to
hold together his network of batteries.
An alarm wails mournfully as his helicopter made of mud is propelled
round and round. At a glance, the motley items moulded to life by his
creative hands catch your attention, so does the notice on faded blue paper
calling for alms.
"Help me," says the message in Kiswahili, "we need rent because our
mother has been evicted."
The note does not indicate why the mother has been slow in paying her
rent, and why her children have to shoulder the responsibility of raising
it.
On closer inspection, one notices an oversized rooster, half the size of
the chopper, its one leg clutching a compact disc. Scanning even closer, one
notices the tiny newspaper cutting celebrating the boy's creativity.
Listening to the Highland 70 man above the wail of the chopper provides
the missing narrative in the Creative Boy's story.
All of a sudden, every picture moulded by those hands of his adopts a
political meaning. The rooster is Kanu's symbol; the alleged eviction
reflects the fate that befell thousands of Mau Narok residents, and which
could become a political watershed.
And the helicopter adopts a new meaning. This week Information minister
Raphael Tuju's helicopter could not be allowed to land at a school in Gem
constituency, where he was seeking support for his new party. Rowdy youths
lit fire on the landing patch then waved oranges threateningly at the
pilot.
The street activist represents the future of politics. Kenyans, tired of
the disparate voices that say nothing, are desperate to hear any voice of
reason.
And just in case the Banana camp alleges bias - as has been happening
whenever a less favourable viewpoint is presented - here's a little
something to cheer them up.
An elderly woman in Embu was asked to pick between an orange and a
banana. Her response: "Whoever saw a child weaned on oranges?"
Those words of wisdom belie another truism. Since adults have failed so
miserably in laying real issues on the line, would it be prudent to leave
the Constitution to another generation? One that will not eat bananas all
their lives, preach treason on streets, and beg for alms in the name of the
mother?

Original_Search_Title: Policemen Injured As Chaos Mars Tuju Rally
Headline: Kenya: Policemen Injured As Chaos Mars Tuju Rally
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Attempts by Information minister Raphael Tuju to campaign for a Yes vote
in Nyanza were disrupted yesterday for the fourth day when he ran into a
hostile crowd near Kisumu.
Scores of people, among them four policemen, were injured when rowdy
Orange supporters confronted the minister in Ahero and Rabuor
townships.
He ran into trouble as he took the campaign for the proposed
Constitution to the No-vote dominated province, with only 24 days to the
referendum in which more than 11 million registered voters are expected to
vote for or against the proposals.
Police lobbed tear gas canisters on the crowd to protect the Rarieda MP,
who had earlier addressed a meeting at the Ahero multi-purpose centre, where
he urged the Nyanza community to support the Government.
Accompanied by former Nyando MP Otieno Karan and a former personal
assistant to Roads minister Raila Odinga, Mr Herbert Ojwang', the minister
said he had founded the People's Progressive Party to strengthen the
community's bargain for the country's leadership.
"Like good hunters, we should scatter ourselves in all directions to
trap the animal. This is what other communities have done by positioning
themselves in different political parties," Mr Tuju said.
During the skirmishes, the minister's official car was pelted with
stones and oranges as he drove from the centre to the nearby Onjiko primary
school where his campaign helicopter had landed. Vehicles belonging to
several other motorists were also stoned.
Escorted by supporters and guarded by riot police, a defiant Mr Tuju
walked for a about two kilometres from the school to the centre amid shouts
of ndizi ndizi (banana, banana)as the Orange opponents re-grouped.
A police officer was injured on the leg after he was hit with a huge
stone during the riot that disrupted traffic on the busy Kisumu-Kisii road
for nearly 30 minutes.
Rabuor primary school, where Mr Tuju later landed for his second
campaign of the day, was turned into a battle field as Orange supporters
armed with slings, stoned his entourage.
School children scampered for safety as pro-orange supporters surged
towards the school, singing anti-government slogans. They aimed stones at
the helicopter but missed.
Political rejects
Mr Ojwang' said it was wrong for some LDP officials to brand PPP
officials as political rejects.
"Contrary to claims by our detractors, it's the current MPs from this
region who are rejects because they bribed their way into Parliament," Mr
Ojwang' said.
And at Kisumu's Central Police Station, a mob of Orange supporters
demanded a permit to hold a meeting in Kisumu on Saturday.
The group that included mayor Priscah Auma walked into the station in
the afternoon to protest at failure by the police to allow the meeting at
the Moi Stadium, where Mr Tuju is expected to address a meeting on the same
day.
Police officers in riot gear surrounded the office as their boss ordered
the group to leave his office. The protestors accused the police of applying
double standards.
The police boss was however unmoved by the charged group and maintained
he would not grant them a licence to hold any meeting within the town on the
same day citing violence threats.
Mr Kilonzi said: "We are not giving any other licence for any meeting in
the town this Saturday. Mr Tuju and his group made applications earlier and
we granted them permission."
Mr Tuju has also been touring Nyanza to popularise his party - the
People's Progressive Party - which he formed recently.
On Tuesday, a helicopter carrying the minister failed to land at Sagam
primary school in Gem because rowdy youths had lit a bonfire on a field
where it was to land.
The minister had earlier addressed an incident-free meeting in Yala
Town, but the aircraft was unable to land since the pro-Orange youths stoked
the fire as the chopper hovered above.
The minister had also addressed a meeting at Muhoroni and asked the
crowd why the community was hostile to him for working closely with
President Kibaki yet his Roads colleague Raila Odinga had also publicly
stated he was a friend to the President.
On Monday, Mr Tuju received a hostile reception in Mbita but still
addressed meetings in Ndhiwa and Nyatike.
Police had to fire in the air to disperse rowdy youths, who attempted to
block the minister from boarding his helicopter, for a trip to Homa
Bay.
The intruders pelted Mr Tuju's car with stones and oranges before they
were dispersed by police.
On Sunday, a similar reception awaited the minister in neighbouring
Migori, where a group pelted his vehicle with oranges before police moved
in.
The minister, however, vowed to continue with the campaigns saying he
feared no one, and that he had not expected a smooth road since he was
introducing a new ideology in Nyanza.

Original_Search_Title: Yes, No Camps Battle It Out On Radio Talk Show
Headline: Kenya: Yes, No Camps Battle It Out On Radio Talk Show
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Protagonists in the Banana and Orange camps got another chance to defend
the positions they have taken on the proposed constitution, live on
radio.
Powers of the President, and whether the Draft Constitution has reduced
or enhanced them, took centre stage as representatives of both camps battled
it out.
The Orange camp, comprising Environment minister Kalonzo Musyoka and MPs
Mutula Kilonzo and Oloo Aringo, were of the view that the draft creates a
more powerful president.
Their Banana counterparts led by Water minister Martha Karua, Assistant
minister Moses Wetang'ula and MP Koigi Wamwere said presidential powers had
been checked.
Aringo said Kenyans had better reject the document, pointing out that
its gains did not address historical reasons that sent Kenyans clamouring
for a new constitutional order.
The Liberal Democratic Party nominated MP said the Wako Draft's major
problem was creation of an imperial presidency.
Aringo said checks and balances meant to control the presidency would
become impossible because Parliament would be "a compliant
institution".
He urged Kenyans to learn to share power, saying that a powerful
presidency could easily degenerate into dictatorship.
He claimed the Draft Constitution gives the president more powers and
that he can emasculate parliament by appointing half of the members from
outside.
Wetang'ula, on the contrary, said powers of the president had been
greatly checked and that the president would only make certain decisions
with the approval of parliament.
He said the president cannot, for example, allocate land since a
commission will be put in place to manage the resource.
He said it was not true that the draft has created an imperial President
as alleged by Aringo.
Wamwere challenged Aringo to point out one instance where the draft
gives the president more powers.
He said the president cannot detain any person without trial as used to
happen.
Kilonzo outlined 15 reasons, why he does not agree with the proposed
constitution. Among them the issue of holidays left out.
He said Christmas Day had been left out, but Wetang'ula told him that
even the current Constitution does not contain the holiday because it is
internationally recognised.
Wetang'ula agreed with the Orange team that the clause that bars a
presidential candidate from the parliamentary race was unfair.
"But we cannot throw out the entire document because of that," he
said.
Kalonzo said the draft was too difficult to amend, saying it would be
impossible to get the one million signatures required for an amendment.
But Karua said Kenyans wanted to make the constitution difficult to
amend to guard against mischief.
She said it would not be difficult to get the required signatures, only
that Kenyans would be involved in the proposed amendment.
Kalonzo said the current stalemate was a result of the removal of the
two-third-majority rule, required to pass the draft, which was replaced by a
simple majority rule.
He said the process was flawed, was in bad faith and a product of
corruption.
The opposing camps were unanimous that politicians' interpretation of
the draft was contributing to differences in understanding the document
among Kenyans, ahead of the November 21 vote.
This was the second of a similar debate, the first having taken place a
week ago, and hosted by the Media Owners Association.
Organised by the Kenya Christian Broadcasting Association, the Big
Decision Debate was transmitted by Hope FM and aired simultaneously on three
other radio stations-Baraka FM, Bibilia Husema Broadcasting and Sauti ya
Rehema (Sayare).
Karua urged Kenyans to read the document, accusing the No camp of
misinterpreting the draft.
She said the Wako Draft was a negotiated document that "supports public
morality" and that it does not support alien cultural practices such as
homosexuality.
Wetang'ula said that MPs agreed, in Naivasha, to have two levels of
devolution - central and district governments, but the Orange camp claimed
that devolution was to be at three levels.

Original_Search_Title: AG Meeting Sparks Row
Headline: Kenya: AG Meeting Sparks Row
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A meeting between some Muslim leaders and the attorney-general has split the Muslim community.
The closed-door talks at Reef Hotel brought together AG Amos Wako and assistant minister Mirugi Kariuki, on the one hand, and Muslims allied to Supkem, including 400 madrassa teachers, on the other. It was organised by the chairman of Supkem's Mombasa branch, Sheikh Juma Ngao, and his Coast counterpart, Mr Munir Mazrui.
But officials of Muslims for Human Rights (Muhuri), the Council of Imams and Preachers of Kenya (CIPK) and even Supkem yesterday said last weekend's meeting was aimed at driving a wedge between Muslims.
Muhuri programmes co-ordinator Hussein Khalid said that only a selected few had attended the talks, which he described as "a campaign meeting."
Mombasa Supkem branch treasurer Sheikh Abubakar Awadh said he had attended the meeting uninvited, claiming that Mr Kariuki had failed to respond properly to questions by non-partisan Muslims and instead had read only articles that favour the Banana supporters.
Sheikh Awadh said it was wrong for his Supkem colleagues to manipulate madrassa teachers by booking them into an expensive hotel and giving them pocket money to convince others to vote for the proposed Constitution.
CIPK organising secretary Sheikh Mohamed Khalifa said that, although the AG had assured Muslims that kadhis' courts were intact, he had failed to explain why they had been left at the mercy of an Act of Parliament.
"It is not true that kadhis' courts have been retained because Parliament could decide on what to do once the proposed Constitution is passed," he said.
But Sheikh Ngao defended the meeting, saying it had been held in seclusion because some disgruntled Muslims had been planning to disrupt it.
He claimed that some of the officials were bitter because they had not been given money to conduct civic education after declaring their intention to vote against the Constitution.
He said those at the meeting were given cash in lieu of breakfast and lunch because they were fasting. The money to host the meeting had been provided by the Constitution of Kenya Review Commission, he said, adding that the chief kadhi should be a member of the Judicial Service Commission.

Original_Search_Title: Why Shouldn't Kenyans Living Abroad Vote?
Headline: Kenya: Why Shouldn't Kenyans Living Abroad Vote?
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Opinion polls have just been released showing the ratings for both the Orange and Banana campaigns based on a "representative" sample of Kenyans. The interviews were conducted "face-to-face in homes".
This made me think of the many Kenyans living abroad, whether in Dubai or New Orleans, and how they are denied their right to participate on important issues back home.
As a Kenyan living abroad, complete with a national identity card and duly registered at my country's embassy, when did I give up my right to participate in important events such as the Referendum?
In the minds of politicians, Kenyans in the diaspora do not seem to have a stake in what is going on, otherwise they would not be disregarded.
The poll results show trends that may influence the referendum outcome. If Kenyans in the diaspora want to vote, what should they do? Should we tell them to "hold on", especially as at least 25 per cent of Kenyans in the country will not vote because they do not have voters' cards? Should they make that expensive journey back home just to exercise their birthright?
Policy towards emigration
According to anecdotal evidence, 90 per cent of Kenyans who live in the West do so illegally. They can't travel back home. If they did, they would not be allowed re-entry to their guest country.
Worse, not much is known about Kenyans in the diaspora: in which countries are, their numbers and socio-demographic make-up, or their places of origin in Kenya. Are they part of the decennium census? And what is our Government's policy towards emigration?
I don't see why we should take the "roadside" announcements given by politicians when they visit places like Berlin or Johannesburg as official Government policy? What services do our bloated embassies offer Kenyans abroad besides organising Jamhuri Day parties and renewing passports? Somehow, for good or bad, being away from home does change you in a fundamental way.
Firstly, more than ever, you acquire a heightened sense of nationality or "Kenyanness", if you like. You are always "so and so" from Kenya.
Secondly, like other emigrants, you are likely to start this complex relationship with a faraway place called "home". This is mostly through online newspapers and Web sites in which one can chat in native tongues.
Thirdly, unlike the middle and upper class back home where a version of Kenyan English is the medium of communication, being abroad teaches you that no matter how well you speak English, you will always have an "accent" and you are a person of "colour". Forget the talk about diversity and tolerance.
Fourthly, after a long period away from home, facts and fiction start to get mixed up. Your mind conjures images of grandeur and heroic acts of sorting out the myriad problems facing Kenya. Last year, as a mature student in the UK, I was amazed that three out of five Kenyans I met harboured political ambitions.
The mood at home resonates in the lives and plans of Kenyans in diaspora. In 2002, when Narc came into power, politicians momentarily won the hearts and minds of Kenyans living abroad, and they said: "If things get better, we will return home."
There are compelling reasons why Kenyans abroad should form an organised "constituency" with clear needs and well-articulated goals.
First, this referendum touches on issues of dual nationality. If you vote "yes", it means there may be a possibility that it will be considered. If "no", then dual nationality and all that comes with it are shelved.
A few years ago, I met a Kenyan woman in South Africa who was completely shocked because her newly-born twin daughters could not inherit her Kenyan citizenship as her husband was a foreigner. The same rule does not apply to Kenyan men who marry foreigners, implying discrimination on the basis of sex. A change on this is desirable.
Empowering families
Secondly, on average, Kenyans in the diaspora contribute to the country's development. At the micro-level, hard-working Kenyans do menial or professional jobs to support their families back home. They pay school fees, hospital bills, wedding and funeral expenses - you name it. Emigrants send remittances back home.
These heroic Kenyans are like non-governmental organisations: they try to empower their families and communities in a fundamental way that our Government has failed to do. Do they, then, not deserve consideration when it comes to the referendum?
Thirdly, at the macro-level, Kenyans abroad contribute to almost 8 percent of the GDP. Is this contribution not enough for them to be given an opportunity to vote on the referendum?
Ms Njoroge is a social demographer who has lived in Europe for 10 years.

Original_Search_Title: No Easy Walk to Nyanza for Banana Camp Leaders
Headline: Kenya: No Easy Walk to Nyanza for Banana Camp Leaders
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Banana team has been having it rough trying to woo Nyanza voters recently.
Its chief strategist for Luo-Nyanza - Information minister Raphael Tuju - has been facing hostile mobs, which have heckled him and stoned his convoy, especially in Mbita and Migori, widely seen as Orange strongholds.
The Rarieda MP is conducting a high profile campaign in the province together with members of his newly-formed People's Progressive Party (PPP).
Despite strong signals that many residents are behind the Orange team, Mr Tuju has insisted no amount of intimidation would stop him from touring the region.
He says he has been meeting supportive people and that the violence he had encountered in parts of the region was not unique since leading Orange leaders, including Cabinet minister Raila Odinga, had also run into hostile crowds in other parts of the country.
After holding high profile campaign rallies in Nyanza, the Orange team has retreated to the far-flung regions.
Besides Mr Tuju, no other Banana camp leading lights have set foot in Luo Nyanza.
Mr Tuju will this weekend address a campaign rally at Moi Stadium Kisumu and PPP elections director Gor Seme Lango says he has received the nod from police to go ahead with preparations.
"We have been doing well in Nyanza, contrary to what some people believe. We shall surprise them," said Mr Lango.
He added: "We have not been disrupting their rallies. Let them give us a break because we have a right to talk to our supporters. We have changed the hearts of many people. We are sure the Orange team will be shocked come the referendum."

Original_Search_Title: Some Voters Want to Learn But Others Unwilling to Be Taught
Headline: Kenya: Some Voters Want to Learn But Others Unwilling to Be Taught
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Failed. That would be the verdict on efforts by the Constitution of Kenya Review Commission to conduct civic education in Kisumu.
But the team led by Mrs Abida Ali-Aroni would not be entirely to blame. Running into hostile and politically charged protesters, the commissioners never stood a fighting chance to teach the people about the proposed Constitution.
In one incident at the Jomo Kenyatta Sports ground last month, rowdy youths ejected two commissioners before a civic education exercise started. They later burnt copies of the proposed Constitution.
Prof Okoth Ogendo, the commission's vice chairman said mistrust by the public was to blame for the hostility that had in the recent past hit some of his colleagues.
In other rural areas, however, trainers appointed by the commission had achieved substantial progress through a series of meetings in churches and barazas.
The commission yesterday said it will have covered enough ground before the November 21 referendum.
Public relations officer Irungu Ndirangu said the commissioners were on the ground to ensure that voter education went on smoothly through churches and community-based organisations.
And in Isiolo, civic trainers started their work last week.
The CKRC district coordinator, Mr Abdulah Jiro, said he had given each group Sh20,000 in advance before the start of the campaign early last week. He said respective groups were to collect the rest of the money from a local bank from yesterday.
But a civic trainer from Ngaremara division, Mr Cosmas Adow claimed that he and his other colleagues had no money to continue with the exercise. Others said Sh100,000 cheques handed to them were yet to mature.
The clause on the rights of children in the proposed Constitution generated debate among men at a civic education seminar in Karatina Town yesterday. Some said it was contentious.
The clause in the chapter on fundamental rights and freedom states: "A child's mother and father whether married to each other or not have an equal responsibility to protect and provide for the child."
Some men wanted to know if the proposed Constitution would be applied in retrospect if passed. Murmurs of disapproval were heard when they were told it would.
The clause allowing women to inherit property was cheered by women but frowned upon by men. Trainers said divorced or unmarried women could inherit their fathers' property.
Civic education has been going on smoothly in most parts of Coast Province despite poor attendance, the Nation has learnt.
Civic education coordinators said most people in the area were interested in getting in-depth information about the proposed Constitution ahead of the referendum.
"We have had some isolated cases of people turning up in the meetings saying that they do not need the education because they have already made up their minds on which side to vote for. But that has not interfered with our work as most people are eager to learn and are demanding more materials to be developed in a language that is easily understandable," said Mr Leonard Gayoye, the Tana River District coordinator.
In Mombasa, the coordinator, Ms Zeinab Ali said all the 20 civic education providers had going on with their duties uninterrupted despite political campaigns.
"Our providers are sticking to the regulations of being non-partisan but of course there are some cases where people would openly say that they do not need the education because of the stand they have already taken," she said.
In Kilifi, those who had attended campaign rallies went to seek clarification on what the politicians had told them.
The district coordinator, Mr Daniel Mwaringa, yesterday said training sessions that started about two weeks ago had been well attended.
In Taita Taveta, attendance at civic education centres was still low because wananchi alleged that the educators were not competent enough to take them through the documents. They said politicians had been able to educate them in a better way.
Mr Mwanyengela Ngali, a Banana camp coordinator, said that commission was biased because it had selected Orange sympathisers to carry out the civic education.

Original_Search_Title: How No And Yes Teams Vary On Campaign Trail
Headline: Kenya: How No And Yes Teams Vary On Campaign Trail
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Banana and Orange camps vary in campaign style and strategy as they do on opinion over the proposed Constitution.
The differences have manifested starkly as the teams scramble for a win in a race that is quickly gaining momentum as November 21 - the voting day - nears.
The Orange side has been campaigning as a team, preferring to traverse the country by air in whistle-stop tours, in which they address crowds in trading centres before holding major rallies.
The leading lights are Opposition leader Uhuru Kenyatta, Cabinet ministers Raila Odinga, Kalonzo Musyoka, Najib Balala, Eldoret North MP William Ruto and a host of MPs, who support the No position.
The Banana team on the other hand appears to have divided the country into zones, which they have assigned to key campaigners, who come from those areas.
Vice-President Moody Awori and Local Government minister Musikari Kombo were at the weekend in Western Province as their Tourism colleague Morris Dzoro, assistant minister Danson Mungatana and MP Suleiman Shakombo hit the Coast trail.
And Cabinet minister John Michuki took the vote-search to his native Murang'a.
It is not clear when President Kibaki will hit the campaign trail to drum up support for the Draft.
The Orange campaigners hit the ground first, moving around long before their Banana opponents started.
Yesterday they released a time-table for their rallies. They will start with a rally in Ngong on Thursday, Kapsabet on Friday, Nakuru on Saturday, Siaya on Sunday and Kisii on Monday.
The Banana campaigners have concentrated on meeting with grassroots leaders from different regions.
President Kibaki met leaders from Rift Valley in Nakuru two weeks ago and then met Maasai and Kamba delegations.
Both teams have experienced hostile receptions in various parts of the country and heroic welcomes in others.
The Orange team has on two occasions taken their campaigns to what are considered to be Yes-vote strongholds - one in Central Province's Thika two months ago and the other in the larger Meru District last week.
Violence in the rallies appears to have subsided in recent days as police keep day-long vigils.

Original_Search_Title: No Camp Demands Action Over Campaign Chaos
Headline: Kenya: No Camp Demands Action Over Campaign Chaos
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange camp has formally written to the chairman of Electoral Commission of Kenya (ECK), Samuel Kivuitu, demanding action against Banana team leaders over referendum violence.
Roads minister, Raila Odinga, yesterday said they had protested to Kivuitu following the violence meted on them at Nanyuki and Wajir campaign rallies on Sunday and Monday.
" We are bitter over the disruption of our rallies by masqueraders hired by Banana leaders after sensing defeat on the referendum," he said.
Raila and his Cabinet colleagues, Jebii Kilimo, Najib Balala, Kanu chairman Uhuru Kenyatta, secretary general William Ruto and former vice president Musalia Mudavadi watched as the police battled the crowds.
Ruto, who is the camp's spokesman, told The Standard they had decided to take up the matter with the commission after the Nanyuki fracas.
"Following what transpired at Nanyuki, we have decided to write to ECK requesting for action against the Banana team which disrupted our rally," added Ruto.
During the Nanyuki rally, the police had to lob teargas canisters to disperse the rowdy youths who engaged them in running battles.
The Orange team pointed an accusing finger to an Assistant minister Mwangi Kiunjuri over alleged incitement of the youth, a fact that the Laikipia east MP later denied.
The incidents come despite a warning by ECK that it would act on leaders out to cause chaos during the campaigns period.
The commission vice-chairman, Gabriel Mukele, recently renewed the warning to politicians not to contravene electoral code of conduct or face a harsh penalty.
"The commission may also suspend politicians who contravenes the electoral code of conduct from campaigning on the referendum," Mukele was reported saying.
Eighty politicians in the Banana and Orange teams have signed the code over the referendum campaigns and promised to abide by it. The politicians from the
Yes group were led by Vice-President Moody Awori and Energy minister Simeon Nyachae while the No team was represented by by Raila and the Uhuru.
The rules stipulate that anyone who violates the code will be liable for a fine of a maximum of Sh50,000 or imprisonment for a term not exceeding three years, or both.

Original_Search_Title: DR Besigye Denied Police Protection
Headline: Uganda: DR Besigye Denied Police Protection
Body_Text:
Kampala — POLICE yesterday declined to offer special protection for Retired
Col. Dr Kizza Besigye who returns from exile today.
The rejection followed a formal request from the opposition Forum for
Democratic Change, the registered party Besigye heads.
Besigye has lived in exile in South Africa since 2001. He arrives today
at 9:05 am aboard a Kenya Airways flight. Yesterday FDC supporters spent
much of the day erecting arches and banners along Entebbe Road welcoming
Besigye home.
While preparing for his homecoming, FDC's party envoy and chairperson of
the Return Besigye Committee, Beti Kamya wrote to government on October 19,
requesting for special protection for the retired colonel.
Police Spokesman Assuman Mugenyi told Daily Monitor yesterday that
Besigye will be treated like any other Ugandan.
"We have had many former presidential candidates return. Herman Ssemujju
is also returning. We cannot go on dividing security. Giving this to Besigye
and that for Ssemujju," he told Daily Monitor by telephone. (Semujju, a
leader of National Freedom Party, has never been nominated a presidential
candidate - Editor)
Mugenyi said Besigye will benefit from the current security enjoyed by
other Ugandans. "Special security will be provided when he is nominated as a
presidential candidate," he said.
Kamya in her letter to Internal Affairs Minister Ruhakana Rugunda on
October 19 said;
"The purpose of this letter is to request you to provide Col Besigye
with security both for his person and residence from the time he arrives in
Uganda until we deem it unnecessary."
Rugunda wrote back on October 25 saying; "I have asked the police to
consider the matter and to take measures they deem necessary."
Police however, have responded with a rejection.
Besigye will be accompanied by FDC supporters from South Africa, his
wife Winnie Byanyima and Ms Salaamu Musumba, acting FDC chairperson and Ms
Beatrice Alaso who flew to Nairobi yesterday.
Besigye, the runner up in the disputed 2001 presidential elections won
by President Museveni, fled the country in the same year citing political
persecution by security agencies.
Besigye is returning to beat the Electoral Commission (EC) registration
deadline of October 28 after the commission announced that Besigye had been
deregistered because his name didn't appear on the new photographic
register.
President Museveni in a recent letter to Cabinet said he had no problem
with Besigye returning, but warned that should there be allegations of his
involvement in criminal activities "the normal course of investigations will
be carried out and the law will take its due course."
His arrest
To counter this, FDC party members say they have lined up 40 lawyers to
fight Besigye's legal battles. If he's arrested on return the lawyers are
ready to work on his release on bail.
At his farewell party in South Africa, Besigye vowed to resist
intimidation from Mr Museveni.
"Every offence, according to the laws of Uganda, is bailable and we are
prepared to bail him (Besigye) should he for some reason or other be
arrested upon return," Kamya told Daily Monitor yesterday at the FDC offices
in Najjanankumbi near Kampala. Asked how much money they had put aside to
pay for legal battles, she replied, "that is classified; but we have more
than would be enough."
Besigye procession
Though FDC has lined up a fleet of cars from all over the country to
transport their supporters eager to receive Besigye at the airport - police
said only five vehicles will be allowed to go through to Entebbe
International Airport.
"You know that Entebbe is an international airport handling both
domestic and international flights. We don't want any interruptions, so we
shall not allow a procession to the airport," Mugenyi explained.
He said the procession will be forced to wait at Muzinga park, just
after Kituburu along Entebbe Road.
On the eve of arrival
Yesterday there was a mini bonanza at FDC offices as throngs of people
marched in to collect paper flags, caps, and T-shirts they would use to
welcome their party chairman.
Several buses carrying supporters from all districts were expected to
arrive yesterday evening and head straight to Entebbe for an overnight party
ahead of Besigye's arrival. Mr Kagulire Sebbowa, the chairman of Entebbe FDC
branch organised the party.
Arches with welcoming messages were also erected at key locations along
Entebbe Road, notably, right outside the FDC offices. Truckloads of banana
stems were visible along Entebbe Road as volunteers helped place them on
poles at the roadsides. There was also another team of volunteers placing
Besigye posters on walls and poles reading, "Col. (Rtd) Dr Kiiza Besigye
welcome home." "One People, One Uganda."
While all this happened, a truck with a public address system made
rounds playing loud music and announcing the return of the party chairman
and calling on all people to come and show their support.
FDC had planned the first rally by Besigye to be held at the
Constitution Square right in the heart of Kampala at 2 pm.
"But I have advised them to change it to Kololo airstrip.
"It's unfavourable given the number, the time and location of the
venue," Mugenyi said.
He also ruled out a crowd escorting Besigye to register at the Electoral
Commission headquarters. Besigye is expected to register as a voter at the
EC headquarters by October 28.

Original_Search_Title: Hostile Crowd Bars Tuju Chopper
Headline: Kenya: Hostile Crowd Bars Tuju Chopper
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Information Minister Raphael Tuju ran into trouble for the third straight day yesterday with his Banana campaign.
Youths lit a bonfire on a landing field in Siaya, preventing a helicopter that was carrying the Rarieda MP from landing.
A hostile crowd armed with oranges waited on a school football pitch in Gem constituency where the pilot made an abortive attempt to land.
Huge logs and jerricans of petrol were ferried to the field in a pick-up moments after the helicopter carrying the minister flew past the school in Nyawara, Central Gem.
But chaos reigned when the helicopter attempted to land at Sagam, with hundreds of Orange supporters carrying dry leaves and twigs which they threw into the fire, fuelling the blaze.
The pilot made an attempt to land in a far corner of the playing field but the crowd surged forward, forcing him to take off.

Original_Search_Title: ZIFA Pay Tribute to Ex-Chairman Rusike
Headline: Zimbabwe: ZIFA Pay Tribute to Ex-Chairman Rusike
Body_Text:
Harare — ZIFA have paid tribute to the association's former chairman Joseph Rusike and said his death had robbed domestic football of yet another seasoned administrator.
Rusike, who was also the Zifa Council president, collapsed and died at his Bulawayo home on Sunday morning.
He was 66.
The former Zimbabwe Saints boss, who is survived by three sons and a daughter, is expected to be cremated in Bulawayo tomorrow.
As news of Rusike's death filtered in the capital on Sunday, a minute of silence in his honour was observed before the start of the Premiership clash between CAPS United and Highlanders at the National Sports Stadium.
Both Zifa and the Premier Soccer League also sent messages of condolence to the Rusike family yesterday.
Jonathan Mashingaidze, the Zifa chief executive, said yesterday his association had been saddened by the news of Rusike's death, which came just a week after the soccer mother body had also lost one of its illustrious leaders, Brian Harry.
Mashingaidze said Rusike's death had come at a time when the former Zifa interim chairman had been expected to form part of the nomination court to prepare for elections to choose the association's new leadership.
"It is a great loss to football, hardly a week after losing Brian Harry, we then lose Joseph Rusike, who was one of our life members. He came into football through Zimbabwe Saints and rose through the Zifa ranks to become Zifa Council president and a life member.
"It is sad that he has passed away before the elections for which he was a member of the nomination court, had been held.
"His contributions were always valuable to football and we hope that the work he did for the game is going to be an inspiration to the up and coming administrators," said Mashingaidze.
At the time of his death, Rusike, a Bulawayo businessman, had been working to ensure the success of the road map designed for Zifa by world soccer governing body, Fifa, which was aimed at bringing lasting sanity to a local game riddled by boardroom squabbles.
PSL chief executive Chris Sambo said he was saddened by the fact that the veteran administrator had not lived to see the conclusion of that road map.
"He seemed very well when I spoke to him last Friday when he was inquiring about Mamelodi's (Fifa official) visit on the road map.
"It is a sad loss to football in Zimbabwe. From my own experience working with him, he was probably one of the most consistent and principled leaders, who was against petty politics in football," said Sambo.
Rusike took charge of Zifa on an interim basis in June 1991 following the resignation of the late Nelson Chirwa.
In the period he was in charge of Zifa between June 1991 and May 1992, Rusike led an executive, which included secretary-general Ndumiso Gumede, treasurer Frank Valdemarca, fixtures secretary Moosa Ismail and committee members Mayford Mawere, Paul Timba, Simon Machaya and Newab Khan.
That same leadership also hogged the limelight for defying a Sports Council decision to suspend them from office for conducting elections "in an unconstitutional manner".
Prior to his appointment as interim Zifa chairman, Rusike had also been the Southern Region chairman in 1987.
In February 1992, Rusike was also instrumental in working with the late former Zifa patron Canaan Banana in trying to resolve the in-fighting between the Morrison Sifelani and Jockoniah Nhekairo factions, which had torn Dynamos apart.
A year later, he was appointed the chief co-ordinator to the parties that were fighting for the control of Zimbabwe Saints.

Original_Search_Title: Minister Warns of Cabinet Clean-Up After Poll
Headline: Kenya: Minister Warns of Cabinet Clean-Up After Poll
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki will be under intense pressure to sack so-called "rebel" ministers after the November 21 referendum on the Proposed New Constitution.
Yesterday, Lands minister Amos Kimunya said President Kibaki could no longer share State secrets with "enemies" who have called him names while on the referendum campaign trail.
He told The Standard there would be a clear out after the referendum because the President would have to streamline the Cabinet to ensure effective delivery of services to Kenyans.
Kimunya's remarks indicate that the stands taken by ministers during the referendum campaigns have come to be seen as a measure of their loyalty to the President.
Those in the No (Orange) campaign have been branded rebels and their Yes (Banana) counterparts are seen as loyalists.
Kimunya used a football metaphor to reinforce his argument that some ministers would have to be sidelined after the referendum.
"Just like in a football club, the players are reshuffled and others tossed to the bench and that is what will happen. It's clear that the President has to clean the Cabinet from the way the ministers have behaved themselves."
He accused his Cabinet colleagues in the Orange campaign of engaging in a game of self-destruction by calling the President names and declaring that he was not fit to rule.
Those perceived as rebels for campaigning for the rejection of the proposed document include Mr Raila Odinga (Roads), Mr Kalonzo Musyoka (Environment), Mr Ochillo Ayacko (Sports), Prof Anyang' Nyong'o (Planning), Mr Najib Balala (Heritage), Mr William Ntimama (Office of the President and Mrs Lenah Jebii Kilimo (Immigration).
Kimunya's statement would appear to be a shot across the bows of the Orange campaign from a section of operatives believed to be close to the President.
Its import is that the President's inner circle could force his reluctant hand into cleaning up the Cabinet.
However when contacted, Raila dismissed Kimunya as a busybody who, though a golf-playing buddy of President Kibaki's, could not dictate Cabinet appointments.
"He is not an assistant of the President's. Nobody knew Kimunya before December 3, 2003," Raila said in reference to his colleague's entry into the Cabinet.
He said he would not take Kimunya's threats of a Cabinet clean up seriously.
" He is a nobody. We cannot listen to him," Raila declared.
Elsewhere, Assistant minister Maina Kamanda hinted that there would be unfinished business after the referendum. "Let us cross the (referendum) bridge before we can talk on that matter."
"I do not want to pre-empt what is going to happen because it is the prerogative of the President," said Kamanda.
"If it is firing them, the President could have done it long time ago. But let's cross the referendum bridge first," he said.
The Cabinet has not met for more than six weeks since the referendum politics began. The meetings were, however, suspended officially recently to allow ministers campaign for the referendum.
Asked to comment, Kabete MP Paul Muite said: "I do not wish to comment on that, but it is the prerogative of the President on matters pertaining to the appointment and firing of Cabinet ministers"
The remarks come only 24 hours after Raila said President Kibaki was still his political friend. "The constitution issue is not a Government project. It is the people's," said Raila.
He said that no minister had powers to intimidate any of his colleagues on crucial matters of the constitution, which is a people's project.
But, the Orange team declared that matters of the review of the constitution were not for the Government, but for the people.
The Orange spokesman, William Ruto, said the Banana team preaches water and drinks wine.
"Why do they tell people to read the document and make informed choices and then intimidate others? The matter of the constitution does not reflect who is loyal to the President and who is not," Ruto said.
He added that those in the Banana team had been defeated and had started introducing useless subjects in the referendum debate.
"This constitution is fake and it must be rejected even if they threaten to sack the other ministers who are with the people," Ruto said.
He said the constitution review process was in a jam today because the Banana team did not want to talk to Kenyans.
Mathioya MP Joseph Kamotho asked: "Who is in charge of this country. Is it the ministers or the President"?
He said it had become a habit for some ministers and assistant ministers to determine the fate of their colleagues in the Government.
"I wish to remind them that the constitutional prerogative of appointing ministers and their assistants rests with the President," Kamotho said.
He said such statements undermined the institution of the Presidency and raised questions on who is in charge of the country.
"If anything, it is the ministers who should be sacked for usurping the powers and authority of the President," Kamotho said.
He said the views of ministers on the proposed constitution couldn't be used to measure their loyalty or disloyalty to the President.

Original_Search_Title: Christian Stations to Air Debate On Draft
Headline: Kenya: Christian Stations to Air Debate On Draft
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Christian radio stations countrywide will today broadcast a live debate on the Proposed New Constitution for three hours, starting from 7.30pm.
The debate, dubbed The Big Decision, has been organised by the Kenya Christian Broadcasters Association and will be aired from Hope FM studios in Nairobi.
The debate's moderator, Reverend Elly Rop, told The Standard in Eldoret yesterday that guests will be luminaries from the Orange and Banana camps.
The Orange team will be represented by Roads minister Raila Odinga, his Environment counterpart Kalonzo Musyoka and nominated MP Mutula Kilonzo.
The Banana side will comprise Energy minister Simeon Nyachae, and assistant ministers Mirugi Kariuki and Moses Wetang'ula.
A spokesman for the Orange Secretariat, Mr David Makali, confirmed the live broadcast is on.
"All of them have confirmed participation, save for Mr Nyachae. But we are optimistic that he will honour our invitation," said Rop, the Full Gospel Church, North Rift, overseer.
He said the show would be different from The Big Debate, organised recently by the Media Owners Association of Kenya. The Big Debate was aired on almost all TV and radio stations.
Rop said the camps will not debate on the proposed constitution but will instead give presentations followed by questions based on their speeches.
"A panel of bishops will pose questions. Guests from opposing camps will also be given a chance to pose questions to each other," said Rop.
He added that listeners would get chances to call in and pose questions, unlike in The Big Debate where a panel of journalists posed the questions on their behalf.

Original_Search_Title: Bid to Disrupt Orange Rally Turns Bloody
Headline: Kenya: Bid to Disrupt Orange Rally Turns Bloody
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Five people, including a policeman and a former Member of Parliament, were seriously injured when crowds tried to disrupt an Orange rally in Wajir yesterday.
The Orange team, comprising Cabinet ministers Raila Odinga, Jebii Kilimo and Najib Balala and Kanu chairman Uhuru Kenyatta and secretary-general William Ruto, watched as police battled the crowds.
The team had taken the No campaign to Regional Development minister Mohammud Abdi's backyard in a bid to woo some of the 82, 000 registered voters in the district into rejecting the Wako Draft at the November 21 referendum.
But as the politicians started addressing the crowds, supporters of the Banana team started waving banners and chanting: "Yes! Yes! Yes!"
The Orange supporters then started chanting "Maya! Maya! Maya! (No! No! No!)" and the shouting match soon degenerated into a free for all.
The rival camps used an array of missiles, including stones, sand and sticks, to fell and beat their targets.
Labour Assistant minister Mohammed Khalif, who was the host in his home district, was seen pleading with the
police to repulse the surging mob as missiles flew past the dais.
Anti-riot police, backed by prison warders, regular and administration police and reservists, then moved in, clobbering and dispersing the Banana supporters.
Wajir Stadium was left looking like a battlefield with trails of blood, used teargas canisters and spent cartridges scattered all over.
Former Wajir East MP, Abdisalan Mohammed, and a police inspector, Jean Kemboi, were among those injured in the fracas.
Last night, The Standard confirmed that Kemboi, who broke a collarbone, was being transferred to Nairobi for further treatment.
Some of those injured in yesterday's fracas were treated at the district hospital.
Area police chief Julius Kitili said six people, including two teachers, had been arrested in connection with the bloody confrontation.
A statement issued by Police spokesman Jaspher Ombati last evening said the six had been charged in a Wajir court with creating a disturbance. The statement added that a councillor had been arrested later and would be charged with assaulting a police officer and creating a disturbance.
"We wish to further reiterate that police will continue to take action against any person who will attempt to disrupt campaign meetings," Ombati said.
Yesterday's disruption of the Orange rally was apparently in retaliation to an incident a fortnight ago where No supporters stoned five Cabinet ministers campaigning for the Yes vote.
The violence accentuated a fresh escalation just days after the start of the official campaign period. Electoral Commission of Kenya chairman, Samuel Kivuitu, had earlier warned that he would not entertain any further hostilities.
Earlier, the stabbing of a security guard at a local lodge where Khalif was organising the rally had cast a dark shadow over the meeting. Police say the guard was stabbed as he tried to restrain a crowd that was demanding cash from the Wajir West MP.
Once calm had been restored at the stadium, Raila lashed out at his Regional Development counterpart for moving from the opposition to the Government of National Unity.
"Mohammud behaved like a hyena scavenging for carcasses that are far-apart," he told the crowd.
He said it was disheartening to see the MP (Mohammud) abandon his people to a "losing bandwagon".
"The croaking frogs cannot not the stop campaign," he said.
He said the Bomas Draft had agitated for a federal system of government, but a clique of a few individuals wanted to lump power on one person.
Kilimo said the proponents of the Wako Draft had admitted the draft was 20 per cent flawed; yet they wanted it passed.
It would be difficult to amend the contentious issues once passed, she said. She said the draft was not specific on number of women representatives in Parliament.
Balala said passing the draft would allow Muslim rights to be trampled further.
He said the Kadhis' courts had been reduced to irrelevance with the introduction of Christian courts.
Uhuru accused President Kibaki of abusing democracy by poaching opposition MPs to his wrangling coalition.
He said the Wako Draft did not cater for the marginalised communities, yet Mohammud supported it "blindly".
He asked residents to receive relief handouts the Government was dishing out but vote No; saying food was their fundamental right.
Ruto said the banana camp had not told the residents what was good in the draft.

Original_Search_Title: Nyachae to Host Councillors in Bid to Counter Orange Team
Headline: Kenya: Nyachae to Host Councillors in Bid to Counter Orange Team
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Cabinet minister Simeon Nyachae has invited all councillors from Kisii, Nyamira and Gucha districts to a consultative meeting in a bid to drum up support for the Wako Draft.
The 147 civic leaders are expected to attend the meeting to be held a day before the Orange and Banana teams' campaign rallies in Kisii.
Nyachae is scheduled to hold a rally at Kiogoro trading centre in his Nyaribari Chache constituency on October 28, while No crusaders will be at Gusii Stadium two days later.
Kisii Mayor Stephen Omurwa, who is co-ordinating the Thursday meeting at the minister's Nyosia home, said all councillors, including Orange sympathisers, had been invited.
"Nyachae, as our party leader, has requested to meet all councillors and talk to them on need to support the proposed constitution," said Omurwa.
Sources told The Standard that the Energy minister wants to listen to the civic leaders' persistent complaints that MPs were isolating them.
"Many councillors have joined the Orange camp as a protest to the manner their MPs were treating them. Nyachae, as the Ford People boss, has accepted to listen to their grievances," said the mayor.
The minister has vowed to use his resources and influence to change the strong wave of the No team, which includes his party MP Omingo Magara.
But some said they would attend the Nyachae's meeting "just hear what he might be having for us".
Elsewhere, Assistant minister Mwangi Kiunjuri has refuted claims that he was behind the violence, which rocked Nanyuki on Sunday during Orange campaign rally.
Yesterday, Kiunjuri said he was not anywhere near the town at the time.
"When they were having their rally, I was 100 kilometres away at Kinamba in Laikipia West attending a Banana campaign meeting, and on Saturday, I was in Naivasha. The last time I was in Nanyuki was on Kenyatta Day," Kiunjuri said.
The No team leaders Raila Odinga and Uhuru Kenyatta had blamed Kiunjuri for the disruption of their meeting.
But the Energy Assistant minister scoffed at the claims, saying they could not convince wananchi.

Original_Search_Title: Goldenberg Report 'Is Ready'
Headline: Kenya: Goldenberg Report 'Is Ready'
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Government will release the Goldenberg commission report before the end of this month, Justice Assistant minister Njeru Githae has said.
Githae said the report would shame some Government critics peddling lies about the Proposed New Constitution.
He assured Kenyans the perpetrators of the multi-billion shilling scam would not be spared: "We will implement the recommendations of the commission in total and the culprits will face the full force of the law."
Speaking at Kinamba, Laikipia, during a Banana campaign rally at the weekend, the minister said some leaders now pretending to be clean had acquired wealth through questionable means.
Addressing the crowd, Laikipia East MP, Mwangi Kiunjuri, claimed the Orange team was using funds stolen during the Kanu regime, adding that individuals will soon be made to account their role in the Goldenberg scandal.
"The Government will soon prosecute these people; we are serious in addressing the past, and we will make them pay for their ill deeds," he said.
Kiunjuri noted it was ministers Raila Odinga and Kalonzo Musyoka who were the first to oppose the cross examination of former President Moi at the Justice Bosire Commission.
And speaking at the meeting also attended by Assistant minister Alicen Chelaite and MPs GG Kariuki and Jane Kihara, Subukia legislator Koigi Wamwere warned the electorate to either retain the current "colonial" Constitution or adopt the proposed draft.
He said the current Constitution is a recipe for dictatorship and warned that if the proposed document is rejected, the country would have lost a chance to have a people-driven constitution.
He said the review process has been turned into a power struggle between the pro and anti-Kibaki proponents, adding that those opposing the draft were self-seeking individuals who wanted to gain power.
Chelaite said selfish individuals who are using ethnicity to settle personal political scores have hijacked the Kalenjin community.
The Rongai MP warned that the community may be shunned by the rest of the country it rejected the new constitution.
"In our history as a community, we have never been put in one basket with people from Nyanza who are ever on the opposing side," she said.

Original_Search_Title: Yes And No Campaign Leaders to Be Fined in Case of Violence
Headline: Kenya: Yes And No Campaign Leaders to Be Fined in Case of Violence
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Banana and Orange campaign leaders were yesterday put on notice over
increasing violence.
Energy minister Simeon Nyachae and Nominated MP Mutula Kilonzo will be
fined should their supporters engage in violence again, the Electoral
Commission warned yesterday.
Vice-chairman Gabriel Mukele condemned violence, saying the commission
would not allow the situation to continue as the poll draws near. "We are
going to deal firmly with leaders of the national referendum committees," he
said. "We will impose fines on them if we find that their members are behind
campaign violence."
The warning came as violence marred Yes and No rallies over the
weekend. It disrupted an Orange meeting when Cabinet ministers Raila Odinga
and Kalonzo Musyoka, Official Opposition leader Uhuru Kenyatta and Kanu
secretary-general William Ruto took the vote hunt to Meru and Nanyuki.
The team needed police intervention when they addressed meetings in
Meru, Chuka, Maua, Kianjahi, Nkubu and Kanyakine on Saturday.
Their rivals were given the same treatment in Isiolo. The following day,
the Yes group faced a hostile reception in Nanyuki.
Rallies in Bungoma, Thika and Garissa were also marred by violence as
the Orange and the Banana teams sought the vote.

Original_Search_Title: No Team Takes Vote Search to Minister's Turf
Headline: Kenya: No Team Takes Vote Search to Minister's Turf
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange team took their campaign to Wajir District - the home turf of
Cabinet minister Mohamed Abdi - and asked the residents to reject the
proposed Constitution, saying it was flawed.
A rival group's attempt to disrupt the rally was thwarted by police who
lobbed teargas canisters as they provided security during the rally at Moi
Stadium in Wajir Town.
The rival group heckled the speakers who included Cabinet ministers
Raila Odinga, Anyang' Nyong'o and Linah Kilimo as well as Kanu leaders Uhuru
Kenyatta and William Ruto, and pelted the dais with rocks before police
intervened.
Six people, who were arrested and charged in court with creating
disturbances, were remanded in custody until next week when their case will
be heard.
Area police boss Julius Kitili said a senior officer was injured in the
shoulder, and was treated at the district hospital and discharged.
But he said his officers did not use live bullets during the
disturbances.
The Orange politicians accused some people in the Government of
attempting to impose the proposed Constitution on Kenyans.
"If the draft Constitution is as good and flawless as some in Government
want Kenyans to believe, then they need not campaign for it," said Mr Ruto,
the Kanu secretary-general. "The document would sell itself to the public."
Others at the meeting were MPs Mohamed Ahmed Khalif, Bonaya Godana,
Justin Muturi, Sammy Weah, Charles Keter, Abdullahi Ali, Yusuf Haji, Samuel
Poghisio, Phillip Okundi and Ayiecho Olweny as well as Mombasa mayor Taib
Ali Taib and former Vice-President Musalia Mudavadi.
The leaders dismissed as "suspect" the issuing of title deeds to the
Ogieks and the downgrading of the Amboseli national park to a reserve.
"Why has it become necessary all of a sudden for the Government to give
goodies to selected communities and for the President to start receiving
delegations at State House, something he has not done before?" Mr Ruto
asked.
The residents were asked to accept relief food from the Government as a
right, but reject the proposed Constitution.
The No team said Kanu leaders in Government had lost credibility for
deserting the party that sponsored them to Parliament. "They now have to
support the Yes campaign because it is payback time," Mr Ruto said.
Mr Odinga criticised the recent opinion poll carried out by the Steadman
Group, which showed the Orange group leading with a 10 per cent margin. It
was sponsored by the Yes group to mislead Kenyans that they (Banana team)
would catch up and get the floating voters, he said.
"If it was a genuine opinion poll, why were Kenyans required to talk
about issues outside the Constitution such as the President's and Narc's
popularity?" he posed.
He dismissed it as diversionary, saying that the people involved were
"cheating Kenyans." The Orange team would soon issue the results of a
similar poll showing the Yes group to be ahead, he added.

Original_Search_Title: Kanu Faction to Back Reforms
Headline: Kenya: Kanu Faction to Back Reforms
Body_Text:
