Nairobi — The Kanu faction allied to Keiyo South MP Nicholas Biwott yesterday
vowed to campaign for the proposed Constitution.
The decision, the group said, followed failure by the Middle Ground
Group to have the November 21 referendum postponed. But they asked President
Kibaki to organise another referendum to thrash out contentious issues in
the document.
Faction secretary-general Amukowa Anangwe said: "The proposed
Constitution, although not perfect, is an improvement on the current one
... and a step in the right direction. It should be welcomed by a majority
of Kenyans."
He was addressing a news conference at Methodist Guest House, Nairobi,
attended by the faction's provincial representatives. Faction leader Biwott
was not present.
The former Energy minister has been non-committal on which side to
support but has criticised leaders for deviating from the relevant
issues.
He has, however, campaigned with the Middle Ground Group to have the
November vote postponed.
Bomet MP Nick Salat, Marakwet West's David Sudi and Keiyo North's Lucas
Chepkitony are among Mr Biwott's allies, who have joined the Orange (No)
group, while Dujis MP Maalim Mohamed and Mt Elgon MP John Serut have joined
the Banana (Yes) camp.
Yesterday, Dr Anangwe said his faction had examined the merits and
demerits of the proposals and decided to side with Banana.
He said the bulk of the proposals reflected the contents of the Bomas
Draft, adding: "For now, we shall vote Yes and if another referendum on the
revised draft is held, we shall also vote Yes because it will be an
improvement," he said. Kenyans had waited for 15 years for a new
constitution and more than Sh7 billion had been spent on the review process
and on the referendum and it would be immoral to discard the Wako draft,
particularly as Kenyans were agreed that 80 per cent of it was good.
Dr Anangwe urged the No crusaders to separate the proposed Constitution
from politics and help Kenyans make informed decisions.
He said the referendum should not be used to settle political scores as
such a recourse would be unreasonable and untimely.
The proposed Constitution was for all Kenyans and political leaders
would be missing a golden opportunity to give Kenyans new laws just because
of selfish interests.
A vote against the proposals would perpetuate misrule and
authoritarianism and such an outcome would empower the president to continue
to rule under the current Constitution, a situation most Kenyans would not
cherish.
The Pope's representative in the country has asked Kenyans to maintain
peace in the referendum campaigns.
Apostolic Nuncio Alain Paul Lebeaupin said every Kenyan had the
responsibility to make a wise decision for the benefit of the country and
the generations to come.
"It does not matter which fruit you eat, but use the taste to promote
democracy for the country you love," he said after leading celebrations at
Wote Town, Makueni, to close the Eucharistic year on Sunday.
The colourful ceremony was attended by hundreds of Catholics led by the
local dean, Father John Makewa.

Original_Search_Title: Besigye Dismisses Museveni 'Scare Tactics'
Headline: Uganda: Besigye Dismisses Museveni 'Scare Tactics'
Body_Text:
Kampala — COL. Kizza Besigye has vowed to resist intimidation from President
Yoweri Museveni when he arrives in Uganda.
The former presidential candidate and leader of the opposition Forum for
Democratic Change (FDC) was addressing a farewell gathering of his
supporters at the Institute of Business Studies in Pretoria, South
Africa. He said Museveni always employs intimidation to muzzle political
opponents.
"I return to Uganda to lead the long march to change Uganda from the
strong arm of dictatorship to freedom," Besigye reportedly told an excited
crowd.
"My return is going to be the beginning of the third phase of our
struggle to end dictatorial rule by Museveni in Uganda."
He said the first phase of the struggle was in the 2001 presidential
elections that he claims Museveni rigged and that the second phase has been
to mobilise international awareness of what is happening in Uganda, which
has succeeded.
"The third phase is going to be both the political and legal struggle
when I return home on Wednesday," he said.
Besigye's return is designed to allow him to beat the October 28 voter
registration closing date and be eligible to contest in the 2006
presidential elections. He contested against Museveni in the 2001
presidential elections.
He lost and filed a petition in the Supreme Court, which ruled that the
polls were ridden with irregularities but upheld the results. He then beat
round-the-clock security surveillance and fled into exile, citing political
persecution. Since then, his imminent return has been announced and put off
a few times. The government has warned that he risks being arrested on
return.
But in the speech that received a standing ovation, Besigye told
supporters not to worry about President Museveni's letter to cabinet of
October 18 that said the government would prefer "long standing criminal
charges" against him.
Gimmicks
"The writing of that letter is part of the continuous gimmicks Museveni
has always employed to harass political opponents," Besigye told a cheering
audience. "He has always employed such gimmicks to intimidate opponents in
order to tighten his stranglehold over the leadership of our
country. "However, I am not going to be intimidated by such gimmicks and I
will return to Uganda."
Besigye said the situation in Uganda has not changed much since he left
the country in August 2001. He said the only things that have changed are
that he and his colleagues have succeeded in forming a political party that
now has a national platform to engage "the regime" politically and
legally.
The second is that since he left the country, many more of his
colleagues who had remained with the NRM have learnt what he realised much
earlier - that Museveni has no plans of democratising the politics of the
country.
Besigye claims that FDC now has 3.5 million registered members who have
been insistent that he returns to Uganda. "However," Besigye added, "The
responsibility for democratising Uganda does not lie with FDC alone. The
entire opposition should both individually and collectively work towards a
more democratic dispensation." Besigye advised supporters not to think he is
the only one who can lead.
"There are many leaders in Uganda, in FDC and the rest of the
opposition" Besigye said, "I have been leading the struggle abroad while
others have been leading it at home. Now I am returning home to provide
leadership on the ground. If I fall, someone will take over. So no one
should think that I am the only leader."
Besigye said that during the 2001 presidential elections when Museveni
claimed to be the cotter pin, the population named him "Ssennyondo", the
hammer to get out the cotter pin. "Today," Besigye said, "Museveni has
realised his declining strength and he is calling himself 'bisanja' - banana
leaves. We are going to strip him of his bisanja because banana leaves are
easier to strip than a cotter pin."
He said in 1986, NRM promised a fundamental change. He said after 20
years in power, Museveni has betrayed this promise to Ugandans.
"Our struggle was launched against the background of a rigged election
yet Museveni has orchestrated the worst electoral rigging in Uganda's
history. In our Ten Point Programme, we promised democracy yet today,
Museveni is consolidating a brutal dictatorship in Uganda.
He narrated the Movement promises which he claims Museveni failed to
fulfill.
"We promised to fight corruption yet today corruption has become the
norm in Uganda; we promised to end nepotism, yet today nepotism is the very
basis of Museveni's rule; we promised a lean, frugal and effective
government, now Museveni presides over a large, extravagant and incompetent
state administrative structure; we promised to improve the well-being of our
people yet today we have grinding poverty all over Uganda; we promised to
end conflict and rehabilitated war ravaged areas, yet today conflict has
been sustained in Acholi and parts of the west; we promised to correct
regional imbalances in development, yet today these imbalances like between
the north and the south have been reinforced."

Original_Search_Title: State House Visit Was a Betrayal, Claims Ntimama
Headline: Kenya: State House Visit Was a Betrayal, Claims Ntimama
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Cabinet minister William ole Ntimama yesterday condemned a Maasai
delegation which on Friday went to State House.
He compared them to those in the Bible who sold their brothers and
sisters for their selfish ends.
At the same time, Mr Ntimama accused a local councillor who, during the
visit, connected him with the Enosupukia clashes, adding that the civic
leader should record a statement with the police. "I was out of the country
during the time and he should record a statement and tell the world who were
behind the killings."
Mr Ntimama, who took his Orange campaign to Narok south constituency of
Stephen ole Ntutu, said the delegation, which was led by Prof George
Saitoti, Mr Harun Lempaka, Mr Ntutu and Mr Gideon Konchella, was aimed at
selling the Maasai who last month held a meeting at Suswa and vowed not to
support the proposed Constitution.
Speaking at Narosura during a home coming party for the local
councillor, Mr Shadrack Lenjir, the minister said any Maasai who will go
against the Suswa declaration will remain cursed by the community.
Mr Ntimama, who was accompanied by 16 councillors led by the Narok
county council chairman John Sayaton, said the Wako draft was aimed at
benefiting some communities while targeting other communities' natural
resources.
The minister in the Office of the President and MP for Narok North said
members of the land commission, as stipulated in the proposed draft, will be
appointed by the President thus giving him powers to manipulate them.
He said no amount of pressure would convince the Maasai to support the
draft.
The minister at the same time said nobody will be allowed to go back to
Mau forest.
Large-scale landowners at the Coast are funding the Orange team, an
assistant minister said yesterday.
Mr Danson Mungatana of Lands and Settlement said the landowners were
saying No to the proposed Constitution because they would lose their land to
local communities if it is passed in the November 21 referendum.
He was speaking at a Banana rally in Malindi Town.
Tourism and Wildlife minister Morris Dzoro urged area residents to vote
for the proposed Constitution because it would give them back the land taken
by foreigners and "big fish".
It would also enable them to enjoy resources in the region "without any
interference from outside, he added.
Assistant Tourism minister Boniface Mganga asked upcountry people to
cooperate with local communities and vote Yes on November 21.

Original_Search_Title: Orange to Hold Final Rally At Uhuru Park
Headline: Kenya: Orange to Hold Final Rally At Uhuru Park
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange team will hold its last rally at Uhuru Park, Nairobi, three
days before the poll date.
Addressing a campaign meeting in Kibera in the city yesterday, Bomet MP
Nick Salat said the November 18 meeting will be the grand finale of the No
campaigns.
Yesterday's rally, which was attended by eight MPs, defended Roads
minister Raila Odinga from the accusation that he was pushing for the No
vote for personal gain.
"On November 18, the Orange team will hold the last rally at Uhuru Park
to drum up support for the No vote," Mr Salat said. "This is when the Banana
team will know that the Constitution is not for Raila or Salat, but for all
Kenyans.
"We do not have a spare Kenya, but we love this country because it is
ours and for the young people."
He criticised the recent Steadman poll, which said the Orange team was
leading with 42 per cent, saying that it was "a ploy to make the Banana team
believe that it was closing in on us, yet we are at 90 per cent."
He added: "The poll is a way of rigging the election; this is why it put
Orange at 42 per cent and Banana at 32 per cent to show that the margin was
minimal. The Government should accept that it is losing out."
He took issue with recent remarks by Constitutional Affairs minister
Kiraitu Murungi to the effect that retired President Moi was sponsoring
Orange rallies.
Eldoret East MP Joseph Lagat speaks against the proposed Constitution at
an Orange campaign rally in Kibera, Nairobi, yesterday. Photos by Stephen
Mudiari
He urged First Lady Lucy to stop criticising Immigration minister Linah
Kilimo for joining the Orange camp. "Linah Kilimo is free to listen to her
constituents because they gave her the votes and have told her the decision
to take," he said.
The rally was attended also by MPs Joseph Lagat ( Eldoret East), David
Koros (Eldoret South), Mutinda Mutiso (Kilome), Reuben Ndolo (Makadara),
Jakoyo Midiwo (Gem), Gor Sunguh (Kisumu Town East) and William Omondi
(Kasarani).
Mr Lagat said the Orange team was not facing any stiff challenge and
that Mr Odinga had declared that he was not interested in the prime
minister's post.
Said Mr Ndolo: "We supporters of Orange must turn out in large numbers
(on November 21) so that we may send the old men home as it is time for them
to retire."
"We know the Orange team is at 80 per cent, and even if we are at 42 per
cent we are still leading," Mr Midiwo said.
Mr Sunguh added: "We have spent Sh4 billion on the Constitution and we
will not relent until the Bomas draft, which is the people-driven
Constitution, is adopted."
Mr Mutiso criticised the Government for refusing to push for the
National Hospital Insurance Bill which, he noted, seeks to make free medical
services a reality.
The MPs claimed that there were attempts by the government to bribe
voters to sway their vote towards the banana team.
Speaker after speaker urged voters to turn out in large numbers on
November 21 to vote against the draft constitution.
They urged voters to reject the Wako draft and instead push for the
Bomas Draft.

Original_Search_Title: Publicity Blitz As Rival Camps Solicit Support
Headline: Kenya: Publicity Blitz As Rival Camps Solicit Support
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Banana and Orange teams are now turning to advertising campaigns as
the referendum date draws closer.
The Banana group was the first to embark on the publicity blitz using
billboards and paid-up adverts to sway the public vote.
And the Orange group has a television advertisement showing a young girl
picking an orange from a tree, and later the same tree sprouts several
fruits, then they tell voters that these would be their future.
The Orange team has packaged an approach which they claim will
overshadow their opponents in the referendum duel.
A key official of the No secretariat, Prof Larry Gumbe, said: "We don't
have the funds that the Yes team have at their disposal, but we have our own
package which is on the way. Just give us one week and you will see," he
said on telephone.
The new strategy comes in the wake of an opinion poll by Steadman Group,
whose survey revealed that the Orange team had a 10 per cent edge over the
Banana group.
The No team claimed Steadman company's survey underestimated their
margin over the Yes camp, which they said was over 40 per cent.
Billboards bearing the Banana symbol of the Yes team, are coming up on
highways and major roads.
In Nairobi, the billboards, telling Kenyans to "vote for the truth" have
sprung up along Forest, Jogoo and Mombasa roads.
Key members of the Yes team claim that the Orange team was using
lies.
Livestock minister Joseph Munyao at the weekend said: "It is easier to
sell a lie than the truth but the danger is that while it is sweeter to
listen to lies, the end result is bitter."
The aim, he said, was to set the record straight that the Orange camp
has been distorting the contents in the new Draft for their own
benefit.
In yesterday's advert on powers of the President, which has become the
focal point of the No campaign, they urged Kenyans to endorse the new
Constitution arguing that it created an "accountable" Executive whose powers
have been tremendously reduced.
It says in part: "Those saying that the proposed new Constitution is
creating an imperial President are not telling the truth. It creates an
accountable Presidency and transfers power to the people of Kenya.
The Orange group, they argue, wants the country to be governed by the
present, colonial Constitution.

Original_Search_Title: Rival Teams to Spend Sh1bn in Vote-Hunting
Headline: Kenya: Rival Teams to Spend Sh1bn in Vote-Hunting
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Banana and Orange teams could spend more than Sh1 billion in organising
campaign rallies and supplying materials to woo voters countrywide,
according to conservative estimates.
The bill could rise significantly as the estimates do not include the
costs of advertising, which began in earnest when the official campaign
started last Friday.
One supplier said they have been charging between Sh175 and 200 for a
T-shirt and Sh100 for a cap and anticipated to sell a big stock.
Although both camps did not disclose how much money they would spend on
the campaign, the Yes secretariat confirmed that they would distribute one
million of each item countrywide, meaning that it will cost them Sh300
million to cover only T-shirts and caps.
The Orange team said a similar amount could be spent on merchandise
carrying the No message through freelance traders cashing in on the
opportunity and funds sourced from its supporters.
A spokesman at the Orange team's secretariat said although they would
distribute one million T-shirts and caps nationwide, they were doing it in
phases whenever funds were available.
"In fact some of the materials are done by business people themselves
who come to sell them at our rallies," he said.
Other costs will be incurred from the purchase of banners and kitting
politicians in flamboyant garb during rallies in their bid to attract the
11.5 million registered voters.
And each side is running a secretariat in Nairobi complete with staff
plus vehicles with the Banana offices having 11 pool cars at their
disposal.
Besides T-shirts and caps, both teams are distributing banners and
scarfs, in addition to meeting the transport costs of their supporters to
campaign rallies. Some of the supporters travel in cars and others in
helicopters.
The Yes team has printed thousands of copies of a simplified version of
the proposed Constitution while the No team has been distributing bangles
with the "No" inscription.
Both teams intensified their campaigns in different parts of the country
at the weekend, with 27 days remaining before the national referendum.
The Orange team took its campaigns to Isiolo where its leaders addressed
a rally, but soon afterwards encountered a hostile reception in Nanyuki
Town.
Led by Roads minister Raila Odinga, official Opposition leader Uhuru
Kenyatta and Kanu secretary general William Ruto, the group had to be
escorted two kilometres out of town by heavily-armed police.
Another group of crusaders championing the No vote campaigned in
Nairobi's Langata constituency.
And the Banana teams campaigned in Bungoma, Kirinyaga, Laikipia West and
in Nairobi's Kamukunji.
The heavy expenses each side had so far incurred came to light when the
Nation visited four suppliers of garments based in Nairobi's Industrial
Area, the central business district and in Westlands.
But neither the Banana nor the Orange team was willing to divulge their
sources of funding, both maintaining that it came from personal
contributions by their leaders and well-wishers.
Each MP in the Banana camp is to be provided with 200 T-shirts and caps
to distribute during the rallies. More T-shirts and caps are to be provided
when these run out.
The suppliers of campaign material said each banner costs Sh150 and if
each side can distribute close to 10 million or more nationwide, it would
cost a further Sh300 million - bringing the total to Sh900 million.
Kitting MPs in rallies is an additional expense. Politicians have been
buying high-quality shirts at Sh2,500 for cotton fabrics and 4,500 for linen
fabrics. Other T-shirts made of lacoste fabrics sell at about
Sh3,000. Assuming that each of the 210 MPs are involved in the campaigns it
would cost under Sh1 million, but only if each MP has one shirt.
But both the Yes and No teams have high profile supporters who are not
MPs.
Other costs include construction of dais and hiring of public address
systems, which both secretariats indicated cost an average of Sh100,000 for
one meeting.
But this does not include hiring youths to maintain security during
campaign rallies plus accommodation for MPs and the youths during weekend
crusades in the countryside, which would run into millions of
shillings.
The Nation visited some clothes designers and found the places a beehive
of activity.
Most of those designing the colourful T-shirts, shirts, scarfs and
banners are well known to the MPs. They have at one point designed exclusive
outfits for the lawmakers, their wives or children.
They use both local and imported materials, mainly from West
Africa.
The expenses have soared as politicians from both camps try to outdo
each other, not only in verbal campaigns, but through flamboyance and
style.
Leaders in the Orange team wear distinct orange-coloured outfits -
mainly T-shirts and caps during rallies.
But the Banana leaders distribute T-shirts to their supporters,
preferring to button up in their well tailored suits.

Original_Search_Title: It's a Hostile Reception for Orange Team
Headline: Kenya: It's a Hostile Reception for Orange Team
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange team yesterday encountered a hostile reception in Nanyuki
town where they had taken the No campaigns.
Led by Raila Odinga, Uhuru Kenyatta, and William Ruto the group had to
be escorted two kilometres out of the town by heavily armed police.
Tension was evident as the convoy of the Orange leaders entered the town
at about 3pm from Isiolo where just two hours before they had addressed a
peaceful rally.
Police had a hectic time trying to restrain Banana supporters from
overunning the No rally at the Kanu grounds in the town centre.
The security officers had to use tear gas to disperse the surging
crowds.
The crowd blocked the entrance to the grounds and engaged a group of
Orange youths inside the grounds in a shouting match.
When the convoy arrived police moved in to disperse the rowdy Banana
supporters with tear gas. But the crowd responded by pelting the police with
stones damaging the windscreen of a police vehicle.
The crowd ringed the grounds with some climbing on top of buildings from
where they heckled the speakers.
MPs present included Joseph Kamotho, Maoka Maore, Musa Sirma, Gideon
Moi, Sammy Weya, Ayiecho Olweny as well as Mombasa Mayor Taib Ali Taib and
former Vice President Musalia Mudavadi.
Police had to provide security for the one hour rally after which they
escorted the group out of the town with the crowd pelting the convoy with
stones.
In Isiolo, Mr Odinga urged voters to reject and not even to read the
proposed constitution saying it does not protect minority rights.
"There is no need for you to read this Draft constitution, which does
not protect your rights," he told the cheering crowd.
He reminded church leaders in the district that the National Council of
Churches of Kenya (NCCK) had clearly said part of the draft is faulty.
Mr Kenyatta said if the NCCK had already pointed out that part of the
proposed document is not good the message is clear that they should vote No
during the November 21 referendum.
Mr Odinga also dismissed an opinion poll by Steadman Group as Government
sponsored.
"Kenyans will not consider the opinion poll because it showed that there
is a very small margin between the Orange and the Banana," he said
But he assured the people of Isiolo that No vote will sail through by 65
per cent during the referendum.
Mr Odinga claimed that in two weeks time, the firm will release another
opinion poll which will indicate that the Banana will be leading.
The Roads minister claimed the Government played a hand in the poll
because it also went ahead to show that President Kibaki's popularity
remained high.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki to Lead Yes Campaign in Search of 1.2m Western Votes
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki to Lead Yes Campaign in Search of 1.2m Western Votes
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki will lead the Banana campaign to Western Province in
early November in a bid to woo the 1.2 million voters in the region.
Vice-President Moody Awori and Ford-Kenya Chairman Musikari Kombo will
host the President.
Kombo, who is already on the ground campaigning for the Yes votes,
yesterday said that the President was scheduled to visit the province, but
the official date had not been confirmed.
However, the Yes team released its programme detailing the campaign
meetings scheduled for Nyanza, Central and Western Provinces.
The Banana team will venture into Nyanza province for a rally in Kisumu
town on October 29.
Justice Assistant minister Robinson Githae told The Standard that all
the top Banana campaigners had been invited to the Kisumu rally by Cabinet
Minister Raphael Tuju.
Tuju is working hard to undo the gains the Orange team led by Roads
minister Raila Odinga and his Planning counterpart, Prof Anyang' Nyongo',
has made in the province.
Githae said that several leaders led by Awori would be in Kisumu to
campaign for the proposed constitution.
After the Kisumu meeting, Githae said, the Banana brigade will head to
Karatina in Central Province for a major rally on October 30.
Awori will lead the Banana team in the rallies that will not be attended
by the President.
However, Githae said that the President would tour the country soon to
campaign for the proposed constitution.
" The President will go to Western, Coast, Central and all parts of the
country to officially campaign for the document," he added.
" We will all go to Western to campaign and deliver the votes for the
Banana camp".
Kombo has already declared that the Banana team has broken ground
against the Orange wave in Western.
Kombo also held a meeting with Bukusu professionals from Bungoma
district working in Nairobi at the Nairobi Club recently to convince them to
support the Yes campaign.
The Orange team is also fighting for a share of the 1.2 million votes in
the province. The team is led by former vice-president Musalia Mudavadi and
Kanu first Vice Chairman Chris Okemo.
" Even if the President comes to Western Province, the ground will not
change. The people are in Orange and that is a closed chapter" said
Okemo.
He urged the Luhyas to unite and reject the proposed constitution and
work out a better deal in a new Government.

Original_Search_Title: Raila's Rally Marred By Teargas As Groups Clash
Headline: Kenya: Raila's Rally Marred By Teargas As Groups Clash
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Teargas and running battles yesterday characterised the last leg of the Orange campaign in the Mount Kenya region as police dispersed rival crowds at a rally in Nanyuki Town.
Cabinet minister Raila Odinga, Kanu chairman Uhuru Kenyatta and secretary-general William Ruto's attempt to convince people to reject the proposed constitution was resisted by crowds chanting "ndizi" (Banana).
It was one of several incidents of violence on the first weekend of the official campaign period that started on Friday.
In Migori, Information minister Raphael Tuju's convoy was pelted with oranges when he went to launch his People's Progressive Party.
Tuju was rescued from the crowd by police, who escorted him to his car.
In Nanyuki, Central Park, which was the venue of the rally, was transformed into a battlefield, complete with a buffer zone of heavily armed police.
Trouble started at around 2.38pm when Uhuru arrived in a convoy of about 50 vehicles from Isiolo.
Chants of "Orange" and "No" were drowned by screams of "ndizi" as a group of youths confronted the visitors.
As soon as Uhuru entered the venue, formerly known as Kanu Gardens, a group of pro-Banana youths started chanting "ndizi", disrupting prayers.
Meanwhile, police had formed a ring around the park to separate the rival groups.
Immediately after the prayers, police fired teargas and the groups fled. But they regrouped and the shouting match resumed.
Laikipia police chief, Jimmy Mnene, ran up and down the "battle zone" as he co-ordinated his officers, numbering about 100. They were drawn from the General Service Unit, the Administration Police, Regular Police and the Anti-Stock Theft Unit.
At one point, Ruto sneezed repeatedly and Raila wiped his eyes as the teargas stung.
The Orange leaders went ahead to address the rally. They accused the area MP, Mwangi Kiunjuri, and the entire Banana team of being behind the chaos.
Uhuru accused Kiunjuri of dividing his constituents over the constitution debate.
Kanu MP Musa Sirma asked Kiunjuri to restrain his supporters from engaging in hooliganism.
Raila claimed the Banana team had panicked after sensing defeat and accused it of using crude methods to "subdue the wishes of the people".
The Langata MP repeated his earlier statement that his differences with President Kibaki arose only from their different stands on the Draft Constitution and that he still recognised him as a good leader.
He said that the No campaign was not a power game and that there was still a chance for all Cabinet ministers to work together after the referendum.
"Some of our colleagues have been calling for our sacking. It is our democratic right to express our views over the Constitution. After the referendum, we will still work together as a team," he said.
Raila said that although 80 per cent of the proposed constitution was okay, the remaining 20 per cent was "poisonous and no sensible parent could give a child food with such quantities of toxin with promises that if he fells sick, he will be taken to hospital".
Former vice president Musalia Mudavadi said those who were supporting the Banana team could not have endorsed the Wako Draft with its mistakes if retired President Moi was still in power.
As Raila was entertaining the crowd with his popular soccer commentary, shots were fired in the air to repulse a group that was marching towards the venue.
At some point, there was a security scare when a drunkard man ran past the police cordon. The crowds fled in all directions, cutting short an address by Kanu Chief Whip, Justin Muturi.
Later, calm prevailed and leader after leader condemned the violence.
Raila said it was unfortunate that it was only in Nanyuki where he had met such violence, adding that in other parts of Central Kenya, he had been well received.
Others at the meeting were Liberal Democratic Party secretary-general Joseph Kamotho, Samuel Poghishio, Maoka Maore, Philip Okundi, Bonaya Godana, Sammy Weya, Oluoch Olweny and Mombasa Mayor Taib Ali Taib.
Uhuru rebuffed claims that by joining forces with Raila, he had betrayed his community and his political ambitions. He said he was his own man and would make an appropriate decision come 2007.
After the rally, the Orange campaigners had to be escorted out of town by anti riot police who had to fire several shots in the air to disperse Banana supporters who were hurling insults at them.
In Migori, police shielded Tuju as Orange youths chanting slogans hurled fruits at his vehicle.
Tuju had landed at the Migori airstrip in a police helicopter and headed to the Maranatha grounds to address a crowd that had converged for the launch of his party.
Earlier, there was tension at the meeting before the minister arrived. A local politician, Mr Musa Ahono, advised the crowd to calm down, saying they should first listen to Tuju's policies to know if the party would bring development to Nyanza Province.
When Tuju arrived, accompanied by former assistant minister Oluoch Kanindo, Samuel Mirotho and Shikuku Owiti, he told the crowd that PPP had not been formed to fight any individual, but to spearhead development projects for faster growth.
"Nobody should cheat you that I have formed a party to fight him or undermine political leadership in Luo Nyanza. My main worry is the abject poverty that our people have been wallowing in for the last 40 years," he said.
He challenged the No proponents to address the escalating poverty instead of engaging in confrontational politics. He criticised Uhuru, whom he said was campaigning against the Proposed New Constitution to safeguard his family's property, especially land.
Tuju also took issue with his political nemesis, Raila, whom he said had not brought any meaningful development projects in Nyanza.
But this did not go down well with the No proponents who started to murmur in low tones as some heckled and jeered.
Tuju said it would be difficult for the Orange team to choose a presidential candidate after the referendum.

Original_Search_Title: Leaders Split Over the Results of Opinion Poll
Headline: Kenya: Leaders Split Over the Results of Opinion Poll
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Political and church leaders as well ordinary Kenyans were yesterday divided on the results of an opinion poll released on Friday.
The poll, by the Steadman Group, shows that 42 per cent of Kenyans would vote No in the referendum, while 32 per cent would vote Yes for the new law.
Vice-President Moody Awori welcomed the opinion poll and termed the results a challenge to the Yes camp, saying it would give them a chance to improve.
Mr Awori who officially launched the Banana campaign in Western Province yesterday called on his colleagues in the Yes camp to work extra hard to ensure the results swung in their favour.
Archbishop Ndingi
Catholic Archbishop Ndingi mwana a' Nzeki was non-committal, saying his prayer was that only the team that can provide the leadership Kenyans want will win the vote. His Mombasa colleague John Njenga said there was a long time as the campaigns officially started only yesterday.
The Moderator of the Presbyterian Church of East Africa Rev Patrick Mungiriria said: "I think the poll was okay but it could have been more accurate if it was conducted after the official campaigns, halfway, end of the month or at the beginning of November."
In Kiambu two ministers and an MP dismissed the poll, saying it did not reflect the situation on the ground. Internal Security Minister, John Michuki said the Banana team was way ahead.
Special Projects Minister Njenga Karume also dismissed the polls as misleading. In Nyahururu, four Cabinet ministers and five MPs allied to the Banana camp dismissed the opinion poll. Justice and Constitutional Affairs Minister Kiraitu Murungi urged Kenyans to disregard the poll results which he said were doctored in favour of the Orange camp.
In Kwale, Coast legislators in the Banana side dismissed the poll results. But they supported the section that indicated President Mwai Kibaki was the most popular candidate for the post. Assistant minister Danson Mungatana took issue with the timing of the polls questioning why Steadman carried out the interviews even before the campaigns kicked.
But the organising secretary of the Council of Imams and Preachers of Kenya Sheikh Mohamed Khalifa welcomed the findings, saying such polls should be encouraged. Opinion was similarly divided in Kisumu, Eldoret, Nyeri, Kisii and Nyamira.

Original_Search_Title: Tourism Minister Now Woos Mombasa Beach Operators
Headline: Kenya: Tourism Minister Now Woos Mombasa Beach Operators
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A day after the release of a report that showed the Orange team in the lead in opinion polls against their Banana counterparts, Tourism and Wildlife minister Morris Dzoro yesterday stepped up his Yes campaign with a morning meeting with beach operators.
The minister urged hundreds of north coast beach operators to vote for the proposed new Constitution because it cares for the welfare of the poor in a more elaborate manner than the current one.
He said that the Government had allocated funds that would be used to construct modern structures where beach operators could easily go about their trade without being harassed by authorities and hotelkeepers.
"We have already set aside funds that would soon be used to put up modern structures in designated areas where beach operators would ply their trade in a tourism friendly environment, unlike the current situation where you (beach operators) work in ramshackle kiosks," said Mr Dzoro.
The minister, who is spearheading the Yes campaign at the Coast, made the remarks in a meeting with north coast beach operators yesterday morning held at Aloha Beach in Mombasa.
The minister promised the traders that the Government would ensure that the hotels did not privatise access roads in future.

Original_Search_Title: Foes Turn Friends in a Game of Musical Chairs
Headline: Kenya: Foes Turn Friends in a Game of Musical Chairs
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The political passions excited by the November 21 referendum have not only created new alliances between political parties. Individual MPs looking for the right place to roost have abandoned their own parties for new alignments.
The governing Narc is itself split. The National Alliance Party of Kenya (NAK), Ford Kenya and their partner, the Liberal Political Party (LDP), are now fierce opponents.
President Kibaki is leading the Yes group at the referendum while Roads minister Raila Odinga has marshalled leaders in the No team. President Kibaki is the face of NAK.
Although the fissures in Narc have been growing wider and deeper since January 2003, it seems the referendum may be the storm to bring the wall tumbling to the ground. To stem the tide in time, President Kibaki last year moved and brought on his side Ford People, led by Energy minister Simeon Nyachae, and a section of Kanu MPs. This alliance is evident in the Banana team. Mr Nyachae is the leader of the Yes committee rooting for the proposed law.
The fallout over the referendum has penetrated deeper in Narc. The Labour Party of Kenya, chaired by Nominated MP Julia Ojiambo, has chosen to team up with the Orange group and abandon NAK. The LDP has also witnessed rebellion in its ranks. Vice-President Moody Awori and Cabinet ministers George Saitoti, Raphael Tuju and Chirau Ali Mwakwere are now firmly in the Banana camp to the chagrin of the party leadership.
Assistant ministers Zaddock Syongoh and Joseph Nyagah have been with the group that wants the referendum postponed until the contentious issues are resolved. The latter has since crossed over to the Yes side.
President Kibaki's own Democratic Party of Kenya, the national treasurer, the volatile Embakasi MP David Mwenje, loudly crossed over to the No team, and promised to challenge his boss in the presidential race in 2007.
In Ford Kenya, MPs Wycliffe Oparanya (Butere) and Julius Arunga (Khwisero) are working closely with the No team. The Official opposition party, Kanu, has not escaped the shifts. Its members who co-opted in government are in the Yes campaign team.
Some Kanu MPs from Eastern and North Eastern Provinces are leading the campaign for the Banana team in their regions. Kanu MPs from Central Province like Mr Kuria Kanyingi, Mr Viscount Kimathi and Mr Patrick Muiruri have abandoned the party chairman, Mr Uhuru Kenyatta. Also in Kanu, Mr Nick Salat and other MPs who were allied to the rebel faction of Keiyo South MP Nicholas Biwott, have gone back to the fold. And Ford-P boss Nyachae has had to contend with defiance from South Mugirango MP Omingo Magara and Wundanyi MP Mwandawiro Mgangha.

Original_Search_Title: Race Takes Focus Away From Tedious Orange, Banana Talk
Headline: Kenya: Race Takes Focus Away From Tedious Orange, Banana Talk
Body_Text:
Nairobi — For the last three weeks, orange and banana referendum campaigns have dominated the lives of many Kenyans especially among the urban and rural poor.
But in middle and upper class circles where food, shelter and the good things in life are not an issue, the talk has centred around the Standard Chartered Nairobi Marathon which takes place today.
Nairobi comes to a standstill with all major roads in the city centre and its environs closed to pave way for over 12,000 runners in the country's biggest mass participation sporting event.
The very brave have entered the full marathon, a substantial number the 21km and the majority in the 10km. The action is in the 5km family fun run with grand children as young as six joining their grandmothers in their 70s.
Conquering the marathon among health buffs is the achievement most prized.
A good example is David Githinji who was warned nine years ago by his doctor to either start exercising or face an early demise.
The Standard Chartered Marathon at its competitive status is thus no longer a social thing but a real test of stamina, willpower and endurance.
Only in its third edition the marathon has proved a spectacular success for various reasons.
But behind the scenes, the generous sponsorship from the Standard Chartered Bank aside, Kenyan athletes are finally being recognised at home as role models worthy of being emulated.
First, Kenya as a major production line for distance runners, has continued to provide elite runners to all major city marathon races, and therefore lining up some of the best was not a big deal at home.
The US$40,000 prize for winners of the men and women's races, compares favourably with some of the big city races of the world. The inspiring story of last year's women's winner mother of four Chemokil Chilapong brought many unknown runners from the woods into the city streets.
At least two marathon runners, Paul Tergat and Catherine Ndereba have contributed greatly at home to improving the profile of the marathon which now is considered fashionable.
Marathons and athletics in general require discipline and once one gets hooked, it becomes a habit hard to give up.
Tergat and Ndereba have also kept a high profile through charity work and the promotion of athletics. Ndereba took over the funding of the Dedan Kimathi Half Marathon in Nyeri and last year attracted substantial sponsorship from a mobile service provider.
Tergat, currently the only sports celebrity who has warmed himself to the corporate world has an impressive track record in charity work.
In social circles, these two stand out.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki is Still My Friend, Says Raila
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki is Still My Friend, Says Raila
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Attempts to disrupt rallies and predictions of victory by both sides in the November 21 referendum marked the first weekend of the official campaign period.
In Meru, Roads minister Raila Odinga said his friendship with President Kibaki was intact. He dismissed claims by the Yes team ministers that the Orange campaign was out to topple the Government.
"Our friendship with President Kibaki is intact, that is why I am still serving in his Government," he said.
At the Coast, MPs supporting the proposed new constitution launched their official campaigns in Malindi town. The MPs, led by Cabinet minister Morris Dzoro, said the new constitution will undo injustices perpetuated against Coast people since Independence.
Kisauni MP Anania Mwaboza said the proposed national land commission would address the squatter problem in the region. The other MPs at the rally were Mr Joseph Kingi, Mr Abdalla Ngozi, Mr Gonzi Rai, Mr Danson Mungatana, Mr Boniface Mganga and Mr Harrison Kombe.
"Coast people stand to benefit most if the proposed new constitution is enacted because in has provisions that will deal with the land problem," Mr Mwaboza said.
In Meru, the Orange team needed heavy police guard as they took the No campaign to Justice minister Kiraitu Murungi's home turf in Meru. In Isiolo town, two people were arrested as they attempted to disrupt a Banana campaign rally.
In western Kenya, the Banana group launched its bid to win the populous region by asking the people to reject attempts by Roads minister Odinga to sway them against the proposed new Constitution.
Retired President Daniel arap Moi also came in for a tongue lashing from the Yes team who warned him to keep off the campaigns.
Roads Minister Raila Odinga. Photo by William Oeri
In Meru, Mr Odinga and other No campaign luminaries, including Official Opposition leader Kenyatta and Cabinet ministers Kalonzo Musyoka and Peter Anyang' Nyong'o braved heckling mobs to address crowds in Meru, Chuka and Maua towns. Later, police in Maua arrested two men who had attempted to disrupt the Orange rally.
One of the two drove into Maua stadium as the No team leaders were drumming up support against the proposed new Constitution. The man stopped the vehicle close to the dais and came out waving a banana. He was then arrested and the vehicle a impounded.
The other man was among the crowd and was heckling as he waved a banana. The two were whisked away by the police. Speaking in Chuka under heavy police guard, Mr Odinga dismissed claims that the Orange campaign was out to topple the Government. But he said they and the Yes minister had differed over the new Constitution because it creates a President with the powers of a king. "A king has his way all the time. He can even take away your wife if he wants."
Mr Kenyatta said the new Constitution was meant to serve future generations and should not be narrowed to a fight between the President and Mr Odinga.
At Kima Church, Vihiga, Vice-President Moody Awori and Ford-Kenya chairman Musikari Kombo urged the Luhya to vote as a bloc to increase their bargaining power in the national political arena.
Mr Awori said the 1.2 million Luhya vote was enough to enable one of their own to ascend to the top political seat, but pointed out they would have to speak in one voice.
Mr Kombo, the Local Government minister, described Mr Odinga as "a political joker who will never provide good leadership.
Speaking earlier in Funyula, Busia, Mr Kombo said the new constitution was a matter of life and death, especially to the people of western Kenya who had much stake in it. But at Bumala market, he failed to address a hostile crowd displaying oranges and sped towards Funyula.
In Ol Kalou, Cabinet ministers Chris Murungaru, Kiraitu Murungi, Amos Kimunya and Martha Karua told retired President Moi to stop meddling in the ongoing campaigns.
The four Ministers claimed that Mr Moi was funding the Orange team in order to destabilise the Kibaki Government.
In Kiambu, Internal Security Minister John Michuki Special Projects minister Njenga Karume urged voters to turn out in large numbers to cast their Yes vote.
In Isiolo police in Isiolo town yesterday thwarted efforts by Orange camp supporters to disrupt a Banana meeting at the local stadium. At least two people were arrested but released after the meeting ended.
The meeting was attended by Cabinet ministers David Mwiraria, Njeru Ndwiga and Mohamed Abdi; Assistant ministers were Joshua Toro and Mohamed Kuti and MP Guracha Galgalo, Maalim Mohamed and Francis Kagwima.
Reported by Walker Mwandotto, Imanene Imathiu, and Mugumo Munene

Original_Search_Title: Leaders Split Over the Results of Opinion Poll
Headline: Kenya: Leaders Split Over the Results of Opinion Poll
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Political and church leaders as well ordinary Kenyans were yesterday divided on the results of an opinion poll released on Friday.
The poll, by the Steadman Group, shows that 42 per cent of Kenyans would vote No in the referendum, while 32 per cent would vote Yes for the new law.
Vice-President Moody Awori welcomed the opinion poll and termed the results a challenge to the Yes camp, saying it would give them a chance to improve.
Mr Awori who officially launched the Banana campaign in Western Province yesterday called on his colleagues in the Yes camp to work extra hard to ensure the results swung in their favour.
Catholic Archbishop Ndingi mwana a' Nzeki was non-committal, saying his prayer was that only the team that can provide the leadership Kenyans want will win the vote. His Mombasa colleague John Njenga said there was a long time as the campaigns officially started only yesterday.
The Moderator of the Presbyterian Church of East Africa Rev Patrick Mungiriria said: "I think the poll was okay but it could have been more accurate if it was conducted after the official campaigns, halfway, end of the month or at the beginning of November."
In Kiambu two ministers and an MP dismissed the poll, saying it did not reflect the situation on the ground. Internal Security Minister, John Michuki said the Banana team was way ahead.
Special Projects Minister Njenga Karume also dismissed the polls as misleading. In Nyahururu, four Cabinet ministers and five MPs allied to the Banana camp dismissed the opinion poll. Justice and Constitutional Affairs Minister Kiraitu Murungi urged Kenyans to disregard the poll results which he said were doctored in favour of the Orange camp.
In Kwale, Coast legislators in the Banana side dismissed the poll results. But they supported the section that indicated President Mwai Kibaki was the most popular candidate for the post. Assistant minister Danson Mungatana took issue with the timing of the polls questioning why Steadman carried out the interviews even before the campaigns kicked.
But the organising secretary of the Council of Imams and Preachers of Kenya Sheikh Mohamed Khalifa welcomed the findings, saying such polls should be encouraged. Opinion was similarly divided in Kisumu, Eldoret, Nyeri, Kisii and Nyamira.

Original_Search_Title: Steadman Poll Doctored, Leaders Claim
Headline: Kenya: Steadman Poll Doctored, Leaders Claim
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Steadman and Associates executive director, George Waititu, has dismissed claims that poll results released two days ago were doctored.
Waititu said Steadman controlled the surveys and could not be influenced even by its financiers.
The survey showed the Banana camp, which supports the proposed new constitution, enjoying the support of 32 per cent of voters compared with the Orange team's 42 per cent, a ten-point lead.
"Anybody who can follow scientific research procedure can go out there and get the same results," he told The Sunday Standard.
He also dismissed claims that the Justice and Constitutional Affairs ministry sponsored the poll, saying the research firm is funded by international agencies, including the American-based Gallup International.
"I understand this is a sensitive issue and many people are likely to find it hard to accept the results. We get many such reactions every time we publish an opinion poll on politics," he added.
Waititu was responding to claims circulating through the mobile phone Short Message Service that, according to the original data collected, Orange team was in the lead with 60 per cent, while Banana had 24 per cent. Sixteen per cent of the voters were said to be undecided.
It was claimed that the data had been changed to 'No'- 48 per cent, 'Yes'- 24 per cent, and an 18 per cent swing vote. According to the poll results released last Friday, 22 per cent of the voters were undecided.
But Waititu, who said he had also received the messages, laughed off the claims.
"We invite our clients to supervise every stage of a research. We also keep the questionnaires used for some time so that those who doubt the findings can inspect them," he said.
He added: "Funding is always a sensitive issue. But even if you sponsored a poll, we would not doctor it in your favour. We have fallen out with some people over this," he said.
Waititu spoke as politicians from the Orange and Banana camps criticised the poll results as unreliable, with each side claiming the study favoured their opponents.
Coast MPs aligned to the Banana camp dismissed the study, terming it a political game.
Tourism minister Morris Dzoro said the study was a plot to woo voters to the Orange side.
Speaking at a Banana rally in Vanga, Kwale District, the minister said the poll results did not reflect the situation on the ground as the study was carried out two weeks ago, yet the 'Yes' team had gained some ground.
He said most voters live in rural areas.
Assistant minister Danson Mungatana said the poll was meant to hoodwink Kenyans to vote 'No'.
"We are not surprised by the results of the Steadman poll. Over the years, they have favoured the opposition. We are not threatened," he said.
The leaders said the proposed constitution would address the land problem at the Coast.
And Lands minister Amos Kimunya said the 32 per cent rating given to the Banana side was too low.
Kimunya claimed 'Yes' commanded a 65 per cent following as it had support in rural areas.
But Kimunya supported the presidency rating for Kibaki, saying the President enjoys massive support.
Cabinet ministers Raphael Tuju and Musikari Kombo and assistant minister Maina Kamanda said 'Yes' had more following than suggested by the Steadman survey.
And ministers John Michuki and Njenga Karume dismissed the poll as misleading.
They said the Banana team has more support than indicated by the poll. "The poll was conducted two weeks ago but we are now ahead," Karume said at a 'Yes' rally in Ndeiya, Limuru, in Kiambu District, attended by area MP Kuria Kanyingi.
But Vice President Moody Awori supported the poll and expressed optimism the Banana team would win.
"We are on a 42km marathon, which our competitors began with speed and we are confident we would overtake them as they gasp for breath ahead," Awori said in Vihiga District.
Banana supporters in North Eastern Province said the poll generally reflected Kenyans' stand.
At a rally in Isiolo Town, politicians led by Dujis MP Hussein Maalim said the Banana team would win.

Original_Search_Title: Orange Leads in Poll
Headline: Kenya: Orange Leads in Poll
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Orange campaigners are running 10 points ahead of their Banana rivals, according to a new opinion poll published yesterday.
The poll shows that 42 per cent of Kenyans would vote No in the referendum, while 32 per cent would vote Yes for the new law.
But there remains everything to play for, with a crucial 22 per cent of voters still undecided on which way to go - enough to swing the result either way in the November 21 referendum.
Four per cent of those interviewed refused to answer the question, put by pollsters Steadman Group.
And Steadman was quick to sound a note of caution over the results of their poll. It's still far too early to get an accurate picture, they warned.
The length of time still to go to the ballot is long enough to see many people switch sides, they said. They could be swayed by powerful campaigning and civic education, the poll group explained.
Also last minute swings - the so-called bandwagon effect - could play a part, as could the level of voter turn-out.
However the poll teams, having established which way people would vote, went on to ask a series of follow-up questions that probed the reasons for their decisions.
Those in favour of the Wako Draft are driven by its contents and about half of them could pick out specific provisions they like. Others say they favour the document because it was basically good and the contentious issues could be amended later, while 10 per cent said that it would boost development and security in the country.
Those against the Draft criticised certain provisions and said it was, "basically bad and difficult to amend later or have taken the position for political alignment," the pollsters say.
The poll also shows around 45 per cent of those had read or accessed summaries of the proposed Constitution while only 15 per cent have read the current Constitution.
An overwhelming 67 per cent would like the referendum to go on as scheduled on November 21 - now declared a public holiday - while 24 per cent feel it should be postponed. The rest were either not sure either way or simply refused to answer.
Sixty-eight per cent of those questioned were against using State resources in campaigning, while 17 per cent approve of it. The rest were uncertain, the pollsters say.
The research shows the original Bomas Draft is more popular that Attorney General Amos Wako's version, with 40 per cent in favour of Bomas and 24 per cent for Wako. Nine per cent like the current Constitution most while the other 21 per cent were not sure either way.
Should the Orange No camp win, 32 per cent felt the current Constitution should be amended, and 25 per cent said the reform process should begin again from scratch. The remainder had no position or did not answer.
The survey revealed that the Orange No-vote is more popular in urban areas while only leading by a small margin in rural areas.
It shows that 90 per cent of Kenyans have not been to any civic education meeting, even as D-day draws near.
The poll did not confine itself to views on the proposed Constitution wrangles.
It looked at a range of other subjects, including the popularity of various politicians who might vie for the Presidency, or try to become Prime Minister should the proposed Constitution be accepted.
The poll shows President Kibaki's popularity has increased to 33 per cent, up from 29 per cent in July, while his nearest rival is Environment minister Kalonzo Musyoka who attracts 21per cent support, just one per cent more than three months ago.
Opposition leader Uhuru Kenyatta, who was in second position in a similar poll in July, is now in third position with 17 per cent support.
Roads minister Mr Raila Odinga attracted six per cent support - yet that was exactly double the level he scored in Steadman's July poll.
Support for the post of Prime Minister is slipping, with 46 per cent of Kenyans wanting a system of government with a Premier, down from 59 per cent only three months ago.
Almost half of those who favour the creation of a PM want to see Mr Odinga get the job. Ten per cent would prefer Mr Musyoka and seven per cent said they preferred Health minister Charity Ngilu.
The majority of those who want a PM say he or she should be the leader of the party with a majority seats in Parliament. Twenty-eight believe the holder should be appointed by the President, while 19 per cent were not sure.
The survey also shows the National Rainbow Coalition (Narc) remains the most popular political group with a 30 per cent following, followed by the Liberal Democratic Party (LDP) with 20 per cent, and Kanu with 15 per cent. But 17 per cent of those interviewed did not have a favourite party.
Forty six per cent of the respondents told interviewers they think the referendum results will affect the 2007 General Election to a large extent while the rest said that it would either affect it "somewhat" or were not sure.
The survey revealed there could be a huge potential vote that will not be realised because 25 per cent of those questioned - all Kenyans aged 18 or over - did not have a voter's card.
The poll indicated that only 13 per cent of those interviewed thought the Government was committed to keeping the promises it made during the 2002 campaign while 26 per cent thought it was not committed at all. Fifty-eight per cent said it was "somewhat committed".
Some 43 per cent of respondents gave media houses approval for fair coverage, 30 per cent said it was "somewhat biased," and 13 per cent said it was "unfair."
The results published yesterday by Steadman Group's managing director George Waititu show 46 per cent of Kenyans would prefer a system of government with a Prime Minister, down from 59 per cent in July.
Steadman said it had briefed both Yes and No teams.
Mr Waititu told journalists at Steadman's Nairobi offices: "If anyone wanted to counter-check, we have the addresses of all those we interviewed, which cannot be given without our approval because of the confidentiality we promised the respondents."

Original_Search_Title: Maasai Delegation Vows to Back Banana
Headline: Kenya: Maasai Delegation Vows to Back Banana
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A Maasai delegation that called on President Kibaki at State House, Nairobi, yesterday scoffed at the so-called Suswa Declaration recently made by the greater Ol Maa community.
The 6000-strong team, led by Education minister George Saitoti, reaffirmed support for the Banana team in the referendum campaigns.
Speakers from Kajiado, Narok, Samburu, Laikipia and Baringo districts said the people who made the declaration, only rubber-stamped the decision of a few.
The delegation included four MPs who emphasised that they would not do the bidding of what they termed self-appointed spokesmen using the community to gain political mileage.
It was preposterous, they said, for anyone to claim that they were selling the community by going to State House.
They were referring to a meeting at Suswa last month attended by Public Service minister William ole Ntimama, at which the community was reported to have vowed to vote against the proposed Constitution.
Mr Ntimama and other leaders had reportedly also accused some local leaders of leading delegations to State House to betray the community.
Also at the State House meeting yesterday were Health assistant minister Gideon Konchella, MPs Stephen ole Ntutu of Narok South, Katoo ole Metito (Kajiado south) and Mr Sammy Leshore (Samburu East) and several councillors.
The leaders had arrived at Nairobi's Uhuru Park in buses as early as 7am before proceeding to State House at noon.
They thanked the President for recently ordering that the Amboseli national park be run by the Olkejuado county council instead of the Kenya Wildlife Service.
In his address, the head of State said the decision was final despite protests and a court case filed by conservationists.
He asked the Maasai not to be misled by the Orange team, whom he described as pumbavu (silly). He reassured his visitors that their land was safe and would not be affected if the proposed Constitution was adopted.
"You Maasai should not be mislead by some people who are pumbavu, that you will lose your land if you vote Yes at the referendum," he said. "Rest assured that no-one in my government will take over your land, and that is final."
The President accused the No team of acting like wajuaji (knowledgeable people), and asked the Maasai to ignore what he called lies by the Orange team, and vote Yes on November 21.
"The councillors vowed not to allow any politician in the region to misguide them, and appealed to the President to split the eight constituencies.
They also wanted major roads in their areas tarmacked and more boarding schools built for their daughters, among other development projects.
The delegation was addressed also by three Cabinet ministers not from the five districts - Dr Chris Murungaru of Transport), Mr John Michuki (Internal Security) and Mr Amos Kimunya of Lands.
First Lady Lucy Kibaki said she was recently shocked to see and hear two Cabinet ministers on television claiming that the proposed Constitution was bad because it supports abortion; while the other minister claimed that it would disenfranchise her people as far as land is concerned.
First Lady Lucy Kibaki yesterday publicly declared her support for the Yes campaign and accused some Cabinet ministers of lying to the public about the contents of the proposed Constitution.
Mrs Kibaki cited claims by some of the No campaign luminaries, whom she did not name, on abortion, land and that President Mwai Kibaki's executive powers would be enhanced to make him as powerful as a king.
Breaking her silence on the referendum debate, the First Lady said: "These leaders want to tell you lies, because most of them do not respect you. They are even saying that the President will have more powers like a king, a king of where? England?"
Mrs Kibaki explained that her silence was because she respected the law and did not want to start premature campaigns, like other politicians, until yesterday which was the first day for the one-month exercise officially.
"People have been asking why me I have been so quite? This was because I did not want to break the law, I leave it up to the Electoral Commission of Kenya chairman Mr Samuel Kivuitu to act on those who breached the law," the First Lady told the 6,000-strong delegation of Maasais who visited State House.

Original_Search_Title: Rival Groups Launch Their Campaigns
Headline: Kenya: Rival Groups Launch Their Campaigns
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Banana and Orange teams launched their official campaigns countrywide with different messages: Vote Yes and have a new nation; vote No to get the people-driven Bomas Draft.
The Orange team sent a high-profile team to Ukambani with most of their leading lights on the campaign trail, where they announced that should they win, they would agitate for the Bomas version that was put together by delegates drawn from all parts of the country.
But groups from the Banana team went in different directions to launch their campaigns in the lead up to the November 21 vote - the first ever in Kenyan history. (See separate stories inside)
Both teams received a boost when two MPs in the Middle Ground decided to take a stand: Ijara MP Yusuf Haji joined the No team while assistant minister Joseph Nyaga declared support for the Yes group.
Mr Uhuru Kenyatta
Yesterday, the Electoral Commission assured Kenyans it was ready to conduct the vote.
The opposing camps, which embarked on their campaigns long before the official date set by ECK, will now face the public in a month-long race before the ballot.
The rallies, which have hitherto been marked with violence and name-calling, are supposed to come to end a day before the referendum date.
The ECK public relations officer, Mr Mani Lamaiyan, said they were prepared for the exercise that will either give Kenyans a new Constitution or retain the current one.
"We at the commission are all set for the work that is ahead of us. Most of the work has been completed and our staff are on the ground preparing for the referendum," he told the Nation on the phone.
The beginning of the official campaign period means the ECK will now monitor closely those flouting electoral rules which prohibit violence, use of public resources and careless utterances.
Commission chairman Samuel Kivuitu has warned both camps against inciting the public to violence and using of public resources to win votes.
Addressing the nation during the Kenyatta Day celebrations on Thursday, President Kibaki appealed to Kenyans to maintain peace during the campaigns and turn out in large numbers to vote.
He told the crowd that the fate of the new Constitution was now in their hands - and he urged them not to be misled by leaders from either side.
Yesterday, Mr Lamaiyan said the commission had trained returning officers and deployed them to the country's 210 constituencies. "They are now busy taking charge of the process in each constituency with assistance of our commissioners who have be assigned to particular regions," he said.
The tasks include recruitment of clerks and organising training for presiding officers who will work under them. Due to the large number of presiding officers, he said, they will be trained in 20 centres identified by the commission countrywide. "It was difficult to bring all of them to Nairobi."
Construction of booths in the 18,000 polling stations to be used for the exercise has started. Each polling station will handle registers of only 500 voters to avoid long queues such as those witnessed in past general elections.
During the polling, a voter will be required to show his or her identity card and voter's card. People requiring assistance to cast their vote (such as those with sight impairment, the ones who cannot read or write), will be assisted by one person of their choice.
No person will be allowed to assist more than one voter, according to a new ECK rule.
To ensure this does not happen, indelible ink will used on the index finger of the person assisting such a voter.
According to national referendum rules, polling in a station can only be stopped on the grounds of interruption by riots, open violence, floods, natural catastrophe, shortage of equipment, or administrative difficulties. But presiding officers should restart the exercise at the earliest practicable moment.
However, ballot papers, which have generated controversy, are yet to arrive, the official said. Although the Orange team claimed 15 million ballot papers had been acquired, Mr Kivuitu put the number at slightly more than 11 million.
The ECK yesterday gave both camps until Tuesday to form referendum committees at the constituency level.
Mr Kivuitu said the decision was agreed upon during a meeting last week with members of national committees of the Yes and No teams.
"We agreed that the constituency referendum committees must be registered by October 25. This day is very close," he said in a statement.
Establishment of constituency referendum committees is one of the requirements of the referendum rules and regulations tabled in Parliament by Justice and Constitutional Affairs assistant minister Njeru Githae in August.
Parliament is expected to debate the rules and endorse them before adjourning. Energy minister Simeon Nyachae heads the Banana team's national referendum committee while Nominated MP Mutula Kilonzo chairs the Orange group's committee.

Original_Search_Title: Rival Camps Welcome Poll But Orange Team Rejects Figures
Headline: Kenya: Rival Camps Welcome Poll But Orange Team Rejects Figures
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange and Banana teams yesterday welcomed the spirit of the opinion poll but questioned the gap separating the two camps.
The No group, which was placed ahead with a 10 per cent margin in the survey by Steadman company, said the gap was higher than it was stated.
Their rivals, the Yes team, took outcome in their stride and said they will overturn the tables within the coming four weeks.
Were the plebiscite held today, the survey revealed, 42 percent of Kenyans would vote No while 32 per cent would vote in favour of the Draft constitution. Twenty-two per cent of voters were undecided over which way to vote.
Cabinet ministers Musikari Kombo and Joseph Munyao said the outcome showed that the euphoria on which the Orange campaigns were riding had begun to fade.
Mr Kombo, the Local Government minister, said the Banana team was gradually gaining ground in their campaigns and the statistics will change in the remaining period.
"The poll results clearly show that the euphoria with which the Orange team started its campaign is now waning and the Banana team is gaining ground. When they started the campaigns earlier, the gap was much higher," said the Ford Kenya chairman.
Kenyans, after reading the new Constitution, he said, were now realising that what they were being told by the No team were lies meant to achieve short term political interests.
Mr Munyao, the Livestock and Fisheries minister, said the results were honest to a certain extent because the Banana camp was yet to launch their campaigns. He said the Orange team was the first to hit the ground campaigning against the Draft even before Kenyans had read it.
"It is easier to sell a lie than the truth but the danger is that while it is sweeter to listen to lies, the end result is bitter. Give us one week and our campaigns will show big strides towards winning the referendum because the people now have developed an interest in reading the Draft Constitution," he said.
Information minister Raphael Tuju (Banana camp) described the polls as "very encouraging". They showed that the Orange camp was disintegrating every week.
The minister said the No team's estimation that they were leading by a margin of 60 per cent was based on crowds which attend their meetings. "But people turn up at rallies to look at clowns, listen to music and satisfy curiosity about some leaders. It does not translate into votes," he said.
The Rarieda MP said he has cancelled his rally in Kisumu today to avoid confrontation with the No team. The rally will now take place on October 29.
However, Orange team members, Cabinet ministers Raila Odinga and Najib Balala and former vice president Musalia Mudavadi questioned the margin in the results.
Mr Odinga dismissed the poll saying it was meant to give misplaced confidence to the Banana side.
He said that judging by the crowds the team has been attracting in their rallies, the Orange team must be leading with over 60 per cent. It is obvious, he said, that somebody was playing around with figures.
He questioned why they conducted a presidential poll saying it was a diversionary tactic when Kenyans are more concerned about the referendum.
Mr Balala said the gap was higher than announced by Steadman and said their own survey had shown that they were leading with 65 per cent compared to the Yes team's 35 per cent.
The Mvita MP said the Orange team was leading in polls in Nyanza, Western, Coast and Eastern Provinces.
Mr Mudavadi, while agreeing with the general thrust of the survey, described the 10 per cent gap as "too polite." The former Sabatia MP said they were leading in polls by more than 70 per cent.
Nominated MP Mutula Kilonzo (No camp) said the margin given was much smaller than the reality on the ground and said the polls had underestimated the lead by the Orange team.
But assistant minister Maina Kamanda and Kabete MP Paul Muite dismissed the poll survey as misleading. Mr Kamanda, the Local Government assistant minister, said the pollsters were not fair to the Banana camp.
"The poll results are unfair to the Banana team, are misleading and should not be taken seriously. I am confident that the Banana team in my rating is higher than 65 percent," he said.
Mr Muite, a Safina MP, questioned those behind the survey arguing that they dictated the outcome. "There is only one thing that I can tell you: He who pays the piper calls the tunes," he said.
Finance assistant minister Henry Obwocha, while questioning the areas where the survey was undertaken, said the Orange team was ahead because it started the campaigns earlier. However, he was optimistic the figures will change because people have now started reading the new Draft which was distributed by the Government.
While declining to give a firm comment, Government Chief Whip Norman Nyagah said the results would assist them in their campaigns.

Original_Search_Title: Banana On Steady Rise, Says Minister
Headline: Kenya: Banana On Steady Rise, Says Minister
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Banana team yesterday said they were rising steadily in their campaigns against the Orange team in the referendum as indicated by the Steadman Opinion poll.
"The Orange team rated us at 20 per cent while they said they were at 80 per cent. Why the sudden drop?" asked Information minister Raphael Tuju.
"We are slow but sure. The Orange team's lies are taking them down as we go up with the truth," said Tuju.
He said claims that the proposed constitution allowed for gay marriage had cost to the Orange campaign popularity.
The Orange team was rated at 42 per cent, a difference of 10 per cent with the Banana team, which was rating at 32 per cent in the polls.
Ford Kenya chairman Musikari Kombo said he was on the ground in Western Province and was confidentt the Yes would win most of the 1.2 million votes in the province.
"We are on the ground and you will see the results at the count of the ballot. We are busy at the campaign and we have broken new grounds," said Kombo.
The Steadman survey also showed President Kibaki remained the favourite compared with Environment minister Kalonzo Musyoka, who rated second, and Kanu chairman Uhuru Kenyatta, who was placed third were elections to be called this month.
Assistant minister Maina Kamanda said the Yes team was above the rating by Steaman.
He said the opinion was biased as a Banana team survey indicated they had a popularity rating of over 65 per cent.
Last week, the Yes secretariat director-general Titus Mbathi said an opinion poll had indicated that the Banana team was rated at 55 per cent against Orange's 45 per cent on the referendum votes.

Original_Search_Title: Narc Would Win If Polls Were to Be Held Today
Headline: Kenya: Narc Would Win If Polls Were to Be Held Today
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Narc, the ruling coalition, has the highest number of followers according to the opinion polls.
Narc, which brings together 13 political parties, takes 30 per cent of the people polled.
Even then, the Government includes Cabinet ministers from the Official Opposition, Kanu.
One of them is Energy minister Simeon Nyachae, who is chairman of the Banana team's referendum campaign committee.
The polls results show that if the country were to go to the polls today with Narc still intact, it would garner the majority votes.
But the Liberal Democratic Party (LDP) is the single most popular party, with a following of 20 per cent of respondents.
Former ruling party Kanu follows with 16 per cent, while the National Alliance of Kenya (NAK) comes fourth with a poor 5 per cent.
President Kibaki's party DP is ranked fifth, with 3 per cent. Also with the same rating is Ford Kenya. Ford People follows with 2 per cent.
LDP's popularity is due to its opposition to the Wako Draft, which many Kenyans see as not representative of their views.
The President's Democratic Party's dismal performance is due to the bad image associated with Cabinet ministers affiliated to it.
Respondents saw LDP leadership as consistent in articulating the common man's rights and will to govern well.
Kanu's rising star is because of its strong opposition to the proposed constitution and articulation of the common man's rights.
The recent eviction of residents of Mau forest and Kanu's efforts to push for Government resettlement of the victims have popularised the party.
It shows that those opposed to the Wako draft unanimously reject NAK affiliated parties, throwing their weight behind LDP or Kanu.
The poll, coming out a day after the campaigns began officially, can be seen as the public's indication on which party they would opt for if the Narc falls out of power.

Original_Search_Title: Orange in the Lead
Headline: Kenya: Orange in the Lead
Body_Text:
Nairobi — An opinion poll on the forthcoming referendum shows the Orange camp
leading with 42 per cent against the Banana camp's 32 per cent.
The poll, conducted by Steadman Group and whose results were released in
Nairobi yesterday, also indicates that President Kibaki is the leading
presidential candidate, with Cabinet minister Kalonzo Musyoka and Kanu
chairman Uhuru Kenyatta taking second and third positions respectively.
On the referendum, the poll, conducted through face-to-face interviews
between October 11 and October 17 in 53 districts, found that 22 per cent of
those polled were still undecided on how they would vote, while four per
cent would not reveal their intentions.
The Orange No camp scores highest in urban areas where it takes 48 per
cent of the vote against the Banana team's 28 per cent.
Those said to be undecided make up 19 per cent.
In the rural areas, the No team still takes the lead, but with a small
margin. It scores 39 per cent against the Yes team's 34 per cent.
Here, those said to be undecided stand at 23 per cent.
The poll results elicited mixed reactions from key players in both the
Orange and Banana camps. The Orange team, led by Cabinet minister Raila
Odinga and Uhuru, dismissed the poll as inaccurate.
"We are much more ahead than that," said Raila, in reference to the
10-percentage margin the poll gave his team. Uhuru termed the poll mere
propaganda while Liberal Democratic Party secretary-general Joseph Kamotho
said the results did not reflect the massive turnout the team has had at
rallies.
But in the Banana camp, Cabinet minister Raphael Tuju said the poll
indicated that his team was "slowly but surely" picking up.
However, assistant minister Maina Kamanda declared that the Yes team was
