above the rating by Steadman. He said the poll was biased as the Banana team
had done its own survey, which indicated that it was at over 65 per
cent.
The pollsters, who say one of their objectives in to provide data for
referendum campaign strategies, warn that there is still ample time
remaining to the November 21 poll.
They point to the impact of campaigns and civic education as factors
that could influence the poll ratings.
They also take note of the large group of voters who remain undecided,
saying that there is no way of predicting how they will vote.
The pollsters also say that succeeding in getting those already
committed to Yes or No position to actually vote on November 21 may be as
important as convincing those still undecided to take a position.
Another key factor that could determine the vote is late euphoria waves
and voting blocs.
The pollsters say that as was seen in the last three general elections,
many voters are likely to be swayed by last-minute swings in opinion.
Other objectives of the poll were to establish the extent of civic
education so far, to establish the people's views regarding the proposed
constitution, to establish the voting intentions and patterns and to gauge
the levels of presidential candidate popularity.
The poll shows that majority of Kenyans wants the planned referendum to
be held as scheduled. Sixty-seven per cent of those interviewed said the
date of the referendum should remain November 21 while 24 per cent would
like the poll postponed.
And majority of those interviewed said material inducements would not
influence how they vote at the forthcoming referendum, with 57 per cent
saying that their voting pattern would be influenced by their own reading of
the draft constitution and other written material.
Thirty-five per cent expect their voting patterns to be influenced by
civic education, 20 per cent look to guidance from religious leaders and 19
per cent would seek guidance on how to vote from political and/or community
leaders.
On civic education, only 10 per cent of the respondents reported having
attended any civic education programme on the draft Constitution.
The pollsters found that 91 per cent of respondents in the rural areas
had not attended any civic education programme.
Only 45 and 15 per cent of those interviewed had read or accessed
summaries of the proposed and current constitutions respectively.
Forty per cent of the respondents said the Bomas Draft best reflects the
views of Kenyans compared to 24 who are of the opinion that the Wako Draft
best captures the views.
Only nine per cent support the current Constitution while 21 per cent
were unsure.
The results also indicate that an increasing number of Kenyans are
losing hope of the Government ever fulfilling promises it made to them
during the last election campaigns.
While in July this year 65 per cent of the Kenyans believed the
Government was somehow still committed to fulfilling the promises, this
figure has reduced to 58 per cent.

Original_Search_Title: Pro-National Conference Group Established in Bad Faith - Parties Conference Chairman
Headline: Nigeria: Pronaco Established in Bad Faith - Balarabe Musa
Body_Text:
Lagos — Chairman of Conference of Nigerian Political Parties (CNPP), Alhaji
Balarabe Musa, has condemned the activities of Pro-National Conference
(PRONACO), saying the body was established in bad faith and that its
proposed People's Conference is "subversive."
Musa, in an exclusive interview with THISDAY in his Kaduna home, traced
the history of the formation of PRONACO, saying Pa Anthony Enahoro undercut
the original movers of the idea "for whatever reason."
He said "long before President Olusegun Obasanjo's National Political
Reform Conference, when CNPP was planning a mass action held in Abuja, we
contacted leaders of all political parties, civil society and all ethnic
organisations for support for mass action. In the course of our
consultations, we realised that CNPP cannot hold an effective and successful
mass action because CNPP's grievances concerned the whole country. So, we
decided that to make the mass action successful, it should be conducted and
controlled by a broader and non-partisan body. We established Nigerians
United for Democracy (NUD) for the sole purpose of conducting the mass
action."
Musa, who is also National Chairman of Peoples Redemption Party (PRP),
said "NUD was headed by Pa Anthony Enahoro as the protem Chairman, Maxi Okwu
as the protem National Secretary and myself as the Chairman of NUD Sovereign
National Conference. Of course, we realised after the mass action that
Sovereign National Conference (SNC) was a serious issue. So, we established
the sub-committee under NUD for this purpose. Our responsibility at the
committee was to consult as many organisations throughout the country as
possible that uphold SNC. We had a series of meeting in Lagos and Abuja. All
the meetings in Lagos were chaired by Pa Enahoro and the committee meetings
for SNC held in Abuja under my chairmanship."
"In the last quarter of 2004, we held our last meeting at Airport Hotel
in Lagos and decided that there should be a democratic election of the
leadership of NUD. Pa Enahoro was asked to convene the meeting for the
election of the leadership.
We identified 30 active political parties, civil society and ethnic
organisations that should be involved in the elections, we said each one of
them should bring I think five delegates to elect NUD leaders. But
unfortunately for whatever reason, Pa Enahoro did not call the meeting for
the election, instead he called a meeting at which PRONACO was
established. We opposed the action and since that time he did not call
meeting of NUD, and of course we couldn't participate in the activities of
PRONACO," he said.
When asked on the probable alternative to the present discordant tunes
over the much-touted people's conference, Alhaji Musa said PRONACO members
"have to abandon it," adding that he "would not attend this conference," and
described it as "subversive."
Musa described the ruling Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) as "the tragedy
of Nigeria," saying "it is the tragedy of democracy in Nigeria."
"PDP did not win a free and fair election, they declared themselves the
winners. Secondly, the PDP led government has not been able to perform for
six years, and they want to deceive themselves that in the next one and half
years they will make the difference. It is impossible. Nigeria is not a
Banana Republic. You can't consecutively for six years and hoped that you
can make the difference in two years. PDP is so bankrupt that even the party
has no respect for its constitution. The President does not respect any
constitution of the party, as far his concerned President Obasanjo is the
party. As it is now, the President has abused all the provisions for
separation of powers contained in 1999 constitution. the legislature, the
judiciary and the executive are supposed to check one another in a system of
separation of power. But President Obasanjo has now consistently transformed
this into only one power-executive even that power has been personalised, it
has become the Obasanjo power, PDP cannot even do anything. Obasanjo decides
at anytime who should be the leader of PDP. He decides what should be the
action of PDP members of National Assembly who are 2/3 of the assembly. PDP
is involved in self-elimination. Even the anti-corruption campaign. PDP is
not helping matter at all," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki Calls for Peaceful Campaigns
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki Calls for Peaceful Campaigns
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki yesterday sent a passionate appeal to Kenyans for them
to maintain peace during the referendum campaigns and to turn out in large
numbers to vote.
And he was quick to defend his own controversial decision to hand out
title deeds to members of the Ogiek community.
"I urge leaders not to politicise land matters and instead support the
Government's efforts in helping the landless to acquire land; it is the
right of Kenyans to own land," he said.
His presentation of the title deeds - which came in the face of a court
order blocking the move - was criticised by members of the No-vote campaign
team as an attempt to get the community to vote in favour of the proposed
Constitution.
But he said the Government was determined to settle the landless and had
already given homes 64,600 families in 76 schemes covering 230,000 hectares
at the Coast.
The Head of State told a large crowd at the Kenyatta Day celebrations at
Nyayo National Stadium, Nairobi, the fate of the new Constitution was now in
their hands - and he urged them not to be misled by leaders from either
side.
To ensure everyone could play their part in this "historical milestone"
the President declared November 21 a national holiday.
"It is not for me to stand up and elaborate what you will do on that
day," he said. "My only point is let us have peace and encourage peace."
Administration Police officer Asnath Mumbu salutes as she marches past
the Presidential dais during the celebrations at Nyayo National Stadium,
Nairobi, yesterday. Photo by Joseph Mathenge .
Mr Kibaki continued: "We have the duty to build the nation, live
together and let not anyone assume that after the vote you will not live
together. Make your neighbour a good neighbour."
The president peppered his half-hour speech with frequent appeals for
peace and for people not to allow divergent opinions about the Constitution
divide them.
In a key passage, he said: "As a social contract governing our social,
economic and political intercourse, the Constitution should strengthen our
bonds as one people and should never be the basis for social divisions and
polarisation of the nation."
To ensure people were well informed about the proposed Constitution, the
Government had made copies of it available to all so everyone could read it
and come to a decision.
The intention, the President said, was to enable the public to
counter-check distortions, misleading information and falsehoods being
spread by politicians on both sides as they campaigned for or against the
Draft.
"We have left the decision to you. We have produced in written form the
kind of Constitution you wanted to have. The decision lies with you. Study
it, consider your own country and make a decision knowing very well it will
have a lasting impact on the nation," he continued.
The fete was the last celebration of the national event as Kenyatta
Day. From next year its name will be changed to Heroes Day.
And yesterday's ceremonies were attended by the widows of two Kenyan
heroes - freedom fighter Dedan Kimathi and JM Kariuki - Mrs Mukami Kimathi
and Mrs Terry Kariuki.
With the President were Vice-President Moody Awori, Cabinet ministers,
MPs, envoys, National Assembly Speaker Francis ole Kaparo, Chief Justice
Evan Gicheru, the Chief of General Staff, General Jeremiah Kianga, Mama
Ngina Kenyatta and Official Opposition leader Uhuru Kenyatta.
There was a moment of confusion among the crowd when key leaders of the
No-vote referendum campaign -Cabinet ministers Raila Odinga, Najib Balala
and Anyang Nyong'o - walked into the stadium sporting orange sashes.
At first the crowd thought they were making a political statement,
reinforcing their campaign against the new Constitution, which has already
seen them wearing orange shirts, caps and tee-shirts.
Murmurings grew
Students from public and private universities sing during yesterday's
Kenyatta Day celebrations at the Nyayo National Stadium, Nairobi. Photo by
Joseph Mathenge
And the murmurings grew as Cabinet ministers Musikari Kombo, Martha
Karua, Mukhisa Kituyi and Amos Kimunya - all firmly in the Yes-vote camp -
also strode onto the dais wearing similar orange sashes.
However, all became clear when it was realised the sashes were those
given to past and current Cabinet ministers so each could pin on the medal
of Elder of the Golden Heart given automatically for serving in the
Cabinet. The majority of the Ministers present wore the sashes, and those
who did not pinned their medals of honour and distinguished service on their
coat lapels.
Taking to the podium at 1pm after the Armed Forces, choirs and Vitimbi
comedians, President Kibaki, who backs the Yes group, immediately urged
leaders to stop dividing people in their campaigns.
Some of them, he said, were misleading the public on the contents of the
new Constitution in their bid to satisfy their personal ambitions.
"I call upon leaders not to divide Kenyans but instead to provide
objective and truthful leadership. I challenge leaders to observe honesty
and integrity during the on-going campaigns," he said.
He urged them to embrace compromise and consensus and to accommodate
diverse interests as freedom fighters had done during the Lancaster House
conference which gave the country the basis of the current
Constitution.
"We should emulate the patriotic and selfless spirit that guided our
national heroes in working out the independence Constitution 42 years ago at
Lancaster House," he said.
The President's call comes in the wake of heightened campaigns by both
the Banana Yes-vote and Orange No-vote groups.
Violence, name calling and accusations of distortion and falsehoods have
dominated the campaigns so far.
President Kibaki, in a move that dimmed the hopes of the Middle Ground
MPs and Protestant churches who want the referendum postponed, said
delivering a new Constitution was a priority of his Government.
"Come out and express your democratic right so as to pull ourselves out
of the past, in the spirit of reconciliation and move forward to build a new
Kenya," he said.
Then, extolling what he said were positive aspects of the Draft, Mr
Kibaki said it would end discrimination against women in leadership and
employment, give young people chance to realise their potential and ensure
slum dwellers lived decently.
Government's achievements
The President went on to give a rundown of his Government's achievements
and commended people for the "impressive" economic growth recorded in the
year. He said he was optimistic the economy would grow by five per cent this
year.
The Government had earmarked 35 road projects worth Sh23 billion in the
last financial year, and since the National Rainbow Coalition had taken
power, 1,233 kilometres of road had been repaired.

Original_Search_Title: Official Campaign Kicks Off Today
Headline: Kenya: Official Campaign Kicks Off Today
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The official campaign period for the November 21 referendum kicks off
today, two months after the Yes and the No teams started premature
campaigns.
But the two camps on the proposed Constitution have four more days to
present their full list of the 210 constituency committee members to the
Electoral Commission of Kenya.
The parallel national referendum committee will then be registered by
the commission on Tuesday.
Each committee member will then be legally bound by the referendum rules
or face disciplinary action.
The campaign officially ends on November 19 while the results will be
announced two days after voting day.
The leading lights in the Yes Camp are President Kibaki, Justice and
Constitutional Affairs minister Kiraitu Murungi and Energy minister Simeon
Nyachae, who is chairman of the Parliamentary Select Committee on the
Constitution.
In the Orange Camp are Roads minister Raila Odinga, Environment minister
Kalonzo Musyoka, Kanu national chairman Uhuru Kenyatta and secretary-general
William Ruto.
The Banana team's secretariat, where the Kenya School of Law used to be
housed, on Valley Road, is headed by the chairman of the Kenya Electricity
Generating Company (KenGen), Mr Titus Mbathi, as the director-general, with
Mr Nyachae, who is the Ford People leader, as national chairman.
The No Camp's headquarters is in Nairobi's Upper Hill area, near
Kenyatta National Hospital, and is headed by Ms Janet Ong'era as director
and Mr David Makali as deputy director and communications manager.
In another development, Kabete MP Paul Muite asked the ECK to strictly
enforce the law as it has promised.
Mr Muite said the country had witnessed violence orchestrated by hired
youths.
He said: "This is the time for the commission to put its foot down and
ensure the regulations are followed."
The MP also appealed to the police to ensure security to stop youths
from causing mayhem. Elsewhere, Mr Makali announced that the Orange camp
would launch its fund-raising campaign next week after the ECK registers its
full list of constituency committee members.
Speaking to the Nation by phone, Mr Makali said: "We were not expecting
the Government to pay for our polling agents at the referendum. We are
waiting for ECK to register our referendum committee so that we can start
raising funds next week.
"At the moment, we have no funds and are relying on volunteers and
wellwishers to assist us."

Original_Search_Title: Referendum Will Be a Fraud On Kenyans
Headline: Kenya: Referendum Will Be a Fraud On Kenyans
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The debate on the proposed new Constitution is taking a decidedly dangerous dimension as we approach the referendum on November 21, and then utterances and conduct on both sides of the fruity divide are not inspiring.
In fact, it appears that in their zeal to win at any cost, the protagonists have thrown all caution to the wind and have no qualms about massacring, twisting or stretching the truth.
But as the truth suffers, so does the best interest of this nation, and it appears that the entire referendum thing is probably an unmitigated fraud on Kenyans.
That Kenyans have for the longest time desired a new Constitution is too plain for argument. Kenyans have condemned the wanton experimentation with their rights that has attended our post-independence socio-political experience, and have long ago decided that the major culprit in the theft of our liberty has been a Constitution all too amenable to capture by the wielders of executive power.
It is no accident that one of the most strident demands of the people during the clamour for a new constitutional dispensation was the radical reduction, rationalisation and reining in of presidential powers which were perceived as an invitation to dictatorship and a ticket to kleptocracy and patronage politics.
An imponderable dilemma
In those circumstances, then, if the question were merely whether Kenyans want a new Constitution, the answer would be a resounding YES. The question that fails to be decided, however, is not that but a mischievously worded one: Do Kenyans want the Wako Draft of the Constitution?
This question poses an imponderable dilemma to all Kenyans, for it presents a Hobson's choice. Whatever the decision will be when the last vote is counted after November 21, the question foremost in the minds of Kenyans will likely be: What have we done?
To my mind, the referendum question as framed is a cruel trick on Kenyans. That we want a new Constitution does not mean it is the Wako Draft we want, just like a "NO" to the Wako Draft is not necessarily a rejection of a new constitutional order or an endorsement of the current Constitution. Yet the question as framed seems to attract either of these fallacious conclusions.
It is a matter much to be regretted that the proposed new Constitution declares itself inviolable. In Article 2(2), it pronounces a textual dictatorship by hoisting itself far beyond the reach of the courts to question its validity or legality. For purposes of clarity, this particular pronouncement is a child of Bomas.
Quite clearly the forces that had the upper hand at Bomas sought to shut out any legal question by those unhappy with the process, composition and product of the National Constitutional Conference. Little did they know that the same provision, so useful and attractive once upon a time, would come to haunt them less than two years later. Herein lies the extreme folly of political opportunism in constitution-making.
Apart from the immunity from challenge and the pretence to infallibility with which the Wako Draft invests itself, the law that births it, the so-called Consensus Act, also puts a monetary roadblock to any attempt to challenge the referendum outcome.
Anybody seeking to question the conduct of the referendum or the outcome must within seven days of filing suit at the High Court deposit a whopping Sh5 million as security for costs. This essentially means that unless one is very rich or does not mind courting financial ruin, he cannot approach the High Court, no matter how the referendum is conducted and even if the result were to be outright rigged.
This iron ring of protection is an acceptable paradox for a Constitution meant to belong to the people of Kenya irrespective of social status or financial weight. It makes nonsense of the Draft's own declaration that one of the values, principles and goals that should inform our body politic is the attainment of institutions of justice that are independent, competent, timely and affordable.
The protective legal hedge that the proposed new Constitution and the latest amendments to the Review Act throw about the referendum and the document are an attempt to circumvent the requirements of Section 47 of the current Constitution.
They are poor attempts, and, far from assuring a safe coming into being of a new Constitution with nationwide acceptance, they are likely to drive a dangerous wedge between Kenyans.
Government has been advised on several occasions and Parliament has been told in clear tones that Section 47 cannot be ignored or wished away. Without amending that section to permit a replacement of the current Constitution by a new, so the advice has gone, the labours are in vain and the proclamation of a new Constitution on December 12, 2005, would be an unlawful act in contravention of the very Constitution that the President swore to protect and uphold.
Most legislators understand the need to amend Section 47 for the presidential proclamation to be lawful, but such an amendment would require two-thirds majority, which is well-nigh impossible in the current highly-polarised Parliament.
Unbelievable cheek
For the Government to ignore Section 47 is an act of unbelievable cheek. The last of the many facets of fraud is the inadequacy of the civic education, a mandatory precursor to the referendum. The process has been slow. It is doubtful the CKRC, despite its gallant efforts, will have done nearly enough before the vote. There are not enough copies of the new Constitution.
Further, whereas there have been attempts to distribute copies of the proposed new Constitution, the current Constitution is not being distributed. Nor is the Bomas Draft, which is being lionised and touted by some as the prototype Constitution.
Upon what basis, then, will Kenyans be able to decide for or against the Draft without the benefit of comparison? Are we not being placed in the unhappy situation of the proverbial blind man in a dark room looking for a black hat that is not even there in the first place?
The situation is compounded by the fact that the atmosphere countrywide is so charged that civic education can hardly be heard above the din of politicians going orange or bananas. Politicians have spewed the most outrageous falsehoods and uttered the most alarming pieces of misinformation in order to sway voters. Attempts by the Electoral Commission to instil some sanity to the debate have fallen on deaf ears.
Mr Kiage is an advocate practising in Nairobi.

Original_Search_Title: Moi Distances Himself From Orange Team
Headline: Kenya: Moi Distances Himself From Orange Team
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The only thing retired President Moi has in common with the Orange team is the conviction that the proposed Constitution is flawed.
In a statement released his Press secretary, Mr Lee Njiru, yesterday, Mr Moi reiterated that, although he is opposed to the proposals, he was not funding the Orange campaigns.
He asked the Press to report accurately, especially at this critical stage of Kenya's political history.
Repeated allegations
Mr Moi
The retired president spoke in the wake of repeated allegations by the Banana camp that he is one of those bankrolling the Orange campaigns to reject the new law in the November 21 referendum.
At a Banana rally in Murang'a on Sunday, Kabete MP Paul Muite claimed the Orange agenda team was to take over the Government through unconstitutional means.
Mr Muite alleged that Mr Moi wanted to return to power through proxy.
Among the leaders of the Orange team are Kanu chairman Uhuru Kenyatta and Roads minister Raila Odinga.

Original_Search_Title: Debate Brings Out the Best And Worst
Headline: Kenya: Debate Brings Out the Best And Worst
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kenyans watching and listening to the Big Debate to get an insight into what some of the leaders on both sides of the proposed Constitution thought were treated to some good arguments - but also some rowdy scenes.
While the Orange team faulted the proposed Constitution on its language, contradictions and loopholes, the Banana team declared it an improvement on the current Constitution and on the Bomas Draft.
Both sides argued animatedly, sometimes getting acrimoniously rowdy and at times giving constructive arguments.
Several times in the 90-minute programme, aired on all television and radio stations on Tuesday night, moderator Rose Kimotho could not control the debate and Planning minister Peter Anyang' Nyong'o and Mr Mutula Kilonzo on the Orange side accused her of bias.
However, she remained firm, appealing to both sides to stick to issues in the proposed Constitution.
The other speakers were Trade minister Mukhisa Kituyi, Information minister Raphael Tuju and Kabete MP Paul Muite of the Banana Yes-vote team.
Nambale MP Chris Okemo was on the No-vote side.
The four journalists who posed the questions were NTV's Julie Gichuru, Mr Peter Opondo (KTN), Mr Herman Igambi (Royal Media) and Mr Charles Kerich (Kiss FM).
At times the debate got personal, particularly between Mr Tuju and Prof Nyong'o, over interpretations of the sections on executive power, land and women's place.
Short cuts to power
At different times during the debate, each side accused the other of wanting short cuts to power.
Said Mr Tuju: "The Orange team does not deal with issues in the Draft... essentially they want to get power and that is it."
And Prof Nyong'o said: "If you really want to know who wants power, Tuju founded a political party recently... as we leave this place, Kenyans know those who want meat to be kept in their mouths and those who want to give power to the people."
There was, however, every attempt by both sides to tackle contentious issues in the new laws.
Each side tried to justify why Kenyans should accept or reject the Draft over issues on the Executive, Parliament, devolution, gender and religious courts.
The legislature was the best-argued topic, free of emotions.
Mr Muite said the senate had been removed by consensus in Naivasha, while Mr Okemo said the intention of a second chamber was to share legislative responsibilities with a lower house.
Mr Kilonzo put the case forward for a No-vote, pleading to Kenyans: "Please reject this Draft. Take the impeachment of a president. It would require support by 75 per cent of MPs in the House, which is impossible, plus the fact that we shall never know the number of constituencies under the new Constitution."
On district governments, Mr Muite argued that power would be decentralised while Mr Kilonzo argued that the governments would collapse because they would have no money.
Mr Tuju argued that the Bill of Rights would be the best on the continent, but Mr Okemo argued that the creation of traditional courts would take away the gains women have already made.
But both sides gave Kenyans hope and optimism for the period after the vote.
They promised that Kenyans would go on as if nothing has happened when the November 21 referendum comes and goes.
Said Dr Kituyi: "We shall start on a healing agenda. The Government will get back to focus again on the development agenda for us to forge ahead."
No vacuum
And if Kenyans do not approve the new Constitution, Mr Kilonzo declared: "There is no vacuum in the country, we already have a Constitution."
What was on trial was a document, and not the Government, and life would go on as usual, said Mr Tuju.
Dr Kituyi said the President had promised to deliver a new constitution and debate over the document was tying people down.

Original_Search_Title: Debate Offered Apt Lessons
Headline: Kenya: Debate Offered Apt Lessons
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The debate on the proposed new Constitution aired live on Tuesday night by nearly all of Kenya's television and radio stations broke ground in many ways.
Protagonists of both sides of the political divide sat down to present their cases and answer questions on why they are pursuing their respective lines.
That it was a pivotal event can probably be illustrated by the fact that in pubs across the country, where patrons were gathered to watch the Europeans Champions league matches on the same night, the satellite TV transmissions had to be put on hold until the debate was concluded.
The local media industry certainly deserves plaudits for organising such an event. But even more plaudits go to the forces on both sides of the Constitution divide who agreed to make their representatives available for the live debate.
The people witnessed that, away from the heat of the political rostrum, Kenya's leaders can, indeed, put aside their hostilities to sit across one another and discuss in a relatively civil manner the issues that divide them. True, some inadvertent words may have been thrown in here and there. Tempers may have flared occasionally.
But all in all, the representatives of both the banana and orange camps did themselves proud by trying to make their cases based on critical analysis of the proposed Constitution.
And what the millions of viewers and listeners tuned in may have gleaned is that the heated rhetoric coming out of the political platform only presents an alarming picture of the real situation.
The point is that our political representatives may sometimes appear at war over critical issues, but they can still sit down and reason together. Thus, many might wonder why, sometimes, the country is presented as one which is almost at war, and yet away from the political platform, the "combatants" can be courteous and civil with each other.
The live debate showed that both sides are agreed on many issues. They agree that the proposed new Constitution is largely good, though it still has contentious issues.
They disagree on whether it should be thrown out entirely or passed with the defects which can then be corrected later. And this brings us to our September 23 question: Why can't our leaders sit down and iron out the few contentious issues before the referendum?

Original_Search_Title: Review Body Boss Accused of Bias
Headline: Kenya: Review Body Boss Accused of Bias
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Constitution of Kenya Review Commission chairperson Abida Ali-Aroni
yesterday came under fire from colleague Alice Yano who asked her to quit
for taking sides.
She said Aroni had compromised the impartiality of the commission by
campaigning for the Draft Constitution and urged her to vacate the
chair.
Speaking from Kabarnet where she is conducting civic education, Yano
said it was unfair for the CKRC boss was taking sides.
Yesterday, Aroni was reported as having asked women to vote for the
Draft Constitution, saying it contained many positive things for them.
While at Egerton University, Aroni said women stood to gain in many ways
by voting for the Draft Constitution and that it would liberate them.
The chairperson has, in the past been accused of taking sides in the
review process and that she favours the Yes camp.
Yesterday, Yano said she had come under fire from members of the public
in Kabarnet, who questioned her impartiality.
Elsewhere, commissioners Phoebe Asiyo and Adronico Adede yesterday
accused Aroni of being partisan. They said it was wrong for Abida to urge
women to vote "Yes" and accused her of "glorifying" the Proposed New
Constitution.
She accused Aroni of contravening the CKRC Act and her oath of office
and urged her to either relinquish her post or retract her stand.
Yano said Aroni had reduced the commission to the level of the Orange
and Banana teams.
"If she feels that she wants to campaign for the Draft Constitution, let
her resign from CKRC and campaign from outside," she said.
She said the credibility of the commission had been eroded and the best
way of redeeming it was for Aroni to quit.
"The chairperson is my friend and I wish her well, but for the sake of
the commission, she should leave or stop such utterances," said the
lawyer.

Original_Search_Title: Debate Draws Mixed Remarks
Headline: Kenya: Debate Draws Mixed Remarks
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Information minister Raphael Tuju has accused No campaigners of bringing
activism into the proposed constitution debate.
However, his Planning counterpart, Prof Anyang' Ny'ong'o, who
participated in the debate organised by the Media Owners Association (MOA),
faulted both the choice of moderator and the venue.
While he encouraged such debates in future, Ny'ong'o said the forum
needed "an independent" moderator and a neutral venue.
But Tuju told a Press conference in his office some leaders in the
Orange camp had a background of activism "saying 'No' to everything."
Ny'ong'o said of the moderator: "It is not good to have a person from
the media, they could be supporting their media."
The Planning minister said the venue from where the debate was
transmitted was not neutral.
He said it was unfair for media personalities, whose stations favoured
one camp, to ask panelists questions on democracy.
Elsewhere, tempers flared as Banana and Orange supporters clashed in
bars, restaurants and other social places in Nairobi as the Big Debate was
aired live on Tuesday night.
Supporters of the two teams jammed social places to view the live
show.
Trade and Industry minister, Dr Mukhisa Kituyi, was hailed for
articulating the contents of the Proposed New Constitution without dwelling
on sideshows.
At another joint, assistant minister Kipkalya Kones found himself in the
middle of Orange supporters.
But Kones continued to take his glass of wine calmly, following the
debate keenly.
And Banana and Orange supporters in Ogembo Town cheered as their
favourite leaders clashed during the debate.
They grouped at various meeting points, including bars, hotels and
boardrooms to watch panelists discuss.
Each time their favourite panelist spoke, viewers cheered to signify
support for either the Banana or Orange views.

Original_Search_Title: Church Group Wants Vote On Draft Put Off
Headline: Kenya: Church Group Wants Vote On Draft Put Off
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The National Council of Churches of Kenya wants the referendum delayed
for six months.
The NCCK executive committee meeting chaired by the Rev Eliud Wabukala
resolved that there were contentious issues in the Proposed New
Constitution, including the powers of the President, which needed resolving
before going to the vote.
Musyimi read a statement outlining why the referendum should be
delayed.
The church leaders also said they would reach out to Orange and the
Banana team leaders to convince them to delay the process.
They said: "The text from Bomas and the Proposed New Constitution have
been criticised for being weak in certain technical aspects. Admittedly, no
constitution is perfect."
Musyimi outlined contentious issues as, the requirement that
Presidential candidates should not simultaneously be Parliamentary
candidates.
"The other strong view from the church that remains contentious was the
design of devolution, without popular consultation, and without professional
assistance," he said.
The church leaders met at the PCEA Guest House in Nairobi where they
resolved to call for the delay of the referendum.
They said a climate of confusion, fear and insecurity had gripped the
country and that the situation was likely to worsen as we approach the
referendum.
Musyimi said: "If Yes or No win at the referendum, Kenyans stand to lose
because of the contentious issues."
The referendum on the Wako Draft is set for November 21 and the official
campaigns start tomorrow.
Musyimi said that the other contentious issues were the treatment of
land issues, the independence of the proposed land commission, and the
status of community land, land inheritance and culture. The Head of the
Anglican Church, Archbishop Benjamin Nzimbi, the Head of PCEA, Moderator
David Githii and the Presiding Bishop of the Methodist Church, Stephen
Kanyaru, attended the meeting, among others.
Musyimi said other contentious issues include the inclusion of past
human rights abuses as a subject to be dealt with under the referendum
instead of seeking other ways of dealing with the matter.
He said that the abolition of the provincial administration and its
overall implication was also contentious.
Musyimi said provisions relating to religious courts had also been
criticised, adding that the provision of equality in the Bill of Rights,
that treats persons who profess Islam differently from other Kenyans, was
also an issue to sort out.
He said the difficult amendment procedure was contentious too.
Musyimi said: "The purpose of this delay is two-fold. First to identify
and resolve contentious issues through national consensus, and second to
carry out comprehensive civic education so that Kenyans are empowered to
make informed choices."
He said it was better to delay the referendum than to proceed with
it.
"Once consensus has been built, the Oranges and the Bananas will make a
very good pudding for everyone's enjoyment," he said.
He added that the church would continue to support the constitution
making process as "we have done during the last one and a half decades."
Musyimi said there was still time to resolve the issues adding, "let us
not proceed to cut the Kenyan baby into two."

Original_Search_Title: Next Debate Should Be More Enlightened, Listeners Say
Headline: Kenya: Next Debate Should Be More Enlightened, Listeners Say
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The quality of the debate was disappointing.
The panellists never brought to the fore critical issues. They were partial and merely engaged in sideshows.

Original_Search_Title: Protestants Want Poll Postponed for Six Months
Headline: Kenya: Protestants Want Poll Postponed for Six Months
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Protestant churches yesterday suggested that the poll be delayed for at least six months and offered to help bring the Yes and No camps together to reach a consensus.
The campaigns, the National Council of [Protestant] Churches executive committee said, had created a situation in which a safe referendum could not be held.

Original_Search_Title: No, They Didn't Die for This Farce
Headline: Kenya: No, They Didn't Die for This Farce
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A capital city not in Africa, mid-last week. We had just concluding a
meeting on development matters. People were milling around, sharing business
cards and "networking".
"We are thinking of coming to Kenya in November, a woman told me. But we
are not sure whether we should, she concluded, somewhat anxiously. What do
you think?

Original_Search_Title: Avoid Politics At Koitalel's Fete, Warn MPs
Headline: Kenya: Avoid Politics At Koitalel's Fete, Warn MPs
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki should not use today's centenary celebrations of legend
Koitalel arap Samoei to campaign for the Banana team, said two MPs
yesterday.
Mr Henry Kosgey and Mr William Ruto said the President was welcome to
grace the occasion, which will give Mr Samoei the status of a national hero
for resisting British occupation of Kenya.

Original_Search_Title: Draft Constitution Debate Goes Live
Headline: Kenya: Big Debate Goes Live
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Hundreds of thousands of Kenyans sat glued to their TV sets or listened
to their radios last night as the Big Debate went live on air.
Top teams from the two camps split over the new Constitution fielded
probing questions put to them by four distinguished journalists in a special
programme broadcast by all major media houses.

Original_Search_Title: Wekesa Quits Neutral Camp for Banana
Headline: Kenya: Wekesa Quits Neutral Camp for Banana
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A leading proponent of the middle-ground group of MPs yesterday declared
his support for the proposed constitution.
Assistant Livestock minister Noah Wekesa said he would support Ford
Kenya chairman Musikari Kombo in his campaign for the Yes vote at the
November 21 referendum.

Original_Search_Title: No Politics Here, Koitalel Anniversary Organisers Say
Headline: Kenya: No Politics Here, Koitalel Anniversary Organisers Say
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Anglican church and the family of the late Koitalel Samoei yesterday
told the Orange and Banana teams to keep their differences from the Nandi
legendary leader's centenary celebrations to be marked today.
There were also sharp divisions between local leaders over the
invitation of President Kibaki. However, MPs from the larger Nandi (North
and South), who are in the Orange grouping, said they would not boycott the
celebrations as earlier feared.

Original_Search_Title: Committee Formed to Plan for WTO Talks
Headline: Kenya: Committee Formed to Plan for WTO Talks
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A committee has been formed to co-ordinate Kenya's attendance and issues
it will advocate during the December World Trade Organisation meeting in
Hong Kong.
Mr James Mwangi, a ministry of Trade official in charge of external
trade, said a sub-committee was involving other stakeholders formed to
bolster the national committee.

Original_Search_Title: Stop Using Children, Say Lobbies
Headline: Kenya: Stop Using Children, Say Lobbies
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Children's rights organisations yesterday asked politicians to stop using minors to entertain guests in campaign rallies.
And school grounds must not be used as campaign venues by either the Banana or the Orange camps, they said.

Original_Search_Title: Leaders Split Over Kibaki's Presence At Koitalel's Fete
Headline: Kenya: Leaders Split Over Kibaki's Presence At Koitalel's Fete
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Nandi leaders are divided over President Kibaki's presence at tomorrow's
centenary celebrations of legend Koitalel arap Samoei.
While one group welcomed the President at the function, another told him
to keep off, saying the festival was a purely Nandi affair.

Original_Search_Title: Live Debate On Proposed Laws Tonight
Headline: Kenya: Live Debate On Proposed Laws Tonight
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The eagerly-awaited live media debate on the proposed constitution will
take place tonight from 7.30 to 9pm.
Dubbed The Big Debate, it will feature Information minister Raphael
Tuju, Kabete MP Paul Muite and Trade minister Mukhisa Kituyi on the Banana
side while on the Orange team will be Planning minister Anyang' Nyong'o,
Nambale MP Chris Okemo and nominated MP Mutula Kilonzo.

Original_Search_Title: Police Thwart Attempt to Disrupt Orange Meeting
Headline: Kenya: Police Thwart Attempt to Disrupt Orange Meeting
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Police dispersed a Yes crowd as it tried to disrupt a No meeting at the
Timau market yesterday.
The intruders were led by four councillors from Meru Central
District.

Original_Search_Title: Banana Group Seeks Strength in Numbers
Headline: Kenya: Banana Group Seeks Strength in Numbers
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Banana team has launched a mobilisation campaign to ensure
supporters of the proposed Constitution turn out in big numbers to endorse
it one November 21.
Three Cabinet ministers and a group of MPs yesterday said the team would
not take any chances to ensure a win.

Original_Search_Title: Draft Row is a Power Game, Says President
Headline: Kenya: Draft Row is a Power Game, Says President
Body_Text:
Nairobi — People campaigning against the proposed Constitution were seeking a
route to power, President Kibaki said yesterday.
They should come out openly and declare their intentions instead of
confusing and intimidating Kenyans, he said.

Original_Search_Title: Go for Orange, Ministers Urge Pastoralists
Headline: Kenya: Go for Orange, Ministers Urge Pastoralists
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Pastoralists should reject the proposed Constitution, key leaders of the
Orange movement said yesterday.
They were addressing rallies in Lodwar and Lokichoggio in Turkana
District. The campaigns were led by Cabinet ministers Raila Odinga, Anyang
Nyong'o and Ochillo Ayacko and Kanu secretary general William Ruto.

Original_Search_Title: Maasai to Visit State House
Headline: Kenya: Maasai to Visit State House
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Maasai leaders supporting the proposed constitution have said they will
seek guidance from President Mwai Kibaki over land issues.
Education minister Prof George Saitoti, assistant minister Gideon
Konchella and Kajiado South MP Katoo ole Metitto said they would lead a
delegation of Maasai leaders to State House before month end.

Original_Search_Title: Orange And Banana in Live Debate
Headline: Kenya: Orange And Banana in Live Debate
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Yes (Banana) and No (Orange) campaigns go head-to-head this evening
in a 70-minute live radio and television debate.
Prof Anyang' Nyong'o, Mr Chris Okemo and Mr Mutula Kilonzo will front
the Orange platform while Mr Paul Muite, Mr Raphael Tuju and Dr Mukhisa
Kituyi will speak for the Banana campaign.

Original_Search_Title: Showdown Looms As Raila, Kibaki Head for Nandi Fete
Headline: Kenya: Showdown Looms As Raila, Kibaki Head for Nandi Fete
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki is expected in Nandi Hills tomorrow at a meeting where
he is likely to come face-to-face with key Orange campaign leaders.
Officially, the President will be the chief guest at a ceremony marking
100 years since the death of legendary Nandi leader, the Orkoiyot Koitalel
arap Samoei.

Original_Search_Title: International Art Agreement Signed
Headline: Namibia: International Art Agreement Signed
Body_Text:
Windhoek — A NUMBER of excited Namibian artists this week witnessed the historic signing of an international artists' twinning agreement between Oruuano Artists union and the Finnish Music Union in the capital.
The long term agreement of musical cooperation was signed with ceremony on behalf of the local union by its president, Banana Shekupe, and his Finnish counterpart, Ahti Vanttinen, under the watchful eye of Seija Kinni, Charge' d'affaires of the Finnish Embassy.

Original_Search_Title: Don't Lean On Tribe, Raila Tells Ford-K Chairman
Headline: Kenya: Don't Lean On Tribe, Raila Tells Ford-K Chairman
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Roads minister Raila Odinga yesterday told Ford Kenya chairman Musikari Kombo to stop dreaming of being president.
Raila said Kombo should stop cheap politics that was likely to lead him nowhere.

Original_Search_Title: Stick to Yes Campaign Schedule, Say Two MPs
Headline: Kenya: Stick to Yes Campaign Schedule, Say Two MPs
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Two Nyeri MPs yesterday told leaders who are supporting the proposed
Constitution to follow the Banana camp's laid down structures and end
squabbles being witnessed in the area.
Mathira MP Nderitu Gachagua and his Mukurwe-ini counterpart Mutahi Kagwe
accused some leaders of usurping roles in the forthcoming referendum, a move
they said might impact negatively.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki Criticised for Ignoring Court Order
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki Criticised for Ignoring Court Order
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki came under attack yesterday for disregarding a court
order against issuing title deeds to the Ogiek community.
The Law Society of Kenya led church and political leaders in condemning
the President's action which, they said, was likely to set a bad
precedent.

Original_Search_Title: 'Yes' is a Vote for Peace, Says Awori
Headline: Kenya: 'Yes' is a Vote for Peace, Says Awori
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Voting in favour of the proposed new Constitution would bring peace and
unity among Kenyans, Vice-President Moody Awori has said.
He was speaking at Malava Primary School in Kakamega District when he
led ministers and MPs in a campaign for the Yes vote in Western Province
yesterday. The province has 1.2 million voters.

Original_Search_Title: Ministers Given Time Off to Carry Out Campaigns
Headline: Kenya: Ministers Given Time Off to Carry Out Campaigns
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Government has allowed Cabinet ministers time off to campaign for
the forthcoming referendum on the proposed Constitution.
Government spokesman Alfred Mutua yesterday said that the State
considers the referendum as a priority agenda.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki Criticised Over Ogiek Title Deeds
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki Criticised Over Ogiek Title Deeds
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki yesterday came under heavy criticism for defying a
court order that stopped the issuance of title deeds to the Ogiek.
But the President also received backing for giving out 12,000 title
deeds over the weekend with Local Government assistant minister, Maina
Kamanda, arguing the court injunction was open to varied
interpretation.

Original_Search_Title: Bulldog Or Not, Kivuitu Commission is Toothless
Headline: Kenya: Bulldog Or Not, Kivuitu Commission is Toothless
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Let us face it. Mr Samuel Kivuitu has good reasons to be angered by
comments that associate his electoral body with a dog - whether a bulldog or
whatever species you call it, so long as it answers to the name dog.
It is even more exasperating to state that this breed of a short-haired
dog characterised by a large head and strong square jaws is flatly
toothless.

Original_Search_Title: Thorny Questions of Land And Religion May Sway Ballot
Headline: Kenya: Thorny Questions of Land And Religion May Sway Ballot
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Away from the political machinations of the Banana and Orange
campaigners, other critical issues that could sway the vote among the Luhya
revolve around land and religious courts.
The question of land ownership, for instance, is a thorny one and the
proposal that women, including abakoko (married women), will be accorded
equal rights to inherit land has not gone down well with many.

Original_Search_Title: Re-Thinking Strategies As November 21 Approaches
Headline: Kenya: Re-Thinking Strategies As November 21 Approaches
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Is the establishment of parallel Banana secretariats in Nairobi an
admission of problems in the Yes campaign? Or is it an indication the Banana
camp is not taking any chances and wants to cover every base?
Whatever the case, eyebrows were raised when a new outfit was suddenly
launched separate from the formal and older Yes secretariat headed by Mr
Titus Mbathi, which has its operational base at the Kenya School of Law
premises in Nairobi.

Original_Search_Title: Regional Politics Dominates Campaigns
Headline: Kenya: Regional Politics Dominates Campaigns
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Judging by emerging trends and recent events, a battle of titans is in
the making. The referendum arena in Western province is dusty in the
scramble for the 1.2 million registered voters.
On the ground, the November 21 referendum has more to do with regional
politics than the merits and/or demerits of the proposed new
Constitution.

Original_Search_Title: Referendum's Chance for Payback
Headline: Kenya: Referendum's Chance for Payback
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Following an extended sulk, Health Minister Charity Ngilu finally
extracted her pound of flesh on Thursday. She is now back in the Banana
camp, though what inducement that drove her to return remains unknown.
The welcome she received may not be so much because of the swell of Yes
votes she is expected to deliver from Ukambani. It was obvious this was
driven more by the need to prevent the calamity of another Cabinet minister
defecting to the Orange garden a la Linah Jebii Kilimo.

Original_Search_Title: Political Heavyweights Face Off
Headline: Kenya: Political Heavyweights Face Off
Body_Text:
Nairobi — For Western province voters - the community that has produced three
consecutive vice presidents between 2002 and today - next month's referendum
will be hinged more on the political prospects of Vice-President Moody
Awori, former VP Musalia Mudavadi and Local Government minister Musikari
Kombo who is also the chairman of the predominant party in the region - Ford
Kenya.
That the province has produced the three vice presidents - Mr Mudavadi,
the late Michael Wamalwa and Mr Awori in that order - is a factor that has
heightened political stakes and expectations among members of the Luhya
community.

Original_Search_Title: Hired Goons Were Involved, Says MP
Headline: Kenya: Hired Goons Were Involved, Says MP
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Three supporters of the banana referendum team are fighting for their
lives at a hospital in Makueni district after they were allegedly attacked
by vigilantes from the Orange side.
They were hurt on Thursday when they crossed the path of No team convoys
on their way to Mombasa.

Original_Search_Title: Orange Team Alleges Death Threats
Headline: Kenya: Orange Team Alleges Death Threats
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Claims that spies are trailing rivals of the Wako Draft and threatening some with death emerged yesterday.
The claims come a week after former President Daniel arap Moi alleged that he was being spied on.

Original_Search_Title: The Biggest Test for Ngilu's Might in Kambaland
Headline: Kenya: The Biggest Test for Ngilu's Might in Kambaland
Body_Text:
Nairobi — It will be the end of the road for Ngilu's presidential ambitions if she does not secure Kamba votes for the Yes team.
On Friday, she held a frenzied rally at the Machakos Mulu Mutisya gardens. Ngilu has been a deciding factor in Kamba politics.

Original_Search_Title: Loyalties to Determine Battle of Titans in Ukambani
Headline: Kenya: Loyalties to Determine Battle of Titans in Ukambani
Body_Text:
Nairobi — In 1972 at Meru High School, Stephen Kalonzo Musyoka persuaded fellow
students not to riot after buttered bread was withdrawn from the menu.
Toeing the official line was until recently a major characteristic of
Kalonzo whose defiance of President Moi - much to the surprise of many - in
the run-up to the 2002 General Election catapulted him to the centre of
Kamba politics.

Original_Search_Title: Banana Rally Cancelled After Murungaru Clashes With MPs
Headline: Kenya: Banana Rally Cancelled After Murungaru Clashes With MPs
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Differences among Banana supporters in Nyeri District resulted in a
last-minute cancellation of a rally yesterday at Othaya Stadium.
The meeting was to be the first for the Banana team in Nyeri District,
which is President Kibaki's backyard.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki Woos Elders As No Team Speaks of Rigging Fear
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki Woos Elders As No Team Speaks of Rigging Fear
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki has declared he will go ahead with handing out title
deeds today as planned, - heightening speculation from the Orange No-vote
team that the gifts are part of a Government move to entice support for the
proposed Constitution.
The title deeds will be given out in Nakuru district in spite of a court
injunction, issued in Nairobi on Thursday.

Original_Search_Title: You've Been Duped, Orange Tells Ngilu
Headline: Kenya: You've Been Duped, Orange Tells Ngilu
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Orange (No) campaign yesterday dismissed as a stillborn project President Kibaki's pledge to appoint Mrs Charity Ngilu prime minister.
Cabinet ministers Raila Odinga and Kalonzo Musyoka and Leader of the Official Opposition Uhuru Kenyatta derided the PM's post in the proposed new constitution as useless.

Original_Search_Title: President in Move to Allay Fears Over Land
Headline: Kenya: President in Move to Allay Fears Over Land
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Government will not pursue former State officials implicated in
human rights abuses when the new constitution comes into force, President
Kibaki said yesterday.
He instead said it would be upon individuals who felt aggrieved to
decide whether or not to move to court and seek redress against those who
wronged them.

Original_Search_Title: Minister Says No Future for NAK If No Wins
Headline: Kenya: Minister Says No Future for NAK If No Wins
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Transport minister Chris Murungaru yesterday said the prospects for the National Alliance Party of Kenya were "unthinkable" if the proposed constitution was shot down at the referendum in November.
The minister said there was no future worth talking about for NAK leaders in 2007 if majority of Kenyans voted against the draft constitution.

Original_Search_Title: West Pokot Councillors Defect to Banana Side
Headline: Kenya: West Pokot Councillors Defect to Banana Side
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A group of councillors from the Rift Valley yesterday defected to the Yes camp.
Twenty-three of them travelled from West Pokot District to Nairobi. Six MPs received them at the Banana campaign secretariat on Nairobi's Valley Road.

Original_Search_Title: Banana Camp Rally Called Off to Avoid Clash
Headline: Kenya: Banana Camp Rally Called Off to Avoid Clash
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Yes campaign team yesterday called-off a rally they had planned for Voi Town to avoid a possible confrontation with the Orange Camp supporters.
There was tension in the town yesterday when it was discovered that both the Yes and No teams had been allowed to hold rallies at the town's Moi stadium.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki Woos Elders As No Team Speaks of Rigging Fear
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki Woos Elders As No Team Speaks of Rigging Fear
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki has declared he will go ahead with handing out title deeds today as planned, - heightening speculation from the Orange No-vote team that the gifts are part of a Government move to entice support for the proposed Constitution.
The title deeds will be given out in Nakuru district in spite of a court injunction, issued in Nairobi on Thursday.

Original_Search_Title: Ngilu's Tough Conditions to Kibaki for Comeback
Headline: Kenya: Ngilu's Tough Conditions to Kibaki for Comeback
Body_Text:
Nairobi — President Kibaki welcomed Health minister Charity Ngilu back to the
Banana camp after hours of hard bargain and concessions.
Ngilu, who spent close to four hours at State House with 120 councillors
and three Members of Parliament from Eastern Province, gave the President
five conditions, which she said her community wanted met.

Original_Search_Title: You Have Sold Us, Kalonzo Tells Ngilu
Headline: Kenya: You Have Sold Us, Kalonzo Tells Ngilu
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Health Minister Charity Ngilu yesterday came under heavy criticism for
allegedly mortgaging her community for her political survival.
Thirteen Orange team MPs led by Cabinet minister Kalonzo Musyoka and
Kanu vice chairman Chris Okemo, termed Ngilu as indecisive, accusing her of
taking matters concerning her community to State House instead of solving
them.

Original_Search_Title: Alamieyeseigha Released, Remains in London
Headline: Nigeria: Alamieyeseigha Released, Remains in London
Body_Text:
London — Sixteen days after he was remanded in prison by a London Court, Bayelsa
State Governor, Diepreye Alamieyeseigha yesterday in London walked out of
Her Majesty Prison in Brixton to the embrace of friends, family and
political supporters, after his lawyers had fulfilled the security
conditions attached to a bail granted him by the Southwark Crown Court.
Alamieyeseigha who was facing a three-count charge of money laundering
was released at about 3.45pm.

Original_Search_Title: Kalonzo Takes No Crusade to the Coast
Headline: Kenya: Kalonzo Takes No Crusade to the Coast
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Members of the Orange team yesterday campaigned in several
constituencies in Ukambani as they headed to the Coast where they will
address several rallies culminating with a final one in Changamwe, Mombasa
on Sunday.
Cabinet minister Kalonzo Musyoka led a team of more than 30 MPs in
urging the people of Ukambani to vote against the proposed new
Constitution.

Original_Search_Title: Court Threat to Politician Over Campaigns
Headline: Kenya: Court Threat to Politician Over Campaigns
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Government's human rights watchdog has made good its threat to prosecute politicians breaking the law in the referendum campaigns.
Assistant minister George Khaniri who supports the Orange campaign and the Banana team's secretariat boss Titus Mbathi are likely to be the first leaders to face court if Attorney General Amos Wako backs recommendations to prosecute them.

Original_Search_Title: Draft Good for Disabled, Says Awori
Headline: Kenya: Draft Good for Disabled, Says Awori
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The proposed new Constitution addresses the interests of people with
disability, Vice-President Moody Awori said yesterday.
"This is not a Banana or an Orange rally, but what is important is to
remember that the draft fully recognises people with disability," he told a
gathering marking the World Sight Day in Nairobi.

Original_Search_Title: Youths Disrupt Review Civic Education
Headline: Kenya: Youths Disrupt Review Civic Education
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Threats of violence marred yet another civic education meeting at
Kisumu. A group of youths attempted to eject constitution review
commissioners from a meeting hall.
The meeting, at the town's social hall, was delayed for several hours as
police struggled to contain the youths who stormed the venue just before the
meeting started.

Original_Search_Title: Fear Over Uhuru And Karume Rallies
Headline: Kenya: Fear Over Uhuru And Karume Rallies
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kiambu police were yesterday making frantic efforts to have a Cabinet
minister cancel a rally he has called in his constituency tomorrow.
Special Programmes minister Njenga Karume's meeting at Ndumberi was
licensed on Tuesday after another called by official opposition leader Uhuru
Kenyatta at Karuri had already received the nod.

Original_Search_Title: Ngilu Backs Yes Team After Meeting With Kibaki
Headline: Kenya: Ngilu Backs Yes Team After Meeting With Kibaki
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Health minister Charity Ngilu yesterday met President Kibaki at State
House then immediately declared herself in favour of a Yes vote in the
proposed Constitution.
"I fully support the new Constitution and I know, because it is a good
document, it will sail through during the referendum," said Mrs Ngilu.

Original_Search_Title: MDGs: Dream Or Reality?
Headline: Rwanda: MDGs: Dream Or Reality?
Body_Text:
Kigali — Some weeks ago, there were media reports of how HIV/Aids NGOs in Uganda had embezzled tons of dollars. These institutions had been established to help the poor in that country access drugs at least to cope with the scourge. The poor man's gift was stolen yet one of the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs) is easy access to health.
At least in East Africa, we have witnessed several referendums in various countries aiming at changing a clause here and there in the constitutions. A lot of money has been spent which could otherwise have been translated into various developmental issues. But why? Simply, because the powers that be have already been corrupted; who said power corrupts?

Original_Search_Title: MPs to Take Break Over Draft Campaigns
Headline: Kenya: MPs to Take Break Over Draft Campaigns
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Parliament is to stop work 45 days earlier than usual - to allow MPs to
concentrate on the referendum campaigns, the Nation has learnt.
But it has also been agreed that the MPs will have to return to work
early next year to make up for the lost time.

Original_Search_Title: Be Wary of Moi Family, Yes Crusaders Tell Kenyans
Headline: Kenya: Be Wary of Moi Family, Yes Crusaders Tell Kenyans
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Yes team yesterday told Kenyans to be wary of families of former
President Moi.
Addressing a Banana rally at Mwala constituency in Ukambani, the team
also cautioned them against the families of former Vice-President Musalia
Mudavadi, Kanu chairman Uhuru Kenyatta and the Cabinet minister Raila
Odinga.

Original_Search_Title: Police Used Too Much Force At Wajir Yes Rally, Claim Muslims
Headline: Kenya: Police Used Too Much Force At Wajir Yes Rally, Claim Muslims
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Muslim leaders yesterday accused police of using excessive force in
handling women at a Banana rally in Wajir on Monday.
They said in a statement the police had been "too hard on harmless
women."

Original_Search_Title: No Sacred Cows in Law Enforcement, Says Ali
Headline: Kenya: No Sacred Cows in Law Enforcement, Says Ali
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Police commissioner Hussein Ali yesterday reassured Kenyans that there
would no sacred cows in ensuring that the law was followed.
The poll must be done in an atmosphere of law and order, he said; "It
does not matter what political camp one belongs to. Those caught breaking
the law will have to face the consequences, including the media."

Original_Search_Title: ECK Boss Takes War to Banana, Orange
Headline: Kenya: ECK Boss Takes War to Banana, Orange
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Electoral commission chairman Samuel Kivuitu yesterday gave the leaders
of the Orange and Banana teams a dressing down for disobeying his order to
stop review campaigns.
Kivuitu told the leaders, who included Kanu Chairman Uhuru Kenyatta,
Roads minister Raila Odinga and Mr William Ruto of the Orange camp, and
ministers Kipruto arap Kirwa and Njeru Ndwiga from the Banana team, he would
soon make them feel "small".

Original_Search_Title: Police Bar Group From Disrupting Banana Meeting
Headline: Kenya: Police Bar Group From Disrupting Banana Meeting
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Police yesterday thwarted attempts by youths allied to the Orange camp
to disrupt a Yes rally in Mwala constituency.
The youths, who were dressed in Yes T-shirts, had welcomed the Banana
team to Mwala divisional headquarters, the venue of the meeting.

Original_Search_Title: Sailing to Muea By Road
Headline: Cameroon: Sailing to Muea By Road
Body_Text:
The stretch of road from Mile 17 to Muea in the outskirts of Buea is known to have been under very bad repairs in recent years. Being a trunk "A" road and the main one leading out of Fako and Douala to other Divisions of the Southwest Province, this road could be said to be one of the busiest in the entire country.
The very fact of the popular Muea Market attracting visitors from many parts of the country, and beyond can only mean that this road is plied by thousands of automobiles of different weights and sizes hence, the need for some particular, if not extra attention to be paid to it.

Original_Search_Title: Real Trade Justice
Headline: Africa: Real Trade Justice
Body_Text:
In the short term, ending export subsidies may mean more expensive food
imports
In a little more than two months' time, the future course of global
trade and development will be shaped at a meeting of trade ministers in Hong
Kong.

Original_Search_Title: Changes to Curb Graft And Tyranny, Say MPs
Headline: Kenya: Changes to Curb Graft And Tyranny, Say MPs
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Banana team yesterday took its campaign to Dagoretti, Nairobi, where they condemned the ruling Lancaster constitution as a document for the rich and dictators.
Dictatorship and corruption would be wiped out under the proposed constitution and presidents would be unable to detain, oppress and give out public land and other resources at will, they said.

Original_Search_Title: Keep Off Moi, Ntimama Tells Yes Ministers
Headline: Kenya: Keep Off Moi, Ntimama Tells Yes Ministers
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Public Service minister William ole Ntimama yesterday told the Yes campaign leaders not to drag retired President Moi's name into the referendum politics.
Mr Moi, he said, was entitled to comment on topical issues because "he is not a slave to keep quiet".

Original_Search_Title: Leaders Blamed for Violence
Headline: Kenya: Leaders Blamed for Violence
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Review Commission yesterday accused politicians of promoting hatred
and disunity in the run-up to the referendum on the proposed
Constitution.
Chairperson Abida Ali-Aroni said the Banana and Orange groups had
ignored issues in the proposals and were preaching party political
interests.

Original_Search_Title: Divided Over Presidential Powers
Headline: Kenya: Divided Over Presidential Powers
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Cabinet ministers Raila Odinga and Kiraitu Murungi are at each
other's jugular once again over the executive powers in the proposed new
Constitution. In an interview with Nation's STEPHEN MBURU, Mr Murungi says
fears that the draft seeks to take the country back to a "strong-man
syndrome" are unfounded. Excerpts:
Why has the issue of the Executive generated so much political
heat?

Original_Search_Title: ECK Accuses Review Commissioners of Taking Sides
Headline: Kenya: ECK Accuses Review Commissioners of Taking Sides
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Electoral Commission of Kenya chairman Samuel Kivuitu yesterday accused
CKRC commissioners of taking sides in the referendum debate.
He said it was wrong for the Constitution of Kenya Review Commission
officials to take sides.

Original_Search_Title: Now Politicians Call for Snap Election
Headline: Kenya: Now Politicians Call for Snap Election
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Calls for a snap election to come on the heels of the November 21
referendum gathered momentum yesterday with key politicians in the Orange
and Banana camps saying it was the only way out of the current
standoff.
Kabete MP Paul Muite said politicians on both sides of the political
divide felt there should be a snap General Election after the November 21
referendum.

Original_Search_Title: Raila And MP in War of Words
Headline: Kenya: Raila And MP in War of Words
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Roads minister Raila Odinga was yesterday again caught up in a war of
words in Parliament after the referendum conflict popped up in the
House.
It was another poorly attended session as members concentrated on the
war between the Orange and Banana teams.

Original_Search_Title: MPs Criticise Kiraitu Over Moi Remark
Headline: Kenya: MPs Criticise Kiraitu Over Moi Remark
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A Cabinet minister and a party leader yesterday warned Justice minister
Kiraitu Murungi against messing around with retired President Moi.
The Minister of State in the Office of the President, Mr William ole
Ntimama, and Kanu chairman Uhuru Kenyatta warned of far reaching
consequences should the Government terminate the former President's Sh2m
monthly allowances.

Original_Search_Title: V-P Arm Twisted, Claim 'No' Leaders
Headline: Kenya: V-P Arm Twisted, Claim 'No' Leaders
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Orange luminaries now allege Vice-President Moody Awori is being arm
twisted into supporting the proposed constitution.
Environment minister Kalonzo Musyoka, speaking in Bungoma and Busia
towns, said the V-P was a staunch Liberal Democratic Party member who shared
a common vision with them, but that his hands were tied.

Original_Search_Title: German Envoy Told Off Over Referendum
Headline: Kenya: German Envoy Told Off Over Referendum
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Justice minister Kiraitu Murungi has told off German Ambassador Bernd
Braun for questioning the use of taxpayers' money to fund the Yes
campaign.
The minister urged Braun to understand the country's political and
economic systems before making remarks.

Original_Search_Title: Orange Crusaders Behind Anglo Leasing, Says Murungaru
Headline: Kenya: Orange Crusaders Behind Anglo Leasing, Says Murungaru
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Transport minister Chris Murungaru yesterday distanced himself from the Anglo Leasing scandal, saying it had been committed by those now campaigning against the proposed Constitution.
He told Planning colleague Peter Anyang'-Nyong'o to look no further than the Orange team if he want to know the people responsible for the loss of Sh7 billion paid out to Anglo Leasing.

Original_Search_Title: Shots Fired As Chaos Hits Banana Meeting
Headline: Kenya: Shots Fired As Chaos Hits Banana Meeting
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Banana campaign team yesterday re-launched its campaign for the proposed new Constitution in North Eastern Province amidst stone-throwing and heckling by some rowdy youths in Wajir's Baraza Park.
Police had to fire live bullets in the air and lobbed tear gas canisters at charging mobs which sporadically pelted the VIP dais with stones while making attempts to invade the Yes rally.

Original_Search_Title: Efforts to Instill Discipline in Drivers
Headline: Ghana: Efforts to Instill Discipline in Drivers
Body_Text:
Madam Theresa Amerley Tagoe, the Member of Parliament for Ablekuma
South, has appealed to transport unions to help stem what she called the
uncouth attitudes of drivers.
Speaking at an educational forum on road safety for drivers at the
Indadfa Park in Accra at the weekend, the MP said the unions have a
responsibility to educate their members on how to communicate with their
passengers.

Original_Search_Title: Shots Fired At 'Yes' Campaign Rally
Headline: Kenya: Shots Fired At Banana Rally As Orange Shakes North Rift
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Gunshots rent the air and tear-gas rose to the sky in Wajir town
yesterday as police suppressed an attempt by Orange youths to disrupt a
rally attended by five Cabinet ministers from the Banana camp.
Stones and oranges landed on the dais at Baraza Park where the ministers
were seated together with an equal number of assistant ministers and several
MPs in the Yes campaign team.

Original_Search_Title: Banana Team Launched
Headline: Uganda: Banana Team Launched
Body_Text:
Kampala — PRESIDENT Yoweri Museveni has launched the management committee of
the Presidential Initiative for Banana Industrial Development (PIBID),
in a ceremony at State House, Nakasero.
The PIBID will be charged with research, development and adding value to local bananas.

Original_Search_Title: Ministers Downplay Kilimo Defection
Headline: Kenya: Ministers Downplay Kilimo Defection
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The defection of Immigration minister Linah Jebii Kilimo to the Orange team was yesterday dismissed as inconsequential.
Eight Cabinet ministers said the move would not stop the Banana team from winning at the referendum and would not affect the group's campaign spirit.

Original_Search_Title: Civil Service Should Be Neutral
Headline: Kenya: Civil Service Should Be Neutral
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The role to be played by civil servants in the coming referendum has arisen pursuant to the assertion by the Minister for Justice and Constitutional Affairs that they shall play an active role due to the fact that the referendum is a government project.
Civil servants, like other Kenyans, have a right to take part in any electoral process whether it is the general elections or by- elections for both presidential and parliamentary elections or even a referendum, as the case may be, as the one slated for November 21, 2005 under section 26, 27 28 and 28A of the Constitution of Kenya Review Act cap 3A. In any event as registered voters they have a right to exercise their constitutional right as mandated by section 28 (2) of cap 3A.

Original_Search_Title: Killing Goose That Lays Golden Egg
Headline: Kenya: Killing Goose That Lays Golden Egg
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The wildlife community has been up in arms since the announcement by
Tourism and Wildlife minister Morris Dzoro last month that the Amboseli
National Park was to be downgraded to a game reserve and returned to its
original Kajiado owners. What is the park to be saved from, you might ask?
Both the timing and the speed of the announcement are worrying. As the
open letter to the President by about 30 organisations pointed out last
week, the move was illegal. It contravenes section 7 of the Wildlife Act,
which states that the minister must consult with a competent authority
before changing the status of any national park.

Original_Search_Title: Churches Urge End to Campaign Violence
Headline: Kenya: Churches Urge End to Campaign Violence
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Protestant churches yesterday came out strongly to protest against the
escalating cases of violence in the ongoing constitution campaigns.
The National Council of Churches of Kenya warned that the deep divisions
emerging from the Orange and Banana referendum debate is leading the country
to a dangerous path of self-destruction.

Original_Search_Title: Police Watched As Chaos Ruled, Claims Kombo
Headline: Kenya: Police Watched As Chaos Ruled, Claims Kombo
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Police were yesterday accused of watching indifferently as helpless
women and children were beaten up during an Orange campaign meeting in
Bungoma.
A man kicks another at Chwele trading centre in Bungoma District when a
No campaign rally turned violent at the weekend. Photo by Jared Nyataya

Original_Search_Title: Nyachae Leads Yes Crusade in Kuria
Headline: Kenya: Nyachae Leads Yes Crusade in Kuria
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Yes team took their campaigns to Kuria yesterday where four Cabinet
ministers led a group of MPs in declaring that the Banana team would soldier
on to win the public vote.
Four cabinet ministers, three assistant ministers and seven MPs vowed to
fight on to convince Kenyans to vote for the proposed new Constitution
despite what they termed "consistent lies propagated by ministers in the
Orange team".

Original_Search_Title: Scorpions' Bosses to Answer Critics
Headline: South Africa: Scorpions' Bosses to Answer Critics
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — ALL eyes will be on the Scorpions today as the elite investigating unit
presents its case to the Khampepe commission in Pretoria on why it should
remain under the control of the National Prosecuting Authority.
Scorpions head Leonard McCarthy and national director of public
prosecutions Vusi Pikoli were at the commission last week when the police
and the National Intelligence Agency (NIA) argued for the unit to be
relocated and its mandate changed.

Original_Search_Title: Have These Ministers Seen Anglo Leasing Ghosts?
Headline: Kenya: Have These Ministers Seen Anglo Leasing Ghosts?
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A fresh row broke out in the Cabinet yesterday when Planning minister Anyang' Nyong'o claimed five ministers were shielding the culprits behind the Sh7 billion Anglo Leasing tender scandals from being prosecuted.
Nyong'o pointed accusing fingers at Finance minister David Mwiraria, Chris Murungaru (Transport), Kiraitu Murungi (Justice) and Amos Kimunya (Lands).

Original_Search_Title: Defection Claims Are Baseless, Says Michuki
Headline: Kenya: Defection Claims Are Baseless, Says Michuki
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Internal Security minister John Michuki yesterday declared that the Banana team would win despite media reports that some high-profile leaders were poised to defect.
His remarks were supported by ministers in the Banana who dismissed Immigration minister Jebii Kilimo's defection to the Orange side as inconsequential.

Original_Search_Title: Register Or Be Locked Out, ECK Tells Groups
Headline: Kenya: Register Or Be Locked Out, ECK Tells Groups
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Electoral Commission of Kenya (ECK) will ban Orange and Banana teams from campaigning in groups if they fail to register their referendum campaign committees within five days.
The ECK Vice-chairman, Mr Gabriel Mukele, said yesterday leaders wishing to campaign for " Yes" or" No" must register with the commission by October 15.

Original_Search_Title: Where 'Facilitation' Pays for Varsity Students
Headline: Kenya: Where 'Facilitation' Pays for Varsity Students
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Kisii is the place to be if you want to benefit from the referendum money, especially if you are young and you look like a university student.
